Original_Search_Title: Kwara Launches Back-to-Farm Project
Headline: Nigeria: Kwara Launches Back-to-Farm Project
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Kwara State Government has introduced "a back-to-farm" programme
aimed at creating wealth and development.
Disclosing this in a paper titled, "Challenges of Wealth Creation and
Human Development," at a forum organised by Labour Writers Association of
Nigeria in Lagos, the Kwara State Governor, Dr Bukola Saraki, stated that
the decision to embark on the back-to-farm programme was to shift from the
present empowerment to making people key players in the over all task of
developing the state.
Governor Saraki stated that through the back-to-form programme, the
state has been able to provide support for about 1,000 farmers in the
production of maize, rice and cassava.
The Kwara state governor said that "apart from providing free tractor
services, the government also provided seedlings, fertilizer and chemicals
to farmers at subsidized rates, saying that even though the programme is
still at the pilot stage, "we hope to improve on the scheme next year,
bringing more people in, especially youths."
On the prospects of the programme Dr Saraki explained that from next
year, there will be a ban on rice importation, and "when this happens, we
intend to make Kwara state, the center for rice production for the entire
country.
"Already there is a proposed private sector investment of about 150
million dollars and we intend to achieve a 50,000 hectares rice farm with 25
fully automated rice processing mills with a capacity to produce about
390,000 tonnes of rice per annum" he said.

Original_Search_Title: Akume Presents N29b Budget to Assembly
Headline: Nigeria: Akume Presents N29b Budget to Assembly
Body_Text:
MAKURDI — BENUE State governor, Mr George Akume, Tuesday presented a budget of
N29.355 billion to the House of Assembly. Akume who christened the 2004
budget as Budget of Continuity, explained to the House that recurrent and
capital expenditure estimates amounted to N17.778 billion and N11.577
billion respectively.
He told the House that the sources of funding for the budget would
include internally generated revenue, value added tax. state's share of
federation account and capital receipts of N1.778 billion, N2 billion, N4
billion and N11.577 billion respectively.
According to him, the state government would further spend N7.07 billion
on salaries of workers and spend N3.428 billion on over head costs to
ministries and parastatals. He expressed concern over the state's huge wage
bills and hinted that varieties of taxes may be introduced to enable
government cope with the situation.
He said only 10 percent of the actual revenue expected next year would
be spent on capital projects, lamenting that last year, the situation was
the same.
He announced that agricultural projects like the fertilizer blending
plant, organic fertilizer plant, procurement of machineries and spare parts
and cassava processing plants would cost the government N2.8 billion while
the establishment of Igumale Cement Factory, Mbatiav Cement Factory and the
three Fruit Juice Factories at Katsina-Ala, Gboko and Otukpo would take
about N2.5 billion.
Akume disclosed that about N2 billion would be spent on commerce,
culture and tourism projects, adding that rural roads would also be
opened-up next year at the cost of N2 billion.Governor Akume said projects
in the water and environment, education and health sectors would attract a
total expenditure of N3.8 billion.

Original_Search_Title: Osun Govt Poised to Change People's Orientation, Wilson
Headline: Nigeria: Osun Govt Poised to Change People's Orientation, Wilson
Body_Text:
The elegant Osun State Commissioner for Finance and Economic
Development, Chief (Mrs) Remi Wilson, is not a green horn in Nigerian
politics, nay Osun state.
She is not a woman who lays back, pleading gender for
non-performance. Since her assumption of office in Governor Olagunsoye
Oyinlola's administration a few months ago, she has brought so many
structural changes into the ministry that are already yielding fruitful
results. She spoke to our Osun state correspondent, Gbenga Olarinoye on a
number of issues. Excerpts.
What has been your experience so far as Osun state commissioner of
finance?
Our achievements cut across all the ministries and parastatals. For
instance in the Ministry of Agriculture we are able to distribute tractors
to all the local government areas and ten others at zonal levels. In the
area of transportation, we have been able to give buses to the NURTW, we
have also assisted our staff to broaden their knowledge in the area of
training by attending courses, seminars and conferences. In the area of
education, the present government has been able to improve on the
standard. You will also recollect that we have reabsorbed those teachers
that were sacked by the last administration in the state. In the area of
generating funds for the government, we have done excellently well. We also
linked up with other agencies for the finance of some of our programmes and
projects. These bodies that are hitherto neglected have been able to assist
us in the area of education, roads construction, health and so many
others.
We have thrown our doors open and that has greatly assisted us to move
forward. In fact all the abandoned projects that have direct bearing with
the people of the state have been taken up. In the area of health, we have
taken a giant stride towards manufacturing our own drugs for our free health
programme, while our hospitals and health centres have been equipped with
personnel and equipments.
We have been paying our workers their salaries as at when due while all
the pensioners have been receiving their pensions promptly. As part of
workers welfare, the state government has bought shuttle buses to transport
them to and from the state secretariat.
How much of the state resources now goes for the payment of workers
salaries?
With the reabsorption of some of the sacked workers by the previous
administration, what now goes into the payment of workers' salaries now is
about 80% of the total revenue.
What have been the major obstacles to delivering on your party's
promises?
The first major problem we encountered was the difficulty of our people
to adapt to the new change when we came on board. Some people are still
skeptical about us while some have implicit confidence in us. It was a
little bit difficult for us to let the people know that there has been a new
government, new hand and a change. But I think it is natural to human beings
that they don't easily adapt to change. But we actually need a change in
Osun state to make progress. That has been the only obstacle and I think we
have since gotten over that.
How have you been coping in a male dominated arena (politics)?
You know one funny thing, I don't believe that sex has any impact in
doing whatever you want to do. For instance if you are in a classroom, you
sit with a male counterpart, you do the same exams and you obtain the same
certificate. Nobody discriminates in marking your papers whether you are a
male child or female child. There is no big deal in women participating in
politics. All you need to do is to believe in God and have focus in
life.
Since when have you been in politics?
I have been in politics since 1978 and I don't think I can be rubbished
by anybody. I will not even give a room for it. I wont beg for money nor
will I ask for any special favour from men, so how can you rubbish me?
Focus what is the main of 2004 budget of the state?
By now, it is a known fact that Osun state is no longer an opposition
state, we now belong to the national body. The main focus of the PDP led
federal government is on agriculture and that is exactly what we in the
state are going to focus our attention on next year.
President Obasanjo has been asking us since 1975 to go back to farming
so that we can have sufficient food to feed the populace and supply raw
materials for our Agro-Allied Industries. President Obasanjo is not just
preaching agriculture he is preaching what he has been doing and
practicing.
We in Osun state we are going into mass production of cassava and we
have started replacing our old cocoa trees with new ones.
Revenue generation in the state
We have been able to modernized the way we collect our internally
generated revenue in Osun state. We no longer have to stat chasing people
all about the way it is done in the past. Virtually everyone in the state is
aware that you have to pay certain amount of money for certain business. Our
revenue officials have been given trainings and what now comes to the
coffers of the government since we came on board has been greatly
increased. But that does not mean that we did not have tax evaders. Even
some of them that have travelled overseas and fully aware of the tax
policies there would come back home only to evade one form of tax or the
other. Take for instance the car wash, the operators uses treated water for
their jobs but when you ask them to pay they start to make one complaint or
the other.
In your view, what is the ideal revenue allocation formulate ?
The federal government has its own reason for having more share than the
state and local governments. But as state governments, we are supposed to
generate revenue internally. Personally I don't believe state or local
government should over-rely on the federal government for revenue. My
observation is that at the state and local government levels we have been
too lazy to generate revenue. That is the reality. All we want is to depend
on the federal government. If it is the federal government that generates
its revenue, it is duty bound to share it the way it wants.
Do you agree with the deregulation policy of the federal government?
I have been saying it both in the public and privately, that since the
federal government has told us the problem it is facing, our oil sector
should be deregulated. I want to be very objective about the policy. At the
10th Nigeria summit the federal government told Nigerians that it wants to
deregulate and we all supported it both the public and private sectors. It
is now funny for some people to now start making noise. People who are
complaining about the deregulation of the downstream of the oil sectors are
only trying to make politics out of it.
You can see efficiency in the sector that has already been
deregulated. Take for instance the telecommunication which brings about
mobile phones or the media which brings about private newspapers, radio and
television stations. If the energy sector is deregulated today you will
witness better performance and greater efficiency.
How do you see President Obasanjo? Do you think he has shown by his
deeds in the past four years that he is the man to deliver Nigeria?
He is a man of vision, a man of courage, a man who match his words with
action.
Some of the works he did as a military head of state are still what we
are still pointing at till today. I respect him and if we all cooperate with
him, this country would be a better place for everybody.
He may not be appreciated today but I believe after he might have left
the office, people will refer to him as an hero. I know definitely that he
would one day be appreciated.
What is your view of the new economic management team at the federal
level?
I sincerely appreciate the minister and the minister of state. They are
very intelligent and a pride to women folks. They are two people anybody
would want to work with. Each time they make any submission on economic
policy, it is always a fantastic one that can hardly be faulted by
anybody. They are good administrators and very committed to the economic
growth and development of Nigeria. I have gained a lot from them.

Original_Search_Title: Saraki to Harvest Farm Produce Tomorrow
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki to Harvest Farm Produce Tomorrow
Body_Text:
Kwara — Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State will return to the farm tomorrow
to harvest the crops he planted in July, this year.
Saraki is also expected to harvest maize at Alapa in Asa Local
Government Council, one of the 53 farm sites cultivated in the state's
Back-to-Farm Scheme.
The 20 hectares Alapa farm site, according to the State Commissioner for
Agriculture and Natural Resources, Alhaji Mohammed Sani Boriya, has mixed
cropping of maize and cassava with 53 other farms locations scattered across
the state.
Boriya said that other varieties of crops grown in the 54 farm
locations, included 527 hectares of maize and cassava, 97 hectares of rice
and 30 hectares of Soyabeans.
On the progress so far recorded on the project, the commissioner
explained that the programme has been a huge success as about 704 hectares
of land have so far been engaged in farming out of the initial target of
1,000 hectares of land targeted for the farm locations.
He attributed the inability to meet up with the initial target to late
commencement of the project, late arrival of fertilizer and farm implements
which, he said, affected the farm yield.
Boriya, however, assured of better farm yield in the next planting
season.
It would be recalled that the back-to-farm project was officially
launched in the state in July as a way of encouraging agriculture and to
also boost local food production.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Wins International Acclaim
Headline: Uganda: Cassava Wins International Acclaim
Body_Text:
Pretoria — Uganda's victory against the cassava mosaic has been showcased as one of
Africa's success stories in Agriculture.
The success against the cassava mosaic, the virus that incapacitates the
plant's ability to feed by attacking and destroying its leaves, was
recognised during an international conference in Pretoria, South Africa
early this month.
The conference "Successes in African Agriculture: Building for the
Future," was organised under the auspicies of Nepad (the New Partnership for
African Development).
Uganda was among the 10 success stories identified in Africa for
averting a food crisis by recovering cassava production to above
pre-epidemic levels within 5 years of introducing resistant varieties.
The mosaic virus appeared in Uganda in 1988 destroying about 80 percent
of cassava production in the country. In order to combat the virus, the
National Agricultural Research Organisation (Naro) imported mosaic resistant
cassava varieties from mainly Nigeria.
The new varieties were distributed to farmers after multiplication. Naro
also put together a coalition of private and public groups to form the
National Network of Cassava Workers (NANEC) and by 1993, NANEC had started
multiplication and distribution of mosaic resistant varieties to
farmers.
In effect cassava breeding, pest and disease fighting efforts have
improved the lives of millions of poor consumers and farm family members
across Uganda, the conference noted.
"Success is possible. Improved varieties of cassava and innovative pest
control strategies, for example, benefit approximately 100 million
Africans," said Ms
Oumy Ndiaye, head of Communication Channels and Services Department at
Technical Centre for Agricultural and Rural Cooperation.
The conference aimed at exchange and dissemination of lessons from the
successes and identification of key processes and technologies that
countries can replicate and expand to achieve sustainable agricultural
growth.
Already African Heads of State and Government have added additional
impetus to agricultural development on the continent by committing
themselves to allocating more resources to agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: Onshore/Offshore: FG'll Take Final Decison Soon, Ekaete
Headline: Nigeria: Onshore/Offshore: FG'll Take Final Decison Soon, Ekaete
Body_Text:
UYO — SECRETARY to the Government of the Federation, Obong Ufot Ekaete has
told the people of the Niger Delta that the Federal Government will soon
take a final decision on the issue of onshore/offshore oil dichotomy but
said the decision reached will be in line with government's new vision on
the economy.
Obong Ekaete who spoke at Onna Local Government Area of Akwa Ibom State
at a reception held in his honour by the 31 Local Government Areas of the
state however urged people of the Niger Delta and other Nigerians to be
prepared to take advantage of the various laudable policies being put in
place by the Federal Government aimed at improving the lot of all
Nigerians.
The Secretary to Government of the Federation explained that the 2004
Federal budget was aimed at alleviating poverty and making life more
meaningful for rural dwellers in Nigerian communities.
"The 2004 budget is designed to support the reform programmes of
government and provide an enabling environment for the private sector to
create jobs and enhance the quality of life of the people.
"The priority sectors of the reforms to be carried out would centre on
agriculture, solid minerals development, manufacturing, oil and gas, tourism
and small and medium scale enterprises," Ekaete pointed out.
Other sectors in which the government's reforms would concentrate,
according to Ekaete, are on the provision of infrastructure such as roads,
water supply and electricity.
"The presidential initiatives on agriculture will concentrate on
cultivation of cassava, rice, maize, and other grains, vegetable oil,
cotton, oil palm and other tree crops, livestock and aquaculture," he
said.
Governor Victor Attah, who was represented by his deputy, Obong Chris
Ekpenyong called on the Secretary to the Government of the Federation to use
his good offices to ensure that the onshore/offshore dichotomy was settled
once and for all by the Federal Government to enable oil producing states
carry on with their development plans for their areas.
Said Attah "with the calibre of the person of Obong Ekaete and the
confidence President Obasanjo has in him, a new chapter and era of better
relations that would change the circumstances of the people of Akwa Ibom for
the better would be actualized in the year ahead. We call on you the SGF to
use your vantage position to fight for the abrogation of the
onshore/offshore dichotomy."

Original_Search_Title: Akume Advises Christian Pilgrims
Headline: Nigeria: Akume Advises Christian Pilgrims
Body_Text:
MAKURDI — BENUE State Governor, Mr George Akume, has called on Christians
performing this year pilgrimage to Israel to pray for the unity, peace and
progress of Nigeria.
Akume whose address to the pilgrims was read by the Secretary to the
State Government, Dr John Orokpo, explained that Nigeria needed prayers for
her to succeed in the various economic , social and political programmes
being pursued for the improvement of the lives of the people.
He stressed the need for the pilgrims to pay special attention to the
spiritual benefits of their journey and advised them not to do anything that
would defile the places that Christ made holy while on earth.
Governor Akume also enjoined the pilgrims to be good ambassadors of the
state and work together while in the Holy Land.
He told the pilgrims to cooperate with the Chairman of the State
Christian Pilgrims Welfare Board, Rev Fr Chris Utov, and congratulated them
for scaling through the screening.
Speaking to Vanguard during a workshop organised for the pilgrims, Rev
Fr Chris Utov, expressed appreciation to Governor Akume for sponsoring
hundreds of pilgrims to Jerusalem every year.
Utov said about 186 pilgrims had been prepared to travel to the Holy
Land on Sunday and added that the entire pilgrims from Benue State would
travel to Israel in about three or four batches.In another development
Governor George Akume is to present the 2004 budget to the State House of
Assembly tomorrow, according to the Commissioner for Information, Mr John
Uzer.
Uzer told newsmen in Makurdi that the various ministries and departments
had been meeting to fine-tune the budget and noted that the House of
Assembly has been duly notified of the presentation on Tuesday.
He hinted that the budget would focus mainly on rural infrastructural
development and completion of on-going projects. According to him, the
budget would also contain various programmes planned for the reduction of
unemployment in the state especially amongst the youths.
It was further gathered that the new projects likely to be reflected in
next year's budget are the Mbatiav and Igumale Cement Companies, the three
Cassava Processing Industries and the three Fruit Juice Factories.

Original_Search_Title: The Environmental Challenge of Developing the Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: The Environmental Challenge of Developing the Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Niger Delta —  Chief DSP Alamieyeseigha, executive governor of Bayelsa State, discusses
how the problems of Niger Delta could be tackled
I feel truly honoured and delighted to be given the opportunity to speak
on the subject matter before you at this historic occasion. Indeed, it is
with profound humility that I accept the privilege of delivering the
inaugural NewsAfrica International Lectures Series here in London.
As a news medium, NewsAfrica magazine has made remarkable impact in the
highly competitive international news magazine industry. The magazine has
not only opened a new vista of qualitative news reporting but has focused a
great deal on the several socio-economic and political problems and
prospects of Africa.
Through incisive and engaging features, the magazine has highlighted the
great potential of Africa and the urgent development needs of this vital
geo-political region of the world. Little wonder, therefore, that NewsAfrica
has sought, through this lecture, to focus on one of most richly endowed and
yet one of the most under- developed deltas in the world.
I believe there are more competent people, experts on environmental
issues, who can speak on this topic more comprehensively. Many distinguished
scholars, advocates and people of conscience from the Niger Delta and around
the world have made commendable efforts at analyzing the situation in the
Niger Delta and proffering solutions to the myriad problems facing the
people and the region.
However, I take solace in the fact that I hail from the Niger Delta area
of Nigeria. I lived there, and I serve the people of the Niger Delta in a
position of authority as Governor of Bayelsa State. I consider myself well
placed, therefore, to speak on the provocative circumstances under which our
people live.
For some time now, the subject of this paper has been on the front
burner of public discourse. This is not by any means surprising.  First, oil
and gas revenue from the Niger Delta region accounts for about 90 per cent
of Nigeria's income. Secondly, the region is of strategic importance to the
global economy and in particular to the Nigerian State. It is the sixth
largest oil exporter among the Organization of Petroleum Export Countries
(OPEC).
The Niger Delta Region The Niger Delta is located in the Southern part
of Nigeria, a geopolitical framework mainly populated by the Ijaw ethnic
nationality.
Spreading over a total landmass of about 70,000 square kilometers, the
region is inhabited by an estimated population of 20 million Nigerians in
2000 communities.
The area is also home to the Ogonis, the Ikwerres, Ekpeyes, Ogbas,
Egbemas, Engennes, and the Abuas of Ahoada division as well as the Obolos
and the Opobo people. In addition to the Ijaws of Western Delta are the
Urhobos, Isokos, the Itsekiris and part of Kwale. In its present
composition, the Niger Delta covers the six states of the South-South namely
Akwa-Ibom, Bayelsa, Cross-River, Delta, Edo and Rivers. This is so even
though the definition given the Niger Delta by the Sir Henry Willink
Commission Report of 1957 is much narrower.
However, the legislation on the Niger Delta Development Commission,
NDDC, in 2000 has further extended the frontiers of the Niger Delta to
include Abia, Imo and Ondo States, thus making the political map of the
Niger Delta to comprise nine states.
The Niger Delta communities have settled in the area for several
millennia, the oldest group having been in the areas for some 7 to 10,000
years. The primary occupations of the people include fishing, farming,
forest product gathering, craft, etc usually at subsistence level.
The immediate source of livelihood for the people of the region has been
supplied by the rich flora and fauna of the area for many generations.
For so long, the people there lived in harmony, and there was evident
balance in the ecosystem.
The Niger Delta is characterized by wetlands and water bodies with
creeks and rivers criss-crossing the entire region. The higher-lying plains
experience 5 , 7 months of flooding in the year, resulting from the
overflowing waters of the lower Niger River in which whole communities and
farmlands are invariably submerged. Flooding and river-bank or coastal
erosion are the bane of the people. The Niger Delta is, no doubt, a
difficult if not an outrightly inclement terrain.
However, the region is endowed with enormous natural resources. It has
the world's third largest mangrove forest with the most extensive freshwater
swamp forest and tropical rainforest characterized by great biological
diversity. Alongside the immense potential for agricultural revolution, the
Niger Delta region also has vast reserves of non-renewable natural
resources, particularly hydrocarbon deposits in oil and gas.
Other non- renewable natural resources include clay pits for burnt brick
making in the construction industry, and silica sand for the glass
manufacturing industry which have however, remained largely untapped.
Part of a World Bank report following a visit to the Niger Delta in 1952
and 1953 declared that the region has great prospects to feed the entire
population of the West African sub-region and have sufficient commodities
for export. Some of the produce highlighted by the report includes rice,
palm oil and cassava, which are in abundance throughout the Niger Delta ,
especially Bayelsa State, the epicenter of the region.
Yet, the Niger Delta remains pervasively poor and underdeveloped,
lacking virtually all forms of social amenities and infrastructure,
including electricity, potable water, medical facilities, roads, shelter,
etc. The area suffers a regrettable legacy of hunger, high and rising rates
of unemployment, communal conflict, youth restiveness and all forms of
social insecurity.
It is evident that nature has done its part by freely depositing
valuable treasures as life support systems in the Niger Delta. What remains
missing is that the Nigerian nation-state is yet to play her role to
overcome, tame and nurture the harsh environment to ensure the overall
well-being of the people of the Niger Delta region in particular, and the
nation at large.
Like the withering plants in autumn, the swamps and mangrove forests in
the Niger Delta have lost their essence. They are dying before their
time. The people of the area have continued to complain bitterly about mass
poverty, hunger and disease, environmental degradation and loss of their
traditional means of livelihood. No one seems to be listening enough.
As Dr Steve Azaiki puts in his book, Inequities In Nigerian Politics:
"the fact that these issues are still staring us in the face indicate
general neglect of the Niger Delta, which now challenges our sense of
justice and precipitates the quest for fairness_e." Given the region's
sensitive and fragile ecosystem and, in spite of the vast resource
endowment, its immense potential for socio-economic growth and its
contribution to the overall development of Nigeria, it remains increasingly
under threat from rapidly deteriorating economic and environmental
conditions as well as social tension.
Clearly, the challenge of developing the Niger Delta is complex but not
insurmountable. The choice of the theme of this lecture is, therefore, apt
and auspicious at a time when all hands must be on deck to identify the root
causes of the problems, and proffer solutions to the worsening physical and
economic crisis in a region of critical global significance. This
presentation seeks to identify the causes of the problem and more. I shall
look at the constraints to developing the environment and proffer
development alternatives in the Niger Delta region.
The complex environmental challenges of the Niger Delta can be clipped
under two broad classifications: i. The difficult terrain, and ii. The
menace posed by multi-national prospecting corporations.
I shall attempt to provide an insight into these challenges within the
context and scope of this paper. I shall thereafter point the way
forward. But, first, let us be acquainted with the historical
background.
Historical Perspective It is tragic that the Niger Delta area has come
to be recognized in recent times as an enclave of social conflict. The
political, economic and social dynamics of the region can only be understood
in the context of the on-going under-development, which has oil exploitation
and exploration as its principal signpost.
The relentless exploitation of the natural resources of the area,
without due compensation for the environmental hazards it has occasioned,
has given rise to youth restiveness, which is primarily aimed at seeking
redress for over forty-five years of neglect and deprivation.
At any rate, the seeds of dissent so visibly displayed in the Niger
Delta today have their root in the distant past, especially from colonial
rule. In 1900, Britain created the Northern and Southern Protectorates which
were merged in 1914 to form the Colony of Nigeria.
The amalgamation of North and South in a "marriage of convenience"
between ethnologically contrasting partners, and a political contraption
that resulted in a federal structure of three regional governments, each
with a distinct geo-ethnic majority group, gave birth to distrust in the
Nigerian political scene. There was wide spread agitation in minority areas
of the North, East and West over their perceived fears of autocratic
domination, neglect, insecurity and discrimination by majority groups, and
the minority groups accordingly demanded for separate states of their
own.
In the twilight years marking the end of British colonialism and the
dawn of independence in Nigeria, the ethnic minority agitator for better
recognition and treatment necessitated the setting up of the Willink
Commission of Inquiry in 1957 to "enquire into the fears of minorities and
the means of allaying them." The Willink Commission Report of 1958, based
upon ample evidence and the personal experiences of members in the course of
the enquiry, observed that: _ethe needs of those who live in the creeks and
swamps of the Niger Delta are very different from those of the interior_eit
is not easy for a government or legislature operating from inland to concern
itself or even fully understand the problems of a territory where
communications are so difficult, building so expensive and education so
scanty in a country which is unlikely ever to be developed.
The Commission concluded: _e. we had no doubt that a feeling of neglect
and a lack of understanding was widespread in both Regions (Western and
Eastern Deltas). We consider that a case has been made out for special
treatment of this area. This is a matter that requires special effort
because it is poor, backward and neglected.
This was the prologue to the establishment of the Niger Delta
Development Board (NDDB) in 1961 "to consider the problems of the area of
the Niger Delta". It has remained the prologue to similar renamed agencies
since then, from the Niger Delta River Basin Development Authority (NDBDA)
in 1976; the Oil Mineral Producing Areas Development Commission (OMPADEC) in
1992; and the present Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) in
2000.
Two negative deviations are worthy of note in the implementation of the
Willink Commission report of 1958. The first false step by the Federal
Government was to deviate from the prescribed composition of members of the
Board established in 1961. The Board, which that government put in place for
the defunct Niger Delta River Basin Development Authority had no
representative from the Niger Delta area.
Thus the ill-conceived Board did away with the aspirations of the people
of area which was vitally needed to make correct policy solutions to the
problems of the area.
The second problem which the government at the centre in Nigeria created
was to establish ten more River Basin Development Authorities in 1976.
By so doing, a serious economic problem was seriously politicized.
Meanwhile, the original Authority- Niger Delta Basin Development
Authority (NDBDA), which was supposed to serve the designated "special area"
was starved of funds, while the ten agencies which did not need to be
created, were generously funded.
Undoubtedly, the Commission understood the challenges of the region but
the colonialists came short of creating the Niger Delta region like the
other existing three regions- the Western, Eastern and Northern regions. It
lacked the political will to do so, and that was to be a major blow to the
peoples of the Niger Delta.
Ecological Impediments It is only a matter of stating the obvious that
the Niger Delta region presents an array of seemingly insurmountable
physical constraints for human habitation. As with most deltas of the world,
the region is fragmented into countless islands surrounded by an intricate
network of rivers, rivulets and creeks. It goes without saying that
infrastructural development would be cost-intensive in such an
environment.
The greatest single problem in the physical environment of the Niger
Delta region is that posed by water. The very low elevations, decreasing to
below sea-level in some parts of the coastal areas, brings about an annual
ritual of flooding in which the rivers overrun their banks and the
floodwaters spread and deposit their sediment in the swamps.
The economic frustration, loss of lives and property in submerged
communities and farmlands is one major source of anguish and despair in the
Niger Delta region.
According to the report of the Civil Engineering Adviser to the defunct
NDDB (1966), the movement of the annual sediment load of the parent river
through the Delta are two of the most important problems to be solved in
connection with the use and development of the water resources of the
delta.
The problems besetting the Niger Delta are truly formidable. There is no
denying the fact that the twin problems of flood and erosion go far beyond
the capability of individuals or communities, and even state governments, to
contain in a comprehensive manner. It requires a strong political will on
the part of government to transform the Niger Delta environment for
sustainable development in the region.
Let me bring to your notice, distinguished ladies and gentlemen, that
land reclamation in the Niger Delta is a costly venture that brings shame to
the development process. Only last year, the Bayelsa State government spent
about $66 million to reclaim a parcel of land before construction work on a
500-bed general hospital project could commence.
In upland Nigeria, that same sum would go a long way to complete a
number of housing estates.
The problems of flood and erosion have remained with the people of the
Niger Delta region forty-three years after Nigeria attained the status of a
sovereign nation, thus stalling the prospects of any form of development in
the region.
The environmental challenges of the Niger Delta region are complex and
enormous, constituting ecological and natural impediments.  Specifically,
they include the following:
a. Sedimentation and Siltation This process is caused by an increase in
tidal wave action and results in narrow creeks and reduction in creek
depth. Whilst the semi-diurnal tidal regime ensures two high tidal floods
and two low ebb tides within the course of each day, wave action along the
coastline results in both depletion and loss of sediments in the beaches. A
good example of this is Koluama (1) and Koluama (2), the off-shore facility
operated by Chevron-Texaco, which was once on land, and is now an off-shore
well.
b. Land Degradation This challenge is related to inadequate waste
management, oil spillage, bush burning, urban industrial pollution, erosion
and inappropriate agricultural practices. The impact of industrial wastes
and oil pollution on fishing and farming activities over the years, have
left deep scars on the economic resources of the peoples of the region.
c. BIO-DIVERSITY DEPLETION This problem relates to air pollution,
deforestation, population pressure, urbanization and over-exploration. The
challenge of air pollution is related to gas flaring and acid rain, as well
as gaseous emissions from variety of sources. The water in this region is
known to have very high iron content.
d. OIL SPILLAGE AND GAS FLARING The proportion of crude oil loss to the
environment is quite disturbing.
A Department of Petroleum Resources report in Nigeria states that over
95% of the volume of oil spilled in the region is not recovered.  This
spells serious health hazards for the communities in the area affected.
It is prima facie evidence that no elaborate and concrete efforts are
made to protect the ecosystem from environmental pollution.
OIL AND ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION In a 1986 lecture at the University of
Lagos, entitled "Oil in World Politics", the late Chief M.O.  Feyide,
Nigeria's former Secretary of OPEC, points out as follows: All over the
world, the lives of people are affected, and the destiny of nations are
determined by the result of oil industry operations.  Oil keeps the factors
of the industrialized countries working and provides the revenues, which
enable oil exporters to execute ambitious national and economic development
plans. The march of progress would be retarded and life itself would be
unbearable if the world was deprived of oil.
That is why oil has become the concern of governments, a vital
ingredient of their politics and a crucial factor in political and
diplomatic strategies. Oil has been given the image of a big business ruled
by naked politics and dominated by ruthless men who are sensitive to nothing
except their profit.
Every known law on environmental safety has been violated in Nigeria.  A
good example of this is the volume of natural gas flared in the
country.
The average rate of gas flaring in the world is about 4%. In Nigeria,
over 70% of associated gas is flared. Nigeria has the notorious record of
25% of all gas flared in the world. There are intense debates about
compliance with the United Nations Agencies 21 and the Kyoto Protocol in
other parts of the world.
Nigeria is a signatory to these and other international
conventions.
Yet, the government does not enforce them because the main victims of
this ecological massacre are the people of the Niger Delta. As far as the
power brokers in Nigeria are concerned, the people of the Niger Delta are
good game; they do not deserve the protection of their national
government.
The deadline for an end to gas flaring was first fixed for 1985. A
policy of gas re-injection was put in place. Pressure from the oil companies
forced the government to abandon the policy. Shell canvassed 2008 as another
deadline. The year 2004 is now being mentioned as the new date. That is only
one year away, yet the programmes on the ground do not indicate that 2004
would be realistic. What is more, none of the 150 flare sites has been
discontinued.
In the oil-producing states of Nigeria, an average of one oil spill
occurs every week. In the delicate ecosystem of the Niger Delta, these
oil-related accidents cause grave damages to the environment and all that it
harbours. Protected by the might of the federal government, the oil
companies accuse the impoverished victims of being the cause of their
tragedy.
Over 1000 youths, women and children perished in the Jesse inferno at
the turn of the century. The figures of the dead in the Odi invasion have
been estimated to be about 2000. In all the cases of major calamities
associated with oil, the Nigerian government has not taken the pains to
calculate the casualty figures nor has it bothered to rebuild the devastated
communities.
One of the most disturbing ironies in the Niger Delta today is that
crude oil for export is transported to Bonny and Forcados through a network
of pipelines covering about 6,000 kilometres. The pipelines are laid across
farms, waterways and fishing grounds. Some pipes cross communities and
living quarters. Not enough care is given to the technical integrity of the
pipes, and so they corrode, burst and cause a deluge of oil spills and fires
that consume plant and human life.
The Federal Environmental Protection Agency (FEPA) was set up in 1989 to
watch environmental abusers. In the case of the oil industry in Nigeria,
however, FEPA is an idle threat. Although the activities of the oil
operators take place in territories belonging to states, they are prevented
by law from penalizing polluters and destroyers of the environment. That is
because oil and mining matters are preserve of the Federal Government as
indicated in the exclusive legislative list of the constitution.
The Challenge Of Underdevelopment The challenge to development in the
Niger Delta region can be better understood when we ask specific questions
and attempt to answer them.
For example, why has there been stagnation of the living standards among
the people of the Niger Delta region for decades? Why has the Niger Delta
remained underdeveloped for decades, despite the region's contribution of
about 90% to the nation's national wealth?
From the onset, all the agencies set up by the Federal Government to
address the developmental problems of the Niger Delta were beset by a number
of constraints including those of legitimacy and transparency.
Allegations of high level corruption were prevalent and there was very
little to justify the resource allocations received in terms of actual
delivery. As such, they were unable to pursue effective programmes.
Besides, the people of the oil producing states were unable to exercise
any significant political influence over the military government at the
federal level in their attempt to redress the situation. Those who paid the
piper dictated the tune.
Except for the NDDC, these agencies clearly lacked vision and planning;
employed improper developmental strategies; and had a poor implementation
focus. Above all, they all suffered from inadequate funding. What is more,
some of the programmes embarked upon to stimulate development have no
commensurate financial backing. Late release and poor management of funds
impede a good number of well-meaning projects and programmes. The result is
that these projects and programmes are abandoned midway without creating any
decisive impact on the lives of the Niger-Delta people.
In the face of constant changes in government and policies, it is
expedient to understand the effects of political power structure in Nigeria
on the developmental process of the Niger Delta.
Since the end of civil war in Nigeria in 1970, until the Obasanjo
presidency, political power remained solely and exclusively vested in the
hands of the Hausa-Fulani and the Middle-Belters with a considerable number
of Yorubas and Igbos in the cabinet.
The minority ethnic groups of the Niger Delta have not been brought
fully into the scheme of things; the occasional few considered for the
Federal Executive Council appointments have had little or no authority to
attract development to the Niger Delta. This unfailing concentration of
power in the three major ethnic groups has contributed in no small way to
the gross neglect of the Niger Delta.
Nigeria's Treasure Trove Like the gold rush of California in 19th
century America, there is a rush for the hydrocarbon treasure of the Niger
Delta. As at the last count, over 20 foreign companies and more than 100
indigenous ones are involved in the business of extracting oil in the Niger
Delta Basin. The rush for the "black gold" as some have tagged crude oil has
opened new chapters in the relationship between the region, its people and
those who seek to benefit from the region's abundant natural resources.
More people and allied industries are coming in to take up space in the
region as they position to participate in the oil trade whether as oil
service contractors, job seekers, oil administrators, law enforcement
officers or private security contractors, environmentalists, researchers,
journalists and development specialists.
The population of the Niger Delta is growing at the rate of about 2000
immigrants per day because of oil. This is in addition to the local
population growth in the region. The implication for social service and
infrastructure is telling: there is a growing demand for housing,
electricity, schools and hospitals. Above all, governments of the region
need to rise to the challenge of training adequate manpower to manage the
various institutions to meet the yearnings of our people for a better
life.
The region's inability to meet the real needs of the people, the
frustrations arising from their inability to convince the central government
and to some extent the international community and donor institutions, as to
their real predicament may be following a familiar pattern that urgently
needs to be reversed.
Allow me to recall that, in 1966, a young man full of promise and
brilliance grabbed a gun and assembled a few like-minded fellows and
announced a revolution by the banks of the River Nun. The revolution lasted
twelve days. That man was none other than Nigeria's civil war hero, Major
Adaka Boro. Twenty-five years later, a middle-aged writer, businessman and
former government administrator launched a movement in his native
Ogoni.
The mobilization of the Ogoni for economic, cultural and environmental
justice through the Ogoni Bill of Rights and Movement for Survival of the
Ogoni People (MOSOP) attracted the attention of the whole country and the
world. Ken Saro-Wiwa, leader of the movement, together with eight others,
were hanged exactly eight years and fifteen days today in a Port Harcourt
prison for reasons connected with the successful agitation by the Ogoni for
survival.  The death of Isaac Boro and the hanging of Ken-Saro Wiwa and his
Ogoni kinsmen did not seem to have deterred the people of the Niger Delta in
the continuing agitation for justice.
The Ijaw Youths Council (IYC) launched the Kaiama Declaration. This was
followed by the Oron Bill of Rights, the Urhobo Economic Charter, the
Ikwerre Charter of Demands, the Aklaka Declaration, among others.  The
primary focus of these bills of right, declarations and charters was the
demand for resource control, self-determination, true federalism,
environmental and economic justice and peaceful co-existence. In all, they
are efforts that have been willfully misunderstood by the powers that
be.
The Way Forward The environmental challenge of developing the Niger
Delta is indeed a great one. By no means will anybody envisage easy
solutions to one single enclave like the Niger Delta that defines wealth and
poverty simultaneously.
However, while it is exceedingly late in many respects for certain
measures to work in the region, a lot can still be achieved if deliberate
and determined steps are taken to halt the time bomb from ticking. It is
therefore my considered opinion that the following suggestions be
considered.
1. The much-needed political will should be cultivated to develop the
Niger Delta, using the Willink Commission recommendations as a basis in many
respects.
2. The draconian laws concerning oil and gas and land use should either
be abrogated outright or amended to foster accelerated development in the
area. At present, all types of decrees, which had been transformed into
Acts, exists to justify the "federation" called Nigeria. The 1999
Constitution; the Land Use Act; the Oil Mineral Acts among others exist to
prevent dialogue among the various people of Nigeria.
3. For Nigeria to survive, the centre should give up some of its powers
to the federating units. At the moment, the centre represents injustice to
millions of minorities in Nigeria especially the Niger Delta. A centre that
does not produce but consumes is an unsustainable centre.
Such a centre can only protect its unfair privileges through the force
of arms.
4. Oil and gas matters should be removed from the exclusive legislative
list and placed on the concurrent legislative list. This will enhance
improved relationship between oil and gas companies and their oil bearing
host communities, which are largely manipulated and short-changed.
5. The oil and gas companies should either alone or in partnership with
the oil-producing companies embark upon small and medium scale
industries. Such industries should be based on raw materials sources from
the local area.
6. There should be massive skills acquisition programmes by the
multinational companies as well as local, state and federal
governments.
7. The large-scale oil and gas based industries such as refineries, gas
plants and the oil and gas companies themselves should have indigenes of the
area in which they operate to serve on their boards and should enjoy 25% of
the equity structure. Furthermore, 80% of unskilled labour needed by these
industries and companies should be drawn from the catchment area. At the
management cadre, 40% of each position should be reserved for the
indigenes.
8. The oil and gas companies should ensure the integrity of their
pipelines and, in times of spillage, the best industry technology should be
employed to effect remediation. In addition, the oil and gas companies
should undertake urgent removal of toxic waste, which is widespread
throughout the Niger-Delta.
9. The oil and gas companies should faithfully implement the 45% local
content proposed for the industry.
10. The community engagement policies of the various oil and gas
companies can be made more favourable than they are now.
11. The federal, state and local governments should pay appropriate
attention to the development of communities responsible for wealth
generation i.e. oil and gas producing communities.
12. The indigenes of host communities should avoid greed and
factionalization in their ranks so that they may not be easy prey to those
who seek to rule them.
13. The Federal Government should establish a Niger Delta Development
Bank. Oil and gas producing states and communities should participate in the
equity structure of such a bank.
14. The federal and state governments and private investors (foreign and
local) should set up industries around the commodities and raw materials
obtainable in the area such as rice, fish export, sharp sand industries,
etc. Of particular interest are the rice fields of Peremabiri and Isampou in
Bayelsa State, which are capable of supplying the entire West African sub
region.
15. The federal and state governments must develop the infrastructure
that will bring riverine filling stations to the rural people to reduce the
high cost of petroleum products.
16. In addressing the environmental challenges facing the Niger Delta
region adequately, it would be advisable to start from the rural
communities, which constitute over 70 per cent of the region's total
population, and harbours most of the oil wells. All the problems associated
with oil and gas exploration ranging from environmental degradation, loss of
farmland and marine life are solely borne by the rural dwellers. It is
therefore necessary that basic amenities be provided for them with some
degree of urgency.
17. One of the biggest challenges facing the Niger Delta people and its
leaders lies in designing an appropriate political and economic framework
that will ensure the protection of local communities. The Niger Delta states
must come together and pull resources for regional development. Investments
in mutually beneficial infrastructure such as railways, communications,
agro-allied industry with special emphasis on fishing, farming and
manufacturing will be ideal. I am convinced that the foregoing suggestions
will certainly raise the standard of living of our people.
Other Delta region such as Mekong in Vietnam and the Mississippi River
in the USA with worse environmental challenges have proved that, with
dedication and political will, the Niger Delta can be fully developed to
avoid what the current chairman of NNDC, Chief Onyeama Ugochukwu, said
frankly about the region: It is essentially the least developed area in
Nigeria_eThe paradox of a resource-rich enclave remaining so pervasively
poor is one of the enduring scandals of Nigeria's development _e_e..
Conclusion: Distinguished ladies and gentlemen, it is ironic that
Nigeria is yet to feel the outcome of resolutions at the United Nations
Earth Summit, which took place in Rio De Janeiro, Brazil about thirteen
years ago.
After 43 years as an independent nation, the central government in
Nigeria seems not to have fully understood the implications of being unjust
to any part of the country, especially by depriving the citizenry of their
God-given wealth. This injustice has given birth to countless problems,
among them a parlous economy and recurrent political instability.
Let me reiterate that the policies of successive administrations as well
as the activities of multinational oil companies have aggravated the neglect
of our land and people. The Land Use Act Decree No. 6, which came into force
in March 1978 appropriated all lands in the name of the Federal Republic of
Nigeria. In 1969, the Federal Government promulgated the Petroleum Act,
which forbade individuals from engaging in mining petroleum resources. This
was followed by Decree No.13 of 1970, which empowered the centre to acquire
all federally collected resources.
Decree No. 9 of 1971 gave the Federal Government all rights to offshore
rents and royalties.
It is obvious from all this that, over the year, our leaders used state
machinery to make laws aimed at dispossessing the people of the Niger Delta
of their God-given resources. It is time to revoke these laws, and release
the Niger Delta from bondage.
Since 1958, when export of crude oil began in commercial quantities,
Nigeria has realised well over 20 trillion dollars as proceeds. Yet, in
spite of this staggering figure, the Niger Delta area remains wretched.
Oloibiri, where oil was first discovered in Nigeria, is now a ghost town
in the very best sense of that description, a carcass rejected even by the
hounds who laid her bare in the first place.
The effects of oil exploration and exploitation are everywhere in
evidence in the Niger Delta. Marine life is virtually extinct.
Indigenous occupational industries are comatose. Erosion is a widespread
menace. Pollution of the very sources of life for the ordinary people
manifests in everything from our fishing equipment to the water we
drink.
The oil companies themselves are certainly not helping the situation by
their flagrant display of insensitivity. At the drilling sites, all the
amenities that make life tick is to be found: electricity, water, telephone
services, high-powdered marine transport vehicles, air transport and health
facilities.
Yet, within a short radius of this drilling haven, there is a stark
hundred per cent disparity in living standards. This is the paradox that the
people of the Niger Delta have had to contend with for almost half a
century. I do not exaggerate when I say that infrastructural provisions that
are taken for granted by other states in Nigeria are luxury to the people of
the Niger Delta. One small step for the rest of the country is, more often
than not, a giant stride for us. That is why the people feel elbowed out of
the race, and why the parameters of competitive growth have to be restored
in our favour.
To protect the people of the region from further hardship, there must be
effective implementation of all environmental protection laws. The people
must be involved in the decision-making process to widen their choices, and
increase their chances of influencing the socio-economic and political
agenda of the Niger Delta region.
I believe that in the face of such a welcome eventually in a nation
where justice and equity reign supreme, the people of the region will feel a
greater sense of belonging.
Distinguished ladies and gentlemen, our environment is our only
inheritance. If we lose it, there will be no basis to measure our
development. Everything begins and ends with the environment. We cannot
afford to look at it in abstract terms. It is the living environment where
policies and politics mix. It is that arena that determines our happiness or
otherwise.
My heart goes out to the management and staff of NewsAfrica magazine,
organisers of this forum, for giving us a chance to consider afresh the
plight of Nigeria's Niger Delta. I am equally indebted to you all for being
such a kind patient audience.
I thank you for your attention, and God bless.

Original_Search_Title: Why People Think Obasanjo is Stubborn, Special Adviser
Headline: Nigeria: Why People Think Obasanjo is Stubborn, Special Adviser
Body_Text:
Benin City —  Some years ago, he was harassed by the military junta for allegedly
drafting the coup speech of Major Gideon Orkar. But today, Professor Julius
Ihonubere, an author and activist, is the chief hunter of those policies and
programmes that drag the wheel of government back.
The Special Adviser to President Olusegun Obasanjo on Policy,
Programmes and Monitoring, in this interview, speaks on his Orkar coup
experience, the president's impatience to fix Nigeria, why some people think
Obasanjo is stubborn and the president's workaholic nature.
Excerpts.
You monitor President Obasanjo virtually 24 hours on a daily
basis. What kind of man is he, some Nigerians think he is a stubborn
president?
Nigerians just try to forget that Obasanjo did not just become president
today. He has been in the army and participated very actively in the civil
war. He was a federal commissioner for works in this country at a time when
we were reconstructing and rehabilitating. All those roads that go to
Idiroko were constructed by him. He has been an international statesman on
the board of Ford Foundation, Rockfeller, the African-American Institute,
member of the Eminent Persons Group, founder of the African Leadership
Forum, etc.
He is one of the founders of Transparency International, many leaders
around the world are his friends and, more importantly, this is second term
in his tenure. So, he knows every wayo any Nigerian wants to play him, so
when they bring those little tricks to him, he has seen the beginning and
the end of their attempt to play games. And sometimes, he is so disappointed
at the people trying to do that, so he gets angry. If you understand him, I
have never had a quarrel with him and he listens to what you say, but I'm
also conscious not to bring stupid things to him. Somebody came to me and
said he wanted to invest in Nigeria, I took him to the president, then, when
we came out of the meeting, he said, 'well, we are going to bring about 10
billion dollars to Nigeria but to do it, we need you to give us about 600
barrels of oil per day to sell, then we add our money..', I said to him,
'then you are a thief.' Of course, he wanted me to take him back to the
president, I said I was not going to do that, get the hell out of this
country. So, if I were to take that kind of man to the president, how would
he react? He will be disappointed. As a professor, and an activist, an
author, you should be able to see that this is a criminal in the making. So,
in my view, if you cannot work hard, you cannot think on your feet and you
do not have the energy, you cannot work with Obasanjo. I do not know where
his energy comes from, he does not sleep and he reads every thing you give
to him. Every thing you give to him, he takes the pain to read and minute
between the pages. He can call you twenty-five times a day when we are
writing his speech to add one word or change one word. And it does not
matter whether it is 2 am or 3 am because he is awake. So, if the president
is awake, who am I to be talking about sleep?
So, I do not think he is a stubborn man, he is just somebody who is
impatient with laziness, with inefficiency, with fraud, with people who
perpetrate injustice and he does not believe in keeping it in him, he tells
you right there and then. If you steal money and he sees you, he tells you
that you are a thief. And I know I have gone to him with information and he
will say, 'do it now.' So, if he calls me, for example, and I do not have
memo pad with me and he says, write a letter to the President of Canada and
you do not have writing pad, he will make a comment that will make people to
say he is stubborn, he is not. He is just in a hurry to fix Nigeria, he is
in a hurry to have good people to work with. So, only those who can think on
their feet can work with him. Take one case for instance, when we just
returned from abroad; at about 2 am, he still carried his files and began to
work. That is the kind of person he is.
Tell us more about the con games some people try to play with the
president...
Like I have said, in the last 15, 20 and 30 years, this country has been
nurtured on criminality within and without. Every Nigerian you meet is
trying to crook you, all those who were abroad will tell you that their
greatest problem are family members. People send money to build houses and
they were sent pictures of other people's homes. They arrive here, there is
no house. You give money to them to open an account for you, they will not
put your money there, they will spend it and tell you stories. And because
of the presidential system, the buck stops at his desk. For example, every
body wants to lift oil and the man is tired of it. He is always upset, 'why
can't people come and say they want to go into agriculture so I can help you
get land, fertiliser and even loan to start it? Why can't you come and say
you want to set up a small scale industry to do plantain chips, cassava for
export or produce starch, why?" Everybody wants to lift oil, people have
come for political appointments, people who do not deserve to even be
walking the streets; and he will ask them: 'You, where do you feel you can
work in this system?'
And they are not looking for appointments where they can contribute to
the growth of the economy, they are looking for appointments where they can
corner some budget, some votes. So, generally, he is impatient with those
people who come and think they are smart enough to play a fast one. But,
luckily, his experience and exposure has enabled him see some of these
quickly.
So, what is it like monitoring Obasanjo's policies and programmes?
Well, the first thing is that at the beginning, there was no money. In
fact, when he was appointing me, he said, 'look, there is a big vaccum in my
government. I take time to take any decision every day and it is like that
from one meeting to the other. If you look at our meeting schedule, there is
no room there for lunch. Lunch is usually around six, seven, eight pm every
day. There is no break from 6am till 8pm, if you do not have breakfast, you
are not going to have it.'
So today, we take decisions at every meeting. Communities will come,
they want roads, they want water, they want schools, they want federal
appointment, they want police stations, and he will tell them, 'this I will
do, this I will not do.' He moves to the next meeting. Previously, nobody
was following up but today, there is a maximum of 24 hours response
time. The only decisions I have not followed are those that were taken
today, because I left Abuja at 7am to come to Benin. But you can be sure
that I will be briefed tomorrow and by tomorrow evening, they will be
followed up. That was one of the loopholes.
Secondly, the institutions that were supposed to be doing monitoring
were moribund. The National Planning Commission by law is the monitoring
agency of the federal government, the Federal Office of Statistics does
monitoring, the Budget office, there is the National Intelligence Economic
Council which also does a lot of monitoring and they are producing their
results. Now, we are trying to revitalise this and streamline the due
process involved in monitoring and streamline some of the work because mine
is a political appointment; at the end of four years when we leave, it is
over. The next president might not think that it is important, he may not
even care because the person there might not be me but when there is a
national planning commission that needs to be transformed technologically,
reinvigorated, and policy wise, redefined so that it can be more effective
in monitoring government policies and actions, there is a problem because a
lot of decisions come from the president to the various ministries that need
to be done. The technological capacity of the monitoring agencies is
extremely weak in all the ministries. There is no local area network, there
is no wide area network, there is no e-mail interconnectivity among the
ministries. So, one ministry does not know what the other is doing. In
Egypt, there is a policy institute within the government, any policy that is
to be adopted goes to that institute first. They analyse it, if it is about
water, they will tell you the implications on roads, for agriculture, for
health, for the Customs because you are going to import those equipment, are
you going to grant a waiver, a subsidy? Most times in Nigeria, after we have
done it, then we see the impact, we now try to change it. We are now making
it to subject some of those things to critical analyses and using the
facility of inter-ministerial council so that you have people from different
ministries that look at these polices. It is only a question of technology
capacity and training. Many officers of these ministries have not gone on
any training in the last 15 years. So, they are out of time. For example,
one of the new strategies of monitoring is committee involvement. If a
borehole is being drilled in your community, if a school is being built
there, you get the community to set up a committee to work with the
monitoring agencies to claim ownership because it belongs to them. They will
protect it and protect it against inferior performance and poor quality. So,
we have just started to introduce that to the public. We are also involving
the civil society in monitoring. The president challenged civil society
groups at the briefing we had in Abuja that they should go back and set up
committees to monitor projects, monitor government, monitor programmes and
government will work with them to ensure that there is delivery to the
grassroots.
Can we know what Obasanjo wants to achieve with his various reform
programmes?
The president is the driving force of the reform agenda. In fact, if you
take him out of it today, probably it will collapse, that is why so many
people are concerned about who succeeds him; will such a person be committed
to the reform agenda? But, for him, it is basically to prevent the economy
from total collapse, re-energising institutions and structures, increasing
and expanding productivity, improving the living conditions of the Nigerian
people, creating jobs, restructuring and re-positioning Nigeria for
greatness and also announcing to the world that Nigeria is open for
business. Like those who were criticising the 8th All-Africa Games and
CHOGM, by the time they see how much money Nigeria has made and will
continue to make and how tourism is going to expand as a result of the
hosting and the signals it sends to the world, they will marvel. Has Nigeria
ever got into the CNN on a positive note? The only time we see Nigeria on
CNN is during riots, demonstrations or coups, but for the first time, we put
50 world leaders in Nigeria and there was not one single car accident, we
should be proud of ourselves. We have demonstrated that we can do things at
world class level. The Queen herself was astounded by the organisation and
the quality of the facilities. And I interacted with many of the visiting
leaders and they were saying that we have set standard that many countries
in the third world and in the Commonwealth will not be able to meet. We
should not be complaining that somebody got contract, somebody has to get
the contract to supply the air conditioners, all Nigerians cannot get the
contract to supply them. But, you see, Nigerians tend to be cynical and
envious of people at the same time. Oh somebody supplied the cars, they gave
us 900 BMW cars, we did not pay a kobo, the only thing they got was the
waiver duty. And they have sold the 900 cars at half the cost price to
Nigerians. I did not get one, you can quote me. I did not even try to get
one because they will think I got it free anyway. But they have been sold to
the Nigerian people. Peugeot was trying to play games and the president told
them to get lost by getting it from BMW free, we did not sell the
cars. Coscharis sold the cars, so what is wrong with that? The cars are now
all over the country, we used them for COJA, we used them for CHOGM. So, the
president is trying to put in place a comprehensive reform agenda that will
empower Nigerians, improve living conditions, expand trade and growth
opportunities, attract investors and consolidate our political structures
and institutions.
There is this belief that Nigeria is not ripe for these reforms
because of the negative effect on the economy and hardship it is imposing on
the people. Nigeria is over mature for the reforms. A Nigerian was mature to
steal three million pounds cash and try to take it out would you say he was
not mature?
Nigerians have embezzled billions of naira in this country, they have
gone abroad and crooked people, they have stolen hundreds and millions of
dollars, they have built some of the most sophisticated houses you can find
any where in the world, that you would not find in Tel Aviv, they are
matured to do that, local government chairmen have shared instead of
building roads, they were matured to do that. They have organised
assassinations, it has to start somewhere, people will always complain,
enemies of reforms and progress will take advantage of the short term
pains. Like the president said, he is not in a popularity contest, when the
results show, Nigerians will know he is doing it for the good of the
country. The deregulation just has to happen soon, we cannot be spending two
hundred and eighty million dollars per year to subsidize petrol. And we all
knew that it was only in Ibadan and Lagos that they were buying at control
price. At Uzzeba in Edo State, I was buying it at N75 per litre. And when
you find it, you will dance, you would even have to bribe to buy it. And the
question is, will the prices come down as competition has entered? Now,
there is a market given price, the refineries are now going to be
constructed. We have given eighteen licences out to Nigerians to build
refineries and more foreigners are coming in. When the people begin to
produce, they want to compete with each other. There were petrol stations in
the United States that will give you free coke when you buy petrol. From
there, free check on your tyres, they clean your windscreen free, give you
towel and all kinds of things for you to come and buy petrol. And before you
buy petrol, you can look around at the prices, it is published, I always go
for the cheapest because I know it is the same technology they are using but
the question is availability and quality control. And that is what the DPPRA
is there to do. On the price of production, the DPPRA will agree on what the
overhead should be, that will be added. And the DPR will monitor them and
ensure they do not go beyond that and any petrol station that goes beyond
that will be closed, some have been closed down now.
People should give Nigeria a chance, what we should be asking is what we
are going to do with the savings. Like that advert on television: Wetin we
go use dis money do? Let it not just be a television advert, NGOs, community
leaders, local government councillors, all representatives, should say that
money, we want to see it on roads, in education, in school, in training so
that we can move forward as a country. Did we not get our independence
before some of the countries that are better than us? Nigerians who are
sending their children to Ghana, are they not embarrassed? South Africa has
taken over our telecommunication, there is nothing wrong in it, it is an
African country. But we too should be exporting to them. This was a country
we fought for, we set up South Africa Relief Fund to help South Africa
become liberated. Today, they are practically dominating everything. They
are human beings like us, they are not better than us, they are not stronger
than us. Americans are not stronger than us, we went to school with them, we
beat them in class. That is what Americans respect Nigerians for, hard work
and delivery. I went there to teach, they were not better than the students
I taught at Ife, in fact, my students at Port-Harcourt were better than the
ones I was teaching. Those people did not know any thing, you have to
spoon-feed them. So we have to put these things together and say every
difficult policy has to start at some point. You cannot make pepper soup
without putting either chicken, fish or something. At one point, you must
put in the meat into the soup or pepper soup. So, in these repairs we are
doing in this country, the only thing we can do is to appeal to all
Nigerians to be patient. Some people will be affected, there will be no easy
money, easy money will not be there. Those were the people
complaining. Salaries have been paid. The Federal Government is not owing
any local government or any state government its allocation as and when
due. So, what is the complaint about? Money in town? That is, no money to
steal, and there will not be money to steal because we want to use that
money to improve the living conditions of Nigerians, for the betterment of
all.
Is there any way your office can advise the federal government to
divert Abacha loot into specific projects rather than distributing it to the
774 local governments as advocated by the civil society for effective
monitoring?
Well, first of all, I want to say that Abacha loot does not belong to
the civil society, it is money stolen from the Nigerian treasury. And the
condition is very clear, all funds generated must go into the federation
account from where it will be appropriated according to the law, according
to the established procedure. So, there will be no preferential treatment or
even federal, state or local government cornering any part of it outside
laid down rules and regulations.
Two, as a mark of respect for civil society, the president, for the
first time, created the Office of the Special Adviser on Civil Society
Matters. Engineer Umaru Abba-Gana and civil society groups have refused to
take advantage of that office. The Office of the Special Adviser on Ethics
and Good governance, Senator Kanu Agabi, is a civil society outfit, they
have not taken advantage of that office. And several other offices in
government that if you look at the roles they are supposed to play, the
civil society has an opportunity there to reach out to government in the
interest of the people. They are not taking advantage of it. I think there
has to be a way in which that has to come in.
Now, in my office, we monitor projects and policies. In Civil Society,
they have a particular area of interest that they think they want to be
involved in. I am following the challenge from the president that they
should come up with ideas on how they want to engage government in
monitoring.
In the extractive industry, Transparency Initiative is just one
additional plank in the anti-corruption, transparency and accountability
reform agenda. As you know, this was originally an initiative that came from
the open society initiative with support of the British Government and DFID,
I was there at the launching in London. I represented the African Center for
Constitutional Government. The books of extractive industries will no longer
be treated as secret documents, they will be opened to the public and
audited account for scrutiny. There is what you call 'publish what you pay,
publish what you earn.' So, a company like Shell will publish publicly in
the newspapers, make their account available to anybody; how much money is
generated and how much money goes to overhead and how much money goes to the
federal government. The Nigerian government in turn, will have to publish
how much it receives from extractive industries - the oil companies. Now,
there will be independent auditing of the figures they submit. The next
stage is the political dimension, when villagers, students, women and youths
now know how much government has received and can now relay that on how much
has been expended on developmental issues and make specific demands. It is
like what we are going to be doing soon, we would be publishing in the
papers how much goes to each local government in the country so that people
in the local governments will know how much the chairman receives each
month. Then, we will do it also to the states. Many Nigerians do not know
how much their local governments are making and how much the state
governments are collecting so that you can now say if you collected this
amount, this is the number of public servants you have, this is your wage
bill, how much has gone into roads, into water, into schools, into health.?
So, in essence, it is a true civil society initiative because it is designed
to bring to the door steps of the ordinary person the resources being
generated by government and how they are being expended. So, there is going
to be a conference in January to bring together the extractive industry,
NGOs, civil societies, the National Assembly and the security forces to
educate them. We will bring experts also who have been involved in the
international aspect of the negotiation and the auditors to talk about what
they have been looking for and how it will be done and what they are
planning to do.
Is the Obasanjo government ready to bite on the issue of corruption
(the arrest of Labour minister, Akwanga and others), or is it a mere scratch
on the surface?
Well, the first thing is that the executive cannot put anybody in
jail. We can only collect evidence and pass it on to the security forces and
the various anti-corruption agencies. And government has been doing that
effectively. And the law says you are innocent until proven guilty and I do
not think it will be fair because you got information that Julius has N20
million in his pocket, you go and grab him because you know his salary is
not up to that. You have to find out how he made the money, investigate him,
cross check, it should take long but it is not the fault of government or
these agencies. It is the law in Nigeria, every case we take to court, they
go and get a court injunction. The judges have been happy at issuing it in
the morning and at midnight and once you get a court injunction in a
democracy, you have to obey. The lawyers have been playing all kinds of
games, like we took Fred Ajudua to court, we brought experts from the United
Kingdom at government expense. We got to court, and suddenly, his lawyers
said they are no longer in the case. The case cannot continue, we paid
airfare, hotel, allowances and they went back and adjourned till they are
able to constitute another defense team.
So, on the side of government, there is no going back on
anti-corruption. And I think, like I have said, this government has put in
place more institutions than any government in this country. All ministers
