increasingly have to source their investments requirements on the capital
markets. While a significant amount of money has been invested in gas
exploration, it ha yet to bring money to the coffer of the
government. However, this will change from 2005 when estimated revenues from
gas could average in the neigbourhood of one billion dollars. Oil will
continue to be the major source of government revenues in the near
term.

Original_Search_Title: Mini Agro-pastoral Show Kicks-off
Headline: Cameroon: Mini Agro-pastoral Show Kicks-off
Body_Text:
Farmers with best agricultural products in the Littoral Province are displaying their know-how.
A mini agro-pastoral show for the Littoral Province has kicked-off at the Bonanjo party house in Douala. The opening ceremony of the mini agro-pastoral show was presided at by the Governor of the Province, Gounoko Haounaye. The mini agro-pastoral show which takes place yearly, groups farmers with best agricultural products in the Littoral Province with the aim of encouraging farming competition and an exchange of ideas in agricultural issues.
At the esplanade of the Bonanjo party house yesterday, many visitors were not only taken aback by the multitude of agricultural products from the Cameroonian soil, but were also fascinated by the festive atmosphere that reigned amongst the farmers.
According to the Littoral Provincial Delegate of Agriculture, Mr. Claude Lacordaire Mbimi, 800 out of the 1000 participants expected to take part in this year's show will be vying 120 prices in addition to envelopes. Of this number, a hundred of the prices are simple and twenty others are special prices. Some of these prices, he added, are trucks, wheelbarrows, cutlasses, hoes, insecticides and manure. He regreated the fact that not all the participants have been able to transport their produce to the exposition site due to lack of finance and poor road network which have always been the major hindrance to farmers in the Littoral.
These farmers, most of them women, emerged victorious at the different divisional competitions that began with preliminary selections at the village level. They have, as such, brought with them agricultural products that will be sold at the end of the show, as well as samples of seeds that for planting. Amongst some of the products being exposed in and out of the fifteen stands set up for the occasion are: white and red yams, cassava, red, white and yellow potatoes, plantains, banana, pineapple, pawpaw, bush pepper, oranges, dry fruits such as pineapples and mangoes which according to Mrs Alice Gnebi, a participant, can be conserved for at least six months. Apart from on-the-spot eatable agricultural products, one can also find natural medicinal products said to be good for farmers in their fight aganist insects. The mini-agro-pastoral show ends on Friday, December 19, 2003 with the award of prices to winners.

Original_Search_Title: Abia State Proposes N20.4b Budget for 2004
Headline: Nigeria: Abia State Proposes N20.4b Budget for 2004
Body_Text:
Abia government has announced a budget of N20.4 billion for its
activities in the 2004 fiscal year.
Governor Orji Kalu, who announced the figure while presenting the
state's budget for 2004, said it represented N3.3 billion or 17.5 per cent
increase over the N17.1 billion for 2003.
Kalu, said next year's budget had been tagged, "budget of optimism,"
because
"we are confident that the problems our nation is going through will
soon give way to steady government."
He gave the recurrent expenditure for next year as N10.5 billion while
the capital expenditure is N9.94 billion.
He said the recurrent expenditure is N5.07 billion less than that of
2003, adding that the capital expenditure is, however, N3.5 billion or 55.4
per cent higher than last year's figure.
The governor said the government planned to break new grounds next year,
stressing: "What I am sure is that 2004 will bring good tidings for our
people".
He said the state also planned to cultivate 700 hectares of cassava, to
be inter cropped with several thousands of palm seedlings.
He said the state had also concluded plans to set up an organic
fertiliser plant in Abia.
Kalu announced that the housing and transport sector would witness a
beehive as the government had made progress in reviving all ailing
industries in partnership with local and foreign companies.
He warned that 2004 would be a difficult year because of federal
government's fiscal policies but gave the assurance that in the end,
Nigerians would overcome, "if we trust in God, shun corruption and believe
in our abilities".
"We cannot actualise our vision of becoming a greater nation when our
people live in abject poverty and want in the midst of plenty," he
said.
He called on the people to rise up and fight for their rights, adding
that "nobody can guarantee a better nation for us except Nigerians do so
themselves".
"I know that the people are wise. They know who their friends and
enemies are," he said, adding: 'The day of reckoning is coming for our
corrupt leaders."

Original_Search_Title: University of Agriculture Stands Up to Be Counted
Headline: Nigeria: University of Agriculture Stands Up to Be Counted
Body_Text:
Lagos — The facility tour of the University of Agriculture, Abeokuta (UNAAB)
that took place recently was a serious matter. Members of the Governing
Council, led by the Chairman, Alhaji Sanni Idris, the Vice Chancellor,
Professor Isreal Adu, other principal officers and staff of the institution
as well as journalists were there.
One of the two Universities established by the Federal Government in
1988, with a tripodal mandate for teaching, research and extension services,
UNAAB had a lot to show. Indeed, it has expanded in terms of Academic
Structure, Programmes, Support Centres, Research and Developmental Centre
(RESDEC), the Agricultural Media Resources and Extension Centres. Other
areas of expansion are the University farms, collaboration with other
institutions, the University Staff School, Library Resources and the
University Consult.
The tour started at the Mini Campus, located at Isale Igbein, Abeokuta,
with the inspection of the University Staff (Nursery and Primary) School
made up Nursery and Primary School, with no fewer than 30 pupils in each of
the classrooms.
The school, which was established in October 1997, has a student roll of
106 and more than 35 qualified teachers.
The team also visited the Centre for Human Resources Development
(CENHURD), which provides consultation services in computer studies, pre
degree courses and a proposed veterinary clinic.
At the pre degree classroom where a lecture was going on, learning was
not too conducive as the classroom was over crowded.
Director of CENHURD, Professor Segun Lagoke stated that the Centre was
established a year ago. He explained that the medium term vision of the
university on information technology is to have a campus wide network with
full voice, video, data capabilities and full Internet access.
The computer laboratory has 50 computers, which students can use for
their practical work, with a web directory booklet on academic
learning.
The Director appealed to the Council to prevail on the government to
support the school's part time program so as to meet the yearnings of the
people for education.
The team later visited the A.G. Leventis Memorial Centre for Learning
(LEMCEL) located at Oke Itoku, where A G Leventis was said to have resided
and started his career in 1822. He was the first Babalashe of Egba
Land.
Director of the centre, said to be newly renovated and furnished at a
cost of N10m, Dr. Taofik Salisu, showed pictures of Leventis' motorcycle,
which he said could run at the speed of 60 kilometres per hour.
Salisu explained that the building was donated to the University by the
A G Leventis Limited and it is being used as a library and museum that will
reflect the history of Egbaland and Abeokuta.
He also disclosed that UNAAB would be using the building as a distance
learning centre, gallery, school of Arts and Textile Design, a Computer and
a cyber cafe that will be open to the public.
The team later visited the University Consult Feed Mill located at Asero
on the outskirts of the ancient city.
Dean of the College of Animal Science and Live Stock Feed, Professor
Funlola Adebanwo said the commercial hatchers hatch 28,000 eggs twice a week
and provide 24 hours service to the public. The College also produces
poultry feeds such as groundnut cakes, grinded bone mill and palm kernel oil
from groundnuts, bones and kernel respectively.
The team was later taken to the permanent site of the Staff School,
still under construction, which is expected to accommodate the secondary
school.
Project Director, Mr. Agboola told the visitors that the project, being
executed through direct labour, started last year, with about N3 million
expended on it so far. He added that all the materials needed for the
construction have been purchased. The first phase of the project will
accommodate JSS 1 to 3 students, a staff room and a temporary office for the
principal. There will also be separate hostels for boys and girls as
boarding would be compulsory for all students.
Director of the Garri Processing Unit, Dr. Dipe Olu explained that
virtually all the process are carried out through the age old traditional
methods, except the grinding of the cassava tubers which is done through the
use of locally fabricated machines.
He said the unit has a contract from the Federal government to produce
and supply 120 tons of Garri, adding that the traditional method of
production not withstanding, they have already produced more than half of
this. The visitors also visited the cashew processing unit, the cold room
for storage and production of ice block and the veterinary anatomy
laboratory.
It was therefore not a surprise when Prof. Adu was named the Best Vice
Chancellor of the year 2002 and the UNAAB council, the second best by the
National Universities Commission. The awards have since been presented.
Born to late Mr. Joseph Adu Fagbohun and late Madam Alice Asake Adu in
the Ajero Compound of Oke-Ita in Igbara-Oke, Prof. Isreal Adu had his
primary education at St. Paul's School, Igbara-Oke and secondary school
education at the Anglican Grammar School, Igbara-Oke, where he obtained his
West African School Certificate in December, 1963.
He later gained admission (as one of the pioneer students) to the Animal
Health School, Moor Plantation, Ibadan, where he obtained the Ordinary
National Diploma in Animal Health and Husbandry in June 1966. The outbreak
of hostilities in the northern part of Nigeria prevented Folorunso and seven
others from starting the Higher National Diploma course in Vom in July
1966. He was consequently posted to Ile-Ife and later Ilesha as Animal
Health Assistant for these Divisons.
It was the same eight 'disappointed' officers that formed the first set
of the Higher National Diploma course in Animal Health at the Animal Health
School, Moor Plantation, Ibadan in July 1967.
As luck would have it, Israel Folorunso Adu was granted direct admission
to the preliminary class of 1968 at the University of Ibadan, Ibadan where
he studied for his B.Sc and Ph.D degrees in Animal Science between 1969 and
1975.
At the Undergraduate level, Professor Adu won the following awards and
prizes:
_The Western Nigeria Cocoa Scholarship award
_The Prestigious 'University of Ibadan Scholar award and;
_The British Engineering West African Company Prize as the best student
in 1970.
At the Postgraduate level, Prof Adu bagged the Rockefeller Foundation
Scholarship and the British Council Fellowship awards which enabled him to
travel to Scotland for several periods, ranging from between 3 and 9
months. Between 1988 and 1989, Prof. Adu was awarded the highly prestigious
Alexander Von Humboldt Fellowship, which facilitated his spending his
sabbatical leave year at the University of Giessen, Germany. Professor Adu
started his professional career as an academic at the Ahmadu Bello
University (ABU), Zaria, where he was appointed Research Fellow/Lecturer II
at the National Animal Production Research Institute in 1976 and progressed
through the Ranks to the position of Professor in 1986.
Professor Adu's area of research interest is Ruminant Nutrition and
Management with special emphasis on the Small Ruminants. At the last count,
Professor Adu has to his credit, 86 publications made up of 62 Journal
Articles, 16 referred Conference Papers, three Chapters in books and five
monographs.

Original_Search_Title: ADF Approves US $31.67 Million Loan to Finance the Fadama Development Project
Headline: Nigeria: ADF Approves US $31.67 Million Loan to Finance the Fadama Development Project
Body_Text:
The Board of Directors of the African Development Fund (ADF) has
approved a loan of US $ 31.67 million to finance the Fadama (low-lying flood
plains usually with easily accessible shallow groundwater) development
project in Nigeria. The project is in line with the Bank's group vision
aimed at fighting poverty and promoting economic development and regional
integration between its member countries.
The project is part of a National Fadama Development Programme and is a
follow-up to the successful implementation of the National Fadama
Development Project (Fadama 1) with World Bank support btween 1993 and
1999. It aims to enhance agricultural production, productivity and value
addition for small holders and rural entrepreneurs in the Fadama areas of
the following six states: Borno, Jigawa, Katsina, Kogi, Kwara and Plateau,
on a sustainable basis.
The project will involve capacity building for participatory planning,
project implementation, operation, maintenance as well as enterprise
development and management. It will also entail the construction and
maintenance of rural infrastructures including rural roads, irrigation
infrastructure, sanitation, agro-processing and marketing
facilities. Finally, the project will provide demand-driven technical
assistance and advisory services to resource users.
The ADF loan will contribute to poverty reduction and food security in
rural areas by improving the living conditions of the rural disadvantaged
people and their access to rural infrastructure. The project will help
farmers increase their incomes as a result of producing crops both during
the rainy and dry seasons in the fadama. Production models prepared for
estimating possible returns from the project indicate that, at full
development, the project will, for instance, result in the following
increases in annual output: 37,000 tons of tomatoes, 7,300 tons of okra,
6,000 tons of pumpkins, 7,300 tons of amaranthus, and 14,600 tons of
onions. Apart from these typical fadama crops grown mostly during the dry
season, the project will also lead to increases in rain-fed agriculture. The
models also indicate potential increases in the production of rain-fed crops
of about 3,800 tons of maize, 200 tons of sorghum, 200 tons of millet,
19,000 tons of cassava, 900 tonnes of cowpeas, 500 tonnes of paddy rice, and
300 tonnes of groundnuts.
The ADF loan represents 88% of the project total cost estimated at US $
36.16 million. It will be used to finance 99% of the foreign exchange costs
and 74% of local currencies expenses.
The Bank Group has since 1971 approved 31 operations in favour of
Nigeria for a total amount of US$ 2212.82 million. Total disbursements
amount to US$ 1,865.85 million.

Original_Search_Title: Association for Food Security Feeds Bombali District
Headline: Sierra Leone: Association for Food Security Feeds Bombali District
Body_Text:
Since the victory of President Kabbah in the May 14, 2002 Presidential
Elections, his vision has remained firmly focused on the Rehabilitation of
the Agricultural Sector so that by the year 2007, at least every Sierra
Leonean would go to bed well fed.
To translate the President's vision into reality, the "Association For
Food Security", a national non-governmental organization with professionals
with years of experience and research in development, extension, crop
production and community work, in collaboration with a component of the
Sierra Leone Reintegration and Rehabilitation Programme (SLRRP) has taken
the daunting challenge in the area of crop production to the Bombali
district.
The 'Association For Food Security' based in Makeni with headquarters at
57 Campbell Street in Freetown has its agricultural extension projects in
the Biriwa and Kamabai sections.
The AFS projects operate in 19 villages, eleven villages in the Kamabai
section and eight villages in the Bumban section, with a total of 500
disadvantaged and marginalized farmers.
Under the AFS programme, the 500 beneficiaries are reported to have
benefited immensely ranging from the acquisition of agricultural inputs and
harvested crops.
Speaking to Standard Times recently in Makeni, Mr. Mohamed Kamara, the
Acting AFS regional officer and a farmer in the Biriwa chiefdom said, before
the commencement of the project in the two sections, the beneficiaries were
supplied with farming tools which includes small hoes, large hoses,
slashers, straight cutlasses, cassava cuttings, potato vines specifically
the Njala white variety, one bushel of groundnut supplied to each
beneficiary, 50kg of husk rice of different variety such as ROC 5, ROC 3,
and ROC 10.
The supply of these farming imputs, according to Mr. Mohamed T. Kamara,
preceded a training programme with two participants mostly women from each
of the 19 villages, who were trained in garri processing.
Following the training programme the 'AFS', according to the trainees,
supplied Start-up kits and other equipment to each section in the following
categories four (4) Garri grater machines; four (4) Shifters; Twelve (12)
rubber bowls, six four each section; Twelve (12) drums; thirty (30)kg
fertilizer for each beneficiary; and jute bags donated by CRS.
In a bid to recover the seed rice and groundnuts which the farmers are
required to repay for expansion to other sections on annual basis, the 'AFS'
formed a village Recovery Committee in the 19 villages with the mandate to
collect the harvested rice, which is 25kg for every 50kg husk rice.
To actualise this objective, 'AFS' constructed four drying floors and
two stones in each section to enhance storage facilities.

Original_Search_Title: Stakeholders Decry Quality of Education At NASU Summit
Headline: Nigeria: Stakeholders Decry Quality of Education At NASU Summit
Body_Text:
The third triennial national delegates conference of the Non-Academic
Staff Union of Educational and Associated Institutions (NASU) was held in
Abuja last week, and provided an opportunity for stakeholders, such as NASU,
ASUU and the NLC to comment on the state of education especially in the
universities. They also offered suggestions on what should be done.
In his welcome address to delegates and guests which included the
president of the Academic Staff Union of Universities ( ASUU), President of
NASU, Comrade Ivor Takor said the union has not relented in its efforts to
get NASU represented on the boards of Universal Basic Education Commission,
Governing Councils and boards of educational, research, teaching and
specialist hospitals. It also wants to be represented on the boards of
libraries and special projects.
Beyond representation, he disputed official claims that the bulk of
government revenue has been committed to education. Takor told his audience:
" I dispute this and hasten to say that these institutions are in a
state of coma as salaries are no longer being paid as at when due and the
infrastructure are completely ruined." He suggested that government should
review upwards the funding of universities in the interest of the public and
youths.
The university autonomy bill was also mentioned by Takor who said, " it
does appear to us that government in an attempt to shirk responsibilities of
educating its citizenry has opted into the experimentation of granting
amorphous autonomy to the universities with the power of life and death
placed at the disposal of the chief executive and the councils".
His view was shared by the president of ASUU, Dr. Dipo Fahina. Fashina
who registered the first appearance of ASUU at NASU's conference said,
"education is being deregulated. Children of workers and the
under-priviledged are being driven out of the system," and added that "the
attempt to castrate university workers though the autonomy bill and
introduce a primitive system which does not operate anywhere in the world
would fail."He pledged to facilitate a dialogue between ASUU and NASU in
order to deepen solidarity in the educational system. Takor agreed with
Fashina when he said that the autonomy bill is "aimed at commercialising
education for internal revenue generation," but warned that "bad laws cannot
prevail in a democratic setting especially when it is set to trap the
down-trodden."
On wages in the educational sector, Takor remarked that, "the education
sector has never had it this bad as salaries, pensions and allowances are no
longer paid as at when due in all the education industries." Besides, he
said, "no one talks about the rehabilitation of the dilapidated
infrastructure or the replacement of obsolete equipment. The entire system
is in decay," he lamented.
Warning that education may soon be priced out the reach of many
Nigerians, he suggested that government should "as a matter of urgency
moderate the activities of the private universities and ensure that the
public is not unduly exploited."
NLC President, Comrade Adams Oshiomhole who was a guest of NASU said the
crisis in the nation's educational sector arose from misplacement of
priority by political leaders about what makes a country great and warned
that the country is doomed if government does not invest properly in the
educational sector. He called on President Obasanjo to change his negative
attitude to education in the interest of the nation.His words: "Comrades, I
was very happy when I heard from the President of the Academic Staff Union
of University (ASUU), that ASUU and NASU must talk and work together, not
for your own benefit, but for the benefit of our country. When our colleague
from Ghana talked about a knowledge-driven world, he cannot be more
correct. Nigeria cannot grow beyond the capacity of its citizens. When
people talk about this country being endowed with abundant human resources,
that is nice and they are talking about the quantity.
But comrades, what makes the difference is not the quantity, but the
quality. That quality is not something that can be decreed with the power of
miracles or magic. If you do not invest in your future, you cannot expect to
reap anything in the future. The way to invest in the future is to invest in
the education of our youths, proper funding of our educational sector from
primary to the university levels".
Reflecting on his educational opportunities, he said, "The reason I
remain personally grateful to the late Chief Obafemi Awolowo, and I will
always remember him, is because coming from where I come from and being born
to the parents that I was born to, I could have been languishing on a
cassava farm because my late father was not in a position to pay primary
school fees.
But comrades, I am not the only one. Even those who have been presidents
or heads of states of our country, ministers, governors, and others, could
not have gone to school if it was left to the market forces. They went to
school because we had governments that appreciated that the way of the
future was to invest in education. The shame, comrades, is that today many
of our leaders are destroying the ladder with which they rose to
power. There can be no basis for any upward mobility if people have no
access to quality education. So, I believe the unity ASUU and NASU need in
the education sector is necessary so that working together, we can prevail
on government at all levels and those in government to keep the ladder that
they themselves used.
The NLC job will be easier if we can, with all our affiliates ensure
that we mobilize and put sufficient pressure on our government to properly
fund our education".
He said: "The truth is that the crisis afflicting our educational sector
flows directly from the poverty of priority at the level of federal, state
and local government. How can we have a country where there is so much to be
used for 'play-play' (sports), but not so much to be used for
education. They call it sports. We ordinary people call it 'play-play'. When
we assembled the entire continent to come and do two weeks of play-play and
we spent over N100 billion, we can celebrate that we have the most
ultra-modern stadium in Abuja, I wish any of our politicians and political
leaders or government officials can say that we have one university that is
ultra-modern. Can you now see glaringly the crisis of priority. That
somebody thinks that our pride as a nation should be in terms of having the
most modern stadium, but cannot boast of the most modern university, most
modern research institution, and yet the future of the world dictates that
if your research is not properly funded, if your people are not properly
skilled, they don't have the capacity to change jobs. They are not
multi-skilled, they cannot benefit from training and re-training . In a
world that is changing constantly, there can be no future for such a
society"."It is a matter of our own enlightened self-interest that we must
continue whenever we have the opportunity to say to government that they
must go back and think again about the right priority for our nation.
For us in the labour movement it is clear that the most important
priority, first and foremost, is education. So, I hope that working with
you, we will have to ensure that the president of the federal republic
re-visits his general appreciation of the place of education in the future
of our nation".

Original_Search_Title: Food Shortage Rocks Moyo District Prison
Headline: Uganda: Food Shortage Rocks Moyo District Prison
Body_Text:
Kampala — MOYO district prison is facing acute food shortage.
The 98 inmates at the facility have been feeding on boiled cassava for
the last two weeks, the district prisons commander, Abdul Aziz, said on
Monday.
Aziz attributed the food shortage to the unfavourable weather in August
and September.
He said they lost crops and livestock to heavy rains in August, which
resulted in flooding.
Aziz said the situation was compounded by the drought in September which
withered crops that survived the August floods.
The district Police commander, Albert Wacha, asked the prison
authorities to seek assistance before the situation got out of hand. He said
inmates were entitled to proper feeding.
Wacha blamed the district local government for neglecting
prisoners.
"It is sad that the district council does not cater for the prisoners
yet the produce from the prison farm generates income for the district,"
Wacha said.
The district secretary for finance, Denis Illi, blamed the prison
commander for waiting for the situation to reach crisis proportions before
alerting them.
"It's sad the prisoners are suffering because the the prison
administration delayed to alert us. We have funds to cater for the
prisoners," Illi said.

Original_Search_Title: Senator Ogunlewe, South Eastern Roads, Ogbaru Road (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Senator Ogunlewe, South Eastern Roads, Ogbaru Road (2)
Body_Text:
The Works minister seems as one willing to dare where others have
failed.
IF I may ask where are the engineering representatives of government,
charged with the supervision of roads found, if not in the Ministry of
Works. And we are told that out of the 65 directors, 45 are from the South
East. The Minister must have been under some kind of pressure to mitigate
his earlier proclamation but the truth does not hide. Even as he was trying
to placate certain interest groups with his retraction, he was also quoted
during his working tour of the federal infrastructure and roads in Enugu
State, as calling on the people to form vigilante groups to monitor the
execution of their roads. In very clear language he warned the "Igbos to
desist from relying on their kinsmen occupying top positions in the
ministry" saying they are not representing them efficiently.
Senator Ogunlewe should stop his futile attempt at covering up the
obvious. Even before he made public his finding, we, the masses, already
know that the deplorable state of our roads might not have been unconnected
with the hanky-panky between top Ministry technocrats and dishonest
construction firms.
Ogbaru road
This brings me to the pathetic case of Ogbaru road which has been a
victim of fake certification of work completion and ungodly neglect by the
state authorities for decades.
When the Federal government announced the approval of N600 million to
rehabilitate the major roads and bypasses in the South East, we expected
Ogbaru road to be included. Apart from the fact that Ogbaru road was
formally adopted as a federal road by the immediate past Minister of Works,
Chief Anthony Anenih, the road is a strategic bypass into Onitsha -- Owerri
road (through Ekwusigo, Ozubulu) and Rivers State through Ndoni. So, for the
Federal government to have thought about rehabilitating bypasses in the
South East without Ogbaru road must have been a gross mistake.
As soon as you cross the Niger bridge into Anambra State, Ogbaru road,
to your right, is the first major road bypassing Onitsha and its nightmarish
traffic situation. The road immediately presents a convenient short-cut for
those who have no business entering Onitsha town or travelling to Enugu or
Anam/Aguleri axis. The road runs through to Atani, the headquarters of
Ogbaru Local Government Area, then Osamala an Ogwu Ikpelle from where it
crosses into Rivers State at Ndoni.
From Atani, Ogbaru road is connected to Onitsha-Owerri road at Ekwusigo
Ozubulu, through a link road built by the colonialist Royal Nigeria Company
for evacuating cash produce through their port on the Niger bank. The road
is now completely in ruins from many years of neglect and effects of
erosion.
The advantage of this road to Owerri bound travellers is that they can
go through Atani/Ekwusigo road and continue their journey to Owerri without
being bugged down with Onitsha traffic. But apart from this exit, the big
take on the Ogbaru road is the passage it provides into Rivers State through
Ndoni to which, but for the accident of geography, is basically an Ogbaru
community. In effect, the larger implication of a rehabilitated or
reconstructed Ogbaru road is that it will provide another good short-cut
into Rivers State without bothering to go through Imo or Abia states.
Though government has named Oba, Nnewi, Uga, Orlu roads to be the
beneficiaries of this initial N600 million vote for the rehabilitation of
South Eastern roads, the Ogbaru-Ndoni and Atani-Ekwusigo roads deserve to be
immediately reconstructed if the benefit of government's intervention on
South Eastern roads would be maximized. Any road networking that does not
take into consideration the Ogbaru bypass is bound to continue to put
vehicular pressure on the rehabilitated roads.
Constructed 32 years ago by the venerable Ajie Ukpabi Asika at the end
of the civil war, Ogbaru road was meant to create access into Ogbaru
hinterland, fondly referred to, as the state's food basket because of its
rich soil which grows some of the best species of yam/cassava tuber,
vegetables, fruits and abundant fish due to its close proximity to the Niger
river.
The road was also supposed to be forerunner of what the oldman hoped
would eventually become a strategic link between East Central State and
Rivers State through Ndoni who are culturally affiliated to Ogbaru.
No sooner than he left office, maintenance of the road became a subject
of buck passing and game playing by subsequent state governments.
A number of times we were told by impeccable sources that funds were
budgeted to rehabilitate the road but all we saw on each of these occasions
were a few tippers of laterite that were washed off with the first couple of
rains. At a time our people were compelled to make representation to the
government, but they were told that the book have it that Ogbaru road had
been constructed.
This was the era of the military when people were not expected to ask
too many questions. So our emissaries picked up their walking sticks in
disbelief, thanked their hosts, and left.
At a stage, when there was no lie to tell again, the state shrugged the
responsibility of the road off its shoulders claiming it is a federal
responsibility because of its inter-state nature. It was at this point that
the road was abandoned and it gradually disintegrated out of existence. All
that is now left of this model road is nothing but what looks like an
overseized bush path with vestiges coal tar here-and-there.
Miles back from civilisation Today, the road represents miles back from
civilisation and the tragedy of it is that the abundant food harvested in
Ogbaru hinterlands can no longer be evacuated easily to the urban centres
because commercial pick-ups cannot venture into the hinterland for obvious
reasons. Farmers now resort to evacuating their farm produce in canoes,
through the Niger River. A number of times, these canoes have capsized due
to inclement tide resulting in huge losses, and where they are landed
successfully, the farm produce are sold at aggravated costs.
What looked like a respite came at the turn of our new democratic
dispensation. Following the unrelenting lobbying by our Ogbaru Federal
Constituency representative, Hon. Okwudili Uzoka, the former Minister of
Works, Chief Anenih, in a widely publicised move, gave his Ministry's
approval to take over the Ogbaru - Ndoni road. He assured our law maker that
Ogbaru road will be amongst those to be reconstructed in the 2001 fiscal
year.
The news sparked off jubilation prompting our leaders to write a letter
of appreciation to the former Minister. We waited in vain. His tenure ran
out and we didn't see even a tipper of asphalt deposited on the road in the
name of Federal Ministry of Works. But we are not blaming him. Perhaps his
soul was willing but his budget was weak.
Senator Adeseye Ogunlewe has taken the bull by the horn. He appears set
to bring succour to the long suffering South Easterners. He came across as a
man that is set to right the decades of wrong. One willing to dare where
others have failed. And for this, he will always have our support and
prayers.

Original_Search_Title: Defector Cautions Against Voting for the National Democratic Congress
Headline: Ghana: I Planned And Executed the Beating of the Tv3 Crew - Ndc Defector
Body_Text:
New Akrade — Sheikh Awal Larry, who defected from the National Democratic Congress
(NDC) to the New Patriotic Party (NPP), has advised Ghanaians not to make
any mistakes by voting the NDC back to power in the 2004 general
elections.
According to him, if some Ghanaians claim that they are hungry under the
present government then they will starve to death should they bring the NDC
back into power because it has no vision and policy with which to govern the
country.
Sheikh Awal Larry gave this advice at the monthly district rally
organised by the NPP at New Akrade in the Asuogyaman district over the
weekend.
He reiterated that the NDC supporters who were clubbed at the congress
at Legon for supporting Dr. Kwesi Botchwey, was ordered by Mr. E. T. Mensah,
MP for Prampram and he is challenging him to take him to the courts.
Also he confessed that he planned and executed the beating of the TV3
crew at Nungua and other journalists in the country during the 2000 general
elections.
These revelations or confessions by the NDC defector stunned some of the
NPP supporters who were hearing them for the first time.
Awal Larry observed that he was blindly following "the wicked" but
President J. A. Kufuor has saved him from them, likewise his other
colleagues.
He called on all Ghanaians to maintain the NPP in power for it continue
its good works saying "President J. A. Kufuor is the best president I have
so far come across who should be allowed to continue his good works."
The Eastern Regional Minister, Dr. Francis Osafo-Mensah, cautioned
supporters against complacency and laziness in carrying out their party
activities to ensure wins come the next elections.
He held that if the party supporters prevent rigging there would be no
way the NDC would win the elections as they are also losing more and more of
their supporters.
The general secretary of the NPP, Dan Botwe, stated that the NPP
believes in free and fair elections hence it will not use its incumbency to
win the next elections.
"As far as the NPP will cooperate with them, it would not allow any
brutalities and rigging on the part of any opposition party. If they promise
to cooperate, we will cooperate with them but if they promise confrontation,
we will also confront them," he asserted.
Mr. Botwe said with hard work from the President and his team of
administrators, he is sure that Ghanaians are going to entrust in his hands
the ruling of this country for another four years. The district chief
executive (DCE) for Asuogyaman, Dr. Godfried Kofi Siaw, announced that since
the government came to power it has constructed 11 roads, 14 schools and
some boreholes in the district.
Again, he announced that under this government's the poverty alleviation
programme the district assembly has disbursed Â¢686 million to residents in
the area, cultivated 140 acres of cassava, while 13 villages and towns have
been connected to the national electricity grid.
The MP-elect, Mr. Kofi Osei, reminded residents of the numerous
unfulfilled promises made by the NDC and charged them to vote him to
represent them in Parliament to bring more development to the area.
He advised them to turn up in their numbers when the voter's register is
open next January and register to enable them cast their votes during the
elections.
Earlier on, the Asuogyaman constituency branch of the NDC stunned the
NPP supporters when they announced their presence at the rally grounds. Some
of the supporters were contemplating whether they were going to cause
trouble.
But they cleared the air by saying that they out of their own volition
decided to attend the rally.
This really demonstrated that the NPP has opened its doors to receive
everybody by accommodating the NDC at its rally at Akrade for the first
time.

Original_Search_Title: Former Vice Chancellor Requests Domestic Manufacture of Drugs
Headline: Ghana: Former Cape Vars Head Calls for Psi On Drug Manufacturing
Body_Text:
Takoradi — A former pro vice chancellor of the University of Cape Coast (UCC) and
once head of the chemistry department, Prof. B.A.Dadson has called on the
Kufuor government to come out with a presidential special initiative on the
manufacture of drugs in the country to stem the ever-increasing capital
flight from the country.
He said every year, the government of Ghana, corporate bodies and
individuals collectively spend trillions of cedis to import drugs into the
country.
According to him, this practice could have easily been averted in order
to build a strong foreign exchange reserve for the country if the Ghanaian
chemist was given a slight capital push as the government has done to the
Ayensu Cassava company to go into the production of drugs since chemists in
the country have the expertise to go into the area.
Prof. Dadson was speaking at the 14th annual conference of the Ghana
Chemical Society, which took place in Takoradi last weekend under the theme,
the role of the chemist in the presidential special initiatives.
Topics like the significance of salt production in the development of
Ghana, a practical management tool for the quality control of products being
manufactured under the PSI and the determination of the state of pollution
in the coastal environment in Ghana, using rain water as an indicator, were
treated by the various speakers who were mostly university professors at the
two day conference.
Professor Dadson noted that because chemists in the country lacked the
resources to go into the large-scale production of drugs, Ghana was being
compelled to import drugs from a fellow developing country like India,
thereby creating jobs for the jobless people in that country at the time
that Ghanaians were also facing similar problem at home.
The university lecturer said even some of the few chemists in the
country have the expertise to manufacture agricultural input like fertilizer
that would have helped our farmers to come out with good yields of food to
feed this country without having to rely on food import but they are unable
to do so because of the same headache- lack of resources.
Prof. Dadson further said what has even appeared to have worsened the
situation was the lack of well-equipped laboratories at the country's
universities to enable lecturers and students studying to become chemists to
go into proper research and come out with quality products that would help
in the advancement of this country.
The former pro vice chancellor noted that, despite all these
shortcomings, more students have now started showing interest in the course
at our universities. This, he continued, meant that very soon, Ghana would
be producing more chemists in the various fields.
He, therefore, called on the government to think carefully about his
suggestion and try to come out with the PSI on drugs manufacture to help
meet the health needs of Ghanaians at less cost, adding that chemists in the
country have the expertise to go into the manufacture of most of the drugs
needed in Ghana.
The government must not lose sight of the fact that economists only
analyse the nature of the economy.
The scientists and engineers do the actual building of every economy, so
the government must pay special attention to them, he advised.
The Minister of State at the Ministry of Trade, Industry and PSI,
Hon. Ishmael Ashitey in his keynote address also noted that the global
economy was being driven by technology and knowledge and that countries,
which have invested in them, are enjoying better standards of living and
those who have not, are condemned to poverty.
We need our chemists therefore to contribute their quota and to liaise
with scientific disciplines to put the economy of Ghana on the accelerated
growth path, he said and added that the chemist and other scientists,
researchers, technologists have all contributed to the industry and the
economy in diverse ways but still, there was room for improvement.
The minister tasked the chemical society to popularize chemistry so that
the country could produce the critical mass of chemists and chemical
engineers to support the PSI and industry in general.
Hon. Ashitey further told the conference that the PSI on salt for
example, has a tremendous potential for a viable chemical industry in Ghana
since the country virtually imports all her basic chemicals at great cost to
the nation.
He called on the chemists to help the government break new grounds in
producing chemicals like caustic soda, chlorine among others for both our
domestic and export markets.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Rising in Drought-Hit Tanzania
Headline: Tanzania: Inflation Rising in Drought-Hit Tanzania
Body_Text:
Nairobi — AS THE government tackles the food shortage that has hit many parts of
the country, the Bank of Tanzania (BoT) is concerned that poor harvests of
food crops will create inflationary pressure for the remainder of the
year.
The inflation rate stands at 4.5 per cent, but pressure started mounting
since July due to low supply, particularly of grains, following a prolonged
drought in most parts of the country.
The annual food inflation rate increased by 0.3 per cent in August to
reach 4.8 per cent compared with July's rate of 4.5 per cent. In June, it
was 4.2 per cent.
According to the BoT Monthly Economic Review for September 2003, the
national average wholesale price for Tanzania's staple food, maize,
increased to Tsh17,215 ($16) per 100-kg bag in August, up from Tsh16,350
($15.3) in July. The highest price was Tsh25,714, ($24.1) recorded in Mara
region on the eastern shores of Lake Victoria.
However, some food items - meat, fish, wheatflour, fruit, bananas,
cassava, and legumes - recorded a fall in prices.
The country's Food Security Information Team (FSIT) warned in its
October report that: "The availability of food staples, maize and rice, and
the outlook for food prices are not promising unless the government and the
private sector inject new supplies into the markets soon."
The FSIT further warned that the food crop prices increases were
jeopardising poor households' consumption in both the rural and urban
areas.
In mid-August, the government appealed to donors to contribute
approximately 45,000 tonnes of food crops to meet part of the 77,500 tonnes
that was required immediately to feed nearly half of Tanzania's 34 million
people who face famine. Another 350,000 tonnes is needed to cater for the
starving population hit by drought from now to March next year.
In order to meet the food deficit, Prime Minister Frederick Sumaye
announced that the Tanzania government was planning to sell 32,000 tonnes of
maize from its Strategic Grain Reserve (SGR) at highly subsidised prices in
imminent danger of famine. Additional donor assistance is being sought to
cover the costs of transportation.

Original_Search_Title: Benue Hosts All Farmers Summit Dec 16
Headline: Nigeria: Benue Hosts All Farmers Summit Dec 16
Body_Text:
Makurdi — All Farmers Summit (AFS) is to hold in Benue State next Tuesday. The
summit is to address issues of how to beef up agricultural production both
for domestic needs and as a viable foreign exchange earner.
Special Adviser to the Benue State Governor on Agricultural Development
and Food Security, Hon Amile Gyuve, who disclosed this in an interview with
THISDAY, said the summit would enlighten the farmers on the governments'
policy thrust and how best they could utilise the policy initiatives for
optimum results.
One component of the summit, according to Hon Gyuve, would be an
interactive session between the farmers and the Benue State Governor, Mr
George Akume. "They would meet face to face with the governor to state their
problems and difficulties in agriculture production." the Special Adviser
said.
Although the attention would be on all agricultural products, government
priority interest on the production of cassava for export is to form the
primary agenda for the summit.
The Special Assistant also hinted that the state government has
established the required diplomatic initiatives and the first team of
industrialists are expected to arrive the state before the end of the
year.

Original_Search_Title: Harnessing Potentials of Nigeria's Air, Sea Ports
Headline: Nigeria: Harnessing Potentials of Nigeria's Air, Sea Ports
Body_Text:
Benin City — Nigeria is said to be loosing huge money in terms of excise and custom
duties due to im-proper enforcement of laws in the country even as the Edo
State Command of the Nigeria Customs Service, said it has generated the sum
of N1.7 billion betweenJanuary and October this year.
Speaking exclusively with the Weekly Trust at the Benin Airport,
Chairman of the Nigeria Economic and Financial Partners, a Non-Governmental
Organization (NGO) responsible for wooing foreign investors to the Niger
Delta, Dr. Patricia Opori said if the Nigerian borders, seaports and
airports are properly manned by the customs, the present revenue generated
by them will increase by not less than 150 per cent.
"Let me give you an example with Benin City and Port-Harcourt. When you
go to any of these cities, you realize that about 90 per cent of the fridges
you find in the markets are the imported second hand fridges that are
classified as contraband. Go to the offices of the big men who uses native
dresses, you discover that they are using the imported guinea brocade which
are also classified as contraband."
"The case of second hand vehicles is the worst. About 70 per cent of the
imported tokunbo (second hand), cars you see in the warehouses were
manufactured more than ten years ago. And I tell you one thing, all the
custom papers they give you as well as the bank tellers for the payment of
customs duty into the bank, the evidence of shipment are all fake. They have
all the required custom papers, stamps and everything in their
warehouses. When you buy a car, they go in, prepare those fake documents and
give you".
Dr. Opori said her organization consisted mainly of Nigerians with
business connections abroad who have investigated the issue and confirmed
the dubious nature of the Nigerian Tokunbo motor dealers, adding that, "of
the five vehicles bought by our members from different dealers in Benin City
and two from Port-Harcourt, none has genuine documents. Even the bank
tellers were forged."
She said if the customs can effectively man the entry points and
possibly raid places believed to be housing contraband goods, confiscate
them and punish both the smugglers, the officers who aided them to smuggle
the goods, the buyers and harbourers of the goods, customs generated revenue
will appreciate more and Nigerian local industries will be encouraged to
increase production.
She said when all means to import vehicles of more than ten years old
into the country are blocked; the importers will be left with no option but
to import those within the specifications that can be brought in through the
authorised routes thereby generating more income.
In his own contribution, a major transporter in the state and an
official of the Transport Owners Association, Deacon Bright Osasume, said in
the past, when customs officers were manning roadblocks at strategic
locations in the state, illegally acquired guns and other contraband goods
were not in mass circulation in the state.
"No transporter then will carry your goods without knowing the contents
and what the law is saying about those goods. Since the customs stopped
mounting roadblocks in Edo State, every person is now free to move anything
from Lagos to Onitsha, Warri, Port Harcourt or even Lagos to Abuja and other
parts of the East and South-South. Edo has now turned to a dumping ground of
all those things, you know you must pass through Benin to all those places."
He blamed government for dismantling the customs checkpoints in the
state which he said has given room for the massive movement of contraband
and other prohibited goods into the state which he said is responsible for
the high rate of crime in the area.
When Weekly Trust visited the major business areas in Benin City, only
Uyi Technical Company and two others were seen selling new refrigerators
with about five new compressors said to be in stock for several months
without patronage as against the numerous stores that sell second hand
fridges and compressors in the country.
A store attendant where second hand fridges, compressors, televisions,
computers and other electronics are being sold, Mrs. Franca Ezeala, told
this reporter that they sell an average of ten fridges a week and that they
sell to even local and state governments offices as well as corporate
bodies, companies and individuals.
However, at number 98C Mission Road, where new fridges are sold, Mr. Ken
Omono, the store attendant told this reporter that the last time they sold
new fridges was before the April general elections when they supplied two to
the state government. He attributed the poor sales to the massive stock of
tokunbo fridges and the poor economic state of Nigerians.
An economists in a private firm, ALSFAT Finances, Mr. Sunday Dimowo said
the major mistake government did in it's bid to curtail the entry and
circulation of contraband goods was for allowing government agencies and
officials to patronize the contraband goods themselves. "Tell me right from
the president to the governors, senators to even councillors, ministers,
commissioners and chairmen of local government, who is not using the
imported guinea brocade? Who among them is using the brocade manufactured in
Zaria or Kano? Look at their wives, who among them is using locally made
fabrics? Go to their dinning table, what you see there are imported
biscuits. The government is now buying tokunbo vehicles as official
cars. Then why should the poor man be punished for doing the same?"
Mr. Dimowo warned that the government should put it's house in order by
cleansing itself before attacking poor traders in the market whom he said,
use their life savings in buying the goods that the customs arrest and
destroy or auction.
The National President of Motor Dealers Association of Nigeria (MODAN),
Elder Benjamin Ughumu, said the issue of tokunbo vehicles should not be an
issue in this country at all, adding that "the customs have sealed up the
border against tokunbo cars and all the items they listed as contraband. You
cannot bring in a vehicle to this country now except through the seaport. If
there is anybody that brings in a vehicle illegally into Nigeria, that may
be the smugglers or customs officials. We are not smugglers."
He said it will amount to a violation of his members rights to free
trade, if the customs decided to raid their warehouses and impound any
vehicle that passed through customs into the country.
"There is no bush path or illegal route you can enter Nigeria with a
vehicle and not a motorcycle without meeting the customs. If they allow
smugglers to bring in vehicles illegally, they must not come and disturb
innocent dealers thereafter."
On the issue of motor dealers forging customs clearance papers, he said
his association does not support illegality. "I doubt if there is anything
like that happening. But if anybody is arrested for forgery and it is
established, I am sorry, MODAN may not have anything to do about that. The
person should clear himself." The Managing Director of a multi-national oil
palm producing company in Edo State, Presco Oil, and member of the
Presidential Committee on Oil Palm Development, Mr. Phil Vandebeeck, told
newsmen that the N50 billion the federal government intend to release was
for enhancement of local production of vegetable oil.
He pointed out that the oil palm producing companies in the country have
the capacity to produce enough oil for both local consumption and export. He
said Presco, which produces 50 tonnes of oil per day, is now increasing its
production to 70 tonnes per day.
He said there is no need for the importation of vegetable oil from any
country.
When contacted for comments, the Customs Area Comptroller of the state,
Dr. Hussaini Suleiman Kangiwa, said the issue of manning the country's
border by security operatives is very difficult due to the porous nature of
the border and the large size of the country.
"When people travel to border towns like Banki/Amchide in Borno State
where the two towns of Banki in Nigeria and Amchide in Cameroun have merged
together, they will understand what we are saying. There are hotels, houses
and shops built along the borderline. You fine such houses or hotels with
two doors, one in Nigeria and the other in Cameroun. When the Nigeria
authorities say no movement or business after 12 midnight, the hotels close
the gates leading to Nigeria and open the Cameroun gates and business
continues and vice-versa. How do you man such places when the people are one
and they eat from the same pot?"
He said the customs doesn't enact laws but they only enforce laws
relevant to their duties, adding, "every item listed as contraband, has a
reason why its importation is banned. The gas in those used compressors is
harmful to human health. Used Cars of over eight years are very weak and
old. At the slightest accident, you find that they are completely damaged
and that affects the passengers in the vehicle. With all the cooking oil we
produce in this country and the surplus bottled water and biscuits, what is
the need of importing other ones. We should not allow Nigeria to be turned
into a dumping ground for all sub-standard and over used products. We should
be seen to be exporting all these things".
He listed the contraband goods as rethreaded used tyres, cars of more
than eight years, gaming machines, cement in bags, vegetable oil in bulks,
used refrigerators, used air conditioners, used compressors, fabrics and
textile materials.
He mentioned other goods as poultry products, cassava and cassava
products, tooth pick in any form, table drinking water, biscuits, spaghetti,
fruit juice, paints, sugar confectionaries, exercise books, envelopes, beer
and toilet rolls.
He also warned against the exportation of maize, timber, raw hides and
skins, scrap metals, unprocessed rubber latex and humps, Artifacts and
antiquities as well as wild animals like crocodile, elephants, lizards,
eagle, monkey, zebra and lions are all prohibited.
He said most of the local industries stopped production because the
markets for their local products, which he described as goods for the
Nigerian environment, have been taken over by "unnecessary imported goods
that are mostly substandard and not good for both our economy and the
environment." He advised used motor dealers to ensure that they buy and sell
only vehicles that passed through the approved entry ports, with authentic
import and customs clearance documents and advised that the vehicles must be
within the approved specification of not more than eight years old for
cars.
"The law is there and there is just no compromise. It is left for the
dealers or smugglers or whatever they call themselves, to operate within the
ambit of the laws or have themselves to blame. Nobody will harass anyone
that does his or her business as specified by the law. When you do anything
different, it is your own business and you are doing that at your own risk
and at your own cost."
For those dealing in contraband goods, he adviced them to keep away from
such businesses and find other things doing before the law catches up with
them. "We can only advise, warn and guide before one is arrested. When you
are arrested, you must be punished according to the law and that is all."
"Look, smuggling activity alone or the illegal importation of contraband is
enough to ruin the economy of a country no matter how rich it may be. These
smugglers use the much needed hard currency to go and bring those
things. They bring them into the country illegally without the government
gaining a kobo from it. When they bring those goods, they deprive the local
manufacturers of similar goods from selling theirs. These local
manufacturers who pay tax to government, employ Nigerians to work for them
and help in developing the economy are now forced to close business." He
said that government's decision to fish out these illegally smuggled
contraband goods even from the hinterland is in the interest of both the
economy and security of the nation. "The effect of this illegal smuggling of
contraband into the country may be partially responsible for the poor
economic situation in the country since a lot of factories have been forced
to close down. Nigerians should expose them and their warehouses to the
authorities."

Original_Search_Title: Abuja Commodity Exchange Begins Stakeholders Meetings
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja Commodity Exchange Begins Stakeholders Meetings
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The management of the Abuja Securities and Commodity Exchange, ASCE,
will soon visit relevant stakeholders in Kaduna, Ibadan and Akure in
pursuance of its programme of strategy interactive meetings across the
country.
A statement signed by the spokesman of the exchange, Chris Echikwu, said
the ASCE management team, which will be led by its managing director, Mallam
Yusuf Abdurrahim, will meet with the members of the National Cotton
Association of Nigeria in Kaduna and the Cocoa Association of Nigeria in
Akure.
"During the inter active sessions, the produce farmers and merchants
will be intimated with the salutary objectives of the federal government's
decision to establish a commodity exchange in Nigeria as well as the
benefits that will accrue to them by its operations," the statement
added.
The commodity producers will also be expected to highlight the problems
militating against their operations, including unfavourable government
policies as well as the way forward in arresting identified problems.
The ASCE team will also visit the Cocoa Research Institute of Nigeria
and the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), both in
Ibadan, with a view to harnessing their inputs into solving the problems
facing the production of some agricultural commodities in the country. The
agro-produce to be assessed during the visit includes Cocoa, Tea, Coffee,
Cassava and Soya-beans, among others.
The next phase of the stakeholders meetings, which will commence next
year, will cover other commodities including Bitumen, Barytes, Kaolin and
Palm kernel, it said.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers of Ghana, 'Ayekoo'
Headline: Ghana: Farmers of Ghana, 'Ayekoo'
Body_Text:
AS THE country celebrates this year's Farmers Day, The Chronicle doffs
its hat to all the long-suffering farmers of Ghana in recognition of the
immense sacrifices they have made in feeding the nation as well as earning
us scarce foreign exchange.
We have them to thank for the recent stability in staple food prices
which has cushioned workers from the brunt of low wages and salaries and the
astronomical increases in tariffs for the utilities.
Indeed, had it not been for the abundance of food, many workers and
citizens would have been unable to eat even one square meal a day when
prices of fuel skyrocketed early this year.
Despite the numerous constraints they face: high farm labour costs,
expensive agricultural inputs, lifting of subsidies in the agricultural
sector and other limiting factors, farmers have continued to struggle on,
using the little available help they can get - and all this without a murmur
of complaint.
What The Chronicle is not happy about, however, is the way farmers are
taken for granted and the non-chalance that has attended their noble
efforts.
Take the cocoa sector for instance. When the NPP government, in its
wisdom, decided to embark on free spraying of insecticides in all
cocoa-producing areas, many citizens applauded them for a decision
well-taken. Here, at last, was a government that was apparently serious
about making the country regain its former status as the world's leading
cocoa producer.
And truly, we are gradually clawing back our lost world market share in
cocoa production. Projected figures for this season indicate that we will
harvest over 500,000 tonnes. With the political trouble in La Cote d'Ivoire,
it may even be possible for us to take the lead in cocoa production.
How then, one may ask, can the hard work of our farmers be brought to
nought because of bad planning by officials in the cocoa sector? The log-jam
of cocoa haulage trucks at the Tema harbour, and presumably at Takoradi as
well, unable to off-load bags of processed beans ready for export, does not
speak well of a nation desirous of regaining her lost image and sends the
wrong signals to our farmers.
Are these public servants saying that plans were not made to take care
of the projected increases in this year's harvest? If this much could not be
done, what about when other cash crops also see increased production?
The government has embarked on Presidential Special initiatives (PSI) to
increase production in cassava, cotton, salt, and others. Are these also to
founder on the lack of initiative and poor work ethics of urban dwellers
living comfortably on the sweat of our hard-pressed farmers?
Surely, farmers deserve better. If the government cannot subsidise
agricultural inputs (even while our so-called developmental partners in the
West continue to fund their farmers), then the least it could do is to
facilitate the work of farmers who toil in the blazing sun day in and day
out.
The Chronicle believes that giving yearly awards to the "Best Farmer" is
all well and good. However, it would be of far more benefit to the country
when every farmer's effort is recognised - not quantitatively but
qualitatively.
When each and every farmer realises that the "big people" in the cities
appreciate their worth and do so not only in words but in deeds, then they
will also continue to brave the odds to feed this nation.
Let our farmers realise that their work can give them happiness. Only
then will this country move forward.
"Ayekoo," farmers of Ghana! God bless you all.

Original_Search_Title: Sunflower Making Lives Brighter
Headline: Kenya: Sunflower Making Lives Brighter
Body_Text:
Nairobi — As a young boy growing up in Kotur village t in Western Province,
Patrick Emoto recalls, he and his peers had a lot of work to do, threshing
millet and sorghum after harvest-time. There were large plantations of
cassava, and potatoes and cassava as well, and cotton provided a dependable
source of income. The Teso, who inhabit the area, lived a fairly comfortable
life. Today, although millet, sorghum and cassava are still the grown in the
area, a lot has changed since Emoto's childhood.
Then, the cotton industry grew steadily weaker and eventually
collapsed. Unreliable rainfall and plagues that cleared livestock followed,
leading to severe malnutrition in the area. Then about two years ago, an
elderly mzungu (white man) entered the scene with flower seeds in one hand
and beans in the other," says Emoto.
"He explained that the beans were drought-resistant and more nutritious
than our local varieties. Besides, both the beans and the flowers were ready
sources of money, and he had a ready market. All we had to do was grow the
crops in large enough quantities.
Several months later, whole locations have taken up soya bean and
sunflower growing. The effects are remarkable: the people look a lot
healthier, and shopping centres that resembled ghost towns have stirred back
to life.
At the Emoto homestead behind Kotur market, we found children picking
seedsout of the compact sunflower heads. Inside his grass thatched house
stood sacks of soya beans.
"When you return, you will probably find that I have replaced this
grass-thatching on my roof with corrugated iron sheets, which I am planning
to buy with the receipts from my crop this season, " said Emoto with a lot
of hope.
"Thanks to Jim Cheatle and his Association for Better land Husbandry
(ABLH), I now dream of doing such a thing" says Emoto, who is in his early
40s.
To facilitate marketing, ABLH encourages farmers to organise themselves
into self-help groups or clusters known as farmers action associations
(FAA). Besides Tesoland, ABLH is active in Bungoma, Lugari, Kakamega and
Vihiga Districts, where farmers have seen their fortunes change through
intensive farming methods and collective marketing of their produce.
Forty-two- year-old Charles Muoshi, of Isiekuti Village in Vihiga
Districtfound relief in ABLH after wasting years on jobs that earned him a
pittance.
"They assessed the soil on my land soil and found it suitable for soya
and other beans. They recommended vegetables such as onions, pepper, egg
plants, spinach and leeks, among others, for the Indian market. I tried my
hand at it and have never looked back," he says.
To attract better prices, he was advised to practise organic
farming. Then to hone his skills, ABLH took him for a course at the Kenya
Institute of Organic Farming.
Today he is a leading member of the Isiekuti Organic Farming Youth Group
and makes an average of sh4,000 every month from the farm. Gone are the days
when he agonised over poorly yielding maize. "We use money from the sale of
the beans and vegetables to buy maize. Now I don't need to get a job," he
says.
When Eliazar Likhaya retired from the civil service after 40 years, he
returned tohis ancestral land close to the abandoned Rosterman Gold Mines
near Kakamega town.
"I planted maize for subsistence and kept a few cows for milk. But I had
no reliable source of income and the little that I had saved quickly fizzled
out. Life was not exciting - until ABLH declared our soil suitable for the
growing of soya beans.
"They came to us with a package that included supply of certified seed
on credit to be settled after harvest, along with a ready market for the
produce. I was among the first farmers to take up the offer," he says.
"Other farmers soon came forward and we formed Rosterman Farmers Action
Association. He has an acre under soya beans and is confident that the crop
will do well. "I made my debut last season with half an acre, and the
returns encouraged me to devote more land to the crop," he explains.
ABLH also initiated a food processing branch, Farmers Own company which,
unfortunately did not do well. that produced a variety of products ranging
from including snack bars, jam, honey and tonutria mchuzi mix- an
alternative to the popular Royco Mchuzi Mix. The Farmers Own Company churned
out produced healthy foods devoid of chemical additives, which made them
popular with consumers. In addition, the food processing company provided
farmers with an added value througha bonus derived fromdepending on the
number of cartons and sachets sold. This prompted the company to shift its
focus from production to to marketing for producer groups.
By so doing, says Cheatle, ABLH has managed to involve thousands of
peasants, drastically transforming their lives.
"Each producer's annual income level has risen from about Sh4,000 to at
least sh30,000," he says.
As a person who has experienced poverty, Cheatle can empathise with the
local people. "With my family going through a financial crisis, I left
school at 17 in search of a living and did anything that could give me an
income," he recalls.
After many years doing odd jobs, Cheatle joined a bank, where he says he
quickly developed a feel for numbers and honed his basic entrepreneurial
skills.
"Nine years later, with two small but successful businesses under my
belt and a little cash in the bank, I decided to further my education and
train as a teacher," he said.
He studied geography at Birmingham University and studying soil science
at the University of Aberdeen in Scotland.
The opportunity to work in Africa first came with an offer in 1970 to
lecture in Geography at the University of Malawi.
By 1980, Cheatle was head of Soil Survey Chemistry Laboratories in
Zambia.
"I worked on issues such as the utility of rock phosphate and soil
fertility maintenance in small-holder systems, learning the importance of
affordable and adoptable methods based based on organic recycling of manure
and compost.
He worked with the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute (KARI) on a
soils' research project before going into private consultancy, which
culminated in ABLH, which he helped found in 1994.

Original_Search_Title: Not Surprised More Ugandans Are Poor
Headline: Uganda: Not Surprised More Ugandans Are Poor
Body_Text:
Kampala — It is now official - the World Bank is admitting more Ugandans are
getting poorer as was reported in The East African issues of December 1 -
7.
This might be news to the bank, which has spent the last 15 years
trumpeting Uganda as 'Africa's success story' but not to the average Ugandan
whose living conditions hardly bear out the annual good news
statistics.
Years of experimenting with the IMF/World Bank's much criticised
policies (cut social spending, privatise and liberalise everything all the
while pumping in the loans) have yielded nothing.
This is hardly surprising because these policies have never succeeded in
lifting a single country out of poverty anywhere in the world. Countries
that have succeeded (mainly in Asia) have had to pick themselves up by their
own bootstraps.
The bank's Mr Suhashan Canagarajah told The East African last week that
there was need to understand why poverty was not falling yet the economy was
growing. Growing economy? Falling poverty?
Anybody who has been watching Uganda's economy since the Movement
government tossed aside its so-called "Ten Point Programme" and
whole-heartedly embraced the bank's policies can only shake his head in
bewilderment.
Despite the bold assurances that the economy has galloped full throttle
between the 1980s and mid 1990s during which time President Yoweri
Museveni's catchphrase was that Uganda's growth rate was second only to
China's, signs of stagnation were everywhere.
Kampala got dirtier and increasingly took on the tired look of a
ramshackled city every passing year. Sugar and salt, though plentiful, once
again became luxuries for many rural families.
The peasants in the countryside, experts of silent rebellion, quit all
pretence of growing the coffee and cotton that paid our import bill and the
salaries of civil servants to become petty merchants in local trading
centres - the exodus from the land had started.
Those who stayed on the land just grew their own food with a bit of
cassava and potatoes for the market to enable them buy necessities.
There is yet another reason why Canagarajah need not scratch his head to
understand why the ranks of the poor have swollen.
How could Ugandans have gotten richer when companies that employed
thousands were sold off for a song to the politically connected only for
their assets to be promptly stripped?
Remember the fate of Uganda Transport Company, Printpak, Foods and
Beverages, Uganda Hotels, Uganda Grain Millers, Uganda Co-operative
Transport Union etc.
The bank's supporters might point to the companies the nation has gotten
out of its liberalisation policy - MTN, Celtel and Tri-Star as
examples.
But slaughtering one's cow and buying another to replace it hardly
represents an increase in one's fortunes.
Yet others would argue, yes there are a couple or so of shiny new
buildings in Kampala and one might add there should be a lot more. After
all, 17 years is a long time.
The World Bank and Finance Ministry it seems have stubbornly refused to
acknowledge that much of the so-called economic growth that seems evident
from the increased number of second hand Japanese cars on Kampala roads and
the construction boom are the result of donor money that has flooded the
country in recent years to address the adverse effects of the bank's
policies, the incessant wars the government has indulged in and continues to
fight, and of course the Aids pandemic.
Talking about the wars, what the bank and the Ministry of Finance has
not bothered to do is calculate the actual cost of the war in northern
Uganda - now in its 17th year - to this economy in terms of lost lives and
output, direct expenses, impact on foreign investment and tourism. And one
must not forget the war in western Uganda and Congo.
In this mix, add the unprecedented and systematic corruption - a more
appropriate term perhaps should be state inspired looting - this nation has
witnessed.
(Uganda is perennially listed among the top ten most corrupt nations in
the world on Transparency International's corruption rating
index). Corruption and poverty are bedfellows - existence of one is a sign
of presence of the other.
Meaningful production, which is what it takes to lift any nation out of
poverty has never taken off during Museveni's rule, perhaps because there
has never been any original, systematic effort by government to revive
commercialised agriculture.
Whatever little has happened has been done by private business people
(for example Mukwano and simsim in Lango, and Aga Sekalala and vanilla in
Mukono), donors agencies like USaid and NGOs.
The Movement government and the World Bank have of course always been
quick to claim the accolades at the slightest sign of success.
Lately, the country is witnessing a disjointed- some might say
opportunistic- attempt to latch onto international poverty fighting
initiatives like the African Growth Opportunities Act (Agoa). Ugandans can
expect scanty salvation from these.
For example, Agoa as a serious poverty escape door for poor countries
has come in for considerable criticism from the experts - one of which is
the initiative's sustainability.
What needs to be done to make a serious dent in poverty in Uganda is not
yet another study looking into its causes.
But rather for Museveni's government to roll up its sleeves and come up
with an original strategy to deal with poverty and the will to execute it-
just like the Mahathirs of this world did done for their countries.
As The East African's Rasna Warah wrote this week, the World Bank like
any other bank at the end of the day is here primarily to make money for its
owners (the rich nations) rather than develop this impoverished
country.
The real sad news though is, if Uganda's growing poor cannot count on
the bank, they apparently cannot do so on this government either whose
energies at the moment seem to be devoted towards securing Museveni yet
another shot at the presidency.
Mr Musoke is a lecturer & communication consultant attached to
Makerere University's Mass Communication Department.

Original_Search_Title: Kufuor Targets $1,000 Per Capital Income in 10 Years
Headline: Ghana: Kufuor Targets $1,000 Per Capital Income in 10 Years
Body_Text:
Accra — The President, John Agyekum Kufuor has charged the Ghana Investor,
Advisory Council (GIAC) to work hard to bring up innovative ideas that would
eventually enable Ghana attain a per capital income of $1,000.
We need necessary domestic and foreign investment, which will enable us
achieve growth in the range of 8-8.5 % per annum, "to enable us attain that
per capital vision, the President told the Council members.
He gave the charge while addressing the fourth GIAC meeting at Akosombo
in the Eastern Region. While Foreign Direct Investments (FDI) has gone down
worldwide, investments into Ghana are going up slowly, but steadily the
President said.
The Ashanti/AngloGold merger has created the largest gold mining company
in the world and will bring $500 million into Ghana. Also Newmont, currently
the biggest gold mining company in the world is investing over $400 million
in three mines in Ghana.
Further, Goldfields Ghana Limited is investing over $160 million in
their Tarkwa and Daman mines.
That according to the President would raise Goldfield's current total
investment from $400 million to $560 million. The Ghana investment Promotion
Council (GIPC) has this year alone recorded 108 new investments, which are
altogether bringing in initial investment capital of $89.86 million as at
the end of the third quarter, the President disclosed.
That compares with 138 projects, which brought in a total of $65.3
million for the whole of 2002. Over the last two years government effort has
jumped cocoa output from 350,000 metric tones and expects it to reach
500,000 metric tones by the end of year.
That boost at expectation has been made possible by two factors,
President Kufuor told the Council.
These are the mass spraying of farms undertaken by government and the
increase in the producer price of cocoa, which currently stands at 67% of
the world market price. The President also stated the first export of
garments to the USA under the Africa Growth and Opportunities Act (AGOA)
adding that there has also been a trial export of cassava starch.
Both fall under the President's Special Initiative (PSI) set up by
government to promote job-creation, especially in the rural areas and also
increase export revenues.
President Kufuor also disclosed that government projected an overall
budget deficit of 2.7% of Gross Domestic Product but the deficit currently
stands at 0.6 of GDP as far and it is expected to go down further.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security 'Under Control'
Headline: Mozambique: Food Security 'Under Control'
Body_Text:
