ferries and boats on the river. Essentially the boats and ferries have to
establish routes within the river which are safer for their movements. These
cataracts however constitute serious threats to the boats and ferries which
themselves are always at the risk of being damaged in the water body due to
obsolete and over used engines.
Aside from these transportation difficulties, the absence of a bridge
across the river Benue at Ibi, has also impacted very negatively on
agricultural activities in the area. As was noted earlier, the people of Ibi
and neighbouring communities are large-scale cereal farmers and
fishermen. Large quantities of these agricultural produce are harvested each
farming season. However the major proportion of the products are either
wasted as a result of the lack of transportation facilities, or are sold out
at incredibly cheap prices to the few marketers who may summon the courage
to put their vehicles on the bad road, and bear the enormous cost of
ferrying the produce across the river or conveying them through the longer
routes to the markets. The vast and fertile arable land has also been left
to waste in greater proportion, as farmers are not encouraged by the
transportation problems. Also, as perishable agricultural products, fish are
sold at very cheap rates to prevent them from rotting.
One might wonder why since its construction in the 1970s, the Ibi
federal road has been left without a bridge across the river Benue. Daily
Trust gathered that there have been several proposals by successive
governments for the construction of the bridge. However, no government since
then has made any practical move to put the bridge in place. According to an
indigene of Ibi who spoke to Daily Trust, President Obasanjo had, during his
campaign for a second term, pledged that he was going to construct the
bridge "but it seems the president has forgotten his pledge."
The remains of the federal road itself is nothing to write home about,
as potholes have overtaken the major part of it. Daily Trust spoke to
Muhammed Nasir Waziri who is the chairman of the disciplinary committee of
the boat and ferry riders association in Ibi. According to him, the people
have been exposed to untold hardship due to the non-availability of any
bridge across the river Benue. "We have made several efforts to get the
government to sympathise with our plight, and put up a bridge here in Ibi,
but we have not succeeded."
According to Muhammed, some people hold the view that the people of Ibi
community are not interested in the construction of a bridge across the
river Benue, because of the commercial gains they are getting from ferry and
boat riding. He however refuted this claim, saying "We are not stupid as to
say that we are not aware of the gains that we can get if a bridge is
constructed here in Ibi. The community would receive a boost and we would be
able to have contacts and relationship with people from outside. So we are
very much in need of a bridge at Ibi here so as to ease the hardship we are
going through."
Daily Trust also spoke to one Muhammed Hussaini who is a commercial boat
operator. According to him, it is equally not true that the people of Ibi
have resisted attempts to construct a bridge across the river Benue. "People
are saying that we are performing ritual sacrifices to prevent the
government from taking any meaningful steps to construct a bridge in
Ibi. How can we be doing such things? When we know how we are suffering as a
result of the lack of a bridge here in Ibi," Hussain lamented, adding that
the people of Ibi are not only faced with the lack of a bridge, but that
they are also compelled to drink raw water directly from the river Benue as
a result of the damage which had since occurred at the water treatment plant
installed in the community by the government.
The story of the untold hardship being suffered by the people of Ibi and
their neighbours as a result of the lack of a bridge across the river Benue,
is as inexhaustible as the list of specific problems that emanated
thereof. It however remains a mystery as to why the Ibi federal road has
been abandoned at the very bank of the river Benue!

Original_Search_Title: Government to Review Kwara Chieftaincy Institution
Headline: Nigeria: Government to Review Kwara Chieftaincy Institution
Body_Text:
Lagos — A Committee to review the structure of the chieftaincy institution in
Kwara State is in the works.
Governor Bukola Saraki, while receiving the Emir of Ilorin, Alhaji Sulu
Gambari, in Council at the Government House, Ilorin yesterday said that the
exercise would be comprehensive and would address all grievances in the
State Council of Chiefs.
Saraki said he was aware that the last review was unjust and highly
politicised. "This administration is not interested in controlling the
affairs of traditional rulers". He told the Emir who had come with a large
entourage to pay the traditional Sallah homage to the Governor.
The Governor promised that Government would act as soon as the State
Council of Chiefs proffers its suggestions in the matter. He said the
government was no intention to interfere in the running of the Council, and
asked the royal fathers to educate Kwarans on the need to shun rumour
mongering and back-bitting.
Alhaji Gambari praised the administration for "taking Kwara out of the
woods", by restoring peace and harmony in the State.
"The first step you took in office testified that Kwara has entered a
new era with a new leadership", the Emir told Governor Saraki.
He pledged the unalloyed support of the Emirate and all Kwarans for the
policies and programmes of the State Government.
The Emir advised the Governor to enlarge the Back-to-farm scheme and
produce rice, yam and potato on a large scale.
The State Deputy Governor, Chief Joel Ogundeji; Chief Judge, Justice
Timothy Oyeyipo; Grand Khadi, Justice Muttalib Ambali; PDP State Chairman,
Alhaji Abubakar Zubair and Chief Imam of Ilorin, Alhaji Mohammed Bashir were
among dignitaries present on the occasion.

Original_Search_Title: Wildfire, Excessive Logging Eat Up 65 Sq Km of Nation's Forest Annually
Headline: Ghana: Wildfire, Excessive Logging Eat Up 65 Sq Km of Nation's Forest Annually
Body_Text:
Koforidua — Available statistics on the country's forest indicate that about 65
square kilometres of forests are lost annually, dueprimarily to wildfire,
poor agricultural practices and excessive logging.
The annual loss of timber, cash and food crops to bushfires alone is
currently estimated at 3% of Gross Domestic Product (GDP). These were
contained in a speech read on behalf of the vice president, Alhaji Aliu
Mahama, at the launching of government's plantation programme at Koforidua
on November 17.
According to the vice president, bushfires have therefore severely
reduced, not only the productive capacity of the forests but have also had
major impact on the other benefits of forests, including estimation of
premium timber species, loss of biodiversity, drying up of water bodies,
soil fertility and loss of tourist sites.
He observed, illegal logging by chainsaw operators and timer
concessionaires have led to huge loss of revenue to the state and excessive
environment degradation.
Consequently, the country is now in a very precarious situation that has
led to the shortage of timber for the wood processing industries and the
attendant threat to job security for workers in that sector, Chronicle
learnt.
"Apart from these concerns, illegal loggers have also recently hired
armed gangs to protect them while they undertake their nefarious activities"
he noted.
Alhaji Aliu Mahama, announced that the government was pursuing the
programme to establish about 100,000 ha of plantations over a period of five
years.
The Minister of Lands and Forestry, Prof. Dominic Fobih, stated that,
under the modified Taungya system, over 17,000 ha of plantations was
established last year, creating 76,000 jobs at the rural and local community
level.
Similarly, several tons of food crops such as maize, cocoyams, yams and
plantains were produced under this programme last year, he said.
According to Prof. Fobih, between January and June, this year, over
30,000 new jobs have been created in the area land preparation.
Earlier, in a welcome address, the Deputy Eastern Regional Minister,
Mr. Gustav Narh Dometey, observed that, quite often, the over-all social and
economic functions of plants are overlooked as the global environmental
situation deteriorates with incredible and amazing rapidity.
He said, the provision of open spaces, recreational parks and green
areas has the settlement planning of Town and Country Planning Department
but, very little effort has been put in by the district assemblies to
develop cities.
The Omanhene of the New Juaben Traditional Area, Daasebre Oti Boateng,
asked the Ministry of Lands and Forestry to ensure that, those given
concessions have a programme for reafforestation.
He also suggested that, farmers should be part of the programme to
ensure its sustainance as well as charged all Ghanaians to help protect the
forest from further depletion.
Ceremonial treeswere planted by various dignitaries to start the
programme at the Koforidua Sports Stadium.

Original_Search_Title: Church Donates Gifts to Less-Privileged Homes
Headline: Nigeria: Church Donates Gifts to Less-Privileged Homes
Body_Text:
Mountain of Fire and Miracles Ministries (MFM) at the weekend donated
gifts worth N2.5 million to inmates of less-privileged homes in Lagos.
The church made the donation through its welfare alms unit- "We Care
Ministry" and also feted the inmates with sumptuous dishes. The event took
place at MFM Onike-Iwaya Yaba, Lagos headquarters.
The inmates who had colourful wears made for them by the minister
laughed and danced across the expansive auditorium of the church. Some of
the inmates of the less-privileged homes who spoke to Eko Trust expressed
their happiness and one of them, Yinka Olaiya said, "I am happy. They gave
me two new clothes. I am wearing one and I have another one for Christmas."
The gifts include a bus to Arrow of God Home, Lekki, television, radio
sets, video CDs, cartons of milk, refrigerators, freezers, electric cookers,
bags of rice, bags of garri, tubers of yam. The statistics of the items run
into thousands.
Some of the recipients include Remand Home, Oregun, Boys Approved Homes,
Lagos State Government Little Saints Akowonyo, Boys Approved School, Isheri,
Rehabilitation and Vocational Training Centre, Isheri, The Good Shepherded
Orphanage Home, Idimu, Distressed Children Transit Home, Idi-Araba and
Remand Home, Agege.
Speaking to Eko Trust after the donation, which was witnessed by notable
church leaders, the coordinator of "We Care Ministry, Reverend Julius
Obileye, said the gesture was MFM's own way of practically ameliorating the
sufferings of the downtrodden.

Original_Search_Title: Traditions Die Hard: Can Arrests Stop Female Genital Mutilation?
Headline: Ghana: Traditions Die Hard: Can Arrests Stop Female Genital Mutilation?
Body_Text:
On September 20 of this year, police officers in Wa, in the Upper West
Region, arrested Fefe Dari for practicing female genital mutilation (FGM) on
three girls. Two days later, the 47-year-old woman pleaded guilty in the Wa
Circuit Court and received a prison sentence of five years. She is currently
serving this sentence in Tamale's prison.
A 1999 study by the Population Impact Project at the University of Ghana
estimated that 69.1% of women in the Wa district have had their genitals
cut. Despite this reported prevalence, authorities have arrested less than
10 people since FGM became illegal in 1994. Dari's arrest was the first in
the Upper West.
If Dari's arrest is an attempt to stop FGM, it raises questions about
how to effectively combat the practice: Why have there been so few arrests?
Is it fair to arrest someone like Dari? And, finally, what is the impact of
Dari's arrest on her community? Dari worked as a farmer in Billi-uu, a
remote village outside of Wa. She and her nephew, Kwesi Nabiaba, farmed
maize, millets, yam and groundnuts.
Dari was married and widowed twice. After her first husband died, she
married his junior brother who died four years ago. Dari has six children -
three girls and three boys. At the time of her arrest, she and her three
youngest children (ages three, five and nine years) lived with Nabiaba.
On September 19, Florence Ali, president of the Ghanaian Association for
Women's Welfare (GAWW), a non-governmental organization, was in Wa
conducting sensitization workshops on FGM. She received a report from a
nurse at Loggu Health Center, on the outskirts of Wa, that Dari had
performed FGM on three girls in Billi-uu on September 13. Someone from the
village had informed the nurse of the mutilation, prompting her to travel to
Billi-uu and verify the accusation.
The girls - who were three weeks, one and a half years, and five years
old at the time - are Dari's relations. The two oldest are her nieces and
the baby is Dari's granddaughter, the first child of her 16-year-old
daughter.
After receiving the report from the nurse, Ali notified the Women and
Juvenile Unit (WAJU) of Ghana Police Services in Wa. The next day, Ali and
police officers traveled to Loggu where they questioned Dari, who was there
for the market day.
According to Ali, Dari denied having performed the acts, saying that
someone from another village was to blame. When the police told Dari they
would detain her until she produced the real perpetrator, she confessed to
the crime.
Ali and the police went to Billi-uu. They walked the ten kilometers from
Loggu to the village, which is not accessible by car. They found the girls
and brought them to Wa District Hospital for treatment. "[The doctors] were
really very scared because they had lost a lot of blood," remembers
Ali.
On September 22, Dari pleaded guilty to "intentionally and unlawfully
infibulating the whole part of the labia minora and clitoris" of all three
girls. The maximum punishment for performing FGM is three years. Because
Dari circumcised three girls, Judge Alhaji Mohammed Ahmed Mustapha sentenced
her to five years of hard labor.
Residents of Billi-uu are angry with the nurses at Loggu Health Center
for Dari's imprisonment. They are angry because a member of their community
was taken away. They know that it was one of their own - a villager - who
reported Dari's acts to the nurses, but the authorities are protecting that
person's identity. The blame, therefore, falls on the nurses.
Hawa Yakubu, a nurse at the clinic, says that the villagers' hostility
towards them has affected their work. "Since the arrest, we haven't been [to
Billi-uu] because we are afraid," Yakubu explains. "We're supposed to go for
immunization programs, but we do not go. A man goes for us."
The nurses' experience illustrates one reason why the police have
arrested so few people for practicing FGM. People fear reporting cases to
them.
Daniel Dorledols, the public relations officer for the Ghana Police
Service in Wa, stresses the importance of keeping an informant
anonymous. "If the informant's identity is known," he explains, "the person
could be attacked." But is a guarantee of anonymity enough? An officer at
WAJU in Wa, who wishes to remain anonymous, recommends that informants
receive protection and a financial reward for their information.
Given the lack of resources to conduct intensive investigations, the
police often rely on informants to catch perpetrators of FGM. "The onus is
on the community members to report," says Marian Tackie, Executive Director
of the National Council on Women and Development, which is a department of
the Ministry of Women and Children's Affairs.
This need for insiders is a result of the law forcing FGM to be carried
out underground. Yakubu, the nurse at Loggu Health Clinic, explains one
tactic people use to keep FGM secret. "They used to let the child grow
[older before being circumcised]," she says. "But now that we've been
telling them [that it's illegal], they do it when [the child is] an infant,
so no one will notice."
Dorledols, of the Ghana Police Service in Wa, disagrees with Tackie on
the effects of the law banning FGM. He argues that the law, combined with
public education campaigns, has led to the near elimination of the practice
in his region. He says there have been so few arrests because there are so
few people performing FGM.
Pat Sutinga, a midwife and nurse in the children's ward of Wa District
Hospital, agrees that there has been a reduction in the number of FGM
cases. She says that after a mother gives birth to a girl in the hospital,
the nurses tell her that FGM is harmful and illegal. (A poster that reads
"Stop Female Genital Cutting" and shows a young girl with a bleeding vagina
is prominently displayed on the wall of the ward.) When the babies come back
for post-natal exams, she says they have not been circumcised.
Like Dorledols, Sutinga believes that public sensitization and the
threat of imprisonment have deterred some parents from having their
daughters cut. However, Sutinga admits that she is not able to examine all
girls in the region. Only nine percent of women in the northern regions give
birth with the assistance of a trained health care worker, according to the
United Nations Population Fund. If they do give birth in the hospital, most
villagers receive their post-natal care in local clinics, out of Sutinga's
reach. However, she says, the clinics send monthly reports to the hospital
without ever mentioning FGM.
"I agree that [Fefe Dari] did not follow the law," says her nephew,
Kwesi Nabiaba. "But it is that she was ignorant of the law."
In court, that logic was the basis of Dari's defense. She pleaded
guilty, but added that she did not know that FGM was a crime.
Dari's critics argue that she could not have been ignorant of the
law. Dorledols, Yakubu and Ali contend that people in the village know the
law. They point to the example of the Billi-uu resident who reported
Dari. "Because there was an informant from the village," explains Dorledols,
"that means that awareness was created."
Nabiaba disagrees: "The whole village was in darkness," he says.
Meetings with ordinary people in the region support Dari's claim of
ignorance. Waiting for a lorry at Loggu, two women from nearby Bintanga said
they had only heard of the law after Dari's arrest. "People were not aware
of the existence of that law," added Mohammed Seidu, a driver at the Wa bus
station.
Tackie argues that "ignorance of the law is no excuse." "Once there is a
law - whether you know about it or not, it is now a criminal offence," she
says. "Just like any criminal offence, you can be prosecuted, whether you
are ignorant or not, because you are inflicting something which is harmful
on another human being."
For others, it is not that simple. The law must be enforced, says Ali,
"but I kind of pitied [Dari] because it may be true that she really didn't
know the law existed."
Whether or not Dari was informed of the law, her arrest raises another
question: Is arresting people necessary to curb FGM or is educating people
on its harmful effects sufficient?
Ali says that stopping FGM is "a question of education." "Everywhere I
go," she explains, "[villagers] don't really know what is done to the woman
[when they are cut]. There was one instance when [a woman] said to me, 'You
see, they have been telling us that it isn't good, we shouldn't do it, but
we don't really know why. But now that you have taken your time to tell us
why, now I can also educate my clients and try to dissuade them from
practising it.'"
Most people interviewed felt educating people about the harmful effects
of FGM must go hand in hand with informing them about the law. And they
argue that the arrest of someone who performs FGM deters others from doing
it as well. In fact, Ali and Tackie hope to expand the scope of the
law. They sent a memo to the Attorney General asking that the law include
punishments for the girl's parents and other community members who
participate in the ceremony.
It is difficult - perhaps impossible - to discern the effects of Dari's
arrest on the prevalence of FGM in Billi-uu. Nabiaba, Dari's nephew, says
that the practice was common before the arrest, but it has stopped
since. "The arrest has scared people because there is a likelihood that
people will report you," he explains.
But it seems highly improbable that a custom would be entirely
eliminated in six weeks. "Traditions die hard," says Tackie.
Nabiaba does admit that the practice is engrained in their
culture. Asked why villagers perform FGM, he said: "It's something practised
by their great-grandfathers, their fathers. I don't know why they do it, but
it is a tradition."
Nabiaba has two wives and says that both of them have had their genitals
cut. All four of his children are boys but if he had a daughter, he adds, he
would have her cut.
If Dari's arrest has deterred others from performing FGM, it achieved
its desired effect. But there's also the chance it has forced the practice
even farther underground and people like Nabiaba are too scared to admit
that residents of Billi-uu still practise FGM.
There are some effects of Dari's arrest that are easier to measure.
Nabiaba says that since the arrest he has been "confused" and has lost
his appetite. He currently cares for Dari's three young boys, who are also
suffering. "They are at home weeping everyday," he says, "and I don't know
what to do."

Original_Search_Title: I Left Ghana Because of Rawlings - 1979 Victim Tells Commission
Headline: Ghana: I Left Ghana Because of Rawlings - 1979 Victim Tells Commission
Body_Text:
Mr. Daniel Ohene Adjei, another victim of the atrocities of the 1979
mutiny started weeping even before telling his story perhaps because just as
he was about to do so he had remembered the painful ordeal.
He told the commission that in 1979, as a trader who went to buy yam
from the producing areas to sell in Accra he had brought in some one million
tubers to the Timber Market when a military vehicle pulled up with some
soldiers in it.
One of the soldiers he said asked him for the price of a tuber of yam he
had picked to which he mentioned one hundred and twenty cedis. The soldier
said it was too expensive but he tried to explain to him that transport cost
and other incidentals are factored into the price of the commodity.
One of the soldiers he said pushed him at this stage and he
retaliated. He was hit and a struggle ensued but he was overpowered by the
soldiers who bundled him into their truck after a bad beating.
In the truck too they beat him until they reached Mitchel Camp,
Tema.
He was locked up in a guardroom for three days and for two days he did
not eat he told the commission. When he was released to go home he had no
money on him and he was half naked. A woman by the roadside he said offered
him one cedi for transport back to Accra and a dress to cover his
nakedness.
He could not go to find out about the status of his tubers of yam
because his father had discouraged him from doing so he told the
commission.
He then left for Nigeria because according to him he did not want to
have anything to do Mr. Jerry Rawlings whose regime was responsible for his
predicament.
He expressed gratitude to the government for making such a commission
possible but for which those of them with bottled-up pain would not have had
the outlet of airing them.
He added that he had forgiven those who wronged him after the
narration.
Proceedings continue today with the retired Major Boakye Djan and his
AFRC mates making a spectacular appearance which is expected to be hot.

Original_Search_Title: Swelling Agitation for the Hyppadec Bill
Headline: Nigeria: Swelling Agitation for the Hyppadec Bill
Body_Text:
The recent snappy tour to the riverine areas of Niger State by the
president, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, to assess the damages caused by the
recent flood is a welcome idea.
Although the visit was brief, it will go a long way to provide hope for
the people of the area to the effect that the federal government has not
totally forgotten them.
Furthermore, the fact that the president could not visit the Shiroro
area where the flood wreaked havoc will give him an idea of the totality of
the catastrophe during the period the flood swept through the state.
It would be recalled that the disaster which is not peculiar to the
state alone, was a general phenomenon all over the Northern region which
regularly suffered from drought.
As can be seen from reports, Kaduna, Katsina, Jigawa and Kano states
were all affected by the flood in various ways.
In a chain of disaster, that assailed these states, nobody knew that the
flood would expand its frontier of destruction beyond these states, until it
suddenly reared its ugly head in Niger State.
Perhaps, the destruction caused by the flood in Niger State can be
described as more horrendous in scale, measured against what happened in the
other states combined.
In the first wake of the flood which occurred in September, thirty-six
(36) villages in Shiroro Local Government Area were submerged.
During the period, livestock, farm produce, houses and property worth
millions of naira were lost .
According to the village head of Galadima Kogo, Alhaji Aliyu Dada, fifty
people died in the flood. Two weeks later when another rain fell, eight (8)
people including two small children also died.
As would be expected from the state government, its legislative arm in a
motion appealing to the federal government to do something, specifically
urged her to hastily sign the HYPPADEC bill into law, so that the disaster
could be controlled in future. No doubt the flood has caused untold
disasters in the states agitating for HYPPADEC these being Niger, Kogi,
Kwara and Kebbi.
Although the President's visit has come and gone, the effect of the
disaster however remains, without eliciting either relief materials or
categorical statements from the president regarding his administration's
next move on the HYPPADEC bill, since it was passed to him for assent.
In the reports credited to him, the President said that he did not
believe that the HYPPADEC bill holds the key to the solution of the flood,
saying his position is based on lack of conviction that the 20-year-old dam
was responsible for the disaster.
But the contrary is the case, as investigations have shown that the dam
contributed in no small measure to the environmental disaster and human
suffering.
Those affected revealed that at the height of the rainy season when the
dam was at full capacity, the National Electric Power Authority (NEPA)
officials released the excess water stored, and the consequence was
immediate flooding of low lying areas.
They added that while this is an annual tragedy, this year's had been
compounded by the River Kaduna which courses through the area, so that when
the dam water was freed and eventually united with that of the river, the
result was the havoc that stared the people in the face.
Perhaps, the lack of adequate knowledge of the impact on the current
disaster informed the levity with which the government is handling the
matter.
In the attempt to unveil the causes of the flood, one came across
political equations impeding the realisation of HYPPADEC.
More potent in the factors inhibiting the assent of the bill by the
president is the personality tussle between IBB versus Obasanjo and
Atiku. In fact, it is being discussed in certain quarters that the HYPPADEC
bill is suffering because General Babangida is harbouring ambitions for the
2007 presidency.
As the saying goes: 'one gesture deserves another,' and since Babangida
was largely instrumental to Obasanjo's election after he was released from
prison by General Abdulsalami, who is also from Niger State, seeking for the
establishment of the commission should not have taken long as Obasanjo is in
power.
Before the soured relationship between the personalities served by
politics, there was a standing agreement between the president and
Nigerlites during the electioneering campaignsthat once the former is
elected, HYPPADEC as a body will see the light of day.
Whether the agreement was documented or whether a gentlemanly one, this
cannot be substantiated. But it would be remembered that the root of the
conflict between the state governor and the president during the latter's
first term, was not so much for seeking a second term. The problem was
traced to the president's stonewalling over the promise made by him to
create the HYPPADEC Commission.
The conflict at that time created the Kure and Mustafa Bello factions in
the PDP, when the latter was alleged to have been undermining the effort by
Kure to realise HYPPADEC in order to prevent him (Kure) from claiming credit
for election purposes in the bid for a second term.
It is almost five years now since the issue of HYPPADEC sponsored by
four states of Kebbi, Kogi, Kwara and Niger has been developing, but it
still remains remains a mirage.
The establishment of this important body has pitched the people of these
states against their leaders, especially Niger State which champions the
cause, questioning the rationale behind their overwhelming support for the
PDP government at federal and state levels.
Their angst has been concentrated on the two former leaders of Nigeria
who are citizens of Niger State for not using their influence to persuade
Obasanjo to sign the bill into law, since his office is being accused of
delaying the progress.
But for a very long time, the people will ever remain grateful to
General Babangida for not only completing the dam project initiated by
President Shehu Shagari, but for also expanding it.
Indeed the reason for the people's angst is not so much because the
president is withholding assent, but for the fear that the effort of those
who gave thought to the project might be in vain if the necessary measures
were not taken to ensure its safety, as it is believed that if the dam is
being used at its present low capacity, it could collapse.
The obvious fact is that it is not being fully utilised presently to
generate electricity, and the other purpose of its agricultural potential
remains completely neglected too.
This negligence is discernible at Gwada village, upon the borders of the
dam site where there is a vast land which can be transformed into an
agricultural treasure, but this has not been done.
In fact, the exploitation of this agricultural potential will generate
not only job opportunities, by extension it will boost the chances of peace
in the already restive country.
Having explained the factors militating against the realisation of the
enabling law that will create HYPPADEC, it would be of interest to make an
exposition on the sorrows and sufferings.
First, the majority of the people affected are Gbagyis, while others are
Hausas. The Gwaris are mainly farmers, while the Hausas are mainly
fishermen.
As earlier mentioned, there are thirty-six (36) settlements that were
submerged by the flood, killing people, destroying food crops, snuffing out
the lives of their livestock, and pulling down their abodes, thus making
them refugees in their own country.
The villages affected are Ibro, Kyaure, Galadima-Kogo, Maiyelu, Guni,
Lagado, Kafana and Alake.
Others are Laga, Dnasa, Layini Kpan, Shiroro Kanufe and Dnabuyi. These
are just a few of the villages whose names were made available to
journalists.
For instance, the village head of Galadima Kogo, Alhaji Aliyu Dada, told
reporters that 50 people were missing in the wake of the flood.
As for an education supervisor in the new Lakpma Local Government,
Alhaji Yahaya Idris, while underscoring the statement of the village head
over the statistics of those that lost their lives, he recounted in
sorrowful detail their ordeal as happened 20 years ago when the dam project
began.
"When the project began in the 80s," he said, "we were re-located to our
present villages. This was done so that the dam work could be successfully
executed," he added.
According to him, compensation then was so small that some of them
received a paltry sum of N300, the highest was N3,000. The education
supervisor further added that the only individual that received N30,000 from
the compensation then was the village head. "Having given us this money, a
piece of land was also given to us where we erected our present buildings to
start life anew. That is over 20 years ago."
"Although we felt we were cheated, there was nothing we could do as we
didn't have people in the government to speak on our behalf then. Imagine
paying somebody who has families a paltry sum of N300!
"Apart from the only primary school in the village, two bore-holes and a
well dug for us then no other project has been established for the past 20
years, and this has exposed us to water-borne diseases, and other epidemics
that usually break out in the villages and other surrounding settlements,"
Alhaji Idris said.
While these time-worn projects are sited in Galadima Kogo, the remaining
35 villages are not as blessed as they have been forgotten by the federal
government which has not made good its promise to establish schools,
hospitals and roads after twenty years.
At Anasa village where the fishermen have been rendered helpless by the
flood, their fate is a gory one after hearing their sojourn.
Anasa village to Kaduna city, the journalists learnt, is a mere three
kilometres through the bush path. But if you decide to go by boat, it is 10
kilometres.
According to them, they are closer to Kaduna than their own local
government headquarters at Erena town having been separated by the great
gulf of water, and before you can access Erena town, you'd have spent two
and a half hours.
During the rainy season when the water is released from the dam, the
fishermen would abandon their houses and hide in their fishing boats to
escape from the fury of the flood, while they float on the water.
They further said that this is their worst experience in 20 years, as
they lost engine boats totalling sixteen (16) at the cost of sixty thousand
naira (N60,000) each.
The engine boats which they claimed were purchased just recently were to
enhance their fishing business which is their main occupation.
In the same vein, their maize and yam harvests exposed in the sun to dry
before they could be stored in the barns, were all flooded by the twin evils
of gushing dam waters and torrential rain, as produce recovered from the
flood showed.
Perhaps, the extent of the damages caused by the flood will never be
fully appreciated due to the inaccessibility of the hinterland.
In some cases, signs of damages are indicated by the crops whose plants
have been swallowed by water as they pop their heads above it. However, in
some areas where the water had started to recede, the crops have already
dried as they have been saturated with water.
The people complained bitterly of being reduced to beggars, refugees and
paupers by natural and man-made disasters in their homeland without
attracting the slightest response from the government of the day, in terms
of relief materials. They said they hope this signal is clear to the
organisation charged with the responsibility of handling emergency cases -
the National Emergency Management Agency.
Dissatisfied with the frustration they have been tangled with, the flood
victims have decided to migrate to another part of the country to start a
new life if the incidence repeats itself again, and called on the federal
government to come to their assistance like the federal government has done
in Katsina, Kano, Kaduna and Jigawa states which had the same problem.
In his own submission, the secretary to the state government of Niger
State, Comrade Adams Erena, who hails from the flood-affected area, said
that the problem of flood has been a recurring decimal in the life of his
people. This problem, he added, has been aggravated by the siting of the dam
in the area.
Recalling the separate efforts made in the past by the community and the
government to get rid of the menace posed by the flood, he said it is a pity
that up till now, those efforts have not yielded any result.
He specifically referred to the early part of the seventies when the
Gwari people were asked to contribute money to buy a boat that would ferry
them across River Kaduna, but that the money disappeared into thin air and
up till today, nobody can account for the position of the contribution.
Comrade Erena who attributed the misfortune of the people to poor
leadership, however, said the provision of the boat was later realised when
the Kure administration came to power in 1999, saying that without him, such
a goal would have remained a mirage.
The scribe to the government explained that the effort to build a bridge
across River Kaduna as a permanent solution was scuttled by the present
administration of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, which was to replace the idea of
crossing the water with a boat as its lifespan is shorter than the
bridge.
He said the idea of a bridge was initially mooted by Babangida's
government, but could not see the light of day due to unavoidable
circumstances.
General Abubakar Abdulsalami endorsed the plan also, but due to his
brief tenure, passed the responsibility to President Obasanjo. The latter
however jettisoned the plan.
In an appeal to the federal government, which was laced with bitterness
over the catastrophe wreaked by the flood, the government scribe called on
the president, General Olusegun Obasanjo, to expedite action on the HYPPADEC
bill by assenting to the law, and asked that he should also revive the plan
to construct the bridge across River Kaduna.
He said he has decided to make this appeal since it is only the federal
government who has the financial wherewithal to tackle this matter of
immense proportion, saying that if the issue is left to the state
government, it has not the money to tackle it.
Comrade Erena said that while these are permanent solutions to the
problem of the recurring flood, a temporary one should be devised to assuage
the sufferings of the people for the meantime by sending relief
materials.
"If the federal government considers these prescriptions, the people
would have found succour in their grimmest hour," he concluded.

Original_Search_Title: Essential Commodity Prices Fall in Jos
Headline: Nigeria: Essential Commodity Prices Fall in Jos
Body_Text:
Prices of some foodstuff and essential commodities in Jos have fallen in
the last one month, the News agency of Nigeria (NAN) reports.
According to a market survey conducted on Wednesday in two satellite
markets in Jos, an average tuber of yam now sells at N80, down from N100 in
October.
A bag of sweet potatoes is sold for N500 instead of the previous
N700.
Similarly, a standard measure of beans which cost N90 last month now
goes for N75, a bottle of vegetable oil costs N140 as against N150, while a
kilogramme of meat remains stable at N350.
The prices of a standard measure of parboiled rice and a bottle of palm
oil are also stable at N130 and N130 respectively.
Some of the traders interviewed attributed the drop in prices to the
availability of the products in large quantities.

Original_Search_Title: Banda Chieftaincy Affair
Headline: Ghana: Banda Chieftaincy Affair
Body_Text:
The Member of Parliament (MP) for Wenchi West, Johnson Asiedu Nketsia
(aka Gen. Mosquito) has denied fueling the protracted chieftaincy dispute in
the Banda Traditional Area but blamed non-resident influential citizens of
the area for the recent clashes.
Mr. Nketsia told The Chronicle on Monday that his message to the two
rival paramount chiefs had been peace. "If there was any problem in the area
I will put 70 per cent of the blame on the so-called influential citizens of
Banda who are staying outside but go there and put fire and then come
out".
Mr. Asiedu-Nketsia was reacting to fresh allegations made by one of the
rival paramount chiefs, Nana Sakyameh Sen-Apem II to the effect that the
recent violent clashes at Banda were of the MP's making.
The chief accused him of advising his rival, Nana Kwadwo Sito I at
Banda-Ahenkro to go ahead and celebrate the Banda Annual Yam festival
despite District Security Council warnings against the celebration.
But the MP said to say that his visit on September 13 ignited the
conflict was "an infantile logic".
He admitted visiting the area on the day in question but said he did so
with the National Democratic Congress (NDC) regional chairman as part of a
planned tour of several constituencies in the area.
According to him the visit was cut short when he received a call from
Accra that he was to travel on a parliamentary team outside the
country.
Mr. Nketsia further admitted the chief mentioned the festival to him but
he told him he was traveling and would send somebody to represent him.
Other allegations made against the MP were that he had never attended
any function at Kabrono, the other rival town but always attended functions
at Banda-Ahenkro. He was also accused of shifting the venue of a programme,
which was supposed to be held in honour of the former vice president John
Evans Atta Mills, who was then visiting the Bui dam in 2000, from Kabrono to
Banda-Ahenkro.
However, Nketsia said the allegations were all false and that since his
election as MP for the area he had tried as much as possible not to involve
himself in the chieftaincy dispute. He had treated both parties with the
courtesy they deserved whenever he visited them.
With regard to the former vice-president's visit, he said there was an
occasion where some of the so-called influential citizens of Banda had gone
to use the former vice-president's visit to support one of the
factions.
Mr. Nketsia called on the factions to reconcile since such disputes did
not augur well for the development of the area.

Original_Search_Title: Is Hausa Poetry in Decline?
Headline: Nigeria: Is Hausa Poetry in Decline?
Body_Text:
Shehu Usman Dan Fodiyo (of blessed memory), was confronted with the
challenge of transforming a society that was enlightened but deeply enmeshed
in immorality, injustice and corrupt leadership.
'Malamai' then were but palace praise singers, while the masses were
left shouldering the burden of paying heavily for the lavish and loud living
of the 'Sarakuna' through taxes. Reforming such a society was to a certain
extent easy, because the people could read and write, hence literary
instruments were deployed by the Shehu and his disciples. Poetry is one of
the effective mediums used by Shehu Dan Fodiyo in making people in Hausaland
understand that the society is ill and had to be changed. Abdullahi Gwandu
and Muhammadu Bello wrote several poems. Nana Asma'u-the daughter of Shehu
Dan Fodiyo was a poet of distinction. In fact, throughout the period of the
struggle that saw Hausaland from deviation to guidance: from injustice to
justice and from corruption to transparent leadership that was the pace for
the evolution of modern Hausa society.
The 'horrible incidence,' i.e., the coming of European explorers,
missionaries who later transformed into colonizers, created a new space for
literary activities in Hausaland. Scholars wrote poems highlighting the
dangers of what became the conquest of Hausalasnd and the subsequent
emergence of what is now Nigeria and the introduction of 'boko.' Writing
gradually changed from Ajami to Roman script. The poetry of scholars like
Aliyu Dansidi, Aliyu Namangi and other notable Hausa poets became available
in Roman script.
Poetry (rubutattun wakoki) has always had an upper hand in the Hausa
literary scene. The best of Hausa writers are concentrated in the poetry
genre. Leading Hausa poets that stood out include Aliyu Dansidi of Zazzau,
Sa'adu Zungur, Aliyu Namangi, Mudi Sipikin, Akilu Aliyu, Na'ibi Suleiman
Wali and Mu'azu Hadejia. Although there were many other poets of
distinction, they were not noticed because they wrote in 'Ajami.' All these
poets were members of a generation far from the present.
The effectiveness of poetry in the Hausa literary scene can be discerned
from the impact made by Malam Sa'adu Zungur. His only collection of poems
contains four poems, i.e, 'Innovation' (Wakar Bidia), 'Welcome to the
Soldiers' (Wakar Maraba Da Soja), 'Careless Talk' (Wakar 'Yam Baka) and the
'The North' Republic or Monarchy (Arewa Jamhuriyya ko Mulukiya). Though
short, the versatility of poetic elements made these poems significant in
form and content. In 'Arewa Jamhuriyya ko Mulukiya' Sa'adu Zungur produced
an informed analysis of the position of Northern Nigeria in what is
'federalism.' With powerful imagery, Sa'adu Zungur projected the problems of
Hausaland like begging, indolence, insolvency, aversion to knowledge and too
much pride. Akilu Aliyu displayed the kind of talent that was entirely
poetic. His famous poem, 'Wakar 'Yar Gagara' (The prostitute) gave an
account of the life of a prostitute leading eventually to the point where
prostitution is a shortcut to a sad end. Na'ibi Suleiman Wali explored the
overwhelming dangers of the much cherished modernity which has promoted
immorality, while dimming the virtues of our values.
This is perhaps the period in which we desperately need more poems from
Hausa literary scenes. This is because, unlike other forms of writing,
poetry can be published and circulated early and less expensively. The decay
of almost everything is apparent in the Hausa society of today.
Developments in Hausa literary scene can best be described as,
copsided. The heydays with Kano market literature (KLM) churned out a prose
only literary era. Best of writers in this class like Bilikisu Salisu
Funtua, Bala Anas Babinlata, Ado Ahmed Gidan Dabino, Balaraba Ramat Yakubu,
were novelists. This era had the advantage of the highest number of literary
associations like Dan Haki, Marubuta Soyayyar Zamani, Raina Kama, Jigon
Hausa, Kukan Kurciya and so many others. It was in this period that we had
bookshops that also doubled as publishers.
More advanced literary criticisms in Hausa came to the fore with what
was known as, "The Great Soyayya Debate,"exposed Hausa literature to
rigorous criticisms thus drawing more attention to the richness of Hausa
literary culture. This is an era of novels.
It is acknowledged that the foundation of Hausa literature is now
isolated or even pushed out of the literary activity. This may often be
attributed to the high level of talent and the linguistic prowess required
in writing a poem especially in Hausa.
Poetry in Hausa is and has been at a standstill for so long. The
prevalence of a culture that tilts more to the screen than the text, thus
Hausa poetry becomes more endangered. This is not saying that there is
absolute dearth of talents, after all Hausa musical scene is ever thriving
with new talents coming-up all over Hausaland, performing as good as Mamman
Shata, Dan Kwairo, Narambada, Garba Supa, Aliyu Dandawo, Sarkin Taushin
Katsina, Dan Maraya, Sani Dan Indo and others.
Now that the Hausa literary scene is more or less consumed by the boom
of fina-finam Hausa with so many writers now comfortable with being
producers, there is need to avoid, 'Mamta Mafari, (forgetting the origin),
producers who were at the beginning writers doing a good service to Hausa
culture. However, the question is, shall literary activity, especially the
poetry genre be left to go away with the storm? The answer here and in the
affirmative is that there is need to sustain and ensure that literary
culture thrives.
This time, more than any other, provides the platform for new and more
'Wakokin Hausa ya rubuce." We have so many topics to put beautiful verses
and stanzas on. Politics and this democracy, education and crimes,
corruption and diseases, poesy and affluence, breach of trust and too much
emphasis on vanity!
Branches of the Association of Nigerian Authors (ANA) in dominantly
Hausa states can intervene. Instead of putting their time and energy into
writing English poems, a bit of their attention should go into putting in
place mechanisms for restoring the vitality of Hausa poetry. New talents
should be encouraged and poetry should be emphasized. The association can
also contribute by collecting Hausa poems written in 'Ajami' script around
our 'Malamai' and 'Tsangayoyi.' Transcribing them into Roman script can be a
step forward in enlightenment.
Technicality and intellectual demands are some of the discouraging
points in Hausa poetry. It takes a lot of originality to write a good Hausa
poem. But this is not supposed to discourage us from trying.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's Vanishing Socio-Economic Visions And Dreams
Headline: Nigeria's Vanishing Socio-Economic Visions And Dreams
Body_Text:
BY October 1 this year, we had had about forty-three years of the
so-called socioeconomic-own-political independence from Britain. One can
reminisce with nostalgia on October l, 1960, how as young primary school
student at St. Matthew's Catholic School, Umuchu, Anambra State, one had to
walk for miles to Aguata Local Government headquarters where we participated
in a parade and march past with a new and unstained Nigerian flag.
We innocently-waived our flags most joyfully and sang in voices without
stain. Life was so good and full of visions and sweet dreams that have now
vanished! There was hope for the future. The ordinary Nigerian loved his
neighbour with all his heart. People, far and near, did their businesses
unrestrained and without boundaries. There was free mixing of people across
the diverse ethnic groups and religions. Nigerians married, traded,
worshipped, farmed, etc.with one another without let or hindrance.
People sojourned from one part of the country to the other without
fear. The young boys and girls attended schools in far places from their
homes and communities. They were never afraid as help would readily-come to
the needy most of the time. The youths had visions, nursed ambitions and
fulfilled their dreams. There was little or no discrimination based on
tribe, religion, section and class. People moved and lived within and across
social boundaries with pleasure. Hate and abuse of fundamental human rights
were anathematic. Life, then, was comfortable and
conveniently-nostalgic. People peacefully-went to bed at night and slept
blissfully. Not anymore today! There was not too much to worry about those
days. Everyone was, almost, his brother's keeper.
Qualified nurses
Schools and hospitals were good and affordable. Though they were not
many, but they were comparatively-enough and -accessible. The hospitals had
many qualified nurses and doctors who ministered well to patients. The
secondary schools and tertiary institutions were well-equipped and
staffed. Students and graduate products from these institutions were
good. Today, many of our educational systems produce yahoos! Those days,
primary six students could write good letters with unblemished
English. Today, it is a major hazard to grade unreadable texts written by
undergraduates and, even, many finished products!
You just pity some of our graduates
who can hardly-express themselves both in logic and language. You watch
with consternation, pity or equanimity when such people speak. These most
poorly-cooked and half-baked disgrace themselves and the avenues or systems
that shamelessly-produced them! That is why, today, houses or buildings
collapse, bridges, roads, dams and boreholes fail, surgical blades are left
in the womb after surgery, commercial injunctions are freely-given by some
judges, etc. That explains why cultism has become rampant in campuses
nationwide; why foreign universities, now, doubt the integrity and academic
contents of our certificates making it difficult for our graduates to be
easily-accepted for postgraduate studies. In those early days of Nigeria,
scholarship awards and grants were available for brilliant scholars or poor
students. There is nothing like that happening today! Instead, any available
funds are quickly-stolen and fraudulently carted away to overseas banks and
no one seems to be punished. What a shame! What a disgrace to statehood and
independence!
And come to think of it, in early Nigeria, there were plans for the
future of the youths as they grew up. Possible job opportunities were
created in teaching, civil service, agriculture, commerce, industry,
etc. Honest attempts were made to interview people and place them on jobs
without bribery and corruption.
It is not so in today's Nigeria! We graduate thousands of young men and
women every year in colleges of education, polytechnics and universities and
leave them to roam about in the streets without jobs or protection. Many
Nigerian youths are withering away in jails overseas while many more die on
their way or in prisons in their efforts to escape from this country in
search of better future elsewhere. It is a shame to see our youths
struggling and suffering for visas at foreign embassies in Lagos and Abuja
for the purpose of running away from their country, Nigeria. Many of them
threw away their Nigerian passports at their destinations and declare
themselves as refugees! These young men and women have been pushed to the
Berlin wall of endurance of tribulations to make them do the worst. There
are no meaningful plans or programmes to absorb our youths into jobs or
ventures whenever they completed their studies. They are left to become
armed robbers, prostitutes, political jobbers, cultists, four-one-niners,
etc. Only a very few of them who are well-connected may become employed
while a few more try to create honest jobs for themselves. In essence, we go
ahead to destroy our youths without any viable future for no just course!
The money we are supposed to use to create jobs for these hapless youths are
shamelessly - and fragrantly - stolen and fraudulently-stacked away in banks
overseas!
The innocent, peace-loving and humble Nigerians, who are
mischievously-described today, as grassroots, had their dreams and visions
all frustrated. They envisioned and dreamed of a great Nigeria where no man
or woman is oppressed and to leave a banner for their children without any
stain. They worked hard for a united and peaceful Nigeria. They prospered
and expanded in their businesses, trades, agriculture, cultures,
environment, etc. Life in Nigeria those years was symptomatic of the
biblical Garden of Eden that was flowing with milk and honey. Foods of
various kinds and types were abundant. Yams, cereals, casssva, bananas,
fishes (mangara and fresh), kwoi, meats of assorted kinds, vegetables,
fruits, etc. were easily-available. Few people went to bed with hungry
stomachs. Today, hunger, poverty and diseases ravage the lands! There was
commercially-abundant palm oil and palm kernel in the east, cocoa in the
west and groundnut pyramids of the north. Agriculture and rural farming had
respect and pride of place in socioeconomic lives. People were not
commonly-sick. Death was an uncommon event. Do not mind the farcical
arguments by some people today who attempt to prove that the high increase
in population may account for the massive increase in morbidity and
mortality. No, we are dying too much these days in this country! Any record
of deaths in Nigeria would waken our leaders to some action no matter how
trivial! There were hardly any mortuaries or coffin-makers those days. But
today, both morbid phenomena have emerged as major enterprising
money-spinners in urban and rural areas of Nigeria. So many dead and dying
must be commercially-taken care of those ghoulish speculators believe.
Whatever infrastructure that was available in early-days Nigeria was
useable. Although most of the roads were narrow and some bridges were
rickety, the drivers were not those reckless and luciferic daredevils you
find tearing away on our national highways and roads today. The earlier
drivers were careful while handling the steering. There was respect and
value for human life. People were not butchered recklessly on our roads as
being done today. Urban water schemes, reservoirs and rural water supplies
that worked, existed. Some of the abandoned dry water taps may still be seen
hanging out today. Many natural springs in rural areas were good water
supply sources. Rural women, youths, children and men worked together to
manage and protect rural water schemes. School children looked clean, neat
and shinny in their elegant uniforms. These rural water schemes served the
people well and were managed properly through culturally-imbued
people-participation in water resources management. Water-borne diseases
were not as endemic and epidemic as commonly-reported in many parts of the
country today. The environment was not as inclement and wicked as it has
become today. People eagerly-looked forward to the oncoming rainy and dry
seasons at the particular periods, knowing too well that each has a
life-saving socioeconomic events attached to it. It is not so today as each
season is, now, highly-dreaded by the people.
Many Nigerians have wantonly-abused, attacked and destroyed parts of our
virgin environment in many parts of Nigeria. As a result, the total
environment has now become enraged and is fighting back viciously and
ferociously. Man has committed heinous crimes against the natural
environment. We are all, now, exposed in Nigeria to multifaceted
environmental angst all over the country. Nigerians have bitten off more
than they can chew in precipitating environmental havoc, thereby, losing
control on its sustainability'

Original_Search_Title: Anambra State People Under Siege By Gully Erosion And Landslides
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra State People Under Siege By Gully Erosion And Landslides
Body_Text:
THE people who live in urban centres far away from home and who cannot
participate in this community exercise should send money for the people at
home to carry out the exercise. Blocked drains should be reopened and
desilted.
Water collection sumps, down-gradient of floods, should be dug to reduce
outflows. Ridges must be constructed around yam mounds to trap water in the
farms. Every compound should have shallow wells dug to collect runoff from
rooftops and grounds and the water allowed infiltrate into
groundwater. Landlords and tenants should keep drains in front of their
compounds open and free of obstructions to water flows. People should be
stopped from mining laterite from roadsides. Escavation of sands from flood
or river channels should be restricted or stopped. These reopen/awaken
stabilized gullies. Every community must ensure that no one or no government
grades any road anymore in Anambra State. Grading of roads deepens and
widens gullies because of the geologic and pedological nature of our
soils. When we grade roads in the dry season by smoothening and levelling
the potholes we improve expansion of existing gullies or start new ones
during rainy season. This is why many rural roads are now very deep,
sometimes, more than a man's height; why many compounds are left hanging by
roadsides. Instead of grading roads, we should fill the channels with red
earth and harden them. The town union leaders, traditional rulers and youths
must watch out for those contractors who construct erosion-causing roads and
stop them from deviant acts before they perpetrate their havoc of
erosion-causation.
Communities should get the contract terms from the government or whoever
awarded the road contract to ensure that proper specifications are adhered
to. They should monitor the road constructions and ensure that the
contractors do not construct narrow and short drainages that they hide in
the bush where they initiate and open up new gullies sooner than
later. These communities should tell their men and women to carry out these
erosion control exercises. They should get the necessary bylaws and enforce
them for their environmental survival and safety. Almost all towns in
Anambra State still have ancient forests around shrines, markets or open
spaces. Presently, there are severe encroachments into these forests through
wood felling and timber exploitation. Many forests have disappeared and are
disappearing fast. Our communities should survey and mark off these forests
to save and protect them. Wherever these forests have been removed or
interfered with, gullies have set in. There are also many abandoned river
channels and ancient dry valleys. Communities must avoid developments in
these places since some of them are geologically-unstable and
environmentally-sensitive.
Where we recklessly-tamper with their existence, we may encounter
emergence of massive gullies or landslides! People may not know but our
laws, statutes, decrees, edicts and bylaws have sections and clauses for
environmental protection and management. Communities and individuals are
advised to resort to court actions to seek redress for damages inflicted by
scavengers and truant environmental nuisances or marauders! Our courts can
award costs for and stop reckless damages inflicted by anyone, governments,
organizations or agencies on any part of the environment be it air, land,
soil, water or rock or biosphere.
People can and should go to court to challenge the vicious atrocities
perpetrated by some people, governments or organizations that cause floods,
gully erosion and landslides. Adjacent towns, communities or cultures can
come together to carry out together the above projects for permanence
otherwise if a gully is controlled in one town and left elsewhere, it may
continue in another town, may spread and fight back! There is a great need
for cooperation and coordination across towns, communities and cultures for
more lasting effectiveness and permanence. If every community can embark on
all the above self-help programmes, floods, soil and gully erosion and
landslides will be given a fair fight in Anambra State and beyond. Many
living-gullies shall be drained of their life-water-bloods, whither away and
die. Few new gullies will have little or no chance to sprout, live and
mature. Anambra State and possibly the Federal governments will, then by
chance, raise the financial resources to deal with the larger and deeper
gullies if they can muster their pride and integrity to do so.
Professor Egboka is of the Department of Geological Sciences Nnamdi
Azikiwe University, Awka.

Original_Search_Title: VP Bukenya's Poverty Dilemma
Headline: Uganda: VP Bukenya's Poverty Dilemma
Body_Text:
Kampala — It has just occurred to me that people who have a very deep attachment
to cows may also have a soft spot for humans who have attributes associated
with cattle.
Now, if you like cows essentially because they are docile, giving milk
to the pigs with the same dedication as they used to give milk to Mr. Jones,
you would probably wish that they did not get much smarter; just in case
they start learning a thing or two about their tragic existence.
Fallen revolutionaries, false prophets, conjurors and miracle workers
are all more comfortable with peasants than with other social groups,
because peasants generally do not have the training and experience to
critically examine the actions and motives of dedicated "performers".
As far as their economic woes are concerned, they have not been taught
enough arithmetic to grasp the nature of the conspiracy by which the rulers,
the legislators, the tax collectors and the privileged middle and business
classes operate.
When a peasant sells his 10kg of beans, walks into a village store and
pays Shs 2,000 for a litre of paraffin, he does not quite understand how he
is helping the lords of Rukungiri, Mbarara or Soroti to build their palaces
around the country and maybe abroad.
He does not even realise that he is contributing to the five-digit
dollar monthly obscenity for Kampala-based top dogs in the petroleum
industry.
Needless to say, the idea that he is helping American and European
executives and shareholders overseas to change their golf bags sounds like a
fable.
The peasant's expertise is in following the tracks of the guinea fowl
that uproots his nuts, or judging that the state of the creeper at the edge
of the forest indicates a mature but shrivelled yam two feet
underground.
But slowly, like he learned the tricks of the monkey that eats his
maize, he is waking up to the tricks of the politicians.
I suspect that you, reading this article, are not a peasant. Neither am
I. So, you and I are probably more akin to the dog or the wolf.
Hitherto, we were told that everything was fine. We mongrels, wolves and
what have you; our feelings and our reflections were largely irrelevant,
because the Movement (an euphemism for President Museveni) had millions of
peasants at its beck and call.
The vice president, Prof. Gilbert Bukenya, was probably dramatising a
little during his conversation with the press this week, when he warned of a
peasant revolt against the elite.
But he was correct when he said that the peasants have reason to be
disenchanted with the status quo.
He confesses that "we" (presumably the princes of the Movement machine)
have been practising the politics of "exciting people", the politics of
"blinding people", the politics of "deceit".
He wants to change that, predicting that once "practical peasantry
development" has started, most of the politicians (of deceit) will "fall
away from the scene."
Will Bukenya be left standing? An intriguing question. For who are the
politicians of deceit?
President Museveni is the avowed champion of the peasants. What is more,
the president perfectly understands the arithmetic that I said was a dark
region to most peasants.
That is why he often explains (as he did this week in the US) to Western
audiences how Uganda is a big "donor", selling to the West cheap raw
materials for the benefit of their multinational processors, workers, tax
collectors and governments.
So, who are the politicians of deceit, the politicians who sing and sing
but have refused or failed to sort out the plight of Uganda's peasants and
workers?
Is it Kananathan of the AGOA fame?
No. Kananathan knows what he is doing; handing out the exact measure of
contempt he knows a morally confused political order will permit.
Which politicians have made sure that the blindness of the peasants is
preserved; that opposition politicians must not be allowed to traverse the
villages to educate the peasants about the politics of deceit?
Which politicians have turned the politics of deceit into a lucrative
industry, instead of raising Uganda to a country that "makes machines which
make machines"; the grand 1986 vision, which I am sure Prof. Bukenya now
secretly acknowledges was the politics of "exciting people"?
But Bukenya cannot eat his cake and also keep it. It is almost
impossible to demand a radical departure without confronting the architects
of the failure.
Attempting this balancing act will probably leave the professor without
sufficient intellectual crispness to persuade the elite. More important, he
will also be useless to the peasants. For, to make any significant
improvements in the lives or our peasants, the entire baggage of Movement
decay and self-perpetuating prodigal waste that grew out of the early
promise must be swept away.
It is naÃ¯ve to believe that it will just "fall away": the vice
president's preferred dream.
But will the professor concede that to be taken seriously in frontline
politics - his otherwise impressive CV notwithstanding - he must work for a
more legitimate democratic condition, with dynamism aimed at banishing the
peasant's blindness?
Or will he continue fidgeting with his contradictory thesis, constantly
mindful that the president has a strong affection for cows?

Original_Search_Title: Ubang: Community Where Men And Women Speak Different Languages
Headline: Nigeria: Ubang: Community Where Men And Women Speak Different Languages
Body_Text:
Ubang, Cross River State — It was Goethe, a German philosopher who said "If you desire to be more knowledgeable than the others, then travel."
Language has been described as the organized system of speech used by human beings as a means of communication among themselves. This makes language a unifying factor of the human race, and enhances the tradition and culture of the people.
But when in a community the Polaric genders are not mutually intelligible yet people live harmoniously, then this can be classified as one of the eight wonders of the world. This may sound untrue and strange, but when Bassey Okon, one of my schoolmates who hails from Obudu in Cross River State, first told us the story of the Ubang community during one of our outings, nobody took him serious, of course for obvious reasons. First, it is (as we believed then) practically impossible for a community to exist where the two genders speak different languages.
Secondly, if ever such a place exists, we felt it cannot be in Nigeria, maybe South Africa or Asia. But the conviction with which Bassey argued his case left me with no option as a tourism reporter but to investigate the story and file my experience.
I arrived Calabar, the Cross River State capital at exactly 1.30 pm on Sunday 16th February, 2003 for the onward journey to Ubang. On my arrival, I made a frantic effort to be equipped with more information about my destination. In the course of my enquiry, I went to the Cross line park situated at the heart of Calabar municipal. There I got every information needed for my journey to Ubang.
Ubang, I was informed is the most remote hinterland of Obudu, in Obudu local government area of Cross Rivers State. Obudu is situated in the northern part of Cross River State, sandwiched by Benue State to the North, Ogoja local government to the West, Obanlikwu LGA to the East and Boki local government to the South, with an area of about 2000 square km. About 20 minutes drive from the Obudu metropolis is the Ukpe/Alege ward, and at the Westernmost part of Ukpe is Ubang. There is no direct vehicle to Ubang from Calabar except to Obudu. Obudu from Calabar by bus costs about N350. By car, it costs between N400 to N500 as the journey may be. From Calabar to Obudu by bus is about 400km and the journey could last some 5 - 6 hours. By car, it is less than that. A rather long journey that takes you through undulating hills of rainforest and beautiful highland scenery of other adjourning towns before getting to Obudu, the tourist heaven of Cross Rivers State. The beauty that makes Cross Rivers state the paradise city of the nation lies in the hinterlands of Odukpni, Akankpa, Baise, Yakur, Obubura, Ikom, Ogoja, Bakwara and Obudu LGA, a replica of the garden of Eden, said Peter Bassey, a driver with the Crossline who spoke with this reporter.
My journey to Obudu however, began on Monday the 17th of February, 2003. At exactly 12.30pm we had left Crossline park through Murtala Mohammed way, heading towards the monumental Calabar gate to confront the scenic view of the Obudu hills to the east, that sprawled towards the Camerouns. As the vehicle disappears into a haze of the rubber and palm plantations of Odukpani, intersected by the linear settlement of Awi in Akankpa local government, you could perceive the cold and hazy weather with a temperature of about 28 degrees. Midway, approaching the Cross Rivers National Park, one would notice Ugep in Yakure local government area, which is reputed to be the largest village in West Africa. Perhaps due to its geographical land mass and large population density, it could be described as a macerated settlement dotted with modern houses begging for recognition.
However, the place is more of a savannah than forest region. Getting to Apiapum in Obubura LGA where the River Cross forms a meander, you notice slight physical changes. The river is seen from the road as a large black and shining body between sand dunes giving an impression of a beach. As you approach the "Cross" bridge at Ikom where the river takes its rise and name, you notice the mesmerizing beauty of the most dense rainforest in Africa. There you also find the Agbokim waterfalls. Ikom, a sprawling commercial town that shares a boundary with the Camerouns is notorious for smuggling Cocoa in the East.
As you move through Obudu road, these settlements peter out into a dense thick dark forest vegetation which houses the largest remaining of the African rainforest with exotic species of apes, snakes, birds and insects. As you advance northwards to Obudu, the forest begins to thin out giving way to Savannah vegetation. Between the hills and valley west wards is Obudu town. I had arrived Obudu by 5.48pm and took a bike to Begiaba village in Obudu district, where I passed the night at Chief Sam Ogar's house, my host. The next day I took off to Ubang by bike.
Ubang town is about 30 minutes ride from Obudu through a dusty and stony road. The town is bounded by Boyobri village in the East, flanked by the Ubang hills to the south, and west to the North, by the plains of Alege. The Ubang community, geographically, is tucked away in a densely forested area as if shying away from prying eyes. It is mostly made up of a linear compound like settlement occasioned by hilly topography.
Unbelievably, Ubang community can boast of a good number of modern buildings. Tree crops like pear, plantain, banana, cashew and coconut plants plants are common sights in all the compounds, indicating an agrarian community. The occupation of the people is chiefly farming and their major crops are cocoa, rice, yam and groundnut. While the men till and cultivate fields, the women help tend them and market products. Hunting is also practiced by the men owing to the abundance of wildlife and forest.
The Ubang people surprisingly are well read, and this is evidenced by two primary schools and a secondary school too. Religion, however, seems to play a dominant role in the history and character of the people. For example, the larger communities are mostly Catholic, while New Jerusalem village to the extreme west is dominated by the Assemblies of God Church. Ubang is made up of four villages, these include Okwurusang, New Jerusalem (although not yet recognised by the government), Ofambe and Okiro village. History has it that about 300 years ago, all the villages were living on the Agbetang hills - 800 metres above the see level - before they migrated to their present location. The first people to descend were the Okwurusang and Ofambe followed by the Okiro who still occupy the foot of the Agbitang hill. Among the villages that make up Ubang community, New Jerusalem is unique. It is a village carved out solely to serve and worship God, a purely Christian dominated village, hence another name for New Jerusalem is the "Holy land." The village has about 100 inhabitants drawn from Okwurusang and Ofambe villages including a couple of visitors. New Jerusalem was founded in 1964 by Pa George Ade, the 78 year old clan head of Ubang. The place where New Jerusalem village is presently located was formerly called Resigeng meaning "peace land." New Jerusalem village is heavily influenced by the Assemblies of God church, which is the only church in the area, and it prohibits the sale of alcohol, cigarette, and the performance of masquerade and heathen practices. The inhabitants are those who have decided to serve God and live by Christian doctrines.
Pastor E. I. Akpoji, sectional leader, Assemblies of God Church, New Jerusalem village, who spoke to this reporter, reiterated that the church has been playing a significant role in the moral upbringing of the Ubang people.
Today, Ubang can be described as a community with a symbolic antecedent of culture and beliefs which had blended with the history and character of the people. Interestingly, there are some superstitious beliefs which the people still hold sacrosanct. For instance, the assumption that it was in Ubang that God first came down to create the universe, and the evidence is the indelible foot print called "Foot of God" found 400 years ago on the Rock of Abdeteng hills, where the Ubang community where said to have migrated from. "It is like a human foot, about five in number on the Rock" said Oliver Ibang, a Chief in Okiarepem clan in Ofambe village in Ubang. Again, there is this dreadful village deity called "Ikwong" which they claim protects them and purifies them from sickness. More so, the culture of the people has it that when a child is born, it is the visitor, not the parents that give a name to the new born child. According to Pa George Ade, 78, "if a child is born, the first visitor that happens to be present during the birth gives the child his or her name. If that visitor is a woman and the new born child is male, the woman has the right to give the child her own husband's name." More significant of Ubang culture is the language differentiation and it is interesting to uncover how this came about?
There are two different angles to this question. Majority of the people hold the belief that it was the Ubang people God first created, thus two languages were given to Ubang. In confirmation of this assumption, Chief Oliver Ibang added that it was a general assumption in Ubang that when God first created the Ubang people he gave them two languages, and when (God) discovered that the language could not go round, he decided to give the rest of the world one language.
In sharp contrast to this assumption, Pa George Ade, clan head of Ubang and founder of New Jerusalem village, argued that the language disparity is a mystery which nobody including himself, knew how or when it started. According to him, "Ubang people were born into it" adding that there was no legacy from their fore-fathers in that regards. He explained that all his children, for example were born and bred in Kaduna, yet they grew up to speak their respective Ubang languages.
The mystery that lurks behind the language disparity is such that when a male child is born, he grows up automatically to speak his father's language and equally understands his mother's language. The case is the same with the female child. According to them, the womens language is said to be very simple to understand. Funny enough, this reporter listened to a conversation between male and female children with the aid of an interpreter to discover that while the children communicate, they maintained their gender differences in words and consonance. Even in the church, said Pa George Ade, when women raise chorus in women's language, the men reply in the male language. The male language is difficult to understand since it is drawn from a rich panoply of village idioms, which they claim no stranger can acquire mastery of, even if he resides there forever. The big question is how do the Ubang people communicate?
Ironically, both male and female communicate to each other effectively. The secret is that they both understand each other when they speak different languages. For example, the word cup in female dialect is called Ugbala, while in the male dialect, it is called Enkor.
Below are some of the lexicons they use in their daily interaction.
Apart from the above lexicon, there are other words that the male and female pronounce after the same fashion. These include farm - tsang, orange - orum, fish - ikwe.
What happens when the male folk speak the female dialect asked this reporter. In reply Pa George Ade said no calamity will befall the male for speaking the female dialect, adding that "it is just abnormal for you to speak the women's dialect as the children will laugh at you." Does the language in any way promote gender inequality in Ubang? Rose Osang, a housewife in Ubang quickly disagreed with such notion saying that the males likewise the females have their respective dialects, the issue of gender inequality does not arise.
