On the same note, Chief Sunday Orim of Abasang clan in Okiro village allayed fears of gender inequality, and reiterated that the people of Ubang are proud of their inheritance saying that the language disparity is part of their identity. He however added that the only problem facing Ubang in general is lack of basic infrastructure from government.
While Amos Iyang Oghni, community leader and president of the customary court in Ubang, bemoans government's activity in the area, he enumerated lack of accessible roads, pipe-borne water, health care centre, electricity, among other things, as problems facing them. He said that Ubang, though having enormous natural resources of Obudu LGA, is marginalized and neglected.
Pathetic enough, Rose Osang told this reporter to use this medium to call on the local government to come to their rescue, adding that it's the women that bear the brunt of problems.
All efforts to reach the chairman of Obudu local government proved abortive as he was said to be away to Abuja as at the time of this visit. But the Honourable R. O. Afung representing Ubang/Alege ward who spoke with this reporter, said that government is doing everything possible to alleviate the suffering of the people, adding that because of the local government's lean purse, projects are undertaken on the basis of priority, such as healthcare, and has also encouraged the farmers to establish co-operatives.
However, plans are in the pipeline, according to him to harness the tourism potentials of the Ubang language disparity to promote rural tourism.

Original_Search_Title: Good Packaging is a Must
Headline: Ghana: Good Packaging is a Must
Body_Text:
The government and, indeed, the entire nation will next month pay
tribute to our hard-working farmers, fishermen and livestock farmers for
their sustained efforts to feed Ghanaians as well as boost the country's
export earning capacity.
This attests to the significant role the agriculture sector plays in the
growth and development of Ghana. For example, available figures from the
Ministry of Food and Agriculture indicate that the sector contributes about
40% of Ghana's GDP and employs more than 60 per cent of its workforce. The
sector again accounts for some 30 per cent of Ghana's export earnings. But
for the huge post-harvest losses recorded yearly by our farmers, which
averages between 30 and 40 per cent, Ghana could have derived greater
revenue from our agricultural produce.
With the exception of cocoa, (our main cash crop), most of our
agricultural produce go waste, particularly during the harvesting season due
to lack of proper preservation, packaging and processing. That explains why
our yams, tomatoes and other vegetables sell cheaply during the peak harvest
season but sell rather exorbitantly during the latter part of the year.
Such a situation is untenable for a country that aspires to stem hunger,
create wealth and reduce poverty. Unfortunately, previous attempts by
government to solve the problem through processing and preservation failed
miserably. But our research institutes, particularly the Food Research
Institute, Industrial Research Institute, Crop Research Institute and the
Biotechnology and Nuclear Research Institute together with the universities,
have taken up the challenge to process some of our agricultural produce such
as yam, plantain, cassava, potatoes and fruits as well as get our farmers
better planting materials and high yielding and disease-resistant varieties
of these crops. The next thing is to add value to our agricultural produce
to keep prices stable and prevent post-harvest losses considerably. But the
crucial stage in our agricultural development is the packaging of our
processed foods and vegetables to be able to sell competitively on the
international market.
It is for this reason that The Chronicle is delighted at the news of the
birth of the Institute of Packaging, which is to promote the networking and
cooperation among stakeholders to develop the science and art of packaging
and marketing in Ghana. It is our hope that the Institute will become a boon
to our agricultural and industrial sectors, which stand to benefit
enormously from its operations. Good packaging is a prerequisite for
reaching out to both the local and international markets on a competitive
scale.
We therefore wish the Institute the best of luck and hope that it will
measure up to international standards and assist in improving the packaging
of our agricultural produce as Ghana strives to become the gateway to the
West African sub-region.

Original_Search_Title: IMO Threatens to Withdraw Autonomous Status Granted Communities If...
Headline: Nigeria: IMO Threatens to Withdraw Autonomous Status Granted Communities If...
Body_Text:
Owerri — IMO State government has vowed to withdraw the autonomous status granted any community or local council area, "if the gesture turns to be a source of discord and loss of precious lives and property for the recipients". The Deputy Governor, Engr. Ebere Udeagu, read the riot act at Nkwo Mbaa, Isiala Mbano local government area, during this year's new yam festival of the people of Ugiri Mbano.
"This state and her people have been known for their peaceful disposition, even in the face of serious provocation. We should do everything possible to sustain this trend", Udeagu appealed. He said that the new yam festival, falls within the state government's cultural re-orientation programme, adding that in line with the state government's agricultural programme, "this administration has decided to distribute fertilizer through the 27 local government chairmen in the state, in preparation for next year's farming season, as well as to ensure equitable distribution of the farm input".
Earlier in his address, the Transition Committee Chairman of Isiala Mbano local government area, Chief Simeon Iwunze, urged the people of Ugiri Clan, to use the occasion of the new yam festival, to resolve to make the area self sufficient in food production, through embarking on aggressive agriculture.
"This Council has already set the pace by distributing palm seedlings to farmers. We are also prepared to do more for farmers, if they show real interest to embrace agriculture", Iwunze assured the people.
While disclosing that his administration has cleared the backlog of staff salaries which he inherited from the past tansition committee, Iwunze also said that he is not owing any worker a dime, as salaries.
"This Council has completed plans to wage serious war against erosion and would embark on full scale road rehabilitation during the current dry season", Iwunze said.
In the address presented by the people of Ugiri Clan, they community requested for, among other things, the citing of a cottage hospital at Ugiri, "to ease the burden of women who travel long distances for childbirth.
They also called for immediate rehabilitation of their roads, which according to them, "are in terrible shape".

Original_Search_Title: A Road Map to a Better Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: A Road Map to a Better Nigeria
Body_Text:
Keynote address to the investiture ceremony of the Organization of Responsible Men and Women for Peace in Nigeria at the Airport Hotel, Ikeja, on Saturday, September 13, 2003, by Alhaji (Dr.) Lateef Kayode Jakande, Chairman, National Advisory Committee.
The chairman of this occasion, The eminent persons on the high table, the patrons and matrons, distinguished guests, ladies and gentlemen, I welcome you to this historic ceremony. It is the first outing of the Organization of Responsible Men and Women for Peace in Nigeria. As the name implies, the organization was founded to promote peace in all possible ways throughout the length and breadth of Nigeria. We all know that this is a herculean task. With conflicts in various parts of the country, violence of different gradations, with a scale of insecurity that has no precedent in our country, Nigeria is in dire need of peace. Peace, in its broadest sense, can be described as the absence of war. But the absence of war is not the only description of peace. Where there is discord, where there is dissension, where there is fear, where there is depression, where there is widespread hunger there can be no peace except the peace of the graveyard. Yet the most desirable state of mind and state of society is peace. So desirable is this commodity, that one of the greatest religions of the world is called Islam, the Arabic word for peace.
Our organization has established a committee whose function is to identify possible threats to peace and to do all that it can to promote the removal or amelioration of such threats. We cannot pretend that the committee can do much more than touch the fringe of this national problem.
Fortunately, the Federal Government has set up a National Peace Forum to undertake a crusade for peace nation wide. The forum was inaugurated on Thursday, September 4, 2003, in Abuja. We pledge our support to this forum and look forward to working together for the peace and stability of our dear country.
Let me say, however, that the basic cause of the national problem is beyond peace Forums and Peace Committees. The President summed it up very precisely when he said recently in Odeda, Ogun State: "Our problem is that we are suffering in the midst of plenty. Despite her abundant rich soil and natural resources, the citizens are still fighting poverty, food insecurity and unemployment. There is hardly any soil in Nigeria that is not rich for good production. If the country could pay attention to agriculture it would soon be richer than most oil producing nations."
My disappointment is that the President did not prescribe any meaningful solution to the national crisis that envelopes this country and which he analyzed so very clearly. I submit that the suffering of Nigerians in the midst of tremendous wealth is conclusive evidence of the failure of our national economic policies. It is not an inevitable lot of the Nigerian people. It is a cankerworm that can be exorcized and killed for ever.
To this end, I propose a new Road Map to a Better Nigeria. The first feature of this road map is a full scale agricultural revolution that involves all sectors of the Nigerian society. I know some functionaries of federal and state governments have been talking of one project or another to boost" farming or "help" farmers but these projects are at best paying lip service to agricultural development. We must move away from the criminal neglect of agriculture which characterizes our national economy.
According to the Central Bank of Nigeria, in the financial year ended 31st of December, 2002 the total revenue in the Federation Account was N1,731,837,.5million. Out of this, revenue from crude oil was Nl,230,851.2million. The non-oil revenue was N500,986.3million made up of companies income tax, Customs and excise duties, VAT and privatization proceeds. There is no mention of agricultural exports. According to the same source, aggregate value of exports in 2002 was N187,419.00 million. Crude oil dominated the market, accounting for 87..9 per cent of total exports. In 2001 it was 91.9 per cent. Non-oil sector was N94,652.65million. Thus agricultural exports are very negligible, virtually non-existent. This deplorable situation can continue only at the peril of the peace, stability and welfare of the Nigerian people.
I call for an agricultural revolution which will include massive subsidy for this very crucial sector of our national economy. I am aware that the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund are opposed to agricultural subsidy in developing countries. But we must reject their objection and proceed with our own economic salvation. Only recently Mozambique, which used to be one of the largest producers of cashew nut in the world market suffered collapse of the industry when the World Bank insisted that Mozambique should end subsidies to the industry.
The irony of the World Bank position is that the United States government buys wheat from American farmers and stores it for use in international politics. In any year when the stores are not exhausted before the beginning of another planting season, the United States orders the farmers not to plant wheat in the new year and pays them for the wheat not planted. Apart from the United States, the European Union spends billions of dollars every year to subsidize agriculture in its member countries. What then is the sense or the justice in opposing subsidies for agriculture in developing countries?
In Europe, the subsidy paid on each cow is $2.50. The United States spends $ 3.9billion on subsidy for cotton growers. Mr. James Wodfensan, World Bank President, gives the size of subsidy to agriculture in developed countries as $350billion annually. This is nearly $1billion a day. Why then must it be a sin for the developing countries to subsidize their agriculture?
Apart from massive subsidy, the Federal Government should establish depots in Abeokuta (for Ogun and Lagos States); Osogbo (for Oyo and Osun States); Akure (for Ekiti and Ondo States); Benin City (for Edo and Delta States); Calabar (for Cross River and Akwa Ibom States); Port Harcourt (for Bayelsa and Rivers States); Jos (for Plateau and Benue States); Kano (for Kano and Jigawa States); Kaduna (for Kaduna and Katsina States); Sokoto (for Kebbi, Zamfara and Sokoto States); Minna (for Niger and Nasarawa States); Maiduguri (for Borno and Yobe States); Yola (for Adamawa and Taraba States); Owerri (for Imo and Abia State); Enugu (for Enugu and Anambra States); Bauchi ( for Gombe and Bauchi States); Ilorin (for Kwara and Kogi States).
In these depots the Federal Government should buy specified crops at fixed prices.
The following crops should be purchased: Maize, cassava, cotton, cashew nut, groundnut, millet , cocoa, soya beans, banana, palm oil, palm kernel, coconut, rice, and sorghum.
Payment should be made on delivery.
This arrangement will provide for all crop farmers an assured market for their products. I have no doubt that several Nigerians now crowded in urban areas like Lagos, Kaduna , Enugu, Port Harcourt will find their way home and revive the abandoned farms of their families and their ancestors.
The Federal Government should then approach the manufacturing sector to get them to buy the crops for use as raw materials. Maize is used by the beer factories. Oil is needed by the soap industry, cotton is used by the textile industry. The world export market should be fully explored for each crop.
For the purpose of mobilization, coordination, supervision, and marketing there should be established a Production and Marketing Board for each group of commodities. For example, tubers, grains, oils, cocoa, rubber and cotton, fruits, livestock, fishery and forestry.
I have no doubt in my mind that this agricultural revolution will impact on all categories of our society. In particular it will impact positively on:
(a)Poverty
(b)Unemployment
(c)Food insecurity
(d ) Industrial growth
(e)Transportation
(f) Building and construction
(g)Commerce
(h)External trade and
(i)Housing
Thus, at the end of this road, we shall have a better Nigeria in peace, stability, prosperity and national cohesion.
Ladies and gentlemen, I am totally convinced that this agricultural revolution will transform Nigeria into a better country of which every Nigerian will be proud. Our economy will blossom in every sector. Prosperity will go round. And our nation will know peace.
A few statistics
Let me give you a few statistics of the great potential of growth which is within our reach. Many years ago, some Malaysians visited Nigeria and discovered our palm tree. It attracted them since they had no palm tree in their country . They took our seedlings to their country. To their greatest delight our palm tree grew in Malaysia and brought a new dimension to the economy of that country.
Today the palm oil is the principal foreign exchange earner of Malaysia. She exports 6,800,000 tonnes of palm oil annually. It has produced twenty byproducts of palm oil. One of these is petrol fuel.
In contrast, the total production of palm oil in Nigeria in the financial year ended December 31, 2002 was 884,000 tonnes, a far cry from the output of the people of Malaysia. Malaysia is now the largest producer and the largest exporter of palm oil in the world. Suppose Nigeria decided to increase production from 884,000 tonnes to 8 million tonnes?
Another crop with which we are all very familiar is cocoa. Before the advent of crude oil, cocoa was Nigeria's principal foreign exchange earner. This is no longer so. The total production of cocoa in the year 2002 was 172,000 metric tonnes. This represents about 7 per cent of world production. Ghana produced 15 per cent while Cote d'Ivoire produced 43 per cent. There is no reason why Nigeria cannot overtake Cote d'Ivoire as the largest producer and exporter of cocoa. The Cocoa Association of Nigeria has set a target of producing l million metric tonnes of cocoa every year. This target, if achieved, will put cocoa ahead of crude oil as the principal foreign exchange earner. That target is achievable. We have the land. We have the people. What we lack is the political will to increase our production from 172,000 tonnes a year to one million.
In the agricultural revolution which I advocate, this target will be pursued and met. This beautiful country of ours is blessed with other crops with equally great potentials. For example, the Benue State branch of the Cassava Farmers Association of Nigeria has estimated that Nigeria can earn N8.4trillion from cassava exports. Targets will also be set for maize, cotton, soya beans, rice, rubber, sorghum, groundnuts, coffee, cashew, millet, wheat, banana, coco nut, yam and cocoyam,. A similar programme will be worked out for Livestock, fishery and forestry.
Finally, in this programme of agricultural revolution, at least 1000 farming settlements will be established in each state of the Federation and agriculture will be made a compulsory subject in all levels of our education system.
Mr. Chairman, fellow Nigerians, I have concentrated in this address on my proposal for agricultural revolution. But this blessed country of ours has enormous mineral resources crying out for aggressive development. They include petroleum, gas, cassiterite, columbite, coal, limestone, gold, silver, diamond, and iron ore. These mineral resources must be fully developed both for domestic consumption and for export.
That is the road to a better Nigeria.
I thank you.

Original_Search_Title: Why Nigeria Economy is in Trouble, By Presidential Adviser on Agriculture Ogbeh
Headline: Nigeria: Why Nigeria Economy is in Trouble, By Presidential Adviser on Agriculture Ogbeh
Body_Text:
Abuja — Presidential Adviser on Agriculture and Food Security, Chief Audu Ogbeh,
yesterday predicted doom for the Nigerian economy in the next three years
except urgent steps were taken to stop importation of agro- based products
and foods which can be sourced or produced locally.
Besides, Ogbeh raised alarm over the rising level of unemployment and
poverty in the country despite the poverty alleviation initiatives mounted
by the Federal Government, blaming the situation on importers of food and
agro-based products which are produced within the country.
Ogbeh, National Chairman of the ruling- Peoples Democratic Party (PDP),
spoke at an expo for Vladimerets range of tractors and agricultural
implements and fertilizers staged at the Nicon Hilton Hotel Abuja by an
agro-based firm, Nigerian Investment Co-operative Agency (NICA).
Reeling out statistics of imported foods and agro-based products, Ogbeh
disclosed that Nigeria spends at least $750 million annually to import rice,
about N400 million on cassava and other related products and another N400
million on sugar and milk.
"The argument has always been that it is cheaper to import every
thing. For it takes one person to bring in a shipload of 10, 000 tonnes of
rice, at the same time half a million Nigerians will produce 10, 000 tonnes
of rice per annum given the very low level of mecahanisation. So for one
ship load of rice that arrives, 500,000 Nigerians are denied their jobs and
earnings" Ogbeh said .
"It's quite convenient to say you can import sugar and beef, where on
earth can you import yams from? Because nobody grows yams as much as Nigeria
and nobody consumes like Nigeria. Where will you import your garri? Nobody
produces as much cassava as we do. We are now producing at 40, 00 tonnes per
annum. This means that we must try to rethink agriculture and also intensify
mechanisation" he added.
Citing the example of the state of Punjab in India which he said has a
population of 29 million but has a total of 400, 000 tractors to support
mechanised farming, Ogbeh queried: "I would like any one of you to tell me
how many tractors your state of origin has?".
The Presidential Adviser regretted that the neglect of the agricultural
sector was responsible for the high level of unemployment in the
country.
"What is the cost of a young graduate who spends seven years to get a
three year -degree and roams the streets for another seven years in search
of jobs. If you look at this in terms of just the value of Naira and the
landing cost, its cheaper but if you look at the over all cost to the
country and the agony it creates, it is certainly more expensive to import,"
he added.
Decrying the level of unemployment, Ogbeh disclosed that about 16
million Nigerian youths were in the primary schools, 4 million in secondary
schools and over one million seeking university education out of which only
250, 000 get admission. He said the universities and other tertiary
institutions were producing annually a total of 500,000 graduates that had
no jobs to do.
This situation, he lamented, has led to increase in the rural- urban
migration which he said was at 5 per cent per annum, placing Nigeria as one
of the countries with the highest level of rural-urban drift.
"Nigeria has the highest migration level in the world now with nearly 5
per cent per annum. It means that cities like Abuja, Lagos and Port Harcourt
will become impossible to manage in the next 15 to 20 years" he noted adding
that this will create a high crime rate as in Brazil which has a high crime
rate because only 18 per cent of the population are living in rural
areas.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Blames Nigeria's Problems On Past Leaders
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Blames Nigeria's Problems On Past Leaders
Body_Text:
Lagos — President Olusegun Obas-anjo has blamed the nation's problems on the
inability of leaders to take decisive actions that would affect the nation
positively.
Speaking through his Special Adviser on Legal and Constitutional
Matters, Chief Ojo Madueke, during the New Yam Festival ceremony organized
by Ndi Igbo in Lagos State at the weekend, Obasanjo said the problems facing
the nation today were created by past leaders who because of fear could not
take decisions that would yield good results.
The president, who enjoined Nigerians to endure the current policies
being carried out, said he understood the hardship families go through in
the nation today.
He, however, reiterated that Nigerians should join hands in building the
nation for a better future.
"I accept that initially some degree of discomforts will be experienced
as occurred in other countries and societies that made the necessary
sacrifice which we are now making. Be more assured that as your president
that I understand and feel, and I can also relate to the difficulties which
families are going through at present but I invite you to join me to see the
light at the end of the turned and to strife through the current policies
which is aimed at putting the nation's resources into more productive use
both for today and for our future", he said.
He urged Nigerians not to consider the pains of whatever they are going
through now but that they should be more concerned about the aftermath of
the deregulation policy adding that the plan is to make every civil servant
own his personal house and car.
"The harvest of new yam, which you experience today is because it was
planted yesterday, without planting today, you cannot harvest tomorrow", he
added.
Speaking on the new yam ceremony, the president said, "In celebrating
the annual Igbo Yam Festival, we rightly celebrate the outstanding qualities
that have defined Ndi Igbo as hardworking and responsible people. I salute
these qualities, especially when they are resourcefully deployed in the
direction of national unity and national greatness as many living Nigerian
nationalists of Igbo extraction has been deservedly acknowledge to have
done".
Obasanjo said the occasion afforded him the opportunity to thank Ndi
Igbo Lagos State for the general support they gave him during the last
elections. Praising the courage of the Ndi Igbo, the president said the
ongoing reforms were intended to facilitate conditions for small enterprise
and wealth creation, attributes which Ndi Igbos have been proudly known to
demonstrate to the benefit of the nation. "These reforms will unleash true
liberalization. When the benefits of these reforms begin to be realized in
no distant future, our nation will prosper sufficiently enough to take care
of all our citizens and not just a few members of the society nor the
privileged elites", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Chieftaincy And Security in Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Chieftaincy And Security in Nigeria
Body_Text:
...an overview of non-centralised East/West Niger Igbo
Governor Chimaroke Nnamani of Enugu State, in this contribution to the
National Conference on Chieftaincy and Security in Nigeria, dedicated to the
40th anniversary of the ascension to the throne of His Royal Highness, the
Emir of Kano, Alhaji (Dr.) Ado Bayero delved into history to highlight the
political system of the Igbo, people of the South Eastern parts of
Nigeria.
Long before this time, I have had the beckoning to grace this great city
of Kano, if only to once again behold the great historical significances
upon which the vast culture of rulership and cohesion has taken place for
well over a thousand years now.
Of course, Kano is a lure, an attraction and a compelling invitation to
a trip in history. It is a reminder of the greatness of our country, both in
vast material as well as abundant human resources. It is a pointer to the
diffusion of entrepreneurial potentials of every part of Nigeria and indeed
among every community of Nigerians.
Indeed, the cosmopolitan proficiency of Kano and the near polyglot
status remain remarkable indexes to measure its possibilities vis-Ã -vis the
boisterousness of the people, who though are ruled by one of the longest
monarchies in the land, continue to reveal a certain class of dynamism and
growth in all spheres of life.
Of course, it cannot be treated lightly that Kano, as a vastly
significant national territory remains one single international centre which
has held attractions for centuries of inter-continental initiatives in
commerce, diplomacy, territorial expansion, military enterprise and more. It
therefore, cannot be surprising that the anniversary of the Kano Monarchy
decided to pursue the course of honour through a broadening of knowledge as
in this elaborate seminar to mark forty years on the saddle. If this is not
the first of its kind, it is the first I have had the privilege of taking a
part in. The gesture itself is suggestive of intention to pursue that which
outlives generations and which will bring about an enhancement of the
system.
Indeed, it is a commendable trip in statesmanship for the Monarchy in
Kano to seek to broaden the knowledge of the citizenry on such knotty issue
as chieftaincy and security. There is no contesting the fact that these are
matters demanding resolution but which must be treated with utmost care and
maturity. This is so because whereas it is easy to ascertain security roles
for chieftaincies in centralized politics in Nigeria, it is not that easy in
such diffuse polities where definite and irreversible class defining
structures are absent and where rank is not permanent as it is open to
all.
And whereas it is easy to view centrality of authorities as running in
tandem with swift and more effective patterns of social coercion, the
prevalence of traditional order of consensus compel consultation in the less
central polities, revealing its own promises in presenting a local version
of democracy which would aid our joining the more global arena of
pluralism.
To that effect, I consider the topic assigned me to divest in two main
dominant factors. One is chieftaincy while the other is security. And for
the typical non-centralised polity, it can, arguably, be difficult to
quickly situate the possibilities of a cohesive, non-coercive, social
control mechanism at confirmation, which is completely diffused,
non-centralised or even unknowingly democratic.
Postulations holding the possibilities of efficacy of single-source
authority as replicable scenarios in places as multi-faceted as a majority
of the Igbo areas of Nigeria can even be contested, if perceived as
incongruous. And for the newcomer or visitor, it is certainly difficult to
discern such factors of social relations upon which a semblance of central
frame for security is built.
Indeed such visitor may not have had the right mind penetration or
sufficient observation to appreciate the dynamics, which keep the society
running. He thus may be circumscribed to pursuing an interpretation of the
patterns of the society on the outlines of centralized and charted
incidences of statecraft. It is even more complex for an observer who may
not have trained his mind in the observation of such evolutionary trends,
some of which present diffusive tendencies manifest in political incidents
tending to multiplicity and attenuation in potency. Consequently a semblance
of culture, to him erroneously stands as one and the same, but in fact, the
trends are vigorously disparate.
But in reality, African societies have witnessed their own fair share of
continuous changes, with each phase of development representing a temporary
or transient movement in the historical and dialectical continuum.
Lately, it has become necessary to compare societies, with reference to
some particular aspects or parts of the whole social system with reference,
for example, to the economic system, the political system, or even kinship
patterns. In most cases, sparing incidents or even accidents of history
bring about very narrow interpretations by a vocal few who mount a voluble
but erroneous claim of insight into the polity in question.
In African Political Systems (1940), edited by M. Fortes and
E.E. Evans-Pritchard, two broad categories of polities are identified. One
is the institutionalized (centralized) political structure in which
cleavages of wealth, privilege and status correspond to the distribution of
power and authority. The other is the segmentary lineage system, which lacks
centralized authority but, of course, wherein there are such systems where
there are no sharp or marked divisions of rank, status or wealth. The latter
model has been variously characterized as stateless or acephalous
societies. But instead of considering such noncentralised polities as
anarchical, we sometimes view them as chaotic and therefore ungovernable. In
a way, since such societies are not narrowed into a straight rulership
pattern, permitting unquestioning exploitation, they are termed
ungovernable. But, perhaps to avoid running away with the charge of being
impolitic, the conveners of this seminar, politely tagged such a societal
model - non-centralized.
In dealing with political systems, we are inevitably dealing with the
structures and processes of the maintenance or establishment of social order
within a territorial unit. Centralized political systems had had to deal
with the issue of a centrally imposed social order through the
instrumentality of organized exercise of coercive authority by means of the
use, threat or the possibility of use, of physical force.
Thus, the centralized traditional African political systems present
little difficulty in this regard. The problematic, however, is in dealing
with such multiplicity of societies as the so-called "stateless", or
"anarchic" or "acephalous" politics, which obviously lack the capacity to
impose social order by means of a pretension to the exercise of coercive
authority, including threat or possibility of use of physical force. This is
especially so, considering that the absence of a coercive authority did not
in any case eliminate altogether the overwhelming potential for conflicts
and disputes or the resolutions of these, even in non-centralised
polities.
In order to put the issues in clear perspective, we have to establish
here that the challenge of the basic subject here is in the matters of
chieftaincy and security, while conceptual puzzles arise from issues of
context associated with stereotypes. These are "stateless," "acephalous",
"anarchical" etc.
In the words of Walter Rodney (1980) "... the word stateless is
carelessly or even abusively used: but it does describe those peoples who
had no machinery of government coercion and no concept of a political unit
wider than... the village. After all, if there is no class stratification in
a society, it follows that there is no state because the state arose as an
instrument to be used by a particular class to control the rest of society
in its own interests. Generally speaking, one can consider the stateless
societies as among the older forms of socio-political organisaiton in
Africa, while the large states represented an evolution away from
communalism (non-centrality) - sometimes to the point of feudalism
(centralised monarchy)."
Elsewhere, 'acephalous' may be employed in describing the political
structure in a simple society, such as a territorial community, which is
united by the rule of law, but lacking a distinct head, without a leader. It
can also be societies in which the largest political unit embraces a group
of people, all of whom are united to one another by ties of kinship, in such
a manner that political relations are coterminous with kinship relations and
the political structure and kinship organization arc completely fused.
There are societies in which a lineage structure is the framework of the
political system thereby being a precise co-ordination between the two, in
such a way that they are consistent with each other, though each remains
distinct and autonomous in its own sphere.
Harold Barclay (1982) characterizes "anarchical" or "anarchic" in the
sense of societies, which do not accept the idea of authority as natural. In
fact, it does not quite appear to occur to them. To that effect, "Anarchy is
the condition of society in which there is no ruler; a society without
government and without the state... it is interesting to note here the
similarity between anarchism and the segmentary lineage system
characteristic of many anarchic polities, especially in Africa. In both
cases, the sum is composed of segments and each segment of subsegment. In
both cases, the most effective authority is in the smallest unit, decreasing
directly as one ascends to broader levels of integration."
Although anthropologists have, over the past several decades,
documented, through their ethnographic research, in innumerable stateless or
acephalous societies throughout the world and throughout time, this paper is
not oblivious nevertheless of the considerable reluctance to define these
societies as pure anarchies. Even amongst anthropologists, there are those
so imbued with their own cultural traditions that they will go to any length
to avoid recognizing these systems for what they are. Since they contend
that social order can exist only in a situation of existence of state,
government and law, they stretch the meaning, nay significance, of these
terms to cover what is clearly not government or state at all.
Peter Hammond (1964) had remarked in this regard:
"Even when the population is large, relatively dense, and somewhat
diversified, the absence of government does not necessarily imply the
presence of anarchy." By the same token, it has been observed that, among
students and historians alike, about the most firmly held myth is the one
that no society can exist, least of all function, without government. Its
mythical corollary that every society must have a head, an identifiable and
visible one at that is also pandemic among the aforesaid group. Thus, the
myth of the necessity of the state and of government continues to hold
decisively true for many. While this might seem inevitable in today's modern
world, there is no disputing the fact that the states and governments have
not always existed in such sense of pursuing absolute and definable
centrality. In most cases, there are many states that are, strictly
speaking, products of recent political history or results of colonial
political engineering or trade-offs.
Prof. M.A. Onwuejogwu, the renowned Nigerian anthropologist, has
questioned the classification of certain traditional African societies as
stateless, a position he shares with scholars like Profs. Lambert Ejiofor
and Ikenna Nzimiro.
Stoutly, they contend "suffice it to say at this juncture that there is
a general recognition in anthropological surveys of a complex web of social
organizations that fall short of states, and more particularly that were
lacking in centralised political authority patterns, pronounced social
stratification and advanced role differentiation. Disputes of intellectual
nature could routinely attend the criteria for the classification of these
models but this can in no way vitiate the substantive defining
characteristics of the system of social organisation that these represent."
They cannot be faulted in the ensuing truism of such defining
characteristics which include communal solidarity, collective action,
horizontal political structures placing premium on leadership instead of
authority, absence of role specialization or class differentiation. etc. It
is our contention here that all of these features or a combination of same
constitute a representative pattern depicting organisation and direction
even as it never presented a scenario of coercion or forcible pursuit of a
one-sided view of governance.
Scattered throughout the continent south of the Sahara, Harold Barclay
(1982) argues, "are dozens of anarchic societies, some of which are the most
populous of all anarchic communities". Among these are a variety of
segmentary lineage systems that are autonomous and self-managing. Social
order is imposed and maintained by means of equivalence and opposition; a
template of diffusion of power that thrives on a regulatory framework of
diffuse sanctions.
An outline of a survey of these polities yields the Anuak, Mandari,
Dinka and Neur (in southern Sudan), the Konkomba (northern Togo), the
Lugbara (parts of Uganda and Congo DR), Shona (Zimbabwe), the Tonga
(Zambia), the Tallensi (Ghana), the Igbo, Tiv (Nigeria) etc. The list is by
no means exhaustive. And at the same time, anarchical.
The character of traditional Igbo social organization (west or east
Niger) is a highly contentious topic, and it will probably remain so for a
long time to come. Characterized variously as a model of "stateless" or
"acephalous" society, and at other times as a quintessential republican
polity, the structure of the Igbo political economy, in its intrinsic
manifestation, is, increasingly, being refracted as "anarchic" by social
scientists. Not a few historians and a sprinkle of anthropologists have
continued to insist that there, in fact, existed kingdoms in the Igbo
traditional setting.
The village - a commune of sorts -provides the fulcrum of social
existence and so underlines the context of Igbo characteristic
diffusion. Yes, the village delicately shapes the Igbo world view,
interaction patterns and social relationships. The Igbo village setting
evinces a complex web of ties and bonds, of roles and responsibilities, of
complementarities and asymmetries, and of equivalence and opposition. It is
usually a tapestry of views, group and individual aspirations bending to the
yearnings of the community assembly - Oha na Eze. In this way, the society
continually strives towards equilibrium and consensus.
Lest we forget, the Igbo traditional society was, at all material times,
a living organism consisting of individuals and groups, of segments of the
extended family and the lineage. Therefore, the diffuse way of living which
we refer to here can actually be located in the concrete realm: in the
people's pronounced sense of social equality; in the prevalence of
horizontal political organization that emphasized leadership in
contradistinction to a vertical, hierarchical and centralized political
structure which emphasized authority; in the unstructured, bonding together
of the village, the lineage and the extended thmily which spontaneously
expresses itself in collective action, solidarity and diffusion of social
sanctions, etc.
But whereas this formed the socio-political configuration of a great
bulk of the lgbo world, centrality of administration was a reality and
indeed prevalent in some noted Igbo kingdoms.
Among these were the Umuezechima group of clans of western Igbo or what
Ejiofor dubbed "Umuezechima Kingdoms", in addition to the so-called "four
Niger States" elaborated in Prof. lkenna Nzimiro's Studies in Igbo political
systems (1972).
The nine village kingdoms of the Umuezechima clan include "Onicha Ugbo,
Onicha Ukwu, Onicha Olona, Obior, Obomkpa, Ezi, lssele-Ukwu, lssele-Azagba,
and Issele-Mkpituime. Nzimiro's four Niger States consisted of Oguta,
Onitsha, Ossomari and Aboh. Other parts of the traditional Igbo society that
established the equivalent of "village kingdoms" were Nri, Arochukwu (East
Niger) and Asaba and Agbor (West Niger)".
Ejiofor therefore argued "The traditional lgbo systems may he divided
into two major types, namely, the democratic and monarchical". He continued:
"west of the Niger, village kingdoms are the rule rather than the exception
in Igbo communities". Furthermore, he observed that even the democratic
model did have chiefs, but quickly added, "these were at best symbolic heads
of village groups ... and their primacy was honorific rather than
jurisdictional". Of this class of chiefs, G. I. Jones noted (in 1950) thus:
"...chiefs of the type envisaged... as "strong chiefs" (except) with a few
exceptions (did) not exist in this region. The people who are usually
referred to as chiefs, and there can be any number of them today, have no
executive, judicial or legislative powers vested solely in their office."
Chief Obafemi Awolowo similarly observed that "save in very few places
there were no natural rulers in Eastern Nigeria of the stature and
jurisdiction of those in the North, West or Midwest" (Benin areas).
But Lugard "manufactured" them, as wealthy and influential persons were
made "natural rulers" by warrant. On his appointment, a warrant chief became
the paramount for a specified area, enjoying the same authority and
privileges and subject to the same limitations as a natural ruler in the
North or West or Midwest. Even this daring innovation succeeded for well
over a decade.
For the purposes of a holistic perspective to the contending issues, I
wish to call attention at this juncture to Prof. Onwuejogwu's seminal
thoughts in this regard which led him to classify the political system of
the Igbo Culture Area into three broad categories, namely, the centralized,
gerontocratic and consensus or non-centralized models.
According to this classification, the centralized model is characterized
by segmentary lineages, theocratic or secular kingship, less differentiated
Ozo or Eze title system and age grades and associations, which could be
located in Nri, Onitsha, Aboh, Ogwashi-Uku, Issele-Uku, Oguta, Agbor and
Arochukwu.
The characteristics of the gerontocratic model include segmentary
lineages, chiefship or headship and sometimes, hyper-gerontocracy, less
differentiated Ozo or Eze title system, hyper-age grades with key examples
as Ibagwani, Ibusa, llla, Okpanam, Asaba etc. The non-centralized model with
Owerri, Mbaise and Ngwa as classical examples contains features such as
segmentary lineages, age-grades, undifferentiated Ozo or Eze title system,
councils and associations. These wield sound social muscles which when
applied can exert maximum weight and compel alteration or proper
conditioning of the individual and group.
Complex and dynamic as the evolutionary trend of Igbo social control
mechanism is, the archetypal political system retains a strong element of
the segmentary lineage system, which is known as "Umunna", which in
extension is as politically potent as it is corrective of deviance.
The principal elements in the comparative differentiation of political
systems in traditional societies are the degree of specialization in roles
that enter into the political and administrative spheres, the number of
structural levels at which authority is exercised in addition to the context
and changing patterns of the social relationship between those who exercise
authority - be it horizontal or vertical - and the rest over whom authority
is exercised.
It is by appreciating that social diffusion with its inherent
non-centrality of features, that we can begin to come to terms with the fact
that although some Igbo communities had managed to construct centralized,
monarchical systems prior to the earliest contact with the white man, on the
aggregate, such communities constituted a minority, albeit a significant one
at that.
Invariably, not one of these communities or kingdoms managed to make the
vital transition from communalism to full-blown centrality. We speak of
centrality in this sense as a specific mode of statecraft, as well as a
system of social organization, including the control of deviant
behaviours. The point can hardly be overstated that the period of transition
from diffuse social system to a level of centrality - in those African
societies that managed to advance beyond non-centrality - was one of state
formation.
The roles of institutions such as the village general assembly, with the
village square (Obodo) as chambers, the age grade society, coupled with
those of the daughters' assembly (Umuada), the masquerade society, the
secret society and the Ozo title society were intertwined, complementary and
inevitably mutually reinforced each other. Their essence and vitality lay in
their functionality, reverence and sheer effectiveness in achieving social
cohesion and broad consensus. In many cases, they served the diplomatic
roles and effectively broke deadlocks for the society to move forward. In
some other cases, they interpret the norms of the society and cry for
enforcement of social control to terminate deviancy. In fact, security in
the typical Igbo setting is matter for social control and reordering.
In each case of course, each village was autonomous and managed its own
affairs in spheres as diverse as religion, festivals, medicare,
administration of justice, exercise of sanctions, etc.
Inter-village relations were characterized by the formation of
federations of two or more villages especially in times of war. The extended
family system provided a second-tier thread that held component units of the
village together, enabling them to share and fend for one another in both
good and difficult times.
The question now is this: In the event that it was largely democratic,
it was completely consensus, it was never centered on any powerful monarch,
how would such threatening social conducts such as crime and other deviant
acts be tackled?
According to Prof. Richard Okafor, social control can hardly ever come
to fruition in Igboland if it is a business of an individual. It comes from
established elements of culture, which "protect precious tenets of the
social environment from erosion or degradation." And paradoxically, though
every Igbo community possesses highly individualized citizenry, the
aggregate life is fully shaped according to the cherished traditions,
hallowed secrets and revered institutions that usually ensure that any
member who violates any of them is severely punished. The yam thief is a yam
thief, it does not matter who his kith and kin are, he must be exiled. The
murderer has murdered a life and must, as determined by the degree of crime,
face death or be exiled.
The most potent of Igbo social control systems, which also underlined
appropriate security measures, is the age-old pattern of stigmatizing crime
or any other deviant behaviour. In the sub-cultures along the Anambra River,
Ojebeogene, Ugwunye and Ezedike clans in northern Igboland, it is common to
stigmatise crime by smear public parade (inya ncha or ite unyi) forced on
culprits. In the case of a thief he is hoisted with the stolen item and
paraded through the village square where every person, in passing or on
invitation, inflicts his/her own form of insult to the culprit.
For such deviant behaviours as immorality, infidelity of the wife (mind
you, never of the husband), abortion, adultery, etc, it is usually made
public by specialist minstrels who mock by mimicking such obscene conduct in
socially reprehensible ways.
In the various okumkpo festival scenes in Afikpo, they move in a
conscious, planned, exaggerated manner, saying unorthodox things; acting
differently than they do, and lampooning miscreants and other deviants. That
way, such culprits are thoroughly discredited and stigmatized to the extent
that they may even leave the community for a long period of time. In some
cases as in some northern Igboland, these are never said directly but
insinuated in ways that stigmatise families and kindred. Such may compel a
family or kindred to force or arrange safe passage of the culprit into
exile.
Indeed, the potency of social stigma is such in Igbo world that families
and kindred can pledge away any item, including precious land, to stave off
the stigma, if possible.
Where there is any semblance of central authority, its job is made
easier by this but such personages who are of elevated social ranks in the
land also pass through the same beam of the searchlight of probity and are
brought through the same trial if found wanting.
The fact of Igbo justice system being a leveler rides the belief that
laws are derived from God (divine) and before God, all men are equal. As
pointed out by Okafor, F.U, such belief formed the bases for consolidation
of the diffuse system, which prevented an undertaking of such phenomenal
structuring of the society for the benefit of some privileged
persons. Perhaps, such supremacy of divine laws over man-made laws actually
sustained long adherence to what is adjudged right for the benefit of
long-held traditions.
In reality, attendant upon the fact of Igbo laws being effective
instrument of social harmony, moral rectitude and political order, natural
laws, some of which are considered divine injunctions, form the most potent
foundation for well being as they further underline the elements of
characteristics of man-made laws such as reasonableness, common good,
sufficiency, legitimacy and harmony with traditions.
This foundation consolidates the security of the society where the rules
are made and known and where deviance is interpreted as a burden of family
and kindred.
And although the societal assembly (0ha Obodo, in the case of the whole
community or ama/ogbe in the case of village wards) may represent a
variegation of interests and competitive factors, the possibility of
positions running counter to divine laws stands as checks or restraints to
would-be dominators. This prevents the emergence of a one-sided pursuit or
centrality of order of enactments, which can confer undue privileges and
create avenues for the subversion of the society.
Indeed, Ndigbo see any form of pre-eminence in the village assembly as
an unbridled outspokenness, which must be checked to prevent
tyranny. According to Victor Uchendu, "they are jealous of their legislative
authority and are not ready to surrender it to a small group of
individuals." To this effect, they consider the validity and security of
their social environment on the strength of divine inspiration (not just the
view of man) in the making of the laws, which informs the pattern of
proclaiming a law with the ofo (signifying uprightness) depicting that every
contribution and indeed the law, have been reached according to the custom
of the land. It is like an oath to uphold the laws of the land and never to
be a part of any form of subversion.
But whereas we can say that these sustained the primordial polities,
what obtains at the moment is at best an admixture of the old values and new
ways, most of which confuse the modern man and induce excuses for violation
of the laws. Colonialism and indeed, post colonial African polities,
appeared confused about what should constitute their social evolution and
political order as in rising to the challenges of complex security questions
negating modern mechanisms for restraining of the unbridled quest.
In the case of the modern Igbo societies, what with the multiplicity of
chieftaincies, the negation of the age old social order which effectively
informed character and decency, has expectedly set the stage for an exercise
of such looming communality affirmation which checkmated unbridled
individual assertiveness.
To worsen matters, the stage set by Luggard himself, as in appointing
paramount chiefs just among the rich and influential without regard to
community feeling or preference, seriously removed any form of credibility
that would have been due some of such emerging chieftaincies. In some cases
actually, Luggard just appointed such oppressive middle men either involved
in the hateful slave dealing regime or the down-pricing of produces of which
they (the middle men) reaped bountifully.
But if we excuse Luggard on grounds of ignorance, (and for the fact that
his indirect rule system collapsed there in his face), the manner of
appointing some of the chieftaincies by successive indigenous
administrations seriously altered community prestige and stabilizing
institutions. It has even been worsened by the failure of a majority of
these chiefs to appreciate their own peculiar social environment. They
rather sought an invention of the pomp and grandeur of the empire situations
in Oyo, Sokoto, Kano, Benin and Borno, by initiating and indeed undertaking
expensive ceremonies as a replication of age-old panoplies.
This done in total negation of the true age- old Igbo political and
social scenario described by Isichei. "One of the things that struck the
first western visitors to Igboland", observed the historian, "was the extent
to which democracy was truly practised".
According to her, "an early visitor to a Niger Igbo town said that he
felt he was in a free land, among a free people". Elsewhere, another French
visitor observed of the people that indeed true liberty existed in Igboland,
although its name was not inscribed in any monument.
So, whereas it was true that the institutions of the old order sustained
the socio-political order of that era, particularly with due respect to the
belief system ordaining ceaseless morality and rectitude, the current regime
of social order faces credibility question on account of rejection of such
restraining elements of the social environment which attenuated excessive
assertiveness. In fact, it is in this area of excessive assertiveness, as
pointed out by Emenne, that a vitiation of the fabric of social order was
regrettably consummated.
But all is not lost. The chieftaincies have shown remarkable abilities
in building welters of information network for government, such that
security can be easily guaranteed with movements and peculiar conducts duly
interpreted by the chieftains. This is even enhanced by the emergence of a
new and wealthy chieftaincy class with backgrounds in academics, business,
professions, law, medicine and the other callings, who are capable of
situating their social environments to suit national security
arrangements.
But in the event that it is finally appreciated that the various
chieftaincies have imbibed what it would take to achieve social order, the
deciding question now is on the extent the nation state can tolerate the
national variety as to accommodate distinct polities in security of the
societies. If that is done, and if it is pursued, bearing in mind that most
of security issues rested on local factors the fabric of which is defined in
locality context, the reversal of trends in deviant behaviours would have
been reached and for which we declare, as usual, in Enugu State:
To God be the Glory.
Governor Chimaroke Nnamani, Executive Governor of Enugu State
contributed this to the National Conference on Chieftaincy and Security in
Nigeria, dedicated to the 40th anniversary of the ascension to the throne of
His Royal Highness, the Emir of Kano, Alhaji (Dr.) Ado Bayero.

Original_Search_Title: Free Ramadan Meals: Students Pray for Shekarau
Headline: Nigeria: Free Ramadan Meals: Students Pray for Shekarau
Body_Text:
Students of boarding secondary and special schools in Kano State have
been organising special prayer sessions for Governor Ibrahim Shekarau, over
the special feeding programme introduced for Ramadan fasting period.
Kano Trust reporter who visited Government Girls College, Gezawa and
Government Girls Arabic School, Tudun Wada, met the cheerful students in
their mosques offering the prayers. A senior secondary II student of GGC,
Gezewa, Hafsat Ibrahim, said they have never tasted good and nutritious food
like those supplied during the fasting period.
"Some of us who come from villages cannot get this type of food and we
are very happy with the governor, but we want him to also help us with
water," they said happily. Another student Habiba Abubakar said, "At first I
was not happy that we are going to do fasting in school, because I know the
food will not be sufficient like at home. But now I feel like I am at home,
you know. There is banana, water melon, fried eggs and yam, tea and bread,
etc. May God help the governor to continue to give the best to schools."
Kano Trust discovered that the students are given three meals between
6:30 pm when the fast is broken and 5:00am when it begins. It was also
discovered that the two hand pumps in the school have broken down, forcing
the principal to buy water worth two to three thousand naira per day for
cooking. The principal, Hajiya Hauwa Yakubu Baba, said the PTA is trying to
tackle the water problem in the school, saying all efforts to get assistance
from the local government has failed.
At GGAS Tudun Wada, the students were so elated that they insisted the
principal convey their personal 'thank you' to the governor in Kano the next
day. One of the students, Sadiya Umar, said "I have not tasted egg since
because in my village, we eat egg occasionally. But now we have everything
here. Alhamdullah.
The principal of the college, Hajiya Mariya Aliyu Waziri, told Kano
Trust, that the new diet programme has completely changed the attitude of
the students even towards academic learning. "Even though we have been
giving them good food before but the additional dietary intake has really
made a difference among the students. We serve them two meals during
breaking fast, that is breakfast and dinner, and then Sahur later at night,"
she added.
A sample of the breakfast menu made available to Kano Trust show's yam
pottage, vegetable and fish with paw-paw and dates is served for breakfast
on Fridays while jollof rice with fish is served for diner, and tuwon
shinkafi (mashed rice) with vegetable soup and fish for sahur.
Kano State Commissioner of Education, Alhaji Ishaq Mahmoud, told Kano
Trust that the government increased the daily budgetary feeding allowance
for boarding schools from thirty to fifty naira per student per meal. He
said that the principals are directly in charge of the feeding and would
report back to the ministry after a week's trial for improvement of grey
areas.
He explained that the governor approved the implementation of boarding
student's directory intake because of the special demands of the Ramadan
period. Alhaji Mahmoud said some parents wanted the schools to close because
of Ramadan but doing so will tamper with the school calendar for the
year.
He added, "Islam did not say you should not work during Ramadan, so the
government decided to alleviate the fears of parents by improving the
quality of food given to the students so that they can feel at ease. Ramadan
is not a static month, it can come in August, in November or March. We
cannot keep changing the calendar or adjusting it."
The commissioner said that all the 43 secondary and science schools in
the state are involved in the special Ramadan diet programme.

Original_Search_Title: DCE Cited in Knife- Drawing Incident
Headline: Ghana: DCE Cited in Knife- Drawing Incident
Body_Text:
It was neither corruption, nor was it bribery scandal, but purely abuse
of power, as the District Chief Executive (DCE) for Kintampo, Mr. James
Kwabena Bomfeh, has been cited in a knife-stabbing incident at Kintampo,
involving members of two factions in the Mo chieftaincy dispute.
It was alleged that Bomfeh ordered the Kintampo police to immediately
release one Bawa Peace, who had allegedly stabbed one Stephen Atta in the
neck with a knife on Thursday October 16, this year at about 8 pm at
Kintampo.
The victim told The Chronicle that after a complaint was lodged with the
Kintampo police, they only arrested the culprit briefly and later released
him on the orders of the DCE.
After the release of Bawa Peace, who is a close friend of Bomfeh, he
went to his victim's house to boast that if he had killed him no one could
arrest him because of his money and the presence of the DCE at Kintampo, and
even said after the incident he went and informed the DCE that he was able
"to accomplish the business."
But the DCE denied the allegations, when The Chronicle met him at his
office to ascertain the truth.
Bomfeh told the paper that he was not aware that Stephen Atta had been
stabbed.
He said the victim sustained the injury as a result of a blow landed on
him during scuffle between him and the suspect. Bomfeh however denied the
allegation that he ordered the police to release Bawa Peace, as alleged by
the victim.
When The Chronicle contacted the District Commander of Police for
Kintampo, Assistant Superintendent of Police (ASP) Thomas Osei, he confirmed
that a report was made to his outfit, but said because the case was still
under investigation, he would not like to comment on it.
But with the paper's further interrogation, the police commander said
the DCE never ordered them to release the suspect.
He said he is yet to call for the docket on the case because, as of
then, he had no more details on in the case, however that after he receives
the docket from the investigator, one Inspector Yeboah, he would then give
instructions on what to do.
At the time of The Chronicle investigations, about seven days after the
incident, no statements had been taken from either the suspect, or the
victim.
The police commander said initially when they heard the case they
thought it was one Mr. Dapaa, until when the victim returned from the
hospital.
Narrating the incident to the paper, the victim, Atta, said on Thursday
October 16 the paramount chief for the Mo traditional area, Nana Elarji made
announcement on a local FM station at Kintampo about his intention to
celebrate this year's yam festival at New Longoro, his traditional
headquarters.
The family head of the chief, Opanin Kwabena Nsiah, countered this
announcement, saying Nana Elarji is not a chief and as such has no
prerogative to celebrate the festival, Atta told Chronicle.
He said the family head further warned that any one who would go to New
Longoro for the festival, goes there at his own risk.
He said due to the volatile situation of the issue, the DCE and the
police commander threatened to arrest the chief if he dared celebrate the
festival.
Atta told The Chronicle that later Nana Elarji brought a letter from the
Brong Ahafo Regional House of Chiefs, which indicated that he could go ahead
to celebrate the festival as the paramount chief of the area.
The message of the letter was announced on the same local FM
station. The victim said following this announcement they came to the
chief's palace at Kintampo to have a discussion, because the next morning
they would proceed to New Longoro for the festival.
After the discussion, he and some friends decided to go to the house at
about 8 pm. On their way, according to him, they met Bawa Peace, with one
Kwame Krah, Issahaku and Koo Nkwantah.
Here, Atta said, Bawa Peace insulted them saying "Look at the slaves,
they call themselves chiefs, we shall see whether you are chiefs."
He said one of his friends called "The Boy" also replied him, saying "we
are not slaves." He added that he Atta advised him not to mind Bawa
Peace.
Later "The Boy" and the other friend left to the house, leaving him
alone. After they left, Atta said he saw Bawa Peace coming toward him with a
knife and Kwame Krah started shouting "Bawa stop that! Stop that!" But Bawa
would not mind him.
Atta said all of sudden Bawa approached him with the knife and raised
his hand to stab him, but he managed to dodge the knife and fell into a
gutter. Before then, Issahaku and Krah were chasing him and all those with
him could not come to his aid because of the knife.
"When I fell into the gutter, Kwame Krah came and held me from behind
and put me on the ground and that gave chance to Bawa Peace the chance to
stab me with the knife."
Immediately after he stabbed his victim, he quickly ran away from the
scene, Atta told The Chronicle.
Later, the victim said he went to the police and reported and he was
issued with police medical form to attend the hospital.
He continued that when he went to the Kintampo hospital he was referred
to the Holy Family Hospital at Techiman, where he was admitted and
discharged him the following day after treatment.

Original_Search_Title: The Police And Anambra Elections
Headline: Nigeria: The Police And Anambra Elections
Body_Text:
By the time Governor Ngige was declared a governorship candidate, it was less than two weeks to the election. So what do they do? They went in during the election and did the abracadabra, as I said, and the "anwansiology" (make belief) and before you knew it, they hammered home a "result" and got it announced.
Dr. Chinwoke Mbadinuju in the Sunday Vanguard of October 5, 2003.
Yes. They hammered home fictitious result and got them announced. The hammering was quickly followed up with chest-beating. Said Chief Christian Uba, the beloved caucus leader of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Anambra State: "God merely used me to touch some lives, and mind you, it is not just the Governor and his deputy. There are also three Senators, 10 members of the House of Representatives and 30 members of the House of Assembly of the State. I sponsored them. I put them there although only 29 House of Assembly members eventually made it. This is the first time in the history of Anambra State that one single individual would be putting every public officer in the state in power." (See the Sunday Champion of June 8, 2003.)
Well, things are currently working out to their dazzling conclusions. Following July 10, 2003, the day Governor Ngige was abducted and announced to have resigned his office, the public began to be let into the details of how one superman managed to fix all the elections in a state that probably boasts more educated people than any other state of the federation. The public came to know about Mr. Raphael Ige, the Assistant Inspector General of Police for Zone 9, who led all of 250 heavily-armed mobile policemen from Umuahia in Abia State to Awka in Anambra State to seize and forcibly remove a sitting Governor from his constitutional stool. The public came to know that Ige, now nicely retired, was the muscle and lead bound impetus to the superhuman wonders of the Anambra elections, a man who virtually abandoned his headquarters in Umuahia and camped in Awka, as if there was no Commissioner of Police in Anambra. With Ige's presence at the head of a major instrument of coercion, and " before you knew it, they hammered home a "result" and got it announced." It confirmed the words of Alhaji M. D. Yusufu, a former Inspector General of Police, who said in a Vanguard interview that the rigging of elections was impossible without the connivance and active participation of the police and the agents of intelligence. There is a lesson to learn at this point. If the overdue local government elections will have any meaning when they come to pass; if the general elections of 2007 will have any credibility whatsoever, if nationals of this country will not again and again be led out into the sun and into the rain to waste their time while the criminal and the impertinent hammered home cooked-up results, then something constitutional must be done to contain and reverse police corruption and impunity.
We have all since discovered, or reminded ourselves, that injustice is like acid burns. It takes both time and committed opposition to master. Even after it is redressed, even after the wounds of injustice are healed, the marks remain, at least as a reminder of the tragic time when a people with vision decided to grope about minefields in blinkers. It is nearly six months since the travesty of elections that shocked the people. Yet the reverberations that came in the wake of the hammering of fake results are still with us. Nicholas Ukachukwu got his day at the Election Tribunal which declared that he, not Ugochukwu Uba, won the Anambra South Senatorial election. Ben Obi also got his day when the Tribunal decided that it was he, not Ikechukwu Abana, that won the Anambra Central Senatorial election. A verdict is imminent in the petition filed by Mrs. Joy Emodi who insists that she won the Anambra North Senatorial election, not Emmanuel Anosike who INEC also declared the winner of a House of Representatives seat contested on the same day.
Virtually all the election results hammered home in Anambra State are in contention. In each case, the Anambra Election Petitions Tribunal is only a first judicial port of call. Irrespective of the verdicts given at this level, most of the cases will progress to the Court of Appeal and thence to the Supreme Court. What this means is that, unless you have the financial muscle, you may not have justice. Flawed elections, an avoidable travesty of the democratic process, have created a field day for lawyers, both SANs and non-SANs. But that is the little matter. The bigger problem is that while litigations are in ascent, little job is being done at the executive and legislative arms of governance. Salary backlogs accumulated from an era now elapsed are not being cleared. The bad roads have not been attended to and have been worsened by the rains now on the verge of cessation. Can anyone mention any noteworthy contribution by any Anambra legislator on the floor of any parliament the past six months? There is a lesson in this. There requires a constitutional re-engineering to ensure that, following future elections, nobody declared victorious is sworn in unless and until any and every legal challenge on the election is disposed of. It is in this connection that we recall the disgraceful conduct of the Senate, which rushed to swear-in certain Anambra "Senators-elect" in flagrant violation of injunctions halting the administration of oaths on them by the Election Tribunal.
To return to the police in Anambra elections, the saying is apposite that yam seedling rots to herald the germination of tendril. The police seems to be undergoing a rejuvenation. Take this report by Charles Onyekamuo in ThisDay of October 22, 2003: "A forensic expert, Mr. Edward Kolawale, a deputy superintendent of police (DSP) has faulted as forged forms EC8A(1), and EC8B(1) (result sheets) tendered by the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) in favour of Dr. Chris Ngige, Anambra State governor, with respect to the April 19 governorship election in some local government areas of the state. The All Progressive Grand Alliance (APGA) candidate in the said election, Mr. Peter Obi, is challenging the results of the election INEC declared in 12 local government areas of the state. But Mr. Kolawale in the witness box at the First Election Petition Tribunal in Awka said a substantial part of the forms were written by one person in relation to "wards/polling stations and that there are evidences of discrepancies, irregularities and gross falsification of figures."
"Kolawale, a handwriting analyst and photographer said he carefully examined and analysed certified true copies of forms "EC8A(i)", and "EC8B(i)" adding that the examination and comparison of the forms sent to him were with one another. Led in evidence by Dr. Onyechi Ikpeazu (SAN), counsel to Mr. Obi, Kolawale said he deployed the aid of a Video Spectral Comparator (VSC) and found some fundamental traits between some of the handwritings on the forms vis-a-vis interpolation, over-writing, super-imposition of figures and outright alternation of figures, amongst others. He said he examined and analysed forms EC8A(1) and EC8B(1) from wards and polling stations in Anambra East, Anambra West, Awka North, Onitsha South, Oyi, Njikoka, Idemili South, Ihiala, Aguata, Onitsha North and Orumba South local government areas and believed a substantial part of the said forms were written by one person in some local governments, while in others the writers varied by two or more."
DSP Kolawole deserves to be cheered for striking a blow for justice. It is proper to hope that the police is clearing a mess it helped to create in the first place. Which is why the fears being expressed in certain quarters on further police evidence are surprising. Some claim that Mr. Kolawole and others of his colleagues have been invited to Abuja, to be dressed down and handed a new code of operations at the Election Tribunals. Others say that Kolawole would be pressured to contradict or alter his evidence. A few are saying that an end will be put to handwriting analysis as part of evidence at the Tribunals. Why? "Because there is the fear that handwriting analysis could be used to void most of the results posted in the elections"! In my view, the fears appear unjustified. Time will tell.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Food Stuff Soar in Jigawa
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Food Stuff Soar in Jigawa
Body_Text:
Two days into the Ramadan period, prices of food commodities have risen between 20% and 100% according to investigations in Dutse, Shuwarin and Hadejia markets.
According to Daily Trust investigations, prices of vegetables such as pepper, spinach, "Yakuisi" cabbage, etc have been increased by certain percentages, while pepper, tomatoes etc increased by about 20%.
For instance, in Dutse markets, vegetables formerly sold for N20 is now N60.
Additionally, prices of fruits have considerably risen by almost 100%. The survey indicates that a piece of pineapple sold at N100 is now N300, while a bag of orange sold at N3000 is now N32,00.
Further checks revealed that prices of pawpaw, banana and other fruits have risen by about 50%.
Vegetable oil however varies between N100 and N110, while eggs now sell at N15 instead of N12 per one.
Daily Trust also gathered in Shuwarin and Hadejia, that prices of grains remained stable, but that of other commodities such as Irish potatoes rose from N200 per unit to N250, even as yam tubers also increased by about 20%.
According to a trader, Mallam Kabir Sakwaya, the increase originated from wholesale traders in Kano. He blamed Southern traders for the increase, and called on market leaders to intervene and ensure the stabilisation of prices.
Scholars however, frowned at the development Chief Imam of Dutse government house, Ustaz Habibu Mohammed, enjoined Muslims to be rational and engage in actions that would make people's life easier at the period of holiness.
He said anybody that makes life difficult or uncomfortable through profiteering will certainly face the wrath of Allah.
Ustaz Habibu added that those that engage in positive actions should await Almighty's good tidings.
Several people interviewed in the market described the development as disturbing, while calling on traders to fear Allah and be considerate in their dealings.

Original_Search_Title: Tussle Over New Kpandai Market
Headline: Ghana: Tussle Over New Kpandai Market
Body_Text:
Accra — Mounting pressures from a section of residents in Kpandai who strongly believe that the "Golden Age of Business", as proclaimed by the President, must be allowed to freely undergo through natural processes of the enduring Positive change refinery in order to achieve the optimum set target of zero tolerance to corruption.
But this believe has created displeasure in the Kpandai area and set the tone for possible violent physical confrontation.
This comes amidst allegations levelled against the executing officials who, as a body, were responsible for the initiation, award and execution of the new Kpandai market project.
The agitation directed at the way and manner the project has been managed from commencement through to completion.
According to a resident, Rueben Kwabena Bitemi, 31 bags of cement were stolen sometime ago, and the culprits were arrested.
What is even embarrassing is that these alleged criminals have been left off the hook. They have not been prosecuted. Aside, the work done is shoddy.
Visual vindications show that the architects and engineers must have a lot of work to do to persuade laymen to believe that construction of the project met any district or municipal standards.
However, it has been reported, that the District Chief Executive of the East Gonja District, Bismark Haruna Dare has made several visits to the area to acquaint himself with progress on projects the Assembly has initiated. What remains a mystery is whether the initiation and commencement of projects awarded in the district meet the least measure of transparency.
A unit committee member, Ababio Wack recently said leaders in the area are taking advantage of media searchlight on the area. "Even criminals openly do what they like and go unpunished," Wack said.
Prospective tenants have also complained bitterly about fees being changed. The rent charged is high. A store is going for Â¢2m and a shed is Â¢ 400,000. Other problems are that prices of their commodities are falling coupled with poor patronage and rising cost of transportation have wiped off their profit margins.
Meanwhile, some residents of the area are threatening to disrupt the commissioning of the project to register their resentment at how the project has been handled by the local assembly and the contractor.
Kpandai is a farming community. The area is noted for the production of yams supplying a sizeable percent of the total national annual production.
But the reoccurring violent clashes, if no schemes are promoted to keep idle hands busy - doing something productive - we could wake up one of these days to another violent explosion in our faces.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo is the Saboteur
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo is the Saboteur
Body_Text:
Abuja —  President of the Nigeria Labour Congress, Comrade Adams Oshiomhole,
is not given to double speak or frivolities. He has consistently stood on
