Activities of the day, which climaxed the week long celebration of the new yam festival, referred to locally as ' Eze-Ugbo', had snowballed into mayhem, leading to a break down of law and order which saw the youths confronting and assaulting law abiding citizens and raping women.
The youths had invaded the works Department of the 'A' police station, where they picked iron rods with the intention to further unlease terror on innocent people on the street of Asaba but for the policemen, who resisted them.
Irked by the violent attitude and the invasion of thier station by the youths, the policemen confronted them, battled them to a stand still and succeeded in dispersing them after throughly tear gasing them.
A police source later confirmed the arrest of 19 of the youlths, condemning their attitude while saying that they went too far in thier invasion of the police station.
That Sunday, the youths were seen on Unebisi Road, armed with dangerous weapons, bottles and threatening innocent people, who scampered to safety for fear of being molested by them.
Reacting to the mayhem at a media briefing in Asaba, the State Commissioner of Police, Mr. charles Akaya, said the rampaging youths also dispossessed an inspector of police of his uniform.
He gave the number of the peugeot 504 saloon car burnt by them as DT18A19, belonging to the State Ministry of Economic Planning.
Akaya, who also cofirmed the arrest of 19 persons in connection with the mayhem, also stated that they would be charged to court after investigation was concluded into the incident.
He berated Asaba yoults over their conducts during such festivals, saying that it has become a common feature during any festival. He said the police would no longer tolerate such acts of lawlessness from any quarters.
Akaya warned that anybody caught unleashing violence on innocent people would be treated as a criminal, assuring that the police would always carry out its lawful duties of maintaining law and order.
In another development, the CP said youths of the town, who invaded Okwe, a surburb of Asaba, and distroyed properties penultimate weekend would be prosecuted.
Though he said no arresst has been made, he stated that the driver of the pick-up van seized by the youths and used for the invasion, was helping the police in the identification of youths involved in the act.
The commissioner further said on September 20, this year, "some youths of Umuaji, armed with dangerous weapons, as guns and matchets, went into Okwe and damaged people's building"
Explaining further, he said a pick-up van with registration No. AM 295 LND and driven by one Emmanuel, was commanded and used while the carnaged lasted, saying that the youths eventually abandoned the pick up vehicle and made good their escape
"This is a clear case of armed robbery and malicious damage. They will be located, arrested and prosecuted as such," the police boss declared.
Beside saying that investigation was being carried out by the police, with the assistance of the driver, Akaya assured that the perpetrators' would soon be fished out.
It would be recalled that the peole of Asaba and their counterpart in Okwe, both of Oshimili South Local Government Area of Delta State, have been at logger-heads over the ownership of parcels of land.
This culminated in a violent confrontation three months ago, and left on its trail burnt houses and several injured people on the side of Okwe.
During the crisis, non-indigenes, residing in Okwe village fled in their hundreds to take refuge at the Government Estate in the area.
Concerted peace efforts were, however, being made lately by the member, representing the council in the State House of Assembly, Mrs. Chinwe Monu-Olanrewaju and the Council's Chairman, Dr. Mordu Ononye, between the two communities.

Original_Search_Title: Refuse Takes Over Karmo Market
Headline: Nigeria: Refuse Takes Over Karmo Market
Body_Text:
Karmo market which is one of the busiest and cheap commodities market in
the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) has become a dumping place for refuse
especially after the weekly Tuesday market when the area is patronised by
all categories of traders and buyers from other surrounding villages and the
city.
With the site of the market in a village which lacks social amenities
and good roads, the market stretches along both sides of the road from the
last gate of Karmo daily market to the bus stop of old Karmo covering a
distance of two and a half kilometres.
The market, due to the density of the population of the village and the
number of visitors may be the dirtiest market in the city since traders not
only throw waste materials around the locality, but abandon their rotten
food stuff after the market, thereby creating stench and health hazards for
the residents of the area.
Apart from the motor park, a small space in which commuter buses
commonly called "molue" park after the daily business with residents, other
parts of the market is nothing to rejoice with in terms of sanitation and
plan.
The motor park which is mostly occupied by food sellers in carelessly
swept every Wednesday even as unsold food stuffs are piled under broken
vehicles at the park making the scene an eyesore especially after a heavy
rain.
While some visitors blame authorities for the deteriorating sanitary
condition of the market, others say the traders, most especially food
sellers are responsible for dumping left over foodstuffs along the
roadsides, though wheel barrow pushers abound on market days.
One of the visitors, Mrs. Jenet Ogbeche who said she lives in Area one,
Garki, not only expressed displeasure over the dirty condition of the market
but also complained of the food stuffs, the refuse and the sellers who
according to her, are not "hygienic" physically irrespective of the kind of
food they sell to people.
"It is a pity that the food sellers seem not to know what is
healthcare. They put their stuffs on refuse and sell to the people. But
unfortunately, people don't even mind from where they buy their items. Even
some of the sellers are so dirty that one would not doubt their food items
are unhealthy. Some even wash their yams, potatoes and fruits in the dirty
waters of gutters and drainage's," Mrs. Ogbeche observed.
Another woman who comes from Gwagwalada, blamed the traders for the
harzards even as she wondered what the money collected from traders was used
for.
"If the authorities, don't clean the market, does it mean the traders
can not clean where they put their commodities. If every trader cleans his
or her surrounding for the good health of the customers the market will
always be tidy," she opined.
But the authorities should do something better and faster. The market
has existed for decades and is supposed to be one of the most patronise in
the city since the rich come to buy their weekly needs from here," the woman
said.
Karmo market is not only famous for cheap commodities but also for
assorted materials because traders on weekly bases come from different
states of the country to sell and buy.

Original_Search_Title: The Search for a Righteous Man
Headline: Nigeria: The Search for a Righteous Man
Body_Text:
Lagos — Cont'd last week
There is also the frightening phenomenon of cultism and cult-related
violence which has claimed many lives and has refused to bow to threats and
pressure from the government and the authorities of the institutions. Part
of the explanation for its tenacity and resilience must be found in the
reluctance of the police and the judiciary to be diligent in arresting,
prosecuting and convicting these common criminals, either because they
themselves are members of senior cults or because they bow to the lure of
financial inducement from the well placed parents of the cult members.
The academic staff are no better, for they now participate in such vices
as grade fixing in return for financial inducement, extortion of money or
sexual favours from students, and compressing a whole semester's course into
a few lectures lasting not more than 20 per cent of the time allocated for
the course, thereby short-changing the students and the nation. The younger
lecturers, who tend to engage in these vices more, are also so deficient
intellectually that they often resort to plagiarism in their lectures,
hand-outs and even academic publications.
Popular Corruption:
Because we the elite have been so irresponsible in the brazen manner in
which we engage in corruption and other vices, we have emboldened the masses
to also emulate our bad ways. In the past, no villager would ever think of
'vandalising a public building or facility in his village simply because it
is left unattended. Now, the story is different: even building blocks are
removed from abandoned government building sites by villagers or local
people who should be the beneficiaries of the project. Their moral reasoning
is: everybody steals government property, so let us also have our share!
Another instance of popular corruption occurred recently when the
Nigerian Government sent an aircraft to evacuate stranded Nigerian citizens
from embattled Monrovia While most of the Nigerians seized the opportunity
to fly into the safety of home, some smart ones among them decided instead
to sell the boarding passes given to them for the flight after due screening
to some desperate Liberians who were trying to escape from the war in their
own country. Upon the plane's arrival in Lagos, Nigerian Immigration
officials stumbled upon the Liberians, and the puzzle was only solved when
the smart Nigerians at the other end, now sensing that Monrovia was becoming
too dangerous to live in, moved into the Nigerian Embassy Compound in
Monrovia and held the staff hostage, demanding to be repatriated home.
Indeed the tradition of honesty is fast disappearing even within family
relationships. How many people sitting in this hall today have been cheated
by their uncles, brothers or in-laws when they made the mistake of
entrusting the building of their village houses to such relatives, only to
be swindled?
Explaining Nigerian Corruption and Moral Decay
Corruption and moral decay in Nigeria can be explained by recourse to
several factors. In addition to those cited in the literature review, the
following perspectives are hereby offered:
Cultural Transition:
Nigerians, as citizens of modern nation-states, are still in transition
from a traditional to a modern socio-political system. Even in the West,
their present system took several centuries to evolve and become fully
ingrained in their psyche. It is too much to expect that within forty-three
years of taking our nation s destiny in our hands we could simply make a
complete transition to another system of moral values. This is why even
among our first and best leaders, there were allegations of impropriety. But
those leaders, may their souls rest in peace, were saints whatever their
faults may have been, compared to the present set of leaders, most of whom
are not mere sinners but devils in comparison. The late Prime Minister of
Nigeria, Tafawa Balewa, for instance, saved money for six years from his
salary as Minister of Transport and Workers in the colonial government
between 1952 and 1957 in order to be able to perform the Muslim
pilgrimage. Some of our present-day State Governors, who are in charge of
relatively tiny portions of what Balewa controlled in his time, now dip
their hands into their state treasuries and rake in millions of Naira to
sponsor themselves, members of their families and official parasites for the
pilgrimage thereby betraying the aspirations of millions of their citizens
who clamour for Sharia in order to return to what they perceive as an ideal
value system which guarantees equity to the ruled and selfless and honest
service from the rulers.
The tensions inherent in this transition from tradition to modernity are
visible in the reluctance of Nigerian leaders to relinquish power, because
deep in our psyche, we believe that between power and the powerful, only
death does them part. It is also apparent in the resistance of our leaders
to any checks and balances on the exercise of such power, especially where
restrictions are applied in their access to or control of public funds. Why
should any body tell the King what he can or cannot use the Kingdom's
resources for? This is in spite of the fact that the Nigerian constitution
is one of the most elaborate in the world in giving details of the principle
of separation of power.
Impact of Military Rule: Loss of Accountability and Job Security
Many of the values of the modem system of governance that we had begun
to imbibe were obliterated by the impact of military rule. The greatest
casualty of this was accountability. The post-colonial civil service was
well-trained to hold political leaders to account, and the civil servants
clearly told ministers and other political heads in writing that such and
such a thing that the had done or proposed to do was not in line with the
G.O. (Government Orders) and must be reversed or must not be done. The
political heads in turn respected the rules and were careful not to bulldoze
their way. However, with the mass purge of the civil service in 1975, which
was targeted at the so-called 'super- permanent secretaries', civil servants
became too timid to caution their political bosses. Accountability was dealt
the final blow that killed it' by the 1979 and subsequent Constitutions
which made political heads 'executive' and also politicised appointments
into senior posts in the civil service. Under the current dispensation, no
permanent secretary, accountant-general or auditor-general would, in his
right senses, dare caution or challenge any action of his political boss,
especially His Excellency himself" Another consequence of the mass purge of
1975 is the loss of job security in the civil service and the resultant
stampede by civil servants to steal as much as possible while still in
service since they could be sacked at any time 'with immediate effect'.
Impact of the Civil War
The Nigerian civil war did a lot of violence to our public morality. It
cheapened human life and took away the shock associated with its loss from
us. It created desperation, especially among the immediate victims of the
war, among us which made the end justify the means, which made survival the
first law of existence and materialism the reason for existence. Crimes and
vices which gained prominence as a direct consequence of this assault on our
collective moral code include armed robbery, prostitution, promiscuity,
advanced fee fraud, drug trafficking and official corruption.
Poverty, Insecurity and Underdevelopment
Nigeria's high level of poverty as discussed earlier is a consequence of
the greed, indiscipline and irresponsibility of the Nigerian elite. After
forty-three years of independence, and therefore forty- three budget cycles,
we are still undeveloped as discussed earlier because we do not use the
resources of the nation for the whole nation 'but for ourselves as the
elite. For example, an analysis of the 2()()3 budget proposals submitted by
the President to the last National Assembly shows that recurrent expenditure
is allocated 66.5per cent while capital expenditure is allocated only 35per
cent. Given our level of underdevelopment, the proportions should be
reversed. When the components of the recurrent and capital budgets are
further analysed, it is found their personnel costs, that is to say the
payment of the salaries and allowances of (Federal) civil servants (the
elite and the sub-elite) account for 45per cent of the whole budget and
67.5per cent of the recurrent budget. Overhead costs, on the other hand,
which should ideally be used to procure tools and materials to justify the
enormous wage bill, account for only 21.6 per cent of the total budget and
35per cent of the recurrent budget. Using this development model, it is
quite clear that we shall never reach the promised land of development.
Additionally, as discussed earlier, there is a wide gap between us and
the common Nigerians. We members of the Nigerian elite live sometimes more
comfortably than our counterparts of similar status even in developed
countries. Compare. for instance. all the palatial houses, the variety of
cars and personal staff of a Minister of the Federal Republic of Nigeria
with the single official car (no official house) of a British Minister. ()r
even compare the level of comfort of a Nigerian university vice-chancellor,
who lives in a free house and is fed at public expense in most universities,
and who has at least three cars and at least six servants paid for by the
university to at the end to his needs with a British vice-chancellor who has
only one official car, one official house and no more.
But the people who really live big are the politicians, especially when
they are in power. the top civil servants, the chief executives of
parastatals and the top members of the military and paramilitary elite. The
accomplished politician or other 'big man' usually has a palatial house at
his main base, which may be his state capital, a regional capital or Abuja
This costs about N25 million to build and furnish, complete with a stand by
Senator and all other accessories of comfort. He also has a befitting house
in is -village, if that is not his main base. This should cost about N 10
million. Then he has a guest-house in another major city (such as a regional
capital or Abuja if that is not where he is usually resident) which should
cost another N10 million. As for cars. there should be at least 7 of them: a
4-wheel drive for his long journeys- cost: N7 million: a posh car for normal
use in the town or city where he is ordinarily resident cost: N5 million;
Madam's car- cost: N3 million; a utility car for the family -cost: N3
million; 3 "olalnbo' cars for the teen-age children cost: N3 million. The
total capital cost of this life style is N66 million. This does not include
the cost of a house in London or New York, which is an optional extra The
recurrent cost of this life-style is estimated to be 141 million per month
to cover feeding, clothing, payment of staff salaries, utility bills,
children's education expenses, social and community taxes et cetera. This
comes to N12 million a year, to which we must add a N5 million provision for
trips abroad for medical check ups, relaxation or spiritual regeneration or
a combination of these. Thus the annual recurrent costs of maintaining this
lifestyle 'will be in the region of N 17 million. If this is added to the
capital costs, the sum total is N83 million for the first year. This money,
stolen (or extorted, if the official is in service) from the public treasury
in various ways, represents the annual income of more than 1665 Nigerians at
the generous rate of 140 per day.
The need to-achieve this kind of life-style is a strong driving force
and fuel for corruption. The need to mountain the life style once achieved
provides further impetus to accumulate more and more, helices the seemingly
insatiable appetite of members of the Nigerian elite for filthy lucre. The
example set by the top political and bureaucratic leadership senses as a
model for people lower down the socio- political scale. This explains why
almost everybody who has the chance steals and steals and steals! J P Clark
puts it graphically in his poem Lyong of the New Millionaires: L9O close to
the desert or forest, Out of which we all come. We fear forest or desert
will overtake Us in our new city stronghold.
So close to the desert or forest Out of which we all come, How shall we
find rest In our new beds of gold?
That land never knows peace uhere a few have too much and many none at
all.
The yam of this world is enough for all mouths which pay daily homage to
the god of the throat enough for the aged awaiting day's dusk and the young
peeping at tomorrow from mother's backs
But alas, men forge unequal knives a few slashing the yam with machetes
greedier than Esimuda's sword leaving mere peelings for the many others
Epilogue: The Road to Rectitude
The decline in public morality and the high level of corruption in
Nigeria are leading the nation to its own destruction. The responsibility
for the low level of morality in the conduct of our public affairs is that
of the elite collectively!! and the ruling elite specifically. Throughout
history and in all nations, the elite always set the rules of public conduct
and determine who gets what and how and who does what and how. Sometimes
they become over-selfish in skewing the rules completely in their favour and
pushing the masses to the wall. When this occurs. the masses, usually led by
a break-away faction of the elite, take over power, sometimes violently and
eventually a new set of elite take over from the discredited ones.
In present-day Nigeria, owing to the greed, indiscipline and
irresponsibility of the elite in slashing the biggest and best portions of
the 'national yam' for themselves, thereby creating hunger, frustration and
desperation among the majority of Nigerians, all the signs are that the
state is on the verge of collapse, unless something urgent is done to rescue
it. The collapse of the state may not take the relatively orderly form of a
revolution. It may simply take the form of violence, looting and anarchy
directed against symbols of the state and all members of the elite. Already,
the foot soldiers for such an eventuality exist in the form of ethnic
militia, area boys and other marginalised groups. During the recent
nation-wide strike against fuel price increases, some of these area boys
positioned themselves along the Kaduna-Abuja expressway and forced all
motorists to chant certain political slogans before allowing them to pass
through. Some of the young men also volunteered this warning to the
motorists in Hausa: 'Set us free before we come into the city of Abuja, for
we are vicious and wicked and our entry will be bloody.' In order to avoid
such a catastrophe, we must, as stakeholders in this country,, engineer a
change of attitude and a change in the ground rules by which the nation is
run through, among other things, the following:
Aggressive and well-co-ordinated poverty reduction, employment creation
and wealth-creation policies which would create more national wealth through
higher productivity and re-distribute it so that the inequality gulf could
be considerably narrowed
Deliberate policies to reduce conspicuous consumption and re-instate
discipline in national life
Improvement in the compensation packages of public officers from the
lowest to the highest to make them more realistic, including severance and
retirement packages in order to improve the sense of security of public
officers and reduce the temptation to corruption
Reduction in the size of the civil service workforce to one third of its
present level over a period of five years in order to have a smaller but
more efficient service which would also cost less to maintain, so that more
money would be spent for real development than for civil service maintenance
Further de-regulation, reform and transparent privatisation of key
sectors of the economy in order to reduce opportunities for public sector
corruption
Inforcement of sanctions against those found guilty of corruption. which
should include the seizure of their loot and appropriate additional
punishment, such as imprisonment
Repatriation of Nigerian looted funds from foreign countries (estimated
to be in the region of $100 billion compared to our total external debt of
about $29) billion) which should be used to liquidate the debt and improve
infrastructure nationally and also create employment for our people
The repatriation of the funds should be preceded by a thorough but
discreet investigation into what each government official has in his or her
foreign account, how they acquired it etc., after which they would be
confronted with the evidence and be given the choice to surrender the money
quietly, in return for protection from public exposure, or risk a full-scale
prosecution in the courts
However, all this and more cannot be done without an honest and
transparently clean political leadership, which, unfortunately, we do not
have in place at the present-time. The search for this kind of leadership,
within the framework of popular democracy and the rule of law, should be the
preoccupation of all of us.
Concluded

Original_Search_Title: Gov't to Gazette Nakivubo Swamp As a Protected Area
Headline: Uganda: Gov't to Gazette Nakivubo Swamp As a Protected Area
Body_Text:
Kampala — PROSSY Nalubwama was under siege. Her belongings were in ruins after the
floods poured into the house as she slept. She held on to a floating empty
jerrycan until she was rescued.
Her ordeal is not strange to the neighbours. They know that her house is
one of those that are worst hit by floods.
This scene in Kalerwe is common in Kampala's suburbs, which are prone to
floods. As the rainy season approaches, the tenants shift to escape the
menacing floods. New unsuspecting tenants usually replace them in the dry
season.
More than half a million people in Kampala, especially the low-income
earners, have been suffering quietly as a result of floods.
"We know about the plight of the people, but it is their fault," says
Robert Wabunoha, a senior environmental lawyer of the National Environment
Management Authority (NEMA).
Wabunoha says many people in Kampala have over the years built houses in
the valleys where water naturally flows. Because the water has nowhere else
to go, it floods people's homes.
Wabunoha says government has come up with new environmental laws to
protect the ecologically sensitive areas such as wetlands.
As the wetlands, which used to hold enormous quantities of water become
no more, the city has begun witnessing a catastrophe.
"It is becoming a routine to lose lives when floods hit the city," says
Paul Mafabi, the assistant commissioner in charge of wetlands in the
Ministry of Water, Lands and Environment.
Mafabi says most wetlands in Kampala have been cleared for human
settlement and industries.
In a recent tour organised by Shelter and Settlements, an NGO, the
low-lying Kifumbira slum that separates Mulago hill from Bukoto was found to
have countless pools of water. The pools provide a breeding ground for
mosquitoes and create a dirty environment that favours cholera. The area
suffered most from past cholera outbreaks.
This is because the latrines are built above water streams. During rains
the area residents usually open a hole to release faeces from the
latrines. The rain then washes away the faeces to streams, from where they
fetch water.
However, not many people have access to toilet facilities. Some defecate
in polythene bags, which they throw into the stream. This has earned buveera
the name: "flying toilet."
There are also heaps of unclaimed garbage because the crowded houses
leave only tiny passages, which can not be accessed by garbage
collectors.
Rwandume Mugizi, the KCC environment inspector, says Kampala has
swallowed up the greenery that once covered the empty hills and
valleys.
"We have a concrete jungle, which does not allow water filtration in the
soil and most of the water runs above the ground," he says.
Mugizi points out that most of the land in Kampala belongs to
individuals. This makes it difficult for the city council to plan and ensure
that houses are built to the required standards.
"The authorities plan for land that is not in their hands."
He says the lack of policies on housing has led to haphazard development
in Kampala and its suburbs. Mugizi also blames old laws that did not
recognise wetlands as important ecological areas. It is only in 1995 that a
law was put in place to protect wetlands. Henceforth it became illegal to
encroach on a wetland.
When the floods hit Kampala early this year, the former minister of
environment, Dr. Kezimbira Miyingo, issued a directive that all houses in
wetlands should be demolished.
But Phoebe Gubya, the KCC environment officer, complains about the
directive saying there is need for compensation. He says landowners claim
that they did not know they were building on wetlands.
However, Mafabi says there is no need for compensation for the houses
built after 1995 when a law was put in place to protect wetlands.
Mafabi says some of the wetlands such as the Nakivubo swamp, purify
waste water before it enters Lake Victoria. In so doing they protect the
lake from pollution.
"Soon the authorities will be mining water from filth as the mouth of
the lake is located a stone's throw away from Ggaba Water Works," he
says.
He says efforts are in advanced stages to gazette the Nakivubo wetland
as a protected area, and to stop fresh encroachment on it.
He says that after gazetting the Nakivubo swamp, the team will move in
to save Kinawataka wetland.
The war to protect wetlands has seen government taking contradictory
positions. As the government prepares to evict over 10,000 yam growers from
the Nakivubo wetland, a few rich owners of houses in Bugolobi who should be
evicted, have been left intact.
In a special report in The New Vision of April 25, it was reported that
the new proposed boundaries were being changed to favour the rich owners of
the houses.
Even the recent directive by Kezimbira came at a time when a
controversial shopping mall, Shoprite, was being put up at Lugogo play
grounds, which is a water way.
Mafabi says some politicians have abdicated from the noble cause of
protecting wetlands because they fear to make unpopular decisions.
"We are making a new law on wetlands to close the pitfalls," says a
source in the ministry of environment.

Original_Search_Title: PDP to ANPP: Don't Politicise Okadigbo's Death
Headline: Nigeria: PDP to ANPP: Don't Politicise Okadigbo's Death
Body_Text:
Abuja —  Kinsmen to consult oracle
Peoples Democraic Party (PDP) yesterday reacted to the raging
controversy over the death of former Senate President and vice presidential
candidate of the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) in the last general
elections, Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, as it advised the deceased's party not to
politicise his death.
PDP's reaction came as the ANPP members in the House of Representatives
also yesterday alleged that Okadigbo died as a result of killer chemical
contained in the tear gas inflicted on him by the police during the party's
rally in Kano last Tuesday.
Also, kinsmen of the politician, the Iguedo clan in the Omambala area of
Anambra State have said they would consult the "Iguedo Oracle" to unravel
the circumstances that led to the death of the late politician.
But the Anambra State government, through controlled by the PDP,
yesterday promised to accord Okadigbo state burial and bear all the expenses
relating to the burial and funeral ceremonies.
The PDP whose reaction was given by its national secretary, Prince
Vincent Ogbulafor, noted that, "government cannot send anybody to go and
kill."
Ogbulafor was reacting to the allegation by ANPP that PDP was
responsible for the use of tear gas by the Police to disperse ANPP members
during their rally in Kano.
The National Director of Publicity of ANPP, Alhaji Ibahim Modibo had
accused PDP of instigating Police against its rally and described the Police
as "the Peoples Democratic Police."
But Ogbulafor in defence of PDP said, "It is very unfortunate that any
time any thing sad happens, they point accusing fingers at the party (PDP)
and the government."
Ogbulafor added that, "government cannot send anybody to go and kill, it
is not done. It is unheard of."
Ogbulafor described death as, "a necessary thing that will come when it
will come, that's why we are all afraid of death, because we don't know when
it will come. We should not politicize his death. It should not be
politicized at all."
Addressing newsmen yesterday at the National Assembly complex, the
caucus leader, Hon Ahmed Salik said he was with Okadigbo at the venue of the
Kano rally when the substance he described as "colourless, odourless and
pepperish in the eye" was sprayed on them. He stated that the chemical made
him (Salik) feel like fainting before he beat a retreat to board a bus where
he said he stayed till the end of the rally.
He said that prior to the spraying incident, the police had cordoned
every entrance to the venue, which caused a surge on the entrance before the
"chemical" was sprayed on the crowd.
Also explaining further during the briefing, a member of the ANPP House
caucus, Hon Ethelbert Okwaranyia said indication that what was sprayed on
Okadigbo was not ordinary teargas, was that the presidential candidate of
the party, General Muhammadu Buhari and Dr. Nnia Nwodo also nearly fainted
when they inhaled the same substance whose effect was different from that of
teargas.
The ANPP legislators therefore asked for a full scale inquiry into the
incident.
Salik who said the party was issuing the statement as a gift on the eve
of the independence day celebrations also congratulated Nigerians for
working for democracy but regretted that they were not getting any good deal
due to the style of administration by the PDP under President Olusegun
Obasanjo.
"The leadership of this country has since 1999 been the worst ever
witnessed on the surface of this earth. The leadership has been
characterised by ineptitude, incompetence, incoherence, abuse of power,
outright violation of the laws of the land as regards the constitution and
other Acts of the National Assembly.
"The leadership of this country has been non-performing, colourless,
lackluster and even primitive. While we live in the 21st century, our
leadership has remained essentially pre-historic with all the trappings of
antiquated megalomania. No thanks to bad leadership, Nigeria has woefully
failed to attain its full potentials of greatness", he lamented.
Meanwhile, the South-east Legislators and other politicians from the
zone will be meeting in Abuja Thursday to take a position over the death of
Okadigbo.
The Iguedo clan, Okadigbo's ancestral home, refers to the communities
said to be direct descendants of Iguedo, the only known daughter of "Eri,"
the progenitor of the Igbo race. It include such communities as Ogbunike,
(Okadigbo's home town) Umuleri, Aguleri, Awkuzu and Nteje. They also
domicile within the Anambra East, and West local governments as well as
Oyi.
The president general of Awkuzu community, Chief Frank Oramulu, who
spoke to newsmen last Sunday during the New Yam festival celebrated by the
community at the palace of the traditional ruler of the town, Igwe John
Nebolisa, said the spiritualists in the clan would invoke the spirit of
Okadigbo with a view to ascertaining who or what killed him.
Oramulu, a veteran labour leader and one-time chairman of the Peoples
Democratic Party (PDP) in Anambra State, said the decision to consult the
oracle was simply traditional.
"Every group of people has their own tradition, we will invoke his
(Okadigbo) spirit and do everything to find out those who killed him and I
tell you, they won't go unpunished," he said.
Oramulu was reacting to the insinuation that Okadigbo's death was
premeditated by the tear-gas cannisters directed at his face by the police
which he inhaled during the ANPP rally in Kano last Tuesday.
"You can't kill an Iguedo son. To kill an Iguedo son is not what you
do. We will go to the Edo mother and tell her that her "Ikenga" (staff of
authority) had been stolen. But if God called him, so be it," Oramulu
said.
Igwe Nebolisa who described Okadigbo's death as "most unfortunate,"
however, cautioned that the people should not mourn as if they had lost
every hope, since according to him, "many other Okadigbos abound in
Igboland."
However, the Anambra State government, in a statement yesterday signed
by Mr. Chuks Akunna, Chief Press Secretary to Governor Chris Ngige, said it
has decided to bear the burial expenses of the late Chuba Okadigbo because,
"the late Oyi was one of Nigeria's finest breed".
The statement quoted Ngige, as saying that even though Okadigbo belonged
to a different political party, the ANPP, the PDP led administration in
Anambra State would accord him all due respect because of his immense
contributions to society, particularly in the area of political
economy.
Ngige described the death of Okadigbo as a great loss to Nigeria and to
Anambra State in particular.
"We in Anambra State have lost a political icon. The political landscape
of Nigeria has been greatly altered by the passage of our great 'Oyi of
Oyi," Ngige said.
The Anambra State government stated that it would ensure the memories of
the late Okadigbo are preserved, that the state government would be
consulting the Okadigbo family at every stage of preparations for the
burial.
In its own reaction to Okadigbo's death, the Nigeria Labour Congress
(NLC) yesterday demanded that a high power judicial enquiry be set up to
probe the circumstances leading to the death. It noted that the police being
accused of complicity in the death has no moral authority to investigate
itself.
Also, the congress warned President Obasanjo against current attempts by
the government to silence opposition. Labour also noted that the current
effort to muzzle the opposition has the capacity to truncate the democratic
process.
Speaking yesterday at the opening ceremony of the Central Working
Committee meeting of the workers organisation, NLC president, Adams
Oshiomhole said the death of Okadigbo is a sad commentary in Nigeria
political history and that the issue raised by the incident must not be
allowed to be buried like it was done with similar deaths in the past.
The NLC president noted that whether or not a linkage exist between
Okadigbo death and police use of tear gas in the Kano rally, that the police
resorted to use of such instrument in disparsing a harmless rally, should be
a source of concern to all those who struggle for democracy.
"If a man who was a leader of the Senate should be treated in such a
manner as to inhale tear-gas then the country is in trouble. It is rather a
sad commentary in our history. Labour is worried by this
development. Indeed, all of us who struggled for democracy must feel
concerned about this.
"It is also worrisome that a rally which has former Head of State,
governors, former Senate Presi-dent could be teargassed," he said.
Oshiomhole noted that this was not the first time police has deployed
excessive use of teargas on citizens who want to register their opinion
about government policies and actions. He recalled the last fuel crises in
which protesters were molested. "By the time we were through with the
peaceful protest, we were counting the number of men lost."
Labour said proper inquiry into Okadigbo's death is inevitable to save
the country from future recurrence.
"Whether police use teargas or not, whether there is linkage between
Okadigbo's death and the use of teargas, we demand for a proper judicial
enquiry into this sad episode.
"And we do not see police doing a proper investigation. Indeed, it is
wrong for police to investigate themselves in a matter they are already a
party and which they have cleared themselves of any complicity. We must get
to the root of this matter," he said.
Meanwhile, the Okadigbo family yesterday formally announced the death of
the late politician.
The announcement signed by Pharaoh Okadigbo (Son) and Dr. Edmund
Okadigbo (Brother) for the entire Okadigbo family in Ogbunike, Oyi Local
Government of Anambra State, reads in part:
"The Rt. Hon. Dr. William CHUBA OKADIGBO passed on in Abuja on 25th
September 2003. He went the way he came, peacefully."

Original_Search_Title: The Proof of Customary Law Lies On the Party Alleging Its Existence
Headline: Nigeria: The Proof of Customary Law Lies On the Party Alleging Its Existence
Body_Text:
By a writ of summons issued on the 22nd day of November, 1985 in the
Port Harcourt Judicial Division of the High Court of Justice, Rivers State,
the plaintiff, who is now appellant, instituted an action against the
defendant now the respondent claiming as follows:-
" (1) A declaration that the defendant is the son of Adele Nlerum of
Oro-Owo in Rumueme and therefore not entitled to any share of properties or
any rights over any properties of Akwaka Kpakani, the plaintiff's Great
Grand Father.
(2) The sum of N15,000.00 being damages for trespass committed by the
defendant when he broke and entered Ekwu-Mgbabokwu land inherited by the
plaintiff from his Great Grand Father.
(3)Perpetual injunction restraining the defendant, his servants or
agents from further acts of trespass on the said Ekwu-Mgbabokwu land or in
any way laying claims to any properties or rights deriving from the said
plaintiff's Great Grand Father, Akwaka Kpakani."
Essentially, the plaintiff's case as pleaded is that the land in dispute
formed part of a larger piece or parcel of land which originally belonged to
his ancestor Kpakani with members of his family who founded the same and
became the first settlers thereon. The said plaintiff's ancestor allocated
portions of the new found land to his three sons, the eldest of whom was
Akwaka. On the death of their father, each of his three Sons inherited the
portion of the land allocated to him. Similarly on the death of his said
sons, their respective portions of the land were inherited by their
succeeding descendants. The plaintiff averred that the land in dispute
formed part of the portion of their family land which was allocated to his
great grand father, Akwaka, and that he inherited the same as the only
surviving male child of his late father. He stated that the defendant was
only the son of D.W.2, his elder sister of full blood, whose husband was
D.W.1, one Adele Nlerum. He averred that the defendant being the son of the
said D.W. 1, who did not belong to their family was not entitled to inherit
the property of the plaintiff's great grand father. The plaintiff claimed
that the defendant unlawfully broke into the land and started construction
work thereon without his prior consent hence this action.
The defendant, for his own part, claimed that the land in dispute formed
no part of the land which belonged to Kpakani.
He averred that the land belonged to the Okpoko family who acquired the
same as a spoil of war after successfully fighting the Rumueme and Rumopara
war. The defendant claimed that he bought the land in dispute from the
Okpoko family in 1978 and was issued with a receipt for the money he
paid. He also obtained a conveyance in respect of the transaction. He
further claimed to have farmed on the land for a while before he erected his
New Era Hotel thereon without let or hindrance from the plaintiff or any one
else. He completed the erection of his hotel on the land in
1981. Consequently he relied on the plea of laches and acquiescence as part
of his defence.
More importantly, the defendant asserted that his father, D.W. 1, at no
time married his mother D.W. 2, under their applicable Ikwerre customary
law. As a result he was a full member of the family of his mother, D.W.2,
that is to say, the plaintiff's family. He contended that as a member of the
family, he had equal rights with the plaintiff and the other male members of
the family in respect of the enjoyment of their family properties including
land. The defendant in support of his assertion relied on the plea of
estoppel as a bar to the plaintiff's denial that he is a member of that
family. He argued that the plaintiff having regard to his various conducts
which he particularised was estopped from claiming that the said defendant
did not belong to the plaintiff's family.
At the conclusion of hearing, the trial judge, Niki Tobi, J., (as he
then was), after an exhaustive review of the evidence dismissed the
plaintiff's claims in their entirety.
Dissatisfied with this decision of the trial court, the plaintiff lodged
an appeal against the same to the Court of Appeal, Port Harcourt
Division. The Court of Appeal in a unanimous decision on the 7th December,
1994 dismissed the plaintiff's appeal as lacking in substance and
unmeritorious.
Aggrieved by this decision of the Court of Appeal, the plaintiff has
further appealed to this court.
The following three issues raised by the respondent were considered for
the determination of the appeals:
"1. Whether the plaintiff/appellant has proved title to the land in
dispute.
2. Whether the plaintiff/respondent's father, D.W. 1, so as to
disentitle the defendant/respondent from inheriting any part of NSIRIM's
property.
3. Whether on the facts of this case the please of estoppel, laches and
Acquiescence avail the defendant/respondent."
The main contention of learned counsel for the appellant, W. Boms
Esq. under issue 1 is that the traditional evidence led by the appellant in
support of the acquisition of the land in dispute by first settlement by his
ancestors was sufficient to establish his title to the said land. He argued
that there were no conflicts in the traditional histories of the parties to
warrant the necessity by the court below to resort to the application of the
rule in Kojo V. Bonsie (1957) 1 W.L.R. 1223. He concluded by submitting that
the evidence of D.W. 3 supported the traditional evidence of the appellant
and did destroy the traditional history relied upon by the respondent. He
urged the court to resolve issue 1 in favour of the appellant.
Learned counsel for the respondent, M.O. Abudu Esq. in his reply
submitted that the appellant completely failed to establish his ownership of
the land in dispute. He pointed out that apart from the appellant, none out
of his four witnesses testified as to the version of the traditional
evidence relied upon by him in proof of his ownership of the land in
dispute. He contended that even the solitary version of the appellant's
traditional history as to how his ancestors allegedly acquired the land in
dispute by first settlement was carefully considered and was rejected by the
trial court. He pointed out that this rejection of the appellant's
traditional evidence was affirmed by the court below. Learned counsel
contended that on these concurrent findings of both courts below alone, the
appellant's claim of title to the land in dispute is bound to fail. He
submitted that the respondent on the findings of both courts below purchased
the land in dispute from the original owners, the Okpoko family for the sum
of N1,500.00. This was on the 14th January, 1978. The respondent also
presented to his vendors the tribute of one goat, a bottle of schnapps, a
carton of beer and 8 tubers of yam as required by customary law in respect
of this purchase. He was subsequently issued with a Certificate of
Occupancy, Exhibit J. in respect of the land in 1987. He completed the
erection of houses on the land in 1980/1981 without let or hindrance from
any one whatever. He urged the court to resolve issue 1 against the
appellant.
It is trite law that in a claim for declaration of title to land, the
onus of proof lies on the plaintiff who must succeed on the strength of his
case and not on the weakness of the defendant's case except, of course, in
cases where the defendant's case itself supports the plaintiff's case and
contains evidence on which the plaintiff is entitled to rely. See Kodilinye
v. Mbanefo Odu (1935) 2 W.A.C.A. 336 at '337, Woluehem v. Gudi (1981) 5
S.C.291, Oduaran v. Asarah (1972) All N.L.R. (pt. 2) 137 e.t.c.
Now, reverting to the appellant's root of title and/or traditional
evidence in support thereof, it cannot be disputed, as pointed out by the
learned trial judge and affirmed by the court below, that there is apparent
discrepancy between the averment in paragraph 4 of the appellant's amended
statement of claim and his viva voce evidence in support thereof. This
pertains to the all important issue of the traditional evidence relied on by
the appellant in proof of his claim of title to the land in dispute. Whereas
by the appellant's pleadings, the land in dispute was founded by and
therefore originally belonged to members of Kpakani family by dint of first
settlement or occupation, the appellant's evidence on the same issue was
that the said land originally belonged to Kpakani who was the first settler
occupier thereof and that it was at a later date that he shared the land
amongst his three children. This discrepancy seems to me fatal to the
appellant's case for if the evidence of a party is at variance with his
pleadings the claim would fail and stand dismissed. Parties are bound by
their pleadings and evidence which is at variance with the averments in the
pleadings goes to no issue and should be disregarded by the court. See
Emegokwe v. Okadigbo (1973) 4 S.C.113, Odumesu v. African Continental Bank
Ltd. (1976) 11 S.C. 261 at 264, Kalu Njoku and Others v. Ukwu Ewe and Others
(1973) 5 S.C. 293 etc.
Issue 2 seems to me the most vital question for consideration in this
appeal. This is because the appellant in this case did not just make a claim
of declaration of title to the land in dispute against the respondent. The
claim is a declaration that the respondent not being a member of the
appellant's Akwaka Kpakani family is not entitled to any share of or right
over the properties of the Akwaka Kpakani family, in other words, even if
the appellant was able to have established original ownership of the land in
dispute in his Akwaka Kpakani family, the declaration he claimed would only
have succeeded if he was further able to establish that the respondent was
not a member of the Akwaka Kpakani family. The case for the appellant was
founded on an alleged marriage between D.W. 1 and D.W. 2 in 1944 in
accordance with Rumueme customary law. The contention of the appellant is
that the respondent, being a child of the aforesaid customary law marriage,
did not belong to the appellant's family and could not therefore inherit any
of the landed properties of the said Akpaka Kpakani family. He claimed that
this is because under the Rumueme customary law, only male children could
inherit the landed property of their deceased father.
The respondent, on the other hand, denied the existence of the alleged
customary marriage between D.W. 1 and D.W.2 and averred that D.W. 2 at all
material times was unmarried and remained with her father's family in their
compound. The respondent claimed that under Rumueme and, in fact, Ikwerre
customary law, children born by D.W.2 in her father's house while unmarried
belonged to her father and are entitled to enjoy all the rights and
privileges of the other children of her father over the latter's
property.
Resolution of issue 2, therefore, would depend on whether, as insisted
by the appellant, D.W.2, the mother of the respondent and sister of full
blood of the appellant was married to D.W. 1, the respondent's biological
father, thus disentitling the said respondent from inheriting any part of
Nsirim's landed property. In other words the issue posed is whether the
appellant proved the existence of a marriage between the appellant's sister,
D.W.2 and the respondent's father, D.W.1.
In this regard, the point must be that native law and custom, otherwise
also referred to as customary law, are matters of evidence on the facts
presented before the court and must therefore be proved in any particular
case unless, of course, they are of such notoriety and have been so
frequently followed or applied by the courts that judicial notice thereof
would be taken without evidence required in proof. See Liadi Giwa and
Another V.
Erinmilokun (1961) 1 S.C.N.L.R. 337. The proof of customary law being an
issue of fact clearly lies on the party alleging its existence. See Taiwo
v. Dosunmu and Another (1965) All N.L.R. (Reprint) 417.
The Court of Appeal was in total agreement with the finding of the trial
court to the effect that no marriage between D.W.1 and D.W.2 was established
by the appellant. Said the Court of Appeal:-
"The learned trial judge after consideration of the evidence and the
applicable law found as a fact and in law that the appellant in whom lies
the burden failed to establish subsisting customary marriage between the 1st
and 2nd DWS.
This being a finding of fact raises the issue and attitude of an
appellate court towards finding of fact by the lower court...In the instant
appeal and after a careful consideration of the facts and the law with
reliance on the authorities coupled with reliance on the credibility of the
witnesses, I am satisfied that the finding of the trial judge is very sound
and not perverse; issue 1 in respondent's brief is that the learned trial
judge was right that the appellant failed to prove the existence of
customary marriage between the respondent's biological father (1st D.W) and
mother (2nd D.W.) and elder sister of full blood with the appellant. I see
no reason to disturb this finding."
I think both courts below are perfectly right in the above findings; in
the first place, it is trite law that a judge having had the opportunity of
bearing witness and watching their demeanour in the witness box is entitled
to select witnesses to believe facts he finds proved and the Court of Appeal
should not interfere with such facts unless they are perverse. See Kodilinye
v. Mbanefo Odu (supra). So, too, this court will not ordinarily interfere
with the concurrent findings of the trial High Court and the Court of Appeal
on essentially issues of fact where there is sufficient evidence on record
to support them and where there is no substantial error apparent on the
record of proceedings unless special circumstances are shown such as
violation of some principle of law or procedure or where such findings are
shown to be perverse or patently erroneous and a miscarriage of justice will
result if they are allowed to stand.
See Chinwendu v. Nbamali (1980) 3 - 4 S.C. 31 at 75, Enang V. Adu (1981)
11 - 12 S. 0. 25 at 42, Nwadike v. Ibekwe (1987) 4 N.W.L.R. (pt. 67) 718) ,
Ilgwego v. Euengo (1992) 6 N.W.L.R. (pt.249) 549 e.t.e have myself given a
most careful consideration to the above concurrent findings in respect of
various material issues of fact in controversy between the parties in this
case and have no reason to fault them. issue 2 maust accordingly be resolved
against the appellant.
There is finally issue 3 which poses the question whether on the facts
of this case, the pleas of estoppel, laches and acquiescence avail the
respondent. As it is my view that the issues of laches and acquiescence
hardly arise on the facts of this case, I propose to consider the question
of estoppel only under issue 3 in this appeal.
Before considering the relevant facts as found by the trial court and
affirmed by the Court of Appeal, it needs be restated that where one by his
words or conduct willfully causes another to believe the existence of
certain state of things and induces him to act on that belief so as to alter
his own previous position, the former is precluded from averring against the
latter a different state of things as existing ~at the same time. This is
how the rule in estoppel by conduct otherwise also known as estoppel by
matter in pals has been stated. See Joe Iga and Others v. Ezekiel Amaktri
and Other (1976) 11 S.C. 1, Gregory Ude v. Clement Nwara and Another (1993)
2 N.W.L.R. (pt.278) 638 at 662 -663.
Turning now to the facts, the learned trial judge after a painstaking
review of the evidence found thus:
"I find the following facts proved in this matter:
1. That plaintiff built a house in his father's compound for the
defendant and his mother (D.W. 2)....... 2. That the plaintiff trained the
defendant
3 That the defendant was not only a member of the Rumukpakani
Development Union but was once a
treasurer of that Union..,.........
4. That the plaintiff and the defendant sued jointly and were also
jointly sued. They jointly sued "for themselves and on behalf of the Nairim
Mgba family of Rumueme." The defendant in that suit is Bekwele Worgu. See
Exhibit C.
5. The defendant still bears the name Nsirim. As a matter of fact he is
so addressed by the plaintiff. See Exhibit M."
He went on : -
"1 have seen from the totality of the above findings of fact that the
plaintiffs is estopped from denying that the defendant is not a member of
his family and therefore "not entitled to any share of properties or any
rights over any properties of Akwaka Kpakani,the plaintiff's
Great Grand Father." lf the defendant was never a part of the
plaintiff's father's family, how did he come about building a house there
for him and the mother (D.W.2). He did so because defendant is his nephew,
being the son of D.W.2 his elder sister. He did so because he believed that
defendant belongs to his (plaintiff) father's family. By conduct he is
estopped from contending the contrary."
He continued:-
"It is clear from Exhibit C that the plaintiff and the defendant sued
together "for themselves and on behalf of the Nsirim Mgba family of
Rumueme." Although plaintiff managed to deny this fact, Exhibit 'c' is so
clear on it. 1 have not the slightes doubt in my mind that from Exhibit C
the plaintiff is estopped from denying the defendant as a member of the
Nsirim Mgba family in Rumueme. He cannot do that in law.
The defendant still bears the name of NSIRIM. This is not a situation
where the defendant unilaterally arrogates to himself the right to use that
great family name, but it is recognised either expressly or tacitly by the
plaintiff. And here, Exhibits C and M. amongst other evidence before me are
relevant.
I have already dealt with Exhibit C: 1 now go to Exhibit M. Plaintiff
wrote Exhibit M. He signed it. And in Exhibit M. plaintiff addressed the
defendant as Mr. E.A. Nsirim and the address is Rumukpakani village, Rumueme
Town, Port Harcourt. Exhibit M bears the same address in respect of the
plaintiff."
The trial court next turned to the respondent' name and commented:-
Defendant bears the same surnamed as the plaintiff for purposes of
repetition and at the expense fo sounding prolix, it is NSIRIM. The spelling
is the same. it is a six letter name. And it is likely to be an Ikwerre
name. And by the evidence before me the defendant has been bearing this name
right from his birth. And he is now above forty years. The question is this:
Why did the plaintiff allow the defendant to use his dear father's name
for so long a time. Why should he now challenge the defendant's right to use
the same after more than forty long years? Again, the plaintiff is caught by
the plea of estoppel and he cannot run away from it in law. No, he cannot."
In the same vein, the court below on this issue of estoppel stated as
follows:- "a-he defence of the respondent was that from the various acts of
the appellant who had presented him as a member of NSIRIM MGBA family that
now that the grapes are sour between them the appellant cannot now state
that the High Court should declare him not a member of NSIRIM's family. The
learned trial judge right in my judgment had justification for refusal to
grant the appellant that respondent is not a member of NSIRIM family.
That court concluded:-
"For the above reasons the attack by appellant on this issue lacks
substance and unmeritorious, as appellant is estopped by his conduct to say
that the respondent should desist to regard himself as a member of NSIRIM
FAMILY."
I think both courts below are again on firm grounds by holding that an
the facts the appellant was estopped from denying that the respondent is a
member of the family of the former. A clear ease of estopped was therefore
established by the respondent in this case and 1 agree from the established
facts that the appellant is estopped from denying respondent's membership of
the Nairim Ngba family.
(From lead judgement by A.I. Iguh, J.S.C.)
Representation:
Wongu Boms Esq for the appellant
M.O. Abudu Esq for the respondent.

Original_Search_Title: The Planned Ban On Important of Rice
Headline: Nigeria: The Planned Ban On Important of Rice
Body_Text:
Recently, the National Chairman of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP)
hinted that the federal government will ban the importation of rice, fruit
juice, and milk by the year 2006.
He lamented that it was costing the government a huge foreign exchange
to import the commodities.
We have no quarrel with this plan, in fact it is our believe that
Nigeria can be self-sufficient in the production of many agricultural
products as well as dairy products if the government shows enough commitment
to the development of the agricultural sector. Indeed, no country can reach
the promised land if the citizens habitually prefer foreign products to
home-sourced ones.
It is infact, this attitude that has discouraged many Nigerians from
agriculture, yet countries that do not experience as much rainfall with
Nigeria are reputed for the foreign exchange they earn through
agriculture.
We are of the opinion that if the ban is carried out, it will not only
help achieve in part the diversification of the Nigerian economy that is
currently too dependent on oil. Diversification will reflate the Nigerian
economy a great deal and reduce the misery living in Nigeria imposes.
In most parts of Nigeria enough rice can be grown to feed Nigerians, and
still have enough for export. The same can be said of dairy products. This
move will, no doubt make Nigerian farmers happy and would encourage them to
work harder.
Two years ago, the Nigerian govenrment made moves to import beans from
Burkina Faso, if that had been done, it would have shown how unserious and
reckless Nigerian leaders can be.
It is gratifying that the ban will not take off immediately, which means
the government can have sufficient time to put some measures in place to
ensure the success of it.
For example, lack of storage facilities is a disincentive for farmers to
produce more than enough fresh milk and some crops like yam. As for fruit
drinks, there are some local companies processing them and making a good job
of it.
Nigerians must be encouraged to produce enough agricultural products and
one of the ways to do this is to farmers against foreign products. Nigeria
should not be wide open to all corners.
Right now, local textile companies are finding it hard to survive since
it is a well known fact Nigeria is a well known dumping ground for foreign
textile materials. Something most be done about this too for if the local
textile companies go under, the unemployment situation will be compounded,
while local cotton growers will have no choice than to find something else
to do.

Original_Search_Title: EBA, Pounded Yam Revive Falcons
Headline: Nigeria: EBA, Pounded Yam Revive Falcons
Body_Text:
A GREAT deal of hope appear in the horizon for Nigeria, ahead of
Thursday's second Group-A match against host USA, as team officials have
moved to solve some of the problems facing the team here.
The girls are now being served African or Nigerian prepared dishes in
the light of earlier problems of food they faced in the hotel. The process
began Monday evening after FIFA acceded to a request by the Nigerian camp to
have a supply from outside the hotel which the players have so suddenly
appreciated.
"Now I can eat eba or pounded yam which I'm used to and no more those
petty things they served since we came," said defender Kikelomo Ajayi, soon
after taking the first meal Monday evening. "I'm better now. It tastes so
well to eat something I know would make me happy and give better energy. I
was near vomitting eating the same things they served," another central
defender Bunmi Kayode said shortly after the meal.
Players had objected to being served often, the continental meals by the
hotel, with many finding the food strange and a times, refusing to eat.
"Now, we are happy that one essential need has been solved for them. How
I wish we had this from the beginning," said Sunmonu Bello-Osagie, who is
with the team in camp.
Both captain Florence Omagbemi and attacker Mercy Akide brightened up
after having the first course of the local food they knew and Omagbemi said
it felt good that this will now be made continuous as per arrangement by
team officials.
"It's better than what we faced. Players are happier with this
arrangement and we appreciate it," she said.

Original_Search_Title: One Person Feared Dead As New Yam Celebrants Clash With Police
Headline: Nigeria: One Person Feared Dead As New Yam Celebrants Clash With Police
Body_Text:
Asaba — AT least one person died and several persons were injured when a group of Asaba youths celebrating their new yam festival clashed with the police. The main police station in the town popularly called the "A" Division was sacked and vandalized while 15 vehicles were smashed in the process. The police confirmed to reporters yesterday that they had arrested 19 people in connection with the bloody clash.
Vanguard gathered that a stray bullet hit a woman when police opened fire in a bid to disperse the rampaging youths who came out in their numbers.
Addressing newsmen in Asaba yesterday, the State Commissioner of Police, Mr. Charles Akaya said "about 5.30pm youths in their thousands marched through Nnebisi road of Asaba while performing the grand finale of their new yam festival. In the process, they unleashed mayhem on innocent road users", adding that "since the beginning of the festival on 15th September, 2003 there have been reported cases of assault of people in and around Umuaji especially women usually between the hours of 7 and 8.30pm.
"These youths", the CP went on, "while marching towards Traffic Light (area) went into Police Works Department and picked some iron rods. When challenged by policemen on duty they vandalized the louvres of offices at "A" Division (police station) and damaged 15 vehicles."
He added, "after being dispersed, they met a police Inspector on their way and dispossessed him of his uniform and also set ablaze a 504 car belonging to the Ministry of Economic Planning. Nineteen persons were arrested for investigation", the Police Commissioner stated.
Akaya further warned members of the public that "the Public Order Act is still in force which demands that procession into the streets should be on police permit. It has been a common incident that during festivals in Asaba, residents are treated to violence", pointing out that "this situation will not be tolerated any longer as offenders and sponsors shall be treated as common criminals."
Commenting on the Okwe clash, the Commissioner said that "on 20th September 2003 at 10.30 am, some youths of Umuaji armed with various dangerous weapons such as guns and machetes went into Okwe and damaged some buildings and a pick up van with registration number AM 295 LND driven by one Emmanuel Ananaro was commandeered and used throughout their rampage which they abandoned while escaping.
"This", the CP said, "is a clear case of armed robbery in addition to malicious damages. They (suspects) will be located, arrested and prosecuted as such."
The Police Commissioner who told reporters that he personally came out when the youths stormed Okwe which is close to his residence to ensure that the situation was quickly brought under control added that Emmanuel, the owner of the pick up commandeered by the rampaging youths "is with us and helping us in our investigation."
Igbinedion laments deductions of $145.7m debt owed by private firm
By Emma Amaize
BENIN CITY- GOVERNOR Lucky Igbinedion of Edo State has described as unacceptable, a situation where deductions are made from the state's allocation monthly for the $145.7 million foreign debt incurred by the Bendel Feed and Flour Mills, Ewu, which had been privatized. He told Vanguard recently in Benin City that some private individuals were feeding fat from the company while the State Government and people of Edo State who received no dividends were bearing the brunt.
"We cannot accept this in a democratic dispensation. Actually, it was when we discovered that some huge amounts up to N200 million was being deducted from our allocation every month that we decided to investigate and we traced part of the deduction to a debt said to have been incurred by the company," he said.
"I will tell you that I was personally shocked at this scandal and the government is already addressing this hideous distortion which the operations of the Bendel Feeds and Flour Mill is causing the people of the state," he asserted.
The state Commissioner for Finance, Mr. Bright Omokhodion who shed more light on the matter stated that "this is the most stunning and excruciating of all the debts the state is said to be owing. Available records showed that the company, established by the State Government some years ago got an export credit guarantee of 1.08 million Australian Shillings and a commercial credit of 508.4 million Austrian shillings millions as at then."
He explained that "going by the Central Bank circular (Pr -- Spem transaction guideline of February 1988) by which all transactions (exchange rate) was to convert at 0.0390 to a Naira, the total of 1.5 billion Austrian Shillings amounted to N72 million but the Federal Government in 1991, abrogated the settlement rate, which was a concession to the industries for repayment.
"At this time," he observed, "the company was yet to start repaying the loan and because of the abrogation which meant no concession to the industries and increasing exchange rate the money started taking geometric flight."
Omokhodion asserted that "by the time the company was privatized, that was before the Igbinedion administration came into office, the settlement rate was not taken into cognizance and its indebtedness was calculated on the Central Bank rate that was abrogated in 1991." He said that those who bought over the company were now enjoying at the expense of the people of the state.
Vanguard learnt that the share structure of the company may be radically altered at the end of the day as the government was not prepared to shoulder the debt burden of a company that is in private hands or the Federal Government would be asked to shift the responsibility of paying the debt to the company. The State Government, it was learnt might even ask for the refund of the money deducted from its allocation from the company.

Original_Search_Title: Wabara, Ogbuewu, Ogunlewe to Attend Arochukwu Festival
Headline: Nigeria: Wabara, Ogbuewu, Ogunlewe to Attend Arochukwu Festival
Body_Text:
Lagos — Senate President, Adolphus Wabara, Minister of Culture and Tourism,
Hon. Franklin Ogbuewu, Minister of Works, Senator Adeseye Ogunlewe and other
dignitaries are billed to attend the grand finale of the 2003 Ikeji
Arochukwu Festival.
National Publicity Secretary of Nzuko Arochukwu, Mr. Ernie Onwumere,
said the ceremony would hold on Friday, at the Amaikpe Square, Obinkita
village, Arochukwu at 1;30 pm, to mark the final new yam festival for the
Igbo. It will feature cultural exhibitions that cut across Igbo the
nation.
"This is the day we come to celebrate culture dances and thanking God
for protecting us throughout the year. The celebration is to mark the end of
a season and the beginning of another. More than 250 communities of
Arochukwu are expected to come together for the celebration of the event. It
is more of a home coming", he said.
He said in 2002 when Aro people commemorated the British occupation of
Arochukwu between 1901-1902, many tourist attractions were opened up and
these would be open to visitors in the grand finale where visitors would be
visiting as a remembrance.

Original_Search_Title: Why Investors Shun Nigeria - Oshiomhole
Headline: Nigeria: Why Investors Shun Nigeria - Oshiomhole
Body_Text:
PRESIDENT of Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), Comrade Adams Oshiomhole has
listed high interest rate, excessive devaluation of the Naira and dearth of
infrastructures among others as reasons why Nigeria is not witnessing both
direct foreign and local investments.
NLC's president said despite forcing Nigeria and other developing
countries to do away with subsidies, developed countries, especially the
United States of America (USA) has been using over 300 billion dollars
annually to subsidise the American farmers. Comrade Oshiomhole who spoke
weekend at the opening ceremony of the National Union of Textile, Garment
and Tailoring Workers of Nigeria (NUTGTWN)'s Annual National Officers'
seminar on strategic planning and policy development skills and capacity
building in Lagos called on government to develop the political will and
deal with smugglers and other economic criminals in the country.
He said: "Why are we not witnessing massive foreign direct investment in
spite of the effort of our government. Several reasons. Why are we not
witnessing sufficient domestic investment. Again, it is like the government
believe that Nigerian's environment is different from other environment and
so we apply some economic logics that appear totally contradictory and that
have failed to simulate investment in Nigeria particularly the prevailing
high interest rate. Interest rate still hovers around 20 to 30 per cent in
the last three to four years. We want people to invest in productive means,
in the productive sector, on businesses that are labour intensive, but
require long time gestation period and yet prevailing interest regime has
priced money out of the reach of investors. So, part of the consequences of
that, is that the structure of interest rate in the country encourages
traders rather than manufacturers. Yet Nigerians shed crocodile tears about
the fact that the Nigerian market has been taken over by imported products
and smuggled goods."
"Nigeria has devalued her national currency on the understanding that as
the naira becomes weaker and weaker, Nigerian goods will become cheaper from
the point of view of foreigners and foreign made goods become more expensive
from the point of view of the Nigerian consumers. And man being rationale
economic being would spend wisely and therefore, devaluation will help to
discourage import and encourage export."
"Today we have moved from one to one around 1986, 1987 before structural
adjustment to N128 to the dollar official market or the parallel market
which is probably the more active market. As at last week, I am told it is
about N141 to one US dollar. We all can feel the pain of devaluation and the
re-distribution of income that has resulted from it. Which may explains why
the government has to address the question of further devaluation of the
naira. So, we Nigerians have gone through the pains and yet it is obvious
that they have reaped no benefit from devaluation. Nigerians, have all
suffered, particularly the middle and the working class, have suffered
massive reduction in our real income."
There has been a substantial drop in real purchasing power in the past
15 to 18 years. Yet,Nigerians have not been compensated in other forms as we
were promised by the mothers and grand mothers of SAP. Today part of the
crisis that Nigerians face is that they are told that once the naira is
devalued and the banking system freed from controls through exchange rate
liberalisation and so on, there is no need to protect the borders. The
market place can then take care of every other thing through the invisible
hands. So, the government has been prevailed upon to liberalise trade and to
quote President Obasanjo, "as a result of these measures Nigerians were
importing tooth picks, even imports pounded yam and amala, as well as
plantain." That was what the president said. I have no reason not to believe
him. So, trade liberalisation has not in any way helped the Nigerian
economy. If any thing, it has led to exporting of jobs and importing of
un-employment. I think it is obvious to all of us why foreign investors do
not see Nigeria as an investors haven. In spite of all the rhetorics about
improving on public infrastructure, the truth remains, that Nigerian economy
is still driven largely by private generating sets rather than public power
supply. Many manufacturers still have to sink their own bole-holes and
source for their own water rather than depend on public water supply."
Comrade Oshiomhole noted that the problem of security, road networks,
power, and other infrastructural amenities must be addressed before Nigeria
could talk of economic growth.
He advised the government to place emphasis on creating conducive
environment for Nigerian manufacturers to take over the local market before
contemplating of taking them to AGOA, adding that US. that preached against
subsidy for developing countries had been subsidising American farmers with
over 300 billion dollars annually as well as imposing a 30 per cent duty ban
on steel import into the US.
He advised the Nigerian government to think more Nigerian than
globalisation or Western imposed economic policies.

Original_Search_Title: Ban On Rice Importation: Will It Materialise?
Headline: Nigeria: Ban On Rice Importation: Will It Materialise?
Body_Text:
Recently the national chairman of the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP,
Chief Audu Ogbeh told the nation that the government will put in place the
necessary mechanism to ban the importation of rice, fruit juice and milk by
2006. He revealed that it is costing the nation quite a lot in foreign
exchange to import the commodities.
This is cheering news not only for our farmers but for the overall
development of the agricultural economy, which also means the
diversification of the national economy, the absence of which has been
contributing to the misfortunes of the economy.
The United Nations World Food Programme has estimated that agriculture
could earn for Nigeria three times what she is getting from oil if properly
developed. It also said that Nigeria has the potential to produce Wheat and
rice enough to feed the West African sub-region, again if well attended
to.
We do not need any expert to tell us that we are capable of being self
sufficient in dairy products with enough for exports. Those who have visited
the Mambilla Plateau in Taraba State must have painfully witnessed how
cattle farmers pour out fresh milk daily for lack of buyers and local
processing plants. When we talk of gas flaring in the Niger Delta, another
evil face of our economy, it has never occurred to us that such wastages
occur to almost all the sectors of the economy. Because of inadequate local
processing dairy products our cattle farmers are being forced to pour out
torrents of fresh milk. Yam, which is one of our stable food products, is
always being wasted due to the absence of storage facilities. And it is not
in doubt that we can produce all the food drinks we need locally given the
right encouraging environment.
If we have been paying half the attention we are paying to the petroleum
industry to agriculture some of the problems we have been facing wouldn't
have occurred. The neglect of agriculture has forced attention on the
petroleum economy and we are painfully been witnesses to its devastating
effect. Elsewhere, the youths have been restive such that they no longer see
evil in human butchering, which they deploy sometimes for the purpose of
looting. The agricultural economy is no longer attractive as it used to
be. Its neglect, coupled with the massive importation of food items have
contributed to this, which in turn has indirectly contributed to the
violence we have been experiencing as a consequence of lack opportunities
for employment including in the agricultural sector. Thus, this neglect has
very serious security implications.
Every where in the world, government employ protective measures through
tariff regime to protect domestic production. In the developed economies of
America and Europe, they not only do this but also subsidize their farmers a
practice which they don't want us to do. They use the World Trade
Organization WTO, IMF and such other multilateral agencies to achieve their
