In this efforts to democratise the Nigerian polity in all facets, there cannot be sacred cows, and halleluja people who do not care about patriotism and nationalism. These guys are simply unfriendly friends within the polity. They need to be sanctioned.

Original_Search_Title: Kiwi Rewards Loyal Wholeselers
Headline: Uganda: Kiwi Rewards Loyal Wholeselers
Body_Text:
Kampala — Farming in Kampala region is to be legalised under a new law being drafted. Currently, under the 1964 Kampala City Council (KCC) Ordinance, farming in the city is still illegal.
Officials at the Ministry of Agriculture office in Kampala, say 30-40 percent of Kampala's 300,000 residences practices a certain form of peri-urban agriculture.
Ms Margaret Ssemwanga Azuba, the Kampala district agricultural extension officer, says that three studies done show that the poultry farming is one of the major agricultural activities.
Azuba says poultry products are mainly eggs, chicken and rabbits reared at the back-yard farms.
"A 1999 study by Mr John Muwanga Musisi, a senior officer in the Ministry of Agriculture at the Kampala office, shows that 70 percent of livestock products consumed in Kampala are produced in and around the city," Azuba said.
Milk from zero-grazed cows is dominant.
"Milk is sold to various city eating houses," she says.
She added that women councils have 32 fish ponds counted under the studies on Kampala urban farming.
According to Muwanga's statistics, 70.9 percent of city households engage in production of crops in and around Kampala; 12.6 raise city poultry products; 6.8 percent handle seri-culture while 5.8 percent on dairy cattle.
"But 40 percent of the eating houses in the city depend on city crops' products. Among these are cassava, yams, vegetables, fruits and a range of others," the report titled: Informal Food Markets, Household Food Provisioning and Consumption Patterns...(1998-99), states.
Azuba said that consultations are underway to collect views on urban agriculture, which will form the law reform content.
Stakeholders include livestock, poultry, aqua-culture and crop farmers; processors; academics; traders, politicians and district officials.
The British Department for International Development is supporting the reform process.

Original_Search_Title: Acacia : A Supplementary Food Market
Headline: Cameroon: Acacia : A Supplementary Food Market
Body_Text:
Bulk buying and weekend purchases are not its main activities.
Acacia is not like other markets in the nation's capital Yaounde where apart from foodstuff; clothes, hard wares and other variety are sold. Foodstuff, fruits, vegetable and other perishables meant for immediate consumption, constitute its main items. Though heavy purchases are seldom made here, Acacia remains a veritable trade centre.
The market is located at the junction, linking Rond Point Express, Carrefour Biyem-Assi and Government Bilingual High School Biyem-Assi. Passers-by may not easily notice the features of an existing market here. This is because the market is surrounded by CAM SIC, tall buildings and neighbouring private homes. The humming and buzzing aspects of most open air markets is absent. What, before now, tilted attention was the choky odour of urine and a pile of other undesirable elements which almost submerged the Yaounde VI council thrash can which was positioned just opposite the police check point. HYSACAM recently took away the can. Acacia does not also entertain "truck boys" who constitute an integral feature of other markets . This is because of its closeness to the main road whereby taxis easily pick up and chip off the goods. That apart, sellers reserve unsold goods in nearby homes.
One feature is peculiar of this market. It is the commonest meeting place for house boys and house girls in the Biyem-Assi area. This, because most supplementary articles are bought from here and often on working days when mistresses make haste to their offices. These house servants usually meet at "Yoghurt Corner" and share the experiences in their various homes. The market is seen as the bedrock of "secret love arrangements". Hence, the origin of unwarranted pregnancies of house girls. It also offers the disgusted servants opportunity to run away from their masters as soon as they receive money for shopping. Acacia is a source of trouble between masters and servants in Biyem-Assi.
Two main foodstuff are sold in two shifts. The first phase begins with excessive growling and barking of dogs which often take refuge in the make-shift shades. The arrival of shade owners at 6 am creates trouble for these animals which often put up stiff resistance. This phase begins with the sale of food items like rice, yams, gari, plantains, tomatoes, vegetable and other perishables Prices are quite high in this market for one reason ; the food is bought from neighbouring markets and sold back here A bunch of plantain sold in Mokolo at CFA 2000, for instance, is worth CFA 2800 in Acacia. Brooms and "bamboo chairs" are however very cheap. This is because travelling agencies like Azimenda, Tabo Express and others which are situated at the neighbourhood transport the articles from the West and dump here directly. The sale of fruits like oranges, grapes, limes, pineapples and others begin at 5: p.m. In fact, business folds up at about 10 p.m. with the violent barking of dogs that need the shades.

Original_Search_Title: Assistant Inspector General Ige - the Artful Abductor
Headline: Nigeria: Assistant Inspector General Ige - the Artful Abductor
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is spring. But signs of an extended drought are still visible.
Although the new yam festival has been marked, the farmlands of Ife Olukotun
AIG Raphael Ige's birth place in Yagba East Local Government Area of Kogi
State, have recorded their poorest yields in years.  Perhaps, because of the
low harvests or because of the cold bringing dry winds that now blows slowly
on the neighbourhood, residents of this medium sized pastoral community
would not hurry out of the embrace of their warm beds. The clear effect of
this, is the near desolate countryside. Hence, from its deserted fields to
its peeling and rioting colours of the walls, this neighbourhood wore an
unmistakable sombreness.
However, if land traffic was light, the same cannot be said of the
sky. Birds of varying plumage give raucous cries as they flew from one tree
to another. In a way, the agrarian community was used to this. But not to
another sound that also came through the air. This time, through the
airwaves. The network service of Radio Nigeria had reported the abduction of
Chris Ngige, the embattled governor of Anambra State by a team of policemen
led by their son Raphael Ige, Assistant Inspector General of police, AIG,
zone nine. A group of elderly men who sat round a radio set in Ife Olukotun
paid little or no attention to the news as they chatted away the day on a
basket of boiled corn and a keg of palm wine. But at the mention of Ige's
name, they exchanged curious glances. "Which Raphael?" One of the elders
managed to find his voice. Indeed, it was a drama that dislocated the quiet
town of Ife Olukotun and environs. "The information tingled the ear of
everyone around here," a young man told TheNEWS in Ife last week.
After the initial shock, apathy now reigns among Ige's kinsmen. And life
goes on in the village as usual. The reason for this is not fetched. "He is
our son, but he doesn't come home often. So not many people know him here,"
an elderly man offered, flashing caffeine-stained teeth. He might be
right. Except for a small, white coloured bungalow, located in the heart of
Ogbo compound, nothing in the ancient town reminds one of Ige's
ancestry. Yet, as a boy this was where his father brought him, yearly for
Christmas and New Year festivals. It was also where his mother operated a
beer parlour until she died about a decade ago. When he was old enough to
have a wife, it was also to the village he went to pick Ebun, (nee Adeyemi)
in 1972.
Although, Ige seldom goes home, having been born and bred in Osogbo, the
Osun State capital, his kinsmen are irredeemably proud of the profile of the
ancient town. A settlement of about 10, 000 inhabitants, people of Ife
Olukotun speak the Iyagba dialect, one of the major sub ethnic groups which
make up the Okun people of North Eastern Yoruba land. The town is commonly
called by its prefix, Ife.  Indigenes of this town are sometimes thought to
have migrated from Ile-Ife to their present site many centuries ago. Another
tradition however claims that Ife Olukotun is the cradle of the human
race. Ade Obayemi, the late professor of antiquities and former boss of the
National Museum and Monuments, argued in favour of the later tradition in
many of his works. As a way of escape from the constant invasions of the
Nupes in the nineteenth century, the people of Ife Olukotun dwelled on the
hills of Oke Ife and Oluwa mountains. They moved to the present site about
the year 1922. Restrained by their geography and history, these former
mountain dwellers are traditional warriors, farmers and hunters. It is
therefore not uncommon to still find among this people, names that depict
these age long vocations.  But tell tale signs of civilization also
abound. Ife Olukotun boasts of a general hospital, a police station, two
public secondary schools, two public primary schools and for power supply,
it is connected to the national grid. But going to or coming from Ife
Olukotun is far from being a pleasure ride. All the access roads either from
Isanlu, Mopa, Kabba all in Kogi State, or from Omuo in Ekiti State are in
bad shape. "You can see that our roads are bad.  This is what we have been
begging government to do for us. We are suffering" Alhaji S.A. Mohammed, the
Olukotun of Ife Olukotun told TheNEWS in pitiable tones.
Raphael's parents were then living in Osogbo as traders. His father was
a cocoa merchant. Royalty flows in his veins. Pa Ige was a descendant of
Ogbo compound, one of the five ruling houses of Ife Olukotun. An adult male
member of any of the ruling houses could aspire to the throne of Olukotun
whenever the stool is vacant. At the moment, the stool is being highly
contested. Perhaps, Ige's legendary assault on democracy in far away Anambra
State would have been better understood, if it had been directed at this
throne, to which he could legitimately aspire on retirement. But an
introvert, and a man said not to have a liking with the staid life of Ife
Olukotun, it was in far away Awka that Ige struck.
Things started taking a bizarre turn when Ige left his Umuahia, the Abia
State capital base and led about 250 mobile policemen to Anambra State
government house just at about half past ten in the morning.  Their mission?
To arrest, or in the words of the AIG "put into protective custody" the
executive governor of the State.
Against all protocols, the AIG forced his way into the governor's office
in a commando style, disarmed the governor's aides and the aide-de-camp to
the governor, Innocent Umeria. Ige then told the governor that he had orders
from above to put him in protective custody. This according to him, is on
the strength of a letter that Ngige signed that he had resigned from office
as executive governor of the state. The governor protested to no avail. His
cell phone was seized and he was immediately driven to Choice Hotel, where
he was held captive and incommunicado for hours. Reprieve came his way when
an aide sneaked in a cell phone with which he contacted the outside
world.
As it turned out, the IG was only acting out a plot scripted by the
governor's godfathers who claimed they were the ones who helped him into
office. There was also the allegation that Ige was acting on the strength of
a N13 million bribe offered to him by Chris Uba, the self acclaimed
godfather and the arrow head of those baying for the governor's head. In a
jiffy, the govenror was dethroned. But that was just the beginning of the
macabre dance. By the time Ngige regained freedom, his deputy Okey Udeh,
supported by speaker of the state house of assembly and Secretary to the
State Government SSG, Uchenna Emordi, had already declared himself
governor.
After a half-hearted attempt to resolve the crises, by the ruling
People's Democratic Party, PDP, Ngige returned to office and all the
principal actors in the coup were dismissed from the party. Ige himself was
punished by compulsory retirement, which came on Sunday 13 July.
But to Ige, the events of that Thursday morning were a familiar
terrain. The AIG enjoys a cordial relationship with Uba, with whom he
hobnobs regularly. He was even said to have attended a party in Uba's house
few weeks before the assault. Uba's admittance of this close relationship
depends on his mood. But in an interview with TheNEWS he argued that it is
not out of place for Ige to be close to him. "At my level in the society,"
he said: "I should know the AIG in my zone, so what's the big deal?" he
asked. Ordinarily, there should be no big deals. However, Ige may have
unwittingly become a tool in Uba's political arsenal. The IG, it was learnt,
had, during the elections, directed Ige to take instructions from Uba. Since
then, Ige has kept his loyalty intact for Uba even in his bid to foist a
hegemony on the people of Anambra.
Before Ngige, another Anambra politician, Godwin Osele had been a victim
of Ige's strong arm. As narrated by Osele, a former chairman of Aguata Local
Government council, he was abducted by the trio of Uba, Chuma Nzeribe,
former Security Adviser to Chinwoke Mbadinuju and Ige on 4 March 2002. The
three men had invaded the chairman's office and executed what amounted to a
"daylight coup." Incidentally, all the three men are major characters in the
current saga. The former chairman said the men, backed by some policemen,
arrested and locked him up in the booth of a car and sped off. Osele said
though he was later released at Government house, Awka, he was not allowed
to return to his office as the Local government chairman because another
person has been sworn-in in his stead. His erstwhile councillors, Osele
recounted, were later rewarded for their co-operation.
Therefore, the retired AIG had the burden of commissioning members of
the eight man Senate committee set up to investigate the Anambra crisis that
he was not in conjunction with his collaborators trying to act out a similar
script on 9 July in Awka.
"All I did was on my own initiative; my own attempt to save the
governor's life. I think I have done my best to protect his life," Ige told
the Senate committee. According to him, he moved to Awka on that day based
on a security report of a threat to the governor's life. His action,
therefore, he claimed was to secure the governor's life. The ex-AIG also
tried to justify other actions he took that day. The disarming of the
governor's ADC, he told Senate was to avoid any irrational outburst from the
ADC who had then become fidgety. "To tell the truth sir, I took the phone
from the ADC. He was jittery, we felt we were not safe from the way he was
behaving."
This version of events was however disputed by the ADC who also
testified before the Senate committee. Reacting to Ige's claim, Umere
insisted that his pistol was taken from him even before he could conclude
his official military salutations to the superior officer.  Umere said the
former AIG also physically assaulted him because of his resistance to the
attempt to take away his boss. The ADC who said the former AIG further got
angry and ordered him to be arrested when he continued to communicate with
the governor in the Igbo language.  He said, "the AIG directed that I be
taken to the office of the Assistance Commissioner of police (operations)
where I was instantly issued with a letter of transfer to Imo State." With
his testimony, the ADC successfully put a lie to the claims of the AIG. Yet,
Ige also contradicts himself in his testimony before the committee. For one,
his claims that he acted alone was a clear contradiction of what he told the
governor that he was acting on orders from the above."
Not many people are attaching any weight to the AIG's tale. The senate
committee that looked into the Anambra crisis didn't spare the AIG
either. "The role of AIG Raphael Ige is ignoble, disgraceful, despicable and
dishonourable, as it was scandalous, shameful and sordid.' The committee
holds that the whole abduction was premeditated and that AIG Ige only
carried out his part of the script. But the committee couldn't prise out of
Ige if he was acting on orders from above as he allegedly told the governor
in captivity.  Indeed the committee wants a judicial commission set up to
probe the abduction saga. It holds that compulsory retirement of the AIG and
was in no way commensurate with the offence. Though the committee had a
closed door meeting with the Inspector-General of police, Tafa Balogun, it's
report is rather silent on the outcome of the meeting.
Those who know Ige well described him as 'quiet and unassuming.' He
often wears a serious mien and seldom spoke. But this taciturnity was
partially an enforced condition. He stammers, and was often a victim of
constant banters from his peers. This was a trait that continued to manifest
in his adulthood. Thus, tried as he did to hide this disorder, it is
apparent even to a visitor that he has a speech defect. "He kept to himself
most of the times, but he is always ready to listen to people" was how a
Chief Superintendent of Police, CSP who had once worked under the embattled
AIG put it.
Some describe Ige as quick-tempered, blaming it as his speech
defects. But Ige's associates say he is not short fused. "However once
angry, it is difficult to persuade him," said the deeply reflective
CSP. This must be his attribute both at work and at home.  It was that type
of anger that defined the latter day relationship between Raphael and his
mother. Accounts are common of how at various times he neglected the aged
woman. A deeply religious man who believes in the power of prophecy, Ige is
said to be found of consulting prophets. So much was his faith in
clairvoyance, that he lives a good part of his life in perpetual fear of
witches and wizards. These twin agents are allegedly like a chimera on his
superstitious mindset. Little wonder, while serving in Minna in the 1980s he
was said to have suffered incubus attacks. In a short time h e became
convinced that his aged mother was the night marauder who constantly preyed
on his soul. And not being one to call a spade by other names, he promptly
accused his mother of witchcraft.
Neglected and treated as an outcast by her only son, Mama struggled to
eke out a living selling beer and allied products at Ilai junction in Ife
Olukotun. But her son's growth in the police was unabated. But like a soul
alien to purgatory, Ige would not set a friendly eye on the old woman. Not
even when it became evident that his suspicious were unfounded. This
strained relationship between mother and child lasted until the former died
in September 1991. She did not die without a parting gift. Most people
conversant with the story are of the opinion, that the mother did not bless
him while in the throes of death. Even then, the fear of witches and wizards
was said to have dictated the direction of his relationship with his
kinsmen. He would rarely come home. In turn, when he was compelled to visit
home for his father's burial in year 2000, many of the villagers shunned
him.  He has not gone home since then. Not even in April this year when he
visited Igbagun, a neighbouring town, for the burial of a friend.
How did Ige acquire his religious sensibilities? Not much can be said
about this. Raphael was brought up in a strict Christian tradition.  He was
born into the ECWA church. It is not clear at what point he abandoned the
church. But with the advent of new generation churches, he soon found
another love. His wife, Ebun was said to be his forerunner in the Synagogue
Church of All Nations, a controversial church led by Pastor Temitope
Joshua. Joshua claims authority over sicknesses, demons and
misfortunes. Ige's wife is believed to be a prophetess in the fold. Little
wonder, when the story of the Anambra coup saga broke, Ige made his first
public appearance in the Synagogue. That was in the night of Wednesday 16
July. Some days earlier, Tafa Balogun, the embattled Inspector General of
Police IGP, who had laboured hard to prove his innocence on the saga had
testified before a senate committee where he disclosed that the police
authority does not know Ige's whereabouts. But at about 11pm that Wednesday
night, Ige and his wife made for the Synagogue. They tried to conceal their
identities under the cover of the night, but the moon gave them away. They
slept in the church and left the following day. A Lagos daily newspaper
quoted an aide of Joshua as saying, "he came here in a blue Peugeot 406 car
with his wife."  Continuing he said, "he was accompanied by a sergeant and a
driver.  He looked sober. He did not discuss with anyone. When he left on
Thursday, he was wearing brown safari suit."
A tough cop, Ige is believed to have impressive capacity for
deception. His brothers and sisters at home would bet that he abhors
alcohol. But those close to him at work alleged that he drank and smoked
heavily.
Unfortunately, not many people are close to the police boss. Ige is a
virtual loner. Luckily for him his mother had only two children. His only
sister commonly called Aunty Iyabo lives independent of his influence in
Ilorin, the Kwara State capital. Ige is alleged to be tight fisted. Iyabo, a
petty trader, makes up for her financial needs by making regular forays into
politics. In this, she has carved a niche for her self-earning the trust of
Olusola Saraki, the strongman of Kwara politics. The Sarakis are said to
have found her an engaging hand in the mobilisation of women. "Look at his
sister, she is not supposed to be suffering if Ige had wanted to help
her. Now she is just on her own."
And so are several of his kinsmen. TheNEWS gathered that apart from his
house in the village, and a police station, Ige has helped about five
indigenes of the town to enter the police force. Perhaps because of the
demands of his profession he rarely comes home nor does he attend meetings
where progress of the village is discussed. But indigenes say he donates
money to the village association whenever the need arises. "He is too
stingy. Several times I have trekked back from his office in Lagos because
the man will always complain of money. In fact many times it is his personal
assistan that will give you transport money" an indigene of his town told
this magazine. He added, "I have received more money from his PA than from
him."
Other people, however, believe Ige is just a man who does not succumb to
societal pressure to live above his income, "I have never believed that Ige
is corrupt, that is why one is surprised to find him in his mess. He is a
Godly man that should not have ended his career in this way."
But Godliness is one thing. Greed is another. Not a few people believe
the AIG is not above board. They point to his properties as proof of an
acquisitive streak. He is said to be an automobile freak.  His favourite
cars are Honda products and Jeeps. He is believed to possess several
varieties of these among other brands. He has also acquired modest real
estate properties at Ilorin, Lokoja and Lagos.  His house in the village
does not even rank as the one of the best.  Painted white and built since
1986 it is a five room face-me-I-face-you bungalow with a detached kitchen
and toilet.
If his country home is small, it does not apply to his buildings in
Lokoja and other places. Located in the heart of Adankolo, a Government
Reserved Area, in the Kogi State capital, the edifice stands out in the
neighbourhood. It is also painted white. Ige was said to have moved in some
of his properties to this house since the beginning of his ordeals. Apart
from serving as a residential quarter, the premises is also home to A-TACOM
water products. The outfit believed to be owned by his wife is a NAFDAC
approved bagged water producing company that is equipped with the latest
technology in the water business. Water produced from here are distributed
to customers in Lokoja and Abuja respectively. Ige has two known properties
in Lagos and some undeveloped plots of land.
Yet Ige's preparation and expectations in retirement may have suffered a
heavy blow by his unceremonious exit from the force.  TheNEWS investigations
revealed that aside other plans, his most important ambition was to retire
to active politicking. It was not clear to what office he intended to
aspire, but those he confided in say he was banking on support from the
erstwhile minister for works, Chief Anthony Anenih. Ige was said to have
served under the President Obasanjo's chief strategist when he (Anenih) was
still in the Nigerian police.
Now with a hazy political future, barring all moves to prosecute him,
Ige may need to look inwards for life in retirement. His five children may
well provide the succour he desperately needs. He has spared nothing in
training each of them. His marriage to Ebun has been blessed with five
children- four females and a male. His first child Tayo is a medical doctor,
she graduated from the University of Ilorin, and the second Kemi, is a first
class graduate of economics from Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria. Toyin,
another female is an undergraduate of the University of Ado-Ekiti, while the
only boy Dare attends University of Lagos. Remi, the last is yet to enter
the University.
Ige, may have lived dual life while in service. This is because his
activities in his Umuahia base contrast with those outside. To many
residents of Umuahia, the Abia State capital, it was a surprise that the
former police boss was involved in the abduction of Ngige. Ige was said to
have been very strict with the rank and file of the police in his zone. He
did not waste time to punish corrupt officers who were caught extorting
money from members of the public. He had either sacked them or directed
their prosecution.
The former Assistant Inspector-General of Police, TheNEWS was told,
maintained an open door policy such that it was easier to see him than even
a Divisional Police Officer, DPO.
A few instances of his non-compromising nature were given. One of such
was his refusal to be influenced during an Ezeship tussle involving the
people of Amuzukwu autonomous community in Umuahia local council area of the
state in August last year. Chief James Akwukwaegbu, a community leader, had
foisted an unpopular traditional ruler on the community, but the people
resisted it, and went ahead to celebrate their new yam festival outside the
traditional ruler's authority. The resistance led to crisis, and Akwukwaegbu
went to lobby Ige to help in sustaining the new Eze. But Ige was reported to
have shunned gratification from the community leader and advised that the
people should be allowed to select their own Eze.
In another instance, Ige was said to have resisted attempts to drag him
into the political crisis that erupted before the last elections in Abia
State between Governor, Dr. Orji Uzor Kalu and the Abuja-based politicians
from the state during the last dispensation.  The Abuja based politicians
included Chief Ojo Maduekwe, Prince Vincent Ogbulafor and Onyema Ugochukwu
amongst others. Each faction had wanted to use Ige against the other, but
Ige reportedly insisted that the crisis was an intra-party affair advising
the politicians to sort themselves out.
According to sources, it was as a result of this that Governor Kalu
celebrated Ige's involvement in the Anambra coup. Apart from championing the
call for the prosecution of Ige, Kalu has, also, been impatient with
President Olusegun Obasanjo's non-committal disposition towards the penalty
for Ige's involvement in Ngige's abduction.
But, the foregoing instances of Ige's seeming sound professional
standing contradict his recent role in Anambra State. The rank and file of
the police in the zone 9 headquarters were said to have rejoiced over his
misfortune because of his strictness. But most residents said they were
taken aback given his conducts and life-style in the state.
Could Ige have been innocent? Or could he have acted on directives from
higher authorities? A senior police officer at the zone 9 who craved
anonymity told TheNEWS that Ige confided in his friends that he received
directives for the assignment from the Inspector-General of Police, IG, Tafa
Balogun.
Another school of thought, however, observed that the ex-AIG mixed with
for money bags and fraudsters who were ready to influence him with huge sums
of money. He was said to have been implicated in a case of 419 Kingpins from
Mbire in Imo State who killed a driver. The 911 lorry, the ill-fated man was
driving reportedly broke down on road and blocked the convoy of the
fraudsters. The fraudsters allegedly took the law into their hands. They
beat their victim and boasted that nothing would happen if he died and that
Ige was their friend. The man died, and the Kingpins talked his death over
with Ige. Nothing eventually happened.
Certainly, Ige did not bargain for the outcome of his last
assignment. He has already relocated to Lagos to pick the pieces of his
life.
Not much is known about Ige's educational background. He attended his
primary and secondary schools in Oshogbo. He took courses in Ahmadu Bello
University, ABU Zaria and Federal University of Technology, FUT, Minna. He
enlisted as a cadet Inspector at the age of 21 on 1st August 1968 with an
Advance Diploma in Public Administration. At the end of his training, he was
posted to the Police College Kaduna. At the beginning of his career,
promotion was slow. A moment of breakthrough came in 1987 when he joined the
rank of senior police officers, SUPOL with his promotion to Assistant
Superintendent of Police, ASP. Thus promoted, he served briefly in Jos
before he was returned to Kaduna.
From Kaduna he was transferred to Bida in the 80s where he served as a
district Police officer. A resident of Bida who knew him in those days
described him as an alcoholic. Benin was his next point of call.  As a
Deputy Commissioner of Police, DCP, he served in Yola and Benin among
others. His journey to prominence began when he became a commissioner of
police first in Zamfara State and later in force headquarters, Kam Salem
House, Lagos. Until 24 May 2001 when he was promoted AIG, he was
commissioner of police in charge of the federal special anti-robbery squad,
an arm of the force crime intelligence department, Lagos, Ige was scheduled
for retirement on 1st August.  However, three weeks to the date, his
glorious career came to an abrupt end in an inglorious manner. Would he ever
reclaim his lost glory?

Original_Search_Title: Kongi And 'Beauty'
Headline: Nigeria: Kongi And 'Beauty'
Body_Text:
ONE elderly man walked up to me a few days back, he carried a worried look and asked me: now that the elephants are fighting ... what do we do? He was pained and he probably expected me to complete the trite saying for him that the grass would suffer. I won't complete it for him because in the current matter my feeling is that the grass should be better for it. Of course, he was referring to the current altercations between Wole Soyinka and Segun Obasanjo - two giant Nigerians in their respective fields.
W.S., as Wole is affectionately called, Nobel laureate for literature is better known as Kongi for his durability. But in fact Kongi is the name of the central character and title of one of the laureate's famous plays - Kongi's Harvest, a political satire. WS himself reminds me of the Kongi character - I think played by Demas Nwoko - gaunt-looking man with a crown of Don King hair profile. W.S. crown of hair and goatee are now luxuriant white; except that W.S. has filled out with a skin complexion that is lighter than the original Kongi.
W.S. is not new to raising dust but in fairness to him he has not, never unwittingly kicked dust without a worthy cause since I first knew him. The first time I became aware of his presence was way back in 1964, when he and his late and lamented friend, James (Aji)Bola Ige engaged in the agitation against a Professor Marshall of the University of Ibadan. I cannot now recall the details of that engagement but I know it was not calculated to endear Professor Marshall to them. W.S. was then teaching at the university, Bola Ige's alma mater, the university he fondly reminded anyone who cared to listen as the university.
It was a time of literary and cultural activities of renascent proportions in Ibadan and the Mbari Club at Ogunpa Oyo was the melting pot for that awakening. Most of the movers and shakers of the literary and cultural scene and the potential ones were there. Among these were to be found W.S. and his inseparable friend, Bola Ige. Of course, Christopher Okigbo, the poet and Ulli Bier, husband of Iya Olosun, Suzzane Wenger, were some of the notable ones. In their wings were late Wale Ogunyemi, Femi Fatoba, Yomi Obileye, Jimmy Solanke, Tunji Oyelana, Betty Okotie, Jimmy Johnson and many others I cannot now remember.
W.S. had not become a universally celebrated person he has now become but his star was on the ascendancy; Bola Ige had started to cause ripples in the political waters and no one doubted that he was destined to be distinguished in his profession (Law) and in his avocation (Politics). Both friends had been coming from farther than 1964 when I made their acquaintance in the course of my professional life. I was then on the Nigerian Tribune in Ibadan. Both men had weathered the political storms together and these have been quite a number.
W.S. has always been found on the side of justice and equity and no-one may accuse him of shyness when it comes to fighting the cause. And he has suffered for it: witness The Man Died. If he feels hurt and justice for his departed friend and comrade-in-arm no-one can blame him. By every consensus, W.S. can be grandiloquent and elegant in his delivery and can, at the same time, be tellingly intense and brutal. It just adds to the reliefs that build up the portrait of W.S. What the elderly man who walked up to me feared was probably the quake that occasioned when a literary giant clashed with a military bull-dozer which is what Segun Aremu Okikiola Obasanjo is.
From what has transpired so far, it is clear that Segun does not like taking things very low (and I mean it seriously, not in the libidinal sense). He has been a man that has had to fight for his life all the way, even if we admit that Providence has dealt him not quite a few lucky hands. He has been used to fighting that for a moment it may occur to us that Don Quixote may have been a fact fictionised.
Segun sees shadows in substances and I bet that his pious picture of closing eyes in benediction may not be true. Segun is likely to be peeping through his eye-lids ... just in case. What some will call all ears. The deprivations in his youth must have taught him to be distrustful of all - without exception. So much so that it has become a reflex with him to fight whether there is cause for it or not. Some people have been uncharitable enough to have remarked that he acts (speaks) first and thinks later. I may not subscribe to that because not a few have attested to his cranial capacity as prodigious even if you concede that Satan himself is highly fertile upstairs.
For the avoidance of doubt and for the record, Segun has been an engineer; an Army commander; commander-in-chief of the Armed Forces of the Federal Republic; head of state and President of Nigeria. He had seen good and bad times - he escaped military execution; got jailed by his junior and left the confines of the prison for the comfort of Aso Rock Villa. He had seen it all.
Baba Iyabo never forgets his roots. He is at home with pounded yam and bush meat and may top that with palm wine. You need to be prepared to shed tears as he regales you with jokes in patent Owu dialect with all the nuances (or is it banalities). Despite all of this, he does not want the chicken to tell the wild cat that there is cataract in its eyes. With him the shaving stick does the kings hair at its own peril. Both men (the elephants) are acerbic in their language and Baba Iyabo especially so. Baba Iyabo has the additional attribute of not only thrusting in the knife but turning it in the wound as well - a most painful thing for those whose bad fortune it is to be at the receiving end.
W.S. might have been hurt but he tried to help restore sanity to the situation - a task that will help all of us but Segun more than all of us. Segun charged. W.S. directed our attention to the lack of attention; to the script by Segun - what may lend credence to the charge that Segun acts before he thinks. A most unfortunate thing if it were true. Shunned of all the fury on all sides what do we have? That the country is awash with political charlatans and assassins who are not imbued with the high notion of the highest good for the greatest number. People who will kill to abort the hopes of many; people for whom filthy lucre means more and people's lives mean less. People who are giving the nation a bad name all over the place.
I submit that, vehement as W.S. might have sounded, it is a patriotic service that ought to be appreciated by the President of the Republic. Obasanjo claims to be a friend of Bola Ige. Even if Ige were not his friend, he was Minister of Justice - Number One Law Officer. It is a sad commentary that Obasanjo has not been able to do anything about it. Even without all that, the buck stops at the President's desk, in this as in all matters.
The most remarkable thing about our President has been his very sharp tongue. Of course, there is his famous bad temper. He can do something about them. He may think he does not need our mandate again, but he would or may need our support for somebody else in future. We may be constrained to judge that person by the company (of Obasanjo) he keeps. From all this, it does not seem to me that the grass has suffered. Rather, it has been made manifest that in democracy all sorts contend. That can only be healthy.

Original_Search_Title: Silver Medal Family
Headline: Nigeria: Silver Medal Family
Body_Text:
Lagos — SOLDIERING is associated with brewery and discipline even as its
practitioners are known as strong defenders of territorial boundaries. But
despite these good characteristics, people still have a way they look at
solders: perhaps with a touch of scorn, contempt and disdain.
This belief arose, maybe because soldiers are seen as unfriendly people,
people solely trained the hardway to shoot and snuff life out of others,
blood-thirsty and ruthless personalities, a people without any touch of
fellow-feeling and a people without a trace of compassion.
In other words, the profession has an associational ring with violence,
killing, maiming, brutality and ruthlessness.
Civilians, especially politicians in the saddle of power dread them a
lot, academics don't fancy them either, so do parents not want their wards
to be identified with men in this 'fraternity' not to talk of their
daughters getting married to them.
Nevertheless, whereas people hold this opinion, these smart looking men
in starched uniform think otherwise. In their view they are like other
mortals - flowing with blood in their veins, inundated with the milk of
human kindness as well as refined sensibilities.
Really, how else do you explain away the fact that a profession full of
diverse professionals comprising among others doctors, engineers,
journalists and lawyers are not imbued with men of substantial humanity; how
do you explain that these men, physically strong, with well-balanced
proportions between their trunks and limbs are not full of humanness.
Little wonder like bees to honey, women go after them. Yes, a flurry of
beautiful women always tug around them, after all, they have all it takes to
keep a home: they are educated, knowledgeable and strong.
Think of such beautiful wives of former Generals like Mrs. Maryam
Babangida, Mrs. Maryam Abacha, Mrs. Ronke Ayuba and a host of others. For
those in the military circle, they understand the meaning of the four letter
word - love - more than other professionals and this effectively explains
why they treat their wives with honour and dignity contrary to people's
expectations.
This assertion was so much corroborated by no other than a retired
one-star General and former Director of Army Information, Brig. Gen. Fred
Chijuka who said the profession is synonymous with respectability.
The General who married as a teacher prior to joining the Army in 1971
said he has since positively altered his relationship with his wife,
Josephine.
"I think I was even more reckless with her before. But since I joined
the Army, I think I have been a little more sympathetic and a little more
careful not to offend her.
"Before I have always tried to 'play hard' on her at times when I am
rally put off, but I think I softened up more when I joined the Army. I
discovered that in the military, we respect women a lot, particularly our
wives," he said frankly.
Speaking to Panache last Tuesday about his marital life that has lasted
35 years, the teacher-turned-soldier narrated with ease how the journey
which began in 1967 culminated in marriage a year after.
Recalling how the journey started, its vicissitudes, the eventual
marriage, and its twists and turns was no big task for the former Army
spokesman who from every indication remembered the game-plan and strategies
he employed to woo his wife with enthusiasm and a measured clarity.
There was this day, he said while he was on holidays that he saw this
girl, slim, beautiful with her hair neatly pushed behind and with a small
belt on her waist. She was just passing through his village road when he saw
her and something in him like a wayward yearning told him to inquire more
about her.
So he asked his mother about the girl, but the answer he got was not so
satisfactory to equate to the organic sense of love that almost immediately
seized him.
In truth, in the brief but fleeting moment he saw her, he felt in him
something strong, even as a feeling of love, hope and optimism thoroughly
enveloped him. Somewhere in the recess of his heart, he had begun to nurse a
feeling that maybe something could happen between them.
To put this feeling, this desire into reality, he continued the inquiry
and this took him to the girl's village as well as to her school, then a
Catholic Teachers' Training College in Ughelli.
At the college, he sent that she be called and on sighting him, she ran
and ran to embrace him, an action she told Panache was done innocently.
"We use to see him, so when I now saw him in far away Ughelli I ran and
embraced him, you know as somebody from the same town. That was all I had in
mind.
"I just embraced him because we were from the same area. I never knew
that the action meant another thing to the young man.
"Later he started asking me questions - ah! ah! so this man has
something up his sleeves," she stated.
For Mr. Chijuka, then a young graduate from the University of Ife, the
warm reception was more than meets the eye. More than anything, it prepared
him over and over on the path of love and confidence.
The action not only satisfied his initial promptings and excitement that
maybe something would come out of this, but it gave him the needed push to
put across his request. Insofar as he was concerned, the deal has been
struck and he was ready to hit the bull's eye in the military fashion.
"Then I said thank God since she embraced me like that it means she
likes me. So I now went home and told my mother I have seen the girl and
that her school was very close to where I was teaching.
"So she now asked are you interested in her. I said yes. She then said,
well if you are interested, you shouldn't worry just go back to your
station. Maybe by the time you come in December, she would be around then we
would open up the matter," he noted.
True to type, everybody returned in December and his father took the
proposal to the girl's family. Ordinarily, it would have received the
readiest acquiescence taking into consideration his pedigree and that of his
family except that Joe as he fondly calls his hearthrob was looking forward
to becoming a reverend sister.
Indeed that was the only snag, so her family demanded for time. Besides
this, he said his family cashed in on other interests like using her auntie,
relatives and relationship. Eventually she jettisoned the idea of becoming a
reverend sister and agreed to marry him.
But did the courtship last, Brig. Gen. Chijuka said no, pointing out
that what prolonged it to 1968 was the Nigeria Civil War.
"When the war was not too bad in our area, we started very serious
arrangement and by November 9, 1968 we had the wedding," he said.
Shortly after, the union produced the first child, Isioma, but not
without the accompanying expectation and anxiety.
"Then my wife was very slim and I was very excited that a slim person
could be pregnant," he said. At this remark, Joe who was busy pretending to
be computing some figures with the calculator, quipped, "say whatever you
want to say." And her husband retorted MTN the best connection.
Their talking to Panache, no doubt, elicited in them the urge to
remember their initial love life, to tell the story the way it is and to try
to make their feeling known. It was a feeling that made them look like a
newly wedded couple and the excitement in their modest business office at
the Tafawa Balewa Square (TBS) was thick.
"Even with her big tummy, she pounded yam for me. She will do all the
house chore without complaining. She was really very strong.
"Like the day she delivered, I was just informed that she was in labour,
but before I could reach home and come back, she had delivered," he said
full of hysteria.
The Catholic family above all has been blessed with six children - four
girls and two boys. Isioma who is resident in United States with her
husband; Ikenna, Ekene, Ifeyinwa, Nneamaka, Onyineye.
Brig. Gen. Chijuka while examining what has produced and sustained the
going family, said it was God, compatibility, love and commitment of all
family members.
"The secret is trust in God. There is no other thing than trust in God
and He has be with us.
"Also, we make these children realise the importance of hardwork. For
example, Ikenna who read Marine Engineering made a second class upper. Ekene
who studied Chemical Engineering made a second class upper, Nneamaka who
read Economics also made a second class upper. I hope the last one,
Ifeyinwa, who is still in school will make a second class upper. So it has
been a combination of all these factors, but mainly God's help and love for
us," he said with humility.
Despite having an understanding wife and parading children with fine
credentials and designations, among other attendant streaks of successes,
Brig. Gen. Chijuka noted that there has not been any major obstacle for the
past 35 years.
Since his children and wife were quite healthy, the only thing he
considered a set back was lack of money.
"God has been of special favour to us because none has ever been so sick
that one would say that this is abnormal. It does not mean that at one time
or the other that they have not gone to the hospital, but there has never
been any serious ailment that would make us to say why is this happening
this way.
"So we have not had any serious obstacles except perhaps money. If you
are looking at my pocket for plenty of money you won't find it. But the
miracle is that we keep going," he stated jokingly.
Reacting to the activities of inlaws and interlopers, Afresco as his
wife calls him, said the family had never witnessed their intervention.
But almost immediately, he paused and with his left hand, touched his
head in an attitude of meditation. And what caused this prayerful outlook
was not far-fetched as he reminisced.
"I remember even as a young man when you marry, there is always these
funny ideas that you would want to be out every time, not looking for women,
but keeping the same company you have been keeping before.
"And then as a teacher, I had habit of coming late especially after
teaching English and Literature and marking certain papers that would almost
get you mad. So at times after marking, I would be out drinking with my
friends in the town.
"So there was this day, she flared up and accused me of coming back
late, so I told her I was going to report her.
"I remember she said, no, let us not take any of our disagreement to
anybody. Young as she was I was highly impressed. So we have never given
anybody room to know what we are doing in our family," he said.
His wife who was still computing some statistical figures abruptly
stopped what she was doing and looked at her husband, her mouth slightly
opened, indicating that she was all along indirectly engrossed in the
discussion.
She herself concurred to this even as she enjoined younger couples to
always have patience.
Patience, Mrs. Chijuka said was the watchword, counselling that
matrimonial problems should not be taken to a third party.
"Women should not be carrying their problems, matrimonial problems to
their friends. Stay in the four walls of your room and discuss whatever the
problem is. Don't tell your friends your husband gives you N10 because she
will tell you her husband gives her N100. Na lie.
"I always tell them stay with your husband and discuss whatever you
feel. Don't discuss your problems with outsiders," the retired teacher
insisted.
The veteran, yes, in marriage, who acknowledged that the family has come
a long way, said she would re-marry her husband time and time again.
Her husband, she said, has got some beautiful qualities.
"Of course, of course, I will go for Fred any time, any day. Nobody
else, no, no no. It must be Fred. So that is it.
"Well you have heard him talk and the majority of Fred is goodness. He
is good. He is a nice man," she reiterated in a measured but laden voice of
enthusiasm.
For the Chijukas, it has been 35 years of little rough edges, but so
much happiness. It has been a relationship of unending understanding and
affection. It has been a sing-song of love and satisfaction.
No doubt, the web of associational memory, especially the symbolic
embrace is still fresh on their mind even as they look forward to more
blissful years ahead.
For the sweethearts, it has been 35 days, nay 35 years of compatibility
and joy.

Original_Search_Title: Eloquent Testimonies
Headline: Nigeria: Eloquent Testimonies
Body_Text:
Last week, The British Council (Nigeria) celebrates its 60 years of
existence as several Nigerians count their blessings from the Council's
stable akeem lasisi At 60, The British Council has many stories to tell. If
it were a human being, no matter how successful it had been, having seen six
decades would have meant that it would have been hosting the aged.
But because it is an agency that symbolises an ideal, a culture and a
dream, the British Council is only waxing stronger with the rolling
seasons. That is why its authorities rolled out the drums in Lagos, last
week, showcasing its achievements since it opened shop in Nigeria in
1943.
Members of the diplomatic corps, civil society, artists, community and
beneficiaries of its projects and programs thronged the Lagoon Restaurant,
Ikoyi, venue of the Diamond Jubilee celebration. They were treated to live
music, dance (drama) and buffet that, quite symbolically, included pounded
yam.
From the British High Commissioner in Nigeria, Mr.Phillip Thomas, to the
Council's Director, John Richards, and other senior officials, came words of
fulfillment and promises of renewed vigour and expanded focus at the
celebration. But their own testimonies were not even as eloquent as those of
Nigerians whose lives have been touched, over the decades, by the
Council.
Telling the British Council's story one after the other, some had gone
as far as declaring the council delivered them from what could have been the
blind alley of their lives.
Accomplished dancer, Arnold Udoka, who told the audience that the
British Council is like a mother to him. In 1985, the Council had sponsored
his advanced training in dance abroad, thus "making me what I am
today. "Having created me" added the international dancer, "I have also
given birth to so many dance children, and these children knew their
grandmother" According to Mr John Momoh, owner of Channels Television, "you
can't talk of Channels Television and its success without talking about the
British Council." The Council has been supporting him since the early stages
of his life. At a time he travelled to see all television stations in the
United Kingdom, on the bill of the British Council. This experience, he
says, procreated his media outfit.
At another level, the British Council scholarship program has
contributed immensely to the educational development of many Nigerians. One
of its beneficiaries, Dr. Anthonia Adindu, who saluted the British
government for committing millions of pounds to the program, noted that over
700 Nigerians have benefited from it since 1984. Chief Medical Director,
Aminu Kano Teaching Hospital, Dr.  Suleiman Wali, shared Adindu's
feelings. Wali's first contact with the British Council was in 1953, when he
was in the primary school.  The Council's library used to organise drama and
debates, apart from providing books for use. When he eventually went for
post-graduate studies in England, the British Council arranged "for
everything for us - including accommodation" Celebrated writer, Cyprian
Ekwensi, and reigning artiste, Bisade Ologunde a.k.a. Lagbaja, also mounted
the stage to tell the British Council story, ostensibly on behalf of
Nigerian literary and entertainment clan. Ekwensi's testimony was laden with
wise cracks that kept the audience laughing and saluting him
intermittently. 'The 82-year-old author of People Of the City, Jagua Nana,
The Drummer Boy, among other novels, had his first contact with the council
in 1946. It was around the time he first met the Council's first director,
Lloyd Williams, "Then, they were more inclined to Literature than
Choreography," Ekwensi said in reference to an international dance project
the council is prosecuting at present. Williams had helped organise the
Scribblers' Club, in which Ekwensi was active. The Council had a nice
reception for he and his fellow Nigerians in England in 1951 as well.
Lagbaja was at the center of entertainment at the Lagoon bash. He is
happy that there is continuity of the adventures of the British Council in
Nigeria. Among all the artistes the Council had brought from England,
Lagbaja confessed, one was "a very big impact" on him - the Itchy Fingers, a
jazz group.
As the United Kingdom's international organisation for educational and
cultural relations, the British Council had existed for about two decades
before it came to Nigeria in 1943. In the 1920s and early 30s, the foreign
office in Britain realised the need for an organisation responsible for the
promotion of British culture, education, science and technology in other
countries, along the lines of existing French, German and Italian cultural
organisations. "This led to the setting up of the British Committee for
Relation with other countries in 1934. The name was later changed to the
British Council for Relations with other countries and then shortened to the
British Council.
The work of the early British Council included support to British
Institutes and societies, and English schools in other countries;
recruitment of university lecturers, support to students and English
teachers; supply of books and periodicals for libraries, lecture tours,
music performances and arts exhibitions. Priority was given to the countries
of the Middle East and Mediterranean region, Europe and Latin America.
Apart from contributing to the growth of the artistes, when some of them
make landmark achievements, the British Council rises to celebrate them. It
did this for Helon Habila, when he won the $15, 000 Caine Prize for
literature. Recently, again, to assist young writers, it flagged off a
mentoring program in which it intends to link some of the budding writers to
established ones abroad. It is equally planning a residency program that
Emecheta is proposed to anchor. The occasion of the diamond jubilee
celebration also proved auspicious for the British Council to roll out a new
set of projects and programs. They include the Jubilee Literary prize:
Jubilee Dance Project; Jubilee Music Showcase; Arts Manager's Conference;
Writer's Workshop; British Film Project and Telling Stories.

Original_Search_Title: Foday Saybana Sankoh as I Got to Know Him During the Sierra Leonean Peace Talks in Lome
Headline: Sierra Leone: Foday Saybana Sankoh as I Got to Know Him During the Sierra Leonean Peace Talks in Lome
Body_Text:
I have just heard about the untimely passing away of former RUF rebel
leader Foday Saybana Sankoh, alias "Okuruba" or The Warrior.
I got to know the legendary Foday Sankoh during the Ecowas-sponsored
Inter Sierra-Leonean Peace Talks in LomÃ© in May 1999.
As a correspondent for the BBC Focus/Network Africa programmes it was an
inevitable professional obligation for me to establish a working
acquaintance and personal relationship with the RUF rebel leadership, as
well as the Sierra-Leone official Government delegation led by the legal
luminary, Solomon Berewah, now Vice-President, in order to facilitate
unimpeded access to the two opposite divergent views of the argument.
It was in the course of this peace process that I got to strike a
harmonious chord with Corporal Foday Sankoh at close quarters.
Corporal Saybana Sankoh was indeed an African traditional warrior, but
he struck me as a real defiant, resolute Bush Man who will go to any lengths
to get what he wants. Damn the consequences. I will illustrate this with an
unforgettable incident which nearly cost me my job and my reputation.
It happened at the prestigious Hotel Corinthia 2 Fevrier in LomÃ©. The
rebel leader confessed to me he was ill-at ease and fed up to the neck with
the monotony of the menu and the glittering life of luxury at the
comfortable five-star hotel in LomÃ©.
"Ebow," Sankoh said on that fateful day while I was nosing round for a
scoop from the RUF rebel High-Command, "I am feeling nostalgic for a return
to bush life. This daily menu of steak and chips, rice and chicken is
becoming too much of a predictable ritual," he confided in me.
Foday Sankoh said he wanted to live in a natural environment of the bush
where one could easily secure fresh fruits, pepper and other basic needs of
life in their pure unadulterated form, with birds chirping on the trees and
cool water flowing freely from the streams.
"I am beginning to feel home-sick of the pure green vegetables, the
appetising fresh red bush meat which you can just shoot down, prepare on
fire, and consume in their unpolluted delicacy," the rebel leader said.
He expressed regrets that even though he enjoyed the liberty to move
freely in LomÃ©, ironically, life in the hotel looked somewhat to him like a
glorified prison.
At this point, I suggested that we could have dinner together in my
house if he wanted. I promised him real, traditional 'kontomire,' vegetable
stew with fresh meat, fish, and crabs with cooked rice, and yams as a
gastronomic complement.
Without hesitation, Foday Sankoh jumped for the bait like fish caught on
the alluring hook.
I saw his epiglottis galloping rhythmically with saliva. His appetite
was whetted beyond the wildest imagination. But at the appointed time, in
the evening, I sensed trouble.
The Togolese security guards refused to allow me access to Foday Sankoh
in his hotel room.
A shouting match ensued, and this brought Foday Sankoh out of his
rooms. He was told by the Togolese security that the dinner could not take
place in my house that evening because we had not made the necessary
security protocol arrangements.
Hell broke loose. Foday Sankoh flew into tantrums. And I later found
myself playing a role of the mediator between him and the men assigned the
duty of ensuring his safety.
When calm was restored, he warned that he would leave the hotel in
protest if he was not allowed to go out and eat whatever and wherever he
wants.
I went back home, for I thought the matter was settled.
It was therefore with trepidation when my mobile phone rudely woke me up
at 2 o'clock in the morning, only to hear the angry voice of Foday Sankoh at
the other distant end.
"Is that Ebow Godwin of the BBC?" Foday Sankoh asked. I
responded. "Well, I have left the Hotel 2 Fevrier in protest over the
security refusal to allow me to eat my dinner outside the hotel," he said
nonchalantly.
To me, it was like a bombshell. I saw myself gradually, but unwillingly,
drawn into the web of diplomatic controversy, and I detested the gloomy
prospect.
"But Foday Sankoh, you cannot do that. Besides, the security men are
right, we have to arrange things properly," I barked out in indignation
without being aware of it.
Yet Foday Sankoh was calm, unmoved. "Is the dinner still intact? If it
is, I have got the authorities to agree to let us have it served in your
home with state protocol in attendance," he said.
By noon time, Corporal Foday Saybana Sankoh was chauffeur driven in a
convoy of limousines under tight Togolese security for the dinner that later
turned out to be another supper.
As Foday Sankoh scooped a mouthful of sumptuous green vegetable stew, he
whispered discreetly into my ears, "President EyadÃ©ma was unhappy about the
refusal of the security guards to allow me to go out of the hotel for my
dinner last night." Apparently President EyadÃ©ma had ordered that the
situation must be rectified immediately.
That was Foday Saybana Sankoh for you. A man whose resolute and defiant
character brought him very close to the promised land, Canaan, in
Sierra-Leone, but paradoxically failed to reach there.
Nevertheless, while out of the bush war, Foday Sankoh never lost eyes
for the good things of life, including beautiful women.
In fact he got himself traditionally "married" to a charming Togolese
lady from Sokode in Central Togo during the Sierra-Leonean Peace Talks.
Unconfirmed reports say that the Togolese "wife," Bataba Victoire, was
believed to have been with Foday Sankoh on the fateful day the tragic
shooting incident turned his political fortunes upside down in
Freetown.
But up till now, all efforts to locate Bataba Victoire have ended in a
fiasco. Now that Foday is dead, the fears of many worried Togolese is that
the mystery surrounding the whereabouts of his Togolese wife may yet remain
buried with Foday Sankoh.
Foday Sankoh told me that he was born in 1936 in a village called
Massang in Moyorsokolifo kingdom in Tonkelele district, northern
Sierra-Leone.
"It is not on the map," he wisecracked. He fondly remembers his mother,
the first in the line of four wives of his father.
"My mother's society name is Yinnosserie or the beautiful woman called
Yinnor," he told me in an interview in LomÃ© during the peace talks.
"I think I was lucky enough to be sent to school, at the age of ten (10)
I started my primary school in Rokes, that is Yohibana area, those with a
railway line," he said.
But while in my child-hood I liked going to the bush to set traps for
animals, fighting with my brothers with whom we used to engage in wrestling
contests. And they used to call me 'Okuruba' or the warrior in our
language," he said.
Foday Sankoh said the language was Timne. He reveals that his name Foday
Sankoh originates from the Poro Secret Society where men learn the secrets
arts of survival.
He told me that the period of initiation into manhood could last for
four years. "Say-Say Bana or Big is my Society name. And it means BIG. But
when you come to see the man, he is a small man. Foday Saybana Sankoh is a
BIG name given to a small man," he explained.
From school, Foday Sankoh briefly worked with the Electricity Department
in Maburka, first as a messenger, then as a meter reader from where he rose
to become an engine attendant.
Later he joined the Water Works Department where an expatriate engineer,
one Mr. Boston, who was an ex-Serviceman, encouraged Foday Sankoh to join
the Army during a recruitment exercise in Maburka. He began military
training in Daru in the Moa barracks.
Foday Sankoh told me that he was posted to Freetown in 1956. After
training, he rejoined the First Battalion where he claimed that he did three
months advanced course at Wilberforce Barracks from where he was deployed to
Tower Hill, First Squadron, then Military Headquarters in Freetown. He
qualified as a signals man.
Foday Sankoh claims that some of his course mates were former Defence
Minister Hinga Norman against whom he fought during the Sierra-Leonean
conflict.
Afterwards, Foday Sankoh took to photography which he claimed he learnt
while he was briefly in Britain to continue with his training in
signals.
It was photography that served as a turning point in his life as a
revolutionary. In 1982, he used his photo studios for undercover activities
to secretly recruit militants for his bush war, which he began in 1991.
When I reminded him about the injustice and atrocities committed by the
RUF rebel movement, he denied them flatly. He in turn accused the local
Kamajors, of committing those atrocities. I knew he lied.
During the LomÃ© negotiations, Foday Sankoh narrated to me the saga
behind his arrest and subsequent detention in Abuja, Nigeria in 1996.
"I do not trust anybody except God, but I am prepared to negotiate with
the devil if only that will bring peace to the people of Sierra Leone," he
told me.
Foday Sankoh recalled how in 1996 he decided to go to Abuja in Nigeria
after the RUF had signed the Abidjan Peace Accords "as part of my plan to
tour the West African Sub-Region to solicit support for the peace plan, and
to halt the hostilities between the Kamajors and the Army."
Foday Sankoh remained bitter till his death about the Nigerian military
dictator, General Sani Abacha.
"Upon my arrival in Nigeria, I was arrested, chained, beaten, and
detained for no apparent reason whatsoever," he told me in LomÃ©. "Abacha
later handed me over to Tejjan Kabbah in Sierra-Leone. Do you call that
justice? He asked.
Foday Sankoh claimed that the late General Sani Abacha confiscated a
large sum of foreign currency, which he allegedly carried on him. When the
news of Abacha's death was announced, Foday Sankoh remarked, "He deserved no
sympathies because Abacha played God. Nobody plays God."
Independent sources say that the monies seized by Abacha were later
refunded to Foday Sankoh by the Nigerian Government under President Olusegun
Obasanjo.
But now that the rest of the story has been cut short by the passing way
of Foday Sankoh, one cannot easily tell whether it is 'Okuruba' or General
Abacha who deserves no sympathy from humanity.

Original_Search_Title: Banished For being 'born again,' monarch dethroned
Headline: Nigeria: Banished For being 'born again,' monarch dethroned
Body_Text:
Penultimate week, the people of Ode Ekiti, the headquarters of Gbonyin Local Government Area of Ekiti State, did the unprecedented when they dethroned their monarch and banished him from the town. What happened? Why was the king's 13-year rulership terminated? At the centre of the crisis was the monarch's alleged disregard for tradition on the ground that he was a born-again Christian
WHEN on Saturday, July 19, 2003, the Olode of Ode, Oba Samuel Adara Aderiye, a first class king in Ekiti State and the most prominent monarch in Gbonyin Local Government Area of the State, his town being the headquarters of that council, woke up, he must have thought it was the beginning of another auspicious day. Better still, he must have thought that since it was another day set aside for Semuregede, a festival marked annually by the people to pray for peace and prosperity of the town, as well as commence the eating of the new yam, the day would pass just like the previous ones - without any untoward incident.
He had spent 13 years on the throne and each of the celebrations of the Semuregede festival had passed the way he wanted, not the way the majority of the townspeople wanted. It was with this mind set that this monarch must have dressed this Saturday, putting on one of his best dresses, the beads dangling on his neck, the crown on his head and the shoes only fit for the royal for a festival which celebration was to continue in his courtyard at about 2.00 p.m.
The gathering storm
By the appointed time when the king emerged at the courtyard, virtually all the townspeople had gathered, having earlier danced round the town as part of the festival. In Ode, nobody jokes with Semuregede Festival as it is celebrated in honour of Oloke, a deity which all sons and daughters hold in high esteem. The belief in the town is that every indigene owes his protection to this deity which was said to have accompanied Ode's founders right from Ife where their ancestors hail from. For this reason, the festival is celebrated with pomp and pageantry with all sons and daughters of the town, even those outside the town, coming home to participate.
Now was the time to commence final lap of the 2003 edition of Semuregede Festival and the king must play a crucial role. Yet another actor who plays a crucial role at this stage of the festival is the Aworo Oloke of Ode, High Chief Sunday Oluborode. By virtue of his office as the chief priest of Oloke, the Aworo, in fact, presides over this stage of the festival. The high point of the festival at this stage is for the Aworo to offer prayers for peace, prosperity for the town and protection of the Oloke for indigenes for the next twelve months. In the course of doing this, the Aworo performs seven rites each of which must be endorsed by the Olode. The Aworo entered the courtyard soon after Oba Aderiye was seated and proceeded with the performance of the rites.
Monarch errs
Trouble began when the monarch endorsed only one of the rites and refused the rest. Although it was customary that the Olode must endorse all the rites, he must have reasoned within himself that if he violated this code in twelve previous editions of the festival and no serious consequences followed, he could as well violate this one and get away with it. And in fairness to Oba Aderiye, he had always told his townspeople that he was a good Christian to the extent of which he asked to be excused from fetish things such as could be found in rites similar to the ones that must be performed during Semuregede Festival. But the Aworo would have none of the king's 'homily' this time and told him pointedly so. The chief priest added that it was a taboo for the monarch not to endorse all the rites, and that if he ended up not endorsing them, it would mean another year of hard times for the people as peace, prosperity and protection would elude them. He did not also fail to remind the monarch that his refusal to endorse the rites was not the only area where he erred but also in his dressing for the occasion. The monarch ought to have wore a robe specially designed for the festival with a white feather clipped to his forehead.
But Oba Aderiye had chosen not to be attired this way for the festival since he mounted the throne. As the Olode stood his ground of not endorsing the rites, the Aworo stormed out of the courtyard. The festival was aborted, the townspeople stunned. But the drama was just unfolding. In a jiffy, the monarch ran from the courtyard into the storey building adjacent to the courtyard which served as his official residence and soon began to address the people from the balcony. He allegedly told the people who were still stunned about what had happened to leave where they stood rooted that he had no apology and that he would not be party to any rites in the town no matter its significance. At this point, some of the townspeople decided to call Oba Aderiye's bluff. They told him it was time for him to abdicate the throne and threatened that his stay a minute beyond that moment would attract the wrath of the entire townspeople. As this went on, militant members of the crowd got hold of cutlasses and began to cut down the trees within and around the palace to signify the 'death' of the monarch.
King flees palace
By the following morning, Sunday, July 20, Oba Aderiye had fled the palace with his entire household. But the people were not done with him yet. They still wanted to hear from him why he chose to trigger the chain of events that led to his dethronement. At dawn, the town crier was sent round the town to mobilise the people for a meeting scheduled for the market square while a search party went for the king. The search party had a hectic time finding the monarch as the team reportedly did not meet him in his personal house when it looked for him there. However, as the team was about departing for the market square to report back to the townspeople, Oba Aderiye was said to have been spotted hiding in his brother's house. He was immediately seized and taken before the townspeople at the market square.
A source told Sunday Vanguard that the monarch's seizure did not come until after both parties had engaged in some stone throwing session and the king overwhelmed by the search party. The smashed window panes of Aderiye's brother's house situated in Ilasa area of Ode was said have resulted from the stone throwing. The inquiry into the Olode's conduct allegedly commenced the moment he was brought before the townspeople with various allegations leveled against him, chief among which was his failure to partake in rites that would have seen Ode being at peace with itself for 13 years.
The inquiry
Whether out of fear or contempt for the people, Aderiye was said to have chosen not to address the gathering. He repeatedly asked the Oji-Iro, the most senior high chief and head of the town's kingmakers, Chief Abraham Adebayo, to help him beg the people and to impress it on them he was willing to change if he was given another opportunity. But Adebayo, who, as head of kingmakers, reportedly installed him as Olode in 1990, declined the request and asked him to address the people himself. The action of the high chief can be understood in the context of his disappointment in the last twelve years when he had had to tell the king to tread the path of his predecessors by doing what the people wanted only for Aderiye to reject his admonition.
The king banished
As this went on, the sound of the beating of drum was heard from a distance. The Olode, interpreting this to mean the imminent approach of some militant youths on a mission to kill him, fled the scene. Instinctively, the people began to chase him until he was pursued out of the town. The unprecedented had happened, the king had been dethroned, banished. Later that evening, the people converged at the market square to consider the fate of the monarch. The meeting which broke into groups asked if each group supported the dethronement and the suggestion that Aderiye should go into exile. The meeting was said to have been unanimous on the monarch's fate. To underscore the unanimity of the decision, one Chief Eyinfe, said to be representing the ruling house which presented Aderiye as king, allegedly spoke at the occasion, aligning the ruling house with the decision. To many indigenes of Ode, the dethronement marked the end of an era, an era which they alleged saw many untoward things happening including avoidable deaths of prominent indigenes like Professor Israel Ojo Adelola, a teacher of political science at University of Ado-Ekiti (UNAD); one Akinola, a retired airforce officer; Mr. Julius Aluko, a retired federal medical officer; Mr. Solo Oba, a senior nursing officer of Psychiatry and Mrs. Adesola, a retired headmistress. Scores of others were said to have died drowning in rivers and auto accidents, incidents the townspeople claimed never happened before the monarch ascended the throne because the king's predecessors performed the "right rites at the right time."
The indigenes hold Aderiye responsible for all the deaths and other unpleasant occurrences that befell the town while his reign lasted, since he failed to perform the traditional rites expected of him during major festivals on the ground that he could not compromise his Christianity and, by so doing failed to ward off evil forces from the town. Sunday Vanguard learnt that a reconciliatory move launched by Oba Ayodele Ige Olokesusi II, the Owa of Egbe, a neighbouring town, was aborted after Ode's high chiefs told him the townspeople's position on Aderiye's dethronement was final. And there is no other way to reinforce their position than the step they have already taken to intimate the constituted authorities - Ekiti State government - about approval to replace the deposed monarch. They wrote the state government last week to that effect.
Running battle
Ode people's battle with Aderiye did not begin with the disagreement over the celebration of this year's edition of Seremuregun Festival. It was a running battle that climaxed with the disagreement over the way the monarch conducted himself during the festival. Last week, the Alase, one of the high chiefs in Ode, Chief Segun Afun, told Sunday Vanguard that the people's problems with Aderiye started the moment he became king about 13 years ago. Afun claimed that the monarch showed his true colours as somebody who would not respect tradition when he refused to subject himself to installation rites. The Olode, according to him, boycotted the two most important aspects of the installation which required him to spend six months in two sacred groves: Odo-Ode and Erepupu. In respect of the Erepupu rites, the monarch was said to have excused himself on the ground that he had a dream that some people had planned to waylay and attack him.
Benefit of doubt
This notwithstanding, the king makers were said to have prepared to give him the benefit of the doubt that he would live up to the people's expectations. But the turn of events soon turned their optimism to pessimism.
The Alase explained that Aderiye, rather than cooperating with Ode chiefs to ensure that all relevant traditional festivals were celebrated and the gods appeased to ensure that things run normally in the town, the Olode claimed he was the modern king, a religious one, who would have nothing to do with the festivals. He listed the gods that must be appeased in the town on annual basis to include the Oloke (during the Semuregede Festival), Orete (small stream which appeasement is believed will prosper the people), Orisa Odo-Ode, Iyemeji, Esin Omo (god that protects children against small pox and other deadly diseases) and Ogun (god of iron). Also celebrated yearly is Odun Ibode by Ifa priests.
The monarch was expected to feature prominently anytime the Odun Ibode festival was being celebrated. Afun stated that Aderiye was a member of the Church of Nigeria, Anglican Communion in the town but faulted his abstinence from participating in the rites during traditional festivals in the town on the ground of his allegation that for each of the festivals, the local government was making available an allocation which he alleged the monarch was collecting. If religion forbids you from carrying out traditional rites, why then would you collect money for the purchase of items for the rites?, the high chief asked.
Sacred items destroyed
In addition to this, the Olode allegedly collected all the sacred items in the palace and destroyed them.
