Of particular mention was a sacred object said to have been installed in the palace to stop premeditated murder in 1933 when a man in Ode slaughtered his wife. Soon after that object was destroyed, a similar incident was said to have been recorded in the town. All kinds of deaths that ordinarily should be averted had necessary rites been performed by the monarch also became the order of the day. Among the killings, it was alleged, was that of a two week old baby said to have been taken to a stream by his mother who slaughtered him.
Alase alleged that Aderiye was unmoved by the situation as every visit to him "to do the right thing" was always met with insults and abuses. A story was told of how the monarch insulted a group of old women who visited him to order that a rite be performed after drowning of indigenes in Ose River became rampant. The Olode allegedly told them that he had no time to listen to a bunch of prostitutes who had nothing to do but gossip all over town. On another occasion, when a group of youths were said to have visited him to show their concern over the frequency of deaths in the town, he was quoted as having asked them if anybody thought he was the person killing the deceased persons.
Aderiye allegedly told them they were mistaken if they thought he would perform any rite to stem the tide and that he was prepared to continue to rule even if deaths depleted the population until only two persons were left.
University teacher dies
At yet another time when some youths under the aegis of Okoro visited him after six of them had died within two months and Professor Adelola had taken ill, the Olode allegedly used uncharitable words to describe the deceased university teacher. He was alleged to have described the don as 'adara nita madara nile' meaning Adelola was useful outside but useless at home. Yet the don was credited as having been instrumental to the admission of many Ode indigenes at UNAD. An indigene told Sunday Vanguard that nobody was surprised that Aderiye allegedly uttered such an uncomplimentary remark about Adelola as he claimed there was no love lost between them. Adelola was said to have consistently opposed the style of rulership of the Olode for which the monarch was alleged to have told the don at a meeting last year that he would not live to witness this year's edition of Ode-Day.
Sunday Vanguard learnt that the last days of Aderiye as Olode actually began with the Adelola saga. When the don died, the youths were said to have met the Olode, hoping they could still persuade him to handle the situation properly in the interest of the town.
An undertaking was reportedly extracted from him to the effect that he would prevent further avoidable deaths in the town. But rather than keeping to his own side of this agreement, the Olode, it was learnt, wrote the police, alleging the youths were threatening his life. It was the same Olode who wrote the police to withdraw his complaint following which he held a truce parley with the youths. That was the situation before penultimate week's Semuregede Festival which climaxed in his dethronement, the spokesperson of youths in the community, Abiodun Ojo, alias Akika, told Sunday Vanguard.
Allegations
Other allegations against the dethroned Olode include his not being friendly with any of Ode chiefs, keeping to himself information relating to emoluments collected on their behalf from the local government, single handedly appointing honorary chiefs for the community and being generally indifferent to the development of the community. The Alase appeared to have several facts to back up the allegations. But the one he could not explain satisfactorily was that which had it that the monarch was indifferent to the development of Ode. Afun agreed that if the meaning of development could be understood within a reasonable context, and facilities like pipe borne water and electricity were attracted to the town while the Local Government (Gbonyin) which has its headquarters in the town was carved out of Emure/Ise/Orun council during the monarch's tenure, then Aderiye attracted some development to Ode.
The Aluko connection
Prominent indigenes of Ode include the late Chief GBA Ainyede, Professor Sam Aluko and his son who served in the immediate past Senate. Asked if these indigenes did nothing to intervene in the crisis before it got to a head, the Alasa of Ode said Akinyede could not have intervened because Aderiye saw the first republic politician as a sworn enemy until he died because he (Akinyede) supported a rival candidate (one Dr. Akilo) when the Olode was to be installed.
Incidentally, Akilo was said to have shown little or no interest in the Olode stool based on his conviction that his religious disposition would not make him a good monarch because he would not be able to perform the rites required of his office. Afun disclosed that Professor Aluko is Aderiye's cousin, saying that if Aderiye was stubborn, as many had been alleging, the professor would have been unable to do anything.
Aderiye's whereabouts
All efforts to get the monarch to react to his deposition proved abortive. There were conflicting information about his whereabouts. While some said he was being sheltered in Egbe whose monarch unsuccessfully tried to restore him back to office, others stated that he was taking refuge in Agbado, another neighbouring town. Yet another claim had it that he headed for Lagos the moment he fled Ode.
Mum was the word when Sunday Vanguard visited his personal house situated in Ilase area of Ode. A woman identified as the exiled king's sister claimed she did not know her brother's whereabouts. She spoke of one Ronke, Aderiye's daughter, who she said was in the position to know the monarch's movement. A visit to Gbonyin Local Government Primary Health Centre in Agbado where the daughter works showed she had not been at work for several days.
Sighted on Lagos-Ibadan Expressway
However, a source, which corroborated the claim that the Olode may be in Lagos, said a green Mercedes Benz 200 marked OLODE was spotted on Lagos - Ibadan expressway at about 12.30 p.m. last Wednesday heading in Lagos' direction. According to the source, a man in royal attire sat at the back seat of the car while a lady, who appeared sleeping, sat with the driver.

Original_Search_Title: Critical Appraisal of Igbo Woman
Headline: Nigeria: Critical Appraisal of Igbo Woman
Body_Text:
IN this discourse, we shall discuss womankind and womanhood from Igbo perspective; evaluate what 'woman' is in the eyes of the Igbo. Every nation or culture has her own place for woman; and nowhere world over that women are not more in chains than the menfolk. The Igbo is no exception, but they have a better place for their women than most cultures. The Igbo woman is conscious of her rights and hardly submits it. In the family, the Igbo woman is seen as a partner and the mother of both her children and the husband. She is the moral teacher of the children. That is why it goes: Onye a turu ilu kowaara ego ejiri nu nne ya nara na iyi (One whom proverbs is weaved to and he asks for meaning the bride price paid on his mother's head is in vain)". The import of this proverb is that the woman is the chief disciplinarian in the family; whose duty it is to be an exemplar. This moral disciplinarian position of the woman in Igbo family is because of the high premium placed on womankind. To demonstrate Igbo regards for women further, one has to read the meaning of some sayings like: "Nwanyi bu nwa (Woman is child)" and "Nwanyi na eme (The woman is able)". Igbo woman is to her husband a companion. She is to be prepared for the task of family-hood by her mother. In the good old days, a girl undergoes what is called Iru mgbede. Iru mgbede is a groom period. It is a time for a matured lady to be taught family hood, and be given beauty and other service attention that is, perhaps why divorce was scarcely recorded in the pre-colonial Igbo. Whether divinely ordained or biologically determined, there is perhaps, a feminine tendency that finds woman or tends to prove woman's total fulfillment in submissive domesticity. And their male folk, while exploiting these shortcomings posit that they do deny woman her freedom with her most useful interests and safety at human heart.
There is another contention that women are naturally unfit to live their world by assuming full responsibility and, therefore, needed masculine prodding, guidance and protection. The notion that a woman can not be herself without a man, social problems and economic upheavals against women, is to say the least not divinely or naturally ordained but a psychological issue.
Ultimate
goal of life
The Adam and Eve story of creationism portends that woman is created to enable man acquire his essence and is expected to be docile; good-humoured arid flexibile under man. The import of this is that man is made to reason and woman is made to feel. Men from their youth are prepared for enabling and un-enabling professions, and marriage is not seen as the ultimate goal of life, but greatest population of women (in this age) seems to be having nothing apart from marriage as ultimate goal. This is so because men see pleasure in this age of Epicureanism as relaxation while women see it as the goal of existence; therefore, she must be advantageously married or be moved to work towards it. But the fact is that a woman was not created to be, merely, "Man's helper" and that the "sexual" should not spoil the human character. Marriage is, to say the fact, not a sexual desire for conquest but an enduring union of hearts for the good of humanity!
Scolding of children, dressing them up with fine clothes for church, and to outshine one's neighbours in finery and parade without values of womanhood that ranges from proven or inspirable essence and reasonability is the job of an epicurean mind and not foundation for sex (real human) advancement. In the old Igbo society before Western bastardization,, the woman does not depend totally on men for subsistence, but outside working in trust with their husbands to erect barns of yams to fend family livings, they (the industrious ones) do have personal means of income. The man may live with the glorified ownership of the yam barn raised in trust and the woman the owner of things like cassava, corns and vegetable planted in the farms. Outside this the woman may own her personal yam barn and cocoyam, as was the case with my mother in her days. That is how an ideal Igbo family subsists itself. We do not have what is called "house wife" as with this age; all hands used to be on deck to get the economy going. That is Igbo woman for you!
We talk of feminism in our world because the Igbo, nay African woman has lost her ideal position. The goal of womanhood to the Igbo transcends childbirth and maternity to Agbara Nwanyi-thing of great beauty, excellence and paragon. Agbara nwanyi is man equivalent and is lolo to the Igbo. She is a lolo (revered one) as an embodiment of womanhood. A woman to the Igbo is a highly principled being. That Is why the Igbo would say; Ana nwanyi chiri ogu ana egbu ya ngworo (A land where a woman led a battle the crippled is not to be spared). This does not by import mean that a woman is heartless but that women are firm-minded to principles. An Igbo woman is known for high sense of value and firmness to a purpose. That is why in Igbo society they have their own institution under Ochi Ora Nwanyi (women leader). They form and judge in their own court only to invite male-folk on a matter of great challenge not as arbiter per se but assistants in arbitration. The cult of the married daughters of the land umuada used to be one of the supreme court in Igbo society; or else they will boycott visiting the fatherland and the ancestors, Ana (earth divinity) and Chiukwu will be angry.
Daughters- in- law of the land, Ndi Iyom could abandon home en-mass to the house of a wealthy one of another village or land to force or drive home their point and their host will feed them and provide good things of life of which their husbands must offset those bills before they would agree to return home. The spirit of Igbo women ingenuity was well pronounced during the Aba Women Riots of 1929 and Biafra-Nigeria War. Igbo women in 1929 went berserk to stop colonial order for women to pay tax. Shooting and killing of their womenlfolk roundabout Eastern Region did not deter them because they had firm resolution. During the Biafra War, Igbo women did every thing possible to sustain the male-folk, ranging from crossing the battle line at risk to trade at the Nigeria side of the divide to all sorts of subsistence moves that carried the besieged Biafra for the three years war! Their role in the post Biafra war rebuilding of the Igbo economy is enormous and inestimable.
The Igbo lost all they had at the
end of the war. Those who had millions in the banks were through Nigerian government order paid £20. This economic barrier saw Igbo men and women back to square one. Their landed properties were declared abandoned property despite that they had certificates of ownership.
Economic
recovery
Most women joined their husbands in the post-war kaikai (gin) production, farming and other menial jobs such that today, Igbo has recovered in economy as they did in population; all because of the efforts of Ochiocha Nwanyis (women leaders), Obidiya (her husband's heart throbs), Osodiakpaku (those who follow their husbands to fetch wealth), Agbara Nwanyis, etc.
This is so because of the enabling Igbo world that is women friendly; the Igbo woman needs kudos whenever the history of Igbo post-Biafra war economy recovery is told. There is Agu Nwanyi - women of lion order! The great exploits of Igbo women in their areas of calling in the contemporary Nigeria like Dr. Ndi Okereke- Onyiuke, director general, Nigeria Stock Exchange; Dr. Ngozi Okonj- Iweala of the World Bank and chair, Board of Afncan Institute for Applied Economics and now, Minister of Finance; Dr. Dora Akunyili, director general, National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC); Mrs Uzoamaka Nwizu, comptroller general, Nigeria Immigration Service and others is a proof of the ideal Igbo woman, Agbara Nwanyi, laden with resolute and firm mind. Herein, it must be pointed out that I am not saying feminism has no ground in Igbo world. To defend womankind is feminism, already. Every community, nation and culture has her own problem of feminism. It is only that some societies are growing above feminism fever. Igbo culture is not a misogyny culture as some uniformed authorities contend. It is a culture that has female-folk at heart while it graduates. That is why the relaxation (in Igbo land) of the culture that makes our windows recluse for almost a year to be ones week or more is a welcomed development. Our culture is favourable to women. The problem of not holding share of one's father's property that most women agitate has a reason. Igbo society is patrilineally ordered. It is meant that a woman should go and raise wealth with her husband for her own family. The import of this is that she should not have the right to inherit her father's property, which if allowed would amount to transferring of property from one village to another.
But our women must not go empty handed! What does Igbo tradition say over this? It says that every married woman is entitled to Idu Uno - that is home furnishing by her family and kinsmen. At such occasion, the bride price receiver, her family and kinsmen and well wishers should come and settle their married daughter with all kind of economic and home appliances. It is done this way to help her build a new home for herself, her husband and children. Our feminism is feminism more of empowerment than emancipation. We have to define feminism to understand our position: "Feminism could in a raw making be said to be a concept and or a cause for women to be given equal rights to those of men". To shed more light will lead us to the coined twins like women emancipation and women empowerment. The former is "to set women free from any limitation and restraint" and the later is "to give moral or legal power and authority to women". Women empowerment is more tenable and materializable in the sense of studying women's plight and tackling it to tolerable degree in conformity with good of womankind; but women emancipation is to a great extent unattainable because there is no how human being could live without some kind of restraints for the overall good.
Nations' cultures have been achieving women empowerment but none has or will achieve women emancipation. The suffragists won vote for American women in 1920; universities in England did not admit women until 1878; except for Oberlin College which first graduated a woman in 1841. In America, most universities, e.g. Princeton University and Yale College barred women until 1969; French Revolution barred women from attending school after the age of eight. All these changed due to women empowerment. Western culture is saturated with male supremacy; even from their religious point of view women are made to feel and not to reason. Or else, how could some Biblical injunctions be explained: "For the woman is not of woman: but the woman of the man. Neither was the man created for the woman; but the woman for the man" (1 Corinthians 11: 8-9). "Let your women keep silence in the churches: for it is not permitted unto them to speak; but they are commanded to be under obedience, as also saith the law. And if they will learn any thing, let them ask their husbands at home: For it is a shame for women to speak in the church (1 Corinthians 14: 34-35).
Religious injunctions
Religious injunctions like this had robbed and taken some shine out of the women folk.Upon this very injuction, orthodox churches (eg. Roman Catholic Church) have not come to terms with the need to ordain women priests. Anglican Communion (Church of England) dabbled into ordination of priestesses in the nineties and it cost her membership and unexpected uproar. This is unwonted with Igbo, nay African religions. We do have female priestesses! Despite that every Igbo is to Igbo religion a potential priest, any Lolo (titled woman) is a priestess. It was the said notion that the western culture is saturated with male supremacy that made the extremist feminists have loathsome mind on marriage. Extremist feminist holds that nuptial union is a legal captivity that a woman has to follow because the society has denied her any pleasing and showing dignity alternatives.
However, marriage is not a legal captivity as the extremist feminists would want us believe. It is a vocational issue. To our tradition, the destination of womanhood is proven essence and reasonability that would crown her agbara nwanyi, or else she is born to .procreate and rot! Real feminism is talking about women empowerment and does not preach that women should be taken out of their families to be men; rather, seen as equal citizens. It does not say that a woman should at all cost choose "day care"to nurturing her child; nor say "she should not breast feed her child because she doesn't want to use falsies (which she must eventually need if she wants something to stand up).
Another, genius has no sex, hence woman-writer, Dr. (Mrs.), Captain (Miss.), Prof. (Mrs.), Bar (Miss) and other unnecessary itemization of "womanism" in every woman break-through is wrong as such could not be said of the male folk: It is, therefore, an attempt to mesmerize woman's identity and status; and should be discouraged. Another aspect of woman marginalization in today's Nigeria is a situation whereby no woman could be allowed to bail person(s) from police station, nor law court; despite that there is no law that says so! Unequal representation in government and under-age marriage is an area waiting for the feminists (irrespective of sex) to tackle to logical conclusion. Great number of under-aged females become victims of ignorance that leads to bad fate due to idleness in the way they are educated. Knowledge is said to be power; give a woman education and empower womanhood!
-Mazi OkwuChiukwu, a socio-political analyst writes from Umuohama, Ukpor, Anambra State.

Original_Search_Title: A Hero Called Charles Oguntoye
Headline: Nigeria: A Hero Called Charles Oguntoye
Body_Text:
IN an era ruined and run by skepticism, it is so simple to cast Charles Oguntoye in the bin of Nigerians due for condemnation. Some are calling for his head, others given a chance would strip him of his citizenship, though he must have been smart enough after so many years to acquire a British passport of convenience. If I know the likes of Oguntoye, he would be surprised that simple business decisions that followed expected patterns have resulted in more publicity that he would have asked for all his life.If he is one of those who never mind whether publicity is good or bad (publicity is publicity, they say) he would not be in the least affected by the emotional outburst about his dealings with Nigeria and Venezuela. He is not known to speak Spanish and we are not giving him any credit for bringing the Venezuelans into this game that dominated sports reports for the better part of the week?What did he do? The now famous British-based, Nigerian businessman, sports promoter extraordinaire, patriot, a football lover with Nigeria's Super Eagles as his niche, a man of action, a man of integrity and an astute match bargainer asked the two countries to play a game in London.
There is nothing wrong with all these attributes. I believe they were what attracted the Nigeria Football Association to him, in the first place. This would not in anyway suggest that the NFA sets out to do business with any serious considerations in mind. Oguntoye could actually sue the NFA for defamation of character, assassination of character, disclosure of business secrets, breach of contract (as in arriving late to the match venue, a factor he could have blamed for the poor attendance) and improper disclosure of the meals that the Super Eagles took before the game.Did they not know that they had a choice to board the next plane back to Nigeria? I even heard that was not a decision they could have made in a hurry. The team could not afford the bus fare back to the airport. The hotel that had their unwanted presence was a last bus stop of sorts. If the owners had insisted on the Super Eagles taking a room or hitting the streets, coaches and pla6yers might have ended up in the kitchen watching plates for their upkeep. Would that not have been befitting training for a team that did not practice?What fault of Oguntoye's was it that the Super Eagles would spend hours on the corridors of a hotel? I considered them lucky - some hotels would not allow you to hang around for more than a couple of minutes. It is not good for business, especially black faces. Luggage strewn all over the place would not be the perfect example of a hotel in business.Extra luck was on their side. It was not winter and the hotel bore their presence with remarkable patience, all 10 hours of it. Coach Christian Chukwu ached from transferring his bowed head from one hand to the other, players watched in dismay, as their coach had no clue about where Oguntoye was. What business did he have with them? His business was with eth NFA, which certified the terms acceptable for the Eagles to depart for London.
Things got better as the hours crawled by. At meal time they knew that they were on their own. Is it not totally dumb to expect that a man who did not bother to provide a roof over your head would feed you? Oguntoye had a game plan, he followed it through, leaving the teams complaining in English and Spanish.When they were lucky to get some fast food, they complained that they had expected something more solid after hours of waiting for a bite. Imagine how ungrateful a team can be! They thought that Oguntoye was going to import some pounded yam to please them? As the owner of the business (called match) in question, was it not within his competence to decide that the Super Eagles could do with some trimming? In response to that allegation, their excellent host served them sandwiches before the match that was postponed for hours as Oguntoye turned his bargaining charms on. He would race from one hotel to the other intriguing the other team with details of his faithfulness to the agreement to a match that was finally played without any of the teams knowing why they had to play.Oguntoye got them to believe that if they did not play, the other would gain a walk over. He must have made it sound like the losing team was giving up the World Cup final without a fight.
Could it be the attraction of travelling to London that made the NFA get into a match without knowing its expectations from the promoter? Nobody could blame Oguntoye on that one. I have it on good authority that the Nigeria-Irish pre-World Cup game that is paraded as his major credential went on smoothly because the Irish FA took over the organisation. Days to the match, worse signs than what happened in London were showing.Nigeria was in London to play against Venezuela, at the instance of Oguntoye and the NFA, which is trying very hard to prove that it can get matches for the Super Eagles. There is no doubt that the farce that took place in London was one of the great A games that the NFA has secured for the national team.Hard as I tried I found out that Oguntoye did the Super Eagles and the NFSA a greet service for which eh deserves our praise. So many of us complain about the slow progress that the Super Eagles make on the monthly FIFA rankings. Oguntoye has given FIFA a good reason to take us at least one notch when the next rankings are released. What else do you expect of a hero? Who among the complaining lot has lifted a finger to help Nigerian football? How many of them can get the Eagles a classy opposition like Venezuela, a South American team? Are we complaining because a Nigerian promoted the match?Many are those who would want this matter top be blown out of proportion. They are talking about eth sanctity of the national team and the discretion that it suffered in London. We are trained to expect worse things. Bad as Oguntoye was, he provided tickets for the team to travel, a hotel room was made available, courtesy of more concerned Eagles supporters, a meal of sandwich was better than nothing we all can admit.No longer would anyone ask who is Charles Oguntoye. He has made his name. He has made his mark. If we cannot get him a national honour in October, the entry may be late, we have ample time to do so next year, by which time he would have organised a couple of other games for Nigeria, with expectedly more finesse along the lines of the experience of the one in London.The NFA has to start accepting responsibility. How could it have sent off Chukwu and the Super Eagles without a penny on them? How much did it know about Oguntoye's excellent match organisation? What role did the secretariat play in accepting a game for which it had not been paid anything? Where did the NFA expect to get the money to pay winning bonuses, or was Nigeria meant to lose the game so that no bonuses would be paid?Chukwu should be the last man to really complain about anything.
He must have had his fill of solid meals since his return. Oguntoye has also aided the resurrection of his slipping career. His hecklers cannot say that he did not beat a Latin American team. The facts are there that he won the match 1-0, at a time of flagging performances.Thank you Oguntoye. From your great handling of this assignment, we have discovered that the food that we give the Super Eagles is wasted. The good accommodation that we provide for their camping is an avoidable drain on scarce national resources. Have they won matches with the same ease as they did in London when we fed them well?I think that we should quickly set up an inquiry on this matter. The sole term of reference would be to establish how the conditions in London made the Super Eagles to win the match against Venezuela. Some people are sounding as if they are sad that we won. They also want to blame Oguntoye for that. Unable to raise convincing reasons to diminish the game, they have taken on Oguntoye and made him their whipping boy, as if he stuck the contract into the throat of the NFA and the Super Eagles.What a country!PS: The next time the Super Eagles are invited for a game (I heard that would be a long time) important items that would be negotiated in the contract would include details of food items to be served. Sandwiches would be forbidden. We would be quartered in villas instead of hotels with waiting areas that a promoter would think is befitting for the national team of Nigeria. Promoters beware or consult Oguntoye, whose next line of business would be a consultancy on how to ruin international games.

Original_Search_Title: A Hero Called Charles Oguntoye
Headline: Nigeria: A Hero Called Charles Oguntoye
Body_Text:
IN an era ruined and run by skepticism, it is so simple to cast Charles Oguntoye in the bin of Nigerians due for condemnation. Some are calling for his head, others given a chance would strip him of his citizenship, though he must have been smart enough after so many years to acquire a British passport of convenience. If I know the likes of Oguntoye, he would be surprised that simple business decisions that followed expected patterns have resulted in more publicity that he would have asked for all his life.If he is one of those who never mind whether publicity is good or bad (publicity is publicity, they say) he would not be in the least affected by the emotional outburst about his dealings with Nigeria and Venezuela. He is not known to speak Spanish and we are not giving him any credit for bringing the Venezuelans into this game that dominated sports reports for the better part of the week?What did he do? The now famous British-based, Nigerian businessman, sports promoter extraordinaire, patriot, a football lover with Nigeria's Super Eagles as his niche, a man of action, a man of integrity and an astute match bargainer asked the two countries to play a game in London.
There is nothing wrong with all these attributes. I believe they were what attracted the Nigeria Football Association to him, in the first place. This would not in anyway suggest that the NFA sets out to do business with any serious considerations in mind. Oguntoye could actually sue the NFA for defamation of character, assassination of character, disclosure of business secrets, breach of contract (as in arriving late to the match venue, a factor he could have blamed for the poor attendance) and improper disclosure of the meals that the Super Eagles took before the game.Did they not know that they had a choice to board the next plane back to Nigeria? I even heard that was not a decision they could have made in a hurry. The team could not afford the bus fare back to the airport. The hotel that had their unwanted presence was a last bus stop of sorts. If the owners had insisted on the Super Eagles taking a room or hitting the streets, coaches and pla6yers might have ended up in the kitchen watching plates for their upkeep. Would that not have been befitting training for a team that did not practice?What fault of Oguntoye's was it that the Super Eagles would spend hours on the corridors of a hotel? I considered them lucky - some hotels would not allow you to hang around for more than a couple of minutes. It is not good for business, especially black faces. Luggage strewn all over the place would not be the perfect example of a hotel in business.Extra luck was on their side. It was not winter and the hotel bore their presence with remarkable patience, all 10 hours of it. Coach Christian Chukwu ached from transferring his bowed head from one hand to the other, players watched in dismay, as their coach had no clue about where Oguntoye was. What business did he have with them? His business was with eth NFA, which certified the terms acceptable for the Eagles to depart for London.
Things got better as the hours crawled by. At meal time they knew that they were on their own. Is it not totally dumb to expect that a man who did not bother to provide a roof over your head would feed you? Oguntoye had a game plan, he followed it through, leaving the teams complaining in English and Spanish.When they were lucky to get some fast food, they complained that they had expected something more solid after hours of waiting for a bite. Imagine how ungrateful a team can be! They thought that Oguntoye was going to import some pounded yam to please them? As the owner of the business (called match) in question, was it not within his competence to decide that the Super Eagles could do with some trimming? In response to that allegation, their excellent host served them sandwiches before the match that was postponed for hours as Oguntoye turned his bargaining charms on. He would race from one hotel to the other intriguing the other team with details of his faithfulness to the agreement to a match that was finally played without any of the teams knowing why they had to play.Oguntoye got them to believe that if they did not play, the other would gain a walk over. He must have made it sound like the losing team was giving up the World Cup final without a fight.
Could it be the attraction of travelling to London that made the NFA get into a match without knowing its expectations from the promoter? Nobody could blame Oguntoye on that one. I have it on good authority that the Nigeria-Irish pre-World Cup game that is paraded as his major credential went on smoothly because the Irish FA took over the organisation. Days to the match, worse signs than what happened in London were showing.Nigeria was in London to play against Venezuela, at the instance of Oguntoye and the NFA, which is trying very hard to prove that it can get matches for the Super Eagles. There is no doubt that the farce that took place in London was one of the great A games that the NFA has secured for the national team.Hard as I tried I found out that Oguntoye did the Super Eagles and the NFSA a greet service for which eh deserves our praise. So many of us complain about the slow progress that the Super Eagles make on the monthly FIFA rankings. Oguntoye has given FIFA a good reason to take us at least one notch when the next rankings are released. What else do you expect of a hero? Who among the complaining lot has lifted a finger to help Nigerian football? How many of them can get the Eagles a classy opposition like Venezuela, a South American team? Are we complaining because a Nigerian promoted the match?Many are those who would want this matter top be blown out of proportion. They are talking about eth sanctity of the national team and the discretion that it suffered in London. We are trained to expect worse things. Bad as Oguntoye was, he provided tickets for the team to travel, a hotel room was made available, courtesy of more concerned Eagles supporters, a meal of sandwich was better than nothing we all can admit.No longer would anyone ask who is Charles Oguntoye. He has made his name. He has made his mark. If we cannot get him a national honour in October, the entry may be late, we have ample time to do so next year, by which time he would have organised a couple of other games for Nigeria, with expectedly more finesse along the lines of the experience of the one in London.The NFA has to start accepting responsibility. How could it have sent off Chukwu and the Super Eagles without a penny on them? How much did it know about Oguntoye's excellent match organisation? What role did the secretariat play in accepting a game for which it had not been paid anything? Where did the NFA expect to get the money to pay winning bonuses, or was Nigeria meant to lose the game so that no bonuses would be paid?Chukwu should be the last man to really complain about anything.
He must have had his fill of solid meals since his return. Oguntoye has also aided the resurrection of his slipping career. His hecklers cannot say that he did not beat a Latin American team. The facts are there that he won the match 1-0, at a time of flagging performances.Thank you Oguntoye. From your great handling of this assignment, we have discovered that the food that we give the Super Eagles is wasted. The good accommodation that we provide for their camping is an avoidable drain on scarce national resources. Have they won matches with the same ease as they did in London when we fed them well?I think that we should quickly set up an inquiry on this matter. The sole term of reference would be to establish how the conditions in London made the Super Eagles to win the match against Venezuela. Some people are sounding as if they are sad that we won. They also want to blame Oguntoye for that. Unable to raise convincing reasons to diminish the game, they have taken on Oguntoye and made him their whipping boy, as if he stuck the contract into the throat of the NFA and the Super Eagles.What a country!PS: The next time the Super Eagles are invited for a game (I heard that would be a long time) important items that would be negotiated in the contract would include details of food items to be served. Sandwiches would be forbidden. We would be quartered in villas instead of hotels with waiting areas that a promoter would think is befitting for the national team of Nigeria. Promoters beware or consult Oguntoye, whose next line of business would be a consultancy on how to ruin international games.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Urged to Take Action Over Accident-Prone Highway
Headline: Ghana: Minister Urged to Take Action Over Accident-Prone Highway
Body_Text:
Kintampo — FOLLOWING the high number of accidents occurring between Kintampo and Babatokuma on the Kintampo-Tamale highway, the leadership of the Ghana Private Road Transport Union (GPRTU) of TUC at Kintampo has appealed to the Minister of Roads and Transport to come and assess the road for possible solution.
In an interview with Chronicle at Kintampo, the branch secretary of the union, Mr.Iddrisu Abubakar, revealed that within the period of seven days, from May 25 to June 1 this year, seven lorry accidents had been recorded at a particular spot termed as an accident prone area near the Kintampo waterfalls.
At the time of filing this report, information reaching our Brong Ahafo desk had it that three more accidents were recorded, involving one person dying at the spot.
He told the paper that it is also estimated that within two years period, since the construction of the road was completed, about 150 lives had been lost at the same stretch of an area measuring about 300 metres.
It would be recalled that about eight years ago, a GTV crew, AGROMAC, doing a documentary on agriculture, had a fatal accident on the same spot.
Chronicle's survey at the road revealed that during construction that portion of the road was sloped to the left hand side towards the Tamale direction.
The reason for the sloping, Chronicle found out, was that the contractors presumed that vehicles from the southern sector of the country are normally loaded with heavy cargo and if the road was raised on the right hand side it would prevent any accident that is likely to occur.
But, according the GPRTU secretary it is not helping because most vehicles from the northern sector too are often loaded with heavy cargo.
The spot is in a curve, Mr. Abubakar said, adding that whenever a vehicle breaks down and parks at any part of the road it is possible that an accident could occur.
To ensure that lives and property are saved the GPRTU has called on the sector minister to have his men back to the road for any corrections to be made.
Later in an interview with the Brong Ahafo Highways Director, Mr. Kassim Nuhu, he said a report would be sent to the head office for action.
He said the number of accidents occurring in the area is a concern to the Highways and therefore assured that something would be done to check further accidents.
In another development, the GPRTU has appealed to the Kintampo District Assembly to relocate the Kintampo yam sellers to a different site, instead of their being along Kintampo-Tamale road, which poses danger to them.

Original_Search_Title: Issues in Fertiliser Scarcity
Headline: Nigeria: Issues in Fertiliser Scarcity
Body_Text:
Lagos — AS the wet season enters its middle course throughout the federation of Nigeria, the media are stuffed with reports of fears of poor harvest due to the biting scarcity of fertilisers in the country. Rainfall is falling regularly, in healthy quantity and at suitable intervals for crops all over the country. God is so merciful that there is no report of drought or even a threat of it from any part of Nigeria. A bumper harvest is on the horizon.
The reports on scarcity of fertilisers are true. The scarcity in turn made the commodity expensive, going for between N3,500 per bag in Kano, and for up to N4,000 a bag in Plateau State, as confirmed by sources in the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development. Why is the country suffering from the reported scarcity of fertilisers? What has the federal government done to ensure that the essential agricultural input is available at affordable price to the farmers of Nigeria? The fact is that many issues are involved in the current scarcity.
The truth, according to very reliable sources in the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development, is that the federal government of the day has awarded contract to 26 suppliers of the commodity who should import it ahead of the cropping season for distribution throughout the country.
The Permanent Secretary, Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development, Mr. Oloche A. Edache, explained that in serving the farmers, the Federal Government is spending the sum of N5.7 billion on 120,000 metric tones of fertilisers or 1.2 million bags of it in the current farming season. And what most citizens do not know is that the 25 per cent subsidy on fertilisers procured by the Federal Government meant to assist the peasant farmers is intact. The subsidy for this year on fertilisers alone is N1.425 billion. Several other agricultural inputs and equipment, such as agro-chemicals and tractors, are also heavily subsidized.
But unfortunately, most of the imported fertilisers for this wet season were stuck in the long queue of vessels on Nigerian waters for nine days during the recent nationwide strike. However, there is likelihood that their cargoes would be discharged within the next few days as the Federal Ministry of Transport reports that the fertiliser-bearing ships have now berthed.
And some of the contractors allegedly diverted the fertilisers they imported for the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development to the black market to cash in on the scarcity. But a number of the contractors, rate as very reliable by the directorate of fertiliser in the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development, such as KASCO of Kano, Bajis Ltd and Fertiliser and Chemicals Ltd, have delivered all or most of the consignment bought from them.
Still, there are other issues or areas of difficulty that negatively affect the process of procuring and distribution of fertilisers by the Federal Government . Although it is widely acknowledged that officials of the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development have efficiently administered the Fertiliser Revolving Fund, it is ironic that some state governments in the country seem bent on crippling it by delaying payment for fertilisers supplied to them by the Federal Government.
Minister of Agriculture, Malam Adamu Bello and the House of Representative Committee on Agriculture then led by Representative Bako Sarai worked hard to persuade debtor state governments to pay up. The effort was relatively successful. The Permanent Secretary, Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development, Mr. Oloche A. Edache also recently sent directors to the states on follow-up visits to recover the debt, which is nearly N3,000,000,000. Seven of the 36 states in the Federation, that is, Enugu, Abia, Akwa Ibom, Imo, Anambra, Delta and Katsina states have responded positively, as each of these states has paid over 90 per cent of the cost of fertilisers supplied to it by the Fertiliser Revolving Fund. Delta State under Governor James Ibori owes the fund the least amount of just over a million naira. Katsina State, whose governor is reputed to be prudent with and cautious in spending public fund, owes just over two million, having paid 99.20 per cent of the N258,618,210 for fertilisers supplied to the state. If there is an award for Governors who excelled in serving the agricultural sector, those in charge should take note of this and act suitably.
Twenty-four other states in the country and the Federal Capital Territory have either made substantial payments to the Fund or are in the process of paying the debt in a jointly agreed way. This willingness to settle the fertiliser debt by the state governments concerned is a clear sign of patriotism and concern for the welfare of Nigerian farmers and the country's desire to attain full food security.
But despite the temporary scarcity of fertilisers, fresh crops, all harvests of the current wet season, are arriving the market, cushioning the fang of the inflation induced by the recent strike in the country that made life a bit more uncomfortable for many urban dwellers. For instance, fresh maize is being roasted and boiled in ever-greater quantity in our cities, with the low-income earners and even the slightly richer ones all benefiting from its hunger-fighting power.
There are plentiful new, farm fresh sweet and Irish potatoes in the market. So also are new yams, cocoyams and cassava. The roots and tubers, which mature early and sold cheaply, constitute a blessing that help in reducing hunger and the subsequent anger in a polity that is willing to exercise its new found freedom of expression with excessive exuberance.
The hardworking farmers of Nigeria constitute a strong factor in stabilizing this country by their consistent contribution to the welfare of its over 126 million citizens. They also contribute at least 43 per cent of the country's US$117 billion annual Gross Domestic Product (GDP) as recorded in the year 2000.
That is why every effort put into agriculture is a wise investment. The Obasanjo administration has in the previous four years rolled out several initiatives and in some cases actually took steps to reinforce the agricultural sector and assure its status as the strongest pillar of the Nigerian economy.
One of these initiatives is the introduction of a means to solve the problem of commodity marketing facing Nigerian farmers, ensure better returns to them and minimise or totally eliminate glut. This gave birth to the idea of creating three multi-commodity development and marketing companies, which would be owned and managed by farmers and other agro-based investors.
The director of Strategic Grains Reserve, Mr. Tunji Ademola, said the three companies, each with an initial share capital of N500 million split into 50 million units and going per Naira each, are the Arable Crops and Marketing Company Plc; Tree Crops Development and Marketing Company Plc and the Livestock & Fishery Development and Marketing Company Plc. The federal government would kick-start the companies with initial 40 per cent equity shares to be divested after five years. The farmers, commodity associations and other agro-stakeholders would own the majority 60 per cent equity shares right from the beginning. President Olusegun Obasanjo has already directed that the N600 million initial equity investment be released. The Director further explained that hundreds of farmers across the country have actually invested in the shares of the three companies, which would take-off effectively once all preparations are complete.
•Salisu Na'inna Dambatta is Assistant Director (Information) in the Presidency.

Original_Search_Title: Serious Landslide in Bayatung
Headline: Nigeria: Serious Landslide in Bayatung
Body_Text:
The devastating effect of landslide in Bayatung "Ukpe Ward" Obudu Local
Government Area of Cross river State has become a routine natural
disaster. The inhabitants of these villages perennially loss their lives,
houses, farms, etc as a result of landslide.
On Sunday, July 20, 203, the landslide almost cause the extinction of
Bayatung village; thank God who never allowed it to happen as most of the
villagers were in the church when the disaster occurred. Although
eyewitnesses say two twins were lost, four adults and 17 houses were buried
during the incident. Cocoa, plantain, banana plantation as well as yam farms
were destroyed worth millions of naira in approximately 1km landslide in the
mountainous village.
We do not need astrologers or magicians to forecast the future of these
people that they will suffer from severe famine and the consequence is
malnutrition of people resulting to what we do not expect; as we are aware
of the effect of malnutrition in children and adults.
Therefore, the ministry of environment and the Niger Delta Development
Commission (NDDC) should come to the rescue of these people. And as well
look for measures to curb this unforeseen circumstance, as consistent
landslide in an area may result to earthquake, which we did not pray
for.
The writer is not only concerned with the recent incident but also other
places affected by this natural disaster.

Original_Search_Title: Those Whom the Gods Want to Kill (1)
Headline: Nigeria: Those Whom the Gods Want to Kill (1)
Body_Text:
What happened in Anambra would only have taken place in
Nigeria.....
IT was one Thursday in August 2000, the citizens of Aguata Local
Government Area of Anambra State gathered at the LG Secretariat for their
first ever new yam festival and cultural extravaganza. All the traditional
rulers with their cabinet chiefs, top private and public sector operators,
members of federal and state executive and legislature from within and
outside the state, past and present governors and all the distinguished sons
and daughters of the local government as well as their friends were in
attendance. I was there in my capacity as a member of the king's cabinet of
my town. It was indeed a distinguished assembly. While Governor Mbaninuju
was breaking the kola-nut (and that is at the core of every Igbo occasion),
there was some commotion as a siren blaring convoy led by heavily armed
policemen, drove with the recklessness of Jehu into the arena and a frail
looking young man alighted just in front of the governor. And while I was
wondering who had the guts to enter so riotously and arrogantly, the
governor abandoned the kola-nut ceremony and started singing praises of the
new entrant, introducing him as Chief Chris Uba (Eselu), and as one of the
most distinguished sons of Anambra without whom nothing would work in the
state! Everything was suspended until he settled down. He not only made a
nonsense of official protocol, he interrupted the kola-nut breaking by the
governor which was an alu (abomination) in Igboland. I noticed how helpless
the governor was, and how from then on, everything revolved around him with
everybody trying to catch his attention. That 'triumphant entry' was my
first and last time of seeing Chief Uba, an unbearable arrogant man who
plays god with reckless abandon, who does not give a damn about crushing
people toes, and who uses his power to humiliate and belittle men, whom
ordinarily, he is not worthy to clean their shoes.
Stupendous wealth
But I have heard about him before then. They said he was stupendously
wealthy, very powerful, well connected (especially at the source) and
influential but nobody told me how he came about his wealth or what he does
and I do not know whether he acquired his wealth through the power and
influence or vice versa. I also heard that he could fix anything and that he
was among the three musketeers that hoisted Mbaninuju on the unlucky people
of Anambra State in 1999. He also emerged as the political leader of Old
Aguata Union, and no one could explain how that was possible in an area that
boasts of people like Dr. Alex Ekwueme (former Vice President), Okwadike
C. P Ezeife (former Governor), Prof. H. Nwosu (former Electoral Commission
boss) and Dr. A. A. Agwuncha (of Eastern Mandate Union). And like the man
who never dreamt of taking an ozo title, he tied the symbolic thread around
the neck, where everybody would see it instead of the normal position, the
ankle where it is not very visible.
Before long, Uba acquired a larger than life image particularly in the
affairs of Anambra State where he has been mentioned in connection with
everything happening there and none of those events are noble. These
included the crises between Mbaninuju, his deputy and the state Assembly,
the Mbaninuju/Offor face-off, the abdication and failed removal of Aguata LG
chairman, the circus-show-like PDP primaries, and the recent election fiasco
in Anambra State. Encouraged by his past 'successes', the repressive state
machineries at his disposal, the much touted and abused Abuja connection,
show that he is in charge in Anambra State, he moved against Ngige. Surely,
the gods wanted to kill him and as it is in their character, they first of
all made him mad-man with power; mad with money; mad with a spirit of
invincibility; and mad with a rabid desire to be worshiped. And because a
dog destined to die ignores the hunter's whistle, he moved against Ngige
even before he had settled down to work, without perfecting his plans and
overlooking the fact that Ngige may be a doctor but he is certainly no
Mbaninuju.
What happened at Awka on Thursday, July10, 2003, would have been
laughable but for its grievous nature. But it did not start that day. It
started when Uba 'single-handedly installed' all the political office
holders in Anambra State (that confirms that there were no elections and
that they were not elected) and they in return publicly pledged
unquestionable, zombie-like loyalty. And being a man without an iota of
humility, he made repeated public shows of the installation and the
resultant allegiance. After a fierce battle that probably started before
inauguration day, May 29, all bordering on direct and indirect 'resource
control', the godfather struck. It was a coup achieved through
civilian-police collaboration. If you think it was not a coup, compare the
statement by Vrutus-the deputy governor- with those of the coup masters, Joe
Garba or Dogonyarro. This is in addition to the invasion and abduction. And
there probably would have been a summary execution but for that phone call
that was also providentially broadcast on Radio Nigeria Network News. But
that was not the first police-civilian coup in the state. The PDP primary
was a coup and so were the victories of Ngige and PDP, the National Assembly
show of shame in which people who did not contest emerged winners, and the
emergence of Mrs. Azodo as the Speaker. Other sordid details of high-handed
political brigandage and chicanery are already public knowledge.
Pockets a state
The wind has blown and we have seen the anus of the fowl! An individual
pocketed a state in the federation, boasts about it and employs the unholy
trinity of fraudulent manipulation. What happened could only have taken
place in Nigeria where every thing is possible, where some people are above
the law, and where police 'services' are available to the highest bidder; in
Anambra where the 'men of timber always look the other way while the rest
are busy chasing containers, where the civil servants' loyalty is as cheap
as that of Judas, where money maketh a man, and where there is always a good
supply of mercenary politicians; and in the PDP which is always its own
opposition, where nothing is ever done transparently and where there is
always a leader in addition to a chairman.
Ngige fought a good fight; he displayed intelligence, courage and tact
but he has a share of he blame. Why and how did he devaluate himself to that
level; why did he politically castrate himself and thus become the first
ceremonial state governor in Nigeria in whose domain appointments,
deployments and dismissals are made without his knowledge! How did he allow
himself to be manipulated to such an extent and how was he able to bear this
humiliation for so long? Surely, he gathered ant-infested firewood and
should ordinarily expect the company of lizards. Also, he who must go to
equity must do with clean hands.

Original_Search_Title: Between Achebe And Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Between Achebe And Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Lagos — "Whenever people have asked me which among my novels is my favourite I have always evaded a direct answer, being strongly of the mind that in sheer invidiousness that question is fully comparable to asking a man to list his children in the order in which he loves them. A paterfamilias worth his salt will, if he must, speak about the peculiar attractiveness of each child. For Arrow of God that peculiar quality may lie in the fact that it is the novel which I am most likely to be caught sitting down to read again." With these words, foremost Nigerian novelist and professor emeritus - Chinua Achebe began his introduction to the second edition of his third and, in my view, most intrusive novel - Arrow of God.
I have not read a novel in years. Like most Nigerians, sitting down to read a book is not one of the many things I am most likely to found doing. I reckon that the burden occasioned by the quest to make ends meet is far too heavy on me to care about such an "inconsequential" thing as reading a novel. But that is not the only reason. Reading a novel is a thing you do in your leisure, when a man is at rest and at peace with himself and his environment.
So far, the Nigerian experience has been very unfair to the concept of rest and leisure. You wake up everyday at 4.30am. You rush out to work. You get to your desk, heave a big sigh and thank God for escaping the traumatic traffic jam and the wrath of miscreants who operate on the highways in the early hours. And just as you are about to settle down to work, NEPA strikes. The whole place gets so stuffy. You are sweating. You are irritable. You lose concentration. But work must go on. So your company's diesel generator is switched on. Other companies down the street turn theirs on as well. Soon the entire environment is transformed into one huge, noisy and smoking locomotive. You can't hear your colleague even if he shouted. You can't think. You can't work. Next, you are glancing at your wrist watch. You just can't wait for it to clock 5pm. You walk down to your 'priceless' Tokunbo car. You have only a quarter tank of petrol left. At least that should take you home and back to work the next day, you reason. You hit the road but you don't get far. The traffic jam, which you managed to evade in the morning, welcomes you back to hell. You are apprehensive because your little petrol is about to burn out in just one spot. You wind down your windows, loosen your tie and resign yourself to fate. You are sweating and angry and confused. You manage to get home at 11pm sometimes 12. You dash into the bathroom for a cold shower but the taps are dry. You are cursing. You are swearing. You are wondering whether God made a mistake for creating you a Nigerian. Of course reading a novel will be the last thing that will go through your harassed mind in that instance.
The first and, as far as I can remember, last time I read Arrow of God, it was primarily to fulfill part of an English Literature course requirement back in the secondary school. In other words, the exercise was purely academic. I read the novel to pass a course, nothing more. That explains why I have forgotten most of the great speeches made by the major characters. I have also forgotten most of the powerful proverbs, the idioms and the general use of words by the master of words himself. But one thing I have not forgotten and which the recent protest by the Nigerian people against the hike in the prices of petroleum products brings to the fore is the central theme of the novel. This theme is summarized thus: no man, no matter how big and powerful and right, can win judgment against his own people.
The character of Ezeulu " the tragic hero of that magnificent novel, his relationship with his deity" Ulu, his relationship with other deities, other chiefs and the people of Umuaro, his high-headedness and frequent I-know-it-all-posture, his inability to stop dancing even when the music had long stopped and his eventual rejection by both the gods and the people is worth a study if one is to understand the tragic path which our dear President Obasanjo and his government may soon toe if he is not careful.
Somehow I found myself walking into a bookshop the other day to pick up a fresh copy of Arrow of God. In spite of myself, I have been reading and re-reading the novel over and over. And I have come to the conclusion that our President can very well pass for a modern-day Chief Priest of Ulu. He has all the funny traits, all the poise, all the pride and arrogance of Ezeulu. President Obasanjo also shares Ezeulu's worst trait of all - a clear disconnection with the people and with reality. Incidentally, this weakness was the immediate cause of Ezeulu's fall.
That President Obasanjo is completely cut off from reality and the feelings of the people he governs is no longer news. But the fact that we still talk about it is indicative of our continued frustration with the man's obtuse decisions and bland style of governance. Last weekend I had cause to dash down to my home town in Imo State. I have always preferred road travels in spite of the obvious dangers. My usual bus ride from Lagos to Owerri and back recorded a substantial increase in cost. But that is not the story. I was stunned to realize that my limited movement within Imo State cost me far more than I spent getting down there from Lagos for the simple reason that petrol, which sells for 34 naira in Lagos, now sells between 70 and 90 naira in most parts of the state. Yet, one of the President's strongest points in his numerous efforts to justify the price increase was that it will remove paucity of products, regiment the prices and make them uniform everywhere. How flawed can an argument of that nature possibly be? The part two of the story is even more pathetic. My sweet mother had taken advantage of my presence to ask me to do the near impossible -- get her some kerosene. I spent a whole day searching for this commodity that has not only become scarce but has also become so luxurious even the likes of Bill Gates will protest. I am still recovering from the shock of having to pay a whopping N6,020.00 to fill the twenty-liter plastic container I had with me. One can then imagine the hell that will be unleashed on hapless citizens should the PPRA carry out their new threat to increase the prices of the products yet again.
Most Nigerians appreciate the fact that we do have a problem on our hands. Some tough decisions ought to be taken to bring back sanity in the oil sub-sector. We are also in agreement that these decisions must be taken as quickly as can be managed. But in taking these decisions, the interest of the people must be fully protected otherwise, it will merely continue to be a case of robbing Peter to pay Paul.
The people of Umuaro in Arrow of God were once confronted with a similar problem. Ezeulu was imprisoned by the Whiteman in a neighbouring land so he was not on hand to perform his priestly functions, one of which is the eating of a sacred yam at the arrival of each moon. The last moon usually coincides with the last sacred yam and the period of harvest. But the people could not harvest their yams because Ezeulu still had three sacred yams to eat instead of one. Tradition did not permit him to eat more than one sacred yam at a time. There was a crisis of decision. Ezeulu was the man in the thick of the storm. If he eats the three sacred yams at once the people will harvest their crops but Ezeulu would have to face the wrath of the gods. If on the other hand he refuses to eat the three yams, the people's harvest will rot in the earth and the nation will record a year of famine.
Every man in Umuaro agreed that it was a problem that required a tough decision and they all sympathized with Ezeulu. But it was also a problem that called for more understanding and consideration in favour of the people, not the gods. After all the gods do not eat yams, but the people do. Ezeulu eventually took his decision in the interest of the gods. The result was that the long-suffering people abandoned him and his gods. They went their separate ways and harvested their yams in the name of a new God.
Ezeulu was cross with the people of Umuaro for allowing the Whiteman to take him away. So he waited patiently to get even with them at the time of harvest. But he failed to realize that his arrest and incarceration was not the making of the people. And even if it was, he still had a greater responsibility as their custodian to always watch out for them. It seems to me that President Obasanjo is holding Nigerians responsible for his abysmal performance in his first four years in office. He is probably punishing us for this. Otherwise I do not see what we have done wrong to deserve his contempt.
The President should go and read Arrow of God. He may choose to disagree with the moral of the story. It is in his character to do so, but he is sure to learn one or two tricks from the persuasive political speeches of the major characters. The President should also read other great books on leadership. He is probably too busy traveling to care. But who says a small bookshelf can't be arranged for him in his traveling Jet? If he manages to read one book in a trip then it is worth it. Rather than engage in cheap blackmail and the award of white-elephant contracts, the new Ministers and Advisers should start reading too. Indeed, the quality of their decisions and advice will ultimately depend on how much wisdom and understanding they are able to garner from books.
Ezeh writes from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Igbagun - Ejuku Road: an Appeal to the FG
Headline: Nigeria: Igbagun - Ejuku Road: an Appeal to the FG
Body_Text:
Igbagun-Ejuku road is one of the major road in OKUN LAND of Kogi State that lead to many towns in that area, such towns include Alu, Oranre, Ife-Olukotun, Panyan and many more. The populace are dominantly local farmers who engage in farm activities such as cocoa farming, coffee, plantain, palm oil, cassava and yam to mention but a few. Unfortunately, there is no awareness of the application of modern technology in these areas and the little among them who have the knowledge of modern technology have no access to it. Common fertilizer is not available for them to use and those that even struggle to get it, obtained it at a higher rate, which means when it's even available there is no means for majority of them to buy.
Moreover, when they are able to harvest the little crops they have in the farm, to transport it to the major market become a big problem because of the situation of the road.
Traveling to these towns during raining season is extremely difficult. These had led to the wastage of the resources God has bless these areas with. They lost millions of naira day in, day out due to access road to transport them to the major market.
Indeed, the former governor, Prince Abubakar Audu had visited the towns several times and a lot of promises have been made to no avail.
The entire communities of these towns are now appealing to the federal government and Mr. President specifically to come to the aid of the people in these area by awarding the contract for the repair of this road.
Inter state trade will be boost if this road is accessible. Through the availability of good road and increase in productivity there will be employment for the unemployed youth and thereby reducing rural-urban immigration.
Finally, through good road, the people will have access to storage facilities to combat the existence of resource wastage.
Mr. President, we pray for your success in all your undertakings to lead us to the glory land as you take on another four years tenure.
Ibrahim T. Hassan, No. 14 Alkani Ibiom Stret, Gwarimpa Estate, Third Gate, Abuja.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Claims 2, Sacks 5 Villages
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Claims 2, Sacks 5 Villages
Body_Text:
Makurdi — At least two lives were lost and over 3000 people displaced in Vandeikya
Local Government of Benue State where five communities were sacked by flood
which struck on Sunday. The local government said it would need N3 billion
to resettle the displaced victims.
The flood also swept along with it "five bridges across Aya and Be,
rivers .It also destroyed a total of "fifty-two compounds and ravaged many
economic trees, domestic animals, food stuff, Yams Cassava and Fadama
farms." Said Hon Joseph Anakaa, transition committee chairman for the local
government. He named the five communities as Dagba and Mbazerem, in Mbaduku
district, and Agera, Agera, Agidi-Mbakena and Gube communities in Mbayongu
district. The communities, he said, are the worst hit by the flood which he
described as "unparallel in the history of the local government area".
Although the local government authorities have "within the confines of
her meager resources, procured some food items which are being distributed
to the affected victims".
"These victims need a lot of support from individuals, organizations and
governments so as top reposition themselves socially and economically." He
said, adding "the meager resources at our disposal can not be adequate
enough to cope with the needs of these victims."
It is feared that the flooding of the river which stretches up to the
Cameroon's may have been occasioned by uncontrolled water situation around
the region.
According to the chairman, victims of the disaster are currently
squatting with friends, relative or in public buildings.
He said the situation neededquick and urgent action if the victims are
to return to live their normal lives.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Elevated to Traditional Export as Ghana Searches for Substitute for Cocoa
Headline: Ghana: Yam Elevated to Traditional Export as Ghana Searches for Substitute for Cocoa
Body_Text:
Accra — Far away in the scorching sun of Northern Ghana, farmers are busily
burning the bush, turning the soil and raising mounds to plant yam, which
has become a staple in many homes in Ghana and in recent times has gained
international acceptance as a major food.
The farmers of Northern Ghana have been cultivating yam for centuries
mainly as a subsistent activity to keep body and soul going. In the past,
trade in the commodity was largely internal, with very few exports to
neighbouring countries. With lack of processing crop and methods of
preserving it, most of the crop goes waste during years of bumper harvest
and becomes scarce and expensive during the lean season.
Last week the Minster of Private Sector Development, Kwamena Bartels
told journalist that government had reclassified yam as a traditional export
because it wants to boost revenue from yam exports. Could government
decision to make yam a traditional export mean the beginning of better days
for yam producers?
Making yam a major export earners has dire environmental
consequences. Yam cannot be cultivated on the same piece of land for two
seasons. Every other year a fresh land is cleared and rid of trees in order
that yams do not compete for nutrients with the tree. It is estimated that
for every yam that is exported, three trees would have been felled.
The elevation of yam as a major export comes in the wake of fears that
cocoa, which for a long time has been the backbone of the economy, cannot
take Ghana into the future.
During the launching of the State of the Ghanaian Economy 2002,
Professor Ernest Aryeetey, Director of the Institute of Statistical, Social
and Economic Research (ISSER) said "Cocoa cannot take us into the future. It
can help us survive today, but its future is bleak." Prof. Aryeetey
suggested that due to the declining performance of cocoa, it was time
Ghanaians found another export to complement the crop. Can yam become a
successful complement for cocoa?
In order to boost export value of yam, former Minister of Trade,
Dr. Kofi Konadu Apraku sought parliamentary amendment of section 12 and
13(a) of the Exports and Imports Act, 1995, Act 503 to empower the ministry
to regulate commercial exports of the commodity. With the amendment, the
Ghana Export Forms, which were being used by exporters, were replaced with
Bank of Ghana Form A2 with the aim of channeling foreign exchange flows from
yam exports through the banking system.
Like cocoa, Ghana has the premium for yam, but has failed to take
advantage of the high demand for the 'Pona' type. According to figures from
the Ghana Export Promotion Council (GEPC) in 2002 fresh yams contributed
13,024 tonnes valued at US$8.2347 million compared to 14,409 tonnes valued
at US$7.785 million, indicating an increase of 5.94 percent in value. This
is considered very low given the potential market for the crop in
Europe.
The Executive Secretary of Ghana Yam Producers and Exporters
Association, Osei Banahene, told this reporter that the association had
expected that with the change in status, the government would have
established a Yam Marketing Board on the model of the Ghana Cocoa Marketing
Board and appointed executives to manage the production and outflow of the
commodity. "The absence of a marketing board has undermined attempts by the
association to bring sanity into the trade", Banahene told this reporter in
his office at the Ghana International Trade Faire Site.
In the view of Banahene because the country is pursuing an export-led
and trade liberalization policies, exporters are not enjoined to come under
one exporting association. He said the absence of control has paved the way
for some unregistered exporters to indulge in unprofessional practices.
Banahene explains that some unregistered exporters use yam as a conduit
pipe to smuggle illegal drugs to Europe. "Once the yams pass through customs
checks undetected the exporters make thousands of dollars from the drugs and
sell the yams far lower than the going price."
The uses of yam to export drugs and the deliberate under pricing by some
exporters have combined to keep the price of the commodity very low on the
international market over the last couple of years. The price has however
improved recently following government decision to enhance the status of
yam. According to Banahene a box of yam is now selling for £20 from
£7 last year. The United Kingdom is the largest destination of
Ghanaian yam because it has the highest concentration of Africans and Blacks
in the Diaspora in Europe. But has the increase in price resulted in
increase in the producer price for farmers?
Perhaps, the biggest challenge facing yam exports is the age-old problem
of Ghanaian exporting without doing market survey. In the case of yam,
supplies over the years have exceeded demand to the extent that at a point
yam becomes cheaper in London than in Accra. "When it happens that way the
prices nose-dive. The price keeps dropping because yam export is a free
range business", complains Banahene, who lauded government policy to
regulate the export of yam.
One other problem facing the exporters is the unwilligness of importers
to pay for supplies. Yam is classified as an ethnic food in the United
Kingdom and mostly consumed by Africans and Blacks in the
Diaspora. Unfortunately, those who consume yam do not control its imports
and retailing. The middlemen and retailers are mostly Greeks, Indians and
Pakistanis, who are accused of robbing the exporters.
Many exporters complain that the middlemen sweet-talk them, but after
taking the supplies and selling they pocket the money. The Executive
Secretary of Yam Exporters alleges that thousands of pounds are still
outstanding in the name of some UK importers in 2001. "It's always in their
interest to ensure that we are disorganized, they use money to induce more
people to go into the export of yam in order to bring the price down."
So, how do all these developments affect the farmers, on whose sweat and
toil the yam business rests? Some exporters have complained that since they
buy the crop on credit from the farmers developments on international market
have a chain effect on the farmers.
Reports gathered by this reporter show that whether yam is a
non-traditional export or a traditional export has not changed the fortunes
of the poor rural farmers. Last week yam farmers from Nkoranza and Kintampo
districts of the Brong Ahafo Region said they had been compelled to sell
their crop at ridiculously low prices because of the bumper harvest this
year. The glut has worsened the plight of the farmers, as consumers prefer
the old stock, which is cheaper.
"It takes us weeks to sell the hitherto fast selling crop even at very
low prices", complains Akosua Woraewaa, Chairperson of the Nkoranza Yam
Producers and Marketers Association. Given the fact that the crop is not
doing well on the local market, what is the guarantee that it would do
better on the world market? Does the commodisation of yam on the
international market benefit the farmer?

Original_Search_Title: Oddities Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Oddities Nigeria
Body_Text:
Nigeria, some (call them cynics) swear can simply be defined as a country which exports what it does not have and imports what it has. As patriotic Nigerians, we can accuse such people of trying to distract us from gains of our nascent democracy but a few examples tend to justify them. Take the recent nationwide strike action called by Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC). It is the 'appropriate pricing' of petroleum products that we have in abundance here, complete with refineries, but would rather import that caused the breach to the peace at home.
But we ignored that and tried to 'export' peace to Liberia by going out of our way to invite Charles Taylor (his harem, combatants, hangers-on and all) here to Nigeria. Don't be surprised if he is not impressed. It is an irony of life that human nature abhors hypocrisy even if it is prone to it. Charles Taylor can see through our ego-massaging offer of asylum, and so is not in a hurry to embrace it.
Of course, we can go back in time and recall the Gowon era when 'money (was) not our problem but how to spend it. 'It was not unusual for then General Gowon to pay the salaries of workers in some of the countries he visited on his highly sought-after foreign trips. But has this ever been true? Has there been any time money was not a problem in Nigeria? If we had doubts then, certainly not now.
The crass poverty the majority of our fellow citizens are subjected to and our rating on the Human Development Index scale will convince any doubting Thomas. Such one find company though in President Obasanjo who was reported to have disputed these dismal statistics at a recent forum because farmers in his home village of Ibogun can afford to eat whole tubers of yam at a sitting! Interesting. Perhaps the President forgot to tell us what quality of water, and the amount of meat and fish they eat the yam with. All these, the experts say, constitute elements of a balanced diet which is in short supply these days.
