a little distance away carrying a sack behind his back.
When the little group had finally caught up with the man, they asked him
what was in the sack and he said it was some yam tubers and firewood which
he bought, but the boy who had followed him insisting that it was his
friend.
The group forced the man to open the sack and almost immediately, the
man tried to escape but was caught and beaten before he was handed over to
the police.
A reliable police source at Gwarimpa informed our reporter that the
police had later discovered that the man who was a member of a syndicate
that was into kidnapping people for ritual purposes.
The man was said to have confessed that he was sent by a woman to bring
a young boy to her. "She did not tell me what she wanted to do with the
boy," the man claimed.
Meanwhile, the residents have the problem of ensuring security of their
children to grapple with in addition to incessant robbery attacks, water
scarcity and erratic power supply to the estate.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Food Stuff Rise in FCT
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Food Stuff Rise in FCT
Body_Text:
There has been steady increase in the prices of foodstuff in Abuja the
Federal Capital Territory. A survey that took Abuja Trust to Wuse market and
old market and Karmo market revealed that the prices of a set of yam tubers
has increased by almost 150 per cent in the last two months a set of yam (5
tubers) which used to sell for N300 about 2 months ago, now sells for
N800.
Other stuffs like garri, white and red beans have also recorded price
increase. Red beans which used to be sold N90 per mudu now goes for N120 per
mudu, while the white one moved from N70 to N90. Rice also had its price
increased per mudu from N110 as at 2 months ago to N140, while a bag of it,
depending on the brand, ranges between N4,100 to N5,000.
The price of palm oil has equally risen per 20-litre jerican form N3,100
to N3500 while groundnut oil has increased N2,500 to N3,200. This wholesale
increment has also affected the marginal price of oil per bottle.
There have also been recognizable increase in the prices of meat. For
example, a cow, which used to be sold for between N30,000 - N35,000, now
goes for N90,00 - N100,000. According to Malam Gasbar Aliyu, a butcher at
Wuse market, the increment has been due to cost of transportation and police
duties. "We normally convey cow from Kano and Borno to Abuja, and this used
to cost us N2,000 and N4,000. Now, we spend N5,000 and N7,000 respectively,
to convey cow from Kano and Borno to Abuja. Beside, we pay policemen very
heavy amount of money on the highway as bribery."
Other traders with whom Abuja Trust spoke also attributed the increase
in prices of foodstuff in FCT to cost of transportation.
However, Malam Audu Adamu, a fruit seller told Abuja Trust that seasonal
charges have also contributed significantly to the rise in foodstuff
prices. "I however believe that in matter of 2-3 months from now, some food
items will begin to record prices fall, while some will stabilize and others
increase also."
A concerned resident, Musa Salihu told our reporter that unless
government shelves the proposed increase in prices of fuel, the
uncontrollable increase in prices of food items would provoke the poor
people to rise against law and order.

Original_Search_Title: University Teachers And the Long Road to Peace
Headline: Nigeria: University Teachers And the Long Road to Peace
Body_Text:
Lagos — After almost six months in the trenches, the Academic Staff Union of Universities (ASUU) finally came out Monday. But this calls for little cheer as its armour is still intact, writes Bukola Olatunji
When on Sunday, December 29, last year, the Academic Staff Union of Universities (ASUU) called its members to embark on a "total and indefinite nation-wide strike" to demand the implementation of the agreement it signed with the Federal Government in June 2001, many prayed and called for a quick resolution of the dispute.
Not a few agreed that the Universities were, and still are in bad shape and in need of urgent attention. But, as some have argued, so are the Polytechnics, the Colleges of Education, the Federal and State Secondary Schools and the pathetic primary schools in their thousands nation wide. But if ASUU decided to take its destiny in its own hands, so be it.
However, like the story of the Tortoise who was caught harvesting his in-law's yams, the support for ASUU gradually turned to condemnations in some quarters.
As the story goes, the Tortoise was caught harvesting his in-law's yams in the early hours of the morning and promptly tied to a stake for all passers-by to see. Every one heaped insults on "the shameless Tortoise" as they went to their farms. But when they found the Tortoise still tied to the stake on their way back home at sundown, everyone turned against "the wicked in-law who wants to kill his daughter's husband because of "a few miserable tubers of yam."
The dialogue convened by the Federal Government, through the Federal Ministry of Labour, collapsed more than once due to the parties' inability to reach a consensus. When the union announced on Monday that it was calling off the strike with effect from this morning, it blamed former Minister of Education, Prof. Babalola Borishade for the collapse of the dialogue, but President Olusegun Obasanjo had also blamed the union for making unreasonable demands, suggesting that dialogue could continue if ASUU could be, "more reasonable."
It would appear that even the unborn Nigerian child would be familiar with the events that lead to the ASUU/Federal Government dispute by now. What every right thinking Nigerian should be bothered about now is the way forward.
It is therefore important to present the thinking on either side for the benefit of all stakeholders, whose various interventions have contributed in no small way to the progress made so far.
Only last Sunday, President Obasanjo repeated for the umpteenth time on national television, one of ASUU's unreasonable demands - than N400 billion or half of the nation's budget.
But in its statement announcing the suspension of the strike, ASUU said, "it will not help the situation to continue to tell the nation that ASUU demanded N415bn at a go when government is in possession of the report of a Government Committee and documents showing ASUU demanded no such thing."
The president also stressed during the media chat on television that the 49 lecturers at the University of Ilorin were sacked for disrupting students' examination. When reminded of the lecturers' position that they were not guilty of the allegation (and their sack letter stated that they were sacked for participating in the strike of May 22nd, 2001), the president said that was what he was told by the then university authority and Governing Council.
Again, ASUU response to this was that, "no problem will be solved by telling the public, without embarrassment, that the lecturers in University of Ilorin were sacked without hearing them and that the accusers were the only ones who deserved to be heard, against the Constitution which the President of Nigeria swore to defend."
Speculations had been rife that ASUU was about to call off its strike last weekend, following its National Executive Council (NEC) meeting held at the University of Ado Ekiti (UNAD).
Dr. Fashina said during Monday's address that the meeting was attended by all branches of NEC. Reports were taken from the National Secretariat of ASUU and from ASUU branches. Reports were also received from the branches of ASUU on the referendum ordered by NEC on the Industrial Arbitration Panel (IAP), which had on, on May 28, ordered the union back to work.
The union took a swipe at the IAP, saying, "without prejudice to the integrity of the individuals who operate the institution, the Industrial Arbitration Panel (IAP) and the general process of industrial arbitration are generally loaded against Nigerian workers especially when government is an aggrieved party."
It therefore called "for concerted efforts by workers' organisations, the Nigeria Labour Congress, and professional organisations such as the Nigerian Bar Association towards a comprehensive review of Nigerian labour laws."
According to it, "this inbuilt institutional bias derives directly from fundamental defects in Nigerian labour laws."
ASUU went further to say that it "considered seriously the calls and pleas of Nigerians, patriotic individuals and organisations on the need to create an enabling environment in which the resolution of all the issues at stake could be resolved in a way that will make a difference in the quaIity of teaching and research in the Nigerian University system.
"Such a resolution, it was noted, was based on the admission by all those who made the appeals and pleas, that the Federal Government is bound to honour the Agreement it reached with ASUU, in June 30, 2001; and that State Universities ought to be
adequately funded and this will best be achieved by the endorsement and implementation of the Agreement by the State Governments.
"Many patriotic organisations appealed to ASUU, attempted to intervene in order to resolve the problems threatening the survival of the Nigerian university system. "
So has the strike been called off?
The union said, "in order to allow the patriotic individuals, organs, and indeed all members of the general public to conclude their positive intervention towards making the Federal Government to honour its own side of the JUNE 2001 Agreement, the State Governments that have endorsed it to do the same, and the few that have not endorsed it to do so and enable their Universities meet the standards envisaged for the university system in Nigeria; and, finally, in order to demonstrate that ASUU as a Union is willing to abide by the laws even under unfavourable
NEC resolved to suspend its ongoing strike with effect from 'Wednesday 18th JUNE 2003. "
What does that mean?
Dr. Fashina said, "if IAP cannot compel government to obey the law, then we know we (the union) are finished with the law."
He went on, "we have suspended the strike in order to give government a chance to resolve the problems through dialogue in an atmosphere of peace. If this fails, Nigerians should lay the blame on the appropriate place, not on ASUU.
"NEC of ASUU will meet within two months to review the situation and to take appropriate steps in pursuance of our resolve to defend the University system using all legitimate means."
The union insisted that the order made by the IAP is directed to both the ASUU and the Federal Government. ASUU having observed its own part of the order, the Federal Government should also follow the path of honour and rule of law by obeying the order as it affects it. For the avoidance of doubt, Paragraphs three, four and five of the order state as follows:
That the First Party (Federal Government) shall not lock out any, some or all of the members of the Second Party (ASUU) pending the final determination of the dispute before the Arbitration Tribunal. The First Party shall not victimize, transfer or terminate any or all the members of the Second Party for offences connected with this dispute pending the final determination of the case before the Arbitration Panel
That both parties shall maintain law and order and keep the peace and shall not engage in any action detrimental to industrial peace and harmony, howsoever.
That the status quo ante the dispute be maintained by all the parties to this dispute until the final determination of the dispute by the Tribunal.
By these provisions therefore, obedience to the order by the FG would require that:
There is no victimization, including withholding of salaries; no transfer and no termination of appointment of any academic staff in any university in Nigeria.
Those victimized already at Unilorin i.e. 49 lecturers should be reinstated, immediately, under any construal of the status quo ante.
That all the financial entitlements of members arising from the non-implementation of the UASS since May 2001 would now be honoured through the full implementation of the UASS.
All areas of the Agreement not in dispute in respect of the work of the dialogue team and the Technical Committee should be implemented forthwith.
Acknowledging "the patriotic role of several individuals who have attempted to intervene to resolve the dispute fairly and in the interest of the university system and Nigeria", with a special mention of some of the individuals, organisations and unions, ASUU called for the immediate resumption of "dialogue on all other areas in dispute, because "the crisis currently engulfing the university system cannot disappear on its own. The only way to make it disappear is to solve the problems."
The union has remained solid to this point, Fashina said, despite "the denial to our members of their salary for close to six months. Nigerian Academics have starved for six months so that the Nigerian educational system and the universities will survive, so that future generations of Nigerians will benefit from public institutions and enjoy their constitutional right to fair and equal opportunity."
He stressed: "We are going back en bloc based on the strength of the resolution of our members. That collective strength will be re asserted if this crisis remains unresolved. It is our historical responsibility.?"
There had been a general outcry and calls on ASUU to go back to work, we have now heeded this call. In response, while ASUU is going back to work, the union is making a general call to all concerned, outside the student organisations, the National Assembly, especially the senate, which has written us to go back to work while it intervenes, the National Executive Council, the Governors, all Past Presidents/Heads of State, Obas, Emirs, Obis, parents, civil liberties organisations, workers' unions, especially the NLC, the media, organised labour, private enterprises and the general public, to prevail on the Federal Government to do what is right by obeying the IAP ruling in concrete terms so as to solve the problems and forestall disruption of industrial harmony, and returning to the dialogue table."
At any rate, Compliance with the IAP order requires the reinstatement of our 49 colleagues at UNILORIN.
While commending "the understanding and solidarity of" the students and their organization (NANS), ASUU pledged its "unflinching support for their right to full publicfunded and highquality education at all levels. We express our solidarity with our students in their resistance against the commercialization of education. Towards this end ASUU will pursue, assiduously, the implementation of all aspects of the ASUU - FGN 2001 Agreement relating to the welfare and rights of our students. NEC decision to suspend the strike for now is a recognition that our students are very dear to us."
The union called on the students to insist that the process of dialogue continues, the National Exco said it has appealed to all lecturers to assist students to cope with whatever difficulties they have in the present circumstances. "Our students should return to the campuses to receive lectures and ensure, apart from other things, that they and their organization NANS act to get the Federal Government to implement the FGASUU Agreement..."

Original_Search_Title: Student Inventor Pleads for Funding
Headline: Nigeria: Student Inventor Pleads for Funding
Body_Text:
Warri — An under-graduate of Obafemi Awolowo University (OAU) Ile-Ife, Osun state, Mr. Orighoye Babaetsetunokan who has invented a number of agricultural tools yesterday pleaded for funding of his research findings by the government at all levels and by public-spirited individuals.
Babaetsetunokan said such financial assistance would enable him produce his research materials enmasse for the use of members of the public. Babaetsetunokan, who is a 300-level student of the Department of Agricultural Engineering of OAU has, so far, invented a palm fruit blender for kitchen purpose, weed clearing machine, cassava peeling machine, vegetable and fruit slicing machine for kitchen purpose, multi-purpose planting machine for cassava stem and yam.
His other inventions include machine for eliminating air pollution during the usage of generator, Pabo Game for relaxation after hectic time on the farm as well as yam and cassava harvesting machine.
Similarly, Babaetsetunokan, an Itsekiri has collaborated with other researchers to invent palm oil processing machine for small scale industrial purpose and cassava as well as yam pounding machine for small scale industrial purpose. To this end, Babaetsetunokan implored the Federal and state governments, corporate bodies and well-to-do individuals in the society to come to his aid by offering financial assistance.
The young inventor who spoke to our Warri correspondent said such gesture would create room for the "mass production for national and international economic development".
He said the research items were not presented publicly as a result of the fear that it could be stolen by powerful individuals who may not attribute the ownership to his team. Continuing, Babaetsetunokan, said the research works would serve as permanent relief to members of the public whenever they are produced en-masse.

Original_Search_Title: Quiet Billionaire Passes Away
Headline: Nigeria: Quiet Billionaire Passes Away
Body_Text:
Ado-Ekiti — Lawrence Ajisafe Omolayo, a billionaire publisher and philanthropist in
Ekiti State dies The rain had just stopped. At a remote corner of a factory
located in the outskirts of Ekiti state capital, Ado-Ekiti, a graveyard
silence reigned. Not a single person was around. Even the dam, which usually
overflows its banks at this season of the year, appeared still. A lone frame
stood bare on its four legs. The tarpaulin, which once covered it, lay
folded up on the misty vegetation overlooking the dam.
Presently, some birds of dull plumage broke the silence, hopping to and
from coconut trees that dotted the entire terrain. It was 30th of May.
Some days before, an old woman had wept profusely at the same spot.  For
several weeks, she had fought back tears as many Ekiti sons and daughters
had done. But now, her anchor could no longer hold, when she saw the lump of
tarpaulin on the grass. The reason for her grief, though nostalgic, was not
far fetched. The lord of the manor, Lawrence Ajisafe Omolayo, a man of
legendary attributes, had joined his ancestors.
Omolayo lost his final battle with death at a specialist hospital in
Abuja 2 March, at the age of 68. Since his death, neither his family nor the
entire Ekiti State has remained the same. Men and women, students, workers,
young and old across Ekiti land and beyond, who had sucked the milk of his
large humanitarian breast still wish the death is not real.
Even the palace of the Ewi of Ado-Ekiti has become less colourful since
the demise. "I have not seen two Nigerians like him," said Oba Rufus
Adejugbe, the Ewi, who had known the late Omolayo for 40 years.  "He is a
bigger loss to Ekiti than to any other group of people including his
immediate family." The Oba should know. An event about seven years ago sheds
some light. On ascension to the throne of his fathers, the Ewi and his
subjects wanted to build a modern and modest palace befitting of his new
status.
A fund raising ceremony was promptly organised.
Olusola Saraki, a doctor and millionaire-politician, was invited as
chairman of the occasion. He donated generously towards the project like
others. The ceremony was winding up when out of the woods, words came that
an "anonymous giver and son of the soil" had sent an amount 10 times in
excess of what any single individual or group had donated. As the master of
ceremony made the announcement, "shouts of Omolayo, Omolayo rent the air..."
That was not a name that rings a bell and Dr. Saraki asked "who is this man
that donated this type of money and we don't know him?" Unfortunately, the
donor was not to be seen at the ceremony, or any social gathering for that
matter.
That was vintage Omolayo. He gave cheerfully and quietly.
About the time the launching was in progress, the philanthropist was in
his favourite steppe behind the factory, munching a measure of groundnut and
roasted plantain, popularly called booli, but which he fondly referred to as
'Ojo and Ajayi'. On a good day he preferred pounded yam and Egusi soup for
lunch. But whenever that was unavailable, "Ojo and Ajayi" took
precedence.
Such was the nature of the silent millionaire of Ekiti who breathed his
last about midnight on a Sunday in March, in Abuja, the Federal Capital
Territory. All his life, he did not believe in media hype about his
deeds. He was publicity shy and never a newsman's delight.  He was born in
Afao, a rustic agrarian village in Ekiti State in 1935. At birth he had no
silver spoon in his mouth. His mother Oloodan Olaribia Aboro and father
Aboro Ajagemo were into petty trading and farming.
The story of Omolayo reads like the script of a typical home movie.  His
childhood was one long painful struggle against poverty. "Right from infancy
he was involved in a wearisome battle for survival, " said his first son,
Sunday Omolayo. The battle paid off.
In 1956, through a dint of hard work and luck he established Omolayo
Bookshop, specialising in the sale of exercise books in Ado-Ekiti.  The
bookshop eventually grew into a large business, under the name Omolayo Group
of Companies, with interests in printing, saw milling, and property
development, among others. By 1990 when he officially stepped aside from
active participation in running of the company, the group had staff strength
of about 800. His printing press churned out exercise books, registers,
drawing books under the name Standard Books. Thus for more than three
decades, Standard became a house hold name in many families in Nigeria,
Ghana and the Gambia. His estimated worth was put at about N10 billion. He
also owned shares in George Stanley Paper mill in the United Kingdom.
Ironically, while Standard books made waves in schools, home and abroad,
the man behind the venture had little Western education. But those who knew
him said he had always demonstrated uncanny skills at arithmetic. "He showed
tremendous love for learning and betrayed flashes of a genius quite early in
life" said Pa Fasuan, a high chief and a relative of the late
businessman. These traits dominated his life. He did his calculations
without the aid of a machine and remained mentally active until the
end.
His parents also noticed this mathematical ingenuity in the lad. Thus at
the age of eight in 1943, his parents withdrew him from the farm where he
had served for about five years and enrolled him at St.  David Primary
School, Afao, for his pre-standard one and two classes.  From 1945 to 1946,
he was in Standard one and two. However, the need to make ends meet forced
him out of school in 1947. During this trying period, young Omolayo devoted
his time helping his mother to hawk kerosene, candlesticks, matches and
cigarettes.
Before he dropped out of school, Omolayo was already reputed to have the
best handwriting among his peers.
He was also the best in calculation. With these qualities in mind, both
mother and child soon saved enough money to return Omolayo to school at
Are-Afao United. He was in the school between 1948 and 1949 for his
standards three and four classes. In 1950, he proceeded to Emmanuel School
Ado Ekiti for his standard five and six, which he completed in 1951. The
17-year old boy sought sponsorship for further studies. But he could not get
any support. Frustrated, he moved to Ikere- Ekiti, where he joined an uncle,
John Awodigede to work as storekeeper in one Mr.
Ayodele's cocoa shop on a salary of 15 shillings.
Omolayo however resigned "because, as he told me, he was earning more
than that hawking petty domestic items," Awodigede recounted. On returning
to Afao, he became a farmer. He made a modest fortune from rice
cultivation. Then, he diversified to cocoa produce and selling, from which
he went to patent medicine in partnership with one Badejo.  A very mobile
entrepreneur, Omolayo's magic year came in 1956. That year, he left his
native home for Ado-Ekiti and established a bookshop at Okesha
Roundabout.
The hardship Omolayo faced in his youth sharpened his philanthropic
instincts later in life. His associates said he was a man who craved for the
progress of others. "He hated to see people suffering and would spend his
last kobo to send a child to school," Sunday who took over management of the
Omolayo Group of Companies in 1990 recalled.  Indeed Omolayo devoted his
resources on the education of his 20 children training most of them to the
university level before he died.
For instance Sunday, his 46-year old first child studied in several
universities in the United Kingdom and India. His chain of interdisciplinary
degrees spans the humanities, business administration and printing
technology. Many of his other siblings also boast similar
accomplishments.
But it was not on his 20 children that Omolayo invested the most. It was
on the children of others, particularly the indigent. Countless university
graduates have benefited from a scholarship scheme, which he
instituted. Those who have benefited and who are still benefiting include
primary and secondary school students.
Those familiar with the Omolayo phenomenon say such gestures are in
consonance with his profile. They might be right. There is a 350-room
complex standing to the glory of the late philanthropist at the campus of
University of Ado-Ekiti, UNAD. The N250 million building housing the vice
chancellor and other principal officials of the university has a revealing
history.
The institution, then known as Ondo State University was at the verge of
collapse due mainly to inadequate office and residential accommodation. The
students, in addition to poor learning facilities, had to grapple with
homelessness. The teachers also bemoaned their lack of office
space. Desperate for a solution, the university authorities approached
prominent indigenes of Ekiti for help to build an administrative block
valued at N1 million. When they approached Omolayo, he asked them to bring
the drawings of the building. The university community was dazed when rather
than give them donation, Omolayo promised to build the complex.
Soon, from one million naira, the cost ballooned to N25 million and
later to N100 million. By the time the administrative block was commissioned
on 30 October 1998, it had gulped over N250 million from the old man's
purse. That is not all.
Omolayo built a private hostel that has facility for about 7,000
students. Till death, students of the school referred to Omolayo as their
father. Little wonder, the university awarded him its highest academic
honour, an honorary doctor of law. Akin Oyebode, a professor and vice
chancellor, UNAD described him as a genius, perfectionist and a stickler for
excellence. "The two- storey building he built and donated to the university
is structurally strong and standard enough to be raised and upgraded to a
six- storey building." Pa Omolayo had no time for frivolities. A man of
serious mien, he abhorred laziness and dishonesty. He avoided social
gatherings like a plague, and rejected several entreaties to take a
chieftaincy title. On each occasion after rejecting a chieftaincy title, he
would remark to his close associates 'Ile Sanmi Dun joye lo, meaning a good
name or contentment is better than titles. He was also fond of saying about
titles and exhibitionism, biisu bata a o fi owo bo je, meaning if your yams
are big and succulent, you have to be discrete in eating them." Whenever any
of his workers stole his money or property, and he knows, rather than take
him to the police station, Baba would simply call him and dress his head
with leaves and make him to dance round the company premises. This
humiliating gesture of bestowing a title was the highest punishment the old
man imposed on a dishonest worker, no matter the degree of fraud
committed. In fact, accounts are common of how Baba would go to the police
station and bail out some of his workers who from time to time dragged
themselves before the police over the sharing of booties they stole from
him. "It is difficult to see a successful businessman in Ado Ekiti today,
who has not passed through Omolayo one way or the other", Pa Ade Ogunniyi,
who said Baba paid his school fees and shouldered all expenses of his
wedding in 1963 told TheNEWS.
Pa Omolayo gave cheerfully to God. He was the largest single donor to
the building of St. Louis Anglican Church, Afao-Ekiti. He was an Anglican by
birth. But the tides of life later brought him to the Apostolic Faith
Church, AFC to which he donated the first site of his factory in the early
1980s.
His battle against the end started in 1980 at the age of 45. That year,
he was struck by an illness that made him to forgo food for days. Mara bouts
tried their luck to restore him to health without success.
He was written off in hospitals. Then one of his workers, Ajayi
Ogunmadeji who had left his service in 1973 over fraudulent practices and
returned after making restitution, prayed for him. He also enjoined other
members of the AFC to join him in prayers. As it happened, Baba was restored
back to health. And thus began his eventual romance with the Apostolic Faith
Church.
The last 13 years of Omolayo was plagued by the complications of a life
threatening hypertension. He first became hypertensive in 1990.  Later the
debilitating condition became a stroke leading to partial paralysis of his
lower limbs.
But he fought on. The second attack left him confined to the wheel
chair.
Again, he resisted death. On the wheel chair, he made a point of duty to
visit the students of UNAD regularly calling them his children. He
subsequently had three other consecutive strokes. But even on his wheel
chair, he continued to work. The fifth one became fatal claiming not only
his working spirit, but also his life.
And thus ended the career of one of the greatest sons of Ekiti
State.

Original_Search_Title: Percy Leads Gospel Singers to Lagos Festival of Praise
Headline: Nigeria: Percy Leads Gospel Singers to Lagos Festival of Praise
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Kaduna State-based gospel musician, Panam Percy Paul, is due in
Lagos for a worship concert tagged: "Festival of Praise".
As banners of the three-day variety show are being hoisted on the
streets of Lagos, the General Overseer of the Gospel Ministries
International (GMI), organisers of the concert, Rev. Moses Kayode Omodunisi,
said praise was an unequal weapon of attracting God's favour.
Under the theme: "Bringing Down His Glory", Omodanisi said the event was
one of the Church's numerous programmes aimed at sustaining the nation's
nascent democracy and attracting God's power upon the devil's mission of
death and destruction characterised by various afflictions of the people in
this end time.
He tasked Christians to understand the dynamics of worship to be able to
understand soul lifting music, which pleases God.
"God does not eat pounded yam. His food is praise. God is so faithful
that our existence is just by His grace and as a person, when I pray, 70
percent of my utterances is praise. I am not a good singer though, but I
praise Him by His titles, I like to call him by His names. Jehovah,
Elshadai, Jehovah Shammah, Jehovah Jire and so on. Beside, we have always
had what we call 'Judah Night', otherwise known as 'praise night' once in
every two months, but more of an internal affair. This time around, we
intend to have it big and so we have invited Dr. Panam Percy Paul to
minister to the people along others", he said.
He said the minister has been so chosen because of his unique
acceptability as a gospel artist and the maturity of his music.
The festival, he said, was one of the Church's techniques of spreading
its divine ministration round Lagos, saying that this year's shower of
praise, holding at its Lagos headquarters Mechanic Village, Aswani, off
Osolo Way, Isolo, would begin with an all night praise from 10pm, Friday,
June 13. It would be followed Saturday at 3pm with a seminar to be directed
by Dr. Panam Percy.
The events, he said, will be rounded off with a praise service on
Sunday, June 15, at 8.30 am.
He gave the names of other members of the festival committee to include,
Host Pastor H. Omotayo, Pastor Bolade Maxwell Osazenhen, Choir Master and
Pastor Gabriel Ukpeh, among others, while support songs, he said, will be
rendered by the GMI Mass Choir.
Reverend Omodanisi enjoined men and women with traits of barrenness or
impotency to come and receive their deliverance at the Lagos Headquarters of
the Church every last Saturday of the month.
He said no one was created by God to be barren, but for the devil and
the sinful ways of men, advising that all those with such problems trust in
God.
He spoke about his calling, saying that his vision was to equip the
young with spiritual life and make them live in the ways of God.
He said the only way the vision of the church would be manifested was
for it to invest in human resources development, cum spiritual development,
stating that the Church has already concluded plans to establish Nursery,
Primary and Secondary schools to take care of the educational needs of
Church's pupils. One of the Church's mission, being to train the youth for
an assured future, Omodanisi said the Church would eventually float a
University of Technology.
Reverend Moses Kayode Omodanisi was a senior lecturer at the department
of Mechanical Engineering, Kaduna Polytechnic. A trained engineer, he
obtained his Masters degree in Mechanic Engineering from the University of
Detroit, Michigan State, United States of America (USA).

Original_Search_Title: Volta Region Security Problems Send Minister to Parliament
Headline: Ghana: Volta Region Security Problems Send Minister to Parliament
Body_Text:
The Volta regional minister, Nana Kwasi Owusu- Yeboah, will today appear before Parliament to respond to two urgent questions concerning the region.
The questions which dwell on the security situation in the region, are expected to be asked by the member for Ho West, Francis Aggrey Agbotse, and Dr. Kwame Ampofo for South Dayi.
The former would attempt to quiz the minister on the causes of the numerous conflicts in the region and what the regional security committee (REGSEC) is doing to ensure peace, while the latter would also ask the minister what necessitated the heightened security situation in the Peki-Tsito disputed land area and measures taken to restore peace in the area.
The conflict areas are Nkonya/Alavanyo, Nkwanta, Peki-Tsito and Abutia Kpota/Abutia Agordeke which disturbances did not only claim many precious lives, but also grossly impeded progress and development in those areas.
The region, lying across the Volta Lake, is heterogeneous in nature with over 200 dialects. It stretches from the Gulf of Guinea in the south and joins the Northern region at Damongo, some 400 kilometres away, and is without doubt referred to as the yam basket of Ghana.
The Volta region which used to be called Trans-Volta Togoland (TVT) before independence can boast of the largest man-made lake in the world and has the highest human settlement in the country at Amedzofe standing at 2,800 feet above sea level, which makes it the coolest spot in the world.
That is not all; the region also has the highest fresh waterfall in the country, Wli Falls, as well as the highest mountain, Afadzato, all in the Hohoe district.
Recently, a member of the Ho district assembly, suggested to the government to take over the Peki-Tsito disputed land area for the establishment of a factory or university or military camp to end the conflict.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Hits Abuja Badly
Headline: Nigeria: Inflation Hits Abuja Badly
Body_Text:
Lagos — Beans, one of the staple food in the country, is among the major food
items, whose prices have risen in the last few months.
A News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) survey of major markets in Abuja showed
that the prices of beans, rice, yams, cooking oil and other condiments have
increased following the onset of the rainy season.
A measure of white beans, which sold for N65 in February, now goes for
N85, while brown beans, which sold at N80, now costs N100.
A measure of various brands of rice, such as Golden Caprice, Caprice,
CIC (white rice), "Tomato" rice, which were sold for N120 and N110,
respectively now cost N150 and N140.
A 50 kilogram bag of the brand, which usually sold between N3,800 and
N4,000, now goes for N4,100 and N4,300 respectively.
A measure of locally produced rice, which was sold for N100, now costs
N120, while a 200kg bag of the same rice is now sold for N14,000.
NAN reports that the prices of cooking oil have also increased, a bottle
of palm oil, which was sold at N110, now costs N130, while the same measure
of vegetable oil, which was sold for N100, now costs N140.
Locally produced groundnut oil, which was sold for N120 now sells for
N150, while a 20 litre of cooking oil now costs between N3,200 and N3,400
respectively.
A crate of egg, which was sold for N350, in recent times now sells for
N400.
A kilogram of beef, which was sold for N350, now costs N400.
A measure of flour, which was sold for N70, now costs N80, the 50kg bag,
which was sold for N2,200, now costs N2,700.
A small tuber of "Onitsha" yam, which was sold for N50 now sells for N80
and a medium size of "Pepa" yam, which was sold for N90, now sells for
N140.
NAN however reports that the price of garri has remained stable, with a
measure of "white" and "yellow" garri still selling for N50 and N60

Original_Search_Title: Poverty in the midst of plenty
Headline: Ghana: Poverty in the midst of plenty
Body_Text:
Accra — The market is no playground for HIPC-ridden Ghanaians these days. The cost of every conceivable item is rising and doing so at such a frightening pace that makes one wonder whether inflation, currently listed in the 20s bears any relation to the reality on the ground. I am still recovering from the price shock that nearly knocked the senses out of my mind when I went shopping for local food items at the Kaneshie Market last week.
A tuber of yam cost c20,000, three fingers of Apem are listed at c5,000, and the mother of all shocks, three tomatoes exchanged for c15,000. The cost of pepper could make the stomach cringe. Ask for fish or meat and the price could induce a headache.
Imported goods like clothing, shoes and electrical items, necessary for a peaceful family life, carry a king's ransom.
We are slowly reaching the stage when the housewife would need a basket to carry the local currency to the market. The bad news for minimum wage earners is that there is no indication that the price rise has will abated soon. If anything at all, the lean season has not reached its peak yet. Indications are that there are more price rises on the cards before the harvest season in July and August.
During the harvest season last year, the Minister of Food and Agriculture Courage Courage Quashigah told Ghanaians that his ministry was buying and stock-pilling food to be released into the system in the lean season. What happened to that lofty idea?
If the food bunkers are still full, it is about time the contents are released to ease the burden of Ghanaians.
These are certainly not the best of times for the wage earner. If you are on Â¢9,200 a day, it takes quite some doing to survive the hurricane currently blowing over the market.
If you have problems with the growing number of beggars on the street, visit the market and you would have the answer ready made. A growing number of people are simply unable to afford the basic necessities of life. Begging is the natural course of action to many.
The other day, while driving through the Graphic Road in Accra, I was accosted by a decently dressed lady begging for alms. When I asked why she had reduced herself into a destitute, her answer set me thinking. Mewura, Enye Mea O. Abrabo No Mu Aye Den. Minni Boafo Biara. (Master, it is not I. Life is difficult and I have nobody to fend for me.)
Life in Ghana is not for the faint-hearted. The economic squeeze is biting hard at people's ability to lead honest lives. I was reading one of the private dailies the other day, when my attention was drawn to a very interesting story from Kumasi about a self-styled man of God who was apologizing to authorities at Komfo Anokye Hospital, for claiming that he had spiritually removed a surgical knife abandoned in a woman's stomach by the hospital medical team who had earlier performed an operation on her.
The pastor's claim was false.
But why would a man of God lie about his pastoral duties? The answer is simple. Most of those parading as reverend ministers and performing miracles are fraudulent characters. They have only found loopholes in the Ghanaian's propensity to seek miracles when times are hard.
If false prophets are many, the answer could be traced to the hardship in society and people's crave to avoid it.
The situation is not aided by stories doing the rounds that a number of state institutions have not paid their workers for two or three months. It looks like this society is fast approaching the situation in our next-door neighbours who are sometimes six months in arrears with their wage payments.
Quite recently, the Conference of Heads of Schools threatened to close down their institutions because government was about two terms in arrears of state subsidy to second cycle institutions.
Alarm bells are slowly ringing that revenue is unable to match expenditure and puts all of us on notice to try and find a way out.
What economic indicators so far suggest is that the state has not been able to manage the economy properly. The Government has a huge burden to try and arrest the situation. I will like to be told a bold plan of action to arrest the situation. If revenue does not match expenditure, then we are on the slippery road to economic doom.
I do not believe the best means of arresting the situation is by getting visiting dignitaries come to praise an economy that cannot cater for the citizenry.
I believe the time has come for the government to lay bare the simple facts about the economy. I have read time and again about state revenue collectors like the Internal Revenue Service, the Customs, Excise and Preventive Service, Value Added Tax Secretariat etc, trumpeting to the high heavens their wonderful achievement of exceeding their target collections. If more than necessary are collected, how come we are unable to cater for ourselves, which tells much about our propensity to overspend.
I will like to believe the state could trim down its expenditure, many of which do not cater for projects.
I am aware of the strenuous efforts being made by government to rebuild the run-down infrastructure and which takes chunks of trenches of cash generated from within. But it is also a fact of life that while the strenuous economic strain is taking a toll on the standard of living of the ordinary man, there is a lot of waste that go on unchallenged.
As I write this piece, companies collecting refuse in the city have given notice of their intention to stop collecting refuse in the capital with effect from the night of Friday, May 30, 2003. They have not been paid for a considerable length of time now.
The health hazard posed to the already hard-up Ghanaian cannot be quantified. We are approaching crisis point on all fronts of the national economy.
I have always held the view that the most effective means of solving the riddle is for the Government to take the people on board in its difficult choices. Many Ghanaians will appreciate the need to sacrifice better if they are convinced that the managers of the state economy are sacrificing as well. I still do not appreciate the need for ministers of state to drive around the country with policemen opening and closing their car doors.
The nation will do with a reduction in imported food items, for instance. That is why I am disappointed that under pressure from the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank, the Government is contemplating removing the extra duty imposed on imported poultry and rice, for instance. The reason given of encouraging consumption of local rice and poultry is as valid today as it was first announced in this year's budget.
We need the IMF and the World Bank to bail us out of our difficulties. But I do not buy the idea that they should dictate what is not practicable in societies those officials come from to us to swallow hook and sinker.
I believe too that the time has come for Ghanaians ourselves to change our attitude to state property. We still treat things belonging to the state as if they are not worth preserving. I was alarmed by a story published in an Accra independent daily on Wednesday, in which some unscrupulous characters in and around Keta are putting the Keta Sea Defence Project into jeopardy. According to the story, some sand contractors are winning sand from the project site and thus ruining the $84 million project aimed at rescuing the people from the hazards of the sea.
I will like to believe someone in authority will call the people to order. In the first place, the police in Keta should be empowered to arrest the intruders to be dealt with according to the laws of the land. The subversion of the project should be a crime that should attract the stiffest of penalties.
There are many other areas of national life, where activities of Ghanaians continue to cause serious financial and material loss to this nation. If we are to win the economic war, we have to brace ourselves for very tough decisions. We cannot afford to continue to be hungry and poor, when a few prudent decisions coupled with vigilance could bail us out of our predicament.
In some parts of the world, the state makes it possible for citizens to feed themselves, have roofs over their heads, attend to medical care without selling family wares and have their children attending school. We may not get to this situation overnight. But as the Chinese say, a journey of over a thousand miles begins with the first step. We cannot afford to continue to be hungry in the midst of plenty.

Original_Search_Title: Violence Rocks Ebonyi
Headline: Nigeria: Violence Rocks Ebonyi
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — In Ebonyi State, 17 people die in a communal clash
Sunday Eguji, 28, an Enugu based businessman, never anticipated any
untoward occurrence on 6 May. Sunday had visited his Inyimagu community in
Abakaliki council area of Ebonyi state to attend to his pregnant wife,
Virginia, who was expecting a baby when rampaging youths from neighbouring
Agbaja community murdered him in cold blood.
Trouble started when a minor argument during a burial ceremony of an
indigene of Inyimagu community on 5 May among the youths of both communities
(Inyimagu and Agbaja) snowballed into a bloody clash. In the course of the
argument, 25 year old Friday Nwaji from Agbaja community was given several
machete cuts by the Inyimagu youths.  Friday died on the way to
hospital.
Enraged by the murder of their kinsman, the youths of Agbaja invaded
Inyimagu community, to avenge the killing.
As the vengeance-seeking youths killed, maimed and destroyed lives and
property in the community, Sunday who, incidentally, married from Agbaja,
appealed to them for peace. But his pleas fell on deaf ears.  He was hacked
down by the irate youths. Having slaughtered him like a ram, the Agbaja
youths proceeded to attack members of the Inyimagu community while setting
buildings ablaze.
Hon. Stephen Uguru, Chairman, Inyimagu Unuhu Development Association,
told TheNEWS that more than 145 buildings were razed. Apart Sunday who was
murbered nine indigenes of the community were attacked with machetes and
axes.
Those on casualty list, according to Uguru, are Christopher Ibina,
Chukwuma Uzor Nwali, Chinyere Nwigwe and Pastor Sunday Mgburuma of the
Deeper Life Bible Church in the community. Property vandalized in the
violent attack included a brand-new Gulf car, yam barns, motorcycles and
bicycles among others.
Uguru said his community has been thrown into misery as a result of the
invasion in the course of which, according to him, losses running into
several millions of naira were incurred. He called on the state government
which he said, had immediately intervened to restore peace between the two
communities to come to the aid of over 2, 000 indigenes of his community
rendered homeless following the burning down of their houses.
On his own part, the father of the deceased Friday, Mazi Nwaji said the
youths of Agbaja invaded Inyimagu out of provocation. He said his son was
not involved in the argument for which he was allegedly killed by Inyimagu
youths. When the Inyimagu people, according to Nwaji, could not show remorse
for killing his son, youths of Agbaja, he said, were provoked to retaliate
his death.
The State Police Commissioner, Dr. Udo Udo Ubam, confirmed the incident,
and said that the police had moved into the troubled areas to forestall
further escalation of the crisis.
Similar violent clashes, also, occurred in Afikpo North council area of
the state. The clash was caused by a renewed land dispute between two
neighbouring communities, Nguzu and Ekoli. The two communities had been
contesting ownership of a plot of land called Ama Nguzu for over 20
years. The Nguzu people tilled the land in the current farming season. In
protest, some youths from Ekoli went and destroyed the crops, and
apprehended two Nguzu boys on 7 May, took them to their traditional ruler,
Ezeogo Okorie Akanu, who immediately ordered the release of the boy.
This magazine was told that on getting home, the humiliated Nguzu boys
narated their ordeals in the custody of their Ekoli captors.  Their story
incensed youths in the area, and they mounted road blocks at
Nguzu-Abiriba-Ohafia junction, leading into Ekoli. The Nguzu youths at the
road blocks reportedly molested and dehumanized Ekoli people who were
travelling home.
Even the traditional ruler of the neighbouring Umunna autonomous
community, Ezeogo Alum Udensi, who was conveying home the corpse of his
relative who died in Aba was said to have been forced back with the
corpse.
Angered by these Ekoli youths mobilized to dismantle the road blocks.
In the process, the Nguzu youths opened fire on them (Ekoli youths), killing
four of them including Idika Aku, erstwhile chairmanship aspirant on the
platform of the All Nigeria People's Party, ANPP, in the local government
area.
An Ekoli youth leader, Ajunwa Ndukwe, at press time last week was still
missing. Nguzu youths were alleged to have seized the corpses of those
killed. A detachment of police despatched to the area, as at last week,
recovered the headless body of one of the murdered Ekoli youths.
Although the remote cause of the violence was traced to the age long
land dispute between the two communities, many say that politics is the
immediate cause. A community leader in the area, Paul Okorie, told TheNEWS
that Nguzu youths attacked Ekoli people following what they believed was a
betrayal of a political pact reached between the two communities.
In the build up to the general elections in the country, some power
brokers in the two communities met and reached an understanding for power
sharing. In the pact, the chairmanship of the council area was zoned to
Ekoli while the membership of the State Assembly was zoned to Nguzu. But,
the People's Democratic Party (PDP) picked the two candidates from Ekoli,
leaving out Nguzu. In the State Assembly election on 3 May, a PDP candidate
and incumbent state legislator, Hon.
Ugorji Amaoti, was declared winner. Having lost hope of clinching either
of the posts, the State Assembly or Council Chairman, the Nguzu people were
said to have viewed it as a betrayal, and felt that they had been schemed
out hence the bloody attack on Ekoli people.
Although the police patrolled the trouble spots on a daily basis, the
area has been under tension following threats of a reprisal attack by Ekoli
people.
Uwana Afikpo South council area of the state, is not spared the orgy of
communal violence sweeping through Ebonyi state. A clash between supporters
of the PDP and the youths of the community on 3 May, resulted in the burning
down of 17 houses wherein over 500 persons have been rendered homeless.
TheNEWS gathered that on the day of the Assembly election, the people of
the area waited in vain at the various polling centres for the electoral
materials to enable them participate in the election. When the youths of the
area got a hint that the materials were in a top politician's house where
thumb printing of ballot papers was allegedly going on. They saw it as an
attempt to disenfranchise them and trooped to the politician's house to
demand that the electoral materials be taken to the polling booth so that
every eligible voter would participate.
Earlier on 29 April, Umeh Igbudu in Ikwo council area of the state was,
also, engulfed by violence which claimed the lives of nine persons including
Nweke Ogodo; Nwali Umanyi; Monday Itumo; John Alieze; Nweke Nome; Daniel
Nwancho; Paul Edeh; Jonathan Alobu and Simeon Ugama.
During the bloody clash involving members of the All Nigeria People's
Party, ANPP, and PDP in the area, many buildings were said to have been
burnt down wherein many indigenes of the area were also rendered
homeless.
Both the state government and the police have been working round the
clock to stem the high wave of inter and intra communal hostilities in the
state. It is, however, feared that unless adequate security is provided in
the affected areas, the pockets of crisis in the state may assume a more
tragic and holistic dimension.

Original_Search_Title: Police Warn On Circulation of Fake N500 Notes
Headline: Nigeria: Police Warn On Circulation of Fake N500 Notes
Body_Text:
The Nigerian Police over the weekend warned residents of the Federal Capital Territory of the circulation of fake N500 notes in the city, advising them to be vigilant in transactions involving exchange of N500 notes.
According to a statement from force headquarters, the police has discovered that some residents mostly in Karmo, Idu and Gwagwa have been alleged to be in possession of large sums of fake N500 notes.
The police stated that such notes were often mixed with genuine N500 notes during transaction especially for buying of fuel and GSM recharge cards.
It therefore warned fuel attendants and GSM recharge card dealers to be wary of such notes and ensure that such fake notes are do not circulate.
The statement asked patriotic Nigerians to report suspects involved in the illegal dealing to the police.
The police assured the residents that it was working hard to get those involved.
The police warning is coming on the heels of complaint over fake notes in circulation resulting in fist-cuffs in some cases between passengers and conductors.
Our investigations revealed that, following the alarm, some residents have refused, outright, to transact business involving N500 notes.
"Like some of these women selling yams, they don't accept the notes if they are to give you some balance. Except you buy exactly N500 worth or N1000 naira, they will not collect the notes" a resident told Abuja Trust.
According to him, the people are aware that such people using the fake notes buy goods worth N50 to N100 and pay with a N500 note expecting to get genuine money worth N450 or N400 as the case may be.
Abuja Trust spoke with some conductors who complained that the development has often resulted in argument between them and their passengers.
"The other day somebody boarded a bus from Idu to Karmo which is just N20 and gave men a N500 note. I told him that he would have asked me if I had change before entering. He started arguing only for us to discover that the money was fake," a conductor told Abuja Trust.
According to him, most of the conductors can distinguish between a fake note and genuine one.

Original_Search_Title: N26m Property Destroyed in Communal Clash
Headline: Nigeria: N26m Property Destroyed in Communal Clash
Body_Text:
Lagos — The clash between Inyimagu and Agbaja communities in Izzi clan of Ebonyi State has left at least 931 persons homeless and over 170 houses burnt with yam barns and other property valued at about N26.6 million.
This was disclosed by the director of Ebonyi State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA), Mr. Hyacinth Ote. Ote spoke with newsmen against the backdrop of his agency's involvement in rehabilitating the victims of the crisis.
The SEMA boss disclosed that only two lives were lost and about 10 injured victims still hospitalised, adding that calm had since returned to the feuding communities following police intervention and that of elders of the two communities.
Ote said that SEMA which was recently inaugurated in the state is facing its first test with crisis, stating that the agency has so far made detailed documentation of the destruction in the area in addition to individual presentation of losses filed by the victims.
He disclosed that relief materials would be supplied to the victims by the Federal Government through the National Emergency Management Agency NEMA as well as from the Ebonyi State government.

Original_Search_Title: Lack of Processing Facilities, Pests, Diseases Hamper Food Security - Aidoo
Headline: Ghana: Lack of Processing Facilities, Pests, Diseases Hamper Food Security - Aidoo
Body_Text:
Accra — The Western Regional Minister, Mr. Joseph Aidoo has said the lack of
processing facilities coupled with the high incidence of pests and diseases,
have been identified as major problems of food security in the country.
His views were conveyed at the commencement of a two-day forum for major
players in the agriculture industry, organised by the Ministry of Food and
Agriculture (MOFA) in collaboration with Care International and the German
Technical Co-operation (GTZ) at Takoradi. "Agricultural extension efforts
need to respond to the needs of the poor and socially disadvantaged farmers
and link them up with research institutions," he said.
Mr. Aidoo said though the government's new objectives of agriculture
extension were meant to ensure equity in the distribution of benefits,
improve rural livelihood and reduce poverty among women, youth and the
physically challenged, no positive efforts had been made to improve the
lives of the marginalized.
He cited hunger, malnutrition and under-nutrition as prevalent in
several communities, adding that "the multiple root causes of hunger and
addressing them are far more complex than ensuring adequate food
production.
The recorded extent of hunger are closely linked to poverty and
therefore poverty and hunger reduction, should be taken seriously, he
added.
Mr. Aidoo expressed regret that rural-urban exodus among the youth had
greatly affected poverty levels in the rural areas and led to the lack of
access to housing, healthcare, good drinking water and inadequate food
production. The situation has been compounded by the lack of electricity,
telecommunications, poor road network and prohibitive transportation costs,
he added.
Mr. Aidoo suggested that MOFA should make agriculture, not only
attractive but also to ensure the planting of modern high yielding
crops.
Mr. Emmanuel Botchway, Western Regional Director of the MOFA expressed
regret that though the Western Region had large tracts of forestlands and
crop species, the region continually registered deficits in maize, rice,
yam, cassava and cocoyam production.
Mr. Botchway said the livestock sub-sector and fish farming, in the
region performed poorly, while yields in cocoa, rubber, oil palm and coconut
were very low.
He said though the region's performance was not encouraging, efforts
were being made to improve rural poverty-reduction opportunities in several
communities.
Mr. Botchway called on stakeholders in the agricultural industry to give
priority to effective and efficient output marketing systems, promotion of
value added agricultural commodities, efficient packaging, pricing, quality
control and standardization.

Original_Search_Title: UN Awards Journalists And Media Institutions for Information Development in Africa
Headline: Africa: UN Awards Journalists And Media Institutions for Information Development in Africa
Body_Text:
New York — The United Nations Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) and its partners today presented Awards to prominent media practitioners and organizations that have made significant contributions to the development of information and communications technologies (ICTs) in Africa.
An initiative of ECA, the African Information Society Initiative (AISI) Media Awards aim to promote journalism which contributes to a better understanding of the information society in Africa. They are given to individual journalists and media institutions based in the continent.
ECA said the sustainable build up of an information society in African countries requires the development of information resources to reflect the needs of each and every sector and stakeholder. The information society also requires that knowledge be disseminated and used by stakeholders, the public at large and disenfranchised groups such as women and the poor to make rational choices and exercise human rights.
Internationally recognized African journalists, media researchers, and experts on ICT for Development issues selected this year's winners: ICT Focus from Ethiopia, Radio Afrique Espoir from Benin, Association Yam Pukri from Burkina Faso, Realities Magazine from Tunisia, Women of Uganda Network, African Languages Technology from Nigeria, Etienne Tasse from Cameroon, Salif Sanogo from Mali, Takawira Musara from Zimbabwe, IT and Telecom Digest from Nigeria, Bianca Wright from South Africa and Brenda Zulu from Zambia.
The Open Society Initiative for Southern Africa (OSISA), the Open Society Initiative for Western Africa (OSIWA), the International Development Research Centre (IDRC) and the International Institute for Communication and Development (IICD) sponsor the awards.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Sellers Clamour for Shops At Proposed Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Yam Sellers Clamour for Shops At Proposed Markets
Body_Text:
The Chairman of the Yam Sellers Association at the New Central Market (Bakassi), Alhaji Suleiman Abubakar has appealed to the authorities entrusted with the allocation of shops at the proposed Wuye and Garki markets to include his members.
The chairman made the appeal in a chat with Abuja Trust expressing his dissatisfaction with the allocations in which none of his members were included in the ongoing distribution of stalls at the proposed markets.
According to him, despite the immense contributions his members made to the growth and distribution of the present market, they are still discriminated against in many facets of the market activities.
Alhaji Suleiman cited an instance by saying, "it is unfortunate that we are treated as second class citizens. Recently, butchers in this market were provided with a loan to support and improve them their financial base, but none of my members has gotten the loan."
Alhaji Suleiman said the authorities should realize the significance of their trade adding that yam is the most patronized item in the whole of new market and as such they should be considered in allocation of market stalls.

Original_Search_Title: Beyond Election Malpractices And Irregularities
Headline: Nigeria: Beyond Election Malpractices And Irregularities
Body_Text:
Lagos — One Yoruba proverbial saying is that, if you do not because of yam take oil, you must because of oil take yam. The relationship between yam and oil is such that it cannot be strained without having some negative effects for both sides.
In the same vein, the application of the Yoruba idiom to the current environmental conditionings of Nigeria's 2003 general elections clearly shows the organic relationship between election malpractices and irregularities, on the one hand, and national cohesion and security, on the other. However, the discussion of the 2003 elections has not underscored the need for national unity, security and development. This is most unfortunate.
Some international election monitors, as well as some defeated candidates have complained that the elections have so far been characterized by electoral malpractices and electoral irregularities. As rightly pointed out by Prof. Ben Nwabueze last week Thursday (The Guardian, May 8, p.8), "electoral practices are to be distinguished from mere electoral irregularities, which relate to noncompliance with prescribed procedure at election, like late commencement of polling, polling outside the statutorily stipulated time, late delivery of electoral materials. Electoral malpractices and electoral irregularities differ not only in their nature but also in their legal consequences; whilst the latter do not in general, invalidate an election, the former emphatically does".
Perhaps more importantly, Prof. Nwabueze said election rigging is a subversion of the constitution, it is treason, robbery of the right of the people to participate in their own government, and the "gravest offence that can be committed against the constitution and people of Nigeria". Consequently, it is a "mocking irony, that a call should have been made for the arrest for treason, not of those guilty of the massive rigging being complained of but of those making the complaint and calling on the people to protest against it".
In other words, there is nothing wrong with complaining about electoral malpractices and irregularities. I cannot agree more here. However, I have problems with the manner of the complaint, with the way it is expressed and with the intended effects. The chief complainant, General Muhammadu Buhari (rtd), threatened national cohesion by calling for "mass action" and by asking the international community to derecognise the Obasanjo government come May 29, 2003.
Without any scintilla of doubt, the constitutional method of complaint in the context of election disputes is to seek redress in the court. By asking the international community not to relate with the Government of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, the ANPP flag bearer, General Buhari, has ab initio assumed that the court would rule in his favour. There is nothing to suggest that the variables being considered by General Buhari are those that will retain the attention of the learned men of the court. Men of the legal profession are indeed learned because they create new bases for the adoption of new laws by interpreting laws in unexpected ways and loopholes in the interpretation and application of laws in such a way that complainants also become the accused or respondents.
Besides, most of the countries being called upon by General Buhari have a policy of not recognizing regimes or governments but states. Although in international practice, a nonrecognized government may not be able to enter into official relations with the Government withholding recognition, there are several factors explaining why the international community would not want to derecognise the Obasanjo government.
Chief Olusegun Obasanjo is an accomplished international player and a seriously needed ally in the implementation of the principle of "responsibility to protect" in international relations. His experience as a military man and as a statesman is needed in the maintenance of regional security. No matter the way electoral malpractices and irregularities is perceived, the need to protect some supranational interests, especially in terms of global peace will always make the international community to condone whatever electoral excesses we may be having in Nigeria. In fact, Europe and America are the international headquarters of election rigging. The only point of difference, when compared with that of Nigeria, is that election rigging in developed countries is very scientific and devoid of violence. Rigging is always fair and not excessive there.
There is also the factor of individual personality. General Buhari did not have good image internationally when he was Head of State. His foreign policy was confrontational and that was why he had diplomatic rows with several countries. Even when he engaged in a verbal war with Britain over Umaru Dikko when he was created as a diplomatic baggage, Margaret Thatcher, then Prime Minister, simply intimidated.
General Buhari by threatening to uncover all the names of Nigerian leaders, especially the military, having questionable accounts in Britain, if General Buhari did not promptly stop his "bullying". That ended the story.
In fact, Europe and America are not at all interested in a disorderly Nigeria. Geopolitically, Nigeria is very important to them in subSaharan Africa. Nigeria plays host to their economic investments. In their thinking, a friendly Nigerian leader is what is needed. If Europeans and Americans were asked to make a choice between Obasanjo and Buhari, the choice cannot but be clear: Obasanjo. This is why, in spite of the electoral complaints and the recourse to election tribunals, world leaders have already taken their final decision, which is to recognize the reelection of President Obasanjo and to give the election international legitimacy by congratulating him and pouring encomiums on the people of Nigeria for generally eschewing violence during the election.
It is also important to put the understanding of malpractice in election and irregularity in implementation of the Electoral Act No.4 of 2002 in the general context of election petitions. First, Part III of Chapter VII of the 1999 Constitution provides for the establishment of Election Tribunals with original mandate to determine petitions brought before them and to the exclusion of any other court. The responsibility of the election tribunals is to determine the validity of an election into the National and State House of Assembly (Section 285). As regards the determination of the validity of the election of a president or his vice, Section 239 gives the jurisdictional competence to the Court of Appeal.
In this regard, Section 134(1) of the Electoral Act No.4 of 2002 provides thus: "an election may be questioned on any of the following grounds, that is to say (a) that a person whose election is questioned was at the time of the election, not qualified to contest the elections; (b) that the election was invalid by reasons of corrupt practices or noncompliance with the provisions of this Act; (c) that the respondent was not duly elected by majority of lawful votes cast at the elections; and (d) that the petitioner or its candidate was validly nominated but was unlawfully excluded from the election".
The main problem here, as noted by Babatunde Fashanu, a Senior Advocate of Nigeria, is that the non qualification of candidates to contest elections has been the main object of dispute in the courts and that the courts have been "emphatic in holding that election tribunals or courts have no jurisdiction to delve into aspects relating to nomination of candidates as electoral body's decision is final on this". Thus, what is the proof that complaints would be admissible for determination by the courts? On malpractice how easy will it be to prove? Can it also be done quickly?
That election monitors and defeated candidates have complained that the elections were marred by malpractices and irregularities constitute only one side of the equation. The other side, which is even more important, is the implication of the complaint, the way it is being expressed, for national unity and security. Put differently, a school of thought advocates "mass action" and "violence". This school posits that Nigeria be made ungovernable come May 29, 2003. Will a new foundation for the destabilization and disintegration of the country not be laid with the adoption of a violent approach to alleged election rigging?
Another school of thought has suggested the establishment of a transition government while a third school has called for a government of national unity. Will the establishment of a national government or a transition government change political attitudes in Nigeria? Are politicians truly committed to a united, virile and developed Nigeria?
In conclusion therefore, there is need for greater caution in the handling of election petitions June 12 1993 election was adjudged the most free and fair in Nigeria's political history, yet the results were annulled. The 2003 elections were more than 50% free and fair, some Nigerians are not only call for the outright cancellation of the election results but also the dismissal of returning officers and the reconstitution of the INEC as conditions for peace in Nigeria. This is a wrong approach to the problem. Rather than destroying Nigeria, defeated and aggrieved politicians should be strategizing now on how to prevent future occurrence of election rigging in Nigeria. They will become better politicians by so doing.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Reconciles Warring Communities in Ebonyi
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Reconciles Warring Communities in Ebonyi
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Ebonyi State government has sent a high powered delegation to reconcile the warring communities of Inyimagu and Agbaja in Izzi clan, Ebonyi,North Zone, where many lives were lost, hundreds of houses burnt, yam barns and other farm produce destroyed in a clash that lasted for three days last week.
The delegation met with Ndi Uke, the decision making body made up of the elders of the clan, who had already initiated a reconciliation procces at Amegu, the ancestral name of Izzi people.
Speaking at the occassion, Senator-elect for Ebonyi, North constituency who hails from the area, Chief Chris Nshii expressed shock at the level of damage and killings a nd noted that in one of the communities more than 70 houses were torched together with several yam bans thereby rendering the victims totally improverished.
He praised Ndi Uke for initiating the peace process between the communities and thanked the state government for its quick intervention in the crisis.
Cheif Nshi attributed the clash to cultism and revealed that cuel groups from the two communities had engaged in similar clashes in the past and the efforts to reconcile them before now had come to naught and therefore charged the federal government to do anything possible to end cultism in the country.
Also, the secretary to the state government, Dr. Icha Ituma who was the leader of the delegation expressed sympathy for those who lost their lives and property in the crisis and promised that the state government would compensate them and urged the victims of the clash to be calm and expect the support and assistance from government.
Ucha said that peace must be enthroned among them because that was the first condition for progress and asked the youths to adhere to the advice of the elders, knowing that they were the future leaders of the land.
State Emergency Relief Agency has been establsihed by government to compensate all those who lost their property in the fracas.

Original_Search_Title: Weekend Extra:- Arebamen: My Sad Journey Into Police
Headline: Nigeria: Weekend Extra:- Arebamen: My Sad Journey Into Police
Body_Text:
LAGOS State Commissioner of Police, Mr Young Arebamen, is an extra- ordinary man. He is so in the sense that where other mortals at the commanding heights of power like him are easily predictable, Arebamen is not.
He is self-effacing, devoid of any official and elitist arrogance and affectation. It has become part of him. In the face of pressure, crises,, danger or even during extreme provocation however, one is certainly going to find Arebamen doing one thing - smiling. This aspect of his character is unique and puts anyone, subordinates, superiors, family members, friends and worried members of the public who come in contact with him at ease. He explained it this way: "I am naturally a philosophical person. I hardly get bothered by things. I believe strongly that in this life, if you worry, you die and if you don't you die still. So why worry your life?"
Arebamen continued: "In all my life, I believe that God is in control of everything that occurs in the lives of men. It has become my training that rather than worry about problems, design solutions instantly to them. It will help in stabilizing the individual". And among his officers and men at the Lagos State Police Command, Ikeja- Lagos where he is the boss, the Arebamen formula is fast gaining currency.
At the command headquarters, a new work culture and ethics are in place since the arrival early last year of "Mwalimu" (the teacher, Arebamen). To many officers and men, they say it has brought efficiency and dedication into the police system in the entire Lagos State in the last one and a half years. Certain factors did the magic. The police boss, on assumption of office, set in motion the machinery for training, re-training and re-orientation of his men. The result was that, ignorance gave way to knowledge, and disillusionment yielded space to hope. The new policy of welfare revived the sagging morale of officers and the rank and file.
It is not unusual to find the police boss behind his desk in the office as late as 3 am, treating files, rummaging through papers, mapping out strategies on how to police Lagos effectively or attending to visitors who could wait to see him. And when Arebamen leaves office by 4 am, he is back three or four hours later at 8 am. "I don't think there is anything really to worry about", he says. "There is so much to be done here (in the office) and you just can't leave it like that. I don't like keeping untreated files on my table because it doesn't help the efficiency of the system. People come here to see me all the time and I don't turn them away. I make sure I see everybody because they have one problem or the other. To some of them, seeing one alone brings some kind of psychological relief to them. So, I try to see everybody no matter how small that person is. I have to do that because that is why I am here. I have no other job except this police work".
On police/public relationship
When he assumed duty about two years ago as the Lagos State Police Commissioner from Zamfara state, fate and circumstance conspired against Young Arebamen to distract him from settling down to his new assignment. His command was under public fire over a few incidents of accidental killing of innocent citizens in Lagos by check point police men. Arebamen's professional skill was challenged. Tension and public angst (anger) against his policemen were at its peak. Young simply deployed his public relation skills, reaching to the angry and uncooperative public and the hostile media. He also reached out to the bereaved families, did a few other moves and within days, he had won the confidence, cooperation and admiration of all. How did Arebamen triumph over incidents that would have exposed the inabilities of most police chief? "I think it is a question of understanding and sympathizing with the people", he quips "The public was outraged by what happened and even if they were wrong and unfair in their reactions, the fact that some people lost their lives during that time is quite painful. Our situation was worse because the people were already too hostile to the police even before the incidents and most policemen were equally angry at this development. So, it was a dicey situation that needed divine intervention and professional tact and strategy. That was how I survived it".
