Arebamen states that the wrong perception of the police by the public is seriously detrimental to improved police/public relationship. Such mind set, he argues, weaken the prospects of understanding and cooperation between the two parties. Said he: "People do forget that the police cannot be an island onto itself; that he is still a part of that society that castigates him often. Nobody was born a policeman. We all came into this job (police) just like any other profession. Sometimes, I think that if the policeman is bad, it means that there is something fundamentally wrong about that society which produced the policeman. Therefore, it will be in the interest of the society that the policeman is seen in a better light. Of all organizations in this society, it is only the police that periodically conducts a self screening exercise and punishes its members who have misconducted themselves.
"There is no other profession that does that . I think that this is commendable. And the policeman who is out there in the sun and in the rain policing lives and properties when other men are in the comfort of the home certainly should be appreciated. For the public comfort and safety, they give their time, their own safety and comfort. You also find out that most members of the public don't obey the laws and they want the policeman to pervert justice for his sake only to come round and be criticized by the same person he has done favour at the risk of his job. So I think it is about time we begin to appreciate the policeman.
2003 polls
As the number one police commissioner in Nigeria owing to the status of Lagos, much was expected of Arebamen during the last general elections. His command had the prospect of engendering violence owing to the concentration of political interests. He rallied politicians, security forces in the state and the electoral officials. Daily, he held unending summits with his officers and men and various groups on how to ensure adequate peace and security during the polls. He deployed internal logistics and organised workshops and seminars in this regard. At the end of it all, the initiative seemed to have paid off. But Arebamen is particularly unhappy that that achievement is about to be vitiated by politicians who are unsettled about the outcome of the polls. This has created political impasse in the polity. "In my own thinking, these elections went well. They were successful even beyond my initial calculations. I would advise that nobody should do anything to hurt this peace. Democracy and violence should not go together. In a democratic set up, people should be allowed to have view-points. In a situation where things are forced down others is not acceptable. Democracy provides that there will be conflicts and in our laws, certain rules and regulations are put in place to iron out such conflicts.
"Our politicians should realise that there cannot be two winners. But there might be four or five losers depending on the turn of things. It takes maturity for the loser to congratulate the winner in the hope that next time, the electorate will consider him. But you find out here that anybody who loses the election wants to pull down the house. When the house is destroyed, there will be no place even for the destroyer to stay. I think we should learn to be good sportsmen and good politicians".
How he joined police
Prior to 1969 when he decided to join the Nigerian Police as a cadet inspector and trained for one year at the Police College, Ikeja, Lagos, Young Arebamen, a native of Irrua in Esan, Edo State was a bank clerk with the National Bank. He made up his mind to quit the bank when he was having problems on regular basis balancing his account figures. This exercise, most times, lasted far into the night. On one of those days, Young and his colleague closed late for work and were heading home when they fell into a deep ditch. He sustained severe injuries which made him resolve to quit the bank.
On graduating as a cadet inspector, he was immediately posted to the then East Central State (now South East) to Nsukka where he was a police D.P.O. (Divisional Police Officer). He was there until the famous 1978
"Ali Must Go" student crisis broke out at the University of Nigeria, Nsukka (UNN). He was later transferred to Ogidi in Anambra State also as DPO. From then to Force CID Alagbon where he served in the S-Squad, arresting policemen who engaged in unprofessional conduct. Later, he was moved to Benin from where he was transferred to Sapele in Delta State.
He was made Police Area Commander Ughelli in Delta Central. He was to move from there to Akwa-Ibom as Officer-in-charge of state CID. It wasn't too long, he went back to Staff College from where he moved to Lagos as Area Commander of Area "F" Ikeja until 1997 when he became the police force public relations officer (Force PP.R.O.). He held the position until year 2000 when he was posted to CID Panti, Yaba from where he moved to state anti-Riot Squad (SARS) Adeniji Adele Lagos. He was there until he was transferred to Zamfara state as Police Commissioner from where he came down to Lagos as Police boss. When he was at the force CID Alagbon in 1986 Arebamen had to go to the University of Lagos where he read management course and bagged his B.Sc degree. He had capped it off with numerous police courses.
Childhood background
Arebamen, though was born in the small village called Ijawuri in Irrua Esan, Edo State but he didn't grow up there. He was raised in Ibadan, Oyo State where he attended More Plantation Primary School CKC. He then proceeded to premier Grammar School in Abeokuta. He later moved to Our Lady's College in Auchi, Edo State where he finished before joining the National Bank in Benin City. From here he joined the police force in 1969. While at Ibadan, Arebamen lived with an uncle who was a chief Inspector in the police but now retired in the village. As a young lad, Arebamen was shaped by his strong Christian background as a Catholic. This aspect of his religious inclinations still remains with him today. It has also helped him in checking his tempers. As a little boy, he was quiet and level headed but went through some difficult upbringing. The top cop in Lagos didn't grow up with his parents and therefore did not enjoy certain privileges and opportunities available to children who were raised by their parents. He was subjected to unpleasant experiences by his foster parents then. The evidence of this unpleasant childhood was a deep, scar etched on his left hand which still lives with him till today. With palpable emotions, he showed the reporter the injury mark. "This is a mark that is going to remain with me as long as I live", says Arebamen. The cause of the injury was that as a little boy living with his foster parents, he broke a tea cup in error and the broken ware was used in cutting his hand.
"I ended up in the hospital with four stitches", he says. "That tells you what I went through". But as a kid, Young knew the value of personal industry. He owned a large farm house where he grew his own crops like yams, corns, etc. "It was a spare government land that I started making use of. After school, I usually went in to tend my farm crops and I felt satisfied when I saw my crops grow well. There are a lot of miracles on earth".
Achievements
Since assuming office about two years ago, Young Arebamen has recorded visible achievements as Lagos state Commissioner of Police. For instance, he says that crime rate has dropped drastically, essentially through the activities of Operation Fire for Fire coupled with other measures he has put in place. Also, about 1,034 stolen vehicles have been recovered with an average of 115 on monthly basis. Abut 4,841 arms and ammunitions have also been recovered. The relation between the police in his command and the Lagos public and also with the press has tremendously improved under his tenure. This is apart from the Police Public Complaints Bureau (PPCB) which he set up. Also Arebamen has sponsored so many interactive and educative workshops and seminars aimed at improving his command's efficiency and police-public understanding and cooperation. "I gave praise to my family members, especially my wife for holding the home front well. The truth is that it makes no sense to outline certain feats when there is always work to do. But my dream is to leave Lagos better than I met it."

Original_Search_Title: Lesotho Host Regional Military Netball Champs
Headline: Lesotho Host Regional Military Netball Champs
Body_Text:
Maseru — The Lesotho Defence Force (LDF) host the second International Military Sports Council (CISM)/ East South Africa Liaison Office (ESALO) Netball Regional Military Championship games ending on the 9th May 2003.
Speaking at the opening ceremony, LDF Chief of Staff, Major General Sam Makoro mentioned that the commanders of the forces should commit their good will for sporting activities so that the duty of the player should match their responsibility as sportsmen. He stated that the commanders should also strive to make games genuinely regional in terms of countries participating in the tournaments.
According to the LetÂolo Kholoane, the Vice President Lesotho Military Sports Council, the purpose of the championship is to foster military friendship through sports in the international forces. He said the success of the games would depend on the commitment of the individuals, organising committee, score keepers, team managers and empires by providing a conducive environment for competitors.
He mentioned that CISM/ESALO should be used as a tool to promote peace, happiness, physical fitness, and cooperation among the military regionally, continental and global thus illuminating suspicions and mistrust within the army.
"We Lesotho Military Sports Council are dedicated to sporting activities, not only within the LDF but also by taking part in the nationally organized sports activities with our Military Athletes," he said.
The CISM-ISALO Representative Lieutenant Colonel Yam Steinbache said Netball provides opportunity for entertainment and unity among sports personalities and the forces and teamwork within the army. He said the high level of sports could prevail within the forces only if the commanders could commit themselves to sports by taking part, and encouraging sportsman.
He said the Lesotho Netball Championship games should be taken as a unifying factor for future generations and the international forces. He mentioned that sports recruits highly disciplined people within the army and their communities.
The Botswana Defense Force cancelled its participation in the challenge. Lesotho plays against Republic of South Africa in the opening game.

Original_Search_Title: She Has Turned Her Garage Into a Food Processing Plant
Headline: Uganda: She Has Turned Her Garage Into a Food Processing Plant
Body_Text:
Kampala — At 64, Angelica Binagwa Kadoma (also commonly called Kaaka, which means granny) has decided not to let old age control her life.
She will not sit back and contemplate on her successes and failures. Instead, she has decided to use the knowledge she acquired before the age of 10 to keep her life moving.
This is what gives her some income and also keeps her mind active.
Kadoma knows only too well that redundancy can cause the mind to meander. Yet, anyone could easily think that this proud mother of five graduates and grandmother of six would sit back and relax.
"Instead of focussing on my failures and successes, I thought I could do something," says this woman who separated with her husband, "a few years ago."
This could have easily paved way for her to probably think of her marriage as a failure, but no. They raised their children together and for the biggest part of her life, Kadoma was her husband's business partner in a textile enterprise.
She had the business skills and this was a plus to what she thought of as a unique idea.
"I thought, why not improve the millet? The young generation seems to have forgotten all about our traditional foods," she says.
She boasts of her millet flour being free from stones and dust, a quality she has achieved because the labour is hired from her village.
Kadoma believes the best people to process our local foodstuffs are not necessarily the schooled, but those who have grown up in a particular food environment.
"They know how to remove the stones and when we are mixing the millet with cassava flour, we don't buy dry cassava. We buy the fresh cassava, wash it and dry it ourselves. That way, you are sure of the hygiene," explains Kadoma.
When she started her business on a small time basis in 1995, people appreciated the quality of her processed food. She then decided to go full swing into processing Ugandan foods.
"I started with foods from my district, and later on began preserving those from other districts," says the woman who is now actively preserving and venturing into many other foodstuffs.
In the heart of Kabalagala, a Kampala suburb, is Kadoma's shop, Komukama Food Products.
In this shop, you will find lots of preserved foods. Apart from the usual millet flour, rice flour, cassava flour, soya flour or a blending of any of these, Kadoma has crafted the art of preserving vegetables and legumes.
She has lots of preserved greens (including ggobe and yam leaves), dried vegetables and French beans.
Kadoma has also ventured into instant foods like instant maize flour for porridge, instant bean sauce and soya milk.
In Kadoma's shop, you will also get home-made salt from bean covers and banana peelings.
Whatever you buy from Kamukama Food Processors, Kadoma provides directions for preparation.
Kadoma is no nutritionist or trained caterer. She says: " I have no special training - I am just putting into practice what my grandmother taught me. My grandmother used to preserve ggobe, pumpkins leaves and even cabbage."
"I just learnt a bit of domestic science, child-care and nutrition. I was working as a social worker, and my job was to teach the community what to eat and how to eat. This was in Toro and Bunyoro. When I left Trinity College, Nabbingo, I worked for three years as a social worker in Toro and that is where I had some training in nutrition," she explains.
Her zeal into this food-processing field was escalated when she visited her children in Europe, the US and India. While abroad, she realised how much yearning people had for their local food. It struck her as something she could do.
Her efforts particularly paid off when she attended the Lugogo trade show. People encouraged her and she has since began marketing her products in a few supermarkets in Kampala and its suburbs.
At her home in Muyenga, her garage has turned into a production room for processed foods. If Gatsby was to visit this ageing woman, they would certainly be impressed.
Kadoma is using technology developed by these people. Inside her garage, there is not much production activity going on, although she has a few bags of millet grain, dried greens and dry beans lined up. Kadoma says her sales dictate when she goes into production and how much to produce.
She has a local dryer which uses the sun. Once there is no sun, there is a provision for a charcoal stove to provide the heat. She also has a cassava slicer and an electric roaster, machinery that eases her work.
Born to the late Stephen Baguma and Miria Matama, Kadoma is the fifth of nine children. When her younger sibling was born, Kadoma began fighting over who should be occupy her mother's laps, and this made her mother send her to her (Kadoma's) granny's home at the age of three.
At nine years of age, Kadoma's father could not stand a spoilt daughter. He decided to get back Kadoma, so that he could discipline her.
For her sweat, Kadoma has tried to get small loans from the women's bank, Cairo bank, but she hates the tension of looking for the money to repay loans. "You are always worried. I wish I could get funding," she says.
She would like to see her business grow bigger. "That is why I always move with my cousin, Angela Kantu. I want her to grow with the business and know how the business runs."
The biggest challenge to her business, says Alfred Agaba, Kadoma's marketing manager is: "Competition. Many people find our prices high compared to other similar foods on the market."
In her free time Kadoma loves to read the Bible. "I cannot say that I am saved, but I am on the way to salvation. I read the Bible and meditate upon the Lord during my free time," she says.

Original_Search_Title: I Knew Lawal Was Doomed-Saraki
Headline: Nigeria: I Knew Lawal Was Doomed-Saraki
Body_Text:
Dr Olusola Saraki, how do you feel now that your son has been elected
governor of the state? Do you feel fulfilled? Do you think you have
succeeded in demonstrating that you control the political direction of the
state?
It's God that controls the destiny of men that made my dream come
true. My dream is to remove the monster that we have put unknowingly. I
thank God that he has helped me to remove him. I also thank all the people
of Kwara State for being understanding. I've never seen this in the history
of Kwara State. Kwara came into existence in 1967. I'm a man of history like
I used to tell you. I love to learn from history, because I want to make
history, I don't want to repeat history. In a modern age one never expects a
man to be crude to the extent of using dangerous weapons to deal with people
who voted him into office. This is somebody who was never a politician, he
was never known, but I've taken it as a test from God, just to test one's
belief in him. I knew there was no way he could win with gun. You can only
win all the time if you are honest and faithful.
How would you describe Gov. Lawal's tenure and was there a time any
of his action drew tears from your eyes?
Yes, it did. It was when I agreed that Bukola should contest. I knew
Lawal can never win because no political leader that did what he did ever
succeeded. So I shed tears openly and within myself when I agreed that
Bukola should go and contest. It was never my intention whatsoever that my
son should go and contest. None of my children was interested in
politics. Bukola is a very intelligent boy, very hard working, calculating
and very bright upstairs.
Do you feel like a very proud father?
Yes, I'm very proud. Yesterday, the four of them came to me to tell me
that they are very proud of me for giving them very sound education. For one
thing, they are very conscious of the name, so they never wanted to live up
to it.
Speculations are rife that this might be your last public appearance
in the public. How true is this?
I think the ovation is high now I want to think of bowing out of public
life.
If you withdraw from party politics, what happens to Kwara politics
then, who takes charge?
quantify my efforts in Kwara politics. You see, I will be in the
background. I feel vindicated. You see I've never had a blood relation in
government since I've been involved, so all I've been doing is for the
welfare of the state and I think I'm one of the most misunderstood
politicians in the sense that people don't understand what I stand
for. People say I enslave them. When you talk of slavery, Lawal says he
wants to free Kwarans from slavery, what sort of slavery? I have no farm,
that they are farming for me and buying yam for me. On the contrary, I give
to them, the needy, I give to them. My pride is to see Kwarans happy.
There are enormous problems in Kwara, where do you start from?The
governor-elect is very capable, he has enough contact, he can manage victory
very well. I believe that he's going to focus on things that matter to
common women and men, not jack of all trades. He should focus on water
supply, agriculture, education, light and roads; these are the things he was
telling me yesterday. He can manage people very well, I can assure you.

Original_Search_Title: Lessons of a Trip Through 32 States
Headline: Nigeria: Lessons of a Trip Through 32 States
Body_Text:
Our political correspondent, Yusuf Ozi-Usman, who was on the entourage of General Muhammadu Buhari's campaign organisation which criss-crossed the four corners of this country in just 34 days, looks beyond the hurly-burly of the political campaigns and reports on other endowments of the country.
Nigeria as a nation, provides a good lesson in diversity, dynamism and resilience. It also provides a case study in careless abandonment of the various sources of strength by leaders, in glorification of narrow desires and acquisition of ephemeral wealth.
This fact becomes incontrovertibly clear if one has the opportunity, like I had, to go through the length and breadth of the country.
In Port Harcourt for example, while oil flows, and provides this country a large source of its foreign exchange earning, the otherwise arable land that spreads out all over the place is devastated as a result of the exploration for oil. The Port Harcourt, and the South-South situation in general is a profound irony.
As a matter of fact, the oil city itself does not cut a picture of any wealth. There are no good road net works, no constant electricity supply and other social amenities to justify its status as the producer or provider of the main source of the nation's wealth.
The same situation obtains also in Odi, a village in Bayelsa State.
Between some parts of Enugu and Ubara in Ebonyi State is nature's work of wonder.
A larger chunk of the land features dizzying, bottomless and frightening valleys and signs of gully erosion. In between the deep valleys and erosion runs a thin, pot-holed path through which the vehicles in which we were travelling passed. Some passengers in the vehicles simply closed their eyes as the vehicle meandered its way through the dangerous path.
A thought flashed through my mind, that if the driver made any slight miscalculation, the vehicle and its contents would end up down there at the bottom of the valley.
However, as we moved from Enugu to Awka, capital of Anambra State, we saw a vast agricultural land, most of which is lined with economic trees. At a stretch of about five kilometres before and after Okigwe in Imo State, are countless trees heavily laden with ripe cashew.
In all other places we visited in Igbo land, including Awka, Umuahia, Onitsha, Enugu and some villages, the arid land looks very flourishing. In fact major parts of the South-East is a region of tropical forests, passing through the tropical rain forests.
The major parts of the region are endowed with such agricultural resources as cocoa, cassava, oil palm, sugar cane, rice, maize, cashew, banana, citrus fruits, rubber, and a group of forest products like gum, resin and tannin.
In Badeji, Kutugi, Kapki and Bida in Niger Sate is also a long stretch of land, covered with forests of mango trees and, sugar cane. So also, is part of Kwara State, especially Bacita where, even there is a sugar manufacturing industry that flourished in the 70's and 80's.
In the South-West, we visited Oshogbo, Ede, Ore, Ogbomosho, Ile-Ife, Ado-Ekiti, Akure, Abeokuta, Ibadan, Oyo, Shagamu, Owo, Lagos and its suburbs, where hundreds of economic trees and smooth arid land, with all its green pasture beg for cultivation.
In Ondo State alone, agricultural products in the late 80's contributed well over 70 per cent of the state's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and generated income and employment for over 75 per cent of the population.
The main cash crops are cocoa, palm oil, palm kernel and timber. Indeed, cocoa from the state constituted the major cash crop and the largest in non-oil foreign exchange earner for the country as a whole in the 80's.
"About 60 per cent of Nigeria's output of cocoa is produced in Ondo State," said Alhaji Tijjani Suleiman, an indigene of the state, who regretted that the emphasis placed on oil has since eroded the prominent position most of the agricultural products played in the nation's economy in the recent past.
There was a vigorous food production programme introduced in the state in 1984 when the War Against Hunger was launched, to boost food production through the involvement of the general public, including civil servants in farming.
According to the Nigeria Year Book 1986, Ondo State government in that year, embarked on direct food production on a commercial basis in Osin, Orin, Ayede, Eporo, Epemakinde and Sobe centres, where a total of 3,600 hectares of maize, cassava and cowpeas were planted.
In Oyo, the tropical nature of the climate that favours the growth of food and cash crops came out vividly. Its vegetation is largely grass land and is suitable for rearing cattle. As a matter of fact, the existence of vast cattle ranches in Oyo town denotes the great potentialities for dairy farming. It is not for nothing therefore, that the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), the Cocoa Research Institute of Nigeria (CRIN) and the Federal Development of Agricultural Research (FDAR) are sited in Ibadan, the state capital to enhance the agricultural activities in the country.
The story of vast agricultural potentialities is the same in all parts of the South-West, except Lagos which has held on to its position as the industrial melting pot of the country, bringing about a sprawling population and heavy traffic.
Travelling around Edo State, including Benin, the state capital, Irrua, Ekpoma, Auchi, Agbede and Afuze, it was clear why the state has become known as the timber and cocoa producing state. Everywhere one turns, a cool green land filled with economic trees; cocoa, timber, rubber and others presents itself.
The state, including Warri in Delta State, produces 80 per cent of the country's rubber and crepe, supplying 60 per cent of Nigeria's total timber, and about 35 per cent of crude oil.
The state is well known for the production of such export products as timber, plywood and veneer sheets, rubber sheets and crepe, palm produce, cocoa and cotton.
The story of green pastoral land in the South-East and South-West turns into something else when it comes to the North, especially the North East where desert encroachment, sand dunes and bad conditions of weather and environment reign supreme.
From Damaturu to Yola, Potiskum, Jalingo, Kaltungo, Nguru,Biu, Mubi, Gujba, Gwoza, Bama, Nangere, Benisheik to Maiduguri, Gombe, Bade, Geidam, Geishua, Fika and Gulani, it is a story of dry land and wilted trees and crops.
Bauchi, Katsina, Daura, Kano, Darazo, Birniwa, Hadejia, Dutse and Birnin Kudu have one thing in common, and that is semi-arid land, even though farming is still the occupation of the majority of the people.
As a matter of fact, despite the semi arid nature of Kano for example, nearly two million farming families are found in all parts of the state cultivating and or harvesting various types of crops, such as millet, groundnut, guinea corn, cotton, pepper, maize, cowpeas, rice, wheat and a wide varieties of vegetable.
The same is true of Kaduna, and Katsina where statistics show that 40 per cent of Nigeria's total annual production of cotton, as at 1991, came from there. The two states, including the towns in them; Kafanchan, Daura and Funtua where we visited, also produce groundnut, ginger, guinea corn, millet, yam, beans and rice.
Yola and Jalingo too, inspite of the excruciating desertification, have the largest arable land in the country, suitable for large scale farming, livestock breeding and forestry.
The River Gongola, River Taraba and River Donga flow through many towns and villages, exposing wide opportunities for irrigation and fishery. In these towns are produced such crops as millet, guinea corn, groundnut and yam.
Places we also visited which are rich in agricultural activities inspite of the unfriendly environment are Jos, Kazaure, Gusau, Sokoto, Lokoja, Okene, Uyo, Zaki Biam, Tarmuwa and Bursari.
These potentialities are so visible and compelling that it becomes criminal and embarrassing to down-play them.
In fact, the question that kept recurring as we passed through these cities, towns and villages in all parts of this country is, is Nigeria this rich?
Nigeria and its leadership have proved right an African adage which says that he who wears the cap, does not know that it is red. This simply means that we do not value what we have and, if we do, we don't make any move or we make half-hearted moves, to show it.
If some of the advanced nations have the fortune of being blessed with one quarter of what Nigeria has been endowed with by the Almighty, they would have taken over the feeding, and by extension, the leadership of the world.
It is a shame that in this jet age and time, and with the enormous natural resources sprawling all over the place, Nigeria is still importing anything that has to do with agriculture and agro-allied products, or even petroleum products.
The major lesson I have personally learnt from all these cities, towns and villages across the country is embedded in a poetic composition which states water, water, water everywhere but none to drink. Nigeria is certainly poor in the midst of abundant wealth.
Another lesson is that millions of Nigerians who otherwise would have been proud to farm are being dissuaded inadvertently, by the government at all levels, by making them to believe that it is only white collar jobs, contracts and even menial jobs that can make them rich.
How much would it cost, for example, for government to create farming settlements in various local governments of the federation for graduates and interested Nigerians?
How much would the government invest in mango, cashew and orange juice factory to process mangoes, cashew and other fruits that are wasting away in Okigwe, Badeji, parts of Abuja, Nasarawa (Keffi) and several other places in the country?

Original_Search_Title: Challenges Facing the African Union
Headline: Africa: Challenges Facing the African Union
Body_Text:
Lagos — The recent inaugural meeting of the African Union (AU), the new continental body that replaced the erstwhile Organisation of African Unity (OAU), which was held in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, is no doubt a welcome development for several reasons. First, is the fact that during the early 60s when the founding fathers of newly independent African States, such as Emperor Haille Sallasie of Ethiopia, Julius Nyerere of Tanzania, Dr. Kwame Nkommah of Ghana, Ahmadu Ahidjo of Cameroun, Dr Nnamdi Azikiwe of Nigeria, Milton Obote of Uganda, Jomo Kenyata of Kenya, Teopold Senghor of Senegal, Abdel Naser of Egypt, Kenneth Kaunda of Zambia and a host of others, founded the OAU, what was uppermost on their minds was the total and quick emancipation of African States that were still under the yoke of colonialism.
Little did they anticipate the existing myriad of complex problems across the continent, ranging from ethnic and cross border conflicts, refugee problems, pervasive hunger and starvation, Aids scourge and incessant military incursion in governance, hence at the conference of Heads of State in July 1964, the OAU rather than open a Pandora box by supporting a territorial re-arrangement for whatever reasons, re-affirmed the South American doctrine of "Uti possidetis" (As you possess, so shall you continue to possess), by accepting the existing Colonial territorial boundaries as the basis for African Unity.
Suffice it to note that as events have shown, their position has no doubt proved quite unpopular as could be seen from border disputes between several post - independence African nations, such as Algeria, Morocco, Kenya, Somalia, Ghana, Ivory Coast, Malawi- Tanzania, Republic of Guinea-Guinea Bissau, Burkina Faso, Mali, Nigeria - Benin and most recently Nigeria- Cameroon. Worthy of mention is the fact that the Colonial powers had drawn national boundaries without regard to Cultural affinities and many ethnic groups found themselves split into different sovereignties. Thus, the Yoruba are found in Nigeria and Benin Republic, the Hausa - Fulani in Benin, Chad and Nigeria, the Masai in Kenya and Tanzania, the Somali in Ethiopia, Kenya and Somalia, the Ewe in Ghana and Togo, etc.
On achieving independence, some of the states wished to incorporate neighbouring territories on the basis of self-determination. Thus, in a situation where everyone wanted to gain but not lose territory, there are bound to be conflicts and continued conflicts far into the future, across the continent, hence the recent birth of the African Union (AU) should be seen as providing the much needed opportunity for African leaders to establish a permanent Boundary Adjustment Commission to tackle and proffer permanent and lasting solutions to boundary disputes among African States, which has been a major source of tension and wars and by implication the prevalence of refugees, hunger and poverty across the continent.
By so doing the resort to the International Court of Justice (ICJ) for settlement of boundary disputes by brotherly African States, such as was the case recently with Nigeria and Cameroon, could be avoided and averted, including the attendant controversy and bad blood.
Secondly, the advent of the African Union is significant and symbolic, considering the fact that at the time the OAU was founded, nations such as South Africa, Zimbabwe, Mozambique, Namibia and a host of others, were still under Colonial domination and thus were not members of the OAU. It is thus quite gratifying that South Africa which has for decades been under the stranglehold of the evil apartheid regime even until the early 90s is not only a major initiator of the Africa Union but is also its first Chairman, in the person of Thaho Mbeki. It is therefore expected that South Africa with its highly developed economy and State of the art infrastructure, comparable to those of the advanced Western nations, would bring its wealth and prestige to bear on the new African Union in the overriding interest of the long suffering masses of this impoverished continent.
Thus, in close co-operation with other notable African leaders like President Obasanjo of Nigeria, John Kuffor of Ghana, Muhmar Ghadafi o Libya, Abdul Diouf of Senegal and others, President Thabo Mbeki, in his capacity as the first chairman of the AU, should seek to urgently resolve the numerous and lingering conflicts across the continent, if the rest of the world community is to regard the new African Union with a pinch of salt, for a situation where a long standing independent nation like Cote d'Ivoire (Ivory Coast), has to rely and depend upon it's erstwhile Colonial master, France, for solution to its internal conflict with the rebels, is indicative of a continent that lacks an effective machinery for conflict resolution. It is for this same reason that many of the smaller and newly independent African nations have had to sign protection pacts and defence treaties with their Colonial masters, from whom they secured independence not too long ago. What a bitter pill to swallow, you may say!
In furtherance of such conflict resolution initiative, the new AU would do well by establishing a standing fighting force, "the African High Command" (AHC) with troops contributed by AU member nations, for the purpose of peace keeping and peace enforcement at short notice, anywhere on the continent, for had ECOWAS swiftly mobilised troops and intervened quickly in the Cote d'Ivoire conflict before the arrival of the French troops, the situation would have been different today.
Needless to overemphasis the fact that the much touted peace deal reached with the rebels in France is not only a slap in the face of the democratically elected government of Cote Dlvoire, ECOWAS and the African Union but runs counter to the pervading tenets of democracy and the rule of law around the globe, since it sends wrong signals to would-be radical elements across the African continent' that resort to force is the best way to settle political grievances.
Furthermore, the establishment of such a standing force would help avoid the pitfalls of the UN Security Council which oftentimes lands it difficult to mobilise troops for its peace keeping/enforcement operations especially where the national interest of the super powers is threatened.
A third and equally potent challenge to the new African Union is the problem of hunger and starvation caused by a combination of factors such as prevalence of drought especially in the arid regions where there is hardly adequate rainfall for most of the year, thus making it extremely difficult to engage in meaningful agricultural activities, ethnic and cross border conflicts, leading to displacement of persons from their homes, absence of year round mechanised large scale farming and dearth of adequate funding and financing of the agricultural sector across the continent.
The on-going self-inflicted situation in Zimbabwe, where the rash and ill-timed decision of President Robert Mugabe to re-allocate white owned farms to land less blacks, has plunged the hitherto food sufficient nation into excruciating hunger and starvation, is instructive in this regard. In like manner, the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), Burundi, Somalia, Ethiopia, Sudan, Ivory Coast, Liberia and a host of other African nations, are suffering the same faith, with no end in sight, as they continue to depend on handouts from the International Commission of the Red Cross (ICRC), other aid donors and multilateral agencies, a situation that would greatly diminish and reduce the pride and prestige of the African Union, if left unattended to.
Therefore, given the gravity and enormity of the problem of hunger and starvation coupled with the chronic and devastating poverty among the African population, at a time when the industrialised Western nations are extending incentives to farmers to reduce their farm acreage and food production, the African Union must evolve deliberate and well- articulated policies to enable its member States boost food production through all year round mechanised large scale commercial cropping, using irrigation to stem the scourge of hunger and starvation. This will also enhance maximum benefit from the Africa Growth Opportunity Act (AGOA), which former President Bill Clinton of the United States of America was kind and benevolent enough to extend to African nations just before leaving office in 2001.
In this connection, it is hereby suggested that the African Union should persuade its member states to adopt the principle of comparative cost advantage in their agricultural programmes and policies. To this end, member nations would be limited to the production of a given number of agricultural and farm products in which they have the highest comparative cost advantage and favourable climatic conditions. In consequence therefore, a nation like Nigeria which currently produces multiplicity of agricultural/farm products, such as yam, maize, millet, sorghum, cassava, cotton, ginger, palm produce, groundnut, coca, rubber, beans, cocoa yam, in commercial quantities, thus a dissipation and duplication of efforts, could be limited to the cultivation of four of these products in which it has the highest cost advantage for exchange with other African nations.
By so doing, the African continent would in the not too distant future overcome the problem of food scarcity, hunger and starvation. Also, under such arrangement, it would become easier to attract the much needed adequate funding and financing to the agricultural sector across the continent, from the likes of the African Development Bank (ADB), the World Bank/lFC, FAO, multilateral agencies and even Commercial Banks. In the same vein, the establishment of an African Centre for Technology Development, for research and development of basic technology for the processing and preservation of food and agricultural products across the continent, would go a long way to reduce over-dependence on imported and expensive foreign machinery for agriculture, as well as reduce cost of food items and make same available to the teeming population on the continent.
Furthermore, the African Union would be well advised to establish a Food and Agriculture Agency to co- ordinate and monitor food production in its member States, as well as create a strategic food reserve for the alleviation of hunger during emergencies in member nations. By the same token, the AU should work in consonance with the appropriate UN agencies and national governments in Africa, to stem the scourge of the AIDS epidemic which is decimating Africa's population. Lastly, the African Union must strive to tackle the prevalent and embarrassing level of corruption in governance among public officials in most African nations.
This could be checkmated by establishing a standing commission on ethics and good governance, to carry out on the spot surveillance across the continent and impose stiff sanctions on Governments and their officials found to be misusing and to have misused and misappropriated public funds, to the detriment of their deprived and suffering populace.
Okoh writes from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Rains Wreak Havoc in Kampala
Headline: Uganda: Rains Wreak Havoc in Kampala
Body_Text:
Kampala — A HEAVY downpour lasting more than six hours yesterday morning left all flood prone spots in and around Kampala drenched.
More than seven houses at the Ntinda Stretcher area were filled with water up to the window, level from the wetland that drains Ntinda and the Kyambogo areas.
It is not clear whether the owners of the houses were around. Across to the left, residents had sought refugee at the neighbour's who was not affected.
The matooke and yam gardens around the area, below the Shell petrol station were washed away.
Police Fire Brigade made responses to a number of areas that were affected by the rampaging waters in different parts of the city.
Joseph Mugisa, the chief fire Officer, said they rushed to Mengo where a man was reportedly swept away by the water. The New Vision could not get details of the man's fate.
Mugisa said they were told that the Electoral Commission offices were flooded.
"We were also not spared here (Fire Brigade headquarters) despite the nearly completed works on the Nakivubo channel around here."
The Kyambogo area on the Kampala-Jinja highway was the most hit because of a yet to be finished drainage channel.
Water levels at that spot rose above the waist of a pedestrian who could be seen trapped at the spot.
Saloon cars were most affected.
Some cars were stuck midway the spot, which stretches from around the turn off to the Pepsi Cola plant to the Concorp offices.
Young men around the area sprung into action, rolling up their sleeves to make a quick Sunday morning buck as they pushed the unfortunate motorists to safety.
The newly installed Crane Access auto teller machine area was submerged.
One resident in Kyebando Kisalosalo zone told The New Vision on phone that her residence was flooded. "I can drive out of my house but not out of my compound," she said. Ends

Original_Search_Title: Early Rains Herald Cool Season
Headline: Nigeria: Early Rains Herald Cool Season
Body_Text:
Blustering showers of rains pelted down on Saturday, at dawn in Abuja, even as residents headed for the polling stations to elect a president for Nigeria.
Early rains, defying earlier predictions of a prolonged dry season, came calling first week in March, carrying along desert-like wind.
However, contrary to fears, rains have since fallen, at least seven times in Abuja this wet season.
According to Malam Biro, a Fulani farmer who also herds cattle in Idbo, near airport area in Abuja, the yams would certainly flourish next harvest season, if the rains continue without a long spell of drought.
The rains are early according to Abuja Trust investigation,.
An according to Engineer Abdulsalami Adaruruku (Cherry) of Piwoyi, another village along Airport road, "the weather has become so cool now, one could sleep without bordering about heat."
Residents, no doubt, prefer the cooling that accompanies these rains to the hotness of the dry season.

Original_Search_Title: Maitama FHA Occupants Condemn Structural Design
Headline: Nigeria: Maitama FHA Occupants Condemn Structural Design
Body_Text:
Occupants of the Federal Housing Estate in Maitama have condemned the structural design of the apartments as built by the authorities, saying, the design is bringing basic facilities into decay.
The occupants, while narrating their ordeals, urged the Federal Housing Authority (FHA) not to repeat the design of apartments built in Maitama anywhere in Abuja.
Investigation by Abuja Trust revealed that most of the apartment built by FHA are now in deplorable condition.
Some of the affected occupants told our reporter that the untimely decay of the apartments in the area was due to lack of foresight by FHA at the initial stage of construction.
Though FHA had sold out the facility to private firms that are now responsible for its maintenance, some of the staff of these organisations lamented that construction materials used by FHA were substandard coupled with conduit wiring and running of water pipes inside walls which consequently weakens structure.
According to a staff of National University Commission, Mr. Ridwan Abdul Salami, who spoke with our reporter, FHA should be blamed for structural defect of most blocks in the area, adding that the connection of water system among flats was in bad condition which usually caused blockage, resulting to gushing out of water on the walls.
"The water system or pipes were passed inside the building I don't know how Federal Housing Engineers at the initial stage planned these building," he said.
Mr. Redwan added that in order to prevent blockage of water system, some of the organisations that bought the FHA buildings in the area were from time to time taking necessary steps to bursting walls as a way of bringing out water pipes and cables for proper functioning.
However, apart from the lack of foresight by the designers, Mr. Ridwan, who has been residing in one of the blocks in the area for the past eight years pointed out congestion of people in a flat, pounding of yam among others as some factors that are contributing to the weakness of the facility.
In a similar reaction, Mr. Lateef Salami, who works with Ministry of Petroleum Resources and who leaves in one of the affected blocks, said FHA had one time admitted its fault during a meeting with occupants but said that it could no longer demolish the blocks after construction.
Mr. Lateef further stressed that FHA was no longer in control of any block except for the monitoring of available spaces and illegal construction in the area.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Food Items On the Increase -Survey
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Food Items On the Increase -Survey
Body_Text:
As Christians get set to celebrate Easter, prices of most staple food items in Abuja have risen.
Surveying some markets in the city indicated that items affected by the price increase included garri, beans, palm oil, groundnut oil and chicken.
A bottle of groundnut oil now costs N150 as against the former price of N120 while a small keg of groundnut oil, which sold for N 600, is now N 650.
A bag of beans now sells for N7,200 as against its old price of N6,800 while a measure of the same commodity is now N80 and N100 for the white and red beans as against their former prices of N50 and N70 respectively.
Live chicken which was purchased for slightly lower prices now sell for prices ranging from N600 to N900 while a kilogramme of frozen chicken which sold for N220 now sells for N270.
A kilogramme of beef, which was hitherto N300, now goes for N400 at the Wuse and Garki village Monday markets.
A big tuber of yam now sells for between N250 and N300, medium size, between N180 and N150 while the small size tuber is sold for between N100 and N120.
According to the survey, condiments such as tomatoes, pepper and onions were also affected by the increase.
Prices of ingredients for salad such as a small size cabbage which sold for N60 as at last week, now goes for N100 while the big one which sold for N120 now costs between N170 and N200.
A bunch of lettuce which sold for N10 now sells for between N20 and N30.
The survey also showed that a measure of rice now sells for N160 even as the price for a 50kg bag ranges from between N4,200 and N4,800, depending on the brand.
According to our further reports, the markets were however, unusually bustling with women and children trying to make last minute purchases.

Original_Search_Title: Polls: PDP, ANPP And Nigeria's Future
Headline: Nigeria: Polls: PDP, ANPP And Nigeria's Future
Body_Text:
The focus of this paper is on the winning prospects of the two main political parties (PDP and ANPP) and their flag bearers, based on the anticipated vision plans of each for Nigeria during the April 2003 general elections.
This intellectual exercise is being undertaken even though we are fully aware of the uncertainties that have so far, engulfed the political campaigns with unpredictable future. We are no doubt aware of the wave of political violence involving assassinations, arson and the desperate posture of the incumbent for the second term re-election at all levels. Very importantly, there are doubts as to the independence, transparency and credibility of the poorly funded INEC whereby it may not be in the position to comply strictly with the provisions of the electoral laws of the land.
In the circumstance, the real struggle for power especially at the federal centre, is between the PDP, incumbent and ANPP, the real front runners. Here we shall reflect on the performance of the incumbent party and what the PDP views as the future Nigeria. On the other hand we shall consider the challenge of the incumbent's performance by ANPP and consider ANPP's vision plan for the future of Nigeria.
As we move to the final stage of the elections, however, the political terrain to be changed has become characterised with suspicion and fear. In the past, civilian-to-civilian transition failed to hold, and this is why we place high premium on election 2003. This time, we hope change shall come about without the shadow of the military that Nigerians hold responsible for all the ills of Nigeria. In this circumstance we are looking forward to real change. So what has PDP and ANPP said about change and how shall each of the parties handle change for the future Nigeria?
At this stage it appears, PDP and ANPP through their flag bearers have rounded up their plans for the Nigeria of tomorrow: about how to keep it up, get it fit or strangulate it under the environment of poverty, corruption, dependence or self reliance, rekindle its hope or leave it to languish in despair, or uplift it to become an effective player in world affairs or allow it to become allergic to internal insecurity and disputes in circumstance of hunger, mono economy and "monopoly money" called the Naira amidst disputed privatisation scheme. Or further still, change to good governance or be ready for a future that we fear?
In most instances the campaigns for elections 2003 had been less about policy issues that lead to progress. Until recently did Nigerians see public notices placed by ANPP calling for campaign based on issues rather than personalities, that tended to dominate the PDP addresses at public rallies. With time fast closing on us, the focus on issues and personalities may not mean much for the choice that Nigerians shall be called upon to make on tomorrow. From our close observation of the ongoing campaigns, it appears PDP government does not like change of whatever nature. This explains the party's philosophy of continuity at all costs. According to PDP, the president must continue, the vice-president must continue, stagnant Nigeria must continue, the lack of infrastructure must continue, the governors must continue, corruption must continue, privatisation amidst public outcries must continue, Nigeria police must continue, ASUU strikes and labour unrest must go on. To the disbelief of the good people of Nigeria, Corporate Nigeria, with its style of PDP funding eve, if the funds come from unclaimed dividends, must continue. The forces of history that shaped it, must continue. The return of PDP presidential candidate to power after 20 years makes PDP to think that Nigeria exists as a constant: constant of violence, constant unchanged and unchanging values, a constant warehouse economy, and a country under one God that brought him back to power to accomplish his work. This is why the man enjoys his pounded yam meal with Okro and bush meat without minding even if the ordinary Nigerians persist in hunger. How much was the cost of a tuber of yam in 1999? And how much is it today?
On the other hand, is it not a valid position to observe that the ANPP maintains that the only constant phenomenon of life is its unremitting motion. In existence, don't we move with change in the security, stability and prosperity of Nigeria and adopt the style of making deliberate efforts for moderating these changes in the interest of the nation. If in the evolution of these scenarios the PDP approach runs off key, ANPP's candidate outshines PDP's flag bearer in the election 2003. Buhari's victory will only be the manifestation of the extent to which his party had articulated a supreme vision plan for future Nigeria.
Some illustration may be useful here. For example, when you take the issues of the Niger-Delta, with particular emphasis on onshore-offshore dichotomy, you will realise that the two presidential candidates disagree more than they differ. Both care about the problems of Niger Delta. Or when you talk about working with the National Assembly for good and implementable budgets, poverty alleviation, food security, a realistic exchange rate for the Naira and how to deal with communal conflicts, you discover differences largely of degree and sincerity. It was a PDP chieftain from Kano who even went out to accuse his party's leadership of being less transparent and sincere if compared with the ANPP flag bearer.
Here, a question might arise as to the background of the PDP and ANPP flag bearers? These are public figures in politics so there seems to be nothing hidden about either of them. For the purpose of this article however, the reader shall know that, both came from middle class family backgrounds. Both went to the army after the secondary education and benefited from professional military training and both rose to the top, and each headed the federal government as Head of State. Each played along in public life after leaving the military and is therefore aware of the trappings of power at the highest levels.
At the start of their search for leadership of Nigeria, each had a supreme decision to make. How much change would they bring about in themselves and to the country? This entailed the extent to which each would be exposed to enable Nigerians to decide. For the PDP candidate, he has been the incumbent (this has its burden). He had been the offspring of sympathy that had earned him pardon and nationwide choice in 1999. His performance in some areas spoke for him and in some cases, incumbency worked against him. In some parts of the country like in Benue and Odi, Niger-Delta region, where he is yet to convince the people that he did not kill their kith and kin on premeditated agenda, his steady repositioning and desire to seek re-election was somehow delayed due to a long consultation with his associates and family members, and in particular his desire to receive "approval from the almighty God." At the end of the consultations he had a safe feel that he would continue as president, with the vision plan for bigger changes. This is the basis for the slogan that "continuity shall secure our democracy."
The ANPP on the other hand focused on what its flag bearer calls "Project Nigeria." The project focuses briefly on among other fundamental issues, achieving the following priority goals:
(i) Stability: through provision of equal opportunity to all Nigerians with emphasis on gender equality, mutual trust and the protection of human rights including freedom of speech, freedom of religion and freedom of expression to make Nigeria to be an effective member and player in international affairs.
(ii) Security: to tackle the entire problems that threaten Nigeria and foreign interests by ensuring comprehensive security in all its dimensions.
(iii) Prosperity: to evolve diversified dynamics and self-reliance in a nation with well-developed infrastructure.
The ANPP flag bearer prides himself of the success he made of the Petroleum (Special) Trust Fund (PTF) assignment before the arrival of PDP government on the scene to scrap the PTF without any substitute. He beats his chest of the presence of PTF vehicles in most government institutions, pharmaceutical drugs in government hospitals and instructional materials in most schools and tertiary institutions across the nation. He feels that the Niger-Delta region shall be freed from the PDP government's neglect, through inaction, corruption and insensitivity at the top. This is why Buhari accuses PDP of insincerity in dealing with the Niger Delta problems including the desire to review onshore-offshore dichotomy bill. This is also why, the ANPP predicts that, PDP shall abandon that bill that has become a subject of intimidation of the good people of the region. As the ANPP chairman promised at the Port Harcourt rally, ANPP government shall address the Niger-Delta on the basis of justice and equity in the best interest of the people of the region and the nation as a whole.
Even though presidential election comes up tomorrow, it appears PDP has dual campaigns: one by the candidate's handlers and the other by the flag bearer himself. This underscores the failure of the candidate to respond to the issue raised at his solo TV presidential debate. At that debate, the candidate did not just avoid answering questions, he dismissed ASUU as being self seeking without the interest of the students, he failed to explain why the Auditor-General of the Federation was sacked when for the first time ever, such a report was produced. The candidate lacked background facts for the clarification of issues, which makes the PDP a party without any plan for bridge-building between the past and the future.
What has come to work against the PDP and its flag bearer has been the decision of the Alliance for Democracy (AD) the Western geopolitical zone-dominated party to endorse the incumbent, the flagbearer of PDP, a Nigerian who hails from the Western section of Nigeria. This action by the AD has been criticised particularly by those who seemed to be linking such endorsement with the kind of endorsement of Abacha by political parties in the mid 1990's. They also link such a decision to ethnic politics that has been the bane of Nigerian politics since independence in 1960. Here it must be recalled that never in the history of Nigeria has the westerners voted for another Nigerian other than one of their own kith and kin, for the highest office in the land. This development may prove disastrous for the PDP in the election. At any rate let us hope that the PDP flag bearer shall not renege on the conditions of the AD endorsement of PDP candidate.
But can we trust any of the candidates? Though PDP considers its flag bearer reliable and trustworthy, other Nigerians find it difficult to accept this because of the inability of the PDP to respect things like its pact with interest groups as was exemplified in the pact with Arewa in 1999. This may be the kind of the outcome of the romance between PDP with Niger-Delta and all those groups who are giving conditions, on the basis of which, PDP shall be returned to power. At any rate, the desire by PDP to convince Nigerians that its words are its bond, if weighed against the USA and the British reports about the level of corruption in the Presidency, the ranking of Nigeria among the most corrupt nations of the world and the re-emergence of fuel queues across the country, makes it difficult for his foes and even the PDP to find words to attack ANPP's flag bearer's character that is found to be based on honesty, transparency, discipline and probity reminiscent of the patriot needed to lead the country at times like this.
At a critical time like this when the incumbent is proving incapable in many spheres - ASUU strike, fuel crisis, insecurity in the land, doubts about the capability of the poorly funded INEC to conduct free and fair elections, and as the same INEC continued to be manipulated by those who know themselves.
But then a little prayer, with the hope of victory by "Baba hopeful" of victory at the end of the vote counting Obasanjo, Buhari, Ojukwu, Gani Fawenhinmi, etc. Major (Mrs.) Obasanjo, Nwachukwu (praise the lord; his wife shouted)
In conclusion, let all the good people of Nigeria know that, for the foregoing projection s to come about, each Nigerian with the voter's card must go out on that D-Day and exercise their supreme civic duty of voting according to their conscience. Beyond that however, the people must protect the votes cast in order to checkmate the ugly spectre called rigging that confers fear and erodes public confidence in elections.
Dr. Audu, a former Executive Director in Daily Times, resides in Abuja.

Original_Search_Title: Fear Swirls As Drought Scorches Crops
Headline: Tanzania: Fear Swirls As Drought Scorches Crops
Body_Text:
DESPITE assurances by President Benjamin Mkapa to the people that there is no food shortage in Tanzania, fear of a severe food shortage still looms large in the country.
Populations in some parts of the country require immediate food subsidies, while other parts need close monitoring because their food situation is not particularly good.
The latest available edition of the Tanzania Food Security Bulletin published by the Government warns of a "localised food insecurity" that remains unsolved despite vulnerability assessment revelations.
The bulletin, which was released on February 24 this year, talks of nine districts that required intervention because food was neither readily available, nor affordable, to 177,000 people in those districts.
The districts - Monduli in Arusha; Simanjiro in Manyara; Mwanga and Same in Kilimanjaro; Korogwe, Lushoto and Muheza in Tanga; Masasi in Mtwara; and Liwale in Lindi -needed 7,215 tonnes of grain between last January and this month.
The situation in the named districts was expected to return to normal in May this year when the harvesting season normally starts. But, as crops wilted in many parts of the country following the prolonged drought, the food shortage is bound to be severer and more expansive.
The Government is considering allowing food to be imported duty-free to fill the shortage that is expected to occur in the next farming season.
Many farmers in Tanzania, particularly those in upcountry regions, depending on selling their food crops to get money to buy other essential requirements for their families.
According to the National Food Balance Sheet of June 2002 to May 2003, the food availability in Tanzania as of January this year was 9.21 million tonnes.
The amount includes 8.57 million tonnes of food crop harvests over the 2001/02 season, and about 634,000 tonnes of the year 2002/03 opening stock.
As at January this year, Tanzania had cereals availability of five million tonnes as against cereals requirement of 5.1 million tonnes. The gross cereals harvest was 4.46 million tonnes.
The Government statistically combines cereals and non-cereal food crops to arrive at the country's food availability figure. But the foods most consumed are cereals because they can be stored for long periods.
Non-cereal foods are those perishable like cassava, bananas, yams and potatoes. These cannot be stored for long, and cannot be transported long distances across country from producing regions to needy ones.
Perishable food crops are mostly consumed by urban residents who are not producing foodstuffs.
This type of food needs immediately supply and use. As such, they are not regarded as the most important foods that can save the country from famine. Tanzania exported about 192,878 tonnes of cereals between July 2002 and March this year. Of the exports, 151,291 tonnes were maize; 38,233 tonnes beans and 3,354 tonnes of rice.
The exports reduced the availability of the commodities. The country's maize requirement annually is at 3,039,000 tonnes while only 2,927,000 tonnes were actually available during the period.
The poor expected harvest in the coming season is due to the unfavourable rainfall countrywide that is causing drought. As a result, there if fear of poor harvests, as well as poor pasturage for livestock.
Tanzania has been experiencing sunny and dry conditions throughout January to March this year. It is expected that the rains may fall in earnest this month, and some parts of the country have already started getting rain.
In most cases, the current rains are of little or no help because local farming needs prolonged rains. Under the circumstances, the Government has called upon farmers to use improved seeds that could match with the scanty rains.

Original_Search_Title: South West : Appeal Court President Denounces Petition Writing
Headline: Cameroon: South West : Appeal Court President Denounces Petition Writing
Body_Text:
Buea (Fako) — The President of the South West Court of Appeal, Buea, Chief Justice Lucy Asuagbor has told the judiciary that "every benefit carries along an equivalent burden of responsibility". Chief Justice Asuagbor was responding to her colleagues of the South West Judiciary corps recently during a new-year wishing ceremony at the Buea Appeal Court premises.
The ceremony, second of its kind in Buea after last year, saw the presentation of new year-wishes to the Appeal Court President and the procureur-general by Magistrates, registrars, Sheriff-bailiffs, barristers and the auxiliary staff.
Responding to demands of better treatment for emoluments, mission allowances and security of magistrates, chief Justice Asuagbor promised to act within legal means to improve on their lot. She, however, admonished her colleagues to desist from petitions and to embrace dialogue in sorting out their differences.
The Appeal Court president listed out some of the achievements made in the South West judiciary including the erection of the Menji (Lebialem) Court of First Instance and High Court with plans to equip them. The acquisition of computers, photocopiers and vehicles for enclaved jurisdictions was re-echoed by chief Justice Asuagbor as a booster for the South West judiciary.
The Appeal Court president announced some six seminars on Francophone OHADA (Business law) intended for the various Anglophone judicial corps this year. The seminars, she explained, would lay to rest the question of the applicability of OHADA law that had lingered long in one of the two Anglophone provinces of Cameroon. Some of the laws that have remained a headcache to the judiciary west of the Mungo include the CIMA law on Insurance, the CEMAC Maritime Code in addition to the OHADA law on business. Legal experts had noted their gradual process of harmonisation in the recent years as yielding fruits.
In an earlier statement on behalf of his colleagues, the vice president to the court of Appeal, Justice Macaulay Moma urged the Procureur-general and the Appeal Court President to "eat with a fork and not a spoon so that we can also enjoy what is left over". He referred to their bosses as having the yam and the knife to make the judicial family happy or not.

Original_Search_Title: They Won, They Lost
Headline: Nigeria: They Won, They Lost
Body_Text:
Lagos — Last Saturday's election threw up new faces and shatteed the ambition of
others. In addition to those who outrightly lost their seats in the National
Assembly, other political leaders equally lost their bases to new
godfathers. The pattern of victory by the three main political parties has
re-drawn the political map of the country.
While the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) ate deep into states controlled
by the Alliance for Democracy (AD) , the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP)
made in roads into PDP areas leaving the AD as the net loser. With the
exception of Lagos state, the PDP swept virtually all the other AD states
even as the PDP itself lost grounds in key Northern States like Kano and
Kaduna to the ANPP. The main opposition party made serious incursions into
Plateau while conceding part of its hold on Kwara State to the PDP.
Victims of the upsets included Senators Olabiyi Durojaiye,Wahab Dosunmu,
Seye Ogunlewe, Femi Okunrounmu and others in the South-West. Former governor
Obong Akpan Isemin also lost his bid for the senate. Senator Bob Nwanunu who
was in the outgoing senate representing Abia central senatorial district on
the platform of the PDP switched camps to the United Nigeria Peoples Party
(UNPP) and lost. One of the political godfathers whose hold is slipping is
Senator Jim Nwobodo in Enugu state. Nwobodo who claimed to have made Dr
Chimaroke Nnamani Governor in Enugu State in 1999 has more or less lost
grip.
The Winners...
Olusegun Obasanjo
Former United States Vice President, Nelson Rockefeller, once said no
politician can claim to be worth the name except he has worked with the
local people and won elections with their help. Perhaps, it is for this
reason that people dismiss President Olusegun Obasanjo when they talk of
politicians of the moment. Though the man is an elected President, his party
did badly in his home base.
However, with the result of last Saturday's National Assembly elections
in which the President led his party into taking 13 out of 18 senatorial
seats in the South West, nobody can now dismiss him as a floating
politician. President Obasanjo has eventually arrived as a politician with a
base, a man who controls the grassroots in his area and one whose name can
be invoked to achieve victory. Incidentally, Obasanjo's PDP took over in the
South West at a time when his closest rival, Muhammadu Buhari looked set to
deliver the whole of the Northwest to the All Nigeria Peoples Party
(ANPP). The President has therefore created a base from which he would
operate. He now has a solid platform of support with national assembly
members who can help him in times of trouble having won the election with
his name.
Muhammadu Buhari
But for the entry of Major General Muhammadu Buhari (rtd) into the
presidential race, it would have been a walk-over for President Olusegun
Obasanjo. His entry not only electrified the political atmosphere, it has
helped to shore up the popularity of the ANPP in the Far North. Last
Saturday's National Assembly results was a testimony of sorts. Nigeria's
democracy has benefitted from Buhari as he has injected credibility,
character and a sense of keen contest into the presidential race and the
polity at large.
Don Etiebet
The easiest prediction to make some two years ago was that the All
Peoples Party (APP) was on its way to the political gallows. That was
correct. With defections to other parties and a party leadership that was
nowhere to be found, APP actually died. From the phoenix came a change of
name and leadership. The All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) was born and
Governor Attahiru Bafarawa of Sokoto State was appointed to head the
Caretaker Committee.
The party rose from the dead - literally. By the time the presidential
convention was held in January, the party had become a new creature - old
things gone, new things on board. It produced Chief Don Etiebet as National
Chairman and a new and vigorous campaign was launched to wrest the
presidency from the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). Although, Etiebet did
not deliver his state or the South-South to the ANPP, the general
performance of the party in the National Assembly elections, especially in
the Far North, is a clear indication that the PDP, for the first time, has a
match. It won in Kano and Kaduna, and took control of some PDP seats in
Jigawa, Sokoto, Borno and Plateau States. But it lost Kwara.
Audu Ogbeh
As National Chairman of the PDP, Chief Audu Ogbeh could easily be said
to be a winner. He assumed the mantle of leadership of the party at a time
of turbulence, when the party was torn by internal strife and mutual
suspicion. Ogbeh, at the time he came brought some measure of credibility to
the party, such that those who wanted to quit, had a rethink. Although the
party became a victim of self-inflicted crisis in the manner it imposed
candidates after the primaries, that it was able to put its house in order
is a credit to Ogbeh.
PDP
The ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) lived up to its pre-election
boast that it would sweep the polls and even poach the states held by the
rival Alliance for Democracy (AD). Although many are protesting the nature
of the landslide in the South West in particular, PDP prepared the ground
for what happened last Saturday by entering into an agreement with AD. While
AD relaxed its guard, PDP went on the onslaught and left AD vulnerable. If
the PDP will overcome the protest that would trail its victory at the polls,
the party would have consolidated. For the victory it has recorded so far,
the party deserves to be a winner.
Olusola Saraki:
"Too close to call." That was the verdict of most political analysts
ahead of last Saturday's legislative elections in Kwara State. Oloye's iron
grip on the state, many thought, has been pried loose by the incumbent
governor, Muhammed Lawal, who had combined with Senator Ishaq Salman of the
ANPP and other political stalwarts within the party in a battle to
'demystify' the Oloye. The battle was fierce, and the stakes high. However,
at the end of voting on Saturday, Saraki was able to prove that in Kwara he
is still the indisputable political grandmaster. His daughter, Gbemisola,
won by a landslide to put an end to Salman's restive stay in the
Senate. However, the elder Saraki still has one last battle to fight. If his
son, Bukola, wins on Saturday, his opponents have every reason to begin to
sing, 'long live the king.'
Bola Tinubu
Few days before the elections, only a few people would be willing to bet
against AD's victory in the southwest. Despite the crisis that has rocked
the party in the last four years, many believe that the Afenifere factor is
still strong enough to keep the party on top. But the results proved
bookmakers wrong. At the end of Saturday, AD had lost all its states, except
Lagos, which remains tightly under the party's control. For this, the state
governor, Asiwaju Bola Tinubu has been able to prove that he is really on
the ground and in charge despite the stiff challenge by the PDP.
Chimaroke Nnamani
The Son becomes a man. Before last Saturday's election, Chimaroke
Nnamani could be said to be fighting the battle of his life. Up against two
formidable forces: his erstwhile political godfather, Senator Jim Nwobodo,
who is also the presidential candidate of the UNPP, and the Catholic
institution in the state. Late 2001, a band of youth invaded the Catholic
Adoration Ground in Enugu. The church leadership believed the invaders were
sent by the governor. For this 'crime', the church believes he does not
deserve a second term. Last week's elections were to be the 'day of
judgement' for the governor. But in the end, the governor's party, PDP,
clinched two of the three Senatorial seats in the state. With this, Nnamani
was able to prove that he has grown to become his own man. Next weekend's
election will however be the final test.
INEC/Guobadia
Before now, the INEC could not be said to be popular among
Nigerians. Its failure to conduct the local government elections last year,
its refusal to register new parties until compelled by the Supreme Court,
its inability to comply with the 2002 Electoral Act were seen as deliberate
acts to favour the government in power. All that has changed with the
orderly and peaceful elections. Not even late voting and pockets of violence
in some states like Rivers, Bayelsa, Delta and Edo could detract from the
success of the polls. And as confirmed by several commentators and foreign
observers and monitors, the Abel Guobadia-led INEC has done a good job
considering the fact that historically, conducting civilian- to-civilian
elections in Nigeria has never been successful. However, voices of protests
are being raised predictably from the AD and ANPP. Is this the usual
Nigerian style of not accepting defeat gracefully or was the election really
flawed?
Tafa Balogun/ Police
The police as led by Inspector-General, Tafa Balogun, deserves
commendation for the success of the poll. Against earlier fears, there were
no incidents grave enough to mar the credibility of the elections.
That the police would do a good job was not unexpected given its
excellent performance during the parties primaries in Abuja earlier in the
year. Tafa Balogun deserves special kudos for his impartiality considering
the nation's experience in 1983, when the then I.G was accused of deploying
the entire police machinery to aid the ruling party to rig the
elections.
The Voters: Keeping Faith Despite All Odds.
Nothing, perhaps, accords an election integrity more than huge voters'
turn-out. And that is precisely what the electorate gave last
Saturday. Regardless of the stature or strategy of candidates and the forces
behind them, there would be no elections if the people didn't go out to
vote.
The enthusiasm of the voters was not spurred by the performance of those
they voted for in 1999. The incumbent National Assembly spent more of its
time and energy on wranglings with the executive and staging one drama after
the other about scandals within the chambers. Last year, for instance, the
national budget could not be passed by the lawmakers.The campaign of those
struggling to supplant them was drab. Some never even bothered to
campaign. Yet the voters came out in their numbers to give the process
legitimacy. They are the ultimate winners.
The Losers...
Umar Na'Abba...
For a long time, the man has been fighting a battle of his life. He has
been described severally as a cat with nine lives. That he is still Speaker
of the Federal House of Representatives may be a surprise to himself and
everybody close to him. It appears his opponents finally waited for him at
the polls. One lesson Na'Abba may have learnt by now is that it is not easy
to fight against the establishment, no matter how strong you are.
In the days leading to the election, the PDP in Kano State pulled the
last sling. Many thought that as usual, the shot was going to miss him. But
he bowed this time around - a loser.
Jim I. Nwobodo
It appears that the fight between Jim Nwobodo and his "political
godson", Dr Chimaroke Nnamani has been won and lost. What will happen on
Saturday may be the final rites. Early in the Administration, the duo parted
ways and declared war on each other. Jim took charge of the greater number
of the State Legislators and made the house unmanageable. Everyone had
thought that the final showdown was going to be this general election as
both sides had on several occassions threatened fire and
brimstone. Surprisingly, Nwobodo took to the costly mistake of election
boycotts. A cleaver way to save face?
Emeka Odumegwu Ojukwu
The self-styled Igbo leader has lost another war. Everybody had thought
that Ojukwu's entry into the race was going to generally change the
political equation in the South Eastern States. Many thought he was going to
be the proverbial god that has the yam and the knife. Results at the last
election have shown that Ojukwu could only be a crowd puller for rallies,
without the knock-out punch. He has this coming Saturday to clear his
image.
