AD/AFENIFERE
The AD/Afenifere is the greatest loser in last Saturday's polls. In all
the South West States, with the exception of Lagos, the PDP made a clean
sweep of the seats.
The erosion of the South West traditional base of AD/Afenifere will lead
to the emergence of a new power block in Yorubaland under the leadership of
younger men. Is this the end of the politics of herd mentality where all
Yorubas troop into one political camp on the instruction of a powerful
leadership clique? They emerged flagbearers through a selection process
conducted by a corterie of Yoruba leadership under Chief Abraham
Adesanya. Their election was more or less a diktat from the Pan Yoruba
organisation, Afenifere.
AD Governors
In the past four years, the AD, the political puppet of Afenifere was
sphintered into two implacable factions. Chief Bola Tinubu of Lagos, more
than any south-west governor, symbolised the split, he on one side, the
Afenifere leaders on the other. He thus became the test case.
While he resisted the PDP onslaught successfully, his four colleagues
crumbled completely. Is this a foretaste of things to come this Saturday?

Original_Search_Title: South West : Muea Farmers Hailed For Quality Food Production
Headline: Cameroon: South West : Muea Farmers Hailed For Quality Food Production
Body_Text:
The representative of the South West Provincial Governor, Rev. Victor Ayuk, has told farmers of Muea in Buea to continue in their quantitative and qualitative food production efforts that have earned them a reputation beyond Cameroon.
Addressing farmers and observers who turned up to mark the, last 22nd World Food Day in Muea, the Governor's representative said he was bearer of President Paul Biya's encouragement to Muea farmers, explaining that a well-fed nation was a happy nation with enough energy to forge ahead in its development process.
Some 97 prizes in cash were distributed to farmers for excellence in qualitative food production as well as animal husbandry. The farmers conducted a magnificent mini-agric show of vegetables, yams, cocoyams, cassava and plantains. Also to mark the Day was a splendid exhibition of livestock such as goats, pigs, and fowls with the Limbe based Non-governmental Organisation (CERUT) making a spectacular show of domesticated rabbits and "Cutting Grass" which are valuable sources of proteins.
The South West Provincial Delegate of Agriculture, Mr. Mbwoge George told the farmers that he was particularly glad with Muea farmers who had taken full advantage of their good soil and the farming techniques offered them.
In his welcome speech the Traditional Ruler of Muea village, Chief David Ikome Molinge called on the State to industrialise farming for better yields. Chief Molinge explained that food from Muea market had found destinations in Senegal, Gabon, Central Africa Republic, Equatorial Guinea and other countries. It was an opportunity for Chief Molinge to plead with the Buea Rural Council to invest in the Muea Market where food is sold on bare mud. The new Mayor of the Buea Rural Council, Mbella Moki Charles was on hand and promised a closer concern to improve on the Muea Food Market.

Original_Search_Title: Forced to Go Home: Rwandan Immigrants in Tanzania
Headline: Africa: Forced to Go Home: Rwandan Immigrants in Tanzania
Body_Text:
Kigali — In early March this year, over 2,300 Rwandans arrived at the Kibungo
transit camp, a centre that receives Rwandan refugees returning from
Tanzania.
These were not the usual refugees. They were Rwandans living within
the local community near the border and had never been in the camps.
When the governments of Rwanda and Tanzania had signed an agreement
to repatriate all Rwandan nationals living in Tanzania as refugees,
these people had not considered themselves part of the target group.
Over several weeks in early March, they had been identified and
evicted as illegal immigrants.
"Most of them left Rwanda in the 1980's, some of them were born
there," Odette Nyiramirimo, the Secretary of State for Social Affairs
in the Ministry of Local Administration told Internews.
When we arrived at the camp, the returnees were placing their
complaints before Nyiramirimo and James Kimonyo, the governor of
Kibungo province.
Internews spoke to some of them.
"I was grazing livestock; they came and beat me up. In the confusion,
I was taken one way and the livestock in another. They took the money
I had in my pocket, and told me, 'Rwandan go home'. They took us to a
school compound and when we were many of us they put us in a pick up
and brought us to Rwanda," a bewildered young man told Internews.
"I do not know Rwanda, I was born in Tanzania, I do not know what to
do," he adds.
The President of the National Repatriation Commission, Sheik Abdul
Karim Harerimana told Internews that the move by the Tanzanian
government caught more than the evictees by surprise." We had not
anticipated this. We asked them to stop the process for some time so
that we can talk with them and work out the modalities of how it
should be done," Harerimana explained.
Internews asked the Tanzanian Ambassador to Rwanda, General Martin
Mwakalindile, the rationale behind the evictions. He explained that
the forcible return of persons categorized as illegal immigrants to
their countries of origin was a national exercise not limited to
Rwandans alone.
"We have very many refugees, Rwandans, Burundians, Somalis and
others. Like all other countries in this region we also have a lot of
illegal immigrants.
Unfortunately, these illegal immigrants are often linked to criminal
activities. They take advantage of the presence of genuine refugees.
In this case, we had Rwandans who were here illegally, and they had
to go home. We are going to continue with this campaign,"
Mwakalindile stated.
At the camp, the evicted Rwandans had barely enough cover from the
rain that pelted them during our visit. Few had umbrellas or warm
clothes.
The reception centre where they were gathered consists of two main
tents and two large open air shelters used for item distribution. On
one side are several make-shift toilets.
Inside the tents women are preparing the midday meals. Most pots have
yams mixed with bananas, or maize and beans. The fires have to be lit
outside, or in a small area in one tent with earth dug cooking
stoves. Due to the congestion, most of the food has to be tended in
the rain.
"There is a lot of pain here; there is also a lot of confusion. The
people here feel that they have been treated as criminals. The
eviction was not handled very well, so we are talking to the
Tanzanian government to see how to handle this better next time,"
Nyiramirimo told Internews.
If the adults are upset, the children seem to be oblivious about it.
They play in the mud and rain, while others queue up at the United
Nations High Commission for Refugees (UNHCR) truck to fetch water.
These are the lucky ones.
"I left behind two children. One is six and the other is five. My
husband was away. He had gone to see a family who were recently
bereaved. I do not know where he is. When they picked me up I had
gone to fetch water. I want to go back and see if the children have
rejoined their father, I do not know what has happened to them," one
woman told the officials.
She is not alone. Several other women in the group have also lost
children and some have been separated with their spouses.
Nyiramirimo tries to reassure them. "Please register yourself, we
talked to the Tanzanian authorities and those of you who left young
children and property will have a period in which you can go and
check on your property.
Those of you who want to come back can come back, those of you who
would prefer to stay in Tanzania must get the proper papers allowing
you to do so," she explained.
Mwakalindile admits that the forced repatriation may not been handled
appropriately.
"We are investigating the allegations that some people were
maltreated. It is possible that some of the officials treated the
people badly for whatever reason; these are going to be punished," he
promised.
He however explains that there are cases where the allegations made
are untrue. "Some said they had lived in Tanzania for over 20 years,
but it was later found that the papers they had were not in order.
What we have told them is if they want to stay in Tanzania they have
to go through the legal process and get their papers. There were
allegations that naturalized Rwandans have been forcefully
repatriated, but I think that these are few mistakes; most members of
the group were illegal immigrants" he added.
The evicted Rwandans were given plastic sheeting, blankets and
cooking pots as well as food to last three months by UNHCR although
they do not fall under the organisation's mandate. They will be taken
to their original districts where the local officials will resettle
them. But that poses a problem. Those born in Tanzania have no idea
where to resettle. Moreover in a small country facing population
pressure, they fear their return could spark land conflicts.
"I have lived out of Rwanda for so long, I am afraid that if I show
up at home and try to ask for land from my brothers we will end up in
a conflict, please advise me what to do," one evictee asks
Nyiramirimo.
"For now, try to talk to your family members and if that fails then
go to the district officials and see if there is communal land that
they can assign you. But even if you do not get land you can find a
way of earning a living. There is a lot of work to be done in
Rwanda," she tells him.
"Some people tell us that we should leave those who are outside to
stay there since we are a small country and our resources are meager,
but we do not agree with them," Harerimana tells Internews. "Every
Rwandan has a right to come back home, Rwanda may be small but there
is space for all of us," he asserts.
In a country where most land holdings are less than an acre, those
returning will have to jostle just to get space to build a house.
Harerimana says that in any other country the returnees would remain
refugees; people who have to ask permission to live there, but in
Rwanda, however difficult their situation, they would be home. One
has to wonder if that is enough.

Original_Search_Title: Safety Nets for Society's Weakest
Headline: Nigeria: Safety Nets for Society's Weakest
Body_Text:
Lagos — The desire to meet the needs of the poor and to keep their hope alive, fired the founder of the Global Success International Fellowship, Brother Chima Emmanuel Okereke to set up the King David Charity Foundation.
Okereke told Thislife recently that the foundation is basically concerned with the plight of motherless babies, widows, orphans and the destitute in the society. According to him: "you must endeavour to feed the hungry before he would be able to listen to the word of God, else it would be an exercise in futility".
The young preacher who is poised to better the lots of the poor in the society has made success of the charity foundation, responding to distress calls. the King David Charity foundation during last December donated 61 bags of rice, 175 bags of salt, several tubers of yam, 40 cartons of milk, 20 cartons of tin tomatoes, drugs, and textile materials to several widows, orphans, and the aged in the society.
After the Idumagbo bomb blast, which claimed several lives, the foundation made a cash donation of N52,000, 37 bags of rice, drugs and other valuable items. On hand to receive the items was the Public Relations Officer (PRO) of the Lagos State Relief Agency, Mrs. Dotun Okegie.
The PRO who represented the State Commissioner for Special Duties, Mrs. Teju Philips, urged other corporate bodies and churches to emulate the good work of the King David Charity Foundation.
Okereke did not leave them with material things only. He prayed for and assured them of God's continuos protection hence they survived the disaster.
"For the fact that your lives were spared by God was an indication that He still loves you despite your misfortune. " Okereke added. He said as part of religious responsibility, "we believe that we should give out our widows mite to the less privileged to alleviate some of their immediate needs. It is about time Nigerians from all walks of life saw it as a duty to demonstrate good citizenship by donating whatever they have to the needy in the society rather than taking pleasures in throwing meaningless parties."
He reminded Nigerians of the universal law of reciprocity, "you give, God gives in return. When you plant a seed, the ground yields a harvest," he said, urging Nigerians to give so that they would be given. He berated those he described as "self-centered men and women with the culture of who cares?" It is his desire that people who are comfortable should be able to put a smile on the faces of others.
He however proffered a way forward. "The poor could be empowered by increasing their capacity to tackle poverty." For example,he said his Fellowship has long embarked on project to establish rehabilitation centre. In the centres, "people would be trained to become self-reliant in various skills and vocations thereby making them relevant to the society". He said the foundation also plans to render a voluntary counselling and testing programmes to fight HIV/AIDS. At the centre, families living with the condition would be counselled and prayed for. He called on the government and well meaning individuals and corporate bodies to lend support to this noble venture.
"These special people need our love, care and support. It is therefore good if once in a while we can spare a thought for them. And the only way we can do this, is to take care of them," he said.
This year, Okereke has planned to hold a crusade at the National Stadium to coincide with his 30 bithday, in December. He said lives are going to be impacted, while his full mission to propagate the works of God on earth will fully commence in earnest in 2004. He likened this analogy to when Jesus Christ started His full ministry on earth.
At his church in Lagos, there have been testimonies to the fact that there has been harvest of miracles of deliverance and healing. Such testimonies include a case of Mr. Benjamin Edigbo of Umuoru Oriuzor village in Ezza North of Ebonyi State. He suffered from chronic insanity and got permanently healed with the use of anointing oil and a miracle handkerchief blessed by Okereke.
Mr. Ignatius C. Ubam, a businessman, who lives in Akesan, Lagos, also received his miracle. He had a terrible battle with the men of the underworld who shot at him and shattered his left leg. Ubam disclosed that he was disposed of cash and his personal belongings, including his car. Having lost hope, he was directed by "a good Samaritan" from hospital bed at the Orthopaedic to Okereke's church. He said the power in the anointing oil knows no bound. In a similar manner, God proved himself worthy of trust as Mr. and Mrs Ojike of Lawal Street, Olodi Apapa, Lagos, who were childless for 17 years, had their hope finally restored when they where blessed with a bouncing baby girl they christened Debora.
For the young man of God, a time like this demands loving hearts and ready hands, therefore people who have deep concern for the plight of others must be heralded and encouraged.
Okereke expressed the wish that Nigerians would learn to be more charitable in thoughts, speech, actions, judgement, attitudes and prayers.
"Think charitably of the less privileged, your friends, your neighbours, your relations, and even your enemies. For Global Success International Fellowship, we are ready to continue to render help to the down throdden.
The evangelist also pledged his continous support to the National Orthopaedic Hospital, Igbobi, Lagos. At present he has moved briskly to assist those with shattered legs. Ubam happened to be one of the benefactors. "I had lost all hope, thinking I was going to have my leg amputated, but God was merciful enough to rescued me", said Ubam.

Original_Search_Title: Blood in the Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Blood in the Delta
Body_Text:
Delta — No fewer than 100 people die in the unsung war in Nigeria's Niger
Delta as soldiers face stiff resistance of youths wielding
sophisticated weapons.
Not many cities have witnessed that type of siege before. Certainly
not Warri, the embattled Niger-Delta city noted for its light crude
oil. From its entry point at Warri-Sapele Road and Warri-Ughelli road
to the city centre at Enerhen junction, the woes of the city were
palpable. In the morning of 24 March, a heavy traffic snarl brought
the ever boisterous city to a sad paralysis. Travelling from Agbarho
to Warri, a distance of less than 15 kilometres was particularly
agonising. At Willy Camp, combined team of eagle-eyed soldiers and
mobile policemen mounted a zig-zag road block that forced motorists
to contend with a single lane. Edafe Fineface, a commercial bus
driver, desperate for a quick trip, did the usual, by skidding his
contraption along the sideways. But he was soon forced back to sanity
by an unusual scene.
A middle aged man, with arms stretched forward and face downward was
lying on his belly, near a motorcycle. Metres away, a battle ready
soldier aimed his gun at him. At the other side of the road and some
distance into the swampy rain forest, another soldier in military
fatigue, manned his machine gun with gusto. Several other soldiers
simply took position as hundreds of commuters, without prompting,
raised their hands in surrender to the occupation forces that had
plagued the entire Warri landscape, as a tripartite war among the
Itsekiri, Ijaw and the armed forces enters its third week. "When will
Warri know peace . . .?" Fineface asked when he found his voice.
Indeed, few days before, deep melancholy had pervaded the air at the
usually lively Effurun Roundabout. That evening as men of the 7th
Amphibious battalion, converged at their favourite rendezvous to laze
away the evening, with booze, their taste buds literally became
bitter. Corporal Musa rendered a story that left no room for a
guffaw. He reeled out gory details of how two other members of the
"joint" were cut down by the superior fire power of rampaging Ijaw
militia. Fraught with the perplexity of a man who had walked through
the valley of the shadows of death, Musa provided a clear narration
of how his company was caught in an ambush off the Shell's Jones
Creek station. "Those boys, I fear them," he continued his account,
"we no even know where dem from comot and dem all carry machine (a
barracks slang for machine gun). In fact, na God save us if not,
things for wowo pass so." The same issue dominated discussions in the
barracks. Wives and wards of soldiers were engaged in hush talks as
news of death of soldiers deployed to the creeks on an escort mission
filtered in.
But not only soldiers, drafted in as peace makers, have suffered
casualties in the war. Both the Ijaw and Itsekiri have been burrying
their kinsmen the way farmers plant yam seedlings. Apart from a total
of ten soldiers said to have died in the zone, the Ijaw said it had
lost 58 men, while the Itsekiri puts their number of deaths at 100 as
at press time. What is more, both sides claim their men are being
kidnapped at an alarming rate. "You must be very careful the way you
move about town particularly about boarding an Okada. They are using
Okada riders to kidnap people on both sides." a youth leader told
TheNEWS in the war weary town. So scarry is the situation that Chief
of Army Staff, COAS, General Alexander Ogomudia had to abandon his
cosy office in Abuja and relocate to Warri to supervise the Delta
operations.
But the attendant economic loss from the war has remained unhalted.
In all, oil conglomerates have cut an unprecedented 800,000 barrels
in daily oil production. This accounts for almost 40 per cent of
Nigeria's production capacity of about two million barrels per day,
bpd. Nigeria is a major player in global oil sector being the sixth
largest producer of the commodity and the fifth largest supplier of
crude to the United States. Most of the oil comes from the much
neglected Niger Delta region of the country. But the zone is familiar
with as much blood as it has known for oil. "Self preservation is the
first law of nature. So we are asking our members to leave the
troubled zone" said Joseph Akinlaja, secretary-general of National
Union of Petroleum and Natural Gas Workers, NUPENG.
The variegated and sometimes exaggerated accounts of blood letting in
the ongoing progrom drops anchor at the Delta sea port of Koko. Like
in 1986 when the rustic Itshekiri community played host to imported
toxic materials from Italy, the Koko shore line has once again become
the focal point of unsightly matter. Headless bodies and dismembered
human parts litter the coast, after making a floating voyage from the
adjoining Islands of Madangho, Ijaghala, Ugboegungun, Aronton and
other Itshekiri settlements along the Escravos bedline. But what
accounts for the current face-off ? That depends on which side of the
divide your informant belongs. However, it is incontrovertible that
the current bloodlettings have both political and economic
underpinnings.
Like a sore-thumb which has refused to heal, the Warri inter-ethnic
wars have remained intractable since its debut in 1997, over the
relocation of newly created Warri South-West local government
headquarters from Ogbe-Ijaw to Ogidingbe. The history of the current
wave of violence has its foundation in the last ward delineation
exercise by the Independent National Electoral Commission, INEC.
According to reports, the Ijaw in Warri-South LGA issued a statement
distancing itself from the voters registration exercise over
perceived imbalance in the ward exercise, which it insisted favoured
their Itshekiri neighbours. Pleas by prominent Ijaw leaders like
Chief Edwin Clark and Chief Tunde Smooth for the group to participate
in the process fell on deaf ears as the militant youth stuck to their
guns. The expiration of seven days ultimatum in early March brought a
fresh perspective to the deteriorating situation in the Delta.
Fighting soon broke out between the Itshekiri and the Ijaws.
In that encounter, the Ijaws claimed they lost four fighters while
opponents also mourned their deaths. But as common in such wars,
anarchy soon became the rule rather than the exception.
Armed youths harassed and kidnapped oil workers offshore, thus
disrupting activities in the nation's cash cow. They even shut down a
helicopter owned by Shell. Finally, soldiers from the 7th Amphibious
Battalion in Effurun were deployed to accompany oil barge to platform
offshore, setting the stage for an imminent clash with ethnic sea
roving malitia. On Saturday 15 March, a detachment of soldiers who
insisted on towing an illegal oil batch along the Forcados axis had a
gun battle with the gun men employed as vigilante. Eye witness
account revealed that the ensuing battle was stunning - the soldiers
were readily overpowered by the combat ready youths. At the end of
the onslaught, three soldiers lay dead with no casualty on the side
of the machine gun wielding Ijaw youths. An enraged Brig. General
Harry Oke, commander of the 4th Brigade in Benin which oversees the
Effurun battalion ordered for a fresh offensive against the youths.
In response to the orders from the Brigade headquarters, Col. Gar
Dogo, commander of the 7th Amphibious Battalion deployed some of his
best hands for another operation. That was on Monday 17 March. But
the militia armed with a fore-knowledge of the impending strike also
turned their spleen on hapless Itshekiri neighbours whom they held
responsible for their imminent woes. Thus while the 7th battalion was
preparing for a showdown at the Escravos tank farm, the bandits
descended on Itshekiri settlements along the Escravos line. Armed
with sophisticated machine guns and rocket propelled grenades, the
disgruntled youths, moving in a convoy of 30 speed boats, invaded and
razed Madangho Ogidingben, Aronton, Ijaghala and Odeugborodu.
Encouraged by the seeming success of the previous day raid, the
youths retreated to their Okerenkoko and Opuraza hideouts in
preparation for what an independent source described as "the final
attack." With a strength of 50 speed boats and high calibre machine
guns, the sea vikings made a bold attempt to capture the Escravos
tank farm. However a combined team of soldiers and naval policemen
numbering about 2000 battled for six hours to put the rampaging
youths at bay. Two days later, the bandits stormed the Itshekiri
village of Upomami and the popular back of Fence settlement located
close to the Chevron Escravos yard. At the end of the attack, scores
of villagers were killed while thousands were rendered homeless.
Modupe Asha, a nursing mother who is among the many victims of the
attack airlifted to Warri by the Chevron chopper recounts her ordeal
to the magazine: "They came from the sea shore burning houses and
shooting at every thing they see. I had to escape with my children
when I discovered that the soldiers could not help us."
Embarrassed by the seeming inability of his men to repel the Delta
insurrection, Ogomudia arrived in Warri last week to take charge of
the operations. Military sources disclose that the COAS, upon his
arrival, summoned all the top military brass connected with the
crisis. The meeting convened, to review field tactics had in
attendance GOC 2nd mechanized division of the army, Ibadan,
Major-General Ndefo; Commander of the 4th Infantry Brigade in Benin,
Brigadier-General Henry Oke; commanding officer of the Naval base,
NNS Umalokun Navy Captain Titus Awoyemi and Dogo, commander of the
7th Amphibious battalion. The magazine gathered that the army chief
was said to have been furious over the death of 12 soldiers in the
hands of "bloody civilians" blaming the death to poor management of
the crisis by officers on ground. The COAS was said to have tongue
lashed the Effurun battalion commander for his slow response to the
situation. In response, the officers told the head of the army that
they were ill-equipped for the operation. The officers informed their
boss that the "V" shaped boats and the limited Offshore Patrol
Vehicles (OPV) in use by the military were not for shallow and rough
Warri creeks as against the flat-bottom high powered supersonic boats
the Ijaw youths used. The military brass maintained that their
inadequate familiarity with the terrain in addition to the high
calibre weapon of the Ijaw did not help matters. At the end of the
frank talk, it was agreed that a naval ship should be sent to the
Delta to clear the area.
The initiative seemed to pay off. Ijaw youths on Monday accused the
soldiers of raiding three of their settlements the previous day. The
activists accused soldiers of firing indiscriminately on residents of
Okpelama, Tebujor and Opuodebubor. They said ten people died from the
raids, leaving 16 others seriously wounded.The military in its
defence denied the charge and argued it has used minimun force in
curtailing the disturbances.
The defence is deliberate. In Warri there is a growing fear among
residents that the city could fall a victim of military tyranny in a
manner that could dwarf the fate of Odi. "We are living in fear of
what might happen next" a trader told TheNEWS at the popular PTI
junction in Warri. Analysts believe that fear is justified. In
November 1999, some 50 army trucks trudled through the forest of
Bayelsa state into the hearts of Kolokuma and Opokuma to avenge the
killing of 12 soldiers by irate youths in Odi. When news about the
killings reached Aso Rock, an enraged President Olusegun Obasanjo
gave the Bayelsa state governor Diepriye Alaimaseiyeigha a two-week
ultimatum to fish out the killers and hand them over to the long arms
of the law or face stiff sanctions. At the expiration of the
deadline, the federal government moved in its war weapons for an
operation whose casualty figure is still a matter of conjecture. At
the end of 26 November massacre in Odi, only a church and a bank
building survived. Nothing that had life remained in the town. Chuba
Okadigbo, then Senate President who had led an inspection tour of the
leveled town was too shocked to find his voice. "We are mourning now.
So I can not say anything" Okadigbo managed to utter. The government
was to demonstrate its capacity to hit an ant with a sledgehammer
when months later it sent troops to raze Zaki-Biam another restive
community in Benue state.
Since the Delta crisis began in 1997, the Nigerian navy and military
have displayed lack of expertice in handling the crisis. In 1998
militant Ijaw youths ransacked and looted the amoury of the Warri
naval base, NNS Umalokun with little or no resistance from officers
and men of the base. The Egbesu Boys, believed to be invincible have
remained a thorn in the flesh of the military. Since the 1998 Kaima
declaration which seek among other items to establish the territorial
annexation of all riverine areas within the Ijaw control, the group
has demonstrated its ability to match force for force with the
Nigerian military.
Yet, the navy has remained ill-equipped to handle the situation.
Investigations by TheNEWS revealed that the Nigerian Navy with 15 war
ships and a strength of about 15,000 personnel including ratings and
officers has remained largely neglected in the last decade. That is
not all. The porous maritime border has over the years provided the
militia the opportunity of having a steady supply of sophisticated
arms and ammunition from the high seas. The real attempt to equip the
navy was first demonstrated during the Shagari regime with the
acquisition of NNS Aradu a MERO 360 multi-purpose frigate to fortify
the fleet of the service. The ship bought at a staggering $350
million, has remained grounded for years. Today, Aradu which means
thunder cannot strike any force of thunder. On land, the military has
proven less effective in handling the intractable orgy of violence
that has plagued the western axis of the Niger-Delta.
Shortly before the recent Ijaw/Itshekiri conflict, soldiers deployed
to keep peace in the Okere area of the city were said to have watched
helplessly as militant youth from Urhobo and Itshekiri engaged each
other in a fratricidal show of shame. According to reports, trouble
began on Friday 31 January at the Warri stadium when PDP delegates to
the Warri south repeat senatorial primaries could not agree on the
format to be adopted for the impending election. However some
aggrieved youths, disappointed that the PDP panel chose the 10 wards
option instead of 12, marched to the Okere area of Warri and
unleashed mayhem on hapless Itsekiris. Several houses belonging to
the Itsekiri were razed along the Ugborikoko Road and Okumagba
Avenue. Smokes graced the Warri skyline. In no time, armed Urhobo and
Itsekiri militia men took positions at the notorious shooting range
at Okere and began what observers described as the "fiercest" gun
battle ever witnessed in the history of ethnic conflict in the town.
Soon, the troubled Okere and nearby area were abandoned as commuters
scrambled for safety. By the end of Friday, five persons, including a
soldier, three young men and a 10-year-old girl were confirmed dead.
On Saturday 1 February, it was the turn of the Itshekiri youth to
extract their pound of flesh. That night malitia Itsekiri men sneaked
into Urhobo lines around Okumagba Avenue burning the Idama hotel
belonging to the influential Benjamin Okumagba, president of the
Urhobo Progressive Union who was said to be away on medical check-up
in the UK. A nearby police station was also razed in the carnage
which left 150,000 people homeless and 150 houses burnt.
However unlike in the past skirmishes in which casualty was limited
to the burning of houses and looting, the recent assault has the
potential of grounding the nation's economy. Though no fresh
hostilities were reported as of late last week the Ijaw boys are
still holding sway in Escravos Waterways despite the reported
deployment of a naval ship. Hostility in Upomami village resulted in
loss of five persons, two soldiers and three Nigerian employees of
Elf Petroleum Nigeria Limited. According to a statement from the
company's Executive Director, Human Resources and Communication,
Emmanuel Chiejina "During the evacuation, March 22, company
facilities and personnel as well as the village of Upomami were
attacked, resulting in loss of five persons." In addition, crude,
worth over $10 million from 10 flow stations belonging to Shell
Petroleum Development Company (SPDC), TotalFina Elf and Chevron
Nigeria Limited, CNL was shut last week due to invasion of men said
to be Ijaw youths."
Early last week, Chevron had closed down 440,000 barrels per day
crude oil flow stations along with 285 million cubic feet of natural
gas in the swampy region, and later allowed its workers to leave the
zone for their safety. SPDC on its part has shut over 10 flow
stations with resultant loss of over 370,000 barrels per day.
Industry watchers fear that if the crisis persists, Nigeria might
miss the rush for its highly rated light crude oil in the
international market. While the international oil market is saturated
with low quality crude, it is Nigeria's cude, high gasoline that has
endeared it to refiners across the globe.
With the Iraqi daily output of about 2.5 million barrels per day
already out alongside Kuwait's over 700,000 bpd from one of its flow
stations located near the Iraqi border, Nigeria, analysts argue
should be the new bride in the industry. But the Warri war has left a
gloomy mark in its trail. Dan Ekpebide, Ijaw activist leader was
quoted as theatening to burn down flowstations currently under the
control of the militia. The group, at press time, had the control of
six Chevron Texaco installations, three Shell installations and two
TotalFinalElf facilities. The group warned that only the withdrawal
of military presence and a revisit of the ward delienation could save
the installations from imminent blow-up. Incidentally, the country
has experienced acute fuel shortages since the beginning of March,
even as a general election is due in April.
While the casualty figure and negative impact of the crisis continue
to mount, leaders at both sides of the crisis intensified their
tirades. According to Biawei Abadebi, president of the Ijaw Youth
Council, a total of 58 people of Ijaw extraction have been murdered
by what he described as the Itshekiri sponsored army attack on Ijaw
villages. "The fact that the Itshekiris are behind this military
oppression and brutality is further evidenced by Messrs Billy, Tueyo
and Omaghomi as drivers of the Navy Gunboats. Right now it is the
Itsekiri boys that are stationed with the army at every strategic
check point to identify the Ijaws for maltreatment," Adadebi declared
in a release which also continued a seven-day ultimatum for the
immediate removal of the Navy Commanding Officer NNS Umalokun, Navy
Captain Awoyemi and Major Irabor of the 7 Amphibious Battalion. But
frontline Itsekiri Youth leader and president of the Iwere
Development Association, Omolubi Newuwumi, has a different view. "Let
nobody deceive you the Ijaws have been the aggressors in this
conflict. Over the years they have accumulated a stock pile of arms
and ammunition. Incidentally, while the whole world focused on the
conflict in the Gulf, the Ijaws unleashed mayhem and genocidal attack
on our people, looting their properties and confiscating their
homelands." Corroborating Newuwumi's views, Alex Eyenho spokesman of
the Allison Ayida-led central Itsekiri Association of Lagos told the
magazine in Warri that the action of their Ijaw neighbours has all
the trappings of a terrorist group. "The United Nations and indeed
the international community should please take note of the prostrate
posture of the federal government in this crisis and help save
Itsekiri nation from the unprovoked hostility of the Ijaw. They must
not wait for another Chechneya in Nigeria before they send assistance
to us." Perhaps the prostrate state of the federal government and the
military over the Warri crisis is excercabated by Col. Emeka
Onwuemegbu, army director of public relations in response to a
question posed by a reporter in Warri last week. "You said you've
arrested some youths with an AK47" how come you've found only just
one rifle, whereas we know that those men have an amoury load of
AK47? And the military spokesman retorted: "I think you should even
commend us for getting something at all rather than condemn our
effort."
Both the Itshekiri and Ijaw and indeed all the groups in the Niger
Delta have had their fate tied to the same apron string of poverty
and economic neglect. The region produces more than 80 per cent of
the country's wealth. Ironically, a large percentage of its people
live below poverty line, unable to afford more than a meal per day,
while affluence practically dwell, in the neighbourhoods. The groups
have deplored this situation and have even watered their struggles
with flow of blood - the likes of Isaac Adaka Boro of Niger Delta
Volunteer Force and Ken Saro Wiwa, the diminutive Ogoni crusader who
added colour to the struggle.
But, the living have found little or no strength in unity. Late last
year, both Ijaw and Itshekiri women defied ethnic differences and
collaborated to wage a non violent war against the oil companies. For
months all over the Niger Delta the women thronged the
multinational's offices to protest the pauperisation of their
husbands. That was then. Now, their men have turned lethal weapons of
war against one another. When will the case of 'brother killing
brother' come to an end? ot many cities have witnessed that type of

Harry Oke, commander of the 4th Brigade in Benin which oversees the
Effurun battalion ordered for a fresh offensive against the youths.
In response to the orders from the Brigade headquarters, Col. Gar
Dogo, commander of the 7th Amphibious Battalion deployed some of his
best hands for another operation. That was on Monday 17 March. But
the militia armed with a fore-knowledge of the impending strike also
turned their spleen on hapless Itshekiri neighbours whom they held
responsible for their imminent woes. Thus while the 7th battalion was
preparing for a showdown at the Escravos tank farm, the bandits
descended on Itshekiri settlements along the Escravos line. Armed
with sophisticated machine guns and rocket propelled grenades, the
disgruntled youths, moving in a convoy of 30 speed boats, invaded and
razed Madangho Ogidingben, Aronton, Ijaghala and Odeugborodu.
Encouraged by the seeming success of the previous day raid, the
youths retreated to their Okerenkoko and Opuraza hideouts in
preparation for what an independent source described as "the final
attack." With a strength of 50 speed boats and high calibre machine
guns, the sea vikings made a bold attempt to capture the Escravos
tank farm. However a combined team of soldiers and naval policemen
numbering about 2000 battled for six hours to put the rampaging
youths at bay. Two days later, the bandits stormed the Itshekiri
village of Upomami and the popular back of Fence settlement located
close to the Chevron Escravos yard. At the end of the attack, scores
of villagers were killed while thousands were rendered homeless.
Modupe Asha, a nursing mother who is among the many victims of the
attack airlifted to Warri by the Chevron chopper recounts her ordeal
to the magazine: "They came from the sea shore burning houses and
shooting at every thing they see. I had to escape with my children
when I discovered that the soldiers could not help us."
Embarrassed by the seeming inability of his men to repel the Delta
insurrection, Ogomudia arrived in Warri last week to take charge of
the operations. Military sources disclose that the COAS, upon his
arrival, summoned all the top military brass connected with the
crisis. The meeting convened, to review field tactics had in
attendance GOC 2nd mechanized division of the army, Ibadan,
Major-General Ndefo; Commander of the 4th Infantry Brigade in Benin,
Brigadier-General Henry Oke; commanding officer of the Naval base,
NNS Umalokun Navy Captain Titus Awoyemi and Dogo, commander of the
7th Amphibious battalion. The magazine gathered that the army chief
was said to have been furious over the death of 12 soldiers in the
hands of "bloody civilians" blaming the death to poor management of
the crisis by officers on ground. The COAS was said to have tongue
lashed the Effurun battalion commander for his slow response to the
situation. In response, the officers told the head of the army that
they were ill-equipped for the operation. The officers informed their
boss that the "V" shaped boats and the limited Offshore Patrol
Vehicles (OPV) in use by the military were not for shallow and rough
Warri creeks as against the flat-bottom high powered supersonic boats
the Ijaw youths used. The military brass maintained that their
inadequate familiarity with the terrain in addition to the high
calibre weapon of the Ijaw did not help matters. At the end of the
frank talk, it was agreed that a naval ship should be sent to the
Delta to clear the area.
The initiative seemed to pay off. Ijaw youths on Monday accused the
soldiers of raiding three of their settlements the previous day. The
activists accused soldiers of firing indiscriminately on residents of
Okpelama, Tebujor and Opuodebubor. They said ten people died from the
raids, leaving 16 others seriously wounded.The military in its
defence denied the charge and argued it has used minimun force in
curtailing the disturbances.
The defence is deliberate. In Warri there is a growing fear among
residents that the city could fall a victim of military tyranny in a
manner that could dwarf the fate of Odi. "We are living in fear of
what might happen next" a trader told TheNEWS at the popular PTI
junction in Warri. Analysts believe that fear is justified. In
November 1999, some 50 army trucks trudled through the forest of
Bayelsa state into the hearts of Kolokuma and Opokuma to avenge the
killing of 12 soldiers by irate youths in Odi. When news about the
killings reached Aso Rock, an enraged President Olusegun Obasanjo
gave the Bayelsa state governor Diepriye Alaimaseiyeigha a two-week
ultimatum to fish out the killers and hand them over to the long arms
of the law or face stiff sanctions. At the expiration of the
deadline, the federal government moved in its war weapons for an
operation whose casualty figure is still a matter of conjecture. At
the end of 26 November massacre in Odi, only a church and a bank
building survived. Nothing that had life remained in the town. Chuba
Okadigbo, then Senate President who had led an inspection tour of the
leveled town was too shocked to find his voice. "We are mourning now.
So I can not say anything" Okadigbo managed to utter. The government
was to demonstrate its capacity to hit an ant with a sledgehammer
when months later it sent troops to raze Zaki-Biam another restive
community in Benue state.
Since the Delta crisis began in 1997, the Nigerian navy and military
have displayed lack of expertice in handling the crisis. In 1998
militant Ijaw youths ransacked and looted the amoury of the Warri
naval base, NNS Umalokun with little or no resistance from officers
and men of the base. The Egbesu Boys, believed to be invincible have
remained a thorn in the flesh of the military. Since the 1998 Kaima
declaration which seek among other items to establish the territorial
annexation of all riverine areas within the Ijaw control, the group
has demonstrated its ability to match force for force with the
Nigerian military.
Yet, the navy has remained ill-equipped to handle the situation.
Investigations by TheNEWS revealed that the Nigerian Navy with 15 war
ships and a strength of about 15,000 personnel including ratings and
officers has remained largely neglected in the last decade. That is
not all. The porous maritime border has over the years provided the
militia the opportunity of having a steady supply of sophisticated
arms and ammunition from the high seas. The real attempt to equip the
navy was first demonstrated during the Shagari regime with the
acquisition of NNS Aradu a MERO 360 multi-purpose frigate to fortify
the fleet of the service. The ship bought at a staggering $350
million, has remained grounded for years. Today, Aradu which means
thunder cannot strike any force of thunder. On land, the military has
proven less effective in handling the intractable orgy of violence
that has plagued the western axis of the Niger-Delta.
Shortly before the recent Ijaw/Itshekiri conflict, soldiers deployed
to keep peace in the Okere area of the city were said to have watched
helplessly as militant youth from Urhobo and Itshekiri engaged each
other in a fratricidal show of shame. According to reports, trouble
began on Friday 31 January at the Warri stadium when PDP delegates to
the Warri south repeat senatorial primaries could not agree on the
format to be adopted for the impending election. However some
aggrieved youths, disappointed that the PDP panel chose the 10 wards
option instead of 12, marched to the Okere area of Warri and
unleashed mayhem on hapless Itsekiris. Several houses belonging to
the Itsekiri were razed along the Ugborikoko Road and Okumagba
Avenue. Smokes graced the Warri skyline. In no time, armed Urhobo and
Itsekiri militia men took positions at the notorious shooting range
at Okere and began what observers described as the "fiercest" gun
battle ever witnessed in the history of ethnic conflict in the town.
Soon, the troubled Okere and nearby area were abandoned as commuters
scrambled for safety. By the end of Friday, five persons, including a
soldier, three young men and a 10-year-old girl were confirmed dead.
On Saturday 1 February, it was the turn of the Itshekiri youth to
extract their pound of flesh. That night malitia Itsekiri men sneaked
into Urhobo lines around Okumagba Avenue burning the Idama hotel
belonging to the influential Benjamin Okumagba, president of the
Urhobo Progressive Union who was said to be away on medical check-up
in the UK. A nearby police station was also razed in the carnage
which left 150,000 people homeless and 150 houses burnt.
However unlike in the past skirmishes in which casualty was limited
to the burning of houses and looting, the recent assault has the
potential of grounding the nation's economy. Though no fresh
hostilities were reported as of late last week the Ijaw boys are
still holding sway in Escravos Waterways despite the reported
deployment of a naval ship. Hostility in Upomami village resulted in
loss of five persons, two soldiers and three Nigerian employees of
Elf Petroleum Nigeria Limited. According to a statement from the
company's Executive Director, Human Resources and Communication,
Emmanuel Chiejina "During the evacuation, March 22, company
facilities and personnel as well as the village of Upomami were
attacked, resulting in loss of five persons." In addition, crude,
worth over $10 million from 10 flow stations belonging to Shell
Petroleum Development Company (SPDC), TotalFina Elf and Chevron
Nigeria Limited, CNL was shut last week due to invasion of men said
to be Ijaw youths."
Early last week, Chevron had closed down 440,000 barrels per day
crude oil flow stations along with 285 million cubic feet of natural
gas in the swampy region, and later allowed its workers to leave the
zone for their safety. SPDC on its part has shut over 10 flow
stations with resultant loss of over 370,000 barrels per day.
Industry watchers fear that if the crisis persists, Nigeria might
miss the rush for its highly rated light crude oil in the
international market. While the international oil market is saturated
with low quality crude, it is Nigeria's cude, high gasoline that has
endeared it to refiners across the globe.
With the Iraqi daily output of about 2.5 million barrels per day
already out alongside Kuwait's over 700,000 bpd from one of its flow
stations located near the Iraqi border, Nigeria, analysts argue
should be the new bride in the industry. But the Warri war has left a
gloomy mark in its trail. Dan Ekpebide, Ijaw activist leader was
quoted as theatening to burn down flowstations currently under the
control of the militia. The group, at press time, had the control of
six Chevron Texaco installations, three Shell installations and two
TotalFinalElf facilities. The group warned that only the withdrawal
of military presence and a revisit of the ward delienation could save
the installations from imminent blow-up. Incidentally, the country
has experienced acute fuel shortages since the beginning of March,
even as a general election is due in April.
While the casualty figure and negative impact of the crisis continue
to mount, leaders at both sides of the crisis intensified their
tirades. According to Biawei Abadebi, president of the Ijaw Youth
Council, a total of 58 people of Ijaw extraction have been murdered
by what he described as the Itshekiri sponsored army attack on Ijaw
villages. "The fact that the Itshekiris are behind this military
oppression and brutality is further evidenced by Messrs Billy, Tueyo
and Omaghomi as drivers of the Navy Gunboats. Right now it is the
Itsekiri boys that are stationed with the army at every strategic
check point to identify the Ijaws for maltreatment," Adadebi declared
in a release which also continued a seven-day ultimatum for the
immediate removal of the Navy Commanding Officer NNS Umalokun, Navy
Captain Awoyemi and Major Irabor of the 7 Amphibious Battalion. But
frontline Itsekiri Youth leader and president of the Iwere
Development Association, Omolubi Newuwumi, has a different view. "Let
nobody deceive you the Ijaws have been the aggressors in this
conflict. Over the years they have accumulated a stock pile of arms
and ammunition. Incidentally, while the whole world focused on the
conflict in the Gulf, the Ijaws unleashed mayhem and genocidal attack
on our people, looting their properties and confiscating their
homelands." Corroborating Newuwumi's views, Alex Eyenho spokesman of
the Allison Ayida-led central Itsekiri Association of Lagos told the
magazine in Warri that the action of their Ijaw neighbours has all
the trappings of a terrorist group. "The United Nations and indeed
the international community should please take note of the prostrate
posture of the federal government in this crisis and help save
Itsekiri nation from the unprovoked hostility of the Ijaw. They must
not wait for another Chechneya in Nigeria before they send assistance
to us." Perhaps the prostrate state of the federal government and the
military over the Warri crisis is excercabated by Col. Emeka
Onwuemegbu, army director of public relations in response to a
question posed by a reporter in Warri last week. "You said you've
arrested some youths with an AK47" how come you've found only just
one rifle, whereas we know that those men have an amoury load of
AK47? And the military spokesman retorted: "I think you should even
commend us for getting something at all rather than condemn our
effort."
Both the Itshekiri and Ijaw and indeed all the groups in the Niger
Delta have had their fate tied to the same apron string of poverty
and economic neglect. The region produces more than 80 per cent of
the country's wealth. Ironically, a large percentage of its people
live below poverty line, unable to afford more than a meal per day,
while affluence practically dwell, in the neighbourhoods. The groups
have deplored this situation and have even watered their struggles
with flow of blood - the likes of Isaac Adaka Boro of Niger Delta
Volunteer Force and Ken Saro Wiwa, the diminutive Ogoni crusader who
added colour to the struggle.
But, the living have found little or no strength in unity. Late last
year, both Ijaw and Itshekiri women defied ethnic differences and
collaborated to wage a non violent war against the oil companies. For
months all over the Niger Delta the women thronged the
multinational's offices to protest the pauperisation of their
husbands. That was then. Now, their men have turned lethal weapons of
war against one another. When will the case of 'brother killing
brother' come to an end?

Original_Search_Title: At a Time Like This ...
Headline: Nigeria: At a Time Like This ...
Body_Text:
SIX days away, the decision hour will arrive. Nigerians will make or unmake this country and themselves on that day. Saturday, April 12, 2003, will be a day of reckoning for Africa's biggest nation with the elastic potential of becoming a world power only if it can get its act right, will be making the crucial decision for its future. The general election which kicks off on that day is generally perceived as what will make or break democracy in the country or if things go wrong as pessimists fear, we may trip over cliff and ram into the dear process of dissolution.
Our main fear which pessimists latch on to, is that the run up to this election 2003 had been ominous. The political environment had become so heated that already assassinations of political opponents had taken place with disturbing regularities. Violence had rocked the country from East to West, and North to South in a fierce contest in which incumbents are bent on a second term while their opponents who perceived a good number of the incumbents as misfits and failures, are themselves fiercely waging a battle to unseat them.
So six days to the beginning of this polls, there is palpable disquiet and concern that there is a great possibility of things going critically wrong. It is against this backdrop that the hour of decision for the Nigerian people becomes crucial. The people will have to decide fundamentally two issues. The first is: have they been satisfied with the performance of politicians who have been in charge of governance in the last four years?
Electoral question
Secondly, is the federation as currently constituted, the best for our country. Although, the second issue is not a direct electoral question, it will form the basis on which the people will have to decide whether or not business should continue as usual. Again our immediate past history will give us an idea as to how best the Nigerian people should decide.
At the time of general elections in 1999, because of the fluidity of the situation and the anxiety to rid the system of military dictatorship, Nigerians in apparent hysteria chose the wrong set of people to take control of their political institution as a result, in few instances, less than credible individuals emerged as governors in several states of the Federation. The same situation could be said of the Houses of Assembly.
But by far, the worst collections of unsuitable characters found their way into the National Assembly. Little did the voters know what error they had made until these people began to perform. To put it mildly, the National Assembly is perhaps the biggest disgrace our new democracy has had to suffer. It was bad enough that known 419ers, drug barons and forgers made up a sizeable number of assembly men. But when they took over control of that institution, it became thoroughly corrupted.
The first speaker of the House was a forger. The first president of the Senate was in similar circumstances in controversy as to whether his name was Evan or Evans. And because the forgery implied in that controversy diminish the respectability of the Senate, Senator Evan/Evans had to go. So bad was the situation that within three years, the Senate had three presidents. We may spare ourselves the ordeal of characterizing the second and third president, because the magnitude of the controversies which surrounded them boggled the mind.
National disgrace
The long and short of the national disgrace which the National Assembly constituted is such that even without an election a third of its members are certainly not going to come back.
The executive arm of government throughout the country will individually and collectively have to answer to the parlous state of affairs, which their abuse of political mandate of the people had wrought in so much debilitating poverty, resulting from criminal mismanagement of our national resources. However, owing to the fact that majority of incumbent have lost touch with reality and the sensitivity of the people they govern, many of them who should be packing their bags to run away in shame for failures, still have the temerity of presenting themselves for re-elections.
Unfortunately, by the nature of our constitution, they have a right to a second term until and unless a court of law pronounces on their ineligibility arising from gross misconduct and unconstitutional behaviour in the course of performing their functions as chief executive of their States. As at the time of going to press, no court in the country has made a pronouncement to that effect. But come Saturday, the supreme court of the democratic process - the electorate will be called upon to make a pronouncement as to who deserves or does not deserve to continue in office or who will be deemed a better alternative to the incumbents who have failed woefully in all strata of our newly founded democratic institutions.
The choice becomes even difficult for the electorate because many green horns and unsuitable people have infiltrated the pack of the multitude seeking to replace the incumbents. There are many who have very good intentions, who are suitable and qualified to occupy the positions they are contesting. Therefore, a great responsibility devolves on the electorate to make a right choice, so that if we are removing incompetent incumbents, we do not replace them with worse materials.
Unarguably, there's a general clamour and determination for change. Yet it is not change for its own sake. But change for the improvement of the quality of those in charge of governance throughout the country. The fear that the electorate may speak a different language than the results of elections, have hopefully, been taken care of by the elaborate preparation of the government and INEC to ensure that the polls are free and fair.
This is why the onus now devolves on the electorate to take the correct decision. At a time like this, let those who go to the polls on Saturday go with their hearts and minds in focus that they are in the process of saving or destroying themselves by whatever decision they will take in casting their votes. At a time like this, it is important to remind the electorate in the words of legendary Aesop's fables which warned: "Those who voluntarily put power into the hands of a tyrant or an enemy must not wonder if it be at last turned against themselves".
At a time like this, we must weigh critically the option of violence and peace and make a choice not only in our personal interest but in the interest of the future of our children and the country. At a time like this, you have to have sense enough to know that those politicians who are trying to bribe you with bags of salt, rice and yam have been the people who have been unable to win your hearts through their good work - reject them.
The same applies to those who are spraying you with naira in their campaign rallies. The question you should ask yourself is where did they get the money from and why should they do it at this time. When you were hungry, they left you starving. When you were sick, they did not give you money to buy drugs. When your children were sent away from school, they didn't come to assist you with the fees.
But now that they need your votes to be in power for another four years, they are spraying you with stolen money. To vote such people back to power will be to commit suicide. But the decision is strictly yours to take. Do you want to be a pawn in the hands of politicians or you want to exercise your right as the ultimate power broker in the democratic system.
If the later is your choice, then you must make use of your head and your heart to vote wisely, at a time like this.

Original_Search_Title: PDP, ANPP And Nigeria's Future
Headline: Nigeria: PDP, ANPP And Nigeria's Future
Body_Text:
Lagos — The focus of this article is on the winning prospects of the two main political parties (PDP and ANPP) and their flag bearers, based on the anticipated vision plans of each for Nigeria during the April 2003 general elections.
This intellectual exercise is being undertaken even though we are fully aware of the uncertainties that have so far, engulfed the political campaigns with unpredictable future. We are no doubt aware of the wave of political violence involving assassinations, arson and the desperate posture of the incumbent for the second term re-election at all levels. - Very importantly, there are doubts as to the independence, transparency and credibility of the poorly funded INEC whereby it may not be in the position to comply sticky with the provisions of the electoral laws of the land. Besides, the foregoing, there are questions being raised about the timing of the 2003 election day that has coincided with the Christian Holy week of Easter. Finally, there is the silence that pervades the future of local councils elections that have been kept on ice as if the state and Federal Governments are immune from the four (4) years term as provided by the constitution as the life span of these tiers.
What happens if elections do not hold at appropriate dates to enable the emergence of elected government at the Federal and state levels? The aim here however is that the elections shall hold. In the circumstance, the real struggle for power especially at the federal centre. is between the PDP, the incumbent and ANPP the real front runners . Here we shall reflect on the performance of the incumbent party and what the PDP views as the-future Nigeria. On the other hand we shall consider the challenge of the incumbent's performance by ANPP and consider ANPP's vision plan for the future Nigeria. In both uses, the focus and pronouncements of the flag bearers of the two parties shall be in the reckoning.
Let me begin this analysis with. the truism that an individual's life can only be truly exposed and comprehended, at the moment; the individual seeks elective office on the platform, of a political party. To me, this truism holds valid in respect of all those seeking elective office at all the tiers of governments in Nigeria today, especially, the presidential candidates who all want to effect change for a better Nigeria.
At this stage it appears, PDP and ANPP through their flag bears have rounded up their plans for the Nigeria of tomorrow: about how to keep it up, get it fit or strangulate it under the environment of poverty, corruption, dependence or self reliance, rekindle its hope or leave it to languish in despair, or uplift it to become an affective player in world affairs or allow it to become allergic to internal insecurity and disputes in circumstance of hunger, mono economy and "monopoly money" called the Naira amidst disputed privatisation scheme. Or further still, change to good governance as a precondition for NEPAD or be ready for a future that we fear?
In most instances the campaigns for election 2003 had been less about policy issues that lead to progress. Until recently did Nigerians see public notices placed by ANPP calling for campaign based on issues rather than personalities, that tended to dominate the PDP addresses at public rallies. With time fast closing on us, the focus on issues and personalities may not mean much for the choice that Nigerians shall be called upon to make on April 19th. From our close observation of the ongoing campaigns, it appears PDP government does not like change of whatever nature. This explains the party's philosophy of continuity at all costs. According to PDP, the president must continue, the Vice President must continue, stagnant Nigeria must continue, the lack of infrastructure must continue, the governors must continue, corruption must continue, privatisation amidst public outcries must continue, Nigeria police must continue, ASUU strikes and labour unrest must go on. To the disbelief of the good people of Nigeria, the Corporate Nigeria with its style of PDP funding even if the funds come from unclaimed dividends, must continue. The forces of history that shaped him, must continue. The return of PDP presidential candidate to power after 20 years makes PDP to think that Nigeria exists as a constant: constant of violence, constant unchanged and unchanging values, a constant warehouse economy, and a country under one God that brought him back to power to accomplish his work. This is why the man enjoys his pounded yam meal with okoro and bush meat without minding even if ordinary Nigerians persist in hunger. How much was the cost of a tuber of yam in 1994 and how much is it today?
On the other hand is it not a valid position to observe that the ANPP maintains that the only constant phenomenon of life is its unremitting motion. In existence, don't we move with change in the security, stability and prosperity of Nigeria and adopt the style of making deliberate efforts for moderating these changes in the interest of the nation. If in the evolution of these scenarios the PDP approach runs off key, ANPP syntax the might to be oscillating towards perfection. In this policy orientation, if ANPP's candidate outshines PDP's flag bearer in the election 2003, Buhari's victory will only be the manifestation of the extent to which his party had articulated a supreme vision plan for future Nigeria.
At the start of their search for leadership of Nigeria, each had a supreme decision to make. How much change would they bring about in themselves and to the country? This entailed the extent to which each would be exposed to enable Nigerians to decide. For the PDP candidate the decision, easy. He has been the incumbent (this has its burden). He had been the offspring of sympathy that had earned him pardon and nationwide choice in 1999. His performance in some areas spoke for him and in some cases incumbency worked against him, in some parts of the country like in Benue and Odi Niger -Delta region, where he is yet to convince the people that he did not kill their kith and kin on premeditated agenda. His steady repositioning and desire to seek re-election was some how delayed due to a long consultation with his associate and family members and in particular his desire to receive approval from the almighty God. At the end of the consultations he had a safe feel that he would continue as president, with the vision plan for bigger changes. This is the basis for the slogan that "continuity shall secure over democracy".
The ANPP on the other hand focused on what its flag bearer calls "project Nigeria". The project focuses briefly on among other fundamental issues, achieving the following priority goals:
(i) Stability: through provision of equal opportunity to all Nigerians with emphasis on gender equality, mutual trust and the protection of human rights including freedom of speech, freedom of religion and freedom of expression and make Nigeria to be an effective member and player in international affair.
(lI)Security: - to tackle the entire problems that threaten Nigeria and foreign interests by ensuring comprehensive security in all its dimensions.
(llI)Prosperity: to evolve diversified dynamic and self-reliance in a nation with well-developed infrastructure.
The ANPP flag bearer prides himself of the success he made of the PTF assignment before the arrival of PDP government on the scene and scraped the PTF without any substitute. He beats his chest of the presence of PTF vehicles in most government institutions, pharmaceutical drugs in government hospitals and instructional materials in most schools and tertiary institutions across the nation. He feels that the Niger Delta region shall be freed from the PDP government neglect, through inaction, corruption and insensitivity at the top. This is why Buhari accuses PDP of insincerity in dealing with the Niger Delta problems including the desire to review onshore - offshore dichotomy bill. This is also why,' the ANPP predicts that, PDP shall abandon that bill that has become a subject of intimidation of the good people of the region. As the ANPP Chairman promised at the Port Harcourt rally, ANPP government shall address the Niger-Delta on the basis of justice and equity in the best interest of the people of the region and the nation as a whole.
Even though election 2003 is very close, it appears PDP has dual campaigns: one by the candidate's handlers and the other by the flag bearer himself. This underscores the failure of the candidate to respond to the issues raised at his solo TV presidential debate. At that debate, the candidate did not just avoid answering questions, he dismissed ASUU as being self- seeking without the interest of the student, he failed to explain why the Auditor General of the Federation was sacked when for the first time ever, such a report was produced. The candidate lacked background facts for the clarification of issues, which makes the PDP a party without any plan for bridge-building between the past and the future.
What has come to work against the PDP and its flag bearer has been the decision of the Alliance for Democracy (AD), the western geopolitical zone dominated party to endorse the incumbent, the flag bearer of PDP, a Nigerian who hails from the western section of Nigeria. This action by the AD has been criticised particularly by those who seemed to be linking such endorsement with the kind of endorsement of Abacha by political parties in the l990s They also link such a decision to ethnic politics that has been the bane of Nigerian politics since independence in 1960. Here it must be recalled that never in the history of Nigeria has the westerners voted for another Nigerian other than one of their own kith and kin, for the highest office in the land.
Audu writes from Abuja

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Scarcity: Fares, Food Prices Go Up in FCT
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Scarcity: Fares, Food Prices Go Up in FCT
Body_Text:
Abuja — Transport fares in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT), Abuja, have shot up significantly as a result of the fuel scarcity prevalent in the nation. Also, prices of most staple food items like rice, garri, yam, fruits and vegetables have increased.
A trip from Abuja to Kubuwa and Abuja to Gwagwalada, which used to cost N40 and N60 now cost between N60 and N80 respectively. Taxis and other private 'kabu-kabu' vehicles plying the metropolis have also hiked their charges from N100 per drop to about N250 while city buses now collect N30 and N40 as against N10 and N20 previously charged.
At the popular new Garki Wuse, Karimo and other markets around the city, a cup of rice (mudu) now costs between N140 and N150 as against the previous N120, five average sized tubers of yam, which normally costs N300 now goes for N450, while fruits and vegetables such as tomatoes, pine apple, oranges and paw-paw are now scarce due to the transport fare situation.
THISDAY checks reveal that the fuel supply situation in the FCT has worsened in the last few days, with several motorists virtually reciding in the petrol stations in a desperate effort to purchase the commodity.
At the NNPC Mega Filling Station in the Central Area of the City, queues of vehicles waiting to buy fuel now stretches to about three kilometres. The same applies to the two fuel stations opposite the NNPC headquarters in Abuja owned by Total and National oil companies.
Many motorists interviewed in the city expressed despair and anger at the situation and called on the Federal Government to take decisive action to address the crisis before it gets out of hand.

Original_Search_Title: Garment Dealer Meets Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Garment Dealer Meets Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — MRS. Emily Lee of the US-based garments company, Emily International,
was in Kampala last week to place orders with Apparels Tri-Star
(Uganda).
According to a press release, Mrs. Lee was also introduced to President
Yoweri Museveni at State House Nakasero, by Tri-Star managing director,
V. Kananathan.
Museveni said Africa has many opportunities and potential for economic
growth, but lacked enough entreprenuers to exploit the abundant
resources. He said Uganda is self-sufficient in food production, but needed
capital goods like machines and manufactured items like medicines.
Emily International is one of the biggest dealers in the global garment
industry. Among its customers are several US retail stores, including Dollar
General, Family Dollar, Fred's Store and Bealls. The company also supplies
grments to Wal Mart Germany, Hannes and Mauritz in Sweden and N.D. in
Switzerland.
Mrs. Lee was accompanied by Abraham Lee and Yam Kwok, both with her
company. Tri-Star, which is based at Bugolobi, will be making a variety of
ladies'wear for Emily International.
Museveni thanked Mrs. Lee for opening up business links with
Uganda. Ends

Original_Search_Title: Queenmums Must Support Aids Victims - Mother Theresa
Headline: Ghana: Queenmums Must Support Aids Victims - Mother Theresa
Body_Text:
Odumase — The First Lady, Mrs. Theresa Kufuor, has commended the Manya Krobo Queenmothers Association (MKQA) for their role in supporting HIV/AIDS victims.
She has further urged queenmothers in the various traditional areas to emulate the example of the MKQA in ensuring the well-being of HIV/AIDS victims.
Mrs. Theresa Kufuor made the appeal when she paid a visit to the MKQA HIV/AIDS orphanage at Odumase Krobo on March 27, this year.
The First Lady, who is also the vice president of African First Ladies Against HIV/AIDS, said every effort should be made to reduce the high degree of stigmatisation against people living with HIV/AIDS.
She urged women infected with the disease not to give up on their situation, but rather seek treatment.
In an address, the project co-ordinator of MKQA, Madam Esther Kpabitey, said the association - by its traditional orientation - prefers to maintain the orphans in homes rather than keeping them in orphanages.
She said by being given home-based care, they quickly overcome stigmatisation and assimilate well into society.
The project co-ordinator appealed for more resources and a permanent structure as their current building belongs to the Presbyterian Church of Ghana.
The MKQA, which consists of 71 queenmothers, has 586 orphans under its care.
The First Lady presented tubers of yam, rice, boxes of chocolate, milk and loaves of bread to the association.
She later visited the Atua Government Hospital to interact with patients and also to acquaint herself with efforts being made by Family Health International in controlling mother-to-child HIV/AIDS transmission.

Original_Search_Title: Stop Foreigners From Humiliating Ghanaiansry
Headline: Ghana: Stop Foreigners From Humiliating Ghanaiansry
Body_Text:
YESTERDAY IT was a herdsman of Fulani extraction setting a Ghanaian's
70-acre yam farm ablaze (reference Chronicle, March 24). The other day, it
was some Liberians turning what is supposed to be a refugee camp into a
training and recruitment ground for combatants. Earlier a Dutch diplomat had
caned a Ghanaian security guard of his with a matchet. Rake up the past
years and you will recall cases of other foreigners spitting on Ghanaians or
pissing into a water container from which workers drink.
So, for how long, shall we continue to suffer these humiliations in this
dear land of us? This is the question on the lips of many Ghanaians who are
proud of their nationality. And certainly we must act to uphold our
dignity.
The view of the paper is that it is not enough to deal with those
foreigners who take our leniency for weakness only when their wicked acts
are trumpeted in such patriotic media as the Chronicle. The only measure
that can be considered adequate is the one that prevents foreigners from
trampling on the rights and freedoms of full-blooded Ghanaians in their own
country.
And we do know that this is possible within our laws and the framework
of the ECOWAS protocol as well as the charter of the United Nations. The
fact that international laws enjoin us to open our doors to foreigners in
the spirit of cross border intercourse does not mean we are bound to gloss
over blatant violations of laws of our country, most of which are binding
even in the home countries of our guests.
On the contrary, our international laws encourage us to inquire from our
guests what jobs they intend doing, how long they intend staying and we are
entitled to withhold some rights reserved to our nationals from
them. Foreigners are debarred from selling in fuel products, operating
security companies and even taxi services.
How come that today foreigners indulge in these activities with impunity
and some of them maltreat their Ghanaian employees and other nationals they
come in contact with? By no stretch of imagination can it be said that Ghana
is enjoined to welcome nomadic herdsmen who export anthrax and other deadly
diseases into our country, rape our wives and sisters and gun down land
owners who repel their onslaught.
Let our soldiers, police, border guards and the district assemblies take
steps to send all these Fulani people back to where they belong. There will
be no point in allowing them to wreak havoc on our environment before we
think of preventing them from destroying our vegetations and crops.
And our landlords, chiefs and farmers who take bottles of schnapps, kola
nuts and such token fees from the herdsmen and give them unfettered access
to their plots should be told that they are acting irresponsibly. If they do
not stop, posterity will never forgive them.

Original_Search_Title: VLTC Suspends Fuel Trans As Lake Level Falls
Headline: Ghana: VLTC Suspends Fuel Trans As Lake Level Falls
Body_Text:
The Volta Lake Transport Company (VLTC) has temporarily halted
conveyance of petroleum products on the Volta lake from the Akosombo port to
Buipe in the north because of the low level of water in the lake.
Consequently, the company will be losing about Â¢1.753 billion in
transport charges that could be earned between March and August this year,
the period when the operations will be on hold.
This reporter's findings, supported by management sources, have it that
bulk cargo carriers making the 415-kilometre south-north trip, are not able
to go farther than Debre, 35 kilometres to Buipe, the last destination.
This is because as the lake dries, rocks surface and therefore threatens
vessel movements.
On March 2 this year, a quantity of 1.4 million litres of petroleum
products on its way to the north was returned.
However dry cargo in the form of cement was discharged at Debre for
onward transportation by road to Buipe. Information gathered has it that
transportation of the products on water is not only cheaper by 40% but also
safer as it is devoid of the rampant accidents and damages associated with
the road transport system.
The VLTC operates four ferry crossings at Kete Krachi, Dambai, Yeji and
Adawso with the Yapei Queen moving from Yeji to Akosombo carrying livestock,
yams, dried fish, exportable products and passengers.
Two other vessels, Buipe Queen and Volta Queen also float on the lake
alongside ten others, some of which are crying for rehabilitation. The VTLC
is a subsidiary of the VRA.

Original_Search_Title: Irin Focus On Host Families
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Irin Focus On Host Families
Body_Text:
[This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations]
ABIDJAN/YAMOUSSOUKRO, 25 March (IRIN) - Thousands of people in Cote
d'Ivoire have had to open their doors to friends and acquaintances displaced
by an armed conflict that broke out in the West African country in September
