2002. From one day to the next, they have had to provide accomodation, food
and sometimes clothing to the internally displaced persons (IDPs).
However, many complain, thet despite their sacrifices - and sometimes
those of the IDPs themselves - they receive no assistance from the
authorities.
Yao Adou Nguessan's two-roomed home in the Ivorian capital,
Yamoussoukro, was just about what she needed, but she had to share her
bedroom with three relatives who fled the central town of Bouake when it
fell to the rebels in September. Her sitting room of six square metres
serves as a bedroom for five other IDPs. All told, 14 relatives had landed
at her doorstep but the other six live in a room she rented for them in the
neighbourhood for FCFA 8,000 per month. [One US dollar is equivalent to
about FCFA 610.]
"With FCFA 1,500 my three children and I were able to have three meals a
day," Nguessan, a seamstress, told IRIN. With the additions to her
household, her daily expenses on food alone went up to FCFA 3500. The
additional expenses add up to a significant sum in a country where many
people earn less than 75,000 CFA francs per month.
New coping mechanisms
She has had to adopt new coping mechanisms. Instead of buying food in
Yamoussoukro, she travels each week to one of the surrounding villages,
where she buys about 250 kg of plantains and yams at much cheaper
prices. However, that is usually not enough so she supplements it with rice
which she purchases in town. For each meal, she says, she needs four
kg. "All the savings I had have gone towards boarding and lodging my IDPs,"
she said. "I also had to foot the delivery bill when one of them gave birth
here."
As at 10 March 2003 there were about 100,000 IDPs in Cote d'Ivoire,
according to Aboke Damaz of the Solidarity and Humanitarian Action Unit at
the Ministry of Solidarity, Health and Social Security. These included over
97,000 people displaced from the hinterland and more than 3,000 "deguerpis",
people displaced after the government ordered the security forces to
demolish shantytowns following the armed uprising on 19 September that
marked the start of the rebellion. The authorities said the shanties had to
be destroyed because they posed a security threat.
The Solidarity and Humanitarian Action Unit receives donations from
institutions and individuals, which it gives to various structures for
distribution to IDPs. These structures include reception centres, where some
IDPs are accomodated, and social centres, where they are registered and
given assistance. They do not include the host families, some of which
accomodate record numbers of displaced persons.
Some homes accomodate as many as 80 people
N'Dri Youbouet, for example, had 80 people in his five-bedroom house
when IRIN visited him in Yamoussoukro in February. They had all fled Bouake,
100 km north of the capital, after rebels took it over in September
2002. Some were relatives, others were simply people from his home
village. "We've really had a hard time looking after the displacees," he
told IRIN.
Since 24 September 2002, when the IDPs started arriving in his home, his
expenses had almost doubled. The amount he spent each week on food went from
FCFA 10,000 to FCFA 18,000. His water bill used to be FCFA 5,000 for every
two months, but increased to FCFA 12,000. His electricity bill jumped sky
high. "I must admit it's been weighing me down both financially and
psychologically," said the former public servant who retired nine years
ago.
Still, he said, he tried to do everything he could to keep up an
appearance of happiness and make sure that the atmosphere in the home was
good so that the IDPs did not think the family was accomodating them against
its will, especially since things were difficult for the IDPs themselves and
not just on the material plane. For example, Youbouet's daughter, Appoline,
had had no news of her husband since she left Bouake with their two
children. "I cannot tell you whether he has been killed or not," she said
with tears in her eyes.
She, like many of the IDPs in the hinterland and their hosts, had the
impression that all assistance was concentrated in Abidjan since, every now
and then, NGOs and other groups could be seen on television announcing that
they had provided aid for the IDPs, but she and her relatives had receive
nothing in Yamassoukro.
Surviving on help from friends
However, in Abidjan, too, host families complain. Often, the little
assistance they receive comes not from an official body but from friends, as
in Bernadette Sia's case. When 19 people fleeing fighting in the west of the
country arrived at her home at the end of October, a family friend offered a
50-kg bag of rice, which lasted one week.
Within weeks, the 375,000 CFA francs she had painstakingly saved over
the years had been used up and eight of the 19 had to look for accomodation
elsewhere. A friend who worked at the African Development Bank (ADB)
headquarters in Abidjan sometimes gave her money - 5,000 CFA here, 20,000
there - but when the ADB relocated its staff from Abidjan in connection with
the insecurity in the country, that assistance dried up.
Damaz, who is in charge of the social centres set up by the Ministry of
Solidarity, Health and Social Security to assist displaced persons,
confirmed that the host families were not receiving aid. "The mission of the
social centres is not to intervene directly in the host families," he
said. However, he added, all registered IDPs living with host families had a
right to assistance.
The big question was whether there was enough food aid to go
around. Aboke said that his ministry planned to provide 23 mt of rice to
IDPs through the 15 social centres between 14 and 27 March. Each IDP has a
right to 250 grammes of rice per day, according to norms set by the
ministry. Giving the example of how hard it was to respond to the needs of
the IDPs, he said the social centre in Abidjan's North Cocody neighbourhood
had registered 6,878 vulnerable persons, both IDPs from the hinterland and
"deguerpis". The centre, he said, would receive 1,600 mt of rice for one
day. Asked what the IDPs would receive after that, he said: "Those are the
difficulties we face".
Urgent need for psychological assistance
But while food ranked high among the heads of households' priorities
where assistance was concerned, followed by sleeping mats and clothing, they
were much more concerned that the IDPs were not receiving non-material help
either. The IDPs' main need, they said, was counselling by psychologists to
ease the traumatic experiences they had gone through. Some had narrowly
escaped death. Others had seen dead bodies scattered along roads.
"Some of us have nightmares at night. We dream about atrocities
committed by the rebels," 19-year-old Fabrice Doly said.
Many of the IDPs had harassing journeys to safety, as in the case of
Fulgence Doly who said he, his wife, three children and 80-year-old mother
walked for 62 kms after an attack on the western town of Duekoue, where they
lived. He was among 47 IDPs who, like Fabrice, shared Emmanuel Doly's
six-bedroom home in the high-income suburb of Cocody with his host's
17-member family. At night, some people slept in the bedrooms while others
made do with the corridors, kitchen, balcony and sitting room. In most cases
bed was either a mat or clothes > heaped on the floor. "Still it's better
than sleeping under the cocoa trees," Fulgence said. He worked with the
National Rural Development Agency (ANADER) in Duekoue. Displacement has
reduced him to a state of dependency.
Dao Pena Nicholas also lives with the Doly's, along with six other
members of his household. He needs to travel frequently to go to the city
centre to fix his pension papers but often he does not have money for
transport. Not being able to do anything for his family was tough, he
said. "Each time they want this, they want that," he told IRIN. "Personally
I'm obliged to leave the house to get away from the pressure. In any case I
don't have the means to do anything for them." Sometimes he goes and hangs
out at the lottery kiosk. At other times he simply walks around the
neighbourhood.
The mental health issue cannot be overemphasized, according to Rosa
Malango of the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
(OCHA). Access to mental health services is essential, given the post-trauma
stress and psychological reactions that result from moral and physical
aggression undergone before, during or after displacement, she told
IRIN.
However, other needs are also important, added Malango, who is a
programme officer at OCHA's West Africa Regional Support Office in
Abidjan. According to the UN guiding principles on internal displacement,
she noted, national authorities should at the very least provide internally
displaced persons with and ensure safe access to essential food, potable
water, basic shelter and housing, appropriate clothing; and essential
medical services and sanitation.
[The UN Guiding Principles on Internal Displacement can be accessed at
http://www.reliefweb.int/ocha_ol/pub/idp_gp/idp.html]
In Cote d'Ivoire, given the sudden nature of the crisis and the high
number of IDPs, about 100,000 according to UN figures, it has been observed
that there is an imbalance between the food-related needs expressed by the
displaced persons and the assistance they receive from the government,
humanitarian organisations and donors, Malango said.
Institutions that do assist the IDPs include the World Food Programme
(WFP), which has provided food to displaced persons in both
government-controlled and rebel-held areas, and the UN Children's Fund
(UNICEF) which runs a project which helps ensure that displaced children are
able to attend school..
Schooling interrupted
Despite such efforts, assistance does not reach everyone who needs
it. For example, some children do not go to school. The authorities had
delayed the start of the school year for displaced children from October to
the beginning of January. However, those displaced after January are often
unable to find places, as in the case of the 26 displaced children in the
Doly household. Then there are the medical needs. Koffi Henry Kouakou, a
retired teacher who suffers from hypotension, lost all his medical papers
when he fled the western town of Facobly over three months ago. Since then
he has gone without medication.
IDPs and heads of host families said they hoped that someone would
eventually pay attention to their plight. "We feel left out," N'Dri Youbouet
said, "whereas the state would never had been able to handle all these
people if the host families had refused to accomodate the majority of them."

Original_Search_Title: When Confusion Reigns
Headline: Ghana: When Confusion Reigns
Body_Text:
Accra — As a people, Ghanaians excel in contradictions. We often say one thing and do the opposite. We build peoples' expectations by what we say and dampen their spirits by what we do.
We justify and carry on with our mistakes, even when everyone is sure that it is wrong. But we are harsh on people who dare point out our mistakes. The national attributes I am alluding to cut across political lines; they are deeply rooted in our historical past and have been perfected since independence. There is therefore no use in purring a political tag on what follows, as it makes no difference which political party is ruling.
For some time now, we have somehow accepted, a la the Washington Consensus, even if grudgingly by some, to run an economy that seeks to make the private sector the "engine of growth". In the process, we are told government has instituted measures to restore fiscal and monetary discipline in the economy. But we know that the economy continues to behave like a "roller coaster", with government deeply involved in setting price controls - tro tro fares, school fees, utility fees, etc. In addition, we keep chanting that the government has also laid solid foundation for attracting private direct investment into the country, but we are hard put to locate the investors in the country. And when the multi lateral financial assistance comes, it is invariably given to companies owned by foreigners.
We pat our shoulders that the country's structural adjustment programmes, initially geared to macroeconomic policy issues relating to trade and exchange rates management and restoration of fiscal discipline, have succeeded and have steadily shifted to growth oriented structural initiatives. But we continue to see nothing but a fragile economy. We are often told that among the results of the realistic policies adopted are stabilization of the exchange rate but the Cedi does not seem to be able to ever hold its own.
We have embraced deregulation of the product markets, yet there is an annual ritual of fixing prices for petroleum products, utilities tro tro charges and their consequential fall outs whilst no one determines the price for plantains, yams, milk, sugar, salt, cement, and other commodities.
We talk about trade liberalization which has increasingly enabled international competition to discipline the price setting behaviour of the local/domestic market, and yet we are preoccupied with checking smuggling across our borders, indicating that trade liberalization is not working and significant price differentials continue to exist between our prices and those of the neighbouring countries.
We have been made to believe that there is a significant improvement in government revenue collection and mobilization, with most revenue collection agencies exceeding their targets, yet the national budget continues to suffer deficits. We continually talk about removal of subsidies from the economy and keep harping on cost recovery in health and education, yet we are prepared to run a mass transit at a loss.
The list of contradictions, in short, is tall. No, I am not doubting that significant programmes have been implemented within the productive sectors of agriculture and industry as well as the social sectors of health and education, my point is that often what we say does not square up with reality. Not because we are a bunch of liars, but rather we do not seem to appreciate serious thinking processes. Until our decisions are based on systematic thinking processes, we shall always be governed by "ad hocracy."
Everyone knows that when petrol prices are increased, literally prices on everything get increased. But somehow concerns get bogged down on fixing prices for transportation and the minimum wages. Everyone forgets that as soon as the petrol prices increased the neighbourhood grocery store without much ado increased the price of ideal milk from Â¢2,500 to Â¢4,000, an increase of 75 percent and the stationery shop upped the price of paper by eighty percent but there was no fuss.
The cassava, maize, amaadaa ke nkate and other produce prices doubled but went unnoticed. Proprietors of private clinics doubled their consultation fees without notice. Some private schools doubled some charges and no one complained, except the parents. All these increases were going to be paid for from one pocket, the worker's daily income, which was being subjected to percentage negotiations. The repercussions are indeed very interesting and require the kind of thinking that is not too obvious.
But is it fair to regulate wages and some prices and leave others free to find their own levels in an economy in which the private sector is meant to be the "engine of growth?"
*Kofi Ankomah (Phd) is a management consultant.

Original_Search_Title: Why I'm Seeking Second Term - Akume
Headline: Nigeria: Why I'm Seeking Second Term - Akume
Body_Text:
Lagos — Benue State governor, Mr. George Akume, Saturday, flagged off his re-election campaign, saying he is seeking a second term "to continue to serve and for an opportunity to complete the projects and programmes" that his administration has initiated.
Specifically, the PDP governorship candidate for Benue State, while promising better service delivery if granted a second mandate, said best point against his other opponents is what he called "four years of invaluable experience as a governor."
I assure you therefore that I stand to serve you better as governor during my second term than any of the green horns seeking election for the first time," he said. Akume, during the flag-off, which took place in Adie town, Kwande local government, identifd the mechinsation of Agriculture and promotion of staple crops, including yams, soya beans, beneseed, citrus and other crops as his target priority given a second chance.
The governor who had on March 15, at the IBB Square declared that "democracy is not about infrastructure but about freedom," also said his administration is presently pursuing a N2.3 billion loan from the Nigeria Agricultural Cooperative and Rural Develop-ment Bank for lending to farmers.

Original_Search_Title: 'Food Can Enrich Us in Everyway'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Food Can Enrich Us in Everyway'
Body_Text:
Lagos — The late sage, Chief Obafemi Awolowo, was right. In his book, The Voice of Reason, he looked at the various calamities that can befall mankind and declared: "The rebellion of the stomach is the worst". Food. There is nothing like it. For more than a decade, another man has been involved in the campaign to ensure that it is taken seriously, that the society insures its own future by giving food the kind of attention it deserves.
Next month, Mr. Harris Aib Igiehon will battle for the soul of Edo State on the platform of the National Democratic Party (NDP). He is a greenhorn in politics, but he has paid his dues in socio- cultural and economic engineering. Through one of his companies Aibtonia Promotions Advertising Marketing Nigeria Limited, he has over the years staged food and beverage fairs, which were geared towards showcasing the local agricultural potentialities of the country and integrating all the food cultures in Nigeria.
What else does one expect from a strong advocate of self sufficiency in food if he finds himself in the seat of power? For one, experience has enabled him to perfect his blueprint for the various programmes associated with the subject of food security. Secondly, his dealings with both the public and private sectors may have prepared him to meet the challenges of governance.
According to Igiehon, everything will be done to encourage mass production of quality food. Hear him; "At the moment, our farming is largely at the subsistence level. That will have to change. If we are going to make any mark at all in this area, we must go into mechanised, large scale farming. We have on the drawing board plans that will put us in a position to feed our population well and also service other markets".
"There is a workable strategy on ground. The department (ministry) of agriculture will be made to rise from its present state of slumber and live up to its responsibilities. The professionals on the pay roll of government will put their technical know how to productive use. Some farming families noted for producing specific crops will have the official backing of an NDP led government to make them excel in their trades for the betterment of the state," he further. For instance, he said some families in Uromi are known for growing citrus crops while those of Ekpoma and Agenebode are recognised quality rice farmers. He said the same can be said of several families across Edo State. Apart from stimulating agriculture, the practice, if supported, will also enhance communal cohesiveness which is needed for speedy development.
Located in the rain forest of Nigeria, Edo State has all it takes to be a bread basket. Igiehon and his team are prepared to take advantage of the natural endowment. The various local government areas in the state will be categorised based on the crops for which they have comparative edge.
When that is done, the governorship candidate explained that resources can then be channeled through the commodity banks to be set up by the instrumentality of established banks. Because of the volume of productive and commercial activities expected in this sector, Igiehon said availability of funds is paramount. "Poultry farmers will have an institution to source capital from, yam farmers will have another bank to do so, and so on," he noted.
When the stakeholders are identified, he told THISlife that the state government will serve as guarantors for the procurement of these loans.
The NDP flag bearer is optimistic that the relationship that will be developed between these financial establishments will be beneficial to all the parties involved and hopefully sustained.
Another remarkable feature of the farming programme of an Igiehon led administration in Edo State will be the establishment of commodity markets. A whole lot of ingenuity has gone into the mapping out of how this will work in a country where persistent, painstaking implementation of policies and programmes are yet to be a virtue. It is anticipated that production will hit an all time peak.
What then would happen to the marketing since the local (state) population would not be able to absorb what is produced ? He said this will become the meeting point for agriculture and commerce. The agency in charge of marketing will be responsible for buying from farmers and selling to buyers around the nation and outside, particularly the West African subregion.
"Commodity markets will be set up as state level," he explained. "They will be run by professionals in capital market and agricultural economists who will regulate the purchase of farm commodities from farmers as well as control marketing and sales of same to larger markets".
These markets will regulate the distribution and prices of the designated goods in order to check things like under or overpricing and also food flight.
"Government guidelines have to be followed so that foodstuff are not rushed outside the state before the people have the chance to consume them. You have to take care of your constituency first. You must not leave things to market forces, which often suppress and oppress the people. The wealth of the people should first and foremost serve the people," he further stated.
He said provision will be made for farmgate warehouses for storage while awaiting onward movement to the markets. Not only that. He noted that farm gate cottage industries will be established with the active participation of private investors to cater for the processing of the raw food (materials) that will be produced.
For instance, a juice plant will be located near a pineapple plantation. Rice and others will also be packaged locally. Jobs will be created that way and prosperity will be enhanced.
Igiehon sees his food production and marketing plan as the major catalyst in the general economic "restoration" of the state.
"We will do what we can to empower our people. That is one way they can have a voice. Land is one of the most important factors in agriculture and the people have access to it. So we can start from there. We are looking at something that is capable of becoming the economic backbone of the state and the nation, something that can enrich us in every way. And we will not let this opportunity slip by, he stressed." Not only has the 51 year old architect made a commitment to push his dream through, his success so far as promoter and marketer may be what Edo State needs to use available natural resources to free itself from economic limitations and fly high.

Original_Search_Title: Osun Chief Judge's Reprieve
Headline: Nigeria: Osun Chief Judge's Reprieve
Body_Text:
A FORTNIGHT ago, the Chief Judge of Osun State, Justice Fasasi Ogunsola during an official duty tour of prisons in the state ordered the release of some inmates in Osun prisons.
Among those released were two teenage girls sentenced for stealing two tubers of yam by a magistrate court. At the time of their sentence, the girls, Rafiat Mufutau and Rukayat Rashidi were 15 years old. Five other inmates namely Adebayo Adeyemi 19, Muri Alimeke 22, Sikiru Wasiu 30, Yisau Akano 35 and Alaba Adewale 40 were equally set free after having spent about five years in prison without trial.
Justice Ogunsola condemned the judgement of the magistrate who convicted the teenagers without considering their age. We agree with him. The teenagers' imprisonment, as minors cannot be sustained in law. The magistrate ought to know better. It is trite law that minors cannot be imprisoned the way the learned magistrate did in the case of the two teenage girls.
Rather, the teenagers should have been tried under the Children and Young Persons Act. The penalty under the latter would have been reformative.
As much as we deprecate stealing as past time amongst our teenagers, it is important to equally remind our courts to act withiA Ogunsola had spent close to five years in prison without trial. This is sentence and punishment without conviction. We are nonetheless aware there are more thousands of unfortunate Nigerians who have spent more than that number of years awaiting trial that is not in view. More inmates are still suffering the same fate in other states of the federation.
We commend Osun State Chief Judge for invoking the power conferred on him by Section 1(1a) of the Criminal Justice (Release from Custody) Special Provisions Act, Laws of the federation 1990 to free unconstitutionally encumbered Nigerians.
The ordering of the release even though a routine legal thing is a fundamental aspect of a judge's judicial responsibility.
The amnesty granted the inmates is laudable because it reminds us that some of our judicial officers have not been doing their jobs diligently . Also, the release re-emphasize the need to drastically re-organise our judicial process since there are so many Nigerians who are Awaiting Trial Inmates who are being unjustly kept in custody.
As an organisation with a firm belief in the rule of law, we call on the government to quickly look into the matter of Awaiting Trial Inmates, setting free those who should be set free, while speedily putting on trial those who have cases to answer.
We call on other chief judges across the federation to emulate the Osun Chief Judge's example.

Original_Search_Title: Ecowas Trade Fair
Headline: West Africa: Ecowas Trade Fair
Body_Text:
Nigerian exhibitors express reservations at quality of visitors
As the 3rd ECOWAS Trade Fair enteres its fifth day today, many of the 66
Nigerian exhibitors have expressed reservations at the level of awareness
created in the country about the fair, which has impacted negatively on the
quality of the people who visited the fair.
Some of the exhibitors who spoke to the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN),
said that the bright prospects created following the large turnout at the
opening ceremony on Friday, sooner became dim as most of the visitors to the
exhibition stands appeared to be students and retail traders instead of the
expected "core" business people.
Those exhibitors, who came for the sole purpose of establishing business
contacts for dealership, lamented that the response so far has been poor,
while others, who came with products to sell, have reportedly recorded low
sales.
Comparing the fair with the one held in Ghana four years ago, Alhaji
Muazu Yakubu of ANNURI International Ltd, manufacturers of leather products,
said that the turnout in Ghana was much more impressive, adding that he also
established some business links there.
Mr. Fatai Adesina, of AS Shine, a honey processing company, said that
due to the poor turnout, he has had to visit the town's supermarkets on a
marketing campaign, and that his effort had yielded some positive
results.
"On the opening day, the fair ground was jam-packed but they were not
the real business people," he said, adding that the present situation could
be attributed to the poor state of the country's economy.
For Olu Shobanke of Stapro Company, manufactures of flour products
sourced from plantain, soyabeans, yam, rice, among others, the situation
appears somewhat different, as he reportedly made some contacts for export
to Paris.
He, however, observed that most of the visitors were people from the
lower strata of the society and not the real business people.
Mr. Paschal Njoku, the secretary of the Manufacturers Association of
Nigeria (MAN) Export Group, suggested that the business inclination of the
people could have accounted for the low turnout so far and not necessarily
inadequate publicity.
He noted the aim of the fair was to expand markets for products made in
the sub-region and Nigerian products, in particular, and expressed the hope
that the objective would soon be achieved.

Original_Search_Title: Chief Judge Seeks Better Interpretation of Law
Headline: Nigeria: Chief Judge Seeks Better Interpretation of Law
Body_Text:
Osogbo — For sentencing two Juveniles over allegations of stealing two tubers of Yam, the Chief Judge of Osun State, Justice Fasaasi Ogunsola, yesterday berated an Okuku magistrate for not interpreting the law accordingly.
Rafiyat Mufutau and Rakiyat Rashidi, both 15 years old have spent six months in Ilesa prison in Osun state for allegedly stealing two tubers of yam.
The two girls regained their freedom on Friday when the Chief Judge visited the prison on an assessment tour.
Justice Ogunsola, after been briefed about the case of the inmates ruled that the juvenile offenders ought not to have been imprisoned by the lower court. He said the magistrate court should have tried them under the children and young persons act which would have been reformative.
Among those who also regained their freedom was a female convict, Bolaji Odelana, who was delivered of a baby girl few months after arriving in the prison in 1998.
Odelana pleaded with the Chief Judge to set her free in the interest of her five year old-daughter.
Justice Ogunsola also set Adebayo Adeyemi, Muri Alimeke who were said to have been mistakenly arrested along with some armed robbery suspects in Ila Orangun in July 2002, free when security operatives raided the town.
Also Wasiu Sikiru, Yisau Akano and Alaba Adewole were freed based on the submission of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) that there was no information to link them with the conspiracy and murder charge for which they have been detained since 2001.
Releasing the inmates, Justice Ogunsola charged all stakeholders in the administration of criminal justice to ensure that accused persons are not put behind bars unjustly.

Original_Search_Title: Where is the Money Coming From?
Headline: Nigeria: Where is the Money Coming From?
Body_Text:
Lagos — We are in a season of fund raising in which public office seekers and
those who want to retain their positions gather friends and contractors to
donate to their campaigns. From Abuja to Asaba to Kaduna, that is the new
vogue in town. With their big babanriga, government people are now making
outrageous donations that tend to confirm Transparency International rating
of Nigeria as the second on the ladder of shame. I must make it clear here
that making donations to political campaigns is a common feature in a
democracy. What makes the present situation deplorable, however, is that
rather than lobby groups, most of the people who donate the highest sums now
are government officials.
At the fund raising dinner for President Olusegun Obasanjo, for
instance, the highest donor was Vice President Atiku Abubakar who parted
with N1.2 billion on behalf of himself and some unnamed friends. Last
weekend, Atiku was again in Asaba where he doled out N35 million at Governor
James Ibori's own version where five people collectively gave the 'Sheikh'
N1 billion within a period of fifteen minutes. Before Delta, Atiku had been
in at least five other states and cumulatively, he must have made a donation
of not less than two billion naira in the last couple of weeks. The amount
of money being declared in recent times as donations have become so
scandalous that one should raise alarm for several reasons.
One, some of the people who make big donations are men without visible
means of livelihood in which case the sources of the money should ordinarily
be questioned. Two, how much tax do these people who donate these scandalous
amount of money pay? Three, do the funds collectors also account for the
money so collected? This question becomes important because we have of
recent seen instances of people who launched fund raising campaigns for
offices they ended up not seeking after collecting tens of millions of
naira.
The last point is the propriety of flaunting ill-gotten wealth in a
society as poor as ours, where a mere N1000 hospital bill sometimes stand
between life and death for many people, a society where millions are
homeless, hopeless and hungry. While businessmen who make heavy donations
can be forgiven since most of them either use it to pay back for the juicy
contracts they received from government or are wetting the ground for more,
I am of the opinion that there is something indecent about public office
holders making outrageous donations, even if, as most of them claim, it is
their money.
That is why I also believe Atiku must be told in unmistakable terms that
he is fast becoming an embarrassment with the way he has been dipping his
hands into his seemingly bottomless pocket. Even when we concede to him the
right to begin his 2007 campaign now, as he is doing by building political
bridges across the nation, the kind of money he has been donating publicly
(this is aside the ones he must be doing privately) can only confirm the
general perception that he has been helping himself to public funds.
Atiku, we all know, retired as a successful Customs Officer (whatever
that means) after which he dabbled into business before joining
politics. But the point remains that he has been in government in the last
four years so we should naturally ask questions as to how he came about all
this money from his "private pocket". Even if he had so many businesses,
which he must have entrusted to people to manage for him, given the
prevailing hostile economic environment in Nigeria, the expectation is that
the businesses should not be doing too well. If that were the case, as it is
with most other businessmen, then where are the billion of naira coming
from?
Of course we are living in a society where people hardly ask questions
and where public office holders get away with anything. But those who can
remember the past would realise also that it is a culture of impunity like
this that led to the fall of the First and Second Republics. Because as
young as I was back then, I can remember that the problem of Alhaji Shehu
Shagari began the moment some of his men began to flaunt their ill-gotten
wealth in our faces. What most of these officials forget to take into
account is that Nigerians are no fools. We may not talk but that is not to
say we have no idea as to where the so-called personal donations come
from. That is why it is incumbent on them to be more discreet about the way
they spend "their" money.
It is even more incumbent on the vice president to be careful with the
way he doles out money. Because he is well aware that the public perception
of him is that of a man who has turned the privatisation exercise to a money
making spinner by using fronts to buy up the whole place. Yet that may not
be the true situation because at a forum I attended last year, Atiku himself
brought up the issue and he was able to defend himself. He sounded very
convincing. He, however, continues to make this kind of outrageous donations
in a pauperised economy as ours, not a few of us poor people will also
continue to ask the question: where is the money coming from? The Vice
President no doubt is a lucky man. And I also hear he, unlike baba, is very
generous to his friends. But he should realise he is our number two citizen
and whatever he does robs off on us as a people. He should learn the wisdom
in a Yoruba saying, and good for him he has a Yoruba wife, that when, as a
farmer, your tuber of yam is growing too big, you cover it with your
palms.

Original_Search_Title: 'We Have to Be More Committed to the Environment'
Headline: Nigeria: 'We Have to Be More Committed to the Environment'
Body_Text:
Lagos — The World Environmental Movement for Africa (WEMFA) in collaboration
with the United States of America based World Institute for Leadership and
Development (WILDEV) identified about 41 world achievers who were receipts
of the WEMFA Award in the areas of Leadership and Development (L&D) and
Environmental and Sustainable Development (E&SD).
Some of recipients include President Olusegun Obasanjo and his wife
Stella, President Hosni Mubararak of Egypt, Professor Nikolai Kirik of the
Ukraine Academy of Sciences, Prime Minister Jean Chertien of Canada,
President Thabo Mbeki of South Africa, former president Nelson Mandela and
Archbishop Desmond Tutu (chairman of WEMFA International Advisory Board);
President John Agyekun Kufuor of Ghana, Chief Mrs. Margaret Ekpo, Chief
Onyema Ugochukwu, chai man of the Niger Delta Development (NDDC), Professor
Jerry Gana, Minister of Information and National Orientation among other
Nigerians and international role models focused on leadership and
Development and environment and sustainable development. Chikas Ohadoma
spoke with the international president of WEMFA, Dr. Steve Azaiki on the
relevance of WEMFA in sustainable development.Excerpts:
How do you describe the event of today?
Yes, today is a great day for Africa, today we honoured the three
distinguished sons of Africa, Nelson Mandela, former President of South
Africa, Arch Bishop Desmond Tutu His grace, the Arch Bishop of former Cape
town and the president of South Africa, Mr. Thabo Mbeki for the World
Environmental Movement for Africa (WEMFA) award for Leadership, Environment
and Development for the year 2001, I consider it a very successful
outing. The South African High
Commissioner to Nigeria Mr. Bangumzi Sifingo received the award on
behalf of the three at a ceremony, which took place at the South African
High commission in Abuja.
How do you feel, being the president of the World Environmental
Movement for Africa (WEMFA)
I feel a great responsibility and privileged. I think that I have the
responsibility to influence, within the organisations to influence the
environmental policies, policies that are favourable towards sustainable
development, not only in Nigeria, but also in Africa. So I think I have a
great responsibility and I feel that I have a responsibility to exercise my
duties as the president of the organisation. The three recipients are
wonderful, highly favoured and respected world citizens and first sons of
the African continent. The event was a practical and physical cementing
block for uniting African leaders with their people through a process of
harmonising our common interests. WEMFA has over the years advocated and
encouraged the governments and citizens of Africa to pursue environment
friendly policies and practices as well as sustainable development compliant
projects through conferences, advocacy, bulletin, public works, seminars,
summits and workshops.
The exploitation of the resources of the environment must be informed by
a sense of belonging and responsibility to preserve the environment for
future generations who also have equal claim and stake to the resources of
our common heritage, the environment. I am calling on world leaders to stop
paying lip service to issues concerning the environment but to show
commitment to the implementation of the outcome of international protocols
resulting from world summits to enable environmental issues to receive its
needed adequate attention because the extent to which we have succeeded in
creating the necessary attention and environmental consciousness, is the
degree to which no project or conference is complete today without reference
to and concern for environmental impact assessment and the need for projects
and policies to be sustainable development compliant.
What is the mission and vision of WEMFA?
Well, first and foremost, WEMFA was encouraging sustainable development,
not only in Africa, but also throughout the world. Our focus is to influence
environmental policies so that they are favourable towards the environment
and sustainable development, we are also to encourage environmental
awareness and cleanliness, education, information dissemination, make sure
that the environment is protected for our children and children yet unborn
and we must make sure that this world does not belong to any particular
person or any particular generation, we have to make good use of it and make
sure that the next generation has better development, so our mission
basically is to create awareness, to protect the environment and to support
sustainable development.
So far, what is the scope of your activities?
Well, so far, we've been able to do a lot, we are involved in the
Niger-Delta, we have done a lot of studies/surveys, done a lot of
conferences, workshop, we've gone into practical projects in agricultural
development, we've gone into skills acquisition in the Niger-Delta, in the
oil producing community to make sure that we can provide some kind of
peaceful atmosphere for the oil company, for instance we are into conflict
resolution, crisis management in flag point areas, we are involved in
desertification we did encourage tree planting. And in other countries of
Africa, we are working with a team of volunteers in Ghana, Kenya, Guinea
Bissau, Sierra Leone and other countries.
Internationally we work with some voluntary organisations in Ukraine,
the former Soviet Union and for now, we believe that in Africa, we are well
positioned to mobilize the continent towards environmental awareness and we
work with a lot of international organisations, foreign countries and
embassies, we work together.
Could you give us a specific programme you are involved in?
Well, basically we did a lot of job with the NDDC, the Niger-Delta
Development Commission, we did some work with Bayelsa State government, we
work with the United Nations Development programme (UNDP), and most of the
things we did are practically people oriented, like I said, we try to
establish farm for school leavers who other wise wouldn't have had something
to do, we also went into tree planting, in various streets of the
federation, we also went into skills acquisition, we have a project we are
working with Women Light, trying to remove sex hawkers from the
street. We've opened up a skills acquisition centre that was supported by
the Netherlands Embassy, and we are working on training then to be self
employed in the areas of fashion design, computer training, handcraft and so
on and so forth.
We also specifically have a project of organising an international
conference to discuss the issues of development in the Niger-Delta, we also
took it upon ourselves to make a study of the Niger-Delta, which
metamorphosed into a conference, to provide a blueprint for the integrated
development of the Niger-Delta, we also basically work with the Bayero
University in Kano in area of desertification, desert encroachment and so on
and so forth. In Ghana we are involved in desertification and study of rear
specie of plants, so that we have a documented plan in Ghana.
In Nigeria also, we specifically target various institutions where we
have several workshops to create environmental awareness, the need to manage
our environment and in some flag point area, we also went into conflict
resolution and crisis management, we have had a lot of community and oil
companies problems and we try to get them involved in sitting down to
discuss their problems and finding peaceful solution to these issues.
What is organisation's position on the onshore/offshore
dichotomy?
We believe that environment and poverty are interwoven and we also
believe that sustainable development can only come when justice and equity
is taken into consideration. We have looked at the issue of
onshore/offshore, WEMFA was one of the first few organisations to submit a
comprehensive proposal to the presidency when this democratic dispensation
came into office, advising them to set up a special commission to look at
the problems of the Niger-Delta and it is our believe that onshore/offshore
should be resolved in favour of the littoral states or the oil producing
States, because it is assumed that the extent of the nautical mile towards
boundary is unanimous with the environment of that particular area and we
believe that the onshore/offshore will also bring some kind of stability,
will encourage the oil bearing communities to use whatever fund that is
accruable to them to develop their area that is very difficult and with
their specific problems they need some extra funding and we believe that
that dichotomy should be abolished as was done during the Babangida time. So
our own position is that it should be abolished, we should return to the
Babangida regime that abolished that dichotomy and encouraged the oil
producing states to be able to develop themselves against the background of
their peculiar environment.
When you talk about development, are you saying that the littoral
States have been marginalised?
Everybody claims in Nigeria that they have been marginalised and to a
large extent, most ethnic groups or states could be right, but we are saying
that the oil bearing communities have their peculiar environment, and they
need specific encouragement, they need to be encouraged because of the cost
involvement in development effort in that area and we also know that oil is
exhaustible and when you take and you don't put anything back, there is the
tendency that sooner or later there will be a vacuum, sooner or later there
will be an emptiness, so you need to prepare for that emptiness, that is why
I believe that they have a particular and specific reason to ask for some
kind of attention so that they can develop, and the cost of development
makes it imperative that they have a kind of special assistance, and this is
not even special assistance, we are talking of let them just have what is
due to them.
If given the offer, what do you think the littoral states should
do?
Well, I believe that development like the economy should be planned, I
hope that most of the states should by now have a programme, a planned
programme of activity because if I am in charge or we have the opportunity
of advising which we have been doing, I think the Niger-Delta proper need a
lot of network of roads, communities need to be opened up, take for instance
Bayelsa State have more farm land than up-land and in that case means that
it will take ten times more to develop a one kilometre road in Bayelsa than
say Abuja or any other part of the country that is up-land. So they need to
have a plan and we need to encourage them to have network of roads and to
also look at the problem of energy, power generation. Of course when you
have constant power generation, and you have network of road, there is easy
movement, then you can now encourage commerce, you can now encourage
tourism, you can encourage industry and so on and so forth, but you need to
put these structures in place, and these structures are expensive, that is
why there is always need to have some specific attention in the
Niger-Delta.
How do you think the international community feel about the Nigerian
environment?
Well, I can say that looking at the last few years, Nigeria has taken
some reasonable and considerable steps, Nigeria has noted for being active
and participatory in the international conference on environment and
development, but we've not been able to put it into practise, as it should
be, that is the only problem we have in Nigeria. But let me just say that in
this democratic dispensation, creating a ministry of environment, the
federal ministry of environment if for nothing, has drawn attention to the
issue of environment in the country. It has created some kind of awareness,
it has created a platform for the implementation of environmental programmes
and projects, now it is left for the implementors to go ahead and practice
what they've put down on paper.
I think the international community has been encouraged by the fact that
Nigeria has now created a ministry of environment, even in the Senate, there
is a committee on environment, just as there is a senate committee on
petroleum, this is a serious encouragement to the international community
that we are beginning to look at environmental issues seriously. I think
that green light is there; it is left for us to practicalise our
commitment. So in summary, I think the international community, are
satisfied with what we have done so far, and they think that we've taken the
right steps and they need to be encouraged.
The world Summit on Sustainable development was held last year and
some agreements were reached in Rio summit of 1992, how could you assess the
progress of Nigeria in implementing the Agenda 21.
Well, like I said, we participated in that conference seriously, at that
time, the present President was not even the president of Nigeria and he was
there as a member of the International community, a Statesman from Nigeria
and also at that time, the honourary chairman of African leadership forum
and a member of the eminent of the commonwealth elite group. So the
practicality of that agenda, part of its implementation is trying to half
into the global fund available for development, creating the Ministry of
Environment, moving ahead to develop some kind of policy on sustainable
development has resulted into encouraging most of the NGOs in Nigeria to
take environmental and sustainable development issues very seriously and we
are a member of a special committee on environment in Nairobi.
We can say that while we have not reached there is that we begin to look
at a lot of issues with perspective of making it more sustainable. Nigeria
has put a lot of pressure on the oil companies to stop gas flaring, the
government has given them some kind of ultimatum 2003, now 2005 and 2008 and
so on and so forth, all in a way to implement some of the outcome of the Rio
summit. Nigeria is beginning to look at some issues of sustainable
development, trying to make sure that instant assessment, which was hitherto
ignored are now put in place so that most of these structures are looked at
from the sustainable part of it. So all in all, I can say that if we say we
have started yes, even though we have gotten nowhere, but the journey is
there.
This award given ceremony is in collaboration with the United States
of America based World Institute for Leadership and Development (WILDEV),
what is the level of their participation?
With WILDEV, We collaborate in the areas of exchange of information,
exchange of technical know how and also in the area of funding. We have had
their members come to Nigeria to practically demonstrate some of these
environmental problems, how it can be resolved, we've had their members
attend our conferences and exchange information and idea and we work
together in a lot of areas like I said exchange of information and funding,
global participation in conferences, joint activities and actions,
declaration on AIDS, we have had a joint declaration on poverty, we have
looked at environment together, we have jointly presented positions about
the world body, the UN, UNO, the UNIFA and various international fora. We
are interacting on one voice, the onshore/offshore conference in Texas,
Houston, we participated together, the South African conference on
sustainable development, we participated together, the Petroleum conference
in Brazil, we've jointly presented a position paper, so we have that kind of
working relationship.
You mentioned funding, like how much have they supported WEMFA
with?
What we've always do, we don't encourage receiving cash, because it is
very difficult to tap into these international organisations to specifically
give you money, but they've given us support in the area of for example,
areas of the awards, the certificates that we use for the awards were
printed in the U.S, the invitation cards were printed in the US and sent to
us and we have had joint visiting exchange programmes and they've been able
to take care of our people when we visited America and we take care of their
people when they visit here, take care of the local expenses, so we have not
actually handle cash to cash, but we've been able to support each other at
various levels.
What's your relationship like with NDDC?
Well, the NDDC is basically an agency set up by federal government to
look at the problems of the Niger-Delta and we have been here long before
the NDDC came to be, we have a lot of information, we have a lot of
materials that is helpful and needed by the NDDC to make their job easier,
we have had a comprehensive study of the Niger-Delta area, we have the
database, we have the blueprint, we have produced on the Niger-Delta, we are
vast to the environment, we have travelled wide, we've talked with the
people, we these kind of information and they cannot just but tap into that
rate of information available, those are the areas of support and
recruitment we've been having with them, when we have all our conferences,
NDDC has been physical, we've always given them the opportunity to tell us
their own part of the story so that others will know their problem and we
can together find solution. We have given them that opportunity severally,
NDDC has at a time asked us to help them sensitise the people on their
programmes and projects in the Niger-Delta area and we have done that for
them, so that is the kind of relationship we have.
Have they given you any monetary support?
Well, sometimes I can say that in most cases we don't request physical
cash, but in the areas of assistance like if we have a conference, we would
want them to support, may be they can take care of the things for the
conference, things for us or may be the reception or the conference hall or
things like that.
What area will you are willing to work on?
Well I feel that we've significantly done a lot of job in the
Niger-Delta, we have another serious environment problem, which is the
problem of desert encroachment and desertification when our trees are being
fallen, our trees are being eaten away, specifically in the north, it is
about time that we begin to focus our attention on making sure of have to
solve desertification because of the future environmental problem that it
will cause, we all know the importance of plants and animals in the
well-being of mankind, we need to encourage a lot of tree planting, we need
to introduce methods that can sustain the northern environment, so we need
to look at that area seriously now.
Do you have any project on water?
Well, environment can never become it unless you bring the issue of
water pollution and sanitation, waste management. Right now, we are working
with the Japanese embassy to sensitise the public on water management, waste
management, sanitation in Nigeria, we are to jointly organise a workshop and
the Japanese embassy has given us the green light to act as facilitators. We
intend to bring the Minister of the Federal ministry of environment and the
federal ministry of water resources into it, so that we can jointly provide
the platform for Nigerians that are going to participate in that conference
in Japan to exchange ideas and go there with more information, so that they
will be more informed about their own situation so that when they get there,
they would be talking with more confidence with a lot of data and
information in their, in their portfolio, so we are trying to create that
kind of platform that we can explain that kind of information so that we
will go there well prepared.
Waste management has become a very big problem in Nigeria, what is
your organisation doing it?
The problem with waste management especially domestic waste is that we
turn out a lot of waste because of the kind of food we eat and that is a
very serious problem and very expensive. To manage waste properly, you have
to have a policy and our policy right now is not working. So I think we have
to start with creating awareness, providing information, the dangers and
hazards of not managing waste properly. When we do that, it is only then
that we can now move into the second phase, which is providing incinerators,
providing dump sites, because when you provide dump sites or incinerators,
you need to have equipment to move them to those sites, but you need to
educate the public first so that you need dump sites and lift wages to weigh
the dump so that you have trucks that can take them away.
But if you don't educate the people and don't create and awareness, you
will still not be able to identify a particular dump site and you also need
to provide the wheel so that incinerators are cleaned because they are very
expensive but I think what we have to do, we are just an NGO, and money is
not there, so our own part is to make sure that awareness is created, people
should know that waste should be managed. We don't have to dump everything,
there are things that can be recycled for example if you go to a country
like Japan, if you are in a hotel it is written "if you need your trowel
cleaned, indicate." Because they believe that if you use a towel for
example, if it is not too dirty, you can use it the next day, even the bed
lining.
Because anytime you go to wash those towels or the bed lining, a lot of
things are done, manpower wasted, the detergent goes to pollute the
environment, the water wastage and so on and so forth, and those societies,
they know this value, so everybody is responsible, in Nigeria people don't
have this information, people don't even know that every bottle of water
counts because we cannot just use things like it will not end, you know we
have to be careful, nowadays when you go to some lakes they are getting
small like fish ponds and they might be causing the problem we have with the
ozone layer, some of these lakes are drying off, so we have to be careful
the way we use everything, you know coming to how we use things. That is why
most items in Europe are recycled, but we don't have those facilities, so we
have to be able to use something and be able to use that thing again. So I
think we will concentrate on creating awareness and providing the necessary
information.
How are you going to go about the problem of waste management?
We will have to reach out to the housewives who are the major
stakeholders in the area of waste management, we have to reach out to them,
we also begin to educate our children in secondary and primary schools on
environmental soundness and waste management. Let me give you an example, in
Russia, if you drop a piece of paper on the ground, you will be surprised
that even a two year old child can recall your attention to it, but in
Nigeria it is normal for any level of person to throw papers, whatever in
his hands on the streets, this is not proper, we need to educate our people,
we must begin to learn to generally educate everybody instead of
apportioning blames.
We must begin to educate everybody, wherever you are, whatever
environment you are, you have to be conscious that you should leave that
place at worse the way it was when you get there, not to dirty the place,
not to create environmental eyesore before you leave a particular place. So
I think the education should be general and better to start with children,
school children, it should be part of their syllables and also make
housewives to be susceptible to information on environmental soundness and
waste management.
During the period of Environmental Sanitation our environment was
much better because of the War Against Indiscipline programme. Why has
Nigerian gone back to even dirtier environment as soon as the programme was
called off?
I think that whenever you force somebody to do anything, you can only
get the temporary result, we must begin to build a foundation, we must
establish the foundation so that it must be permanent and that's what we are
talking about, we must get the people educated, we must begin to work away
from forcing people to do something. You can force somebody to do something
for a period of time, but anytime that force is withdrawn he gets back to
his former position, but when you are able to educate the person with the
foundation, you will get a better result.
What has been your contribution towards food security?
I think that Nigeria has the potential to feed itself, even to export,
the problem we have with Nigeria is that we are all dependant on government,
nobody wants to go into agriculture, even the traditional farming method has
broken down, everybody from the villages are moving into town looking for
white collar job, looking for government jobs, the most lucrative industry
now is politics, public and the civil service, that's what is available
now. Nobody wants to do any other thing, take for instance in the
Niger-Delta, studies have shown that we have to go back and look at our
traditional system. We also need to go back and encourage those villagers
that need to grow food for Nigeria to go back to the farm, I think we need
to do a lot of job, we also need to give some dignity to agriculture, we
also need to make people understand that farming is a noble profession.
I think these are activities we need to do because studies have shown
that the right potentials available in the Niger-Delta can feed the entire
West African sub-region. We had a serious study and when you look back, we
remember the groundnut pyramid, the cocoa in the Western part of Nigeria,
even palm oil, I was in Malaysia, palm oil is one of the major revenue
generating product in Malaysia and we all know the history of how Malaysia
got its palm from the Nigerian shore. So the potentials, because of our
detached geographical terrain, because of our diverse land structures
arrangement, the North, the South, the West, basically you can produce
everything in Nigeria.
Fruits, paw paw, pineapple, oranges, mangoes etc. food crops, what have
you, look at yam, cassava, plantain, banana, we have everything even Irish
potatoes, sweet potatoes, they are all available. Look at the Plateau where
you can even have apple, so what is left for us is to go back and educate
our people. Look at our educational system, we will make sure that people
are encouraged to look at agriculture more positively and to see agriculture
as a noble profession, we also need to go back to the villages and encourage
them to do their normal subsistence farming, you know when you discuss
agriculture, people will always look at the subsidy, fertilisers and so
on.
Those are bureaucratic; those are the things that are meant for those
who don't even practice agriculture to get rich from. When you are talking
about fertiliser, you are talking about fertilisers that do not even get to
the farmers, fertilisers that get out of this country by the cartel; we
always look at agriculture in what amount of money can be provided to the
farmers when those people are not farmers. The agriculture we are talking
about, should be like the agriculture we used to have in those days when our
parents felt proud and thought that that is the only way they can sustain
their family and their economy.
That is the way agriculture should be seen, it should not be seen in
this kind of bogus bureaucratic arrangement of subsidy, providing loans to
farmers, all those noise, that is not what agriculture is all about,
agriculture is about first and foremost making people understand that there
is pride, there is dignity in farming, that farming is a good profession, it
is noble, that farming is sustainable, that farming is what you need to keep
your immediate family alive, healthy and you can provide even enough for the
education of your children, that is how we should look at farming.
Have you tried to empower some of the young farmers in your area?
Well, I will not say no because financially we have not been able to do
that, but we have always provided a platform for example we make sure that
young school leavers are brought together in a kind of programme, we call it
agricultural programme, school to learn where they are taught modern farming
method, they are encouraged to go into farming, they are encouraged to use
better seedlings, high breed and so on and so forth, those are the areas
that we've been involved, but we have not been financially involved, we have
been involved in providing the knowledge and giving them the opportunity to
learn modern techniques.

Original_Search_Title: Muluzi Declares No Third Term Announces Transitional Arrangements
Headline: Malawi: Muluzi Declares No Third Term Announces Transitional Arrangements
Body_Text:
Sanjika Palace-Blantyre, Malawi — My fellow Malawians,
I once again wish to address you on some issues of great national
importance, including the current food situation, the need for food
diversity and hard work, the need for unity, maintaining peace; and the way
forward as we move towards the 2004 general elections.
As you are aware, last year I declared a state of disaster over Malawi
quite early in the crisis and appealed to the international community for
assistance because of the food shortage problem that had affected the entire
country. I wish you to know that Government did everything possible to
import maize into the country in cooperation and partnership with our donors
to address the hunger situation.
On its part, Government imported 255,000 metric tonnes of maize through
the National Food Reserve Agency (NFRA). There are now 207, 234 metric
tonnes in stock at the National Food Reserve Agency. So far NFRA has issued
47, 766 metric tonnes to ADMARC for sale. I understand that out of this
amount 35, 804 metric tonnes has already been sold leaving a balance of 11,
962 metric tonnes, which is in ADMARC depots and markets throughout the
country.
My fellow Malawians, it was necessary to assist those who needed food
because hunger reduces the ability of people to work effectively in their
efforts to fight poverty. I am aware that not everyone received the free
maize due to some logistical distribution problems.
Nonetheless, the fact that there was and that there is still enough
maize in the country is in its self-gratifying and very important. I wish to
express my appreciation to those governments and international organizations
that responded favourably with the necessary food and non-food
assistance.
The real challenge too remains how we could move away from a repeat of
any hunger situation to food self-sufficiency. As some of you may know,
Government supplied free farm inputs to over 3 million households by
re-introducing the Starter Pack Programme. The overall maize production
forecast is that Malawi is likely to have 1.9 metric tonnes this year. This
is an improvement over last yearÂ¡Ãs production of 1.6 metric tonnes.
Nonetheless, this yearÂ¡Ãs production has been affected by drought in
some parts of Karonga, Chitipa, Mzimba, Salima, Balaka, Nsanje, Chikwawa,
and Machinga, among other areas. I wish to appeal to every Malawian to
carefully use any maize and other food crops they have to avoid a repeat of
last yearÂ¡Ãs hunger.
Yes, there is need to sell some to generate cash, but this must be done
wisely. In this regard, I would like to appeal to all leaders in their
various categories, at religious level, political level including
traditional chiefs, and NGOs to sensitise people against wanton selling of
the little harvest that they may have.
I have oftentimes asked you to diversify your food production as well as
eating habits. I wish to repeat that appeal now.
It is very important that we also learn to eat other foods such as rice,
potatoes, pumpkins, bananas, cassava and yams instead of relying solely on
maize nsima all the time.
I have never hidden the fact that Malawi is one of the least developed
countries in the world. It is for this reason that poverty eradication has
remained the centrepiece of my governmentÂ¡Ãs development policy.
I however strongly recognize that our fight against poverty cannot be
achieved effectively without high productivity of food and cash
crops. TodayÂ¡Ãs highly developed countries in North America, Europe and Asia
have forged ahead because the people there work for longer hours.
It is in this regard that I wish to urge you, my fellow Malawians, to
change our attitude towards work. Let us develop good work ethics and let us
work extra hard. No country anywhere in the world can progress economically
where people do not apply their full potential. Hard work is the superior of
capital or any human social and economic development.
Let us have a sense of belonging to our nation, adapt collective
responsibility and stand on the wheel of competition. Let it be upon each
one of you to use your creative effort to work very hard and do the best for
your country. Let it be everyoneÂ¡Ãs duty to work very hard and improve
oneself, in turn that will improve the economy of Malawi.
It is sad to note that there are some of us who are still asking, Â¡ÃWhat
is it that the nation of Malawi has done for us?Â¡Ã The question should
rather be, Â¡ÃWhat have we done for our country?Â¡Ã This is our country and no
one, and I mean no one will emancipate us from this poverty situation.
I am pleased to inform the nation that the inflation rate has dropped to
10.5 percent from JanuaryÂ¡Ãs 14.8 percent due to continued decline of maize
grain and flour prices. This is a very welcome development, but we need to
work harder so that it drops to a single digit.
I wish to appeal to the banking sector to address the issue of lending
rates that are choking private sector development. In a country where oneÂ¡Ãs
business returns are very low one cannot expect much private sector
productivity. The private sector can only take off to greater heights if the
bank lending rates are lower and conducive.
I have several times reminded you that peace and unity are pre-requisite
to any meaningful social, economic and political progress. There is an old
saying that remains true even now: Â¡ÃUnited we stand. Divided we fall.Â¡Ã
As I have said before elsewhere, I am a strong believer in peace and
unity. I want all Malawians to regard each other as sisters and brothers of
Mother Malawi. I condemn regionalism and any form of tribalism. We are one
people and we have to be one. We need to always remain united and co-exist
peacefully as one people. I thus wish to appeal to all leaders of churches,
mosques, political parties and civil society organizations to preach love,
peace and unity.
It is in this context that in Chikwawa I talked about the need for Young
Democrats in whatever form and from whatever party that they must desist
from acts of violence. I would like to repeat that condemnation
now. Political violence is a threat to any young democracy and any political
leader must not tolerate it. Politics must border on competition of ideas,
viewpoints and delivery of service. It must not be a competition of
violence.
We are in a democratic Malawi and I want Malawians to know the
difference between Young Democrats and the former Youth Leaguers of the
one-party regime. I want Young Democrats in whatever form and whichever
political party to be promoters of peace, love, unity, and development. I
therefore appeal to all Young Democrats to desist from acts of violence and
in a credible manner turn their energies into peaceful and developmental
activities.
In particular, I wish to call upon all members of my party, the United
Democratic Front to always remember that as a ruling party they have a duty
to be exemplary in avoiding violence even when provoked. The UDF is a party
of peace and we are the present custodian of this countryÂ¡Ãs peace and
stability.
I urge the police to carry out their duties and responsibilities to
maintain law and order. They have a duty to bring to justice anyone and I
mean anyone engaging in any form of violence without fear or favour. I ask
them to do so in their most professional and ethical manner regardless to
which political party or pressure group the perpetrators of violence
belong.
Since I have made myself perfectly clear on this matter, I would hold
the police and any party leader accountable should they fail to take
appropriate action in the face of political violence from any quarter.
My fellow Malawians, I also wish to address you about the way forward as
we approach the 2004 general elections. I would like all political parties
to brace for a peaceful and successful 2004 general election.  We must start
preparing for the elections now. Normally, those poorly prepared easily cry
foul and turn violent. We have chosen the path of democracy and peace. We
must therefore be democratic and peaceful in our conduct.
Immediately after the outcome of the 1999 general elections, a
journalist asked me whether I was grooming someone to take over as a
president of Malawi or not. My answer was Â¡ÃNoÂ¡Ã because we had five years
to go. I believed that the talk would have diverted our focus from many,
many crucial developmental issues such as poverty, HIV/AIDS, provision of
health, water, education and infrastructure facilities to our people.
Even when people were debating the need to change the Constitutional
clause related to the number of terms a president of Malawi would be in
office, I refused to comment. And I have never commented on the matter
because I believed that they were only exercising their democratic rights. I
have repeatedly informed the nation that I am a true democrat.
As a true democrat, I fought for democracy, the respect for human
rights, good governance, the rule of law, freedom of worship and
association, freedom of the press and all the tenets of guarding
democracy. And because I strongly believe in the freedom of expression, it
would have been improper for me to comment on this constitutional
matter.
As Malawians you were entitled to express your views on the issue
because there is freedom of expression in this country. Now that we are
about thirteen months to the May 2004 general elections, I find it very
necessary to tell the nation the way forward to ensure a smooth and proper
transition for a successful 2004 general elections and beyond.
At a joint meeting of the National Executive Committee of the United
Democratic Front and the entire cabinet comprising 54 members, the issue of
succession was thoroughly discussed. The United Democratic Front as a
democratic party, everyone was given an opportunity to speak his or her mind
on the issue. And those who wanted to stand as a Presidential Candidate for
the UDF in the 2004 General Elections were given the opportunity to declare
their interest.
Three members declared their interests. These were Honourable Aleke
Banda, Honourable Dr. Bingu Wa Mutharika and Honourable Harry Thomson. After
thorough deliberations on each candidate, a vote was taken and the following
were the results:
1. Honourable Aleke Banda 0 vote 2. Honourable Harry Thomson 0 vote
3. Honourable Dr. Bingu Wa Mutharika 54 votes.
I am therefore pleased to announce that the National Executive Committee
and the cabinet have proposed Dr. Bingu Wa Mutharika as a candidate to stand
on the UDF ticket in the 2004 Presidential Elections. Dr. Bingu Wa
Mutharika, was previously Deputy Governor of the Reserve Bank of Malawi and
is now Minister of Economic Planning and Development. He has a distinguished
background in Monetary Economics, a Masters Degree in Economics and a
Doctorate Degree in Development Economics.
He hails from Kamoto Village, Traditional Authority Chimaliro, in Thyolo
District. Dr. Mutharika, served as a senior officer at the World Bank, the
United Nations, PTA and COMESA.
When I formed the United Democratic Front in the early 1990Â¡Ãs he became
one of the founding members of the UDF External Wing of Zambia. I appointed
him as shadow Minister of Physical Planning and Surveys.
According to the Malawi Republican Constitution, a Presidential running
mate is normally the prerogative of the Presidential Candidate. After full
consultation, Dr. Mutharika proposed Dr. Cassim Chilumpha as his running
mate in the 2004 Presidential Elections, a proposal that the National
Executive Committee and the Cabinet has unanimously accepted.
Dr. Cassim Chilumpha has a doctorate Degree in Business and Corporate
Law. He is a Member of Parliament for Nkhotakota South and has served in
various cabinet portfolios.
These are proposals, suggestions and thoughts from the UDF governing
body. There will be need for these names to be discussed at the UDFÂ¡Ãs
convention, which will be heard soon.
The UDF National Executive Committee and the Cabinet have furthermore
considered restructuring the party to provide for a separate party governing
body. Both the National Executive Committee and the Cabinet have requested
me to consider the position of the UDFÂ¡Ãs National Chairmanship after the
2004 General Elections. This is a matter I will give due consideration when
the time comes.
All UDF members wherever they may be should have comfort in the fact
that should I accept the National Chairmanship of the UDF, I will give the
party and its members all my possible support. I want the UDF to remain
strong so that it wins the 2004 general elections and beyond. I hope that
this will remove the fears some of the UDF supporters may have had.
Let me appeal to all Malawians that from this threshold, we should focus
our attention not on politics, but on development issues that affect our
social and economic welfare. I have extensively travelled to many areas of
Malawi and I feel very concerned with the poverty level. Despite our
tireless efforts, we still have to work together and very hard to eradicate
poverty. This is my concern and my concern will be to continue improving the
living standards of people for the better.
Let me state here that this is not my farewell speech. The General
Elections are many months ahead of us. But it became incumbent upon me to
make this statement so that we all concentrate on finding solutions to the
many challenges affecting our nation. For example, the HIV/AIDS problem, the
need for giving our people more schools, hospitals and clinics, more roads
and empowering them to produce enough food. This should be our agenda from
now onwards. It is therefore very important that we all pay our attention to
these issues and pull together our potentials and efforts.
In my life, I have always put God first. It is hence my humble prayer
that the Almighty God should continue guiding and blessing us all. I believe
that it is only through God that the democracy, peace, unity and stability
that we have enjoyed during the last nine years, will grow and
flourish. This is very important not just for us but also for our children
and their children.
I thank you all for your attention. May God bless you all and bless our
nation.

Original_Search_Title: N10m Goods Razed in Market Fire
Headline: Nigeria: N10m Goods Razed in Market Fire
Body_Text:
Osogbo, Osun — More than N10 million worth of goods were destroyed when fire gutted the Atakunmosa Market in Ilesa West Local Government, in Osun State.
The incident occured in the early hours of last Wednesday destroying 25 stalls in the market.
The cause of the fire was not immediately known but reliably sources blamed it on electrical fault.
Among the goods destroyed were food stuffs, including bags of rice, beans, garri, several tubers of yam, cassava and fruit items.
Also destroyed were 20 grinding machine used for commercial purposes.
Chairman, Transition Committee of Atakunmosa Local Government, Hon. Wale Webster-Esho, who visited the scene, lamented the level of destruction.
