He appealed to the victims to remain calm. Webster-Esho gave the assurance that the local government would come to the aid of the traders by re-constructing the destroyed stalls and providing cash to put the victims back in business.
He called on the state government to provide infrastructural facilities for the traders in the market.

Original_Search_Title: Relief As Food Prices Drop
Headline: Nigeria: Relief As Food Prices Drop
Body_Text:
Rivers — Port Harcourt residents may heave a sigh of relief as prices of food
items have dropped considerably, compared to their prices same time last
year.
According to a News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) survey in Rumuwoji Market,
Diobu, a bag of rice, which sold for N4,500 last year, now sells for
N3,900.
Meanwhile, half a bag of the same item has fallen from N2,250 to N1,850
since the beginning of the year.
NAN reports that a bag of beans, which previously sold for N6,500, now
sells for
N6,000, while a bag of pepper has dropped from N19,000 to N16,000.
The survey showed that a bag of garri has dropped from N7,000 to N4,000,
while a basin of the commodity, which sold for N1,000 last year, now goes
for N850.
According to the survey, the price of palm oil also dropped from N3,300
to N1,900 for a 20-litre container, while a 20-litre container of groundnut
oil has remained at N2,500.
A bag of Ogbono, which cost N28,000 last year, now sells for N24,000,
while the price of a bag melon has dropped to N19,000, down from the
previous N20,000 last year.
NAN reports that of all the commodities, only tubers of yam have
recorded an increase, with a heap of the produce selling for N22,000, up
from the N18,000 for which the commodity was sold for last year.
A rice dealer, Mrs Ngozi Orji, attributed the fall in prices to the fact
that no major festival had ever been celebrated in the state since the
beginning of the year.
She attributed the seasonal increase in the prices of food items to
festivities, during which almost everyone shops for food items.

Original_Search_Title: My Plan for Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: My Plan for Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Onyema Ugochukwu chairman, Niger Delta Development Commission, NDDC, talks to Newswatch editors on his commission's achievements, problems and master-plan for promoting development in oil-producing states. Excerpts:
Newswatch: What has your commission achieved in the last two years?
Ugochukwu:  You know the circumstances of the establishment of NDDC, the law setting it up was a bit controversial.  And at the end, it was established through a veto over the president's objection.  The principle thing that Mr. President complain about was the financial contributions to be made by each of the major stakeholders.  He had wanted the federal and the state governments to contribute to it.  The oil companies of course, were also to contribute and some funds were to come from ecological funds.  The National Assembly excluded state governments in the bill they passed which was what Mr. President objected to.  There was agreement before the inauguration that an amendment to the bill be sent back to the National Assembly.  The agreement was with the governors of the nine oil-producing states.  That amendment is to provide for the state governments to contribute funds.  Unfortunately, that amendment is still with the National Assembly two years after it was submitted and nothing has happened.
So in terms of funding the NDDC received from the Federal Government 10 percent of the amount due to the state governments of the nine states from the Federation Account.  The law says 15 percent actually but what we have got so far is calculated at 10 percent.  The oil companies pay three percent of their total budget.  We and them are having an argument over whether it is three percent of their operating budget or three percent of the total budget.  We have not received any funds at all from the ecological funds.  Part of the reason, of course, was a certain error in the wording of the NDDC Act itself.   It says that the NDDC is to receive 50 percent of funds in that account due to the nine member states.  The minister in charge of ecological funds, then said there was no money due to any state from the start.  He claimed that he had consulted the then attorney-general, the late Chief Bola Ige.  But the amendment now took charge of that.  The amendment is still out there.  Meanwhile, the ecological fund has been reduced in the new revenue formula to one percent.  So we have not got any funds from that place.
Newswatch:  When eventually the amendment takes place, are you going to get the funds retrospectively?
Ugochukwu:  No, I don't think so.  The issue is that the Supreme Court judgment on revenue-sharing affected the Federal Government's own share of funds.  Secondly, the bill now before the National Assembly which was submitted by the Revenue Mobilisation and Fiscal Commission also reduced the percentage share of the Federal Government and also reduced the ecological fund to one percent instead of two percent that it was.  The reduction itself should mean that the money available may be applied in a different way from how it was being applied before.  That is the situation.
But your question is, what have we achieved? I will like to put it this way.  We have been able to design a strategy for addressing the problems of the Niger Delta on a sustainable basis.  This is in line with Mr. President's directive for the preparation of an integrated master-plan for the whole region.  A master-plan which will provide a framework for all agencies working in the region to harmonise their activities.  These agencies, of course, will include the Federal Government, the state governments, the NDDC itself, the oil companies, local governments and communities.   They will also have a shared vision when there will be a reduction in the duplication of projects, duplication of efforts which are ultimately wasteful.
We have gone very far with that master-plan which we awarded a German firm which is working with a Nigerian firm.  What we wanted to do with the master-plan was to develop 26 sectoral master-plans.  For instance, we have take agriculture. Even agriculture is broken into two water resources, urban sanitation and so on.  Our consultants have been appointed for these sectoral master-plans and they are nearly all Nigerians.  We have more than a hundred consultants working in the different sectors.  The idea is that they will go down to communities, we want a bottom-up approach.  We start by talking with the communities for them to make an input, that is the vision of their own development.  Then we come up to local government, to states and then we have the holistic one.  That is the first part of the strategy.
The master-plan will be ready in September, this year. But bearing in mind the situation out here, the restiveness and the very high level of poverty in this area, we developed an interim action plan which involves building projects, facilities in the health area, schools, roads, water, shore protection and channelisation, jetties and so on.  We actually say that this interim action plan is a crisis management programme.  We did not expect  that the people will be patient until the master-plan will be completed.  So we have to begin to address some of their basic needs.  In choosing what we address, we take into account projects that would fit into the master-plan when it is ready.
Newswatch:  Do you consult the communities before you carry out these projects?
Ugochukwu: Yes. When we started, we went on tour and talked to communities.  We asked them to tell us what they want in their place, the things they want and that since we couldn't do all of them at the same time, they should prioritise.  That is what they have done with some communities bringing their own proposals.  These are the project areas.  There is the human development part of the interim action plan, which deals with engaging the youths in a more positive way in skills acquisition and entreprenuership developments.  There are several programmes we have on hand, some of them will start running very soon.  For instance, in the computer skill training programme, we started sending out letters to some of the people who applied.  We expected that about 10,000 people can be trained in one year under that programme.  But unlike in the past when people who  go through  this acquisition come out and nobody employs them, we want follow-up with micro-credit schemes to enable those who want to set up whatever they want to do.
Not just in computer, we have agriculture programme which includes fisheries, crop development, transportation, co-operatives formed by youths to run either bus or boat services within this area and even waste disposal.  Those are being developed.  In terms of the projects we have done, I think we awarded 740 projects.  Many of these are roads and they are under construction really and they are doing very well.  The work of course, was delayed by the rains.  In this area, you can hardly work on a road during the rainy season but all the contractors are taking advantage of the dry season to make as much progress as possible.  Actually many of the smaller roads are ready.  There are some here in   Rivers   State   which are ready.  The others like I said, are school buildings.  We have a prototype design, six classroom blocks with office for the principal or head teacher and a store with a VIP toilet attached.  The VIP toilet is not the normal VIP, it is the ventilated improved pit toilet.  Not that we are building marble toilets in the school.  We are also building jetties where we have committed a lot.  We have committed a lot into water supply.  It is ironical that if you take a state like Bayelsa which is surrounded by water  but here is no water to drink.  Even in the state capital, the first thing that strikes you as you drive in is the number of people selling water with trucks.  The NDDC is fast tackling the water project we  are doing in Yenagoa which should have a capacity of 100,000 gallons.   It is almost ready, it should be ready within the  next three months or so.  One of the roads we  are building also will lead  from the   East-West Road   to Yenagoa.  Right now, if a Bayelsan, say from Kaima ,wants to go to his state capital, he will first have to drive  to   Rivers   State   at Maima and then turn.  So, that road we are constructing will be the first access that is entirely within the state.  We are doing a lot of electrification also.
There are so many of the projects.  We try to document these projects so that we can track them.  There are electrification projects in 28 communities in Rivers and Bayelsa states which are going on now.  Some are old OMPADEC projects which were abandoned and we have taken up and they are making very good progress.  There are the hospitals of course.  Very many of them.  As part of the human development scheme, we also have free medical programmes which we run in association with pro-health and other charitable medical organisations.  They bring in doctors from all over the world including Nigerians who come and give their time for a brief period.  We go from zone to zone to do this programme.  It has been very, very successful really.  So these are the things we think we have achieved.
Newswatch:  I have always wondered if NDDC is just a change of name for OMPADEC.
Ugochukwu:  No, it is not just a change of name.  The OMPADEC was more in line with all the previous attempts to create an intervention agency in the Niger Delta.  Starting with the Niger Delta Development Board of 1962 through to the River Basin Development Authority to the 1.5 percent fund to OMPADEC.  They tell you it's a crises management approach in the nature of what we now call our interim action plan.  There was no planning, no master-plan or any effort to develop a master-plan that will harmonise activities.  And because there was no plan, there was no sustainability in the sense that if we take OMPADEC, when we came, we inherited from OMPADEC 1,300 projects that were not completed.  They were not functional and nobody was using them.  So our own approach is to say if these projects can be made useful, we will make them useful.  And a lot of the projects I am talking of were part of these abandoned projects - that we have completed and made them useful.  So there is a great difference between us and OMPADEC.
We have made the master-plan as the first step because without the plan you cannot sustain it.  If each person who comes in to build a school or whatever and nobody uses it, it is a waste.  A lot of water works even when they were commissioned have already broken down.  No plans to maintain them.  They couldn't sustain them.  I was talking about the master-plan and the bottom-up approach which is a major departure point for the NDDC.  We believe  that unless the people own the process of their development then there is no way anybody can sustain it.  That is why we want their involvement.  The people must be in the driving seat  of their own development.  So our own is to involve them right from conception, design and implementation.  And that is the only way you can sustain any development plan.  If the people don't own it, they can't sustain it.
The other departure point for us arises from the act itself.  The NDDC is conceived as a partnership.  A partnership of various agencies and stakeholders who will  unite in developing  this place.  The president said at our inauguration that the NDDC is not to take over the functions of other agencies or government, that there is nothing in the law that says the NDDC alone should  be responsible for developing the Niger Delta region.  We have made a great effort to forge a partnership with all the agencies.  We go on very well with several of the state governments, with the oil companies, with the World Bank, the UNDP. They want to be our partners in providing certain services in the area.  I believe the   United States   will also participate.  There is a delegation from the   United States    which will be in the country later this month.  They have asked to meet with us, so that we will be the platform for the aid they want to bring into the Niger Delta.  Last November, for instance, we attended a partly World Bank-sponsored conference in   New Orleans ,  USA  , comparing Rivers Mississipi and the   Niger  .  And the purpose was also to learn from the experience of the Mississipi because its root is almost like that of the River Niger in the Delta area and then to see ways where we can work together in developing the course of the River Niger.  I was thinking that one of our biggest achievements is that we have developed sufficient credibility for major world bodies, the World Bank, the UNDP.  They also want to work with us, for us to be the platform for their involvement in the Niger Delta.
Newswatch: There is some evidence to show that some of the indigenes sabotaged the activities of OMPADEC in terms of contracts and there is also the feeling that all major contracts must be given to the indigenes.  What is your experience so far?
Ugochukwu:  There is a design that all contracts must be done by the indigenes of the beneficiary communities.  I think that is part of the history of this area.  In the sense that you may even have two neighbouring communities and if you locate a project in one and you get a contractor from the neighbouring community, the youths will not allow him to work.  There are so many instances.  But our policy was to localise the execution of the job as much as possible.  This was why we run the contract award process from the state offices so that the people in the state will be the main beneficiaries.  These are for small or medium projects.  We also apply the policy, which says that where an inherited OMPADEC job was completed up to 85 percent, preference should be given to the original contractor to complete it.  For major projects, our policy is to go for the best.  When we were involved with the discussions, we are still involved, on the possible dualisation of the   East-West Road  , we went for Julius Berger to at least do maintenance while the new road is being designed and constructed.  You would have seen Julius Berger if you came through the road from   Port Harcourt   to Warri.
About communities saying that they are not benefiting, you see we are in a petition zone and frequently people are writing when they don't know who got the job.  But our own study shows that we probably achieved up to 85 percent of jobs localised in the communities.  This has helped to dampen the hostility which normally occurs when any contractor goes to work in these areas.  It usually occurs once they begin to clear the ground. In many places they charge you bush clearing fee, then you must employ a number of youths, depending on the size of the project and your negotiating ability, you will have to employ up to 30 youths from the community who didn't have to do  any work but you will pay them the salary.  It is called sitting allowance and upon all these problems people disrupt the work.  But we have had a very peaceful experience in areas where these things are localised.  They know the people who we are giving the job.  It has served us well.
Newswatch: Has there been any case of indigenous contractors from these localities taking the money and abandoning the job?
Ugochukwu: No. Our payment system is totally different from what operated before.  We give you a job, before you get any money, you give a bank guarantee.  We make it easy for people by committing the full amount, if we start a job we keep aside the full amount with the bank.  We also involve the bank in the supervision of the project itself because if the contractor walks off, we recover our money from the bank.  So the banks are involved in the supervision of the projects.  But we also insist that they do not put a heavy burden on the contractor by charging or cooking up the books and saying they are giving them overdraft.  With our contractors, any bank that charges more than the two percent we agree, we help the contractor to recover.  Many banks have tried.  In fact, there are many cases.  But there have also been many cases when we forced the bank to return the money to the contractor.  So we haven't had any abandonment.  We are doing intense monitoring.  For instance, during the Christmas week, we formed nine teams made up of board members, directors and staff with the engineers, one for each state.  It was Christmas week between the 26 of December and 2nd of January, our people were touring every project to assess what phase of completion because we need to complete whatever we start.  And now, we have been reviewing what needs to be done where a job hasn't been completed.  We have established that we have completed more than four  hundred and something projects.  But for us it is not the end of the job.  The end of the job is, is it commissionable?  Even  if it is completed, is it commissionable, is it going to serve the people?  On that basis I think we now have 231 completed. Nobody has abandoned our  job yet.
Newswatch: When you commissioned those projects and hand them over to the communities, what do you do to ensure that the projects will be maintained and managed to function well?
Ugochukwu: We have been conscious of that.  Ideally, we will like to hand over to some levels of government.  Either the state government or the local government.  When this is not possible, we hand over to the community development associations.  This question is very important especially in respect of water.  What we found is that the first day the man who commissions it drinks a glass and that's the end of it.  We want our contractors, especially for water, to run the plant for six months during which a community youth will be trained to run it for them.  And then we are developing a programme for periodic maintenance of those that will not be handled.  We want them to function.  In terms of schools, most of the schools that we have been involved in are located in existing schools.  Out prototype building is really beautiful.
Newswatch: You talked about co-operation between NDDC and other levels of governments in order to minimise duplication of projects and that you will hand over some of these commissioned projects to them.  Have you considered a situation where a state government  that is not controlled by PDP will not maintain those projects? Don't you envisage the type of conflict that could lead to the breakdown of some of these projects?
Ugochukwu:  What you are saying is very, very true.  You know these are political days, differences in parties even in states run by PDP government, we do have problems of state governments believing that they should approve NDDC projects.  It was quite bad until the governors were then constituted as an advisory committee-the nine governors with two people from outside representing national interest.  I am a member and the managing director is the secretary.  It helped to improve the communication system.  It is not working completely well.  There are sometimes, because of political differences or ambitions, people want to run down what is being done.  There are hostilities that develop.  Also sometimes NDDC proposes a project at the request of the communities, we have many instances where the state government runs in and either awards a contract or starts doing something there.  It has happened so many  times.  The experience is that in a lot of the cases even when the contracts have been awarded, we keep getting letters from the communities that we should take over our projects.  But we have representatives of every state on our board.  That is the nine-member states and we try to use them to improve community and government relations within their states.  The way we see it is that we are here to work in partnership with the state governments.  It is true that many state governments see us as competitors rather than collaborators. Many of the state governments are actually writing us that they will like to commission our projects and take them over. But there are places where of, course, the state governments will say NDDC has not done anything, if it has they are not interested, so that is where our maintenance programme comes in. Newswatch: In 1992 the Justice Belgore Commission that looked into the problems of the Niger Delta came out with a report that talked about the master-plan on a short, medium and long-term and Etiebet's Commission set up by Abacha in its report quoted copiously from the report of Justice Belgore. I wonder whether you have had the chance to look at these two reports because they seem to be germane to the problems of the Niger Delta.
Ugochukwu: I personally have not looked at them. I know of them. There is in fact, an earlier master-plan which was commissioned in 1960 by the Niger Delta Development Board. The master-plan was done by a Dutch company then. The minister of science and technology was telling me about it the other day and I have asked our people to try and locate it. The master-plan consultants would probably have got these. Part of their job is to find out what anybody has done in the region. There is also another one of the report of the NDDC, the Onosode group which was funded by the oil companies. You know the report has not been printed. The cost of printing it is enormous. But we have involved them as sector consultants in our own master-plan. So they are participating. The great improvement in our own is that most of the earlier work concentrated on the core Niger Delta. It was Rivers, Delta, Bayelsa. It didn't include all these other states. Now there are nine states, it's a bigger area. We also tried to complete an abandoned satellite mapping project. I think it was abandoned by OMPADEC. We would ensure that the map shows everything on the ground, including houses. Now part of the work of the consultant handling the GIS - Geographic Information System, it will be computer-based, is to now go on the ground and confirm what those pictures are showing and it will be updated so that if for instance a place is a bush and say tomorrow houses come up there, it will be updated in the computer. That segment has already been done.
Newswatch: You talked about the human angle to this problem in the area. I am wondering,  is it ever going to be possible for an organ like yours to re-orientate the youths in this part of the country to do something other than just sit down and be collecting money?
Ugochukwu: You know a few years ago there was restiveness in this area, violence. Unfortunately, as we talk now, there is violence in Warri and all that. So it is going to take time to re-orientate people. Our own reasoning is that a lot of the problems was caused by poverty and unemployment. So you have a whole lot of angry young people who have no jobs and probably no hope, that is why they engage in all those things. Our approach is to engage them in a constructive dialogue. We talk to them. The president has approved the creation of a new directorate which we are setting up now to address the issues of youths, women and so on. We have involved them in the jobs we have done. We have had seminars with them, where we tried to identify how many youth groups are legitimate. Of course, what you find here is that anybody can make a letter heading, put one name and come to NDDC to threaten people. But we now have sense of the size of youth groups. So what we do with some of our jobs, where they are not technically complicated a group of youths, each group, you give us names of youths who form a team, bring your company and register, you work on this, it's for that group. That way we have got a lot of them involved.
Secondly, there are smaller projects, like when we were supplying science equipment in schools, but we didn't want each person to buy the equipment because then we will not be sure of the quality once it is engraved NDDC. Each school gets enough equipment for physics, chemistry, biology and agriculture laboratories. So we now target the schools as a contract, the delivery of these things, the building of the cupboards to fit them and we gave it to the youths and the women. They make some money, they have some income. I think because of our policy of also asking for performance we have greatly increased the banking habit among young people of these areas. Many of these people were people who never had anything to do with banks. I think it's working. In general the violence has diminished.
Initially, when we came, they used to come and they will tell you there will be no peace without them, and we will reply that there will be no development without peace. And in many cases we will tell the communities, if you don't want the projects, you are the beneficiaries we will go away. We are not going to let you blackmail anybody or terrorise them. In many places, they tried it, we threatened to call off the project. Sometimes they kidnap the contractors' workers. When the contractor is gone away and they see what is happening in other communities, they come back to us, can you please tell the contractor to go back. This has happened in many places. We know Warri is probably the most violent place within the area. There is a road  there in Warri which  we are doing, the youths after demanding all the usual ground-breaking, employment allowance and the others, they were still harassing the contractor, then he packed his things and left. Of course he reported to us. Then the community met with the youths again and wrote us. This is in Okere,  in fact, Okere is the centre of all the youth restiveness. The community and the youths now wrote to us to please ask the contractor to come back, they will not disturb him again.
Newswatch: I think we are missing something here. The fundamental peculiar problems of the Niger Delta. What exactly are the peculiar fundamental problems of the Niger Delta that your commission is supposed to be addressing other than building of schools, roads, water, etc. What are your statutory responsibilities?
Ugochukwu: Let me address even these basic facilities you mentioned. We engage in that because they don't exist. They don't exist in these communities. There are also so many communities in the area. Maybe I should start afresh. The Niger Delta is a very, very difficult terrain. It starts from the ocean and goes into the blackish swamps into the fresh water swamps and then down here the river begins to meander and turn all over the place. There are no roads. The poverty level is extreme really. In fact, I used to wonder why people settled by the bank of the river. And what happens is that the canoe or the boat is the means of travelling. So you get down at the bank, you have to walk to your house. So everybody tries to stay near the river. There is land behind but nobody wants to move that far because of the means of transportation. So this creates its own peculiar problem. Building the facilities in these areas costs enormous amount of money because of the nature, the rivers and so on. It costs a lot amount of money. But if you take what I just told you, the Federal Government has built a road from the   East-West Road   to Abonema. It is now possible to drive from   Port Harcourt   to Abonema. Even with   Port Harcourt   traffic problems, you can leave   Port Harcourt   and you are in Abonema or Buguma or Degema in less than one hour. The only route you used to go to that place is the river which takes about two hours even when you are using a speed boat. There were no roads inside the towns and so there weren't cars. There were cars in Buguma, I think rich men used to ferry their cars to their towns and then drive from there to Degema. Now taxi parks or bus parks have developed in Abonema but it has to be right at the end of the bridge.
To illustrate again the problem of the terrain, if you take that road most of it is bridge. You will first cross the Somberiro while it is moving east, it comes out again somewhere else, you cross it when moving west. So there are, I think four longish bridges across that  river at different points. So the cost of building a road is enormous. Communication is the next  most  important problem that the people have. If we are able to build roads in these areas, things will change. The woman who catches her fish say at Oworokoto in the Andoni area cannot get it here. Sometimes before they get here the fish has gone bad, you know the problem. Whereas if there were roads, these people will then be able to move their goods, it will help to improve life. The second problem of course is employment arising from lack of industrial base. This is not peculiar to the area but this is an area that is richly blessed with resources that can form the basis for the industrial development of the area. We are looking at that ourselves in the sense that we want to help create sustainable livelihood. You see the problem when people complain here about the effect of oil production, they leave out pollution. The gas that is flared here causes acid rain. And the gas in the Niger Delta area forms about two million tonnes of carbon dioxide into the air everyday. It is a major threat to the world climate. A lot of this comes back as acid rain. It destroys crops and fishes. The people of these areas are fishermen and farmers. In the Ogoni area which used to be a large provider, a food basket, yams and so on, now food is going into Ogoni from outside. People are taking in yams to sell in Ogoniland.  These are farmers.
Now the oil industries that are at the base of the problem cannot employ all of them. So their livelihood is affected, the cause of that decline for their livelihood cannot provide them alternative livelihood. So we are looking at a scheme. There are several schemes already, which should help these people to run their own businesses. That is why we are running the entrepreneurship scheme using materials or raw materials available in their locality. And then building new livelihood, new employment. So the problem is enormous. We need massive investment of money to turn around this place. The way I see it is this, we are not necessarily the one that will have to build these things. But we want to create an environment that will attract both Nigerian and foreign investors to come here and help also in developing the place.
Newswatch: How much have you spent so far in the development projects, how much have you collected and how much of the OMPADEC debts have you been able to liquidate?
Ugochukwu: No, you know the Act setting up NDDC says it will not inherit OMPADEC debts. Ahead of the setting up of the NDDC, the president set up a committee headed by Imoke, the presidential committee on OMPADEC projects, which came up and tried to verify the debt. The law says we should not inherit the debt because they are supposed to have been settled and so we are not inheriting any debt. In 2001, we had a budget of N36 billion and in 2002 a budget of N40 billion. That represented our hopes but all the money we have got in two years up to December 2002 is N44 billion. We generated more than one billion of that by depositing our money in interest-bearing account. So what should have come from all the other sources is a little less than N43 billion and that is more than two years. Like I said earlier, if we award a contract we set aside the full amount for it. We do that so that lack of money will not become a reason for not completing the job. So many projects were abandoned here, in fact, all over   Nigeria  , by people who over-estimated their cash flow and by the system of course because everybody who comes into a place leaves what his predecessors started and starts a new one, invariably he never finishes them and then things go up. So we decided that we are not going to contribute to abandoned projects in this area. So we set aside money. That money is in the bank and we earn some interest on it.
In terms of commitments, we are looking at N35 billion on roads and all other projects. That we have actually paid. I don't have the exact figure but I think it is probably under N20 billion because we pay by milestones. You get to an agreed milestone then you are entitled to a part of the payment even when the money has been set aside. That is how we run it. But if you look at the money, and this is the amazing thing because sometimes people say the NDDC has not developed the Niger Delta, sometimes even state governments say eh' what is the NDDC doing in two years it hasn't developed the Niger Delta? First of all, like I said earlier nobody says the NDDC alone should develop the Niger Delta. But if you look at the amount of money that has been made available to us in these two years, it is an average of N22 billion per annum. If you are to do a per capital calculation with the 20 million people who live here what it will come to per head is about one thousand per head. If you go on the basis of budget which is of course unrealised that will be N76 billion over two years giving an average N38 billion. Now if you do the arithmetic it is under two thousand naira. Now compare it with say the Federal Government budget for the whole country say one trillion naira and you apply the population of about one hundred million, the Federal Government budget will come to about ten thousand naira for every Nigerian. Then you look at some state governments here who budget N60 billion and the population is somewhere around three million, the average is N20,000 per head. Our budget is poorer than any other level of government you can think of on a per capita basis. Then when you consider what we have actually done it shows you the kind of problem one has to deal with.
Newswatch: Does NDDC have any special programmes to solve the peculiar problems of Ogoniland?
Ugochukwu: It is not a special programme different from what we are doing elsewhere but we do treat the Ogoni area as a special area. The law setting up the NDDC says that we should have regard for oil production levels in allocating our spending. The Ogoni area used to produce about 30,000 barrels of oil up to the early 90s. These wells have been shut. So technically the area doesn't produce oil now. But we have taken the position that the Ogoni area is a special area and therefore must not be tied to this kind of calculation which we use in deciding what any state gets relative to the other, what any local government gets relative to the other. We do all these calculations. But we treat the Ogoni area as a special area.
Newswatch: Let's look at other national dimensions. Have you been able to make peace with your state governor?
Ugochukwu: Well, we are talking. You see the process of going to the PDP convention that was held early January was very, very difficult. Governors obviously wanted to be re-elected, other politicians wanted relevance or wanted to run. You know all these things created their own conflicts. In the end, the PDP itself intervened more or less and nearly every state gave the party structure in the state to the governor even when the  governor didn't have the credentials to merit that. For me the main objective was, I work for the president, to contribute to his re-nomination and re-election. For that objective alone, we had to put aside our egos or any other considerations and join in the reconciliation to ensure that most Abia votes will go to the president. So we are exploring ways of developing a relationship that will take account of the grievances of everybody.
Newswatch: How are you looking at the entire presidential elections with the numerous political parties? How do you think the election will go?
Ugochukwu: With so many parties, it will create problems for INEC. I keep imagining what the ballot papers will look like. That is one. And you talk of the election costs. A lot more. With the number, the cost will probably have tripled. I think the major parties will be successful, and of course the most major of the parties is the PDP whose candidate President Olusegun Obasanjo is with his vice-president,  Atiku Abubakar. I believe the Obasanjo administration has done well. Last elections we campaigned on the basis of the leader we can trust. In general, I believe the president has lived up to that promise because he has put in everything he has. It is working for   Nigeria  .
His achievements of course are enormous. We live in a country where people have short memory. For instance, nobody remembers now when we used to sleep at petrol stations. Nobody remembers how much bribe they had to pay to get a telephone. He achieved both quantitatively and qualitatively enormously for this country. Let me give you an example of what I mean by qualitatively. You see since he came, the gas project at Bonny had moved from two trains to  three to four  and the fifth one is under construction.   Nigeria  's gas potential is bigger than the oil potential. Now because of the quality of governance we have, people are willing to invest that is why we have grown so quickly in less than five years to the fifth train which by the time the fifth and sixth are completed and based on the people in the industry, Nigeria would have become the biggest shipping of gas to the world. So there is a strategic development of a new area. You know this argument of on-shore off-shore of a thing, the discovery 160 to 200 nautical miles out in the sea are enormous.  Virtually every oil company in the world is operating out there. They are bringing in money to develop this place. That is not strategic rather they are quantitative. But this gas thing for example, deregulation instead of talking of maybe we now have GSM, we will like to talk about the effect that the policy of deregulation has had, generally. He has the courage to insist on genuine privatisation. And it is succeeding. In all those areas, where government became an obstacle, private investors are putting in money and providing the services. I have no interest in being the owner of NEPA or NITEL. What I, as Onyema Ugochukwu want is service at a reasonable amount. Now what we used to have with NEPA for instance, every year government, instead of NEPA generating money and paying my dividend, the government uses our money to sustain NEPA. At the same time I never get the service which was my only objective. So the policy thrust has a multiplier effect in so many areas.
Newswatch: At the end of the day what is your vision for the Niger Delta and what would you like to be remembered for as chairman of NDDC?
Ugochukwu: First of all, when we got here, we had to try to define our mission. What everybody thinks we are supposed to be doing here is to facilitate the emergence of a Niger Delta which is proposed economically, politically, peacefully, environmentally friendly and contributing adequately to the resources of Nigeria. We were also conscious that these cannot be done over- night. So knowing that we were appointed only for four years, we are focusing on setting standards and I think we have achieved a lot in that area building partnership and then developing the master-plan which  will now provide the framework for other people to carry out. In the sense that if we have an integrated master-plan which the people themselves contributed to and own, then it wouldn't matter whether I am here or not because the people know, they own it, they drive it and so we can go on. If we can establish that attitudinal change that we know it is lacking then I think we would have achieved something.
Newswatch: Well we have four Igbo presidential candidates. Do you think we are going to have an Igbo president this year. What do you think of the Ekwueme challenge?
Ugochukwu: First of all, I do not believe in a tribal president. I want to be ruled by a Nigerian who protects my interests as a Nigerian. I do not define myself in tribal relationship. This is how my life is. And I do not believe that an Igbo president is more capable than any other Nigerian who is president in protecting my interest simply because he is from Igboland. I expect my interest to be promoted and furthered no matter who is president. But if you look at the challenge of the respected former vice-president, one was a little unhappy and sad at the reaction that has followed the convention. Chief Ekwueme is a sportsman and the convention which I saw take place, was by any standard very transparent. I believed you watched part of it live, and that Chief Ekwueme himself did say that there were areas where things were being done wrong and that he made suggestions and the suggestions were adopted. I think he said so on T.V. that day. I respect him. I still respect him a lot but I am a little saddened that the rancour has continued.
Newswatch: Talking about the press, which is also part of your constituency. How have we  performed in the three and half years of democracy?
Ugochukwu: I think the press has been its usual vibrant self and has assisted this democracy to actually develop. You will recall that I was doing national orientation then and I used to go out there.  I used to urge the people to stand on their rights, to defend their rights and to demand from their governors what was due to them. The governor is not doing you a favour by providing a road neither am I doing anybody a favour here. It is my duty and the people have the right. The press has been in the fight all along of course. When there is military, the press fights for the return of democracy. When there is democracy, after a military rule, it is a new system. The military mentality exists. People will break the law without even knowing that they are breaking the law. That may include us in the press and lot of democracy is inconvenient. But what is happening is democracy. So I think the press has helped to popularise this notion of freedom as a right within the law by insisting and reporting. Nobody decides for you what to publish. But there are of course areas where one can say there is increasingly a lack of humour. There were columnists, I used to read. In fact, I have old copies of such columns but what you find now is too much seriousness  throughout the paper.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Scarcity: Prices of Food Items Go Up
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Scarcity: Prices of Food Items Go Up
Body_Text:
Lagos — As the fuel scarcity persists in Lagos, prices of food items have gone up considerably.
A check by correspondents of the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) in some markets in Lagos showed that prices of food items like yam, garri, tomatoes and meat have gone up by about 100 per cent.
At the Tejuosho Market in SuruLere area of Lagos, a tuber of yam that was sold between N200 and N250 last week, now goes for between N300 and N350, while a basket of tomatoes that was previously sold for N600 now goes for N1,200.
Mrs. Titi Okolawo, a trader at the market, blamed the situation on the scarcity of fuel, saying that she paid about N600 to transport 100 tubers of yam from mile 12 market as against the previous price of N300.
Another trader, Lasisi Raji, a meat seller at Oyingbo market, said the prices had risen significantly compared to what obtained last week.
He said that an average size cow that was sold for between N35,000 and N40,000 last week was now going for between N45,000 to N60,000.
Raji also complained of low sale as a result of the high cost brought about by the scarcity of fuel, saying: "I used to sell about 70 pounds of meat per day, but now I sell only about 25 pounds".
At the Oyingbo Market, Mrs Felicia Otu, a restaurant owner said that she was now operating skeletal services because of the drop in patronage and the increase in cost of food items.
NAN investigation, however, showed that the prices of food items, such as rice and palm oil have either gone down or remain stable as a 50 kg bag of rice is still being sold for between N3,800 and N4,500, while the price of palm oil has gone down to N2,400 as against the previous price of N2,800.

Original_Search_Title: The Senate: The Change We Want
Headline: Nigeria: The Senate: The Change We Want
Body_Text:
As the nation prepares for another election, Ndigbo wants a Senate that can produce some dividends of democracy.
It is rather too unfortunate and shameful that while other senatorial districts of other geopolitical zones count their gains as the first term of our new democratic dispensation draws to an end, the South East States of the Igbos count their losses in terms of lack of Federal presence due to the under representation of our interests at federal level of government, especially the senate. For instance, while the South-South Senators smiled home with the NDDC and imminent abrogation of the on-shore off-shore dichotomy, with the former bringing in its wake a commendable wave of socio-economic development, such as the Niger Delta University, the people we sent to Abuja have only the stories of their fight-to-finish battles of supremacy, protracted bad blood, galore of senate presidency coups, infamous furniture allowances and often misdirected and self-serving bouts with the presidency to tell us. At their home state level, most of them can only boast of instigations of melancholy, confusion, chaos, unwarranted wars against their home governors, undiluted political gansterism, sponsorship of malicious junk journals, plus irresponsible busy-body, poke-nosing and rude intrusion in the running of their state governments. They can only boast of bicameralizing their states legislatures, massive importation of menacing, trigger-happy, red-eye policemen, provocative display of federal opulence, stage managed demonstrations, disorientating and mesmerizing of some ill-informed masses, as well as arrogant display of federal might in the negative.
In view of all these, I, for one, have always postulated that rather than place the blame on Obasanjo and crying over spilt milk, we of the South-East should start re-engineering our fate and repositioning ourselves for more federal benefits and peaceful states. And the first step is by ensuring that only mature minds with reliable and respectable antecedents are elected into our federal legislatures, especially the Senate. It is therefore a thing of joy that we of Enugu East senatorial district have made the pace-setting and all important first step in the right direction with the emergence of Owa Enugu, Chief Ken Nnamani as PDP senatorial flag bearer, having suffered a peculiarly dark period of gross under representation in the just-ending four-year season. The emergence of Owa (Igbo word for the moon), is very symbolic in the sense that the moon is not only an element of light in Igbo, but also enjoys a pride of place in the socio-cultural lives of the Igbos. It is not for instance, by accident that the Igbo traditional calendar depends entirely on the moon. The same with the Igbo Festivals (such as the new yam festival) and even certain sacrifices and rituals which must either take place during a crescent, half or full moon. But very important is the fact that the moon is a powerful uniting force, bringing the old and young together; and that it is a traditionally sacred and gentle phenomenon which neither hurts nor scorches. Owa Enugu has displayed these qualities by taking up the role of a father, a uniting and stabilising force in the volatile terrains of Enugu politics. And this mature, charismatic and galvanising quality is what the South East needs at the federal level if we must not count losses again in 2007.
Campaign/Promises
Also, we need a senator whose promises we can count on. Being the season of campaign, it is not unusual for the political office seekers to come to bamboozle the electorate with a recitation of their age long, crammed promises in order to steal our votes. They would promise to build bridges, tar our roads with silver and gold, build Eldora do hospitals where our wives would deliver their babies without the slightest labour pain, make our educational system soar till it kisses the third heavens, turn our rural areas into New York city, as well as drill three boreholes in each compound, from where we shall fetch water, wine and honey. Then our unemployed would be richer than the drug barons of the South Americas. No doubt, these are wonderful promises. Yet, at this point of my life, experiences have taught me to take a politician's promises based on what he was able to give to the society before seeking any political office. This is because it is only a man who has helped himself that I can trust to help me, and a man who has served his immediate society that we can trust to serve the wider society.
For example, when Owa Enugu's campaign leaflet emphasizes on a science and technology-based education and building of more technical colleges, I believe it because he has offered five university scholarships to deserving youths between 1986 and 1990 and because he too is an accomplished scholar with chains of higher degrees from the best of American universities, not one of those who either go to buy or lobby universities for honorary doctorate degrees. When it says Chief Ken Nnamani will come up with legislations aimed at revitalizing the industries and carry out an urban renewal of Enugu which has lost its glory as the cradle of modern civilizations in the East, I believe it because he has constructed 50 HT NEPA poles for the extension of electricity from Ebony substation to his town, Amechi-Uwani at the cost of N5.8 million in 1994. He also donated a 1.5 million 500 11/0.415 Kva transformer to his people in 1986, thereby saving the community from a long time epileptic rural electrification. He further sponsored the extension of NITEL underground cable for 100 digital telephone lines to Amechi-Uwani in 1998 to the tune of 1.3 million. I will have no doubts at all because he had as well built an ultra modern civic centre for his people in 1986. Again, when the campaign leaflet promises he is to push for legislations that will revitalize the ailing industries and attract new industrial projects, I need not doubt such because he is an industrialist himself, an accomplished international businessman who is also a director and managing director/CEO of chains of local and foreign companies. In my own candid estimation, I believe that if a man can serve his people so selflessly with his own limited personal resources, it is such a man that the people of Enugu East senatorial district can trust to deliver. The logic is that a toothless child who chewed palm kernel with its gum will definitely chew an iron rod to flour if given the opportunity to grow. May it also suffice to say that the house of sins and scandals which the Senate had reduced itself to since the inception of this dispensation is largely attributable to the preponderance of unpatriotic politicians, ex-convicts and prisoners, fraudulent contractors, progenitors and genitors of corruption and either retired military maladministrators or those who had collaborated with the military in looting the nation and unleashing blatant anarchy on the land- as ambassadors, ministers, directors, and even as master-planners and executors of the infamous five-million march for Abacha. The Senate then becomes a house in dire need of the restoration of dignity and gentle men who would not resort to fisticuffs to settle minor disagreements, thereby desecrating that which is held sacred and in high esteem. We need in the senate, men who can bare their clean palms to the sun and have the moral justification to fight corruption to finish.

Original_Search_Title: Why Obasanjo Should Not Be Re-Elected, By Nwobodo
Headline: Nigeria: Why Obasanjo Should Not Be Re-Elected, By Nwobodo
Body_Text:
Abuja — United Nigeria Peoples Party (UNPP) presidential candidate, Senator Jim
Nwobodo, has given reasons why Nigerians should not re-elect President
Olusegun Obasanjo iduring the forthcoming presidential elections,
emphasising that Obasanjo's a second term would plunge Nigeria into deeper
socio-economic and political problems.
Nwobodo, who unfolded a 14-point agenda, if elected President, urged
Nigerians to discountenance the slogan "continuity will secure our
democracy," saying no single individual can lay claim to being an embodiment
of democracy.
Addressing a world press conference yesterday in Abuja, the UNPP
presidential candidate described 1999 to 2003 as a "most traumatic period
for Nigeria," and wondered why a President with such a dismal performance
would choose to seek re-election.
"His approach to governance is imposition of siege and brutal
destruction of the peoples' psyche. When a man cannot elevate his nation to
a position of honour and prosperity, he has no basis whatsoever to seek
re-election. A leadership that cannot lay foundation for true nationalism
and patriotism must be adjudged irrelevant and anachronistic," Nwobodo
stated.
He accused Obasanjo of lacking the will to "nurture in the minds of the
led, such virtues as national pride, conviction and the will to carry on in
the spirit of a people with one destiny."
Nwobodo averred that Nigerians were today left in a state of doubt as to
whether or not their condition would improved going by the socio-economic
problems facing the country.
"In 1999 a bottle of coke cost N20, today it is N40; in 1999 a bag of
rice cost N1,850, today, it is N4,300; before 1999, a tuber of yam was below
N100, but today, it is over N200, and so it is for other consumable items
such as garri, beans and others."
"In 1985-1992, Nigeria's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth average was
seven per cent; by 1996-1999, it was 3.5 percent; in 2003, it had dropped to
1.9 percent. Although the per capital income increased nominally but what is
the value of naira to dollar? In 1999 the dollar was N85 but today the
dollar is N140. What this means is that the inflation and exchange rates
have wiped out any gains from the per capital increase.

Original_Search_Title: Making Effective Use of High Yields
Headline: Ghana: Making Effective Use of High Yields
Body_Text:
THIS YEAR has been acclaimed as one of bumper harvests. Plantains, cereals and tubers have been produced so much that prices have remained relatively stable, in spite of such tough economic measures as hiking prices of petroleum prices by as much as 94%. That is something we all praise our farmers and agricultural policy makers for.
But will the positive effects of the bumper harvest last? Signs point to a negative answer. All around in the forest zones where the early rains have started falling, storms accompanying those rains have razed down thousands of plantains, most of them with bunches of the fruit on them.
This time, each year, the early rains - falling after the long dry spell and the intense heat of the spell- cause thousands of tons of cassava to rot. This year, it has started.
And the combined effects of these wastes are that by the fourth month of the new year, the markets will be empty. The hunger will reach its peak in May and linger on until around August when new cereal will ripen.
It is undisputed fact that if we are able to save the plantains from falling and the cassava from rotting, the country will not experience any serious hunger throughout the year.
What the paper is calling for is a method to decrease the loss of foodstuffs through the ways already mentioned. Effective staking of the plantains should be taught the farmers by agricultural extension officers with the view to reducing the number of those that fall.
Elsewhere, research has led to the evolution of species of crops that are more resistant to weather inclemencies such as the strong winds that blow in the first quarter of the year. Given the calibre of scientists who man our research centres and tertiary agricultural institutions, it can be assured that they must have long found solutions to the problems that lead to losses of agricultural produce.
If they have already innovated plantains that can produce all-year-round fruits without being destroyed by the winds, then they are urged to introduce them to growers.
The Chronicle also draws attention of the sector ministry and dealers in foodstuffs as well as transport operators to the impending hunger season. How they are able to distribute the stock-piled maize, rice, yams and other foodstuffs still available from the bumper harvest is very important.
Our roads are bad and incomes are low in several places, including the farming communities who produced the food. If conscious efforts are not made to distribute food equitably, most parts including farmers who require a lot of energy during the farming season to plan their seeds will be left starving.

Original_Search_Title: My Clearance, Credit to Judiciary - Nyame
Headline: Nigeria: My Clearance, Credit to Judiciary - Nyame
Body_Text:
Rev Jolly Nyame, of Taraba state was one of the governors caught up in the controversy over whether he was qualified to contest again having served previously as governor under President Ibrahim Babangida's short democratic transition period. For a while, there was confusion with his party, the PDP, giving conflicting pronouncement on his fate; eventually the matter was allowed for judicial interpretation, which finally came and to the relief of Nyame and others they were cleared to run. In this interview with Paul Odili he reviewed the past weeks of tension and power play in his party, and how he feels with the court judgement. Excerpts:
With the court clearing the way for you to contest April election, how do you feel?
Well, the truth is that I feel normal. I personally feel that the judiciary has taken their normal and usual position in the society - being independent and giving justice to whosoever justice is due - so I feel normal and I think I must give credit to the judiciary.
What was your initial reaction when you read Col. Yohanna Madaki's statement in the print media that the party has asked you to step down?
Actually, I heard him on the NTA before the print media, and as soon I heard what he said, I realized it was his own position because I did not and still do not believe that the party will be naive of the constitutional position of my situation or of the three other governors, although they are not party members. I personally believe that the party that PDP, under the leadership of Audu Ogbe, a man I consider knowledgeable and who is vast in administration, can take the position of the Judiciary. I also know that he cannot assume the position of the opposition. So to stop me from contesting is to assume the position of the judiciary and the position of the opposition. So, I knew it was a joke. When I phoned him, he immediately said I should take it easy, that the national working committee is going to meet shortly, and that I should wait for the outcome of the meeting. And so, when Venatius Ikem came back with the real position of the party, I knew that I was right.
Now that the storm is over, which is the way forward?
Well, I want to believe that the position of the judiciary is final, and whosoever is still aggrieved, well, he can still go further. But at the same time, I want to believe that whosoever wants to go further would be very naive because the ruling is very clear. The constitution was given a very good interpretation, so even if one is a layman, he would really understand the position of the judiciary. And the only thing that we need now at this point in time is for PDP to consolidate and give me all the necessary moral backing for me to go ahead because Taraba state is one state that I'm sure, we in PDP can clinch at any given day.
Given all the uncertainties that came with the problem of your constitutional eligibility to contest elections, hasn't this in some way dwindled the strength of your party in the state?
No, Instead it has given the party more strength in the state. I remember when I came back from Bauchi when Tom Ikimi in his usual characteristic manner disqualified me on his own. He assumed the position of the judiciary, and he disqualified me. When my aircraft touched down at the airstrip in Jalingo, there was nobody to receive me, and as I was driving from the airport to the government house, the entire state was in a mourning mood, and all the delegates that came for the election went back home immediately, they said they were not ready for any congress until the PDP is ready, they should let them know. It was then that I knew that my support was strong with the grass root. When PDP cleared me to contest, and also fixed another date, that was when all the delegates came back to Jalingo. In fact, all these have further strengthened the PDP in my state, and I also now know that I have the total support of the people because of this.
It's three and half years now, and your term is almost up, what would you say were the major achievements of your government?
In a nutshell, I think I've been able to bring Taraba state from where we were before I was sworn in May 29, 1999 - which was practically in the woods - to a plain field where Taraba state is going to realize its proper position compared to other states of the federation. I have put a lot of emphasis on agriculture and health care delivery. Virtually all the local government areas, it's either we've renovated the hospitals in those areas, or we've put in new referral hospitals in those areas. And what we've also been able to do was identify what we could do for the masses to upgrade their standard of living, and since Taraba state is agrarian in nature, we've decided to put a lot of emphasis on agriculture. We were also able to improve their capacity to produce more food, which meant they would be able to sell more food, and make more money to be able to send their children to the hospital whenever they are sick; they would be able to send their children to school also. And the results are showing already because we find that the results of the junior and senior secondary schools have been very encouraging. For instance in one village on the Mambilla Plateau called kakara (I'm not sure of the right name) one of the students got nine credits. Nine credits! Before now, this feat was practically impossible. I cannot give you a percentage of the performance rates but when you come to Taraba, you'll see that there has been a serious and remarkable change from the way we met the students and their performances compared to the position now. So in the area of agriculture, in the area of health care delivery, and in the area of empowering people to see the need to send their children to school, I think we've done remarkably well.
When you come back for the second term, what would your immediate task be?
When I come back for the second time, my immediate tasks will be to encourage the growth of small-scale industries, to industrialize the state in the area of agriculture. We in Taraba state now produce a lot of food, and it's either we sell it so cheap when we start harvesting or they spoil. So with the introduction of machinery they will process it and then can conserve it. This will help the farmers have a proper price for their commodities. Take, for example, yam. In fact, all the yams that are processed and exported as pounded yam all come from Taraba and Benue state, and it is being processed in Lagos. Why can't we process it here in Jalingo or Wukari? So, one of my tasks would be to process this right here in Taraba state, so that the farmer could also use it for himself when he wishes to, or even sell when the need arises, and this will be a way of earning hard currency for himself. The industrialization of Taraba state will take most of my attention.
Baba Adi was your strongest opponent in the PDP, what is your relationship with him now that you've won the party ticket?
One thing that people need to understand is that the politics in Taraba revolves around personalities. The people of Taraba state, those adherents of the PDP have a lot of confidence in me. So when you look at the result - what I scored and what other people scored - you would see that the PDP revolves around my own personality, and follows from the dividends of my performance in office these past three years. Baba Adi is one of the PDP adherents in Taraba state. But he does not live in Taraba state, rather he lives outside Taraba state, so whatever he wants in Taraba state he can only have it if he realises that he has to come back and stay with the people and contribute his own quota in whatever way he can, no matter how small. I know whatever he can do, he cannot compare himself with government, but he should go and contribute something either in the economic or for the educational development of our society. He is a perfect gentleman. Anytime we meet we crack jokes. Even during the primaries we were cracking jokes with one another. But I want to believe that eventually he would realise this fact and he would come back, and we'll all join hands together. Virtually all the aspirants except three of them, we're all now together and we've enlarged the campaign organization except Baba Adi, Prof. Said Abubakar and Bala Adamu. But the rest are now working as a team.
The general impression is that you're not in good terms with the Vice- President and Gen. Danjuma, how true is this?
It is not true. One, for me to contest again, I had 100% of Gen. T.Y. Danjuma, not 99.9% of his support and I know this because he's said time without number, that his sole candidate in Taraba state is Jolly Nyame. And every time he makes these pronouncements, I was not always there, so this shows you the position between T.Y. Danjuma and myself. Let me also tell you about my personal relationship with his family. If I wish to visit him and his family, I do not book appointment to see him in the house, I go straight into the house. His wife one day me in his presence that ' this is your senior brother's house, you do not need to call before coming to this house because, you do not need an invitation to come to this house. Just come straight.' So this is the position between T.Y. Danjuma's family and myself. Secondly, about the Vice- President (laughs), anybody who thinks I have a bad relationship with the Vice- President is only joking, we have come a long way together. He brought me up, and he was the one that took me to the late Yar'adua. And I remember too, about a week or two ago when the Ooni of Ife stood up to say something about one of the meetings we had with Yar'adua, late Abiola, the Ooni and myself, four of us. He (the Vice- President) brokered peace between Yar'adua and Abiola, we were the four people that did this. So when I came and introduced myself to the Ooni, he stood up, and went to Atiku Abubakar, the Vice- President, and Atiku Abubakar told him that, Shehu Yar'adua is his grandfather in politics, and I, his father in politics. You can quote me anywhere. So to say that I have a sour relationship with either of these men is simply not true. I think it's just mere political talk.
Is it true that there was some sort of agreement between the PDP governors to return the president and his vice, and in return, you will all get automatic endorsement for a second term?
Well, that is not true because, if it is true, why is it difficult for the governor of Anambra state to come back. If it is true, why are they making it difficult for governor of Delta state, and the governor of Bayelsa state, and other governors from coming back for a second term? The truth of the matter is that, before the national convention, we came together to rub minds with the presidency, and we all realised that continuity would help promote democracy in this country. Anything short of that will only encourage disunity and we may only disrupt democracy if we are not careful. Democracy and the country in general were our primary considerations.
About thirty parties are now seeking power do you think it is healthy?
It is a welcome development in the sense that the people have got varieties of choice to make. At the end of the day, after all the elections - from the local government, state to presidential have been over, then we would be able to tell which is strong or not. In fact, there should have been no need for INEC to register parties, but allow parties to float themselves, and at the end of the elections, only then would the parties know their positions. So I consider it healthy because democracy encourages freedom of choice.
General Buhari is the presidential flag bearer of the ANPP, don't you think he is a big threat to your party come April?
Well, I don't believe so. Gen. Buhari was once head of state of this country, and he must have left his footprints in the course of his governance of this country, and I think he wants to prove himself worthy again by coming back. But all of us have seen what General Olusegun Obasanjo did before and what he's doing now. As far as I'm concerned General Buhari is no threat because the PDP as a family will forget all these rancour left and right, and unite at the dying minute. We know where we're coming from and where we are going. Gen. Buhari is doing his best, and I congratulate him for that but I want to assure you and I want to assure the people of Nigeria that President Obasanjo is coming back to finally take the country to a position where it would be envied by other countries of the world.
A lot of people have expressed doubts about INEC not being competent to conduct the forthcoming elections in the country, what is your reaction to this?
When people are not in the position to assess their strength, they would want to start discarding their followers even before they fail. So those who are not sure of victory want to find fault left and right. I believe if you're sure of your own position, INEC is there. INEC has already put out a timetable, but we're not there yet. Why complain that the bridge has a weak foundation when you've not even reached the bridge. Why don't you come close to the bridge or get on the bridge first, only then can you say the bridge has a faulty foundation. We have not even reached the date set for the elections, some people have already started finding faults with INEC. But I must state categorically that INEC is fully prepared, if they are not, they will not come out with a timetable.
Governor Mbadinuju has just been disqualified as the flag bearer of the PDP, what is your reaction to this?
Well, I think it is a pity. But like I said earlier on, it is a family matter. Audu Ogbeh is the chairman of this party, along with his lieutenants, whatever they do shows was a common position that has been adopted by all. You can be dissatisfied as a member of the PDP, but I want to advise that we should not loose our head. I remember when he called me one day and told me that 'your issue might end in court, and I want to start preparing your mind towards the decision of the court.' So you see, he's a perfect gentleman. He prepares your mind even before you get there. So I said to him, 'Mr. chairman, I 'm open to whatever is the position of the judiciary, and I beg of two things: One, that the party should not assume the role of the judiciary. It should allow the judiciary carry out its duty. And two, the party should not assume the position of the opposition. Allow the opposition to face me come April 19. And I must stress that the party must have discussed exhaustively with the governor of Anambra state before any decision is taken. And I want to believe that the party would not, as the print media says, 'dumped' Mbadinuju, far from it. I want to believe that the party would find a way of utilising the governor of Anambra. I must also stress that, there is no governor in the PDP that is completely useless, it is not possible. It is just like the Hausa saying that, 'however slim a camel is, if you slaughter it, it will fill a basket.' Mbadinuju, I know very well will contribute to the growth of the party in Anambra state and at the national level too.
A lot of big wigs have been migrating from the PDP to other parties. Doesn't this pose a serious problem for the party, especially at this eleventh hour?
As people leave PDP, more people are coming to the PDP. Like in my state, a former minister of internal affairs left ANPP and moved to PDP. So, I want to believe that the free movement from one party to another is very healthy for this democracy. So in no way can this movement from one party to another ever be a threat to the PDP. And I'm sure that most of the people that have left will soon come back to the party. I've also been rightly informed that the party is working very hard to make sure that Rochas Okorocha comes back to the party because, Rochas is one young man that I see has a huge potential; but unfortunately, he was misunderstood by some few PDP people in his home state and that affected his relationship with the presidency.
Dr. Alex Ekwueme has gone to court claiming that there were some irregularities in the just concluded PDP primaries. Don't you think this will further dent the credibility of the party?
It's difficult really to comment on a big person like Dr. Alex Ekwueme. He is by far my senior and I have no reason to say anything against him. If he's aggrieved he must have a reason. But at this point in time, sometimes you'll have to take things easy not because you're a coward but because of the bigger picture - the need for democracy to be enshrined permanently in this country. If we're going to have a repeat of the national convention, I can bet you that it may be chaotic. Sometimes you come to agree that, 'well, let me take it easy. If it is not me, somebody else will take it.' Awolowo, was someone that wanted so badly to be the president of this country but he didn't get it. With all the things he did it the west, if he had been the president of this country, he would have done so much for this country. But he died in the cause of that. The same thing goes for the late chief Abiola. I was one of the people who really contributed in his making. Like I told you, the Oni of Ife brokered peace between Yar'adua and Abiola. I was the only SDP governor that was invited, and I can confidently say I was the instrumental to the making of Abiola politically. And I must mention that the man did not know the difference between a Yoruba man and a Hausa man, he did not know the difference between a Muslim and a Christian. At a time he was even fighting for a bigger cause - Africa. In fact, he would have made a very good president, but he died in the struggle. I want to believe that Alex Ekwueme has a lot to show the PDP. So I would want to use this opportunity to call upon him and his supporters to come together and see where PDP has fallen short, and see how best we can all help correct it.

Original_Search_Title: Cost of Love
Headline: Nigeria: Cost of Love
Body_Text:
Are You Getting Ready to get married? Here is the idea of what it will
cost you to do so in Nigeria
When James Agwu, 38, a petty trader, from Afikpo in Ebonyi State , saw
Eunice Okorie, his heart leaped for joy. He had found somebody he would like
to spend the rest of his life with.
The two lovebirds did not waste time in making their intention known to
their parents who gave their approval for marriage. But that was not to
be. Agwu could not afford the bride price. Still lamenting over the matter,
Agwu told Newswatch: "They asked me to pay N70,000.00 as the bride price and
also list of items that I must buy to qualify to be a husband. I did not
have that amount and was told to forget Eunice.
But Harmony Mark, 37, a computer engineer, can count himself
lucky. Without much ado, he performed all the marriage rites his in-laws
asked of him, although he did not find things easy. That was in 1998. Still,
Mark spent not less than N100,000 on traditional marriage before his
heart-throb could be released to him. Mark, who hails from Arochukwu in Abia
State , said he was made to provide a box and buy some wrappers like george,
hollandis, blouses, wristwatch and give some amount of money to the bride's
mother.
Agwu and Mark's experiences above represent what many potential husbands
are made to go through before they can get married in Nigeria . Intending
couples pass through several stages of marriage rites before they can be
called husband and wife. This includes traditional, religious-church or
mosque, and registry marriages. The commonly practised is the traditional
marriage, which involves the man going to see the bride's parents to
indicate his interest to marry her. Thereafter, the bride's family would
give him a shopping list which, in most cases is a must-list and which must
be met before they could give him their daughter in marriage.
Newswatch investigations showed that bride price vary from one place to
another and from family to family. But spending so much money in marriage is
a matter of choice. In addition to fulfilling what the tradition of the
bride demands for marrying her, some men lavish money to throw
'society-wedding' just to show off wealth.
In Okposi community in Ebonyi State , the suitor is culturally required
to take two gallons of palm wine, two kola nuts, a carton of beer, a bottle
of hot drink, a head of tobacco, and dry fish to the family to officially
request the hand of their daughter in marriage.
He would then be given a list of things to buy for the marriage
proper. This includes: 24 gallons of palm wine, 12 kola nuts, five bottles
of hot drink, two goats, four packets of cigar, dry meat, and four heads of
