tobacco. These are meant for the father of the bride.  He would also give a
bushel of rice, wrappers and blouse, shoe, head-tie, and some money for the
mother. Okposi culture demands that the suitor gives some money depending on
how much a family is willing to accept, to the male children born in both
the bride's paternal and maternal home. The bride price is not fixed. It is
as the family of the bride wants. But the intending couple would spend a lot
of money buying clothes, shoes, jewelleries for them to look good on that
day. He would also hire musicians, traditional dancers to liven up the day
of their traditional marriage. The two families would invite their friends
that must be properly fed with food and drinks, making the ceremony highly
expensive. In some cases, the couple spend as much as N200,000 or more.
In Ihiala, Anambra State , bride price is generally not more than
N5,000, even if the bride is a graduate or even Ph.D degree holder. Ihiala
custom demands that the suitor brought kola nuts, two gallons of palm-wine,
two gallons of ngwa-wine to the family of the bride. He would also be asked
to give some money to the mother and father of the bride.
Yoruba tradition demands that the suitor bring drinks, kola nuts, a cow,
orange, banana and alligator pepper to the family of the bride. As for the
bride price, the suitor is normally asked to give whatever he can because
putting a price means selling their daughters. But the man has to buy five
different bundles of clothes for the woman.
In Erei, in Baise Local Government Area of Cross River State, a suitor
is allowed to pay as much as he can afford. So bride price and list items
for marriage are regarded as a way of bringing together the two
families. Bartholomew Inamang, 45, a police inspector with Oke-Odo in
Abule-Egba area of Lagos State , an Erei indigene, told Newswatch that Erei
custom demands that a suitor brings a bottle of hot drink to the bride's
family to find out whether they would allow him to marry their
daughter.
If they accept him, the family would then give him a list of items for
the marriage proper.  The items on the list include: three cartons of beer,
three crates of soft drink, a bottle of wine, two bottles of hot drink, two
bottles of local gin, kola nuts, three tubers of yam, three jars of palm
wine and N300 for prayers. "Our custom demands that wealthy suitors buy
'lion' wrappers like george, hollandis, blouses, head-tie for the mother of
the bride. He will also give, if the bride is the first daughter, a he-goat,
two different types of wrapper, a carton of beer, a crate of soft drink, a
bottle of hot drink, a bottle of local gin, two tubers of yam, kolanut and
N150 for prayers to the grandmother. Bride price is between N2,000 to N5,000
depending on how much the bride's family is willing to collect and what the
suitor can afford.
Newswatch also gathered that marriage in Igalaland is dependent on
individuals and families.  A source told Newswatch that while marriage is
cheaper for the Muslims in the place, their Christian counterparts spend a
lot of money on list items depending on the family. He said his family made
him to spend close to N300,000 because they wanted him to show off his
affluence. "The wealthier ones could spend up to N500,000 on that," the
source disclosed.
For Esan in Edo State , Newswatch learnt that bride price is N200
irrespective of the bride's qualification but those who wish to throw party
could spend more.
Getting married does not come cheap in Muslim areas of the North,
either. In most cases, the suitors have to buy one or two big boxes filled
with expensive cloths for their brides and complement that with equally
expensive jewelries. Bride price is mandatory.
Many traditional rulers, pastors, marriage counsellors and some people,
who spoke to Newswatch blamed couples and families for the prevailing high
cost of marriage today. Eze Nwabueze Ohazulike, Ezendigbo, Lagos State ,
said marriage costs much these days because couples no longer stick to what
the custom stipulates for marriage. Ohazulike blames high costs of marriage
on high taste of intending couples and their families, especially the
highly-placed who often want to use the opportunity to tell the world that
they are rich. "You see, they spend fortunes buying the highest quality of
clothes in the market just to wear on that day. At the end of the day they
throw a big society party that would be the talk of the town years
after. What annoys me is that they would tell you that they spent N700,000
or more getting married. Here, would you say that the amount they spent was
what the culture of their communities demand for marriage? No way,"
Ezendigbo said.
The traditional ruler also faulted the general belief that marriage is
costlier in Igboland. He told Newswatch that he gave out his daughter in
marriage last year and he collected only N60 and his wife got N40. He said
that buying of hollandis, george, a box, soap and shoes were only gifts to
the in-laws which is not mandatory.
Musibau Ajuwon, Baale of Ogunlowo Alagbole, also distanced the
traditional institution on the high cost of marriage saying the individual
couples and their families were responsible. He told Newswatch that the
Yoruba traditional marriage was not costly if people do only the things
their culture demanded. Ajuwon further stated that the act of throwing
owambe parties to celebrate marriages was not compulsory, as the woman would
automatically become the man's wife the moment the traditional marriage
rites were performed.
But many couples could want to go beyond the traditional marriage. They
go to ridiculous extent to show off their wealth.
Jacob Ebun, 50, a pastor and marriage counsellor, said that the amount
of money a couple spends getting married in church depends on the way and
the nature of marriage. He told Newswatch that church marriage could become
costly if the couples decide to buy rings, suits for the groom and his men,
gowns for the bride and the bridal train, clothes for little bride and
groom, flowers, shoes, and other wedding accessories. Money is also spent
hiring and decorating cars and halls for the wedding.
After the church service, they spend a lot of money entertaining their
guests with drinks and foods. Photographs, cake and wines also gulp money
from the couples. Musical instruments and players are also hired for
entertainment. Some "close down the street" to have enough space for their
guests at the ceremony. Here, the bride's and the groom's families and
friends are often identified on the type of aso-ebi they
wear. Alternatively, Ebun said couples could just present themselves before
God by meeting the pastor or imam with their rings for marriage blessing,
and they would be pronounced husband and wife.
At a typical society wedding the couple could spend between N500,000 and
N1 million depending on the social standing of the couple and their
families.
Ebun blamed extravagant spending on marriage on women who compel their
grooms to perform three marriage ceremonies. "Some couples do traditional,
church and registry marriages for security reasons. You know, the
traditional institution does not give any inheritance right to the woman in
case of the death of the husband. It is only the statutory marriage, which
includes church and registry marriages that accord such rights to the
woman. You can see why the clamour by brides," the marriage counsellor
said.
Newswatch found out that women married under the Marriage Act (cap 218,
LFN, 1990) enjoy more legal rights than those married under the Customary or
Islamic Law.
Rita Edoh, 35, a tailor, believes that marriage costs so much these days
because some brides want to wear costly accessory during wedding. She said
many of those who came to her to sew wedding gowns often "chose costly
materials. You know, ladies like showing off on their wedding day. We have
wedding gowns of low quality materials, which are mainly patronised by poor
couples." Edoh has wedding gowns costing N2,000. There are others for N5,000
and N10,000, while there are also very expensive ones.
Getting married at the marriage registry does not come cheap
either. Each registry, Newswatch learnt, has devised a means of collecting
illegal money from intending couples. Mark, who took his heart-throb to
Ikeja Marriage Registry to legalise their marriage after their traditional
marriage, said that apart from the registry registration fee of N2,500, he
was made to pay some unreceipted sum of money.
Officials at the marriage registry also asked him to give them a carton
of Okin biscuits, two bottles of wine and additional N1,500. On the whole,
Mark said that he spent about N10,000 at the registry alone. Thereafter, he
went home to entertain his guests.
Newswatch also found out that getting married at Ikeja Marriage Registry
is costlier than other registries in Lagos State . Yaba Registry collects
N1,500 while Mushin and Ikoyi registries charge N1,300 and N700,
respectively to wed couples.
But Blessing Anike Olanloye, registrar, told Newswatch that Mark's claim
was false. She said it was not their practice to collect biscuits or wine
let alone money from those about to wed.
But Etim Bassey, 40, a businessman, told Newswatch that he also paid the
same items before the registry could wed him last year.
Also at Ikoyi Federal Marriage Registry, couples would not be advised to
come with flowers on their wedding day but if they were to wed there they
would be compelled to hire flowers dropped by those already wedded at
exorbitant price, otherwise the marriage would not hold.
A man who wishes anonymity narrated his experience to Newswatch. When he
was going to wed at Ikoyi Marriage Registry, nobody told him about flowers
until the marriage was to start. He spent the last money on him to hire
flower and could not get money to go home. "They didn't tell me to come with
flower or money for "offering." After paying those two bills, my pocket was
empty," he said.
High cost of marriage has become a source of worry to many youths in the
country. Those who spoke to Newswatch explained that these costs were a
great hindrance to marriage. Benedette Iruanya, 39, a spinster and bank
official, blamed the growing number of unmarried girls on high cost of
marriage in the country. Iruanya told Newswatch that prostitution and single
parenthood were also being promoted by this trend. Speaking in the same
vein, Ini Eka, 37, a businessman, said that, "marriage is made so exorbitant
and thus out of reach for the common man. This is why you have so many
eligible bachelors and spinsters not getting married. Some of them choose
the easy way out by impregnating a lady and living with her without going to
see her parents or getting married properly. He said that some men resort to
quarrelling or even beating up their wives frequently after paying highly to
marry them.
Newswatch gathered that more people preferred going for only registry
marriage once they agree to be husband and wife. G. O. Olaopin, former
registrar of Ikeja Marriage Registry, said there is an upsurge in registry
weddings. According to her, "there has been an increase in the number of
people opting for registry marriage as reflected in the increase in revenue
generated for the council."
On December 28, 2002 , alone, about 30 couples wedded in Ikoyi Federal
Registry. At Ikeja Marriage Registry, 15 wedded on December 21, 2002 .

Original_Search_Title: I Pump Iron And Lift Weights - Igbinedion
Headline: Nigeria: I Pump Iron And Lift Weights - Igbinedion
Body_Text:
CHIEF Lucky Igbinedion, Edo State Executive Governor is an amiable personality no matter the situation he finds himself.
A hard worker and sports enthusiast, he cuts the picture of the perfect role model to the up and coming.
In this brief interview, he speaks about his other side when he is not entangled in the matters of state and governabce. Excerpts:
"IT is quite a difficult task to outline my daily routine especially now that the heat of politics is on and electioneering campaigns are at fever pitch.
There are days I do not sleep until around 4.30 am, to be exact any day I get as much as four hours sleep, I consider myself privileged.
Basically, I rise as early as the job dictates, usually around 6.30 am if I went to bed late, and 5.30 am if I slept early.
I perform my personal early morning devotion after which I tune on the radio for foreign and local news. My favorite stations are the BBC, VOA and Edo State broadcasting Service (ESBC ).
I am a regular listener to the daily programmes of the ESBC especially the one titled Facts You Must Know. Afterwards I move over to the gymnasium for a thorough work-out.
I pump iron and lift weights after which I head for the bathroom. By 8.30 or 9.00, I am at the office. I love to stroll down, but I often drive down. I love to stroll since it has to do with fitness..
My day at the office is that of the typical chief executive. I do so much consultations, have meetings and dialogue to settle differences. There are also several travels to be done, to Abuja, Lagos and all the LGAs.
I believe it is God that gives good health, all we can do is try our best to stay healthy. I do everything in moderation - no smoking, no drinking, no excesses. Usually I skip breakfast but I never forgo lunch. In actual fact it is my main meal for the day.
My favorite meal is pounded yam with any good soup. In the evening, I may be content with hot fresh fish pepper soup and I am OK for the day.
For my leisure, I love sports naturally. I am a sportsman in all totality, that is, to the core. I play football, tennis and squash. I am also into weightlifting and boxing. I am a strong believer in general body fitness and I work out every morning and evening. In addition to sports,I love music especially Jazz.
Nigerian music also appeals to me irrespective of its origin - Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa or Edo. I listen to music a lot particularly while traveling by road since it keeps me cool, calm and collected.
One pastime I have never really come to terms with is watching the movies even though I am an ardent fan of action films.
But since I became governor, I have never had the time to watch even one movie."

Original_Search_Title: Nema Should Sue KCC
Headline: Uganda: Nema Should Sue KCC
Body_Text:
Kampala — HERDSMEN HAVE been given six months to quit various wetlands in Soroti by the National Environmental Management Authority.
This is right and proper because wetlands need to be protected throughout Uganda as they conserve water, regulate rainfall and filter polluted discharges.
However NEMA needs to become more aggressive in Kampala. Massive encroachment is continuing in the Bugolobi, Bunga and other swamps. Farmers are growing yams and other crops in these wetlands and slumdwellers are also encroaching on the swamps. But these are temporary groups who can eventually be expelled.
The biggest problem is that Kampala City Council is still giving planning permission for new housing plots to be carved out of the wetlands.
KCC is putting personal gain before the national interest. If Bugolobi and Bunga swamps are not protected, the source of Kampala's drinking water will become increasingly polluted. The National Water and Sewerage Corporation will have to use more chemicals to purify Kampala's water supply and will charge consumers more per litre.
Yes, NEMA has raised these issues with KCC as has the Wetlands Protection Unit. But KCC has ignored them.
NEMA must now go on the attack to protect Kampala's wetlands in the national interest.
Firstly, as it has done in Soroti, NEMA should issue eviction notices to all people who have built or are building properties in Bugolobi, Bunga or other wetlands within Kampala.
Secondly NEMA should sue KCC for massive damages for dereliction of duty in failing to protect the environment of Kampala. Any funds awarded in the High Court can be used to protect the source of Kampala's drinking water, the wetlands that are now being wrecked with the collusion of KCC. Ends

Original_Search_Title: Armed Robbers Kill Suspected Informant
Headline: Ghana: Armed Robbers Kill Suspected Informant
Body_Text:
Accra — Police on Wednesday said a 45-year-old man who was shot dead by an armed gang at Flamingo Junction, near Russia, in Accra on Tuesday, could be a victim of vengeance by the robbers.
Police told the GNA in Accra that the man whose name was only given as Mufu must have been gunned down following the arrest of a hardened armed robber, Philip Gbotsui.
The armed robbers suspected the man to have informed the police on the hideout of one Gbotsui, who is about 25 years old and was believed to be behind many armed robberies in Accra.
Gbotsui, who was described as a "top class armed robber", was arrested on Monday night near Gloryland Hotel, Odorkor, after a struggle with the police.
The Police also believe that Mufu was gunned down by a group of armed robbers who thought he was an informant, but police deny that he was an informant.
The Police sources said at around 9:00pm on Tuesday a Police Patrol team heard that there had been a shooting incident at Flamingo.
Upon reaching the scene they saw Mufu lying in pool of blood with empty bullet shells by his side.
The sources said eyewitnesses said Mufu, in the company of a lady, was buying fried yam at the Flamingo Junction when an unregistered car with five men suddenly pulled up.
One of the occupants got out of the vehicle and tapped Mufu on the shoulder. Another occupant with a Pump-Action gun fired at Mufu while two others also pulled pistols and fired at him.
They got back into the car and sped off leaving Mufu dead and one Daniel Amenu, who was also in the company of Mufu with a shot in the thigh.
The Police sources said they believe the car, which was ash in colour, is either an Opel Astra or a Hyundai Excel.
Meanwhile the lady in whose company Mufu was before the incident has "vanished into thin air".
Police also said the armed robbers are planning to kill two other people they suspect are informants, but declined to give their names for security reasons.
Police said they are meanwhile continuing with investigations into the robberies believed to have been committed by Gbotsui.
Police said they have arrested one Adamu, another notorious armed
robber, at the Kaneshie Overhead upon a tip-off.
He managed to throw away a weapon to some accomplices before his arrest.

Original_Search_Title: Fuji Godfathers Battle for Survival
Headline: Nigeria: Fuji Godfathers Battle for Survival
Body_Text:
Lagos — Fuji godfathers, Sikiru Ayinde Barrister and Kollington Ayinla, are
having an intriguing challenge to cope with the change in the trade.
AKEEM LASISI writes that their new albums - Precious Mum and Boya
Lemo - point to this fact.
Even in music where talent largely governs the market, change is
inevitable. Hence, despite the huge potential and fame that the duo
of Alhaji Sikiru Barrister and Kollington Ayinla have as fuji
'superstars', time and change have thrown up a growing bunch of
younger voices whose grip on the music is clear and square.
But the song on the minds of the two who nurtured the music brand
into national heritage seems to be never-say-die. On the one hand,
all the younger fuji artistes, ranging from the new 'King' Wasiu
Ayinde through to the no-holds-barred generation of Abass Akande
Obesere, and to the tiniest star like Shanko Lomo never fail to
salute Barrister and Kollington as their professional fathers. On the
other, however, is that the fuji warlords have never ceased to
release albums, even if the sales of such have become a shadow of
what they used to be.
At the close of last year, Barrister released Precious Mum and
Kollington, Boya Lemo. The Ibadan-born Barrister, who is largely
regarded as the ultimate creator of fuji and who eventually baptized
himself Mr. Fuji, sees in Precious Mum a life-time opportunity to
celebrate the life and death of his mother, Alhaja Odere Sifawu, who
had died in November 2001. Boya Lemo (which translates as, I doubt if
you know) is the Ilota, Kwara-born Kollington's own response to the
government functionaries's insensitivity to the plight of the voters.
Neither of the albums hit the market with a ripple, but both
musicians manage to remain their veteran selves. For Barrister, he
feels he might have committed a crime if he fails to deify the mother
to whom he was phenomenally attached throughout his music career. He
visits his childhood since when the old woman had always been there
for him. In literal and metaphoric sense, he establishes how the
mother transfers to him the power (spiritual power, which she located
in the Koran, of course) that has seen him through the thick and thin
of life. And he effectively does what makes him the hero: he
praise-sings the dead mother in such a way that the Yoruba believe
she cannot but burst into dancing on her way to the other world.
Interspersed with the praise of his dead father, that of the fuji
patron murdered last year, Alhaji Bashiru Bamgbola, a.k.a. Hadji
Bashy, and the praise of the multitude who graced the mother's
funeral, the tribute that keeps the beat low and sustained traverses
the whole of the first side of the album.
"If you have been a fan of Barrister," says Lagos-based Mr. Fatai
Jimoh, "you would not be surprised that he dedicates the whole of the
Side A to the mother. In the first place, the man is never tired when
he is paying tribute, even in his earlier albums.
Another thing is that in his music, he had always prayed that he
survive the mum. It was as if he had a special fear that it was going
to be the other way round. You know, he even said he was like an
Ogbanje child, and that if the mother died today, and he died the
following day, he would still have been fulfilled - rather than being
the other way round." Incidentally, the Side B of Precious Mum is as
overtly political as what Kollington does in Boya Lemo. The latter,
who only recently returned from a musical forage abroad, stridently
accuses the ruling politicians of neglecting the electorate even when
they (rulers) are flaunting abnormal personal social transformation.
He takes up the Assembly men who had plotted to impeach President
Obasanjo, singing that they are toying with the unity of the country.
The import of Boya Lemo is the artiste's doubt as to whether the
politicians realize that the masses of Nigeria are under serious
socio-economic burden.
Compared to what obtains in Precious Mum, the beat in Boya Lemo
habitually shrieks on the fast lane.
Being openly partisan is Kebe-n-Kwara's long-embraced trade. The way
he canvassed votes for the late Obafemi Awolowo, and M.K.O. Abiola in
Ijo N Be, he salutes the South-West governors in the track Gomina Ile
Yoruba, canvasses vote for the Lagos State Governor, Tinubu, in Vote
Tinubu, and joins issues with the opponents of Kwara's Mohammed Lawal
in Lawal No Shaking. The idioms he directs at the Shola Saraki's camp
are pointed and sharp.
All that is in Kollington's character. Barrister is the one who seems
to have surprised many fuji fans when, in the four tracks on Side B
of Precious Mum - Gov Bola Tinubu, Gov Lam Adesina, Gov Segun Osoba,
and Gov Adeniyi Adebayo - he openly canvasses the Alliance for
Democracy's re-election bid. It is not that Mr.
Fuji had been apolitical in his music career. In the Second Republic,
he had leaned towards the rightist National Party of Nigeria (NPN),
celebrating Chief Adisa Akinloye, Lamidi Adedibu, Dr. Omololu
Olunloyo, among others. In fact, some accused him of getting some
contracts in Abuja when the bobble burst for the NPN. But he never
waxed a record to openly and directly campaign for any candidate. Not
even for his benevolent MKO Abiola, whose presidential bid he had
promoted only during live performances. But now, the music appears
changing, and many people read commercial/advertising interest into
what Barrister does for the second term seeking governors in his
latest album.
Although the big masquerades have had to bow to the wind of change,
they nevertheless remain fulfilled.
They have made their marks both within and outside Nigeria. Their
names are never mentioned when old stars that have gone
professionally and financially bankrupt are being chronicled, for
instance, as especially evident in Barrister's effortless decision a
few years back to go on self-exile abroad, as the problem of national
security became too much for him.
He shuttles between home and abroad with ease.
The younger fuji freaks are reputed for indulging in erotic songs
which, ironically, happen to be the taste of the time, but which the
two leaders seem to be too old to successfully trade in. Since they
carved fuji from Were (the Awurebe music pioneered by the
Ibadan-based Dauda Epo-Akara) about 40 years ago, they have
separately taken the music through a series of stages of adaptation.
Incidentally, both had first found themselves in the military at some
point in their lives. Barrister is always quick to recount how he was
shot in the leg in the Civil War. Since he got his foot on the path
of showbiz, however, determination and tenacity have made his huge
talent blossom. Through challenges and travails he rose to the centre
of the stage, winning honours, both at home and abroad. At different
times, he was rumoured to have committed murder, got involved in drug
trafficking, while, in the 1980s, another woman openly lay claim to
being his mother. Such incidents only kept Barrister going, while
becoming materials for tracks in such albums as Okiki, Garbage Series
One, and Aiye.
Barrister stands out as a Yoruba musician who manifests intense
depth. He is painstaking in the treatment of his subjects, and there
is a natural missionary in him, as reflected in his art that preaches
as much as it entertains. He has an intense knowledge of the Yoruba
art of praise singing, and this marks his music out any day. It is
the combination of these and other traits that gave birth to such
classics as Aiye, Suuru, Oro Idibo, the Garbage Series and New
Dimension.
Kollington's strength doesn't lie in any painstaking treatment of
issues or subjects, and he has been criticized on this. But he makes
up by being a public voice. If he were a poet in the English
tradition, he would have fallen in the league of the Niyi Osundares,
or the Reuben Abatis, if a columnist. From one album or track to the
other, he races from one topic of public interest to the other. He
announces changes in government, new policies, he praises governments
or condemn them, depending on how they perform, and does not mince
words when he wants to champion a social cause. That is why listening
to his old songs brings forth the memory of military take-over,
change in currency, elections, MAMSER, census, Bakassi (in the
beginning), Mandela's travail and triumph, June 12, among others.
Kollington's fuji is light. In fact, he championed what has now
become Saje - the erotic genre - when he was singing eating yam,
Kaya, Mi Jolojolo, Ropopo, etc. His beat is habitually hot and is
characterized by regular change in tempo. He prides himself as having
introduced jazz, piano and bata into fuji music, while the titling of
his albums is usually peculiarly abstract - Kaya, Lakukulala,
Kasabubu and Tukasa. His fans remember Owo, Megastar and Yoyo as some
of the hits of the fuji star who also experienced his own times of
trial as in suffering armed robbery attack, his house being caught by
fire, and other forms of family tragedy.
Between both fuji artistes lies a traditional rivalry that has caused
a lot of sensation (and at times criticism) in the industry. Since
they have been eating from the same market, the friendship that ought
to exist between them is eternally stained with suspicion, envy and
jealousy that occasionally assumed bitter proportion.
Barrister's claims that he created fuji and initiated Kollington into
the trade have always stirred the latter's ire. He insists that since
he did not learn the trade from Mr. Fuji, nobody should lay claim to
being the lord of the craft.
When Barrister thus sees himself as Aare (the president), Kollington
answers 'The General', which was the title General Sani Abacha
bestowed on him, ostensibly through the prompting of Chief Alex
Akinyele, a fan of his, when Akinyele was the Information and Culture
Minister, for the musician's role in sensitizing the people on public
matters.
Also, when Barrister was crowned Dr. of Fuji, which he later
amplified to Dr. of Music, Kollington invoked for himself the title
of Professor Master.
But it is in their music that the rivalry is most manifest,
especially in the past. Abuses, threats and curses sometimes became
the in-thing, and Kollington's tongue is assessed to be sharper,
while his rival's mind is known to be more serpentine. You could be
very sure that one would reply to an attack he suffers in the other's
song. They manipulated each other's thought to the delight of the
market. Thus, when Barrister sings metaphorically that orin ti sun
siwaju (the game has changed its colour, sort of), Kollington replies
that it is dead musicians who had snatched music from Barrister. When
Barrister, in Okiki, enumerates all the scandals that were woven
around his person, and likens them to a mark of fame, his rival only
likens him to a tortoise who is always embroiled in scandals. When
Kollington returned from his first musical tour of America, he sang
that none of his band members got missing - in response to
Barrister's allusion to the fact that one of his boys, Tunde Balenga,
had temporarily missed his way when they were abroad. And when
Barrister, who prefers to hit his rival rather proverbially, sings
that he has become werepe, the nettle seed that is too hot for every
skin, Kollington replies he has become the palm oil that neutralizes
the power of the itching plant.
With such an eventful history at their back, both fuji stars can, no
doubt, look back at their career with nostalgia and an occasional
grin.
They are happy the young ones - including Barrister's son, Adewale -
are keeping the flag flying. And an acceptance of such a wind of
change is what Kollington sings in Boya Lemo. In one of the tracks,
he hails the new voices, and declares that the future belongs to the
young.

Original_Search_Title: Is Government Getting Serious?
Headline: Nigeria: Is Government Getting Serious?
Body_Text:
Lagos — In 1999, the Obasanjo government promised to cause a revolution in the agriculture sector. It's main objective, it says, is to improve food security, which means "enough food for an active healthy life." Among other things, the government promised to give farmers "necessary support to cultivate and produce more per hectare per season, by such measures as timely supply of agricultural inputs; to "rehabilitate" and "reactivate" irrigation facilities and encourage small size irrigation facilities by farmers. It also promised to support and encourage livestock production with a view to making the country self-sufficient in meat products.
While these lofty promises were made almost four years ago, it is only recently that the Federal Government could be said to be taking concrete steps towards delivering on them. The recent government decision to ban importation of certain categories of poultry products is one of these moves. If anyone was in doubt, government made clear its intetion when it ordered the destruction of N500million worth of poultry products imported into the country a few days ago. However, while such actions as setting meat on fire makes for good television and maybe good politics, it does not fill the space that the banning action will create on the people's table neither does it translate into concrete incentives to local farmers of poultry to produce more chickens.
Only few days ago, the House of Representative Committee on Agriculture announced the approval of N50billion subsidy for the agricultrural sector, mainly as support for farm imputs. It is interesting to note that this announcement passed almost unnoticed. Not even the stakeholders have cheered, what by all standard is a major support for the sector. There are two possible explanations to this apathy: farmers are not aware of the implications of such support for their enterprise or they have become cynical to such policies. Afterall, huge sums of money have been voted in the past without anything to show for it.
In Nigeria, majority of farmers still live from hand to mouth, they are mainly subsistence farmers. However, even those that are into farming as business continue to grapple with the problem of lack of access to credit and market. Much of what is produced in Nigeria's rural farms are wasted annually. Interest rate still stands around 30 percent and most famers do not have the required collateral. Because most of the farmers are illiterate rural dwellers, they have no knowledge or access to modern techniques that could boost production, with relatively lower labour input. The Agricultural Development Projects (ADPs) jointly financed by the World Bank, the Federal as well as the state governments with a focus on integrated rural development, which was created in 1975, used to provide extension services and training to farmers. The objective of the ADP was to "increase the productivity and income of small farmers." All the ADPs are dead now. How can the current government efforts address these problems? How will N50billion tackle, for example, the problem of poor storage; and how will it expand the agricultural market? In essence, how will the increased productivity that is likely to result from these support, if they were targeted, be accommodated and translated into real income? How will it be ascertained that it is not only the rich farmers that benefit from the support?
N50 billion is not one hell of money. Is it? The Nigerian Agricultural and Co-operative Bank (NACB) started in 1973 with a capital base of N570.3 million to provide credit facilities to farmers. USD1 was equal to 60kobo 30 years ago. Nevertheless, it is a bold move that is worth building on. And perhaps, another significant side of it is the implications it will have for Nigeria's commitment at the World Trade Organisations. Under this regime, non-tariff measures, like domestic support to farmers, are regarded as market distorting and it is enough for some countries to go to court. Don't ask how much Americans are awarding their farmers every year. That is the question that will have to wait till somebody begin to invoke Uruguay. Abimbola Akosile asks...
What Can You Do with N50bn
Mr. Akeem Oyegunle - Auditor, Obasanjo Farms, Otta, Ogun State:
"Government needs to recognise the actual farmers, the real agriculturists. They also need to embark on mass importation of farm implements and distribute these to the actual farmers at the barest minimum amount possible. The Government also needs to resuscitate the farm settlements as in the late fifties and sixties. These settlements should be provided with basic infrastructural amenities like light, good water, and roads so that the dwellers there would not suffer.
At the settlements, the workers should be encouraged on mass crop production like cocoa to regain our lost position in the agricultural genre. The settlements should be a joint affair, where agricultural agencies should educate the farmers on the use of modern day implements. Government should also employ agricultural science graduates to go back and enlighten the farmers on given targets and agricultural methods".
"On disbursement, the subsidy should be made available through farming associations and cooperatives, with the processes publicly aired so that no association official would be able to embezzle the funds. The disbursement should be adequately publicised and the figures involved made public records so that all beneficiaries can approach their individual associations and collect their entitlements".
"Government should concentrate on all the sectors since they are equally important, but special focus should be paid to arable crops like yams, beans, rice, maize e.t.c. to ensure adequate production of food to sustain the growing population. If the subsidy policy is implemented with all sincerity, there would be widespread growth and success in agriculture in Nigeria and food would be available for everyone".
"There must also be focus on research. Let professors in educational institutions research into each agricultural product to determine their durability. As an interim measure, there should be greater construction of grain silos to increase storage. The subsidy will have a multiplier effect, which will bring out positive development, if it is done by government and carried out as conceived by project initiators".
Official, Lagos State Ministry of Agriculture (Maize Processing Unit, Oko- Oba, Lagos):
"Until I see visible signs, I will not believe any Government policy or moves. All along, money has always been released but it ends up in the wrong pockets through diversion. It never gets to the root, some people will still divert the money. Government is not sincere.
The problem is not the money but the implementation of action for which the money is being budgeted. We have that peculiar problem in this country, where at the planning stage, everything is going well, until some official decides to embezzle the money, and then nothing gets done. All the same, it is a welcome development."
"Government, while implementing the subsidy, should concentrate on soil analysis and research for planting; adequate storage and poultry farming; education and enlightenment of farmers, and provision of agricultural implements. They should also carry out a survey to determine each agricultural sectoral need, and based on that, they can allocate the money (subsidy). But the people in charge of disbursement must be reliable people who can make the difference".
"On importation and ban on frozen poultry products, we have enough poultry products in this country. Any importation is due to only laziness and lack of focus. We have enough to feed ourselves in this country. It is because agriculture has been relegated to the background and associated with poverty. The players in the sector should be re-orientated to change their mind-set and thus be more productive".
Kolade, an Agric-Economist Graduate, now turned Banker
"I have graduated in agricultural economics from university since four years, and I must be frank with you, I'm very cynical about the whole matter. That subsidy will never filter down to the lower levels for the farmers to be able to enjoy the supposed benefits of the package. As a reasonable man, you want to ask, "Will the subsidy come in form of imputs like fertiliser, chemicals, improved seedlings, or in cash?. A rational mind would also wonder what Government is actually subsidising, whether it is actual agricultural production or not".
"If you get a reasonable answer, then you can take a risk and get more involved or not. Look at me; I'm now a banker out of frustration because there was no avenue to practice what I learnt in the university. If I had a chance to go back into the agricultural subsector, I would want to look before leaping. It would be suicidal to go back without knowing what the government actually plans. Government has given subsidy several times and nothing happened in the industry, no improvement".
The Government, if indeed they intend to subsidise the sector, should focus on fisheries production now that it has banned importation of frozen poultry. Also livestock production should be well subsidised so that there would be enough meat for Nigerians, and also crop production should be enhanced, especially income earners like cocoa and other cash crops so as to reduce the pressure on oil, and thus allow the other sectors of the economy to grow again as in the days before the advent of oil discovery. Government should focus more on food crop production, and there should be adequate infrastructure to ensure the products get to the market and the desired population. Provision of infrastructure would make it easier for subsistence farmers who provide the major food products in the country".
"Government should also try to revive the agricultural extension services sector which was well utilised during late Chief Obafemi Awolowo's era. This really helped to boost agricultural production in the then Western region, a method which can be employed all over the country. The government should also focus on areas of comparative advantage, e.g. rice in Tiv- land, maize in the middle belt, cocoa in western Nigeria, and so on. Each region will mass- produce its own assigned products and the network would then ensure adequate and equable distribution to all corners of the country, thus alleviating hunger and high food prices".
Mr. Silas Omomehin, Agricultural Scientist & Consultant, Lagos
"The subsidy is a very welcome development. No nation in the world can boost its agricultural production without government intervention. The subsidy will change the face of agriculture and make it more competitive. It will help solve the problem of smuggling of poultry products".
"To make the subsidy very effective, Government should invest the funds in capacity-building in sustainable agricultural production systems i.e. how to produce sustainably using the best agriculture practices. Also they should channel funds into imputs e.g.. tractors, fertiliser, seedlings, farm implements, raw materials especially for feed for animal production.. Thirdly, Government should focus on post-harvest storage and market development, and also infrastructural support. Good roads should be constructed to help the farmers bring their products to the market themselves, thus eliminating the issue of middlemen and subsequent jacking up of foodstuff prices beyond the common man's reach. Storage should be particularly tackled since an average of fifty percent of agricultural products in the country are wasted yearly to poor storage facilities. This brings about the issue of agricultural processing where extra eggs can be converted into egg powder for later use, and so on".
"The disbursement of the subsidy should be in phases to avoid glut and possible inflation. It should be done gradually through farmers' associations, organised private sector players like banks, finance houses, investment firms, agro-related Non- Governmental Organisations (NGOs) and consultancies, Agricultural Development Programmes; and also pumped into educational institutions like universities, research institutions e.t.c. There must be an articulated plan of action. Lots of people don't need the physical cash, but rather imputs so these should be made available to them to enhance production. Raw materials must also be made available at very affordable prices.
Official, Lagos State Agricultural Development Authority (ADA), Oko- Oba, Lagos State:
"Government should go beyond subsidy and take responsibility and also encourage farmers to produce enough. Subsidy would be a welcome idea, but the first focus should be agro-processing, which must be encouraged. We have enough resources to feed the country in poultry products; there are so many poultry farmers in Lagos who can produce in adequate quantity to feed the nation. Government should address priority areas like agricultural extension services because it is all encompassing, and also mechanisation. Right now mechanisation is about two percent effective and this is responsible for our problems. It (mechanisation) can never be practised half-way, but fully. Government should look for or establish villages and commission cultivation with the farmers paying only a token for the use of mechanised implements. A tractor right now is going for six million naira, which is out of the reach of an average farmer, so if these tractors are made available to the farmers at token fees, it would boost production tremendously".
"Storage is also vital and cuts across all aspects of agricultural production. The grains and food crops should be adequately stored after cultivation and harvest, while the poultry and animal products should also be well stored after production e.g. the abattoir where we get our daily meat should be kept in good condition".
"The subsidy can be disbursed through the cooperative societies and associations to ensure even and adequate distribution in area where cash is directly involved. The issue of comparative advantage is also very important in the revival of the agricultural sub-sector of the country. By now, Nigeria should be talking about agricultural regionalisation, where each area or region would be allowed to produce based on the crop or product it knows how to produce best. For instance, cassava thrives well in the south-west, the North-central has cereals, the core north has groundnuts, and so on. Each of these regions should be given equal assistance in funds to produce maximally. This is what is killing us; we want to produce everything in one location, and at the end of the day, nothing gets done. Nigeria should be able to do any kind of agricultural production, by merely looking at the climatic and geographical diversity of the country".
"Under the same arrangement when talking about poultry, the cage system thrives well in the south-west, while the north is known for free-range system. There are poultry and processing plants in Oyo State that can process up to one million birds. These methods can be encouraged in all relevant locations so that adequate poultry production can be ensured and enough products made available for consumption. Nigeria must also go back to the issue of farm settlements, where all types of people from graduates to core rural farmers can be employed to produce en-masse agricultural products. The Government can start the initiative, while the organised private sector can come in, on seeing the success of the whole scheme. If there is money and success involved in the schemes, the private sector will surely come in and also invest in the projects and schemes".

Original_Search_Title: Who Needs Tax Exemptions?
Headline: Nigeria: Who Needs Tax Exemptions?
Body_Text:
The renewed efforts of the Obasanjo administration towards self sufficiency in the agricultural sector surely has little to do with the re -election bid. The administration's special interest in agriculture is simply an extension of Operation Feed the Nation, a feat which never quite was done.
As Operation Feed the Nation continues, too many factors stand in the way of its success, the more prominent of which are under mechanization of the farming process and the momentous waste due to bad storage facilities. As we face another mid year of acute food shortage, the Federal Government announced a reduction in tarriffs (up to 2.5%) as well as tax waivers for up to seven years for any farmer, local or foreign, who is willing to invest in large scale mechanized farming for the production of rice, wheat and maize. Rice, of course, takes predominance. If we are shocked by the amount of money which was spent on rice importation last year, we will be even more shocked this year. Higher tarriffs have not discouraged the consumption or the importation of rice. Rice is still the cheapest staple in most Nigerian cities, proving to be far cheaper than garri, plantain, yam and other yam and cassava based staples, and the demand for it will always exceed that for others. The only thing, in short, that will prevent Nigeria from consuming and importing long grained rice is the availability of locally produced, cheaper rice in quantities that will be worth the while of the farmer, the retailer and the government. In this regard, the plan, if it is well implemented, should turn the Nigerian rice industry around, boosting activity in the agric engineering as well as the retailing sectors and helping to keep at least 600 million naira every year in the pockets of Nigerians. But then, Nigeria's problem has never been the shortage of brilliant ideas. The problem has always been in the amount of dedication, as well as the sincerity with which they are implemented.
Not that this is a particularly brilliant idea. There is nothing at all original about reducing or removing tarriffs and taxes in order to boost agricultural production in some sector or the other by attracting the investor. It is, in fact, what the success of every country which has succeeded in food self sufficiency has been hinged upon Every country assists her farmers either by the donation of seedlings, fertilizers and other materials as has been the case with both the federal and state governments in Nigeria over the years, by tax exclusion as is being proposed or simply by government grants. Still, since it is not a step back, it can only be a step forward.
Naturally, the first person to benefit from the scheme would be the president himself. His Otta farm has survived many travails, including (and I quote)'journalists'. Mechanized, large scale cereal production is a step which would make sense, particularly with regard to taxes, which is an issue which limits the benefits of the option. If large scale cereal production is meant to flood the country with more food than we can eat in a few years, it might (and only might) bear fruit. If it is to alleviate the difficulties farmers face to make food production a feasible venture, it is doomed to pay only the farmers who already have means. How do youn give tax consessions to a man who did not pay tax in the first place, and how do you reduce Tarriffs from zero to two and a half percent?
Otokoto
Of Money Rituals and Ritualists
The judgement passed on the Otokoto seven seemed, to those who have followed the Otokoto saga all this while and to the good people of Imo State who had put their foot down and refused to be bought, a fresh assurance that justice can be gotten in this world, and even in Nigeria. Of course, it does not bring the young boy and the last of the victims back. What it rather does is to make nonsense of the incidence which had resulted in the criminal suits in the first instance. Rituals, and murder on a grand scale to support it. There have been tales from time immemorial about how people are used to make money by some fantastic ritual. No one who has actually seen it has come out to give their own testimony, and it would not be very difficult to get most people to admit that the closest they've ever got to seeing money falling off someone's head or ears or where ever has been in Yoruba movies and, more lately, the reels of films that are reeled out en masse for public consumption by our celluloid industry in what are generally referred to as 'home videos'. Because of this rather than in spite, belief in the viability of killing for rituals with the aim of making money has gained even more credibility, and, as with virtually every thing else, the current economic situation of the country has been blamed for its existence. But there will always be doubting Thomases, and so there are those who claim that the organs stolen from the owners during a ritual murder indeed makes someone very very rich, but not in the way it is thought. Trading in human organs and their exportation abroad for organ transplants and other medical uses is big business, and will continue to be for as long as cloning has not reached that level. In order not to show their hit men the way in this lucrative business, the international organ traders would rather leave them on a wild goose chase by leaving them in the dark about the real relationship between the murders and the cash. What ever be the case whether some god can magically transform a dead person's head into a machine in the Printing and Minting, or that there is a world market for auctioning kidneys, hearts and ovaries just as for oil, gold and the like a ritualist should be a lot more invincible than the Otokoto saga would have us believe. For people who have magically squeezed cash from corpses supposedly by some dark forces, it is incomprehensible how they could not overturn a simple court judgement before it even came out of the judge's mouth.
If the State or someone else related to it has something to hide in the Bola Ige murder case, It is certainly not going about it in the smartest way. Only one thing is certain. It is by no coincidence that new generation human rights lawyer and activist Festus Keyamo was picked up only a short while before the trials were to have begun, but no one knows yet exactly what the connection was. The common suspicion is that the lawyer and Chief witness in the Ige case was put away in order to prevent him from testifying, and, great as the temptation is to believe that explanation, it just continues to rub down badly. It is just too simplistic, and it is more of experience than common sense that informs that the invisible and even the visible powers that be in this highly competitive power terrain did not arrive tops and remain there by being simplistic and leaving loose ends untied, and Keyamo's 'Tales out of Jail' ascribes to these invisible hands desperation more than anything else. This somewhat strengthens the conspiracy theory, which had not got much credibility to start with, that Keyamo is actually working for the government or 'the larger picture' and is being pushed perpetually in our faces to distract us. Yet this still fails to answer questions as to how a government, which is seeking re-election on the most part, would be brave enough to make history as being the first even in Nigeria to arrest and detain a man simply for being someone's legal counsel.
The Ige case appears to be falling apart even before it has started, and Keyamo has already declared his disbelief that justice would prevail. In the mean time, nothing still is being done about the NNPC fire. The consolation though, is the air of sophistication which his newly acquired beard lends him. Now, he looks like a true radical, and he might want to keep it.

Original_Search_Title: Empowering Women for National Development
Headline: Nigeria: Empowering Women for National Development
Body_Text:
Lagos — Touched by the prevalent poverty among rural women, a non-governmental organisation is providing micro credit facilities to women at the grassroot with the conviction that it will empower them economically, writes Funso Abdullahi
A large percentage of the country's population live in the rural areas and most of these people do not have access to white collar jobs, they survive mostly on agriculture and petty trading.
Because of the poverty level in rural areas most of them do not have capital to start any business at all but they only need just a few thousands of naira to start a business. It is for this reason that the Women Organisation For Gender Issues (WOGI) has been involved in micro credit programme.
WOGI grants small loans to women who have viable business proposals and such loans range from N5,000 to N20,000. The beneficiaries of the loans are expected to repay within a 12-month period. The loan is interest free.
Recently when WOGI presented its programme of activities for this year and its past achievements, its co-ordinator, Mrs. Stella Adaku Odife, said it found out that more than half of these rural women pay their loan within the stipulated period.
WOGI informed that under its micro credit programme, over 250 women have received micro-credits. Under this programme WOGI grants small loans to those who have viable businesses.
Because of the ban on imported poultry this year, WOGI's emphasis is to ensure that rural women provide enough poultry meat for themselves and a surplus for sale to city people.
Loans repayed are pooled and lent to other women who are also desirous of starting their own trade or ventures. In some cases those beneficiaries whose trade required additional funding for expansion are given another loan from the repayments.
Odife attributed the success rate of the micro credit scheme to the communal nature of the villages where borrowers are well known to each other, they strive to re-pay on time to enable others benefit.
Because of the success rate of the micro credit scheme, WOGI plans to start a women's bank. Already, a study has been commissioned to examine the feasibility of the proposed bank. If established, the bank is expected to provide loan at reduced rates to deserving rural women. Such loans would be granted to those interested in agriculture, such as poultry keeping, yam and cassava cultivation as well as soya milk making.
In addition, young girls are trained to acquire skills like tailoring/dress making, hairdressing, soap making and soya milk production. It hires resource persons to teach requisite skills to the women. For skills like tailoring, hairdressing that take longer time, WOGI sponsors girls to be trained under apprenticeship programmes. The girls are provided with sewing machines and hairdressing equipment after apprenticeship and are expected to repay the money used to purchase the equipment within one year to enable others benefit.
Under the skill acquisition programme, a group of seven women was also trained as caterers. WOGI, at the end of their apprenticeship supplied them with modern catering equipment.
WOGI said it has been impressed with the scheme so far as most beneficiaries repay their loans within the stipulated time.
According to the organisation the exceptions include those provided with hairdressing equipment at Nkwelle-Ezunaka in Anambra, who could not repay the cost of their equipment within the stipulated time due to lack of electricity for a period of over nine months in a year.
It said similar problems were faced by women trained in the production of soya milk as poor power supply meant that they lacked the means for the preservation of the milk.
The organisation, which has its headquarters in Anambra State, has acquired land at Nkwelle- Ezunaka, a town about 10 kilometers from Onitsha, to build a skill acquisition centre, which will be equipped for training of young women. It also hopes to establish more centres in other states.
WOGI has also held workshops both in the cities and in the rural areas. One of the topics covered in the workshop was in the area of politics. Building on the prevalent lack of interest in politics by women, WOGI educates women to take interest and participate in politics, and by possibly seek elective offices.
It said that participation in politics might translate to joining political parties who have the ambition and for others to ensure that they register to vote and actually cast their vote during election. It noted that since it is believed that there are perhaps more women than men in Nigeria, if women should take advantage of their numbers by participating fully in politics, more women would probably get into positions that would facilitate a change in policies affecting them.
This way, Mrs. Odife said more women would be found in the judiciary, senate as well as state legislative houses. If and when women are found in policy making positions, obnoxious laws that militate against women could easily be changed.
Odife informed that WOGI found out in its programmes that one of the greatest setbacks of women is ignorance, even when there are laws in place. She said women do not even know such laws exist, law on battery, the law of bigamy and even the fundamental rights as enshrined in the constitution. Therefore she said education on such rights is provided at WOGI workshops.
Determined to make the best use of her law degree, Mrs. Odife explored other avenues and this led her to team up with three other lawyers in 1998 to set up the Women's Organisation for Gender Issues (WOGI), an organisation involved in grass root mobilisaton of women.
Odife speaks passionately about WOGI activities, especially in the eastern part of the country. "Thank God that I have little means, anytime I travel, women mostly come to me for help, I was really touched on a particular occasion when a woman came to me that she wanted to start selling groundnut that I should assist her financially. I asked how much she needed, she told me she needed about N500 to start the business. Most of this women need just a little money and with good management they make it." She added: "So I thought to myself that I will be able to help more women in this situation if I can solicit for funds on their behalf, that it will go a long way in cushioning the effects of poverty on them and sometimes their family."
She explained that generally, about 90 per cent of conflict between husbands and wives is on housekeeping allowance, children school fees among others.
"But if the women are empowered financially like the men, they will be able to assist in taking care of financial matters in the home," she stated. In her view, women can buy more freedom, more happiness by working hard.
To empower women, she said WOGI has designed about 10 programmes. She said in local governments like Nkwelle-Ezunaka and Nsugbe in Abia State, the women were encouraged to form co-operatives, were they are divided into three sets.
"For the first set, we bought big pots, spoons and other items for them to use for outside catering parties. They help in the cooking and get paid. They also hire out the equipment," she explained.
Another set of women, she recalled, were helped to start off a poultry farm. "We gave them one week old chicks and money to buy feed for three months," she noted, adding that another group got materials for garri making while some were also given money to hire labourers to assist them in farming.
Apart from all these, Mrs. Odife is also interested in party politics though she is not yet a card carrying member of any political party. She is now interested and willing to support any woman who wishes to be elected into political position.
"Women are ripe for governance, they should try us out first at the local governments for quick reforms of the Nigerian society."
Odife explained that the more women are elected into political offices, the more issues affecting women are treated with dispatch. On its part, she said WOGI has been sensitising women to go out and vote during elections.
She however conceded that women have had a better deal since 1999, as more women were given political appointments, which has created more awareness since the 1995 Beijing Conference.
One area which Odife identified, as bane against women is violence against them. She said it is no exaggeration that "Nigerian women have been the butt of discrimination in a male dominated judiciary, government and society."
According to her, one key factor in wife battering is an improper view of women. "Whichever culture, the men have been brought up to believe that men are number one.
"They are trained to believe that they are superior to women and that it is their right to punish, discipline or intimidate them. In Africa, the man is considered to have the right to treat his wife like a mere object just as a measure of his manliness and honour," she regretted.
Mrs. Odife said it has been found out that the key to achieving a final solution to some of the family squabbles that have resulted in wife battery is the economic empowerment of women.
This finding led WOGI to extend its operation to granting of micro credits in addition to the training and skills-acquisition programme, which has been its primary focus.
She however advised that to reduce wife battering, non-governmental organisations should provide temporary habitats for abused women until the man comes along for settlement.
The positive side of this arrangement is that "it is a fact that not many men can care for children and handle household chores in the absence of their wives. In few cases, when the woman leaves for a couple of days, the man suddenly realises how dependent he is on her for household management. At the point when he goes in search of his wife, the NGO would be in a position to counsel the man," she stressed.

Original_Search_Title: Niger Police Kill 5 Robbers
Headline: Nigeria: Niger Police Kill 5 Robbers
Body_Text:
Minna — Men of the Niger State Police Command have killed five robbers in separate incidents in Niger and Edo States.
Briefing the press in Minna recently, the acting Police Commissioner, Mr. Cyril Njirize, said the first robbery took place at Aviele in Edo State in a luxurious bus travelling from Minna to Onitsha.
An escort attached to the bus, he said, noticed the suspicious movement of a passenger in the bus and apprehended him.
The passenger, according to him, was seen by the police when he bent down to remove something under the seat and withdrew frantically. But the escort curiously conducted a search under the seat and discovered three tubers of yams.
It was then discerned that one of the yams had been clearly sliced into two and a space enough to accommodate a pistol was hollowed out where a locally made pistol was hidden.
The discovery, Njirinze said, eventually led to another yam where some bullets were similarly concealed.
When the bus was stopped, the passengers noticed that three persons who were suspected to be accomplices of the robbers wanted to escape and two of them were shot dead while the third one ran away, he said.
In a similar case, which happened along the Kaduna-Suleja road, he explained, four people had joined a Peugeot vehicle at Suleja, along with others going to Kano when they observed that other passengers were carrying huge sums of money.
The acting police commissioner said when the vehicle reached Sabon-Wuse, a few kilometres from Suleja, one of the four men pulled a gun at the driver and a struggle ensued.
Unfortunately for the bandits, the Operation-fir-for-fire squad emerged abruptly and clearly blocked them from their escape bid.
Three of them who jumped from the car into the bush were shot while the remaining one was arrested.
The suspect who claimed to have come from Anambra State for the robbery operation gave their names as Azubike Aneke, Cosmas Eze, Uche Chioma and Sunday Okafor. Also recovered from them were a pistol and a single barrel gun.
The police, said the police commissioner, are still investigating the case of the escaped robber at Aviele in Edo State.

Original_Search_Title: Senatorial Candidate Pledges Quick Oil Exploration in Anambra
Headline: Nigeria: Senatorial Candidate Pledges Quick Oil Exploration in Anambra
Body_Text:
Enugu — THE United Nigeria Peoples Party (UNPP) Senatorial candidate for Anambra North Senatorial Zone, Chief Alex Inoma has pledged to work for the quick exploration of Petroleum products discovered in the state since 1949. Speaking with newsmen on the programmes he intends to pursue if elected into the National Assembly, Inoma said exploration of the oil deposit would enable Anambra State to enjoy oil producing status which would lead to increased revenue. "The reactivation of petroleum exploration in Anambra North would be my priority if elected into the Senate. I will vigorously pursue the return of oil companies to this zone for the exploration of oil which was discovered since 1949. This will also entail including our state amongst oil producing states rather than the strategic reserve list," the senatorial candidate said.
Inoma, an international businessman, said he would take advantage of his foreign contacts to bring in power generating plant in a joint venture agreement with Nigeria in the private sector in order to ensure constant electricity supply to the zone especially the commercial town of Onitsha.
According to him, this would come in the form of barges situated on River Niger and Anambra River for the sole purpose of generating electricity into NEPA transmission liens and feeding the communities in the locality.
On the problem of unem
ployment, Inoma noted that both past and present representatives at the National Assembly had not given adequate attention to giving the youth federal employment. "it is my aim to liaise with various arms of the Federal Government in charge of employment to ensure that our right full share of jobs is obtained for equal distribution amongst local government areas in my senatorial zone," he said.
The UNPP senatorial candidate said that since Anambra North was one of the largest food baskets of the nation, he would within his tenure as Senator adequately support fish farming and provide cold storage facilities through private sector investments and initiatives. He said that he had already set up the machinery for large scale seasonal loan disbursements to rice and yam farmers without interest adding that this would commence as soon as he was sworn in as a senator. "Fertilizer will be made available at subsidised rates and its distribution improved upon so that there will be no middlemen involved," he said.
Inoma who also lamented the dearth of infrastructure in the senatorial zone, however, said that he would liaise with the Federal Ministry of Works during his first year as senator to tackle the two major roads in the zone, the Onitsha-Anaku road and Onitsha-Atani road. He also promised to pursue the construction of the Oyi-Ayamelun link road as well as other internal roads within the seven local government areas in the zone.
He also pledged to pursue the reactivation of existing water boreholes and execute new ones in addition to the installation of water treatment plants along the various rivers especially the Anambra river. "Effective representation at the senate entails the ability to articulate sound ideas and lobby to have them passed as bills or motions for the individual and collective good of the people. I intend to contribute my quota in refocusing the Federal Government towards a humane and grassroots approach to issues that affect the masses and for sustainable development," Inoma said.

Original_Search_Title: Audit Report: Business As Usual?
Headline: Nigeria: Audit Report: Business As Usual?
Body_Text:
We continue our serialisation of the Auditor-General of the Federation's report recently submitted to the Clerk of the National Assembly.
Federal Ministry of Education (Continued from yesterday)
The examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Kaduna, revealed the following:-
(a) Amounts totalling N1,423,770.00 were paid to some contractors and members of staff for the supply of various items and as refunds of out-of-pocket expenses incurred between May, 2000 and January, 2001. The relevant supporting documents such as invoices, receipts and Stores Vouchers were not attached to the payment vouchers. It is therefore difficult to accept the expenditures as legitimate charges against public funds.
(b) Amounts totalling N4,261,935.00 were paid between May, 2000 and January 2001 to some members of staff for the settlement of the college expenses. A scrutiny of the payment vouchers showed that the actual total amount signed for and collected by the payees was N3,068,513.00 resulting in an overpayment of N1,193,442.00 which should be accounted for.
(c) An amount of N42,779.00 was paid to a contractor in January, 2001 for the supply of food items. The payment voucher was supported with a photocopy of an old invoice for N150,000.00 dated 25th October, 1999 and store receipt voucher dated 21st October, 1999. This fictitious payment of N42,7799.00 should be recovered from the officer who authorised such payment and recovery particulars furnished for audit verification. In addition, all officers involved in the processing of the payment should be appropriately disciplined.
(d) 2 cheques for amounts totalling N250,000.00 were issued in April and June 2001 in favour of the Principal without raising payment vouchers contrary to the Financial Regulation which stipulates that all payment must be properly vouched before payment is made. The total amount of N250,000.00 should be refunded by the Principal.
These irregularities have been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose comments are being awaited.
During the audit examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Kiyawa, it was observed that:
(a) An amount of N498,000.00 was paid to the Principal in November, 2001 for the purchase of an equipment to the Home Management Department. The equipment should have been procured through the use of Job Order or Tendering procedure. Moreover, the equipment could not be traced into the stores records or produced for my verification. Consequently, I cannot accept such payment as a proper charge against public funds.
(b) Another amount of N689,000.00 was paid to the Principal in November, 2001 for the purchase of adding machine, typewriter and duplicating machine. Audit investigation revealed that the duplicating machine was yet to be supplied as at the time if audit inspection. The Principal should please refund the cost of price of the duplicating machine not purchased.
The irregularities have been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose response is being awaited.
During the examination of the accounting books maintained at the Federal Technical College, Lassa, the following observations were noted:-
(a) 9 payment vouchers for amounts totalling N7,850,058.81 were raised and purportedly paid for hiring of labourers to clear the college premises. The physical inspection of the college revealed that no clearing was effected to justify such colossal expenditure. Consequently, I cannot certify that the expenditures were legitimate charges against public fund.
(b) 3 payments totalling N2,821,000.00 made to the Principal, during the year under review, for the printing of Treasury and other forms were wrongly classified to the Sub-head for Personnel Costs, contrary to the Financial Regulation which stipulates that expenditure will be classified in accordance with the Estimates and votes must be applied only to the purpose for which money is provided. Furthermore, there was no authority from the Accountant-General of the Federation for the printing of the Treasury Forms in accordance with the Financial Regulations.
(c) Similarly, 2 payment vouchers for amounts totalling N2,506,800.00 were raised and paid from the Personnel Costs vote to members of staff for alleged Duty Tour Allowances. The narration for the payments was not specified on any of the payment vouchers. It is therefore difficult to accept the expenditures as legitimate charges against public funds.
The irregularities have been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose reaction is being awaited.
The examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Maiduguri, revealed that irregular loans for amounts totalling N3,587,253.71 were granted to Officers of the College, Federal Pay Officer, NITEL's Territorial Manager and the Publisher of the College's magazine between April, 2000 and November, 20001 from the Domestic, Feeding and Capital Accounts.
As at the time of audit inspection in December 2001, the loans were yet to be recovered contrary to the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
This irregularity has been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary and his comments are being awaited.
While examining the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Nise, it was observed that:-
(a) An amount of N3,223,783.80 was paid to a contractor as part payment for a contract valued at N12,520,000.00 in respect of the Perimeter Wall Fencing of the College. The audit inspection of the project revealed that the work was shoddily executed even though the interim payment certificate was signed by the Project Officer. The cement used was poorly mixed, thereby causing major cracks on the wall and the blocks to be wearing off. The contractor should therefore be made to do a satisfactory job or refund the money so far collected.
(b) Six payment vouchers for amounts totalling N1,661,473.75 paid to various contractors between February and July, 2001, were not accompanied with supporting documents such as receipts and invoices contrary to the Financial Regulations.
These issues have been taken up with the Permanent Secretary and his reaction is being awaited.
While examining the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Ohafia, the following observations were noted:-
(a) 10 (Ten) payment vouchers for amounts totalling N2,246,271.00 paid between February and April 2001 for various contracts were not supported with the relevant documents such as Contract Agreements, Job Orders, and the contracts did not pass through any competitive tendering procedure in accordance with the provisions of the Financial Regulations. Furthermore, amounts totalling N112,313.55, representing the mandatory 5 per cent withholding tax, were not deducted from the amounts paid to the contractors resulting in a loss of revenue to the Federal Government.
(b) Out of the balance of N7,604,171.92 in the Capital Account Vote as at April, 2001, amounts totalling N2,900,000.00 were expended between February and April, 2001 for various supplies and construction without recourse to contract procedures as stipulated in the Financial Regulations. Moreover, the amounts were not posted into the cash book and vote book. Consequently, it is difficult to accept the payments totalling N2,900,000.00 as proper charges against public funds.
(c) A payment voucher for N500,000.00 paid in March, 2001 to a contractor as part payment for furnishing the Principals's Office was not supported with the necessary documents such as - Contract Agreement, Store Receipt Voucher and Store Issue Voucher. This irregular payment was made from the Domestic Account of the college without any authority in accordance with the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
All the issues have been taken up with the Permanent Secretary whose response is being awaited.
While examining the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Okigwe, the following observations were made:-
(a) A total amount of N3,625,833.32 under the Capital Allocation Vote in 2000 was paid to the Principal without reference to any payment voucher number or service rendered instead of spending the amount on Capital Projects.
(b) 36 payment vouchers for amounts totalling N6,523,224.45 paid between December 1999 and December 2000 were not produced for my inspection. Consequently, I cannot certify that the expenditure on them were just and proper charges against public funds.
(c) 33 part-time teachers were employed by the Principal between November 1999 and April, 2000 - 6 months, without any authority from the headquarters and a sum of N998,930.45 was paid to the teachers for the period. However, there was no evidence to confirm that the part-time teachers signed and collected their salaries. Furthermore, the amount paid to the teachers in the last 3 months - February to April 2000 was N169,287.55 more than the amount paid from November 1999 to January 2000. The issue should be investigated and the amount totalling N998,930.45 should be recovered from the Principal.
(d) A colossal amount of N4,000,000.00 was paid to 50 casual labourers in December 1999 and July 2000 for cutting of grasses and clearing of students hostels. It is amazing that a college that has some labourers on its payroll could engage outside labourers for everyday from January 1999 to May, 1999 and April 2000 to be cutting grasses.
There was no evidence to authenticate that the casual labourers signed and collected their wages during the period. Furthermore, the payment of N400,000.00 in July, 2000 would appear to be duplicated. Consequently, I cannot accept the expenditure of N4,000,000.00 as legitimate charges against public fund.
