(e) Amounts totalling N570,500.00 were paid as loans to 37 members of staff between October 1999 and December, 2000. None of the loans has been recovered from the beneficiaries as at the time of audit inspection in February 2001. Furthermore, the loans were not recorded except on the payment vouchers which the beneficiaries signed and collected contrary to the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
(f) A sum of N3,057,538.00 was paid to various officers of the college as refunds out-of-pocket expenses. However, lit was observed that all the necessary attachments or supporting documents were not attached to the payment vouchers to confirm the authenticity of the transactions. It is therefore difficult to accept the payments as proper charges against public fund.
(g) A total amount of N1,747,110.00 was paid to the Principal and some members of staff as refunds of out-of-pocket expenses. Only N886,776.15 could be accounted for leaving a balance of N861,146.85 to be accounted for.
(h) 171 payment vouchers for amounts totalling N2,528,821.42 paid between January and September, 2000 were not posted into the Cash Book in accordance with the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
(i) 33 payment vouchers amounting to N756,430.00 paid between January and September, 2000 were not signed by the Principal, who was the Officer Controlling Expenditure, in accordance with the provisions of the Financial Regulation.
(j) An amount of N250,000.00 was paid for the purchase of 5000kg of yams in February, 2001. Although Store Receipt Voucher was raised apparently to satisfy payment requirement, the items could not be traced into the Store neither were they accounted for.
(k) A sum of 200,000.00 was paid to the Bursar in February and May 2000 from the Domestic Account to enable him purchase some unspecified domestic items and renovate his apartment. Audit investigation revealed that the bursar was not living in government quarters and no domestic items were purchased for the kitchen.
The irregularities have been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose comments are being awaited.
During the audit examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Otobi, the following observations were noted:-
(a) 27 Domestic Account Receipts Nos. 0002953 -0002979 issued in acknowledgement of receipt of school fees totalling N40,500.00 on 30th January, 2001 were not posted into the cash book.
(b) The total collection of N75,000.00 vide Domestic Account Receipts Nos. 01201-01250 was posted into the cash book as N7,500.00, resulting in a balance of N67,500.00 to be accounted for.
(c) Collections vide Domestic Account Receipts Nos. 005401-005423 and Nos. 005434-005450 amounting to N57,330.00 and N22,154.24 respectively could not be accounted for during audit inspection.
(d) Bank Reconciliation Statements have not been prepared for the bank accounts being operated by the school since April 2000 contrary to the provisions of the Financial Regulations. Zit is therefore difficult to ascertain the true periodic balances of the accounts and this could lead to non-detection of possible fraudulent practices.
(e) The debit closing balance of N160,819.26 in the Other Charges Cash Book as at 28th December, 2000 was brought forward as N61,678.19 on 2nd January, 2001. Similarly, an amount of N120,259.43 being closing balance (credit) as at 28th December, 2000 was brought forward as N6,601.83 debit balance in the Boarding Account cash book on 2nd January, 2001.
In the absence of the Bank Reconciliation Statement, the irregularities could not be explained.
(f) Cash advances totalling N640,358.00 granted to 7 members of staff between May 2000 and April, 2001 still remained unrecovered as at the time of audit inspection in June 2001. Some of the officers collected advances more than eight times without retiring the previous ones negating the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
The attention of the Permanent Secretary has been drawn to the above-mentioned irregularities and his comments are being awaited.
At the Federal Government Girls' College, Sagamu, it was observed that a personal advance of N141,490.00 was granted to an officer in February, 2001 out of which an amount of N110,590.00 has been retired leaving a balance of N30,900.00 to be retired as at the time of audit inspection in December, 2001.
The Permanent Secretary has been requested to recover the outstanding advance furnishing me with recovery particulars. His response is being awaited.
During the audit examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Technical College, Shiroro, the following anomalies were noted:-
(a) An amount of N189,000.00 was irregularly withdrawn from the school's Domestic Account. This was done by detaching the documents that were used to support the payment of the same amount in the year 2000 and attaching them to another payment voucher that was raised on 31st January, 20001 to withdrawn the amount of N189,000.00.
(b) A sum of N358,000.00 was paid to a member of staff on 27th December, 2001 to settle what was termed "outstanding liability on foodstuff purchased for the college". This sum was paid from the school's Domestic Account. The originals of the payment vouchers and the relevant attachments showing details of the liability paid, were not produced for audit inspection.
The Permanent Secretary has been requested to recover the amount irregularly withdrawn and comment on the disciplinary action taken against the culprit; and also furnish me with the details of outstanding liability that was paid. His response is being awaited.
While examining the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government Girls College, Umuahia, the following observations were made:
(a) Cash advances totalling N2,677,470.00 granted to 3 members of staff of the College between September 2000 and January 2001 for the purchase of foodstuff and fuel had not been retired at the time of audit inspection in March, 2001. Incidentally, one of the officers had been granted 19 advances, which had not been retired.
Furthermore, those services might have been better rendered through the award of contracts after competitive tender procedures or through the issuance of Job Orders.
(b) Amounts totalling N276,122.00, being Withholding Tax due on 9 contract payments totalling N5,522,444.00 and effected between January and December, 2000 were not deducted, thereby resulting in a loss of revenue to the Federal Government.
(c) Monthly allocations for overhead and capital costs were withdrawn en bloc from the Federal Pay Officer's Account through payment vouchers raised in favour of the Principal without the necessary supporting documents and deposited into the Domestic Account. The amounts deposited were then spent through the Domestic Account Cash Book while the Treasury Vote Book indicated on en bloc amount. This irregular procedure would not indicate the actual payee, amount on each voucher, the sub-head and purpose of payment contrary to the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
The irregularity has been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary and his comments are being awaited.
At the Federal Government, Wukari, 14 copies of revenue receipts issued between 28th September and 8th December, 2001 for amounts totalling N4,673,334.83 could not be produced for my verification. It is therefore difficult to ascertain the correct amount of revenue collected for the Government during the period.
The issue has been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose comments are being awaited.

Original_Search_Title: Those Who Killed Bola Ige Will Not Go Scot-Free - Ajayi-Obe
Headline: Nigeria: Those Who Killed Bola Ige Will Not Go Scot-Free - Ajayi-Obe
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chief Phoebe Chiadikobi Ajayi-Obe, SAN, the second female to be conferred with the prestigious rank of Senior Advocate of Nigeria is publicity shy. JUDE IGBANOI tracked down the unassuming Senior Advocate in her chambers at Ibadan last Friday and she had a lot of advice for younger lawyers, women and even our belligerent politicians
You were conferred with the coveted rank of Senior Advocate of Nigeria in 1989. Being the second woman to be so honoured, how did you receive the news?
I was very, very happy. My family was very happy. I knew that it carried a lot of responsibilities and I was prepared for it.
What would you say marked you out for this award?
I wouldn't say that I am better than anybody. I believe that at that time it was on merit. They looked at your character generally, your performance at the Bar and your contribution to the development of the law.
As a matter of fact, the Chief Judge of your state must write something positive about you. The Justices of the Court of Appeal where you practice must have their input. The Supreme Court as well, they have to assess you all round.
Not only in your performance at the Bar but you must be generally accepted as suitable to be elevated to that coveted position.
There seems to be something peculiar about law practice in the city of Ibadan. Producing the first two female SANs is something unique. What is the secret or can we pass it off as a mere coincidence?
It is not a coincidence. The standard of practice in Ibadan is very high. Distractions in Ibadan are very few, unlike Lagos or any other city.
In Ibadan if you want to practice law, you practice law. You don't mix it with any other thing, like business, e.t.c When we go into practice in this Ibadan, we are in practice, though the money is not there in Ibadan as it is in Lagos. So, we live moderately here and we have good practice. That is the only secret I can attribute to it. The social life in Ibadan is moderate and healthy compared to Lagos and other cities. That gives us time to do our work. Most importantly, a stable home. If you have a stable home it will help in everything that you do.
Success comes easily when you have a stable home. When you have time to look after your children, you plan properly, your husband is understanding and accommodating, so you are free to do your work. Of course, you will achieve success.
Up till date, there are only five female Senior Advocates of Nigeria. There is no woman at the Supreme Court and there are just a few at the Court of Appeal. Would you say the Nigerian Bar and Bench have been fair to the women folk?
So far, they have not been fair. The question is, who takes the decisions?
Mostly men. Women are trying, but the society is not helping. I wouldn't say that only five women are qualified to be Senior Advocates of Nigeria. But some of them will reach the last stage, and when the result comes out their names won't be there.
It's not as if they are not qualified. Many Nigerian women are qualified to be in the Supreme Court, to be Senior Advocates, but there are only five female SANs in the whole of Nigeria. I know it is not fair. We deserve more.
As for the Supreme Court, how do you get there? Women cannot nominate themselves. We can't send ourselves to the Supreme Court! We are even lucky that we have a few of us in the Court of Appeal.
I remember when Honourable Justice Bello, CJN, was about to retire, he came here and I asked him, when are we going to have a woman in the Supreme Court? He said to me that left to him as a person, we would have had one. Even at that time, we were to have a female Chief Judge in Kaduna. He told us that he couldn't do that because the Moslems would not accept a female C.J. That the Alkalis will not accept her as their head. Fortunately, he had to make her the first female Justice of the Court of Appeal to compensate her. So you can see the obstacles that women face.
As a word of advice to younger female lawyers in Nigeria, what does it takes for a woman to reach the peak in a male dominated profession like law?
In every profession a woman starts from minus zero. I advise them to start from there to the top. Before a woman is recognised, she was to work harder and excel and do much more better than her male counterparts.
It's only when she does that she will be recognised. If she is at par with men she doesn't stand a chance.
So, I advise them to work very hard. they should believe that the sky is the limit. One day they will be recognised.
The issue of declining standards in practice is one that has been of concern among senior members of the bar in recent times. What was practice like in your earlier days and how can it be improved upon now?
Well, in our earlier days a lawyer was looked at as learned, respectable person in the society. Law was his life, his profession and he lived on it. If he worked very hand he got results.
Today, law is not as lucrative financially as it used to be. There are some, a few that find it very lucrative. But you find that some lawyers mix law practice with other professions to make ends meet. Of course, the law itself must suffer. This is because you have to prepare your cases very well, prepare your address very well, interview your clients and witnesses in your chambers.
You must prepare before you go to court. When you get to court you must deliver.
If you are preparing a document, you must prepare that document looking at precedents and authorities and strive to make it a masterpiece.
That gave us joy in those days.
Today, to some lawyers, what gives them joy is how much they have got out of it. Of course, if that is the case the standard must come down.
But we are doing our best to see that the standard does not go lower. In Ibadan, the standard of practice is higher than any where in this country. When you talk of lowering standards, Ibadan is still much better and we are doing our best to ensure that the young ones who want to practice law really practice law. We have juniors and we make sure we bring them up to continue in that direction.
Coming back to the women issue, women empowerment and women in politics. The first female Speaker of a State House of Assembly in Nigeria was frustrated and hounded out of office in Benue State. Now, the only female Deputy Governor in the present dispensation has just suffered the same fate in Lagos State. Currently, the only female Presidential aspirant, Mrs. Sarah Jubril is not taken seriously. What is really wrong with the womenfolk in Nigeria? What is your assessment of the Nigerian woman?
Well, I can say that it's not women themselves that are the problem. It is the society. For instance, if a woman is a Deputy Governor, if her party does not give her the support, if her governor does not give her encouragement and there is no rapport, you find that she will be a lone ranger among men who are interested in ousting her.
I am not holding brief for the Deputy Governor that she is a perfect person. But she is not the worst Deputy Governor in the country. I think, when you find that two people are unable to work together, it won't be exactly because this is a woman. But it's worse because she is woman and, of course they will ask 'in the first place, how did she get there?' So, she can stand a chance with them.
You see, I will advise women, if you want to go into politics, go into politics on your own merit. If you get there, look at the office and do your best to carry out the responsible that office imposes on you. Another thing you have to look at is that we are advocating that women should support women in the coming elections. We are advising that women whenever we find a woman capable, we should support her. We are advocating that.
We are not saying that we don't have other women who don't rate women high. But we are trying to educate ourselves. For instance, when we had our Zonta meeting recently, we invited Professor Sowunmi to talk on this issue. It generated a lot of debate. Part of the debate was that we women are our own enemies. We advocated that we set out to educate women that we need to support ourselves. If we don't support ourselves, nobody will support us. We can only be stronger if we support ourselves. Men who will want to pull us down will not succeed. We are at that and by the special grace of God we will get there.
The death of Chief Bola Ige was quite traumatic for both the legal profession and the government. Now, the suspects are undergoing trial here at Ibadan. You, being based here, must be conversant with the goings on. Given the political dimensions that the case has assumed, would you say that justice would be done at the end of the day?
You see, I want to believe that a court will do justice any time. The moment we suspect that justice will not come from the court, the common man is finished.
I want to believe that a judge once called a judge will do what justice demands in every matter that comes before him. So, I won't say that justice will not be done. Justice is not just who wins or who lose.
Losing a case may still be justice and winning it could be justice. So, I want to believe that justice will be done in the end. Of course the judge can make a mistake. Why do we have the Court of Appeal? Why do we have the Supreme Court? If a judge at the High Court makes a mistake, you go to the Court of Appeal. If the Justice there makes mistakes you go to the Supreme Court. If they confirm the judgment there, you leave it to the final arbiter.
God is the final arbiter. But I believe that the people who killed Chief Bola Ige will not go scot-free. They will not! Even today, they are suffering for it. Even if they are not the people facing trial, the Bible says, what you sow, you will reap.
There is no way they can be happy and joyous and live joyfully for the rest of their lives with that on their conscience.
I observed that you have a Christian Fellowship here in your chambers. Now, there is so much God - consciousness in Nigeria today. We have churches in every nook and cranny of our cities, yet there is still so much crime, violence and corruption in Nigeria. Is it that we are not praying hard enough? What exactly is our problem?
The way I see it is that if we had not turned to God, the situation would have been worse. Definitely, the situation would have been worse.
God has been answering our prayers. Look at the time that Abacha wanted to translate into a civilian president. God intervened. Look at all other instances. If we had not turned to God, this country would have gone to pieces. What we are going through in this country and what we are getting away from, other countries would have gone to war to destroy themselves. Look at the way things are in the country but we still have two or three meals in a day. I think God is still in control. The children of God who are real children of God have our prayers answered. That is why things have not got worse. But I believe that things will inprove.
You know that some of these killings, robberies and so on is as a result of unemployment and hunger. Hunger! The only thing we are complaining about is the killing of innocent people.
Again you find that before they go into these operations they are no longer human beings. They have taken something that removes them from humanity, so they behave like animals. Can you imagine somebody robbing you of your car and still killing you?
It makes no sense! It's just because the man is not himself. But I believe that with the number of Christians praying everyday God is at our rescue. We will not give in. We will continue! I believe that God will come to our aid. God has been with us, that is why this country is still one.
It has been said that there is this age old mutual mistrust between the Igbos and the Yorubas which makes the proverbial handshake across the Niger a mirage. As an Igbo married to a Yoruba, how have you been able to strike a balance between your ethnic and cultural leanings, being married to a Yoruba and building a stable home in the heart of Yorubaland?
When I first came to Ilesha, my husband's uncle used to call me Kobokobo! After a year or two, I was passing through the front of his house and he called me his daughter.
He said, 'You are my daughter, you are no longer Kobokobo'. He went into his house and brought some yams and gave to me.
I think the differences between the Yorubas and the Igbos are strictly political. Outside of the politics, the two tribes have very good rapport. There are very many Igbos married to Yorubas, just like you have many Yorubas married to Igbos.
Disagreements only come in through politics. I listened to the Primate (Mbang) last month. He said that the problem of Nigeria will be solved when the South unites. I took him seriously because the present politicians will not be there forever.
The younger people know what the problems are. If you go outside this country, Nigerians are Nigerians. They don't say, 'this one is Yoruba or this one is Igbo! I believe that its a question of time and God will answer our prayers. I also believe our leaders should remove self and think of country first. That is their problem. Self. They want to satisfy self first I want to rule, I don't want to support a Yoruba man. An Igbo man must not get there. That is self.
I really believe that it's a question of time, it will all be well. My father-in-law loved me very much. I got away with a lot of things because I am not Yoruba woman. So, that made things easy for me.

Original_Search_Title: Campaign for Clean Environment Falling On Deaf Ears
Headline: Ghana: Campaign for Clean Environment Falling On Deaf Ears
Body_Text:
Accra — Despite the campaign by the Accra Metropolitan Assembly (AMA) to educate traders and food vendors to sell their wares in a clean environment; most of these people still sell their wares in filthy areas.
One example is the big gutter running from old Lido to Orion Cinema at the Nkrumah Circle is full of rubbish with an army of flies. The smell from the gutter is unbearable; and yet food vendors and traders are selling their wares right by the gutter.
Traders, hawkers and food vendors as well as their customers create the refuse in the first place when they throw empty rubber bags and water sachets into the gutters. Garbage, dirty water, urine and even night soil all get dumped in the gutters.
This is the practice at Lagos Town, Maamobi, Agbogbloshie, Kasoa to mention a few places. "Left-over" food such as rice and the skins of plantain, yam or cocoyam also end up in the gutters, instead of in a receptacle.
A Ghanaian friend who normally buys food from the food vendors at the Nkrumah Circle has stopped buying from that area because of the rubbish and the flies that come to settle on the prepared food.
In European countries, where they have efficient sewerage systems, all the gutters are covered, yet the citizens are still educated against throwing rubbish or refuse about.
In Ghana where our gutters are exposed and all refuse finds its way into the gutters, the education campaign must be intensified.
BOROUGH CONCEPT
Accra with a population of about three million has for many years been faced with the problem of managing waste. The process of waste management begins with the storing of the waste at the household level (not in gutters).
In the U.K. and other European countries structures made with cement are mounted in front of houses where refuse is kept. The waste management department of the borough or council then collects and transports the refuse for final disposal every morning.
Accra is growing and AMA alone cannot manage the refuse or waste in the city. Accra generates 1,500 to 1,800 tonnes of waste daily, but AMA says it is able to collect only about 1,200 tonnes daily. The rest remains in gutters and open spaces. Accra should be partitioned into about eight or ten councils or boroughs and each should take care of its area.
District Assemblies in the Accra Metropolis are non-existent so far as waste management is concerned. They are not mentioned anywhere. It is therefore the council or "borough" arrangement that could resolve waste disposal and enforce local sanitation in the Accra metropolis.
In the U.K. the Greater London Council (GLC), which is like the Accra Metropolitan Assembly consists of London county halls, which are called boroughs. The urban centres here need further partitioning for more efficient management. The boroughs can enforce bye-laws better because they have a smaller area to manage.
LORRY PARKS.
A visit to Makola Market and Tudu Lorry Park in Accra will give a real insight of indiscipline in Accra. Traders, food vendors, hawkers, drivers and their passengers have taken over the whole area including pavements and it is impossible to walk either on them or the road.
Children selling iced water in sachets and other wares have occupied the road in front of U.T.C., the Fire Service station, Kimberly Avenue etc. and motorists find it difficult to use this road. It is chaotic.
Years ago, all these areas were clean and orderly. No one was allowed to sell on the pavements much more on the roads. Sanitary inspectors showed up all the time to stop traders from creating insanitary conditions. They also saw to it that open gutters were free from waste by cleaning them with brushes and water and that refuse collection was done every day.
The population growth seems to have overwhelmed the city of Accra and some other urban centres. The only solution will be to partition Accra.
The boroughs would ensure that services are provided to residents. They will also provide proper street numbering to identify areas.
AMA's campaign to educate traders and food vendors to sell their wares in a clean environment would not be effective because the assignment is too much for AMA alone to handle.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Foodstuffs Remain High
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Foodstuffs Remain High
Body_Text:
There has been a steady increase in the prices of foodstuffs after Sallah, Christmas and new year festivities in the Federal Capital Territory.
A market survey of the two main markets in Abuja conducted by the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) showed that prices of foodstuffs have increased, as opposed to the expectation that they would crash after the festivities.
NAN found that a "Mudu" (measure) of yellow gari which sold for N50 during the festivities last December, now costs between N55 and N60, while a bag which costs N2,500, now shows a N700 increase.
A "Mudu" of white gari which sold for between N45 and N50 last December now sells for N55, while a bag now costs N1,800 up from N1,700.
A "Mudu" of imported rice which sold for N110 during the festivities, now costs N120, while a bag, which was sold for N3,300, now costs N3,750 and N4,100, depending on the brand.
Brown beans which was sold at N80 per "Mudu" last December now costs N100, while a bag, which sold N8,300 now costs N14,000.
White beans which costs N60 per "Mudu" is now N80, while a bag, which was sold for N10,500, now costs N12,500.
A bottle of vegetable oil was sold for N100 last December, but now costs N120, while that extracted from "Kulikuli" which was sold for N110 now costsN140.
A bottle of palm-oil which was sold for N90 is now 140.
Twenty litres of vegetable oil, "Kulikuli" oil and palm oil now sell for N4,200, N5,500 and N5,600 respectively.
A large tuber of yam which was formerly sold for N70, now costs N150, a medium size tuber, which was N50, now costs N100, while the tiny ones which sold for N40 a piece, now costs N70.
The price of a kilogram of meat, however, remained unchanged at N350.
Most traders attributed the increases to the high cost of transportation and increased demand for foodstuffs since the festive period.
Consumers who spoke with NAN, however, complained about insensitivity on the part of the traders.
A civil servant, Okey Chima, said the traders ought to have known that a very large percentage of Abuja residents were civil servants who had spent their money during the festivities.
"They should have sympathy for us as we have not been paid any salaries since Christmas. Coupled with that, many of us have school fees to pay," he lamented.
Another resident, Mrs Juliana Usman, said she was optimistic that prices would fall by the time demand reduces.
"Nobody has money to throw around at this time of the year and demand is bound to fall," she said.
Visits to some other markets on the outskirts of the metropolis showed that businesses were picking up as more traders returned from the Christmas and new year vacations.

Original_Search_Title: Checking Fire Disasters in Abuja
Headline: Nigeria: Checking Fire Disasters in Abuja
Body_Text:
Last Friday at about 6:30 pm, some parts of the Wuse Old Market in Abuja went up in flames recording a second incidence of fire outbreak in a major market in less than one month.
In the last week of December last year, barely three weeks ago, the new Wuse market had its share of fire, which the market has become notorious for. According to reports from the Federal Fire Service, on the day the fire outbreak occurred at Wuse new market, all the stations of the service had gone to quench one fire outbreak or the other before the new market incident brought them together later. What this means is that about four cases of fire incidents occurred that day in the Abuja city centre.
Unlike the Wuse new market, which has had over 17 (seventeen) cases of fire outbreaks, the old market has not been known for five disasters. The only recorded of fire outbreak that occurred there before that of last Friday was around Easter period in 2001 but it was only restricted to a shop which got burnt completely.
Last Friday's fire, Abuja Trust gathered started at 6:30 pm at a gas shop and spread to over 48 shops. There are speculations in some quarters that another 40 to 45 attachment shops were also affected.
The whole stations of the fire service turned out to the venue. The zone 3 Wuse, Garki Area 10, Federal Secretariat all other stations came with their tanks except the Nyanya station which has been off for the past one year due to breakdown of their appliance, which is said to be under repair.
The fire was later brought under control after over five hours with the help of NNPC fire service. The Julius Berger (Nig) Plc refused to send their tank or mobilise their fire service because, as Abuja Trust gathered, the management had instructed that the tank should not be released.
Since December 2001, Abuja has been inundated with fire outbreaks, which the fire service put at an average of three every week.
It all started early in December 2001 when the AIT/Ray Power lost its Abuja centre to fire.
The office, which was situated at the top floor of the Labour House, was gutted by fire, which was said to have broken out as a result of fluctuation in power supply to the station.
The fire was said to have originated fromthe TV studio where the network of cables caught fire and extended to the whole of the office.
Modern broadcast equipment worth millions of naira were lost in the inferno.
In less than two weeks after the incident, the Wuse new market was gutted by fire. According to a source, the fire broke out in a boutique where a seller was trying to put on a faulty generator. The attempt to bring the generating set to life resulted in a spack, which then ignited some of the material on sale leading to spread of the fire.
The fire did not only render so many people who lived in the market homeless but their means of livelihood were completely lost to the inferno. Those who could not contain the reality of the event were said to have jumped into the fire.
Last year, fire incidents which seem to have been restricted to harmattan periods and dry season occurred several times. On the 13th of February 2002, the new building that is to serve as the headquarters of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs under construction caught fire.
The wooden scaffold and other wood works got burnt completely. But for the arrival of firemen at the scene, more harm would have been wrecked on the building.
Abuja Trust gathered that the fire was suspected to have originated from the kitchen of the laboures at the building who once in a while roast yams and eat same with red palm oil.
On 14th February 2002, one of the floors of one of the three storey buildings at plot 1954 Cotonou Crescent, Wuse zone 6, popularly called Nigerian Social Insurance Trust Fund (NSTF) Wuse estate was lost to fire.
At about 9:30 am, that fateful morning, thick smoke started coming out from one of the flats at the top floor. Then there was fire coming out of the roof, people began to gather outside especially at the express road overlooking the building. Cars passing by started parking to catch a glimpse of what the fire was all about. It took the combination of efforts of the fire service, the NNPC servicemen and those of Julius Berger to bring the fire under control.
According to sources at the fire service, fire outbreaks occur regularly in Abuja. Last year many fire outbreaks occurred but the irony about them is that people don't know about them; people only know about fire outbreaks when markets are involved. Places affected include MTN Network station Wuse II, SCC (a construction company) building in Wuse II, the homes of General T. Y. Danjuma Minister of Defence and that of Mamman Yusuf.
On March 27, 2002, the Edo State Governor's lodge was gutted by fire right in the presence of the governor, Chief Lucky Igbinedion.
There were also fire incidents at the American Embassy and the police workshop in Gaki II. Abuja Trust gathered that the police have acquired fire fighting appliances, which have not been mounted. It seems the fire service command in Abuja anticipated the current fire incidents in Abuja which may have prompted it to recruite over 100 firemen in March 2001 and sent them for training in Lagos.
The training ended in July 2001, were it not for the additional fire fighters, the story would have been different in most of the fire outbreaks in Abuja.
In this 2003, just three weeks into the year, there have been three major incidents of fire in Abuja. The first was in Kado, near the estate where a life was lost.
Abuja Trust gathered that the Kado fire incident was unfortunate as the victim was said to have been locked inside on his own request. His friend whom he visited was going out and the boy requested that he should be locked inside to avoid being disturbed since he wanted to sleep and was not planning to go anywhere. His burnt bones were gathered at the foot of the door to the bedroom. It was the first time in two years according to official reports, that somebody was to die in a fire incident in the city.
On Thursday, 16th January, a part of the Supreme Court quarters was gutted by fire only for the Wuse old market to be visited by the same fate the next day Friday 17th January 2003.
Abuja Trust gathered that the men of the fire service are always incapacitated in the event of fire outbreak because of either lack of equipment or the obsolete nature of the ones available.
"When we go to the fight fire you see onlookers applauding, "Berger", because they often think that Julius Berger fire fighters are doing better than us but in most cases we are the people who make use of the equipment" one firemen who pleaded anonymity had told Abuja Trust.
According to him the service is greatly hampered by the unavailability of fire fighting equipment: "even at that, we are often the first to arrive on any scene of fire outbreak" the fire service man said and asked government to equip the fire service with modern fire fighting equipment.
Abuja Trust investigations revealed that the four fire service stations in Abuja situated in Wuse Zone 3, Garki Area 10, Federal Secretariat and Nyanya have only 4 trucks.
Wuse Zone 2 has truck number FG 443 B20 with a capacity of about 2000 litres, which takes only about 5 to 7 minutes to discharge in the event of fire outbreak. That is why more time is lost in going back to refill them when fighting the fire.
The Garki Area 10 station which is also the headquarters has truck number FG 438 B20 with a capacity of about 1000-1500 litres, which takes less than 5 minutes to discharge in the process of fighting fire.
The Federal Secretariat has truck FG 445 B20 with the biggest capacity of 8,500 litres and takes about 30 minutes to be discharged using hose reel or 15 minutes when delivery hose is applied. In most fires the delivery hose which has greater pumping pressure and quantity of water pumped out is often used.
The Nyanya station has not been functional in the last one year but Abuja Trust gathered that repairs are being effected now on the equipment. Abuja Trust gathered that a new station would soon be opened at station house, Abuja.
In most fire incidents in Abuja, the NNPC firemen, Julius Berger and sometimes the with Airport firemen join hands to bring such fire under control. One of the NNPC fire trucks with number AH 436 BWR has about 10,000 litres capacity bigger than any of the ones possessed by the fire service. Julius Berger sometimes comes with a truck serving as reservoir to the main truck.
However, Abuja Trust gathered that despite efforts to contact Julius Berger to help in the Friday fire outbreak, the construction company refused because management had instructed otherwise. This made the situation worse especially when Garki truck was said to have had a flat tyre in their second round of going for refill (it went for refill four times). Wuse station truck was said to have ran out of diesel leaving only the NNPC truck and that of Federal Secretariat station.
Going by the quantum of losses incurred by individuals and even government to fire and sometimes to thieves during such incidents, it has become imperative for government to equip the fire service very well to enable them tackle any outbreak.

Original_Search_Title: Post Harvest Losses - a New Dimension
Headline: Ghana: Post Harvest Losses - a New Dimension
Body_Text:
Accra — The phenomenon of post harvest losses of produce following bumper harvests had an added dimension in the last farming season. It was estimated that about 30 per cent of food crops was left to go to waste at the farm gates, but this sad situation has been extended to the markets.
A Ghana News Agency (GNA) survey indicates that large quantities of plantain, banana, cassava and oranges have flooded the major food markets of Techiman, Goaso, Akrodie and Sunyani in the Brong Ahafo Region; Tepa, Mankranso, Obogu, Nintin, New Edubiase and Kumasi in Ashanti Region; Asesewa, Begro, Adawso, Kade, Asamankese and Koforidua in the Eastern Region; Mankesim, Dunkwa-On-Ofin and Cape Coast in the Central Region and Sefwi Bekwai, Tarkwa, Asakrangwa, Juabeso and Sekondi-Takoradi in the Western Region.
Most of the food crop farmers interviewed said this year's harvest has been unprecedented in recent history and attributed it to the very good rainfall last year and the extension of credit facilities to farmers to expand their farms.
Madam Akua Obiyaa, an octogenarian farmer of Buabeng in the Central Region, told the GNA that this year's harvest could be compared to those of the 1940s and 1950s when there was so much to eat that most of the food produced went to waste.
It is clear that the country was not ready for the harvest. Visits to the Agbobloshie, Dome, Mallam Atta and Nima markets in Accra showed that large quantities of plantain, banana and oranges were getting rotten.
It is this new dimension of farm produce going to waste even at Accra markets that is worrying.
This could be attributed to the fact that most Ghanaians appear to have forgotten their normal eating habits according to Madam Obiyaa. She said in the past Ghanaians ate the staples that were in season but this practice has been abandoned by many people.
She explained that plantain becomes scarce in May, June and July because of rainstorms in February and March that destroy crop.
Plantain begins to appear in August and reaches peak supply in November and December, she said, and suggested that the period should see the consumption of dishes prepared with plantain - red-red, kakro, tatale, kelewele, roasted plantain, fufu, ofam, akankyei and ampesi.
She was of the view that if these were eaten and the cravings for rice controlled, there would not have been the present waste.
Indeed cassava also has its cycle. It becomes unsuitable for fufu in February and March because it begins to regenerate after the first rains.
It is then suitable for konkonte, agbelema and gari for the months of April, May, June and July. In the forest belt cocoyam comes in to support the processed cassava dishes making it possible for fufu lovers to continue to enjoy their meal.
Fresh maize appears in late April or early May to supplement feeding - mostly in the afternoon.
By August new yam also appears to give respite so that the maize could be allowed to mature. The "sun maize" - planted in September is spared the ordeal of being consumed fresh and is allowed to mature because other staples would be in season at the time of harvest.
It would be in the collective interest of Ghanaians to adapt their eating habits to suit the staples in season to reduce the huge expenditure on food.

Original_Search_Title: Otokoto Seven: Judgment Day Beckons
Headline: Nigeria: Otokoto Seven: Judgment Day Beckons
Body_Text:
Lagos — Owerri, Imo State capital, was rocked to its foundation in 1996 when a teenager was allegedly killed for ritual purposes in what has come to be known as Otokoto episode. This week, a high court in the state capital will pass judgement on seven suspects, the last set of those indicted, writes Chuka Odittah
On Thursday, attention would be fixed on the serene town of Owerri, Capital of Imo State. That day, judgement is expected to be delivered on an eight-year old case involving seven murder suspects who were said to have played key roles in the killing of 11-year old Master Ikechukwu Okonkwo, in Owerri for ritual purposes in 1996.
The suspects in order of their appearances are: Alban Ajaegbu (first accused), Sampson Nnamito (second accused), Ebenezer Anyawu (Third accused ) and Rufus Anyanwu (Fourth accused). Others are Lawrence Eboh (fifth accused), Chief Leonard Unaogu (sixth accused), while Chief Vincent Duru (alias Otokoto) is the seventh accused person.
The seven have since 1996 been standing trial in a long and convoluted case over their roles as ascertained by police investigation in the cold blood murder of Master Okonkwo, a teenager who until his death was subventing his poor guardian's income by hawking groundnuts.
Before now, the presiding Judge, Justice Chioma Nwosu Iheme had in the last three years been battling to steer the case to a logical end. Just last November she presided over the close of defence of counsel to the accused persons who pleaded that his clients were not guilty as charged. Defence counsel to the seventh, fourth, second, and fifth accused persons, Mr. J.C. Uwajuronye pleaded with the court to discharge his clients, arguing that evidences advanced by the state counsel C. N. Akowundu, were not enough to pronounce them guilty.
However, in his plea at the close of state prayed the court not to heed the prayer of the defense counsel. He argued instead, that about 36 exhibits had been tendered before the court to prove that the accused were guilty as charged. The state counsel further argued that exhibit 30 (autopsy report issued by Dr. R. C. Nwiwu of Federal Medical Centre (FMC), Owerri in respect of master Okonkwo) and exhibits 36 (second confessional statements of late Innocent Ekeanyanwu dated 20/9/96) among 34 other evidences tendered before the court were enough reasons for the court to pronounce them guilty.
Counsel to the accused, countered the motion in the argument that such evidence alone, particularly those which cannot be verified from their sources (deceased) were insufficient and unacceptable in law to pronounce sentence on persons suspected to have carried out the act. It was on the basis of these arguments that Justice Iheme fixed final hearing for January 23.
With the pronouncement of death sentence earlier on the three policemen on August 8, 2002 by retired Chief Judge of Imo State, Justice Lawrence Alinor, it is evident that the state judiciary is bent on proving its mettle as true custodian of justice. The three officers, namely Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP), Obasi Chukwu, Sergeant Dele Baribo and Christian Nmezi, a constable, were sentenced to death by hanging by Justice Alinor, who found them guilty in court.
Alinor, after a careful review of the case which lasted several years, said he was convinced beyond doubt that the three police officers colluded to cover up facts leading to the murder of Master Okonkwo by proxy.
In his judgement, Justice Alinor said the condemned policemen were responsible for the death of late Innocent Ekeanyanwu, who was detained on September 22, 1996 for beheading the teenager at the Otokoto Hotel. Justice Alinor in his judgement, said that the police officers were guilty as charged because they attempted to cover facts surrounding the killing of the boy by eliminating Ekeanyanwu on the orders of other unidentified suspects connected with the killings. Alinor declared that based on evidence available to the court "late Ekeanyanwu was killed with dangerous weapon applied on him by the three policemen rather than food poisoning as speculated."
The presiding judge held that there was no truth in the allegation that Ekeanyanwu died of food poison after he allegedly ate a meal of pounded yam served him by prison authorities. He rather disclosed that the deceased (Ekeanyanwu) died after repeated torture by Obasi, Dele and Christian, all policemen who were supposed to protect him in custody.
The chief judge's death pronouncement on the policemen threw the court premises into commotion as wailing and weeping from members of the families of the condemned policemen rented the air. The Judge said the judgement was to serve as deterrent to others and to prove that the men had enough to work in the interest of the civil populace who they were originally hired and trained to protect. He described their conduct as a betrayal of public trust.
Deputy Superintendent Chukwu was the first investigating police officer (IPO) drafted to immediately to investigate the allegation surrounding the killing of Master Okonkwo. He was accused of recieving one million naira gratification to quash all traces, including Innocent Ekeanyanwu, which could lead to the discovery of those who requested for the head of late Master Okonkwo.
The killing of Master Okonkwo triggered a chain of violent protests in Owerri in 1996. Indigenes of Owerri, particularly the youths, women and children, took to the streets enmass to burn and destroy properties believed to belong to suspected ritualists in the town. This was the beginning of what is the Otokoto saga today.
The fearful Otokoto drama reached its peak in 1997, when about six men were publicly executed by firing squad over alleged armed robbery charges and ritual killings. Those executed were, Obichiozor Duru, Chidi Onuoha and Amanze Onuoha (brothers); Collins Ndulaka, Sydney Onyechege and Mac Donald Ebere. Former Military Governor of Imo State, Colonel Tanko Zubairu (rtd) quickly signed their death warrants in an effort to rid the state of the rising cases of suspected ritual killings.
The condemned men, who were notorious in Owerri and environs as dreadful personalities said they had no apology for their actions. They were so unrepentant that when they were being conveyed in a Black Maria to the execution site, they sang, "Today, today, tomorrow no more, if I die today, I will die no more". The execution and cummuting of Patrick Okorafor's life imprisonment to jail term, appeared to have calmed frayed nerves as well as put an end to episode of ritual killings in Owerri and environs.
Nevertheless there are still reported cases of persons found to have been murdered for ritual. Police Commissioner in the state, Mr. Benneth Oghomone, while reacting to last year's reported killing of one Miss Lynda Oguamanam in Anara, Mbano Local Government Area for ritual purposes, explained that the matter required careful investigation before any conclusion could be reached. He explained that cases of ritual murder were about the most complex to handle because of the usually hard to find convincing evidences to show that such a thing happened. He however, said that only when evidence like somebody was caught in the act exist or where well proved evidences exist would police prosecute.
With the end of prosecution of the surviving seven suspects, there is a general apprehension on what the law might say with respect to the roles alleged to have been played by the likes of Duru, Unaogu, Ajaegbu, Nnamito, Egwuekwe, Anyanwu and Eboh.
The sixth accused, Unaogu is charged with being privy to the beheading of Master Okonkwo but, he denied the allegation. He said that he was not at home for three weeks before the alleged killing took place.
"They have peddled rumours that I am linked with the human head saga even when I was never home for at least three weeks before the incident", Unogu pleaded.
Counsel to the state, however, argued that going by exhibit 2l (first confessional statement of late Innocent Ekeanyanwu dated 20/9/96) and tendered before the court, showed that Unaogu was alleged to have been at home.
The state counsel said "Refer to Exhibit 21 where Innocent Ekeanyanwu stated that it was three weeks ago from August 19, 1996, when the sixth accused who was then at his home, Eziama, Ikeduru met with Innocent Ekeanyanwu in the afternoon in the sixth accused's house and asked him to get a human head."
The seventh accused, Duru (alias Otokoto) was also charged for having first hand knowledge of the killing of Master Okonkwo. He was alleged to have ordered Nnamito (second accused) and Ajaegbu (first accused) to proceed with the beheading of Master Okonkwo, being the owner of the Otokoto Hotel where the killing took place. Akowundu said that the confessional statements of the principal culprit, late Ekeanyanwu guided the court to believe that both Ajaegbu and Nnamito connived to murder the young Ikechukwu.
According to the confessional statement of late Ekeanyanwu tagged exhibit 36: "When the boy, finished drinking the Coca-Cola he slept off. When the boy dey sleep, I go tell our director Vincent Duru that we don get one small boy he said we should look for. He then directed me to kill him as we dey kill before and cut off the head and the penis and bring to him. When I get back to my room, I told my room mate, Sampson Luamuta and Alban Ajaegbu what Chief Vincent Duru, MD of the hotel said. Then I cut one shot stick, tie one end with cloth and enter my room. As I entered my room I told Sampson Luamuta and Alban Ajaegbu to help me hold the boy's legs and hands. As the boy woke up and was about to cry. I forced that cloth end of the stick into his mouth to prevent him from shouting or crying.
"From my room, myself, Sampson and Alban Ajaegbu carried the boy inside the bush behind our room in the Hotel premises. At the bush, I brought my matchet and cut off the boy's head, after cutting the boy's penis", he said.
The fourth accused person, Anyannwu (director of Otokoto Hotel) and third accused, Egwuekwe (security supervisor of Otokoto Hotel) were also charged for murder. Egwuekwe, security personnel of the infamous hotel was charged for not upholding the rule that forbids hawkers from gaining entrance into the hotel premises. He was further charged for deliberately neglecting the rule in order to facilitate the killing of Master Okonkwo.
The fifth accused person, Eboh was charged for actively participating in the beheading of the young hawker as shown in exhibit 22, statement of Ajaegbu dated 24/9/96. As the court prepares its judgement which many anticipate would be free and fair, fears are that Owerri may never be the same again unless the long arm of the law treats fairly and appropriately with all those found to be guilty.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Trend Reverses in Third Quarter 2002
Headline: Nigeria: Inflation Trend Reverses in Third Quarter 2002
Body_Text:
Against the backdrop of declining inflationary rate in the second quarter of 2002, the composite Consumer Price Index (CPI) statistics for the month of November 2002 has indicated a resurge of inflationary pressure in the economy. The CPI was 4956.2 in November which represents an increase of 3.3 percent over 4797.6 recorded in October, 2002.This is also a reversal of the three consecutive months trend ended October.
The rise in the index, according to the Federal Office of Statistics (FOS), resulted from increases in the prices of food items, kerosine, appliances, vehicle maintenance, medicaments and other services. The urban and rural indices rose by 0.7 and 3.8 percent respectively.
FOS report indicated that in the 12 months to November, 2002, the average consumer price level went up by 12.2 percent. This was substantially higher than 5.4 percent recorded in the month of October 2002. The urban and rural indices showed increase of 10.2 and 12.6 percent accordingly over the same period.
The percentage change in the average composite CPI for the12 months period ending November 2002 over the average of the CPI for the previous 12 months period was 13.2.
This means that a fixed market of consumer goods and services purchased with N100.00 during the 12 months period ending November, 2001 sold for N113.2 on the average for the corresponding period ending November 2002. It also indicated a slight decline when compared to 13.6 percent obtained by making a similar comparison in October 2002.
From the foregoing, the Composite Food Index (CFI) rose by 2.1 percent in November 2002. The increase in the index was largely as a result of increases in the prices of yam tuber, plaitain,rice, oil, vegetables, fruits and sweet potato.
The level of the CFI was higher than the corresponding level a year ago by 7.3 percent. The average annual rate of rise of the index was put at 14.6 percent for the 12 months period ending November 2002.
All items less farm produce index which exclude the prices of agricultural products rose by 4.8 percent in November, 2002.
In the 12 months to November 2002, the index rose by 19.7 percent and the average annual rate of rise of the index was 10.7 percent for the 12 months period ending November 2002.

Original_Search_Title: Towards Sustained Food Security
Headline: Ghana: Towards Sustained Food Security
Body_Text:
A Combination of Favourable Weather, Sound Agricultural Policies And the
Hard Work of the Ghanaian Farmer, the Country is Enjoying a Relative Bumper
Harvest Unprecedented in About a Decade. Plantains, Cassava, Yams, Maize And
Rice Are in Abundance And Their Prices Are Fairly Affordable to Employees
And Other Lower Classes of Our Society.
This paper deems it fair to say kudos to the Kufuor administration and,
in particular, the sector minister, Major (rtd.) Courage Quashigah. We doff
our hats to you for a good job done so far.
Having said that, we wish to draw attention to the fact that the
Ignatius Kutu Acheampong government and a few others that have ruled this
country started well by engendering the production of plenty food. But as
the problems of governance grew more complex, they lost control and the
effective management of the agricultural sector was sacrificed, just as it
happened to several other sectors.
It is also pertinent to note that favourable weather comes but once a
while, at times once a decade. If the rainfall distribution last year was
ideal for food production, this or next year could be bad What contingency
plans are we making against a rainy day or rather a year of drought?
The Chronicle wishes to suggest that the Agriculture ministry ensures
that the food stuffs we cannot eat now are not left in the fields or markets
to rot, but they are put in cans, sacks and cribs against a famine
season. We are not unaware of the ministry's attempts to encourage business
people to buy and store maize in particular, but what we are saying is that
the policy must not only be made to succeed but it must be expanded to cover
the tubers, plantains, and other cereals than maize.
In view of the obvious need to avail the farmer competitive prices in
such bumper harvests as we are currently experiencing if they are to remain
encouraged to produce more, the paper urges the ministry to take the matter
into very serious consideration. If the economy cannot support reverting to
the days when agricultural production was subsidized by the state, at least
some measures must be implemented to ensure that the farmer does not suffer
as a result of serious price falls.
While it is considered necessary that the increases in fuel prices which
hangs around the neck of the nation has to be implemented, it is also deemed
essential that the effect it will have on the margins of farmers and
middlemen will be some what taken care of by the authorities.
For a genuine self-reliance in food production, it is also desirable
that the government will de-emphasise the importation of rice, sugar and
other items which can be grown locally. Already the opposition has
criticized the government a lot on this, and it is hoped that the ministry
will take heed and put in place all what it takes to provide lasting food
security.

Original_Search_Title: Woman, 40, Raped
Headline: Ghana: Woman, 40, Raped
Body_Text:
Agogo — A 70 year old farmer from Kwame Addo in the Asante Akim North section of the Afram Plains had the unforgettable shock of his life when his 40 year old wife was brutally raped by a Fulani herdsman in front of him and their little child.
The Assembly member for Kansanso in the Afram Plains, Opanin Kwadwo Oduro, who briefed the Member of Parliament for Asante Akim North, Mr. Kwadwo Baah-Wiredu, last Friday during the latter's tour of the area, said the incident happened a month ago.
According to him, the victims were returning to Kwame Danso from a visit to Dawiaso, when the unidentified Fulani man ambushed them, and at gun-point, ordered the woman to un-strap their little child from her back following which he forcibly raped her in their presence.
Consequently, the farming communities in the area have appealed to the government to expel the Fulani herdsmen without delay to avert any further humiliation and loss of lives.
Oduro and the chiefs who were obviously terrified at the herdsmen, said the latter have shot at others and burnt their fields to allow green grasses to sprout up to feed their cattle.
The MP, in responding, said since the advent of the NPP into office, the Asante Akim North District Assembly has spent Â¢80 million through the services of the security agencies to deal with the situation, which succeeded in flushing them out.
He disclosed that his ministry's concern is to equip rural areas with telephones and good roads. To this end, he and that of communications and the mobile service providers, would work to see how best district assemblies could provide rechargeable handsets to be used as communication centres, depending on network coverage.
Baah-Wiredu, who is also the Minister of Local Government and Rural Development, cited locations like Kansanso in the Asante Akim section of the Afram Plains and Kwasidoi in Akwapim South where there are some available networks which can be exploited in emergency situations if equipped.
H advocated that rural communities should contribute tokens of their produce like maize, beans, groundnuts or yam to be sold by the district assemblies to generate funds to complement government's efforts in developing their communities.
He said their first Aid Kits could be stocked and replenished from that, and requested each community delegate two young females to enable the assembly sponsor their training at Agogo Hospital as First Aiders who can administer primary drugs.
This measure, according to him, will replace the property rates at rural communities which may find it more convenient I contributing five tubers of yam than parting with Â¢1,000.
On the roads, Baah-Wiredu hinted that a team from Switzerland is due in the country on February 17, which will test rural roads with CONSOLID, a form of chemical.
Another team from South Africa which specialises in converting used tyres into bitumen suitable for rural roads is also expected soon.
The Agogo Area Police Commander ASP Yorkyire, advised the people to report those cultivating Indian Hemp (wee) to the police. He urged those involved to desist from it or face the full rigours of the law if caught.
The warm reception accorded Baah-Wiredu and his entourage which included the DCE at Kansanso, was a paradox in that the NPP campaign team was given a hostile and serer physical beating in the 2000 elections which culminated in injuries and vandalising of vehicles.
As a reciprocal gesture to the visit and words of reconciliation from the minister and DCE, Mr. George Frimpong, the chief and people installed the latter as the Nkosuohene (Development Chief) under the stool name, Togbe Ngoefie.

Original_Search_Title: Emphasise health, safety isuues, unionists tell NLC
Headline: Nigeria: Emphasise health, safety isuues, unionists tell NLC
Body_Text:
Count — We continue our serial countdown to Abuja 2003 with an interview on
the assessment of the leadership of the NLC, its failures and other
expectations of workers.
President of National Union of Chemical, Footwear, Rubber, Leather and
Non-Metallic Products Employees [NUCFRLANMPE], Comrade Moses Gbadebo: How would you assess the leadership of the NLC from 1999 to date?
Well, in reality, not only Nigerian workers, but every right thinking
Nigerian knows that the only functional organisation we have in Nigeria for
now is the Nigeria Labour Congress [NLC]. Looking back and appraising the
events of the past four years, one would surely score the incumbent
executive very high. They have delivered not only on the issue of workers'
welfare in the area of minimum wage, they have also ensured that Nigerian
government is not going out of its way to milk the Nigerian masses to
death. We are all witnesses to the issue of the attempted increase in prices
of petroleum products. But for NLC intervention, by now, we should have been
buying a litre of petrol for N100.00. The quick and swift intervention of
NLC on the matter saved the day for Nigerians. Two times, the NLC led
Nigerians to fight government to a standstill. We made the opionion of the
people known to the whole world that we cannot have something in our domain
and still pay international prices for it. By my own, and my union's
assessment, and I am sure by the assessment of all Nigerians, the leadership
of NLC has performed creditably well.
Even with all these, which areas do you think needed improvement?
It is only God that is perfect. As such, there are areas that exigencies
of the time have brought into focus and we hope and believe the leadership
of the NLC will look into. Yes, it is good to have a reasonable wage, it is
good to have conditions of service that are fantastic, but the incident of
the Ikorodu fire has taught us a big lesson that not only Naira and Kobo
that is important. There are other things like health and safety which are
very important because, it is those who live that can spend good pay that
they earn. So, we hope the next dispensation/regime would have a standing
committee on safety and health that could work along with the Ministry of
Employment, Labour and Productivity, which is hardly functional in this area
to ensure that factories inspection is done periodically and
effectively.
Again in the area of social life, NLC should deepen its collaboration
with other civil society groups. I believe it is very important for labour
to carry all strata of the society along in its campaigns especially, on
issues that affect all Nigerians. We can only succeed in doing this when we
have other civil society groups like pro-democracy groups, human rights
activists, students union and the like, including our colleagues in the
senior staff associations. If we come under one umbrella, we can effectively
checkmate any anti-people policy of government.
Comrade Jeje Adeajo, General-Secretary, National Union of Postal and
Telecommunications Employees [NUPTE]:
Achievements- "We think they have done their best. After Comrade Hassan
Sumonu, we have not gotten another versatile president like Sumonu. In my
own opinion, it might be because of circumstances, it even looks as if Adams
has performed more to our expectation than Sumonu. Though, Sumonu tried his
best, and what might be responsible for that is that, Sumonu had 42
industrial unions and later 41to deal with, whereas Adams has 29 industrial
unions. But this leadership under Adam has done its best. They have not
disappointed me and they have not disappointed the National Union of Postal
and Telecommunications Employees [NUPTE].
What should normally happen is that there should be cohesion within
them. The in-coming executive should ensure that they don't work at cross
purposes. Some will not speak up when they have grievances. This we would
like corrected. People should always speak up. Adams is somebody you can
easily reach and talk to. Since I know he would come back as the president
of NLC for the second time, I expect that there should be unity and people,
in the executive should always trust one another
Intra-Union Dispute - "The point is, how many unions have problems? They
are few. The question you should ask yourself is: why is that it is the same
unions that continue to have problems? Something is wrong with those
unions. The set up or organisational structure is faulty. It is those unions
that should sit down and see how they can solve their problems and work like
other unions who don't have problem in their unions. Out of 29 industrial
unions, how many unions have problems? They are about two or three. So, it
means something is wrong with those unions themselves.
Privatisation:"In that area, we have our misgivings but, what we see is
that, if we don't have representatives in the higher echelon of the
privatisation committees and so forth, we will not even know what is going
on. They will be forcing issues on us. But, since we have Adams there, at
least, there are times we would say this is not acceptable to the congress
and things like that. What we need is that, if any organisation is slated
for privatisation, anybody representing NLC at the top echelon should always
get in touch with the unions involved so that they don't work at
cross-purposes. That is our opinion.
Areas of Improvement -They should improve on the very remarkble
achievement of this executive. Though, we have not gone to the conference,
but some of them or majority of them who are in the present executive are
likely coming back, it might not be the position they are holding but they
are likely coming back as National Administrative Council [NAC] of
Congress. So, they must improve on this present achievement.
They should avoid rancour, since we preach to politicians too that they
should not regard election as a do or die affair, I will advise that we too
should not look at election as do or die affair. Without holding office, one
can still be useful to NLC and workers. I don't hold any position in the
NLC, but I believe that I have always tried not to obstruct what they are
doing. I see them as good and capable people. I don't go out to criticise
them unnecessarily. That is what all of us should be doing, once we do that,
there will be unity among all of us. It will make the NLC better for workers
and Nigerians in general.
Dr. Deji Akinwalere, President of National Union of Local Government
Employees [NULGE]:
I think we should thank God that we have the new improved NLC that we
have now during this democratic dispensation. The NLC has come out as the
voice of the people on serious issues that affect the welfare of the masses
like increase in fuel prices, wage matter, etc., and has been able to do
this successfully. You can see from every indication that workers are having
a better deal during this period. The people too are really benefitting from
the services of the NLC. We have a government that could have become a
monster doing things without consultation, without any hinderance or
restraint in imposing policies that would aggravate the social, economic and
even political crisis in the country if the present leadership of the NLC
had not been there.
The leadership of the NLC has made the NLC to come in as the only viable
opposition. NLC has been forthcoming in bringing into the front burner
serious economic and political issues for discourse. It has been able to
engage both the National Assembly and presidency in some of these issues and
I think at the end of the day, the Nigerian masses are the better for
it.
Areas of Improvement - "Maybe in the forthcoming year, NLC can still
improve on all it has done in the past three years, especially, the economic
issues. This can be taken deeply. The welfare of workers can still be
improved upon because inflation is on the rise daily and we have to ensure
that impediments that are put in some of the agreements on wage are
removed. You can see that this government is not keen on the welfare of
workers. I think the major challenge now is the 12.5 per cent increase which
this government in its typical double-speak is saying that they have agreed
and they have not agreed to implement. I think this is the major challenge
that NLC will have to face in the new year.
But giving labour antecedents, I think it is a battle we are prepared to
fight and Nigerian workers are ready to support the leadership of the NLC to
make sure that we are given our dues in the new year.
I think there is one area we should commend the leadership of the NLC
too. That is, the registration of a political party, the Party for Social
Democracy [PSD]. NLC has been able to prove a point in conjunction with some
civil society groups and other groups that it is not the business of
government to register parties. That is, as soon as people express their
intention, we should create a level ground for everybody to operate. It is
the people that will decide which party they would vote for and the one that
can survive, not Mr. President or Mr. INEC. This is a major historical
landmark that for the first time, we are having a party that can be called
baby of the NLC. This is a sign of greater things to come because, we are in
an era where labour cannot really remain neutral. We have to identify with
the political fortune of this country. We have to identify with the
political trend in the nation. That is a commendable achievement that we
must acknowledge.
Joeseph Akinlaja, General-Secretary, National Union of Petroleum and
Natural Gas Workers [NUPENG]:
I give them pass mark compared to previous leadership in the area of
decision based on collective leadership and in the execution of decisions as
well as being courageous and being forthright. The last four years have been
eventful. In summary, I score them high mark.
In the areas of wage increase, in the struggle against casualisation and
above all, in moderating the prices of fuel. Those three areas are very
critical to be rolled into one piece within the period. It is
commendable.
Areas of improvement - Yes, in the area of politics. The leadership did
not give sufficient attention to workers involvement in politics. The way
they mobilised workers for the increase in wages and remuneration of
workers, the way they mobilised against casualisation and the way they
mobilised against fuel prices increases. They did not mobilise, they did not
sensitise Nigerian workers to enable them know their own innate ability to
get involved in political participation.
In-coming executive -Well, it will be a very useful experience. It is
going to be a challenge because this leadership performed creditably well
and everybody will like to see us to continue with the same tempo and even
improve upon what they have achieved. With the carlibre of people whom I
know filed in their papers for positions in the congress, I have no doubt in
my mind that we will improve on the performance of the present NLC
leadership.
Continuation of the struggle against casualisation and then, the
sensitising of Nigerian workers and indeed, labour movement, Nigerian masses
and the progressives towards politics in order to reduce the level of
commercial stealing that is going on in the nation's politics and the
monetisation of politics. That will be my preferred areas of
activities.
We will make presentation to government in the area of non-monetary
benefit to workers in order to make meaningful the wages and salaries of
workers. Previous governments have paid lip services to agriculture and we
will like to pressurise government and its policy makers to focus attention
on food. When we were young, we selected which food to eat among plenty. We
may decide not to eat cocoyam but yam or dodo [fried plantain] not to eat
just plantain. But plantain is almost out of the menu of the ordinary
person. To eat dodo is a problem. These are not foreign exchange related
matters. Nigeria is blessed with fertile land and we have also up to three
cropping seasons in most part of Nigeria. Yet, we still have the propensity
of importing rice, processed foods and the likes. A government should know
that the aged ones in the rural areas and in the farm are getting older and
they are dying away through natural attrition. The young ones are getting
more and more educated and they are moving to the cities in search of white
collar jobs. Therefore, there is a vaccum in the rural areas. A focused
government will begin to think and work on mechanised agriculture to cover
all the shortfalls between the production of the peasants that are aging and
are dying away and the people who are unable to come to town and cities as
well as those already in the town looking for jobs. This has to be drummed
into the ears of government and its agents.

Original_Search_Title: Alliance for Democracy in Delta Stands for Change, Says Oganah
Headline: Nigeria: Alliance for Democracy in Delta Stands for Change, Says Oganah
Body_Text:
Prince Tonnie Ositadinma Oganah, (Barrister-at-law), steps through the opened glass-door, into the overtly-lit newsroom of Vanguard, with the mien of a man braced up to rule the world. When he later divulges news of his plans to run as Chairman for the Aniocha North Local government area, under the flagship of the Alliance for Democracy (AD), one wonders if such lowly political office wasn't beneath a man of such over-reaching mental capability, an elevated educational base and a decorous character.
As it turns out however, Tonnie Oganah seems to understand the consequence of the words in the Holy Book when it says, "...never despise a humble beginning." The fact that he is coming in under the flagship of AD in a geopolitical zone that enjoys an overwhelming support for the PDP, leaves him undaunted.
The first question fielded to the pleasant-looking, fast talking ex-TV presenter as we settled down to the interview was: Why grass-root politics? What's the dream? He takes in a deep breathe before unleashing the words like the trained lawyer who has taken up the challenge of being his own advocate:
"For years, specifically after I left UNIBEN and was called to the bar, 1989, I have been in private, legal, consultancy practice. I have also done some media work, writing articles for publication and anchored a TV programme, somehow, as a way to crusade for good governance and still, I don't feel as if I have made enough impact on the society.
Yes, I have gotten a few awards, and maybe I am probably luckier than some of my peers. But beyond that, there comes to a stage in a man's life where you must crave bigger challenges. But again in going after bigger challenges, we think small and hence, we are starting smaller as Chairman of Aniocha North council in Delta state, by God's grace and hopefully make a good shape of things there and go on further. So, for me its about crusading for social justice, it's about positive change, it's about progress."
But isn't this just the same sing-song the people have heard from other politicians in time past, who leave them sparsely better of than they were at the end?
There is a mistake people keep making. First, I think it is important to probe into the past of any individual going for public office because I believe you can only give what you have. For example if all my life, I have been a nonentity who now comes out to promise heaven and earth, you would know that I am just fooling myself. But if all my life, I have tried to showcase an example of what the Nigerian youth should be, and based on the endowment God has placed in me, I tried to make a difference in my immediate environment, if I over the years I have made friends who are accomplished in their own rights and on whom I can call upon anytime and would listen to me, and I come from a family background that you would not right off as a useless family, then you can be sure that I would deliver.
Our politics is like this today because people hardly bother to scrutinize people coming out for political offices. You can only give what you have! People must be ready to ask the right questions at all times. Ask what people do for a living, check out their live-styles, what they do in private, then you would be able to separate the wheat from the chaff. And I daresay, I am the wheat!
If the fact of background is anything to go by, what do we say about those who based on their antecedents, get the support of the people but later become indulgent with greater position, responsibilities and power?
Many are sons and daughter of great men and women that ever lived in this part of the globe. Children of people who have left indelible footprints on the sands of time. When you remove the surname of such people, what do they have left? Those are hardly the kind of people I am talking about. If a man said he went to school, then you should be able to ask such questions like who were his mates in school, what was he known for on campus and in his private life? How does he relate to his wife and family, if any? How does he relate to his environment? What values does he hold there. If he says he is a Christian, does he go to church regularly, does he try within the realm of human ability to follow the dictates of Christ? His he a flight-by-night character? What is his reason for being in public office is it for the public good? I am sure if we ask ourselves, people can provide adequate answers. People know where other individuals come from; we all have origins. Yes, if you are from the south-west people from my village might not know you but people from your village know about you. People from my village know about me, my family and lineage.
As it is now, we are up against the incumbent. I am an AD party member and AD is in the opposition in Delta state. I am not going to come like that over-night, no. I hope to displace them by my pedigree. I hope to displace them by my programmes. I tell you what, during the primaries that I won overwhelmingly, I published my CV. I listed my clients. My source of income is known - these are things you can verify. That compelled other contestants to do the same. Like the Yorubas would say, amo egbe arawa (we know those in our ranks)! So you know your level. There is one thing you need to understand though, its about the fear of God. If you have the fear of God, you would not kill or steal.. The salary of local government chairman is about N350.000 (three hundred and fifty thousand naira). That's okay for anybody whose taste are moderate.
