And you would not need to steal money to make ends meet. People come to visit me in the village, they bring yams, chickens ,goats and stuffs like that, not to talk of as a man in office. They pray for you everyday and I think that is important. It can't all be about amassing wealth. It is about integrity, it is about service.
Why AD for a state like Delta?
The last time people were talking about parties. Now the story is different, it is now about the personality - who is involved? And don't you forget there are over twenty political parties in Nigeria as of today and AD remains head and shoulder, the most credible. Even when we disagree, we don't kill each other. We go to court, we are civilised people. And that is the legacy laid down by chief Obafemi Awolowo, for me the greatest Nigerian that ever lived. The most politically informed and educated. It is not as if the guys from the south-west have more testes or that the ladies have extra pair of boobs. So, my dream is to Afeniferize Anioma politics. To make people so aware that it would be difficult if not impossible to bribe them to sell their votes. The Yoruba land is that part of Nigeria, where money plays the most minimal role. If they say this person is going in, then that person gets the ticket. Unlike other parts of Nigeria. In the Ibo land for instance, the person with the biggest amount of money wins. But in Yoruba land, if they say you are not going, forget it, but if they say you are going, even if you don't have money, they would rally round just so that you can get there. That is creating awareness. That is what I call cultural and political sophistication, I would like my people to be like that. I would like to give my people free medical care and free education, integrated rural development, empowerment for the people, as part of my programmes in AD. And the only way to jump start education for me is to do what my parents did for me when I was young and that is to give the children free extra lessons. You discover that when this children close at two o'clock, they do not get to open their books again until the next day. If they make chairman, I am going to keep them in school till five o'clock.
And how do you plan to achieve this?
At that age, of course, they burn up a lot of energy, they would get hungry. I would figure a way to feed them. I would not need to give them MR. Biggs, no, I would give them basic meals and a town in my area, we call it Obvior, produces the best rice in Nigeria. At that age, they need a lot of protein, we have fish in the area and I would not need to import jack! I am keeping the money within the system and by doing that I would have created an avenue for more job-opportunities. People would have to come and teach them. If the regular teachers are interested in teaching them, they get an extra pay for that, but if not, we look for people who have done well in school to come and teach on part time bases. If you do that, standard of education would improve. I can't do much to help the older generations that are over sixty, seventy. They are gone. But we can affect my generation and the next coming after us. On campus was involved in the publication of the best magazine called "Campus Trends ", so I know how students live. And we know that to get students out of hardship and frustration, we have decided to pay local government subventions. And we figure that even though the local government is poor, we figure we can pay each person, N25,000 (twenty-five thousand naira) every year and believe me we can do it. We are going to have a pilot scheme for a hundred student per town a year, and that translates to N25 million per year, about the amount we would be making in a month. So, these are the things I plan to do to enhance education because for me, it is the only legacy you can give.
But again we are going to do borehole and grade roads, we cannot tar the roads because of the enormous money involved, but it would be good enough to make it motor-able to help farmers take their goods to the market. And then again, I am enhancing communication, that's why you have here, commercial satellite phones. MTN has helped me and now more than 50% of Aniocha-north have phones. For those who can't afford it, we would figure a way to have commercial phones where people can pay a minimal amount to reach important places they want to call. The idea is to bridge communication gap. Because hitherto, people pay as much as N500 to write letters to take to their siblings in Lagos and Abuja, and that's not good enough.
What if budget allocation is not big enough to help you to fulfil the dreams?
I have done my homework. The last time, the highest the local government got in a month was N35 million, while the lowest was about N11 million and the average come to about N25 million per month. Most of my programmes, the bursary, the teachers and the extra lessons would be taking much money but since we are paying the teachers from the local government, we are keeping the money within the system.

Original_Search_Title: Ashanti File
Headline: Ghana: Ashanti File
Body_Text:
To say that Mr. Kweku Owusu, proprietor of Kweku Owusu Enterprise Limited,(Tema and Kumasi) is a patriot is an understatement.
In actual fact, he is a patriot par excellence who we must get to know, respect and admire for his belief that well placed individuals in society should be sensitive to other people's problems.
By his deeds and belief, which is the basis of his success in life, he has won for himself recognition in society. He is more renowned in developmental issues.
The 39-year old businessman is a real patriot which patriotism is based on his rich experience from his sojourn to seek fortune in Britain, Holland, Belgium, Germany, Italyand USA over some years. He left the shores of Ghana at the age of 16.
Back in Ghana in 1992 he started with the importation of cars. He made a good gesture of donating a Mercedez benz car to the late Dr. Hilla Limann in 1996 but this made the government of the day uncomfortable sending wrong signals to it (government). The result was that Owusu was robbed of four cars in a matter of days at the alleged instance of the powers that be shortly after the donation.
Coupled with the frustrations by activities of CEPS then, he lost interest in the car business and left it eventually to venture into Cold Store operations.
He started with the distribution of frozen turkey and chicken parts and imported sea food while serving as commercial farmers and environmental engineers as well.
Kweku Owusu Enterprise Ltd. is also into the distribution of cereals like rice, maize and wheat. The company is also into the exportation of sawn timber and timber products.
Managing Director Owusu is the only Ghanaian member of the 3000 membership International Aid and Trade Organization (IATO).
One may ask: What makes Kweku Owusu a patriot and so different from all other businessmen?
He is an agent of change and development. Indeed Owusu is deeply committed to wealth creation and, therefore, is the very embodiment of the NPP government s positive change policy.
To sustain the government's policy of Golden Age of Business, Owusu has suggested that Parliament, and for that matter, government should consider the establishment of a Commission to embark on the training of personnel to monitor business concerns (both public and private) and offer expert technical advices on management towards a sustained growth in the economy.
He noted that most commercial outfits had collapsed because the proprietors lacked the requisite managerial skills to sustain the businesses for the benefit of the nation as a whole. "Entrepreneurs should be equipped with managerial skills to sustain productivity and economic growth," he advised.
Towards this envisaged growth in economy Owusu, in his own small way, has already initiated a special poverty reduction package in Kumasi for which about 1,200 people, mainly women traders of all categories have benefited. He has personally guaranteed loans to be advanced to a group of 200 traders in Kumasi.
About 380 women also enjoyed his benevolence last November by opening job avenues to enable them earn decent living.
According to Owusu, he is negotiating with the Ministiy of Women and Children's Affairs for the disbursement of Â¢1.6 billion through the First Allied Loans and Savings bank in Kumasi to selected women traders in the Kumasi metropolis.
He also intends to establish a Tomato processing plant in Kumasi to absorb a workforce of 150 which project a three acre piece of land has already been acquired.
The installation of the $800,000 plant is to take off this year.
As a commercial firmer, Owusu has acquired 10 tractors for the cultivation yams in the Afram Plains this year all in the name of creating jobs and wealth for the unemployed youth. Negotiations for land acquisition for this project is underway.
The patriot he is, Owusu has criticized the release of funds by the AIDS Commission to NGOs who organise supposed anti-AIDS educational campaigns once or twice in a year and think they are combating AIDS. This approach at fighting AIDS is not enough neither is it helping to reduce the menace he noted saying "the most effective means of fighting AIDS is to create jobs to divert people's attention from promiscuity."
According to Owusu the greater the reliability and dependence on condoms the greater the promiscuity among the youth.
Owusu observed that most of the funds provided by the AIDS Commssion to these NGO's to fight AIDS end up in funding the establishment of private businesses which become defunct in a matter of days for selfish motives and do not benefit the society in any way.
As a devoted lay Prison Minister, Owusu has injected several millions of cedis out of his own resources into the rehabilitation of inmates of the James Town, Nsawam, Koforidua, Akuse, Ho and Kpando prisons.
He has suggested the establishment of an open prison where all defaulters of fines could go (and not necessarily mingle with hardened criminals in the prisons). T
his category of prison inmates could be made to serve public institutions to defray such fines imposed on them while the state benefits from their services at the same time.
He believes this measure could save the nation millions of cedis that might have gone into their maintenance in the prisons.
That is Kweku Owusu, a native of Offinso-Denten in the Ashanti region for you, the man whose major frustration in life is to see fellow human beings wallow in abject poverty.
His simply message, therefore, is that all and sundiy,( from the highest government official to the lowest peasant farmer and no matter ones economic standing) should be his brother's keeper for the good of mankind." We are all part of the solution to the problem of poverty, squalor and disease.
The time to stop these is now. Let's make the effort and success will crown our efforts and make the world a better place to live in" he advised.

Original_Search_Title: Non-Oil GDP Up 7 %, By Kpakol
Headline: Nigeria: Non-Oil GDP Up 7 %, By Kpakol
Body_Text:
Abuja — CHIEF Economic Adviser to the President, Dr. Magnus Kpakol, yesterday, took a retrospective view of year 2002 and gave a pass mark to the nation's economy, saying the non-oil sector real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) recorded a 7 % growth rate. Dr. Kpakol at a press briefing in Abuja, pointed out that in spite of the difficult world economic outlook last year, which witnessed great losses, "the Nigerian economy remained resilient," and as such, the success story of the Nigerian economy must be told to attract foreign investors to Nigeria. "World stock markets suffered a third consecutive year of losses in 2002, dropping about 20 per cent, the worst annual performance since 1994," he said, explaining that with stock markets in America, the United Kingdom and Germany recording losses of between 17 and 43 per cent last year, "investors will be searching for better opportunities in 2003. We want Nigeria to be a visible destination for a significant amount of these flows of investment."
The Presidential Economic Adviser expressed satisfaction that the industrial sector also recorded about 60 per cent capacity utilisation over the 2002 fiscal year and that with improved infrastructure expected in the new year, the sector would record even greater heights. Dr. Kpakol was optimistic that with more Independent Power Producers (IPPs) going into operation, the industrial sector would be in a greater position to leap much higher in the new year than it recorded in the preceding year.
He pointed out that although the oil sector witnessed a decline in the year under review, owning to the cut in OPEC quota, the nation's economy got a boost from the non-oil sector where, the adviser said the nation has great potential. According to him, the average inflation performed much better than was even anticipated at the beginning of 2002 when a 4 % target was set for the year. Inflation rate stood at about 13 per cent. Year 2002 started with an inflation rate of about 18.9 per cent. Dr. Kpakol attributed the falling rate of inflation to the bumper harvest from last year's farming activities, which has made falling prices of food items, particularly, yams and grains possible. His words: "In general, the deceleration in inflation was led by sharp declines in food prices, in particular, yam tubers, plantains, beans, and sweet potatoes, as good rainfall and supportive government activities resulted in bumper harvest in 2002. Government incentives in terms of fertiliser support, access to loans, and improving infrastructure are also helping to assure food price stability."
On the foreign reserve, Dr. Kpakol said gross level stood at $8.1 billion at the end of December 2002. He attributed it to what he referred to as "the success of the Dutch Auction System (DAS)," introduced by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) in July 2002 to arrest the volatility of the foreign exchange market. "The gross foreign reserve level at year end topped $8.1 billion, with net well over $7.7 billion. Given the success of the DAS and the stability in the foreign exchange market, we expect our foreign reserve position to continue to be strong in the coming period," he said.
The Chief Economic Adviser said government was very proud of the way the CBN has been handling the nation's monetary policy. Dr. Kpakol who commended efforts of some Nigerian private sector operators urged all well-meaning Nigerians both at home and in the diaspora to invest in the nation's economy as a way of improving the lot of all Nigerians and as road map for foreign investors to be attracted to the Nigerian economy. "We all must have faith in this collective project, Nigeria. Unless we have the right environment and invest ourselves, the highly expected foreign direct investment inflows would not happen."
The Federal Government, he said, was consulting with Nigerians in the diaspora with a view to persuading them to return home and invest in the already liberalised economy. The adviser expressed optimism that the 2003 economic outlook make life better for more Nigerians than ever before, particularly, given the vigour with which the economy was being expanded beyond the oil sector.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Pushes for Debate
Headline: Ghana: Minister Pushes for Debate
Body_Text:
...over constitutional clause barring gov't interference in chieftaincy matters
The Western Regional Minister, Hon. Joseph Boahen Aidoo, has called for a debate and possible review of the portion of the 1992 constitution that bars the government and its officials from meddling in chieftaincy matters in the country.
He said this particular clause was inserted into the 1969 constitution by its framers because of the autocratic nature of then CPP government when the president or any of his officials sitting in Accra could just get up to say that a particular chief has been destooled.
According to him, currently no sitting president or his minister under the fourth republican constitution can just get up to say that a particular chief has been destooled and therefore there is no need for such a clause to remain in our constitution for some of the chiefs to exploit to their advantage.
Speaking to the Western press corps at the end of the year party he organized for them at his official residence in Sekondi last December, Hon.
Aidoo said his decision to call for the review of that particular clause in the constitution stemmed from the fact that some of the chiefs are using it to prevent government officials from intervening in chieftaincy disputes that threaten life and property in their traditional areas.
Hon. Aidoo, who is also the Member of Parliament for Amenfi East, said the chieftaincy institution has its own rules and regulations which must be followed by chiefs and their subjects, but some of them sometimes either deliberately or not do flout these rules and regulations resulting in misunderstanding which usually leads to disputes in their traditional areas.
He regretted that anytime chieftaincy matters would raise their head in some of these traditional areas and a government official responsible for the well being of the people tried to come out with measures to ensure peace and unity, he is immediately accused of meddling in chieftaincy matters.
"When some of these chieftaincy disputes start occurring in areas under your jurisdiction you cannot sit down for people to lose their lives. Definitely you have to go in but this laudable idea is misinterpreted to mean that you are meddling in chieftaincy matters," he told the journalists whose questions prompted him to make the comment.
Hon. Aidoo, who is seeking re-election to represent his people in parliament, made a special reference to Bamiako, a town in the Nzema East district of the Western Region, where one person was shot dead and several others injured in a chieftaincy dispute that rocked the town in December, last year.
According to the regional minister, when Awulae Angama Tua-Gyan, the omanhene of the Bamiako traditional area, announced the date for celebration of his annual yam festival, he received intelligence report from the security men on the ground that the festival was allowed to come on as planned there would be fatalities.
The regional minister further told the reporters, who kept on asking more questions on the seemingly unending chieftaincy disputes in the region, that after he had received the intelligence report he also informed the Nzema East security council and asked them to take up the matter.
He said the district Security Council also met all the feuding parties, including the Omanhene, Awulae Angama Tua-Gyan, on how to ensure an incident free festival.
As part of the security measures, the Omanhene was also asked not to ride in a palanquin during the day of the festival, which he agreed.
Hon. Aidoo however regretted that having agreed earlier not to ride in a palanquin as advised by the security men, the Omanhene reneged on his promise and allowed himself to be carried in a palanquin during the day of the festival and this resulted in the violence that occurred that day, which also led to the shooting to death of one person and injuries to several others.
The minister admitted that the Western Region had been bedevilled with a number of chieftaincy disputes but said if what the chiefs themselves have told him is anything to go by then the disputes may soon be over.
"In fact, I have met the chiefs and they themselves have assured me that the problem will be brought under control," he told the reporters, who were given a gastronomic treat by his outfit.

Original_Search_Title: Okeke: Mysterious Birth, Harvest of Miracles
Headline: Nigeria: Okeke: Mysterious Birth, Harvest of Miracles
Body_Text:
Lagos — When the history of the Global Success International Fellow-ship for all in Christ Church is chronicled, the name of the founder, Brother Chima Emmanuel Okereke, would feature prominently in so many ways. Founded in April 4, 1998, the church is located at the Eleganza Plaza Suru/Alaba Bus-stop, along Mile 2/Orile Road, Badagry Expressway, Lagos.
Okereke did not only found the fellowship, but nurtured it to a high spiritual pedestal. His spiritual activities have always remained symbiotic. A peep into available records of the evangelist who would not want to be addressed as pastor but simply as 'Brother', shows a true man whose life has been a study in miracles and divine calling.
The fellowship, which started from a two-room apartment could now lay claim to having over 5,000 worshippers when in full service. It has continued to grow stronger on a daily basis. Going by the public perception, it has turned out to be a centre of attraction where ailments and various degrees of problems have solutions proffered. It is appropriated to state that the miracles of healing and deliverance witnessed on a daily basis at the Global Success International fellowship has been quite remarkable as lost hopes have been rekindled.
People who came to the church weeping left the Church with joy and smiling as healing and deliverance of various degrees, ranging from the dreaded disease HIV/AIDS, Parkinson disease, barrenness, visa problems and other related issues of sundry nature were permanently taken care of. A particular case was that of Mr & Mrs Ojike of No. 25, Lawal Street, Olodi Apapa, Lagos. Mrs. Ojike had been barren for the past 17 years, and had been to many places in search of the fruits of the womb, but all to no avail, until they were directed by a member of the fellowship who had suffered same fate. Prayers were said for them and counselled to remain steadfast to God who will see them through in their request. After some few months, they came back to testify to the goodness of the Lord with a bouncing baby girl whom they named Deborah. This is one of the harvest of miracles that happens there.
Okereke despite all the successes is disturbed about the Nigerian polity. He said, for instance, that Nigerians are caught in mid ocean given the rate of crimes in the society today. He appealed to those in authority to shun negative thinking, cheap blackmail, corruption, indiscipline, and disloyalty to constituted authority in running the affairs of this great nation if they want to reap God's harvest.
The need to turn to God he stressed, is very important at this crucial time of the nation. "I believe the word of God works if you hold on to it, our God is a merciful and loving God having kept us peacefully together in the face of so many crisis that could have resulted to war but all these were averted," he said. Citing instances in other countries with little crisis resulting to war, he concluded that: "God is really a Nigerian as His love endureth forever".
Okereke who is also blessed with the gift of prophecy said, the country would begin to experience relative peace in the year 2008 as the present dispensation is yet to be properly rooted. He said, for the coming elections to be hitchfree, he urged Nigerians to embrace peace and pray very hard . He advised that politicians should imbibe the culture of altruism, as the interest of the nation was more than an individual. "Insincerity is the bane of all the trouble we have at the centre today," he pointed out.
The 29 year-old who is still single said he was careful about marriage in order to avoid conflict with the Holy Spirit.
Chima had both his primary and secondary education at Awa community in Oguta Local Government Area of Imo State. He is a part-time degree student at the University of Lagos.
In an effort to extend assistance to people and the needy beyond his church, Global Success International Fellowship runs a charity organisation that is concerned with the plight of the motherless babies, widows and orphans in the society. The vision which gave birth to the organisation was borne out of conviction that something needed to be done for these groups of people for a better society. By this activity, he said the organisation reaches out to the public through donation of materials. In a demonstration of its love for charity, the church last christmas distributed about 61 bags of rice, 175 bags of salt, 13 tubers of Yam, 40 cartons of milk, 20 cartons of tin tomatoes, drugs, textiles materials and toiletries to 250 widows and orphans in the society.
Chima said, the Global Success International fellowship has established a rehabilitation centre. In these centres, he said people would be trained to become self reliant in various skills and vocations thereby making them relevant to the society. He called on the government, well meaning individuals and groups to lend support to the activities of this noble venture. With this anticipated support, Okereke said, "in a short time everyone will change the society for the better".
He was born on December 10, 1973, to Chief R. S. N, Okereke and Chief (Mrs.) Dorathy Okereke, who are devoted Catholics. His father was the former vice chairman of the Oguta Local Government area of Imo State between 1994 - 1996. Chima revealed that his birth was characterised with mystery which could only be interpreted by God himself.
"An Angel of the Lord appeared in an army uniform to reveal my birth to my mother. He said the Angel later disappeared after the revelation. Uptill date my birth has remained a mystery," Okereke told Thislife.
With this development, he became a child to watch right from his birth. The young Chima needed unfettered freedom to plot the graph of his adolescent life. He could not hide his precocious talent. He was poised to face the challenges of life with tenacity of purpose, those who knew him said. This in-built purpose principle later formed the pillar of his outstanding achievements in the spiritual realm. In consonance with his feat, he declared that his callings has been approved in Heaven. "My Power is from God", said the charismatic preacher. As a modest man of God, he never allowed earthly materials to becloud his spiritual mission.
To many, Okereke is described as the Caleb of the present generation in view of the energy and dexterity he devotes to God's service. His operational philosophy could be described as perseverance and excellence as he is not only organised but keeps all documentation's meticulously and expects perfection in all things done by himself or his followers. "I have always done things according to God's inspiration," he noted. For the man of God, this is the age of action, and manifestations of the sons of God. Of demonstration of God's power through the word proving that Jesus Christ is alive now and is same yesterday, today and forever.

Original_Search_Title: Wakiso Women Battle Hunger
Headline: Uganda: Wakiso Women Battle Hunger
Body_Text:
Kampala — IF famine struck in the country, lives of residents in nine villages of Bukasa parish, Wakiso would not change.
The residents, most of them women, have set up communal gardens to enable them fight poverty and hunger. With the assistance of Hunger Project Uganda (HPU) which provides loans and seedlings the residents boast of between three to six acres of gardens.
HPU identifies the land on which the residents set up their communal gardens. Recently, Dr. Fitigu Tadesse, the Hunger Project's regional director for Africa visited the gardens and was happy to see the healthy maize, vegetables and yams grown by the women.
Tadesse advised the women to set up their individual gardens in their homes and also learn how to save.
He noted the need to grow a variety of crops for nutritional purposes. HPU operates in eight African countries.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Now a Traditional Export Commodity
Headline: Ghana: Yam Now a Traditional Export Commodity
Body_Text:
Accra — Fresh yam has since November 20, 2002 been classified as a traditional export commodity, the Ministry of Trade and Industry said on Tuesday.
A notice to exporters and the general public by the sector Minister, Dr Kofi Konadu Apraku said the use of the Ghana Export Form for export of Non-Traditional Goods would no longer apply to yam export.
The notice said all yam exporters should therefore use the Bank of Ghana Exchange Control Form A.2.

Original_Search_Title: Vietnamese Agriculturists to Help Boost Food Production
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Vietnamese Agriculturists to Help Boost Food Production
Body_Text:
Brazzaville — Sixteen Vietnamese agriculturists have arrived in the Republic of Congo (ROC) to take part in a programme to help the country attain food self-sufficiency. Another 33 Vietnamese are expected in the next few months.
The technicians, engaged for a three-year tour of duty to pass on their knowledge to local farmers, met Agriculture Minister Jeanne Dambendzet in the capital, Brazzaville, on 3 December.
"With the help of these technicians - who have already demonstrated their expertise in agriculture, fisheries and animal husbandry - we will move towards attaining food self-sufficiency," she said.
The Vietnamese will also teach fish conservation techniques. The ROC's food-security plan focuses on improving water management, proliferating its exploitable resources, and conducting an analysis of socioeconomic constraints on the attainment of these goals. The country intends to improve the cultivation of cassava, yams, bananas, rice, and potatoes.
If the programme succeeds, it will enable the nation's 3.2 million people to eat more locally produced food. According to the UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation, 32 percent of the population suffers from hunger. The agriculture ministry says the country spends 100 million francs CFA (US $166.38 million) a year on food imports.

Original_Search_Title: So Tall, a New National Assembly
Headline: Nigeria: So Tall, a New National Assembly
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The 1999/2003 Senate and House of Representatives were like a person who
is dreaming of fighting or running away from an attacking lion while he or
she is sleeping with legs folded at the knees. However much he or she wills
to throw blows at the beast, or dash away across a flat landscape, the
actions will not come at all. There will be a desperate struggle to act but
a leg or arm will not be effective. The National Assembly started in 1999 by
falling into a sleep touched by the sweetness of corruption through
apparently unrestrained spending of public money. Onto the scene stalked a
lion as the "Executive"; roaring and showing its fangs. Much as they fought
to slay it and win nationwide public acclaim, a wall of public cynicism rose
to shield the executive.
The National Assembly, for example, grabbed a sling called "the
Constitution" and shot it towards the forehead of President Obasanjo. Not
only did the hand pulling the string and the stone never seemed to let it go
off, but there were no cheering national crowds urging them on. The wax from
"Ghana must come" bags, and reports of senators awarding contracts worth
many millions of naira kept gumming up their elbows.
Part of the problem of the National Assembly was the failure to
interpret the material meaning of their claimed mission of "defending the
constitution", or that budget passed into law must be implemented. Those
talked to about this handicap blamed it on the deliberate silence of the
media which had been bought by the Presidency. But even their most
sympathetic observers could not dispel the nagging and sour thought that the
representatives merely wished to supervise awards of contracts to themselves
for eating up budget funds. Others saw in their anger not heroic reflexes to
defend the welfare of the masses, but a silent creeping of a cobra called
the "Arewa Consultative Forum" for a lethal serpent's strike at Obasanjo's
political legs.
This raises the question of which language the 2003/2007 National
Assembly should use in talking with President Obasanjo. The so-called
"hardliners" (or the bitter tongue) want to constantly insist on them
swinging the constitution as whip to lash and despise the president. Those
who fear for explosion of chaos if a constitutional coup were to happen,
would resort to saying na constitution we go chop? There is no common
language here.
And yet the constitution must remain the anchor of the new
politics. This constitution must not be an abstract on mystical fetish which
is distant and does not send off steams of heat of the heart, will,
aspirations and common sense and decency of the communities (and not
individuals), who make up the "Nigerian people." The measure of the distance
of the constitution, so far, from the heart in the hearts of the people is
the cavalier way the National Assembly sought to amend the
constitution.
As a starting point, therefore, the new National Assembly must
continuously build a sense of sanctity and gravity in the imagination around
the constitution. Since they are modelled structurally on the American
system, they should tease out from the Americans the genius with which they
have built a hallowed aura of steel around their constitution. Yet they need
not go gallivanting to Yankee land if they were capable of looking at the
communities that elected or "selected" them. Each Nigerian community has
ancient codes of morality and civics whose potency was recently displayed by
the "traditional rulers and leaders of thought" from Abia South Senatorial
District over the Wabara - Imo affair. It is the duty of this National
Assembly to build the bridge between the constitution and the heart in the
hearts of community values and civic morality all across Nigeria.
A critical bridge which American legislators have over the years built
between their ritual activities as legislators, is the link they nourish
with their constituencies. Before the days of internet, Congress allocated
four billion dollars each year for free daily telephone and postal mail
between members of Congress and their individual constituencies.
Members of the Congress didn't ask for the money to be paid to them. It
was paid directly to the US Postal Service. That is how important the sound
of the voices of voters inside the corridors of legislatures at state and
federal levels should be for the legitimacy of governance. The bridge to the
constitution is built of the voices of people in individual constituencies
flowing to national, state and local government legislatures.
This National Assembly must also climb to the hill of farming ideas; of
making deep and wise reflections over public affairs a dignified
vocation. In their midst are people like Comrade Chukwumerije and Professor
Jibril Aminu who once upon a time were not only people "fascinated with
ideas" (to quote Ali Mazrui) but also galvanised the imagination of diverse
peoples across the African continent. The season of drought in ideas and
visions for firing the imagination of peoples, both young and old, has
lasted for too long. During the thirst, peddlers of easy tribal jingoism and
religious bitterness and anxieties have usurped the historic duty of the
politician to sketch horizons, for the ambitions of whole generations. Bread
hawkers have also flourished.
It is time elected politicians planted large banners inside each chamber
proclaiming: "Not pounded yam and tuwon gero alone will Nigerians flourish;"
and the National Assembly tangle with the executive.

Original_Search_Title: Start Preparing Animals And Crops for Easter
Headline: Uganda: Start Preparing Animals And Crops for Easter
Body_Text:
Kampala — During this Christmas season, hundreds of farmers throughout the country
have reaped the benefits from their investment in livestock.
Pigs, goats, cattle and chicken are selling like hot cakes and fetching
handsome returns. Even ducks, sheep and turkeys are raking in thousands of
shillings.
That having been said we need to plan for the times ahead. The next
festive season is about four months from now.
This coming Easter is a chance for you to start earning good money.
You can start small and grow to bigger scales by the next Christmas
festival. For Easter, you can plan for the products that require shorter
maturity periods such as pigs and chicken.
For crops, you could go in for horticultural crops, as well as short
maturing crop varieties such as sweet potatoes, Irish potatoes, tomatoes and
others.
For those farmers interested in rearing chicken, the planning should
begin now. Start preparing the chicken house immediately. If you are going
in for broilers, make sure that the booking is made at the day-old chick
suppliers this week.
You should time the collection of your chicks so that they are
eight-weeks old during the Easter weekend.
Receive more informative discussions with the veterinary staff at the
chick suppliers. Take notes during the discussions, and take particular note
of the health of the birds, especially the timing of the vaccination rounds
and the feeding regimes.
Look beyond Easter and go for the big time which is next Christmas. Here
we can plan for most crops and animals. Choose a particular animal such as
cattle, goats or sheep.
Christmas means agonising over the colossal amounts to be spent.
Here is an opportunity to turn the tables to your advantage. Turn the
spending spree into a cash-minting season, and reap from your sweat in the
coming year.

Original_Search_Title: How Not to Relate With HIV-Positive People
Headline: Nigeria: How Not to Relate With HIV-Positive People
Body_Text:
Lagos — The slogan used to be that 'AIDS Kills'. However, stakeholders now
dispute this slogan. What kills, they said, are the social stigma,
discrimination and the lack of funds for drugs and nutritional
requirement.
It was not surprising that the Nigerian Red Cross Society has tailored
its campaign along these lines in recent times. Right from the beginning of
2003, the organisation has embarked on programmes to reverse the social
stigma and discrimination currently being suffered by those who have tested
positive to the dreaded virus.
Apart from a sub-regional meeting on HIV/AIDS and other health problems,
the organisation has organised a debate for secondary school students across
the Lagos metropolis on the topic: If Your friend Has HIV: What Will You
Do?.
As a follow-up, the organisation launched a commemorative stamp on May
8, to fight against stigmatisation and discrimination with the saying: Know
the Truth About AIDS. Stigmatisation Kills. It Could be You.
Thus, this year's World AIDS Day commemorated recently further gave the
organisation the opportunity to reinforce its campaign to reverse the ugly
trend.
Presenting the background information to the current problem of HIV/AIDS
in Nigeria, president of the Society, Mr. Emmanuel Ijewere first spoke on
the perspective of the role of the Nigerian Red Cross in reducing spread of
the disease. They include advocacy, mobilisation and campaign, home-based
care and support for people living with HIV/AIDS (PLWHAs), among
others.
Ijewere, who was represented at the occasion, by the organisation's
National Information Adviser, Mrs. Olumide Ogunlesi, explained that the
results from the 2001 HIV/syphilis sentinel sero-prevalence survey report,
showed conclusively that Nigeria has reached the explosive stage of the HIV
epidemic, where all are at risk of infection. The survey, sets Nigeria's
overall HIV prevalence rate at 5.8 per cent.
Worst hit are young adults, aged between 15-19 years, ranging from about
four per cent in the south-east zone to more than 10 per cent in the
south-south zone. The sentinel study of 2001, showed that HIV in Nigeria is
not an urban phenomenon. The highest HIV prevalence was found in rural sites
in Enugu (13.6 per cent) and in Benue (15 per cent). In all zones except
north central, the HIV prevalence has increased since 1999.
"AIDS has taken a heavier toll on Africa than in any other part of the
world. It is estimated that 46 million people are infected by the HIV virus
worldwide, out of which more than half live in sub-Saharan Africa. Among
those infected are a large number of women and children. Majority of the new
cases of adult infections affect those between the ages 15 - 24 years. The
scourge has been left to continue to ravage mankind because of the silence
on the truth about AIDS", Ogunlesi said.
According to a recent report by the National Intelligence Council (NIC),
Nigeria is one of five the "next-wave" countries that will double or triple
the number of global HIV/AIDS cases by 2010. The others are China, India,
Russia and Ethiopia. The rapidly growing HIV prevalence in these countries
combined with their large populations will cause an explosion in the number
of worldwide AIDS cases.
The report further speculated that the social and economic impact of
AIDS in Nigeria will be similar to the hardest hit countries in Africa and
that by 2010 the disease would decimate government and business elites,
undermine growth, and further discourage foreign investment. The NIC also
theorises that the country might have as many as 15 million cases by 2010,
three times the official estimate, and speculates that the number of AIDS
orphans has already reached 2.6 million.
Despite disparity in the exact figure of those affected, experts do
agree that Nigeria already has the highest number of HIV infected adults and
AIDS orphans in West Africa.
"A sad truth about AIDS is that stigma and discrimination have many
different forms, but the end result is the same. The infected and sick, and
often their families, are shunned, ostracised, even denied access to social
and medical services.
"In the most extreme cases, HIV-positive people have been killed when
they revealed their status. Children often suffer the most, either losing
their parents at an early age, or through abuse associated with
superstition", she further said. However, each statistic made available, she
said, is a chilling reminder that the world has not, to date, been able to
stem the pandemic.
"It is evident that unless we address the questions of stigma and
discrimination, unless we fight this disease on every level, all programmes
will be less effective and the pandemic will continue to ravage communities
everywhere", she noted.
Accordingly, she said the Nigerian Red Cross was prosecuting the
HIV/AIDS war using a three-pronged strategy namely peer education,
home-based care and counselling.
However, the Red Cross was optimistic that by 2005, 1.4 million young
people between the ages of nine and 17 would have been encouraged by 2,600
peer educators to practise safer sex behaviour. The project is also to
improve the conditions of life for people living with AIDS across
twenty-four states of the federation.
"The task ahead may not be easy, but the Red Cross is undaunted, as this
is one battle that must be won. We need to understand that we are all part
of the solution. Silence is not a solution. Silence is not an
option. Silence kills. Discrimination kills. Let us work together on this
global campaign to make a real difference in people's lives. Let us be
courageous to pass on the truth about AIDS", she added.
Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer, Nestle Nigeria Limited,
Mr. Klaus P. Wachsmuth, spoke on the relationship between Nutrition and
HIV/AIDS.
"We identified hunger and malnutrition as the number one public health
problem in Nigeria. Good nutrition for all individuals, but especially
people living with HIV/AIDS requires the consumption of an adequate amount
of macro nutrients (proteins, cabohydrates and fats) and micro nutrients
(vitamins and minerals)", he said.
Macro nutrients A deficiency, he said manifests itself in the weight
loss and wasting that is typical for AIDS patients. Those symptoms occur as
a result of reduced food intake, poor absorption of nutrients, changes in
the metabolism that affects cell growth, enzymatic processes and immune
system reactions. Because of these effects, an HlV-infected person has an
additional nutrient requirement: 10 to 15 per cent additional energy intake,
50 to 100 per cent increase in protein intake as compared to a non- HIV
infected person, among others.
For energy needs, he said, an active non-HIV-infected adult requires
approximately 2070 kilo calories per day, while an HIV infected adult
requires 10 to 15 percent more energy per day (or approximately 400
additional kilo calories for a man and 300 kilo calories for a woman.
Also for protein needs, a non-HIV-infected man requires about 57 grams
per day and a woman requires 48 grams per day while an HlV-infected adult
needs approximately 50 to 100 per cent more protein for a total of 85 grams
per day for men and 72 grams per day for women. The same is true of the
micro nutrients.
He said consuming micro nutrients (especially Vitamins A, Be and Bz,
iron and zinc) was important for building a strong immune system and
fighting infections. He explained that Vitamin A deficiency is associated
with higher maternal-child transmission rates, faster progression from HIV
to AIDS, higher infant mortality and child growth failure, advising that
such vital nutrient could be obtained from diary products, as vegetables,
kidney, eggs, fishes, sweet potato, palm oil, carrots and pawpaw.
"The B-group vitamins play important roles in immune regulations and
deficiencies play a role in disease progression. The most promising results
are for Vitamin A and the B-group vitamins. Vitamin B (niacin) and B
supplementation have been shown to improve survival and reduce disease
progression.
"Nestle, as a respected and trustworthy food, nutrition, health and
wellness company is very much concern about malnutrition and HIV/AIDS
especially in the developing countries such as Nigeria and Kenya. It is in
the light of this that Nestle is involved in social partnership with the Red
Cross to reduce the spread of infection and minimise the impact of HIV/AIDS
in Nigeria", he stressed.
Thus, presenting HIV/AIDS Questions and Answers brochure which was
produced by the Nigerian Red Cross Society in collaboration with Nestle
Nigeria, he explained that it was to further efforts to improve the
knowledge and understanding of HIV/AIDS and sexually transmitted infections
in Nigeria.
"Our goal is to build the right understanding, guide people to interact
appropriately and encourage safe sexual behaviour. We wish to promote,
through this publication, greater involvement of civil society and ensure
proper dissemination and understanding of HIV/AIDS in the local community",
he said.
He stressed that the brochure would be published in three major Nigerian
languages: Hausa, Yoruba and Igbo and distributed widely throughout
Nigeria.
"I would like to assure you that Nestle will continue to support
building a better knowledge and understanding of HIV/AIDS as well as
facilitate positive behaviour change to reduce the human toll of AIDS-a
tragic reality being experienced by families and communities in
Nigeria. Nestle's vision is a world where an increasing number of people
live a healthy life style. I believe the vision is achievable", he
emphasised.
With the overall goal of the project being to contribute to the
reduction of the spread of HIV/AIDS, particularly among youths and improve
the quality of life PLWHA, it is expected that other corporate institutions
will follow suits to further help improve knowledge and understanding of
HIV/AIDS and Sexually Transmitted Infections (STI's) among youths, and
provide basic home care support for PLWHA.
Experts said the virus has continued too ravage the nation due to
stigmatisation and discrimination of those confirmed positive many of whom
have resorted to spreading the virus indiscriminately as punishment to a
hostile society.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Sector Produces 713,000 Tones of Cereals
Headline: Angola: Agriculture Sector Produces 713,000 Tones of Cereals
Body_Text:
Luanda — Some 713,000 tones of maize, rice and other cereals were produced during the 2002/2003 agricultural season countrywide, what represents a slight increase in comparison with the previous year, Agriculture and Rural Development Minister Gilberto Buta Lutukuta said on Tuesday.
Mr Lutukuta was addressing the workers at the end of the year greeting ceremony and added that maize production in 2003, reached about 619,000 tones comparing to the 47,000 of the year 2002.
Massango plus massambala production reached only 83,000 tones, this year, due to the heavy drought registered in southern Cunene province, he added.
He said the country is still registering a deficit of 47 per cent in the cereal availability, what corresponds 650,000 tones, in physical units, adding that 6,8 million tones of manioc were produced.
Angola has produced, so far, about 93,000 tones of beans, 64,000 of peanuts and 819,000 of sour and sweet potatoes.
Agricultural Deputy-Ministers Dario Katata and Zacarias Sambeny, national directors among other entities witnessed the act.

Original_Search_Title: Kanu Behind Woes in Narc, Says Transport and Communications Minister Michuki
Headline: Kenya: Kanu Behind Woes in Narc, Says Transport and Communications Minister Michuki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The former ruling party, Kanu, is fueling the current political squabbles within the Cabinet.
Transport and Communications Minister John Michuki said Kanu was behind the wrangling.
Speaking at Muguru ACK Church during the Kangema leaders meeting , Michuki said the "squabbles are giving an impression that all was not well within the coalition Government".
"Mburi ya ruanio ikuaga ona ruanio (Goats that bleat since birth continue to do so after death," he said.
Michuki likened those dissatisfied with the Narc performance to the proverbial wolf in sheep's clothing.
He however praised President Mwai Kibaki's stand of maintaining silence as the squabbles in the Cabinet took centre stage, saying "one should not join the fight amongst his children as it would be difficult for an outsider to know who the father is".
Ruling out existence of other political parties within the coalition, Michuki said he and other leaders, including Roads and Public Works Minister Raila Odinga, were elected on a Narc ticket.
He said Kanu was still harbouring dreams of coming back to power and that was why it was allegedly using money looted from the country's resources to destabilise the Cabinet.
On aid resumption, Michuki said the Cabinet would be intact as he and other ministers will be busy implementing the election pledges.
He said Kenyans must have renewed hopes as the Government was implementing its pledges. Sweet potatoes planted today cannot be harvested a day after, the minister said.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agency Sponsors Nutrition Workshop for West Africa in Burkina Faso
Headline: West Africa: UN Agency Sponsors Nutrition Workshop for West Africa in Burkina Faso
Body_Text:
Improving the diets of West Africans facing nutritional deficiencies
will be the focus of a workshop next week in Burkina Faso sponsored by the
United Nations and research institutions.
"Food-based approaches for a healthy nutrition in West Africa: the role
of food technologists and nutritionists" is slated to meet from 23 to 28
November in Ouagadougou, the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) said
today.
"Nearly 35 per cent of (West African) children under 5 years of age are
stunted, a sign of chronically poor diet, while about 5 to 15 per cent of
young children are wasted, a sign of acute hunger," FAO said.
"At the same time, overweight and obesity, which can lead to diabetes,
heart disease and some types of cancer, are emerging problems among some
urban populations in West Africa."
The most widespread deficiencies among the 226 million West Africans are
vitamin A, iron and iodine, the UN agency said, but these can be prevented
by adding palm oil, citrus fruits, sweet potatoes, papaya, mangoes, green
leafy vegetables, legumes and animal flesh to the diet.
FAO said it would urge better food production and dietary
diversification, as well as increased support for appropriate storage,
processing, and marketing.
The other workshop sponsors are the University of Wageningen in the
Netherlands, the University of Ouagadougou, and the Institut de RecherchÃ©
pour le DÃ©veloppement (IRD) in France.
The countries whose representatives are expected to take part in the
workshop are Benin, Burkina Faso, Cape Verde, CÃ´te d'Ivoire, the Gambia,
Ghana, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Liberia, Mali, Mauritania, Niger, Nigeria,
Senegal, Sierra Leone and Togo.

Original_Search_Title: Sweet News On Potato
Headline: Kenya: Sweet News On Potato
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyan researchers and farmers are part of a team that has won a
prestigious award for efforts to alleviate vitamin A deficiency.
Vitamin A for Africa (Vitaa) scooped the Consultative Group for
International Agricultural Research Science Award for outstanding
partnership in promoting the orange-fleshed sweet-potato. Vitamin A
deficiency is a leading cause of early childhood death and a major risk
factor for pregnant and breastfeeding women.
The condition weakens the immune system, making victims susceptible to
deadly diseases like measles, malaria and diarrhoea.
However, it remains one of Africa's most important and most treatable
health problems.
Prof Edward Karuri of the University of Nairobi's Department of Food
Technology and Nutrition, who is also chairman of the Kenyan steering
committee, received the award and a token $10,000 (Sh780,000) for his
colleagues at a ceremony in Tanzania last week.
Mr Ian Johnson, a World Bank vice-president and chairman of the
consultative group, presented the award.
Vitaa includes 44 local and international institutions and agencies
promoting the potato's development and consumption.
The partnership provides community groups in Ethiopia, Ghana, Kenya,
Mozambique, South Africa, Tanzania and Uganda with new orange-fleshed
varieties that prevent vitamin A deficiency.
Local institutions involved in researching the potato's effectiveness
and its development included the University of Nairobi and the Kenyan
Agriculture Research Institute.
Unlike the traditional white-fleshed sweet potatoes, Vitaa varieties are
rich in beta-carotene, a micronutrient the body uses to produce vitamin
A. Prof Karuri says the partnership is recognised for its new ways of using
the potato, including juice and flour for weaning breastfeeding
children.
Vitaa, co-ordinated by the International Potato Centre (CIP), based in
Lima, Peru, aims to combat vitamin A deficiency, which blinds and kills
between 250,000 and 500,000 children in Africa a year.
About two-thirds of the children develop xerophthalmia, a
blindness-inducing disease resulting from lack of Vitamin A, and die within
a year of losing sight.
Though long a staple on the continent, the African sweet potato's flesh
has no beta carotene (a precursor to vitamin A), unlike its sweeter
yellow-orange-fleshed relative.
Vitaa works at the community level, targeting mothers' groups and other
producer organisations in areas where sweet potatoes already grow.
"Sweet potato is a women's crop grown mainly for family use and a
supplementary household income," says Vitaa project co-ordinator Regina
Kapinga, a Tanzanian agronomist based in Uganda.
"One of the reasons we are drawing so much attention these days is
because Vitaa offers a simple solution to a major public health
problem. Right now, our only difficulty is to meet the demand for planting
materials," she said.
Health officials have been trying to tackle Africa's vitamin A
deficiency problem for more than a quarter of a century, says Prof
Karuri.
"In black Africa, three million children under five already suffer from
a vitamin A-related blindness known as xerophthalmia, or dry eye, and many
more are at risk. Two-thirds of those affected may die within months due to
the collapse of the immune system," he said.
Globally, an estimated 250 million pre-school children may be vitamin
A-deficient. Public health experts believe that addressing the problem could
reduce by more almost 25 per cent the overall mortality of children under
six.
Studies also show dramatic reductions in maternal mortality when
pregnant women receive vitamin A or beta-carotene supplements.
"Such supplements have helped millions," said Prof Karuri, "but delivery
is expensive and supplement programmes are hard to administer."
Diet-based interventions, he said, are also problematic because many
good vitamin A sources, such as fruits and vegetables, are too expensive or
not regularly available to African consumers.
"Vitaa recognises orange-fleshed sweet potatoes as a promising solution
to vitamin A deficiency in Eastern and Southern Africa because, apart from
being rich in beta-carotene, they're easy to grow and affordable," Prof
Karuri added.
To encourage widespread adoption, plant breeders at the International
Potato Centre (CIP) are working with Vitaa partner agencies to introduce 42
improved orange-fleshed breeding lines.
"These are the first in a series of new sweet potatoes to emerge from a
six-year multi-million-dollar breeding programme," said CIP Director-General
Hubert Zandstra.
To find out if African consumers will accept the new varieties, Vitaa's
partners in Kari, the International Centre for Research on Women and CIP
have field-tested several orange-fleshed plant types.
They found that African women will accept them in place of white-fleshed
sweet potatoes once they recognise their nutritional benefits. The centre
studies also showed that children like its sweeter taste.
Vitaa works with other agencies in its seven member countries. Each
country seeks to introduce orange-fleshed varieties. But Uganda's efforts
are probably most advanced.
Last year, about 40,000 Ugandan farmers received samples of improved
orange-fleshed sweet potatoes for planting through the Buganda Cultural and
Development Foundation.
"What we're seeing is a groundswell of support for this common-sense
agricultural approach to a major public health problem," says economist
Joachim von Braun, director-general of the US-based International Food
Policy Research Institute.
An impact case study by economists at Michigan State University and CIP
suggested that orange-fleshed sweet potatoes could benefit some 50 million
African children.
CIP is one of 16 international agricultural research centres that make
up the global network known as CGIAR, which, each year, gives awards to its
scientists and researchers with innovative practices.
Additional reporting by Zephaniah Ubwani in Arusha

Original_Search_Title: Food Cooked in 'Kaveera' Bad
Headline: Uganda: Food Cooked in 'Kaveera' Bad
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT is lunchtime, the local shed where local dishes are sold is packed to
capacity. A customer calls out and makes his order, loud enough for everyone
to hear. The cook, with beads of sweat trickling down her face, lifts the
cover of one of the saucepans and proceeds to unwrap the huge polythene bag
covering the food, picks a plate and piles on some steaming matooke.
She then uncovers another saucepan, undoes the polythene covering, wipes
her hand on her apron, picks a sweet potato and lays it on the plate with
the matooke. The cook then opens the lid of another saucepan and serves some
groundnut sauce before walking through the benches to deliver the order to
the customer.
Has it ever occurred to you that such meals could lead to illness for
the rest of your life.
In many of the local food stalls, the food is covered with buveera as it
cooks. The 'logic' behind this method of cooking is that the food gets ready
faster and is kept steaming hot. Once in a while, there have been complaints
of the food assuming the colour of the buveera, but this has not stopped
people from going ahead and eating it.
However, research has revealed that under certain temperatures,
polythene can disintegrate and release chemicals that are detrimental to
human health.
Polythene is made up a combination of carbon and hydrogen and thus is a
hydrocarbon. It is a tough, translucent, waxy and thermoplastic (can be
repeatedly softened by heating).
According to Andrew Othieno, the senior environmental inspector of
National Environmental Management Authority (NEMA), when polythene bags are
made certain properties with different compounds are added to make them suit
the purpose for which they are made.
One of the components that are added include chlorine. Buveera
disintegrate under temperatures of less than 1,000 degrees centigrade and
begin to release some toxic fumes.
These toxic fumes contain chemical compounds known as dioxins and
furans. They are also called unintentional by-products because they are
released by mistake as a result of combustion. These chemical can also be
created by car exhaust fumes, and emissions from the incineration of
hospital waste, municipal waste, hazardous waste and wood.
Dioxins and furans have been blacklisted internationally because they
pose a serious threat to human health and the environment.
These categories are known as Persistent Organic Pollutants
(POP's). There is scientific evidence that exposure to even low doses of
POP's can lead to cancer, damage to the nervous and immune systems,
reproductive disorders and interference with normal infant and child
development.
Thus many countries have come together to put an end to the production
of this unintentional combustion by-products, together with 10 other POP's
including pesticides and industrial chemicals.
This has led to the (POP's) Stockholm Convention, a treaty that sets out
control the production, import, export, disposal and use of POP's.
In Uganda, efforts have been made both by NEMA and Government to try and
reduce the use of polythene for instance in the last budget, a high tax was
imposed on buveera to serve as an economic disincentive. Alternative
measures have been proposed such as using paper bags or totally banning the
use of polythene.

Original_Search_Title: Crisis in the Cupboard: Big Threats to the World's Crops
Headline: Ghana: Crisis in the Cupboard: Big Threats to the World's Crops
Body_Text:
Accra — The plentiful food supply we take for granted has come at the expense of agricultural diversity. Now the bugs and blights are taking their revenge
Oranges are a big deal in Brazil. They account for 400,000 jobs and $1.2 billion in yearly exports; seven of every 10 glasses of orange juice drunk worldwide are squeezed from Brazilian fruit. The fortunes of JosÃ© Luis Cutrale, said to be Brazil's richest man, were made in orange juice. But now the source of this bounty is in peril. Two years ago a mysterious disease began attacking the roots and veins of orange trees. In a matter of weeks the trees starve to death and the splendid leaf canopy withers into a gnarl of barren branches as brittle as witch's hair. Scientists call it "sudden death?
To Marcos Schrank, a SÃ£o Paulo grower who recently destroyed 12,000 stricken trees, there's another name for it. "This"-he nods at a stretch of bare dirt, as red and raw as a wound-"this is like AIDS?
Actually, it's more like tristeza da Zaranja ("the orange's sorrow"), a virus that ravaged the Brazilian countryside in the 1940s. In a few years, the disease killed all varieties of Brazil's orange trees except one: the limao cravo. Brazilians hailed the limÃ£o cravo as something of a miracle, and farmers made it the foundation crop of the orange industry's renewal. Of course, they knew-as farmers everywhere know-that planting only one kind of anything is folly. History is rife with single-crop catastrophes, from the locust storms of Biblical Egypt to the Irish potato famine. But they risked it anyway. By the late l990s, Brazil had overtaken the United States in orange production. Today, 85 percent of Brazil's 200 million orange trees are grown from limÃ£o cravo rootstalk. In an ironic twist, scientists believe that the new virus maybe a relative of the orange's sorrow, returned to claim the one tree spared in the previous epidemic. Says Nelson Gimenes, a plant pathologist at research lab Fundecitrus, "The risk of catastrophe is enormous?
Ever since hunters and gatherers swapped spears for seeds, farmers have waged war with germs and pests. Usually, producers have prevailed over the pathogens. But today the stakes are infinitely higher. Old-style farms, with a mosaic of different vegetables, fruits and cereals grown on a single plot, weren't terribly efficient, but the diversity of crop types helped insure against catastrophic losses due to disease. In the rush to feed the world's billions, diversity has been sacrificed on the altar of efficiency. Agriculture is now an industrial- scale enterprise, with huge estates dedicated to the cultivation of a single crop-so-called monoculture farming.
Today, fewer and fewer farmers produce massive quantities of supercrops that smother the land in a seamless carpet. Intensive irrigation and synthetic fertilizers pump up yields. But these vast spreads are sitting ducks for pests and pathogens, which farmers fight with ever-growing doses of insecticides and weed killers. The most powerful new tools to defend the gains of the green revolution-namely, genetically modified crops-are taboo in many nations. That leaves farmers with a handicap. If some kind of solution isn't found, many scientists say, food crises will grow more frequent and disruptive. Eventually we may find that the food supply we take for granted may falter. "How long will it take before we have an ecological disaster?" says David Tilman, an ecologist at the University of Minnesota and a longtime scholar of monoculture farming. "We can't afford to have a SARS in agriculture? The green revolution, based largely on monoculture fanning, saved much of the world from the threat of starvation due to overpopulation-at the expense of diversity. According to Jared Diamond in the journal Nature, the first farmers chose from 200,000 species of wild plants and 148 wild animals, but today only 100 plant varieties and just 14 different animals are routinely cultivated-the ones that humans could most easily tame, reproduce, manipulate and profit from. Orderly expanses of a single crop like wheat or soy are convenient to plant and reap, and make big profits for breeding companies, which can sell tons of superseeds. Streamlining the garden has long been the most efficient way to put food on an ever larger number of tables.
It still is, but the gains have begun to falter. From sprawling Kansas wheat fields to southern China's rice fields, farmers do almost daily battle with legions of microscopic enemies: blight, bunt, blast and bugs that rip through their fields like fire through kindling. In the United States, pests and diseases devour $90 billion worth of food crops a year. Worse, these ills hitch rides around the globe aboard ships, airplanes and trucks, stuck to the soles of shoes or merely riding the winds. Customs keepers and health inspectors do a heroic job of holding the invaders at bay, but their efforts are laughable. U.S. port officials intercept some 13,000 exotic plant diseases every year, but check only 2 percent of incoming freight, according to a National Academy of Sciences report.
If mobility makes germs more dangerous, monoculture makes farmers more vulnerable. Like families, single-crop plantations are stamped with nearly identical genes. "If there is an inherited defect, all of the siblings are vulnerable," says Zhu You-yong, a plant pathologist at the Phytopathology Laboratory in China's Yunnan province. "We may not be able to see it now, but in the future monoculture is going to be a threat to the world food supply?
The dark side of monoculture is becoming more and more apparent. When southern corn-leaf blight tore through the one-of-a-kind corn farms in the 1970s, U.S. farmers tallied $1 billion in damages. Late potato blight, a cousin to the fungus that devastated Ireland, is even more in-tractable today than two centuries ago, costing potato plantations $2.7 billion a year worldwide. 1
For small farmers, the impact of disease can be even more devastating. In the vast and populous Indian states of Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, West Bengal and parts of Uttar Assam, rice cultivators plant almost nothing but the Mahasuri variety, leaving them wide open to disease. Chinese peasants lose 10 percent of their harvest to rice blast, while wheat rust claims 20 percent of their grain. And while North American potato potentates grumble that blight has cut profits, in impoverished Russia potatoes are the next meal. In the Indian state of Andhra Pradesh, where a rampant bollworm infestation adds to the already crushing debt of small farmers, 10,000 desperate peasants have committed suicide in the past decade. Geoffrey Hawtin, head of Global Conservation Trust, a Rome-based research agency, puts it starkly. "For the little guy, a failed crop means you starve," he says.
Producers large and small have generally shrugged off the fight against bugs and bacteria as part of the trade-off for a brimming silo. No longer. Rice yields have been stagnant in Asia for more than a decade. With water growing scarcer, irrigation has actually retreated, down 10 percent per capita in the past 25 years. As the green revolution loses momentum, pressure on farmers to compensate with ever-bigger harvests will be even greater. Says David Pimentel, an agricultural ecologist at Cornell, "We are bumping up against the limits of the model? Demographers expect that not only will the world's population swell to 9 billion by 2050, demand for food will surge nearly 2Â½-fold, driven by rising incomes.
To wrest more food from the earth, producers will either have to pump more yield out of the same tired plots-blitzing the soil with fertilizers and pesticides-or push cultivation onto weaker soils and chop down forests. Each harvest will bring more erosion, pollution, salinization of water and waterlogging of the soils. "We are on a trajectory that is not sustainable in the long run," says Tilman.
In China now, plants bred for disease resistance last five years before the germs catch up. Tilman reports that maize hybrids in the United States typically remain disease-free for four years-half the shelf life of 30 years ago. Bugs can beat the best insecticides in a decade. Spraying fungicides and pesticides now accounts for a quarter of all costs for banana growers. Fertilizers are double agents:
"We're feeding plants and making them more nutritious, and the pests like that, too," says Pimentel. The world, he says, loses 40 percent of potential food production a year despite the use of 2.7 billion kilograms of pesticide.
The banana may be the worst casualty of the modem plantation system. Some 500 million Asians, Africans and Latin Americans depend on it for calories and income, yet few crops are as cursed. Emile Frison, head of the International Plant Genetic Resources Institute in France, estimates that 40 percent to 50 percent of the world banana harvest is lost to insects and diseases. Ten millennia ago, the banana was a big, starchy thing stuffed with seeds as hard as stones. But early farmers carefully culled and cultivated the rare edible fruits and, over time, begat an elegant golden crescent with a filet of sweet, smooth flesh. Of 500 varieties, the only banana people in rich countries ever see is the Cavendish, a huge cash crop.
The problem is, the perfect Cavendish banana is perfectly sterile. Centuries of careful breeding have shrunk the once virile pits to a sexless streak of black dots (farmers breed it by cloning). In the wild, sexual reproduction equips a plant with a mixed bag of genes that get jumbled again and again with each generation-making it a moving target for disease. The chaste Cavendish is a pushover. Black sigatoca, a once rare fungus, is now an epidemic. Cavendish growers spray their crop with fungicides up to 50 times a year, at a cost that experts say may price this banana out of the market.
It's not only wonder foods that are threatened. Malaysian customs inspectors pore over baggage as though they were looking for weapons-grade plutonium. What worries them is microcyclus ulei, the rubber-tree leaf blight. Together, Malaysia and its Southeast Asian neighbors produce 92 percent of the world's natural latex, which synthetic rubber has never equaled for elasticity and heat resistance. So far leaf blight has not spread beyond the West Indies and Central and South America. But the Asians are taking no chances. To this day travelers from Brazil, where the blight is endemic, may not set foot on Malaysian soil without first spending several days in quarantine in Europe. There is even a blight brigade on call, ready to defoliate an entire plantation at the first hint of infection. If the leaf disease ever makes the jump to tropical Asia-scientists have little doubt it will eventually- "the world rubber supply would collapse," says Eurico Pinheiro, a Brazilian rubber expert.
The task for scientists is to find a way of producing food efficiently while keeping the planet healthy at the same time. Genetic engineering may help boost immunity in sterile plants (the banana) or those in which virtually all varieties (potatoes, papaya) are vulnerable. Brazilian agronomist Manoel Texeira engineered papayas to withstand ringspot virus, which causes $35 million in damages a year. But Brazil has outlawed transgenic crops. "It's extremely frustrating Texeira says.
Another way out may be to take a gamble on diversity. Thirty years ago, at the height of the green revolution, a few prudent policymakers and geneticists scoured villages and forests for varieties of plants that had been left behind. Some 6 million samples are on ice in more than
1,000 gene banks worldwide. Zhu believes that after millennia of discarding crops, farmers can use these lost seeds to resurrect variety in their fields. When he urged peasants in Yunnan to plant two different strains of rice, thus imitating nature's genetic firewall to disease, losses to blight and blast dropped significantly.
Experiments like Zhu's offer a way out of the monoculture trap, but will they catch on? History is not encouraging. Only a few wild plants-strawberries, blueberries, pecans-have been added to the human diet in hundreds of years. "Everyone knows that to grow more food we need to go back to the well of diversity," says Tilman. "We need to be looking at agriculture not in the near future but over the next 10,000 years? That kind of perspective usually comes only in a crisis.

Original_Search_Title: Ugandans Scoop International Award
Headline: Ugandans Scoop International Award
Body_Text:
Kampala — Two Ugandans on October 29 scooped the Consultative Group on International Agricultural Reasearch (CGIAR) award in Nairobi.
Rajab Ssetyabula, Joweria Ssekiyanja and Dr. Regina Kapinga, a Tanzanian of Vitamin-A for Africa were presented with the CGIAR Partnership award by Kenyan Vice President, Moody Awori.
Awori in his speech emphasised that African nations had to step up their technological know-how so as to improve crop yield. This, he said, would raise the Gross Domestic product because most African economies depend on agriculture.
Mr. Ssetyabula was proud to explain to the delegates how their product became a success: "NARO Uganda gave us some genetically improved sweet potato vines to plant and we multiplied them.
Eventually, we started selling them around Masaka, Kampala and Luweero. People would come from very far to buy these sweet potatoes. This is because the potato boost the immune system of especially children and HIV/AIDS patients. Some people bought them because of their deep orange colour," he said.
Vit-A for Africa is supported by the International Centre for Research on Potatoes (CIP). Ms Joweria Ssekiyanja makes various products from these vit-A sweet potatoes which include juice, jam, pancakes, mandazi, biscuits and porridge flour.
One litre of juice costs sh2000 while a 250-gramme jar costs sh1000. The products are available at Makerere Nutrition Centre and Nyimbwa Health centre in Luweero. "These products are pure and organic and therefore do not have side effects to the human body nor the environment," Dr Kapinga said.

Original_Search_Title: Trade in GM Crops May Be Allowed Soon
Headline: Kenya: Trade in GM Crops May Be Allowed Soon
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Genetically modified crops may soon be available for commercial use in
Kenya.
A biosafety framework workshop for Members of Parliament was yesterday
told that trials on the crops were going on.
The crops under trial are sweet potatoes, maize and cotton. Several
other food security crops will be put under trial once a Bio-safety Bill
becomes law.
Assistant minister for Agriculture, Mr George Khaniri, said the
government issued biosafety regulations and guidelines in 1998.
The assistant minister announced that genetically modified sweet
potatoes were under a greenhouse trial by the Kenya Agricultural Research
Institute.
MPs from all political parties are attending the two-day workshop.
He also announced that maize and cotton were also under trial before
being accepted for field and commercial use.
Mr Khaniri however said all the approvals were being done without an
existing policy.
"This indicates there is a serious need to have the draft policy
approved before things run out of control," he said.
The assistant minister said the development of the Bio-safety Framework
would result in the formulation and drafting of the National Biotechnology
and Bio-safety policy as well as Bio-safety Bill, which would eventually
become an Act of parliament.

Original_Search_Title: Atlantic Survivors Want More of the World
Headline: Cape Verde: Atlantic Survivors Want More of the World
Body_Text:
At close to 3,000 metres, the Pico do Fogo volcano is by far the most
imposing landmark in the arid Cape Verde Islands off the coast of West
Africa. The village of Cha das Caldeiras, within its vast crater boasts
several vineyards, a wine cooperative and a French-owned restaurant.
The volcano, which last spewed out a stream of molten lava in 1995, is
proving to be an important source of revenue for Fogo, a barren tropical
island in the Atlantic Ocean which has few other natural resources.
"Over 30 per cent of the seats on planes flying into Fogo are taken by
tourists," the island's mayor, Eugenio Miranda da Veiga, told IRIN. He
welcomes this influx of money-laden visitors and recent investments in
tourism made by returning emigrants who made their fortune overseas.
But da Veiga acknowledges that Fogo, like the other eight inhabited
islands in the Cape Verde archipelago, still suffers from a chronic lack of
development.
"There is not much economic capacity here and that means very high
levels of unemployment," he said. "Our only richness is the imagination of
the people."
Fogo has a population of around 40,000, most of whom are concentrated in
its small capital, Sao Filipe, a neat pleasant town of white-washed houses
and stone-paved streets.
Here there are clear signs of investment - new bars, hotels, a cybercafÃ©
and new houses under construction.
But away from the coast, a string of poor villages reveal a tougher side
to the island. They are inhabited by poor subsistence farmers, who depend
for survival on money sent home by relatives who left to seek work in Europe
and the United States.
Cape Verde, 450 km west of Senegal, forms part of the drought-prone
Sahel belt. Rain falls for only two or three months a year and sometimes not
at all.
Local farmers produce meager quantities of beans, corn, sugar cane and
sweet potatoes, but the island nation seldom manages to produce more than 11
percent of the food needed to keep its 430,000 people alive.
Public sector salaries are low, often less than $150 a month and the
water supply highly erratic.
Teachers readily admit that primary school attendance rates are only
