feed and to provide seeds for non-cereal crops.
"The funding of these projects is essential to strengthen the capacity
of vulnerable farmers, to make them more self-reliant and less dependent on
food aid," said Anne Bauer, director of Rome-based FAO's emergency
operations division.
Veterinary services in rural areas are poorly equipped for vaccination
campaigns. FAO has proposed strengthening veterinary services and training
veterinary staff
"Where necessary, vaccines should be provided to protect animals against
diseases," FAO said.
Drought has killed many farm animals in Ethiopia. FAO will help set up
fodder banks to feed animals during dry periods.
The food agency intends to hand out more than three million sweet potato
cuttings and vegetable seeds to the poorest families in SNNPR. About 50,000
farm families would benefit.
The number of hungry people has risen over the past decade in Ethiopia,
despite an increase in aid, FAO said.
Annual agricultural growth averages 2.4 percent, compared to a
population growth rate of 2.8 percent. This increases Ethiopia's annual food
deficit.
"There has been a trend towards declining investment in the (food)
deficit areas, whilst surplus producing areas were continually affected by
volatile prices that are hindering further investment," FAO said.

Original_Search_Title: Maganjo Cleared for AGOA
Headline: Uganda: Maganjo Cleared for AGOA
Body_Text:
Kampala — AN inspection team from the Whitaker Group visited the Kawempe-based
Maganjo Maize Millers early this week.
The Whitaker Group, a consultancy firm helping locals exploit the Africa
Growth Opportunity Act (AGOA), is owned by a former assistant US trade
representative, Rosa Whitaker.
Maganjo is one of the four companies that have been cleared by the firm
to benefit from the AGOA initiative. AGOA allows for duty/quota-free access
to the US market.
Whitaker's Susan Linye, said they had identified five Maganjo products
that could compete in the US domestic market.
These include Soy drink, Kwik instant porridge, Maganjo high protein
baby porridge and sweet potatoes.
Linye commended Maganjo for their packaging, but advised them to improve
on labelling.
Maganjo currently exports 200 five-kilogramme packets of maize flour
every month to the US by special arrangement.
"We send 200 packets of flour to the United States and 50,000 packets of
two and five kilos to John Whirler trading company in the UK every
month. Our brands compete fairly in East London," Alex Luggya, the sales and
marketing manager said.
He said: "We are ready for the challenge though we do not know how big
the US market is. They saw our competing brands in the UK that is how they
identified the company."
The visitors were led by Susan Muhwezi, the special presidential advisor
on AGOA and Wasswa Hashim, a commercial advisor.

Original_Search_Title: Luapula Constituency: Putting Facts Right
Headline: Zambia: Luapula Constituency: Putting Facts Right
Body_Text:
To most Zambians, the name Luapula Constituency is usually associated
with the whole of Luapula Province.
And to some extent, some people have ended up believing that it is a
parliamentary constituency representing the people of Luapula Province.
But all that is nowhere near the truth!
Though the name may suggest otherwise, there are more than 11
constituencies in Luapula Province and five well-established districts. The
Luapula Constituency falls under Samfya District which has three
parliamentary seats; Chifunabuli, Bangweulu and Luapula Constituencies.
Each of these has its own unique characteristics - geographically,
historically and economically.
CHIFUNABULI CONSTITUENCY
This constituency, with its name derived from Lake Chifunabuli, is
located in the northern part of the district. called Abena Ng'umbo, ruled by
five traditional chiefs belonging to "Abena Ng'oma clan - Chitembo,
Mwansakombe, Mwewa, Mulongwe of Mbabala and Mbulu of Chishi Island on Lake
Bangweulu.
The constituency covers Mundubi, Chitembo, Mashitolo, Lubwe Mission,
Masanta, Shikamushile, Chibingila, Mwewa, Miponda, Chibuye, Mbilimamwenge,
Chamalawa, Mwansakombe, Filipo, Thomas, Nambale, Chikuwe, Kasaba Mission as
well as Mbabala and Chishi Islands.
The traditional ceremony celebrated in this area is called Ukwanga which
includes people and chiefs of the Bangweulu swamps and the two islands;
Chishi and Mbabala. This ceremony is usually held in Chief Chitembo's
area.
The occupation of abena Ng'umbo is mainly fishing conducted along the
Ifunge Peninsula on the Kasaba river, Kamponda and Umumana Katongo.
There are no fishing activities in certain areas like Chibuye, Miponda
and Chamalawa. Usually these areas are referred to as "Ku masonde" and are
popular for farming activities for crops like groundnuts, sweet potatoes,
millet and cassava which is the staple food. Maize is also grown nowadays in
most parts of Ng'umbo land.
In the late 1970s, the UNIP government introduced a livestock scheme at
Kasaba Mission in Chief Mwansakombe's area and a farmers' training centre at
Nshungu in Mbilima-mwenge's area.
The Kasaba livestock scheme with the help of the Netherlands government
which used to second volunteers from Holland, was a haven of animals like
cattle, poultry, sheep and goats but this project was abandoned. The
infrastructure has since collapsed.
The constituency also boasts of a secondary school which offers boarding
facilities at Lubwe, a police station at Chief Mwewa's palace and some two
hospitals, thanks to the missionaries based at Kasaba and Lubwe
Missions.
In the old days, these included a leprosarium at Kasaba which no longer
functions.
There are two post offices, one at Lubwe and the other one at Mwewa
where even the only police post is found.
Rural health centres are spread in various places like Miponda,
Shikamushile and Chibuye.
Zesco has done a commendable job by providing electricity while Zamtel
phones have been installed in the area.
Prominent businessmen and politicians in the area include the departed
souls of Alex Kaunda Shapi, Luke Mwananshiku and archbishop of Kasama
Archdioceses Clement Chabu Kasansha after whom St Clement secondary school
in Mansa was named.
The current archbishop of Kasama James Spaita also hails from this area,
including prominent Lusaka lawyer Bupe Mwansa of E.B.M Chambers and Ndola
Lawyer John Kabuka.
Chifunabuli Constituenc y is second to Samfya Boma in terms of
development and prosperity. The current area MP is Kennedy Sakeni (MMD) who
is also Home Affairs deputy minister.
The author went to St Clement secondary school together with Mr Sakeni
who was a year ahead and late Mwansabombwe MP Mr Songobele Mungo.
Other former MPs in this constituency were Clement Mwananshiku, Ndalama
and Lusaka lawyer Ernest Mwansa.
BANGWEULU CONSTITUENCY
This constituency covers Samfya Boma, Mwamfuli village, Kapata
Peninsula, Katanshya, Panta Point, Chinsanka, Chief Mulakwa, East One,
Twingi Mission, Mabo Kunda, Twingi Mission, Chikunyu, Iyongolo, Kalasa
Mukoso and some areas along the Chinese built Tuta road.
It is the only constituency scooped by an independent candidate during
the 2001 tri-partite elections - Joseph Kasongo who previously held this
seat as MP in the UNIP government.
There are three chiefs in the Bangweulu constituency; Kasoma Bangweulu,
Mulakwa and Kalasa Mukoso. The people of the Bangweulu constituency are
called abena Kabende.
Like their counterparts in the Ng'umbo area, the main occupation of
abena Kabende is fishing and subsistence farming with their staple food
being cassava while maize is also grown on a small scale.
The constituency has two boarding secondary schools at Twingi Mission
and Samfya Boma which also has a day secondary school.
Rural health centres are located at Kasanka and the Zambia Flying Doctor
Service is in Chief Mulakwa's area.
Apart from Samfya Boma, most areas of the Bangweulu constituency have no
electricity, although in some areas there is solar power.
A modern market and trading shops are being built by the locals, a main
trading port for fishermen from the Copperbelt and far off places like
Kabwe, Nakonde and Lusaka who come to this area to buy fish.
There is urgent need for the Kapata Peninsula to be electrified by
Zesco. Zamtel should provide telephones while ZNBC should introduce TV
transmission to enhance development in this highly populated area of Samfya
district.
If Zesco can electrify Kapata Peninsula, institutions like Twingi
Mission, Twingi boarding secondary school and the health centre at East One
will greatly benefit from this project.
Since the Bangweulu Constituency has vast tracts of land not fully
utilised by the local people especially on the Tuta road, the Government of
the day, should liaise with the area MP and local chiefs to enable
interested people gain access to the land for farming purposes.
The other previous MPs were Scott Matafwali, Dan Pule and Enoch
Chikamba.
LUAPULA CONSTITUENCY
The Luapula Constituency should have been called "Lunga" Constituency
because the people of Luapula Constituency or the Bangweulu swamps are
called "Unga" tribe.
To reach Luapula Constituency, one does not need a car but a canoe,
popularly called "Ubwange" in a local language while a paddling stick is
called "Inkafi" or "umubale." There are no roads and motor vehicles in the
whole of Luapula Constituency because the area is surrounded by water from
the Bangweulu Swamps.
There are basically eight Islands on Bangweulu swamps with the main
Islands being Kasoma Lunga, Bwalya Mponda, Kalimankonde, Matongo, and
Nsalushi.
Traditional chiefs of the Bangweulu Swamps are Kalimankonde, Kasoma
Lunga, Bwalya Mponda while Nsamba of Nsalushi Island and Matongo are Unga
chiefs.
The main occupation of the Unga people is fishing. There is a lot of
fish in these swamps and most traders from the Copperbelt commonly known as
"abasulu" flock to the swamps to trade in fish.
Often, fish is exchanged with "Salaula" (second hand clothes), salt and
mealie meal.
Firewood is scarce on the Islands. People travel as far as Chilubi
Island, Chinsanka, Katansha, Mayuka and Mulikashi in Mpika District to fetch
firewood.
Thus, there is need to re-introduce tree planting for wood fuel by
reviving the nursery which was based at Mwananjelwe's village, Chief
Nsamba's area.
This nursery was introduced by the colonial authorities, under the Unga
Native Authority to transform the Islands on the Swamps into a forest so
that the natives as they were called then, could use the trees in various
ways including domestic chores.
However, this project was discontinued in the late 1970s and to date,
there is no trace of the nursery.
There are no major agricultural activities on the Islands, although rice
and cassava are grown including sweet potatoes on a small scale.
There is need to introduce rice farming on a large scale and this can be
achieved by encouraging experts from countries like China, Korea, or
Japan. Crops like maize cannot do well because of the sandy soils.
The other possibility is to introduce livestock animals, like cattle,
sheep and goats since the Bangweulu swamps have similar geographical
features comparable to the Barotse plains in Western Province.
At the moment, there is not a single edible domestic animal apart from
chickens.
May be it is time the people of Bangweulu swamps formed a co-operative
to cater for local fishermen, by accessing loans in form of fishing nets,
outboard motor engines and banana boats.
In 1969, the UNIP Government gave boats and five horse power Seagull
engines with the assistance of the Chinese government, to fishermen at
Mbabala Island, Chishi Island and the Bangweulu swamps; a project which was
launched by the then Republic vice- president Simon Mwansa Kapwepwe.
With the New Deal Government of President Mwanawasa and the
re-introduction of the Co-operative Bank, the forming of a co-operative
would definitely be of great benefit to the Islanders.
The Bangweulu swamps also constitute a game management area, but the
local people have not benefited from the Zambia Wildlife Authority (ZAWA),
like their counterparts in the Luangwa Valley of Eastern Province.
Even local game assistants are paid peanuts ranging from K20,000 to
K30,000 per month.
There are only two basic schools at Nsamba and Kasoma Lunga Islands, the
rest are primary schools found at Matongo, Itala, Chisale, Kalimankonde and
Mutamwina while there are no schools at Kabulu and Mandwe Islands.
With no single secondary school in the area, pupils have to depend on
schools situated on the mainland. As a result, most pupils end up only in
grade seven and girls are usually forced into early marriages.
There is no hospital in the whole swamp area apart from under-staffed
rural health centres only found at Nsamba, Kasoma Lunga and Bwalya Mponda,
usually manned by unqualified medical personnel. Serious medical cases have
to be referred to Santa Maria on Chilubi Island or Samfya.
But all in all, the people of the Bangweulu swamps are very hard
working, friendly, and accommodating. The only thing they need now, is
proper and selfless representation in Parliament so that they can also
benefit from the national cake.
The author is grandson of Chief Nsamba of the Unga people of the
Bangweulu Swamps.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Permanent Secretary Calls for Farming System That Mitigates Weather Fluctuations
Headline: Zambia: Agriculture Permanent Secretary Calls for Farming System That Mitigates Weather Fluctuations
Body_Text:
Lusaka — WE need a farming system that mitigates effects of seasonal weather
fluctuations, agriculture permanent secretary Elizabeth Phiri said
yesterday.
Opening the Small Holder Access to Processing, Extension and Seeds
Project (SHAPES) workshop organised by Prevention Against Malnutrition
(PAM), Phiri said SHAPES seed growers had produced 600 metric tonnes of
grain and legume seeds in the 2002/2003 farming season.
She said the first two seasons of SHAPES (2000/2001 and 2001/2002) were
marred by drought and floods that affected the southern half of Zambia
leaving more than two million people food insecure.
"This situation contributed to the exhaustion of the coping abilities of
the affected households and increased the security of food insecurity
drawing government's attention to humanitarian aid," she said.
Phiri said despite the situation, the majority of the 3,400 smallholder
farmers targeted by the project during the 2001/2002 season were food secure
as a result of adopting the technologies promoted by the project.
She said in the 2002/2003 season, more than 4,500 smallholder growers
benefited from the programme and more than 85 per cent of them had enough
food for consumption and managed a surplus for sell.
She said in the 12 districts that SHAPES operated, each operational
district had cassava and sweet potato nurseries for multiplication
purposes.
Phiri said demand for cassava production and processing as a major
complimentary staple and food security had increased resulting in SHAPES
failing to cope with increased demand for training in cassava processing and
storage.
She appealed to donors to extend their support to SHAPES for another
three seasons so that experiences of the project could be consolidated and
facilitate continued assistance to other farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Biotech Can Bring Green Revolution to Africa, Expert Says
Headline: Africa: Biotech Can Bring Green Revolution to Africa, Expert Says
Publisher: United States Department of State (Washington, DC)
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — (The following article appears in the September 2003 issue of the State Department's electronic journal, Economic Perspectives. The issue is titled: Agricultural Biotechnology. The entire journal can be viewed at: http://usinfo.state.gov/journals/journals.htm)
THE ROLE OF AGRICULTURAL BIOTECHNOLOGY IN WORLD FOOD AID
By Bruce Chassy, Professor and Executive Associate Director of the Biotechnology Center at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
Biotechnology has the potential to play a key role in reducing chronic hunger, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, which missed out on the "green revolution" of the 1960s and 1970s, says Bruce Chassy, professor and executive associate director of the Biotechnology Center at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. He urges more public investment in agricultural research, education and training at the local, national and regional levels.
Food aid is one of several global mechanisms created to deal with hunger and food insecurity. The need for food aid around the globe varies from specific responses to acute and episodic shortages to long-term donations of food to abate continuing chronic inability of some regions to become agriculturally self-sufficient. While agricultural biotechnology is not a panacea to food insecurity, it is likely to play a vital role in the delivery of food assistance and reduction of hunger for generations to come.
THE GLOBAL NEED FOR FOOD AID
The U.N. Universal Declaration of Human Rights declares the right of access to food and freedom from hunger as a fundamental right.
Although we live in a world of unprecedented prosperity and technological development, 800-850 million people are malnourished. More than 200 million of these are children, many of whom will never reach their full intellectual and physical potential. Another 1-1.5 billion humans have only marginally better access to food and often do not consume balanced diets containing sufficient quantities of all required nutrients.
The majority of this nutritionally at-risk population lives in developing countries. Most, perhaps 75 percent, live in rural agricultural regions. Most are very poor. There is a well-recognized link between poverty and hunger. In fact, family income is probably the single most important determinant of adequacy of access to food. The World Food Summit in 2002 reaffirmed a commitment made by the international community five years earlier to halve the number of hungry people by the year 2015. That goal will not be met unless agricultural productivity and personal income can be improved in the world's poorest regions.
It is argued by some that eliminating poverty is more important than producing more food since there is more than enough food produced in the world to feed everyone. Economists tell us that there is a surplus of food in the world - or at least a surplus of grain that when tabulated as potential caloric intake could theoretically adequately feed the current global population. But the sad lesson of both recent and ancient history is that adequate food supplies do not reach everyone. The large number of hungry people proves that. It is pointless to argue whether poor agricultural productivity or extreme poverty is more to blame when people are starving. What is clear is that if the rural poor can produce a surplus of food in a more efficient and sustainable manner, there will be adequate food supplies, increasing income and the opportunity for supporting rural development.
While most experts would agree that the only long-term solution to hunger is economic development and the elimination of poverty, people who are food self-sufficient through local or regional agriculture will not go hungry. Unfortunately, neither the required increases in agricultural productivity nor the necessary rural development will happen overnight. The question then becomes "What do we do in the meanwhile?" The short-term solution for the hungry is food aid. But even food aid has become politicized as skeptics have charged that it is simply a way for rich over-producing nations to eliminate the surpluses produced by their heavily subsidized farmers. The skeptics also assert that food aid robs local farmers of markets and makes them hungrier. These arguments ignore the daily reality faced by hundreds of millions of hungry people for whom the immediate alternatives are simple: continued hunger and ultimate starvation or the acceptance of food aid.
ELIMINATING CHRONIC HUNGER:
A ROLE FOR BIOTECHNOLOGY
The Green Revolution of the 1960s and 1970s helped India and China and other Asian countries become agriculturally self-sufficient net exporters of food in the last three decades. The increased productivity has been accompanied by increases in personal income and stimulus to national economies. Similarly, through application of new technology, agricultural productivity per hectare has doubled in most developed countries in the same timeframe. The development of new high-productivity agricultural technologies resulted from investment in agricultural research performed in government laboratories, research universities, and non-governmental institutes such as the Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research (CGIAR) centers scattered around the globe. A crucial element of success has been the deployment of effective systems of outreach education and technology transfer. Research and technology transfer has also taken place in the private sector.
For a variety of complex reasons, improvements in agricultural productivity did not take place in all developing countries. Quite the contrary, some of the least developed countries are now even less able to produce sufficient food. There, the Green Revolution never happened. While civil unrest and political corruption may have contributed greatly to this phenomenon, from an agricultural point of view, the failure lies in the lack of investment in and adoption of new technologies and management practices. Often this occurred because there was not sufficient attention paid or investment made in research to develop effective local or region-specific strategies and technologies.
Sub-Saharan Africa is a region where growth in agricultural production has not kept pace with expanding need. As a whole, the region has some of the poorest and most depleted agricultural soils. Only 4 percent of the farmed land is irrigated. Significant areas of agricultural land are at risk of becoming desert while in some parts of the region excessive humidity and high temperatures contribute to a high incidence of disease and pests. Weeds such as Striga stifle yields. Droughts are commonplace in some parts of the region. Outright crop failure is common and poor yields are endemic. There is clearly a need to develop crop varieties and management strategies that are more productive under these conditions. High on the list of desired traits are crops with enhanced resistance to environmental stresses such as drought, temperature and salinity; enhanced resistance to diseases and pests; and improved agronomic properties and yield potential. The heavy reliance on a few staple crops makes biofortification - the boosting of the vitamin and mineral components of foods to enhance the nutritional value - an attractive strategy as well.
Recent advances in molecular biology and genomics greatly enhance the plant breeder's capacity to introduce new traits into plants. Commercial applications of agricultural biotechnology have already produced crops such as Bt-maize, rice, potatoes, cotton and sweet corn (sweet maize) that can protect themselves against insects; virus-resistant papaya, squash and potatoes; and herbicide-tolerant crops such as wheat, maize, sugar cane, rice, onions and beets that allow more effective weed management.
There is accumulating evidence that these biotech crops can be more productive and profitable for farmers. Major reductions in costs for labor, energy and chemicals have been documented. The crops have also proven to be environmentally-friendly, particularly with regard to biodiversity, reduction of agricultural chemicals in soil and water, and decreased exposure of workers and communities to chemicals.
There is also an emerging international consensus of scientific and regulatory opinion that crops derived through biotechnology are safe to eat as food and feed and beneficial for the environment. These and other promising technologies are now being directed at improving the production and yield of African staple crops: banana, cassava, maize, millets, oil crops, peanut, potato, rice, sorghum, soybean, sweet potato and wheat. Protein-enhanced sweet potatoes and potatoes and carotene-enhanced rice and oilseeds promise to improve the nutritional value of the diet. Thus, over the long term, agricultural biotechnology promises to play a crucial role in the improving agricultural productivity and reducing the environmental impact of agriculture leading to agricultural sustainability and food security in many regions of the world. While it would be foolish to say that agricultural biotechnology alone will solve the world's food problems, it would be equally foolish to assert that food insecurity can be eliminated without agricultural biotechnology.
In recent years, there has been a significant change in the organization of agricultural research directed at improving food security. It is now recognized that research needs to be done at local, national and regional levels in order to address specific agricultural challenges and produce new varieties appropriate to local agriculture and customs. This change is particularly focused on utilizing and expanding local scientific and agricultural human and capital infrastructure that can work in partnership with international scientists and funding. Although the path is clear and there are numerous successful examples of these kinds of international partnership, global funding for such activities falls far short of that the level required to achieve global food security in the next decades.
RECENT CHALLENGES POSED BY ACUTE FOOD SHORTAGES
Widespread local or regional crop failure often leads to acute food shortages and hunger. The reason for episodic events can be as varied as flood, droughts or civil war. The United Nations, national governments and an assortment of nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) often respond by mobilizing an immediate food aid program. Food aid distribution can be hindered by lack of infrastructure for storage and transportation of food, and there are often concerns for the security of aid workers.
Recently, a new obstacle to food aid distribution has been identified. Repeated crops failures in Southern Africa have placed millions of people in six nations at risk. In response, the United States offered food aid that included substantial shipments of maize. The maize supply in the United States is approximately 30-35 percent insect-protected Bt-maize developed through biotechnology. This variety of maize had been approved by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) and the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as safe for consumption as food and feed. It was commingled with conventional maize in the U.S. commodity system. However, since the intended recipient nations did not use biotech seed varieties and imported few commodities such as maize, they for the most part lacked specific laws and regulatory systems with respect to foods produced through biotechnology. Genetically modified (GM) maize was an unapproved food in their regulatory systems. In light of the global scare campaign against GM foods, several countries hesitated to accept the aid. Ultimately, intensive international consultation and fact-finding satisfied all of these countries save Zambia, which continued to refuse GM food aid. One obvious conclusion to be drawn from this experience is that regulatory systems and training need to be in place before the need for food aid again arises.
PUBLIC INVESTMENT IN RESEARCH, EDUCATION AND TRAINING
What the experience of recent decades has taught is that agricultural biotechnology can be a powerful tool in the development of improved crop varieties for developing countries. The promised benefits can only be realized in a permanent and sustainable manner when the countries that benefit play a role in defining the need, developing the solution and implementing the education and technology-transfer systems. Each nation must decide what agricultural goals are in its national interest and what technologies are consistent with consumer acceptance and customs. Shared ownership leads to good stewardship.
Partnerships that lead to shared ownership can solve another challenge to applying technology. One major concern about agricultural biotechnology is that the seeds are owned and sold by large multi-national corporations who might eventually exert external domination and control local seed markets and farmers. An additional problem is that developing countries may have limited access to intellectual property rights that would provide them access to modern agricultural technologies such as new seed types. To help counter these challenges and promote public sector uses in developing countries, a consortium of public universities and public sector institutions has recently announced the formation of the Public Sector Intellectual Property Resource for Agriculture (PIPRA). PIPRA will work to make public-sector research available to more of the people who want it and insure freedom to operate. Multi-national corporations have also demonstrated their willingness to donate their technology and expertise to such efforts.
There is a holistic answer to all these food security needs and concerns. The global community needs to invest more capital in creating agricultural institutions and infrastructure in countries that face food security challenges. Investment must be made in legal and regulatory systems, agricultural research, transportation and processing systems, and education. The success of the Land Grant University system in improving agriculture and contributing broadly to society in the United States over the last 140 years demonstrates that the development of human capital and educational systems is as important as scientific discovery. The creation of institutions and public/foundation funding mechanisms would create a platform for international collaboration that is open to government, university and private-sector collaborators. If the world community is to arrive at its stated goal of food security for every person, it must put aside ideological and political divisions and pragmatically embrace each and every technology that leads to sustainable food security.
Note: The opinions expressed in this article do not necessarily reflect the views or policies of the U.S. Department of State.

Original_Search_Title: Current Crop Outlook Optimistic
Headline: Rwanda: Current Crop Outlook Optimistic
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Unless unfavourable rains develop during the latter part of this current
agricultural season harvest should be normal in Rwanda, FEWS NET reported in
its 18 August update on food security in the country.
"It is estimated that Seasons C [July-November] will contribute about 15
percent of annual food production, which significantly alleviates the
severity of hunger periods in October-November," it reported.
It said that because of recent sparse rainfall, there should be no need
for distribution of sweet potato vines for planting, unlike in 2002, except
for parts of Bugesera region. FEWS said that the UN Food and Agricultural
Organization (FAO) planned to distribute 6.3 million cuttings of Creolina, a
cassava variety moderately resistant to the mosaic virus, in October.
It said there was a current "severe shortage" of mosaic-resistant
varieties of cassava, especially in Bugesera where the root tuber was the
main source of food and income. Distributions of cassava cuttings will be in
Bugesera and Mayaga regions, and the provinces of Kibungo and Umutara.
"This quantity should be enough for planting about 630 hectares, but
this falls short of current needs not only in Bugesera but across the
country," FEWS reported.
A joint team comprising officials of the Ministry of Agriculture and the
FAO made a food assessment mission in June and confirmed that production at
the national level was "essentially satisfactory", FEWS reported. However,
it said, Bugesera region in Kigali Province experienced a major shortfall of
production. The team also found localised pockets of production shortfalls
in Umutara, Kibungo, Gikongoro, Butare, Kibuye, Cyangugu and Ruhengeri
provinces.
FEWS said the situation in Kikongoro and Ruhengeri provinces were
becoming "increasingly worrying".
It added, "The shortfall of the WFP [World Food Programme] food pipeline
in Rwanda remains a serious concern. From August 2003 to January 2004 there
will be a gap of 4,885 mt if new donations are not made now. Otherwise, the
ongoing protracted relief and recovery operation which includes food
assistance for refugees, nutrition centers and drought affected [areas]
(i.e. Bugesera) will need to be reduced."
FEWS said the situation in Bugesera prompted the ministry, international
relief NGO CARITAS, the Rwandan Red Cross and others "to provide free food
distribution to the households most affected by the production shortfall in
the worst hit sectors of Gashora and Nyamata districts as early as June",
FEWS reported. In addition, it said, the ministry provided goats to affected
residents so that they would not sell their assets, thus helping to maintain
farmers' livelihoods.
"The mission recommended that the households most vulnerable to these
localized shortfalls be considered for food assistance by WFP and partners,"
FEWS reported.
The team also recommended that FEWS, WFP and partners monitor closely
the provinces falling short of their minimum daily requirements of 2,100
kilo calories per capita - namely Ruhengeri, Gisenyi, Butare and
Gikongoro.
FEWS NET, the Famine Early Warning Systems Network, is a USAID-funded
activity.  [Full report on
http://www.fews.net/current/monthlies/report/?submit=y&m=1001002&f=rw]

Original_Search_Title: Of Unity, Peace, Democracy And Development
Headline: Malawi: Of Unity, Peace, Democracy And Development
Body_Text:
Blantyre — Opening address by His Excellency the President Dr. Bakili Muluzi to
the National Conference of the United Democracy Front, Chichiri
Conference Hall Blantyre, 8th August, 2003.
On this most auspicious day, as we gather to begin the proceedings of
the 2003 National Conference of the United Democracy Front, I stand
here in all humility to salute you all in the name of Unity, Peace,
Democracy and Development.
I welcome you all with joy and warmth, and I welcome you with
profound appreciation for your noble presence. You have travelled
from far and near, you have come from every corner of our beloved
land, you have come from villages, towns and cities, you represent
all walks of life, the young and the old, women and men, the poor and
the not-so-poor.
You have come, not because you have nothing to do in your various
homes, but because you have answered the call of national duty. You
were called to national duty by your beloved party, the United
Democratic Front, the party of Unity, Peace, Democracy and
Development, and I thank you for answering that call without
hesitation.
I wish at this moment to take the opportunity to welcome our special
guests, those who have come here from across our borders in response
to our invitation for them to come and be with us at this conference.
We thank you for accepting our invitation and we cherish your
presence as a mark of your solidarity with the goals that our sister
parties share, namely, the uplifting of the lives of our people,
under the common banner of Unity, Peace, Democracy and Development.
Although you have come here as observers, we hope that your presence
will provide us with an opportunity to share ideas and experiences.
We feel we should be learning from each other, and there is no reason
why we should be trying to re-invent the wheel when you may have
already found some answers to the common challenges that we face in
trying to respond to the aspirations of our people.
I wish also to extend a warm welcome to the Members of the Diplomatic
Corps and Representatives of International Organisations here
present. You are our partners in development, and your presence here
serves to underline the confidence that we have in our relationship
with you, and appreciation of your work in Malawi.
Before I proceeded let me recognise here the presence of the Second
Vice President, the Right Honourable Chakufwa Chihana, who is also
here in his capacity as President of the Alliance for Democracy
(AFORD). You are by now all aware that the UDF is working very
closely with AFORD and, with the support of this Conference, will
continue with this cooperation in the forthcoming general elections.
Mr. Chairman, Ladies and Gentlemen, I invite you to take a moment to
reflect with me on our party the United Democratic Front, and where
it has come from. The UDF was formed to fight against the one party
dictatorship and bring democracy and genuine development to the
people of Malawi. Anybody who remembers those dark days of our
struggle will agree that we have come a long way. As we sit here
today, let us remind each other that what we have achieved so far has
been the result of the dedication and sacrifices of many who are not
here with us today.
Heroes of the United Democratic Front and indeed of the country as a
whole are many and varied in their backgrounds and contributions. The
first heroes we must salute are the unknown ones, the ones whose
names will never appear in the history books.
Many perished in the cruel prisons of the MCP; many were thrown to
the crocodiles. Many, although still alive, have not made it to any
prominent positions, but are living their anonymous lives with the
satisfaction that Malawi is a better place to live in now because of
the part they played in the struggle. These are honourable men and
women who deserve our gratitude.
Then there are all those other heroes whose names we are fortunate to
remember. Names like the late Edward Bwanali; the late Dr. Namkumba;
the late Professor Chilibvumbo; the late Alhaji Shaibu Itimu; the
late Wenham Nakanga; the late Collins Chizumila; the late Chakala
Chaziya; the late Dr Mapopa Chipeta; the late Dr Mzunda; the late
Bundaunda Phiri; the late Chenda Mkandawire; the late Greenwell
Mponela; and more recently the late Emmanuel Chimphenzu and the late
Chanache MP who was taken from us only a few days ago and many other
Members of Parliament and party officials too numerous to mention
here.
In remembering all these gallant departed fighters mentioned here and
those not mentioned, may I request you all to stand and join me in
observing one moment of silence in their memory.
(May their souls rest in peace)
Mr. Chairman, Ladies and Gentlemen, in reflecting on the goals and
achievements of our party, I believe we should focus our minds on the
themes of Unity, Peace, Democracy and Development, which happen to be
the themes chosen for this conference. These are values that I
personally strongly believe in. Although these are separate concepts
they are in fact interrelated in very crucial ways. We need Peace,
Democracy and Development, but to achieve these we need Unity and a
sense of nationhood.
For Malawi to exist as a nation we all must first accept the
fundamental concept of unity. A nation cannot be called a nation
unless its various and diverse parts are considered parts of one
entity. When I speak so frequently about unity, the aim is to drive
home this point.
This nation of ours made up of many different parts: three regions,
many districts, many chieftaincies, many villages, many families,
many language groups, and many cultural traditions. This nation of
ours is made of Christians and Muslims, and even within these
religions there are many groups and denominations. Our nation, like
all nations today without exception, is a cultural mosaic.
But despite this great variety we are one nation, and because of this
great variety we must all work hard to ensure the survival of this
sense of nationhood. Unity and nationhood requires the person who
lives in Nsanje to care about what is happening to the person in
Chitipa, and the person who lives in Mangochi to accept the one who
lives in Kasungu as his brother.
Sometimes our differences are more pronounced than at other times.
Sometimes those who do not wish our nation well may seek to divide us
by constantly reminding us of our differences. Some within our nation
may seek to achieve their purely selfish goals by dwelling on and
exploiting our differences.
At such times it is the duty of all of us who believe in nationhood
to preach the importance of unity and remind each other that we are
all members of one family. Let us thank God that we have lived in
unity ever since this nation came into being, and I call upon every
member of the UDF to be in the forefront of preaching, promoting and
practising unity and tolerance.
Mr. Chairman, Ladies and Gentlemen, we need Unity, Democracy and
Development, but we can only have these if we also have peace. The
notion of nationhood requires that the members of one nation be able
to live with each other in peaceful harmony. This may sometimes not
be easy when the differences I have outlined are allowed to stand in
the way.
We often think of peace first and foremost as the absence of war and
conflict. We know for a fact that where there is war people are not
free to work, and therefore these can be no development. Where there
is war there is mutual fear and suspicion, so there is no unity. And
of course where there is war there cannot possibly be democracy.
There are many factors that have retarded democracy in Africa, but
prominent among them is war. We are all only too familiar with the
death and destruction which wars have caused in too many African
countries. We do not live on planet Mars. We live in this same world
and on this same continent, and when conflicts break out, we members
of the UDF must take an interest and ask ourselves what has caused
those conflicts. Then we must look within ourselves and ensure that
we do not engage in practices or injustices that can lead to
conflict.
Our Members of Parliament, from the UDF, the MCP and AFORD, sit in
parliament to discuss issues on which they may not always be in
agreement. Sometimes they even get very angry and shout at each
other. When this happens, do not get upset. It is better that they
shout at each other in Parliament and shake hands afterwards, than
that they shoot at each other on the streets or in the bush as is the
case elsewhere. Your party, the UDF, fought for democracy in order
that differences may be settled in a way that maintains peace for our
nation.
Of course, peace does not only mean the absence of war. For there to
be peace, all our citizens must feel at home and a sense of
belonging. During the one-party era, there was really no war going
on, but I ask you, was there peace? Did my brother Chakufwa Chihana
here have peace when he was locked up at Mikuyu? Did the Malawians
who run away to foreign countries have peace in exile? Did the poor
woman who could not go to the market because she had no MCP card have
peace?
Did the parents who could not find money to send their children to
primary school have peace? Did the mother in the village who had no
borehole to provide safe drinking water for her child have peace?
Did the young people who could not wear bell-bottom trousers have
peace? Did the Jehova's Witnesses who could not worship the way they
wanted have peace? Did the journalists who could not report what they
saw have peace? Did the university lecturers who were not free to
read certain books have peace?
This then, is what the UDF believes in, this is our ideology namely,
that if we are champions of democracy, then we must also champion
peace not only as the absence of war, but also as the presence of the
justice and the freedoms that are required for every citizen to fully
realize themselves. For peace to be maintained, we must continue to
safeguard the material and spiritual well-being of every individual
citizen. For there to be meaningful peace, the needs of the people
must be addressed through pro-poor development initiatives.
This is the UDF philosophy, and that is why we not only fought for
freedom, human rights and democracy, but as a party in government we
have also tirelessly pursued policies that are aimed at benefiting
the poor, such as introducing free primary education so that even the
poorest child has access to education.
Here is what we did. We increased the number of primary school
classrooms from 19,909 in 1993 to 52,022 in 2002/2003. We increased
the number of teachers' houses from 15,708 in 1993 to 29,603 in 2003.
We increased the number of qualified teachers 23,443 in 1993 to
31,122 in 2003. We increased the number of textbooks distributed for
free from 1,413,200 in 1993 to 11.4 million in 2002/2003.
We did these things because when education is more easily accessible,
there is peace.
We are honest enough to admit that we faced some hurdles in our
efforts to achieve food security. But despite unexpected difficulties
related to the vagaries of weather and unfair world market prices, we
made significant achievements made in the agriculture sector.
First and foremost we introduced the famous and highly successful
Starter Pack Programme, in which hybrid maize seed, legume seed and
fertilizer are distributed to smallholder farmers.
This initiative along with others has had a major role in achieving
this year's 2.2 million tonnes of maize production, more than last
year's. Since the scheme started, a total of 10.66 million metric
small holder farmers have benefited and have produced a total of 10.9
million metric tonnes of maize.
We also introduced the Agricultural Productivity Investment Programme
(APIP) aimed at making fertilizer and maize seeds more affordable to
smallholder farmers. The programme has benefited more than 1.2
million farmers since its inception in the 1997/98 season.
To ensure that smallholder farmers purchase inputs like fertilizer,
we established a sustainable rural financial credit system through
the Malawi Rural Fianance Company (MRFC), covering tobacco and maize
growers. The financial institution has positively contributed to the
increase in maize and tobacco production.
We also liberalized smallholder burly tobacco production and
marketing, thus enabling more than 600,000 small holder farmers to
benefit through increased cash incomes. We established a credit
guarantee scheme to improve the uptake of inputs for tobacco and this
has benefited over 1044 clubs who in turn contributed a total of 42.1
million kgs of burley, amounting to K4.3 billion.
We liberalized agricultural markets and diversified smallholder
agricultural production into more drought-tolerant food staples
including cassava, sweet potatoes, millet, sorghum and legumes, which
have contributed significantly to food security.
To ensure increased production of cash and food crops, we liberalized
fertilizer and seed markets to promote private sector participation
in importation and distribution, thereby promoting competition and
ensuring availability of fertilizer in the country. To this end, the
National Fertilizer Buffer Stock, which used to be idle for years,
was used to promote the development of the fertilizer private sector,
to the benefit of large, medium and smallholder farmers throughout
the country.
We supported irrigation development through the promotion of treadle
pumps, motorized pumps, sprinkler and gravity fed schemes, and river
diversion irrigation technologies. To achieve this, we increased the
allocation thousand-fold from K1 million prior to 1994 to more than
K1 billion now.
We have increased the area under irrigation from 35,000 hectares in
1994 to 62,000 hectares in 2003. We have earmarked 100,800 hectares
by irrigation, reaching out 336,000 farm families. To date, we have
developed 180.93 km of inland canals, we have distributed about
12,500 treadle pumps and we will distribute 46,000 treadle pumps this
season. We have constructed three new irrigation schemes, namely,
Bwanje covering 800 hectares, Miyombo covering 80 hectares, and
Khwisa covering 40 hectares, all of which will benefit over 12,000
farm families.
Besides the new schemes, we have rehabilitated Domasi, Lufira, Wovwe,
Hara, Chonanga, Bua, Kasitu, Likowa, and Lifidzi. We are promoting
sustainable investment and development of small-scale irrigation by
local communities in which 65,000 hectares will be under irrigation,
benefiting about 650,000 farm families.
In line with our policy that no stone should be left unturned, we
have also ventured into new non-traditional crops with the
introduction of a new Macadamia clone for estates and small holders
farmers such as, new cassava and sweet potato varieties, new
groundnut varieties and new rice varieties. Furthermore we have
successfully promoted the use of low cost soil fertility to improve
technologies in manure making where 1,2327,44 farm families used
manure on 365,589 hectares.
In an effort to regulate imports of livestock and livestock products
and to protect the local industry, we have taken measures towards the
revival of the local industry, which had previously been facing stiff
competition from imports from other countries. We have introduced tax
exemption on livestock production and imported inputs to promote
investment in the livestock sector. We have conducted research on
using local materials for making livestock feeds.
We are doing all these things to ensure food security, because when
people have food in their stomachs, there is peace.
Mr. Chairman, Ladies and Gentlemen, water in life, and your party's
performance in the provision of water demonstrates how much we really
believe that without water there can be no life.
As opposed to the about 9,000 boreholes drilled by the previous
government in 31 years, we have drilled no less than 21,020 boreholes
in less than 10 years, servicing an additional population of more
than 625,000.
In addition to this, more Malawians now have access to piped water,
Mulunguzi Dam on Zomba Mountain was constructed to guarantee reliable
water supply to the people of urban Zomba and surrounding areas
putting an end to an age-old problem. More than 15 old rural gravity
fed piped water supply schemes have been rehabilitated, while 26
district urban water supply schemes have also been expanded.
This had increased coverage by Rural Piped Water Supply Schemes
covering more than one million people within the nine years that your
party has been in power, compared to the previous regime, which
supplied to only to under a million people in 31 years.
In this short time the UDF government has also constructed more than
130 small community dams and five medium dams to conserve water for
water supply, livestock, irrigation and recreation.
To ensure adequate water supply to urban areas in Malawi Regional
Water Boards have been established. We have also promoted private
sector involvement in protecting and rehabilitating catchment areas
of Lunyangwa dam in Mzuzu, Chitete dam in Kasungu as well as
Mulunguzi in Zomba.
We have carried out studies to increase water supply for the Lilongwe
and Blantyre water boards. In addition, community water, sanitation
and health project in Thyolo and Phalombe districts are being
implemented. The Lilongwe-Dedza groundwater development and
Mangochi-east water projects are both underway.
To ensure the success of water supply programmes, we have
strengthened hydrology, scientific and laboratory services of the
Ministry of Water Development, where new hydrological and laboratory
equipment, vehicles, watercrafts and materials have been procured.
The water resources board, the Integrated Water Resources Development
Plan for Lake Malawi and Shire river system, and the Songwe river
course stabilization project have also be instituted.
District centre sanitation rehabilitation and upgrading are underway
in Karonga, Mzimba, Kasungu, Salima, Liwonde, Mangochi and Zomba
districts. Training of water point committees throughout the country
on issues of water hygiene under the community based management
programme has been successful.
We have done all these things, because when people have access to
safe drinking and sanitation water, there is peace.
To safeguard the health of our people we built Mzuzu, Central
Lilongwe, Chikwawa, and Nkhata Bay District Hospitals, and we built
health centres at Mitundu, Kabudula, Malembo, and Chadza.
Construction work for Chiradzulu, Thyolo District Hospital is
expected to finish this year and Chitipa District Hospital and Zomba
General Hospital constructions will definitely be completed next
year. We have also embarked on the construction of Nkhotakota and
Balaka District Hospitals. We are doing this because when people have
easy access to good medical treatment, there is peace.
We have embarked on programmes to redress shortage of personnel in
the health sector. Government and non-governmental health teaching
institutions are now operating at full capacity with double annual
intakes while the College of Medicine has tripled intake this year
from 20 to 60 to redress shortage of personnel in the health service
sector. For example, 500 nursing personnel graduated this year, which
is the largest ever number of graduates in one year; 100 clinical
offices and 188 medical assistants are expected to graduate very soon
together with laboratory, radiology, pharmacy technicians;
representing at least a 100% increase. When people have easier access
to nurses and doctors there is peace of mind.
We enacted the Labour Relations Act, ratified the International
Labour Organizations' conventions, and accorded our workers the
freedom to form associations to defend and watch their interests
through the Malawi Congress of Trade Union because when there good
industrial relations, there is peace.
To ensure freedom and greater choices for the people, we liberalized
the airwaves by introducing a second channel on Malawi Broadcasting
Corporation (MBC) and licensed five private and community radio
station, namely, ABC Radio in Lilongwe, Radio FM 101 in Blantyre,
Radio Maria in Mangochi, Dzimwe Community Radio in Mangochi and
Capital Radio 102.5 in Blantyre.
To enable more Malawians to communicate with each other and the rest
of the world, we increased telephone lines from 35,000 during the MCP
era to no less than 110,000 lines.
Today Anachisale in Chiradzulu in now better able to speak to
Anyagondwe in Rumphi.
But for Anaphiri in Chikwawa to see for herself what Anachanze in
Lilongwe is doing in her village, we went on to introduce a public
Television Broadcasting Service for the first time in the history of
Malawi.
To safeguard the freedom of the people we scrapped censorship of
newspapers, books and other publications. Today Malawi has more 20
newspapers as against only two that had been wholly controlled by the
MCP for more than 31 years, and these publications are free to
criticize the UDF and the government because that is exactly what we
fought for.
We promote these freedoms because when people are free to say what
they want, when we tolerate each other's views then there is peace.
The proliferation of newspapers, magazine and private radio stations,
many of which specialise in maligning the UDF and the government, is
one of the most important gains of democracy under the UDF
government. Malawi is probably the only country in Africa where the
only daily newspapers are both independent and definitely not
pandering to the whims of ruling party.
These newspapers can never claim to be afraid of pointing out the
wrongs that they see in government and the ruling party.
Almost on a daily basis the front pages and opinion columns are full
of such criticisms and even castigations. We may not always agree
with what they say. But democracy requires that the public have
access to different and opposing views, and we as the ruling party
have a duty and are determined to promote such freedoms.
Members of the UDF must be proud of this because it demonstrates the
freedom that prevails under the UDF government. The journalists who
expose mistakes and misdeeds are our friends and the friends of
democracy. If they did not care about the UDF they would not point
out when something is going wrong, and they would not come in such
large numbers as they have done today!
Let us therefore cherish the freedom of the media as one of our proud
achievements. And the best way to celebrate this freedom is to listen
to both the media and those who admonish us, and to protect and
safeguard their freedom, building on the positive achievements but
also learning from past mistakes and taking into account what our
critics say.
I could give more examples, but there is really no need, because you
can find a more detailed account in the Report, which has been
submitted to you by you the outgoing National Executive Committee of
your party. They have submitted the Report because as a democratic
party you have the right to demand that your Ministers explain
whether they have fulfilled the pledges contained in the manifestos
of 1994 and 1999.
I have deliberately placed all these issues under the theme on peace
mainly in order to emphasize that peace does not mainly inorder to
emphasize that peace does not mean only the absence of war. I could
easily have placed the same issues under the themes of Democracy and
Development.
After all, when we talk about democracy we include the expectation
that governments must look after their people and provide for their
needs. The development initiatives are in fact a human right as
defined under democratic principles. People have a right to eat. They
have a right to safe water. They have a right to jobs. They have a
right to health facilities. They have a right to health facilities.
They have a right to freedom of expression: Development, Democracy,
Unity and Peace. These things go together.
My Chairman, Ladies and Gentlemen, one important thing to remember is
that democracy is not just one ideal to be realized in one go, once
and for all. The voting or candidates' debate is only a small part of
democracy. Democracy is a journey, or better still a culture which
evolves over a period of time.
In the past 10 years we have proved that democracy is not at all
foreign or alien to Malawi, as we used to be warned in the one-party
era. Malawians are just as capable as anyone else of carrying on
democratically. And from all the castigations, mudslinging,
name-calling, investigations, allegations, and the formation of
political parties almost every day, there is no doubt that democracy
is safe and sound in Malawi, especially under the very tolerant
leadership of the United Democratic Front.
We have made a very good start, but we do know that democracy can
only flourish in certain conditions, and these include Unity, Peace,
and Development.
Mr. Chairman, ladies and Gentlemen, throughout my life I have always
been guided by the belief in the existence of God. Indeed I owe
everything I am to Him, and therefore I put Him first. I wish
therefore to thank the Almighty God for providing me with the
guidance and good health I needed to serve the Party which is so
close to my heart and the people of Malawi that I love so dearly.
I have been able to do what I have for the party and the country
because of God but also because of the support that you all given me.
I want you to know that I am extremely grateful for that support, and
I have never taken it for granted.
My fellow members of the UDF, hold your heads high and be proud of
the fact you are members of a party that has made a very positive
difference in the lives of Malawians. As a ruling party, which will
stay in power for another fifty years to come, you must not be
surprised when people look at you with envy and jealousy. Yours is
the largest party and the ruling party, so naturally a lot of bashing
will always come your way. But you really have a lot to be proud of.
My message of advice to you is that sometimes the ground on which you
walk will be wet, slippery and rough. But you must always take your
inspiration from the late Dag Hammarskold, who once said and I quote:
"Never look down to test the ground before taking your next step;
only he who keeps his eye fixed on the far horizon will find the
right road." End of quote.
Mr. Chairman, Distinguished Guests, Ladies and Gentlemen, with these
few words, I declare the UDF national Conference, officially open. I
wish you happy deliberations.
May God bless you all. And bless our beloved nation.
By President Bakili Muluzi of the Republic of Malawi

Original_Search_Title: There's a Zulu in My Soup, And He Looks Tasty
Headline: South Africa: There's a Zulu in My Soup, And He Looks Tasty
Body_Text:
The fourth annual V&A Waterfront Winter Food Fair got off to a frivolous start last night as chefs from five city restaurants were given the opportunity to showcase their culinary know-how and artistic flair.
Called Food as Art and hosted by Giggling Gourmet Jenny Morris, teams from Balducci's, Jewel of India, Balducci's Royal Sushi Bar, Baia Seafood Restaurant, and the Commodore Hotel were given an hour in which to slice and dice their raw food and vegetables into a delectable artwork.
The judging panel, which included the Cape Argus's Andrew October and Bianca Coleman, had a difficult time choosing a winner as the chefs pulled out all the stops to impress.
Kerry Correis and Raynor Damons of Balducci's prepared a "gamey" piece, which they said reflected the restaurant's philosophy.
"We used mainly venison, because Balducci's has an African and wildlife theme," said Damons.
Jewel of India presented a more conservative piece, with chefs Arun Shinde and Rajendra Singh presenting favourite North Indian dishes like pulav rice and butter chicken.
Jason Sun of Balducci's Royal Sushi Bar, created a sushi garden with a South African sushi flag as the centrepiece.
Gert Biedenhann and Nabeelah Kriel of Baia, opted to use only fruit and vegetables for their display.
"This is the first time we've done something like this and it was fun," said Biedenhann.
But the design that attracted the most attention and was the eventual winner, was the Commodore Hotel's Zulu, made from seaweed, lemon grass, ginger, spring roll pastry, chillies, sweet potatoes and a host of other vegetables.
Chefs Markus Ferreira and Chris Wheeler said they had not created anything like it before. "What can be more African than the Zulu," said a delighted Ferreira.
The fair, which will run until Sunday at the Marquee on Market square, is a celebration of the best food and cuisine that South Africa has to offer.
With more than 35 exhibitors from across the province, the fair offers the ideal chance for small businesses and gourmet food producers to show their wares.
A highlight of the three-day jamboree will be the celebrity cook-off tomorrow that will see a local politicians take on sports celebrities. This will be in aid of Feedback, a charity that distributes excess food.
Other gastronomic delights include the Food Theatre; a top-class demo kitchen; gourmet food producers' stands; a wine bar; tea tastings; a pub with coffee and cake and a big screen; and a Kids Corner.

Original_Search_Title: Resettlement Process Highly Flawed
Headline: Angola: Resettlement Process Highly Flawed
Body_Text:
Luanda — The Angolan government and the United Nations are failing to ensure the
safe and voluntary return of millions of Angolans to their homes, Human
Rights Watch said in a new report released today.
The 29-page report, "Struggling Through Peace: Return and Resettlement
in Angola," documents several incidents of government authorities using
violence, or the threat of violence, to drive people out of camps where they
had been living sometimes for years.  The Human Rights Watch report also
raises concerns about reported incidents of rape and other sexual violence
against internally displaced women and returning refugees.
"The end of the conflict in Angola is a blessing for millions of
Angolans who can now return home," said Peter Takirambudde, Executive
Director of the Africa Division of Human Rights Watch. "But unless urgent
measures are taken to stop these abuses, peace will be a curse instead."
Hundreds of Angolan refugees have spontaneously returned to their homes
since the ceasefire of April 2002, but millions of internally displaced
people, refugees and ex-combatants remain in exile, in transit or in
temporary resettlement sites within Angola.
Rather than paying special attention to children, women, and vulnerable
groups, the Angolan government has granted preference to ex-combatants for
resettlement. The government has also failed to provide people with identity
documents that would help them get access to humanitarian assistance, which
is in any case inadequate.
"Angola is an oil-rich state with the resources to help its people,"
said Takirambudde. "There's no excuse for the way they have been suffering."
Angolan law has incorporated international standards on the protection
of the displaced, but the government has failed to respect that law in
practice, Human Rights Watch charged.
Landmines have killed or maimed hundreds as they have tried to return
home, Human Rights Watch said.
"U.N. agencies have also failed to take effective steps to ensure the
protection of Angolans from human rights violations. Human Rights Watch
urged the High Commission for Refugees (UNHCR) and U.N. Human Rights
Division in Angola to increase their outreach to vulnerable groups whom the
Angolan government has ignored in resettlement efforts. Human Rights
Watch also appealed to the donor community to fully fund the
U.N. interagency appeal for Angola.
The Human Rights Watch report urges the Angolan government and
international agencies to ensure reasonably uniform conditions in the areas
to which the internally displaced, refugees, and former combatants will
return, and to pay special attention to the needs of women, children and
other vulnerable groups. Most importantly, the Angolan government must
respect international and domestic law requiring the voluntary basis on
which displaced people should be resettled.
Findings: Forced Return
Human Rights Watch found that local authorities have forced internally
displaced Angolans to return to their home areas by violence or threat of
violence. One such incident occurred in transit center Cambabe II, in Bengo
Province. Local administration and police forces entered the camp in
September and October 2002, and burned the internally displaced Angolans'
homes and 10 acres of crops. With their homes and crops destroyed, the
displaced people had nowhere to go except their home areas, which were not
ready to receive them.  Most fled immediately, without stopping to gather
the animals or possessions that had survived the fire.
One Angolan humanitarian worker who was present at Cambabe II and
witnessed the burning of the displaced persons' homes, told Human Rights
Watch:
They were forced to leave the area because the Government wanted to have
the land for its own agricultural projects. The [internally displaced] lost
ten acres of crops- sweet potatoes and manioc.  Burning houses was part of
the Government's policy.
In another case, the government threatened to suspend assistance to
people in the camps they had been living in for many years. Another
humanitarian worker reported:
Local administration determined that the camps [at Bengo II] had to be
emptied. They told us that the return process was officially open and people
should go back to their areas of origin. However, they did not provide
transportation or other assistance and they threatened to suspend current
assistance. And then, in July 2002, there was a general embarrassment, when
WFP food distribution was temporarily suspended in Bengo and Feira [transit
centers].
Marlene V., 28, told Human Rights Watch that local authorities
instructed her family to leave Bengo II and go to Sanza Pombo (their place
of origin), even though they wanted to remain at Bengo II. She said:
I don't have any one there. My mother and father passed away and my
children are going to school here [in Negage]. In Sanza Pombo there are no
health centers or other services. My husband went there and told me so.
In Bengo II, there were about 12 families from Sanza Pombo that did not
wish to return. Jorge S., 33, told Human Rights Watch their reasons for
remaining:
We have been here since September 1999. Here we have a house and land to
work on. 'Return' means go to a place where roads don't even go.
In other cases, displaced people have been prevented from moving where
they want to go, in particular to Luanda, the capital city.  Helena S., 29,
a displaced woman interviewed by Human Rights Watch in U_, where she had
been living as a displaced person for years, told us that local authorities
had been preventing her from moving on to Luanda, where five of her children
and other family members were living. She told a Human Rights Watch
researcher:
I have not seen my mother for seven years. We were separated during the
war. I am from Mbanza Kongo. Here [in Negage] I don't have land.  I don't
have anything. I have five children in Luanda and two here with me. I wanted
to go to Luanda where I have family but they told us to wait. I have been
waiting for ten months. I've been waiting [ever] since there was finally
peace.
The report is available online at the following location during the
embargo period: http://docs.hrw.org/embargo/angola0803/ using the username
"angola" and access-code "idp2k3"

Original_Search_Title: Agriculturists in Ekunha to Benefit of Fertilizers
Headline: Angola: Agriculturists in Ekunha to Benefit of Fertilizers
Body_Text:
Ekunha — At least 12 tonnes of fertilizers have been distributed for farmers at
Ekunha district, a potential agricultural region, some 42 kilometres of
Huambo city, to prepare the 2003/2004 season, starting in September.
The fertilizers will allow the coverage of a area of 30 hectares of land
of maize, bean, potatoes, sweet potatoes plus horticulturals.
Benefitting the good are farmes located at the headquarter, Quipeio
village and 30 other small villages.

Original_Search_Title: $5m for Farmer-Based Organisations
Headline: Ghana: $5m for Farmer-Based Organisations
Body_Text:
The government has set aside $5 million under the agriculture sector services investment project (AGSSIP) to provide capacity assistance to farmer-based organisations.
Trade and Industry and Presidential Special Initiative Minister, Mr. Alan Kyeremanten who announced this in Accra yesterday said this forms part of government's measures to address the long-term capital inadequacy facing entrepreneurs in the agriculture sector.
He said he is convinced that an intergraded strategy for enhancing access to financial support would be crucial to agribusiness development and that is why the government sourced the World Bank funding for AGSSIP.
The Minister was launching the 19th National Farmers Day celebrations, which falls on December 5 this year yesterday. The theme for the occasion is "enhancing agricultural and food security through improved agribusiness".
He said the inadequate funds to the sector by financial institutions might be due to low loan recovery rate, high cost of administering agricultural credit and exposure of agriculture to the vagaries of the weather.
He said medium to large-scale enterprises could also access credit facilities including merchant bank's business loans portfolio guarantee scheme supported by USAID, Agricultural Development Bank's (ADB) performance Bond, Barclays Bank's Cooperative Loans and revolving overdraft, ECOBANK's Medium Term Enterprises Loans and Ghana Commercial Bank's Buyer Commercial Line for Capital Goods.
He said the state is no longer directly involved in the procurement of inputs and production, processing and marketing of agricultural products. "Government's role is to provide the necessary environment for the private sector to take up these responsibilities," he added. He said the businesses in the sector have realized that they could no longer count on the state's direct support and are now beginning to negotiate their interest with the government in a free market setting.
He was of the view that if agriculture and related economic activities were handled by the private sector in a business-like manner, backed by appropriate technologies and economies of scale, the country would certainly be on the road to rapid growth and development.
Major (rtd) Courage Quashigah, Minister of Food and Agriculture, said that plans are far advanced to improve the rice industry, beef cattle, poultry, grasscutter rearing as well as the establishment of mechanization service centres in all the ten regions.
Apart from that, he said that groundwater development for irrigation and human consumption, which involves the construction of dams and boreholes, would soon be undertaken.
To ensure food security throughout the year, he said his ministry is liaising with researchers, agro-industrialists, agribusinessmen and financial institutions as well as development partners.
Major Quashigah said with the assistance of the FAO a dairy training centre has been established at Amrahia in the Greater Accra region where people are being trained in milking cattle under hygienic conditions which is sold to a local factory for the production of fresh milk, yogurt, butter and cheese.
In order to create market for the farmers, government has embarked on vigorous campaign to promote the consumption of local foods at official functions by encouraging caterers to use local rice, gari, cassava and sweet potato flour for pastries and diary products desert.
He said the district assemblies have been tasked to add value to agricultural products for which they have comparative advantage. This he noted would help improve the lot of farmers and fishermen in the rural areas.
The minister appealed to organizations and individuals to donate towards the celebration to motivate farmers and fishermen to produce more in the years ahead.
The ADB has pledged to donate fully furbished three-bed roomed house as a prize for the best farmer to be built at any location in the country of the winner's choice, while National Investment Bank has also pledged a double decker Toyota pick-up for the first runner-up. WENCO, a fertilizer manufacturer, also pledged about Â¢110 million for the celebrations.

Original_Search_Title: Colonialism : the Good, the Bad And the Ugly
Headline: Zambia: Colonialism : the Good, the Bad And the Ugly
Body_Text:
To many former colonial states in Africa, colonialism is usually
associated with suppression, retarded economic development and erosion of
the indigenous culture.
However, some scholars have discovered that colonialism was not enterely
bad. Other social scientists have even gone further to state that even other
evil practises like slavery had a silver lining.
Are they right ?
The controversial, conservative Indian scholar Dinesh D'Souza who is the
most authoritative supporter of the benefits of colonialism has in several
books and articles argued that colonialism has gotten a bad name despite
greatly improving the lives of the colonised people.
And true to the scholar's acute observation, colonialism till recently
has never had a balanced analysis. The only books that have flooded the
market on the subject are all anti-colonialism.
Among the leading Western scholars is Edward Said, Gayatri Spivak,
Walter Rodney and Samir Amin. Third World intellectuals who are stringently
anti-colonialism include Nobel Prize winning playwright Wole Soyinka,
Chinweizu, Ashis Nandy and Franz Fanon of the Wretched of the Earth
fame.
Like D'Souza has rightly pointed out, these scholars, despite
highlighting the evils of colonialism, have turned a blind eye on the many
benefits of colonialism or are simply ignorant of its advantages.
Many of the things people have been made to believe about colonialism
for many years are half-truths. Many scholars have hastily concluded that
colonialism and imperialism are distinctively Western evils.
The other much-mouthed but debatable claim is that as a consequence of
colonialism, the West became rich and the former colonies became poor.
A close study of development of nations will reveal that some countries
which never colonised other countries are rich. On the other hand, a number
of poor countries in the Third World countries are poor not wholly as a
result of colonialism but other factors which include mismanagement, poor
policies and mediocre leadership.
The other fallacy is that descendants of colonialism are worse off than
they would have been had colonialism not occurred. The cruel truth is that
many Third World countries citizens would be far worse off if were it not
for colonisation.
One only needs to look at ' undisturbed ' societies like the Pygmies of
Ituri forestin the Democratic Republic of the Congo and other aborigines who
have never had a serious social intercourse with the dynamic Western culture
to realise what many societies could have been if the colonialists had not
come.
In his classic Marxist book The Wretched of the Earth, Fanon points out
that, "European opulence has been founded on slavery. The well-being and
progress of Europe have been built up with the sweat and the dead bodies of
Negroes, Arabs, Indians, and the yellow races." Really Mr Fanon ?
The point Fanon and other proponents of such notions miss is that in the
first place colonialism is not unique to the West. In traditional Zambia,
owning slaves was practised in many parts of the country. Vernacular names
for a slave abound in almost all Zambian tribes.
The fact that the slave traders commercialised slavery does not
criminalise the practice as being more evil than slavery that was practised
by our own chiefs and other traditional leaders long before we heard of
notorious slave traders like Mlozi or Tib Tibbu.
In other parts of the world former colonies like India had been
colonised by other people before the British hoisted their Union Jack in the
country and forced the natives to sing God Save the Queen.
The Indians had been invaded and conquered by the Persians, the Afghans,
Alexander the Great, the Mongols, the Arabs and the Turks. In short, the
British as a colonial power was preceded by six other colonial powers some
of them racially-related to Indians.
Globally other once colonial powers included the Egyptian empire, which
among their subjects were the Jews who now control America, the Persian
empire, the Macedonia empire, the Islamic empire, the Mongol empire, the
Chinese empire, and the Aztec and Inca empires in the Americas.
The once mighty Ancient Greece was for example conquered by Rome and the
Roman empire itself was once conquered by the Huns, Vandals, Lombards and
Visgoth from northern Europe.
If the Industrial Revolution had first taken place in Africa, maybe an
African country could have colonised a European state ! Remember, the
greatest nation in the world-America was once colonised by Britain. England
itself was conquered and ruled by the Normans from France.
World history before the League of Nations which culminated into the UN
is full of fights by a mightier nation to dominate a smaller or weaker
nation.
Back home tribal wars in Southern Africa saw stronger tribes like the
Ngonis and Bembas defeating smaller tribes and imposing their cultures on
them.
The scenario was no different in Europe which had seen many empires at
one time dominating the West or the world. We should understand that at one
time the West was not a dominant and powerful civilisation it is today.
The West has become an affluent and dominant political and economic
global force because of inventing three institutions : science, democracy
and capitalism.
This led to the West subduing the rest of the world between 16th and
