this country. The only thing is just to fight for your own survival."
On consumer boycott she says: "This doesn't work in Zimbabwe. ...Even
if prices rise like a flooding river, we struggle to buy the
commodities if they are available. You can't boycott what is not
available."
The situation gets worse every day. Foodstuffs are expensive and
diffcult to find. Disposable incomes have shrunk to such an extent
that many people no longer buy clothes and other items they consider
secondary. "Business is just bad," says a shopkeeper in Harare.

Original_Search_Title: ICRC Distributes Aid Relief to 4,000 Families in Shabunda
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: ICRC Distributes Aid Relief to 4,000 Families in Shabunda
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) completed on Monday
the distribution of 210 mt of relief aid to some 4,000 families in the
formerly inaccessible region of Shabunda, South Kivu Province, eastern
Democratic Republic of the Congo.
Shabunda and the surrounding region remained cut off for months due to
insecurity, thereby limiting commercial activity and residents' access to
fields surrounding the town. This prevailing insecurity also forced the ICRC
to twice suspend its operations in the region during 2002. However, having
recently obtained necessary security guarantees from the Rassemblement
congolais pour la democratie in Goma and Mayi-Mayi militias in Shabunda,
ICRC was able to carry out this operation.
Several recent evaluation missions enabled the ICRC to identify the
needs of the 25,000 or so residents, and to thereby focus its intervention
on supporting the resumption of agricultural activities and improvement of
nutrition.
A total of 19 flights of a DC-3 aircraft of the ICRC in the course of
one week enabled the delivery of 28,000 kg of improved groundnut seeds,
12,000 kg of beans, 10,000 kg of sweet potato cuttings, 4,000 kg of maize,
6,400 kg of soap, 3,000 kg of iodised salt, 4,000 market gardening seed
kits, 4,000 hoes and 4,000 sheets of cloth.
In a statement issued on Tuesday, the ICRC said authorities had enabled
it to complete this operation and to carry out its mandate of assisting
victims of armed conflict. It also said that the success of this initiative
was due, in large part, to close collaboration with local Red Cross
staff.

Original_Search_Title: Memorable Night As Artistes Pay Tribute to Late Mutuma
Headline: Zimbabwe: Memorable Night As Artistes Pay Tribute to Late Mutuma
Body_Text:
FRIDAY 21 February was a beautiful day for Zimbabwean music. They were all there the young and the old, the good and the bad, those who have arrived and those who are still on the way.
From the electrifying and well-choreographed Africa Revenge to charismatic Oliver Mtukudzi, the cool Summer Breeze, vintage Jabavu, effervescent Amagents, prodigal (and perhaps repentant) Andy Brown, lively Busi Ncube, and the good old Pied Pipers an almost Who's Who of Zimbabwean music all there to honour a man who on that night showed that even in death, he had friends real friends.
Seeing them give their all for free when they could have been making money elsewhere, one could not help but feel that this was the beginning of good things for Zimbabwean music in general and jazz in particular.
The place was Jazz 105, the home of Zimbabwean jazz. The occasion was the Jonah Sisonke Benefit Concert.
And the stage was set for a night to remember, a night to celebrate jazz and the life of one of our own. Some danced to remember, others to forget.
By the time I arrived at the venue Summer Breeze were already doing their thing. Fari Sumaili was a joy to watch and listen to on guitar. His intricate jazz chords, accompanied by the subtle melodies of Moses Kabubi's keyboards, serenaded by the riffs of a well-handled bass guitar and generous helpings of a saxophone sound set the stage for an unforgettable night.
Jazz 105 is not the biggest venue around, and the temperature was already beginning to rise. By the time Summer Breeze left the stage, we were all quite warm musically and it was apparent that such a disciplined and mature performance could only be the beginning of auspicious things.
Africa Revenge did not waste time in living up to the night's promise of fulfilment. Comfort Mbofana, who wore an appropriate skullcap and so effortlessly performed his duties as the master of ceremonies for the night, fittingly introduced them as "a band that is going places". Tazz and Willis, those fine and focused young men from the City of Kings, would have made Mutuma proud to be from Bulawayo, for with the able and youthful assistance of Ruth on backing vocals, Zex Manatsa's son on lead guitar, the two Mparutsa brothers on drums and keyboards, Master Pablo Nekapa on bass guitar, they sang beautifully and danced so breathtakingly, their harmonies soaring and and soaking Mutuma's night and, above all, talent. They gave us songs like Emawemi, Sihlobo Ungaqaqazile Mfo Wethu, uSomandla Ukubhekile among others.
And then it was time for Too Open. Yes, this band is called Too Open, and they played their own brand of jazz driven by the wizardry of their enterprising lead guitarist and inspired by the presence in their ranks of some of Mutuma's former band members in Sisonke.
Sisonke fittingly took to the stage to rapturous applause. And by this time the drink was beginning to have an effect on me and those others who were affording it the chance to o so, which was more or less everyone.
The sequence of events correspondingly became livelier, apparently not only to me because there was a lot more swaying of bodies and tapping of feet around me. But I remember that this Sisonke that drove and was driven by Mutuma, brought him right back that night. And those of us who saw him bade him goodbye.
Sisonke set the mood by playing a beautiful instrumental of one of Mutuma's songs from their album Sisonke Mdala.
Jairos Hambahamba, that effervescent keyboard player, immaculately dressed in flowing West African regalia, gave us a beautiful rendition of the title track Sisonke Mdala and we sang along.
But when he sang Pafunge, we raised our voices even higher threatening to blow the roof off with echoes of Pemberai Pemberai! Ndinotenda, Pemberai Pemberai!
Patience Musa was there too, her soulful Zvirinani urged on by the guitar playing of Jimmy "Dludlu" Buzuzi.
Then there was Bob Nyabinde, that irrepressible headmaster from Kwekwe, making what I thought was a surprise appearance, having been missing from the original line-up. Accompanied only by his guitar, he strode onto the stage, looking a little unsure, I thought. But the people cheered him on and his anguished delivery of Swing Low, Sweet Chariot was to me both a prayer and a send-off for our Jonah.
Earlier Nyabinde had said something quite sad but which also bore testimony to the love of Mutuma: "When I played in Harare for the first time, it was with Jonah at the Terreskane."
Then along came muzukuru Brown. As he took to the stage he coaxed his guitar with his usual dexterity and broke into the song Ndoenda. First, Busi Ncube joined him on backing vocals, then Mary Bell, then Sam Mataure on percussion, followed spontaneously by various other musicians.
And when Potato hopped onto the stage and shook the house with his gravelly tribute to Mutuma, the crowd went wild.
Jabavu were on fire! Those veterans of jazz were really hot. Singing with such flaming passion, Mukoma Philip Svosve delivered another beautiful song, Rudo Chete. Dumi Ngulube and Amagents were there too. Ngulube himself was inspired on keyboards, churning out some new material and older tunes such as Echoes From the Mountain and Shungu Mwanangu among others.
I did not watch Mtukudzi, due to urgent business that could not wait. In fact, I went out just as he was taking to the stage and tuning his guitar. But I have no regrets because I know he did justice to his name and to his presence, which on its own would have been enough without a performance. Chirikure Chirikure, Friday Mbirimi, and the Pied Pipers probably performed after I left. If they did, I know they too gave a good account for themselves.
For me, the point had been made there was no need to belabour it.
Over $500 000 had been raised. The truth had been well told. Mutuma had friends. And they all came. Some to give speeches, like Oliver Hawadi who, turned out immaculately in a white tuxedo, gave moral support just as he had done during Mutuma's living years.
Admire Taderera, whose love for jazz is well known, also gave a moving speech.

Original_Search_Title: Late Planted Crops Expected to Improve After Countrywide Rains
Headline: Zimbabwe: Late Planted Crops Expected to Improve After Countrywide Rains
Body_Text:
Harare — MOST of the late planted crops, which had been suffering from moisture
stress, are expected to improve countrywide following rains which have been
falling in some parts of the country.
In its fortnightly report, the Department of Agricultural Research and
Extension Services (Arex), however, said the rains could not save most of
the early planted crops, which had reached a permanent wilting point.
The department said most of the early planted maize in Beitbridge and
Gwanda South was a write-off while in Hwedza, Chikomba, Chegutu and Seke
districts, the crop was severely affected by the dry spell.
In Kadoma district, harvesting is already underway and green mealies
from the early planted maize are being sold.
Late planted crops in the same area are expected to recover if more
rains are received.
In Manicaland, Mashonaland West and East, and Masvingo provinces, the
maize crop is reported to be in a fair condition.
In most parts of Matabeleland, the maize crop is in a poor state except
in Insiza district, which has a good crop.
Murewa district suffered a heavy blow from a recent armyworm invasion,
which destroyed 330 hectares of maize although the pest is now under control
countrywide.
In Kariba, Makonde and some parts of Mashonaland East province, yields
are expected to be low due to the shortage of top-dressing ammonium nitrate
fertiliser.
Mhunga, sorghum and rapoko crops have been negatively affected by the
dry spell, but the crops are expected to recover.
In Beitbridge, sorghum and mhunga are a complete write-off. Groundnuts
are in a poor condition in Matabeleland South, but doing well in
Insiza.
Low yields of groundnuts yields are expected due to reduced hectarage,
resulting from seed and fertiliser shortages.
Soyabean is expected to improve in provinces such as Manicaland,
Mashonaland East and West, where the crop has reached the early reproductive
stage.
Sunflower and cotton are moisture stressed countrywide, but are expected
to improve with the current rains.
Tobacco farmers are reaping and curing the crop. However, in Masvingo
reaping and curing has been delayed due to inadequate structures.
In Hurungwe district, some farmers have shifted from growing burley to
flue-cured tobacco while insignificant plantings of burley are reported in
Mashonaland West due to low market prices.
Paprika is experiencing a flower drop in Manicaland due to the dry spell
but the irrigated crop in Insiza is doing well.
Rice in Buhera has been affected by moisture stress while sweet potatoes
are in fair condition in Mutasa district and planting is still underway in
the rest of Manicaland province.
Livestock condition is generally fair to good in Masvingo and Manicaland
provinces, but poor in Matabeleland South.
More and more cattle are being reported dead in the region due to
starvation.
Some of the animals are being affected by the outbreak of tick-borne
diseases although dipping is taking place. Animal pastures, which were in a
poor state, are expected to improve following the recent rains.
However, water supplies for both human and livestock consumption are
adequate except in Matabeleland South where people are travelling for more
than 10km to the nearest water source.

Original_Search_Title: A New Import Policy
Headline: Nigeria: A New Import Policy
Body_Text:
THERE are strange things coming into Nigeria. I mean imported into Nigeria. We see all manner of items on our streets and in our markets: Orange juice, cornflakes, chocolates, sweets, sausages, asphalt, LPG, diesel, petrol, petroleum products, gas, groundnut oil, sugar, salt, sardines, corned beef, wheat, apples, potato chips, furniture, paper and paper products, plastics, even alcohol, tobacco, and so on and so forth. The irony is that there is nothing that Nigeria is importing;that it does not have and cannot produce.
As a matter of fact, some of the things we import, the initial raw materials are actually exported from here, processed and re-exported to us. An example is chocolate. There is no where in the temperate regions where cocoa grows, yet Switzerland, France, America and England export chocolate to us. Another is plastic. This is basically made from crude oil (our major export). Again, Nigeria has no business flaring gas and then importing LPG, or importing bitumen, gold, diamonds, tin, bauxite or even copper, things that we have in abundance in our soil. Nigeria has no need for imported cornflakes or corned beef or even sardines. Nigeria has probably the best shrimp in the world, and some of the best fish, yet we import these items. There is no part of the motor vehicle or motor cycle that cannot be produced in Nigeria, yet we import cars in millions. Nigeria has great limestone deposits, yet we import cement. We have bountiful oranges, pineapples, tangerine, bananas, yet we import fruit juices. Up till recently, Nigeria imported bottled water, perhaps still does and even chicken when we know that the Nigerian fowl is one of the fastest in the world. Thank God, President Olusegun Obasanjo is a farmer, yet a lot more can be done in that sector.
There is virtually nothing that Nigeria does not have. We have incredible cotton, amazing hard wood, yet we import furniture; we have clay and marble, yet we import floor tiles and marble floors. It is simply unbelievable. We have acquired tastes for things that we do not need and despised and discarded things that we have. Nigerians have acquired tastes that are beyond their competences due to maladjusted western influences. Why a black man ignores natural oranges, palm wine and revels in imported Five Alive and in Champagne, I do not know.
I remember having a great desire for caviar until I discovered that it was nothing more than raw fish eggs. Can you imagine paying so much for raw fish eggs just because it has been exposed to some neo-colonialist propaganda? Why allow the Western world to continue to run our lives, hoodwink and blindfold us? Now South Africa is taking over our economy. UAR does not own, manufacture or produce anything, yet we import from them in millions of dollars. The palm oil we import from Malaysia was introduced to them by us, yet palm oil has become the main stay of the Malaysian economy. Nigeria has tons of men, yet it imports Father Christmas.
You will be surprised to know that thankfully today Nigeria has knowledge in most things, what then really is the problem? There is really no problem. Nigeria has everything, the only thing that lacks is good, honest leadership.
If Nigeria loves itself, it should stop playing politics, stop sharing power and stop stealing money but should look for and encourage good leadership. Nigeria does not need to acquire good leaders, there are many of them hiding in their homes, unwilling to expose themselves to the elements and vagaries of Nigeria. We should go and look for them, give them leadership roles and then import the little bit of knowledge we do not have. It is unfortunate Nigeria has been held at the throat by a local cabal who really are going nowhere. These people make it their life style to steal the nation's resources and will do anything not to allow Nigeria or Nigerians to go anywhere.
This is a wicked and selfish cabal who also think that Nigeria is theirs to rule and ride as they please, for all time; a cabal that has introduced and enthroned corruption and nepotism and was at a time insisting on Sodomism as a pastime of high rulership; a cabal determined to waste and who would rather destroy than have anyone benefit at all; a cabal who thinks that leadership is personal enrichment, aggrandizement and total oppression of the masses.
I was talking to a friend recently who lost a contract in a major oil company, even though he had won the bid on merit, the reason being that he did not know that he had to see top people in government in order to get a major contract in an oil company. He had naively thought that the bid would be decided fair and square. That's Nigeria for you, a nation where merit is irrelevant and your yardstick for success is the kind of contacts that you have in high places.
Another friend was telling me recently that corruption was a bona fide citizen of Nigeria. That it cannot be removed, destroyed or deported nor have its passport seized or taken. As a matter of fact, it can go to places presidents cannot go. He pointed my attention to our various party conventions - he told me I should have seen corruption there. He (corruption) was in action. He did all the voting, he issued the ballot papers, he did the counting, he won the elections. He was in charge assisted by Ghana must go bags. Nigeria is a country that even when you try to do it right, the nation wants to destroy you, to eat you up, strangulate you. Any leader in Nigeria that wishes to preserve his life and position is made to bow to the dictates of corruption - because corruption and selfish ambition are in charge.
The losers of any of the various party primaries elections are only crying foul because they were out-corrupted. Many of them then left for other parties where the power of the level of their corruption could hold water. However, regardless of the fact that they have moved to other parties, no amount of intervention can help them this time around, because they have joined them to enthrone corruption.
We can do better for ourselves.
Ighodalo is a chartered accountant and pastor, (Christ Church Parish, Gbagada), RCCG

Original_Search_Title: Chefs Serve Up Local Fare for Foreign Fans
Headline: South Africa: Chefs Serve Up Local Fare for Foreign Fans
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — SOUTH African restaurateurs and hoteliers are set to dish out exotic
indigenous cuisine to international cricket fans.
The South African Tourism and the Federated Hospitality Association of
South Africa this week launched a month-long campaign called the Warm
Welcome in Soweto as a celebration of local cuisine.
About 50 Fedhasa restaurants, hotels and lodges countrywide accepted SA
Tourism's invitation to participate in the campaign.
One of the restaurateurs, Sakhumzi Maqubela, owner of Soweto's renowned
Sakhumzi restaurant, is offering tourists traditional African fare "that
will bring them back for more". At Sakhumzi's, which is located on the
township's tourist route, international cricket fans can tuck into samp,
pap, steamed bread or a stew of their choice.
Christina Martin, owner of Christina's Restaurant in Durban, is offering
ostrich bobotie with sweet chilli sauce, a deboned chicken stuffed with
vegetables representing the rainbow nation, sweet and sour pork on a bed of
crushed sweet potatoes, and fresh mango mousse .
David Robinson, owner of Weldon Kaya in Plettenberg Bay, has devised a
special cocktail - a Kaya Cooler - as part of his welcome to visiting
cricket fans. - Suthentira Govender

Original_Search_Title: Commend Museveni; Don't Just Criticise Him
Headline: Uganda: Commend Museveni; Don't Just Criticise Him
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni's reported recommendation that Uganda should
prepare for a return to pluralism has opened a new chapter in the country's
political life. This chapter, I predict, is going to expose the nature and
character of our political actors.
To the best of my knowledge, Uganda suffers from a deficiency of honest,
consistent and democratic leaders. The political culture of this country
rotates around self-aggrandisement, arrogance, manipulation and deceit.
Uganda's politicians have turned the country into a man-eat-man society,
and the unwritten policy is that "you either eat or you are eaten".
The reaction of political opposition leaders to President Museveni's
recommendation to open up the political space is testimony that our country
needs a political-cultural revival.
Since The Monitor broke the news on Wednesday, 19 February, several
politicians have reacted hurriedly to the president's remarks.
Of all things, one would not have expected our opposition politicians to
quickly jump into the fray, setting tough conditions for President
Museveni. Reaction is not bad, but the type of reaction matters. Most of the
opposition leaders have doubted the president's move, while others have
demanded that he expedite political reforms.
Much as I would agree with politicians calling for reforms, I totally
disagree with their approach. Why the hurry? In Teso, we have a saying that
"never attempt to kill a rat when it has just showed up its head from the
hole in which it was hiding." If you do so unsuccessfully, the rat will
slide back into the hole and disappear through its underground routes.
It will be difficult for you to get that rat unless you dig up all its
underground routes. The digging is, of course, tedious and most times
unmanageable. Mark you the rat knows its routes better. Moreover, it will
eat your crops such as cassava, groundnuts and sweet potatoes.
If a farmer does not have skilful ways to exterminate the rat, he should
be rest assured of a poor harvest and wasted efforts.
Now back to politics. It is absurd that politicians are all over the
media, chastising, rebuking and even abusing President Museveni for his
proposal to open up.
Such politicians are not different from a farmer whose crops have been
destroyed by rats but when he cites a rat getting out of its hideout, he
screams loudly, decrying how bad it has been, even before it is dead.
Why on earth do we have such political leaders?
I believe President Museveni's recommendation should be embraced and he
should be skilfully encouraged to actualise his proposal. Making unnecessary
comments to the effect that the president has been forced by donors and
pressure from opposition politicians to open up shows how egocentric some
opposition leaders are.
If I may ask, did donors force President Museveni to entrench a
monolithic system in Uganda? Did donors send Mr Museveni to the bush? And
can donors stop Mr Museveni from going back to the bush?
What most Ugandans want opposition politicians to deliver is an open
political environment, different from the Movement's "individual merit"
system.
Many people have suffered in pursuit of that cause; some have even lost
their lives. If President Museveni has seen it wise to grant us multiparty
politics, we should commend him for that and forget his past stance.
The best our power-hungry opposition politicians should do is, not to
discourage the president by making provocative statements, but to lobby him
to continue with his proposal, given the negative reception some Movement
hard-liners have shown.
As a young journalist, it has been my desire to practice my risky trade
in an open and free society. The Movement's dominance of politics in this
country has been a pain in my neck, and that is why I welcome President
Museveni's change of heart.
Opposition politicians should accept that Mr Museveni has done a good
thing to recommend that the country goes pluralistic. Whether this will come
true or not depends on how well they play their cards against the hard-line
movementists.
The fear in this country has been that President Museveni is against
political parties. Now that he appears to have changed his mind, why can't
political parties seize the opportunity?
Let us accept that although pressure can bear on President Museveni to
adopt pluralism, the most civilised and non-fatal way of achieving it is
through talking.
President Museveni is now talking, so the opposition should encourage
him to talk more. Don't discourage him through ridicule and criticism for
the consequences might be detrimental to ordinary Ugandans.
The issue is not whether donors have forced President Museveni to open
up or not, but that the president seems willing to open up.
Why do the opposition politicians want to discourage the rat from
getting out of its hideout?
They should be advised that they could spoil things if they aim at
humiliating President Museveni.
Most people had felt that President Museveni would rather go back to the
bush than open up, but now that he appears to have changed his mind, let us
thank him for it.
At least he recognises that Uganda is for us all, not just the
Movement.
Opposition politicians need a change of character. They should oppose
for genuine reasons, not to be noticed.
President Museveni would die a hero if he went ahead and opened up the
political space for other political interests to participate. Many will
oppose him, but remember, not all who say, "Jesus is Lord" actually believe
in him.
Some might even claim they are "more movement" than he, the
chairman.
After leading the country for 20 years in 2006, Ugandans would be
willing to forget President Museveni's mistakes if he granted them total
political freedom. His achievements are visible to all but he would rather
quit the stage when he is still a darling.
Forgive our opposition leaders, especially those who speak to excite
people. Go ahead and open up. If you do, the true character of our
politicians will be exposed. Medals await you.
The author is bureau chief of The Monitor in Mbale.

Original_Search_Title: Technology is the Seed of Food for All
Headline: South Africa: Technology is the Seed of Food for All
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — WE HAVE most of the toys but we don't want you to play with them. That
seemed to be the unspoken message from European opponents of biotechnology
at a two-day conference in Brussels recently.
To the European public, the men and women advocating biotechnology are
either arrogant and evil scientists tampering with nature, or plain foolish
- as one delegate put it, monkeys playing with matches.
Philippe Busquin, European commissioner for research, acknowledged that
scientists are suspected of having a secret agenda, but countered that while
science and technology move on, much of the world still lives in
poverty.
He sketched the dilemma facing developing countries: if they produce
genetically modified products, such as sugar, they risk losing European
markets. If they don't, they risk being uncompetitive.
The recent experience of Zambia seems to indicate that developing
countries can't even accept GM food as a gift for fear of offending European
importers.
But genetic modification is not about boosting exports. What the
developing world seeks is improved crops to feed people - with foods they
know.
At present, 12 crops provide 90% of mankind's diet. Biotechnology
provides the opportunity to develop a far wider range from nature, including
local crops favoured by indigenous peoples and of little interest to giant
seed companies and commercial producers.
The developing world owns the vast majority of the gene bank. But, as
Kenyan scientist Florence Wambugu pointed out, having the biological
diversity is of no use if you do not have the technology to unlock it. And
the First World has the technology.
Maize, cotton, canola and soya are the main commercial GM crops being
grown at present, but a cassava plant that is immune to pests, for instance,
would benefit Central Africa, where it is the staple food.
Plants can also be modified to increase their vitamin and mineral
content in order to reduce malnutrition. This is done by introducing a gene
into a plant - which could already have about 25 000 genes - in order to add
another characteristic. The hybridising of plants has done this over
centuries, now it can be done at once.
There are 3 500 varieties of cassava alone, and the entire botanical
gene pool is vast.
Such biotechnology is seen as essential to feed the growing population
of the planet without destroying its resources, although all agree it is
just part of an agricultural programme that must include traditional
knowledge, soil conservation and organic farming.
Professor Ismael Serageldin, director of the library of Alexandria in
Egypt, pointed out that the world would need 40% more grain to feed itself
by 2020, and subSaharan Africa would need 10.5% more by the same date. As
organic peasant farming cannot meet the demand, the only way to achieve this
is through increased yields.
There is not much more arable land left on the planet - unless
biotechnology solves the problem of farming on acid soils, which make up 40%
of the world's arable lands.
If sugar cane became more resistant to cold, far more could be grown to
produce both food and ethanol as an alternative to petrol.
But African and other delegates at the Brussels conference were adamant
that they set their own biotechnology agendas.
The needs of ordinary farmers, especially women, who produce 80% of
Africa's food, must also be taken into account.
There are already a number of African research projects like the
University of Cape Town's Professor Jennifer Thompson's drought-resistant
maize and Wambugu's work on virus- and weevil-resistant sweet potatoes.
And a number of diseases - such as Sigatoka disease in bananas, mosaic
disease in cassava and Aflatoxin mould on harvested maize - could have
devastating effects on food crops if no genetic "cure" is found for
them.
But opponents of GM crops argue that there is no food shortage in the
world - only distribution problems.
Louise Fresco, of the UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation, said that
although there was no convincing evidence of negative effects of GM
organisms on humans so far, that was not proof that there were none.
Scientific evaluation of risks all along the food chain was the only way
to win the public's acceptance.
But genetic modification is happening irrespective of whether Europe has
a moratorium on its products, and the Brussels conference only just avoided
descending into the European row that has been going on for two
decades.
"We are using it, our governments are supporting it, we are here to ask
how you can help," an irritated Wambugu told delegates.
David McConnell, of Trinity College, Dublin, expressed the fear that
Europe's polarised debate was in danger of being exported to the rest of the
world. "We are going to be doing great damage to the developing countries,"
he warned.
Biotechnology has yet to produce substantial results of interest to
developing countries, but this will change as they do their own
research.
It is happening, Pandora's box is open.

Original_Search_Title: Good Old Tastes of Home a Must for 'Fussy' Ozzies
Headline: South Africa: Good Old Tastes of Home a Must for 'Fussy' Ozzies
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — THE Australians are not only the most difficult cricket team to beat on
the field - they are also the most demanding in the dietary stakes.
The reigning Cricket World Cup champions have kept hotels on the hop
with their requests for imported beer, sandwich spread and no oil or fat in
their meals.
By contrast, 1999 Cricket World Cup runners-up Pakistan, India, England,
New Zealand and South Africa are proving a roll-over, in the hotel stakes,
at least. Heinz Kerber, executive chef at the Sandton Sun, said this week
the Australians were the "fussiest" of his World Cup guests.
The Sandton Sun is part of the Southern Sun group of hotels, which is
the official host of all the cricket teams.
Said Kerber: "The Australians are the most strict by far. Their
breakfast is not allowed to have any fat or oil in it." The team also
requests lots of cereals, fresh fruits and yoghurt.
Team members turned up their noses at Marmite and local beer, insisting
only their local beer, Fosters, and the Australian equivalent of Marmite,
called Vegemite, would do.
"They told us right from the start that they do not like the taste of
Marmite, so we had to import Vegemite especially for them," said
Kerber.
"They also were not very keen on our local brew and we had to find
Fosters for them."
He said the team had also brought along their own energy drinks, which
were stocked next to the Fosters in the fridge in the team's common
room.
Jonathan Rose, media manager for Australia, confirmed that the team had
strict dietary requirements, saying that every player's diet was set by a
dietician.
He confirmed team members had asked for Fosters beer and Vegemite. "Home
is where the heart is," he said.
Kerber said Pakistan was the only other team that had special dietary
requirements, but these were relatively simple to fulfil.
"The meat has to be sourced at halaal butcheries.
"Aside from this, we have had to make sure we do not ever serve team
members pork," he said.
Kerber said the hotel had also stocked up on exotic teas to please its
Asian guests.
"When it comes to the Asian sides, we know they like their teas, so we
have made sure we have good Ceylon and Jasmine teas available at all times
in the team rooms," he said .
Hotel staff members had treated all the teams equally, Kerber said.
However, they couldn't help showing a little favouritism towards "their
boys", the South African players.
The hotel assigned South African cricket captain Shaun Pollock the same
bedroom in which he stayed previously and also made sure that his favourite
sweets, Jelly Tots, were in a jar in his room, even though he had not
requested this.
The other team members were also given rooms they had occupied
previously.
But despite this "favouring" of South Africa, all the teams said they
were happy with the service they were receiving.
The New Zealand cricket team said they had been bowled over by South
African food.
"The food here has been great. The only thing we make sure they give us
lots of is protein and energy-replacement bars.
"But something the team loves the most is the double-thick chocolate
milkshakes. They have become a real favourite with team members since we've
been here. The steakhouses have the best milkshakes we've ever tasted," said
cricketer Daryl Tuffey.
He said the team rooms in the different hotels had also proved a
winner.
"Table tennis has been a real winner with us. We've had
mini-championships among ourselves. Some of the guys even brought their
PlayStations and X-Box sets and have been playing non-stop in their spare
time," said Tuffey.
Team manager for England Andrew Walpole said his team had been glutting
themselves on the golf courses and were also enjoying the food.
"A lot of the guys love a game of golf and South Africa has beautiful
courses. So we have been doing a lot of that in our spare time," he
said.
"The food here has been great and adventurous. The pap is interesting,
but we still prefer potatoes. But we've enjoyed the game dishes, as well as
the braais."
Pakistan team manager Shaharyar Khan said his team were having no
trouble at all finding food, despite their dietary requirements. " There is
no problem because we have found a lot of established Asian restaurants in
whichever town we have visited," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Outreach Programme for Non-Traditional Exports
Headline: Ghana: Outreach Programme for Non-Traditional Exports
Body_Text:
Accra — The Ghana Export Promotion Council (GEPC) in collaboration with Ministry
of Food and Agriculture has embarked on an outreach programme to district
assemblies to expand the production base of selected non-traditional export
products.
Mr. Gerald Nyarko-Mensah, head of the General Promotion Division of
GEPC, who made this known in an interview with ADM yesterday, said 25
district assemblies are taking part in the programme, which is being carried
out in the Central, Western, Eastern, Ashanti, Volta and the Greater Accra
regions.
He said the Central Region is cultivating sweet potatoes and bird's eye
chilli pepper, Eastern, Ashanti and Western regions are cultivating pepper,
Greater Accra is cultivating mangoes and the Volta Region is cultivating
pepper and vegetables.
"Apart from the cultivation of pepper in the Ashanti Region, additional
products are being developed. These include traditional textiles such as
kente, brass ware, wooden handicrafts as well as ceramics," he said
He said in the middle of last year a nursery for plants was established
at Baifikrom near Mankessim in the Central Region and Dodowa in the Greater
Accra Region. He said GEPC has targeted about 200 acres for sweet potatoes
and 2,500 acres for bird's eye chillies.
Mr. Nyarko-Mensah said each district assembly is expected to give the
seedlings free of charge to farmers who would be interested in cultivating
them in the various assemblies. He said GEPC would train the youth of the
districts that would be interested.
After cultivation, GEPC would buy the products from the farmers and
export them. However, he said, farmers who would like to export the products
themselves, would be assisted.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Vice Minister Appraises Work
Headline: Angola: Agriculture Vice Minister Appraises Work
Body_Text:
Huambo — Angolan vice minister of Agriculture and Rural Development, Zacarias
Sambeny, travelled today from Londuimbale to Bailundo, two districts of
central Huambo province.
The trip is meant to appraise the results of the 2002/03 farming season
as an element in the fight against famine.
The official that began last Friday a three-day working visit to the
interior of Humbo province, is also travelling this afternoon to the
district of Katchiungo.
Any of the districts included on the deputy minister's visiting agenda
has a strong potential for the production of maize, beans, potato, sweet
potato, horticultural crops, fruits, Soya and wheat.
During the visits, the deputy minister is holding contacts with the
administrations and local peasants, from whom he is receiving information on
the work being carried out in terms of the current agricultural season.
This is the first season in an Angolan of peace, after 27 years of civil
war.

Original_Search_Title: Back to the Land Call
Headline: Gambia: Back to the Land Call
Body_Text:
Banjul — The relentless call by President, Yahya Jammeh for the public,
particularly the youths to engage in agricultural activities as a
means of promoting food self-sufficiency and providing self
employment has been heard by almost everyone both in the urban and
rural areas.
It is prudent and essential call from the Head of state, which we
support and declare that benefits can be achieved if land is more
effectively utilised in the country. This activity will enable us to
feed ourselves instead of depending on imported foodstuffs and
provide some form of gainful employment for our youths.
Unfortunately, the youths do not appear motivated or zealous to go
and farm.
Let me highlight that some of us in Fajara area who have enough land
undertake small scale farming during the rainy season to grow
cassava, sweet potatoes, groundnuts, black eyed bears, okra and
maize. Back to the land call sounds ideal theoretically, but the many
constraints faced in farming cannot be over looted, for they are
many. First of all it is a problem to find reliable, hardworking and
willing workers to help on our mini farms and gardens.
There are numerous pests, which continuously attack plants - such as
termites, and white bug that actually destroys cassava and banana
plants and even citrus trees. There is also another bug which leaves
a black film or layer on the leaves of the affected plants and the
bug which used to affect mango trees two years ago but tends to
recur. Presently, at the moment, it seems that some pest attacks
bananas because many of these plants look poorly unkempt with leaves
drying up and are not producing good fruit. We are plagued by these
different species of pests throughout the year and sometimes even
after one has had the plants sprayed at a high cost. The white bug
know as the mealy bug is most resilient.
The president has demonstrated how effectively and efficiently the
land can be utilised to grow melons, rice, maize, cassava, egg plant,
bananas and more, on his farm at Kanilai. We have been impressed with
the bumper harvests of these crops at his farms. It must not be
forgotten that he has all the facilities to enjoy farming. There are
men and women at his disposal, who are willing to offer their
services to assist him on his farm because so many of them wanted to
be seen helping the president. Even the Lebanese community who are
mostly business orientated, were actually viewed on the television
busied harvesting president's maize on his farm, a most unusual and
humorous spectacle.
As regards pests, the president was seen using his own spray on the
farm. I hope that water spray was not mistaken for innecticide spray.
The president has all the facilities available at his doorsteps and
workers at his "Beck and call" which makes farming rewardingly
enjoyable and unimpeded by lack of funds and other frustrations that
constrained ordinary men or women. Little wonder he is so
enthusiastic in his untiring, "Back to the land" proclamations.
On GRTS's 'Bizor Business talk', which is produced and presented by
Lamin Manga, Mr. Momodou Ceesay, proprietor of the Gambia
Horticultural Enterprise (G.H.E) of Old Jeswang was interviewed and
he highlighted some of his frustrations and constraints in farming.
He dilated on the citro products programme where over one thousand
lime trees were planted, which succeeded for a while and the
limejuice exported to U.K.
He described the interest charged on bank loans as high and difficult
to pay back unless one can guarantee to make profits on the business
even at its infancy stage, which is not feasible.
Acquiring land for large scale farming is laborious and difficult to
obtain loans for private individuals, because banks are not willing
to give out loans unless one produces documents, describing the
project, and outlining details of its viability and so on. Employing
suitable workers presents problems because most people could not
afford to invest on menial jobs involved in farming. But to work for
President Yahya Jammeh, is quite another matter, because they would
be proud to work on his farm to show up themselves to Jammeh.
In the past, there have been lots of Gambian farmers, who have
ardently tilled the lands, among them are men like Rev. J.C. Faye (a
politician) and minister of religion) Mr. Sam Old Field of Tabokoto,
Mr. Samhurack of Sukuta, Sanjally Bojang of Brikama. More recently,
Dr. Sam Palmer, Mr. Omar Dibba (a past Economist) Dr. Fred Oldfield,
Dr. Lamin Sidibeh (the Psycologist) Mr. Steven Bidwell, just to list
few names. Also, Dr.
Lenrie Peters embarked on commercial farming and used to export
vegetables and mangoes to the U.K. He successfully grew Irish
potatoes for the local markets.
Definitely, we would all be competing with President Yahya Jammeh if
we have all the facilities and implements that he has at his
disposal.
Sangol farms, Nemakunku and Citro Products enterprise are added to
the list of farms.

Original_Search_Title: Second Year of Drought in Gaza
Headline: Mozambique: Second Year of Drought in Gaza
Body_Text:
Maputo — At least 119,500 people in the southern
Mozambican province of Gaza are regarded as in an "emergency
situation" because of the drought that has hit the province for
the second consecutive year, reports Wednesday's issue of the
independent newsheet "Vertical".
Maize fields have withered in the scorching heat, and lack
of rain. Along the banks of the Changane river, a tributary of
the Limpopo, the paper notes, one can note field after field of
withered crops.
Small dams have dried up, and the only rain the province has
seen in recent months has been light showers - quite insufficient
for crops such as maize.
According to Felix Tivane, Gaza provincial coordinator of
the country's relief agency, the National Disasters Management
Institute (INGC), the "most critical areas" are the districts of
Guija, Massingir, Mabalane, Cicualacuala and Chigubo, all in the
Gaza interior, in the area usually referred to as the upper
Limpopo.
"The drought victims in Gaza", said Tivane, "have no food
reserves, except for those living near the banks of the Limpopo,
Save and Nguaneze rivers, and some small lakes. They manage some
agricultural production by resorting to the humidity of these
areas".
What has proved a life saver has been the food-for-work
programme, which covers about 80 per cent of the 119,500 people
affected. Under the programme basic foodstuffs, such as grains,
beans and vegetable oil, are provided by the UN World Food
Programme (WFP), and by various NGOs. In return for the food, the
beneficiaries work on building access roads, drainage channels,
schools, clinics and houses for teachers and health workers.
Many of those working on these programmes are women heads of
household (Gaza has a high percentage of its men working in South
Africa). 
 The deal is that one member of each household works, and
then receives food for five people - 75 kilos of grain, 7.5 kilos
of beans and 3.5 litres of vegetable oil per month.
Tivane estimates the food needs for drought victims in Gaza
at 1,792 tonnes of grain, 179 tonnes of beans and 89,6 tonnes of
oil a month. 
 He said the government has been recommending that peasants
switch to drought resistant crops such as sweet potatoes and
cassava, and that the farmers also plant cash crops such as
cashew trees. Tivane claimed that experimental planting of
cashews in Chicualacuala was giving "positive results".

Original_Search_Title: Zambia Episcopal Conference Secures K5bn Worth Relief Foods
Headline: Zambia Episcopal Conference Secures K5bn Worth Relief Foods
Body_Text:
THE Zambia Episcopal Conference (ZEC) has secured relief commodities from its cooperating partners amounting to $190,000, (about K5 billion) for distribution.
In a joint statement released in Lusaka yesterday ZEC president Bishop Telesphore Mpundu and director for development Bishop Paul Duffy, said the gesture was in response to the national food crisis.
The statement said about 3,800 tonnes of assorted foods comprising mostly white maize and beans had been bought locally mainly from small scale farmers as a way of providing market for them.
Others include sorghum, cow peas, cassava cuttings and sweet potato vines.
The beneficiaries of the relief commodities will be mainly the vulnerable groups in the community with emphasis on orphans, terminally ill and the aged.
The exercise is being carried out by the Catholic Commission for Development of the Catholic Centre for Justice Development and Peace and being implemented by diocesan development teams.

Original_Search_Title: Food Deficit Threat Causes Concern
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Food Deficit Threat Causes Concern
Body_Text:
Abidjan — Cote d'Ivoire's government is worried that food insecurity might increase this year as a result of a precarious economic and food situation in rural areas affected by a four-month-old armed conflict.
Massive population flight from productive zones to safer areas has led to a drop in farming activities, the minister of agriculture and rural development, Danon Djedje, told journalists on 17 January. This has reduced food stocks in the areas hosting the internally displaced persons (IDPs), Carmel Obou, information officer in the agriculture ministry told IRIN.
To prevent a food deficit, the Ivorian government was to mount an emergency food project called 'Projet riz pour tous' (Rice-for-all Project). The project, expected to cost an estimated 4.25 billion CFA (US $7.2 million) seeks to offset the long delay in the start of the current planting season in the war zones and areas near the frontline between government troops and rebels of the Mouvement patriotique de Cote d'Ivoire (MPCI).
Carmel said the government would use one billion CFA to subsidise the rice-for-all project. The remaining 3.25 billion CFA would go to agricultural groups that will be cultivating the cereal on a total area of 125,000 ha.
Preparations for the project were underway in government-controlled areas in central and west-central Cote d'Ivoire and around the economic capital, Abidjan. The first phase of planting, due to start in April, is expected to yield 26,623 mt of rice while the state is to import about 500,377 mt.
The 2003 rice harvest had been estimated at 617,000 mt before the rebellion began in September 2002. According to the agriculture ministry, annual consumption is estimated at 1,145,000 mt.
Cries of alarm have already been sounded in some of the areas directly affected by fighting between loyalist troops and rebels. A local newspaper quoted the mayor of the western town of Duekoue as saying that about 7,000 displaced people in his area would run short of food if nothing was done.
Crops have been rotting in fields in the central region of Sakassou, about 310 km north of Abidjan, and the scene of fierce fighting in the early days of the insurgency. People there are afraid to go to harvest their plantains and bananas, Kouadio N'Guessan Paul, a farmer from Sakassou, told the local press.
Many of the people displaced from such areas had sought refuge in the capital, Yamoussoukro, 2666 km north of Abidjan. A state crisis committee in Yamoussoukro said it needed over 2.7 million CFA (just under US $5,000) per month to feed 1,200 IDPs living in the town itself.
According to the committee, 400 host families were providing accommodation to other IDPs in the Yamoussoukro area. Information provided by these households shows that their combined monthly food bill was over 24 million CFA (almost US $41,000), a member of the crisis committee, which is led by the prefect of the region, said.
In the southwest, where the second largest port, San Pedro, is located, food aid donated by NGOs, religious groups and charitable persons had benefited thousands of IDPs, the Red Cross in San Pedro told IRIN. More was however needed to feed children and adults.
In rebel-held Bouake, about 111 km north of Yamoussoukro, more and more people had reportedly been reduced to destitution. According to various testimonies, households have had to cut back on the rice, sweet potatoes and plantains they eat so as to make sure their members obtain a minimum of food each day. The few proprietors of local restaurants and other food sellers told local reporters that they sometimes gave out food for free and were worried because they risked going bankrupt.
In government-controlled areas, there have been significant price hikes. Irie Lou, chairperson of the Agricultural Producers Cooperative of Cote d'Ivoire in a radio interview on 21 January, urged retailers to keep prices at pre-crisis levels.
Salaries, she argued, were no longer enough for families when they have to board and lodge IDPs. In many cases, the number of persons per household had more than doubled.
In November, the World Food Programme (WFP) launched an appeal for $6.6 million dollars to provide food for people who need it in Cote d'Ivoire. At Monday's press conference, WFP said it only had enough food to assist the affected populations until March.
WFP has been providing for 175,000 persons through the distribution of emergency food rations in the north, center and west of the country, WFP information officer Sam Oussou told IRIN.
Should the crisis continue, it will have severe consequences for Cote d'Ivoire and its neighbours, WFP's emergency assistance coordinator, Gemmo Lodesani, said.

Original_Search_Title: Over 300 Tons of Seed Distributed in 2002
Headline: Angola: Over 300 Tons of Seed Distributed in 2002
Body_Text:
Kuito — At least 326 tons of assorted seeds were distributed in Angola's central Bie province in 2002 by the Irish Non-Governmental Organization "CONCERN".
The information was released by CONCERN's provincial director for agricultural projects, Eduardo JonatÃ£o AntÃ³nio, who said the donation included maize, beans, soya and pumpkin seeds.
According to him, farming implements were also distributed as a means to help improve and increase production in the region.
The source also said about 49.735.00 displaced families benefited from the donation, including residents of the districts of Kunhinga and Kuito.
The same source stressed that for this year CONCERN will engage in distributing and buying seeds to grow sweet potatoes, plant slips of cassava and deal in small animals breeding.

Original_Search_Title: Opposition MPs Boo President
Headline: Zambia: Opposition MPs Boo President
Body_Text:
Lusaka — OPPOSITION members of parliament yesterday booed and jeered President Levy Mwanawasa when he told the House that his new deal government had opened up offices for all the MPs.
And President Mwanawasa told the House that the on-going presidential petition would take a long time for it to be concluded.
Officially opening the National Assembly, President Mwanawasa disclosed that so far 29 offices had been secured and taken up by MPs.
"I am disappointed, however, that in some cases, Honourable members have refused to take up offices secured for them on grounds that they had to meet the cost of running these offices claiming that I had said that the offices should be given free of any charge," said President Mwanawasa as the opposition MPs responded on top of their voices "Aaah...aaah. Where? Where?"
He appealed to the MPs to appreciate the need for the councils to be charging nominal fees to maintain the offices.
President Mwanawasa told the House that it remained certain that the presidential petition would not be concluded soon.
He said the continuation of the election court proceedings had robbed the country of opportunities for all Zambians to forge ahead together.
President said the presidential petition had led to the wastage of other resources.
He said poor Zambians would continue to suffer due to politicians' habit of politicking and throwing insults at each other.
"At the pace the hearing is progressing, it seems clear to me that this case will take long to be completed," said President Mwanawasa amid murmuring from the opposition MPs. "As President of the Republic of Zambia, I have resolved not to allow these court proceedings to inhibit my government work or allow these selfish politicians to divide the country."
President Mwanawasa asked politicians to stop making political capital out of the food shortages and acute hunger in the country.
He warned that making treasonable statements and inciting violence would only make those involved answerable to the law.
President Mwanawasa said Zambia required men and women of vision and integrity to join hands with the ruling party in taking the country forward.
He reaffirmed his desire to appoint some of the opposition MPs as ministers in his government.
"I am still convinced that this nation would benefit from their contribution as members of the executive," he said.
President Mwanawasa extended an olive branch to all serious political parties in the country to begin the process of working together on all national issues across political affiliations.
"I intend to work closely with all opposition political parties, opposition members of parliament, civil society, NGOs, the Church and indeed citizens who want to take Zambia forward," he said.
President Mwanawasa said he intended to start inviting some of the opposition MPs to accept ministerial appointments in his government.
He said the opposition parties should not only want to come together in order to become strong and sole purpose of removing him from State House.
"That kind of alliance shall not succeed but shall only seek to weaken the government to the detriment of Zambia," President Mwanawasa said. "While I wish to commend UPND, FDD and ZRP for their efforts in forming the alliance, please come together so that you can strengthen your parties for the sake of enhancing development for our people but not to simply remove and replace me with anybody else because that alone cannot change the lives of the people for the better."
He said the opposition alliance would fail if the ultimate aim was the presidency.
President Mwanawasa said it was sad for the country to see some leaders desperately riding on tribal inclinations in order to survive both politically and economically.
On governance, President Mwanawasa said his government would continue to facilitate the training of investigating officers in human rights, criminal investigation, prosecutions, counselling, crime management, gender violence and child abuse.
President Mwanawasa said it was clear that the Constitution had serious conflicts, inconsistencies and lacunas.
He said his government was concerned because it wanted a constitution which reflected the feelings of Zambians and stood the test of time.
President Mwanawasa disclosed that the Church had pledged to work with his government in formulating an ideal constitution.
He described the meeting as very friendly and beneficial to both parties.
President Mwanawasa urged Zambians to make their submission with the Law Development Commission.
He said the nation was greatly concerned at the long delay in concluding cases involving suspects in the plunder of the country's resources.
President Mwanawasa told the House that the Task Force had concluded a good number of cases but failed to proceed any further because of the indemnity stay of execution case still pending in court.
He defended the appointment of Mark Chona as the executive chairman of the Task Force saying the move was to avert continued conflicting style of leaders of the command which was rotating between the Inspector-General of Police and the Commissioner of the Drug Enforcement Commission.
"In many cases, it became clear to me that progress was being hampered by conflicting styles of command," he said.
On tourism, President Mwanawasa told the House that he had directed tourism minister Marina Nsingo to deal with the problems surrounding the safari hunting.
He said safari hunting was a major source of revenue for both Zambia Wildlife Authority (ZAWA) and the local communities.
President Mwanawasa said safari hunting had economic implications on the hospitality industry and several other sectors of the country.
He said the ban on safari hunting since 1999 had resulted in increased poaching due to diminishing resources.
"I am, therefore, directing all the key institutions under the co-ordination of the Ministry of Tourism, Environment and Natural Resources to quickly take all the necessary steps to resolve all outstanding issues affecting safari hunting so that normalcy can return to this important sector," he said.
President Mwanawasa further called for the promotion of Zambians' participation in the tourism sector.
According to President Mwanawasa, 73 per cent of the K36 billion raised by tourism sector investment last year was by Zambians.
He said Zambian investors created 862 job opportunities for Zambians.
President Mwanawasa called for the establishment of a tourism faculty at one of the country's universities for advanced studies in tourism and hospitality management.
Nyampala Safari director John Matimba commended President Mwanawasa for taking keen interest in tourism.
He asked Nsingo to follow President Mwanawasa's directive so that injustices committed against the sector could be resolved.
"We have no way out apart from giving back the concessions to the seating operators," said Matimba.
Four local safari operators have dragged government to court for awarding majority of the safari hunting blocks to the foreign operators.
President Mwanawasa called for timely presentation of the Auditor General's annual reports to the House.
He said late tabling of Auditor General's reports encouraged theft, corruption and abuse of office in the public service.
"To this effect, I would like to see the report for 2002 tabled this year," he said.
President Mwanawasa demanded for follow-up actions based on the recommendations given.
He said government was determined to come up with an electoral process that was cardinal and acceptable.
President Mwanawasa said the process of coming up with acceptable electoral rules that would eradicate incidence of elections disputes would be completed before the next presidential and general
elections.
On the media, President said a free political society could only exist with a free new media.
He said democracy could only thrive if ideas, views and opinions had a channel for their effective expression.
President Mwanawasa directed information minister Newstead Zimba to ensure that installation of television transmitters in rural areas under the rural television project was completed before the end of this year.
Opposition MPs, again booed President Mwanawasa when he told the House that he had fulfilled almost what he promised in the education sector.
"I am pleased to report to this August House that in the education sector, we have delivered on almost all the promises I made last year," said President Mwanawasa as the opposition MPs responded "No!"
He disclosed that groundwork had been laid for the establishment of a third university in Kabwe.
On HIV/AIDS, President Mwanawasa said AIDS patients occupied more than half of the hospital beds in major hospitals in the country.
He advised Zambians to abstain from the behaviour that was likely to put them at risk of being infected by the HIV virus.
President Mwanawasa said threats of industrial unrest was discouraging foreign investment.
He urged the labour movement to ensure that all existing channels were exhausted before workers resorted to industrial action.
President Mwanawasa said government had started the process of enacting and strengthening and or amending gender discriminatory laws and procedures to make all forms of discrimination such as gender violence, rape, spouse battering and child abuse punishable with stiffer penalties.
He disclosed that government would this year implement the gender policy.
President Mwanawasa disclosed that government was considering opening up few more missions in countries where benefits would be justifiable.
He commended officers from the armed forces for their excellent performance and achievement in their role of preserving, protecting and defending the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the country.
President Mwanawasa said Zambia would remain active in matters of conflict resolution and peace keeping.
He said the policies and targets contained in the 2002 economic programme were intended to address the deepening poverty situation.
President Mwanawasa said the government was clearly mindful of the economic conditions that prevailed in 1991 when the MMD government came to power.
"Equally, we acknowledged the structural and policy shortcomings," he said.
President Mwanawasa said the government had a daunting task of attempting to achieve a sustainable transformation of the economy.
"Mr Speaker, I must remind the nation that the task of attaining a sustainable transformation of our economy to one in which all our citizens are not only stakeholders but beneficiaries, is far from being achieved," President Mwanawasa said. "However, it essential for all of us to remain optimistic since this nation has abundant natural resources."
President Mwanawasa said government's task was to create the right conditions for economic activity to attract investors and create employment for the people.
He said all the policy and sectoral objectives in 2002 were aimed at supporting the achievement of high economic growth, employment creation and poverty reduction.
President Mwanawasa said the targets were to be achieved through diversification in production and in exports.
He said the government's macroeconomic objectives in 2002 were aimed at achieving economic growth of at least 4.3 per cent, reducing annual inflation to 13 per cent from 18.7 per cent recorded in December 2001 and maintaining a stable exchange rate.
President Mwanawasa said the government further targeted to limit the domestic fiscal deficit to 3.1 per cent of gross domestic product (GDP) from 4.7 per cent achieved in 2001.
He said the lowering of the budget deficit was expected to contribute to the reduction of the high bank lending rates thereby encouraging the private sector to borrow for productive investment.
President Mwanawasa said pro-growth incentives were introduced in 2002, which included the lowering of excise duty on diesel from 60 per cent to 30 per cent and on electricity from seven per cent to five per cent.
He said in the social sectors, the government endeavoured to maintain its commitment to provision of social services at 36 per cent of the discretionary expenditure.
President Mwanawasa said preliminary estimates indicate that the performance of the economy in 2002 did not meet these expectations.
He said growth was sluggish, unemployment continued to be high while macro economic conditions were characterised by high inflation and interest rates and depreciation of the kwacha.
President Mwanawasa said the execution of the budget faced numerous difficulties emanating from increased expenditure arising from maize imports and wage adjustments.
"This resulted in the suppression of some expenditure items, which unfortunately included some poverty reduction programmes," he said.
President Mwanawasa said even though revenues were on target for 2002, the increased expenditures resulted in a higher than programmed fiscal deficit by the end of the year.
President Mwanawasa said the situation contributed to the loosening of the monetary conditions and an increase in inflation to 25 per cent in December 2002.
He said export revenues recorded a decline during 2002 mainly due to a fall in metal export revenues resulting from weak metal prices on the world market.
He said as the government implements its programmes this year, there would be need to apply the scarce resources efficiently and in a transparent manner.
President Mwanawasa said accordingly, the government was developing an integrated financial management information system to address the problem.
He said in addition, the government was developing a medium term expenditure framework that would provide for a planning policy.
President Mwanawasa said the government would this year look for ways in which industry, service providers and farmers could be encouraged to extend their work schedule.
He said the restructuring of most of the ministries has facilitated the rightful placement of qualified human resource.
President Mwanawasa said there was need, however, to look at the conditions of service to attract and retain the right calibre of personnel.
He said he was glad that the Transitional National Development Plan (TNDP) was launched on December 6, 2002.
President Mwanawasa said the emphasis of the plan was on economic growth, job creation and poverty reduction.
He said the re-introduction of national development plans does not mean that the government was reverting to socialist principles.
"To the contrary, the idea is to consolidate the development of the private sector and purposefully co-ordinate our national development efforts," President Mwanawasa said.
He said implementation of the TNDP would remain a pipe dream if the plan is not reflected on the ground.
President Mwanawasa said the plan was set in a regional context so that members of parliament could have a role to play.
He re-assured that there was no contradiction between the TNDP and the Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper (PRSP).
President Mwanawasa said the PRSP was a strategy for growth and poverty reduction in the country while the TNDP on the other was a comprehensive medium term plan document which encompasses the PRSP.
"Thus in preparing the TNDP, all sectors and priorities in the PRSP were brought into the TNDP," President Mwanawasa said.
He said the TNDP also means that the annual budget would have to indicate what resources were being allocated to each province and district for development programmes.
President Mwanawasa said the government's challenge this year would be to set up measures to raise economic growth and employment as outlined in the TNDP.
He said to support growth objectives in 2003, the government would aim at restoring macroeconomic stability, enhancing governance and improving productive infrastructure and human capital.
President Mwanawasa said in line with the Public Sector Reform Programme (PSRP), the government would endeavour to continue with the process of recapitalising all strategic state owned companies in an effort to make them operate profitably.
"However, while government is committed to making the remaining parastatals viable, some management tendencies in these companies do not reflect this," he said.
President Mwanawasa said the government was anxious that the parastatals are operated in a manner that reflects good business acumen.
"I therefore urge all boards and their chief executives in these parastatals to relentlessly pursue quality management," President Mwanawasa said.
He said because development would remain a pipe dream as long as the issue of debt was not adequately handled, he was directing that a comprehensive debt policy and strategy be urgently developed.
President Mwanawasa said the policy would guide the country on issues of contracting and management of debt.
In terms of agriculture, President Mwanawasa said in trying to achieve national and household food security for both rural and urban areas in the country, the government would promote all-year round agriculture production at competitive costs.
He said the government would ensure that existing agricultural resources are maintained and improved upon through innovations such as conservation farming, crop diversification and rotation.
President Mwanawasa said the government intends to transform the resettlement schemes located in all the nine provinces into centres of high agricultural production.
"To this end, government will provide the necessary support in terms of basic infrastructure, credit and markets to boost agricultural production in these areas," President Mwanawasa said.
He said in order to complement food production, he was directing the Zambia National Service (ZNS) to aggressively implement its mandate of food production.
President Mwanawasa said ZNS would have to make use of the Airport Farm to grow both irrigated and rainfed crops.
He said he had further instructed ZNS to acquire more land for both cropping and ranching throughout the country.
President Mwanawasa said in an effort to increase investment in agriculture, the government was making more land available for farming.
He said in that regard, the government had set up an inter-sectoral land identification committee which he said had so far identified potential farm blocks in all provinces to cater for large, medium and small scale farmers.
President Mwanawasa said with a view of making agricultural inputs affordable to farmers, the government had established a national fertiliser support programme to distribute seed and fertilisers to vulnerable but viable small scale farmers.
He said the programme would continue so that more farmers could benefit.
President Mwanawasa said other efforts of making inputs cheaper include the food and security pack under the Programme Against Malnutrition (PAM).
He said the policy of diversification in the agricultural sector had started yielding results.
"For example, in Luapula Province, our farmers are producing crops which do not need costly chemical inputs such as sweet potatoes, cassava, millet, beans and groundnuts," he said.
President Mwanawasa said the government would take advantage of the withdrawal last year of the Crop Marketing Authority (CMA) Bill to consult further.
He said the government would specifically look at concerns of separation of the food reserve and crop marketing functions in the CMA.
President Mwanawasa urged the Ministry of Agriculture and Co-operatives to quickly review the CMA Bill so that it goes back to Parliament in the shortest possible time.
He said the government had put in place measures aimed at improving the development of fisheries by promoting sustainable fishing methods and promoting aquaculture.
President Mwanawasa said the yearly fish ban was assisting greatly in the conservation of fish resources.
He said the government would take steps to fill the gaps in the agricultural system by creating special purpose vehicles to promote crops of high potential in income generation.
President Mwanawasa said diversification of Zambia's mineral resources was critical to the country's development.
He said the exploitation of mineral resources required a strategy that could attract local and foreign investment which could exploit the non traditional minerals such as gemstones, agro and industrial minerals.
President Mwanawasa said Zambia's high potential for unexploited mineral deposits offered vast prospects for generating economic growth and increasing incomes and employment.
He said exploitation of the vast resources was being hampered by the lack of the necessary infrastructure particularly in remote parts of the country where mineral occurrences are found.
President Mwanawasa said he was directing the Zambia Consolidated Copper Mines Investment Holdings (ZCCM-IH) to take a more active role in resuscitating Maamba Collieries.
He said if ZCCM-IH had taken a pro-active interest in Roan Mine Antelope Corporation of Zambia (RAMCOZ), the situation could not have reached the point of closure.
"Even the pull-out of Anglo American Plc at Konkola Copper Mines could not have taken us by surprise," he said.
President Mwanawasa said the government, through the ZCCM-IH was already collaborating with First Quantum in the development of Kansanshi copper ore bodies in North Western Province.
He said the mine was envisaged to be developed into phases to start this year once the major constraint of infrastructure was addressed.
He said because the government attaches great importance to small scale mining, it was establishing a gemstone exchange to help small scale miners sell their stones.
President Mwanawasa said Zambian companies need to take steps to ensure that the quality and price of their products are competitive under the African Growth Opportunity Act (AGOA).
He said if they did not do that, the country stood to lose especially that preferences under AGOA are time bound.
President Mwanawasa said on implementing the Export Processing Zones (EPZ), the government would also provide fiscal incentives to specialised lending institutions to encourage them to provide development finance for long term projects.
He said local investors would benefit from incentives to be provided in the Investment Act which is due for amendment this year.
"Other incentives will cover joint venture partnerships between Zambians and foreign investors in order to facilitate indigenous Zambian entrepreneurs in order to facilitate indigenous Zambian entrepreneurs access to investment capital and technology," said President Mwanawasa.
UPND vice-president Sakwiba Sikota asked President Mwanawasa to stop commenting on the on-going presidential election petition.
He asked President Mwanawasa to respect the judicial process of the country if he was leading a government of laws.
Sikota advised President Mwanawasa to take Law Association of Zambia's (LAZ) advice on the matter.
"As a lawyer, I expected him to stay away from the issue and stop commenting on the case because it was before the court," said Sikota.
Chipangali UNIP member of parliament Lucas Phiri commended President Mwanawasa's decision to extend an olive branch to the opposition MPs.
He said the move was progressive because it would get rid of tribal politics and hatred.
Former UNIP member of the central committee General Kingsley Chinkuli said President Mwanawasa's speech was the beginning of a brighter future.
He said it was good that President Mwanawasa had expressed his willingness of having an inclusive government.
"Once we have an inclusive government, that is the starting point of development," said Gen. Chinkuli.

Original_Search_Title: U.S. Trade Rep. Zoellick Discusses First Day of Agoa Meeting
Headline: Africa: U.S. Trade Rep. Zoellick Discusses First Day of Agoa Meeting
Body_Text:
Port Louis — Press Roundtable with U.S. Trade Representative Robert Z. Zoellick
Mauritius.
Robert Zoellick: Just a few opening remarks. AGOA is something that is
very important to the United States because it fits the heart of President
Bush's concept of an expanding circle of development. It's been two years
since AGOA passed and it's already a proven road to success, as you've seen
with some of the examples that we've discussed.
Now it's a question of expanding on that success. At this meeting, what
we've already been doing and what we'll be doing over the next two days, is
to roll up our sleeves, share experiences, identify problems, and seek to
figure out how best to solve them. These are very practical working
sessions; people are not giving a lot of long general speeches. We have the
key people here from the United States and sub-Saharan Africa to get down to
work. We have trade people. We have development people. We've got people
from finance ministries. We have a number of important representatives from
the private sector from Africa as well as from the United States. I was told
we have here representatives from US apparel firms that cover about 90% of
Africa's exports to the United States. I'm going to try to meet them this
evening around the reception because I want to hear what they have to say
about some of the questions that we'll be discussing.
Just to give you an example: one of the important issues here will be
what the United States can do in dealing with some of the apparel questions
as our quotas come off in 2004 and create new competition for Africa.
We also have the issue, as many of you know, that AGOA has a time period
in which a number of the African countries can use third party fabric as
part of their apparel exports, but that expires in 2004. Some countries have
been developing yarn industries and textile industries, so they may be
interested in that expiration so that you can encourage the development of
this added industry in Africa. But others are dependent on that third party
fabric, so we're getting into some of these issues.
We're very fortunate -- I'm very pleased -- that we have a number of
members of Congress, including some from the House of Representatives' side
that are really key to getting things done. You've got the Chairman of the
Ways and Means Committee, Bill Thomas. That's a very powerful committee in
the US Congress. That's the committee that is responsible for passing all
tax legislation and all trade legislation. What they also represent,
something that I think is a very good signal, is that after AGOA was passed
in 2000, we learned some lessons about some things that we could improve on
and so last year we passed AGOA II. hope that some of these discussions
might create the basis for an AGOA III -- to show this is a living,
breathing economic partnership.
We have people here like Andrew Young and Carl Masters from Goodworks
International. This shows both the breadth of support that we have in the
United States for AGOA, but also shows the combination that is important to
achieve success by drawing on a wider segment of people interested in
Africa.
Now, the remarks that I have made, in bilaterals and my opening session
and will make at a special session tomorrow, also keep recalling that
whatever the United States and sub-Saharan Africa do at AGOA has to be seen
