in a global context. And we have a lot of business to do in the WTO
negotiations. We want to try to discuss with our African partners where
those negotiations stand and how we can try to work together.
We're very fortunate that while I'm from the US Trade Representative's
office, I lead a delegation that frankly has every office that can make a
difference. We've got an Under Secretary of Agriculture here. We've got
Andrew Natsios, the head of our AID office, which is important to have the
support related to trade. We have Sam Bodman, who is the Deputy Secretary of
Commerce, and has very good strong business background in engineering,
chemicals and also finance, and we have people from our trade and
development administration. We've got people from our Treasury
Department. And what I pointed out to my colleagues on the team is that we
are here as problems solvers. The starting point of that is to listen, to
try to understand more, how AGOA is working and how it can work better.
There's no sense of loss or sense of pride about that everything has to
be working well. There's nothing that I know of in life that can't be
improved and that's why we have these meetings; it's to make sure that we
fix the things that aren't working, and learn from the successes and expand
them. My job is to be the chief problem solver, but we do so as a
partnership. So, I'll be happy to take your questions.
Sydney Selvon (Financial News): You spoke of "AGOA III." What would
be the basis of new agreements?
Well, you know, one of the reasons that I'm here is to hear from
Africans, because they are the ones that are working with AGOA and can
figure out how it can be strengthened. Also, obviously, it's important that
we have some of the members of Congress because those are the people that
push the legislation through. But in general I would say there are issues
related, at this point, according to what I've heard here, to extending
dates, extending the life of all the AGOA provisions, and particularly the
question of the third party fabric provisions that would expire in
2004. Now, you're from Mauritius, and so you understand some of the balance
here. I was told that there was to be a cornerstone laying event later this
week where an investor is putting money into Mauritius to try to develop the
additional yarns and fabrics that could then be used throughout Africa. So,
from the Mauritius point of view, maybe there's a question of whether that
should be extended.
This has happened in South Africa as well. The members of Congress
shared with me that they stopped at one apparel plant that was thoroughly
the value chain; in other words, it had created the material, the textiles,
and also the apparel, which would show that it could all be done in
Africa. They also stopped in another one in Namibia, which is just under
AGOA II qualified for these extra benefits, and they needed to do some of
the imports of the third party fabric.
What I've said to some of those who might worry about the extension or
think that maybe we shouldn't extend it, is that we need to keep in mind
that economics and trade are not a zero-sum processes; in other words if you
can expand apparel and have good quality goods at lower prices, sometimes
you can also expand the market. People will get more clothes, they will have
more diversity in their clothes, and they'll change the lines. I see this as
an area where I hope that we can really do both: we can try to make sure
that in Africa we develop some of that textile and yarn and fabric industry
but also we help those, like Lesotho and others, who've been very
successful. We don't want to lose that success.
Keep in mind this also includes the environment where the United States
is committed, under our Uruguay Round agreement, to remove our quotas under
the multifiber agreement. That's a separate issue, but that's going to
happen at the same time, which means there are many countries around the
world, which have urged us to remove quotas, because it doesn't allow free
competition. We're now removing those quotas. Our average apparel tariff is
about 11% or 12%, which varies by product line, so an AGOA country will
still get a benefit if it has zero per cent as opposed to the 12%. But that
will make also tougher competition with China and India and others. So
that's another key question.
That's one of the reasons I want to meet a number of the apparel
companies because from talking to some of them in Washington, I've realized
they want to have diversity of sources and China will clearly be the leader
in this, but they want to have multiple sources. It also depends on where
you are in terms of the product. They feel that China will certainly be
successful on some of the low-end products and some of the African
countries, including Mauritius, have been moving up the chain a little bit
in terms of production. So, it gives you a good feel. Often, as a government
official, you discuss the general thrust of policy, but we're getting into
very practical issues here. I keep coming back to the point. I've dealt with
trade, economic and finance issues, private and public sector, and one of
the lessons I learned is you've got to talk to the people who are doing
it.
Matt Rosenberg (Associated Press): South Africa and Mauritius are in
good shape in terms of being able to cope with the end of the AGOA textile
package in 2005. Other African countries are not as strong. Will you support
extending the fabric package rules?
Well, that's what we're discussing with the Africans.
Rosenberg: But if it were up to you? Would you extend it?
Well, this is something we have to discuss with the members of
Congress. What I have expressed in the sessions was that I think this is a
very important issue. I mentioned that, in another context, I have talked
with Chairman Bill Thomas in the early part of the year about considering a
possible package of issues related to AGOA. I said let's hear what the
Africans have to say about it. But I personally have some sympathy with
those that are worried about making sure they have enough access to the
fabric components. So that's where I start out but I want to listen to
others.
Anthony Moreland (AFP): What is the likelihood of an extension of the
fabric package?
It's too early to say, in part because there's a lot of support for AGOA
in the United States, from the President to key people like Bill Thomas. But
whenever you pass trade legislation you get interests that don't want to
face the competition. Our apparel industry has lost some 600,000 jobs since
1994. We managed to get a number of those members from apparel districts to
vote for this the last time, members from my party I might say, because the
President pushed them very hard and, frankly, it would help us if we could
get a little more bipartisan support from Democrats as well as
Republicans. In our constitutional system, a law has to go to the House as
well as to the Senate. So I can't tell you that today but I can tell you
this: the meetings that we're having here help us get more information, they
help us pull together a coalition of people in the apparel industry, they
help us find out maybe some of the arguments that Africans can help make in
this case.
This is why it's a real pleasure to have someone like Andy Young, who is
very well respected throughout the US community that cares about Africa, who
can also bring back the message. Part of what we are doing with all this is
building political support at home for Africa getting the facts and
arguments straight.
Adam Roberts (The Economist): Where is the agricultural side of AGOA
headed in terms of subsidies and sanitary and phytosanitary regulations?
What is the next step?
You talk about two things. For a start, you raise a very important
general point that I've tried to stress, which is when everyone always talks
about AGOA in terms of apparel there are 6500 hundred tariff laws and a lot
of them don't have anything to do with apparel. It's natural that people
focus on apparel because that's often a first level manufacturing
industries. There's another reason: the history of the apparel industry is
one where the distribution side works back from the retail end to the
producer, in other words you've got retailers here finding the production
sources. That doesn't happen in many other industries. Although as I noted
in the larger forum, it is starting to happen in some areas where you see
global sources of information technology. So part of our interest in this
dialogue, obviously in this ongoing forum would be to get people to try to
move towards diversification.
Within that context, and let me give a specific example that I raised in
one of the sessions -- I don't know whether it was public -- that when you
have a 16-fold increase in South African auto exports, and that's BMW - a
quality product. BMWs are being made in Germany, in the United States, in
South Africa, and there's a potential for automobile component businesses
throughout SACU and elsewhere. We need to expand more along these
lines.
Now in agriculture: Our market under AGOA is very open under
agriculture. So the question is what is called sanitary and phytosanitary
standards. We, as a country, are quite vigorous in trying to explain, around
the world, that SPS standards have a place, which should not be used as a
protectionist measure. Our Department of Agriculture conducts for many of
the products what are called pest risks assessments. This where I think the
whole process is useful. One of the things we've been identifying is that
this is a kind of funnel in the process.
People want to export things but they couldn't get the pest risks
assessment. One of the things that I announced today was that three of the
regional hubs would have a specific person from this office (called APHIS)
for plant and health inspection service. They would be dedicated to this
function in working with the countries to get the product through the pest
risk assessment. We're also adding 20 people to the staff in Washington to
help deal with not just these issues but also other issues. Frankly, I'll
tell you why I also see another benefit in this for the United States. I run
into these problems all over the world where our products get blocked by
this, and if we can get more support from Africans to recognize that this
should not be a barrier and if we can help demonstrate our credibility by
solving some of their problems, that's a win-win situation.
You also mentioned subsidies. I'm really glad you've mentioned this,
particularly from The Economist, because in your most recent edition you
published a statement of defense of subsidies by the French Agriculture
Minister and I was shocked, I must say, shocked that there was barely a
comment on this even though if I had made such a statement about the United
States, you would have justifiably ripped me to shreds. What's going on
right now in the WTO is that our ability to move forward on agriculture,
services, manufacturing, the whole package, depends on Europe and Japan. Now
to be fair to Europe, Japan is just as bad and it won't move unless Europe
moves. Europe is at least willing, some Europeans, are willing to recognize
the stakes.
The members of your Commission came forward with a proposal that they
tried to work under the current CAP reform and they tried to say "Here is
where we can cut " but if you disaggregate the numbers you see a lot of
averages which is a sign you're not getting at very serious cuts. But
they're trying to go as far as they can with the current common agricultural
process. They're right now putting forward new plans for a common
agricultural policy. I have great respect for commissioners Lamy and
Fischler. They're trying to move this in the right direction but you've got
member states there that are going to basically block them.
Part of what we need to do is get a message from the developing world to
countries like France that say: ÂThe age of colonialism is over, thank
you. We would like to be able to be free to sell our products. The Americans
are willing to cut their subsidies; all they're asking is that the Europeans
cut their subsidies which are three to four times as high to get a little
closer to the Americans' subsidies.' So that's one of the other things we'd
like to push forward. And I hope the next time you tell your editors: "Let's
be a little fair."
Zeenat Hansrod (RFI): How do you reconcile AGOA trade advantages
and preferential treatment with the WTO? Is the US going to replace AGOA
with WTO?
The WTO system has provisions to offer preferences to developing
countries. It's a way, as we're seeing in AGOA, to help bring very poor
countries more effectively into the trading system. But, at the same time
AGOA was conceived with the notion that we would build on it towards a more
reciprocal and deeper based economic partnership, which we're starting to do
with our free trade agreements with the Southern African Customs Union. So
the two are loosely supportive; they give a start to less developed
countries, and here Europe and others have done things, every thing but arms
proposals, is step to try to do that. The reason why, in my view, is that
eventually moving to free trade agreements offers a greater stability. We
just talked about expiration dates as a loss, they can expire unlike
agreements that have a long term stability that are there unless countries
decide they want to walk away from their overall agreement. Therefore, it's
very important in terms of creating a deeper economic relationship.
Now your question about replacing, with all due respect: this is a
leftover European view from ages of colonialism mercantilism, and I'll be
very clear on this because I saw recently that the French Trade Minister
Loos made a comment about the US free trade agreement with Morocco, it
reminded me somewhat of 1905. The suggestion was that Morocco should not
have a free trade agreement with the United States but only with Europe. I
have never ever said anything to that degree when Europe goes to Latin
American or elsewhere. In fact, quite to the contrary, I say we need more
competition. If Europe does free trade agreements around the world, I
welcome it. If we're too slow, it's our problem not anybody else's. I really
believe this is not the view of the Commission. I don't think the Commission
is talking about free trade agreements in this way. I hope the Commission
steps up to its responsibility and points out to Minister Loos that this is
an antiquated view and inappropriate in the trading system of today.
It's good for Africa to have trade relations with Europe, the United
States, India and China. We want to offer more opportunity here; not try to
constrict it. Let me give you an example. The Mauritian Trade Minister said
" We're opening US markets and we're getting non-Americans investors here."
And I said: "That's great! If you can draw investors from BMW to come to
South Africa, from Taiwan or other places and it creates jobs in Africa,
that's a plus." We don't live in the world that it seems the French Trade
Minister lives in. Those days are over. It's not colonialism, it's not
mercantilism, we're moving to an open trading system. I believe the
Commission shares our view but now and then some people in Europe seem to be
stuck two hundred years ago.
Jean-Marc PochÃ© (Le Mauricien): There is some concern in Mauritius
about free trade agreements. They may not be good for us. Your comment?
We do not try to push a country or region into a trade agreement. We
only pursue free trade agreements with those that are interested. As you
properly point out, about 92% of the trade from AGOA countries comes to the
United States duty free. Then you might ask: "Why are the SACU countries
interested in the free trade agreements?" There are a couple of
reasons. One, as I said, trade preferences can be changed. Secondly, trade
preferences; because there is an uncertainty, they are probably less useful
in a more stable investment climate.
One of the reasons why a number of countries are interested in having
free trade agreements with the United States is that we make them very
comprehensive. We cover services; we cover intellectual property; we cover a
series of issues that make it a fuller economic relationship. That helps
assure investors that this is a good place to put their money. Because of
that, certain countries also can see how they can fit this into their own
development strategies. In the case of the SACU countries, clearly a number
of them want to use a free trade negotiation with the United States to help
them support their own reform process, because these are not easy to
undertake.
In addition, these agreements promote regional integration within SACU
and, unlike the European Union, which just did its agreement with South
Africa, we from the start want to include all the countries. I just met with
the minister from Botswana, and I visited Botswana the last time I was in
Africa, because we see the SACU agreement as a help to an extremely well-run
economy like Botswana, but a small economy in terms of people, be linked to
a larger system.
To return to your key point, we're not pressing people to do free trade
agreements. They come to me. We use the preference agreements to basically
open the door and then for those who want to take the next step, we explain
to them when we do a free trade agreement it's a serious exercise. We're
dealing with everything from the digital economy to agriculture, so one
doesn't undertake these lightly.
Alistair Leithead (BBC): You recently described the EU ban on GMO as
"luddite". What will you take to negotiations with the EU on GMO?
It's my view that we've been patient as long as we can. This moratorium
has been in place for about four and a half years, I've been in office for
about two years, I've discussed with my colleagues in the Commission --
there are some four that have responsibility for this -- the fact that I
understand the political difficulty and that's one reason we've tried to
work to see whether the process would change. As you probably know, the
Commission is trying to change the process. The Member States have
resisted. I believe that we've waited long enough, that this is
anti-scientific, and that it is not only undermining the future of our
agricultural trade, but also Africa's. As I was coming here I was looking at
some of the research being done on sweet potato crop, in terms of dealing
with some of the pests that really reduce the production. It's true of
cassava, as well. Let me just point something back to the Europeans. I
noticed there was a report from the French Medical Association in December
that said that biotech crops were safe, they were useful, and that the
French Medical Association suggested that the ban should be lifted. I'm in
agreement with the French Medical Association and I hope that others in
Europe can join with us.
Matt Rosenberg (Associated Press): What about IPR issues. Driving
around Mauritius it seems there is a Ralph Lauren factory outlet on every
corner. Is the US concerned about counterfeit clothing coming from
Africa?
We work with the companies that try to deal with their trademarks and
other intellectual property rights areas. I haven't really heard that as a
major concern from these companies. The companies that I have talked to see
Africa as a very important production center. In fact a number of them have
come to me and said that after the quotas come off, China will obviously be
very competitive, that in terms of having diversity of sources, they would
like to do some private sector capacity building. The question is where will
it take place? We're doing a free trade agreement with Central
America. There's textile apparel production there. We hope there will also
be in Africa.
Stephane Saminaden (L'Express): What sort of delay might there be in
extending AGOA? When might we expect to know if it will be extended?
Well, that, ultimately, will be for the Congress to decide. I think from
the administration's point of view, we're very pleased with AGOA's initial
success. For two years we've made good progress. We know there's more work
to be done and we're sympathetic to the idea that this needs to be in place
for a longer period of time. Now how and when do you do the extensions, how
long do you do the extensions, those sets of issues are issues that we need
to talk with Africans about and the Executive Branch needs to talk with
Congress about. But let me say it this way: we've just got this off and
running, it's doing a good job, so for my view we need to push it and keep
it going.
David Mageria (Reuters): We have heard a lot about what Africa wants
from AGOA, but what does the US want from AGOA?
Well, as I said when I was in Pretoria, US exports to the region have
grown after AGOA. There was about a 21% increase of US exports. We can check
that back for you. I believe there is opportunity for growth here. I believe
as you see in Mauritius and as you see in South Africa and other economies,
that you develop economies that will buy more from the United States. As I
use some of the examples, these people earn more and they develop more
production, they buy more machinery, and that's the economic benefit to the
United States. But there is also a bigger interest. And that is: the world
economy will not be complete if there are 800 million people left out. And
there will be a lot of problems that flow from that: health problems,
potential famines, security problems. You need to have a strong economic
base for society to have effective political systems. You've seen a
tremendous change in Africa in the past decade in terms of the spread of
democracy, but obviously it's still very, very fragile, Economic strength
will also, we hope, will help support the political process, the process of
tolerance, as you've seen here in Mauritius, the process of multiparty
systems as we have here and you have in Botswana and elsewhere. While it may
seem like we're most interested in helping Africa's economy, you may ask
why? We feel that the world and we benefit if Africa partakes more of
economic growth and opportunity.
Here's a key point, maybe referencing back to my comments about
mercantilism and colonialism. We don't believe that trade and economic
growth are zero sum. I keep explaining this to colleagues. If people get
greater incomes they'll buy more and everybody can buy more. This notion
that if you import, "you lose," is an outdated notion. When the US we buys
more apparel from Africa and it's good quality at lower prices, who benefits
the most: low-income people in the United States because they must use a
bigger share of their family budget on clothing. One the points that I have
made in the African context about cutting tariffs is that about 70% of the
tariffs the developing countries pay on manufactured goods, that's
industrial and most consumer goods, they pay to other developing
countries.
Part of the challenge here, which we can do through AGOA, SACU, and
others, is breaking down barriers among developing countries. What would
that do? It would means that their people will have better quality; lower
priced goods and their producers will have lower priced inputs. It also
means that the economies will grow together. Sorry for the strength of
feeling on this. There's no reason why when one country grows, the other is
disadvantaged; it's quite to the opposite. I met with a minister, as I've
mentioned, from Botswana, I said: " How's your economy doing?" He was
telling me a little bit about it. He said one of the most important things
is to get the US economy growing more again. The US economy has been growing
more, Japan's been sunk, Europe is stumbling along, and so for a lot of
these countries, not only in Africa but in Latin America, or South East
Asia, it's very important that we all grow together.
Ms Zeenat Hansrod (RFI): How far does the US want to go in terms of
opening up the energy sector?
In our country, that depends on what the private sector firms do, I know
it's a little different in France. We believe there are energy resources
here. We hope one creates the right investment climate. We hope that US
firms, French firms, Russian firms and other firms take advantage of it
because we'll be better off.
[Crosstalk, unintelligible question]
You say "you" in a way that reflects, I haven't invested because I
can't, I'm a government official. We are trying to create an enabling
environment for private investors to put their money. You must have spent a
lot of time in France; it really works very differently for us. We don't
tell energy where to go. Energy companies will come to us and say: "If you
can help make sure that the tax law works, or that there is a fair the
production sharing arrangement, we would like to develop these resources."
But our system works a little differently than yours, I guess.
Anthony Moreland (AFP): An official at the African Development Bank
expressed anger about US agricultural subsidies, specifically about the US
Farm Bill. What is your response to African leaders who criticize US farm
subsidies?
It's a good point. I'm glad you raised it. I'm glad you mentioned the
African Development bank. In Latin American, when we do our trade agreement,
we're trying to work with multilateral development banks to help fit the
trade agreements into not only both trade capacity building but also long
term development. In this region, I hope we can do more of the African
Development bank as well as the World Bank on that it shows how we look at
trade.
There is a lot of disinformation about the Farm Bill. To start out, the
fact that our projections for the farm spending over the next two years are
about 20% less than the prior three years. You might ask how this
happened. What a lot of people overlooked was that when the prior Farm Bill,
which was called The Freedom of the Farm Act, passed in 1996, was
supplemented each year with annual supplements. That boosted the
spending. One of things that we and the Congress were trying to do in this
Farm Bill was do it once, without annual supplements . You might justifiably
ask, "Will you keep your word on this?" There was a rather interesting test
last year because there was a drought in the upper northwest states and in
the midst of an election year, somebody pushed for an extra five billion
dollars and the President didn't go along with it.
Secondly, if you look at the sums on the Farm Bill, about 75% to 80% of
that money is spent for food aid and conservation programs. And thirdly,
there's been a lot of disinformation about this too, it didn't affect our
tariffs or anything like that in terms of closing the market.
Now, the second part of this, and this is what I'll freely acknowledge
and is why I was pushing on agriculture, is we're willing to cut those
subsidies, but we will not cut our subsidies unless the European Union and
Japan cut theirs. Let's take the key elements: Exports subsidies, which are
the most pernicious form because there you really paying people to buy your
goods. The European Union spends between two and five billion dollars a
year, that's "b" as in "boy. We spend about 15 million dollars, that's "m"
as in "man." We would be delighted to eliminate those right away. Our
proposal was to eliminate them over five years. Second, what is called
domestic support, a lot of people kind of mix these up, these are the
subsidies that you spend on your domestic programs that distort trade and
the WTO has rules about this. Our cap under the WTO rules is 19.1 billion
dollars. The European's cap, depending on the euro exchange rate, ranges
between about 62 and 69 billion dollars. Now that was a compromise the
Uruguay Round made to start to get those subsidies under control, but you
can see there's a rather huge asymmetry there.
The Japanese have about a 30 billion dollar cap. Our proposal was to put
a new limit of about 5% of overall production and in that case it would
still allow the Europeans more spending than we would have and bring them
down about 12 billion. And it cut ours about in half. Here is the other
interesting thing: this doesn't rule out subsidies that would go under the
so called green box, which would be subsidies that hats are dealing from
production, and if you look at the proposal the Commissioner Fischler had
last year, he would bring the European subsidies that are production
distorting down to about the level that we would make in our proposal. So
it's something that could be done if the Member States would get on
board. Third, you have tariffs. This is the one that's most challenging
because we proposed a rather bold cut using a formula that had been used
before in manufactured goods. It's called the Swiss 25 formula, it would cut
tariffs around the world by about 75% and it would mean that no agricultural
tariff would be higher than 25%.
Now, you look at a lot of the tariffs there in rice, 900%, 500%, and
300% and we have high tariffs too. But we can get our farm community to back
that if we get others to commit to these cuts. That's why my answer to
Africans is first, as I said, if you look at the facts you'll see that it
was not the nightmare that some of you thought. But we agree with your need
to cut subsidies and if you want to help us this administration is the one
developed country that is now saying we will cut subsidies if others cut
subsidies. So join with us because who knows how long that opportunity will
last, I hope it'll be through to a first and second term, but you never know
and we put a lot of political capital behind passing trade promotional
authority to regain the negotiating authority presidents have lost for years
for this exact reason. So the moment to act is now and one of the themes
that I'm trying to emphasize is the Doha negotiation in my view will stall
if we don't get significant progress on agriculture. That would be, in my
view, a tremendous missed opportunity in agriculture, as well as all the
rest of the negotiations. So we may be having another ministerial soon in
Japan. I think if Europe moves, Japan will follow.
(end transcript)

Original_Search_Title: Putting Farming in Evangelism Programmes
Headline: Zambia: Putting Farming in Evangelism Programmes
Body_Text:
Tracing The Seventh-Day Adventist Church's way...
On the shoulder of a stony hill behind Ndola's civic centre is a newly built concrete building whose foundation is set on hard rock. Every Saturday, from as early as 07.00hours Christian faithfuls congregate at this imposing but unplastered building to worship, and renew their fellowship with God.
Recently, on a bright Sunday morning, a sizeable group of Christians from various parts of Ndola met at the place. The only difference was that this time the men, women and a few youths - did not meet to worship as is usually the case on Saturdays.
In place of Bibles the expectant congregants were clutching exercise books, plain papers and anything capable of being written on in their hands and armpits.
The expression on their faces and the tone of their voices testified to the seriousness they attached to whatever occasion had brought them together.
They were participants to a one-day seminar on small-scale farming. The seminar, whose theme was "Farming as a Business" was organised by the Seventh-Day Adventist Church Men's Organisation (AMO), Ndola Central congregation.
Coordinator, Aswell Kazembe, said the church had realised that it was important for Christians to be actively involved in farming to help reduce poverty and hunger in their homes, communities and the nation as a whole.
Christians should lead the way because food security was one of the biblical principles of Christianity.
It was a requirement for all followers of Jesus Christ to get actively involved in income generating activities, especially farming, so that they could produce enough food and bless others.
The emphasis of the seminar, which was punctuated by Bible readings and prayers, was changing the church members' attitude towards farming and encourage them to treat the activity as a business rather than a mere way of life.
"We have realised that the current food crisis in the country is a challenge to us as Christians to lead the way by regarding farming as a serious economic activity.
"If all the Christians realise that God has placed an obligation on them to till the land and produce food for consumption and sale, there will be no hunger in Zambia," Mr Kazembe said.
The first phase of the seminar had been held earlier at Mukuba Hotel where participants were given basic lessons on farming and, the role it was supposed to play in the development of household and national economies.
In the second, more serious phase held at the church, the participants learnt modern farming practices, prominent among which was conservation farming, dubbed the "farmer's magic wand."
Participants were told where to seek extension services to ensure maximum yields.
It was also emphasised that a Christian's home was the last place to hear of hunger as God, through the Bible, has revealed the secret riches in the soil and how to tap them.
Scripture was quoted from Genesis and Deuteronomy to drive home this point.
The main resource person was Shadrack Mungalaba, an agronomist at the Copperbelt provincial headquarters of the Ministry of Agriculture and Co-operatives, with vast experience in crop production.
He was also the congregation's first church elder for 2002.
Some of the topics tackled were soil chemistry and physics, soil nutrition, crop behaviour and rainfall patterns and their relation to farming.
Others were the effects of traditional farming methods on crop yields, crop rotation and simple farm management.
Mungalaba appealed Christians to ensure that they also grew other crops like beans, sweet potatoes, groundnuts, cow peas and beans besides maize.
He said the more crops a farmer planted the more money he would earn from the harvest.
Crop diversity was the key to food security.
During the seminar church members learnt that poor crop yields were mainly caused by traditional farming methods which have been practised by small-scale farmers over a long time.
Almost all of these practices are destructive as they lead to destruction of the land's ecology.
Some of the identified bad traditional farming practices were burning of residue, over-tillage of the soil, wrong size of ridges, poor weed management, use of wrong seed varieties and poor spacing.
Mungalaba emphasised that no fire should ever be allowed on a piece of land used for farming because it destroyed the soil's nutrients, dismantled its natural texture and killed organisms which played an important role in the ecosystem.
"Residue is the life of any piece of land on which crops are expected to be planted. Dry maize stalks, maize jackets or covers, dry weeds and other plant life should be left on the farm to decay. They replace nutrients into the soil which were lost in various ways," Mungalaba said.
"There should never be a time when there is no activity on your farm. After harvest you are supposed to start preparing the land for the next farming season, and that should begin with spreading of residue across your farm," he said.
The earlier the land was prepared the more chances there would be for a good yield.
Many farmers had lost opportunities to record good harvests because they started preparing land in November when it was supposed to be a planting month. Land preparation should be done between July and October.
"If you want to have a good yield, you should start working on your land in July. If possible you should plant your maize or groundnuts in October or by the first week of November," Mungalaba told his attentive audience.
Agriculture and Co-operatives Minister Mundia Sikatana should have been there to witness the enthusiasm displayed by the church members in this new role they were adopting at this seminar.
Throughout the seminar, Mungalaba appealed to the farmers and those who were contemplating going into farming to seriously consider going into conservation farming.
"I can assure you that will never make a mistake if you practise conservation farming. It's simply the best method."
" The beauty of this kind of farming is that it gives you maximum yields on unbelievably small pieces of land while giving back to the soil the nutrients it lost to the crops and other things like erosion," he said.
The participants also learnt that tilling their land every season was a bad practice which led to poor yields as the soil's chemistry and physics were destabilised.
The best method was to use the same ridges for at least three farming seasons, then apply agricultural lime.
"The idea is that after harvesting you will not need to plough or hoe the land for the new planting season. You will just make holes on the same ridges and plant your seed.
"The plant will benefit from the abundant nutrients left over from the last season," he said.
Because of the informal method in which Mungalaba handled the topics, they generated a lot of interest from the participants who intermittently asked him several questions.
The participants were surprised when Mungalaba condemned the practice of breaking ridges from the last season in half to form new ones in the spaces that had been left between the old ridges.
"That's a very bad way of preparing land. I know that is what you are all used to and do every season. You see it almost everywhere. But you should stop today.
"Maize is a shallow-rooted crop which utilises the nutrients found in the top soil. When you bury the residue that had collected in the depression between the ridges to form a new ridge there the nutrients will be buried too deep, beyond the maize roots's reach," he said.
By the time the ridge reached a 'satisfactory' height most of the top soil which was rich in nutrients would have been buried and the seed would be planted in the red, poor sub soil.
At the end of the seminar there was a lot of excitement among the participant many of whom immediately resolved to become small-scale farmers this season, while those who were already farmers pledged to apply whatever they had learned.
"The church should organise more such seminars. I have learned so much today that I now feel motivated to take farming seriously, not only as a means of meeting my family's food requirements, but as a serious business enterprise," said Chishimba one of the participants.
Mungalaba, who thanked participants for their attentiveness, also appealed to them to include farming as a business in their evangelism programmes as individuals or groups.
"As you evangelise on the soon coming of Christ and the need for people to prepare their hearts through repentance, I want you to also tell other Zambians that there is a blessing in farming as it is a God given commission," he said.
It is encouraging that the church has joined President Mwanawasa's crusade to encourage Zambians to take farming seriously at small-scale level as the only hope for the country's stagnant copper-driven economy.
Ndola's SDA central congregation should be commended for the initiative.

Original_Search_Title: Looking Forward to Peace
Headline: Sudan: Looking Forward to Peace
Body_Text:
[This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations]
Just about everyone in Yei, a sprawling rural town in southwestern
Sudan, is talking about prospects for peace these days. You can scarcely
walk through the town's bombed-out streets without overhearing vendors, for
example, discussing their hopes for an agreement at ongoing peace talks in
Nairobi, Kenya, between representatives of Sudan's government and
rebels.
"We are really so happy about this peace deal," says Johnson Okut, who
sells grilled cassava at a roadside stall. "If there's a final agreement I
will be able to go back to my homeland in Bahr al Ghazal for the first time
in 20 years. I'm ready to go; I'm just waiting for them to finish this
thing."
If all goes to plan Okut and the more than 4.5 million other Sudanese
displaced by Africa's longest running civil war (including 570,000 refugees)
could be about to get the peace and stability which some of them have been
dreaming about for nearly 50 years. The war between Sudan's military regime
and the rebel Sudan People's Liberation Army (SPLA) has cost an estimated
two million lives and plunged the bulk of the country's 38 million
inhabitants below the poverty line.
If a hoped-for deal between president Umar al-Bashir and SPLA leader Dr
John Garang is concluded, Sudan stands its best chance of becoming a stable
nation since independence from Anglo-Egyptian rule in 1956. The picture is
fragile - not least because of insecurity in western Sudan's Darfur
province, currently the biggest threat to the vision of a peaceful
Sudan. Nevertheless, for the first time in 20 years, Sudanese are talking
about lasting peace as something possible.
TIMID HOPE
Despite the general optimism, the mood in south Sudan is not
euphoric. It is much more sober - the mood of a people who are tired of war
yet uncertain about peace and, even if they get that, faced with the tall
order of rebuilding a devastated country.
Natalino Losuba Mana, a southerner who runs the Norwegian People's Aid
(NPA) operation in Yei county, says the Sudanese will be waiting to see
concrete results from the peace deal. "No one is celebrating yet," he
says. "We'll wait to see these promises of peace fulfilled rather than
living on hope. We still fear they will make a very good agreement that will
never get beyond the paper."
If the deal does make it beyond the paper, it will in principle release
some US$200 million from the United States for reconstruction. Other donors
are also likely to contribute. But it is not yet clear how the money is to
be spent, nor is it clear how much it will cost to rehabilitate the south -
which, neglected under colonial rule and at war for most of the time since,
has suffered underdevelopment for over 100 years.
According to Dr George Leju Lugor, senior development officer for the
non-governmental Catholic Relief Services (CRS) in the south, "even in the
event of peace, the people here are visibly traumatised by the war. And
after all the signing and celebrating the new deal, now for the hard part:
the challenge of starting development. We have somehow to rebuild ordinary
life".
Perhaps the most daunting aspect of that challenge is that few people
have an ordinary life left to go back to.
"I fear our next war will be against redundancy," says Lugor, "the hard
part is finding things that can give the southern Sudanese a living. We are
talking about hundreds or thousands of us who joined the struggle and have
only known war. They don't know what else to do."
In Yei, a town with a population swollen by the displaced to around
70,000 people, that redundancy is visible. Hundreds of bored-looking youths
take shade under the rows of mango and palm nut trees. What makes it all the
more frustrating, say stakeholders, is that the area has such
potential. Lush and well watered, Yei's fertile land could become the bread
basket of Sudan if it is developed, some people say.
But reaching that stage means reversing the near total devastation of
the south's economy. "People in these areas have got used to living on their
basic coping mechanisms, doing the bare minimum to survive," says Mac Maika,
NPA coordinator for Yei and Juba counties. "That's the trauma of war: there
is no point in producing an excess for trade because tomorrow you might be
fleeing again".
DEVELOPING THE LAND
The NPA - ever keen to distance itself from what World Food Programme
(WPF) boss James Morris calls the "quick fix, band aid mentality" - has
already invested a lot in improving food production skills with long-term
agricultural training programmes. Ladis-Laus Ongaro, an agriculturalist
working on a training project with NPA for farmers in Yei, says the biggest
challenge is finding a market for farmers' goods.
"There are a number of farmers producing a surplus in this part of the
south. They are looking for a market but can't find one because of all the
insecurity and poor infrastructure", he says, "they urgently need a reliable
trading structure - obviously they will only be persuaded to grow an excess
if it fetches money".
Ultimately, development workers say, southern Sudan will only become
self-sufficient in food production and distribution if key features of its
infrastructure are repaired. Top of the list are the country's transport
links.
ROAD BLOCKS TO GROWTH
A frequently made complaint of the southern Sudanese about the neglect
of their region is the state of the roads. Poorly built and un-maintained,
they are regarded as one of the biggest impediments to reviving the region's
economy. The road from the Ugandan border to Yei, for example, covers a
stretch of barely 80 km, yet the journey takes over three hours in a sturdy
Toyota land cruiser.
"This stretch of the road we call the 'disco sections' ", quips an NPA
driver, as his vehicle judders along the appalling dirt track linking Yei
with Uganda's West Nile district. "We call them that because they force
everyone in the car to dance along with the bumps".
Yet the impact of southern Sudan's bumpy roads on its struggling economy
is no joke. It cuts the area off from valuable trade with other regions and
countries, as would-be freighters focus on easier, more profitable
routes. This renders the few goods that do pass into south Sudan from
Uganda, Kenya, Ethiopia or the Democratic Republic of the Congo extremely
expensive. "Any truck drivers or cross-border traders are going to be asking
themselves: Do I bother driving goods into Sudan, where I waste so much time
and my vehicle gets damaged?" Maika says.
Projects for grading roads are underway, however, and international
organisations operating in the county say the time it takes to travel from
the Ugandan border to Yei town, for instance, has already been cut by almost
half (it apparently used to take five hours) because a section of the route
was recently overhauled.
EDUCATION, PLEASE
But more than the roads, the south Sudanese complain about
education. They often talk of having been deprived of the education which,
they say, people in other African nations have enjoyed. They also describe a
feeling of being disconnected from the wider world, partly because of the
lack of education but also because of an absence of information media
tailored to southern Sudanese.
Many southern Sudanese, of all ages, are keen to return to studies as
soon as things settle down. Ely Dada, a part-time casual security guard for
senior SPLM figures, says he urgently wants to go back to school. "We have
lost a lot in terms of education from this war. Schools closed and some of
us dropped out to join the struggle - I dropped out of senior 1 in 1982. For
me that is the most significant thing about this peace deal - it means that
we can now start our education all over again", he says.
But Paul Mac, the SPLA commander who controls Yei and Juba counties in
the extreme south of Sudan, claims that the SPLM are addressing the urgent
need for more schools in southern Sudan. "In Yei county alone we now have
five primary and two secondary schools governed by SPLM using the Ugandan
English-speaking curriculum. Before the SPLA took the town in 1997, there
were no schools - so you can see the progress slowly being made. We are just
awaiting funding to build more when the deal is signed".
Much will depend on just how generous that funding turns out be. If
substantial development money is released on the back of the peace deal,
schools could for the first time be part of ordinary life in south
Sudan.
SELF-DETERMINATION
For many of the south Sudanese, the solution to decades of neglect is
clear: they want the right to determine how their area is to be built and
run, something to which the Khartoum government has agreed in principle at
the peace talks.
Silvanos Yokosu, journalist for the Sudan Mirror, a newspaper in
southern Sudan, feels many southerners are likely to want a separate
state.
However, none of the people IRIN interviewed in Yei dismissed the idea
of a united Sudan after a six-year interim period after which, under the
terms of the draft peace accord being hammered out in Nairobi, the
relationship between the south and the north is to be decided by
referendum.
What does the future hold? No one knows but many are guardedly
hopeful. "It is always possible this war could end in a happy marriage,"
says Yokosu, "but that will require a big change in attitude - on both
sides".

Original_Search_Title: Nampula Investors Released
Headline: Mozambique: Nampula Investors Released
Body_Text:
Maputo — Two foreign investors detained over the weekend in the northern
Mozambican city of Nampula were conditionally released on Monday
afternoon.
The two are South African citizen Gary O'Connor and his wife Tania
Skytte, who is a Danish national. They had been jailed on trumped up charges
of abducting children, brought against them by local prosecuting attorney
Francisco Cuamba.
But the real reason for the harassment of the couple seems to be a
dispute over land. The Nampula provincial government allocated abandoned
land to them for a project to construct a poultry farm.
This clashed with the interests of a neighbouring catholic convent which
had also applied for the land, but which saw its application turned down. A
Brazilian nun has been leading a campaign against O'Connor and Skytte which
included the allegation of kidnapping children, for which there is not the
slightest evidence.
The Nampula court on Monday decided to release the couple against their
identity and address - an implicit recognition that they are unlikely to
attempt to flee the country. No bail was required, but the case was not
simply thrown out, as the couple had hoped.
Tania Skytte's brother, Tobias, who is the third partner in the poultry
project, told AIM on Tuesday that they are still being harassed by the
Brazilian and her friends. "Last night we found them snooping round our
property", he said.
O'Connor told AIM "They've been looking for evidence against us, but
they can't find anything".
He noted that recently some of the nuns had spotted one of the project's
workers digging a hole. They had rushed over, demanding to know what he was
doing. "They hoped to find some bodies or something", said O'Connor, "but
the worker was just digging up cassava".
As for his days in the Nampula jail, O'Connor said that he and his wife
had not been mistreated. "Conditions are not good in the jail - it's very
overcrowded", he said. "They treated us quite well, but it wasn't a pleasant
experience".
"The case is still going on, and we'll wait to see what the authorities
are going to do about it", O'Connor added.
The Danish ambassador, Mats Sandal Jensen, accompanied the proceedings
on Monday. He had spent the whole weekend in Nampula, and only returned to
Maputo on Tuesday morning.

Original_Search_Title: Large Scale Farming Will Address Agricultural Problems - Governor Saraki
Headline: Nigeria: Large Scale Farming Will Address Agricultural Problems - Governor Saraki
Body_Text:
Governor Bukola Saraki is into serious negotiations with South
African experts for the transformation of the agricultural sector in Kwara
State. He has also, just concluded an education summit in the state and has
listed some on-going efforts to extend the positive impact of his
administration to the rural sector. In this interview with our editorial
team, of Is'haq Modibbo Kawu (Editor, Daily Trust), Suleiman Mohammed and
Henry Omunu. Governor Bukola also bares his mind on the lingering political
crises in Kwara State.
In which ways does the background of acrimony that was there when
you came on board impact on actions being taken by your government?
It impacted so much. It was between the period of the elections and
swearing-in when I had the opportunity to kick start what we wanted to do in
the best ways to do them. We devised policies against abuses in Kwara State
and that's where the start was. We have been dealing with the agitations for
capital, the environment, and providing jobs. So far we have provided over
4,000 jobs. Even the water problem. To show that this government is
committed, we did not start to build new boreholes. we started by reviving
what was put on ground by previous administrations. So it was not the case
of abandoning what we inherited and start building our own. We repaired
about 200 of the boreholes that were already in existence. This is a
fact.
And one of our policies is that we want to see some of the previous
projects to completion. For example we must provide water. So whether it was
the government of Muhammed Lawal that started it, if it is going to provide
benefits to the people of Kwara State, fine. There are projects we believe
are not in the interest of the people of Kwara State. For example the 330
ward clinics. we are not going to finish it, because we don't have the money
to maintain the infrastructures neither do we have the money to complete
them. Even after completing them, to provide drugs will be another
problem. So we attend to each of the projects purely on the values they have
to the people.
Your Excellency, some people feel that there must be a revenge
content in your agenda by coming to power. How would you react to this?
It is possible for people to look at it like that. It is possible for
some people even within your community. That is the way the world goes. But,
easily, I believe that it should go along with decency, because you make
yourself vulnerable to issues that are not supposed to be driven at. And
that is a very very bad one, because you can be distracted. You see, these
things are wrong. For example the issue of water contract that was awarded
at that time for over 1.4 billion naira to improve the capacity from 4
million gallons to about 12m gallons per day. The previous government spent
the whole 1.4 billion naira and the result was still 4 million gallons per
day. The ordinary people, including students of the University of Ilorin,
protested that they don't have water, and really, they don't have. Now these
are the kind of issues that we are addressing and many more. And if anybody
sees that as a revenge mission, well, posterity will judge. Those are the
kind of issues that impact on the people, and for those kinds of issues, we
are going to recover everything given to contractors and ask them to go back
to site and deliver water. But on the issues that have no impact on the
people, I don't think we can just get up and begin to look at them, we were
not voted in because of revenge.
The electorates are the ones to decide how the government is going to
perform and what would happen if they don't perform. So, I don't think we
have been voted to carry out any revenge.
In essence are you now probing the water project contract?
The point I am saying is that such are issues we have to look into. Now
the federal government is warming up to take over the second phase for
us. In Kwara, we need a production of about 30 million gallons of water per
day.
According to WHO if your population is about 2.2m, you need about 30
million gallons of water per day. By the time we do phase one and two, the
one the federal government is doing is 12 million gallons. Together, these
would be 24 million gallons. But now, we are still at 4 million. So, by the
time people ask what have we achieved, what do we say? One of the issues in
Kwara State, which everyone would easily tell you is the issue of water. For
example we have to think of how we are going to look for another N1.2bn,
especially having known that this money was taken and these things were not
done. So if the contractors can come and step the capacity up to 12 million
gallons per day, that is what we want. So, probing is only the means, if the
contractor cannot come to site to provide us the 12 million gallons. And
what I am interested in is that we have water production of 12 million
gallons per day.
Your administration has uncovered monies that was alleged to have
been misappropriated by the last government in the state. So how much was
this amount, and how much have you been able to recover?
We don't really have figures. There are too many issues we have to talk
about. We talk about SPEB i.e. State Primary Education Board etc. These are
key areas. And if issues come up as we move along, fine. SPEB, in the sense
that we set up panel at the early stage to look into what was happening. The
executive secretary during interrogations told us that over the period,
about 534 million was delivered to the governor in cash. We are looking at
the white paper and we will take decision on that.
And on the issue of the local governments. Local government came about
because when we came in June, we met N240 million every month as salaries of
local government staff. We did some pay parade, we suspended some DPS,
treasurers. We did another pay parade, suspended some others, and today from
210 million every month, we only pay 140 million. There is another
self-confession that 70 million which they used to pay as salary, there was
nobody they were paying the salary to. So, those are issues that need to be
accounted for. And those are the specific areas. Unfortunately in the area
of the local government, we have set up auditing firms to look at local
government account and to be able to tell those that need to pay money back
to the local governments. I think sometimes the local governments were
allowed to inflate recurrent expenditure at the expense of capital
expenditure. At the end of the day, there was no development. And we are
hoping now that at the end of the day we will be able to save Kwara State of
about N2.8 billion in these four years.
You seem very committed to agriculture. I was there in July when you
launched your programme. I also know that you have been having relationship
with the white farmers of South Africa and Zimbabwe. What stage have you
reached now, and then, have you factored into this relationship the
historical problem that the white farmers have had with Africans in these
countries where they live?
First of all, yes, agriculture is our number one priority, for major
reasons. We believe through agriculture, we can increase level of employment
and enhance job security. And in doing that, we can employ the best
strategies. As you know majority of farmers in Nigeria today are peasant
farmers that need capital or access to information technology or even best
practices than the older generations. So if you look at these factors, they
do not encourage large scale farming, and as such the best way is for us to
look round and see where best can we get such incentives.
And, the areas of poultry farming and cattle rearing and diary
products. These are three areas that we believe people in the Southern part
of Africa have quite an expertise and have knowledge. We look at other areas
too, like vegetable etc, having comparative advantage. The market is more
favourable for export. We are working with South Africa especially in
poultry farming because they have poultry union, they are making good
turn-over and making good gains as they sell frozen chicken at about $1 per
kilo, while here in Nigeria we are selling at $3, to $4 i.e. N400 to
N500. So there are lots of opportunities and we are hoping to exploit
them. That brings us to the contentious issues. Some of the issues people
raised on Zimbabwe. You see we will learn from them of course. When we go
into detail of the history of some of the past. I honestly believe that we
have to be very sincere with our selves today in Nigeria especially in Kwara
State, that unless we take to large scale farming, we will not be able to
raise our GDP above the 5 to 7 per cent we talked about. We will not be able
to utilise the opportunity we have in agriculture as the largest employer of
labour. Now these chaps from South Africa when they came to Nigeria what did
they found out? They found out that every hectare of land in Kwara here was
only yielding 1.5 tones of cassava. In South Africa, you get 79 tones. Why?
We have better variety of seedlings, we have 1600mm of rain, they have only
600 mm. They use fertiliser. But they found that our land here does not
require much of that.
So there is the terrain here, mind you they have the expertise, we have
the terrain, so lets work together. So I use this as a comparism, when we
discovered oil in Nigeria, I see that as an asset; an asset that has been
there for Nigerians. So we did not wait for those in the Niger Delta area to
be drilling, we opened it up for the white oil employers to come, but we
have been able to work out a relationship that ensures our control and
ownership of the assets. But in processes of doing it, we have been able to
develop that sector and that is what I believe we need in agriculture. for
as along as we depend on just small-scale farming, we will not be able to
develop agriculture. So my views are that we need those that will be coming
and do large-scale farming and of course you know conditions of this kind of
problems and issues. I believe that it can work.
What is the thrust of your administration in the area of rural
transformation, i.e rural roads, electrification etc?
First of all, we talk about poverty reduction. When we talk about
-poverty reduction in an environment where you don't have enough resources,
the focus is then to empower people so that they will be able to have their
own resources. Now what we are doing on rural roads for example, is to
identify roads where we would assist in reducing the cost of bringing
produce to the town. Even the issue of water which I have talked about,
within the next two or four years we will be able to address and improve
water supply in the rural areas.
We noted that Kwara State is not integrated economically. For
instance, if you live in Kiama, you have to go through Oyo or Niger states
to come to Ilorin, the state capital. It seem the Kiama road, is
abandoned. Is there any effort to open the road given the agricultural
potentials of the area?
That is what we are dealing with. What we are hoping we can do is the
grading of the roads leading to Borgu that has border with the Republic of
Benin. And again, that also have it's economic value because the farmers are
there. The House of Representatives sub-Committee came over, we spoke with
them as well, we are looking at them to assist us. Also talking about road
network, of course there is the Ibadan-Ilorin road. Unfortunately I don't
know why the federal government has not adequately funded this road. It is
very unfortunate. That road too is economically positioned with great value
to receive this kind of treatment. And now the contractor keep on
complaining that the level of funding the government is giving them cannot
complete the construction of the road at all. We are going to make sure that
in 2004 money is provided for the completion of work, because people are on
the road on daily basis. It is even being taken over by trailers, thus
putting commercial activities at great risk. I think the road needs to be
completed as soon as possible.
You just concluded Kwara State education summit. Why did you do it?
There are certain things that became glaring as we continue to do our
own review of education. I was sitting with the registrar of University of
Ilorin then I asked what percentage of students, students from secondary
from Kwara State get into the university, then he said only 10
percent. Since the university of Ilorin is situated in Ilorin we are
supposed to get higher than that, and majority of our youths do not meet the
criteria, and then also in our primary school level there is over crowdness,
no materials. So we said let us begin to see what it takes for us to improve
the quality of our education. And there are also some reforms at the primary
school level, we are also looking at the issue of introducing what I can
call Western education at a level together with Qur'anic education because I
believe we also need to prepare the children for the outside world to see
that they can combine all. Even in Pakistan or Egypt, Western knowledge does
not stop Qur'anic education, it further strengthens and complement it.
Your visitation panel to the Kwara State Polytechnic just submitted
its report to you. I know that the polytechnic had serious problems of
cultism in the recent years. In the last regime the students were used as
thugs. What are the issues that have been discovered and what are you going
to do to reposition the school?
Many issues have been discovered. One, we found out that we need
policies behind science and technology studies. The admissions did not take
cognisance of that and what they do now is that they have been admitting
those in the arts department. We found out that 90 percent of those trouble
makers are in the Arts. So we want to go back, there is a bill before the
state Assembly where we will redistribute the ratio between students from
the arts and science.
One important thing now is that the students have now realised that they
have a government that does not have sacred cows. Anybody, parent or child
involved, or lecturer would be dealt with. We are passing a new bill to the
House of Assembly on the process and condition of guarantors and we are now
looking at the overhead to be able to reduce it. There is a recommendation
of about 9 million a month, which will help.
More importantly now, is that we have invited the private sector to look
at the accommodation on the campus. the fact that the majority of the
student live off campus is the problem. if we can get more students to live
on campus, it will further reduce the problem.
Talking now about Northern institutions, the Bank of the North is
supposed to be re-capitalised, how far have you gone?
I think that process has started. I am sure by middle of next quarter
majority of the states would have paid their own parts.
Other Northern institutions are not functioning properly. The
Northern Nigerian Investment Company for example. What exactly are you
thinking, about re-invigorating these institutions?
My view is that the situation of these institutions are a reflection of
the economy at large. they are not isolated. There are some factual issues
that resulted to that. So what is most important now is that we must try,
and find an enabling environment in the private sector for those Northern
individuals that have the resources to invest. There are opportunities in
Nigeria today, there some policies the government can put in place to
protect the Nigerian business sector. There is nothing more important than
national interest. Look at today, we have seen how USA backed down on the
issue of tariff despite the fact that it is a country with strong economy,
but even now it is still bringing in policies to protect its institutions,
and that is a developed country that has the resources. So, the developing
countries, have to look at how they will protect their resources. Like in
India, that was what they did and after they stabilised. They said okay, we
have opened up the economy whoever want to come and invest should come. That
is what needs to be done particularly those who have the resources.
You have something you always have to contend with for the good or for
bad. How much influence, how much does your father lean on you in the
administration of Kwara State?
What is his interest? He is a man who has been in politics for about 40
years now. He has made his own efforts to make life better for the people of
Kwara and Nigeria at large. So the interest of empowerment and providing a
better life is the interest of our administration. The goal is to provide
better life for the people so there is a common goal in that area, and as
such, as far as our own policies are concerned, I don't believe that there
is a need for a lean. Whether you stay in the country or outside you still
provide jobs for 4,000 people and I am sure they must engage, everybody
else. We are interested in the betterment of the life of the common
man.
So don't you have to carry him along to deliver the system?
For the purpose of what? You see you have to analyse your objectives, if
it will improve the lives of the people, yes you do have to. But you see
these are kinds of perception, images that people create that of things that
needs to be done.
Finally you are coming from the private sector. How has that
experience politically conditioned your work in Kwara State?
I think it has. I think it's my private sector background who have kept
me focused. It is not by chance, I have a road map and I am following it. I
think that is the kind of thing you have in the private sector. What are
your targets? These are the kind of things that will make it possible for a
very realistic approach to what we do. I suppose we don't wake up to find
out that we are creating overhead tanks then the following day we are
creating this and that, we don't do that kind of thing. We sit down, we take
every issue in place, what is the first line of approach and I think it is
this private sector backing that enables one to succeed.

Original_Search_Title: Kapeka Farmers Reap Big From Salim Saleh's Land
Headline: Uganda: Kapeka Farmers Reap Big From Salim Saleh's Land
Body_Text:
Kampala — Farmers in Kapeeka subcounty in Nakaseke, Luweero, have made a fortune following a series of bumper harvests on free land which Lt Gen Salim Saleh lent them to do farming, two years ago.
Saleh lent out 150 acres of tilled farm land to UPDF veterans, LCs and youths. Each of the beneficiaries was allocated two acres near the UPDF heros' cemetery, near Kapeeka town.
The New Vision learnt recently that several of the beneficiaries have constructed houses, bought cars or started businesses using the money they got from crops harvested from Saleh's land.
The beneficiaries planted mostly cassava, maize and rice which did well on the newly opened fertile land. The LC3 chairman, Christopher Kiwanuka bought a car from the sale of his cassava which he planted in 2001. Other farmers have built new houses.

Original_Search_Title: A Royal Father's Development Drive for His Kingdom
Headline: Nigeria: A Royal Father's Development Drive for His Kingdom
Body_Text:
Lagos — For His Royal Majesty, the Dein of Agbor Kingdom, Delta State,
Kiagborekuzi 1, everything must be done to ensure the development of every
facet of his kingdom. The task is enormous, but it must be done, whatever it
would take to do just that.
The 26-year-old royal father, who was the star guest at the opening of
the Belgian Week, at Eko Le Meridien Hotel, last week, had expressed concern
at the neglect his people had suffered over the years and the need to
reverse the trend.
The traditional ruler, who was resplendent and stole attention at the
occasion, also bore his mind on the problem of youths' unrest, saying that
it was a fallout of the neglect being suffered by the people.
Agbor, his main domain, he said, deserves to have good network of roads,
and otherl infrastructures, among others.
"It is rather unfortunate that Delta State has been in the news for the
wrong reasons due to the restiveness in the Warri axis of the oil rich
state. The state, however, is only 12 years old, so, we ask for patience and
understanding on the part of the public as the state goes through what might
be referred to as teething problems", he said, pointing out that inspite of
the restiveness, Delta State remains one of the best states in the country
for investment, naming the limitless areas, yearning for investment in the
state to include the agro and agro-allied sectors, marine, non-metallic
minerals, polymer, mining and mineral processing, wood, craft, packaging,
and textiles.
"The state, in addition to being the largest source of oil and gas in
the country, is blessed with many more natural resources and raw materials
that can make it a haven for investors. We have oil palm, kaolin, lignite,
silica, clay, cassava, ethanol, spices and tomato, the government and people
of Delta State would willingly provide land, necessary infrastructure and
conducive atmosphere for prospective investors. So, we invite them to come
and take advantage of these resources and hospitality of Delta State to
invest there", he said.
With the Belgium community in Nigeria and guests present now all ears,
listening to him, Kiagborekuzi launched into a brief history of his Kingdom,
now known as Agbor:
"Agbor Kingdom is one of the oldest Kingdoms in Nigeria, having had her
first King, Ogele, in 72BC. We presently sit on the throne of our third
dynasty, the Dein Dynasty, which started in 1270 AD as a hereditary
rulership. That is why it was possible for us to be crowned in 1979, when we
were barely two and half years old. This earned us a place in the Guinness
Book of Records as the youngest crowned monarch in the world at that time",
he said.
The second dynasty in Agbor, he said, was between 600-9000 AD, a period
in which the palace of the first dynasty, the Ogele dynasty, was moved.
He explained that Agbor was related by blood and marriage to Ile-Ife,
stating that history had it that it was in Oduduwa land that Agbor's first
King found himself on entering the present day Nigeria and indeed was given
his first wife from there to make the Queen of Agbor then a Yoruba
woman.
Agbor, he said, was also blood related to the Binis, who are
incidentally related to Oduduwa. He said the present dynasty and Kings of
Benin and Agbor were blood brothers, explaining that Agbor moved from the
present day Benin to the Niger River basin.
"This can clearly be seen on examination of the present rulers in the
geographical area, who got their independence from Europeans. The royal
families of these rulers are commonly referred to as Nmu-Dein, which means
children of Dein", he said, pointing out that in the earliest times, the Oba
of Benin and the Dein of Agbor (Obi of Agbor then), were the recognised and
most powerful kings in that axis of the country.
"Agbor has always been involved in the government of this country. At a
point, our grandfather, His Majesty, Obi Obike A. Gbenoba, while on the
throne, served as Minister along with the Ooni of Ife and Oba of Benin", he
further explained, saying that Agbor has two indigenous languages, the Oza
and Agbor, to make for similarities between the two Kingdoms.
Closely related to that, he said, is the entire Delta State, which he
described as the melting pot of ethnic and cultural diversity, making for a
very rich and exciting young state, noting that there were many similarities
between Nigeria and Belgium.
He said in Europe, Belgium was at the heart of activities as Nigeria was
to Africa.
He, however, said that Nigeria has a lot to learn from the Belgian
experience such as clear constitutional policies and the acceptance and
involvement of the monarchy in governance.
He said he was committeed to fostering better relations between Nigeria
and the outside world, and increased international trade to enhance
Nigeria's fortune.
He consequently called on President Olusegun Obasanjo to continue his
fight against corruption and entrench transparency in governance.
He called for urgent attention to be given to the plight of the youths
in the Niger-Delta, especially as it concerns unemployment, which he blamed
for idleness and the often recourse to militancy and attendant civil unrest
in the area. He also called for infrastructural development in the area of
road rehabilitation, provision of pipe borne water, and rural
electrification, among others to make life easier for all while advising the
youths to shun vices, and educate themselves, show respect to their parents,
and put their lives in God's hand.

Original_Search_Title: Marauding Elephants Invade Kilifi
Headline: Kenya: Marauding Elephants Invade Kilifi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Twenty marauding elephants on Friday night invaded farms in Kilifi
District.
The elephants destroyed crops worth thousands of shillings, at Kaembeni
and Kahingoni villages in Vitengeni division.
Speaking to the Press yesterday, the residents called on the Kenya
Wildlife Service to address the issue.
The residents said the elephants have destroyed crops and threatened
their lives.
Speaking at Matano Manne trading centre, they said the elephants had
destroyed maize, cassava, coconut trees, bananas and paw paws among
crops.
The residents further said that they carry out their activities amidst
fear of being attacked.
Led by Mwahera ward councillor Byron Changawa Charo the residents said
the jumbos have terrorised them for the last two months.
They said they would kill the elephants if a solution is not found
soon.
A farmer Baray Baya said the elephants invaded his farm last Friday and
destroyed cassavas.
The elephants are believed to have strayed from the Arabuko Sokoke
forest reserve. Vitengeni District Officer Wilson Kimaiyo confirmed the
incidents. He said the Provincial Administration and KWS would take drastic
measures to relocate the elephants.

Original_Search_Title: Road Safety Wagons Go West
Headline: Cameroon: Road Safety Wagons Go West
Body_Text:
After Yaounde-Douala, Transport Minister targets the Bafoussam-Bamenda Axis.
Statistics from the Ministry of Transport show that human errors are responsible for 70 percent of road accidents on major inter-urban highways in the country. These road mishaps which characterise the Christmas and New Year season have largely been attributed to reckless driving caused by alcoholism, excess speed, fatique and road worthlessness of vehicles. Road users' attitudes on the highways was also identified as a major contributor. It is against this background that the Ministry of Transport launched a four week sensitisation campaigns to ensure an accident-free festive period in the country. The crusade which began on the Yaounde-Douala axis, two weeks ago, moved to the Yaounde-Bafoussam-Bamenda highways, last Tuesday. The call remained unique: "the protection of lives on our highways is everyman's duty, not only the government, the Transport Ministry or drivers," the Minister of Transport, John B Ndeh, preached from Ebebda through Banakane, Bafossam to Bamenda. At each stop, he handed over the campaign trumpet to his administrative and judicial collaborators.
Traffic policemen on the Yaounde-Bafoussam axis say the sensitisation campaigns have made an impact within that zone. Police officials told journalists that there has been a marked improvement on road worthiness of most vehicles, the rate of overloading and less incidence of violation of road safety controls. The situation on Bafoussam-Bamenda lane was, however, not the same. Many a vehicle rattled and swallowed up the road with smoke, while others were grossly over loaded. A Toyota Hilux transporting bunches of plantains, coco-yams, firewood and over 25 passengers in the same cabin from Bouda to Bamenda su mulsulted, a few hundred metres, ahead of the minister's road safety convoy. The car was derailed by its weight on a sharp bend. The driver successfully dashed into the bush, leaving many wounded and no document to identify him.
The road safety campaign field report show that, apart from recklessness, many other factors contribute to the rampant road accidents in the country. Driver complain of very low and irregular salaries, which induce them to overloading for extra allowances. They also said numerous "extortionist barriers" erected on the highways by the police, gendarmery and councils cause drivers to speed excessively in order to recover the sum lost, as bribe, to these road safety officials.
