Once the officials are satisfied with the booty, drivers can go on any speed. The loading condition and the road worthiness of the vehicles become a minor matter. A check on the activities of these officials may reduce the rate of these mishaps.
Drivers also hold that many of their colleagues operate with faked documents. They revealed that most driving licences are bought from senior authorities in the Ministry of Transport and ficticious driving institutions. Reacting this, the Director of Land Transport, Mr Josue Youmba said, the rule stipulates that a professional driver must pass through a driving institute and be awarded licence only by a jury which constitutes experts from several ministries and the civil society. He said the list of candidates selected for the driving test must be published one week before the jury meets. This is to check fraud. Qualified driving schools, Mr Youmba said, must be approved by the Transport Ministry after having shown proves of qualified trained staff, satisfactory infrastructure and equipment. He said to check corruption and maintain efficiency, routine controls are carried out in these institutions every year.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture, Alternative Revenue Earner in Ebonyi
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture, Alternative Revenue Earner in Ebonyi
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a State that lacks Federal Government presence in terms of
investment, Ebonyi State is poised to make agriculture its alternative
source of income. Before the creation of the state in 1996, it was a general
knowledge that it produced the 'Abakaliki Rice'.
Apart from rice production, the State, in the past was also known for
producing and exporting palm kernel, palm oil, yam, livestock and poultry
products.
Shortly on assumption of office, the Governor, Dr. Sam Egwu visited
Israel for the purpose of attracting technical assistance in agricultural
development and food production. That relationship led to the signing of
memorandum of understanding with the state government.
It is believed that the administration, focusing on agriculture is to
ensure that the state regains its status as the food basket of the
nation. This is being done through the rehabilitation of poultry production,
large-scale oil-palm plantation and rice production, as well as provision of
agricultural inputs for farmers to improve their individual capacities.
In a recent interviwed with THISDAY, the Commissioner for Information
and Youth Orientation in the State, Dr. John Otu explained that the governor
has slightly shifted focus from investing on education as was paramount
concern when he came to power in 1999, to the development of agriculture and
industrial base for the people.
He said that Egwu's greatest achievement in his first term as governor,
was the mental and psychological liberation he gave the people of Ebonyi. He
stressed that most of the hawkers that are found on the streets of Abuja and
Lagos are back home, maximising the free education policy of the
government.
The aggressive move embarked by the government into developing
industrial base for the state, Otu said, is to absorb the citizens on
graduation from various studies.
It is obvious that the use of fertilizer by farmers is relatively new in
Ebonyi State, therefore, he said there is the need to acquaint the people
with modern techniques of application. Infact, with the total completion of
the State Fertilizer Blending plant, which would be producing about 40 tones
per hour, farmers in the state and its environs would be buying it in large
quantities and at subsidised rate, since it is the only blending plant in
the whole of south-east. Apart from that, it is also expected to shore-up
the state's revenue base.
Interestingly, Otu revealed that government has set up an
inter-ministerial committee to see that the blending plant comes on stream
soon and subsequently plans for its privatisation. When operational, it is
also expected that it would employ about 2,000 workers.
The influx of people to Ebonyi State is also attracting more business
men, as a result, rice millers are under pressure of increasing production
to meet demand. It is in appreciation of this that the state government, the
Commissioner said, initiated various measures aimed at ensuring the
mechanisation and mass production of this staple food. Government, he said
is also revamping the rice mill in other to modernise it and make the
'Abakaliki Rice' a global brand.
Otu disclosed that Chinese investors have acquired 10,000 hectares of
land to complement the state's agrarian drive in three geographical zones of
the state for rice planting. "Because Ebonyi is noted for rice production,
we want to optimise our potentials in that regard. The state is going to be
pacesetter in that crop the coutry", the commissioner boasted.
It was not only in rice production that the government has thrown its
weight at. Its agricultural programme also embarked on the modernisation of
the Nkaliki poultry. The project is about the largest in West Africa with a
capacity to producing 16,800 day-old chicks and 8,000 broilers a week.
The poultry, contracted to Dizengof, an Israeli company, would not only
be a source of revenue and foreign exchange earning for the state, but also
a major avenue for the employment of requisite manpower and to meet the
challenges of protein production of the country.
Touching on almost everything Egwu's government has also embarked on,
what may turn out to be the biggest palm plantation in Nigeria. The
construction of a standard oil palm nursery has been completed. This also
has the capacity of producing two million palm seedlings per annum. The
plantation, located at the Vertinary Investigation Centre (VIC) Ezzamgbo, is
equipped with the modern irrigation facilities.
The nursery is the first phase of the administration's massive oil palm
production pragramme. This is in addition to the 4,500 improved oil palm
seedlings developed by Ebonyi State Agricultural Development Programme
(EBADEP). It is believed that the oil palms, which were developed from high
yielding oil palm species from the National Institute for Oil Palm
Researchers (NIFOR) Benin, are being sold to farmers and various
agricultural agencies. Plans are underway for the establishment of some tree
nursery such as plantain, Banana, Ireculia SPP (Ukwa), Irvengia SPP (Ogbono)
and several other plants.
To fully tap the huge resources in the state, the Otu explained that due
to the huge deposit of limestone in the state, government is also interested
in having its own cement factory, outside the Nkalagu factory jointly owned
by the five eastern states. As a member of the government entourage to
China, the commissioner said a Chinese company has indicated interest of
carrying out a geological survey at the central zone of the state where
there is large deposit of limestone.
The Wanshi Cement Company from China, he said would not only invest on
cement production, but would delve into provision of social amenities in the
state. Otu added that the state prepared to turn the natural blessings
endowed in the state to wealth for the overall interest of the people.

Original_Search_Title: Nutrition, Poverty And the Rights Issue
Headline: Nigeria: Nutrition, Poverty And the Rights Issue
Body_Text:
"HUNGER kills." This is the message a Lagos taxi driver conveys to the world through large, vivid inscription on a front panel of his rickety cab. As he meanders through the dusty streets of the city spreading his two-worded message, another set of inscriptions with the message that "Ignorance is death" is displayed. To the casual observer, these messages probably mean nothing more than the cab driver's own way of justifying his way of eking out a livelihood. But to the mind that is more discerning, the apparently simple messages connote weightier significance. Indeed, there is hunger as well as ignorance in Nigeria and the world at large. Both features combine to entrench under-nourishment, ill health, misery and death. Although, medical professionals and dieticians continue to maintain that a balanced, healthy diet is necessary for health maintenance, there is nevertheless a counter- argument that the majority of the Nigerian population cannot afford to eat a balanced, healthy diet or is unaware of the fact that certain healthful ingredients are limited or nutritionally unavailable to them.
To start with, nutritionists are worried that too many people are subsisting on variations of distorted diets which are either too little or too much in nutrient level, or with too great a loss of nutrients, thus making the individuals easy prey to ill-health and disease. "Nutrition is a key factor of good health," admits Dr. Rosemary Anyanwu, Nutrition Researcher at the Nigerian Institute of Medical Research (NIMR). "The effect of nutrition is not only on growth and physical development, but also on cognitive and social development. A malnourished child is not only more vulnerable to disease, its cognitive development will be in a peril, especially during the first three years of life. Stunted physical growth is closely linked to reduced mental development. Adequate nutrition begins with healthy eating habits and numerous studies have shown that increasing fruits and vegetables in diet can decrease risk of debilitating diseases such as cancer and heart disease."
Studies have shown that increasing fruits and vegetables in diet can decrease risk of cancer and heart disease. For calcium and protein needs, a few servings of dairy products, calcium supplements and /or alternative calcium-containing food sources are recommended daily. An array of whole grain products, such as the cereals, rice, yam, maize, potatoes or cassava are essential in providing various nutrients, calories and carbohydrates. Since each food has a slightly different nutritional profile, Anyanwu argues that consuming an array of different foods each day is recommended. "For each nutrient, consideration should be given to function, metabolism, dietary intake patterns, requirement levels, and toxicity. Basal requirements, safe intake levels, recommended dietary allowances, and tolerable upper intake levels are also to be established. A healthy diet should also include adequate fluid intake considering that approximately two quarts of water are lost each day from normal functioning of the body. At least eight glasses of water, are recommended to replace fluids lost throughout the day."
Adequate nutrition is the one containing the essential nutrients of a balanced diet which comprises the basis of all human nutrition comprising between 45 - 50 substances essential for maintaining normal growth and health. These include protein for building body tissue and synthesizing enzymes, some hormones and other complex substances that govern body processes. Illnesses and infections that lead to an increased loss of nitrogen from the body necessitate replacement by a higher consumption of dietary protein. Infants and young children also require more protein per kilogram of body weight. A protein deficiency accompanied by energy deficits results in a form of protein-energy malnutrition called marasmus - characterized by loss of body fat and wasting of muscle, fats and carbohydrates for energy. Others are vitamin A and carotene, vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin E, vitamin K, the B-complex vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, vitamin B6, pantothenic acid, biotin, vitamin B12), folate, antioxidants and minerals like calcium, iron, zinc, selenium, magnesium and iodine.
The 1992 World Declaration on Nutrition, made at the International Conference on Nutrition (ICN) in Rome, recognized access to nutritionally adequate and safe food as being essential for an individual's welfare and for national, social and economic development. However, today, millions of hungry people are dying more as a result of inequity of access to or knowledge about how to obtain adequately nourished meals among households and individuals, than to food shortages or non-availability.
The figures are daunting. According to UNICEF, half of more than 10 million deaths every year among children under five are associated with malnutrition; 170 million children are underweight, 208 million are stunted, and 49 million are wasted; over 900 million people suffer from goitre, 16 million are severely retarded, and another 50 million suffer other forms of brain damage, due to a deficiency of iodine. But it does not end there.
In its annual Food Insecurity Report, entitled the State of Food Insecurity in the World 2002 (SOFI 2002) the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), notes that "a smaller increase in world food production would suffice if its growth were accompanied by more equitable access, achievable through redistribution and production of the food itself, and of the means of producing it or of the purchasing power needed to buy it, to those currently on the lower rungs of the food access ladder." This failure of the fight against hunger as confirmed by the reports, show a lack of progress toward meeting the goals set during the 1996 World Food Summit in Rome. From the same report, in the 1990s, the number of hungry people declined by six million a year on average, and that at this rate, it would take 60 years to reduce the number of hungry people in the world to 400 million not later than the year 2015 - the target set at the Summit.
From the report, the goal of halving the number of hungry and undernourished people is hinged on the achievements of countries such as China, Indonesia and Thailand in Asia, Nigeria in Africa and Brazil in Latin America, in sustaining remarkable growth in food availability to reduce the proportion of undernourished in the total population from 37 per cent in the late 1960s to 17 per cent at the end of the last century. However, the decrease was not sufficient to halve the actual number of undernourished in the developing world, estimated at 956 million in 1969-71 and now as high as 777 million in 1997-99 - the FAO's latest three-year average estimate.
Worldwide, the latest estimates indicate that 860 million people were undernourished between 1998 and 2002. This figure includes 13 million in industrialized countries, 35 million in countries in transition and 799 million in the developing world. The latest figure of 799 million for developing countries represents a decrease of just 20 million since 1990-92, the benchmark period used at the World Food Summit of that period. But a closer examination reveals that the situation in most of the developing world is even bleaker than it appears at first glance. While countries like Nigeria, Ghana and Peru have all achieved marginal reductions of more than three million under nourished persons each over the last decade, rapid progress in a few large countries like China, Indonesia, Viet Nam and Thailand, have failed to redeem the situation. If these six countries are set aside, the number of undernourished people in the rest of the developing world has increased by over 80 million since the World Food Summit benchmark period.
In several ways the issue of nutrition has attained both a health and a human-rights perspective. But then, where lies the connection? What are the primary links between nutrition, poverty and health as seen from a human-rights perspective? In the view of Dr. Gro Harlem Brundtland, erstwhile Director General of the World Health Organization (WHO): "Nutrition is a key universal factor that affects, as much as it defines, the health of all people. For proper functioning, a regular intake of the proper mix of nutrients, safely ingested, is essential for growth, development, work, resistance to infection, and attainment and enjoyment of physical and mental well-being."
Continuing, Brundtland says "It is no coincidence that the idea to establish the WHO emerged in 1948 from the same process that identified the universal value of human rights, and WHO's mandate is also universal in calling for equity in the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of health as one of the fundamental rights of every human being without distinction of race, religion, political belief, economic or social condition. Her point is that if we were to point to one single factor necessitating the need for nutrition rights, poverty would take preeminence. "Poverty is responsible for ill-health in the world today. It is the main obstacle to the attainment of good health. Poverty leads to ill-health and ill-health breeds poverty. Where there is structural poverty and ill-health there will be poor development, poor nutrition, poor health, and poor human rights. Only the proper mixes of nutrients under clean and safe conditions guarantee the ideal or "gold standard". But in a world where inequity, poverty, underdevelopment, as well as mal-distribution and inadequate access to food, health and care still prevail, tragic consequences are imminent."
Putting the issue in another perspective, Dr. Janet Akindele, a social scientist avows that "We are talking more of the 'right to adequate nutrition' rather than 'nutrition rights'. The concept of nutrition rights was developed in the context of the Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC) and developed by UNICEF which defines nutrition rights as the combination of access to food, health and necessary care - the three components necessary to guarantee adequate nutrition".
She notes that failure to eliminate hunger is already undermining efforts to reach the Millennium Development Goals set by the UN. "Hunger limits school attendance so hopes for achieving universal primary education and literacy, for example, will be thwarted as millions of hungry children continue to suffer from diminished learning capacity or are forced to work instead of attending school. Worse still, low birth weight, protein energy malnutrition, iron deficiency anaemia and iodine deficiency disorders are all linked to cognitive deficiencies.
Looking at the matter from yet another angle, Fred Eke, a lawyer, opines that the line should be drawn between biological nutritional needs and fulfilling individual food rights. "The law can be the bridge between the hungry and their nutritive rights, it can also be the obstacle. While many human needs can be met by autocratic powers without consultation, if people have no chance to influence what and how they were being fed, then their right to adequate food is not being met, even if they get all the nutrients their bodies need. Human rights are mainly about upholding human dignity, not about fulfilling physiological requirement. Dignity does not come from being fed, rather it comes from being liberated from poverty, being able to provide for oneself and having the right knowledge and information about the right food combinations.
In any well-structured society, the objective is to move toward such conditions under which all people can provide for themselves."
On the basis of this formulation, Eke says democracy is an important factor in the realization of the human right to adequate nutrition and all other human rights. "Fulfillment of human rights requires a democratic social order. A democratic social order is one in which individuals can play an active role in shaping the conditions under which they live. Democracy is about participation and just as the human right to adequate nutrition must be seen in the context of the right to an adequate livelihood, this cluster of rights, in turn, must be viewed in the broader context of all human rights. Livelihoods may be adequate in terms of specific measures of income, health care, housing, etc., but this must not be achieved through means that violate other human rights because human rights are indivisible." "An explicit human-rights approach to health and nutrition thus means that mechanisms and procedures are gradually put into place to ensure that the values advocated are underpinned in human-rights laws and subsequently incorporated into national laws. Technical links between health and nutrition with the way we think and speak about human rights need to be reviewed. The bottom line is that the professional, institutional and operational bridges between health and nutrition and human rights remain to be further refined."
Agbo Areh, an agriculture scientist has this to say: "Nutrients come from the soil, forests, seas and lakes, and nutrition, as a process, is linked to complex interdependent issues like production, the environment, economic structures, social organization, and human roles and behaviour.
He is quick to point out that while focusing on the crucial role of nutrition, the complex interplay between nutrition, health, poverty and social rights should not be resolved in isolation. "Nutrition is akin to development and one of the factors relating to the essence of development is poverty. Other factors like unemployment, low income, unsuitable shelter, sex-based discrimination, and inadequate knowledge and other barriers to providing the vulnerable with care, converge to determine nutritional status."

Original_Search_Title: Houses Everywhere, But None for the People
Headline: Nigeria: Houses Everywhere, But None for the People
Body_Text:
Lagos — Various governments as well as private developers are constructing
state-of-the-art housing estates across the country. The more these houses
are built, the more many Nigerians are retiring under bridges for their
inability to afford decent accommodation.
Mr. Olumide Akintayo, this sunny morning sat under the Ijora Bridge, a
place that has become his home for the past two years. At one corner of the
space there are two weather-beaten 'Ghana-must-go' bags apparently
containing his personal effects. His wife, Toyin, was busy cooking breakfast
of beans and yam at a makeshift kitchen under the bridge. Their three
children-a girl and two boys--were playing with their peers within the
environment.
Akintayo served the Nigerian Railway Corporation (NRC) as a technician
for 20 years. During those years all effort to get a house of his own was in
vain as salaries were hardly paid regularly and when due though he lived in
one of the staff quarters. On disengaging from the NRC, he relocated his
family to Badia area of Ijora, were they got a one-room apartment at the
slum. It was not long they moved in that the landlord sold the premises
without adequate notice to the tenants.
With no tangible means of livelihood, he could not get another
accommodation before the bulldozer from the new owner of the property came
to pull the place down. With neither friend nor brother or neighbour to run
to, Akintayo headed to the Ijora Bridge. His intention was to stay there for
few months, gather himself together, get a job and then look for befitting
accommodation.
However, for the past two years he has been living under the bridge with
his family without any hope of leaving the place soon. He is now regretting
that if the corporation he worked for had put in place an arrangement for
staff to own house or paid salaries regularly he would not have been in the
present mess.
Akintayo is not alone in such a predicament. Around him are other
occupants who might be in similar problem. Across the breath and width of
Lagos State and other cities around the country there have been a gradual
emergence of a colony of under-the-bridge squatters. Yet around the poor
Akintayo and other low and middle class Nigerians, governments and private
housing developers are building various exotic housing estates, but all for
high-income earners. One of such estates recently commissioned is the Lagos
State Millennium Houses in Lekki in which a duplex is going for N17
million. Little or no attempt is made for low and middle class
citizens. Even when there was deliberate attempt, they were priced beyond
what they can afford.
A United Nations report in 1996 declared metropolitan Lagos a
slum. There is still a strong belief that people are too poor to save or to
repay housing loans. In the second republic, this led to heavy housing
subsidy by allocating a standard plot of land in Lagos at the rate of N1,
000 for high density, N1,500 for medium density and N2, 000 for medium
density and the sale of Shehu Shagari core housing at N6, 000.00 and N15,
000.00 respectively for one-bedroom and three-bedroom bungalow- a
paternalistic approach that could not be sustained."The fact is that there
is no adequate housing policy or adequate implementation of the existing one
by various governments for the benefit of the poor masses and the people at
the rural areas. Housing programme in this country has never favoured this
segment of the society. Corporate organisations are also guilty in this
matter for their inability to provide housing accommodation for their
staff," Tony Edike, a quantity and estate surveyor told THISDAY.
In a paper titled 'Housing Financing in Nigeria: Need for
Re-engineering', Dr. Timothy Olugbenga Nubi of the Department of Estate
Management, University of Lagos and the National Coordinator Ideal Habitat
Cooperative Housing Initiatives, said it has become glaring that most of the
urban population live in dehumanising housing environment, while those that
have access to average housing do so at abnormal cost. Nubi, who is also
national co-ordinator, Ideal Habitat Co-operative Housing Initiatives, said
that rent in major cities of Nigeria is about 60 per cent of an average
worker's disposable income.
This is far higher than the 20 to 30 per cent recommended by the United
Nations, he noted. He explained that the rate of demand for new houses was
in part predicated on the rate of formation of new houses and in part on the
rate of replacement of old housing stock. With estimated population of 110
million according to the 1991 census report, he said Nigeria needs to
produce 720,000 housing units per annum based on an estimate of nine
dwelling units a year per 1,000 of population. This is a very big challenge
to the building industry, Nubi added.
According to the Federal Office of Statistics, Lagos, despite Federal
Government access to factors of housing production, the country could at
best expect 4.2 per cent of the annual requirement from the government in
Abuja. Substantial contribution is expected from other public and private
sectors, while it is acknowledged that private sector developers account for
83 per cent of urban housing. Unfortunately, the private sector is saddled
with numerous problems which make supply always fall far short of demand. Of
these, the most limiting is finance.
Nubi explained that finance has been part of housing problems, but he
ranked land and building materials higher. The drought of information and
working knowledge of housing finance operation is a major problem today, he
further stressed.
Housing, he said, is the first area to suffer in a tight money market
since neither the builder nor the consumer could readily obtain finance for
housing. Actually, many builders have difficulty obtaining capital for their
projects even in normal times.
It was to tackle the housing problem that the Federal Government
established the National Housing Fund (NPF) to collect funds from the
Mandatory Saving Scheme. Again out of the N300 million loan approved by the
Federal Mortgage Bank of Nigeria (FGMBN) between 1999 and 2001, only N100
million was advanced. The problem in this case was not availability of fund
but stringent measures to prevent default. Hence, the housing problems
persisted, the housing expert stressed.
FMBN started operation in 1977 with the following main functions:
Provision of long-term credit facilities to mortgage institutions in the
country; the encouragement and supervision of the activities of the mortgage
institutions.
Others are provision of long- term loan to individuals and property
developers for housing, produce saving facility, and carry out research on
mortgage finance. These activities have been marred by administrative
ineptitude, political instability and unco-ordinated policies.
No doubt, housing is regarded as a social problem. But while it is
admitted that housing must be provided or improved eventually, many national
economic development programmes regard housing as a form of investment that
could be postponed. The longer it can be put off, the reason goes, the
better the result for the nation as a whole.
"There are portions of the world in which the satisfaction of demand for
housing and housing improvement, is a rewarding private business which does
not lack eager participants and which seems to produce, as in the United
States, a general level of housing welfare. Nevertheless, in many parts of
the world, housing is regarded as a demand, which cannot and should not be
satisfied by the market and, hence, must be distributed as a form of public
largesse," Nubi further explained.
The perception of housing as either 'economic good' or 'social-good', he
added, would determine the "level of investment and government involvement
in its production and distribution. When seen as a 'social good', housing
market is subjected to political uncertainty and statutory
intervention.
"In such a circumstance, private involvement is discouraged as investors
will be unwilling to take the risk. Our land policy is an example of such
restraint. The 1996 Rent Control Edict in Lagos was a discriminatory
one. The rent control law was limited to low-income housing. This
discrimination led to investment in high income housing as shown in Lagos,
Abuja amongst other places", he emphasised.
In 1979 the World Bank intervened to assist the country. This led to
housing projects in eight states of Nigeria with Bauchi State having a share
of N 24.6 million and Imo State, N63.8 million. The 1980 - 1985 Fourth
National Development Programme also proposed a budget of N1.9 billion for
housing. During this period N600m was spent on housing construction. The
failure of these housing programmes and the ever-increasing housing needs
led to the promulgation of National Housing Policy of 1990.
Edike told THISDAY that it was to encourage workers to save towards
building their houses that the former National Providence Fund (NPF) was
established to collect funds from employers and employees towards their
retirement. This gives them access to long-term funds and put them in good
position to finance housing development. NPF collects funds from employers
especially government (organisations and large business concerns) and
employees and therefore have long-term obligation since employers can only
receive their benefits or retirement gratuities only after leaving their
jobs.
Thus, NPF usually looks for investment that offer long-term prospects
and are inflation proof like real property development or acquisition. They
also offered loans on long-term basis to building societies and mortgage
institutions.
However, according to Edike, corporate bodies have a lot of role to play
in the housing policy by making sure that their staff are not only
accommodated while on active service but even when they are retired. He
added that by 1979, it had become evident that despite the fact that most
companies make huge profit, there was total neglect of the need to solve the
obvious housing problems of their workers. This compelled the promulgation
of Employees Housing Scheme (Special provision) Decree 54 of 1979.
The main provision of the decree was that any employer of up to 500
employees should provide minimum of 50 housing units out of which
three-quarters should be made available for non-executive staff. The decree
put in place a structure for identification of such category of employees
and implementation of the decree. It also provided for the establishment of
a Housing Loan Board by the government. Like policy in Nigeria meant for the
lower and middle class, it never worked or lasted.
The high cost of houses in the country may not be unrelated to the cost
of building materials, but Nubi explained that where per unit cost is
abnormally high "as we have today, the simple implication is that few people
will be able to afford it. The limited finance will not be able to spread
around the potential home owners".
He added that the gap between income and shelter cost in Nigeria is very
wide, and has eliminated the low-income earners from the housing
market.
"High cost had been attributed to the following: rising cost of building
materials, inflation rate in the economy, high space and quality standard
adopted by designers, fees of professional involved in housing designs and
construction, excessive profit of contractors and 10 per cent interest
payable to NHF.
"The sale of two-bedroom bungalows at Otedola Estate in Lagos, which,
according to the former Military Governor of Lagos State, Col. Buba Marwa in
1999 was the cheapest anywhere and the subsequent sale of two- bedroom flat
at Ikorodu by LSDPC at N1.7 million, is a bad omen", he stressed.
As Nubi stated, without adequate will to implement previous policies to
provide cheaper houses for the poor, no change will be achieved, unless
existing structures were re-engineered as appropriate and an enabling
environment provided without further delay.

Original_Search_Title: Army Unit In Ketu to Remain
Headline: Nigeria: Army Unit In Ketu to Remain
Body_Text:
The Federal Government has directed that the military unit stationed at Mile 12, Ketu, Lagos, should remain there so as to ensure the security of persons and property in the area.
Vice President Atiku Abubakar gave this directive when the national president and founder of the National Traders and Market Leaders Council of Nigeria; a non governmental organisation (NGO), Alhaja (Dr.) Adetutu Odunsi, led a 140 man delegation on his office on a courtesy visit in Abuja today.
Abubakar stated that government would take steps to stop the incessant molestation of members of the association by some state government who accuse them of political partisanship.
Vice Preisdent, Atiku Abubakar said that government since its inception had given a lot of incentives to farmers through the distribution of fertilizer at subsidized rates. This, he said, has ensured a growth rate of more than 5% in the agricultural sector, adding that subsidy on fertilizer at present is put at 25%.
Abubakar told the delegation that President Olusegun Obasanjo recently approved the sum of Three billion naira (N3b) for the purchase of excess grains from farmers to ensure that farmers do not lose their crops and to motivate farmers to greater efforts in the next planting season.
Government, he said has also approved the sum of two hundred million naira (N200m) for the purchase of tubers such as yams and coco yams.

Original_Search_Title: Politicians Angry at UN Plan to Make Kenyan Commander of Peace Force
Headline: Nigeria: Reps Ask FG to Rebuff Okonkwo's Replacement As Ecomil Commander
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE House of Representatives Committee on Army has urged Nigerian defence authorities to stop the planned replacement of General Felix Okonkwo, the Nigerian commander of the ECOWAS Military Force in Liberia (ECOMIL) with a Kenyan General.Chairman of the House Committee on Army, Rep. Ado Dogo at a press briefing in Abuja yesterday, also revealed the discomfort of Nigerian troops in Liberia with the American imported meals they have been feeding on. The troops, he said, want local foods such as garri, yam.Mr. Dogo who just returned from an assessment visit of the Nigerian contingent in Liberia also disclosed logistical constraints facing the troops, notably finance and transport.
Observing that Nigeria's contribution of 1,580 officers and men to ECOMIL's total strength of 3310 officers and men should give the country a commanding role in the peace keeping operation, Mr. Dogo said:" Presently, General Okonkwo is the ECOMIL Commander in Liberia. He is expected to surrender such leadership to General Obande of Kenya when UN takes over the activities of ECOMIL in October 2003."The delegation learnt that General Obande is not in anyway better than General Okonkwo and moreover, Nigeria has the largest contingent in the peace keeping operation, why cant the country lobby for the retention of General Okonkwo as the Force Commander?"
Observing the discomfort of the Nigerian troops with American imported meals, the Committee chairman said: "The food presently supplied to NIGCON comes from the U.S. and most members of the contingent are usually not at home with it. They instead prefer home food such as garri, yams, apu etc. "Rep Dogo also disclosed other operational constraints facing the Nigerian delegation in Liberia such as transport and poor finances, but assured that the country's troops were giving a good impression of the country and its military even in the face of the limitations.

Original_Search_Title: NNPC/Shell Oil Pipeline Ruptures, Explodes in Ogoniland
Headline: Nigeria: NNPC/Shell Oil Pipeline Ruptures, Explodes in Ogoniland
Body_Text:
Crude oil spilling from a ruptured Shell Petroleum Development Company
(SPDC) joint venture oil pipeline burst into flames in Ogoni land, scorching
yam fields and spreading thick, black smoke for miles, residents and
activists disclosed last week.
Residents of Gio, in Khana Local Government Area of Rivers State, 40
kilometers east of Port Harcourt, said they first learned of the spill when
they awoke to an explosion early Friday and saw flames leaping from the
pipe.
In a bid to put out the blaze, Shell workers raced to the scene but were
denied access by angry villagers who demanded compensation for the spill, a
company spokesman who spoke on condition of anonymity said.
The spokesman explained that Company officials were negotiating with the
community to repair the leak, but this had not achieved any results as at
press time.
Ledum Mitee, leader of the activist Movement for the Survival of Ogoni
People, disclosed that the flames had died down but that rolling smoke made
it difficult for nearby residents to breathe.
Crude was also flowing into nearby streams, polluting drinking water,
Mitee said.
Mitee blamed the spill on corroding pipelines that have not been
maintained since Shell's pullout.
Shell suspended drilling in Ogoniland since 1993 as a result of protests
by villagers who accuse the oil giant and the Nigerian government of
colluding to cheat them of oil royalties.
Shell pipelines continue to transport crude through Ogoniland, a region
with about 500,000 people in Nigeria's swampy southern Niger Delta.
The cause of the latest spill has not been determined as the area
remains inaccessible and the strike action embarked upon by Shell workers
makes it difficult to reach concerned officials for their immediate
response.
While reacting to the development, the company's External Relations
Manager, Don Boham disclosed that the leaking pipeline has been clamped and
that efforts are being made to ascertain the degree of damage to the
surrounding farmlands.
On the issue of compensation, he pointed out that a different asset team
would be in-charge of that , adding that as soon as the degree of damage is
ascertained and those affected identified, steps would be taken to remedy
the situation.
It would be recalled that Shell senior staff union Petroleum and Natural
Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria (PENGASSAN) embarked on a strike
action last week to protest what they described as the company's EP
Globalisation policy.
The workers are also protesting the re__cpLocation of the SAP server to the
Hague , pointing out that this was unacceptable since it would put vital
information regarding the country's petroleum sector under the control of
foreigners.
The striking workers are also of the opinion that the Globalisation
policy of the company would cost Nigerian staff their jobs , while making
the same jobs available to foreigners .While speaking on the development,
the Lagos Chapter Chairman of the union, Celestine Ebigwu noted that
Europeans were using the issue of Globalisation to solve their unemployment
problems at the expense of Nigerians.
The union chairman also disclosed that the company has started bringing
in non-technical expatriate personnel to do jobs which were hitherto
reserved for Nigerians and lamented government's lack of concern over the
development.
In Brunei, the policy of Shell Globalisation was rejected by the
government , the chairman disclosed , adding that the situation remains
unchanged in Nigeria because government has failed to live up to
expectation.
Meanwhile , the federal Government say about 300,000 barrels of crude,
more than one-tenth of Nigeria's exports are stolen daily by criminal rings
who syphon the fuel from pipelines using everything from buckets to
sophisticated pumps.

Original_Search_Title: Food Glut, But We Must Make Hay...
Headline: Ghana: Food Glut, But We Must Make Hay...
Body_Text:
Last weekend, GTV did a report on the current food situation in Ghana and informed Ghanaians that there is a food glut in their country.
True, though, Ghanaians are wont to complain about all manner of things, so far, nobody is complaining about food - that is its unavailability.
GTV's team went to some of the "food baskets" of the country and the picture presented was a largess brimming over.
At least the basic staples like maize, yam, cassava, not imported fare, are in such abundant supply that, the farmers are having difficulties in disposing of their stocks.
On camera, some of the food distributors expressed frustration, however, about the transportation and other logistical problems hampering the distribution of the food to other parts of the country.
They appealed to the government to step in and help with the problem of storage and distribution.
Good news, we are told, is no news and so even in the midst of this cornucopia, the stories doing the headlines in the newspapers and FM stations hardly mention our full bellies as one of the small mercies we should be thankful for!
The problems of storage and distribution have bedeviled Ghanaian agriculture for decades. To that we must also add irrigation.
Luckily for us, the past two years have not seen us go through debilitating "lean periods" as food supply has been fairly stable year round; but we cannot take that for granted.
Food security cannot be left to chance alone. With the rain-fed agricultural system that obtains in Ghana, we require extensive storage and processing capacities to take care of the kind of "excess" our food growers and distributors are now "complaining" about.
This is one area the Ministries of Food and Agriculture and Private Sector Development can team up on to enable viable food storage, processing and distribution companies, employing modern methods and techniques to step into the breach.
The state-owned Food Distribution Corporation has gone the way of similar state-controlled bureaucracies - moribund, but that does not mean that Ghana does not need well-organised and resourced food distribution companies to trade in food and also act as the nation's strategic food buffers.
As we enjoy our glut, we must not forget that just one failed rainy season and we are back to square one...

Original_Search_Title: Afenifere Never Supported Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Afenifere Never Supported Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Lagos —  The leader of Afenifere, Chief Abraham Adesanya, was cornered at the
socio-political group's headquarters in Lagos by a crop of journalists in
the heat of the fuel price hike crisis.
Excerpts:
Today marks the beginning of an indefinite strike action called by
the labour movement in the country, as a result of the hike in the prices of
petroleum products. What is your view about the present state of things?
The PDP government spent a lot of money during the last election to
solicit for people's support. They used money to buy electorate's votes. The
news that came to me from all over the country was that in many places, they
were distributing money, even when the voters were on the queue, waiting to
exercise their franchise .
How can that happen and you would expect the government not to go
into debt?
How do they get this money back, except you ask the people to pay
more. You use one glorious name to ask people to pay more, like increasing
petrol price. The proceeds will obviously replenish government purse, the
amount they have indiscriminately spent. The pity of it is that, it is not
only people who got this money from them that are now paying back. You and I
will now be paying back.
If you choose the wrong person and he is treating you in a way that he
knows, you deserve the government you have chosen for yourself.
From the situation of things at the moment, it is believed that
people are ready to continue to stay at home if only to demonstrate that
they object to the increases. How do you assess the reaction of people so
far?
Well, the reaction of the people is what you yourself has been
seeing. It is something you can point to, it is something you can hear. But
from the newspaper reports I have read, the PDP said they are going on. They
are the people who got us into this debt. So, they say we must go on with
what it takes to make us run into this debt, which you too must pay. And how
do they do that? Increase in fuel price. Even if you don't have a car, when
the price is increased, you are also to pay. You will pay. I will also
pay. The market woman will pay, the farmer will pay. That is the evil of
voting for the wrong people, the wrong party.
Well, so far, Mr. President has said the decision is irreversible.
What is your stand on this?
How can somebody say the action he takes on behalf of his principal is
irreversible? Isn't that a case of the tail wagging the dog? The agent
saying that well, you my principal , you are talking rubbish.
But sir, Afenifere, which you head, supported Chief Obasanjo in the
last presidential election. Why this sudden turn-around to now label him as
anti-masses?
What has that got to do with the petrol issue? The point you must note
is that Afenifere never took any official stand. You wouldn't see it in the
handout of Afenifere that, this is what they are going to do about
Obasanjo.
It is a question of guess work. And if that were so, then we must reap
the benefit of what we sowed.
The UAD (United Action for Democracy), has said that civil
disobedience will be a continuous exercise until this present government
falls. Do you support this view?
Well, I wouldn't know. But Yoruba have a saying that "Ohun ti eniyan o
ba ni je, enu e lo maa fi ko" (whatever somebody will not eat, he will
reject with his mouth). If they feel that what they are getting, they don't
deserve it, it is their duty to make a change.  But what you sowed you will
reap.
That is the immutable law of karma. If you plant orange, you don't get
to your farm with a view to harvesting mango. Or you plant cassava, you go
back there with a feeling that you will harvest yam.  You are wasting your
time.
The argument has been propounded that owing to the virtual absence
of a viable opposition, this has given Mr. President more than enough
latitude to do whatever he desires without being checked. Do you share this
opinion?
I wouldn't know. But what I know is that those people, either
representing us in the National Assembly, at the states assemblies, the
local governments, they are deemed to be the servants of the people, the
agents of the people. The people are the principal, those they put there are
their agents. Can a river rise higher than the sun? When your agents tell
you that he is higher than you are, then there is a coup. Your agent is
saying that well, what I say must be carried out, no matter what you
think.
When a servant is telling his master such a thing, where do we then go
from there.
In those good old days, when the civil servants or the government wrote
a letter to you or me, do you know how they usually ended it?  "Your
obedient servant". But nowadays, they are not our obedient servants, they
are your masters. If it is not so, how can people do something , take for
example, this fuel price increase and you seek the opinion of the high and
the low, including the medium, and they say this thing is not right, and you
say no, I will force it down your throat. May the good Lord deliver us.
As a leader, what will be your reaction to the move being made by
the PDP-controlled Federal Government to restructure the local government?
The PDP wants to be the master of all. How can any government come
around to say it is appointing some people to go and do a council reform or
whatever they call it? They are the people to appoint those who will do it,
and when they sit and do it and realise that it is not right for you, like
this increase in fuel price, they say that is what they want to do whether
it is right or wrong. We are all powerless.
We have said, let the people come together and decide on what to do.
They are now saying they are going to appoint some people to sit over the
sensitive issue, and come back with their report. Is that the democracy for
which many of us paid with our own blood?
When we are asking for the national conference, you remember the time
we've been asking for it, some obas, traditional rulers came together to say
they must do something about this matter. Representatives went to Abuja to
talk over this matter. There are six zones in the country, four of these
zones agreed that we should have national conference. The remaining two
didn't say there shouldn't be, but they lied and said well, we didn't know
this is what we were coming to discuss. So, we still have to go back to our
people, and the other zones took them on. For example, they knew of that
conference before we in the Afenifere knew about it. And these are the same
people who said, let us go back to tell our people. In spite of that, four
zones agreed that we should have national conference. Two said no or half
no. And these two won the battle. Even our president never liked it.  They
never took any step to carry it out. And do you people still feel proud that
we are practising democracy in this country?
In the light of all these sir, people out there are calling for the
resignation of Mr. President from office. What is your stand on this?
Which people are you talking about?
The fuel price hike protesters?
Oh well, that is their opinion.
Alright then, what is your own opinion sir?
My opinion?
Yes sir?
Let me think about it. I don't talk before I think. I think before I
talk. I never thought of resignation before I came here, but now that you
are telling me that some people are saying so, what is my own opinion. Isn't
that what you are asking?
Those who are saying resign, must have thought over it for some time.
They must have slept over it. You haven't allowed me a minute to think over
it and you are asking for my opinion.
Would you say you are disappointed in the government of Chief
Olusegun Obasanjo as a person?
As a person, I don't like this petrol hike. I don't like him saying that
well, the will of the people should not prevail. I don't like it at
all.
But so far, you don't think he is taking the right step?
If I think he is taking the right step, I wouldn't have said the hike in
the prices of petroleum products should be reversed.
Recently, Chief Obasanjo made public, his decision to make one of
your own, Chief Cornelius Adebayo, a minister in his new cabinet.  What is
your reaction to this?
Well, if Obasanjo is of the view that he wants to take some opinion,
that certain opinion should be represented in his government, who am I to
say he shouldn't. To get these people there is one thing, to consider what
they say there, is another. So, let us wait and see.
The AD has said Chief Adebayo is on his own if he takes the
appointment.
So, what is the position of Afenifere on the issue?
What I understand is that Chief Adebayo was taken to represent the
North-Central. Well, I don't think I will be wrong if they think the
North-Central from where he comes, should be represented and they take
him.
One thing I must make you all understand is that I have implicit
confidence in C. O. Adebayo, absolute confidence. I know Adebayo is not
somebody who picks up appointment just because of money.

Original_Search_Title: South West : Farmers Exhibit Know-How at Agric Show
Headline: Cameroon: South West : Farmers Exhibit Know-How at Agric Show
Body_Text:
Mamfe (Manyu) The Manyu Divisional Chief Town, Mamfe, was in a two day festive mode recently as food crops and life-stock farmers throughout the South West Province converged to exhibit their best products at what was termed a "mini-agricultural show with a provincial touch".
The Mamfe show, organised by the South West Provincial Delegation of Agriculture was the third after Tombel in Kupe Manugouba and Menji in Lebialem Divisions.
The best species in food crops and fruits like cocoyams, plantains, yams, cassava, potatoes, oranges etc and livestock like goats, fowls, fish and pigs were the biggest pullers of the anxious crowds. Almost everything exposed at the Mamfe show especially the oranges, garri and eru were all bought even before the close of the first day, except the barely eleven months old pig which probably, because of its extraordinary 400 kg weight chased away many a prospective buyer. But this pig did not see the end of the show as it was slaughtered and displayed in pieces for easier purchase at the Mamfe central market.
Opening the show, the First Assistant S.D.O for Mamfe, Mr. Nwafua Lawrence Forwang said it was to limit the headaches of inadequate mechanisation, lack of capital, preservation facilities and low quality production that government, through various action plans was training and employing technicians to assist farmers, encourage them through best farm competitions and agro-pastoral shows.
On his part, the chief organiser of the show, South West Provincial Delegate of Agriculture, Mr Mboge Mboge George, saw the objective of the show as one aimed at bringing the farmers closer in order to learn from each other. Announcing a CFA 250,000 government support to young farmers and a farmers' grant of CFA 4 million, Mr Mboge said much more was on the way in form of inputs under the HIPC programme.
The Provincial Delegation of Livestock, Animal Breeding, Fisheries and Industries, co-organisers of the show, was represented at the occasion by Mr Njoume Abrie Njumbe, Animal Industries Engineer. The mini agro-pastoral shows, which have a provincial touch, come up twice in the year in two different divisional headquarters.
The second and last for this year has been slated for Mundemba in Ndian Division in November.

Original_Search_Title: Hour-By-Hour Report On Wulensi
Headline: Ghana: Hour-By-Hour Report On Wulensi
Body_Text:
FRANCES ASSIAM, ZIEDONG CONCEDE DEFEAT
The people of Wulensi will forever remember the battle for that
constituency. For, it was the day the political juggernauts and hoodlums
descended on the town and almost tore it apart in the name of politics.
Certainly, the battle was one of many contenders but which ended up
being drawn between the New Patriotic Party (NPP) and the National
Democratic Congress (NDC). For, at the last count, the other contesting
parties had paled into insignificance and the NDC, lay supine lagging behind
the NPP by 894 votes difference.
The flurry and scurrying that heralded the election died down by 5:00
p.m. when voting ended. It was supplanted by a mish-mash of high hopes and
palpable anxiety as party functionaries, agents and brawny henchmen waited
for the countdown to the declaration of results.
As the EC officials rushed in with ballot boxes from the various polling
stations to the Wulensi community centre that had been turned into a
makeshift collation centre, party officials stood in groups assessing their
chances with all kinds of analysis.
Officials of the NPP felt relaxed but cautious when after the contents
of the first 50 ballot boxes were counted by 11pm. They were clearly leading
with about 1,300 votes, having swept all the polling stations in the Wulensi
township.
Then came a long and anxious wait for the remaining 15 ballot boxes,
which had to be ferried by boat to the centre from the 'overseas' area of
the constituency. It was during the long wait that two prominent members of
the NDC were overheard making defeatist remarks.
The national women's organiser, Mrs. Frances Assiam, conceded defeat
saying, "The NPP has done well in winning most of the vital votes in the
Wulensi township. They are clearly winning."
The deputy general secretary of NDC, Mr. Bede Ziedong, also said: "They
(NPP) have won from my calculations."
When the 15 ballot boxes finally arrived from the "overseas" area around
midnight and counting of ballot papers re-started, the results announced by
the Electoral Commission from the various polling stations mostly favoured
the NDC. In fact, an example is when the NDC polled over 200 votes at one
polling station as against only five votes for the NPP.
This placed the NPP on tenterhooks. Alarm was visibly written on the
faces of the top brass of the NPP, including the General Secretary, Dan
Botwe, and the Northern regional minister, Mr. Ernest Debrah, who were
witnesses to the near calamity.
One NPP supporter, not happy about the turn of events remarked, "We now
know these Bartors," apparently referring to the voters from the 'overseas'
area, whose votes were clearly threatening the NPP's lead.
The climax came when the Electoral Commission announced the final
results. According to the EC, a total of 16,798 votes were cast, out of
which 16,230 were valid votes, while 568 votes were rejected, representing a
58% turn-out. Registered voters in the constituency totaled 28, 899.
In the end, NPP's candidate, Wulensi Kofi Karim, secured 8,197 votes to
top the seven contestants, followed by Saadan John Njigur, NDC, 7,303 votes;
PNC's Okpanul Daniel Niwubui with 319 votes; Mumuni Dawuda of DPP, 227
votes; Independent candidate, Alhaji Musah Ziblila, 75, votes; Egle Party's
Inusah Nantogmah with 56 votes; and independent candidate, Oggah Magan
Peter, 53 votes in that order.
The very tense atmosphere in the Wulensi township in particular on the
day of the by-election, was defused by the heavy presence of military and
police personnel who set up barriers at strategic points to ensure law and
order.
There were brief warning shots intended to send a message out loud and
clear. This seemed to have worked as "macho" men from both the NDC and NPP
sent to Wulensi were scampered into their hideouts by the close of the
election.
As expected, the chief scribe of the winning NPP, Dan Botwe, was happy
with the final results, although he accused the NDC "of trying to cause
confusion in order to disrupt the by-election." He thanked NPP supporters
for their untiring efforts leading to the victory.
On his part, the NDC general secretary, Dr. Joshua Aryeh, accused the
NPP of vote-buying and intimidation, as well as using its incumbency to
advantage. He cited that the timing of the announcement of the appointment
of a Konkomba, the Saboba/Chereponi district chief executive, as the deputy
Northern regional minister, and the switching-on of street lights at Lungni,
an NDC strong-hold, a day before the election, worked in favour of the
NPP.
Hell nearly broke loose between the national women's organiser of the
NDC and some supporters of the NPP soon after Dr. Aryeh gave his impressions
about the election to newsmen. This followed an attempt by the NPP
supporters to heckle Dr. Aryeh. But Ms. Assiam would not countenance the
disrespect being shown to her chief scribe. A war of words ensued which
nearly generated into a fracas.
Wulensi, a farming community noted for yam production, was one of the
most severely hit areas during the 1994 Northern ethnic conflict between the
Nanumbas, Dagombas and Gonjas on the one hand and the Konkombas on the
other.
A cross section of people interviewed after the by-election were
generally happy that a Nanumba had emerged as the new Member of Parliament
(MP) for the Wulensi constituency.
According to the interviewees, this will balance the ethnic divide in
the area, since a Konkomba is also the MP for the Bimbilla
constituency.
The rumour making the rounds at Wulensi before the by-election alluded
to a calculated plan by the Konkombas to carry through an agenda, which
would ensure that the electorate in Konkomba settlements vote massively for
their compatriot, Mr. Saadan John Njigur, the NDC candidate.
They argued that the election of another Konkomba MP in addition to the
MP for Bimbilla, would give Konkombas a high leverage in Parliament and
ultimately their ethnic identity. But this was not to be.
In the same vein, the Nanumbas feared that if another Konkomba won the
Wulensi seat, this would amount to Nanumbas being marginalized on their own
land, as their tribe would not have a representative in Parliament.

Original_Search_Title: OAU Ile-Ife At 40
Headline: Nigeria: OAU Ile-Ife At 40
Body_Text:
Lagos — Obafemi Awolowo University stands out as first among equals with its
dogged commitment to the pursuit of excellence.
It is not by accident of history that Obafemi Awolowo University,
Ile-Ife, since its establishment has been passionately committed to
the pursuit of excellence. This goal represents the dream of the
founding fathers of the university. The visionary leadership of the
Action Group government under its deputy leader, Chief S.L. Akintola
,led to the setting up of the first regional university in Nigeria
called University of Ife in 1962.
This progressive move was to meet the growing demand for higher
education and to provide the much needed high-level manpower in both
the public and private sector of the economy. From the point it
admitted 244 students as its first set of intakes on September 22,
1962 at the old Nigerian College of Arts, Science and Technology, now
Polytechnic Ibadan, it remained a regional university under the
control of the Western Region. However, the ownership of the
university changed when the Federal Military Government through
Decree 23 of 1975 took it over.
At the demise of Chief Obafemi Awolowo, one of the brains behind the
establishment of the university, the institution went through another
metamorphosis, as the federal government in recognition of the
sterling qualities of Chief Awolowo and his contributions to national
development, especially in the field of education, renamed the
university as Obafemi Awolowo University in May 1987.
In its quest to meet the aspirations of its founding fathers, the
university has continued to affect positively, the socio-political
and economic spheres of the nation. It has remained as an agent of
change in terms of revolutionizing university education in the
country by setting high academic standard in deference to the rich
tradition and heritage that brought about the Ife idea. In essence,
the university has succeeded in producing graduates with skills to
independently solve problems in the work place and the outside world
generally.
Great Ife, as the university is fondly called by its products, is
indeed a great citadel of learning. Its pioneering efforts in science
and technology has produced good records acknowledged locally and
across the world. The university has to its credit the production of
the mechanical yam powder and Ife siffa 202, a garri processing
machine. It has equally through research efforts in biotechnology
produced new varieties of beans popularly referred to as Ife Brown.
For their invaluable contributions to knowledge, faculties like
pharmacy, humanities, engineering, medicine and social sciences of
the university have been designated centres of excellence. The
Regional Centre for Training in Aerospace Surveys, RECTAS, is located
in the university in recognition of the leadership role it has been
playing in aerospace and energy research.
To demonstrate that it is committed to the training of graduates who
are not only academically sound, but physically fit, the university
has provided the enabling environment and necessary encouragement for
her students to participate effectively in sports. This has in turn
placed her in the dominant position in sports in Nigeria and the West
African sub-region. Its sportsmen and women have been winning most of
the medals on offer in major competitions like Nigeria University
Games, NUGA, and West African Universities Games, WAUG. Arts and
culture is not left out as the university has devoted its energy to
the provision of a global platform to fully give expression to Yoruba
language and culture.
In all intent and purposes, the university has recorded landmark
accomplishments in its forty years of existence by providing a
liberal and qualitative education. This effort has yielded beautiful
harvests today.
With well over 50,000 graduates passing through its grill, majority
of whom are constantly shining like the constant star in their chosen
professions, Obafemi Awolowo University has indeed lived up to the
expectations of its founding fathers.

Original_Search_Title: Economists Seek Ideas to Fight African Food Shortage
Headline: South Africa: Economists Seek Ideas to Fight African Food Shortage
Body_Text:
Johannesburg —  Policymakers meeting at Durban conference know that the need for
economic recovery on the continent is urgent
WITH a recovery in agriculture central to a wider African economic
recovery, it is likely that policy makers will be looking to this week's
conference of agricultural economists being held in Durban for some fresh
ideas.
The urgent need for African economic recovery is clear. SubSaharan
Africa is the only region in the world to have experienced a large increase
in the number of undernourished people in the past 30 years.
If this trend continues, Africa cannot possibly meet one of the
Millennium Development goals of halving hunger in 30 years.
There have been some gains in African agriculture recently, but there
have been some spectacular disasters too.
Nine years ago, Zimbabwe's President Robert Mugabe opened the meeting of
the International Association of Agriculture Economists, a body which brings
together international economists from research, academic and financing
institutions.
Then, Zimbabwe was an example to Africa for both small- and large-scale
agriculture. Today the country is in deep crisis with more than half the
population dependent on food aid.
Zimbabwe is likely to be discussed at the conference this week, although
it is not on the formal agenda.
In many ways Zimbabwe shows the limitations and frustrations of
agricultural economists and that agricultural recovery is firmly in the
hands of politicians in certain countries.
"Economists can turn around lots of things, but not fundamental
governance problems," says the outgoing president of the association,
Joachim von Braun, who is also the director-general of the Washington-based
International Food Policy Research Institute.
He says there are issues that donors and governments should address as a
matter of urgency to bring rapid, proven results to the problems and
challenges facing African agriculture.
Market access to advanced industrial countries is one of the issues near
the top of his list for agricultural recovery, but the institute's team is
not hopeful the World Trade Organisation talks in Cancun next month will
solve this problem.
If agricultural protection is lifted in developing countries, a number
of studies suggest that Africa will see a $3bn annual increase in national
income, as agriculture makes up 30% of the continent's exports.
Von Braun would like to see greater public investment in rural
infrastructure, such as roads, storage facilities, and banking.
Although agriculture makes up between 25%- 50% of most sub-Saharan
countries' gross domestic product, investment in agricultural infrastructure
is only between 4% and 6%.
During the 1980s and 1990s most African governments embarked on
fundamental economic reforms to their pricing regimes to lower the
discrimination against their domestic agricultural sectors.
Most governments have depreciated their exchange rates and allowed farm
gate prices to rise, giving farmers a greater incentive. But the lack of
infrastructure continues to bedevil efforts to make markets work more
effectively.
Price volatility is far greater where infrastructure does not allow
farmers to get their produce to larger markets, and allow consumers a choice
of where to purchase products.
In some situations, says David Orden, a senior research fellow at the
institute, prices can vary by as much as a factor of four, depending on
local conditions. This makes it very difficult for farmers to be guided by
prices.
The institute's team underscores the need for improved seed, resistant
to drought, to make recovery possible.
Von Braun says he is somewhat encouraged by the development of the
Nerica rice variety because of its reduced need for water as well as its
higher yields. But, he says, what is really required is greater drought
resistance in Africa's staples, particularly maize, sorghum, cassava, and
sweet potatoes.
Because of Africa's mainly dry conditions, pests and complex diseases,
he believes that in the long run the continent cannot do without genetically
modified crops if there is to be any sustained agricultural recovery.
Many African countries are taking their time to decide whether or not to
allow modified varieties out of fear that their export crops could be
excluded from European Union markets.
But governance remains the overarching issue for Von Braun. Economic
reform is not enough when there are problems of rule of law because
agriculture can only grow if there is long-term stability, he says.
The institute is hosting an AllAfrica conference in Uganda next year, to
discuss assuring food and nutrition security on the continent by 2020.
The theme of the Durban conference is Reshaping Agriculture's
Contributions to Society.
Delegates will grapple with the fact that demands on the agricultural
sector are continually increasing.
In developing countries, agriculture, fisheries and forests are required
to supply expanding demand. But they are also being asked to address chronic
rural poverty and food insecurity problems, and become more environmentally
benign.
In transitional economies the task of structural and institutional
adjustments remains at the fore, as do the rules of trade engagement between
different groups of countries.
The challenge is to reshape agriculture and the food chain to meet the
demands of society.
The mission is to bring together economi sts and leading policy makers
to debate and generate a better understanding of the way the new agenda
might be met.

Original_Search_Title: Pyrethrum Production Falls Despite Demand
Headline: Kenya: Pyrethrum Production Falls Despite Demand
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Since 2002, the industry has also been experiencing graft-related
problems, leading to the more than 200,000 farmers in the country calling
for the liberalisation of the subsector to enable them to look for
alternative markets. At present, pricing and marketing of the produce
undertaken by the PBK, which also under the Pyrethrum Act that came into
force in 1964, licenses production and marketing of the produce.
Despite demand on the international market rising, local producer prices
have been dropping. The dropping of prices to Ksh160 from Ksh162.5 per
kilogramme of dried flowers with 2.2 per cent pyrethrin content and from
Ksh73 for flowers with 1 per cent pyrethrin has contributed to the slump in
production.
Kuresoi Member of Parliament, Moses Cheboi, said recently that farmers
in Nakuru district could earn far much more than Ksh1.2 billion per year if
the running of the industry was freed from the monolithic management, which
he said had failed to look for new markets in Asia and the Common Market for
Eastern and Southern African (Comesa) free trade area.
The chairman of the Pyrethrum Growers Associations Samuel Kihiu, shares
Mr Cheboi's sentiments, blaming PBK for the loss of traditional markets in
Europe and the US, which have been encroached on by Australia and
Tasmania.
The drop in pyrethrum production since 1993 has affected farmers'
earnings, with some opting to venture into alternative crops like banana,
sweet potato and wheat production.
According PBK data, production dropped from 10,000 tonnes in 1994 to
7,500 tonnes in 1995, then 6,220 in 1996 before it exports were affected by
El Nino and La Nina weather vagaries from 1997-1999. It recovered marginally
from all-time low of 3,490 tonnes to 5,480 tonnes in 2000.
The decline in production was accentuated by theft of equipment owned by
farmers' co-peratives by officials. Mr Kihiu said that the machinery of the
country's only factory in Nakuru, bought in in 1934, require modernisation,
as some of its equipment is obsolete, to improve efficinecy.
Experts say that Nakuru district, with the potential to produce over
20,000 tonnes per annum can meet the demand of the world market, which at
present is estimated at about 20,000 tonnes earning Kenya over Ksh10 billion
per annum in foreign exchange. The other pyrethrum growing regions in Nyanza
province, West Pokot district and Nyandarua district have the potential to
produce over 10,000 tonnes, almost the same quantity Tazania, Uganda,
Rwanda, Australia and Tasmania can produce.
The study blames the under-performance of the industry on inefficiency
in the pricing and marketing of the produce, the high cost of inputs and
"low adoption and adaptation of modern technology - techniques, planting
material, harvesting and storage."
The study by Dr Chepkwony noted that, because production is labour
intensive, farmers in Kisii had opted to grow bananas, which fetch them
Ksh91,000 per acre per annum compared with Ksh7,500 per acre per annum from
pyrethrum. In Nakuru, pyrethrum some farmers have shifted to sweet potato
farming from which they earn about Ksh65,000 from an acre.
Against this backdrop, Dr Chepkwony observed, farmers would earn more if
the industry were liberalised to enable them to sell the produce to the
factory directly without having to go through PBK, which charges levies on
their earnings in addition to falsification of the pyrethrin content of the
flowers delivered through it.
Following claims of graft, which forced the chairman of the board of
directors, Patrick Mararia, to resign on allegations that he sanctioned the
irregular transfer of Ksh150 million to the collapsed Euro Bank and
suspicions of planning to defraud the board of Ksh7 million.

Original_Search_Title: Agribank Misplaced Its Priorities: Industry Players
Headline: Zimbabwe: Agribank Misplaced Its Priorities: Industry Players
Body_Text:
Harare — AGRICULTURAL industry players last week alleged the Agricultural Bank of
Zimbabwe (Agribank) had "misplaced priorities" in the disbursement of a $60
billion loan facility it was given by the government soon after converting
into a fully-fledged agricultural development bank in October.
The players told The Financial Gazette the bank should have funded an
equal number of potential farmers in every district for the production of
seed on every crop to boost seed availability.
The bulk of the funds from the bank are funding cereal production, with
maize taking up $40 billion, while cotton has been allocated $15 billion. A
total of $5 billion would be channeled towards the production of
drought-resistant sorghum and millet. There was no al__cpLocation for small
grain.
"There has to be a national participation in seed production or in the
next three years we will have an extinction of some of the crops.
"The bank just gave out money to anyone who wanted to farm without
considering that the country is facing a major seed deficit on all
crops.
"We require a serious seed management programme and that's where most of
that money should have gone to," said one of the players who spoke on
condition of anonymity. This year alone, there is a maize seed deficit of
more than 30 000 tonnes while that of groundnuts is more than 25 000
tonnes.
The land reform programme displaced 90 percent of white commercial
farmers, who produced seed for the country, thereby creating a serious
national seed gap.
Another farmers' representative said the agricultural bank gave out
haphazardly the loans without considering the capacity of the
applicant.
"The bank should have targeted specific people who have shown a previous
commitment to food production to resuscitate the agricultural sector.
"Or else, they should have distributed the money equally among all
districts and then make an assessment of which district would have produced
more. It seems their greatest concern is on whether or not the people return
the money and not on reviving the sector," the representative said.
The agricultural sector is expected to shrink by more than 50 percent
owing to a shortage of inputs, capital and the disturbances caused by the
chaotic land reforms.
However, Agribank chief executive Taka Mutunhu said in a statement: "The
bank has throughout its network in the country committed/lent out almost the
entire $60 billion disbursed to it by government to fund eligible farmers in
the smallholder A1 and A2 categories.
"It is obvious to the bank and with regret that not all who applied and
are deserving farmers will be successful in securing loans given the huge
demand on the limited resources."
Prior to its transformation, Agribank operated as a commercial bank
since mutating from the defunct Agricultural Finance Corporation (AFC) four
years ago.
The bank, which adopted the AFC's bloated debtors' book, has had a rough
ride as a retail banking entity and has failed to publish its financial
accounts since its transformation.
Mutunhu added the bank would not assist new farmers without an official
permit letter or legitimate Ministerial Offer Letter as proof of their
tenure.
Most farmers have applauded the existence of the Land Bank but many are
worried about accessing the funds.

Original_Search_Title: Dire Need of Food Aid for One Million Victims in Western Sudan
Headline: Sudan: Dire Need of Food Aid for One Million Victims in Western Sudan
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The UN World Food Programme warned today that more than one million
people are now displaced by the conflict in Greater Darfur, western Sudan,
and are in desperate need of food assistance.
"The conflict in the Darfur States has caused enormous human suffering
and destruction of property," said Bradley Guerrant, Deputy Country Director
for Sudan. "We are facing a major humanitarian crisis which we must respond
to. These people were already poor and marginalized - now many of them are
totally destitute."
In November, WFP increased its appeal by US$39 million to assist the
growing number of displaced people over a period of five months.  The twelve
month emergency operation which began in April is now valued at US$180
million, to assist more than 3.8 million people affected by war and drought.
The operation is only 35 percent funded and the number in need keeps
rising.
Hordes of newly displaced people stream daily into camps around the
capital of the three Darfur States - North, South and West. They are living
in makeshift tents and have limited humanitarian assistance.  All of them
recount the horror of their houses being burned, food stocks looted and
being forced to flee with little or no possessions. Many arrive wounded or
with severe burns, but medical care in Darfur is very restricted.
Since February, the conflict has brought widespread killings, damage and
displacements throughout the region. A recent assessment carried out in
South Darfur by several humanitarian agencies established that 46 villages
out of 62 had been burned to the ground, while the rest had been
looted.
