did not post 341 offer letters to the beneficiaries, but instead, issued his
own offer letters to favoured people. The DA has since been dismissed. In
Gwebi/Hunyani, the DLIC and PLIC failed to resolve the problems there,
resulting in a lot of people being allocated land unofficially.
3.3 Land Al__cpLocation
3.3.1 Planning seems to have been largely a desk exercise as it failed
to take cognisance of the infrastructure existing on some farms. On such
farms as Stirling Vale and Dichwe in Makonde District, coffee and citrus
plantations were cut up into small units and allocated to separate
individuals rendering them unviable. Concern was expressed at the uprooting
of coffee plants at Dichwe farm.
3.3.2 At Hunyani and Yomba (Zvimba), Two Tree and Manyamba (Makonde) as
well as Blackmovale and Impalavale (Kadoma), the demarcation of plots failed
to consider the irrigation infrastructure already on the farms to the extent
that it is difficult to operate the system unless re-planning is done.
3.3.3 The production of specialised crops such as flowers has also been
severely affected particularly in Zvimba, since most of the farms where such
activities were taking place were demarcated for A1. Most farmers in A1 lack
the knowledge and wherewithal to undertake such activities. The following
farms in Zvimba are a case in point: Mimosa (A1), Mpandaguta (A1), Muhacha
(A1) and Nicortina (A1).
3.5 Decongestion
3.4.1 Statistics from the data collection teams and comments from
interviewees suggest that decongestion had not significantly taken place in
the communal lands. This situation had been worsened by some land reform
beneficiaries who maintained duel homes i.e. both in the communal areas and
in the new resettlement schemes owing to the uncertainty of tenure referred
to earlier.
Productivity
3.5.1 While some people especially in Makonde realised good harvests,
production was generally constrained by a number of factors which included:
lack of adequate financial resources, lack of farming implements for
tillage, lack of adequate inputs and drought.
3.6 Provision of Agricultural Inputs
3.6.1 A sizeable number of settlers benefited from the Government input
support programme. However, many expressed concern with the way the inputs
scheme was being administered by the GMB. They complained that inputs such
as fertiliser and seed often came late or were inadequate. Some pointed out
that some of the agricultural inputs were often given to undeserving persons
who sometimes ended up selling them. There was therefore need for the GMB to
institute follow up mechanisms to ensure the inputs are used properly.
3.6.2 The DDF was failing to cope with the demand for tillage and was
being accused of favouring influential people at the expense of the ordinary
people.
3.6.3 Most farmers in both A1 and A2 models had very little resources
for farming activities and largely depended on Government support. The lack
of resources such as draught power and adequate farming inputs was a major
constraint on crop production.
3.7 Role Played by Agro-business
3.7.1 Many settlers were of the view that some agro-businesses operating
in the province were playing an important role in providing input support
services. However, some expressed concern at the operations of certain
companies which they alleged were charging exorbitant prices for the inputs
they supplied.
3.8 Tenure
3.8.1 The uncertainty in the security of tenure resulted in
beneficiaries being unable to secure loans for investment and also being
hesitant to invest their own money on the plots they would have been
allocated resulting in reduced production.
3.9 The Environment
3.9.1 Gold panning was becoming a major issue, especially in Kadoma
district as it competes for land with agricultural production. The
precedence of the Mines and Minerals Act over other land use legislation was
questioned. There was thus need to make it compatible with legislation
governing land use practices.
3.10 Infrastructure on the Farms
3.10.1 Instances of conflicts regarding the use of tobacco barns and
other infrastructure such as irrigation facilities were observed e.g. at
Maine farm (Chegutu), Chinomwa (Zvimba), Two Tree farms (Makonde) and
others.
3.10.2 Infrastructure on most farms, such as the farmhouses, barns,
sheds etc. were in a state of neglect or had been vandalised. Many settled
farms had irrigation infrastructure but very few were functional. The
infrastructure needed to be rehabilitated.
3.10.3 There was shortage of social infrastructure i.e. schools,
clinics, roads, water supply points etc.
3.11Governance
3.11.1 The issue of governance in resettled areas was of major
concern. Many farmers expressed dissatisfaction with the role of the
committee of 7 that was responsible for local administration in A1 farms as
these, especially the chairmen often abused their powers. There was thus
need to clarify the terms of reference for these committees. In A2 farms it
was mostly the Rural District Councils, which were responsible for local
governance, however, settlers still had difficulties in the areas of dispute
settlement, and security.
3.12 Former Farm Workers
3.12.1 Generally, former farm workers did not benefit from the land
reform programme in the province, for various reasons. However, at Katawa
(Zvimba), some former farm workers have been allocated 2 hectare plots.
3.13 Issues Peculiar to the Gwebi/Hunyani Area.
3.13.1 Virtually all the officials, in both Zvimba district and at
Provincial level highlighted the need to have the problem of illegal
settlements in the Gwebi/Hunyani are resolved.
v) MASVINGO
1. Background
Masvingo province which has a surface area of 56 566 square kilometres
has a population of 1 318 705 (2002 Census) and is made up of the following
administrative districts: Bikita, Chiredzi, Chivi, Masvingo, Mwenezi and
Zaka. Of the seven districts, farms in Bikita are part of the Save Valley
Conservancy and the Chiredzi River Conservancy and were thus delisted,
whilst Chivi and Zaka did not have any commercial farms.
The Province falls within Natural Regions III, IV and V.
2. Statistical Data
3. 2.1 A total of 649 farms measuring 2 622 147 hectares were gazetted
for compulsory acquisition in Masvingo Province. One hundred and ninety-six
(196) of these were delisted for various reasons including indigenous
ownership, church ownership, protection under the BUPA, designation as
conservancies, plantation farms (sugar estates), and stateland. A total of
23 772 households were resettled under the various resettlement models
during the Fast Track on 1 439 912 hectares.
4. The following tables give a statistical overview of the land
acquisition profile and settler emplacement in Masvingo province as at the
end of July 2003.
Table 1. Number of farms in the Province
Notes:
The following reasons were given by the District Teams for the
variations in the statistics:
(1) Chiredzi District: Variance of 9 farms because 9 farms belonging to
Masvingo district were captured in Chiredzi district records.
(2) Masvingo district: Variances of 5 farms because farms were recorded
under Chiredzi district.
(3) Mwenezi District: Variances of 4 farms because 2 were recorded twice
whilst 1 farm was listed under three different names.
All the farms in Bikita w3were delisted, as they are part of the Save
Valley Conservancy and the Chiredzi River Conservancy. According to policy
conservancies were not supposed to be acquired for resettlement.
Table 2. Land Al__cpLocation Patterns and Take up Rates
Notes:
Statistics could not be established by the District Teams and in the
case of Mwenezi District Model A2 settler emplacement, the gender breakdown
of beneficiaries could not be obtained. Please note that Bikita is not
included on the table because all its farms were delisted whilst Chivi and
Zaka are excluded because they do not have any commercial farms. People from
these districts were accommodated in other districts during the Fast Track
resettlement exercise.
Table 3. Land Distribution by Gender
Notes:
The Mwenezi district team could not establish the gender breakdown for
the Model A2 beneficiaries. However, available statistics from other
districts depict that a very low percentage of females were allocated land
under the Fast Track land reform programme. The table does not capture the
war veterans who were resettled under the programme.
Table 4. Unofficially Settled Farms by District
Notes:
Does not capture households informally settled on Chidza and Chevden
farms which could not be established
Nuanetsi Ranch
Discussion with the Provincial Administrator and Chief Lands Officer at
the time of report writing indicated that there has been no official
settlement yet on Nuanetsi Ranch. They noted that the following plans have
been made to address the issue.
l 150 000 ha had been made available by DTZ for official resettlement of
people.
l 54 000 ha of the offered land have been planned for A1 plots and A2
subdivisions as follows:
A2 - 20 beneficiaries for livestock farming
100 beneficiaries for irrigation
A1 -= 6 500 households for irrigation on plots between 0,5 and 10 ha in
extent
*10 000 - 14 000 ha for maize irrigation scheme by ARDA
l Ministry of Lands Agriculture and Rural Resettlement were currently
conducting head count to facilitate al__cpLocations once pegging had been
concluded.
Table 5. Land Ownership Profile after Fast Track
Notes:
l Excludes areas of Chiredzi Research Station, Mbizi Quarantine Station
and Nandi (ARDA) farm in Chiredzi district whose exact sizes could not be
obtained.
l Does not include areas of Makoholi Research Station in Masvingo
district
l Does not incorporate areas of Gokomere, Muzero and Morgenster Missions
in Masvingo district as well as areas of Mbungo and Mukaro farms in Gutu
district whose sizes could not be ascertained.
l 3. Emerging Issues.
l 3.1 Reactions to the Fast Track.
l Stakeholders who included the Governor, Government Officials,
traditional leaders, war veterans and new settlers were generally satisfied
with the overall land redistribution exercise. They acknowledged that
Government had managed to empower the majority of the people in Zimbabwe
through land al__cpLocation. There was however consensus among those interviewed
that more land was required for resettlement especially from districts like
Bikita, Chivi and Zaka which did not have any commercial farms.
Although quite a substantial number of people had been resettled from
the communal areas, interviewed chiefs indicated that potential settlers on
their lists had not been exhausted. Officials explained this development
indicating that there was competition for resettlement between the communal
people and people from the urban areas.
3.2 Institutional Arrangements
3.2.1 Implementation of the programme was spearheaded by the PLIC
chaired by the Governor and the District Land Identification Committees
(DLICs) chaired by District Administrators (DAs). However, officials noted
that departures were later observed in Chiredzi where there was interference
from war veterans and politicians; Masvingo district where war veterans
politicians and traditional leaders had an overriding influence over the
operations of the Committees; and Mwenezi, where war veterans and
politicians interfered with Committee business.
This phenomenon led to unprocedural land al__cpLocations.
Gutu district is an exception in the Province in this regards as the
composition of the DLIC followed the laid down policy. Land identification
and al__cpLocation were therefore done procedurally.
3.2.2 Laid down procedures for land identification were generally
followed at the inception of the programme. Departures were however noted
where instructions to acquire conservancy farms such as Angus, Chigwete,
Mkwazi and Mukazi in Bikita district were issued by the Provincial
authorities. After these farms were settled, outbreaks of foot-and-mouth
disease were reported as settlers' cattle came into contact with Save Valley
Conservancy buffalo. Agro-industrial properties and farms protected by
country to country agreements were also observed to have been gazetted for
compulsory acquisition.
3.2.3 Impact of Fast Track on Decongestion in Communal Areas
Stakeholders who were interviewed stated that the impact of the
resettlement programme on decongestion in the communal areas was
minimal. This was attributed to the following reasons among others:
l Land shortage in the Province because two of Masvingo's districts do
not have any commercial farms.
l Failure by the new settlers to surrender their communal homes and land
holdings.
l The accommodation of people from the urban areas at the expense of
communal people.
Gutu district was however an exception as all the stakeholders had come
together in the A1 settler selection process. Settlers had therefore been
selected according to the ward system thereby achieving a measure of
decongestion. It was still necessary though to undertake a communal land
re-organisation exercise for the full impact of the resettlement exercise to
be felt.
3.4 Plot Take up Rate
The average plot take up rates for Model A1 and A2 were 95% and 79%
respectively. Take up constraints were evident in Chiredzi district where A2
beneficiaries experienced difficulties with former cane growers who had
provisional orders from the courts barring the new farmers from taking up
their land. The same difficulties were encountered over land under wildlife
management.
3.5. Production
3.5.1. Crop Production
Generally, the Fast Track had a great impact on cropping in the
Province. Resettled farmers responded positively to the need to grow a whole
range of crops including high value cash crops such as cotton, paprika and
wheat over and above the staple crop maize. Reasonable yields were realised
despite the drought conditions. Settlers indicated that the advent of
Cyclone Japhet benefited the late planted crops such as cotton and
sorghum.
Significant yields were realised by resettled farmers who averaged 0,6
to 0,7 tonnes/hectare for sorghum and h0,8 to 1 tonne/hectare for cotton in
Chiredzi district. According to the Chief Agricultural Extension Officer,
yields can be increased through timely distribution of inputs in adequate
quantities.
3.5.2 Sugar Can Production
While many parts of the Province are suitable for cattle ranching, it
was noted that many properties which had been assigned to such ranching had
now been demarcated into plots for cropping. This called for replanning and
reorganisation of the properties in question such that they would revert to
the original land use patterns.
3,6 Agricultural Inputs and Support Services
Most beneficiaries acknowledged receiving inputs through the Grain
Marketing Board scheme but expressed concern at the late delivery of these
inputs in inadequate quantities. Some settlers therefore wanted to
participate in loan financing schemes for the procurement of inputs (seed,
fertiliser and agro-chemicals) if these were made available. These settlers
also wanted to use such schemes to procure tractors and other agricultural
equipment.
The new farmers particularly A2 farmers also bemoaned the lack of
security of tenure under the Fast Track programme as this limited their
capacity to borrow. They implored Government to urgently address this
issue.
Most settlers were not satisfied with the delivery of services by AREX
extension staff and called for the introduction of skills development
programmes for interested resettled farmers.
3.7 Infrastructural Support
Most of the stakeholders who were interviewed were of the opinion that
infrastructural requirements such as schools, clinics, boreholes and access
roads were hardly adequate. However, satellite schools and clinics had been
established in all the districts using homesteads in the former commercial
farms. The shortage of this infrastructure was cited as one of the reasons
for the duel ownership of land in the communal areas and resettlement
farms. Schools and clinics were available in communal areas and this
therefore encouraged settled farmers to maintain their communal homes.
The irrigation rehabilitation programme was popular among beneficiaries
who had irrigation infrastructure on their al__cpLocations. Concerns were
however raised over the apparent delays in the processing of applications
and hence there was a call for the decentralisation of the disbursement of
funds under the programme.
3.8 Governance
Committees of Seven were observed to be evident on resettled farms even
though they were mostly ineffective. It was noted that their authority had
been undermined by influential individuals calling themselves 'Base
Commanders'. This was particularly evident in Gutu and Masvingo
districts. This feature had the negative effect of unprocedural land
al__cpLocations particularly in Masvingo district, and the occupation of
homesteads on A1 farms by individuals instead of these being used for
community purposes such as satellite schools and clinics. Poaching,
stock-theft, indiscriminate tree cutting and vandalism had also been
observed because of the absence of a governing authority on resettled
farms.
There were boundary disputes in the districts of Chiredzi, Bikita and
Gutu involving some chiefs in these districts.
The institutional presence of Rural District Councils (RDCs) was not
very evident in resettled areas.
3.9 Environmental Issues
3.9.1 Illegal Gold Panning
Illegal Gold panning was observed along Runde River on the Development
Trust of Zimbabwe (DTZ) Nuanetsi Ranch. It was believed that approximately 1
400 people who were part of the 4 193 families that are settled unofficially
on this property are responsible for this activity.
3.10 Former Farm Workers
Some 128 former farm workers were resettled. Others are assumed to have
taken up employment under the newly resettled A2 farmers particularly in the
Chiredzi can growing area. It was also believed that the rest may have gone
back to their original homes or taken up alternative employment
elsewhere.
4. Issues Peculiar to The Province
4.1 Conservancies
Masvingo province had large tracts of land under wildlife management on
designated conservancies such as Save Valley Conservancy, Chiredzi River
Ranch Conservance and parts of Bubiyana Conservancy covering Bikita,
Chiredzi and Mwenezi districts. These conservancies were the preserve of the
whites. Many in the province bemoaned the policy vacuum in this sector.
4.2 Gonarezhou National Park/Ndali Boundary Dispute
National Parks and Wildlife Management Authority and the Ndali people in
Chiredzi district are engaged in a long-standing boundary dispute. An area
of about 16 000 hectares was occupied by the Ndali people. The land in
question formed part of the above cited National Park. The matter required
early resolution (see Chapter 7 above).

Original_Search_Title: Why is Poverty On the Rise?
Headline: Uganda: Why is Poverty On the Rise?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Last week, there were reports that poverty levels in Uganda, which had
declined between 1992 and 2000, had started rising again.
Uganda's Finance Ministry and the World Bank as well as the public are
concerned about the reports which show that, in spite of the growth in the
national economy at an average of 5 per cent over the past three years,
poverty levels were rising.
The Ministry of Finance, as a result, is trying to investigate the
possible causes for this.
In 2000, only seven million people were below the poverty line,
depending on one meal a day, but at present they have risen to nine
million.
Rising figures of poverty should be taken seriously as they also show
the widening gap between the haves and have-nots and question the
government's poverty eradication programmes.
The government has to explain what has happened to the funds that have
been channelled through the Poverty Eradication Action Plan (PEAP),
otherwise the public will not be blamed for thinking the al__cpLocations were
hot air.
However, the reasons for the rise in poverty rates should not be
strange, especially to the Ministry of Finance, which has over the years
ignored the plight of the people in villages and those leaving
colleges.
The government should know that you cannot fight poverty by ignoring
qualified people leaving colleges.
For example, although bureaucrats in the Ministry of Finance say that
agriculture is the backbone of the economy when it comes to allocating
budget funds, it is not given that priority. It is the Ministries of
Defence, Education, Health and Finance that take the lion's share.
It is not strange, then, that poverty started rising during when the
government's focus shifted from projects aimed at lifting the standards of
the people to those favouring the middle class.
Meanwhile, the people of Gulu sub region, where a rebellion has been
going on for the past 17 years, have lost their means of production. They
depend on handouts, although Gulu is a rich agricultural area that used to
supply Kampala with rice and sorghum.
The government, instead of wasting resources on electioneering now and
then, should spend on projects that directly impact on the poor.

Original_Search_Title: Give Us Tax Concessions - GBL
Headline: Ghana: Give Us Tax Concessions - GBL
Body_Text:
The Managing Director of the Ghana Breweries Limited (GBL), Mr. Segun
Adebanji has appealed to the government to give some tax concessions to beer
manufacturing companies to promote a solid local business for the
product.
Speaking with a section of the media in Tamale last week, he explained
that before the company could export its products it must first build a
solid local base.
Mr. Adebanji said it had come to the notice of the company that some
people were using the GBL's Bluna soft drink bottles in bottling their
products whose colour look like the original Bluna and appealed to the
public to help GBL to apprehend such culprits.
He said GBL spent Â¢three billion in purchasing 900 tonnes of locally
produced maize for beer production. He said this represents 25 percent of
the total raw materials requirement of the company.
Mr. Adebanji, who was explaining the company's policies, strategies and
expectations, said GBL's raw materials requirement was expected to increase
next year, following an increase in beer consumption pattern in the
country.
He said the Company's machinery had now been structured to use maize for
the production, adding that GBL was exploring the possibility of using
sorghum as an additional raw material for beer brewing.
"This is good news for maize farmers since the problem of marketing of
their produce would then be addressed", he said, adding, "Our efforts should
therefore encourage them to increase their production levels to keep the
company in business."
Mr. Adebanji urged the government to reduce the excise duty on beer to
make the industry to grow, saying, "right now my company pays 50 percent as
excise duty while those who import hard drinks into the country pay only
five per cent as excise duty".
"There is no equity in the distribution of tax on beer and this is
affecting our finances and expansion of our business," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Gains, But Watch Complacency
Headline: Ghana: Economic Gains, But Watch Complacency
Body_Text:
THE MID-YEAR review of the 2003 budget and the economic performance of the NPP administration, as expected, has given additional momentum to the debate on the direction of the economy.
While the government and, largely, the business community believe that the economy is on course, given the macro-economic indications, others, especially the opposition NDC, see things differently. Although they acknowledge the fact that there is considerable improvement in the macro sector, they assert that this does not translate evidently in the fortunes of the generality of the citizenry. This makes the economy fragile still, in spite of the gains made.
We believe that both positions are a true reflection of the economic situation. It is refreshing to note, however, that for the first time, there is almost unanimous acceptance of the fact that the economy has gained some measure of stability. The politicians on both sides of the divide, the economic experts and members of civil society groups are all agreed that the cedi has stabilised fairly against the currencies of our major trading and development partners. Added to this is what has been described in financial circles as the remarkable improvement in the country's foreign exchange position.
No less a person than Dr. Joe Abbey, the Executive Secretary of the Centre for Policy Analysis CEPA), the economic think-tank, has been quoted as hailing the three months foreign exchange reserve accumulated by the government as a landmark achievement in the history of the management of the country's economy.
He said the situation puts the country in a strong position to hold its own against any unforeseen development on the international commodity market, especially with regard to oil and cocoa.
Coming from a person like Dr. Abbey, a veteran economic analyst, who was himself at the helm of the national economy both as a civil servant and Commissioner (Minister) in times past and whose present organisation (CEPA) has not reserved words in its criticism of government's policies, we cannot help deeming his observation, genuine and sincere.
In spite of whatever gains have been made however, we think this is not the time for complacency. The government should be the first to acknowledge that the economy is still fragile - still far from the NPP's touted objective of making it so buoyant, it would reflect in the "pockets" of even the ordinary person.
Again, both government and the opposition agree that the nation needs to achieve and sustain an economic growth rate of between 7 and 8% to reach the level of desired buoyant and middle income status. The present under 5% growth rate that has been our bane over the past several years does not engender much hope and confidence in the investment community.
Critics of the government point to the increasing numbers of our youth still hawking petty wares in the streets and the fact that agriculture, which is the backbone of our economic advancement, is still unable to rise above its present level of subsistence. We believe that so long as agriculture remains rain-fed and largely unscientific, so long would our economic growth only crawl, if not stagnate, and our young people remain in the streets.
Agriculture holds the potential and, indeed, the key to a breakthrough in the country's economic fortunes if we can place the right emphasis on it by making it attractive enough to draw productive young persons into embracing it as a career. Whichever way one looks at it, it is untenable and unacceptable to let these young boys and girls waste their youthfulness in the streets while the land lies fallow and we keep importing even our staple food.
The breweries have reportedly started using maize in the production of beer. Last year, Ghana Breweries Limited alone is said to have purchased 900 tonnes of locally produced maize, representing 25% of its total raw materials requirements. This may be good news for our farmers because it guarantees them ready market and income. But it could be counter productive if not sustained and the breweries have to turn to the import market.
What about developing a scheme geared solely towards attracting the youth into maize and sorghum production - with the assistance of the breweries to meet their needs in a sustainable way?
We are aware of the Sorghum project, which the breweries initiated a couple of years ago. But we can not be sure of its future if the aging farmers are the ones to be relied on to produce.
Let the government summon the will and the resources to give agriculture its rightful place in the scheme of things now and it will make the required impact on the economy even before the end of a possible second term of Mr. Kufuor's administration.

Original_Search_Title: Allow Foreign Investors in, Raila Urges Kenyans
Headline: Kenya: Allow Foreign Investors in, Raila Urges Kenyans
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans should stop opposing foreign investments, Roads and Housing
Minister Raila Odinga said yesterday.
Raila said Kenyans had adopted a culture of blocking any development in
their areas, particularly the construction of industries.
He said that there were cases where foreign investors had suffered heavy
financial loses after the locals opposed the establishment of certain
projects.
He cited the Sondu Miriu Power Project in Nyanza Province and the
titanium mining in Kilifi as some of the projects that had stalled due to
resistance from the local people.
"Investors can go to Uganda, Tanzania, Sudan, Russia, the United States
of America and all other countries because doors remain wide open to them,"
Raila said in Kakamega. "But we have to attract them by giving them total
support when they come in the country."
The minister said that Kenyans should be ready to sacrifice some of
their resources, including the environment, if development is to be
realised.
He stated that there was no place where a factory can be constructed
without having some effects on the environment.
Raila said the government was aggressively wooing investors for the
purpose of creating jobs.
Meanwhile, Raila revealed that a new variety of sorghum would be
introduced in Nyanza as raw material for the production of spirits and fuel
at the Kisumu molasses plant.
He said that production of the sweet sorghum would be concentrated in
Siaya, Bondo and parts of South Nyanza districts.
The Minister said that sweet sorghum farming would also be extended to
Budalangi in Busia District, adding that sugarcane produced in Nyanza would
not sustain the operations of the molasses plant.
Addressing Siaya town residents on Friday evening, the minister said
that the first phase of the Kisumu molasses plant would be operational in
the next six months, while phases two and three would be ready in two
years.

Original_Search_Title: Saudi Arabia Donates 100 Tonnes Assorted Food
Headline: Zambia: Saudi Arabia Donates 100 Tonnes Assorted Food
Body_Text:
THE Office of the Vice-President has received 200 tonnes of sorghum, 100
tonnes of assorted food and 210 cartons of medicine from the Saudi Arabian
government.
Deputy Minister in the Office of the Vice-President Rose Banda commended
the Saudi Arabia for its assistance in ensuring food security in
Zambia.
Ms Banda said the donation would improve the food insecurity experienced
by hunger- stricken communities, especially in the southern part of the
country, due to prolonged drought.
Ms Banda said the Government was still concerned about the hunger
situation that continued to hit some parts of Zambia.
"The hunger situation in the country has continued to persist in some
parts of the country and the Government is making frantic efforts to address
the situation. I wish to thank the government of Saudi Arabia for rendering
assistance," she said.
Ms Banda described the gesture by Saudi Arabia as a manifestation of the
warm relations between the two countries.
Saudi Arabian ambassador to Zambia Talat Radwan said his government
would continue to offer support in tackling the food shortages in
Zambia.
Mr Radwan expressed hope that most of the donated items would reach the
Southern Province where the food crisis was said to be most serious.
Mr Radwan also commended the effort by President Mwanawasa to restore
the country's economy to improved levels.
"It is in this regard that my government strives to enhance the
bilateral relations that are currently existing between the two countries
through such donations to help Zambia restore food security and tackle
poverty," he said.
-The United Nations World Food Programme has welcomed recent
contributions to its appeal for Southern Africa, but expressed concern that
its operations were still only 38 per cent funded.
WFP's appeal was for $311 million and the organisation still needed $193
million to meet the needs of 6.5 million people in the region who required
food aid through to June 30 next year.
This is according to WFP regional director Mike Sackett in a new release
yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agency Builds School for 1,700 Refugee Children in Southern Chad
Headline: Central African Republic: UN Agency Builds School for 1,700 Refugee Children in Southern Chad
Body_Text:
Bangui — Some 1,700 children from the Central African Republic (CAR) who had been learning under trees at a refugee camp in southern Chad will soon take their lessons in classrooms built by the Office of the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), an official told IRIN on Thursday.
"The classrooms will be ready for use in two weeks," Emile Segbor, the UNHCR representative for CAR and Chad, said.
He had returned to the CAR capital, Bangui, after visiting Camp Gore in southern Chad between 28 October and 2 November. The camp hosts 13,000 CAR refugees.
Segbor said workers recruited from among refugees were currently making desks and completing the school's roofing.
When complete, he said, the red brick-walled and zinc-roofed classrooms would accommodate 1,400 primary school pupils in the mornings and 300 secondary school students in the afternoons.
He said the primary classes would be taught by CAR refugee teachers and the secondary school classes by both CAR and Chadian teachers.
Segbor said the agency was planning to build a similar school at Camp Maro in southern Chad, which is hosting 15,000 CAR refugees.
Thousands of CAR nationals, mainly from the north, fled their country between October 2002 and March 2003 following fighting between government troops and rebels loyal to former army chief of staff Francois Bozize, who is now the CAR head of state. Bozize seized power on 15 March from President Ange-Felix Patasse.
At least 41,000 refugees are reported to be in camps in southern Chad. Apart from Gore and Maro, two towns near the CAR border, there are other smaller refugee camps in southern Chad.
Segbor said all the refugees were receiving food and medical aid, and that they had access to safe drinking water. He added that the refugees were also engaged in farming and that the UN Food and Agriculture Organization had provided them with bean and sorghum seeds as well as farming tools. The refugees had opened a market in the camp to sell their produce, he said.
Despite the CAR transitional government's efforts to repatriate all its refugees, those in southern Chad are hesitant to return due to insecurity in their home areas in the north.
A CAR mission headed by Social Affairs Minister Lea Doumta toured the camps in September to convince the refugees to return home.
"For the time being, repatriation is not one of our immediate plans," Sebgor said.

Original_Search_Title: Customs Gives 2-Wk Deadline On Contraband
Headline: Nigeria: Customs Gives 2-Wk Deadline On Contraband
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) has given supermarkets and shop owners nationwide a two-week deadline to dispose of contraband or risk seizure. The Federal Government had in August, last year, banned the importation of some products, food items and other consumables into the country.
"We are inundated with reports of flagrant display and sale of banned items in supermarkets, shops and market places across the country," the NCS said in a statement in Abuja yesterday.
In the statement, Comptroller-General of the NCS, Alhaji Ahmed Mustapha said the service would on expiration of the ultimatum raid such shops nationwide. Mustapha said the enforcement team of the service would confiscate the banned items "whereever found" and also prosecute the offenders.
He directed Customs Area Controllers (CACs) to use the local media in their areas to educate and warn members of the public on the impeding action. The Customs boss also advised all formations nationwide to ensure strict compliance with the directive.
Among items outlawed were sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum, vegetable oil in bulk, mosquito repellent coils, retreaded or used tyres, gaming machines and second-hand clothing. Others are used refridgerators and air-conditioning units, used compressors, bagged cement, vehicles of any kind through the land borders, vehicles above eight years from date of manufacture, textiles of any kind through the land borders, printed fabric (Ankara) and frozen poultry.
Also banned are cassava, cassava products, containerised goods through the land borders, fruit juice in retail packs, table water (sparkling/non-sparkling), toothpicks, spaghetti, noodles, biscuits of any type and drugs and other regulated products through the land borders. Government had also banned importation of sugar, all kinds of canned and bottled beer, toilet rolls, confectioneries, exercise books and envelopes, Barytes and Bentonites with effect from July 1, 2003.

Original_Search_Title: Customs Begins Seizure of Contraband Nationwide
Headline: Nigeria: Customs Begins Seizure of Contraband Nationwide
Body_Text:
Lagos — ALL supermarkets and shop owners in the country who still stock and sell
contraband goods have been given two weeks, beginning yesterday, to dispose
of the products or risk prosecution.
Government had in August last year banned the importation of some
products, food items and other consumables into the country.
But, Comptroller-General of Nigeria Customs Service (NCS), Alhaji Ahmed
Mustapha, in a statement yesterday and made available to the News Agency of
Nigeria (NAN) in Abuja, warned that at the expiry of the deadline, such
goods will be seized.
Some of the items outlawed are used fridges and air conditioning units,
used compressors, used vehicles and those above eight years from date of
manufacture, bagged cement and others.
"We are inundated with reports of flagrant display and sale of banned
items in supermarkets, shops and market places across the country, Alhaji
Mustapha said.
Mustapha said the enforcement team of the service would confiscate the
banned items "whereever found" at the termination of the deadline and also
prosecute the offenders.
He directed the Customs Area Controllers to use the media in their
locality to educate and warn members of the public on the impending
action.
The Customs boss also advised all formations nationwide to ensure strict
compliance with the directive.
The NCS had recently raided warehouses in some popular markets in Lagos
and Kano and impounded contraband textiles and other items valued at
millions of naira.
Among items banned were sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum, vegetable
oil in bulk, mosquito repellant coils, retreaded or used tyre, gaming
machines and second-hand clothes.
Others are textiles of any kind through the land borders, printed fabric
(ankara) and frozen poultry.
Also banned were cassava, cassava products, containerised goods through
the land borders, fruit juice in retail packs, table water
(sparkling/non-sparkling), toothpicks, spaghetti, noodles, biscuits of any
type and drugs and other regulated products through the land borders.
Government also banned the importation of sugar, all kinds of canned and
bottled beer, toilet rolls, confectioneries, exercise books and envelopes,
Barytes and Bentonites with effect from July 1, 2003.
The ban government had said, was intended to boost local production,
enhance the performance of the national economy and safeguard the health of
the citizenry.

Original_Search_Title: Kony Nips Epuripur in the Bud
Headline: Uganda: Kony Nips Epuripur in the Bud
Body_Text:
Kampala — Waste : Farmers are abandoning ripening crops in the gardens and fleeing for dear life
The presence of Kony's LRA rebels in northeastern Uganda may cause huge economic loss to the Teso economy.
As the Iteso grapple with the loss of lives, the farmers are even more worried about the looming reality of the loss of their livelihoods.
the growing of epuripur, the sorghum used in the production of Eagle beer is likely to fail as a result of the rebels' invason of the region.
The sorghum, introduced in 2002, had started flourishing and the farmers in Teso had started reaping handsome dividends from it.
However, LRA's intensified attacks in Teso since June might nip the harvest in the bud. Kony's attacks compel the farmers to abandon their farms and homes for dear life.
The majority of the farmers from especially the affected sub-counties of Katine, Arapai and some parts of Gweri in Soroti district and from Katakwi and Kaberamaido have been forced out of their villages into protected camps. Some farmers have actually fled the region and are roaming the streets of Kampala in search of jobs.
The massive exodus of farmers at the time of harvesting the first season and planting the second one of epuripur, which started in August has greatly affected the progress of the crop. Currently, in Soroti alone, the number of internally displaced people (IDPs) stands at 136,112. In the rural areas they are more than 24,000.
The National Agricultural Advisory Service coordinator in Soroti, Charles Aben says, "It's a very good programme, well integrated and has market linkages".
He says in Soroti alone, the Epuripur fetched
sh75m from 2000 acres during the second season, last year. He says, early this year, during the first planting, the district raised sh120m from another 2000 acres.
"If the insecurity had not interfered with the harvesting of the crop, especially in Katine, Arapai and some parts of Gweri sub-counties, we expected to get more," Aben explains. in those sub-counties, the Epuripur was not harvested.
He says many farmers, particularly in Soroti had picked interest in the crop because it is easy to grow.
To minimise losses, the Nile Breweries officials have considered decentralising the growing of the Epuripur, which, of course, means an economic slap in the face of the Iteso farmers.
Ian Mackintosh, technical director for Nile Breweries says 50% of what his company used for the production of Eagle beer, was planted in Teso and only 20% of the expected yields was achieved.
Teso region is comprised of four districts, namely, Kumi, Soroti, Katakwi and Kaberamaido.
"Most of the crop was lost in Katakwi and Kaberamaido districts.
We also lost part of the crop to farmers in the affected sub-counties of Soroti districts. The loss was mainly due to the current insecurity in the region," Mackintosh explains.
"We are however, not pulling out of Teso completely. We shall continue to support the farmers especially in areas which are still secure," he says. He says if the security situation improves, the company will concentrate in Teso as the main production region for the sorghum.
He adds that the Epuripur was to offer an alternative source of income to the Iteso farmers who had for so long relied on cattle. He says his company needs 3,500 tonnes of the Epuripur every year.
there are countries like Ethiopia, Zambia and Tanzania which have expressed interest to manufacture Eagle beer. He says there is an opportunity for these counties to import the crop from Uganda.
Nile Breweries has a guarantee agreement with the farmers to ensure that if they produce high quality seeds which it would buy at sh300 a kilogramme.
The company sells Epuripur seeds at sh200 a kilogramme and also gives credit facilities to farmers who cannot afford paying cash.
In the open market, the Epuripur seeds are sold at sh8,100 per kg which is much more expensive.

Original_Search_Title: UN Food Agency Distributes Canadian-Donated Seeds to Needy Families
Headline: Ethiopia: UN Food Agency Distributes Canadian-Donated Seeds to Needy Families
Body_Text:
Some 70,000 families in two drought-stricken Ethiopian provinces are receiving food crop seeds that can be planted late in the delayed rainy season in the hope that they will not need emergency food aid, the United Nations Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO) said today.
"Thanks to generous funding from Canada, some 350,500 drought-affected people in the regions of Amhara and Tigray are being given seeds of late-planting pulses, such as chickpeas, lentils and vetch," FAO said in a release. "Late-planting carrot, cabbage, tomato, onion, beetroot and spinach seeds are also being distributed to selected farmers, women and youth groups."
Prolonged drought and poor and delayed rains had led to the widespread loss of high-yielding maize and sorghum, the FAO Emergency Coordination Unit in Ethiopia said.
The distribution of food crop seeds would help to improve household nutrition and give the families both a food safety net and a source of income, thereby reducing their dependency on emergency food aid, FAO said.
Ethiopia already has 13.2 million people needing urgent food aid, "due mainly to structural causes, poverty and recurrent natural disasters," it said.
In return for the agricultural help, the Ethiopian Government requires families to do work that will benefit their communities, such as repairing water management projects, FAO said. Occasional but intense floods have destroyed some of the canals, small dams and diversion weirs set up as part of such projects in both provinces.
The repairs, however, were becoming too complex for local farmers and they would have to be trained, it said.
"There is an important educational component to this project," FAO said, "Farmers will be trained in modern crop production techniques, seed selection and water management procedures. By assisting today's food-insecure households, we may be producing the self-sufficient, self-reliant households of tomorrow."

Original_Search_Title: Chinese Firm Set to Start Clearing Land for Irrigation
Headline: Zimbabwe: Chinese Firm Set to Start Clearing Land for Irrigation
Body_Text:
Harare — CHINA International Water and Electric Corporation (CIWEC), the company contracted by the Government to clear 150 000 hectares of land for irrigation at Nuanetsi Range in Masvingo, has moved in some of its equipment to start clearing the land.
The Chinese company is now expected to start clearing the land any time soon after the Government supplied it with fuel for the bulldozers.
This followed the company's appeal to the Government for diesel since it did not have sufficient supplies.
Masvingo Governor Cde Josaya Hungwe confirmed that CIWEC was now expected to start clearing the land after finally moving the equipment on to the site.
"They have brought their equipment to the site now and they can start clearing the land any time from now.
"We delivered fuel to them through the Agricultural and Rural Development Authority (ARDA) after they indicated that they had no diesel to start the work," said Cde Hungwe.
"We are already putting preparations in place by looking for unemployed youths to cut the huge trees that would be felled during the clearing exercise."
Cde Hungwe said CIWEC would begin by clearing 28 000 hectares in the Mupapa and Mukume areas.
The Central Mechanical and Equipment Department, he said, would clear 4 600 hectares in the next six months under phase one of the project.
ARDA, which was offered 20 tractors to do the ploughing once the land is cleared, would be expected to plant dry land crops such as sorghum and millet, while about 4 000 hectares would be put under irrigated maize with water coming from Muzhwi and Bindamombe Dams.
The project is expected to continue with an average of 20 000 hectares being cleared every six months depending on the availability of funds.
The Chinese company had promised to start work at Nuanetsi in September but was unable to do so after it failed to move its equipment on time because of lack of fuel.
But work is now expected to start after Government made available $5 billion for the project in the supplementary budget.

Original_Search_Title: UNHCR Briefing Notes: Distribution of Food to Sudanese Refugees Begins
Headline: Chad: UNHCR Briefing Notes: Distribution of Food to Sudanese Refugees Begins
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
We have begun distributing emergency food aid along Chad's eastern
frontier, where thousands of Sudanese refugees, mostly women and children,
are encamped in a dozen sites after fleeing the rebellion-torn Darfur region
in western Sudan. A UNHCR relief convoy offloaded supplies of sorghum and
oil on Monday at Toumtouma, 42 kms north of the border town of Adre, center
of our emergency operations. On Wednesday it proceeded to Kolkol, 25 kms
away.  This supplementary diet is for the most vulnerable group of refugees
numbering around 1,000 in the two sites. Chadian military escorts went with
the convoy.
We had earlier handed out plastic sheeting for shelter, blankets and
jerry cans to these refugees. Local refugee committees were told to bring
carts and donkeys to transport the food supplies from the World Food Program
to their encampments in the semi-arid border region. Each family group of
six received three sacks of sorghum and four jerrycans of oil.
The Red Cross, our main implementing partner, will handle general food
distribution. We are awaiting the deployment of the Red Cross staff in the
region where security is one of our major concerns. There are land mines
scattered in the area and there have been reports of cross-border
raids. However, further delays in the general food distribution would
prejudice the fragile health of the refugees and their peaceful coexistence
with the local population during this fasting month of Ramadan.
The first Sudanese refugees came into Chad in April following an
uprising by the Sudanese Liberation Movement (SLM) in Darfur state earlier
in the year. By July, the Chadian government said some 65,000 Sudanese had
fled into Chad.  On Sept. 4, the Khartoum government and SLM hammered out a
40-day cease-fire while peace talks were being conducted.
Early this month, UNHCR launched an appeal for $16.6 million dollars for
emergency assistance to the refugees. The amount would cover provision of
shelter, health services, water and sanitation facilities through the
yearend in a remote region where there is little basic infrastructure.

Original_Search_Title: Royal Bank Column - Sowing the Seeds for Success
Headline: Zimbabwe: Royal Bank Column - Sowing the Seeds for Success
Body_Text:
Highlights Turnover up 213% from $2 056 billion in prior comparable
period to $6,43 billion for the six months to August 31.
Gross profit increased 320% from $741 million in the prior period to $3
111 billion for the first half of the trading year.
Headline EPS rose 2 264% from $0,95 in the prior period to $22,45 for
the first six months of the trading year.
Inventories up 425% from $2 894 billion in the prior comparable period
to $15 208 billion for the six months to the end of August.
Nature of business
Seed Co is in the business of developing and marketing certified crop
seeds.
The majority of sales are in the form of its hybrid maize seed as well
as significant sales of wheat, soyabean, barley, sorghum and groundnut
seed.
The company is heavily invested in research and development from which
it develops its own seeds through various research stations.
The group holds a dominant position in the Zimbabwean market with an
approximate 85% market share.
They also have an Export Processing Zone (EPZ) processing and packaging
plant specifically for export.
The group has extensive operations throughout the region where it does a
significant proportion of its business. The group has operations in
Botswana, (established to drive the African expansion), Zambia, Malawi and
South Africa (in the form of a joint venture with Syngenta Seed Co).
Results commentary
The group produced an excellent set of results for its half year as
evidenced by the 2 464% increase in profit after tax from $142,9 million in
the prior comparable period to $3 665 billion for their first half,
significantly ahead of inflation for the period.
This coincided with an increase in operating margins from 36% in the
prior period to 48% for this period.
The rise in profit was largely driven by a huge increase in other
operating income up 1 772% from $346,5 million in the previous comparable
period to $6 485 billion derived mostly from net exchange gains of $5,9
billion, the sustainability of these earnings has to be questioned.
Cost of sales as a percentage of turnover 48% for the current set of
results represents an improvement on the prior comparable period where the
figure was at 64% an indication that the group is watching its costs.
However, a major concern was the sharp rise in overheads which grew by
411% from $862,8 million in the prior comparable period to $4 413
billion.
With local overheads rising 315% ahead of the rate of inflation, while
regional overheads were up 480% this management attributed to the
devaluation of the local currency and increased production in these
markets.
The percentage of turnover derived from the region declined as compared
to the prior period from 74% to 69%.
Local turnover was affected by 33% decline in local winter cereal sales
while the region experienced a 6% decline in real volumes due to last
season's stockout.
There was a notable increase in Return on Shareholders Equity (ROE) from
5% in the prior comparable period to 62% for the first six months to the end
of August, indicating a great deal of value created for shareholders.
This has been accompanied by an increase in Return on Assets (ROA) from
4% in the prior period to 10%, indicating an improvement of the use of their
assets.
Inventories increased 425% from $2 894 billion in the prior comparable
period to $15 208 billion for the first six months of their trading
year.
This indicates that the group is in a strong stock position for the
upcoming season, the added effect of this will be control future costs.
There has been a significant increase in bank borrowings from $1 445
billion in the prior period to $14,13 billion for the first six months to
August, most of which was for the purchase of seed for the upcoming
season.
Consequently, the current ratio is at a less than comfortable 1.04
compared to the prior period where the ratio was 1.68.
Operational review
The majority of sales in the first half came in the form of cereals in
the Zimbabwean market which represented approximately $2 billion of the
turnover while the remaining $4 billion emanated from Zambia and Malawi
mainly in maize seed.
There has been a notable improvement in margins regarding this season's
current prices.
In the previous season seed was bought for $100 000 a tonne and sold at
$170 000 a tonne a margin of 70% while this season they were purchasing at
$1 million a tonne and selling at $2,5 million a tonne, a margin of
150%.
In the region the group was buying at US$400 and selling at US$1
100.
The company had not released any seed until the new prices had been set,
unlike the previous year where the company sold 30% of their produce at
lower prices.
Local seed supply has been slow primarily due to late planting and a dry
spell experienced in January.
The group's regional operations all performed well save for the
Mozambique operations which however, saw its losses narrowing.
Outlook
Prospects for the upcoming season remain low with the group expecting
40% of its normal local deliveries.
However, overall deliveries should be 90% of those last year with the
slack taken up by the regional operations.
On the right is a chart showing the breakdown in estimated production by
region for the upcoming season.
An average of management's projected estimates
While Zimbabwe, as said earlier, is going to be severely affected, it
still represents the largest supplier to the group with an estimated 28 000
tonnes.
Management is expecting Zambia to supply between 18 000 and 20 000
tonnes for the upcoming season with Malawi expected to produce 7 000
tonnes.
South Africa and Mozambique should both supply 3 000 tonnes each.
As the company is unable to export any of its Zimbabwean supply, the
group is likely to sell the majority of its regional supply on the regional
markets, which represents 54% of total supply.
On the research and development front the Zimbabwean research team is
nearing the release of seven new maize hybrid seeds with improved disease
resistance and yield.
Also in the pipeline are new strains of wheat, soyabean and
groundnuts.
Regionally the new research site at Mpongwe in northern Zambia is now up
and running, and the research site south of Maputo is also making progress
in producing varieties suitable for the south eastern coastal belt from
Mozambique to Kenya.
Recommendation and valuation
We believe the stock to have excellent prospects for the medium to
long-term. Traditionally, the group transacts 80% of its business in the
second half of its trading year and management indicates this year should be
no different.
With a possible long overdue devaluation, the group may be poised for
additional windfall exchange rate gains.
The stock is currently trading on a rolling PE of 7.49x.
We have revised our forecasts upward to forecast $110 EPS for their full
year to the end of February, which gives the group a cheap forward PE of
2.9x.
We recommend the stock to be a strong buy for the medium to
long-term.
This article has been issued and prepared by Barnfords Securities (Pvt)
Ltd (Barnfords). The company is a financial consultant to the Royal Bank of
Zimbabwe Ltd (Royal).

Original_Search_Title: Agri-Input Shortages Causing Concern
Headline: Zimbabwe: Agri-Input Shortages Causing Concern
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — With preparations for the coming planting season already underway in
Zimbabwe, there are growing concerns over the critical shortage of
agricultural inputs.
According to preliminary projections by the Seed Security Network (SSN)
of the Southern African Development Community (SADC), Zimbabwe will face a
maize seed deficit of about 40,000 mt in the 2002/03 season. SSN noted that
current production was pegged at 20,000 mt, with the remainder expected to
be imported from South Africa, Malawi and Zambia.
The prospects for groundnut, sorghum and millet seeds were equally
bleak, and a deficit of 1,300 mt was forecast for sorghum. Millet seed from
commercial sources was expected to fall almost 80 percent short of the
estimated national demand and a 2,500 mt shortfall for sugar beans and
groundnuts was projected.
The Famine Early Warning Network (FEWS NET) noted that "even if the
rainfall situation turns out to be good", the critical shortage of inputs
would seriously affect Zimbabwe's 2003/04 agricultural season.
Access to seed and fertiliser by smallholder farmers was being hampered
not just by limited supplies, but also by the increased prices at which
these commodities were currently trading. The past twelve months saw the
price of hybrid maize seed rise from Zim $192/kg (about US 2 cents) to Zim
$1,428/kg (about US $1.80).
The escalating foreign currency shortage continued to limit the
availability of other critical agricultural inputs, the most important of
which were spares for the repair and maintenance of agricultural equipment,
fuel and fertilisers. Consequently, yields for the 2003/04 cropping season
would be below normal, even if climatic conditions turned out to be
favourable, FEWS NET said.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Tasks Private Sector to Emulate NB
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Tasks Private Sector to Emulate NB
Body_Text:
Lagos — President Olusegun Obasanjo has challenged the private sector to emulate
Nigerian Breweries Plc in contributing to the improvement of the economy by
attracting foreign investors.
Commissioning NB Plc N40 billion Brewery at Enugu, he said his
administration was still committed and undetered in its effort to ensure
massive foreign investment into the country.
He expressed surprise at the speed with which the project was completed
and described the NB Greenfield projects as a symbol of confidence and trust
which the world's largest brewery - Heineken of Netherlands has for
Nigeria.
Nigerian Breweries managing director and chief executive, Mr. Festus
Odimegwu commended the Enugu State government for its encouragement and
support which facilitated early completion of the project.
With the investment of N40 billion in its world class brewery plant,
Nigerian Breweries Plc and its parent body Heineken of Netherlands, have
brought a turning point to the economic life of Enugu State and its
inhabitants which hitherto was dependent on government.
According to him, the plant which was built with the state of the art
equipment has the capacity to produce 300 million hectolitres of beer and
could be further extended.
Odimegwu said the plant had already absorbed some bright young students
from technical schools across the the country and staff.
According to him, Ama brewery has provided 280 staff with permanent jobs
and 100 workers in various businesses engaged by Nigerian Breweries in the
fields of catering, clearing, fork lift truck maintenance among others.
According to him, the brewery takes interests in the surrounding area
and sees it as its moral duty to contribute towards improving the welfare of
those living in the neighbourhood.
According to him, the Ama brewery has an average production capacity in
the excess of 1.1 million crates of beer per week and uses only natural
ingredients such as water pumped from five wells constructed 200 metres
below the ground, malted barley and hops imported from Europe as well as
maize and sorghum produced by local farmers.
He said completion of the brewery will enable NB meet the demand by its
market and fully prepare for growth in future.
He said while the brewery's flagship products like Star, Gulder,
Heineken, Legend, Maltina and Amstel Malta were the pride of Nigerian
Breweries, the Ama brewery is certainly another for NB to be proud of.
He attributed the success stay of the new brewery to heavy investment of
Nigerian Breweries in the recruitment of young technical professionals
straight from technical schools with additional training.

Original_Search_Title: Bank Launches Multi-Million Dollar Project
Headline: Zimbabwe: Bank Launches Multi-Million Dollar Project
Body_Text:
Harare — RENAISSANCE Merchant Bank has launched a multi-million dollar
Nhimbe-Jakwara Wealth Regeneration Project, an outgrower sorghum scheme set
to benefit more than 1000 farmers from three provinces.
This project brings together players from the corporate world, the civic
society, the farming community and the financial services sector.
"The Nhimbe-Jakwara Wealth Regeneration Project aims to increase the
marketing options for red sorghum farming outputs for men and women in the
rural areas.
"In the past, the marketing for sorghum has been limited to one player,
but with the entry of one of the key players in the Nhimbe-Jakwara Wealth
Regeneration Project, TJ Commodities, the playing field has widened giving
farmers wider and more playing options," said Renaissance in a
statement.
TJ Commodities is a brokering firm that sources markets for red
sorghum.
Renaissance has availed a grant to TJ Commodities to embark on this
project.
The financial institution joins other firms such as the Zimbabwe
Industry Tobacco Auction Centre, Tanganda Tea Company, the Cotton Company of
Zimbabwe and Farmers' World which have provided support to farmers across
the country.
This comes at a time when farmers are finding it hard to procure inputs
due to the rising costs.
Zimbabwe's economy is agriculture-driven and the support by players from
the private sector is expected to help revive its sagging fortunes.
Renaissance has financed the purchase of sorghum seed and is also
doubling as the financial advisor for TJ Commodities.
At least 1 119 farmers covered under the project are expected to produce
about 630 tonnes of red sorghum next season.
There has been a decrease in the number of traditional suppliers of red
sorghum, thus, the new players will be expected to fill the gap.
Sorghum is a drought tolerant crop which makes it the best alternative
in areas that receive little rainfall or in the event of a drought.
"They (TJ Commodities) purchase the sorghum seed and transfer its
ownership to Care Zimbabwe. They also identify viable and lucrative markets
for communal farmers.
"But the beauty of their involvement is the fact that they pay for all
the produce collected from the farmers whether produce is sold or not," said
Renaissance.
TJ Commodities would also train the farmers on quality control and
production techniques.
Care Zimbabwe and TJ Commodities staff often undertook field visits to
assess the progress being made by farmers.
The sourcing of lucrative markets for the farmers would come as a relief
for the sorghum growers.
Most of the small-scale farmers do not get value for their produce
because they do not have the capacity to source the best markets for their
produce.
They have been vulnerable to unscrupulous middlemen who buy their
produce for a song then sell them at exorbitant prices.

Original_Search_Title: Loan Terms Relaxed
Headline: Zimbabwe: Loan Terms Relaxed
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Cabinet Taskforce on Inputs Supply and Distribution has directed the
Agricultural Development Bank of Zimbabwe, formerly AgriBank, to drop all
stringent conditions, such as collateral, when extending loans to farmers on
the $60 billion facility.
Stringent conditions imposed by the bank had made most farmers unable to
access any loans.
The chairman of the Taskforce on Inputs Supply and Distribution Cde
Ignatius Chombo said the Government had put in place measures to ensure that
the facility was not abused.
"We have set up a committee that is going to operate 24 hours monitoring
the situation in the provinces and districts so as to ensure that there is
no double dealing," said Cde Chombo.
He said the committee would compile a list of all those who would have
accessed loans from the bank, including the name of the farm and other
details to check on whether the same farmer could have received financial
assistance from other organisation for the same project.
"It's not going to be possible for people vakaita semashefu (high
ranking officials) to get loans from the bank because those can afford to
buy the inputs on their own. People are concerned that the little that we
have should be shared fairly," he said.
Cde Chombo said the new bank was supposed to complement and not
complicate the process of accessing funds to buy inputs.
He said his taskforce agreed that an offer letter from the Ministry of
Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement and a workable business plan were
the prerequisite for farmers to get loans from the bank.
Zimbabwe Farmers' Union vice president Mr Wilfanos Mashingaidze, who
also sits on the taskforce, yesterday commended the move taken by the
Government in removing obstacles that were hindering farmers from accessing
loans from the land bank.
"The issue of collateral should never be a barrier to farmers who are in
need of funds. The farmer's business plans should be enough to allow him to
get a loan," said Mr Mashingaidze.
The decision to scrap collateral and other stringent requirements was
reached during a meeting of the Cabinet Taskforce on Inputs Supply and
Distribution held in Harare last week at the Ministry of Local Government,
Public Works and National Housing offices.
Officials from the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural
Resettlement, Zimbabwe Farmers' Union, Zimbabwe Indigenous Commercial
Farmers Union, National Railways of Zimbabwe, the District Development Fund
and Agricultural Research and Extension attended the meeting, which was
chaired by the Ministry of Local Government, Public Works and National
Housing secretary Dr Vincent Hungwe.
It was noted during the meeting that bottlenecks still existed in the
operations of Agribank as farmers were expected to pay a 40 percent deposit
of the total amount they required.
Support was being restricted to certain type of farming activity,
especially in the Midlands where the bank is only supporting those in cotton
production.
The bank also required quotations from reputable companies only, of
which demand for inputs was unlikely to be satisfied by the companies, a
finance sub-committee noted.
Other obstacles noted by the sub-committee were that one was required to
be an account holder of the bank in order to benefit yet the opening of an
account usually takes long and does not take in to consideration the time
factor.
The bank was also refusing to accept recommendation letters and forms
signed by Agricultural Research and Extension officers.
The stringent requirements in accessing loans resulted in very few
farmers being able to acquire loans to buy inputs for the 2003/2004
season.
As a remedy, the taskforce directed that although the bank should
consider the farming activity in a particular region or province,
disbursement of funds should not necessarily be restricted to a particular
farming activity.
The taskforce said that the loans being applied for by farmers should be
disbursed in trunches based on AREX officers' reports on progress on the
ground.
The bank was also directed to give cash to farmers instead of
cheques.
Members of the taskforce recommended that priority of foreign currency
al__cpLocation should be given to local fertiliser companies, which need US$6,5
million in three installments for them to produce the required quantities
rather than importing the same quantities for US$14 million.
On the farm preparations for this season, the committee advised that the
fuel situation had not improved and this was hampering farm activities on
resettled farmers.
At least 4 976 tonnes of seed and 3 285 tonnes of fertilisers have so
far been distributed to all the country's provinces with Mashonaland West
getting the largest amount of 1 252 tonnes of seed and 810 tonnes of
fertiliser.
However, farmers face a serious shortage of inputs, particularly maize
seed after the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe failed to source in time the US$30
million ($24,7 billion) to the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural
Resettlement to buy inputs for the 2003/2004 agricultural season.
Only 32 000 tonnes, far short of a national requirement of 100 000
tonnes, are available at a time when the demand for maize seed has risen
owing to the land reform programme that resulted in thousands of landless
people being resettled.
AgriBank, recently converted into a fully-fledged land bank, is expected
to complement Government efforts to assist farmers.
The Government extended a $60 billion facility to the bank to finance
this year's agricultural season, which is now being disbursed through out
the country's provinces.
As part of the bank's mandate, the $60 billion would be made available
as production finance for at least four crops with $40 billion being set
aside to finance maize, $15 billion for cotton input while $5 billion would
be availed for sorghum and millet production.

Original_Search_Title: TCCIA Stresses Agricultural Output
Headline: Tanzania: TCCIA Stresses Agricultural Output
Body_Text:
A DECLINE in export earnings, worsening balance of payments, mounting
debts and slow economic growth are the major characteristics of the
Tanzanian economy.
The Tanzania Chamber of Commerce, Industry and Agriculture (TCCIA)
proposes that salvation lies in improving agriculture, a leading contributor
to the country's economy.
It notes that although the agricultural growth has comparatively shown
some encouraging signs in recent years, its overall performance is still
unimpressive in alleviating poverty.
"Agriculture's barter terms of trade, that measure the relative change
in agricultural producer prices compared to the price of industrial goods
have also not changed significantly over the past decades," TCCIA notes in
its Challenges for Agricultural Incentives in Tanzania document.
TCCIA says agriculture grew at 3.3 per cent in 1985 and went on
increasing. It grew by 5.5 per cent in 2001.
The combined volume of main food crops -maize, paddy, cassava, wheat,
beans and sorghum/millet-have grown at 3.5 per cent per year, while export
crops have grown at 5.4 per cent, according an Economic and Social Research
Foundation report of 2001.
This has largely contributed to an encouraging performance of the
economy as the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) in real terms grew to over 5.6
per cent in 2002 compared with 4.9 percent in 2000, according to the
Quarterly Economic Review of 2002.
Notwithstanding, however, distorted macroeconomic (monetary, exchange
rate and fiscal) policies, together with price and non-price factors have
all been identified as constraining factors to the development of Tanzania's
agriculture.
TCCIA cites past market interventions, distorting agricultural price
incentives that have not encouraged growth or investment in the sector
resulting in an inefficient al__cpLocation of resources and lack of comparative
advantage as some problems that faced the sector.
Others are the persistent problems of poor technology, lack of
diversification into new crops and failure to commercialise existing
production systems. These limit the scope for developing agriculture.
However, there are some particular problems such as lack of proper
marketing policy, uncontrolled market liberalisation process, lack of market
information for product prices and alternatives markets.
Others woes are the failure to make impact analysis for the enacted
marketing policies, failure to empower farmers to make rational decisions
and lack of reliable sources of quality products.
"There is also lack of agricultural support services such as credit
system for producers, traders and processors, prevalence disincentives such
as poor transport system, high-priced electricity and water supply, poor
research and extension services," the document points out in part.
TCCIA also describes lack of government commitment, including lack
policies for sustainable growth, lack of clear marketing and trade policies,
difficult regulatory procedures, and lack of clear campaigns on growth in
agriculture and trade.
There is also a heavy reliance on donor-driven programmes.
"Given the importance of agriculture as the mainstay of rural
livelihood, the country should recognise that the sector must grow faster if
rural poverty reduction is to become a reality," the chamber insists.
Tanzania forms part of the 75 per cent of the population of the world
that has the per capita income of less than $400 a year. Although per capita
income is only a rough estimate of real income, there is no doubt that real
poverty is predominant in these low-income countries.
Associated with low per capita income are low life expectancy, high
infant mortality, low percentage of children of school age in school,
nutritional deficiency, lack of access to the minimum needs for health
services, education and housing, among others.
Taking into account that currently the most productive sectors of the
economy among others are agriculture, industry and mining, more emphasis
needs to be vested.
However, when trying to design strategies for improving the rural
sector's economy one has to think not only in terms of income generating
potential but more so in terms of the impact of the generated income to the
rural mass life as a whole.
This includes the income distribution factor and its forward and
backward linkages.
Poverty, as assessed by the poor, hinges on the importance of secure
land tenure, availability of agricultural inputs, credit and suitable
technology, good transport, access to markets, and the potential to
save.
If the main concern and objectives are to develop the rural masses
through improved income level, then developing the agricultural sector is
the main and the best approach and means of attaining objectives of reducing
income poverty, improving human capabilities, survival and sound well-being
as well as containing extremely vulnerability among the poor, the chamber
proposes.
Meanwhile, officials say Tanzania's total debt stock as at end of last
June stood at $8,150.8 million. $7,331.3 million of the debt was the
external component while $819.5 million was domestic.
The external debt committed increased by $24.1 million, rising from
$7,606.6 million as at June 2003, to $7,630.7 million in July 2003.
Out of the total amount committed, disbursed outstanding debt was
$6,296.5 million, while the committed undisbursed debt was $1,334.2
million.
To improve the situation, TCCIA proposes that since close to 85 per cent
of the country's labour force is engaged in agricultural-related production
activities and the sector contributes 45 per cent to total production and 75
per cent of exports, the situation calls for need of having development
strategies aimed at alleviating poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Sorghum Bonanza
Headline: Uganda: Sorghum Bonanza
Body_Text:
Kampala — FARMERS in the district have earned sh120m from the sale of the epuripur
sorghum variety to Nile Breweries Limited (NBL) this season.
Charles Aben, the Soroti district agricultural officer, said on
Wednesday the district sold 400 tonnes of the sorghum to NBL at sh300 a
kilo.
"We could have even done much better if some of the sub-counties had not
been disturbed by the insurgency caused by Joseph Kony's (left) Lord's
Resistance Army rebels," Aben said.
He said that the district supplied 400 out of the 600 tonnes NBL bought
up.
NBL uses the this sorghum variety to brew its Eagle brand beer.
Aben said farmers from the sub-counties of Katine, Arapai and Gweri did
not harvest their crop. He said that Katine was one of the most promising
sub-counties where a large acreage of the crop had been cultivated.
"Most of the farmers were forced to abandon their homes and were living
in the camps for the internally displaced persons at the time of
harvesting," Aben said.
"This time we expect a better harvest since the farmers have realized
the benefit of cultivating the sorghum," he said.
NBL is subsidising farmers to grow the sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Forex Crisis Continues As Tobacco Output Drops
Headline: Zimbabwe: Forex Crisis Continues As Tobacco Output Drops
Body_Text:
Harare — The 2003 tobacco-selling season officially closed on Monday with a total
of 79.9 million kg having passed through the auction floors at an average
price of US$2.26 a kg.
Final clean up sales will be held on October 29 of which about 500 000
kg are expected to be sold pushing the total seasonal mass that would have
gone through the floors to 81 million kg.
This represents a 51 percent decline from last year's output of 165.7
million kg and a peak of 236.7 million kg in 2000.
Although the price remained unchanged at US$2.3 a kg and the number of
growers shot up by 66 percent to 25 600, thanks to the influx of new
farmers, the decline in output can be attributed to the decline in the area
cultivated by 33 percent to 46500 hectares from 69000 hectares last
year.
The biggest area to be devoted to tobacco cultivation of 95613 hectares
occurred in 1998 and this saw an output of 216 million kg passing through
the auction floors.
This year's output of 81 million kg only earns the country some US$183
million which is 51 percent lower than US$375.7 million earned last year and
way below a peak of US$593.4 millionkg that was achieved in 1996.
Tobacco output is not expected to improve next year because the amount
of seed sold is not significantly different from that sold last year.
According to the Zimbabwe Tobacco Association (ZTA) seeds for 2003/04
amounting to 335.58 kg have been sold which is slightly higher than 328.25
kg sold during the same period last year.
Large-scale farmers have bought 145.30 kg of seeds (down 20 percent )
and small-scale farmers have bought 190.28 kg (up 30 percent ).
If all the seed was planted, a maximum of 90 million kg could be
achieved.
This is, however, unlikely due to high production costs, among other
factors.
