Large-scale farmers are estimated to actually plant around 20 000
hectares, down 20 percent on last year and this gives an output of between
40 and 42 million kg.
Small-scale producers, although having actually purchased more seed than
last year as noted above, will be adversely affected by lack of affordable
chemicals, fertilisers, tillage services and insufficient easy
term-finance.
For instance, due to inflation-propelled cost increases, the ZTA
estimates that it now costs Z$35 million to grow one hectare of tobacco
compared to Z$4 million last year.
Owing to these cost factors, area under cultivation is expected to drop
by 36 percent from 25 000 hectares last year to 16000 hectares and this is
consistent with an output of between 14 and 16 million kg.
The total crop estimate for the 2003/04 season is estimated to be in the
region of between 55 and 60 million kg.
The other viability problem afflicting tobacco growers concerns the
overvalued exchange rate which remained fixed at Z$800 per US dollar
throughout the selling season against a high and rising inflation
environment which as noted above has resulted in a phenomenal increase in
production costs.
As a result, the rate of return per hectare declined by 29 percent to
US$3885.5/ha from US$5446/ha last year.
Using the official exchange rate of Z$800 per US dollar that applies to
the tobacco sector, this means that in local currency terms a hectare of
tobacco yields some Z$3 million which is way below the Z$35 million cost
calculated by ZTA.
This means that profitability or some break-even will only be realised
if one uses an exchange rate of at least Z$9 000 per US dollar.
This means the Government has to either review the exchange rate or
aggressively assist farmers with subsidised inputs or very cheap term-loans
through the Agricultural Development Bank.
This means that the Z$60 billion loan facility whereby Z$40 billion of
it is for maize and the balance for cotton, millet and sorghum should be
expanded to include tobacco.
Moreover, the money for the said crops is inadequate.
Although one could argue that tobacco growers are covered by the Tobacco
Growers Trust (TGT) funds, the money is also inadequate. This year growers
have been heavily under-funded by the Reserve Bank as only US$6 million
instead of US$36 million is available as money from money from the 20
percent retention scheme of the tobacco export proceeds.
Most of the US$6 million has been used to pay-off last year's debts and
buy some inputs using a cheap conversion rate of Z$800 per US dollar which
then yields less value in terms of inputs bought against a background of
ever-rising input costs.
These developments in the tobacco industry are worrisome given that
tobacco is the major foreign currency earner constituting some 35 percent of
total exports.
Gold is the second major export earner contributing some 13 percent as
of 2000 although the figure might have declined due to continued viability
problems in the sector.
Although Zimbabwe is said to be moving towards world leadership in
platinum production, it will be some before that happens let alone to
surpass gold which in turn is lower than tobacco in terms of foreign
currency generation capacity.
Moreover, minerals by nature are non-renewable resources, which cannot
be relied upon in the long-term, again leaving tobacco to be the crop of
last resort.
Given that the crop has been doing well for a very long time in terms of
contribution to the economy the government should put in place measures to
ensure that it remains a crop of first resort. Tourism is another important
foreign currency and is showing some signs of recovery if figures for the
first half of this year are anything to go by. However, at US$75.7 million
in 2002 the sector cannot be compared to tobacco, which as already noted,
earned some US$183 million this year under difficult economic
conditions. With other foreign currency earning commodities like gold,
platinum and tourism not expected to surpass or replace tobacco this means
that the economy's foreign currency crises should not be expected to improve
in the near future. This means that Government should urgently implement
foreign currency boosting measures, which range from putting in place a
realistic exchange rate system to re-engaging the international community
like the IMF and World Bank to improving the political environment so as to
encourage foreign investment. At the same time the Government should
implement sound macroeconomic policies like reducing inflation through tight
monetary and fiscal policies so as to contain the ever-increasing production
costs.

Original_Search_Title: Relief Agencies Distribute Food in Urban Centres
Headline: Zimbabwe: Relief Agencies Distribute Food in Urban Centres
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — World Vision Zimbabwe has announced a new urban school feeding programme, which the organisation aims to launch in November.
The feeding scheme, funded by the UN World Food Programme (WFP), forms part of World Vision's urban intervention in Zimbabwe.
Zvidzai Maburutse, the World Vision Zimbabwe Relief Deputy Director, said in a statement that the programme was set to kick off at the beginning of November. "We are going to use the lessons drawn from this pilot program to expand into other urban schools," he added.
This is the first time the organisation will be implementing a school feeding programme in an urban environment. Previously its focus has been on the rural areas, where an estimated 4.4 million people currently need food aid.
Maburutse said the intervention was coming at a time when food insecurity in urban centres was becoming increasingly desperate, and school children had been identified as one of the most vulnerable groups.
"Urban vulnerability, fuelled by the declining economy, has continued to slide and households have been forced to come up with strategies to survive. It is estimated that 1.1 million people in urban centres are in urgent need of food aid," World Vision said.
The school feeding intervention will augment already existing programmes being run by other humanitarian agencies in the city.
Last month Catholic Relief Services launched a Market Assistance Pilot Program (MAPP) to reduce urban vulnerability in Zimbabwe's second city, Bulawayo.
"The MAPP programme, which is funded by the United States Agency for International Development (USAID), is bringing in 20,000 mt of sorghum into the country. The sorghum is going to be milled and placed in strategic retail outlets and sold to the public at subsidised rates," World Vision said.
The WFP is also running supplementary feeding in 37 clinics dotted across the city, reaching out to over 25,000 children under five years of age.

Original_Search_Title: Zeltis Turns Corner With R10m Boost to Profit
Headline: South Africa: Zeltis Turns Corner With R10m Boost to Profit
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — INVESTMENT holding company Zeltis says it is turning the corner and
expects to post a profit in its next set of year-end results.
The group, which specialises in providing financial services, has taken
a lot of stick in the media because of its poor performance.
Zeltis MD Rodney McLeod said yesterday there was a R10m improvement in
profit in its results for the year to June.
While revenue for the year dropped from R148,5m to R141,4m, Zeltis
turned a R2,4m operating loss into a R3,2m profit.
Its net loss came down from R12,9m to R745000.
McLeod said the improvement in its results was on the back of its
recruitment agency, Denverdraft, which was restructured to lower costs.
Long term debt was up from R510000 to R3,2m, while cash was down from
R3,2m to R971000.
McLeod said next year's results would show an improvement in the balance
sheet.
Although the group was turning around, McLeod still was not happy with
the way it was structured.
He said that Zeltis had created two empowerment recruitment operations
and was close to introducing another that would enable it to operate in the
northern parts of the country.
He said he was happy with the way the empowerment operations had
performed.
The group's dispute with Awethu Breweries the second-largest sorghum
beer and mageu brewer in SA was still continuing.
The dispute began over an amount owing from the sale by Awethu of RMD
Financial Services to Zeltis.
McLeod said Awethu was under a lot of stress and could not afford to
give Zeltis its holding in RMD Financial Services.
The results were delayed because the group was hoping to conclude a
dispute with one of its debtors.
McLeod said the dispute would be most likely be concluded before the end
of the year.
Zeltis closed unchanged at 2c with no trade taking place in the group's
shares on the JSE Securities Exchange SA yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: Lack of Foreign Currency Hampers Seed Purchases
Headline: Zimbabwe: Lack of Foreign Currency Hampers Seed Purchases
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Zimbabwe's newly settled and communal farmers face the prospect of this season's expected good rains falling on empty fields if the government does not distribute seed in time for the first plantings at the end of October.
The official Herald newspaper reported on Tuesday that the US $30 million needed by the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement to buy inputs like seed and fertiliser for distribution throughout the country, was yet to be released by the country's reserve bank.
According to the Herald, the bank had raised the precious foreign exchange using tobacco revenues as security, but the money would not be released until the government had met certain terms. It was not immediately clear what these were.
Government spokesman Steyn Berejena told IRIN that Zimbabwe's land reform programme, which saw thousands of white-owned farms transferred to black farmers, resulted in an increase in the amount of acreage to be planted this year and a corresponding shortage of seed.
The government has a programme to buy seed from seed houses for distribution to new farmers through the Grain Marketing Board (GMB) and the Agricultural Rural Development Authority.
"Government will need more seed this year to make sure enough is channelled to the needy farmers," Berejena said, adding that the farmers were hoping to use the predicted good rains to make up for the last two years' drought-induced losses.
"The main thrust [for the government] is to assist newly settled and communal farmers because they don't have the resources to buy seed on their own," he said. "It has to be available in rural shops for purchase, or obtainable through a soft loan from the GMB by the end of October, when the maize planting season starts."
He said the government had already sourced 10,000 mt of maize seed from neighbouring countries, and now just needed to pay for it. Up to 32,000 mt was currently available, but another 56,000 mt was still needed.
"Although many other seeds like millet and sorghum are needed, the emphasis is placed on maize seed for food security," Berejena said.
"We are raring to go - the seed has been found, we just need the money to pay the supplier," he explained.
Economist John Robertson told IRIN there was a possibility that the Reserve Bank simply did not have the foreign currency the government needed, as many business people were holding onto their currency in anticipation of another devaluation.
The Zimbabwe dollar was last devalued in February from Zim 55 to US $1 to a selling rate of Zim 848 to US $1, with the promise of devaluations every three months.
"It is likely that exporters are holding back on repatriating their earnings and waiting for a devaluation, and it is unlikely that the currency will be sourced from the parallel market at a rate of Zim 5,000 to US $1, which would make the seed very expensive."
He added: "It is a disgrace that Zimbabwe is importing seed ... we used to be an exporter. The country also runs the risk of importing seed not suitable for germination in this climate."

Original_Search_Title: No Cash for Inputs
Headline: Zimbabwe: No Cash for Inputs
Body_Text:
Harare — FAILURE by the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe to release in time US$30 million
($24,7 billion) to the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural
Resettle-ment to purchase inputs has adversely affected preparations for the
2003/2004 agricultural season which is now only a few weeks away.
Sources in the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement say
only 32 000 tonnes of maize seed were available and this falls short of
national requirements by 56 000 tonnes.
The country needs at least 80 000 tonnes and the demand for seed has
risen owing to the land reform programme.
The Government had put in place a programme to import 15 000 tonnes of
maize seed from the region and had requested US$30 million from the central
bank.
The Agricultural Rural Development Authority was allocated $15 billion
to import 9 200 tonnes of maize seed.
The Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, Cde Joseph
Made, said yesterday the failure by the Reserve Bank to release the money
for inputs had dented preparations for this planting season.
"Once again the Reserve Bank is failing to release the foreign currency
that we require to purchase the maize we have ordered from our neighbouring
countries," he said.
Cde Made said teams that were sent to scout for maize seed in the region
had managed to sign contracts in South Africa and Zambia for the supply of
10 000 tonnes of maize seed.
However, this could only be procured if the Reserve Bank releases the
foreign currency which was also required to purchase millet and sorghum
seed.
"They (Reserve Bank) actually said they had found the US$30 million and
we then sent out our teams to Malawi, Zambia, Kenya and South Africa but up
to now we have not received anything," Cde Made said.
He said the provision of maize seed was very critical especially after
predictions of a good rainy season.
However, acting Reserve Bank Governor Mr Charles Chikaura said although
the funds were now available for disbursement, the Government was yet to
meet certain conditions as required by the lender.
He said a Ministerial Inputs Committee tasked the Reserve Bank to
provide the foreign currency for the procurement of maize seed.
But given the tight foreign currency situation in the country, Mr
Chikaura said, an arrangement was put in place to source foreign exchange
from external financiers.
He said the bank's External Loans Committee approved a US$30 million
facility against the security of future tobacco sales.
But the disbursement of the funds was to be triggered by a cash payment,
which has since been made from current tobacco sales.
"Funds are now available for immediate disbursement subject to
Government meeting certain conditions precedent as required by the
lender. These conditions are being discussed between the Ministry of Lands,
Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, who are the ultimate beneficiary in this
facility, and a local bank, who are acting as an agent for the external
financier," he said.
The shortage of inputs has adversely affected farming preparations and
farmers resettled under the A1 and A2 models as well as those in communal
areas have been failing to secure maize seed, fertiliser, tillage and other
farm inputs.
Cde Made said communal farmers were, however, less affected in terms of
tillage as they could still use their ox-drawn ploughs.
The minister said the country needed at least 100 000 tonnes of maize
seed to adequately provide for farmers this season.
He said the normal seed consumption for the country was 80 000 tones,
but there was need to get an extra 20 000 tones to cater for some areas that
would need replanting.
Cde Made said the little seed that was being released on the market
mainly by Seed Co was not adequate and the 10 000 tones secured from Zambia
and South Africa would go a long way in reducing the shortage.
Mobilisation of resources for supporting the agricultural sector this
season was based on targets set by the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and
Rural Resettlement.
Expectations were that the Government would finance up to $536,7 billion
with the private sector contributing over $657 billion of the requirements
for the inputs and services.
Imported maize seed would go a long way in covering the deficit that was
created because of the high demand.
The ministry had so far purchased $20,8 billion worth of agricultural
inputs and these were being distributed throughout the country. It had set
up provincial, district and ward distribution committees to facilitate a
smooth distribution of the inputs.
The tillage programme was allocated $10 billion and to date $2,9 billion
has been disbursed to DDF and Arda.
At least $60 billion was allocated to Agribank, which has been
transformed to a land bank, for on lending to farmers and out of that, $20
billion had already been disbursed.
The country has been divided into three regions for the administration
of inputs and tillage programmes. These are the eastern region, which covers
Manicaland, Mashonaland East and Harare; the central region comprising
Mashonaland Central and Mashonaland West and the western region, which
covers Matabeleland South, and North and the Midlands.
Zimbabwe Farmers Union vice president Mr Wilfanos Mashingaidze said
although preparations for this season were at an advanced stage, farmers
were facing a critical shortage of tillage.
He said tractors from the District Development Fund were failing to cope
up with the demand.
Mr Mashingaidze urged the Government to provide more diesel to farmers
and the DDF to enable them to prepare adequately for this season.
"Although most farmers recently received maize seed which was
distributed by the Government, the amount is not enough. There is so much
activity on the ground but the lack of tractors and maize seed is affecting
most farmers," he said.
Mr Mashingaidze said while the country was facing a shortage of seed,
there was a lot of maize in Hurungwe but because of the shortage of diesel,
this could not be ferried to the Grain Marketing Board.

Original_Search_Title: Malnutrition Claims 40 in Bulawayo
Headline: Zimbabwe: Malnutrition Claims 40 in Bulawayo
Body_Text:
Bulawayo — More than 40 people, the majority of them children under the
age of five, have died of malnutrition in Bulawayo in the last two months as
hunger begins to take its toll on Zimbabwe's urban population.
According to a council report released last week, a total of 43 deaths
due to malnutrition were recorded in the months of August and September
alone in the city, Zimbabwe's second largest town.
The Bulawayo City Council's Health, Housing and Education Committee says
of the total number of deaths, 12 people died in August while the remainder
- 31 people - died in the month of September alone.
The report further states that of the total number, 23 children aged
between one and four years died of malnutrition in September compared to
seven in August, making the category the most affected age group.
The deaths come hard on the heels of reports of hunger stalking
villagers countrywide and reports that many families in the most remote
parts of Zimbabwe were going for days without food.
In some instances, families in Bulawayo say they have resorted to having
porridge with only lemon juice but no sugar or bread for lunch while both
lunch and supper are combined and usually taken at dusk.
Zimbabwe is facing its worst humanitarian crisis in years with more than
six million people in dire need of food aid countrywide.
However most food stuffs are gradually getting back onto supermarket
shelves but most of the basic commodities are now far beyond the reach of
many families as prices are sky-rocketing on a daily basis.
Currently, 2 litres of cooking oil and a loaf of bread have gone up to
$19 000 from $7 000 and $1 500 from $1 000, respectively.
The increases mean that a family of five now needs between $250 000 to
about $650 000 per month to survive, which is far higher than most
salaries.
The indiscriminate price increases have forced the government to
reconsider its position on price controls. Price controls were scrapped
officially in May because they were believed to be the cause of the
unavailability of products on the market.
Through the Market Assistance Pilot Programme, the United States Agency
for International Development (USAID), threw a lifeline to urban dwellers
last month when it put up 20 000 tonnes of cheaper sorghum meal onto the
market.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Hinders a Hunger to Learn
Headline: South Africa: Poverty Hinders a Hunger to Learn
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — In a rural corner of South Africa, a grandmother is raising eight
children on R700 a month - and her biggest worry is paying school fees
IT'S 6.30am. Suzan Dolo sniffs her snuff, with her right hand clutching
a hand-made grass-broom. At 64, she still sweeps her sandy 100m' yard before
sunrise - the first activity every morning for the women of Vergenoeg
village, 80km northwest of Mokopane in Limpopo.
"You lot are going to be late," she screams at five of her eight
grandchildren. "Ms Makgotlho is already on her way."
They don't have any means of measuring time. "Our wireless [radio] is
not working any more," says Cylia, 16, a Grade 9 pupil at Nakonkwetlou
Secondary School. Thus Perpetoa Makgotlho, principal of Modisha Primary
School, is their only chronometer.
Salome and Pula didn't sleep at home the previous night. Pula, 21, the
truant and delinquent, hasn't been to school, where he is doing Grade 10,
for two days. Salome, 20, has been asked by a relative to babysit for two
days.
N ine-year-old twin sisters Elizabeth and Francinah are bathing in a
plastic wash basin. Cylia and Suzan, 14 - named after her granny - are the
first to get up, prepare the fire and sweep the mud veranda before having
the basin to themselves. Manoko, 11, is next in the basin queue.
Manoko is combing her hair , using a piece of a broken mirror to look at
her dark-skinned face and beautiful eyes. "I would like to become a nurse,"
she responds to my question, while shyly hiding her face behind her
hands.
They all wash in the tiny, weather-beaten, thatched-roof hut. It smells
of sweat, sorghum, the cow dung used to polish the mud floor and smoke from
a makeshift fireplace.
All of them, including Salome's daughter, depend on their granny's R700
monthly state pension grant.
"Please eat, you cannot go to school on an empty stomach," says
Dolo. Their breakfast is black tea without bread. "I can't give them
anything. I don't have a cent to buy dry bread. They will have to go on like
this until I get paid on Monday," she says, sneezing continuously from the
snuff.
Food is the least of Dolo's problems - school fees are her biggest
worry.
The mother of seven of her grandchildren is in Tembisa, on the East
Rand, looking for a job. She has left the children under the guardianship of
Dolo. The granny is not prepared to say anything about their fathers.
Pula's parents died and he has been living with Dolo ever since.
Her husband died in 1979 and she has known nothing but poverty for the
greater part of her life. However, the two schools still demand about R500
in fees a year.
"Every time we get paid, I have to settle my debts at the local store
for groceries I took the previous month," she says. "I pay about R260 each
month."
This is for a bag of mealiemeal, sugar, washing powder and tea. She
spoils her grandchildren by buying them a chicken on payday - the only time
they can lick their fingers besides Christmas.
After she pays R10 to a burial society she buys other basic groceries
amounting to about R250. A n electricity recharge card costs R30, even
though they only use it for lights to allow the children to do their
homework.
On weekends, Dolo supplements her pension with the sorghum beer she is
brewing. "If business is good, I might rake in around R150 and I will buy
tsotsi [a 15kg bag of mealiemeal] and pay more fees," she says.
A brownish liquid is leaking from a big brown clay pot in the hut, "a
sign that the beer is maturing", she says.
"All that I want is to see my grandchildren doing something for
themselves in future and not ending up like me and their mother," she
says. "I want them to be nurses, teachers or to be like you [a journalist]."
A large portion of her income goes on school fees. "If I don't give them
[the schools] anything, I'm likely to meet my grandchildren at the gate
returning from school. The schools send them home every payday to get
money. They say they must not return until they get money. If I don't pay
until the end of the year, they won't get their report cards.
"I tried to tell the principals that I'm struggling but they say I'm not
the only one struggling."
However, Makgotlho says her school doesn't chase away children but "just
sends them home to remind their parents of the fees".
Such incidents are common in rural areas where principals take advantage
of illiterate and ignorant parents such as Dolo, says Limpopo Education
spokesman Freddy Greaver.
Asked whether he has told parents about exemption for those who can't
pay, Nakonkwetlou principal Malesela Lekalakala says parents are frustrated
by the bureaucracy involved in applying for exemption and end up not
applying.
He adds that the government does not supply enough teaching aids and
that funds have shrunk, which means the school has to depend solely on
fees.
Solly Mabusela, chairman of the Global Campaign for Education, says this
is the reason why the campaign's international lobby group has been calling
for the total scrapping of user fees.
"The state does not have [enough] machinery to monitor the situation on
the ground," Mabusela says. "There will always be such incidents and
loopholes."
It is a warning to Dolo's grandchildren that their education might be
limited as tertiary institution's fees rise higher and higher.
Alternatively, says Firoz Patel, acting deputy director-general in the
Department of Education, they might have to wait until a new funding policy
is implemented. "We want to ultimately ban school fees in poor schools," he
says. "This could be as soon as 2005."
In the meantime, Dolo will have to share her house, her pension and the
income from her sorghum beer with her grandchildren - and hope they will
somehow rise above the poverty that restricts their lives.

Original_Search_Title: New Traditional Beer Plan for Mbeya
Headline: Tanzania: New Traditional Beer Plan for Mbeya
Body_Text:
TANZANIA Breweries Limited has authorised the establishment of a small
traditional beer brewery in Mbeya.
The US$600,000 investment is expected to be ready by the end of 2004,
and will be run by 15 persons. The plant is expected to produce 200,000
hectolitres of liquor annually, brewed from corn and sorghum, when it begins
operations.
The plant is being put in modular phases that will allow for easy
expansion.
"We feel there is opportunity in this market," says the TBL chief
executive director, Justice O'Donovan. He says that, while barley and malt,
which are the main ingredients in beer brewing, are more costly - and take
longer to grow - maize and sorghum are readily available, relatively cheaper
and take a short time before harvesting.
Traditional liquor commands a sizable share of the alcoholic beverages
market in neighbouring Zambia and Malawi.
The Mbeya brewery is expected to be commissioned by the end of June
2004, and is intended to provide the population within the area with
commercially produced traditional beer that would meet both hygiene and
value needs.
The one-litre returnable bottle will be used in an effort to keep the
product price affordable, whilst simultaneously being kind to the
environment.
Local contractors have been employed to build the factory, and employees
will be predominantly drawn from the area.

Original_Search_Title: Better Working Conditions of Workers
Headline: Ghana: Better Working Conditions of Workers
Body_Text:
The Minister of Private Sector Development, Mr. Kwamena Bartels, has
urged manufacturers to consider the working conditions and health of staff
of Ghanaians.
He said that would prevent tragedy, like the one which happened recently
at the Pioneer Food Processing Company Limited.
In a speech during the launching of the Bakers Inn Bakery, Tuesday, the
minister cautioned the company to work closely with the Factory Inspectorate
Division of the Ministry of the Manpower Development and Employment to
ensure that industrial standards and safety are maintained.
He noted that "profit considerations should not be the driving force for
venturing into this kind of business," but that the company is to use the
right raw materials to produce to specifications of the standard weight and
adherence to lifespan of its products on the shelves of supermarkets.
He was pleased that the management of the company has come to complement
the efforts of local bakeries with their special brand of bakery
products.
But Mr. Bartels was fast to say that; "it should therefore not be
construed as an attempt by this company to edge local producers of bakery
products out of business."
He advised that the company, when it establishes itself on the market,
should explore the possibility of teaming up with Ghanaian research
institutions to undertake research into the use of other local staples, like
maize, millet and sorghum, in the production of bakery products.
The Deputy Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Kwadwo Afram Asiedu, also
cautioned the company not to indulge in unauthorized capital flight which,
invariably, would touch the nerve center of the country's revenue collecting
agencies.
He said the company should strive to fulfill its promises and operate
within the confines of the laws of Ghana and help to improve the lives of
workers as well as satisfying other social and civic responsibilities.
According to him one feature of the company's business that gladdens his
heart was its proposal to introduce the mechanism of franchise in
Ghana.
This, he submitted, "will be a golden opportunity for the Ghanaian
business people to embrace new mechanisms in modern day trading, such as
franchises, mergers, acquisitions and joint ventures or cooperatives."
He advised that Ghanaians discard their individualistic attitudes in the
way they do business since that has been a veritable bane to the enhancement
of the nation's business culture.

Original_Search_Title: Produce Gains Hurt By Quality
Headline: Uganda: Produce Gains Hurt By Quality
Body_Text:
Kampala — Uganda Grain Traders (UGT) Limited, a local consortium of produce
exporters has secured a sh1.1b from the European Union (EU), to facilitate
the training of farming groups in raising quality standards John Magnay, the
Chief Executive Officer (CEO) UGT told Business Vision recently, that the
agreement has already been signed and the first disbursement of 50,000 euro
(about sh200m) will be received this month.
The second phase is expected to start early next year with the
disbursement of 450,000 euro.
"This will be supported by quality control and post harvest equipment
and a SMS Hotline marketing system to allow farmers to get up-to-date
information and sell product direct to UGT at export quality," Magnay
said.
In the recent past however, demand pressures and a higher price offers
from Kenya caused local traders to sell wet crops, raising concern over the
quality of maize presently exported by Uganda.
Magnay said that last season's planting was delayed resulting in a late
harvest.
The crop is said to be wet with subsequent damage to quality with high
fungal disease and discoloured grains.
"But because there is a shortage in Kenya and therefore, high demand for
maize, farmers are now selling their crop before drying well. Our biggest
problem now is the moisture content. There is no time for farmers to dry
their maize or beans well," Magnay said.
Also the high price for maize in Kampala, presently between sh240 to sh
255 per kilo at the border with Kenya, has encouraged farmers and traders to
sell the crop hurriedly.
Magnay however, said that there is very little maize available for
export, except the add-hoc border trade to Kenya.
The high demand from World Food Programme for northern Uganda feeding
programmes and Rwanda, Burundi and Congo is expected to dominate the market
for maize and beans for the coming season in January 2004.
He said there is good prospects for prices and the trend would now be
towards contract growing of specific crops for export and local
processing.
Currently UGTL, a consortium of 16 companies, is buying maize, soya
beans for export and White Sorghum for the brewing of local beer by Uganda
Breweries Limited (UBL). UBL last year signed a deal with UGTL to supply
seeds to eastern farmers.
"We supplied seeds to the farmers last year and we are having a very
good harvest," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Literacy Classes Help Desert Nomad Women
Headline: Niger: Literacy Classes Help Desert Nomad Women
Body_Text:
Self-assured, 25-year-old Aicha Mooha stood up, made her way to the
blackboard and added up three numbers in front of the class. The instructor
said "well done" as she chalked up the correct answer, prompting a round of
applause from the other 20 "students" at this literacy centre for nomad
women in the Sahara desert.
Sitting on mats covered with blankets in a thatched shelter to provide
shade from the burning sun, the women learning to read and write ranged in
age from six to over 60.
Their families had settled in the small village of of Iguizran Malolan,
45 km from the town of Agadez in central Niger, for the duration of the
rainy season. But soon they would be off again with their herds of cows,
sheep, goats and camels, scouring the bleak landscape for pasture.
"I have learnt how to count, read and write," Mooha said. "After the
training, I would like to start my own business - a boutique where I can
sell clothes and ladies wear in Agadez," she added.
Mooha's story was cut short by the cry of her one-year-old daughter. She
was being looked after by Mooha's mother, Fatima, who was also a student in
the class.
As Fatima handed back her grand-daughter, she remarked that had she
known the importance of education 20 years ago, she would have sent Mooha to
school.
"I did not know the importance of education, but now I know," she
said. "I know education is important for women because ladies who have been
to school understand life better," Fatima added.
She regretted there were no schools in the village when Mooha was
growing up, but the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) has helped to
change that.
UNICEF has helped to launch 2,847 literacy centres for girls and women
across Niger. It provides teaching materials and pays the teachers' modest
salary of US $21 a month for the first year and half their salary for second
year. After that, it is up to the local communities to keep these makeshift
schools going with their own money.
Literacy centre turns lives around
One of the instructors at Iguizran Malolan, 25-year-old Abdulrahaman
Ibrahim, said the literacy centre had revolutionised the life of women in
the village. "It turns their lives around," he told IRIN.
"When they start they know nothing, but after two months, you can see
progress because they start to know how to read and how to write and you see
there is a complete difference."
"At first especially for the older women, it is very difficult but they
get used to it and they like it," he said, adding that it "becomes a chance
to catch up with a missed opportunity".
Ibrahim who wore a white worn out T-shirt, a pair of black trousers and
old white sandals, was lucky enough to complete primary school in another
nearby village. However, his parents could not afford the fees for him to go
on to secondary school.
He said young girls were attending the literacy classes in Iguizran
Malolan alongside adult women because of a lack of schools nearby. In any
case, many of the girls were late starters in education and this made it
difficult for them to fit in with classes dominated by younger pupils, he
noted
"Most of them are over age and cannot fit in easily in the normal
schools," Ibrahim said.
Semi-nomad families
The families in this small village are semi-nomads who move around the
region with tents, grazing their flocks for half the year. But they settle
in one place during the rainy season between May and October.
This provides an opportunity for UNICEF to offer them a four-month
literacy programme. The women are taught to read and write in Tamachek, the
language of the Tuareg ethnic group which is spread right across the
Sahara. But they are also given information about health issues, including
nutrition, malaria, child vaccination, breastfeeding, disease control and
HIV/AIDS.
At the same time, the nomad families are encouraged to plant onions and
other vegetables in the oasis during their brief period of settlement for
sale as cash crops.
In some centres women, UNICEF helps the women to set up "cereal banks,"
small cooperatives where they can pool their stocks of sorghum and
millet. The reading and writing skills and the arithmatic which they learn
at the literacy centres help them account for their grain stocks.
Three-year programme
The complete literacy course is spread over three years, but is
implemented in three modules of four month's each. Mooha's class was doing
its first year.
UNICEF has helped establish development skills and literacy training
centres for girls and women all over Niger. However, those in the desert
centre and north of the country are unique because of the challenges of
running them for nomadic populations.
There are only 6,000 primary schools catering for 1.8 million
school-aged children in Niger, which is one of the world's poorest
countries. But these are not enough to educate its population of 11 million,
most of whom are illiterate.
90 percent of women illiterate
According to UNICEF, only 36 percent of boys and 26 percent for girls
enrol in primary school and 90 percent of all women in Niger are
illiterate.
Women, it says, suffer the weight of tradition and are generally
excluded from decisions affecting their lives. Most are confined to the role
of housewife and spend their lives preparing food, fetching water, tilling
fields and bringing up children.
UNICEF hopes that access to education will give women greater
opportunities in life and allow them to manage their own affairs. That in
turn should earn them greater respect from their menfolk and help them move
towards a situation of equality.
"Girls education is high on UNICEF's agenda," UNICEF Representative in
Niger Adjibade Aboudou Karimou said.
UNICEF helping government establish policy guidelines
UNICEF helped to open nearly 1,200 new literacy centres for women in
Niger last year. It also produces pamphlets in local languages that are
specially designed to be used in adult literacy programmes.
Some feature cartoons to educate people about health issues such as
HIV/AIDS prevention and immunisations are also produced.

Original_Search_Title: Controversial Themba Mliswa Quits Sport
Headline: Zimbabwe: Controversial Themba Mliswa Quits Sport
Body_Text:
Harare — THEMBA Mliswa, the controversial fitness trainer and once Dynamos
interim chairman, has quit sport to concentrate on farming.
He is now a full time A2 model farmer.
In an interview yesterday, Mliswa said he is no longer interested in
anything to do with local sport.
"I am a professional and have a degree in sport. I do not want to get
involved in the circus that we call sport in this country," he said.
"I have worked selflessly to improve sport but my efforts have gone
unnoticed. So I thought it was better to try my hand in feeding the nation."
Mliswa said he was now devoting all his energies into "making money
through farming".
"I am a proud beneficiary of the land reform programme and have no
regrets about it," said Mliswa.
He said he was now growing crops at the 400-hectare Spring Farm in
Karoi, some 210 km west of Harare.
The farm was previously owned by Jenny Parsons who moved left sometime
last year after the farm was listed for compulsory acquisition by the
government.
Mliswa, who controversially inherited some infrastructure at the farm,
claims he has made several improvements to the farm which has a primary
school, six teachers and a full-time health worker.
He said he employs 120 people and has bought 650 head of cattle.
Mliswa says activities at his farm are diverse and he is into tobacco
farming, seed maize, sorghum and maize production, among other
activities. He said he has invested close to $2 billion since he took over
the farm.
But where did he get that kind of money?
"I have made savings since my days in the United Kingdom and my family
also supported me financially. My bank has been very helpful in getting me
off the ground too," he said.
His farming adventure has not been without incident. He was in April
arrested for allegedly assaulting Parson and another farmer, three policemen
and a deputy sheriff's official resulting in him spending over a week in
remand prison before he was released on $50 000 bail.
The matter is still pending before the courts.
"I believe the law will take its course on that matter but at the moment
I am just doing my work.
"I am very excited and work on the farm is going on well. We're
preparing the land and things look good."

Original_Search_Title: 56-Year Wait for House is Over
Headline: South Africa: 56-Year Wait for House is Over
Body_Text:
Eighty-six-year-old Headman Ngxamane of Khayamandi dreamed of living in a proper house for 56 years - yesterday his dream came true.
His face lit up with a smile of gratitude and satisfaction when he saw his new home.
Ngxamane said he had never owned a proper house.
"I am so grateful to receive this brand new home, this makes me very happy."
Ngxamane said his only regret "is that my dear wife, who passed away 14 years ago, cannot be here to share this joyful moment with me. I know she would have been so excited".
Three hundred and eight families in Khayamandi, Stellenbosch, received keys to their new homes from the Western Cape Department of Housing during a ceremony yesterday.
The lucky beneficiaries of the neat but modest dwellings were part of a group of almost 2 000 local residents, including school children, unemployed youth and the elderly, who had turned out to witness the historic event.
The emotional ceremony featured a keynote address by housing minister Sankie Mthembe-Makhanyele, traditional dancing and singing, the tasting of newly brewed sorghum beer, tree planting and the ceremonial hand-over of the keys.
Speaking from in front of a marquee filled with local leaders, dignitaries and officials from the provincial housing department, Mthembu-Makhanyele urged residents to take pride in the new developments in their surroundings.
She pledged to find the resources for the further renewal and rejuvenation of Khayamandi, which spreads up one of the Hottentots Holland Mountains.
"Khayamandi," said the minister, "is one of the best pilots of this government's programme to provide low and medium cost housing to our people."
She was particularly proud that the housing al__cpLocation programme had taken note that "33% of the beneficiaries of the new al__cpLocation are women, many of whom head up single parent households".
She said young people should take note of the changes for the better and urged them to pursue education to become doctors, teachers, priests, scientists and business people.
With Stellenbosch mayor WF Ortel and Western Cape housing MEC Nomatyala Hangana flanking her, she then accompanied Ngxamane across from the main tent to his new home.

Original_Search_Title: Partners Support Decentralization in Timbuktu Region
Headline: Mali: Partners Support Decentralization in Timbuktu Region
Body_Text:
New York — UNDP and several partners are working with the Government and rural
communities in Mali's northern Timbuktu region to help people gain skills in
planning and managing development activities.
The project supports the Government's efforts to improve lives and
livelihoods through decentralization, giving localities greater authority
and resources for development. In the arid and semi-arid Sahelian
environment, the challenges are great.
Agriculture is a mainstay of the region, including cultivation of rice,
sorghum, tubers, as well as raising livestock. About 60 per cent of
population lives below the poverty line.
The Ministry of Interior began the five-year initiative in 1999,
encompassing 27 municipalities with 444 villages in the Timbuktu, DirÃ©, and
Gourma-Rharous districts.
UNDP, partnering with the Belgian Solidarity Fund (FBS), the UN Capital
Development Fund and the Food and Agriculture Organization of the UN, is
providing technical assistance. The Government is providing 2 per cent of
the US$8.6 million budget and the communities about 5 per cent.
The project is training elected officials, local government staff and
central government employees, as well as community leaders, members of civil
society groups and private service providers. It is also reaching out to
citizens through the media to raise awareness about decentralization.
To promote wide participation in local decision making on development,
the initiative has helped communities set up planning committees. These
include community council members, as well as local men and women serving as
resource people. The committees set local development priorities - such as
construction of schools, health centres, irrigation systems and roads - and
track their implementation.
"Thanks to the project, we are happy to see that decentralization is
taking shape in the region," said Segui KantÃ©, Cabinet Director of the High
Commission of Timbuktu. "We salute the initiative for helping build the
capacities of local elected officials through training."
This positive assessment was reinforced by the results of a recent
survey by Radio Jamana, which found that local people view the project as
contributing the most to the region's development.
For further information please contact Mahamadou Coulibaly, UNDP Mali,
or Nicholas Gouede, UNDP Communications Office.

Original_Search_Title: IT Software And Service is a Major Area of Co-Operation
Headline: South Africa: IT Software And Service is a Major Area of Co-Operation
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — THE information technology (IT) sector is an area of significant
cooperation between India and SA.
India's National Association of Software and Service Companies says that
with India recognised and accepted as a global IT player, and SA promising
to be a growing market in the Southern African Development Community, the
opportunities for IT cooperation between the two countries are immense.
The Indian software industry spearheads the country's export initiatives
in global markets. The association says that one in four international
Fortune 500 companies outsource their IT requirements to Indian software and
services companies.
India is emerging as a major hub for investment by IT companies. The
major Indian IT players in SA are Tata Consultancy, Tata Infotech and Sahara
Computers, with a number of others having a presence in the South African
market.
India also sees opportunities for co-operation in the mining sector, in
both technology and equipment.
India's gold jewellery industry and diamond cutting and polishing sector
are major players in value addition to South African gold and diamonds,
while there are potentially good markets for Indian leather and footwear in
SA.
Rice, wheat and sugar are other areas of export potential for India. In
recent years, India has emerged as one of the largest international
exporters of food grains, especially wheat and rice.
SA imports substantial quantities of rice from India.
The Spices Board of India has been active in promoting trade in spices
by participating in South African food and hotel exhibitions and through
direct contacts with South African traders.
India has skills, technologies and experience in the fields of small and
medium enterprises development and employment generation programmes.
India, which has an office of its National Small Scale Industries
Corporation of India in Johannesburg, envisages providing training to many
potential entrepreneurs, as well as marketing machinery to generate job
opportunities through small businesses.
In the construction arena, South African companies such as Group 5,
Intertoll and Interpark are active in India.
Indian companies such as the Hindustan Construction Company, as well as
the Overseas Construction Council of India, have indicated interest in
co-operating with South African companies.
The UB Group of India is the largest Indian investor in SA. The group
initially invested $20m in a black-owned brewery, National Sorghum
Breweries.
It has also invested $6m in a tourism project, the Mabula Game Lodge,
and has since expanded its operations and investments in SA.
The Tata group's operations in this country consists of three
companies.
Tata Africa Holdings is responsible for bringing in investments to
SA. It is exploring a number of investment projects, particularly in
mining. A major ferrochrome production plant, which Tata plans at Richards
Bay, is at the stage of environmental scrutiny.
Tata Automobiles imports, assembles, distributes and sells Tata trucks
and buses. The third Tata company, Concilience Technologies, is an IT joint
venture between the Tata Consultancy and South Africa's J&J group of
companies.
Indian companies with operations in SA include Ranbaxy; Cipla;
Millennium Pharma; Eicher International; NIIT; Aptech; Interglobe Air
Transport; Sahara Computers; Allied Chemicals; Shalimar Enterprises; Godrej
Sara Lee; SPAT; Grashim; L&T Infotech; Zensar Infotech; Atlas Software
and Zee TV.
Indian public-sector enterprises based in SA include the State Bank of
India; Exim Bank; Bank of Baroda; the National Small Scale Industries
Corporation; and the Government of India Tourist Office.
Shriram Industrial Enterprises has invested 7m in a vehicle
airconditioner manufacturing unit near Durban.

Original_Search_Title: Up Close And Candid: JAK and JJ: Reconciling the Irreconcilable?
Headline: Ghana: Up Close And Candid: JAK and JJ: Reconciling the Irreconcilable?
Body_Text:
"...as far as the apparent differences between President Kufour and
Ex-President Rawlings are concerned, we need to be less schizophrenic and
dig a little bit deeper into the background and philosophy of the two
gentlemen in order to understand why things are what they are now."
When it is said that Ghanaians are peace-loving people it is a fact not
a clichÃ©. It is this preoccupation with maintaining peace that must be at
the core of recent calls by civil society organisations such as the Ghana
Bar Association (GBA) and the Christian Council of Ghana (CCG) for the
current president of the Republic, Mr. John Agyekum Kufour, and the
immediate past president, Mr. J.J. Rawlings, to put aside their differences
and work together for the sake of the nation.
At its last general meeting held in Elmina, the GBA passed a resolution
in which the Association noted "with serious concern the mistrust, animosity
and unhealthy relations existing between His Excellency President John
Agyekum Kufour and Ex - President Rawlings which needed to be tackled. If
the two cannot sit together and take decisions together (sic) then it means
that there is something wrong".
I support any efforts that will consolidate the fledgling democratic
governance in the country.
However as far as the apparent differences between President Kufour and
Ex - President Rawlings are concerned, we need to be less schizophrenic and
dig a little bit deeper into the background and philosophy of the two
gentlemen in order to understand why things are what they are now. Is it
indeed worthwhile to reconcile two people whose worldviews are as different
as day and night? We need to know what the two sons of the soil stand for to
guide us to fashion a strategy to 'reconcile' them.
I do not pretend to have any great insight into or understanding of the
two very important men of our country. We will delve into their
philosophical positions and policies.
Mr. Rawlings by all accounts is a populist, a gun-totting pseudo
revolutionary who burst onto the Ghanaian political scene first in May 1979,
then June 1979 and again December 1981, promising to make Ghana a corrupt
free society.
For good measure and as recent revelations at the National
Reconciliation Commission (NRC) have confirmed, hundreds of innocent
Ghanaians either lost their lives and/or properties/livelihood. His
conversion to multi party democracy since the early 1990s does not hide the
fact that Mr. Rawlings believes in using violence as an instrument to effect
change in society.
Under his administration, especially during the PNDC years, Ghana
recorded one of the worst human rights abuses in her short history. The
abduction and killing of the three judges and the ex Army Officer were duly
calculated to undermine the rule of law and to intimidate those who dared to
challenge his administration.
Mr. Rawlings is miffed that no due recognition is given to what he
perceives as major achievements under his government.
Yes, it is true that between 1981 and 2000 some major programmes were
initiated. For example, the structural adjustment programme (SAP) was aimed
at removing distortions in the economy and positioning the country to embark
on market driven economic development. His own government through reckless
spending and excessive corruption undermined the initial gains, resulting
from the SAP.
There were massive infrastructure development beginning with the
modernisation of the road network in Accra, rehabilitation of some major
roads such as Tema-Akosombo, Kumasi-Sunyani, Kumasi-Mampong,
Yamoransa-Anhwia Kwanta, Anwhia Kwanta - Kumasi, Kintampo-Tamale and
Aflao-Ho.
His administration implemented a radical local government reform
programme. Overall Mr. Rawlings' record is not impressive. There was no
appreciable growth in national wealth and in personal incomes.
So it is utterly strange that Mr. Rawlings will lead a crusade to vilify
the Kufour administration, calling it the worst government the country has
ever had. On what basis did he make such an assertion one may ask? President
Kufour is well educated, suave and politically sophisticated.
He cut his political teeth in his early 20s and has been politically
active even during the rough and tumble of military regimes. He is a liberal
democrat by conviction and subscribes to the principles of the rule of law,
respect for human rights and human dignity and a strong believer in free
enterprise. He is an excellent political strategist who continues to be
under-estimated by his opponents.
He does not pander to populist demands. His decision to implement the
highly indebted poor country programme (HIPC) and the astronomical increase
in the price of petroleum products did not endear him to some Ghanaians. But
those decisions had to be taken to enable the country embark on sustainable
growth and development.
President Kufour's commitment to good democratic governance has led his
administration to initiate an impressive array of reforms such as the repeal
of the Criminal Label Act, the National Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS), the
proposed Freedom of Information Bill and the Whistle Blowers Bill should
further deepen democracy in our country.
The amendment to the Bank of Ghana Act, the proposed Information and
Communications Technology (ICT) policy, the Presidential Initiatives for
Cassava, Salt, Textiles, Sorghum, Cotton, Oil Palm as well as the creation
of the Ministry of Private Sector Development should go a long way in
deepening market reforms and in strengthening the development of free
enterprise and modernisation of the economy generally.
The Kufour administration has initiated several infrastructure projects,
including road construction such as Tema-Sogakope, Accra-Kumasi, Tetteh
Quarshie interchange, Circle- Ofankor six lane road and many others. And
very soon work will start on Mallam- Kasoa-Yamoransa road.
Mr. Rawlings' method of governance may have worked in the 1980s and
parts of 1990s but it has long been discredited. He ought to accept that he
has played his part. He should therefore sit back and allow others to take
the country to another level of development.
This is the sort of message the GBA and the CCG should carry to him!

Original_Search_Title: Maize Harvest Set to Increase
Headline: Zimbabwe: Maize Harvest Set to Increase
Body_Text:
Harare — The Government, in conjunction with private organisations, has launched
an operation code-named Gutsaruzhinji to ensure more than 3,5 million tonnes
of maize are harvested in the 2003/2004 season.
Under the operation, the Government will share with private
organisations the provision of seed, fertiliser and tillage services to
farmers.
The Cabinet Taskforce on Agricultural Inputs Supply and Distribution
recently went throughout the country accompanied by various private
organisations to assess the needs of farmers.
The chairman of the taskforce, Cde Ignatius Chombo, told The Herald last
week that farmers had the potential to produce more than 3,5 million tonnes
of maize if they were given enough assistance.
The farmer' success in meeting the target would also depend on the
availability of rain.
However, the Department of Meteorological Services has predicted a good
rainy season.
Cde Chombo said at least 5 400 tonnes of maize seed had already been
distributed to farmers as a first step to achieve the targeted harvest.
The maize seed was part of the 9 000 tons that was recently acquired by
the Government under the input distribution scheme.
More seed was expected to be purchased from the Sadc region using the
US$30 million ($24,7 billion) set aside for that purpose.
Cde Chombo said the Govern-ment had acquired several thousand tonnes of
fertiliser that was being distributed to farmers.
At least US$2 million was advanced to Sable Chemicals to boost the
company's capacity to import more fertiliser from countries such as South
Africa.
Cde Chombo said the District Development Fund was given $2 billion for
the repair of tractors to ensure land for A1 and communal farmers was tilled
in time.
"The message we want to send to the farmers is that it is now the time
to plough and we expect them to be busy ploughing," he said.
"Each province has targets set for the crop output and we expect them to
achieve the targets given enough rains."
The expected maize output would ensure there is enough food to cover at
least two years since the country consumes nearly 1,8 million tonnes of
maize each year.
The more than three million tonnes of maize were expected to be
harvested by farmers in the A1 and A2 schemes, communal farmers and others
in different resettlement schemes.
Farmers had already promised they would plant more than three million
hectares of maize only if they got enough seed, fertiliser and tillage
services.
Cde Chombo said A2 farmers would not get tillage assistance from the DDF
as they would apply for loans under the $60 billion facility from the
Agricultural Develop-ment Bank.
He said the operation to produce higher yields would be extended to
other crops with a targeted harvest of 400 000 tons of wheat and 450 000
tons of sorghum this season.
Each province was given a target of the maize harvest depending upon
indications of the number of hectares likely to be planted.
Mashonaland West was expected to plant at least 592 000 hectares of
maize, Mashonaland Central 437 218 ha, Mashonaland East 618 000 ha, Masvingo
460 000 ha and Manicaland 432 000 ha.
Matabeleland North would plant 234 000 ha, Matabeleland South 266 000 ha
and Midlands 486 000 ha.
Zimbabwe's maize production dropped to 1,54 million tonnes in 2001 from
2,1 million tonnes in 2000 because of drought and floods.
But the projected harvest for this season could be achieved considering
that the Department of Meteorological Services has predicted a good rainy
season.
Newly resettled farmers have pledged to plant more hectares of maize to
ensure the country becomes self sufficient in food.
The importation of maize seed would help in reducing the deficit of 50
000 tons that was expected because of the high demand created by new farmers
resettled under the land reform programme.
Non-governmental organisations were expected to chip in and import seed
for distribution.

Original_Search_Title: Experts Worried Over Strange Disease
Headline: Nigeria: Experts Worried Over Strange Disease
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ten-year-old Susannah Jackson is dying of what may be the world's newest and oddest disease. An illness so rare and mysterious that science has not yet come up with an official name for it.
Sitting outside her family's mud hut, near the small town of Lui, Sudan, Susannah is gripped by a series of brain seizures which force her neck to arch forwards, down, and then up again.
The disease is puzzling to doctors and scientists. No wonder people in this isolated corner of southern Sudan call it "nodding disease."
"We have no clue as what is causing this. It's like a detective novel and a murder mystery, because it's fatal," Dr Mickey Richer, a tropical disease specialist from UNICEF said.
So far, almost 300 children are known to have caught the disease in one small region of the country.
Bizarrely, the seizures normally occur when the sufferers start to eat, or when it is particularly cold.
When Richer asked for a bowl of sorghum to be placed in front of Susannah, the "nodding" began almost immediately, and stopped when she finished eating.
Curiously, Susannah does not react if she eats unfamiliar food such as a chocolate bar for instance.
Susannah's eight-year-old brother, Jacob, is now in Lui's crowded hospital with the same disease at a more developed stage.
He shows signs of being mentally retarded and physically stunted.
During a particularly fierce seizure he threw himself into a fire, and is now being treated for severe burns on his leg.
Two other children in the same ward have almost identical stories.
An hour's drive from Lui along a rutted track lies the village of Amadi, a silent cluster of huts trapped in a forest of thick green grass.
Amadi is now considered to be the epicentre of the disease - 12 per cent of children here are affected.
About 12% of children in Ramadi are affected. Last year, experts from the World Health Organisation did neurological scans on some of the children, enabling them to confirm that this is a specific and unique condition.
Twelve-year-old Ruben Nicholas stands in the middle of the village, gripped by a sudden seizure.
It looks as though someone is forcing his chin down onto his chest.
"He is nodding three times a day," says his father, Nicholas Lado.
"The disease stops him from growing. His brother died from the same disease, in August."
So what could be causing this horrific outbreak? If this had happened in western Europe, it seems likely there would be at least some answers by now. Here, in poverty-stricken southern Sudan, families still do not know if they should be trying to quarantine their children. Courtesy: bbc.com

Original_Search_Title: Aid Focus Shifts to Mitigate Impact of HIV/Aids
Headline: Eswatini: Aid Focus Shifts to Mitigate Impact of HIV/Aids
Body_Text:
Mbabane — The impact of HIV/AIDS on Swaziland's agricultural production has forced
aid agencies to adjust their programming in a bid to mitigate the effects on
food security.
"Food shortages in Swaziland are compounded by the country's high
HIV/AIDS adult prevalence rate, and it is therefore essential that the
special nutritional needs of vulnerable people are met," said Angela Van
Rynbach, World Food Programme (WFP) Country Representative.
WFP's operation in Swaziland is part of a regional emergency operation
in Southern Africa that also covers Zimbabwe, Malawi, Zambia, Mozambique and
Lesotho.
"I think it's clear to everybody by now that you can't separate food
from AIDS," added Sarah Laughton, WFP Emergency Coordinator in
Swaziland.
"Ever since I arrived, there has been a back and forth whether WFP
should remain here. Because Swaziland is ranked as a lower-middle-income
country, we would normally not be here," she told IRIN.
Although, on paper, Swazis should have the collective wealth to purchase
their own emergency food relief supplies, in reality two-thirds of the
population lives in chronic poverty, according to a recent country profile
by the United Nations Development Programme.
Swaziland's rating by the World Bank as a lower-middle-income country,
which should separate the kingdom from the truly poorest nations, is based
on gross domestic product. But Swaziland has one of the world's largest
divides between rich and poor, with considerable wealth concentrated in the
royal family.
Aid workers have criticised income averaging to determine the nation's
qualification for assistance, and stressed that a more accurate measure of
need is the number of poor and foodless Swazis.
Currently, 156,750 Swazis are provided with food aid rations from WFP,
up from 132,000 in July. As stored food from last year's harvest is
depleted, an estimated 217,000 Swazis will need food assistance by January,
before the summer crops can be harvested.
About 40,000 additional Swazis receive food assistance through feeding
schemes and nutritional supplements for pregnant women distributed at
clinics. In total, about a quarter of the nation's population will require
assistance by early next year.
WFP is close to ensuring its presence in the country after the current
emergency operation is concluded, and will likely include Swaziland in a
three-year Protracted Relief and Recovery Operation (PRRO), said
Laughton. "The goal is to move away from emergency assistance, and toward
food self-sufficiency."
Neighbouring Mozambique will be included in the PRRO project, as well as
Lesotho, Malawi and Zambia.
AIDS is the main reason WFP will remain in Swaziland. Weather forecasts
predict average rainfall that could break the cycle of drought which has
devastated smallholder farmers on communal Swazi Nation Land - where 80
percent of the population lives under palace-appointed chiefs.
"If it does not rain, our crops die. There is no money for irrigation
and pumps. Fortunately, there are some rains now, and we are planting,"
Mandla Hlatswako, an HIV positive farmer in the rural Luve area north of
Manzini, told IRIN.
But Hlatswako says there will be no one to weed or harvest his fields if
he is incapacitated by illness.
Harvest yields of maize, the national staple food, and other crops are
expected to decline for the fifth year. The loss of agricultural workers due
to AIDS in the commercial sugar and citrus industries has had a negative
effect, but small family farms are hardest hit.
"First the father, and then the mother, dies of AIDS - we have more
child-headed households in the rural lands. The grannies may survive, but
they cannot work the fields. The older children drop out of school, but they
are not able to farm as well as adults. This is why harvests were smaller,
even where there were adequate rains last year," said Albertina Nyatsi of
the Swaziland AIDS Support Organisation.
The national cereal supply for 2003/04 is estimated to be 78,100 mt,
about a third of the 205,800 mt required. Food relief agencies will provide
24,300 mt, and 127,700 mt of food will have to be imported
commercially.
This week, WFP received what it called a generous donation of US $2.2
million in food and cash from the Swaziland government.
"This is the first donation Swaziland has ever made to WFP. It will
cover procurement, distribution and monitoring of a total of 5,952 mt of
food," said Van Rynbach.
Aid will be targeted at the most vulnerable, such as orphans and the
elderly, in the parched central and eastern regions.
"The days of massive distribution of bulk food are over," said
Laughton. "We are seeking ways to link our activities to HIV/AIDS."
Working with government's National Emergency Relief Committee on
HIV/AIDS (NERCHA), WFP is assisting 40 chieftaincies in the eastern drought
belt to cultivate fields. The harvests will feed orphans in the areas, most
of whose parents have died of AIDS.
Chiefs are being enlisted in education programmes to promote
non-traditional edible crops that are drought-resistant, like sorghum,
cowpeas, and cassava.
Food assistance is going to neighbourhood care points in impoverished
townships, as well as 59 clinics in the eastern Lubombo and southern
Shiselweni districts, where food rations go to pregnant and lactating women,
and undernourished children under five years of age.
"This is all useful, but for now it's limited. Under the new PRRO, we'll
be expanding. We'll see how we can assist with home-based care programmes
for people with AIDS, and provide for the clinics," Laughton said.
Government's National Emergency Relief Task Force, which provides WFP
with data on Swazis in need and carries out its own food relief work, is
also changing its tactics.
"Like the WFP, we are looking beyond the weather, and considering the
long-term impact of AIDS. The effect is the same: fewer cultivated fields,
inferior harvests, less food, and hundreds of thousands of vulnerable
people," said Ben Nsibandze, director of the task force.

Original_Search_Title: Agribank Disburses $60bn Loan Facility
Headline: Zimbabwe: Agribank Disburses $60bn Loan Facility
Body_Text:
Harare — THE $60 billion agricultural facility extended by the Government to the
Agricultural Development Bank of Zimbabwe (Agribank) to finance this year's
agricultural season, is now being disbursed throughout the country's
provinces, bank officials said yesterday.
Agribank chief executive Mr Taka Mutunhu said the financial institution,
which was successfully converted from a commercial bank to a land bank, has
already started disbursing the funds.
"As part of the new bank's mandate, $60billion would be made available
as production finance for at least four crops.
"$40 billion has been set aside to finance maize while $15 billion has
been allocated for cotton input support scheme.
"A further $5 billion is being availed for sorghum and millet
production," he said.
The funds were available for all sectors in agriculture and distribution
would be done through the bank's three administrative regions in all the
provinces.
"Farmers from A1, A2, small-scale commercial, communal and old
resettlement sectors who are interested in production of any of the above
crops can now approach their nearest Agribank office with their
applications," said Mr Mutunhu.
The financial institution has been tasked by the Government under the
National Economic Revival Programme to provide all forms of agricultural
credit to farmers at concessionary rates.
Special focus is being put on newly resettled farmers who, for the past
two years, have failed to access funding from established banks and
financial institutions.
Although efforts to get a comment from the Minister of Lands,
Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, Dr Joseph Made, proved fruitless
yesterday, authoritative sources said the Government was satisfied with the
transformation of the former commercial bank into a land bank.
"In fact, the transformation of Agribank was long overdue. We expect the
bank to play a pivotal role in bankrolling the agricultural sector," said an
official.
The financial institution's facility comes barely a few weeks after the
corporate sector pumped in a whopping $230 billion into the agricultural
sector.
Tobacco Sales Limited has availed $50 billion while National Foods
Limited and Innscor Africa Holdings Limited contributing $90 billion each
for contract growers involved in tobacco, maize and wheat production.
Identification of contract buyers who will participate under the three
schemes is already in progress.
There have been calls for other players in the private sector to provide
additional lines of credit to newly resettled farmers.
Farming organisations such as the Indigenous Commercial Farmers Union
(ICFU) and the Zimbabwe Farmers Union (ZFU) have, in the past, urged the
Government to put more financial resources towards the agricultural
sector.
The president of the Indigenous Commercial Farmers Union, Mr Davison
Mugabe said at least $600 billion was required to fully finance this year's
cropping season.
The Grain Marketing Board has also started disbursing at least $45
billion for the crop and livestock programme, which was allocated under the
recently approved Government's supplementary budget.
Support schemes have also been set up for crop and livestock development
programmes in a bid to increase productivity in both communal and newly
resettled farming areas.
Syfrets Corporate and Merchant Bank is also involved in the mobilisation
of funds for the agricultural sector through the floatation of agro-
bills.

Original_Search_Title: NOTAP Proposes Solution to Petroleum, Agric Products Shortage
Headline: Nigeria: NOTAP Proposes Solution to Petroleum, Agric Products Shortage
Body_Text:
The National Office for Technology Acquisition and Promotion (NOTAP) has
proposed solution to petroleum and agricultural products shortage in
Nigeria.
In a statement issued to Business Day, Engineer O.E. Essien of NOTAP
said that lack of abundant supply of petroleum for transportation and
generation of electricity and enough agricultural products is due to lack of
political will by government.
He added that all other stakeholders especially the private sector need
to invest abundantly in agriculture to produce raw materials for alternative
petroleum products bio-fuel food for human consumption.
Engineer O.E. Essien who is a deputy director in NOTAP made this
assertion in a paper he presented titled "Commercialisation of liquid
Biofuel Technologies; a case study of Nigeria" at a seminar organised by
NOTAP headquarters in Abuja recently.
He noted that there is a need for government and all stakeholders in
technology development to focus their attention to huge capital investment,
development and commercialisation of liquid biofuel as it is done in some
countries like Brazil, USA and Canada.
He explained that liquid Biofuel is a non-gasline and fossil fuel of
petroleum products derived from complicated processing of botanical and
biological substances or allied products like agric residues, grains,
tubers, sugarcane short hardwoods, oil seeds and animal fats etcetera.
He added that these liquid biofuels include fuel ethanol and biodiesel
from any vegetable oil, yellow grease or animal fat that can fuel a diesel
engine according to data published by AG Biotech in 2002.
According to him, the most vital and popular raw materials for biodiesel
include Canolas, Soya beans and palm oil, emphasising that these raw
materials could be abundantly produced in Nigeria to meet the human
consumption and industrial demands like the production of biofuel.
He said that the raw materials for production of fuel ethanol also
include, corn, maize and sorghum, which are largely produced in Nigeria,
adding that the bye-products such as lignin, chemicals, fusels oils glycerol
are of economic value and can be used to increase return on investment.
He called on government to design policies and legislation in this
regard, pointing out that opportunities or creation of employment is the
ultimate in this venture.

Original_Search_Title: Mbeya Sorghum Farmers Find Market in Local Brewery
Headline: Tanzania: Mbeya Sorghum Farmers Find Market in Local Brewery
Body_Text:
THE brewers of a traditional beer in Mbeya municipality, the Mbeya
Traditional Beer Breweries Ltd (MTBL), have created a market for sorghum,
Business Times has learned.
The hardy crop - which many farmers refused to grow as it had never had
a reliable market in the region - has finally found sustained use in the
brewing of Mwamba beer.
A survey by Business Times has shown that most peasants in the region
are only slowly becoming aware of this good news. The few who were
interviewed in the survey also confirmed that sorghum farming is now bound
to be profitable.
As a farm crop, sorghum is resistant to adverse weather.
In an exclusive interview with the MTBL plant manager, Sichilima
Kazonda, it was found out that the new brewery, which commenced production
only a month ago, uses a mixture of white sorghum (70 per cent) and white
maize (30 per cent) to brew Mwamba. Kazonda pointed out that the plant is
producing only
one brand of beer at the moment: Mwamba - ki-Swahili for a rock. The
product is of a high quality, is hygienically brewed and has qualified for
TBS standardisation.
Incipient production is currently 2,000 litres a day, although the
brewery has the capacity to produce 20,000 litres per day at the full
swing.
In that regard, the manager urges farmers
In the Southern Highlands region to capitalise on this opportunity and
plant sorghum with a will. The company pays
Tsh150 for a kilogramme of sorghum delivered.
Apart from providing a ready market for sorghum, the brewery has created
more working opportunities in the locality. And, a hygienic product is more
readily accessible to consumers who had in the past been relying upon beer
brewed in backyards under dubious health and environmental conditions.
The venture provides direct employment on the premises, and indirect
employment to wholesalers and retailers.

Original_Search_Title: Drought Affects 85 Percent of Crops in Shinyanga District
Headline: Tanzania: Drought Affects 85 Percent of Crops in Shinyanga District
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Close to 85 percent of maize, sorghum and groundnut crops in Tanzanian's
northern district of Shinyanga has been affected by drought, leaving
thousands of people without enough to feed their families for the next six
months, according to the Presbyterian World Service & Development
agency.
"The ongoing drought is expected to further diminish pastures and water
sources and consequently affect livestock production," the agency, a
Canadian development and disaster relief body, reported on Wednesday.
The Canadian agency said it had been supporting agriculture and water
programmes in the district together with the Africa Inland Church of
Tanzania. The Canadian agency said as part of a coordinated effort to
provide food relief in the region, it and the Canadian Foodgrains Bank were
helping the Tanzanian agency target the villages of Buganika, Ngwigumbi,
Wigelekelo, Wishiteleja, Masanga, Ngwanhalanga and Bulekela for food aid
programmes.
"Many families are down to one meal a day," the Canadian agency
reported. "Children have stopped going to school. Families are selling
whatever they have to buy food. Some young girls are being forced to marry
into wealthy households so that their families can buy food with their
dowries. Others are turning to prostitution."
It said that crop production for the next farming season would also be
affected, as people eat the seeds intended for planting, and travel to other
areas to look for work instead of working their own farms.
Therefore, it said the Tanzanian agency would provide maize and beans to
10,000 people, particularly targeting children and pregnant women. The
beneficiaries will receive 12 kg of maize per month/person for six
months. Women and children will also receive 1.4 kg of beans per
month/person for six months. The food distributions will occur in October
and December 2003, and February 2004.
In addition, the Canadian agency reported, maize and sorghum seeds would
be distributed to 1,200 drought-affected farmers so fields in the seven
target communities -- approximately 1,000 acres - could be replanted. Every
family will receive 4 kg of maize and sorghum seeds.
Some two million people face food insecurity in Tanzania due to poor and
