required to carry households through to the next harvest was expected to be
covered by the Government's flood victims aid as well as during the next
drought aid scheme.
In Western Caprivi, even though some fields were apparently destroyed by
wild animals during last season, no assistance of any kind was received by
the victims.
The report said that during the entire drought relief programme (from
November 2002 through June 2003), food relief was only received twice in
that part of the Caprivi Region, once in December 2002 and in February
2003.
In Eastern Caprivi, substantial crop losses occurred mainly due to
drought while disastrous flooding aggravated the food situation.
Flood waters in Eastern Caprivi have already subsided, with land
preparations anticipated to commence around August/September 2003.
Newfis said Government was set to deliver about 90 tonnes of maize seed
and 10 tonnes of sorghum seed.
Earlier, about 30 tonnes of maize seed and 10 tonnes of sorghum seed was
dropped off at Katima Mulilo.

Original_Search_Title: Areas of Food Shortfalls Persist Due to Fighting
Headline: Central Africa: Areas of Food Shortfalls Persist Due to Fighting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Despite generally favourable growing conditions, areas of food
shortfalls persist throughout the Great Lakes region due largely to
hostilities in certain areas, the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation said
on Thursday in its latest "Foodcrops and Shortages" report.
In Burundi, FAO said that harvesting of 2003 second-season food crops,
mainly sorghum and beans, was well advanced. A good bean crop was
anticipated in the main growing regions of Kirundo, Muyinga and Ruyigi, in
the north and east of the country, where prices of beans have declined by
about one-half with the arrival of the new harvest into the
markets. However, in other regions, excessive rains and hail damaged the
late-planted bean and sorghum crops in May, and by insufficient rains
earlier in the season.
FAO said that the provinces worst affected by localised crop losses were
Kirundo, Muyinga, Karuzi, Cankuzo, Makamba, Mwaro, Kayanza and Muramvya. The
agency said that food aid was being provided to populations affected by an
escalation in civil conflict in April and May.
In the Democratic Republic of the Congo, FAO said that harvesting of the
2003 second-season food crops was underway in eastern and northeastern
regions. However, favourable growing conditions notwithstanding, production
was expected to be sharply reduced following the escalation of the civil war
in these areas, mainly in Ituri District. In mid-June, FAO and the UN World
Food Programme jointly approved an emergency operation to assist 483,000 of
the most affected people in the northern and eastern parts of the country,
including the internally displaced, returnees from neighbouring countries
and other vulnerable groups. The operation, to last six months starting
mid-June, would provide 32,236 mt of cereals and 9,934 mt of beans.
In Rwanda, FAO said that harvesting of the 2003 second-season food
crops, mainly sorghum and beans, was underway. A satisfactory harvest was
anticipated following abundant rains in late April and May, which improved
conditions for crops previously affected by erratic
precipitation. Production of Irish potatoes and bananas was also expected to
be normal. However, reduced crops have been harvested in the Bugesera region
of Kigali Rural Province, where rains were insufficient and the food
situation was likely to deteriorate in coming months. Localised crop losses
are also reported in parts of Rukara District, in Umutara Province,
northeastern Rwanda.
In Tanzania, FAO warned that prolonged drought conditions in several
parts of the country had affected a large number of households, with an
estimated 1.9 million people in need of food assistance. [See IRIN story,
"Government appeals for 45,000 mt of maize", at
http://www.irinnews.org/report.asp?ReportID=36197]
In Uganda, FAO reported that the humanitarian situation in northern and
eastern parts of the country continued to worsen due to escalation of
conflict. It said that recent fighting between government forces and rebels
had displaced more than 820,000 people, bringing the total number in need of
emergency assistance to more than 1.6 million people.
Meanwhile, in the Central African Republic, FAO said the food security
situation was still precarious, and that food production was not expected to
increase this year due to reduced planting and seed shortages, following
population displacement caused by an October 2002 to March 2003
rebellion.
"Foodcrops and Shortages" is issued five times per year by FAO's Global
Information and Early Warning System on Food and Agriculture, with special
attention to countries that face exceptional food shortages or where the
prospects for current crops are unfavourable. If emergency interventions are
underway, details of the total numbers of beneficiaries and emergency food
aid requirements are also provided. [For the complete report, go to
http://www.fao.org/WAICENT/faoinfo/economic/giews/english/fs/fstoc.htm]

Original_Search_Title: Zambia Breweries Wants Sorghum
Headline: Zambia Breweries Wants Sorghum
Body_Text:
Kampala — BEER breweries in Tanzania and Zambia are interested in buying the
recently developed Epuripur sorghum variety. Nile Breweries Limited (NBL)
uses it to make the Eagle Lager brand.
Ian Mackintosh, the NBL technical director, said on Friday, "They have
seen our example of using the sorghum and want us to export it to them."
Mackintosh said Tanzania Breweries Limited and Zambia Breweries Limited
had made feelers to NBL over the possibility of a deal.
He said however that NBL would first have to consider their own
requirements before making a decision.
"If the crop harvest is good we may look at it (exports) and if not, we
shall keep it for ourselves," Mackintosh said.
Epuripur sorghum was developed by Serere Animal and Agricultural
Research Institute in Soroti. NBL later found it suitable for beer brewing
and contracted farmers in the Teso region to grow it.
Last year, a host of farmers took up the challenge and to date Epuripur
sorghum is been grown in 13 districts.
Mackintosh said although 5,000 tonnes of the sorghum was expected to be
bought from the farmers this season, about half of that may be unavailable
for use due to the present insecurity in the region.
Last month, the Lord's Resistance Army rebels crossed into Teso region
from northern Uganda and displaced several thousand people.
NBL sources said out of the 12,000 acres planted during the prevailing
season, Teso region had cultivated about 6,900 acres.

Original_Search_Title: President Museveni Okays Genetically-Modified Organisms (GMO) Foods
Headline: Uganda: President Museveni Okays Genetically-Modified Organisms (GMO) Foods
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has allowed the importation of non-contestable
forms of Genetically-Modified Organisms (GMOs).
He said foods that have been processed could be brought into the country
because they cannot contaminate native plants.
GMO plants or animals contain genes which have been artificially. The
highly contestable GMOs include living organisms such as seeds or plants
that could alter native plants through cross-pollination.
Museveni was presiding over the launch of a multi-million-biotech lab at
the Kawanda Research Institute last Saturday.
Museveni, who was speaking publicly about the controversial GMOs for the
first time, said studies and debate on the living GMOs should go ahead.
He said experts should isolate characters from wild plants through
biotechnology (manipulation of genes) to make crops with high yields and
disease resistance.
He cited clonal coffee, which gives five times as much yield as the
traditional coffee, as a break-through which has increased production and
incomes of farmers.
Kisamba Mugerwa, the Minister of Agriculture, said commercial releases
of GMOs had become controversial because of their potential risks.
Mugerwa said biotechnology would enhance food security, especially in
the poor countries.
He said there were suspicions that biotechnology applications had
potential threats to bio-diversity.
Museveni said George Bush, the US president, had urged him to take on
GMOs during his visit.
Museveni said he pointed out to Bush that he had to study the GMOs more
before he took a position.
"I am now fully moblised to accept biotechnology," Museveni said amid
applause from the staff of the National Agricultural Research Organisation
(NARO).
Museveni lauded NARO for making over 600 crop varieties including
sorghum, which has replaced barley in the making of beer.
But he warned against abuse of biotechnology, saying the Government was
against GMOs becoming a problem.
Mugerwa said the Government was in the process of formulating a policy
to regulate biotechnology and address biosafety concerns.
Mugerwa, while quoting Maxwell Mauder, a researcher, said genetically
modified crops, which cover 52.6 million hectares of land globally, were
fast becoming a major component of agriculture.
"The reality is that biotechnology cannot be wished away or ignored,"
Mugerwa said.
Prof. Kayanja, the NARO chief, said research would dispel the fears
about bio-technology.

Original_Search_Title: US $2.6 million Needed to Restore Livelihoods, FAO says
Headline: Central African Republic: US $2.6 million Needed to Restore Livelihoods, FAO says
Body_Text:
The UN Food and Agriculture Organization
(FAO) has appealed for US $2.6 million to distribute farming, livestock and
fishing inputs to vulnerable populations in the Central African Republic
before the start of the next planting season in April 2003, the agency
announced on Monday.
It said more money was urgently needed for the Organization to help
improve the livelihoods of some 210,000 farmers, fishers and livestock
owners, who had been affected by continuing civil unrest in recent
years.
Since 2001, in collaboration with other UN agencies, non-governmental
organizations (NGO's), and more recently with Italian funding, FAO has
implemented two projects aimed at assisting communities whose agricultural
activities have been disrupted by the civil unrest in the country.
In June 2003, as part of its Technical Cooperation Programmes, FAO said
it approved two more projects to assist the country's agriculture and
livestock sector.
An FAO assessment in May and June estimated that 30 percent of the 2003
cassava crop had been lost due to civil strife. "Civil unrest keeps people
from working in their fields," FAO said.
With farmers' normal food reserves depleted, FAO said that an estimated
60 percent of this year's corn, rice and sorghum crop may also have been
lost through the consumption of seeds by the population since the Agronomic
Research Institute and its seed centres were looted.
"In addition, there has also been an increase in the number of children
suffering from diseases and malnutrition," the Rome-based food agency
said.
Several parts of the country were occupied by various armed factions
leading to the ostensible division of the Central African Republic into
three regions.
"This virtual partition has serious humanitarian implications for
2,200,000 people who have been either directly, or indirectly affected by
the conflict," the agency reported.
The three new FAO project proposals for the Central African Republic are
designed to help people resume their normal productive activities in order
to meet their basic food needs and to increase the long-term self-reliance
of households and communities in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Thanks Teso Residents for Action Against LRA
Headline: Uganda: Well Done On LRA, Teso
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni on Wednesday addressed residents of Teso region on The Voice of Teso radio about the recent invasion of the region by rebels of the Lords Resistance Army (LRA). Below, we reproduce the Presidents full address
"I greet you all the people of Teso. Yoga. First of all, I am very sorry about the suffering you have gone through in the last two months on the account of thugs of Kony who have been causing so much damage to the people of northern Uganda and they came here to try to spread their killing.
As I told you, the other day, at a public rally, these bandits' coming to Teso was not because they were growing in strength. It was actually because they were losing in Acholi. They therefore thought that if they came to Teso and Lango that should force us to de-concentrate the forces we have concentrated in Acholi area.
We have got two whole divisions there reinforced by a lot of LDUs. These are the 4th and 5th divisions. These are quite large army groups. These are permanently dealing with the Kony problem in that area. They (rebels) thought that by coming to Teso and Lango, we would re-deploy or deploy away the 4th and 5th division from the north into Teso and other areas of southern Uganda. We did not do that, because we brought other forces from other parts of the country. These are the ones we have deployed.
Fortunately, for the case of Teso, we had a very strong support from the people. We also had the support from the Arrow Group; meaning volunteers. Of course the terrain in Teso is also good. It is not sustainable for banditry activities against a well-organised force, which UPDF is as of now.
Unlike the first insurgency in Teso in the 1980s, the UPDF now is much better equipped than it was at that time. So there is no way anybody can sustain an insurgency in an area like Teso because the terrain is open. It has no mountain and has no forests. It is not possible for anybody to sustain an insurgency here. Therefore these bandits thought they could come and disturb Teso as they have been disturbing Acholi. It shows how bankrupt they are. They are not even able to plan for their criminal activities.
The army was of course not here in Teso when these people made their incursion. We didn't have UPDF units in this area. We had only some LDUs and some ASTU in Obalanga and some ASTU in Kapelebyong. These are the ones, who initially did not perform well. However, as we brought in more and more troops, it was clear that it would take us a short time to clear these bandits especially using the support of the "wananchi". That is exactly what is happening now. After a few encounters, first of all with the air cover units of the air force, mainly the helicopters that we have been attacking these bandits in a number of places like Abarilela and some other places.
You see I am beginning to learn some of these names of these places where the helicopter managed to get the bandits. Then some of the infantry battalions have also got in touch with these bandits. Like the other day; a few days ago, one of our battalions, the17th battalion which is normally based in the 2nd division in Mbarara; but which we have now deployed in this area got in touch with these bandits ata place called Apopong. Apopong is to the northeast of Amuria towards Adacar.
They really gave them (bandits) a good beating. The journalists were yesterday (Tuesday) taken to see the 31 bodies of the bandits who remained there, including some of their leaders. But many many many went with injuries. Some of them will die. And there were also a few other contacts. I mean clashes. That is what we call it in military language a contact. But you should not take it in the ordinary sense. We had contacts in a few other places and as a consequence of these, all the bandits have fled Teso areas. By yesterday (Tuesday) all the organised groups of the bandits had fled the Teso areas.
They were in Otuke in Lango and some of them are even nearing Pader. I have heard of a few splinter groups, which got separated, from these main groups when we attacked them. like I heard of a group that is in the direction of Kapelebyong. They were about 10.
I also heard of another group. They were a few people near Orungo. But there is even a group north of Amugu. That is in Lango now, which yesterday ambushed some of our soldiers who were a bit careless. But otherwise the bandits have fled. It will not be easy for them to come back. If they come back, they will die. That is what I am here to ensure. That any bandit who comes to Teso will die because it is easy to operate in this area because of the clear terrain.
Therefore in about a week, I will be coming to you or through the division commander; I will be able to tell the people to go back to their villages. However, I want you to give us about a week from now so that we put in more forces in the area. We want to put in more forces and more equipment so that once the people go back to their villages; the terrorists do not disturb them again.
I would like to thank the people of Teso for their maturity and for their strong support for the army. This is an example of patriotism and commonsense. When you allow bandits to infiltrate your area you ruin your own development opportunities.
The people of Teso since the other insurgency have started making big strides to develop their area. I can see the Government has built a lot of schools and has brought a lot of boreholes. But more importantly, the people themselves have started developing homestead incomes.
I hear you now grow sorghum called Epuripur. That sorghum that was developed by our researchers in Serere and this is now being used in making modern beer. This beer in the past used to be made from (pause).. What do they call them. sorghum there in Europe..called.. Eerr.. barley. So the people of Teso are now saving us from squandering money in importing barley to be used in making beer, because it can be made from sorghum. This we had known all along, but it is good you have implemented it. But what is important is that it brings incomes to your homes.
The people of Teso started growing potatoes and sending them to Kampala and they got money out of that. I even saw somebody who built a very good hotel in Kumi town from potatoes.
That is why I stopped the other day when I was coming here. I stopped to wash my hands and have a rest. It is a very nice hotel. The people of Teso have also grown fruits.
I visited a number of people. One in Katakwi and another one here in Serere who is growing fruits. And incidentally, this can be a lot of money; because fruits, especially the oranges have got not only the regional market but attracts international markets as well. Now that man, like the one I visited on Serere side is getting quite a bit of money from oranges. Therefore the people of Teso are making very big strides and I am not surprised that they do not want to be disturbed by fools like Kony. People who do not have any purpose; killers; criminals like Idi Amin who died the other day. We are going to ensure that these criminals are crushed once and for all.
We are going to mobilise the Arrow Group into battalions. We shall be having one battalion in every three sub-counties in the sub-counties, which are affected. But if it becomes necessary, we can even have a bigger number.
There is a lot of enthusiasm to join this battalion of LDUs, which we now call the Arrow Group. But we have to think of the budget. We must give them some uniform, we must give them guns and we shall give them some allowances. They cannot work for nothing. They have to get allowances. Because of that we shall have controlled numbers.
But we have selected a number of sub-counties, which could be vulnerable to banditry, when they come back to these areas or have been vulnerable to the Karimojong rustlers who have also been disturbing the border sub-counties in Katakwi. All these we shall arm these LDU battalions so that they protect the people against cattle raiders from Karamoja and also in case some of the bandits try to come back; although they (LDUs) are not going to be alone.
There is also going to be a strong contingent of UPDF here with mobile elements including tanks, which will never allow these bandits to come to this area again. I myself after staying here (in Soroti) for a few days, I will move on to follow the track of those bandits and ensure that we eliminate them from Uganda completely. I would also like to inform you that we have forces in Gulu and Pader. In fact Gulu is now quite peaceful because of the work of the 4th division, the whole division of the army reinforced by the LDUs in that area. The problem has been with Pader. There had been quite some activity there, including Kitgum. We are now going to concentrate in those areas after we have cleared Teso and Lango.
The purpose of my talking to you today was to tell you that I hope you will be moving back to your homes in a week.
The bandits are largely cleared out of the Teso area as I speak.
But in case there are some few remaining and in order to allow us to bring more equipment to the area and to ensure that we guard against re-infestation of the area, I would like you to give us a week so that we can completely ensure the safety of this area. Eyalama noi. Thank you very much.
'You let bandits infiltrate your area, you ruin your development opportunities'

Original_Search_Title: Museveni to Flush Kony Out of Teso in One Week
Headline: Uganda: Museveni to Flush Kony Out of Teso in One Week
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni (right) has asked the people of Teso to give
him only one week to flush out LRA rebels from the region.
He said the people displaced by the rebels should be able to return to
their homes within a week, report Kenneth Oluka and Nathan Etengu.
"I would want you to give me just one week so that we put in more forces
to guard against a re-infestation of the area by the bandits," Museveni said
in a 10-minute address on Voice of Teso radio station.
He did not take any questions. He said all the organised rebel groups
had by Tuesday fled towards Otuke in Lira and Pader.
He said they had suffered heavy losses during battles with the UPDF and
the Arrow Group, a local militia.
"The area, as I speak now, is free of bandits. But in case there are a
few remnants and in order to allow us bring in more equipment to the area
and ensure we are guarded against a re-infestation of the area, I would like
you to give us a week so that we can ensure the safety of this area,"
Museveni said.
He said he was in Teso to ensure that any bandit who tried to
destabilise it was killed. "In a week's time, I will be coming back to you
and I will be able to tell the people to go back to their villages,"
Museveni said.
He said the Teso terrain was conducive for fighting any form of
insurgency. Museveni said more troops were to be deployed in the region. He
said the Government would support the recruitment of more volunteers to join
the Arrow Group.
He said the people of Teso had started making big strides to develop
their area. He thanked the Iteso for developing homestead incomes
particularly for undertaking the cultivation of the Epuripur sorghum used in
the brewing of Eagle beer.

Original_Search_Title: Food Crop Prospects in the Sahel Mixed, FAO Says
Headline: West Africa: Food Crop Prospects in the Sahel Mixed, FAO Says
Body_Text:
Abidjan — Food crop prospects in nine West African Sahelian countries are mixed with delayed onset of rainfall in Cape Verde, large-scale outbreak of grasshoppers in Guinea-Bissau, grain-eating birds in Mali and desert locusts in Niger, the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) reported last Thursday.
Prospects are favourable in Burkina Faso, Chad, The Gambia, Mali, Mauritania and Niger following generally widespread rains in July over most producing areas, FAO's Global Information and Early Warning System on Food and Agriculture (GIEWS) said.
According to the report, small-scale breeding of desert locusts was expected with the onset of the summer rains in Chad, southern Mauritania, northern Mali and Niger. Large scale grasshopper infestation would however occur in Southern and Central Senegal.
Early crop prospects in Senegal, which received limited rainfall until August, would be uncertain, GIEWS added in its third 2003 report on weather and crop conditions in the Sahelian countries of Western Africa.
"As of mid-July, estimated cumulative rainfall totaled 40-60 percent of normal in much of Senegal's groundnut basin and mostly dry conditions still prevailed in the north," GIEWS said.
Burkina Faso experienced widespread rains in July, permitting satisfactory crop development, the report said. Although rains decreased in June and late July, crops were growing satisfactorily, pastures were regenerating countrywide and no pest activity had been reported.
In Chad, crops such as millet and sorghum were developing well due to widespread rains and pastures were regenerating countrywide. Reported locust occurrences would remain below threatening levels, GIEWS said.
According to the report, rice was growing well in The Gambia, millet and sorghum were doing well in Mali. Crops including millet and sorghum were also doing well in Mauritania and Niger where rainfall was generally widespread above 2002 levels in meteorological stations.
In Guinea-Bissau, large scale grasshopper infestations on sorghum, millet and maize compromised crop prospects, GIEWS said. "Over 50 percent of cereal production across the north is officially estimated to be at risk," it said.
The grain eating birds in Mali "are reported on about 205 hectares of crops in the Office du Niger zone. Army worm attacks are also reported in the Office du Niger and treatments have been undertaken," the report said.
The nine countries covered by the report are drought-prone nations belonging to the Permanent Inter-State Committee for Drought Control in the Sahel (CLISS). They lie within the Sahelian zones and receive rainfall ranging from 250 - 1100 mm annually.
The full report can be found at: http://www.fao.org/docrep/005/J0159e/J0159e00.htm

Original_Search_Title: World Vision Provides Food Aid in Drought-Stricken Nyamata
Headline: Rwanda: World Vision Provides Food Aid in Drought-Stricken Nyamata
Body_Text:
Nairobi — World Vision announced on Wednesday that it had been distributing 14.5
mt of food to 700 families in the drought-stricken Nyamata District of
Kigali Rural Province in Rwanda.
The distribution started in early August after World Vision found that
about 12 families with children it sponsored had migrated to neighbouring
villages. A rapid survey in Nyamata showed that about 8 percent of the 1,150
children World Vision supported in the area were malnourished. The NGO said
that the Rwandan government, which had a policy against free food aid,
approved a proposal to support the families of children cared for by World
Vision, while the UN World Food Programme was encouraged to continue with
its school feeding programme and food-for-work project.
Nyamata Vice Mayor Theobald Kayigamba described the aid as timely
because most of the crops that had been planted had withered.
World Vision Programme Coordinator Peter Twahirwa said with the next
rains due in October, his organisation was working on a food security and
environment conservation proposal to secure funding for farm inputs, and to
teach better farming techniques.
In April 2003, the USAID Famine Early Warning System Network (FEWS)
reported a possible food crisis in low-altitude areas of Bugesera Region and
in Kibungo Province in eastern Rwanda, particularly in the Mayange and
Muyenzi sectors of Nyamata District, Kigali Rural Province, where high
temperatures deplete soil humidity faster than in higher altitudes. At the
time, FEWS said the season had started very late, with rainfall poor and
erratic, causing sorghum to wither and bean cultivation to be delayed.
On 7 August, WFP signed a US $35.4-million deal with the Rwandan
government, under which WFP would deliver 53,566 mt of food over the next
four years. The agency said it would also provide operational aid valued at
$28.7 million over the same period.
[See related item, "WFP in US $35 million agreement with Kigali", at
http://www.irinnews.org/report.asp?ReportID=35883]

Original_Search_Title: $2bn Input Credit Scheme Unveiled
Headline: Zimbabwe: $2bn Input Credit Scheme Unveiled
Body_Text:
Harare — A NEW player in the cotton industry, Grafax Investments, has unveiled a
$2 billion input credit scheme to help farmers across the country.
The facility comes on the heels of a further US$3 million ($2,7 billion)
set aside for inputs by Rarefields Investments, a major stakeholder in
Grafax.
Although Grafax is a new entrant in the local industry, it has had a
long association with the cotton industry in India.
"The outgrower scheme will be targeted at the newly resettled farmers
especially the A2 model farmers who have larger hectarage and can grow
bigger areas of cotton, said Graftax chairman Mr Walter Mzembi.
The company will finance between 6 000 and 8 000 farmers depending on
average land size put to cotton.
On average each farmer is expected to grow at least five hectares, while
small holder growers are expected to put at least two hectares under
cotton.
"The cost of inputs will be recovered at the time the farmers market
their crops.
"Farmers will be at liberty to sell any surplus cotton over and above
their contracted quota to whoever they so wish," said Mr Mzembi.
The company is already involved in the provision of agricultural inputs
countrywide.
The unveiling of the $2 billion credit facility comes at a time when two
major players, the Cotton Company of Zimbabwe and AFSI Agricom Holdings
Limited have announced their intention to withdraw their inputs assistance
programmes.
Cottco announced on Monday said it had withdrawn its $3 billion inputs
assistance programme to cotton farmers due to "unfair" practices where
private buyers were purchasing the crop it financed contrary to agreements
reached earlier.
Furthermore, Cottco was unhappy that new players were given Export
Processing Zone status for their gins yet they were not contributing much to
the promotion or production of the cotton crop.
The company could not get the same status for seven of its nine gins
which were not considered as new ventures under the EPZ Act.
FSI Agricom yesterday said it was also reviewing its involvement in the
outgrower schemes.
The company supported cotton, soya beans, sorghum, maize, paprika and
tobacco farmers with a combined total of about 59 000 hectares.
"Due to the problem of side-marketing the company has been prejudiced
considerably.
"In light of this our company, just like Cottco, will be reviewing its
involvement in outgrower schemes," said an FSI Agricom statement.
It said the problem of side-marketing was compounded by the absence of
legislation to limit produce buyers to only those financing the growing of
such crops.
However, on the cotton front FSI would continue to lobby the authorities
to put in place a legislative framework that would be "in the long term
interest of a vibrant cotton industry".
"It is unsustainable for companies financing growers to end up with less
volumes that they planned for due to poachers who reap where they did not
sow," said FSI Agricomm.
However, Graftax is confident that its programme will go some way in
developing cotton production.
"The involvement of Grafax at each stage of the value chain of cotton
production will ensure an increase in the number of growers, increased
output, better incomes for the farmers and high quality exportable
lint.
Cotton production is expanding in this country with a number of
companies coming to the fore to support growers.
Production figures are expected to increase this year, after a 33
percent drop last year.
There are prospects of goods rains this coming season and more financial
support will result in better yields.

Original_Search_Title: Advice to Eat Vermin Infuriates Kalangala Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Advice to Eat Vermin Infuriates Kalangala Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Minister of State for Agriculture, Dr. Kibirige Ssebunya, has said residents of Kalangala districts had attacked him when he advised them to eat monkeys in order to stop them from destroying their crops. This time he has urged Masaka residents to eat birds for the same reason.
Eddie Ssejjobawrites that Ssebunya said this at the Masaka Agricultural show at the Liberation Square after farmers complained that birds had become a menace to their crops.
One of the farmers, Nnalongo Florence Mulindwa from Butenga, told the minister that birds had become a problem to the growing of a new variety of sorghum supplied by Nile Breweries. She said the beer company was trying the sorghum in the area birds were destroying the crop.
Ssebunya said eating the birds was one of the sure ways of getting rid of them. "I advised the people of Kalangala to eat the monkeys and they attacked me, thinking I was joking, but my friends in the north had to resort to eating eagles to stop them from disturbing them and they are no more," he said.
He said people especially from the central region should learn to eat some of the vermins like edible rats, monkeys, birds and insects.
Ssebunya thanked Nalongo Mulindwa for the hard work and promised to get her market for her maize and beans.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Ahead of Budget
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Ahead of Budget
Body_Text:
DELTA chief executive officer Joe Mutizwa says volumes in the three months to June 30 held up very well particularly lager and sorghum.
"Lager volumes, in June alone, were 5% up on last year and 14% ahead of budget and overall margins increased to 33% from just under 30% last year," Mutizwa said.
He said despite the depression in soft drink volumes due to the cold weather, he was hopeful soft drink volumes would recover in the remaining period to year-end as the warm weather returned.
Soft drinks contributed only 6% to group bottom line in the just-ended financial year to March 31.
Turnover in the three months under review increased to $50 billion compared to $103 billion achieved in the whole of last year.
The chief executive said sorghum volumes were almost 12% ahead of budget for the three months under consideration.
Recovery in the packaging division, MegaPak Zimbabwe, was very strong and volumes were ahead of budget and up on last year, he said.
Megapak volumes for the year ended March 31 were slightly over 4 000 tonnes converted with turn-over increasing to $6,7 billion which the company said reflected problems in the country with fuel and water supplies.
Mutizwa said this division's major challenge was to expand capacity so as to meet growing demand and plans were underway to address this.
Megapak Zimbabwe incorporates Megapak (injection moulding), Megapet (PET bottle blowing), Blowmoulders and Megatank (large plastic tank manufacturing).
Mutizwa said market response on the new Castle Milk Stout in terms of penetration was gratifying and ahead of expectation.
"We are very bullish about the Castle Milk Stout brand and we are glad that although it is too early to say much, market response has been good," said Mutizwa.
Export volumes for barley malt were in line with budget while lager beer exports were 19% up on prior year though off a small base.
New markets for barley malt, lagers and plastic packaging are being developed in the growing markets of Angola and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mutizwa said.
"Initial indications are promising although it will take a while to exploit the full potential of these markets," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Still On COJA-PAN Affair
Headline: Africa: Still On COJA-PAN Affair
Body_Text:
Lagos — We have listened to the explanations of COJA on Saturday, July 26, 2003 and other write-ups in the dailies and weeklies and, especially, the excellent and well-balanced THISDAY Editorial of Sunday, August 3, 2003. This write-up dwells on the negative medium and long run economic effects of allowing massive brand penetration of goods that we can produce at home on the economy.
We have had cause to state losses arising from nonpayment of tariffs and VAT in past write-ups. We are wondering if charity can be embodied in sponsorship with a gift of 900 cars for sports events - is that our way of classifying charity? How about the loss of profit margins by the indigenous importers of BMW cars? We understand it is between 25 and 35 percent profit margin per car. What will the markups for the various 900 BMWs spare-parts be between 2003 and 2007 when spare-parts consumption will become issues? Or is BMW following the guideline of also sponsoring a minimum of thirty per cent of the values of the 900 cars as spare-parts? Or will it be sold at lower/preferential rates? We should remember that BMW dealerships are based mainly in Lagos and supported flimsily in Port Harcourt, Abuja, Kano and Kaduna. Not so with Peugeot because it is fully represented in all the 36 States capitals and strongly at Abuja.
There is insistence that dividends of democracy entail freedom of choice and such freedom being warranted especially where cost benefit analyses shows it. If Nigeria should allow for freedom of choice. she will be importing cowpea, sorghum, maize, all categories of cooking oil and juices/fruits drink, all of its rice requirements, poultry meat, eggs, textile materials, sugar among many other items. Yet, Federal Government from time to time bans the importation wholly, or in components, of some of these products. Nigeria will lose her Independence if it allows freedom of
choice to reign fully. While I was in Lagos, Rivers and Delta states in June 2003, the discussions I had showed that local manufacturing outfits cannot compete with imported Malaysian, Indonesian, India and Thailand products any longer unless there are massive protections. It is not so with Peugeot cars at all. The 306, 307, 406 and 607 are all embodiments of new generation technological developments in the automobile sector. They are superb cars that are used as both pools and statutes' cars in the Federal Ministries, parastatals and some private sectors.
If the present administration allows the BMW cars to remain, then, it will open the floodgate for massive or systemic/systematic justifications of importations of what we can rationally and locally produce. Then, by 2006/2007, we would have witnessed the demise of most import-substituting industries, closures of many factories especially in Lagos, Ogun, Oyo, Kaduna, Kano, Jos, Enugu and Port Harcourt as a result of this short run myopism. These will then, be counted against the present administration as its visible failures. Therefore, we implore the federal authorities to initiate a rapid rethink on the BMW issues and direct that only Peugeot should be used. We believe the President is very sincere and desires to see avenues for growing job creation that will continuously employ our qualifying youths and also, generate sustainable promotional avenues. These will not come to pass if industries and factories are closing up because of massive dumping of imported products that they can produce.
Peter Okaiyeto, Zaria

Original_Search_Title: Government Appeals for Help for Drought-Ridden Afar Region Despite Recent Rainfall
Headline: Ethiopia: Gov't Appeals for Help for Afar
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The Ethiopian government has appealed for more help for tens of
thousands of pastoralists in drought-stricken Afar region - scene of
widespread devastation after failed rains last year.
According to the government's emergency arm, the Disaster Prevention and
Preparedness Commission (DPPC), some 740,00 people need support.
Afar - on the northeast fringes of Ethiopia, bordering both Eritrea and
Djibouti - was one of the first areas of the country to be hit by the
current famine.
The government now estimates that the region lost more than half of its
cattle in the drought.
"Years of recurrent drought have hit hard people's main source of
survival and caused massive deaths of livestock in 2002," the DPPC
said.
It said that pasture in the region was at critical levels in most areas
and water resources had "considerably reduced". This had prompted unusually
early migration.
Officials also stated that the region lacked medicines and health
services to cope with any serious outbreaks of disease.
The warnings came in the government's Special Early Warning System
report, which was released to journalists on Thursday.
In it, the government stated that food aid and emergency support to the
country should be extended to an additional 2.4 million people because of
poor rains earlier in the year.
Some 600,000 people - on top of the 12.6 million hit by the famine -
will need food aid, the report concluded.
The report is an assessment of the food needs of the country up until
December 2003 following the short (belg) rains (March-May).
The rains are crucial, according to the DPPC, for maize and sorghum -
staple foods in Ethiopia - as well as coffee production which is the
country's biggest export earner.
But humanitarian organisations are starting to report that the country
is now receiving good rains which fall during June and September across the
country.
The US government's Famine Early Warning System (FEWS) said "good
rainfall distribution since the beginning of June has made this year better
than last year".

Original_Search_Title: More People in Need As Drought Worsens
Headline: Namibia: More People in Need As Drought Worsens
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Some 400,000 people may be in need of general food aid distributions in
drought-hit Namibia, the country's Emergency Management Unit (EMU) told IRIN
on Thursday.
Gabriel Kangowa, deputy director of the EMU, said that "this year's
drought is more severe than last year's" and the number of people in need of
aid had risen.
Last year around 345,000 Namibians required food aid, which the
government was able to provide without making an appeal for international
assistance.
Kangowa said he would be handing a report on the current situation to
the cabinet on Tuesday and they would deliberate on how best to
respond.
"There might be a need for us to appeal - depending on our own resources
- but that decision will be taken by cabinet," Kangowa said.
The EMU had planned for a "free food distribution programme to all
drought-affected communities ... We are still receiving figures from the
regions, but we think it may be about 400,000 people affected," Kangowa
added.
Meanwhile, the government's flood relief aid to communities in the
northeastern Caprivi region was coming to an end, he noted. Some 12,000
people in 22 villages were affected by the flooding, which occurred in May
after a period of prolonged torrential rainfall in the Democratic Republic
of Congo caused the banks of the Zambezi river to burst downstream in
Namibia.
"The situation [in the Caprivi] is 95 percent back to normal, [but] we
still have some places that cannot be accessed by road. Those are places
still surrounded by muddy areas - you cannot drive through by truck, and
there's no water, so you can't use boats. There are some places the 4x4
trucks cannot negotiate, so we've been flying food to those areas - but the
distribution of food aid has come to an end," Kangowa said.
He added that "the non-food items from the Red Cross and other donors
are still being distributed but will be finished soon".
Because the flooding had destroyed crops in the region, the government
was providing the affected communities in the Caprivi with agricultural
inputs for the coming cropping season.
"We bought seeds from a company in South Africa, 90 mt of maize seed,
which is already on the way. I talked to the company this morning and they
say the first truck might arrive Friday or Saturday in Katima [Mulilo, the
provincial capital]," Kangowa said.
He explained that the early arrival of inputs was critical as the
"planting season runs from mid-July up to the end of this month, so they
[should be able to] plant and produce their own food".
"We also bought sorghum from a local cooperative, 10 mt of sorghum,
which will be picked up probably next week and taken to Katima so they can
start planting," Kangowa added.
He noted that while "the assistance relating to the floods has come to
an end, these people will also be included in general food distributions
[because of] the ongoing drought in this country".

Original_Search_Title: Caprivi Flood Aid Nears End
Headline: Namibia: Caprivi Flood Aid Nears End
Body_Text:
Windhoek — GOVERNMENT flood relief aid to about 12 000 people in the Caprivi Region
is scheduled to end this week.
People have been receiving aid since being affected by floods three
months ago.
EMU Deputy Director, Gabriel Kangowa, yesterday expressed satisfaction
with the way the relief distribution had been conducted, saying it had been
in line with what was planned.
Progress reports are scheduled to be discussed at a meeting at Katima
Mulilo tomorrow.
Decisions will also be taken on whether or not it is necessary to extend
the provision of aid to the area.
But Kangowa said it was possible the affected people might receive
assistance from September under the drought relief programme, an issue he
said should be discussed by Government next week.
Although the flood waters have receded significantly in many areas, the
villages of Muzii, Nsundwa, Nankutwe, Namiyundu, Itomba and Mbalasinte are
still not accessible by road.
The Regional Emergency Management Unit (Remu) based at Katima Mulilo
said it had completed the third round of food delivery and was now only
distributing items such as blankets and mosquito nets.
Kangowa told The Namibian that the last of the promised assistance -
about 90 tons of maize and 10 tons of sorghum seed - should arrive in the
region by Monday.
This should give people have enough time to plant in preparation for the
next harvest season.
The purchase of veterinary medicine valued at about N$400 000 has been
approved for the vaccination of animals which might have contracted diseases
as a result of the floods.
A month ago Imakusi, Lisikili, Sisuha, Malindi, Schuckmannsburg,
Nakabolelwa were only accessible by helicopter but this is no longer the
case.
Impalila and Kasika are also accessible via road from Botswana.
Councillor for the Katima Rural Constituency, Leonard Chaka, told The
Namibian that with the waters having subsided in many areas, it was possible
that people would be able to sustain themselves again soon through fishing
and farming.
The Red Cross Society, which worked extensively in the area at the
height of the floods, has also completed its distribution of aid.

Original_Search_Title: Concern Over Low Food Yields in South
Headline: Somalia: Concern Over Low Food Yields in South
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A food security watchdog says lower than expected cereal production has been recorded in southern Somalia due to poor and erratic rains during the April-June (Gu) season.
In its latest report, the Food Security Assessment Unit (FSAU) - a joint project of the EC and the UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation - said "erratic and unevenly distributed" rains had impacted on the cereal production in southern Somalia.
"Poor rainfall occurrence and distribution negatively affected maize crop establishment," it said.
The report added that due to "poor irrigation infrastructure and inaccessibility", production levels had been lower than in 2002.
A Somali agronomist told IRIN that since the collapse of the Somali government in 1991, no-one has been able to desilt the riverbeds or manage the sluice gates on the rivers or adjoining canals, all of which contributed to seasonal flooding which destroyed what was left of the irrigation system.
The two most-affected areas were the southwestern Bay Region, which normally accounted for 70 percent of the country's sorghum production, and the southern Lower Shabelle Region, which normally accounted for 60 percent of maize production, the agronomist said.
"This is the worst production from the two regions in the last two years," he added.
The FSAU report also blamed the low production "on soil insects (white grubs), grasshoppers, stem-borers and rodents (rats)" in many parts of southern Somalia.
According to the report, the low yields mean that in areas like the southern Hiran region, "around 30 percent of the agro-pastoral households in Belet Weyn district will be facing a [food] deficit of 10-25 percent".

Original_Search_Title: Arresting the 'Witch Weed'
Headline: Nigeria: Arresting the 'Witch Weed'
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Striga is the dread of many farmers, not only in Nigeria, but across Africa. In line with its mandate of generating technology to enhance food production, the University of Agriculture, Abeokuta (UNAAB) is at the fore front of arresting the 'witch weed' and putting more money in the pockets of farmers, writes Bukola Olatunji
It is called 'Moto moto' in Swahili and 'Uta uta' in Hausa. They both mean the same thing - 'Fire fire' because of its burning effect on grains such as corn, rice, cow pea, sorghum, etc. The Yoruba call it 'Orisapoka' or 'Osapoka' for short (the oracle that kills the grain). The striga, otherwise known as the 'witch weed' looks like a harmless, weak weed that even a six-year-old child can uproot and throw away without a second thought. But it is far from that.
So devastating is the effect of the weed that a pan- African Striga Control Network (PASCON) was formed in 1988. This later metamorphosed into the Pan -African Parasitic Weed Control Network, maintaining the same acronym.
Linked to this network is the Semi Arid Food and Grain Research and Development Project (SAFGRAD), whose headquarters is in Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso.
Coordinator of the project in Nigeria, Professor Segun Lagoke, said as at 1986, sorghum loss due to striga was estimated at about $250 million per annum in Africa.
Through a SAF-GRAD/Korean Government project, the Ibadan, Oyo State-based International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA) has development different species of corn that are resistant to the weed. These are tested by UNAAB's team led by Lagoke, after which "useful materials are picked and tested with farmers, through what is known as 'On Farm Demonstration of Striga Management Technology. The Institute of Agricultural Research (IAR) at the Ahmadu Bello University (ABU) Zaria also coordinated by Lagoke oversees the project in the northern parts of the country. The project has about 3000 Plots in West Africa, 160 of which are in Nigeria.
Three years ago, the project moved to the Odo Erin community (made up of other smaller communities) in the Odeda Local Government in Ogun State where a high prevalence of the Striga had been reported.
After an initial skepticism, the farmers embraced the researchers who had come to 'deliver them' from the scourge of the Striga. The years later, all participants had a good story to tell such that when the principal officers of UNAAB, led by the Vice Chancellor decided to visit the community last Thursday to see how far the project had gone, to were received by the community at the junction of the untarred road that led to the farms and the Abeokuta- Igbo-Ora road, with singing and dancing.
The group of men and women were led by the Baale of Odo - Erin Community, Mr. Ayinla Adesina and Baale Ayooyo, Mr. Jimoh Kolapo, among others.
Research officer at UNAAB's Research and Development Centre (RESDEC), Dr. (Mrs.) Biola Phillip explained that the Striga may be under the soil for years without manifestation. but as soon as the corn is planted and begins to grow, the weed poisons it and takes all its nutrients.
A Director of AMREC, Dr. Yaya Adeoti, explained further that unlike other plants whose stomata close at night, the Striga's stomata never close, so the weed transpires 24 hours a day and therefore keeps absorbing nutrients from the host tissue.
But the striga is not completely useless. Professor Lagoke said the weed is known to induce contraction in pregnancy.
The hybrid corn (hybrid) was found to be resistant to the weed, but was not popular with the farmers because they have to go back to the source for more grains each time they want to plant.
This led to the production of the open pollinated varieties such as 'Across 97' which could be replanted by the farmers.
The weed has also been found to hate high concentration of nitrogen, so the farmers were taught how to apply first the NPK (Nitrogen Phosporus and Pottasium) fertiliser and six weeks later, a second application of urea fertiliser.
Groundnut, which cannot be attacked by the striga, also provides a good cover or acts as 'trap crop' for the grains.
With different plots showing the yields of the treated and untreated seeds, the difference was clear for all to see. Lagoke stressed that all farm produce goes back to the farmer. Indeed, Dr. Phillip said "the economics of the project is the main thing. It is to put more naira in the pocket of the farmer.
One of the farmers, Mallam Mohammed, who said he had been farming in the area for about 10 years, said he was at the verge of giving up maize farming, due to low harvest, when the researchers came. He was pleased with the 'Across 97' variety, he said, because he could see the difference on his farm.
Baale Adesina lamented that farming in the community had become an even more arduous task as there are no longer young people in the village to help on the farms. He therefore requested UNAAB's assistance in providing tractors to help on the farms for a fee even as they are willing to and have been paying for their supply of fertiliser.
Baale Kolapo also requested for the purchase of their cassava by the university, while the entire community also needs potable water, motorable roads for smooth movement of their produce to the market.
Another farmer, Mr. Amos Adesina prayed fervently for the success of the project and all participants in it.
Prof. Adu promised to take their requests to the government for attention. he also promised to buy the farmers' grains, cassava and cashew nuts, provided the prices are reasonable. He said the mandate given to UNAAB was borne out of government's concern for the plight of farmers. He therefore enjoined the farmers to continue to make use of the knowledge they hadacquired through the programme and also encourage their colleagues in other communities to embrace it.

Original_Search_Title: Zambia Records Rice Deficit
Headline: Zambia Records Rice Deficit
Body_Text:
THE Ministry of Agriculture and Co-operatives has estimated a rice
deficit of 6,500 tonnes this year.
According to the Zambia National Food Balance for the 2003/04 marketing
year, the ministry has projected a further 2,000 tonnes wheat deficit.
The crop production estimates report by the ministry and the Central
Statistics Office (CSO) indicates that the total national rice requirement
was 17,244 tonnes but only 10,744 tonnes were available.
The report shows that the 6,500 tonnes was the import requirement to be
made by the private sector to meet the commercial market demand.
National human consumption for rice has been estimated at 16,707
tonnes.On wheat, the report shows that the total quantity of wheat available
is 137,968 tonnes against the national requirement of 140,000 tonnes.
Rural production of wheat for the season was estimated at 135,000 tonnes
while 2,000 tonnes would come from commodity traders.
National wheat consumption is estimated at 131,000 tonnes and a further
1,500 tonnes is required for seed production.
The report anticipates that the wheat deficit will also be met by the
private sector importing the commodity to meet commercial market
demand.
No deficits are expected of sorghum and Irish potatoes, with available
and required crop standing at 56,000 tonnes and 132,000 tonnes
respectively.
Some 348,000 tonnes surplus from cassava flour is expected from a total
production of 958,000 tonnes while total requirement for various uses is
609,000 tonnes.

Original_Search_Title: 'Customs Does Not Aid Smuggling of Textiles'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Customs Does Not Aid Smuggling of Textiles'
Body_Text:
The Nigeria Customs Service has refuted a recent allegation by local textile manufacturers that it was aiding and abetting the smuggling of printed fabrics (Ankara) into the country.
"It is an unfair allegation against us because we are fully implementing the federal government's prohibition order on the importation of some items into Nigeria and this is earning us commendations from President Olusegun Obasanjo and other stakeholders in the textile industry," Customs spokesman, Wale Adeniyi, said at the weekend in Abuja.
Reacting to the allegation by the Nigerian Textiles Manufacturers Association, Adeniyi told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) that the service had in recent times seized contraband worth millions of naira.
The seizures were made at the land borders in Katsina, Seme and Idi-roko and some of the seaports not designated for importation of some items, he said.
"Only recently, we impounded two large containers of textile materials at Onne Port for illegal importation through that port," Adeniyi said.
He, however, explained that government had allowed the importation of other types of textiles through Apapa and Tin Can Island ports in Lagos, noting that "anybody caught contravening the law will forfeit the items, as well as face legal action".
He also said the Customs had last month established an enforcement strike force to tackle smuggling nationwide.
About 300 officers, the spokesman said, were undergoing intensive training at the Police College, Ikeja, to prepare them for the task of dealing with smugglers.
Part of the anti-smuggling campaign has also been the mounting of a campaign to educate importers and other Nigerians on items banned from coming into the country, as well as on the dangers of some of the items to the health of the nation, Adeniyi said.
He appealed to Nigerians to always avail the customs of information on activities of smugglers along the country's entry points.
"We are ready to make good use of such information, so as to decisively deal with the smugglers and those circumventing government's fiscal policies".
NAN recalls that the government had, in August last year, banned the importation of sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum, vegetable oil in bulk, mosquito repellant coils, used tyres, gaming machines and second-hand clothes.
Other banned items are used fridges and air-conditioning units, used compressors, bagged cement, vehicles of any kind through the land borders, vehicles older than eight years, textiles of any kind through land borders, printed fabrics (Ankara) and frozen poultry, among others.
NAN reports that smuggling has, however, continued, with the perpetrators attacking customs officers at several border points with dangerous weapons.

Original_Search_Title: Volume of Rain Revives Crop Hopes
Headline: Gambia: Volume of Rain Revives Crop Hopes
Body_Text:
Banjul — Last season's dashed hopes for rich agricultural yields, have been
sufficiently revived by the so far "impressive volume" of rainfall this year
according to a senior agro meteorological officer at the Department of Water
Resources.
Speaking to The Independent earlier this week Peter Gibba said this
year's volume of rainfall into August is reviving much hope and expectation
to the effect that crop yields would be register a "big boost capable of
turning last year's story of crop failure around".  He said if the momentum
with which rain has been falling since June holds, remarkably positive
changes would be realised as far as the agricultural output of farmers are
concerned.
According to Mr. Gibba, the country should expect what he called normal
patterns of rainfall which he said would determine the level of agricultural
productivity for the season. He said so far, this year has registered about
15 percent of rainfall compared to this time last year when the paucity of
rain was already beginning to adversely affect the crops. The chief agro
meteorologist forecasted that with high pressure winds from the Atlantic
Ocean, more rain is expected to fall especially in the western part of the
country, which is recording the highest rainfall yet, followed by the
eastern part and the middle of the country.
Meanwhile the agrometeological bulleting of July 2003 has revealed that
significant rains have been recorded throughout the countrywide since the
rainy season started. Breaks of up to three to five consecutive days were
only observed in the western third of the country with the frequency of
rains much improved compared to previous years. As at July 10, the country's
average rainfall stood at 147.3mm, which is 15 per cent above last year's
average of 128.0mm and 35 percent above the long-term (108.9mm) during the
same period.
On the agrometeological situation, the bulletin indicated that the rainy
season has progressed with increased rainfall amounts as maximum and minimum
temperatures continue to drop as in the previous two decades.
Maximum temperatures recorded were 340 0C at Kerewan in the Western
third, 35.7 degree Celsius at Kaur in the middle third and 34.0 degree
Celsius at Sare Soffie in the Eastern third.
On the agricultural situation, it mentioned that ploughing and planting
of late millet, sorghum, up-land rice and groundnuts is continuing in the
Western Division. In CRD North and parts of NBD maize is in mid vegetative
development, generally about 50 centimetres high while early millet is at
the tilling stages of development while in URD re-planting of cereals
performed in some areas is being affected by grasshoppers.
Crops that were not affected are progressing in their development.
Countrywide, weeding is in progress except in areas where ploughing is
dominating.
The bulletin added that presently, no major outbreaks of pests were
reported countrywide. However, pockets of grasshoppers and leaf beetles were
reported in the Basse area, eating up germinating seedlings, which resulted
to re-planting in affected fields.

Original_Search_Title: FG Approves N1.2bn for Maize Mop-Up
Headline: Nigeria: FG Approves N1.2bn for Maize Mop-Up
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Government has approved N1.2 billion for the purchase of excess maize from farmers during this planting season.
Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development, Mallam Adamu Bello, who disclosed this yesterday when the management of Syngenta Nigeria Limited, an agro-chemical firm visited him in his office, also expressed government's commitment to biotechnology and the use of genetic grains.
Bello said that action was necessitated by the fall in price of maize and added that it was also as a continuation of government's Buyer of Last Resort Programme, which commenced in 1999.
The minister, who said the programme is used to mop-up excess grains resulting from a depressed market, noted that over 100,000 metric tonnes of grains comprising of maize, sorghum, millet and paddy rice were purchased between December 1999 and May 2002 thus relieving farmers of financial stress.
Bello said government's main concern about biotechnology is on the protection of its citizenry from hazards that may arise from the use of genetic grains.
He, however, added that "Nigeria is a naturally blessed country and what it needs is highly improved seed varieties and best agricultural practises."

Original_Search_Title: FG Approves N1.2bn for Maize Mop Up
Headline: Nigeria: FG Approves N1.2bn for Maize Mop Up
Body_Text:
The federal government has approved the release of N1.2 billion for the purchase of excess maize from farmers during this planting season. Government also reiterated its stand to be cautious on issues relating to biotechnology and use of genetic grains.
Agriculture and Rural Development Minister, Mallam Adamu Bello, disclosed this when the management of Syngenta Nigeria Limited, the foremost agro-chemical company in Nigeria and some of its international staff paid him a courtesy call in Abuja.
The minister said that the maize mop-up is necessitated by the fall in prices and is in continuation of government's buyer-of-last-resort-programme which commenced in 1999.
According to him, the programme is to mop up excess grains resulting from a depressed market, and that between December 1999 - May 2002, over 100,000 metric tonnes of grains comprising maize, sorghum, millet and paddy rice were purchased.
Earlier, Syngenta, Chief Executive Officer in Nigeria, Mr. Patrick Ikemefuna, introduced his Kenyan and South African counterparts and said that the company has been in operation in Nigeria since 1964 and has undergone various transformations including the changing of its name from CIBA-GEIGY to NOVARTIS and now SYNGENTA.
In another development, the agric minister has said that the ultimate goal of the present administration is to move the Nigerian farmer from his present production level of subsistence to surplus agriculture.
He made the declaration when members of the Board of Directors of the Nigeria Agricultural, Co-operatives and Rural Development Bank (NACRDB) paid a courtesy call on him in Abuja.
Mallam Bello said that the bank is the foremost credit institution available to farmers and has a crucial role to play in the realisation of this goal with 70 percent of agricultural enterprise dominated by peasant farmers.
Earlier, the Board's chairman, Alhaji Isa Tata Yusuf, gave a run-down of the bank's achievements during the current year. This include the granting of N1.1 billion loan of which N693 million is for micro credit.
He said that since year 2002, the bank has approved N4.6 billion of which N2.5 billion went to eight states for on-lending purposes.
On the recovery of bad debt, the chairman said that of the N5.9 million owed, about 40 percent has been recovered and that the bank was able to mobilise deposits from rural areas to the tune of N57 million from 2,436 accounts.
The chairman informed the minister that the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), has paid the sum of N3.6 billion for its first call equity. CBN has 40 percent equity shares in the bank.

Original_Search_Title: Isiko Fits Well in Basoga's Shoes
Headline: Uganda: Isiko Fits Well in Basoga's Shoes
Body_Text:
Kampala — WHEN a bull dies, another one will surface. "He shares a lot in common with his predecessor. He is an outspoken orator and a shrewd politician," his colleagues remark.
"Hon. Asupasa Wilson Isiko Mpongo, has fitted well so far in the late Basoga's shoes," Davis Gole Kirya, says of him.
They have a lot in common, they resemble, both attended the same schools and university, they both studied education and they all love music and drama and were both journalists. Constituents of Busiki elected a new Member of Parliament who to many, is as hard working as his predecessor, the late Basoga Nshadu.
"He is a down-to-earth man, he associates more with ordinary people in his constituency. He is a hard working man who has made himself a household name in business. He has mobilised the youth into business and agriculture. Busiki is a business hub in Iganga.
"There is a lot of commercial farming, courtesy of his mobilisation skills and business acumen. He employs many people in a string of businesses he owns," Besi Kaduyu, a business lady in Iganga, says.
"I would not describe him precisely, but the few weeks he has been in Parliament he seems to have fitted in so well. He is assertive and serious," Tom Bagalana, MP Bunya South confides.
Like his predecessor, the new 'bull' of Busiki is proud, talks with pomp in a carefree manner and in a commanding voice and chipping in Lusoga words occasionally.
Isiko was born in Busiki, Magada sub-county, Iganga district in 1961 to Mr. and Mrs. Ignatius Mpongo. The third born in a family of six siblings, Isiko started his education at Kalamira Primary School, four miles away from their home in 1968. In primary six in 1973, he went to Kisoko Boarding Primary School where he completed his PLE with a first grade. In 1976, he was admitted to Busoga College Mwiri.
In 1980, he joined Iganga Secondary School for A-level, but hardly a year into his course he was suspended. Later in 1981, he joined Kiira College, Butiki where he completed his A-level in 1983.
In 1984, he was admitted to Makerere University for a degree in Education majoring in literature and English and graduated in 1987.
He taught at Wanyange Girls' School briefly and later went for military training at Mbarara military training school from 1988 to 1989.
The biggest challenge he faced at school was his small size, which worked against him. "They used to call me cartoon. I was very tiny. Any one could tease me. My father bought me a pair of Bata shoes size two, which I wore up to primary seven. My size really hampered me, but at the same time, it helped me to be assertive and out-spoken. I was very tiny and short but bright. My only weapon was my mouth and brain," the man who is now a giant recalls.
He says he joined the army because it was perceived as a stepping-stone to politics.
"I had political ambitions. To be successful in politics, you needed some military background. Literally, the army was in charge. I joined the army to lay a strong foundation for my political career," he explains.
He also says that Major General Jeje Odongo, who was commandant of Mbarara training wing then and a family friend, inspired him to join the army.
He served in the NRA from 1989 to 1993, working with the directorate of information and public relations. He received some journalistic training at Uganda Management Institute in 1992. He was a sub-editor and writer for Tarehe Sita and weekly military bulletin. In 1993 he voluntarily resigned from the army and joined politics. His first attempt at a big political position was in 1994 when he stood for the Constituency Assembly (CA) elections, but was unsuccessful. In June 2001 he made a second attempt at politics but came second to the late Basoga Nsadhu.
At the time of Basoga's death, Isiko was working with Uganda Revenue Authority as a senior revenue officer. Isiko openly admits his love and passion for the Movement, promised to promote the system in memory of his predecessor.
"Basoga loved the Movement and I will promote the values he so treasured. He actually proposed that the Movement should rule for 1,000 years - who knows, I might be the one to continue with that vision. Basoga was a great a figure, a man who held different forces in Busiki in his palms. When he died, divisions sprung up along - clans, religion and loyalty. My biggest task is to unite my people," he explains.
He acknowledges the vast problems his people face and alarming poverty levels, but says he plans to transform his area socially and economically.
"My constituency has a great economic potential, which needs to be exploited. I am mobilising my people to grow cotton, groundnuts, maize and sorghum because there is instant market these crops do not require sophisticated technology to grow them.
"Education is very poor. We have only two government-aided secondary schools and not even a single vocational school. The road network is in a sorry state. We have only one tarmac road just passing through the district. However, I am optimistic that things will be brighter because government is committed to development," Isiko explains.
Throughout school, Isiko held various leadership positions. At Mwiri, he was a house prefect and timekeeper.
At Makerere, he was students' representative for Nsibirwa Hall and was on the university students' guild council. He was a great debater and his skills won him an award. He also wrote a play The Evasive, which won him an award at the national theatre. After losing the 2001 elections, Isiko went into business and private work. However, when bi-elections came, following the death of Nsadhu, he contested in the race, which attracted 10 candidates. He beat them with 13,506 votes. His closest rival, John Wadada, polled 6,000.
He married Joyce a financier in 1988. The couple is blessed with five children.

Original_Search_Title: EuronAid Launches a Triangular Cereal Purchase Operation
Headline: Ethiopia: EuronAid Launches a Triangular Cereal Purchase Operation
Body_Text:
EuronAid has purchased 24,387 MTs of sorghum from the Sudan with the
help of funds donated by the European Commission [EC] to Ethiopia. EuronAid
launched the triangular purchase operation according to arrangements made by
the euronAid Headquarters, following negotiations among EC and
Ethio-Sudanese authorities. The tripartite negotiations addressed such
issues as customs and border formalities and procedures of passage of
trucks, among others.
According to EuronAid officials, the purchase operation aims at the
repayment of relief grain borrowed by EC's partner NGOs in April 2003 from
government reserves to ensure the timely supply and distribution of relief
food.
EuronAid's triangular purchase operation has won the support of the
officials of the Ethiopian and Sudanese governments. EuronAid officials say
the operation, which has come at the backdrop of quiet favorable bilateral
ties, is going as planned mainly due to the strengthening relations between
the two countries.
Ethiopia and the Sudan, who share an over 1000 km border and a
longstanding relations, have recently further reinvigorated their ties by
embarking upon various bilateral engagements that have brought them close to
each other. The current operation, which marks the start of cross-border
initiatives negotiated by EuronAid and Ethio-Sudanese authorities, is
believed to boost existing trade and investment between the two
countries.

Original_Search_Title: Annual Inflation Drops
Headline: Zambia: Annual Inflation Drops
Body_Text:
Lusaka — ANNUAL inflation rate has reduced from 21.9 per cent to 20.2 per cent
during the month of July.
The Central Statistical Office (CSO) census and statistics director
Dr. Buleti Nsemukila disclosed that metropolitan low, high income and
non-metropolitan groups were recorded at 20.8, 21.8 and 18.8 per cent
respectively for July as compared to 23.2, 23.5 and 20.2 per cent for the
month of June.
Dr. Nsemukila explained that the downward trend in the food index was
largely due to the significant lower prices of mealie meal, maize grain and
vegetables.
"Additionally, price decreases occurred in dried beans, shelled
groundnuts, sorghum and fresh kapenta," Dr. Nsemukila said. He also
disclosed that the monthly inflation rate registered a reduction of -0.1 per
cent for the month of July.
Dr. Nsemukila disclosed that monthly inflation rates for the
metropolitan low, high income and non-metropolitan groups were recorded at
-0.5, -0.1 and 0.1 per cent respectively.

Original_Search_Title: Jumbos, Hippos Destroy Crops in Siavonga Raising Fears of Food Shortage
Headline: Zambia: Jumbos, Hippos Destroy Crops in Siavonga Raising Fears of Food Shortage
Body_Text:
ELEPHANTS and hippos have destroyed 135 hectares of crops in Siavonga
district, raising fears of hunger among peasant farmers.
Siavonga district agricultural co-coordinator Mark Chombe confirmed the
development in Choma yesterday.
Dr Chombe said the crops, which were destroyed during this farming
season by the beasts included maize, sorghum and millet.
He named the most affected areas as Lusitu, Simamba and Chalokwa in
chiefs Chipepo and Simamba's areas.
Dr Chombe said a team sent to the area by his office found that about 70
per cent of the crops were destroyed in the most affected areas.
He noted that the elephants were crossing from one of the game parks in
Zimbabwe and were wreaking havoc on the Zambian side, while the hippos were
from lake Kariba.
He further said the physical inspection conducted by his office
indicated that the least affected areas had five per cent of their crops
destroyed by the animals.
Dr Chombe said the most affected area was Lusitu which had 118 hectares
of crops destroyed.
He said Bakasa Agriculture Camp had 17 hectares destroyed by the
elephants.

Original_Search_Title: KPMG Chairman is No Affirmative Appointment
Headline: South Africa: KPMG Chairman is No Affirmative Appointment
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Auditing firm KPMG South Africa has appointed Moses Kgosana chairman of
its board in what the firm calls "a bold move to achieving real
transformation".
"I do not regard myself as an empowerment or affirmative appointment.
You can take away my skin colour and on the basis of what remains, I will
still be the suitable candidate for the job," says Kgosana.
Besides transformation, KPMG says, the appointment will enhance its
corporate governance practices. The firm has separated the position of chief
executive, held by Tom Grieve, and that of chairman.
Kgosana is one of the founders of KMMT, a black owned auditing firm,
which competed its merger with KPMG last year. "We brought in twelve (plus
one white) highly skilled partners to the firm and a lucrative mainly public
sector client base to which KPMG had very little access."
"I was empowered when I started my firm, not now."
Born in Pretoria 45 years ago, Kgosana completed his articles with what
is today Deloitte & Touche. Before starting his firm, he worked for the
Nedcor Group and National Sorghum Breweries.
Kgosana says he will continue to spend most of his time at clients'
premises.
"I have not thought of whether I will get a bigger office or not because
that is irrelevant. I do not earn any fees when I am here."
He currently heads the Consumer Markets division which has clients like
Telkom, Post Office and the SABC. The division is the third biggest
contributor to the R1.1bn turnover operation. It makes up between 15% and
20%, says Kgosana.
His appointment means he will now have to service other divisions like
financial where the firm audits close to 80%.
Kgosana is appointed at the time when the auditing profession is under
tremendous pressure both internationally and domestically. This follows the
Enron collapse in the United States and investigations by the SA Receiver of
Revenue Services of some of the members.
He says both do not worry him because the Enron saga was a lesson which
everyone had to go through and the SARS issue has no basis.
He says the acquisition of the businesses of Andersen is now
complete.
There were some minor hiccups when the tax partners in Cape Town left us
the focus now is on growing the business and driving transformation. "This
we will do through training our staff, recruiting from outside and mergers
with smaller, mainly black firms," he says.
Despite having been expelled from school in 1976, Kgosana is not into
politics.
"The only reason I was expelled is because of my uncle Philip who was
one of the PAC stalwarts."
But he supports the government's policies. "Trevor Manuel, Tito Mboweni
and Alec Erwin are doing a superb job."
On empowerment, Kgosana says there is no other way the economy could
advance except ensuring that more black people are allowed into the main
stream at all levels. "There is also no problem with black entrepreneurs
seeing opportunities and becoming millionaires"
He says he spends some time, when ever he can, with people like
presidential economics adviser Wiseman Nkuhlu and SA Institute of Chartered
Accountants Ignatius Sehoole on ways of getting more black CAs.
He says it is not easy. When you get them, they are too expensive
because there is a limited pool and they get enticed into lucrative
jobs. "Even myself, I get a lot of tempting offers."
But he says he has done his bit for the industry - even at home.  "When
my son did not know what to do after matric, as his father I chose
accounting for him and I know it is the right thing to do. He's studying
accounts at RAU now."
The father of three says he still has a lot to offer and when his
four-year term expires, he would not reject a renewal. When not visiting
clients or networking with his friends from the industry, he spends time on
his farm.
"It is a hobby, I make no money from it."
