He also plays golf of a 15 handicap and drives "just a bakkie".

Original_Search_Title: Heineken Surveys New Openings
Headline: Ghana: Heineken Surveys New Openings
Body_Text:
A DELEGATION from Heineken International, parent company of Ghana Breweries Limited, has visited the country to undertake a study of new business opportunities in the country.
In particular, the delegation is conducting a survey into the possibility of setting up a multi-million cedi project in Kumasi for the training of Ghanaians in Microsoft Certificate (MSC) and other information, communication and technology (ICT) related courses.
This was disclosed when the delegation held discussions with the Vice-President, Alhaji Aliu Mahama, at the Castle.
The Heineken International delegation included Mr. Jean-Francois van Boxmeer, Executive Director in Charge of Africa; Mr. Thomas de Man, Managing Director, Africa Department; and Prof. M. Looijen, Emeritus Professor of the University of Leiden and Consultant to the proposed MSC/ICT training center project.
Those who led the delegation to the meeting with the vice-president included Dr. Sir K. Donkor Fordwour, Member of the GBL board, Mr. Ishmael Yamson, Chairman of Unilever Ghana Limited; Mr. S. O Adebanji, Managing Director of GBL and a number of other GBL executive.
Alhaji Aliu Mahama commended Heineken International on its decision to undertake the Kumasi project. He further urged Heineken and GBL to study the prospects of expanding into fruits and food processing as well as the cultivation of Sorghum as a raw material for GBL products.
Such moves, the vice-president said, would help create jobs and conserve foreign currency.
Mr. Boxmeer described the Ghanaian investment climate as favourable and said Heineken International remained open to all ideas that would promote the welfare of its companies and the environments in which they operate.

Original_Search_Title: 'FG Commits N3.2bn to Food Production Annually'
Headline: Nigeria: 'FG Commits N3.2bn to Food Production Annually'
Body_Text:
President Olusegun Obasanjo has stated that the federal government has committed $26 million (N3.2 billion) per annum to developing biotechnology to promote food production.
President Obasanjo was quoted to have made this declaration in the recent edition of an American publication, Food from Genetically Improved Crops in Africa.
He noted that "the 21st century will be biological and diverse with biotechnology being the kingpin of the process," adding that biotechnology has been used to create many improved crops.
He cited insect resistant crops, reducing the need for pesticides or delayed ripening in tomatoes, and virus resistance in potatoes among others.
He said through biotechnology, industrialists and scientists are expected to replace an existing copy of a gene with another copy that may be slightly better, noting that new molecular and genetic techniques will make genetic modification or improvement of crops even more special industrial processing more precise.
He added that through rational approaches to traditional plant breeding, "we will be able to improve African crops, such as cowpeas, sorghum, cassava, and millet, that have not received much attention so for," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Indian Brewer Not About to Sell Its SA Sorghum Interest
Headline: South Africa: Indian Brewer Not About to Sell Its SA Sorghum Interest
Body_Text:
Cape Town — United Breweries of India was not planning to sell its stake in United
National Breweries (UNB), the SA sorghum beer company, and press reports in
India that speculated on the sale were inaccurate, UNB president and chief
operating officer Rajan Ranganathan said yesterday.
He was commenting on a report from the Economic Times in Mumbai in March
that United Breweries planned to sell the SA business because of a decline
in sorghum beer consumption .
Two weeks ago The Telegraph of Calcutta reported that United Breweries
planned to dispose of UNB, among other assets.
AK Ravi Nedungadi, chief financial officer of the United Breweries
group, was quoted in the article as saying it was looking to divest control
in the company and would sell its 75% stake in it "if they get a reasonable
price".
After a capital restructuring last year, in which United Breweries
bought out R46m of minority shareholders, United Breweries holds 74,99% of
UNB and Marriott Merchant Bank 25,01%.
Ranganathan said SA management had discussed the reports with its
holding company . What was true was that UNB was looking for a black
empowerment partner and held discussions with a number of groups but had not
found a suitable partner.
UNB has turned around in the past year and is now making profits, so it
would not be a good time to sell it. UNB management believed it could
increase profits further, Ranganathan said.
This was not because sorghum beer consumption was growing, since it was
a mature market, but because there were more efficiencies to be wrung out of
the business.
According to UNB's recent submission to public hearings on the Liquor
Bill, the sorghum beer market in SA is estimated at 1,7billion litres a
year.
Ranganathan said UNB's sales revenue was about $70m a year.
United Breweries is India's biggest brewer but its position is being
challenged by the new joint venture between Shaw Wallace, India's
second-largest brewer, and SABMiller, which has brought its six regional
breweries into its partnership.

Original_Search_Title: Country has Produced Enough Food - Sikatana
Headline: Zambia: Country has Produced Enough Food - Sikatana
Body_Text:
Lusaka — ZAMBIA has produced enough food and a modest surplus for export,
agriculture minister Mundia Sikatana has said.
And the Food Reserve Agency has received K15 billion to purchase maize
from small scale farmers, said Zambia National Farmers' Union (ZNFU)
president Guy Robinson yesterday.
Briefing the press in Lusaka yesterday, Sikatana said results of crop
forecast conducted by his ministry and the Central Statistical Office
indicated the total maize production in the country stood at 1.2 million
metric tonnes representing a more than 100 per cent increase compared to
last year's output.
He said in addition, there was a maize carry-over stock of 100,156
metric tonnes from last year's imports as well as winter maize
production. "This brings total availability for the country to 1.3 million
metric tonnes. Total maize requirements for human consumption is estimated
at 1.2 million tonnes," Sikatana said.
He said the country would have 120,000 metric tonnes of surplus
maize. Sikatana also said cassava production increased from 850,626 metric
tonnes to 958,113 metric tonnes of flour equivalent.
He said sorghum and millet production also increased by two per cent
from last year's figure while rice production had decreased by eight per
cent. Sikatana attributed the favourable crop production during the last
farming season to favourable weather conditions in most parts of the
country.
He also attributed the success to early and effective input distribution
programme by government and the civil society and the positive response by
farmers to government call for increased agricultural production. Sikatana,
however, observed that there were some areas especially in Southern Province
which recorded food deficits because of drought and floods.
"I wish to appeal to traders and farmers to ensure that all tradable
agricultural produce in the country is safely stored and marketed
accordingly," Sikatana said.
"In view of the foregoing, government will allow for some modest export
of surplus produce in order to inject more liquidity in the agricultural
economy." He warned that any exports would be closely monitored so as not to
allow domestic shortfalls.
Sikatana said some of the surplus maize may be exported to Zimbabwe
where there was an expected food crisis. However, he said the country should
not risk its food security and that little surplus should be taken care
of. Sikatana said all farmers should not allow anybody to take their crops
for a song because it was hard earned.
He said agriculture was tough business and produce should not be given
away easily. Sikatana said the government recommended floor price of K30,000
per 50 kilogram bag of maize was the standard price below which farmers
would be disadvantaged.
He said exports must be done sparingly and warned government would take
stern action to ensure crops were not abused. "The ministry is making all
efforts to get inputs for the coming season to be distributed on time. We
have already sent fertiliser to Southern Province," Sikatana said.
"The fertiliser support programme was abused last year and we will not
allow it this year."
On the Crop Marketing Authority, Sikatana said it was still on the door
step. He said the Bill was still going through various ministries for their
endorsement. "We hope we are not doing it merely for the change of
names. FRA may continue because it is successful.
But we will still present the Bill in Parliament," Sikatana said. He
also said the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) was given an impression that it was
a non performer but it was now on its feet. Sikatana disclosed that the FRA
last year raised about K17 billion.
And in a statement, Robinson said ZNFU had confirmed the K15 billion
funding and that FRA was now moving into other designated areas to buy maize
while government had assured they were mobilising more funds. However,
Robinson said for the future, the country needed to improve statistics on
maize stock movement.
"Currently, we do not have any one stop place to know how much maize
comes into the country, how much is exported, how much is processed into
mealie meal or stockfeed and what is in stock in the country," he said.
Robinson said these statistics, together with expected production
figures would assist the country make timely decisions on whether to allow
exports and when to import and stop importing.
"It will also assist the country in planning marketing activities,"
Robinson said. "Without maize stock movement statistics, it is difficult to
achieve correct and timely decisions. In the process industry suffers."

Original_Search_Title: Kebbi Farmers Predict Bumper Harvest This Year If...
Headline: Nigeria: Kebbi Farmers Predict Bumper Harvest This Year If...
Body_Text:
Birnin — Farmers in Kebbi state have predicted a bumper harvest in this year's farming season.
"Despite delay in the commencement of rain in some parts of the north, we still hope and predict a bumper harvest at the end of the season," said Mallam Abubakar Daniya, a rice farmer.
He said that crops, especially millet, guinea corn, rice and sorghum among others are expected to yield immensely this season.
According to him also, despite the unavailability of fertiliser that made farmers to devise local substitutes of improvising manure, yet they are hopeful, Abubakar Daniya added.
However, a visit to some rice producing areas in Ambursa and Dukku along the river Rima bank, Daily Trust observed that the crops planted within the month of April and May looks healthy and free from pests and diseases.
Some of the farmers who spoke with our correspondent only expressed fears and prayed that the yearly visiting and dangerous parasite, "quellae birds," should not come around the area this year.
While appealing to the state government to as a matter of urgency, take measures to ensure that the birds are finally dislodged every season because they cause serious havoc to farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Wholesale Commodity Prices Show Mixed Trend At Kariakoo
Headline: Tanzania: Wholesale Commodity Prices Show Mixed Trend At Kariakoo
Body_Text:
WHOLESALE prices of most commodities decreased while others rose
slightly during the second quarter of this year at the Kariakoo Market in
Dar es Salaam.
According to the market's price reports of between March and July, the
slight increase in prices was experienced in frozen Nile Perch, sorghum and
Irish potatoes.
A source from the Kariakoo Market Corporation said the decrease in
prices of most commodities was caused by rises in supplies since last month
when the crop harvesting season reached its peak.
The source predicts that wholesale prices of most commodities will
continue to decline following increased supplies of such commodities.
Reports show that last March, a 70-kilogramme bag of cassava was sold at
Tsh28,000. By the start of this month it decreased to sh26,000. The price of
a 100-kilogramme bag of onions decreased to sh70,000 from sh80,000 during
the same period.
According to the reports, a 60-kilogramme bag of tomatoes is sold at
sh24,000 compared to sh25,000 in the first quarter of this year.
Prices of a 200-kilogramme bag of tilapia, 50-kilogramme bag of sardines
from Kigoma and a piece of cured Nile perch have decreased to sh230,000,
sh130,000 and sh4,000 respectively from sh280,000, sh150,000 and sh4,500 in
the first quarter of the year.
Prices for ripe bananas and pineapples also declined during the period
under review. Last March a bunch of ripe bananas was sold at sh24,000 while
a pineapple was sh450. Now the prices are sh20,000 and sh400
respectively.
However, prices of a 60-kilogramme bag of oranges, an 80-kilogramme bag
of sardines from Lake Victoria and a 100-kilogramme bag of wheat remained
constant during the period under review because supplies are
smooth. However, the wholesale price for few commodities such as frozen Nile
perch, Irish potatoes and sorghum increased during the period under
review.
According to the reports, 100 kilogrammes of frozen Nile perch from Lake
Victoria cost sh90,000. In the first quarter of this year, the price was
sh85,000.

Original_Search_Title: Seed Availability Could Be a Problem, Says SADC
Headline: Southern Africa: Seed Availability Could Be a Problem, Says SADC
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The outlook for seed availability for the 2003/04 growing season is mixed, warns the latest Southern African Development Community (SADC) Food Security Network Ministerial Brief.
Zimbabwe, with about 5 million people said to require food aid, is still the country most affected by food shortages and a deficit of 40,000 mt of maize seed is anticipated.
"Soybean seed should be adequate, and a surplus 1,000 mt of cotton is expected. However, a deficit of about 3,000 mt of open pollinated sorghum seed is expected. A further deficit of 120 mt of groundnut seed and 1,100 mt of millet seed is also foreseen," the report noted.
The critical seed shortages were attributed to a combination of climatic factors, human and animal diseases, and the land reform programme which had resulted in role of the "formerly strong commercial sector in certified seed production" not being sustained.
"Resettled farmers are yet to acquire experience in seed production. Some of them lack essential resources for seed production. A further drawback experienced this season concerns the security of seed crops. Most of the crops are in deficit due to thefts of seed from fields reported by seed growers and seed companies," the Seed Security Network noted.
The brief adds that due to the magnitude of the deficit, seed would have to be imported from other sources in the region.
However, the report notes that there are "difficulties in moving seeds between countries in the region. Current seed regulations in the SADC region are not friendly to regional seed trade and continue to act as barriers".
The SADC Seed Security Network was working to harmonise seed regulations so as to foster intra-regional seed trade.
"Elsewhere in the region, current information indicates a mixed pattern. Prospects for seed availability for the growing season 2003/04 are largely favourable for South Africa, Mozambique, Malawi, Seychelles and Mauritius, which have adequate seed.
"Detailed information from South Africa is difficult to obtain. However, the general indication is that the country will have enough seed for both local and export demands. Botswana and Zambia will experience a slight reduction in seed and require to source seed from within the region," the report said.
Information on seed prospects for Angola, Namibia, Lesotho and Swaziland was not yet available.

Original_Search_Title: No Health Facilities in Rebel-Controlled Abyei County
Headline: Sudan: No Health Facilities in Rebel-Controlled Abyei County
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An NGO survey of rebel-controlled Abyei county has found that there are
no health services available to a population of about 32,000 people, forcing
them to walk for between two and three days to access medical care.
About 60 percent of people relied on traditional healers and "spear
masters" who performed witchcraft, the Irish charity GOAL reported, with
about 20 percent opting for formal health care in Abyei town, where there is
a hospital, or neighbouring Twic county, which has primary health
clinics.
A lack of money and the distance involved determined where people went,
GOAL reported, adding that those who were unable to walk had to be carried
on stretchers.
Community leaders had set up "emergency health posts" in rebel-held
Abyei three years ago, but there were no medicines and no salaries available
which meant they were unsustainable, GOAL programme officer Martina Collins,
told IRIN.
Malaria, diarrhoeal diseases, respiratory infections, malnutrition and
measles were the main causes of mortality in the area. Two thirds of people
had no blankets and slept around fires, exposing themselves to mosquitos and
malaria as the fires died down and the smoke - which acts as a repellant -
thinned out, GOAL reported.
Despite a food surplus in the region, almost one quarter of the children
surveyed appeared to be malnourished. This was due to a poor diet of
sorghum, lacking protein content, which young children were being fed from
when they stopped breast feeding, said Collins.
There was also no immunisation available for children apart from
polio. The only children who had been immunised - eight out of 116 surveyed
- were returnees from northern Sudan.
Abyei has suffered several decades of conflict between the Ngok Dinka in
the south and the Misseriya Arabs to the north. Although it has been
peaceful since late 2002, thousands of Dinka remain displaced. Five camps in
government-controlled northern Abyei house an estimated 70,000 people, with
a further 50,000 scattered throughout the province of Bahr el Ghazal.
The Abyei Community Action for Development (ACAD), a local NGO,
estimates that 1,300 households have returned to southern Abyei so far this
year, while another 750 people are expected within the next week. The
returnees, mostly from Khartoum, were visibly better off than local people
who had not been displaced, Reuben Haylett, a medical coordinator with GOAL,
told IRIN.
Many others who had returned, had chosen not to stay due to the lack of
services, and moved on to neighbouring Twic county or to the IDP camps in
northern Abyei, Deng Mading, director of ACAD, told IRIN.
A regional conference held in Agok, Abyei county, in early June
expressed "outrage at the subhuman conditions" in which the IDPs were
living.
The over 700 conference participants - no government representation was
present - called on the international community to guarantee "free movement
and adequate protection and security" for the repatriation of the IDPs. They
also asserted that they were "part and parcel" of southern Sudan and
identified the Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army (SPLM/A) as their
"legitimate representative" in any negotiations with the government.
The status of Abyei is currently one of the most contentious issues in
negotiations for a national peace agreement in Sudan. The SPLM/A insists on
the right to hold a referendum in Abyei on whether to join southern
Sudan. In theory, this would precede the main southern referendum, on
secession, to be held six and a half years after a peace agreement is
signed. The government has so far insisted that Abyei is a local issue and
does not require negotiation with the SPLM/A.

Original_Search_Title: FAO Delivers Agricultural Aid Shipment to Nuba Mountains
Headline: Sudan: FAO Delivers Agricultural Aid Shipment to Nuba Mountains
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The UN Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO) said on Tuesday it had
successfully delivered a substantial amount of seeds and tools by road to
more than 10,500 households in a previously inaccessible rebel-held
territory in southern Sudan.
The FAO said it had distributed about 130 tonnes of sorghum, maize,
sesame, cowpea, groundnut and vegetable seeds to various parts of the Nuba
Mountains controlled by the Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army
(SPLM/A).
Farmers also received some 13,400 agricultural tools, including hoes,
axes, machetes and sickles, to support the resettlement of returnees and
boost food security in the region, the statement said.
The delivery of the urgently needed agricultural aid to the disputed
region was made possible by an internationally monitored ceasefire agreement
signed between the Sudanese government and the SPLA/M in January 2002, it
added.
Anne Bauer, the FAO's director for emergency operations and
rehabilitation, said the operation was "a real breakthrough for people who
have been living under insecure and extreme conditions" in the Nuba
Mountains. "This project will make farmers and their families less dependent
on food aid and could be a contribution to peace and stability in the
region," she said in the statement.
The FAO said it had also distributed about 154 tonnes of local crop
seeds and more than 16,000 hand tools to an estimated 12,500 displaced
families and returnees in areas controlled by the Sudanese government. "In
total, the delivery of seeds and tools is expected to result in the
production of 8,200 tonnes of food in 2003, and to improve livelihoods and
food security - a contribution to peace," the statement said.
The Nuba Mountains region, geographically located in northern Sudan, but
whose people have identified with the SPLM/A struggle, has been a zone of
conflict since 1985.
As a result of the war, a large part of the region's population has been
forced off the plains and is living in insecure and precarious conditions,
cultivating the limited agricultural land on the hills.

Original_Search_Title: Kwazulu Liquor Bill May Grant Sunday Sales
Headline: South Africa: Kwazulu Liquor Bill May Grant Sunday Sales
Body_Text:
Durban — The KwaZulu-Natal provincial government is expecting widespread debate
over new liquor law proposals, following the release at the weekend for
public comment of a draft bill which will also open the door for Sunday
trading.
In terms of the constitution, the licensing of liquor retailers and
micromanufacturers, and the licensing of sorghum beer manufacturers and
retailers, are provincial competencies.
A national Liquor Bill was introduced to the National Assembly in 1998,
but past president Nelson Mandela referred it to the Constitutional
Court.
The national bill, aimed at replacing the existing "outdated and
inefficient" law, is being reworked. It currently contains a default
provincial bill should provinces not enact a provincial act within a year of
the national Liquor Act being passed.
KwaZulu-Natal economic development and tourism MEC, Roger Burrows,
released the draft provincial licensing bill for public comment on
Friday. Following consideration of written comments, the draft will be
submitted to cabinet and then to the provincial parliament. Burrows expected
the law to be in place by December.
He said he hoped the new legislation would bring unlicensed
establishments on board, thus "levelling the playing field and making the
industry easier to police". These included the shebeens, whose value Burrows
placed at billions of rands.
However, he expected "considerable debate" over proposals to grant
licences in terms of the retail sale of liquor for consumption on and off
the premises.
This would cover buying alcohol at a restaurant, but taking home the
unopened bottle. Burrows said this category "took into account what was
already happening".
The legislation also opens the door for Sunday trading, with Burrows
saying the provincial liquor licensing authority would consider the
restrictions on trading days and hours. However, there would be provision to
prevent outlets selling alcohol in socially unacceptable places, such as
next to a church during services, or next to a school.
Burrows also said applicants needed written consent from their local
authority for a trading licence before a liquor licence would be
considered. This covered shebeens opening in residential areas, where the
surrounding residents were unhappy with its existence.
Burrows criticised the current law, dating to 1989, as "outdated and
extremely inefficient in its practical application". He said governments
over the past two centuries have successfully used liquor laws for
separation and suppression.
The draft bill proposes reducing the current 25 liquor licence
categories to six. These include onpremises retail consumption; offpremises
retail consumption; onand-off-premises retail consumption; retail sales in
terms of a special licence (alcohol sales in a temporary beer tent);
micromanufacturing (small breweries and wineries) and the manufacture and
sale of sorghum beer.
Public comment on the draft bill must be submitted to the department by
August 15.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agriculture Agency Delivers Sorely Needed Seeds And Tools to South
Headline: Sudan: UN Agriculture Agency Delivers Sorely Needed Seeds And Tools to South
Body_Text:
New York — With 300,000 households in southern Sudan in dire need of seeds and
tools, the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has for
the first time delivered a substantial amount of both items by road for tens
of thousands of people in previously inaccessible rebel held areas in the
Nuba Mountains.
"It is a real breakthrough that FAO succeeded in delivering seeds and
tools by road to people who have been living under insecure and extreme
conditions," Anne M. Bauer, Director for FAO's Emergency Operations and
Rehabilitation said in a statement released in Rome today. "This project
will make farmers and their families less dependent on food aid and could be
a contribution to peace and stability in the region."
More than 10,500 households benefited from the assistance in the region,
controlled by the rebel Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army (SPLM/A),
where many people have been forced off the plains by war and are now living
in precarious conditions, cultivating limited agricultural land on steep
slopes and flat land on the top of and in between hills. The delivery became
possible after a ceasefire agreement between the Sudanese government and the
rebels in January 2002.
FAO distributed 130 tonnes of sorghum, maize, sesame, cowpea, groundnut
and vegetable seeds to different parts of the area. Farmers also received
13,400 agricultural tools in the project, which supports the resettlement of
returnees and the early rehabilitation of agriculture.
FAO has also distributed 154 tonnes of local crop seeds and more than
16,000 hand tools to displaced people and returnees in areas controlled by
the government. This relief aid will improve the food situation of an
estimated 12,500 farm families.
In total, the delivery of seeds and tools is expected to result in the
production of 8,200 tonnes of food in 2003.
The project is financed by the governments of Norway as the main
contributor, the Netherlands and Sweden. FAO said some 300 000 households in
southern Sudan are in dire need of seeds and tools, estimated at nearly $8.6
million, to resume farming in the upcoming season starting this month. So
far, FAO has received $1.7 million.

Original_Search_Title: Emergency Relief Assistance for Government
Headline: Gambia: Emergency Relief Assistance for Government
Body_Text:
Banjul — The FAO, the Department of Agriculture and the Government of the Gambia
in collaboration with the ADB recently signed an emergency assistance to
meet the best level of the communities by putting $500, 000 at the
government's disposal.
A total population of 235,000 people, a majority of which are women and
children are at risk of starvation if the effects of last year's drought are
not adequately addressed.
The Secretary for Finance and Economic Affairs, Famara Jatta said during
the signing ceremony that The Gambia government does not have the required
resources to assist affected communities and therefore, needed international
contribution in order to improve the nutritional status of those in the
affected areas. He said the provision of seeds, such 360 metric tones of
early millet, 72 mt of late millet, 176 mt of Sorghum Maize, 270mt of paddy
rice and 640mt of unshelled groundnut will improve the food production to
the level of 2001.
Furthermore, he said the deepening of 52 wells and the construction of
30 new ones will increase the availability of safe drinking water in the
affected communities.
Mr. Jatta who described the agricultural sector as the engine of growth
in the government's P.R.S.P. admitted that it is a weak sector that requires
significant investments in order to enable it to play a leading role in the
economic growth of The Gambia. The effects of the drought he pointed out if
not addressed in time will further weaken an already weak sector, which will
result in increased poverty and malnutrition. The provision of seeds and
construction or deepening of wells, he said will assist in speeding up the
recovery of the sector especially with regards to food security.
Furthermore, Mr. Jatta recommended that the Fund provide from the
Special Relief Fund (SRF) a grant not exceeding US $500,000 to the
government of the Gambia.
Prior to the disbursement of the fund he said FAO shall have provided to
the bank acceptable to the fund into which the proceeds of the grant shall
be deposited, he pointed. He added that the bank and the government of the
Gambia shall have signed a protocol of agreement pursuant to which the
government will agree to have the emergency operations implemented as set
out.

Original_Search_Title: On a Bumpy Road Towards Democracy
Headline: Mauritania: On a Bumpy Road Towards Democracy
Body_Text:
Nouakchott — Last month's bloody coup attempt highlighted the fact that beneath a veneer of multi-party democracy, Mauritania remains very much a traditional society where control of the army and membership of clan and family are the real keys to power.
The pro-western government of President Maaouiya Ould Sid Ahmed Taya, an army colonel who seized power in a coup 19 years ago, survived a two-day battle with rebel soldiers to the relief of neighbouring governments and Mauritania's Western donors.
Many Mauritanians condemned the coup, but they regarded it as being motivated more by family vendettas among the ruling elite than about "bread and butter" issues affecting the country's largely poor population.
One French-trained economist said that while Ould Taya was not a popular figure, Hanenna's coup attempt had attracted little popular support. "Between the bad and what could have been worse, Mauritanians want neither," he told IRIN.
On the night of 8 June, rebel military units, led by a former army colonel, Saleh Ould Hannena, rolled a column of tanks into the capital Nouakchott and began shelling the presidential palace.
State radio and television went off the air and for 36 hours the capital of this desert nation of only 2.5 million people echoed to the sound of gunfire.
However, military units loyal to Ould Taya eventually crushed the uprising and Ould Taya reappeared on TV screens to confirm that he was back in control.
The rebels never said why they wanted to overthrow Ould Taya or what they stood for and the government has not publicly identified the coup plotters. One official dismissed the bloody battle which left 29 people dead and dozens wounded, as "just a bump on the road."
But Mauritania's neighbours and western donors took it seriously enough. Within days, French foreign minister Dominique de Villepin jetted in for a lightning visit, followed shortly after by Morocco's King Mohamed VI. Sighs of relief and messages of support, meanwhile came from the United States, Senegal and Algeria.
Although Ould Taya was quick to announce that presidential elections would go ahead as planned on 7 November, security in Nouakchott has been beefed up since the coup attempt. Heavily armed soldiers have mounted guard outside the presidential palace, radio and TV studios and other government buildings. And at night military check points have been thrown up across the city of one million people to search cars and check the identity of their occupants.
Initially there was widespread speculation that the coup attempt was partly a reaction to a month-long crackdown on Islamic radicals by Ould Taya.
Diplomats feared that had he been overthrown, Mauritania might have severed its unpopular diplomatic relations with Israel, loosened its ties with the United States and become more closely identified with the Arab world.
But many Mauritanians suspect that the coup attempt was more about family and clan rivalries, than fundamental issues of government policy.
Ould Hannena has gone into hiding, but many of his relatives in government have been sacked or arrested.
Victims of the purge included the head of the Supreme Court, the mayor of the northern port city of Nouadhibou, the deputy director of the government news agency, the minister in charge of women's affairs, the national secretary of the ruling Republican Social Democrat Party (PRDS) and several military officers.
The joke around town is that most of these people stood accused of "kinship" with Ould Hanenna, rather than any active role in the rebellion.
Ould Hanenna himself was drummed out of the army in 2001 and was subsequently seen driving a taxi and selling cars. No official reason was given for his discharge, but he was widely believed to have been linked with a group of officers that wrote a letter to the president seeking better pay and conditions.
Little is known about Ould Hanenna's political beliefs, although he is suspected of sympathy towards the deposed Baathist regime of Sadaam Hussein in Iraq. Like the man he tried to depose, he is a member of the fair skinned Bidan elite.
He hails from the Ayoun region, near the eastern border with Mali and received military training in the Middle East, where Mauritania formerly sent cadets to military academies in Egypt, Saudi Arabia and Iraq. The fugitive coup leader is also believed to have opposed Ould Taya's controversial decision to establish full diplomatic relations with Israel in 1999.
Mauritania provides a delicate bridge between the Arab states of the Mahgreb and the black nations of Sub-Saharan Africa and ethnic tensions smoulder just belong the surface. There are therefore many vested interests in maintaining the stability in this former French colony, now an Islamic Republic that uses Arabic as its official language.
Increasingly it has looked to the Arab world rather than Sub-Saharan Africa for inspiration. Mauritania first withdrew from the CFA franc zone to establish its own currency in the 1970's and then left the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) in 2000.
Two thirds of the population are Moors, who are culturally closer to Mauritania's northern neighbour Morocco than Senegal to the south. During the early years of independence Morocco claimed Mauritania as a southern province. It only recognised the government in Nouakchott in 1969.
But a third of Mauritania's populations are Black Africans who live near the southern border with Senegal. They belong to the same Wolof and Fulani ethnic groups that live in greater numbers on the south bank of the Senegal River, which forms the border between the two countries.
The Moorish community is sharply divided between the "Bidan" or white Moors, who dominate government, and the "Harratin" or black Moors, who were formerly slaves and today still generally occupy a lower rank on the social scale.
Slavery was only abolished by law in 1980 and ethnic tensions erupted into communal fighting in 1989 that led to many southern blacks being forcibly deported to Senegal and Mali.
Ould Taya has, on the surface at least, begun to modernise the country.
He legalised opposition parties in 1991 and drafted a new constitution that turned Mauritania into a multi-party democracy. Over the past decade he has also liberalised the economy, which is dependent on exports of iron ore and revenues from licencing foreign fishing boats, and has presided over a modest improvement in living standards.
According to the African Development Bank, the economy grew by four percent a year between 1990 and 2000. The United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) estimates that the number of Mauritanians living below the poverty line fell from 56 percent of the population to 46 percent over the same period.
Now there are hopes that offshore oil will start flowing in 2005 and help to spread the wealth currently enjoyed by a small urban elite more widely. Woodside Petroleum of Australia struck a promising oil reservoir with its Chinguetti exploration well in May 2001, since then other companies have been gearing up to drill more.
But behind the facade of modernity and progress, drought and famine still plague subsistence farmers in the southeast of the country. Opposition leaders question the economic success story told by official statistics. They accuse Ould Taya of running a corrupt government packed with members of his own family and clan that has failed to tackle the country's real problems effectively. "Tribu" the French word for tribe, crops up continuously in political discussions.
"The fundamental problem is to know if the State is fulfilling its primary roles? Is it promoting economic justice and social equality? Democracy and human rights?" said Hamidou Baba Kane, an economist and parliamentarian of the opposition Rally of Democratic Forces (RFD).
He expressed fears that the hope for oil boom would only widen the gap between rich and poor. While a minority of western-educated men wears smart suits and drive around Nouakchott in expensive cars, most of the city's middle class are growing slowly poorer and beggars line the streets. And among the drought-hit millet and sorghum fields of the south, many subsistence farmers and their families are quietly starving.
Although Ould Taya boasts that he introduced multi-party democracy to Mauritania, he has continued to ban political parties that he accuses of "threatening state authority" and detain their leaders. Critical newspapers have been shut down for similar reasons.
Political analysts say that as a result the opposition remains muzzled, weak and divided.
"The job of the opposition becomes very difficult under a tyrannical regime," Kane, the RFD leader, said.
Cheick Saad Bouh Kamara, a law professor at Nouakchott university, told IRIN that after 19 years of strong-arm government by Ould Taya, Mauritanians were yearning for change, but did not want to plunge the country into armed conflict to achieve it.
Kamara, who is vice-president of the International Federation of Human Rights, said Mauritanians wanted "peace at all costs, but not at any price."
So far three opposition politicians have announced plans to challenge Ould Taya in the November presidential election, including for the first time, a woman. Aicha Mint Jiddana, a 43-year-old businesswoman, is campaigning against female circumcision and forced marriage.
But it remains to be seen whether Ould Taya's western friends will pressure him into running a freer, fairer and more transparent poll than the last presidential election in 1997 which was boycotted by most of the opposition.

Original_Search_Title: Port Expects 13,000 Tonnes of Relief Cargo
Headline: Africa: Port Expects 13,000 Tonnes of Relief Cargo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A total of 13,484 tonnes of relief cargo for neighbouring countries is
expected at the port of Mombasa.
According to the World Food Programme, countries faced with an acute
food shortage as a result of adverse weather and internal strife include
Uganda, Ethiopia, Somalia, Democratic Republic of Congo and Sudan.
A Kenya Ports Authority official says a consignment of wheat and sorghum
imported by Care International is destined for Somalia.
World Food Programme says one fifth of the Ethiopian population is faced
with a serious food shortage.
Its bulletin says more than 1.3 million Ethiopians will require food aid
this year while another three million are likely to be affected later,
bringing the total number to than 4.3 million.
Violence in northern Uganda has left more than 800,000 people in urgent
need of food aid, a number WFP says is a significant increase on the 520,000
displaced people the agency was assisting last July.
In Sudan, two decades of armed conflict and recurrent drought have left
3.3 million civilians displaced and their livelihoods destroyed.
In the DRC, hundreds of thousands lack food after being displaced by
civil war.

Original_Search_Title: Country Expects One of Its Worst Ever Harvests
Headline: Namibia: Country Expects One of Its Worst Ever Harvests
Body_Text:
Windhoek — THE severe floods in the Caprivi Region, poor rainfall, a shortage of
seeds and damage done to crops by elephants and birds have resulted in
Namibia expecting one of the worst harvests this year.
The latest bulletin of the Namibia Early Warning and Food Information
System (Newfis) issued yesterday forecasts that Namibia will have to import
116 400 tons of cereal this year.
"There were numerous problems that caused poor cereal production this
season apart from the dry-spells. It was observed that the lack of rainfall
from mid-February through March brought about a situation whereby most
late-planted crops could not reach maturity and of those that did, the
grains were of poor quality," the bulletin says.
It notes that other problems include a shortage of seeds, particularly
in the Caprivi and Kavango Regions where most farmers were unable to retain
seed from the previous season while drought-relief seed was delivered very
late.
The Quelea birds were particularly damaging in the Omusati Region while
elephants caused havoc - most notably in the Kavango region, the report
says.
One of the worst blows was the late flooding in the eastern part of the
Caprivi Region.
"In Caprivi, the devastating floods in the eastern part towards the end
of the season worsened the already poor harvest particularly in areas around
Ngoma, Itomba and Bukalo. The flooding aggravated the food insecurity
situation as most crops were not harvested by then and consequently
perished," the bulletin says.
Crops kept in traditional grain storages were ruined by the floods while
some livestock drowned. Others were trapped by water and starving.
Quelea birds feeding on late planted crops were reported in the
north-central regions of Omusati, Oshana, Ohangwena and also in the Kavango
Region where they led to the reduction in already poor yields.
"There were serious cases of damage by birds in many parts of Omusati
region especially in Onesi, Ruacana and Etunda," Newfis says.
The unit adds that this year's production of sorghum crop, especially in
the Omusati Region, was the worst since Independence because of poor rains
that prevented it from reaching grain formation, let alone maturity
stage.
"The yields for maize this season are the lowest since 1998/99 cropping
season, ranging from 40 kg/hectare in Kavango to 160 kg/hectare for Caprivi
Region. This is particularly poor for Caprivi given the fact that maize is
the staple food crop for the region," the bulletin says.
Given the reduced area planted and the low average yield estimate, the
bulletin says coarse grain production was estimated at 91 100 tons,
comprising of 32 700 tons of white maize and 58 400 tons of mahangu/sorghum
combined.
The expected output was about 67 per cent of the 1999/00 production
season (135 500 tons).
The estimated cereal production, together with operating stocks, give a
total availability of 148 700 tons for the 2003-2004 marketing year, made up
of 88 500 tons of mahangu/sorghum, 39 500 tons of white maize and 20 700
tons of wheat.
Assuming a projected national population figure of 1,97 million and an
average per capita cereal consumption of 125 kg, the national cereal food
use was provisionally calculated at 246 300 tons.
The deficit is calculated on the difference between the domestic cereal
supply of 148 700 tons and domestic utilisation of 246 300 tons.
The bulletin said commercial millers planned to import about 116 400
tons, comprising 45 000 tons of wheat and 71 400 tons of white maize.

Original_Search_Title: Kyerematen Commends GGL Sorghum Initiative
Headline: Ghana: Kyerematen Commends GGL Sorghum Initiative
Body_Text:
Accra — Delving Hainsworth to his officeAn initiative by Guinness Ghana Limited to develop 10,000 acres of sorghum- farms in northern Ghana has received commendation from Mr. Alan Kyerematen, Minister of Trade, Industry and President's Special Initiative.
The commendation came after Mr. Devlin Hainsworth, Managing Director of Guinness Ghana had briefed the Minister on developments within his company during last Tuesday's courtesy call on Hon. Kyerematen at his offices in Accra.He described Guinness as a role of a Good Corporate Citizen that had delivered on its social responsibilities regardless of the political situation or Government in power.
Hon. Kyerematen likened the Guinness sorghum project, which will employ 5,000 rural farmers to the various President's Special Initiative such as the production of starch at Bodjwase in the Central Region which so far has employed more than 10,000 farmers.
The Minister promised to be in touch with stakeholders in the Guinness sorghum project in order to share experience and ensure the project's success.
He urged Guinness, on the other hand, to interact with his ministry for co- operation in areas such as human resource development and raw, material sourcing.In his briefing, Guinness Ghana CEO, Mr. Devlin Hainsworth said the sorghum project was initiated in 2000 with the objective to produce 3,000 metric tonnes of the cereal as substitute for imported barley in the production of Guinness Foreign Extra Stout.
Guinness was fully committed to sorghum in Ghana in demonstration of which the company has already converted its cookers and other civil works at the Kaasi (Kumasi) brewery at a cost of more than $1 million to receive the local raw material.
Mr. Hainsworth announce the acquisition of a £10million bottling line to be operational by the end of the year. The company, meanwhile, is to undertake its biannual maintenance exercise and upgrade its existing production line.
Hon. Alima Mahama, (Hajia), Deputy Minister of Trade, Industry and President's Special Initiatives, Mr. Agyapong, Chief Director and Mrs. Pamela Djamson - Tettey, External Affairs Director of Guinness Ghana were among those present at the meeting.

Original_Search_Title: GMB Food Procurement in Limbo
Headline: Zimbabwe: GMB Food Procurement in Limbo
Body_Text:
Harare — A REGIONAL food security assessment organ has warned the Grain Marketing Board (GMB) could fail to execute its food procurement obligations due to "inherent inefficiencies and corruption".
"Reduced deliveries to the GMB, together with inherent inefficiencies and corruption within the parastatal are expected to limit the government's capacity to provide food," the Southern African Development Community Famine Early Warning Systems Network (Fews) said in its latest report on the country's food security situation.
The GMB is the sole legal procurement agency for maize grain, both locally and offshore.
Zimbabwe's southern parts are facing a serious threat of famine due to poor harvests.
The government has already declared a disaster in the south, encompassing mainly the two Matabeleland provinces.
In its food security report, Fews warned that the parastatal would even be constrained from making substantial imports by the current foreign currency shortages in the country.
"It's capacity to import is going to be reduced further by the poor export performance that is expected to continue throughout the current marketing year," the report said.
GMB has in the recent past managed to get an average 13 percent of the estimated national maize production, with projections hinting a rocky spell for the parastatal due to a host of factors, the report said. Major constraints likely to affect GMB's market operations include a lower maize producer price offered by the parastatal, limited deliveries by smallholder farmers due to poor harvests and non-production by large-scale commercial farmers.
This week, Agriculture Minister Joseph Made said the GMB would ensure it moved closer to farmers to reduce transport costs.
This, he said, would ensure transport costs do not erode the farmers' margins.
The Fews report said staple cereals - maize, wheat and sorghum - remained critically in short supply throughout the country, and this was pushing prices up, forcing sellers to evade GMB to sell on the parallel market.
Farming industry players said non-governmental organisations were trying to avert the disaster by marshalling enough food aid.
It is understood the government is pleading with donors for food aid but it will take some time before more assistance starts trickling in as donors such as the WFP have to forward the request to the UN.
Food aid from non-governmental organ- isation has played a critical role in averting famine in the country, currently going through its worst economic crisis in history which has been worsened by erratic rainfall in the past three years.
Many large-scale farmers have been replaced by peasants and non-farming urbanites in a move designed to de-racialise commercial farming. That has reduced farming output. Some have not occupied pieces of land allocated to them as they do not have adequate funds to buy inputs and machinery and develop required infrastructure.
The government says the uptake for the commercial farms has been in the region of between 70 and 75 percent.

Original_Search_Title: Who is to Blame?
Headline: Zambia: Who is to Blame?
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The single most important cause of poverty in Zambia is not drought, HIV or witchcraft but unemployment. The single most important cause of unemployment is MMD mal-administration (and we are just being polite).
A lot of people believe that structural adjustment policies are imposed on Africa but the IMF as we know, does not have an army. Who is it that forces the president of Nigeria to privatize firms and raise petrol prices? When he was finance minister, our own Kebby Musokotwane once said in relation to IMF conditionalities that when we sign the agreement, it ceases to be an IMF document and they become OUR conditionalities. How can it be otherwise?
We all know that SAP does not work for us we even call it (Satana Ali Pano) and yet we allow our government (in what way is it our government?) to continue to exchange lead for gold or 100 million dollars in externalized profits, debt repayment and skills for 1 million dollars in aid.
Yes European imperialists are greedy but they cannot help themselves, they have always been like that. What is our excuse for getting into a situation where the priority of the Zambian government is NOT feeding the children or providing medicines to the sick but turning land, water and even citizens into commodities for foreign investors to speculate in? No fellow Zambians, it is not the IMF to blame. We have brought this death sentence upon ourselves and we have executed ourselves for no reason other than maybe stupidity. Most people call it suicide but we call it MMD government.
And Zambians are not alone. Virtually any assessment of the human condition whether done by the UNDP (Human Development Report), World Bank (World Development Report) or the Commission for Human Security (Human Security Report) reveals the miserable failure of all the world's leaders to provide fellow human beings with freedom from fear and freedom from want.
We in Africa have always known the colonial and neocolonial armies to be anything but our protectors. How many Africans can approach a police roadblock and not feel anxious that their lives may well be in danger, or at least their wallets? We refer to our hospitals as departure lounges and the Ministers of Health are not ashamed of themselves. But exactly what do we need such ministers for? We really must learn to bring our politicians to account. They cannot serve Satan (SAP) and their fellow citizens at the same time.
On May 26 and 27, the speaker of the South African parliament Dr Frene Ginwala and Africa Institute of South Africa convened a civil society meeting to review the new Human Security Report. Vice President Jacob Zuma participated in this meeting and gave us a lot of evidence that Africans die for nothing (not his words). All over Central Africa the Musevenites and other militarists hold millions of people to ransom refusing to accept peace because they feel when they put their rifles down they will cease to be important. Just imagine that! For the egos of some drunken politician our countries cannot know peace. We cannot even blame the UN for its ineffectiveness without seeing that it took an African Secretary General to make it completely useless. So at the end of the day, we must be our own liberators for that is how it has always been. No amount of Swedish funded Pajeros is going to bring clean water, schools or jobs to rural Isoka.
At the same meeting in Pretoria, I could not help being frightened of the future for the SADC youth who spoke about their situation in different countries. When I was their age, I did not have the fears that they have that they may not have a future. Clearly between 1980 when I left university and went straight into a government job and today, someone has sold the future to foreign investors.
Let us suppose that our elected leaders in parliament took their responsibilities seriously, would they allow an IMF economist to read the Minister of Finance's annual budget speech before they did? Policy Framework Paper indeed! Prisons are overcrowded yet no one dares think that prisoners could make the bricks and build the new prisons themselves. Oh, no, we need a foreign investor to think such thoughts.
Of course once in a while a Mundia Sikatana will show that you can grow maize (and why not millet or sorghum like our ancestors did?) without a World Bank loan but a Sikatana cannot think for everyone else in government.
Our presidents go to G8 summits and complain that they cannot access the US and European markets because African produce is discriminated against and cannot compete against the inefficient but subsidized European descendants of feudal lords and US agribusiness (not forgetting the slave owners). Why or why can't African presidents say we shall subsidize and protect our agriculture until the global playing field is level?
Someone once sang that we bring poverty upon ourselves (kuchula kuifwaila) and poverty is a choice. It would be our choice if we did not have such unpopular (in every sense of the word) governments. They preside over death and dictatorship and still manage to win elections.
How is that possible? As more Zambians go to another civil society seminar or conference and share woeful tales about how unfair this capitalist world bank regime is, just ask yourself why you continue to put up with it!

Original_Search_Title: Kasempa Council Workers Go Without Pay for 25 Months
Headline: Zambia: Kasempa Council Workers Go Without Pay for 25 Months
Body_Text:
UNIONISED workers at Kasempa District Council have not been paid their
salaries for over 25 months.
Council secretary Johnson Shindano said those in management had also not
been paid for 30 months.
Mr Shindano said the council was failing to pay the workers as their
revenue base had shrunk after the local authority sold most of the
assets.
"We do not have so much resources to raise capital as most of the
ventures in which the council used to raise money have been sold off," he
said.
Mr Shindano said the wage bill for Kasempa District Council was pegged
at K12.5 million per month.
He said the council in the past depended on the collection of rentals
from council houses which were sold to private individuals.
Mr Shindano said the local authority had introduced a flat rate which it
collected from the houses which had not yet been evaluated.
He said the Local Government Act which was in the process of being
revised should be done quickly so that the local authorities could be
empowered in terms of revenue.
He said Kasempa District Council was now undertaking agricultural
projects to expand the revenue base.
Mr Shindano said the council grew maize and sorghum last year and there
were plans to grow other cash crops such as cotton and paprika.

Original_Search_Title: Local Brew And Thatch Grass the Top Earners for Kavango Villagers
Headline: Namibia: Local Brew And Thatch Grass the Top Earners for Kavango Villagers
Body_Text:
Windhoek — TRADITIONAL beer and thatch grass are the top money spinners for many
villagers in the Kavango Region.
The information is contained in statistics of a survey detailing incomes
of residents of rural Kavango, east of Rundu.
Charlie Paxton, who works for Rossing Foundation's Craft Development
Programme in the Kavango and conducts the surveys, said of the beer brew:
"It's a significant income in the area".
However, beer making is limited to women, and they brew it in addition
to other household chores.
In her latest survey, Paxton found that women brewing their ow" beer
from mahangu and sorghum make nearly N$17 500 a year, compared to N$17 600
for the sale of thatch grass for making roofs.
In addition, other type of beer making bring an income of nearly N$5 000
for individuals.
Paxton pointed out that what makes beer relatively lucrative is not
mainly because of the alcohol in it but "it's the cheapest source of
food".
She said the traditional beer is rich in carbohydrates.
Paxton explained that the figures represent "the maximum potential
earning, if they were making beer as a [full-time] job".
Most brew for only a few days in a week.
Other income earners are (per year): Harvests per 10ha N$9 000 Ploughing
N$8 400 Local grass N$6 300 Vetkoekies N$3 700 Handicraft N$3 200 Reeds N$1
400 Fish N$800 Firewood N$400

Original_Search_Title: More Sorghum Needed
Headline: Uganda: More Sorghum Needed
Body_Text:
Kampala — Although the Eagle Lager beer project has kicked off well, there is
still shortage of the Epurpur sorghum required to produce the beer for the
whole country.
Ian Macintosh, Nile Breweries technical director last week said the
demand for Eagle beer had exceeded expectations. He said the beer now
commands 10% of the market share.
Macintosh was briefing the Uganda Investment Authority executive
director Dr. Maggie Kigozi, who toured the factory to appraise herself on
the success the Eagle project had achieved todate.
The project began in 2002, has so far seen the successful investment of
upto sh1.5b in equipment, sourcing of appropriate land, seed multiplication
programme, farmer recruitment and training.
Macintosh said they expected between 300 and 400 tonnes of sorghum by
the end of the year.
He said this would help increase the production of the beer. He said
sorghum quantities will then have to grow as the beer grows in the
market.
He said they decided to look out for local ingredients because the beer
market had stagnated.
"The economy was going through a slow period in the last couple of
years, taxes were high and the environment was very competitive," he
said.
"We also needed to grow the market and make beer more affordable, but
most importantly also, we are committed to the growth of Uganda through
Poverty Eradication Action Plan, Plan for Modernisation of Agriculture and
other projects," Macintosh said.
Kigozi said the area towards the east was badly affected after cattle
rustling.
She said the Epurpur project would fill the gap.
"We are glad you could replace imported barley with local sorghum. We
are happy that you responded to the Government's call to buy locally,"Kigozi
said.

Original_Search_Title: GMB in Need of $160 Billion for Imports Bill
Headline: Zimbabwe: GMB in Need of $160 Billion for Imports Bill
Body_Text:
Harare — The Grain Marketing Board (GMB) urgently needs to raise about $160 billion to meet its obligations including cereal imports, but sources said it might be unable to mop up enough cash from the local market, where the National Oil Company of Zimbabwe (Noczim) is frantically trying to raise $60 billion for fuel imports.
This comes at a time when Zimbabwe needs to import an estimated 540 000 metric tonnes of maize, which form part of an expected cereal deficit for the current consumption year, said the sources who expressed hightened fears that the GMB could fail to meet the import requirement due to foreign currency shortages.
Noczim and the Zimbabwe Electricity Supply Authority (ZESA), among other key parastatals, have failed to meet their import obligations because of a biting foreign currency shortages.
Currently the GMB is saddled with a $60 billion debt, which includes $26.6 billion carried over from last year.
The entire debt is under-witten by the government, but industry sources said this could now become a stumbling block to the GMB's hunt for fresh capital.
This is understood to have forced the government into talks with millers for a possible bail-out for the GMB.
The $26.6 billion, which should have been financed by the Treasury as part of the government's subsidy to the GMB, was carried over from last year as deferred expenditure, according to official government sources.
This situation is not helped by the fact that the GMB's producer price for maize of $130 000 per metric tonne (MT) is now chasing soaring prices of between $220 000 and $250 000 per metric tonne on an emergent black market.
The sources indicated that pressure was expected to begin mounting at the end of this month when the National Crop Forecasting Committee (NCFC), the government and the joint Food and Agricultural Organisation(FAO)-World Food Programme(WFP) supply assessment mission release their reports on the situation before the end of this month.
The FAO/WFP mission and the NCFC are finalising their estimates on Zimbabwe's cereal production for 2002/2003.
The government, which pledged to subsidise the GMB's price for maize grain to millers, has been dogged by consistent financial problems.
Any hopes of the government making a meaningful contribution to solve the GMB's financial problems hinge on prospects of a supplementary budget which could be brought to parliament anytime soon.
According to preliminary estimates, Zimbabwe's maize harvest could lie between 757 000 and one million metric tonnes, giving a total cereals harvest - including staple cereals wheat, millets and sorghum - of around one million metric tonnes.

Original_Search_Title: Shortage of Seeds Bodes Ill for Food Security
Headline: Ethiopia: Shortage of Seeds Bodes Ill for Food Security
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Ethiopia faces a seed shortfall of about 30,000 metric tonnes which, aid agencies say, needs to be covered to avert another food crisis.
Farmers have been complaining that they have no seeds and the planting season is quickly running out. The country requires some 23,000 mt of cereal seeds and 7,000 mt of pulses.
A humanitarian source working in the seed sector said that unless farmers were able to plant seeds within the next few weeks there could be a disaster next year. "We need to secure the seeds and distribute them as soon as possible so the farmers can plant them," the source said on Tuesday. "This is becoming a matter of urgency."
International donors were holding talks on Wednesday to try and overcome the shortfall - which totals around US$7 million. George Mburathi, head of the UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation in Ethiopia, stressed the importance of seeds in averting future crises.
"Food is important for survival but people have to be able to produce enough to eat for next year," he told IRIN. "Time is now running out. "If nothing is done in the next two weeks it will be very difficult for the seeds to reach the farmers because once it starts raining it will be difficult to move. This situation is more than critical. The critical time was when we appealed in April for extra seeds."
The UN's Emergencies Unit for Ethiopia has continually warned of the potential danger the country faces from the lack of seeds. It has called for the distribution of seeds to hungry farmers as a "top priority". It is already too late to plant corn, the planting season for both sorghum and barley runs out in a matter of weeks, while wheat must be sown by July. Ethiopia now faces a food shortfall of some 1.5 million mt, needed to feed one-fifth of the population.
Meanwhile Germany has announced that it is increasing its emergency support to Ethiopia by US $2.4 million to more than US $7 million, according to a statement from the German embassy in Addis Ababa. The additional funds will be earmarked for areas in the hard-hit provinces of Oromyia and Afar.

Original_Search_Title: Epurpuri Plants Not Ready Yet
Headline: Uganda: Epurpuri Plants Not Ready Yet
Body_Text:
Kampala — Nile Breweries has announced that once they have enough sorghum, their
Eagle Lager brand will start selling in the Kampala market.
Dr Kigozi (L), and Mr Mackintosh take a look at the sprouting epurpuri
(Sorghum) plants in a field in Kayunga recently. The sorghum is used for the
manufacture of Nile Breweries' Eagle Larger brand (Photo by Wandera Owora
Ojumbo)
Mr Ian Mackintosh, the technical director of Nile Breweries told the
press at the brewer's premises in Njeru on Friday. He said Eagle has not
sold in Kampala because of the low sorghum production, the raw material used
to make Eagle Lager.
"Once we have enough sorghum in our stores, we shall provide something
for Kampala," Mr Mackintosh said.At the same function, the new owners of
Nile Breweries, South
African Breweries Miller (SAB MILLER) led the Executive Director of
Uganda Investment Authority Dr Margaret Kigozi in a tour of the brewery and
the farmers who are participating in the growing of sorghum.
Dr Kigozi appreciated the work Nile Breweries was doing in uplifting the
welfare of the people. "We shall always support those who come in to help
the local people.
The only way to improve on household incomes is to get involved in such
projects," she said.Mr Peter Stuttard, the financial director of Nile
Breweries said the sorghum project was doing well in the Eastern districts
of Soroti and Kumi, but they were expanding to other parts of the
country.
"Our mission is to involve the locals in production so that we reduce on
the cost of importation as well as cut down on production costs," Mr
Stuttard said.
He said the only way to solve the high costs of beer is to use locally
produced raw material.

Original_Search_Title: I Have Started Right - Mumba
Headline: Zambia: I Have Started Right - Mumba
Body_Text:
Lusaka — I HAVE started right and will end very right, declared Vice-President Nevers Mumba yesterday.
Representing President Levy Mwanawasa at the opening of the 46th Copperbelt Mining, Agriculture and Commercial Show under the theme: Diversification for a brighter future, Vice-President Mumba said Zambia had reached a new position politically and economically which called for frankness with partners on issues that frustrated them.
"Coming back here in Kitwe reminds me of being home, my home. I am happy that on my first official duty I had to come here. I have started right and I will end very right," he said.
And in a speech read on his behalf by Vice-President Mumba, President Levy Mwanawasa said government was geared to achieve economic diversification in key sectors of agriculture, tourism, manufacturing and mining.
President Mwanawasa said agriculture must be a centre stage of economic development policy.
He said government was committed to a good crop marketing system and that with the announced maize flow price, farmers will be encouraged to grow further this coming farming season.
"All maize produced will be purchased. FRA (Food Reserve Agency) will purchase all the maize through farmer organisations and primary co-operatives," he said.
President Mwanawasa said for economic diversification to work value had to be obtained on all production.
He said Zambia had a comparative advantage in the SADC and COMESA region owing to its central position.
President Mwanawasa said least developed nations failed to meet standards of those developed because they lacked finances to set up laboratory facilities to enhance standards.
He called on developing nations to collaborate.
President Mwanawasa said 14 months ago when the Anglo American Corporation pulled out of KCM, hopes and aspirations of the Copperbelt people were doomed.
He assured that once negotiations with new preferred bidders for KCM and Ramcoz were concluded, the Copperbelt will be vibrant.
President Mwanawasa said Zambia was endowed with abundant natural resources and good climate suitable for increased agricultural production.
President Mwanawasa said the Copperbelt should accelerate agricultural production to contribute to national food security.
He said given the favourable rainfall in the 2002/2003 agricultural season, he was happy to announce that the crop focus exercise undertaken by the Ministry of Agriculture and Co-operatives and the Central Statistics Office showed that maize production had increased from 6,166 metric tones in last year to 1,157,861 metric tones this year.
President Mwanawasa said production of other crops such as sorghum, beans and cassava had also tremendously improved.
He said government would continue with the fertiliser support programme to maintain increased crop production.
President Mwanawasa said in the last season 120,000 farmers benefited from the fertiliser support programme and another 200,000 vulnerable but productive households received inputs.
Later, Vice-President Mumba presented the overall stand prize to African Explosives.
Copperbelt Mining, Agriculture and Commercial Show chairman Bill Osborne said agriculture on the Copperbelt was vibrant but needed government support.
He said this year's show went to emphasise that the diversification had become a password for economic future of Zambia.
Osborne said it was hoped the task force on economic diversification would come up with programmes and proposal that would be backed and financed by government.
"We don't want to see diluting of the task force efforts through the formation of different other agencies working to the same end. The Zambia Investment Centre which facilitates investment has the capacity to work with the Task Force," he said.
Osborne said the Copperbelt went through another difficult year economically but that now things were looking a little rosier.
He said the recent announcement of KCM selection of a strategic equity partner "Sterlite" was positive news.
Osborne further said the news of J&W in Chambishi and Ramcoz appeared like the Copperbelt would be in for a period of sustainable development.
"I am sure government would have learnt from the past that now it fully verified new investors and contracts of sale are water tight," Osborne said. "Let's believe the new investor and existing ones prove to be true to their words, then we will have a stable and growing economy on the Copperbelt and make significant contribution to growth of the national economy."
Osborne said the mining industry portrayed a picture of hope and was still an important part of economy for many years to come.
He said the industry and commerce outside mining still found things tough going and were still looking forward to the mining industry to gain buoyancy.
Osborne said Zambia was truly a land of opportunities yet 90 per cent of the population was unemployed but the tax rate was very high resulting in stifle of the economy.
He said the more government raised taxes it lowered revenue gained from taxes because such eroded spending power.
"By lowering taxes the economy will grow and so in return will government revenue," he said.
Osborne said energy and fuel costs were still very high.
"We have abundant hydro-electric power and cheap to produce but expensive due to inefficiency of Zesco," Osborne said. "It's sad Zesco sells power at a cheaper rate to our neighbouring countries than they do to local consumers."
Osborne said interest rates too were an area of deep concern to all sectors of the economy.
He said the cost of borrowing for capital was not going proportionately, and as such impacted severely on farmers and other industrial sectors.
Osborne said investors could borrow at 10 times lower than local entrepreneurs.
"Lower interest rates substantially and reap benefits of stimulated economy," said Osborne.
Earlier during a luncheon, Chambishi chief operating officer Ed Munnik said patience had waned among investors in mining following government's failure to a level incentives and the operating field.
He said there were difficulties the mining industry was going through in Zambia which government needed to address.
"We need all the help we can get from government for the industry to grow. It is important that the mining industry is helped. There are outstanding issues to be addressed," Munnik said. "We have been fighting very hard to resuscitate the mines and we need your support."
Munnik said Chambishi Metals was changing hands and hoped the new owners J&W would continue investing more in the mine.
He said Chambishi Metals shareholders sell of company had entirely nothing to do with results of the relationship between government and Chambishi.
Munnik said the company had a wonderful stay in Zambia and hopeful new owners would move on especially that there was a drastic improvement of infrastructure such as roads and lodges in Zambia in the past few years.
In response, agriculture minister Mundia Sikatana said the new deal government was a listening government.
He said government would not abandon investors with good intentions.
"We will grow nearer to you to appreciate your problems," he said.
Sikatana said most things last year were put on hold due to hunger.
He said now that hunger was over, government would concentrate on other issues.
"This time last year it was gloomy. We gathered as very hungry Zambians. It was shortly after we gained enemies over refusal of GMOs," Sikatana said. "Don't forget it's impossible for a nation which is starving to concentrate on anything else."
Sikatana said challenged investors to look for technology that would add value to minerals they produced.
"We are positive when we sit with new investors, genuine investors not infesters, together we will turn the economy of this country," Sikatana said. "As government, we will live to regret during this time of abundant peace, which we tend to take for granted, if we don't develop the economy."
Sikatana said Vice-President Mumba had joined politics where there were specialists in mudslinging and others with ulterior motives.
"I do not say you should be thick skinned. Whatever, happens on the political horizon take it but believe in your principles, rely on truth," Sikatana said. "We must agree to differ peacefully. Never resort to experiments that have caused havoc in neighbouring countries."
Sikatana said he was joyous that the new deal administration was determined grow the economy.

Original_Search_Title: Anti-Aids Message Reaches King's Warriors
Headline: Eswatini: Anti-Aids Message Reaches King's Warriors
Body_Text:
Mbabane — In a traditional kingdom that takes seriously its customary social structures, the anti-AIDS message is this week being targeted at young Swazi men belonging to King Mswati's warrior regiments.
"About 45 percent of Swazis in their twenties are HIV-positive, the highest demographic group among an adult population that officially is 38.6 percent HIV-positive. Young men are the most vulnerable," health worker Mduduzi Simelane told IRIN.
Simelane and health motivators from the Ministry of Health and various NGOs are handing out AIDS literature, and dispensing advice on where to get blood tests and counselling at a venue where this has never been attempted: the warrior barracks at Mswati's royal village, Engabezweni.
Each May, the warrior regiments spend a fortnight at their traditional compounds, rethatching beehive huts where they live, engaging in symbolic tribute work like harvesting the king's sorghum field, and handing down traditional skills like shield making and knob stick carving to younger generations.
"Every Swazi, boy and girl, man and woman, belongs to the regiment of his age mates. But lately we have grown alarmed by the decimation of what should be the most vital of the regiments, the Inkhanyenti [Swazi star, the regiment of the king's sons], where the youngest warriors are members," said Balonja Mngomezulu, an elder from the Inyatsi (buffalo) regiment.
Mgnomezulu will not specify the cause of the deaths - in Swaziland it is still taboo to blame a fatality on AIDS because of the stigma attached to the disease. But it is permissible to advocate ways to avoid the virus. Mngomezulu and other warrior elders responded favourably to health motivators' requests that they have access to the regiments when they assembled for royal duties.
"We must educate these young men about AIDS, because they will listen to us. What they hear at school does not have the same authority as what we tell them, because we represent the king. When we speak to the warriors, it is the king who appointed us who speaks to them," one Inkhanyenti elder explained.
The regimental leaders tell the warriors they must listen to what the health motivators have to say. Because women are usually not allowed into the warrior barracks, just as men are usually barred from the women's regimental gatherings, male health motivators are pressed into service.
"Speaking to the young men is a matter of making them see the danger of multiple girlfriends. That is what is spreading AIDS, the polygamous mindset," Aubrey Masuku, a community officer with the AIDS Information and Support Centre in Manzini, told IRIN.
"However, we are careful not to condemn polygamy, because it is legal in Swaziland and a lot of the older warriors are polygamists. We have to avoid antagonising anyone," Masuku said.
Middle-aged warriors are also approached by health motivators, who engage them in conversations about suspected AIDS-related deaths in their communities. Older Swazis are vulnerable to HIV through the custom of "kuteka", where a widow enters the homestead of her late husband's brother to become one of his wives. If the deceased died of AIDS, the disease can infect a new family.
This month Mswati appointed as health minister a traditionalist, Chief Sipho Shongwe. He is expected to ease the AIDS message into chieftaincies where resistance to condom usage and blood testing is intense.
"Swaziland is largely a rural nation. Four out of five Swazis live in the countryside under chiefs. So it is natural that the worst of AIDS can be found in rural lands," said Dr John Kunene, the principal secretary in the Ministry of Health.
Mswati has long advocated condom usage as a means of AIDS prevention. Health motivators visiting the regiments this week carry with them cartons of condoms for distribution.
