"All the young men say they don't need a demonstration with our rubber figures; they know how to use condoms. The challenge is actually motivating them to do so," said health worker August Malambe.
Malambe and his colleagues report that the warriors are receptive listeners, and actually enjoy the diversion from wood gathering and water fetching chores the elders have them do. "We don't lecture the boys, we talk. We motivate, and the key motivation is to recognise the value of life, and desiring to hold on to it," Malambe said.
One sign that the message is getting through: As they parade in their warrior finery, carrying cow-hide shields and wearing antelope loin skins and pink regimental beads, young members of the regiments have added a new touch to their fur anklets by inserting shiny gold condom packets that reflect the sunlight - providing hope for an AIDS-free future.

Original_Search_Title: Wheat Imports Dock At Mombasa
Headline: Kenya: Wheat Imports Dock At Mombasa
Body_Text:
Nairobi — More wheat imports continue to arrive at the port of Mombasa to replenish the country's dwindling grain reserves . A conventional cargo vessel MV Glorious docked at the harbour last week with 21365 metric tonnes of wheat, and so did Mv Kyma under the agency of Barwil Bat Haf Agencies with 31,000 metric tonnes of bulk wheat.
The two vessels are discharging their cargo at the Grain Bulk Handling Services (GBHL) facility adjacent to berth Number One.
MV Maersk Arizona under the agency of Seaforth Shipping (Kenya) is sailing towards Mombasa carrying 4357 metric tonnes of bagged sorghum.
Another shipment of 13200 metric tonnes of relief cargo arrived aboard MvWilson on Tuesday. It is under the agency of Seabulk Chatering and Shipping (CBULK).
More cargo imports are expected to dominate at the port with Mv Jolly Gialo , a vessel under the agency of Interocean East Africa Limited (IOEA) bringing in 100 car units.
Mv Modern Peak whose agents are Diamond Shipping Limited (DSS) brings in the largest vehicle unit imports of 420 tomorrow.
Mv Grand Quest also under DSS docks at the port on Friday with 162 units. On the same day, Mv Orange Horizon will bring ashore 270 car units.

Original_Search_Title: Concerns Over Winter Wheat
Headline: Zimbabwe: Concerns Over Winter Wheat
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Prospects are looking grim for Zimbabwe's winter wheat harvest with many farmers unable to plant or tend their crops following equipment theft and foreign currency and fuel shortages.
Last year the country produced 230,000 mt of wheat - over 100,000 mt down from the previous year - but indications this year are for a vastly reduced crop of winter wheat and barley, Commericial Farmers Union (CFU) President Colin Cloete told IRIN on Thursday.
He said theft of irrigation equipment, transformers and electricity cables was rife, to the extent that the government had banned aluminium exports in a bid to stop the stealing.
Zimbabwe's wheat is grown under full irrigation in winter, with most of the water coming from private and state dams and some boreholes.
Production in the past has accounted for about two-thirds of the national demand of 400,000 mt.
Cloete said farmers have had to overcome chronic diesel and fertiliser shortages brought on by the foreign exchange crisis.
The minutes of a CFU meeting last week said farmers were told the country's fuel situation was one of "hand to mouth" and fuel company representatives were unable to give farmers any meaningful advice.
Two large fertiliser companies reported they had zero stocks, their product was sold soon after manufacture and back-order lists were lengthy. This was due to the lack of foreign exchange to import ingredients required to manufacture fertiliser, reduced power supplies and transport constraints. Monitored prices also meant manufacturers could not recoup their production costs.
"Supplies of fertiliser for the winter cereal crops are very short because of these factors. Even if prices come right there will no extra product," the CFU report said.
Cloete added that slow tobacco sales were also quelling hopes of a boost to the country's foreign exchange supplies.
As an alternative to scarce maize, many Zimbabwean consumers have substituted bread as a staple.
"It there's no wheat, we'll just have to go without bread. We are really concerned this year," Cloete said.
The last Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS NET) report said cereals like maize, wheat, millet and sorghum remained in critically short supply in the major markets throughout the country for much of April 2003.
A food supply mission is in the final stages of assessing Zimbabwe's cereal production for 2002/3 but early indications are that overall production will be lower than last year.
For more details:
FEWS-NET: http://www.fews.net/centers/current/monthlies/report/?f=zw&m=1000914&l=en

Original_Search_Title: Food "Monetization" Aims to Reach Urban Poor
Headline: Zimbabwe: Food "Monetization" Aims to Reach Urban Poor
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Deliveries of food relief in Zimbabwe have almost exclusively focused on rural communities, bypassing the needs of the urban poor.
Providing aid to towns is complicated by the difficulty of properly targeting the food-insecure, and the logistics of distribution, relief workers say. As a result, urban households in Zimbabwe have been largely left on their own to struggle with the impact of severe food shortages and spiralling black market prices.
An initiative by the Consortium for the Southern Africa Food Emergency (C-SAFE), which still awaits funding, seeks to increase the access of poor urban households to affordable food supplies by using the market - rather than food handouts.
C-SAFE, made up of the NGOs World Vision, CARE and Catholic Relief Services, is proposing a three-month pilot programme in Zimbabwe's second city of Bulawayo to sell 15,000 mt of US Agency for International Development (USAID)-supplied sorghum through small-scale traders. An added bonus is that profits from the sales would be ploughed back into development projects.
Maize and wheat are Zimbabwe's staple foods, but their importation and domestic marketing is under the monopoly of the state-run Grain Marketing Board (GMB). Confronted by the country's food crisis, fuel shortages and administrative problems, GMB has been unable to come even close to meeting the demand, humanitarian officials say.
In urban areas, price controls - the government's answer to rising prices - have further distorted the market, with basic commodities being re-directed onto the more expensive parallel market. But despite escalating prices - Zimbabwe's inflation rate last month jumped by 41 points to reach 269 percent - the lack of availability of maize and wheat remains a more significant problem.
"We aim to target the poor by using a market mechanism. Sorghum [a less preferred commodity] is the [food] option to pre-select those desperate enough to buy," Steve Goudswaard, C-SAFE project manager in South Africa, told IRIN.
Sorghum is more freely marketed than maize or wheat, although the government did impose a price freeze on the grain last year. C-SAFE would need to work with the Ministry of Commerce and local authorities to determine its price, Walter Middleton of World Vision explained.
According to the C-SAFE proposal, with funding secured, imported sorghum would be milled and bagged in Zimbabwe. From there, supplies would be directed to street corner grocers as another tool for targeting poorer households. Credit facilities and an auction system for tender bids could be used creatively to help ensure the participation of small-scale traders.
"This innovative and untested approach is inevitably risky. Assumptions about such things as the effectiveness of sorghum as a self-targeting commodity, the capacity/willingness of private sector agents to participate, and government cooperation/approval, all require testing," a project assessment report noted.
"In addition, the availability of resources (particularly sorghum) that are currently available for this intervention are quite limited."
Bulawayo was selected as the pilot city for the project because of consumer familiarity with sorghum, the acuteness of the food shortage in the drought-hit region and the available milling capacity.
At the ration rate of 10 kilos per person per month, the planned importation of 15,000 mt of sorghum for Bulawayo would feed 500,000 people for three months, Middleton calculated. If successful, the scheme would be replicated in other cities, he added.

Original_Search_Title: Two Pupils Drown in Floods Havoc
Headline: Kenya: Two Pupils Drown in Floods Havoc
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two primary school pupils have drowned as floods submerged seven villages in Tana River District.
They were pupils of Kitere Full Primary School in Garsen, local chief Sahid Buya said.
"The pupils were in a group of seven who were going home for lunch on Monday when it happened," the chief said.
Mr Buya said the bodies had not been recovered. The other five were rescued by villagers.
Local councillor Hijab Mohamed said seven villages had been affected by the floods. Kinyadu, Bahati, Muguveni, Mnazini, Kitere, Baomo and Tholoni had been submerged and hundreds of acres of maize, cassava, bean and other crops swept away, he said.
Garsen District Officer Anthony Sunkuli said more than 300 people had been displaced.
He appealed to villagers who had not left the banks of the Tana River to move to higher ground as more flooding was expected.
The chairman of the Pastoralists Youth Development Initiative, Mr Ali Bure, said although the government had taken food and medicines to the affected areas, they were inadequate.
"We are appealing for more help, especially tents, blankets, mosquito nets, medicine and food," he said.
Mr Bure also called for more medical personnel, saying those in the affected areas could not cope with the increase of patients.
In Kitale, Vice-President Michael Wamalwa said more than Sh15 million had been raised following his appeal to help flood victims.
Speaking at the Immaculate Catholic Church, he said the government was doing everything possible to contain the floods.
Yesterday, the VP toured Budalang'i in Busia to survey the situation. He appealed to well-wishers to continue contributing clothes, tents and food.
Meanwhile, Turkana faces an acute shortage of food and fuel after the collapse of Wakor Bridge which links the district with West Pokot and the rest of the country.
More than 100 lorries carrying food, fuel and other supplies for the district, the Kakuma Refugee Camp and war-torn southern Sudan are stranded at the bridge, 70 kilometres from Kapenguria Town.
Transport between Lodwar and Kitale has been paralysed since the bridge was swept away by heavy rains last week. Matatu (commuter taxi) operators plying the route doubled fares and did booming business from Kitale and Kapenguria to the bridge, and from Lodwar and Lokichogio.
They demanded a fare of Sh1,200, up from Sh600, from Kitale to Lodwar, a distance of 300 kilometres.
Some matatus ply between Kitale and the bridge where passengers alight and wade through the water to board vehicles on the other side.
"Loaders at the bridge charge up to Sh200 to ferry passenger/luggage across the river," a conductor, Mr James Buluma, said.
Turkana District Commissioner Njenga Miiri said food and fuel would run out by the end of the week.
"I am consulting the disaster office in the Office of the President to see what they can do to help us," Mr Miiri said. The DC said southern Sudan relief operations could also be affected.
Some Sh20 million will be required to build a permanent bridge across Wakor River, local roads engineer Francis Muriuki said.
West Pokot Agriculture boss Philip Ting'aa said transporters had only two alternatives: Use the more than 1,000-km road through Eldoret, Iten, Biretwa, Chesegon, Lomut and Lodwar, adding an extra 12 hours to the journey, or through Uganda on the Kapenguria-Kacheliba-Arlale-Uganda route.
Lorry drivers complained that they had exhausted their Sh2,600 each allowances and threatened to return the goods to Mombasa if their employers did not provide them with more money.
Lorries that were ferrying relief food donated by the World Food Programme are stranded at Ortum, Chepareria, Kapenguria, Kitale and Eldoret, awaiting the repair of the bridge.
A litre of petrol at Ortum costs Sh70, a bottle of soda Sh45 and mineral water Sh80. Travellers stranded at the bridge said they bought a 20-litre jerry can of water at Sh40. Traders said they had incurred huge losses as a result of extra transportation costs for loading and off- loading of goods and payments to people who ferrying the same across the river. Police were yesterday busy supervising the movement of people and goods at Wakor to prevent any looting or any attacks .
Mr Ting'aa said apart from two people who were washed away by the floods on Friday, more than 60 goats and three cows were also swept away. Mr Ting'aa said hundreds of acres of sorghum and maize had also been affected by the heavy rains but thanked the Anglican Church of Kenya for providing assorted variety of seeds worth Sh2.4 million to locals for planting this season.
But the rains have been a blessing to pastoralists who had migrated to Uganda in search of pasture for their livestock. They have started to return.
Reports: Patrick Mayoyo, Peter Kamau, Lucas Barasa and Edward Koech

Original_Search_Title: Overflowing Zambezi Floods Homes And Fields
Headline: Zambia: Overflowing Zambezi Floods Homes And Fields
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Flooding has hit communities along the banks of the Zambezi river in southwestern Zambia and across the border in Namibia.
The authorities in Zambia are still collecting information, but so far at least 120,000 people have been identified as needing help in Kalabo district which borders Angola. Figures are not yet in for the other affected districts.
Rising flood waters are normal along the path of the Zambezi. But vastly increased levels this year caught communities unawares in Senanga, Lokhulu and Zambezi West, forcing them to flee their homes, Webby Mulubisha, permanent secretary in Zambia's Office of the President, told IRIN on Tuesday.
The Zambezi's headwaters begin close to where the boundaries of Angola, the Democratic Republic of Congo and Zambia meet. Water levels were higher than usual this year due to increased rainfall.
"It came all of a sudden and was one of the highest levels seen since 1958. Crops were flooded, and when people move quickly they don't carry a lot of belongings, especially food," Mulubisha commented.
"At the moment we are zeroing in on food as an immediate need," Mulubisha said, adding that the government was already delivering maize to Mongu, the capital of Western province, for distribution. "Some people are still in their homes and some are displaced, but they don't have enough food at the moment."
Zambia is one of six southern African countries needing urgent food aid following drought-induced shortages last year. Although this year's crops look more promising, floods brought by cyclone Japhet in March to the southern regions have already compromised food security in some households.
Mulubisha said although the government was indebted to NGOs, church groups and the World Food Programme for their support, it still needed help airlifting relief food and supplies to dry areas for distribution and would "welcome any other assistance".
Across the border in Namibia's eastern Caprivi region, about 12,000 people have been affected by the vastly raised water levels of the Zambezi, and two people were killed.
Gabriel Kangowa, head of the country's Emergency Management Unit, told IRIN the government was working with NGOs to provide emergency relief to the families who needed food, medical supplies, tents and mosquito nets.
As a temporary measure, clinics were being supplied with extra medication and one extra nurse per clinic, and school classes were expected to resume soon in tents, as the schools were still flooded.
Kangowa said many people lost their maize and sorghum crops in the flood, with only those who had harvested early escaping the devastation.
Many people in the affected communities were already dependent on relief food following a drought and were now cut off from these supplies.
The government was providing relief food to about 345,000 people, mostly in the Caprivi region, who faced critical food shortages.
Communities had been warned about the rapidly rising water but did not expect such high levels and were reluctant to leave their homes.
With roads inaccessible, relief workers were using helicopters and river boats to ferry supplies.

Original_Search_Title: Caprivi A 'Water World'
Headline: Namibia: Caprivi A 'Water World'
Body_Text:
A FRESH wave of floods from the Zambezi River at the weekend swamped
several communal plots that sustain up to 1 000 villagers at the Ibbu
and Mahundu settlements in eastern Caprivi.
Over 25 000 villagers have now been severely affected by the floods -
the worst in decades.
Entire villages have been submerged while an estimated 80 000 cattle
and small livestock are affected.
On Saturday, a team led by Gabriel Kangowa, head of the national
Emergency Management Unit (EMU) visited settlements at Mahundu and
Ibbu which border the Linyanti-Chobe River south of Katima Mulilo.
Speaking to The Namibian, Kangowa said the floods had covered maize,
millet and sorghum fields.
However, he added, the villagers had already harvested their crops.
Access by road to the two areas is expected to be reduced to almost
nil in the coming two weeks, said Kangowa.
Villagers at the two fishing villages at Masikili and Nakabolelwa
have made an impassioned appeal for food aid particularly, maize meal
and cooking oil.
They say people in the area are starving.
Dozens of villagers have already been stricken by malaria because
most of the villagers are too poor to afford mosquito nets and
mosquito repellents.
Some huts in the area are completely covered by water.
This week the World Food Programme (WFP) is expected to provide five
helicopters that can land on both land and water.
The choppers will be used to distribute flood relief aid.
At present, humanitarian assistance to thousands of villagers at
Musanga, Lisikili, Schuckmannsburg, Namiyundu, Nankuntwe, Nsundwa,
Muzii, Mpukano, Impalila, Kasika, Mbalasinte, Ivilivinzi,
Nakabolelwa, Masikili and Ngoma is being hampered by accessibility
problems.
On Saturday The Namibian visited Masikili and Nakabolelwa where many
cattle are grazing in grass that is almost completely covered by
water.
Almost all the islands have been stripped bare of grass by displaced
cattle which have now turned to eating grass sticking out from wide
expanses of flood water.
A number of areas where villagers managed to drive cattle before
floods cut off all access routes are already overgrazed while tension
is brewing between those living in the flooded areas and those
staying in upper, drier areas.
Gerard Sinvula, headman at Kahonono Village in the Nakabolelwa area,
said hundreds of elephants wandering between Namibia and Botswana ate
some of their crops.
EMU's Kangowa said because of transport problems, Government was
looking beyond its borders, particularly South Africa, to see how it
can deal with the distribution of food, tents, fodder, firewood and
other supplies to affected communities.
An Erongo-based company has pledged to donate 100 lifejackets that
will today be distributed to the villagers whose main mode of
transport is canoe, Kangowa added.
Humanitarian officials are also expected to buy a large consignment
of water purification tablets as people are drinking contaminated
water.
Apart from malaria, they can fall victim to a variety of water-borne
diseases.

Original_Search_Title: ICRC Assists Flood Victims in Somali Region
Headline: Somalia: ICRC Assists Flood Victims in Somali Region
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The International Committee of the Red Cross has started an urgent
relief operation to assist the victims of flooding which affected the
population living along the Wabe Shabelle River, in the districts of
Kelafo and Mustahil, situated in the Somali National Regional state
(SNRS).
According to the press release sent to The Daily Monitor in the past
days, heavy rains fell in the upper escarpments of Bale and Western
Hararghe areas and in several zones in the SNRS. Consequently, the
Wabe Shabelle River started to swell in a significant way until it
burst its banks inundating a considerable part of the territory.
The excessive rainfalls had a devastating impact on the area.
Villagers were obliged to seek refuge in the surrounding hills and
those living in the lowest part of the territory, mainly near
irrigations canals used for flood recession agriculture, had their
houses destroyed along with cultivated fields of sorghum and maize.
Already present in the region with its logistic bases and despite
huge difficulties to reach the affected areas, the ICRC rapidly
reacted to bring emergency aid to the population hit by this new
calamity. Two airplanes transported tarpaulins, blankets cooking pots
and jerry cans from Nairobi to Goode, while food came from the
warehouses situated in Dire Dawa. This first stage involves 3,300
families, which correspond to some 23,000 people.
"The first assessments were conducted by road when possible, and by
boat. On this basis, distribution started in 5 camps of displaced
persons from Mustahil town and in 3 villages along the river," the
release stated.
Dr Pierre Gratzl, the ICRC medical doctor who was among the first
shelters workers to arrive in the flood-hit area reported that beside
the lack of food, shelters, blankets and day-to-day utensils, a
possible health risk of malaria and water-born diseases was imminent.
To avoid this scenario, kits of medicines and mosquito nets are on
the way to be distributed. Contacts have also being made, for
facilitating access to seeds when the floods recess.
Assessments that were delayed because of the very difficult road
condition are also planned very soon in East and West Imi districts.
The ICRC is closely coordinating its intervention with DPPC (Disaster
Prevention and Preparedness Commission) and other involved
humanitarian organisations in order to optimise the humanitarian
response and to avoid duplication of efforts.
Ethiopian Red Cross Society (ERCS) staff and volunteers are also
giving hands in the various logistic bases.
The ICRC is ready to increase its assistance on the basis of the
needs assessed and is planning further actions to reach the suffering
population living in the most isolated sites.

Original_Search_Title: 10,000 Taita Families Faced With Starvation
Headline: Kenya: 10,000 Taita Families Faced With Starvation
Body_Text:
Nairobi — About 10,787 families in Taita Taveta are faced with starvation, local District Commissioner Chege Mwangi said yesterday.
Mwangi said the families urgently require famine relief.
He said the prevailing bad weather was to blame for food insecurity in the district.
The DC was speaking at Wundanyi Stadium after he flagged off participants in this year's Freedom from Hunger Walk.
He said the district has received 4,000 bags of maize and 1,000 bags of beans to be distributed to famine stricken residents.
The DC said the food supply from the Government was not enough and urged residents to come up with specific programmes to make them self-reliant in food.
Mwangi said the lower zones of the district were the most hit areas.
The DC advised residents to diversify farming activities and plant drought-resistant crops like cassava and sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Sorghum Beer Trade in the Eastern Cape Largely Illegal
Headline: South Africa: Sorghum Beer Trade in the Eastern Cape Largely Illegal
Body_Text:
Bisho — More than 90% of the outlets for sorghum beer in the eastern Cape are
operating illegally.
This was revealed yesterday (subs: Fri) by the senior vice president
of the United National Breweries (UNB) Paramjit Gill during the
company's presentation to the public hearing on the Eastern Cape
Liquor Bill of 2003.
Gill said there were about 150 000 illegal traders of sorghum beer in
the province. Many of these were also producing sorghum beer. The UNB
brewery in Butterworth was the only licenced producer.
He said that there were "basic problems in treating sorghum beer like
other liquor products". He recommended that the sorghum beer trade be
kept out of the preview of the proposed Liquor Act for the first ten
years.
He said the UNB was "willing to assist" the Liquor Board to assist
current and prospective traders to comply with the proposed Act.
He suggested that the province introduce a "simple permit system
instead of licenses." He said that the application form should be
easy to fill in because there was "a lot of illiteracy" in the
business and the fee should be "nominal".
Standing committee chairperson Andre de Wet said the UNB seemed to be
"willing to participate and to bring some form of legality to a place
where illegality is rife". He urged the UNB to make "concrete
proposals" for the legalising of the sorghum beer trade.
However, chairperson of the Eastern Cape Liquor Board Mnyamezeli
Ntshiba pointed out that the sorghum beer trade "does not have more
than 5 licensed" outlets in the province.
"You (UNB) sell to the outlets that are not licensed," he said.
Ntshiba added that an "outcry" from communities over illegal shebeens
prompted the inclusion of a clause in the bill empowering inspectors
to enforce adherence to the rules.

Original_Search_Title: The Namibian Goes One On One With the President
Headline: Namibia: The Namibian Goes One On One With the President
Body_Text:
President Sam Nujoma on Tuesday gave an interview to The Namibian,
the first with this newspaper in four years. It took place at his farm,
Etunda, 30 km south of Otavi. Topics ranged from the serious to the
light-hearted, near and far, fresh and refreshed.
Following is an abridged version: When he entered the hall where the
interview took place, the President asked why the journalists had not joined
him the previous day, when the interview was first scheduled to take place,
for a trip to the Caprivi .
It's a very strange kind of [a] situation where you have flooding
without rain ... it is dry and floods are all over.
Ja, you can start the business now.
Thank you very much President for giving us an opportunity to speak
to you.
[Hands over birthday presents - books].
Yes, thank you very much.
Yes, sit down
Mr President, I had some prepared questions, but before we go there
how much of your time can we take?
Well uhh, I'm always controlled by my colleagues who are my officials
who make my appointments.
(One hour is allowed.)
I guess then we'll have to cut some of the questions I wanted to ask,
umm...
Ja, but before you ask questions, I always want to talk to people who
are honest and truthful. Your newspaper, The Namibian, has been distorting
what I have said in the past and it is not good to deal with people who are
dishonest. It does not help you to tell lies.
And The Namibian also has a habit of insulting other heads of state. You
see, if you don't respect people don't expect other people to respect
you.
We are brought up in a society where we respect everybody, including
children.
But your newspaper, because you are probably brought up in a different
society where you insult elders... Even if you don't like that President or
that particular individual, you don't need to insult or to tell lies.
It does not make your newspaper popular by insulting people or by
thinking that you are more intelligent than them. Respect for human beings
is what in return makes people read your newspaper or to respect you.
So, but I want you first to take an oath here that you are going
precisely to say what I'm going to say. There will be not one word
misquoting me in reply to your questions. Without that I will not give you
an interview.
Mr President I undertake that we are going to report what you are
saying and that's why we brought some recorders to make sure where my
fingers cannot catch up with your speech the recorders can take down
accurately .
Ja, but take an oath that [states] 'yes I'm going exactly to write what
you are saying'. Raise your hand.
(The reporter reluctantly raises his right hand.) Yes, Comrade
President I'm going to write what you are saying, at least to the best of my
ability .
Okay, fine. Let's go ahead.
Firstly, I wanted to ask a little bit about your interest in
farming. How did you end up choosing this area? What do you like about
it?
Well, we were brought up [by] farmers in communal areas. When I was a
small boy, my first task was to look after the goats, and when I had grown
up, [I looked after] calves and cattle.
This is how I was brought up, and this is what motivated me to, umm,
purchase a farm.
The reason I got this particular farm is because it's closer to the
triangle of Otavi, Grootfontein and Tsumeb, where in Namibia normally we
have good rainfall because of the Otjikoto Lake and the Guinas [lake], and
this triangle has got more trees than the rest of the country. We farm with
livestock.
Do you have some game too? I saw ostriches.
Yes, we have some game. I tried to farm with ostriches but they demand
[too] much time. They are very sensitive when they are small, extremely
sensitive.
If you just say to one of them 'hoh', two of them drop dead. If there is
a thunder storm when it is raining, whoo, three or four of them die. They
even need more care than when you look after a human baby.
We also have game in this area, mainly kudus [and] eland. One can
introduce other game as well like springbok. But because it is very bushy,
springbok don't like to be in the bush because they are afraid of jackals,
cheetah or leopard. So one would need to debush, then you can introduce
them.
Who are your immediate neighbours and how do you get along with
them?
When I came here after Independence all these farms were occupied by
whites only, but I initiated contact with them.
We had a braai here and I explained to them the importance of working
together as farmers, and also to protect our area from being invaded by
thieves and botsotsos who may disturb peace. Now we have become
friends.
On my birthday on Saturday, at the party, all the farmers came, not only
in Otjozondjupa Region but also from other regions in the country. So it
went on very well.
Comrade President, if I can move on to something else. I have been
told, for instance, when chairing meetings [and you want to move fast] you
often use the term dacco??? What does it mean?
Well, d'accord is a French word, to say okay... we use it just to say
that's acceptable.
Where do you get all the energy at your age?
I was brought up like that by my parents. My father woke me up round
about three or four o'clock to go and work on the field to cultivate our
mahangu, sorghum, ground nuts so we [can] always have plenty of food.
We ate our [dinner] at 6.30-7 o'clock. At 8 o'clock we are in
bed... even now, after 8 o'clock news I go to bed. So, by even 3.30, I'm
already up. Today I woke up at 4 o'clock. I was just reading.
So it's very healthy to go to bed early [he laughs].
The youth of my time, unlike yours, seem to let go of leadership. We
seem to have energy only up to a certain point and [then] organisations
begin to collapse. What is your recipe for successful leadership?
Well, I think the reality is that one must be responsible for what is
happening around you and you must concentrate on what you are supposed to
do.
You don't need to sneak out and leave your work unfinished.
So, time management is very important.
This is what we learnt during the war of liberation.
How can we maintain that legacy? It seems as if today we do not have
something like you had to focus us [on] and live up to what we should be
doing.
In the first place you are in a different situation. Our generation has
grown up under colonial oppression. You are in a liberated Namibia.
You can travel anywhere you want in Namibia. But before Independence you
could not travel without any pass. You had to go to the colonial authorities
to be given a pass.
If you are a Herero and you wanted to go to the North, they'd say 'you
are not an Ovambo, what are you going to do in Owambo?' So, you are in a
free Namibia, you have the right to travel anytime to go anywhere even to go
overseas. We had no right to go overseas.
I had to jump bail and escape the country to go to New York to petition
before the Fourth Committee of the General Assembly of the United
Nations.
You are in a free and independent Namibia. It is up to you really to
make up your mind [about] what it is you want to achieve in life.
Many of the youth today would say 'I'm not interested in politics'
what would you say to that?
Well, in a free and independent Namibia you can become a businessman,
you can become a farmer, you can become a fisherman. If you are a geologist
you can go and find more minerals in Namibia.
[As a farmer] now that we have Ramatex... if you want money, you [can]
produce more cotton and you get money from Ramatex because the company
cannot run without cotton.
Talking of Ramatex. There was a strike a few weeks ago. What do you
say of the workers who are complaining that they are getting low pay. Must
they too just work hard and not complain?
In the first place, Ramatex is just being established. How do you demand
a salary increment in a company which is being established? Ramatex is not
complete yet and the workers are also being trained. They are under
training, people who are training them came from Asia.
It is [a] completely meaningless or negative attitude of those
workers. They must understand that, first of all, Namibia is no longer a
colony.
If you are employed you must be qualified in order to earn a genuine
salary, but if you are not qualified, of course, you cannot be paid a higher
salary.
The workers are just under training and yet they are going on strike to
demand higher salary. Until you are qualified and be able to produce... you
can't [demand higher pay]. Those are realities of life.
What kind of leaders do you propose for a future Namibia?
Well, in Swapo as a party we have structures drawn up.
The [Swapo] Youth League when they are grown up, like errr...  Paul[us]
Kapia and all others, they join the ranks of the main party, and (Swapo)
Pioneers will become youth.
After you and beyond do you have a specific candidate or candidates
in mind?
Oh, yes, the leadership of Swapo is there. I'm the president, I have a
vice president.
If I go all of a sudden, the vice president takes over. If the vice
president is not there the secretary general is there...
If I can move on to some of the concerns that are facing this
country. HIV-AIDS for one. It's a problem which you are concerned about.
How else can you get the message across?
Well, we have a Government programme spearheaded by the Ministry of
Health and Social Services. First, you must sleep alone if you want to avoid
HIV-AIDS infection.
Secondly, there are protective measures. Use condoms if you cannot
afford to sleep alone, and thirdly, you stop alcohol and drug abuse. And
also to avoid things like homosexuality, and all these.
Once you indulge in those dangerous things, surely you will not escape
being infected by those. Those are the things that you must keep away
from.
High profile personalities have been asked to go for testing and
make their status know. Would you encourage your team in Government to do
that?
Privacy is allowed. This is a free and independent country. People have
their own feelings.
The Ministry of Health gives us directives to avoid contracting
HIV-AIDS. So you can't make a policy and say all members should go for
testing.
What should be done to those who deliberately infect other people?
Well, I think those are committing a crime. Once they are found [out]
certainly they can be punished.
How happy are you with pace of land distribution?
Well, we are just 13 years [into] Independence. We achieved Independence
after a racial war. The colonialists who used to occupy land, some of them
are still here.
Some of them have become Namibian citizens. So, it's difficult to take
land from a fellow Namibian.
Some commercial white-owned farmers don't want to sell the farms or they
raise the price of the land so that the Government [cannot] purchase the
land.
But historically it's a known fact that when the whites came here they
did not bring even a piece of the land.
They came here with empty hands, now they are millionaires. So,
therefore they must be prepared, if they are Namibian citizens, to share the
land with their fellow Namibian citizens.
We have a big land if we just accept each other on an equal basis and
[are] prepared to share the land, because we are not a small country like
Zimbabwe, for example.
I just gave an example of Zimbabwe. Zimbabwe is a very tiny country and
it has about 14 million people, whereas we have 800 000 square km of
land... and therefore we also have a small population.
The Government will continue with the policy of purchasing the land on a
willing-buyer, willing-seller basis.
We certainly will not allow from now on absentee landlords to occupy the
land while they are in their own countries.
We need the land for our people. [We] will expropriate the land owned by
absentee landlords. But we will pay compensation to those farm owners for
what they have developed.
How does our strategy differ from the one in Zimbabwe?
We've no quarrel here except that I would wish to see more commercial
white farmers donate land to the Government to settle their fellow Namibian
citizens who are landless.
This is the only way we can maintain peace and stability in the country
and development.
When you talk about maintaining peace and stability, on Friday you
went to Kaoko to welcome thousands who joined Swapo.  The opposition is
ineffective. Some would argue this amounts to a virtual one-party state and
that Namibia is in danger of getting into economic decline and suppression
of human rights [like other] one-party states.
I think you are wrong there. The Constitution of the Republic of Namibia
is very clear that we are a multi-party state. We are not a one-party
state.
Anybody can establish a political party, can present his or her own
programme of economic development or whatever to the electorate and say vote
for my party because this is what I'm going to do for you.
That's what politicians are doing. So, Swapo had it's own programme of
economic development...
We hardly see your comrades in Swapo turning down whatever
assignment you want to give them in Government. I'm tempted to know since
former Prime Minister [Hage] Geingob turned down your offer to go to the
Ministry of Local Government, how is the relations between the two of you?
In Swapo, there is discipline. The constitution of Swapo clearly states
that the cadres of the party, whether in the leadership or ordinary members,
they have to follow the rules.
And as regards to the Ministers, of course the Constitution of the
Republic of Namibia clearly states that the President of the Republic
appoints the Ministers, as well the Prime Minister, Deputy Prime
Minister. So, if I appoint a minister who does not like it, it's well and
good.
It's discipline that makes Swapo popular.
You helped, I understand, create or at lease bring about The
Namibian newspaper during the days of the struggle.  I am a bit curious to
know what was your relationship with my editor (Gwen Lister) like and how it
has changed to come to a stage where Government has even banned advertising
in The Namibian.
Well, my aim and objective as an individual and collectively with my
colleagues is to help whoever needs the help.
Because I happened to know many people in the world during the 30 years
of my being in exile, I was able to recommend The Namibian at that time. So
there is no problem in introducing anybody who may wish to do some
business.
I'm still doing it. I don't need to control anybody. Everybody may have
their views, Namibia is a free country.
Freedom of expression is allowed and so everybody does what he or she
thought is correct. But I just want to caution you as a young man that you
must be honest, you must be truthful. Otherwise, if you do not respect other
people, nobody will respect you.
That's what life is.
Formal interview ends and, while waiting for lunch, the President
took The Namibian's journalists around his farm to show them his
experimental garden.
What do you make of the sole superpower in the world, the United
States, and how President George W Bush uses that power and influence around
the world?
Well, I think he's a disaster. He's a disaster. Because they have more
weapons, they are attacking small nations.
The US cannot be compared with Iraq. You bomb, you kill women and
children. You bomb them for what? Maybe one can say this is an oil war. They
want Iraqi oil.
Every nation is afraid, if they want our diamonds they may just start to
provoke us. Even countries like France and Germany don't agree with the
Americans. I think, definitely, the Americans must be isolated in the world.
And they violated the United Nations Charter.
They are a permanent member of the Security Council, who are entrusted
by the Charter to maintain world peace and security. Now they are the
violators of world peace and security. Now the United Nations [is] almost
paralysed.
It's a disaster, whatever power they try to think they are. I don't know
what they want to achieve. But certainly the world is now living in a state
of uncertainty and fear, because they don't know where the Americans will
turn next.
It's bad.

Original_Search_Title: 'Relief Food Exercise Must Go On'
Headline: Zambia: 'Relief Food Exercise Must Go On'
Body_Text:
VILLAGERS in Siavonga in the Southern Province have appealed to Government not to stop the relief food exercise as most crops failed due to less-than-expected rainfall in the area.
Moba village headman Amos Siabungu said in an interview his subjects could not harvest anything this season because their crops failed.
Headman Siabungu said in the few areas where harvest was good the crops were destroyed by wild animals.
He said a head of 15 elephants crossed into Zambia from neighbouring Zimbabwe and were destroying the crops and terrorising villagers.
Some villagers are now depending on a wild fruit called Mabuyu for food.
"I am appealing to the Government not to stop sending relief food to our area because there are places where crops failed," he said.
He said although some villagers had enough food to last until the next season, a large number of people would have to depend on relief food.
Headman Siabungu was, however, hopeful that with the introduction of the crop diversification policy by Government, good crops in millet and sorghum would be harvested.
He appealed to the ministry of Agriculture and Co-operatives to begin distributing farming inputs in readiness for the coming season and to help the farmers prepare adequately.
Headman Siabungu also appealed to the Zambia Wildlife Authority (Zawa) to crop the elephant population in the area.
The elephants were a threat to human life and were destroying homes, he said.
Meanwhile, villagers in Chifunabuli constituency have called on Zawa to crop crocodiles as they are posing a threat to people living around Lake Bangweulu and the surrounding lagoons, reports ZIS.
The villagers complained to area member of Parliament Kennedy Sakeni during meetings held at Mwewa and Mundubi basic schools that they were unable to fish freely.
But Mr Sakeni said Zawa indicated to him that they would only crop the reptiles after carrying out a census of the crocodile population.
He said he would follow up the issue vigorously to ensure that fishing and tourism activities resumed in the area.

Original_Search_Title: $120bn Lost Due to Farm Disturbances
Headline: Zimbabwe: $120bn Lost Due to Farm Disturbances
Body_Text:
The government's chaotic land reforms have cost the country more than $120 billion in lost revenue this agriculture season alone and resulted in a rapid decline of crop yields in what could be a virtual death of commercial agriculture, the Commercial Farmers' Union (CFU) said yesterday.
Neil Wright, the CFU's chief economist, was elaborating on a CFU report highlighting the impact of the land reform programme, under which most white-owned land has been taken over by the government ostensibly for the resettlement of landless blacks.
The report, based on information gathered in February, but which was updated this month, said Zimbabwe's commercial agriculture and related input industries were on the verge of collapse and that farm output would be scaled back by between 40 and 60 percent this year.
About 200 000 farm jobs were also lost in 2002.
Wright said the chaos on the farms had cost Zimbabwe more than $120 billion and the situation could worsen if lawlessness persisted on the farms.
The CFU report says the government's present land policies, combined with lack of security and a collateral base for credit, have seriously hit production of virtually all commodities.
"Key input industries for agriculture are also on the verge of collapse and others are operating at well below capacity due to a substantial contraction of their main markets, foreign exchange shortages, interruptions in the supply of raw materials and price controls undermining their production ability," the report says.
The report adds that fertiliser companies such as Sable Chemicals, Zimphos, Zimbabwe Fertiliser Company and Windmill are operating below capacity due to problems in mobilising foreign currency to import raw materials, especially potash, and the inability of the National Railways of Zimbabwe to move these into the country quickly.
This situation, the report noted, had seriously affected stockfeeds imports and chemical production companies had had to drastically rationalise their products.
The CFU report said investment in tractors, farm machinery and irrigation equipment was insignificant due to the uncertainty prevailing in the commercial farming sector.
The industry survives on servicing equipment and selling spares. The CFU said between October and December 2002, only eight tractors were sold by all dealers but prior to 1997, around 1 600 tractors were sold every year.
The CFU reported that prior to the planting period for the 2002/2003 summer crop season, farm equipment valued at $23 billion was seized, looted or vandalised.
About 453 commercial farmers, out of 4 137 who worked the land before the start of the controversial land reforms three years ago, were still operating their farms, but this number was likely to fall if the government continued with its farm seizures, it noted.
"As of February 2003, the number of members no longer farming when compared with January 2000 was 2 098," the report said.
"According to a survey undertaken in February 2003, only 453 members were still fully operational on their properties, while another 666 were partially operational."
The organisation said maize production had fallen from 810 000 tonnes in 2000 to an estimated 80 000 tonnes due mostly to viability concerns, theft and the seizure of maize kept on farms.
It said despite the government's claims that the land reforms were completed in August 2002, the acquisition procedures were still being implemented in a lawless and disorderly manner, with illegal occupations, interruptions to production, theft of moveable property and human rights violations continuing.
Doug Taylor-Freeme, the CFU's vice-president, yesterday said evidence on the ground pointed to a bleak future for Zimbabwe's agriculture.
"Commercial agriculture production has been severely affected," he said.
"Soya-bean production has gone down from a record 162 000 tonnes to 30 000 tonnes. Most farms that produced agriculture products are sitting idle and more jobs will be lost. Maize seed production has been seriously reduced and a shortage looms in the coming season."
The report said Zimbabwe exported about 20 000 tonnes of soya beans in 2001 but this year the country needed to import the crop because of the sharp decline in output.
The report said the production of crops such as groundnuts and sunflower had dropped to insignificant levels, while wheat, sorghum, barley, coffee, sugar cane, dairy and ostrich production had also drastically gone down.
Zimbabwe's food security and foreign currency earnings have been hit by the decline in agricultural output, the report said.
Close to eight million Zimbabweans are in need of emergency food aid because of the impact of the land reform programme and a drought that has hit southern Africa.
"Commercial farming operations have been reduced substantially since the 1999/2000 season when fast-track resettlement began," the report noted.
"These developments have had and will continue to have severe repercussions on Zimbabwe's food security and foreign exchange earning ability in the short to medium-term."

Original_Search_Title: Localised Crop Failures Expected
Headline: Zambia: Localised Crop Failures Expected
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — While the overall food security outlook for Zambia appears to be better than last year, it is expected that there will still be pockets of need due to crop failures.
The latest report by the office of the UN Resident Coordinator, based in Lusaka, warns that "more than 40 percent of rural households are facing total crop failure" in a few districts of the Southern province.
The report said a March assessment conducted by the government, the UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) and NGOs, "suggests pockets of crop failure" mainly in the south, but also in isolated parts of the west and north.
CARE Zambia Emergency Coordinator Miles Murray told IRIN on Tuesday "the districts mentioned [are mainly] lower [in altitude] and hotter and have received less rain" than the higher northern parts of the country.
"However, the outlook for [total crop production] for the country seems to be pretty much back to normal," he added.
The UN Resident Coordinator's report found the districts most affected were: Luangwa, Gwembe, Sinazongwe and Livingstone districts in Southern province; Shangombo and Mongu districts in Western province; and Chilubi in Northern province.
Other districts at high risk of crop failure included: Sesheke and Itezhi-tezhi, also in Southern province; and Nyimba, Chama and Isoka in Eastern province.
"In Gwembe, only 10 percent of the crop planted along the riverbanks in October-November survived until the satisfactory February rains, but [were] finally washed away and buried by flooding along the riverbanks. In some areas, land is no longer suitable for cultivation due to huge deposits of rocks and sand," the report explained.
The south of the Kazungula/Livingstone area is a low-rainfall area with low agricultural potential.
"Several parts of Luangwa, including Kavalamanja camp, are facing almost total crop failure for maize. However, the few attempts at sorghum and millet are doing well. Elsewhere in the district, the maize crop is patchy. Complete and total crop failure is expected in Sinazongwe due to two dry spells in December and January," the report added.
An absence of rains in April meant that the third replanted crop was unlikely to succeed there.
While in the west, "due to late planting, some crops have been affected by floods that usually happen in April".
"In Shangombo district, the total crop failure is mainly on maize and a bit on the sorghum planted after the 20 January. The performance of cassava, sorghum and cowpeas can be described as average. In Mongu and Chilubi districts, crops have been adversely affected by lack of rain at critical stages of plant growth as well as poor access to inputs," the report noted.
Results from the FAO/World Food Programme (WFP) joint crop and food security assessment will be presented next month, together with key results of the WFP/Vulnerability Assessment Committee survey.

Original_Search_Title: State Pledges to Assist Farmers
Headline: Zimbabwe: State Pledges to Assist Farmers
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Government has pledged to increase its support to farmers for the production of maize, cotton, tobacco, paprika and wheat in an effort to boost foreign currency inflow into the country.
Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, Dr Joseph Made, made the commitment on Friday when he officiated at a function to launch a Farm and City Centre franchise shop in Norton.
"We are working around the clock on the matter. Last season we did better than the previous season in making inputs available to the farmers.
"As I speak, about 85 percent of wheat seed has been delivered to the farms and we would like to commend the seed companies for their support.
"It is our hope that fertiliser companies would also take heed and make every effort to make it available to the farms," Dr Made said.
Maize was also vital to improve on the country's strategic reserves.
"We have also granted permission for some enterprises to import seeds such as sorghum, sugar beans and cowpeas.
"The policy in my ministry is that they should approach us and we talk about the seeds but we would not allow the importation of seeds that is GMO (genetically modified organism) based," said Dr Made.
He also commended the relationship between Farm and City Centre and the Zimbabwe Farmers Union which has seen more than 350 000 farmers through the credit card scheme.
The opening of the franchise shop is part of the company's expansion drive in working with businesspeople operating nearer to farmers.
The group was expected to open a number of shops throughout the country under franchise arrangements.
"We are not going to have a specific number of shops across the country, and the number would depend on the interest that would have been generated.
"We can only stop after we have met the requirements, that is a shop at every point in the country," said Mr Philip Chimukute the franchise manager for farm and City Centre.
Under the franchising system, the franchisee would receive advice from Farm and City Centre.
"The franchisee would also use our corporate colours, layout of the shop and product procurement.
"We also help in product procurement to make sure that there is uniformity of prices for all our shops across the country," said Mr Chimukute.
The franchisee would have to raise capital to start the business.
"The proprietor will have to come up with the initial capital which we will top up through our procurement facilities.
"We also assess the building on which a businessperson would like to establish a shop and the viability of the business by looking at the catchment area," Mr Chimukute added.
It is hoped that the franchising system would boost the performance of the small to medium businesses across the country.

Original_Search_Title: Why Africa Only Has Four Months to Rescue Zimbabwe
Headline: Zimbabwe: Why Africa Only Has Four Months to Rescue Zimbabwe
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Now that President Thabo Mbeki and his colleagues, Nigeria's Olusegun
Obasanjo and Malawi's Bakili Muluzi, are at last engaged in a serious effort
to resolve the Zimbabwe crisis, it is time to look ahead to a post-Mugabe
era and try to assess the country's recovery prospects.
How much of the economic damage inflicted by Mugabe's madness is
permanent, how much is recoverable, and how long might a recovery process
take?
Even a rough assessment reveals one stark fact which Mbeki and his
colleagues should note. Time is of the essence. There is a tight window of
opportunity, between June and September of this year, when a fairly swift
recovery will still be possible. After that it will become rapidly more
difficult and full recovery will be less likely.
Already some of the damage is permanent. Zimbabwe has suffered a serious
loss of skills, especially in the industrial sector and in the
professions. Some 60% of the country's trained professional people -
engineers, accountants, lawyers and doctors - have left the country. Few
will return.
Keep up with the latest headlines on WhatsApp | LinkedIn
The industrial sector has been the worst hit. Scores of enterprises have
been forced to close down and others have moved to neighboring states in a
disinvestment process that eclipses anything South Africa experienced during
the apartheid years.
One estimate is that half a billion U.S. dollars of industrial
investment has left the country annually for the past three years. Again, it
will be hard to persuade any of these enterprises to return.
The mining sector has suffered the least harm. Although mining is being
badly disrupted by power cuts at the moment as foreign electricity
suppliers, Eskom included, demand up-front payments which the crippled
Zimbabwean fiscus cannot meet, the mines have suffered no structural damage
and could quickly be brought back to full production.
And ironically, although the agricultural sector was the target of
President Mugabe's wildly disruptive "land reform program", which has been
the root cause of Zimbabwe's economic collapse, it, too, could be
substantially revived in a relatively short time.
This is because what Mugabe unleashed was not in reality a land reform
program at all but a mindless spasm of legalized theft and vandalism which
brought agricultural production, and thus the economy as a whole, to a
standstill.
But the wonderfully fertile land is still there, and given bold policies
to reverse what has been done it could be brought back to levels of
production within a few years where Zimbabwe could at least feed itself and
pay its way once again.
Some permanent damage has been inflicted in agriculture too. A quarter
of the evicted commercial farmers, including some of the most valuable
producers of specialist crops like seed maize, have emigrated to prosper in
Australia and New Zealand, and they will not return. But three-quarters of
the farmers are still in the country, having moved into the cities and towns
and clung to their title deeds in the hope that things will change.
At the same time, between 60% and 70% of the farms from which these
commercial farmers were evicted are now vacant, abandoned by the black
"settlers" who were unable to operate them because of a lack of expertise,
financial resources, labour and government back-up.
Travelling across the country, as I did recently from Harare to
Bulawayo, reveals a remarkable sight of lush grazing - the livestock
population has been decimated to a quarter of what it was only three years
ago - and unworked farmland. Large fields which used to yield crops of
maize, soya bean, sorghum and tobacco, now lie unploughed and overgrown with
weeds.
A leading Zimbabwean rancher and agricultural specialist, Michael Clark,
describes what he saw during a recent trip through the once thriving
Masvingo and Chatsworth farming areas. "Although there were a few isolated
settler huts," Clark writes in an e-mailed memorandum, "there was nothing
else. No crops, no cattle, no people, no wildlife, no farmers, no
production."
Through most of the rest of the country it is the same, he says, "just
an empty void." Clark describes one farm he visited which used to run 22,000
head of export quality cattle, and which is now deserted.
What this means is that with a bold and determined reversal of policy,
the farmers who are still in the country could be returned to their
unoccupied farms. A substantial part of the agricultural economy could be
revived.
This should then be followed by a legitimate and sensible land reform
program, with land properly acquired with fair compensation for occupation
by authentic black farmers, who should be provided with the financial
support to acquire the seeds, fertilizers and other equipment they need to
make productive use of it.
But speed is critical.
Zimbabwe is bankrupt and to get it up and running again it needs to have
a tobacco crop in the ground by September. Do that, says John Robertson, the
country's leading independent economist, and Zimbabwe could possibly match
its 1999 export earnings of US$600-million next year, compared with the
miserable US$150-million it earned from tobacco sales this year. This would
be a lifesaving injection of foreign exchange, the essential starting point
on the road to recovery.
But, Robertson warns, to achieve that requires getting the bulk of the
available commercial farmers back on the abandoned farms by June or
July.
In other words, there is no time for further timidity and egg-dancing by
Mbeki, Obasanjo and Muluzi in their dealings with Mugabe.
More critical still, if a settlement is delayed until next year it will
be too late to plant food crops for the 2004 harvest. Starvation, already
ravaging the rural population, will then become worse with no chance of
recovery until the 2005 season.
The timeline is achievable. What is needed is a swift agreement that
Mugabe should retire immediately, with a guarantee of immunity from
prosecution for all his crimes to ease him on his way, and for a new leader
with some local and international credibility to take over the leadership of
the ruling Zanu-PF party.
The former Finance Minister, Simba Makoni, whom Mugabe fired last year
for suggesting the Zimbabwe dollar should be devalued, would clearly be the
most competent successor, but he lacks a solid support base within the party
and other old party hacks are hungry for the job. Again Mbeki and company
should use their influence to get the right man appointed, for international
credibility is vital to the recovery process.
That done, negotiations should then take place between the new Zanu-PF
leadership and the opposition Movement for Democratic Change (MDC) for a
joint transitional administration to take over and prepare the ground for
internationally supervised elections to take place, preferably before the
end of the year.
That would be enough to win international endorsement of the deal. The
World Bank and International Monetary Fund would then come on board, and
with the support of the police and military the reconstruction program could
begin even before the election.
But if there is any further diplomatic soft-shoe shuffling, another
agricultural season will be lost and the misery will turn into catastrophe,
for Zimbabwe and the region.

Original_Search_Title: Emergency Food, Seed Supply Continues
Headline: Ethiopia: Emergency Food, Seed Supply Continues
Body_Text:
The emergency food relief jointly launched last February by the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) and the Ethiopian Red Cross Society (ERCS) is continuing in different regions of Ethiopia, it was learnt. The principlal aim of this significant operation is to prevent the total distitution of the agricultural and pastoralist population living in areas of the country most affected by last year's erratic, poor or nonexistent rainfalls," the ICRC said.
On the occasion of the round of distribution that took place in March and the first half of April, some 600,000 received food rations and seeds in Fedis, Gursum, Boke and Darolebu woredas of Oromia region, Irob woreda and Adigrat of Tigray region, and Dale, Borcha, Humbo and Kindo Kusha of Southern Nations, Nationalities and Peoples' region. In total about 1,700 MT of local improved seeds (mainly maize and sorghum) and 11,500 MT of food (cereals, pulses and oil) were distributed. In addition, an fifth round of distribution took place in zone 3 of Afar region and Mieso and Afdem woredas of Somali region for some 118,000 persons who received some 2,200 MT of food.
"This assistance brought by hundreds of Red Cross volunteers and staff will reduce the impact of the food shortage suffered by the families until the next harvest and the recoverey of grazing areas for the cattle. As a consequence of this essential aid, the humanitarian situation in the areas covered remains relatively stable with the threat of famine being strongly reduced," the ICRC said.
The belg rainfall, which is still under way in several areas in the country, will enable the farmers to start planting the local variety of seeds provided by the ICRC and the ERCS and help them maintain the household economy, which mainly depends on agriculture.
In the coming months, the ICRC and the ERCS will continue to distribute the remaining assistance and will closely monitor the effects of the rains. However, this joint operation is not limited to emergency food and seeds distributions, medium-term projects such as the rehabilitation and construction of water wells and rainfall catchments have already been launched.

Original_Search_Title: FAO Seeks Food Security for 400 000 Zim Households
Headline: Zimbabwe: FAO Seeks Food Security for 400 000 Zim Households
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Food and Agricultural Organisation has appealed for US$8.5 million from international donors to improve the food security of 400 000 agricultural households in communal and peri-urban areas in Zimbabwe, it was learnt this week.
According to a report entitled United Nations Consolidated Inter-Agency Appeal in Response to the Humanitarian Crisis in Southern Africa, the project is expected to run until June this year and would be implemented through local non-governmental organisations.
"This project aims to improve household food security through supporting and increasing existing productive capacity for agricultural production in the most vulnerable communities in communal lands and peri-urban areas," the report says.
The beneficiaries of the project will be people in 40 districts out of the 58 districts in the country hard hit by drought in the past year and in peri-urban areas of Harare, Bulawayo and other towns.
The Zimbabwe Food Security Assessment for 2002/03 says that out of half the country's 14 million people who require food aid, a total of 2.6 million of the severely affected are in communal areas.
The World Food Programme will also assist in the provision of food aid through the food-for-work programme that will see the construction and renovation of irrigation facilities to enhance water facilities.
The project will also ensure that there is possibility of a food-for-grain swap and in this case, a maize-for-sorghum swap.

Original_Search_Title: Once Lush Buhera Forests No More
Headline: Zimbabwe: Once Lush Buhera Forests No More
Body_Text:
Harare — ABOUT 10 years ago, thick forests characterised Chikwekwete village in the Buhera District of Manicaland.
One could hardly see the next homestead that was less than a kilometre away because of the lush vegetation.
Pasture for livestock was easily available not far from the houses, although the cattlemen preferred driving to a river about two kilometres away where they could also have a drink of water.
It was risky then to leave livestock unattended for long, because it would be difficult to locate them when they strayed into the thick bushes.
Young children were also not allowed to walk alone in the bush, as wild animals would prey on them. Criminals also hunted people in these bushes for ritual purposes or just petty crime like mugging.
During those years mountains were a sacred place where no one passed any remark no matter what they saw. Rural folk would go there to get firewood and also pick wild fruit quietly and only speak using sign language.
Passing remarks it was believed, would anger ancestral spirits that would punish offenders by either disappearing or getting lost in the hills for days.
These mountains were rich in all sorts of wild fruits such as hute, makwakwa, maroro, nhengeni, mauyu, tsambatsi and matufu.
Rural farmers grew sorghum (mhunga) which was also favoured by birds.
Wildlife and humans lived side by side in harmony.
Those were the days when meat was also always available as cattle bred in abundance because of the rich pastures and chicken laid so many eggs because of the abundance in seed products.
The younger generation also spent their afternoons trapping quelea birds, which travelled in swarms using a sticky adhesive known as urimbo which was smeared on tree branches.
These birds were a delicacy.
However, Buhera District in general is a sorry sight. It is so dry and that lush green vegetation is no more. The quelea birds don't grace the skies anymore.
Although Buhera District falls in region five that has low rainfall in Zimbabwe, land conservation activities in the 1970s had made it possible for rural folk to produce food all year round without anyone going hungry.
It was a punishable crime to cut down trees and there were areas designated for pastures and cropping.
The rivers were flowing with fresh waters and vegetable gardens graced the landscape during wintertime.
The Zunde Ramambo system of storing grain by chiefs was also in place. This grain was collected by all villagers to a central granary at the chiefs' homestead that would feed orphans, widows and also to feed the village when there was a low yield.
As Buhera people round up their harvesting this season, some have already started cutting down trees as usual for brick moulding and firewood. The practice still continues unabated.
The thick forests, which used to cover Chief Makumbe's area only a few years ago, have since disappeared.
Livestock has to be driven as far as Gumbunga that is five kilometres away for pasture and water.
However, other villagers said they had turned into brick moulding to save the forests that have dwindled in the area.
But they still have to cut tree trunks into pieces that are placed in a 'brick oven' to harden them.
A thousand bricks costs $5 000.
It is estimated that a full brick oven constitutes at least 20 000 bricks.
Villagers have become desperate for wood fuel as paraffin that is an alternative is no longer readily available. Paraffin stoves have collected rust as paraffin has joined many other basic commodities that have become scarce.
Village head Mutizwa Munyimi said he was not happy with the way his people were cutting down trees especially the Mukamba tree, which he said, was used for different purposes.
The Mukamba tree can be used to manufacture various types of household and office furniture, which included desks, tables, cabinets and wardrobes.
He added that people who had migrated from cities to rural areas in the last two years had worsened the situation.
"Many people have returned home following retrenchments and these are cutting down trees to put up houses and cattle pens. They also need wood fuel and the scenario is just very distressing," Mr Munyimi said.
Apparently, many newly resettled farmers had the tendency of hacking down trees completely oblivious of the need to keep small woodlots on their premises, as conservation strategy.
Some villagers cut these trees for firewood, which they sell at Murambinda growth point, where there is a ready market.
It is also sad to note that residents of Murambinda growth point have invaded neighbouring farms for firewood because of the acute paraffin shortage.
Some of the residents could be seen emerging from the farm forests carrying bundles of firewood and others armed with axes as they went about hunting for the commodity.
Poverty was cited as one of the major reasons that had driven many unemployed folk into this desperate measure.
But this is actually complicating matters that will be difficult to rectify in future.
"Some of the young people are still very young and supposed to be in school but you see them during school days pushing wheelbarrows of firewood for sale.
"Others have already dropped out of school to engage in this activity after both parents died," said village head Munyimi.
Buhera District, like many other areas in Zimbabwe is also hardest hit by the HIV/Aids pandemic that is claiming at least 3 000 lives a week.
One villager admitted he cut down trees for sale as firewood as a source of livelihood.
"I am aware that what I am doing is against the law but what other alternative do I have to ensure my family and orphans from my two brothers who died from Aids do not starve. It is a matter of life and death," he said.
A few years ago the University of Zimbabwe came up with a stove that would use as little wood fuel as possible but it seems this idea was not marketed effectively.
The stove popularly known as Tsotso stove could also use cattle dung for cooking.
"It is important, therefore, that alternative sources of energy be encouraged before more trees are lost," Mr Munyimi said.
He said unless villagers were given alternative options, trees would continue to be chopped for various domestic purposes including erection of open grain silos, thatching and fencing poles.
Unless the villagers are taught about the importance of conserving the country's flora and fauna for sustainable development, there would be no hope of salvaging the little that is left.
The availability of such information should facilitate formulation of appropriate strategies and measures to combat deforestation, which has resulted in many districts losing more than 80 percent of their woodland cover.
"We have no option but to use firewood. Now the problem is where to find it.
"We can't sit back and relax when firewood is available in the farm," said Ms Tarisai Mariseni of Murambinda growth point.
Although villagers are encouraged to grow quick maturing trees such as the bamboo, it would seem there is no systematic approach to ensuring this goal is achieved.
The Tree Planting Day which, is commemorated on December 1 is a platform to remind the nation of the need to conserve nature but it would seem there is no concerted effort by stakeholders to ensure replanting of forests.
The Natural Resources Board played a very big role in providing seedlings for woodlots for a very long time since independence but it seems there is not much being done in that area anymore.
They held competitions every year where villages that had showed efforts to conserve their natural resources were rewarded in both cash and kind.
However, environmental conservation is gaining prominence in Zimbabwe and legal reforms aimed at strengthening the monitoring systems are also underway.
Environment Africa a non-governmental organisation has launched a campaign against deforestation by encouraging planting of more trees in both rural and urban settings.
The organisation has so far planted both indigenous and exotic trees in Guruve, Victoria Falls and at some schools in Harare.
Environment Africa information officer Mr Andrew Hartnack said their main aim was to cover all rural areas.
"We are planting and distributing Jatropha seeds, an exotic tree which is used in oil and candle manufacturing.
"We are also distributing seeds for fruit trees and shade trees," Mr Hartnack said.
Environment Africa also gives lecturers at various schools on how best Zimbabwe can save the environment.
In an effort to raise environmental awareness among all citizens, the Ministry of Environment and Tourism in conjunction with the Institute of Environmental Studies at the University of Zimbabwe are also working on a national environmental policy document.
The Minister of Environment and Tourism, Cde Francis Nhema recently urged all sectors of the economy to invest in public environment awareness programmes.
Cde Nhema urged everyone to take concerted measures to ensure that people who engage in illegal activities that affect the environment such as gold panning and poaching were dealt with according to the law.
The Environmental Management Act, which was passed by Parliament last year, also has a provision for environmentally friendly projects.
Members of Parliament have been tasked to reinforce these messages to their various constituencies as they played a major role in ensuring that the multilateral environmental agreements that the Government has ratified are enforced.
However, the fact remains that for as long as there is no immediate solution to the indiscriminate cutting of trees, Zimbabwe is set for a natural disaster.
Gulleys and siltation in Buhera's major rivers like the Save are a clear example of massive land degradation and deforestation.
Something has to be done as a matter of urgency to rectify this land disaster.

Original_Search_Title: Special Report On the Northern Crisis
Headline: Uganda: Special Report On the Northern Crisis
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Since Ugandan President Yoweri Museveni came to power in 1986, Uganda has been the envy of many developing nations, achieving high economic growth rates and posting encouraging social development indicators, including stemming the upward trend of HIV/AIDS infection rates in the country.
But these indicators are primarily a reflection of the development of the southern and central areas of Uganda. In the north, a very different situation prevails.
For the last 17 years, residents of the northern Acholi subregion (comprising Gulu, Kitgum, and Pader districts) have lived under the terror of killings and abductions by the Lords Resistance Army (LRA); an insurgent group, whose leader Joseph Kony is seeking to replace Museveni's government with one ruled by the biblical Ten Commandments.
The LRA insurgency has made children its main target in the war against Museveni's government, and has committed some of the worst atrocities against civilians. The group routinely attacks villages in 'Acholiland', killing civilians and abducting children to be forced into its ranks or to be used as sex slaves for commanders. Yet, due to Uganda's overall macroeconomic successes, the LRA insurgency remains an under-reported conflict, and its toll on the Acholi society has continued to be underestimated.
The horrors of captivity
Children who have managed to escape from LRA captivity tell horror tales of the extreme physical and psychological violence with which they were initiated into the ranks of the rebel army.
Peter Ochan, 14, was abducted while asleep and made to walk through the night and the next day, before he and other abducted children were finally allowed to rest. During the subsequent ten months he spent in LRA captivity, the clubbing of children to death by rebel commanders became a daily ritual in the bush.
Ochan, now accommodated at the Gulu Support the Children Organisation (GUSCO), a rehabilitation centre set up to help children returning from captivity, tells of how he and others were forced to carry the decomposing body of a boy killed for trying to escape.
"We carried the body on our shoulders everywhere we went. It was smelling very badly. We were told it was part of our training. The commander then told us to scoop out the brains from what was left of the body and show around to others," he said.
Sixteen-year-old Tony Ulanya was also abducted when he was only ten and stayed in Kitgum district for only three months, after which he was made to walk to southern Sudan in a group of some 300 abductees, where he was forced to undergo military training. Of the three hundred abductees, only about 50 survived. The rest died of diseases or lack of food, or were murdered by rebel commanders, he says.
Ulanya has met Kony several times and describes the rebel leader as a "possessed" man. "He would preach to us on Sundays for many hours. He always told us that he spoke with the angel who told him that he would one day be the president of Uganda and the young people he has abducted, and the children born in captivity would take over the country. Sometimes he leaves people and stays on his own for a long time," he said
Opportunities to escape from captivity are extremely rare for many of the abductees. Many do not dare attempt escape, fearing reprisals against themselves or their families should they be re-abducted.
