A few have managed to do so when under intense attacks by the Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF), but many are killed in the crossfire.
It is even more difficult for child mothers to return because as 'wives' of commanders they are much more closely watched. Their movement is also restricted by the need to care for their children, who cannot survive weeks of walking in the bush with little food or water.
Patrick Komakech, who is at Kitgum Concerned Women Association (KICWA) - another rehabilitation centre - was lucky to escape two weeks after his abduction from school in Kitgum early in 2003. But many children who tried to do so were recaptured and killed as the rest watched, as a "lesson on what would happen to us if we tried to run away".
"Two boys tried to escape. They were beaten to death with sticks. A child whose feet were too swollen to walk any further was tied up and left behind to die. He was left there and told to eat soil," Komakech said.
Rehabilitation
Those who run the rehabilitation centres for returning abductees such as GUSCO and KICWA say most of the children who escape are often emaciated, weak from diseases, and severely traumatised.
In the bush, good food like chicken and goats is reserved for commanders, while children only eat sorghum or wild fruits. "In Sudan, we had to kill people, steal their animals and take stored grains in order to survive," Komakech said.
Returning abductees are required to first report to the Child Protection Units set up within the UPDF, before being taken to rehabilitation centres such as GUSCO and KICWA. While under the control of the UPDF, many said they had been under pressure to join the army.
Fifteen-year-old Denis Nyero, who was rescued by the army, told IRIN he had refused to give in to the pressure, but many boys who were with him at the child protection centre had agreed to remain behind and join the army.
The rehabilitation centres are, however, just a temporary respite for returning abductees. Once return to their families has been facilitated, the children continue to remain under constant risk of re-abduction, which would mean almost certain execution at the hands of the LRA.
There are also children in the centres who were born in LRA captivity, and have no parents or relatives to care for them because they were either orphaned or separated from their parents in the bush.
One positive aspect of the human tragedy in the Acholi subregion is that the community usually welcomes child returnees, despite the atrocities they may have committed against their own people. Even where children's parents are dead, the extended families will always accept them back without attaching much stigma to them, according to the KICWA centre manager. "I do not remember of any children who have been rejected by their families," said Christopher Arwai, who heads the KICWA centre
The problem of acceptance has occurred mostly with child mothers, because relatives are often not willing to support their children, according to Julius, a programme manager at GUSCO. "Our major problem is with the child mothers who often find it difficult to get support from their families. When they are with two or three children, they become an extra burden for the families," he said.
Night commuters
In an attempt to find some improved security, thousands of people from surrounding villages walk long distances at night to shelter in the safety of Kitgum and Gulu towns, where they occupy public spaces such as churches and schools. It is estimated that some 8,500 children commute every night from their villages to stay overnight in Gulu town.
The phenomenon of night commuting is a direct consequence of the fear which the LRA has brought to the community. On some nights, the available shelters are overstretched, forcing most commuters to sleep on pavements and verandas. "We come here out of fear. Our parents do not want us to be abducted. But we are suffering. We only eat one meal at home. We don't get any supper," one child commuter told IRIN.
In Pader, parents have been sending their children to neighbouring Kotido District in the hope they would be looked after, "This illustrates the kind of desperation here. Parents sending their children off to another district where they have no relatives. We have never seen this before," Mads Oyen, the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) child protection officer in Uganda, told IRIN.
Operation Iron Fist
News of the launch of a major military operation to eliminate the LRA, following an agreement signed between the Sudanese and the Ugandan government in March last year, initially raised hopes that the insurgency would be brought to an end and normal life would return to the 1.4 million residents of Acholiland.
The operation, code-named "Operation Iron Fist", prompted humanitarian agencies to make contingency plans to receive an estimated 3,000 children who were to be rescued by the Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF) from LRA rear bases in southern Sudan.
It was not until June, when Kony's group sneaked back into northern Uganda and intensified attacks on civilians, that it became clear Operation Iron Fist had not succeeded in its mission. The group intensified its attacks on villages and camps for Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs), killing and abducting even more children.
A child abductee who was in the bush under LRA control when Operation Iron Fist [www.updfironfist.co.ug] got under way told IRIN how Kony had ordered the rebels to step up attacks and kill as many civilians as they could. "We extended our operations in Kitgum and Pader. In Agoro, [a county of Kitgum District], we abducted 200 people. Kony ordered us to kill all of them. We did as we were told. After that, we split into smaller groups. Some went to Gulu, but mine stayed in Kitgum," the boy said.
According to Oyen, there had been at least 6,000 abductions since the beginning of 2003. This rate, according to UNICEF is the highest since the insurgency in northern Uganda began. "The abductions that have taken place since the period after June [2002] are the highest levels of abductions ever," Oyen said.
Christopher Arwai believes that many more children have been abducted from northern Uganda during the entire period of the insurgency, most of who may never be accounted for.
Humanitarian Overview
Rebel activities in northern Uganda have forced up to 800,000 people to flee their homes and move into camps protected by the Ugandan army.
Gulu hosts the bulk of the IDPs, estimated at about 400,000, and in Kitgum camps have spontaneously emerged to accommodate some 100,000 IDPs.
The humanitarian situation in Pader District, where almost the entire population has been displaced and cut off from humanitarian access by insecurity, is considered most dire - an estimated 82 percent of the population in the district are now living in camps. In addition, cattle rustling by armed Karamojong 'warriors' launching raids from neighbouring Kotido district has increased insecurity still further.
A visit to Awer camp, situated on the outskirts of Gulu town, revealed a critical situation. Although the camp's official capacity is 11,000 IDPs, the actual number of displaced persons living in the camp has more than doubled in the past few months, currently standing at 24,000.
Cases of malnutrition have also risen sharply in the region in the past few months, where the capacity of humanitarian agencies to provide emergency food, water, sanitation and health facilities has been overstretched. Attacks by rebels on food stores, who routinely empty granaries and steal domestic animals, only serve to aggravate food shortages further.
Valerie Julliand, head of office for the UN-OCHA Regional Support Office for East and Central Africa, stressed that the special circumstances of children in the northern Uganda conflict required urgent international attention.
"We get the feeling that the situation in northern Uganda is not getting its due attention and needs to be brought back into the international radar. The violence has no limit," she said.
Julliand pointed out that the devastation in Acholiland was already cancelling out much of the progress Uganda had made on the socio-economic front. "We know that wars produce lost generations. These ones are not only lost, they are completely ruined. It is devastating because of the deterioration of the human mind and spirit," Julliand said.
In addition, the nature of the LRA, being a fragmented rebel army made up largely of traumatised children, makes it exceptionally difficult for humanitarian actors to meet with, and to promote humanitarian principles with, the rebels. "It is the purest form of terrorism one can imagine. In some other countries, you can meet rebels and educate them on humanitarian principles, and somehow you don't have the impression that these efforts are in vain," Julliand said.
On-off peace talks
Although both the government and the LRA have expressed commitment to starting serious peace talks, the situation on the ground has often raised questions over the prospects for real progress.
Even after declaring several cease-fires to pave the way for talks, the LRA has sustained attacks on civilians and increased forced recruitment of children into its ranks. For its part, the Ugandan government has indicated that it wants to bring an end to the insurgency by using both diplomatic and military means.
Although parliament passed a law in 1999 offering amnesty to surrendering rebels, few hardcore LRA mebers have taken advantage of it. Most beneficiaries of the amnesty so far have been abducted children who have escaped from LRA captivity, with only a handful of commanders giving themselves up under the terms of the amnesty.
Some observers believe the attitude of the Ugandan military is an obstacle to negotiating peace in northern Ugandan. Carlos Rodriguez, a Catholic missionary in Gulu and a member of the Acholi Religious Leaders Peace Initiative (ARLPI) [www.acholipeace.org], criticised the government's military methods against the LRA which, he argues, have led to the deaths of innocent civilians and the abduction of children.
"The army tells us that they have killed rebels. But when you look at the so-called rebels, most of them are women and children. We don't think the solution is to kill the same people whom we have failed to protect from the rebels in the first place," Rodriguez said. "You don't use helicopter gunships to kill guerrillas. You end up killing many innocent people," he told IRIN.
Mistrust between the government and the rebels has frustrated efforts by the peace team appointed by Museveni to negotiate terms for peace talks, and even to make meaningful contact with the rebel commanders.
Lt-Gen Salim Saleh, a former senior commander in the Ugandan army and currently head of the government's peace team, said the rebels had failed to honour several agreements to meet with the team, but also expressed some concern over the approach of some elements within the Ugandan military. "The president is totally committed to peace. He does not fear to negotiate. The frustration I get is the big pressure from the military. They think they can end this conflict militarily," Saleh told IRIN.
Some aid agencies working in the north have criticised the international community for allowing Museveni's government to keep the humanitarian crisis in the north on the back burner, partly because of his economic successes in other parts of the country.
For example, they have expressed concern over the government's recent decision to re-allocate 23 percent of funds from other ministries to defence, seen by some as indicating a preference for a military solution over a peaceful settlement in the north.
"The government has put almost no resources to humanitarian response, leaving humanitarian organisations to fill the gap. The Ugandan government needs to be reminded of its responsibilities," one aid worker told IRIN.

Original_Search_Title: Falling Millet Harvests Bad for Food Security
Headline: Tanzania: Falling Millet Harvests Bad for Food Security
Body_Text:
A LIMITED market for some food crops has been cited as the major problem currently facing crop production in semi-arid areas of Tanzania.
Consequently, production of the crops, which include sorghum and pearl millet, is declining fast and may lead to food insecurity in the areas in future.
This has been established by a review of crop protection in semi-arid Tanzania done by the National Research Institute of the United Kingdom.
Areas reflected in the review programme include Dodoma, Singida, Shinyanga and some parts of Mwanza, Arusha, Tabora, Iringa and Mbeya regions in the central semi-arid zone. Others are parts of the south eastern regions of Mtwara, Lindi and Morogoro.
The results show that sorghum and pearl millet are good for food security purposes, and are also well-suited to Tanzania. But they not preferred as food in most areas of the country.
Speaking in an exclusive interview with Business Times in Mwanza last week, a natural resource economist, Dr Joseph Hella, said there is an urgent need for stakeholders to find a way to tackle the problem.
He said his study of the effects of globalisation and trade liberalisation on small-scale farmers in Tanzania has established that, although millet and sorghum have a wide range of advantages, few farmers are interested in growing them.
The other advantages are that the crops are indigenous in most places - and are suitable in areas where other food crops such as maize and rice cannot perform well because of insufficient rainfall.
"Under the system we have in place - of globalisation and trade liberalisation - everyone, even a farmer in the village, is looking at the immediate benefits of activities they are carrying out. They do not think about other benefits," he pointed out.
However, the economist - who comes from the department of agricultural economics and agribusiness of the Sokoine University of Tanzania in Morogoro - emphasised that ignoring cultivation of the crops could cost the nation a lot in terms of food security.
This is given the fact that they account much for the food status of the country, especially in the semi-arid and the southern regions.
To intervene, he recommended stakeholders to chart out strategies under which the setback can be solved. This can be done through promotional campaigns aimed at encouraging processors of different food and other products (such as alcoholic drinks) to use the products as raw materials.
The NRI review identifies other bottlenecks as being weeds, a shortage of improved seeds, low yields, as well as little support from politicians and researchers.
Other crops whose production has been declining in the past few years are cotton and grapes.
The findings, however, give a healthier result for others, particularly horticultural crops such as tomatoes and other vegetables.
The results also show good performance in the cultivation of grains, cassava, rice, maize, sunflower and perennial crops.
There are many factors attributed to this positive situation.
Some of these are good markets and prices, preference by consumers, political support, availability of local processing and increasing demand in and outside the country.

Original_Search_Title: FSI Builds Empire Renting A2 Farms
Headline: Zimbabwe: FSI Builds Empire Renting A2 Farms
Body_Text:
MORE names of Zanu PF cronies renting out farms taken under the fast-track land reform programme emerged this week.
Investigations by the Zimbabwe Independent revealed that of the 634 model A2 farmers whose properties are being operated by FSI Agricom, over 400 are allocated to either ruling-party officials, war veterans' leaders, or businessmen with close links to government.
FSI Agricom Holdings (Pvt ) Ltd and CFI Holdings (Pvt) Ltd have entered contractual agreements with A2 beneficiaries to run theirfarms. The contracts are, however,bound by a government Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) of September 17, 2002 signed by the two companies.
In Mashonaland West province, where provincial chairman Phillip Chiyangwa was among the first people to offer his farm to FSI Agricom, over 150 farmers are sub-letting their farms.
Prominent figures renting out their farms include war veterans' chairman Patrick Nyaruwata and secretary-general Andy Mhlanga who are both occupying Bramfield Farm.
Sources said FSI had planted about 105 hectares of soya beans on the farm, which is ready for harvesting.
"The two started benefiting from the FSI contract last year when the company planted about 100 hectares of wheat," sources at FSI said.
"We will be harvesting the sum-mer crop in the coming weeks and making the land ready for wheat."
FSI provided financial support to the two war veterans' leaders covering inputs, planting and harvesting of the crops.
Another beneficiary of the A2scheme with close links to govern-ment in the same province is Tsitsi Mugabe, the wife of Davison Mugabe who is the chairman of the Indige-nous Commercial Farmers Union.
Papers seen by the Independent show that in Mashonaland Centralbeneficiaries from the FSI arra-ngement include Agricultural perm-anent secretary Ngoni Masoka and writer Alexander Kanengoni.
In Manicaland, Zesa boss Sydney Gata is a beneficiary and in the Midlands, deputy minister of Home affairs Rugare Gumbo is also a beneficiary.
Sources said the Mashonaland West scenario was a microcosm of the wider national scenario in which senior ruling party and government officials seized farms only to rent them out.
"Provincial chefs are manipula-ting loopholes in the land reform programme with a good number of them renting out their farms to government organisations such as Arda and the District Development Fund where FSI has not come into picture," a source said.
Documents seen by the Indepe-ndent show that FSI Agricom, which has quickly become an agricultural giant, divided the country into four regional areas - Central, Northern, Eastern and Western - for its purposes.
In a statement to the Independent, FSI Agricom managing director Ivan Savala said the September 17, 2002 MOU has given his company a mandate to produce for the country to ensure food security.
"In terms of this MOU, FSI Agri-com was required to produce the following quantities of crops: 30 000 tonnes of maize; 30 000 tonnes of soya beans; 30 000 tonnes of sorghum; 90 000 tonnes of wheat, and 60 000 tonnes of seed cotton," Savala said.
"Pursuant to this agreement FSI supported 634 A2 farmers growing the first three crops.
"Under our cotton out-grower scheme alone we supported 25 000 A1 and A2 farmers," he said.
Savala said that the agreement provides for FSI assisting A2 bene-ficiaries with inputs as well as management where necessary to ensure that the objective of food security and production is met.
Savala said FSI as an agro-processor has provided a window of opportunity for A2 beneficiaries. Precise contractual agreements are tailor-made to suit individual requirements of each beneficiary, he added.

Original_Search_Title: Gov't Says Food Security Programme On Course
Headline: Nigeria: Gov't Says Food Security Programme On Course
Body_Text:
The nation's Special Programme on Food Security (SPFS) has recorded some "substantial yield increases" in rice, maize, sorghum, cow peas and tomatoes.
The minister of agriculture and rural development, Alhaji Bello Adamu, disclosed this on Tuesday in Abuja while speaking to a 29-man team of Chinese experts who are in the country for the programme.
Adamu said that under the programme, a total of 42,250 broilers and 44,750 layers were raised.
According to him, 347 packages involving ram fattening, small ruminants upgrading and pig production were successfully executed by farmers, through the programme.
He disclosed that already, field activities have begun in about 109 selected sites across the country to further enhance the programme.
The SPFS is expected to improve the livelihood of the rural poor through improvements in their methods and prices of products, he said.
It is also to assist through the provision of portable rural water supply, water harvesting and control, aqua culture and soil fertility.
The SPFS is an initiative of government to pursue nationwide implementation of the programmes under the Unilateral Trust Fund it signed with the United Nation (UN) on May 11, 2000.
The programme, he said, is to be implemented over a five-year period at a total cost of 45.2 million dollars.
The South-South co-operation is an initiative within the SPFS aimed at enhancing an effective solidarity among developing countries.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) reports that China is expected to provide a total of 500 experts to strengthen the implementation of the programme. NAN

Original_Search_Title: Harvesting Enters Into Full Swing
Headline: Zimbabwe: Harvesting Enters Into Full Swing
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Agriculture Research Extension (Arex) says harvesting, shelling, drying and marketing of the early-planted maize crop are the major activities in all provinces as the 2002/2003 agricultural season comes to an end.
Arex said in its fortnightly report that the late planted crop in Mashonaland Central Province was at the hard dough stage and the condition was fair to good.
The late-planted crop in Beitbridge, Gwanda and Matobo districts ranged from knee height to soft dough stages and the condition was fair to good although it still needed some more rains.
"In the Hurungwe District of Mashonaland West, the crop is being artificially dried and either being consumed or sold and wild animals are reported to be destroying crops on many of the A1 and A2 farms," Arex said.
It said cases of grain theft had been reported in Mashonaland West and East provinces.
It said reaping of Virginia tobacco had been completed and the major activities were curing, grading and marketing.
"Some farmers are now busy preparing their seedbed areas in Mashonaland East Province and shortage of diesel is also affecting transportation of crops from fields to barns."
Arex said barn space shortage and dilapidated infrastructure were affecting curing and baling of the crop in Mashonaland Central Province.
It also said grading and preparation for marketing of the burley tobacco was in progress but the crop was becoming less popular in Mashonaland West Province as the farmers now opted for the Virginia type which they claimed was more viable.
"Picking, pest control, grading and baling are the major activities on the cotton crop with some farmers already delivering their crop to the markets," said Arex.
It said there was great concern by farmers over the delayed announcement of the official price of cotton in Mashonaland Central Province.
Arex said harvesting and marketing of the early-planted edible bean crop was in progress while the late-planted crop was podding.
In Matabeleland South, more crops were planted during the February wet spell and the late planted crop was unlikely to yield grain due to inadequate moisture, Arex said.
"The bulk of the small grains crops, sorghum, mhunga and rapoko, were planted late but most of them were ready for harvesting," it said.
Quelea birds were a problem but efforts were being made by some farmers to chase them away. In Makonde District an outbreak of heliothis was affecting the maturing mhunga crop.
The body said very little of the sunflower crop was planted this season in Matabeleland South Province due to seed shortage and also inadequate moisture at planting.
The crop in Zvishavane had been affected by the dry spell but harvesting of the crop was now in progress.
"The soyabean crop is now being harvested and dried but in Insiza District, the crop performed very well while the majority of the crop is in fair to good condition."
It said the paprika crop in Mashonaland Central and West provinces was being reaped and cured and some farmers were reported to be grading their produce in readiness for marketing.
Arex said grazing was adequate in most provinces although in some districts of Matabeleland South Province, it may not be enough until the next season.
It said water supplies were adequate for both livestock and domestic use in all provinces except for Matabeleland South Province where small dams were low and rivers were not flowing. - Ziana.

Original_Search_Title: Mengo to Rear Edible Rats
Headline: Uganda: Mengo to Rear Edible Rats
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Kabaka's government has embarked on rearing emisu (edible rats) after getting ready market in Japan and several African countries.
The kingdom will also invest in commercialising banana juice production, exporting matooke, bogoya and sweet bananas. The kingdom will also strengthen growing of sweet sorghum for power generation, animal feeds, brew, sugar, fertilisers and syrup.
These are some of the 15 projects contained in the seven-page report on agriculture, animal husbandry, fisheries and environment, presented to the Lukiiko by a minister, Lukulwase Nsamba, recently .
The measures aim at increasing food production, income generation, poverty alleviation and preserving the environment.
Nsamba said he was working closely with the Uganda Wildlife Authority to rear the edible rats.
He said USAID-IDEA Project and Cape to Africa Limited would fund the commercialisation of banana juice through growing improved bananas from which the juice is made.
Nsamba said plans were underway to open a factory that will improve the juice for export. Mengo, working with Tec tank company of Canada, plans to plant 10,000 acres of sweet sorghum.
The report said funding is expected from the United Nations Global Environment Facilities the World Bank, and the National Agricultural Research Organisation.Ends

Original_Search_Title: Manicaland Farmer Wins $1m
Headline: Zimbabwe: Manicaland Farmer Wins $1m
Body_Text:
Harare — A farmer from Manicaland, Mr Timothy Jelani, won $1 million in the Farm
and City Centre (FCC) Reap Big award in a draw that was conducted in
Chegutu, yesterday.
Six other farmers won a heifer each courtesy of FCC while 90 others
walked away with a variety of prizes sponsored by various companies in the
same competition.
Speaking at the ceremony, the Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural
Resettlement Dr Joseph Made said the support by FCC was complementary to
Government's efforts to support small holder farmers.
"Since 1999, your company has been awarding prizes worth millions of
dollars in the form of ploughs, veterinary products, fertilisers, seeds,
cement, stock feeds, knapsack sprayers and crop chemicals.
"I also note with appreciation the inclusion of heifers in the prizes
because livestock is an important part of agricultural production," said the
minister in a speech read on his behalf by the secretary in the ministry, Mr
Ngoni Masoka.
The Reap Big Award promotion is linked to the FCC/ Zimbabwe Farmers'
Union joint venture cash discount card scheme aimed at providing
agricultural inputs at competitive prices to assist new farmers.
The winners were spread across the country.
Dr Made said because of the changes in the agriculture sector, there has
been a rise in demand for inputs.
"However, due to high inflation, agricultural inputs are beyond the
reach of many resulting in threats of reduced production and the food
security status of the country.
"To address this problem my ministry has stepped up the financial
resources available for the crops and livestock inputs loan scheme.
"Since December 2000 the Government has availed $30 billion, as a
revolving fund. The fund benefit farmers through soft loans for inputs such
as seeds, fertiliser, pesticides, irrigation equipment and tillage
services," said the minister.
Some of the enterprises supported under the scheme include cotton, beef,
dairy, poultry, piggery, maize, sorghum, wheat, millet, tobacco and soya
beans.
The chairman of Agri-business, Mr Walter Mzembi, said the joint venture
between the two organisations would eventually empower the farmers.
"Agriculture production is vital for the protection of the sovereignty
of any country because if you fail to produce enough for the country some
donors would come in through food aid and open up the country to
manipulation. This smart partnership is commendable," said Mr Mzembi.
The President of ZFU, Mr Silas Hungwe, pointed out that the partnership
with FCC was beneficial to both the farmers and the business community.
"We have enjoyed the partnership and we hope it would continue," said Mr
Hungwe.
Some of the companies that sponsored awards include Wind-mill, Crest,
Zimplow and National Tested Seeds with 10 prizes each.
Stock feeds manufacturing firm Agri Foods also sponsored some of the
awards together with Agventure, Vetco City Glass and Reckitt and
Benkiser.

Original_Search_Title: Horrors of LRA Child Captivity
Headline: Uganda: Horrors of LRA Child Captivity
Body_Text:
[This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations]
KITGUM, 24 April (IRIN) - Fifteen year-old Patrick Komakech lies on a
bed, grateful for his freedom and more importantly for his life.
He was brought to a rehabilitation centre in the northern Ugandan town
of Kitgum after fleeing his captors, the rebel Lord's Resistance Army (LRA),
two weeks after they abducted him from his school. He was among the lucky
ones. Others in his group who had tried to escape, were recaptured and
killed in front of their counterparts "as a lesson on what would happen to
us if we tried to run away".
Children are the main target of the 17-year LRA insurgency which has
terrorised civilians in northern Uganda. The group, led by Joseph Kony,
seeks to topple President Yoweri Museveni and replace his government with a
regime governed by the Biblical Ten Commandments.
The group routinely attacks Acholi villages, killing civilians and
abducting children to be forced into its ranks and girls to be used as sex
slaves for commanders.
GRUESOME TALES
Children who have managed to escape from LRA captivity tell horror
stories about the extreme physical and psychological violence with which
they were initiated into rebel ranks.
Peter Ochan, 14, was abducted, when he was asleep and made to walk the
whole night and the whole day, before he and other abducted children were
allowed to rest. During his 10 months in captivity, the clubbing of children
to death by rebel commanders became a daily ritual in the bush, he
says.
Ochan, now accommodated at the Gulu Support the Children Organisation
(GUSCO), a rehabilitation centre set up to help children returning from
captivity, explains how he and others were forced to carry the decomposing
body of a boy killed for trying to escape.
"We carried the body on our shoulders everywhere we went," he said. "It
smelt very bad. We were told it was part of our training. The commander then
told us to scoop out the brains from what was left of the body and show them
around to others."
Another escapee, Tony Ulanya, 16, says he has met LRA leader Joseph Kony
several times and describes him as a "possessed man".
"He would preach to us on Sundays for many hours," he told IRIN. "He
always told us that he spoke with the angel who told him that he would one
day be the president of Uganda, and the young people he has abducted and the
children born in captivity will take over the country. Sometimes he leaves
people and stays on his own for a long time."
FEAR OF ESCAPE
Opportunities to escape from captivity are extremely rare for many of
the abductees. Many do not dare to escape for fear of reprisals, either
towards them or their families.
Relishing his freedom at the Kitgum Concerned Women's Association
(KICWA), Patrick recounts some of his gruesome memories.
"Two boys tried to escape," he says. "They were beaten to death, with
sticks. A child whose feet were too swollen to walk any further was tied up
and left behind to die. He was left there and told to eat soil."
REHABILITATION
Those who run the rehabilitation centres for returning abductees, such
as GUSCO and KICWA, say most of the children who escape are often emaciated,
weak from disease, and severely traumatised.
"In the bush, good food, like chicken and goats was reserved for
commanders, while children only ate sorghum or wild fruits," Patrick
recalled. "In Sudan [where the LRA has its bases], we had to kill people,
steal their animals and take stored grains in order to survive."
The rehabilitation centres are however just a temporary respite for
returning abductees. Once their return to their families has been
facilitated, the children remain under constant risk of re-abduction, which
would mean certain execution by their captors.
Mads Oyen, the UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) child protection officer in
Uganda told IRIN that there had been at least 6,000 abductions since the
beginning of this year, translating to 12,000 a year.
"The abductions that have taken place since the period after June [2002]
are the highest levels of abductions ever [since the insurgency began],"
Oyen said.
Christopher Arwai, who heads the KICWA centre, however believes that
many more children have been abducted from northern Uganda throughout the
insurgency, many of whom may never be accounted for.
[See also Peace Process Crumbling in North at:
http://www.irinnews.org/report.asp?ReportID=33613 ]

Original_Search_Title: To Fight Hunger, Modernize Farming
Headline: Senegal: To Fight Hunger, Modernize Farming
Body_Text:
New York — For the first time ever in Senegal's history, tens of thousands of
farmers from across the country marched on the capital on 26 January to
voice their grievances and anger.
They converged on the largest stadium in Dakar to demand policies to
improve their harvests, boost their incomes, reduce rural poverty and lift
them from the status of "second-class citizens." By their action, they
shattered the common urban perception of Senegalese villagers as silent and
passive. "Farmers, more than ever before, are mobilizing to better their
conditions of life," declared Mr. Mamadou Sall, president of the largest
national farmers' group, the Conseil national de concertation et de
coopÃ©ration des ruraux (CNCR).
The organization presented government officials with a "Farmers'
Manifesto." It noted that agricultural productivity remains very low and
that most rural people live below the national poverty line.
"Agriculture has been in crisis since the end of the 1970s, and this has
led poverty and food insecurity to become generalized throughout the rural
areas," the manifesto said. Affirming the CNCR's own determination to change
such conditions, it also appealed for solidarity from all social groups in
Senegal and asked the government to open talks with farmers to develop
long-term solutions.
Addressing the rally, Minister of Agriculture Habib Sy promised to take
the farmers' concerns directly to President Abdoulaye Wade. He expressed the
government's openness to dialogue with farmers, and explained the measures
it already has initiated. Many farmers welcomed Mr. Sy's pledges, but also
wondered about the extent of the government's commitment.
Only a few months earlier had President Wade vowed to begin systematic,
long-term efforts to "modernize" agriculture, more than two years after his
government was elected into office. It took successive poor harvests and
reports of widespread rural hunger to finally bring the plight of villagers
to the centre of the policy debate.
That debate is just beginning, and easy solutions are not apparent.  The
challenge of transforming Senegal's low-yielding agriculture into a vibrant
productive sector that can raise rural living standards and improve food
security faces complex constraints. These include the grinding poverty of
the farmers themselves, insufficient government resources for rural
investment, limited access to water, environmental degradation, pressures
from external financial institutions for rapid liberalization and
unfavourable world market conditions for Senegalese farm exports.
A difficult legacy The problems facing Senegalese farmers are not
recent, and affect both food growers and producers of cash crops, above all
groundnuts. Production methods on the country's 440,000 farms have long been
very simple, as most farmers cultivate small plots with just hand tools and
almost no fertilizer or other inputs besides rain.
Although agriculture provides livelihoods for around 60 per cent of the
population and accounts for 18 per cent of gross domestic product, it
receives only 10 per cent of all public investments. For many decades ?
including the French colonial era ? the groundnut sector absorbed the
lion's share of public financing for agriculture. Since independence in
1960, almost all the government's training, subsidies and agricultural
extension services have been oriented to groundnut production.
Such support has not led to much improvement in rural living standards,
even in Senegal's main groundnut zones. Most villages lack running water or
electricity. In many areas the soil has eroded or become acidic. A
succession of droughts hit hard in the late 1970s and early 1980s, bringing
more environmental degradation and rural impoverishment.
By the mid-1980s the government began to adopt new economic policies at
the urging of the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and World Bank. These
eliminated some agricultural extension services and gradually ended
fertilizer subsidies. Too poor to buy fertilizer outright, many farmers took
out loans at the start of the planting season, but then were left deep in
debt when the rains failed or crop prices dropped unexpectedly.
Over the past five years, fertilizer use for groundnut cultivation has
declined further, from 45,500 tonnes in the 1997/98 season to just 25,000
tonnes in 2001/02. Yields have plummeted accordingly, and with them farmer
incomes. "For us, the sale of groundnuts is the only source of monetary
income," says Mr. Ali Mballo, a farmer.
According to the UN Development Programme's 2001 national "Human
Development Report" for Senegal, about 85 per cent of the rural population
lives below the poverty line. This is considerably higher than the rate of
60 per cent for the country as a whole.
Drought depletes grain stocks Discontent over such conditions led many
rural voters during the March 2000 presidential election to shift their
support from the old ruling party to the opposition coalition of Abdoulaye
Wade, which promised sweeping Sopi ("change" in the Wolof language). In the
new government's brief period in office, it has in fact introduced numerous
reforms, including efforts to combat corruption, improve the delivery of
essential social services and strengthen Senegal's democratic
system. President Wade has emerged as one of the main developers and
promoters of the continent's development strategy, the New Partnership for
Africa's Development.
Many of these changes have not been felt in Senegal's countryside,
however.
Making matters worse, the first years of this decade have been marked by
inadequate rainfall. This contributed to a steady decline in total national
cereal production, from more than 1.2 mn tonnes of millet, sorghum, rice and
maize in the 1999/2000 agricultural season to just 835,000 tonnes in
2002/03, according to Ministry of Agriculture projections released in
December (see graph).
For two years, many farming households were able to manage by using
their domestic grain stocks or selling off some livestock or other goods to
buy more food. But by 2002 the situation had worsened dramatically. Grain
sheds in many villages were virtually empty.  Unexpectedly heavy rains in
northern Senegal in January caused serious flooding, which destroyed crops
and killed several dozen people and more than 100,000 head of livestock. The
early rains for the 2002/03 growing season, expected in June, did not
actually begin to arrive until August in most of the main groundnut and
grain regions. With many farmers already producing at very low levels, these
calamities pushed major parts of the Senegalese countryside toward
near-famine conditions.
'Sweeping liberalization' Parallel to these difficulties, groundnut
farmers suddenly found themselves caught in a breakdown of established
marketing arrangements, precipitated by a policy shift.  The government of
President Wade had inherited economic agreements signed by its
predecessor. These included a commitment in 1995 to privatize the
state-owned groundnut enterprise, the SociÃ©tÃ© nationale de commercialisation
des olÃ©agineux du SÃ©nÃ©gal (Sonacos), although at the time no buyers could be
found and it remained in state hands.
Following the political change in Senegal, the IMF stepped up pressure
to move ahead with the liberalization process. But the new government
initially emphasized state action to boost farmers' incomes and stimulate
rural economic activity, in part by using Sonacos to aggressively promote
groundnut cultivation. During the 2000/01 season, Sonacos borrowed massively
in order to buy from farmers a record level of 600,000 tonnes of groundnuts
at a relatively favourable price. (Production that year reached a record
total of more than 1 mn tonnes, with the remainder of the crop bought by
informal groundnut merchants, called bana-bana.) This injected significant
financing into the countryside. But for Sonacos it proved
unsustainable. Because the price paid to groundnut farmers was well above
world market levels and a significant minority of farmers had trouble
repaying the loans they received from the enterprise to buy fertilizer and
other inputs, Sonacos was saddled with a sizeable debt.
A number of Senegal's donors reacted sharply. In April 2001, a European
Union official warned that failure by Sonacos to liberalize the distribution
of groundnut seeds could jeopardize EU aid to the country.
Then in July, IMF officials visiting Senegal demanded that the
government stop subsidizing groundnut marketing and bring prices closer to
world market levels. The following month, Sonacos agreed to cut the price it
paid to farmers from CFA145 to CFA120 per kilogramme and to sharply reduce
its direct purchases, moves that shocked many groundnut farmers.
In November of the same year, a cabinet meeting further agreed that the
government would privatize Sonacos during 2003. As a step in that direction,
it also simply dissolved the SociÃ©tÃ© nationale de graines (Sonagraines), a
wholly owned subsidiary of Sonacos which had played a major role in the
collection and transport of groundnuts from the farmers' fields to Sonacos's
processing plants (which transform the nuts into cooking oil). The
authorities expected that licenced private marketing agents, known as
opÃ©rateurs privÃ©s stockeurs (OPS), would step into the void.
The result was a disaster for groundnut farmers. Even with significant
financial advances from Sonacos, some of the nearly 500 licenced OPS could
not arrange transport to collect the groundnuts from farmers' fields.
Many did collect the nuts, but paid the farmers only in vouchers, not
cash.
Months later, many farmers still had not been paid. This meant that they
could not buy seeds or fertilizer for the next planting season, clear their
debts ? or pay for food or other household essentials.
As farmers in some parts of the country began to set up roadblocks
across national highways in protest, the CNCR and other farmers' groups
became increasingly militant. Many blamed their plight on the government's
policy shift. "One of the big problems facing agriculture is the sweeping
liberalization of our national economy," said Mr. Samba GuÃ¨ye,
secretary-general of the CNCR.
Some also noted the problem of low world market prices for groundnuts,
cotton and other Senegalese agricultural exports. Senegal holds second place
in the world groundnut market, behind the US. But, notes Mr.
Serigne Amadou Camara, in charge of managing the government's state
enterprises, "Our main competitor, the United States, subsidizes its
price. We don't have the means to fight that." Turning 'peasants' into
'farmers' In August 2002, the government finally began to respond. Taken
together, its initiatives point towards a continued pursuit of market
liberalization and promotion of the private sector, but with more emphasis
than before on state investment in rural infrastructure and public
programmes to safeguard farmers from the vagaries of the market and the
weather. In various statements, President Wade emphasized the need to
"modernize" Senegal's underdeveloped, low-input agriculture. The aim, he
said, is to transform Senegal's subsistence "peasants" into market-oriented
"farmers" capable of producing more for both domestic and external
markets.
President Wade and other officials acknowledged that Sonagraines was
liquidated too hastily, in response to external pressures. The decision,
however, could not now be reversed, they said, and the challenge is to
ensure that the new marketing system functions better. The president ordered
the judicial authorities to take legal action against "thieves and
swindlers" who took farmers' crops but failed to pay them, as Sonacos
tightened up its licensing criteria.
Among the other steps that President Wade announced, some were designed
to tackle immediate food shortages, while others have longer-term
development implications. These included: ? Significant short-term relief
assistance, financed directly by the government, to help victims of flooding
and drought, with distribution supervised by a high-level oversight
committee composed of farmers' leaders, media figures and political party
representatives.
? The establishment of local grain stocks, to speed emergency responses
to food shortages in Senegal's 11 regions.
? Greater emphasis on promoting agricultural diversification, to end the
"tyranny of mono-cropping," as President Wade termed it. At the national
level, there already have been important steps to stimulate more production
of cotton, sesame, soya, sunflowers, rice and other food and export crops,
so as to reduce the economy's dependence on groundnuts. In the fields,
production of more than one type of crop can give farmers greater
flexibility and better protect them from market and weather
fluctuations.
? The digging of 50 water retention basins to better preserve and
utilize rain, along with more irrigation schemes. Senegal has the potential
to irrigate 240,000 hectares in the Senegal River Basin, thanks to the Diama
and Manantali dam projects. So far, however, only 70,000 hectares are
irrigated (up from 36,000 three years ago).  Smaller-scale projects are also
under way elsewhere in the country, especially involving rice.
? The initiation of a cloud-seeding project designed to induce
additional rainfall. This would utilize Moroccan expertise, already
demonstrated by successful programmes in Morocco and Burkina Faso.
? Acceleration of plans to connect all of Senegal's villages to the
national electricity grid.
? The construction of new agricultural training schools and colleges
around the country.
At the end of August, President Wade toured most of Senegal's regions,
to explain his government's initiatives and hear directly from farmers and
livestock herders about their problems. Many complained about the new
groundnut marketing arrangement, the high cost of inputs, the poor quality
of health services and the absence of an effective food security
system.
At one rally in Saloum, Mr. Wade pledged that "we will never abandon the
countryside" and insisted that for his government, agriculture and
stockraising will be "two priority areas of development."  Elements for
success Many farmers reacted to the government's pledges with hope, but also
a measure of caution. They have heard high-level promises before. Many are
waiting to see what is included in the new agricultural law that the
government is drafting for discussion and approval by the legislature.
Nevertheless, the crisis in Senegal's villages and the authorities'
initial responses have already stimulated greater public debate about how
best to transform the countryside.
In the policy realm, President Wade has been arguing for a pragmatic mix
of market mechanisms and state support and regulation, an approach that
enjoys wide backing in Senegal, even if there are differences on the precise
nature of that combination. Mr. Wade frequently talks about the benefits of
markets and private initiative, but he also insists that in an inequitable
world market in which rich Northern countries subsidize their farmers to the
detriment of producers in the South, countries like Senegal should also be
permitted to subsidize agriculture.
At a minimum, many Senegalese argue, governments should be able to
invest in rural electrification, roads and other infrastructure essential to
improving agricultural productivity and rural living conditions.
"Everywhere in the world," says Mr. Mamadou Cissoko, a CNCR leader, "it
is public investments that create favourable conditions for agricultural
production and soil regeneration." In particular, the CNCR maintains, such
investments should be targeted towards small-scale family farmers, the vast
majority of the rural population.
There also is broad agreement on the need for more active involvement by
farmers, livestock herders, village artisans, women and rural people.
The CNCR, which has been especially energetic in voicing rural concerns,
is only one of the better-known national organizations.  There are literally
hundreds of agricultural cooperatives, village associations and producers'
groups around the country. The national cotton farmers' association has
acquired a 20 per cent ownership stake in the government's cotton marketing
enterprise, and the CNCR is hoping to acquire a share of Sonacos. "In the
new vision of agriculture," President Wade has said, "I intend to involve
everyone." With Senegal's limited water sources and fragile topsoil, talk
about intensifying agricultural production through greater use of fertilizer
and other inputs naturally raises concerns about environmental
sustainability.
Learning from past mistakes, especially in the irrigation projects in
the northern river valleys, there now is greater emphasis on careful
environmental assessments, use of organic fertilizers and technologies that
safeguard rather than harm Senegal's natural resources. President Wade is
promoting the notion of "ecological security," which combines environmental
management with poverty reduction and local empowerment, including
participatory mechanisms and the strengthening of land rights, especially
for rural women.
Such social considerations must be at the heart of any agricultural
policy, argues Prof. Moustapha KassÃ©, a prominent Senegalese development
expert.
In narrow economic terms, it might make sense to liquidate unprofitable
state enterprises and allow the groundnut sector to decline. "But what will
the farmers do?" he asks. How would the country prevent an increase in rural
poverty and even greater migration to the cities?
"It is impossible to transform agriculture without a fuller social
vision," Prof. KassÃ© concludes. "Today, it is vital to elaborate an
agricultural strategy that is global, coherent and focused."

Original_Search_Title: Siavonga Harvest Under Threat From Jumbos
Headline: Zambia: Siavonga Harvest Under Threat From Jumbos
Body_Text:
THE Catholic Commission for Peace and Justice (CCPJ) in Siavonga district has said people in the area will continue depending on relief food if elephants destroying fields are not cropped.
Lusitu CCPJ branch coordinator Robson Simweemba said people in the area worked hard to ensure that there was enough food for them but the harvest was under threat from the elephants.
Mr Simweemba said the elephants were now destroying crops which survived the recent floods that swept away maize, millet and sorghum early this year.
"Elephants in Lusitu area of Siavonga district are destroying crops. What this means is that if they are not cropped then even this year there will be hunger in the area," he said.
Mr Simweemba said the animals were not only threatening food harvest but also human life.
He said last month a young boy was almost crushed to death by an elephant when it found him scaring animals using an empty tin.
Mr Simweemba also said sorghum seeds which Care International gave to peasant farmers were not good for regions like Lusitu because they were the late maturing type.
The branch coordinator said people needed fast maturing crops because the area did not have a long period of rainfall to last up May.

Original_Search_Title: Diversify, Southerners Counselled
Headline: Zambia: Diversify, Southerners Counselled
Body_Text:
SOUTHERN Province Minister Chilufya Kazenene has said crop diversfication is one way the region will enhance food security.
Speaking when Care International staff paid a courtesy call on him in Livingstone, Mr Kazenene said maize was no longer doing well in the area because of persistent drought the province was experiencing.
Mr Kazenene said Government had introduced drought-resistant crops but because of late delivery of inputs last farming season, the crops did not do well.
He said the drought-resistant crops introduced by Government included cassava, cowpeas, finger millet and sorghum which survived on less rainfall than maize.
In the past, the province used to be the bread basket of the Zambia because of its rich soils, but that status had been changed by drought.
Mr Kazenene said Government had embarked on a programme of restocking cattle which had been wiped out by corridor disease.
He said he had met Southern Province members of Parliament to discuss how best they could implement the programme.
He commended Care International for its humanitarian activities in the province which had saved a lot of people from starvation through its relief food distribution exercise and appealed to other non-governmental organisations to emulate it.
Care International Drought Recovery Project manager Evans Mwengwe said his organisation had embarked on long-term measures that would alleviate the suffering of people in times of drought.
Mr Mwengwe said Care International had given out free drought-resistant crop seeds and fertiliser to farmers in the province last farming season.
He said more than 5,000 farmers had benefited from the scheme and hoped there would be more of such crops in the next farming season.
He pledged Care International's support to Government projects that would enhance food security even when there was less rainfall.

Original_Search_Title: Arda Targets Derelict A2 Farms
Headline: Zimbabwe: Arda Targets Derelict A2 Farms
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Agricultural and Rural Development Authority has reportedly asked for more agricultural prime land for its operations, targeting A2 farms that were not taken up.
Recently Arda turned over 5 000 hectares of land into a greenbelt under irrigation in the Lowveld and Zambezi Valley to beef up the country's maize seed stocks.
Although details were still sketchy with the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement and Arda currently working on the modalities, The Herald understands that the organisation wanted access to land that was not taken up under the A2 Model.
This comes at a time when the Government has expressed disappointment over the reluctant farm uptake by many successful applicants under commercial schemes namely A2 Model, peri-urban, and small-scale commercial farming.
No comment could be obtained from Arda chief executive Dr Joseph Matowanyika.
However, a senior Arda official indicated that the ministry was in the process of identifying suitable land, especially in the prime agricultural areas for maximum agricultural use by Arda.
"We have been utilising all the land available to us. We have the capacity to fully utilise commercial land lying idle.
"In terms of knowledge, we can fully utilise the land if the Government gives us more.
"As Arda we utilise our land throughout the country. Our culture is we produce throughout the year and we are optimistic that we will succeed," said the official who refused to be identified.
Commercial farming operations and marketing director for Arda Mr Raphael Zuze said the authority had planned "big things" for the winter crop programme.
Mr Zuze said Arda had dedicated 4 000 ha of land in the Lowveld to winter maize seed, 4 500 ha to winter wheat, 1 500 ha of sugar beans and 1 190 ha of sugarcane crop, in addition to 800 ha of land to winter wheat seed.
The move was aimed at boosting production of Zimbabwe's seed crops following a severe shortage of seed last season.
So far, Arda has put more than 600 ha of land under maize seed production, hence the need for land in some of the country's prime agricultural areas.
The authority has also put 550 ha of land under tea, 306 ha under coffee and an unspecified percentage of land under horticultural crops, cotton, small grains and soyabeans among other crops.
The move to alleviate the seed shortages in Zimbabwe was a diversion of its traditional farming operations, which were dominated by cotton production, especially in its Lowveld estates and soyabeans in the Highveld areas.
Mr Zuze said seed production has become the authority's major operation after entering into agreements with Seed Co, National Tested Seed, Pannar Seed, Agpy and Pioneer.
In addition to seed production Arda runs successful diary and beef operations with at least 2 768 dairy cows and 2 716 beef cattle respecti- vely.
The authority was also into ostrich production with 360 birds in some of its estates.
The farming operations director said Arda had dedicated 4 000 ha under maize, in addition to 654 ha for seed maize production, 1 500 ha of sorghum, 110 ha sorghum seed, cotton seed 500 ha, soyabeans 1 000 ha, commercial cotton 281 ha, tobacco 240 ha and potatoes 100 ha.
Mr Zuze added that the reasons for putting a small hectarage under maize seed production was that seed firms chipped in late in December when the programme was expected to have kicked off in August.
The winter crop programme, according to Arda, would kick off on April 21 and 28 for the winter wheat and maize in the Lowveld and Highveld areas respectively.
In other areas such as Chirundu however, clearing of bushes was currently being undertaken to extend the land for irrigation purposes.
Mr Zuze said Chirundu was suitable for maize production.
Officials from the National Parks and Wildlife Management Authority, Arda and Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement last Wednesday were assessing the irrigation site at Chirundu.
The irrigated land in Chisumbanje, Middle Sabi in the Lowveld and Muzarabani, Mushumbi Pools and Chirundu along the Zambezi River, is bound to salvage the country from perennial seed shortages, unemployment, hunger and poverty, effectively turning Zimbabwe into the breadbasket of Southern Africa.
If done on a large-scale, Zimbabwe could soon become a major producer of maize seed, wheat and other crops in the region.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Yield to Improve
Headline: Zimbabwe: Maize Yield to Improve
Body_Text:
Harare — ZIMBABWE will harvest close to 1,3 million tonnes, from the summer maize crop, leaving just one third of requirements to be met from imports or the winter harvest.
A long dry spell in the middle of the season reduced the harvest. But good rains at the end of the season produced a considerably higher harvest than predicted early this year, when only 500 000 to 1 million tonnes was expected.
According to preliminary estimates contained the Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWSNET) monthly report, Zimbabwe expects a maize harvest of 1,289 million tonnes. This is almost three times the size of last summer's harvest and not much smaller than the 2001 harvest of 1,54 million tonnes.
Maize deliveries have started trickling in at the Grain Marketing Board and in a few weeks white maize-meal should be available.
An earlier report of The Herald indicated that although the GMB was still working on the 2002-2003 maize crop harvest estimates, agricultural experts projected figures from as low as 500 000 tonnes to highs of more than a million tonnes.
Drought struck much of Southern Africa last season, forcing humanitarian agencies to appeal for food aid for more than 14 million people in the region.
Regional food security agencies last year said the Sadc region faced a maize shortfall of up to 3,67 million tonnes.
South Africa was the only country in the region with a surplus.
However, this surplus of 746 000 tonnes was not enough to cover the import needs of all Sadc countries.
Zimbabwe has since January 2002 committed US$207,9 million for the importation of 1,1 million tonnes of grain.
A total of 886 749 tonnes of grain were delivered into the country while another 245 738 tonnes already ordered are still to be delivered.
Imports came from South Africa, Kenya and Argentina. Zimbabweans eat between 1,8 million and 2,1 million tonnes of maize every year.
The Agricultural Research Extension (Arex) in its latest fortnightly report said the bulk of the late planted crop was at grain development to maturity stage.
Artificial drying of maize in most parts of the country was going on primarily for home consumption while in some areas like Hurungwe there were reports that farmers were already selling some of their grain on the informal market possibly to meet pressing family financial needs.
Arex said crop conditions were generally fair in most parts of the country save for Lupane and Hwange districts where there was a high crop failure.
Harvesting of the early-planted cotton crop was in progress in Manicaland, Lowveld, Mashonaland West and Nkayi but the bulk of the crop was at boll development to splitting stages in all reporting areas.
Reaping of the irrigated Virginia tobacco crop was almost complete in Manicaland and Mashonaland East and West provinces and curing was now taking place.
Reaping of the irrigated burley tobacco crop was now almost complete in Manicaland province while reaping and curing of the rain-fed crop is continuing in all areas.
Harvesting of small grains which include sorghum, mhunga and rapoko was in progress in some parts of the country but the bulk of the crops were now ready for harvest in Mashonaland East Province while they were at grain development stage in Manicaland and Mashonaland West provinces.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger Alert After Failure of Long Rains
Headline: Kenya: Hunger Alert After Failure of Long Rains
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans will be hit by a food shortage in the year, the government has
warned.
The production of food has plunged due to poor rains this season,
National Security minister Chris Murungaru revealed yesterday.
But he was quick to assure the public that no one would starve because
the government would bring out the 1.5 million bags of maize, beans,
sorghum, millet, among others, in the strategic food reserve.
The reserve, which is supposed to stockpile 3 million bags of various
foods has, however, a shortfall of 1.5 million bags.
The minister promised that the government would spend Sh260 million to
buy food to plug the gap.
"Farmers will have a direct market for their maize next harvest season,"
the minister added.
He was speaking in his office after he registered for the Freedom From
Hunger Walk slated for next month.
The anticipated drought and food shortage are bad news for the
economy. Depending on the severity and length of the drought, it could
destabilise government finances massively, posing serious macro-economic
consequences with serious implications for the success of the economic
recovery programme.
With the gap in the budget for this year expected to go beyond Sh69
billion, drought and food shortages might force the government to borrow
heavily in the next financial year, with major consequences for the level of
inflation, interest rates and the cost of doing business.
Droughts also spell doom for the power sector with knock-on effects for
other productive segments of the economy. In 2000, a drought brought down
the water levels in power generation dams, causing serious power shortages
and at a great cost to the economy.
The Meteorological Department has already predicted water, food and
pasture shortages accompanied by disease outbreaks in the next three months
because of poor rains.
North Eastern Province and parts of the Rift Valley are in the grip of
famine, which is expected to get worse by the end of the year.
Studies show that famine has a cyclical pattern, occurring every four to
six years in East Africa. Last month, the Drought Monitoring Centre-Nairobi
(DMCN) warned that the Greater Horn of Africa sub-region would experience
severe drought during the long rains.
Meteorologists fear that this year would be like 1995, when the long
rains were extremely poor.
Yesterday, Dr Murungaru said the government was watching the situation
very carefully and would soon appeal for relief food from donor
countries.
He appealed to Kenyans to help one another during famine.

Original_Search_Title: HIV Devastates Output
Headline: Uganda: HIV Devastates Output
Body_Text:
Kampala — When AIDS enters a family, famine finds a fertile ground, a new survey has revealed.
The survey found out that families affected by AIDS produce less food because the disease lowers their ability to dig.
The Food and Agriculture Organisation of the United Nations, and the National agricultural Advisory services commissioned the research that was undertaken by Dr. Chris Laker, and Agnes Ssekiboobo of Makerere University Institute of Social Research. They covered fishing, cattle-keeping and mixed farming communities in Masaka, Iganga and Luweero.
Affected families reduce cultivation of crops that require much labour, and instead resort to crops like pumpkins and sorghum that can be left to grow without much tendering.
This affects the amount and variety of their consumption, and reducing their income. On the other hand, unaffected families are gradually shifting from food crops to cash crops to widen their income bases, but maintaining a percentage of food crops for home use.
Resulting from weakness, and lack of time, the affected families are not able to prepare their land properly as the activity is left to women and children who are unable to dig the soil to required depths.
The researchers, while delivering the findings of their study at a stakeholders' workshop recently, said all the three sub-sectors in agriculture had suffered tremendous effects of the disease.
In the fishing communities, fish catch is sharply reduced because fishermen resorted to shallow-water fishing instead of going into deep waters. They avoid fishing at night, when bigger fish are caught.
The women abandoned labour-intensive fish preservation methods such as salting and smoking, which give a higher market price. They resorted to sun-drying, which leads to lower market prices and more spoilage. Widow and orphaned households were most affected, while widower headed households were also affected though slightly because the men had to take up feminine roles. In pastoralist communities, food insecurity was worse as access to foodstuffs is depended on purchasing power, yet the quality of herds was deteriorating due to poor management by affected families. "The families were resorting to nearby grazing grounds which have poorer quality pastures, because of inability to take their animals to far grazing grounds.
This resulted in less production of milk, and increased animal diseases due to congestion of the animal herds, yet there are limited resources for treating them," the research says.
NAADS Executive Director, Dr. Silim Nahdy said, "The effects of HIV/AIDS on agriculture had for long been ignored, but this research has revealed a lot and we hope the information will help us find a solution to avert the trend."
He said HIV/AIDS strategies and policies had been neglected.

Original_Search_Title: Grass: the Fibre of Life
Headline: Zambia: Grass: the Fibre of Life
Body_Text:
From now until mid July, most of the grasslands will remain deep
green.
Immediately after the rains, the wild pastures are so health that a
single acre can produce tonnes of protein rich fodder.
Towards the end of the cold season, the grass will begin to revert to
yellow stubble, hence nature demands that some of the herbivores migrate to
allow the grasslands to rest and replenish.
Others that do not migrate move into the open woodlands that have
islands of grass. It is also a time when the plains are set on fire.
Although fire is usually considered destructive, it is necessary for the
savannah. Fires breakout in the dry season, this encourages new grass to
grow and thus maintaining the savannah.
It is advisable, however, to carry out early rather than late burning,
as this is less destructive.
The savannahs sustain a large variety of game, both large and
small. Some of these are elephant, giraffe, buffalo, zebra and numerous
antelope as well as the tiny grass mouse.
The grazers are mainly antelope, wildebeest, hartebeest, oribi, impala,
gazelle, reedbuck, roan and sable antelope.
In this category there are also buffaloes and zebras represented in good
numbers.
Wildebeests are the main players in most of the grasslands in East and
Southern Africa. When feeding, they always mutter and grunt.
The zebras are a common feature on the plains, they are not ruminant, so
they are able to handle tougher grass. With their sharp cutting teeth, they
can cope with the tough fibrous stems of tall grass.
Buffaloes on the other hand can handle tough grass to an extent. They
are bulk feeders and to help them with the digestion, they drink a lot of
water daily.
It is the savannahs that draw hordes of tourists to view the treasures
that are present in the form of hoofed mammals and their attendant
predators.
There are always the ravenous hunters; the ones that keep a check on the
numbers of herbivores.
Most tourists are actually keen on seeing the predators than the
herbivores.
Among these are packs of wildogs, the slender bodied and fleet footed
cheetahs, that run down their prey with devastating strides.
Leopards, almost invisible in the dappled shade of trees, watch and wait
for the unwary prey to approach the woodland belt.
Also present are lions which are kings of their grassy world; the
plentiful spotted hyenas which keep tabs on the weak, sick, crippled and
young.
Whenever an opportunity avails itself, be sure the hyenas will be
there. These predators determine who would live and who would die.
In general grasslands are the central regions of several continents. On
the edges of these grasslands, they mix with forests.
In addition, scattered clumps of trees often grow in grasslands where
there is enough water.
Tropical grasslands are known as Savannahs which grow in warm lands
nearest the Equator.
Savannah differs from the temperate grasslands, which grow in cooler
parts of the world and are known by several names.
They differ in the length and kinds of grass, which in turn vary with
the amount of rainfall and soil types.
The temperate grasslands of North America are called the Prairies. These
once hosted and boosted millions of bison, a large North American mammal of
the buffalo tribe.
There are numerous species of grass and man uses some of these for
food. Important grass foods for man are maize (corn if your are American),
rice, wheat, sugar cane, millet and sorghum.
Bamboos, the largest species of grass are heavily utilised in building
and furniture making while other wild species of grass are used for
thatching.
It is for this reason that grass must be managed and utilised wisely as
we all depend on it.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Deliveries Start
Headline: Zimbabwe: Maize Deliveries Start
Body_Text:
Harare — MAIZE deliveries from this year's harvest have started trickling in at Grain Marketing Board depots countrywide with a total of 2 200 tonnes having been delivered so far since the marketing season opened on April 1.
This has raised hopes that people might soon start getting white maize meal from the shops, without having to queue.
GMB acting chief executive Colonel Samuel Muvuti told The Herald in an interview that the GMB had received 1 607 tonnes at Nandi in Chiredzi, 299 tonnes in Middle Sabi and 94 tonnes in Chipinge.
"Deliveries are now coming in," he said.
"We hope deliveries will continue to increase as the marketing season progresses."
The late rains received between January and March have brightened prospects of a good harvest.
If the deliveries continue to improve, they would be a relief to many who have not known white maize meal for months as they have been relying on the much disliked "kenya" yellow maize meal which was being imported.
Col Muvuti hailed the Government for announcing the new maize producer price saying this would help farmers plan ahead.
The new producer price for maize and other small grains like sorghum and millet was set at $130 000 a tonne and that for wheat at $150 000 a tonne.
This new price, Col Muvuti said, would encourage farmers in natural regions four and five to plant small grain crops on a large scale.
"Farmers in dry regions should be aware that these crops can earn them more money because they are on demand in countries like Botswana and South Africa," he said.
Col Muvuti could not be drawn to disclose this year's projected maize output, but agricultural experts are giving conservative figures from as low as 500 000 tonnes to highs of more than 1 million tonnes.
"We are still working on the estimates with all stakeholders involved," he said cautiously.
"We don't have a maize forecast for this season as yet."
GMB is working on measures to ensure all maize grown this season would be accounted for.
Col Muvuti said GMB was now prepared to pay farmers timeously while the new prices were expected to be an incentive to farmers to sell as much maize as possible at its depots.
He said the GMB was setting up more collection points to reduce distance and transport costs for farmers.
The GMB, he said, had approached the Ministry of Energy and Power Development to enable it to get preferential treatment for fuel supplies.
This would enable the body to move grain quickly to depots.
Col Muvuti urged farmers to approach their nearest depots so that they could be assisted to procure empty bags.
He warned that side marketing would not be tolerated at all and that those caught trading in maize illegally would face the full wrath of the law.
According to Statutory Instrument 235A of 2001, only the GMB is allowed to trade in maize and wheat.
"These are controlled products," he said.
"Those found flouting these regulations will be dealt with severely. GMB and law enforcement agents are on high alert to make sure these regulations are adhered to."
The GMB was also tightening loopholes to prevent abuse by unscrupulous dealers intending to buy maize at $9 600 a tonne from its depots and re-selling it to the board at the new rate of $130 000 a tonne.
"Measures have since been put in place to make sure that this does not happen," he said.
"Anybody caught trying this will be punished severely. Its tantamount to sabotage."
GMB also plans to set a cut off date to stop selling maize to the public in areas where there are reasonable harvests.
These include Mvurwi, Chiweshe and some parts of Mashonaland East, West and Central and Manicaland provinces.
The Grain Marketing Board has already started rolling out winter farming inputs such as wheat and maize seed, fertilisers and chemicals.
"We are targeting to put 100 000 hectares under wheat and another 100 000 under maize irrigation this winter," Col Muvuti said.
"Zimbabwe has no reason to import wheat at all if we put our act together."
Zimbabwe consumes about 400 000 tonnes of wheat a year and Col Muvuti said if wheat was to be grown on 100 000 hectares, a harvest of between 500 000 and 700 000 tonnes could be realised, leaving a surplus for export.
He said the GMB was importing inputs from South Africa to meet the needs for inputs for the winter farming season.
However, he expressed concern that local seed and fertiliser companies were not doing enough to help farmers prepare for this year's winter cropping.
Drought struck much of southern Africa last season forcing humanitarian relief agencies to appeal for food aid for more than 14 million people in the region.
Despite propaganda from the United States government that the Zimbabwean authorities had failed to guarantee food security for its citizens, the Government has since January 2002 committed US$207,9 million for the import of 1,1 million tonnes of grain.
A total of 886 749 tonnes of grain were delivered into the country since last year while a balance of 245 738 tonnes is still to be delivered.
Col Muvuti said maize imports would continue as the GMB wanted to build up the country's strategic grain reserve, which had dwindled as a result of the drought.
Zimbabwe, which is traditionally self-sufficient and an exporter of surplus food, had in the past not needed international food aid.
Last year's drought hampered maize production and forced the country to import grain from South Africa, Kenya and Argentina.
In 2001, the country's maize production dropped to 1,54 million tonnes from 2,1 million tonnes in 2000.
Zimbabwe consumes between 1,8 million and 2 million tonnes of maize a year.

Original_Search_Title: The Day Lusitu Residents Received a Harmer Mill
Headline: Zambia: The Day Lusitu Residents Received a Harmer Mill
Body_Text:
Hunger has taken root in Lusitu area of Siavonga district.
Like in many parts of Southern Province, people are now feeding on
sorghum as relief food donated by the Government has since run out.
Even so, the sorghum meal is not prepared at regular intervals --
sometimes once a day. It is the issue of rationing, or is it economising?
The problem has, in fact, been worsened by the presence of elephants
from neighbouring Zimbabwe which are uprooting crops from the fields.
The beasts have become Lusitu residents' enemy number one because the
animals are frustrating people's efforts to grow food and stop depending on
the Government for food handouts year in year out.
As if that is not enough, the residents have been walking long distances
before getting to the nearest harmer mill. At times, up to four
kilometres.
Thus most of them have resorted to grinding the sorghum on the rocks
before preparing nshima out of the sorghum mealie meal.
But such woes are likely to end soon, thanks to the Zambia Shall Be
Saved Foundation.
The Nevers Mumba-run foundation last week rescued the residents from the
agony of covering long distances, at times on empty bellies, to get to the
nearest harmer mill point.
The foundation donated the diesel-run harmer mill worth K15 million to
Chipepo residents in the same area.
Presenting the machine, Dr Mumba, who is also opposition National
Citizens Coalition (NCC) president, said the gesture was intended to
mitigate the problems of the people in the area.
Though the foundation could not provide relief food, he felt the machine
was a long-term investment that would continue serving the people for many
years to come.
"As a foundation, we help provide long range solutions to the challenges
you are currently facing in Southern Province," Dr Mumba told a gathering of
up to 50 people.
"There is no pain that we cannot endure, and there is no enemy we cannot
defeat.
"Therefore, hold together during this hard time and bury your political
and tribal differences to deal with the enemy of hunger. And we shall
overcome, soon and very soon."
Since the beginning of this year, the foundation has distributed three
harmer mills in Southern Province, but Dr Mumba said they were working on a
programme to distribute six more before the end of the year.
