He said the total cost and expense of the 10 harmer mills in the
programme stood at approximation of K90 million.
Community Development and Social Welfare Deputy Minister Catherine
Namugala thanked the foundation for helping the poor citizens of the
country.
Government was appreciative of any effort aimed at saving lives.
"As Government, we are very grateful for the gesture. We shall now work
on a programme to ensure hunger is averted in Lusitu," Ms Namugala
said.
She said among the measures Government was working out were to encourage
more women to go into skills training, provide more harmer mills and help
build a strong partnership between churches and non-governmental
organisations (NGOs) in the area.
The deputy minister urged the residents to guard the property jealously
and ensure smooth operations.
Headman Moba (Amos Siabungu) called on the Government to send more game
rangers to the area to prevent further deterioration of the crop in their
fields.
He said unless urgent measures were taken to prevent the elephants from
destroying crops in their fields, hunger would continue prevailing in
Lusitu.
"Many families are affected by these 15 elephants from
Zimbabwe. Government should help us with a game ranger to protect us from
these animals," he said.
A visit to some fields later found some residents re-planting their
crops which had been grazed by the wild animals.
Many of them, however, said they found it unwise to re-plant the crops
when they were not sure of its security.
Ms Namugala assured the residents that she would take the issue to the
minister of Tourism for further action.
And so, the residents are still waiting for any effort that will help
prevent hunger from spilling into the next season.

Original_Search_Title: Many Kicks At Witblits Festival
Headline: South Africa: Many Kicks At Witblits Festival
Body_Text:
Philippolis, Free State — Eating krummelpap and kaiings on the lawn of Philippolis' town
square is but one of many small joys afforded by the annual
Witblits Festival, held this past weekend in the small Karoo town.
The traditional crumbly maizemeal dish, eaten with fried-out
pieces of fatty mutton meat, tastes of the small veld bushes on
which the district's sheep flocks are raised.
Across the street the town's historic Dutch Reformed church
towers with its impressive clock tower. The dazzlingly white
building replaced the church built by the London Missionary Society
in the early nineteenth century to convert the local San population
to Christianity.
In front of the church, pupils from the local high school sells
umqombhoti by the "scale" for their school fund. One "scale", a
generous helping served in blue plastic pails, costs R1.
Saleswoman Happiness Paliso reckons one would need around three
"scales" of the African sorghum beer for sufficient "kick" (any
previous witblits consumption not taken into account).
The yeasty aroma of farm breads baking in clay ovens in the
museum yard blends with the fumes of witblits being distilled in
another corner.
"A nippy, please," asks a headclothed woman at the witblits
tasting counter on the museum stoep, producing a R10 note from
under her blouse.
The local pawn shop in the main street advertises several
"Witblitz Specials" - from garden chairs to a curious plastic
container on short stilts.
A poster describes it as a "makoekoe" (corrugated shack) bath.
Suddenly a smoky blast rings out from the rocky outcrop above
the town square. It is from one of two ancient cannons, after
almost two centuries still "guarding" the town from their elevated
posts.
The cannoneer is Free State historian Nico Fourie, who taught
himself from old books on weaponry how to fire the big guns with
black-powder loads.
They are among only a handful of authentic muzzle-loading
cannons still known to be actively used worldwide, says Fourie.
The two Philippolis cannons were among six ordered around 1860
by legendary Griqua chieftain Adam Kok II from their British
manufacturer. They were to serve as artillery against hostile
tribes on the Kok company's trek from Philippolis to Griqualand
East (Kokstad).
Kok left his Philippolis stronghold in 1862 after selling what
is most of the southern Free State today, to the then Free State
republican government.
Fourie suspects the cannons arrived too late to be taken on the
trek. They were later found in town in a storehouse of the Port
Elizabeth agent through whom Kok ordered them.
When Fourie first became interested in restoring and firing
them, he found them in a spotless condition, purportedly never
used.
Fourie now fires them as part of the yearly Witblits
festivities.
Oom Jan Witsokkies, last inhabitant of the dilapidated "ghost
town" Waterkloof, just south of Philippolis, gets his festivity
kicks from a different source. He bypasses the witblits counter at
the museum to buy a bottle of consolation at the local coop before
moving on homewards./NP

Original_Search_Title: President Launches Scheme to Assist Tobacco Farmers
Headline: Zimbabwe: President Launches Scheme to Assist Tobacco Farmers
Body_Text:
Harare — President Mugabe yesterday launched the Tobacco Growers Trust
Mechanisation programme which will enable tobacco farmers to acquire
equipment and other inputs at affordable prices.
The programme is targeted at tobacco farmers who are in dire need of
machinery and have the potential to increase the production of tobacco and
other crops if provided with the requisite machinery.
Tobacco farmers needed equipment to produce about 400 million
kilogrammes within the next three seasons, according to Tobacco Growers
Trust chairman Mr Wilfanos Mashingaidze.
The expansion of the tobacco output would enable the country to earn
more than US$1 billion per year in foreign currency. Tobacco is the
country's chief foreign currency earner.
The programme was born out of a realisation that the major limitation to
the growing of quality tobacco was lack of adequate machinery for land
preparation, crop production, transportation, curing and irrigation
equipment.
"This was more critical in the developing sector of the producers who
are the communal farmers, resettled farmers, including the A1 and the A2
model farmers as well as the indigenous commercial farmers," said Mr
Mashingaidze.
Through the mechanisation programme, viability of the tobacco crop would
be enhanced.
Speaking at the launch of the programme at Kutsaga Research Station in
Harare, President Mugabe said the Government requires complementary support
from the private sector in order to enable full exploitation by the farmers
of the opportunities arising from the agrarian reforms.
He said the effort by the trust was a worthwhile endevour that
complements the Government's own mechanisation programme being implemented
through the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement.
"I cannot over-emphasise the importance of mechanisation in our agrarian
reforms which are taking place in the age of modern agriculture," he
said.
"Mechanisation can, indeed, be described as the backbone of agriculture,
for it ensures that farmers make optimal use of their land, through
increased production, the diversification in high value crops and the
consequent yielding of improved household incomes, while significantly
enhancing household, national and regional food security."
He commended the launch of the programme saying it came at a time the
Government had successfully completed the first part of the fast-track land
reform exercise. The focus was now on consolidating the gains made so
far.
"With the larger part of the land now in the hands of our own people, we
are reminded often that the land will not yield anything if we just sit on
it. In order to realise the desired economic empowerment of indigenous
Zimbabweans and thus give substance to our independence, we have to work the
land, fully aware that Government is committed to providing a conducive
environment for farmers," the President said.
He said the announcement by the Government of new producer and
pre-planting prices for maize, sorghum, millet and wheat was meant to
cushion farmers and make them realise real dividends from their labour.
The Government has since 2000 assisted farmers through the
implementation of the Crops and Livestock Inputs Loan Scheme.
Through this programme, the Government availed $30 billion as a
revolving fund, which is lent to farmers as soft loans, for inputs such as
seed, fertilisers, pesticides, irrigation equipment and tillage
services.
Enterprises that benefited under the programme include those dealing
with beef, maize, sorghum, wheat, millet, groundnuts, soyabeans, tobacco and
cotton.
"In addition, cognisant of the role that tobacco plays in earning
foreign currency and the need to augment that role in the context of the
on-going reforms, my Government has, through the Reserve Bank, introduced a
20 percent foreign currency retention scheme emanating from the sale of
tobacco," said Cde Mugabe.
The foreign currency retention scheme, the President said, was aimed at
providing foreign currency to tobacco farmers for the importation of
relevant inputs, which can ensure enhanced tobacco production.
The President appreciated the fact that the Tobacco Growers' Trust was
formed to manage the scheme and that through the 20 percent retention
scheme, the trust managed to procure an initial 200 tractors at a cost of
US$4,6 million.
The scheme was designed to assist and encourage tobacco farmers.
Under the scheme, farmers would be able to acquire equipment at a
reduced price. A 60 horsepower tractor, which costs about $20 million at the
domestic market, will be acquired at just $1,5 million.
It is envisaged that the acquiring of new equipment would enable farmers
to increase the tobacco output and boost farmers' returns on investment
capital.
"I would like to urge the Tobacco Growers' Trust to continue managing
the revolving fund on behalf of the tobacco farmers in a transparent manner
as this effort must not go to waste. We want to revive and grow our economy,
and the farmers' contribution is greatly appreciated," President Mugabe
said.
He urged other players in the tobacco industry to emulate the trust and
come up with initiatives equally promotive of increased production in other
enterprises and encouraged teamwork to ensure that the land and agrarian
reforms are a success and become a significant contributor to the growth of
the economy.
TGT is an umbrella board representing all the six registered tobacco
growers' associations.
It was created in May of 2001 by the Government through the Minister of
Finance and Economic Development and is administered by the Reserve Bank of
Zimbabwe.
The purpose of the trust is to manage the pooled 20 percent foreign
currency from tobacco sales income.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Corp Launches Beverages Subsidiary
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Corp Launches Beverages Subsidiary
Body_Text:
Harare — ZIMBABWE Stock Exchange-listed Delta Corporation Limited has integrated its beverage manufacturing divisions under a subsidiary to be known as Delta Beverages, company officials said yesterday.
The move is part of Delta's unbundling process, which started three years ago and has already lead to the disposal of Zimbabwe Sun Limited, Pelhams Limited and OK Zimbabwe Limited.
The unbundling process will enable Delta to concentrate on its core beverage business.
Under the arrangement announced yesterday, United Bottlers, National Breweries and Chibuku Breweries will no longer retain their names and logos but will now be re-branded under the Delta Beverages brand.
Delta Beverages will run a manufacturing division involved in the production of lager, sorghum beer and carbonated soft drinks.
The subsidiary will also have a 100 percent shareholding in its operations division, comprising sales and distribution as well as transport.
Delta Corporation, which has a 28.8 percent interest in African Distillers Limited, will continue to hold 100 percent shareholding in malt producer Kwekwe Malting, 51 percent in Food and Industrial Processors (Pvt) Limited and 51 percent in Megapak (Pvt) Limited, through Delta Beverages.
"We have re-engineered Delta Corporation to provide a synchronised efficiency in its systems, packaging, delivery and training," the group's chief executive, Joe Mutizwa said.
"This integration will help ensure the survival of Delta in an environment which has become increasingly challenging," he added.
Following the restructuring, Delta's customers will be able to procure beverages from a single distribution centre.
Analysts said the consolidation of Delta operations would create a highly focused conglomerate, enhancing shareholder value.
The analysts said the move was likely to increase efficiency and reduce Delta's overhead costs.
They however pointed out that the group might need to increase its distribution centres countrywide to avoid congestion and delays, something Mutizwa said the company was already undertaking.
"I think we are likely to see more efficiency, reduction in costs and the company's income will increase, which will enhance shareholder value," an analyst with Kingdom Stockbrokers said.

Original_Search_Title: ICFU Embarks On Training Programme
Headline: Zimbabwe: ICFU Embarks On Training Programme
Body_Text:
Harare — The Indigenous Commercial Farmers Union has embarked on a major training
programme to help farmers across the country to grow maize, sorghum and
burley.
ICFU president, Mr Davison Mugabe, said the training programme was
funded by Delta, which availed $2 million to the organisation towards the
end of last year.
The Zimbabwe Farmers Union, ICFU's sister organisation, also received a
similar amount from Delta.
"We have already completed the training of the trainers and the next
phase is that of training the farmers and we hope that it would start very
soon.
"The programme to train the trainers started in February this year and
we were mainly looking at special crops such as burley, sorghum and maize,"
said Mr Mugabe.
He added that although they were looking at the specific crops, farmers
would still be able to use their experience on growing other crops.
"You will find that one who is taught to grow burley can use the same
training to grow wheat. Therefore, the training programmes will be
beneficial in several respects to the farmers.
"Because of the nature of the training programme, we want to make sure
that the entire country is covered," he added.
The training programme would also benefit farmers who intend to plant
crops for the coming winter crop season.
There is an urgent need for the farmers across the country to grow as
much grain as possible to boost the country's reserves which have been
depleted because of low rainfall in the last two seasons.
Mr Mugabe added that they wanted to complement the financial support
they received from Government.
"We appreciate the support we have been offered by the Government and
anticipate that more farmers will be able to plant more crops this season
particularly the winter crop," he added.
The farmers received a boost last week when Government announced new
producer prices for wheat and maize.
From the start of the new marketing season, the country's farmers will
benefit from a significant increase in the price of wheat, maize and other
grains.
The producer price of maize, sorghum and millet will rise to Z$130 000
(US$157) per tonne from Z$28 000 (US$35) and the pre-planting price of wheat
has been increased to Z$150 000 (US$182) from Z$70 000 (US$85).
The pre-planting price, is a signal of the Government's commitment to
ensuring viability in wheat production.
It is anticipated that farmers are likely to seriously engage in wheat
production this year.

Original_Search_Title: New Skirmish in Beer War
Headline: Southern Africa: New Skirmish in Beer War
Body_Text:
A long-standing war of words between Namibia Breweries Limited (NBL) and
SABMiller has intensified, with the Namibian brewery claiming their South
African counterpart's intended launch of a 'clear sorghum beer' represents
unfair competition.
A consignment of Copper Crest heading to Namibia was impounded by
Namibian customs authorities last month for unknown reasons, according to
South African media, possibly for not paying customs duties.
Director of Customs in Namibia's Finance Ministry, GL Kabozu, as well as
the Permanent Secretary in the Ministry of Trade and Industry, Andrew
Ndishishi, could not be reached for comment.
SAB says its Copper Crest sorghum beer should be charged import duties
at the same rate as traditional sorghum beers, but NBL feels it should be
taxed at the higher rate associated with Western beers.
Standard beer is taxed approximately 20 times higher than traditional
sorghum beer, according to NBL.
"SABMiller launched Copper Crest in the Namibian domestic market to try
and abuse a loophole in the Customs and Excise definition of sorghum beer
...
Despite SABMiller's effort to associate Copper Crest with traditional
sorghum beer, it should for all practical purposes, be defined as a clear
beer," said NBL in a press release.
"Copper Crest is a sorghum product containing 70 per cent sorghum.
The customs authorities have formally classified it a sorghum product
under the current excise classification," said Peter McLoughlin, Marketing
Director of SAB, in an official reply yesterday.
He added that SAB was within its rights to respond to the impounding of
Copper Crest with a legal challenge, but would prefer an amicable solution,
and would pursue a non-confrontational course for now.
NBL claimed the Copper Crest importation was one of many "strong-arm
tactics" that SABMiller was employing to eliminate it from the market.
These, it said, included "dumping" beer on the Namibian market by
selling it at below-cost prices and manipulating the retail market.
SAB said this was not so.
"Namibia Breweries continues to make unfounded allegations on dumping
which have absolutely no legal basis.
Castle Brewing in Namibia will from time to time offer price
promotions.
This is accepted competitive practice," said McLoughlin.
The latest series of statements is reminiscent of earlier battles when
NBL successfully lobbied Government in the 1990s to prevent SAB from
establishing a brewery in Namibia.
A SABMiller spokesperson told South Africa's Financial Mail that failure
to launch Copper Crest could well condemn it to becoming SAB's "most
expensive beer" as the group spent more than R20 million on installing the
sorghum process at its Durban brewery.
McLoughlin said Copper Crest had been developed for a specific type of
consumer need that existed in many African markets, including Namibia.
He said South African liquor laws precluded SAB from launching a sorghum
beer in South Africa, but not in Namibia.
NBL quoted industry analysts as saying NBL has been chipping away at
SAB's South African market share, which is said to have dropped from about
98 per cent in the late 1990s to closer to 95 per cent now.
SAB has denied this, quoting the Nielson statistics firm, which said in
November 2002 SAB controlled 97,8 per cent of the market and NBL 1,7 per
cent.
In February 2000, NBL said its biggest challenge in the new millennium
was to break SAB's dominance in the South African market.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Begin Reaping Good Rewards
Headline: South Africa: Farmers Begin Reaping Good Rewards
Body_Text:
Cape Town — Income received by farmers continues to increase every year, and gross income from all agricultural products last year rose by 29% to at least R68bn, says Agriculture and Land Affairs Minister Thoko Didiza.
During debate on her budget votes in the National Assembly yesterday, Didiza said field crops contributed 38%, animal products 37%, and horticultural products 25%.
This was as a result of marked increases in producer prices of almost all primary agricultural products, in particular that of maize, flower seed, sorghum, wool and vegetables. Also, the production volumes of maize, grain sorghum and sunflower seed also increased.
Didiza said another "exciting development" was the increase in net farm income and cash flow of farmers, which was resulting in the improvement of the debt position of SA farmers.
"Indications at this very hour are that this has resulted in a R2,6 bn decrease in farm debt."
With regard to SA's land reform programme, the minister said it was important for the stability of the country that there be co-operation between government and commercial farmers rather than apportioning blame or attempting to score "easy victories".
She said the intention was not to reverse apartheid "today".
But, she said, it was important to keep in mind that land was taken away from many black South African's without compensation.
Turning her attention to the high food prices, Didiza said the food price monitoring committee she had appointed would recommend "specific actions for government to take in the case of identified uncompetitive behaviour within the supply chain".
She said: "In addition, they are expected to ensure also that the benefits of lower prices as a result of our stronger rand are filtered through to the consumer.
"I believe that through this form of monitoring and analysis, we are also trying to encourage and nurture a situation that is more predictable and sustainable for government and consumer and that would enable us to be more proactive."
With regard to the implementation of the agricultural sector plan, she said her department, the National African Farmers Union, and Agri-SA had established an institutional framework to oversee the process of implementing the plan.
Botha in turn challenged Didiza on her statements after a visit to Zimbabwe earlier this year when she was quoted as saying that things in Zimbabwe were looking promising.
"This must surely be one of the most monstrous miss-statements of reality that one can possibly imagine. How the minister who normally displays rationality could bring herself to say such a thing as well as give material support to that disaster, defies belief.
"The only promise that the citizens of Zimbabwe can be certain of is poverty and the most monstrous human rights violations caused by a land destruction programme that has no element of reform whatsoever in it," Botha said.
Botha said that if it was still the government objective to acquire 25-million hectare for black farmers by 2015 "It is therefore quite clear that either budgetary provision for land reform must be dramatically increased or the target date for land reform must be drastically amended."
During her agriculture budget vote Didiza said that the food price monitoring committee which she appointed would recommend "specific actions for government to take in the case of identified uncompetitive behaviour within the supply chain".
"In addition, they are expected to ensure also that the benefits of lower prices as a result of our stronger rand are filtered through to the consumer. I believe that through this form of monitoring and analysis, we are also trying to encourage and nurture a situation that is more predictable and sustainable for government and consumer alike and that would enable us to be more proactive."

Original_Search_Title: Struggling Awethu Breweries Goes After Meatier Market
Headline: South Africa: Struggling Awethu Breweries Goes After Meatier Market
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — AWETHU Breweries, the secondlargest sorghum beer and mageu brewer in SA, plans to shift its focus to the meat industry.
The company has been hard hit by small-scale brewers and illegal home brewers, industry sources say.
Awethu, which has reported a drop in turnover from R8,527m to R821604 for the interim period to December 31, said a bad-debt provision had cost it R1,42m.
The company reported a loss in earnings a share of 2,39c from 0,93c a share. It also reported a net loss of R2m compared to its previous net profit of R786708.
Awethu MD Tony Ford said the directors were in talks to finalise payment from the sale of the Ventersdorp mill and were in litigation to collect an amount owed by RMD Financial Services.
The directors had continued the policy of renting out the Carletonville and Marizaan breweries as well as the Ventersdorp mill.
"Unfortunately, the guarantees for the sale of the Ventersdorp mill have not been met," said Ford. "As soon as these two debtors can be collected, we are sure Awethu will be in a great position to invest in new profitable businesses."

Original_Search_Title: Despite Food Aid And Rains, Drought Persists in the Horn
Headline: East Africa: Despite Food Aid And Rains, Drought Persists in the Horn
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Amid growing concern over internal displacements in northern Uganda and
drought in both central Tanzania and Kenya's North Rift region, the food aid
appeals of the governments of Eritrea and Ethiopia for 2003 have reached
slightly over 897,000 million tonnes.
The amount is still insufficient to meet the emergency requirements for
2003, and most of the food pledged is yet to arrive in the countries for
timely distribution, the Greater Horn of Africa Bulletin for February has
said.
"The current food aid distribution in Eritrea is being provided at
reduced rations of 60 per cent of the assessed needs," says the report,
noting that Ethiopia's food pipeline is only secure through mid-May."
Kenya's Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock says existing reserves,
plus this season's harvest, will make the cumulative national maize stocks
sufficient to meet the country's food needs through June 2003.
And although the availability of pasture and water for livestock in
January this year was generally the most favourable in four years, some key
pastoral areas such as Baringo, West Pokot, lower areas of Koibatek,
southern Turkana and pockets of Kajiado, Mandera and Samburu districts
continued to have little pasture and water due to poor rains.
Livestock prices remained high in January compared with the five-year
(1997-2001) average. Although this enhances the recovery process of
pastoralists, selling livestock means their asset-base is much reduced and
it will take several successive good seasons to build up. Therefore, a good
2003 long-rains season is critical in consolidating the recovery
process.
Maize prices in Kampala dropped by 21 per cent between December and
January, due mainly to new harvests from eastern Uganda reaching
markets. Conversely, Dar es Salaam and Nairobi maize prices stabilised in
January to December levels and have been consistently below the 1997-2002
average.
Kenya's 2002/03 October-February short-rains season had varied outcomes
across the country. The rains were favourable in the eastern and northern
pastoral districts but poor in the northwestern agro-pastoral
districts.
Meanwhile, civil insecurity remains high in northern Uganda, with
increased numbers of internally displaced people (IDPs). Together with low
harvests from the previous season, high food insecurity and acute rates of
malnutrition are reported.
In addition, the movement of Karimojong pastoralists to northeastern
areas of Katakwi district has displaced people, heightening the risk of food
insecurity. Limited access to agricultural fields by the IDPs in these areas
could result in low crop cultivation and production, leading to an increase
in food aid requirements.
Between August 2002 and January 2003, millet and sorghum prices were 10
per cent higher than in the previous two years, signifying lower market
access for households dependent on market purchases.
Livestock prices in Karamoja region have declined by more than 50 per
cent, worsening the terms of trade for those selling animals to buy
food.
In Rwanda, despite unfavourable rainfall conditions at the commencement
of the season in September/October 2002, crop production is generally
satisfactory following improved rainfall, mostly average and above during
the season.
The mission estimated the 2003 production at 16 per cent higher than the
average of the past three years for the September-December season.
The northern and eastern parts of southern Sudan remain highly food
insecure due to fighting and insecurity, returnees from Ethiopia, and poor
harvests attributed to low rainfall and pest infestation last year. Fighting
in the north of Liech State displaced a further 50,000 people in
January.
This is in addition to a population of about 50,000 displaced from Raga,
the Democratic Republic of Congo and the Central African Republic who fled
to the southwestern areas of Ezo and Tambura in 2001.
In Somalia, the good performance of the October-December (Deyr) season
improved prospects of a good harvest in most parts of the country. This has
reversed the poor food security conditions following the below average crop
production during the April-June (Gu) 2002 season.
Overall, current conditions for agricultural production are favourable
for most of the Greater Horn of Africa countries. The Drought Monitoring
Centre's March-May 2003 seasonal forecast predicts near-normal rainfall in
the equatorial Horn. The current moderate El Nino rains are expected to
significantly weaken in the coming months up to June.

Original_Search_Title: "I Wanted My Son to Go to School"
Headline: Ethiopia: "I Wanted My Son to Go to School"
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — For millions of children all over the world going to school, having
decent meal and clean place to live in are luxury.
According to ILO about 246 million children are involved in child
labour. These children are exposed to the worst form of working
condition. They are engaged in works that are hazardous to their
health and general well being.
The major factors for child labour are poverty, excessive population
growth and lack of educational opportunities.
Natural disasters, economic turn down, armed conflict and HIV/AIDS
are also other factors, which force children into child labour.
Child labour is rampant both in rural and urban areas. Young school
age girls work as housemaids, young boys and some girls work as
shoeshine, taxi assistants and prostitution is also in the list of
jobs that children are involved in, in urban areas.
Quite a number of children go into the above-mentioned businesses
sometime due to the brutal treatment and harassment they are
subjected to by parents and stepparents.
Most rural children work on farms, herding animals, weaving carpets,
hauling bricks, making toys and many other overwhelming tasks.
Although no country is immune from the problem of child labour, it is
to a larger extent practiced in the developing countries. Ethiopia is
one of the poorest nations in sub-Saharan Africa, where nearly one
third of the children aged 14 and under are involved in child labour.
We are witnessing a large number of child labour cases all over the
country, the largest portion being concentrated in urban areas, and
big towns.
In rural and semi urban areas of Ethiopia children work on small
farms owned by individuals, plantations, retail trade of small
articles and in producing souvenirs for tourists mainly around the
major tourist sites.
One such place is the small village of Kimboye, which is located 68km
west of Addis Ababa. Kimboye is situated on the side of the main road
that takes to Ambo, the next bigger town.
Children of Kimboye are known for their handicraft-making, different
kinds of toys made from dried sorghum straw. The children display
their products on the side of the main road so that passers-by can
purchase some.
The fact that Ambo is a place most frequented by tourists for its
climatic attraction, hot springs and the crater lake of Wonchi
located near by, have helped the children of Kimboye sell some of
their products.
Zerihun Birru, 14 is one of the boys who makes straw toys and sells
them to support his family. He lives with his uncle, and three of his
cousins. "It has never been easy for me" says Zerihun, displaying
hopeless expression on his face, "I have been doing this since I was
9 years old. I have been helping my uncle, who has been farming and
making toys for years. I used to sell what he made until recently.
When my uncle got sick and couldn't go to the farm I was forced to
take over and do every thing all by myself."
Although making toys is considered as a side income-generating job,
some families totally depend on it for their livelihood. This forces
most children to be totally engaged in this work allowing them no
time and energy for their schooling.
Zerihun continued telling his story, "I dropped out of school when I
was on 4th grade. Since my uncle stayed at home I had not enough time
to study and do my homework. I have to work very hard and convince
the tourists from Ambo and Addis to buy some of my toys so that I
could get some money for the day."
Getting the raw material, which is the straw of sorghum is
problematic by itself. Since sorghum is a seasonal crop the making of
toys is also seasonal. The children have to wait for the next crop
season to get the straws in abundance.
Asnake Challa, 11, is one of Zerihun's colleagues engaged in toy
making. Asnake lives with his mother, father and two younger sisters.
As an oldest child he is expected to take care of his family.
Asnake attends school, which though he doubts will ever continue. "I
haven't dropped out of school yet" said Asnake "For me it is really
hard to work and study. I am busy selling the toys to the tourists
and most of the time I don't have enough time to prepare for my
schoolwork. Unless there is some change I don't think I can continue
with it, since at this very moment school is no longer a priority for
my family and I."
Although families believe that their children have to go to school,
they are forced to accept the fact that the children must perform
those tasks under the circumstances that prevail.
"I didn't want my son to be on the streets waiting for tourist to
come and buy" Asnake's father Ato Challa said.
"I wanted him to go only to school, play with his peer groups and
enjoy life at this age. But I cannot support the family by myself and
Asnake has to help out in what I do. I believe education is the key
to escape from poverty. But it is unlikely for my son to continue his
school."
Another fact about these children is that what they manage to sell is
not enough to sustain their basic need, such as food.
They sell one piece of toy for 5-10 birr for local buyers which is a
little more than one dollar, but if they got lucky they might sell a
piece for 35-40 birr for foreign tourists. But this happens once in a
while and makes not that much difference at all.
On the other hand there are people from the capital who buy these
toys at a very low price and sale it in big hotels and souvenir shops
twice or three times the original price. This leaves the children
with nothing.
Because of all such mentioned reasons these children will end up
physically and psychologically exploited. They are physically weak,
malnourished and easily exposed to disease.
They are also mentally weakened for they are pressured into thinking
of how to get a penny more out of a sale.
Lack of education will inevitably also contribute for their inability
to lead a better life in the future.

Original_Search_Title: GM Pays the Bills
Headline: South Africa: GM Pays the Bills
Body_Text:
Seven of every 10 South African cotton farmers have switched to genetically modified (GM) varieties. The others still plant conventional cotton and use pesticides and chemical fertilisers. South Africa has no market for organic cotton. The GM farmers produced 25 130 tonnes of cotton in the 2001/02 growing season.
On the Springbok Flats, north of Pretoria, Willem van der Walt runs a mixed farm producing sorghum, cotton, rotational crops and pigs. He is a firm GM convert.
"It is absolutely essential," he says. "Our production costs have decreased 40%. Zero tillage allows for greater water retention in the soil, we use fewer pesticides and our maintenance costs for machinery are less."
Van der Walt insists his farming methods are environmentally sensitive because he uses less pesticide.
"The production of organic cotton is possible, but you need long-term market agreements to make it attractive," he says.
Klaus Sall, a Danish organic cotton specialist who works with a foreign-funded project that hopes to introduce cleaner technology to the South African textile industry, reinforces the point. Cotton-growing is the first point in a long production line that produces the clothes we wear.
"If the customer wants it, South Africa can make it," says Sall.
A small but growing demand for organic cotton is creating viable niche markets in the United States and Europe. Nike marketed its first 100% "organic" range of clothing last year and now wants 5% of all its products to be made from certified organic cotton by 2010. The company buys 600 tonnes of organic cotton a year, but this is increasing by 100 tonnes every year.
Other companies that are following the trend include LL Bean (US), Mabrouc (Switzerland), Ikea (Sweden) and Marks & Spencer (Britain).
Though South Africa has no market for organic cotton, Sall believes local producers should not be discouraged because there is already a global demand for 10 000 tonnes a year.
Turkey is the world's largest producer of organic cotton, though it has no local market for the crop. It hasn't been an easy ride for the Turks, whose small-scale farmers have relied on contracts with foreign companies committed to the organic varieties.
Uygun Aksoy, chairperson and founder of the Turkish Association for Organic Agriculture, says the government was pessimistic about the project and farmers resisted converting their land to organic farms, which took three years.
Yields first declined by 15% to 20% before stabilising. "But these losses were offset by long-term price increases of 20% to 26%, because buyers were prepared to pay a premium for the organic materials," says Aksoy.
Bo-Weevil, a Dutch organic manufacturer, believes customers in Europe are prepared to pay more for the environmental benefit of organic cotton, but this has not yet been the experience of countries such as South Africa and Australia.
Thabo Tshabalala, national education secretary of the South African Clothing and Textile Workers' Union, says local consumers will be prepared to pay more for organic products once they know the costs of using chemicals in the manufacture of textiles.
"It's like medical insurance," says Tshabalala, citing the inherent environmental and health risks of non-organic production methods, "you are prepared to pay for it, though you never know when you will need it."
Karen Lundbo, the coordinator of the Textile Cleaner Production project who is trying to encourage local retailers to introduce organic ranges, also believes that a local demand will be established.
"Price is an important factor, but the fact that companies like Nike are mixing organic with conventional cotton shows that the manufacturers are actively doing something about it."
Time can be a fickle agent, but farmers such as Van der Walt know that GM now pays the bills.

Original_Search_Title: Huge Hike in Maize Producer Price
Headline: Zimbabwe: Huge Hike in Maize Producer Price
Body_Text:
Harare — THE government has hiked the maize producer price by 364 percent and announced a new pre-planting price for wheat that farmers' organisations yesterday said would ensure growers' viability and encourage deliveries of the crop to the Grain Marketing Board (GMB).
However, the government did not increase the selling price of the crops, which analysts said would result in the fiscus directly subsidising consumers at a time Treasury was battling to raise money to finance other expenditure.
Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement Minister Joseph Made said for the 2003 marketing season, which begins on April 1, farmers would sell their maize, sorghum and millet to the GMB at $130 000 a tonne, up from the $28 000 that was given to the growers last year.
Made said the new price would ensure that farmers realised a return of 60 percent.
The minister said the government had also increased by 114 percent to $150 000 a tonne the pre-planting price for wheat in a bid to boost output of the crop, which has fallen in the past two years because of drought and a controversial land reform programme.
The price will be reviewed in September before farmers harvest their crop.
Made said the government would also introduce a $7.5 billion winter crops inputs loan scheme to help more farmers grow wheat.
The money would be used for the procurement of wheat seed, fertilisers and crop chemicals, as well as for the payment of electricity and water tariffs, tillage services and harvesting.
Officials within organisations representing the country's farmers said the new wheat measures would encourage growers to produce more of the crop.
According to Made, a crop forecasting committee is in the process of assessing the national wheat crop for this year, but the government expects farmers to deliver between 200 000 and 300 000 tonnes of maize to the GMB.
He said growers were expected to retain most of their output for consumption.
Zimbabwe Farmers' Union president Silas Hungwe said the prices announced by the government would encourage farmers to deliver their crop to the GMB.
But he said the GMB had to increase collection depots in farming areas so that farmers were not forced to sell their crops to private buyers. The GMB is the sole buyer and seller of maize and wheat in the country, but many farmers sell their crop to private buyers who offer more money.
George Hutchison, a senior official of the Commercial Farmers' Union said: "The price for maize is very encouraging, farmers will be delivering to the GMB."
Agriculture Bank of Zimbabwe chief executive Taka Mutunhu said the new prices would give farmers more disposable income, enabling them to repay loans borrowed from banks.
But analysts said the government's failure to raise the selling price of maize and wheat from $9 600 and $29 500 a tonne respectively would adversely affect the operations of the cash-strapped GMB.
Made said the selling prices of the grains would remain static in a bid to protect consumers by curbing increases in the cost of flour, mealie meal and bread.
"What this has highlighted once more is that the government is willing to let the GMB play its role as a service provider but there is a big price to it," an economist with a Harare merchant bank said.
"The difference between the buying price and the selling price is too much to make economic sense and is indicative of the structural rigidities that we find within parastatals," he told the Financial Gazette.
Consultant economist John Robertson added: "This amounts to a direct subsidy when we all know that the government does not have the resources for those kinds of subsidies.
"By solving one problem of ensuring the viability of farmers, the government has created another one. I am not sure of where they will get the money."

Original_Search_Title: Wheat And Maize Producer Price Increase
Headline: Zimbabwe: Wheat And Maize Producer Price Increase
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — From the start of the new marketing season on 1 April, Zimbabwe's
farmers will benefit from a significant increase in the price of wheat and
maize, Agriculture Minister Joseph Made announced on Wednesday.
The producer price of maize, sorghum and millet will rise to Zim
$130,000 (US $157) per mt from Zim $28,000 (US $35) and the pre-planting
price of wheat has been increased to Zim $150,000 (US $182) from Zim $70,000
(US $85).
"As this is a pre-planting price, it is a signal to farmers of the
commitment government has of ensuring viability in wheat production. Farmers
will therefore start the wheat production season with the assurance that
this price will be reviewed," Made said.
He noted there would be no change in the price of the
government-controlled Grain Marketing Board (GMB) selling price to millers
and the maize price will remain at Zim $9,600 (US $12) per mt and wheat at
Zim $29,500 (US $36).
"The government's intention is to contain the price of flour, maize meal
and bread so that it is affordable to consumers," he added.
George Hutchison, general manager for commodities in the Commercial
Grain Producers Association, said the move would encourage farmers to take
their harvests to the GMB, the official buyer in the country and would give
farmers enough to pay for their inputs and make a small profit.
"Because of food shortages, a lot of maize is being retained by [mainly
communal] farmers who are keeping it for their own use," he told IRIN on
Thursday.
"Commercial farmers' maize production is right down - they have produced
maybe 100,000 mt this year," Hutchison said. "This year the majority of
maize was produced by small-scale communal farmers. If the government hadn't
increased the prices, very little would go to the GMB."
According to the latest Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS NET)
report, most of the current maize crop is performing badly due to poor
rainfall and the lack of inputs. Crops are expected to be 20 percent less
than last year.
FEWS NET estimated that Zimbabwe would have to import between 840,000 mt
to 1.3 million mt of maize to fill the estimated pre-harvest deficit for the
2003/04 marketing season, while deliveries of existing stocks from the GMB
throughout the country were erratic.
Hutchison cautioned that the pre-planting wheat price increase, though
significant, might not be enough to spur an increase in May's
plantings.
An increase in wheat production is important this year after last year's
dismal crop and subsequent shortages.
"It is an expensive crop to grow. It's a winter crop and needs full
irrigation. The increase should have been around Zim $200,000 (US $250) and
the current price is still not enough of an incentive," Hutchison
explained.
However, the optimism generated by the producer price increase was
tempered with concerns over where the fiscus would find the money to
subsidise the consumer price.
The Zimbabwe government is currently battling an economic crisis that
has left it struggling to procure basic supplies like fuel due to serious
foreign exchange shortages, and its debt arrears preclude further
International Monetary Fund lending.
Economist John Robertson said that although the government's low
consumer price appeared to be a relief for consumers, in reality only a few
well-connected people would access the commodities at the lower price. Due
to shortages the average consumer would have to "negotiate in the back
streets and pay three times that price", he alleged.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Producer Prices Increased
Headline: Zimbabwe: Maize Producer Prices Increased
Body_Text:
Harare — GOVERNMENT yesterday made sure the new maize harvest would be delivered to the GMB by raising the producer price of maize from $28 000 to $130 000 a tonne.
And to encourage farmers to plant winter wheat, the pre-planting price of wheat was more than doubled from $70 000 to $150 000 a tonne.
At the commercial exchange rate of $824 to the US$, farmers are receiving roughly world prices for their produce, almost US$160 a tonne for maize and more than US$180 a tonne for wheat.
The moves, which were welcomed by farmers' organisations, are likely to see an increase in the delivery of maize and wheat to the Grain Marketing Board.
This will result in an improvement in the supply of maize and wheat to millers, which will in turn see an improvement in the supply of maize meal and flour to consumers.
The Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, Cde Joseph Made, told journalists in Harare that the pre-planting price of wheat would be reviewed at the beginning of the marketing season for the winter crop in September.
He said there would be no change in the GMB's selling price to millers and that this was the Government's way of subsidising consumers.
The GMB will continue selling maize to millers at $9 600 a tonne and wheat at $29 500 a tonne.
"The intention is to contain the price of flour, maize-meal and bread so that it is affordable to consumers," said Cde Made.
The new producer price of maize would also apply to sorghum and millet to encourage farmers to grow more of these small grains.
"These small grains contribute significantly to food security, particularly in the drier parts of the country," said Cde Made.
"This will encourage producers to exploit the relative comparative advantages that exist in the production of these commodities, while at the same time enhancing food security."
Cde Made said the new prices would put agriculture on a firm footing in line with the requirements of the recently launched National Economic Revival Programme.
He said the Government would continue tightening statutory instruments that made maize and wheat specified products.
"Stringent measures will be taken against farmers, institutions, individuals, companies traders and non-governmental organisations in violation of these provisions," said Cde Made.
"Government's objective remains very clear - it is to have basic food items derived from grains, maize and wheat available to every Zimbabwean."
Stakeholders in the farming sector yesterday said they were excited by the new prices that would ensure farmers deliver more products to the GMB.
"We are very happy with the new prices," said Zimbabwe Farmers' Union president Mr Silas Hungwe.
"But the GMB must ensure they set up more collection points near the farmers to prevent dealings with unscrupulous people."
Indigenous Commercial Farmers Union president Davison Mugabe said the prices would help new farmers invest in equipment.
"Our members are going to produce more," he said. "We are happy at the timing of this increase just at the beginning of the marketing season."
Commercial Farmers Union representative Mr George Hutchison said the new prices were encouraging and would result in farmers delivering more to GMB.
Agribank chief executive Mr Taka Mutunhu said a recent research carried out by the bank revealed the farmers' major handicap was low producer prices.
He said the situation would change with the new prices.
Zimbabwe Fertiliser Company general manager Mr Richard Masvaire said the new prices would ensure farmers afford inputs.
"We hope the gazetted prices of fertilisers will be reviewed in line with this development," he said.
GMB acting chief executive Lieutenant Colonel Samuel Muvhuti said they would turn grain distribution points into collection centres in anticipation of increased maize delivery.
"We urge farmers' unions to educate their members on the need to support the Government by bringing grain to depots," said Lt Col Muvhuti.

Original_Search_Title: UNDP Donates N3.3m to Jigawa Agric Development Programme
Headline: Nigeria: UNDP Donates N3.3m to Jigawa Agric Development Programme
Body_Text:
THE UN Development Programme (UNDP) has donated N3.3 million to Jigawa Research Institute. A statement signed by the Public Relations Officer of the institute, Mr Isyaku Muhammad, at the weekend, noted that the money was targetted towards improving farming methods and promoting agricultural output in the area. It said that part of the money would be used to train researchers in various fields of agriculture.
He added that the institute planned to engage some 50 staff on various courses both within and outside the country to prepare and make them more efficient in handling research projects. Already, he said, N1.65 million had been released to the institute by the UNDP for the training of 25 staff of the institute.
He expressed appreciation over the gesture and urged the state government to release its counterpart fund for the project to boost the training programme. Earlier, the director of the agency, Dr Hilton Gomez, had said the institute recorded progress on the researches carried out in the state.
The agency, in collaboration with its technical partner, Embrupu, a Brazilian agricultural research institute, had introduced improved varieties of maize, wheat, beans and sorghum seedlings to boost the state's agricultural base.Gomez gave the assurance that the new technologies would transform the agricultural output of the state and urged farmers to support the programme to achieve its objectives.

Original_Search_Title: United States Delivers Food Aid to Three NGOs
Headline: Ghana: United States Delivers Food Aid to Three NGOs
Body_Text:
Accra — Three Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) in Ghana are to take delivery of 25,000 metric tons of wheat valued at approximately $5 million under the United States Government's Food for Peace program.
The NGOs are the Catholic Relief Services (CRS), Adventist Development and Relief Agency (ADRA), and Opportunities Industrialization Centers International (OICI).
H.E. Mary CarlinYates, US Ambassador to Ghana, witnessed the offloading of the commodity at the Tema Harbor on March 18, 2003. She was accompanied by Ms. Sharon Cromer, Mission Director of USAID/Ghana, and Country Directors of the beneficiary NGOs.
CRS will receive 12,500 metric tons, while ADRA and OICI will receive 9,850 and 2,650 metric tons respectively.
The wheat will be sold to local flour mills and the proceeds used to improve food security in the country, particularly in the three northern regions.
The sales, which are payable in local currency, will assist the country to save its foreign exchange earnings that are required to import the wheat.
The Office of Food for Peace, through funding provided by United States Public Law 480, Title II, makes available food aid donations to numerous NGOs to address the needs of food security in various countries.
The beneficiary NGOs will use the money generated to fund projects such as training in modern methods of agriculture and micro-credit to farmers to increase rural incomes through higher crop yields.
They will also utilize the resources to provide wells to improve access to clean drinking water, enhance child survival, and increase enrolment and attendance in primary schools. In addition, the resources will be used to support vulnerable groups in Ghana, including orphans, the elderly, and the physically and mentally challenged in society.
Annually, the Food for Peace program imports approximately 50,000 metric tons of food commodities. The total cost of the food averages $10 million each year. The commodities include wheat, wheat soy blend, bulgur wheat, soy, fortified sorghum grits, and vegetable oil, of which approximately 80% is sold to generate local currency to support food security programs in the country.
The program has also contributed to the socio-economic development of Ghana.
For example, in the Northern Region, ADRA farmers have increased crop yields from 290 kg to 850 kg per acre over a five-year period, increasing their net profits on maize from approximately Â¢760,000 to Â¢2.6 million per acre.
And over a four-year period, the total enrolment in primary schools assisted by CRS increased by 43%, while last year, the CRS girls' education promotion project, which provides take-home rations as an incentive for attendance, exceeded its target of 48,300 girls by 7.2%.

Original_Search_Title: UN Responds To Urgent Needs In  Blue Nile - First Time Ever
Headline: Sudan: UN Responds To Urgent Needs In  Blue Nile - First Time Ever
Body_Text:
Khartoum/Nairobi — The World Food Programme (WFP) and the United Nations Children's Fund
(UNICEF) have started the first-ever operation to deliver essential
humanitarian supplies to meet the urgent needs of 115,000 drought and
war-affected persons in the Blue Nile State, eastern Sudan.
The area was recently opened to aid agencies following lengthy separate
bilateral negotiations between the UN, the Government of Sudan and the
SPLM/A in mid-January. This was followed by a joint assessment conducted in
February by UN Agencies and NGOs, in collaboration with the Government of
Sudan and SPLM/A.
According to the assessment, over 90 percent of the total population are
in need of food assistance.  The situation is said to be worse for the
internally displaced persons (IDPs), of whom more than 60 percent are
children and women whose survival is challenged by the combination lack of
food and frequent bouts of illness.
"Food is one of the most pressing needs in the area," said Ronald
Sibanda, WFP's Country Director for Sudan. "War and drought have taken a
vicious toll on the population, with many people risking hunger-related
death if food is not provided urgently."
The assessment also found an exaggerated dependence on the seasonal
water sources that are gradually disappearing with the progression of the
dry season.  Household latrines were non-existent.  Many primary schools
need urgent rehabilitation, and they operate without textbooks and other
basic supplies.
Blue Nile State, which up to 1997 was a net exporter of grain, faces a
very insecure food situation with over 17 percent of the population
displaced between 1997 and 2002. The massive displacement and death caused
by the prevailing insecurity in the region, has resulted in a significant
loss of assets, particularly livestock.
The situation was aggravated by low rainfall last year. The harvest was
well below normal; poor pastures and lack of water had a devastating effect
on livestock; and even fishing was affected because of the low water levels
in the rivers. Families rely on very limited supplies of sorghum from the
scarce harvest and wild roots. Most livestock died and prices of food,
especially sorghum are reported as very high and soaring with exchange
levels at one goat for two bags of grain as opposed to one goat to six bags
a year ago, hence the urgency of making as many relief deliveries and
interventions as possible in the coming months.
During the initial intervention, which will target both government
controlled and SPLM/A areas of the state, WFP will truck and airdrop a total
of 934 tons of various relief food commodities to cover the needs of over
79,000 people for one month. UNICEF will provide essential drugs, water and
sanitation supplies, such as water pumps and latrines, as well as teaching,
learning and recreation kits to support the education and basic development
needs for over 2,500 children in ten basic schools.
At this first stage, WFP will distribute a one-month ration to areas
identified as critically food insecure by the recent assessment. Food
distribution teams will continue to assess the situation on the ground, and
WFP will provide food as needed in the months leading up to the next harvest
in October 2003. Food will be provided through general distribution and
Food-For-Work projects, whereby participation in rehabilitation projects is
rewarded with food.
The UNICEF supply of essential drugs, primary healthcare and maternal
and child health kits are expected to meet the basic health requirements
over a three month period. In addition, supplies of Unimix, corn-soya blend
and BP-5 high-protein biscuits will be used for therapeutic feeding of 70
percent of the severely to moderately malnourished children over a six-month
period.
Plastic jerry cans and pieces of soap will be distributed to 1,000
families to address immediate water collection and hygiene needs. Within the
month, UNICEF will support the construction of 120 hand pumps to provide
safe drinking water to about 30,000 persons. Initially, 25 latrines will be
constructed in schools to provide safe sanitary means of waste disposal for
at least 5,000 pupils.
School supplies will be distributed to 2,500 children in primary schools
to assist in re-establishing normalcy in children displaced from their homes
by the war.
"Families have lost most of their resources and access to food and other
basic services is extremely limited," said UNICEF Representative, JoAnna Van
Gerpen. "This is having a terrible impact on populations also suffering from
lack of drinking water and poor health care.  People are very vulnerable to
diseases, in particular women and children, whose lives are seriously at
risk.  It is therefore crucial that both the Government and SPLA adhere to
their commitment to a cease-fire, especially to this humanitarian access
agreement with the UN."

Original_Search_Title: Vulnerable Rely On Food Aid
Headline: Zimbabwe: Vulnerable Rely On Food Aid
Body_Text:
Mutare — On Mutare's tobacco auction floor rather than bails of golden-leafed
burley, sacks of maize and corn soya blend are neatly stacked in row upon
row.
Burley tobacco never really took off in the Mutare area in eastern
Zimbabwe, and the town's auction floor closed at the end of last year's
growing season after a decade of operation.
Its new tenants, however, are extremely busy.
Mutare is one of four transhipment depots for the World Food Programme
(WFP). Food aid trucked through South Africa or carried by rail from the
Mozambican port of Beira arrives at Mutare to be off-loaded. The relief
supplies are then repacked onto vehicles heading to food distribution points
in the seven districts served by the warehouse, as part of the WFP's
emergency operation to feed more than half of Zimbabwe's 11.6 million people
in urgent need of assistance.
Diana Sibanda was just one of the 2,844 recipients at Chiranda primary
school last week who had waited patiently for the delivery of WFP's monthly
ration. She had struggled hard not to be in this predicament. Since November
last year she had planted three times, only to see the rains stop early each
time, and her maize crop whither in the sandy soil.
"I can pick one or two cobs to roast, but the crop has failed," she told
IRIN.
As a widow, Sibanda has been classified as "vulnerable" by WFP and
therefore entitled to 10 kg of maize meal, 1 kg of pulses, 1 kg of corn soya
blend - used as a nutritious porridge - and a little vegetable oil each
month. This, she said, had been the difference between life and death.
"When I saw my crops fail I felt pain and worried how I could live. I
feel gratitude [for the aid], without it we would have died," she
explained.
Tendai Mujiwechi said that before the WFP programme began in October,
she was surviving on un-ripened fruit - mangoes and paw-paw - which she
would boil. "They taste good when you are hungry," she told IRIN.
In Mutare, as in much of rural Zimbabwe, subsistence farmers scrape a
living growing a little maize and sorghum. The area is dry, and more suited
to ranching, but both Sibanda and Mujiwechi said in a good year they could
grow enough to produce a small surplus for sale.
But three consecutive years of poor rains exhausted the few resources
they had to fall back on. With even the wealthier farmers facing a poor
crop, opportunities to work in their fields as wage labourers, a traditional
coping strategy, dried up.
"As old as we are, we really grew scared because of the hunger,"
Mujiwechi said.
Alongside the vulnerable fed by WFP - deemed as the elderly, widows,
women-headed households, orphans and people living with HIV/AIDS -
increasing numbers of the just plain poor have appealed to be included on
the distribution register.
Supplies of cheap price controlled maize in Mutare, provided through the
government's Grain Marketing Board, were described as "erratic" by aid
workers.
Throughout the region is the evidence of the failure of the rains during
the crucial months of November and December, when the maize crop should have
matured. In the small plots that surround each homestead, rows of scorched
and shrunken stems stand abandoned.
Compounding the bad weather were problems at the village level over
access to seeds and fertiliser during the planting season last year. Maize
seeds, when available, were late in arriving in Mutare. Both Sibanda and
Mujiwechi said they had been forced to plant without fertiliser.
Complaints by producers over the fixed price of seed and problems with
the government's input support programme had delayed deliveries.
In a warning of worsening food insecurity in 2003, the Harare-based
Famine Early Warning System said in a report last month that due to
"inadequate inputs and poor rainfall distribution", crops were in "a much
worse situation than last year, when more than half of the country
experienced a complete crop failure or well below-average yields".

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Lira Goods Fall As Business Slow
Headline: Uganda: Prices of Lira Goods Fall As Business Slow
Body_Text:
Kampala — The continued military operation against Lords Resistance Army rebels has slowed produce business in Lira town.
Prices of common farm produce like maize, beans, millet, simsim and peas have fallen as the armed operations continue.
But there is strong demand for beans and maize from the World Food Programme, which want 4500 tonnes of beans and 2250 tonnes of maize to be taken to Pajule and Pader.
Continuous attacks on the Lira-Pader-Kitgum and Lira-Kotido roads mean that farmers can no longer freely transport their produce in the three districts, produce dealers said.
"These districts used to supply simsim, greenpeas, sorghum and they would buy beans from here. But now there is a lot of beans in store," Mr Fadhil Ogole, the treasurer of Lira Produce Buyers Association, said.
"Only simsim and groundnuts are good business now. G-nuts is almost out of stock because of limited supply while simsim hit a record high of Shs 940," he said, adding that they now buy simsim at Shs 850 a kilogramme and sell it at Shs 900.

Original_Search_Title: Best Cereal Production for Years in South
Headline: Somalia: Best Cereal Production for Years in South
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A food security watchdog says southern Somalia has recorded the best cereal production in years, due to good seasonal rains.
In its latest report, the Food Security Assessment Unit (FSAU) - a joint project of the EC and FAO - said "very good rainfall with high intensity and frequency" in the region had led to the "best ever recorded" cereal production in the post-war era.
It noted that cereal production following the deyr rains (October-December) had registered almost an 80 percent increase on the 1995-2001 period.
The best production for sorghum was recorded in Bay region, while Lower Shabelle contributed most of the maize production, said the FSAU report.
According to the report, the good harvest had relieved pressure at the household level in the regions of Gedo, Hiran and Bakol "which were areas of food security concern".
However, it noted that some areas in Middle and Lower Juba were experiencing food problems as a result of insecurity "which has caused disruption to migratory patterns and looting of assets".
The report also called for close monitoring of the Gedo region, which has experienced three consecutive dry years and where the economy has deteriorated.
Furthermore, it warned of "pockets of food insecurity" in the Sool plateau in the self-declared republic of Somaliland and the Addun pastoral area in the self-declared autonomous region of Puntland. These areas had experienced successive dry seasons.
"This, combined with additional factors specific to each area, has created vulnerability," the report stated.

Original_Search_Title: Analyse Subsidies Benefit
Headline: Uganda: Analyse Subsidies Benefit
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE UGANDA Revenue Authority is challenging the exemption on excise duty
claimed by Uganda Breweries and Nile Breweries for their Citizen and Eagle
beers.
The Finance Act states "all locally manufactured beer other than beer
made from malt is exempt from excise duty".
So Nile Breweries has smartly decided to start brewing Eagle Beer using
sorghum from Teso while UBL is selling Citizen using Kenyan barley.
Prices have been slashed to 800 shillings a bottle because of the tax
exemption. The two beers are believed to have immediately taken up to a 20
percent share of the Uganda market.
However, the URA is upset because it is losing tax revenue and may not
meet its collection targets.
It is estimated that the lost excise duty could eventually run as high
as 20 billion shillings a year.
Yet Nile Breweries has set an excellent example by buying all the new
epuripur sorghum that Teso farmers can produce. This is a useful horizontal
linkage in the economy.
Government has also invested heavily in the Tristar and former Nytil
textile factories and the Uganda Grain Exporters complex to try and
kick-start these vital economic sectors. In effect, they are being
subsidised.
Such subsidies should only be made in the context of a proper
cost-benefit analysis that clearly indicates that the nation will profit in
the medium term.
If the URA thinks the cost of the local beer exemption is too high,
perhaps government should seek a middle ground.
For instance, government could restrict the total amount of excise duty
exemption to a certain percentage of the total amount spent on sorghum or
barley from Ugandan farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Six Killed in Cyclone Japhet Flooding
Headline: Zimbabwe: Six Killed in Cyclone Japhet Flooding
Body_Text:
POLICE have recovered the bodies of six people who drowned in the wake of vicious floods triggered by Cyclone Japhet in Masvingo province.
Police spokesperson Inspector Learn Ncube said they were still searching for more bodies that are missing while the six had been positively identified.
Since the onset of Cyclone Japhet last week, houses, crops and bridges have been destroyed in Chipinge and Chimanimani districts.
Temporary tent shelters set up for victims of the catastrophe at 3 Infantry Battalion camp were not spared from the harsh weather either.
In Chipinge the most affected areas are Maparadze, Deruko and Mutechana villages, where some roofs were blown off house, while outside toilets collapsed.
Morris Sakabuya, the district administrator, said the maize and sorghum crop planted along the Musvazvi and Chikuu rivers was a complete write-off. The Musvazvi Bridge along the road to Mahenye was also damaged.
Meanwhile, the situation is still critical in Musambakaruma village in Kariba where 45 people were air-lifted after the cyclone-induced floods destroyed their houses. The heavy rains have cut off communication lines at Siakobvu Police Station where telephone poles were uprooted.
Isaac Mackenzie, the MP for Kariba, said the area was still inaccessible. In Chimanimani, the most affected areas are Rusitu and Shinja. Edgar Nyagwaya, the district administrator, said the roof of a clinic in Muchadziya was blown off, while several houses, outside toilets, crops and trees fell victim to the raging cyclone.
In Masvingo, over 200 people displaced by the floods are reportedly facing serious food shortages.
A government source said: "It is difficult to assist the families because the floods have not been declared a national disaster."
This would have opened the way for State funding.

Original_Search_Title: Agric is not just farming - Quashigah
Headline: Ghana: Agric is not just farming - Quashigah
Body_Text:
Accra — The Minister for Food and Agriculture Major Courage Quashigah has said agriculture is not just about farming but rather encompass a whole range of areas where business is brisk.
"Agriculture is business and not a way of life, Major Quashigah told the media last Tuesday in Accra adding, "The tyres under your car, the paper on which your are writing on, and the cosmetics you are wearing are all products of agriculture."
He said Ghana has been endowed with so many resources that if people were to take agriculture seriously and invested in it, Ghana would not need to go anywhere else for assistance or aid.
The government he said is changing the curricula for agricultural training up to the university level.
He said hitherto the training only focused on agricultural production to the detriment of the business side.
Students would now be trained with equal emphasis on the economic aspects of agriculture.
The Ministry is to ensure food security for the Ghanaian population and facilitate the production of agricultural raw materials for industry as well as for export. It is also to coordinate and formulate as well as implement policies, programmes for the food and agricultural sector.
Ghana's agriculture he said is made up of five major sub sectors.
These include food crops, forestry, livestock, fisheries and cocoa.
Major Quashigah said several factors were responsible for the inefficient production of food in the past.
Unclear policies, weak institutional arrangements, poor infrastructure, inadequate financial support as well as social and environmental problems.
To correct the imbalances in the agric sector the Minister said Directors of the Ministry, scientists and agricultural experts, industrialists and development partners under the chairmanship of Professor Kwadwo Asenso Okyere was charged in the last quarter of 2001 to formulate a new policy direction to address the problems.
The Minister said the objective of the group was to transform Ghana into a leading agribusiness and industrial country in Africa by the year 2010.
However he said Ghana's agricultural industry has passed the cross roads into a more focused and robust stage where the goals and objectives for the sector are more realistic.
The concentration now he said is on the production of selected local food items that contribute to nutritional requirements for proteins and carbohydrates, fats, fibres vitamins and minerals that are found in fish and meat products.
Others are vegetables, legumes oils and fruits.
The Minister said to facilitate the production of agricultural raw materials for industry; the ministry is focusing on sugar, textiles, leather, tyre, paper and cosmetics.
Other are areas are canning, chocolate, soap and beverage industries.
The policy will facilitate farmers' access to inputs at reasonable costs and ensure economic prices for their produce.
With the assistance of the Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO), the Ministry has developed 20 hectares of land for the cultivation of millet, sorghum, rice, plantain and vegetables through improved water control systems at Bolgatanga, East Gonja, Tano, Jasikan and Dangbe East districts.
Production
Major Quashigah said the Ministry is assisting 330 farmer groups to intensify the production of rice, maize, sorghum, millet soyabean, cowpea or vegetables.
Again as part of the current national drive towards promoting the use of locally available raw materials in industries, the project during the period under review provided funds for the purchase of four tonnes of sorghum seed for the distribution to participating farmers in the Guinness Sorghum project districts of the three northern regions.
In 2002, the minister said a total of 2,800 tonnes of sorghum grain was produced for Guinness Ghana Ltd to advance their experiment of using sorghum in the brewing of Guinness.
The Minister also said an amount of 2. billion cedis has been provided for the importation of essential spare parts to enable Agric Mechanisation Limited to rehabilitate about 1,000 broken down tractors.
"The spare parts have arrived and the company is presently taking delivery to commence work," the Minister said.
The lack of social amenities in rural areas including lack of processing and storage facilities have also made farming unattractive to the youth.
In order to entice the youth into agriculture, the Minister said efforts are being put into place to develop amenities so as to attract the youth into agriculture.
"In order to get our fishermen involved in the management of marine and Inland water resources, community-based fisheries management committees have been formed in the fishing communities.
The ministry, he said, has already bought a crawler dozer at the instance of the Ashanti Fish Farmers Association and is digging fishponds on request.
He said the Ministry has completed a pilot Aquaculture Centre that is expected to produce fingerlings for fish farmers in the middle belt of the country.
"It consists of 10 concrete tanks, nursery ponds and 24 earthen ponds for production of fingerlings," the minister said adding, "a mechanised borehole has been established at the centre to act as a back-up supply of water.

Original_Search_Title: Finance, URA Disagree On Exemptions for Beer
Headline: Uganda: Finance, URA Disagree On Exemptions for Beer
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Uganda Revenue Authority (URA) is contesting a decision by Ministry
of Finance to offer excise duty exemption to brewers producing beer using
raw materials obtained locally.
URA is said to have refused to accept the exemptions claimed for Eagle
Beer and Citizen Beer produced by Nile Breweries Limited (NBL) and Uganda
Breweries Limited (UBL) respectively.
URA do not agree with the breweries' interpretation of the Finance
Act. Sections 3, 5 and schedule Three of the Finance Act 2002 states that,
"All locally manufactured beer other than beer made from malt, is exempt
from excise duty.
However, when contacted last Tuesday, Annebrit Aslund, the Commissioner
General declined to comment on the issue and referred Business Vision to the
finance Planning headquarters.
The rift erupted following a recent introduction of Eagle beer, by the
South African owned Nile Breweries, It costs sh800. An average of sh600
below other locally made beers.
The beer that was launched in November last year is made from locally
grown sorghum called epuripur and is presently distributed and consumed
mostly upcountry. In response UBL also launched their Citizen beer.
Citizen is produced in Kenya, using barley. It also costs sh800 and
popular to consumers outside Kampala.
The launch of Eagle Beer raised concerns from the industry and prompted
reactions from URA to send a team of experts to NBL.
Aslund wants the finance ministry to review the decision, but finance
minister Gerald Ssendaula has yet to comment on the issue.
"Government has for sometime been looking for crops from which people
can get income. To promote these crops, we decided that any consumable
produced from locally obtained raw material will be free of excise duty, and
this does not apply to Nile Breweries Limited (NBL) only," Lawrence Kizza
Commissioner Tax Policy told Business Vision last week.
Epuripur is at present widely grown in the eastern and northern parts of
the country. It is a high yield crop that matures in 120 days. It fetches
good money for farmers, because it sells for sh300 a kilo compared to sh120
a kilo for red sorghum.
Critics say the tax break for Eagle beer would make other beer products
uncompetitive in the market. NBL's exemption could also cost the Government
about sh20b in taxes over one year.
However, Kizza said the tax break cannot be quantified, because it
constitutes a very small portion of the total volume of requirements for
production of a beer.
"I am not sure where they got their figures from. However, the policy
applies to any company that sustains a project that uses local raw materials
for production. In this way we are also trying to encourage investors to
create market for local farmers. We tried it with another variety of sorghum
called Serena and it failed, epuripur seems to be successful," Kizza
said.
Excise duty in Uganda applies to four products namely beer, soda,
cigarettes and spirits. Excise duty on all imported alcohol and spirits is
presently 60%.
In his confirmation letter to NBL, August 5 last year, Kizza wrote that:
"This is to confirm that there is no excise duty on beer made from
sorghum. The 60% excise duty in the Finance Bill 2002 is applicable only on
beer made from malt. The position of extending the excise duty on beer from
sorghum will be reviewed in subsequent years after the beer has taken-off."
Kizza said the tax break to NBL is in line with the Poverty Eradication
Action Plan (PEAP), which requires Government to be in partnership with
central and local governments, civil society, donors and the private
sector.
