Nile Breweries Marketing Manager Mike Olsen told Business Vision that
issues of unfair competition do not arise, because the market is being
shared equally. He said Eagle beer is just 20% of their total sales,
representing 10% of the volume of beer consumed in the market. Uganda's beer
market is at about four litres per capita, compared to South Africa at 65
litres per capita and Tanzania at around 15 litres per capita.

Original_Search_Title: We Have Made Modest Gains in Agric - Quashigah
Headline: Ghana: We Have Made Modest Gains in Agric - Quashigah
Body_Text:
AS PART of the Ministry of Food and Agriculture's measures to promote the consumption of local foods, it has made foreign exchange savings of about $1million over the past one year.
At the latest in a series of meet the press held in Accra yesterday, the sector minister, Major (rtd) Courage Quashigah, explained that, this was made possible by the Accra Brewery Limited using 2,500 tonnes of locally processed maize grits.
He said this decision was taken after maize and sorghum were satisfactorily experimented with in the brewery industry. Also a total of 2,800 tons of sorghum grain was produced for Guinness Ghana limited to advance their experiment of using sorghum in the brewing of Guinness stout.
The minister also said his ministry had committed an amount of Â¢900 million to the rehabilitation of the Vea and Tano irrigation projects and an amount of Â¢30 million had also been allocated to repair a broken down valve at Bontango in the Northern region. It is also making efforts to entice the youth into agriculture through the Village Infrastructural Project (VIP), Agricultural Engineering (AE) and Rehabilitation of Irrigation Facilities (RIF).
Maj. Quashigah explained that, the VIP is aimed at reducing drudgery, making farming technically enjoyable and financially rewarding to attract the youth. The project, which was initiated in 1998, has been re-focused to concentrate resources in selected rural communities to make an effective impact.
He stated that the project had made considerable advances in building the capacity of the district assemblies, pilot area councils, communities and groups in the identification and preparation of development plans and the construction and maintenance of rural infrastructure.
The project has been able to recruit 3,400 district assembly staff and 191 area council members, who constitute opinion leaders in communities and have been trained in participatory planning. Also an amount of Â¢133 billion-worth of various sub-projects that facilitate agricultural production are under construction in all the 110 districts.
He added that, physical infrastructure sub-projects costing an estimated Â¢176 billion in rural water, transport and rural post-harvest have been initiated and are at various stages of implementation.
The ministry has been able to construct five dams, one irrigation system, 67 boreholes, one village pipe system, 74 small water pumps, 105 kilometers feeder roads and farm trucks, 68 markets,24 sets of processing equipment provided, 27 Intermediate Means of Transportation (IMTs) provided for farmers, 26 storage facilities and 15 village storage warehouses.
The minister said that efforts to promote agricultural engineering, assembly installation and trial of 46 tractors, as well as 72 power tillers, under the Japanese grant are also underway. Also included are motorized heavy-duty carrier tricycles, rice mills, corn shellers, manual tricycles and other agricultural machinery.
The engineering department of the ministry, in collaboration with the Dunkwa Gold Fields engineers, has also fabricated rice threshers, trailers, grain dryers, a rice mill a grain cleaner, a power reaper, a cassava grater and sugar cane crusher. These technologies have been introduced to other fabricators and farmers for onward duplication and usage.
The sector minister also suggested that ancient African practices should be wholly incorporated into the modern ways of achieving development in the country.
According to him local resources should not be discarded so quickly and that thorough research should be conducted for the realization of its full potentials.
"Don't throw your local resources away so quickly, let us investigate" the minister emphasized.
He noted that it is necessary to go back and adopt the practices of the good old days and to modernize them to suit current developments making rounds in the world.
"Sankofa! Let us go back to adopt what our grandmothers were doing," he advised.
In the area of packaging of products in the country, Maj. Quarshiegah observed that Ghana is greatly loosing, as packages of products on the market are not attractive to entice potential internal and external consumers.
He therefore encouraged producers to give their products eye-catching packages to enhance their marketing.
In education, the minister hinted of the possible change in the curriculum in the universities in the country. The curriculum, he noted, would inculcate the study of agribusiness, a tool to make students marketable in the sector.
This, he said, would improve on the agricultural practices in the country and thereby introduce food sufficiency as well.
As a result of the very scanty number of youth in agriculture, the ministry is also working vigorously to get more of the youth into the sector in order to increase food production in the country and also supporting the research institutions to produce high-yielding local produce on the market, the minister noted.
Major Quashigah further urged local producers to go into food production, processing and marketing to create jobs in the sector.

Original_Search_Title: Poor Marketing Hindering Products
Headline: Ghana: Poor Marketing Hindering Products
Body_Text:
Accra — Major Courage Quarshigah (Rtd), Minister of Food and Agriculture (MOFA), says Ghana is endowed with all the necessities, but development would only materialise if Ghanaians develop a taste for Ghanaian products and support them.
"We do not need anything from outside this country to survive as a nation and yet we have been so confused by the so-called foreign goods that we so readily condemn everything Ghanaian in favour of what other people elsewhere are producing."
The Minister said this when he took his turn at the meet the press series for state ministers in Accra yesterday to inform the public about their mission and achievements.
He identified poor marketing strategies including labelling and packaging as factors hindering the marketing of Ghana products both locally and on the international market.
He said in order to create market for farm produce in Ghana and achieve import substitution, the ministry is actively promoting the utilisation of selected crops through processing, value addition, packaging and presentation.
Major Quarshigah said MOFA seeks to promote sustainable agriculture, thriving agribusiness, and improved human livelihood through research and technology development. To accomplish the stated mission, MOFA is pursuing six main functions including; ensuring food security for Ghanaians, facilitating the production of agricultural commodities for export and facilitating the production of agriculture raw materials for industry.
The minister explained food security as, "good quality nutritious food, hygienically packaged and attractively presented, available in sufficient quantities all year round, and located at appropriate places at affordable prices". He said the ministry has therefore proposed the National Strategic Food Security Stock to keep food surplus produced in the country.
He said the stocked food would be supplied to the public in times of crisis, like floods, earthquakes, drought or would be used to stabilise food prices when there is a bad season in a particular year.
In line with this, MOFA supported the private sector to purchase and store two hundred and twenty thousand mini bags (11,000 tonnes) of white Grade 2 maize as buffer stock in 2001. He assured farmers of ready market and encouraged them to produce more.
He said the ministry with the assistance from FAO has initiated the Special Programme for Food Security (SPFS). Under the initiative, the ministry has developed 20 hectares of land for the cultivation of some cereal products, plantain and vegetables in selected districts, whilst under the Farmer Fields Schools (FFS), 330 farmer groups are being assisted to intensify their production in those areas.
He said as part of the national drive to promote the use of locally available raw materials in industries, funds have been provided for the purchase of four tonnes of sorghum seed for farmers in the Guinness Sorghum Projects in the three Northern Regions to help Guinness Ghana Ltd in their experiment to use sorghum for brewing Guinness.
He said to stem down problems associated with livestock development in Ghana, the ministry has had discussions with the Poultry Farmers Association and other stakeholders to develop strategies that would revitalise the poultry industry to make it economically viable to farmers.
He said in order to attract the youth into agricultural production, there should be incentives and facilities to entice them. He promised that MOFA would pursue measures in the various sub-sectors including rehabilitation of irrigation facilities, Village Infrastructure Project (VIP) and agricultural engineering to entice the youth into agricultural production.
The minister said to ensure uniformity and transaction efficiency in marketing, the marketing services unit of the Statistics Research and Information Directory (SRID) has been tasked to undertake a review exercise to promote the standardisation in agricultural produce marketing in Ghana.
The minister described as "half digested information" the allegation that last year, the government imported more rice into the country than it intended to do. He explained that not all the rice imported into the country was meant for Ghanaian consumption rather, the rice was only being passed through to the neighbouring countries of Burkina Faso and Cote d'Ivoire.

Original_Search_Title: Tracing the Roots of Waragi
Headline: Uganda: Tracing the Roots of Waragi
Body_Text:
Kampala — It has been given all kinds of names. Many people call it waragi. Others call it enguli. There are places where it is called enkangaali. Yet in some places it is known as andiro. It is also referred to as Lira-Lira. In Teso it is called ewaragi.
Some creative folks, claiming that waragi is curative, refer to it as Â¡Â¥choroquineÂ¡oe and sometimes Â¡Â¥quinineÂ¡oe.
At least every ethnic group in Uganda has coined a name for it. In Masaka and some areas of Buganda, the more concentrated brand of the potent gin has been Â¡Â¥baptisedÂ¡oe amandule or Kasese-Kasese, while the less concentrated type is called regular.
So where did this wonder drink originate?
Mzee Wakulira Ssemuju of Masaka says the history of the drink can be traced back to the days just after World War I had ended.
Â¡Â§When the World War I veterans returned to Uganda, they told stories of a Â¡Â¥war-ginÂ¡oe which they had tasted and learnt to make,Â¡Â¨ says Mzee Ssemuju.
Since the war attracted men from almost all parts of the country, they started making Â¡Â¥war-ginÂ¡oe; oblivious of the fact that they were actually introducing a drink that would forever posses the fair men and women of this land.
Mzee Ssemuju says the locals found the word Â¡Â¥war-ginÂ¡oe a mouthful, too hard to pronounce, and it ended being pronounced as waragi Â¡V a name it has taken up to date.
In neighbouring Kenya it is called changaa, and across Africa it is known by different names.
Today waragi is not only an important drink for low-income earners, it is also a source of income for a sizeable number of families in the country. It is probably the most easily affordable alcoholic drink around.
A tot of waragi goes for at least Shs 100 in most parts of the country, and today jerrycans upon jerrycans of enguli are transported into Kampala and other Ugandan towns from rural areas.
The raw material for its production varies from region to region and this could be the soft landing for its popularity.
In Buganda it is made from banana, in Teso it is made from cassava, in some areas it is made using sorghum and the urban brewers distil it from sugar molasses.
And thus, owing to the different raw materials used in different places, the smell, level of purity and alcohol concentration of the product may vary, but the Â¡Â¥knock-downÂ¡oe effect is more-or-less the same.
At a graduation party in Masaka recently, a woman whose son had just graduated said in her speech, Â¡Â§With five children to look after, it has not been easy to raise money for school fees. I have distilled waragi all my life to raise money to send these children to school.Â¡Â¨ The woman got what Americans would describe as a standing ovation.
Indeed, tales of how whole families have survived on waragi as a source of livelihood are not few.
But distilling enguli, transporting, consuming or being in possession of it or the equipment for making it is illegal, according to Ugandan laws. Chapter 96, Volume 3 of the Enguli (Manufacture and Licensing) Act states in part: Â¡Â§No person shall manufacture, sell or otherwise deal in enguli, have it in his possession, drink or have any apparatus that make enguliÂm.Â¡Â¨
Mr John Matovu, a lawyer in Masaka, told Sunday Monitor recently that any police officer can arrest any person at anytime in any place, if they are found to have broken the Â¡Â¥enguli lawÂ¡oe.
However, for some incomprehensible reason, the law seems to have been put aside. Waragi brewing seems to be on the increase, unabated.
Interestingly, the people who are supposed to arrest the lawbreakers are some of the faithful waragi drinkers.
Mr Joel Okwir, the Southern Regional CID Officer, said the problem is enormous because many police officers also take the forbidden drink.
Mr Okwir, however, speaks of a time when government used to issue licenses to some people to make waragi strictly to be sold to Uganda Distilleries Ltd.
Sunday Monitor has established that this actually happened in 1979 after a tragedy in which a number of people died after consuming some quantities of methanol in waragi.
It had become a habit by some brewers to add some methanol in the distillate to make it more concentrated.
Waragi is associated with a number of health problems. According to Dr Gertrude Nakigozi of Masaka Hospital, continued consumption of waragi can lead to heart and liver diseases like hepatitis, hypertension and bleeding from the gastrointestinal tract.
Dr Nakigozi also says waragi consumption blurs vision and can be responsible for low blood sugar and destruction of the brain.
During the days when law enforcement against distilling waragi was effective, people disappeared into the swamps or forests to prepare it. Today, however, a man will even announce to his friends that he will not attend the village council meeting because he will be distilling waragi.
It has even become normal these days for people to openly pledge a jerrycan or so of waragi as a contribution for a relativeÂ¡oes wedding ceremony. A parent in Buganda may demand some bottles of super as part of the bride price.
Mr Charles Irama, a prosecutor in the state attorneyÂ¡oes office in Masaka told Sunday Monitor, Â¡Â§I have been doing this work for the last six years, but I have not had any Â¡Â¥waragi caseÂ¡oe brought before me.Â¡Â¨
In some villages in Masaka, some micro-finance institutions lend money to women groups who brew waragi as a means of poverty alleviation.
Just as there were no records at Masaka Police Station of how many people have been arrested and prosecuted for distilling waragi during the last five years, there were also no figures of deaths directly linked to waragi consumption at Masaka Hospital.

Original_Search_Title: First Lady Gives Seeds to K'jong
Headline: Uganda: First Lady Gives Seeds to K'jong
Body_Text:
Kampala — MRS. Janet Museveni, wife of the president, has brought smiles of hope
to the famished faces of one Karimojong community with a donation of seeds
and agricultural equipment worth sh10m.
The donation is to benefit 150 households who are president Museveni's
'neighbours' under Morulinga Women Agricultural and environmental project,
27km west of Moroto town.
Museveni pitched camp in Morulinga near Kangole township from November
2001 to mid 2002, for community mobilisation and to launch the disarmament
exercise in Karamoja.
Dr. Robert Limlim, the UNICEF Deputy Country Director in Rwanda,
represented Janet Museveni as chief guest at the project launch and
hand-over ceremony on Monday.
The ceremony was, however, overshadowed by a wailing crowd desperate for
food.
The donation included 150 hoes, 150 jerrycans, 1,200 seedlings, 800kg of
Serena sorghum, 400kg of beans, 200kg of cowpeas, 600kg of maize seeds and
two bicycles.
It also included ploughing of 200 acres of land, through a donation of
2,000 litres of fuel form Shell and Total oil companies.
Margaret Lalam, Mrs. Museveni's personal assistant who delivered the
items, said the project would expand to cover all the 450 households.
She said it was an initiative by Mrs. Museveni to improve the welfare of
Morulinga community.
Mrs. Museveni said the project was the first of its kind in
Karamoja.
She said it was aimed at demonstrating modern agricultural methods to
the Karimojong so that they could become self-reliant.
"I am aware of your tremendous food demands and other social
problems.
My humble contribution to you is just a way of identifying with you and
putting our hands together for the good of our families and country,"
Mrs. Museveni said in her speech delivered by Limlim. It was punctuated by
many Karimojong proverbs.
She told Karimojong men to support the women in their efforts to
alleviate poverty and ensure food security.
She said it would help them avoid famine that the region has suffered
for the last two years.
Mrs. Museveni urged Karimojong women to actively participate in
reconciliation and peace building efforts.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Revamps Agriculture
Headline: Malawi: Govt Revamps Agriculture
Body_Text:
Blantyre, Malawi — Government has worked hard to improve the Agricultural sector because
the sector contributes 38 percent to the countryÂ¡Ãs economy.
Since 1994, the government has encouraged irrigation farming as a way of
averting food shortage when there is drought.
The government also encourages winter cropping, contract farming and a
civil service credit system.
However, the most significant has been the government initiated Starter
Pack Programme, which has benefited over 3 million households. As a result,
the forecasts predict that MalawiÂ¡Ãs maize production will be 1.9 million
metric tonnes compared to last yearÂ¡Ãs production of 1.6 million metric
tonnes.
The government has placed special interest in agriculture because it
contributes 90 percent of the countryÂ¡Ãs export earnings.
Malawi is an exporter of primary produce and net importer of industrial
goods. Its major exports include tobacco, tea, sugar and groundnuts.
Malawi adopted an export oriented growth strategy following independence
based on large scale agriculture and agro-based manufacturing using foreign
capital and managerial skills.
The economy was directed at supporting private and parastatal projects
in the productive agricultural sector through the provision of essential
infrastructure, utilities and supporting services.
Until the mid 1980s when structural adjustment reforms were introduced,
parastatals and Press Holdings Limited operated in all key sectors of the
economy, often enjoying monopoly positions.
Government control and regulation of the private sector was also
significant. The country enjoyed a high economic growth rate up until 1979,
driven by tobacco and tea exports.
GDP growth for the first 15 years of independence averaged six percent
every year.
Between 1979 and 1982, MalawiÂ¡Ãs economy went to recession. To get the
economy back on track, the government believed the smallholder agriculture
sector needed to grow, management of public resources needed improvement and
private and public sector wage controls had to be relaxed.
Further liberalization of the foreign exchange, an overhaul of the
monetary and tax systems and improved incentives for foreign and local
investors were also introduced.
Since the early 1990s, economic reforms aimed at, among other things,
attracting foreign investment, have been stepped up.
Price controls on all commodities have been eliminated and privatization
of the parastatal sector is well under way.
MalawiÂ¡Ãs main economic activity continues to be the agricultural
sector. The major exports include tobacco, sugar, groundnuts, rice, tea,
coffee and cotton. Other crops include maize, sorghum, millet, pulses root
crops and rubber.
The government has also been quick in helping those in dire need of food
because of natural calamities.
Food production in Malawi has been on the decline for the past two
seasons due to unprecedented flooding. With poor harvest, the majority began
to run out of food long before lean months of January to March every
year.
The government proceeded to import 255,000 metric tonnes of maize
through the National Food Reserve Agency (NFRA). Later government also gave
a boost to the Safety Nets Programme by putting MK113 million into the Cash
for Work, a Public Works Programme under the Malawi Social Action Fund
(MASAF).
The supplementary feeding programme for Under-Five children, lactating
mothers, pregnant women, school feeding programme, food for work assets and
food for disaster mitigation programme were carried out too.
Government will continue to improve agriculture in the country because
it generates most of the foreign exchange and employs the majority of the
labour force.

Original_Search_Title: UCE to Help Farmers Market Their Produce
Headline: Uganda: UCE to Help Farmers Market Their Produce
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A COMMODITY exchange, which started working in Kampala last month, is expected to open up opportunities for domestic, regional and global trade for Ugandan farmers.
Deborah Mwesigye, the UCE manager of mobilisations, said that, due to the lack of proper market information, some farmers invest in crops that do not reward them adequately, while other crops experience extreme price fluctuations. The UCE initiative is expected to offer farmers better prices for their produce based on market information.
Among the commodities being traded the UCE are coffee, cotton, maize, beans, groundnuts, soybeans, peas, sorghum, honey, vegetables, wheat, chillies, vanilla and okra. It is expected that the exchange will improve producer prices by leaving middlemen out of the marketing chain.
"There is a serious marketing problem, with price fluctuations, a paucity of market information and unscrupulous middlemen who offer farmers low farm-gate prices," said Alex Rwego, the UCE manager. Since it started operations early last month, the UCE has so far been involved in the sale of 50 tonnes of maize, 20 tonnes of soybeans, 30 tonnes of red bird chillies, 20 tonnes of sesame and 10 tonnes of pineapples.
Mr Rwego said: "The lack of market information is a significant impediment to market access for farmers. It increases transaction costs substantially and reduces market efficiency. For any crop, the marketing chain consists of multiple middlemen, taking a margin of the profits at each stage of the chain."
With the liberalisation of agriculture, the collapse of marketing boards and the withdrawal of government support to agricultural producers, there was a need for an institution to provide a convenient physical trading mechanism, he added. Zambia, Kenya, Egypt and South Africa are among the other African countries with commodity exchanges. Officials say middlemen increase the time and costs of sourcing agro-commodities from farmers.
"They transport small volumes of ungraded and varying quality produce, take risks and want to benefit from the buyers of the farm produce as well as farmers," said Ms Mwesigye.
She said UCE would manage market-price risks and its activities would evolve towards hedging against price risks and options. It would also provide an arbitration facility in case buyers or the farmers defaulted on their agreement.
"In Uganda, commodity prices can fluctuate by as much as 500 per cent. There is no mechanism of price-risk management in the agro-commodity trade. Farmers also lack access to market information that would enable them to make informed decisions, which is why they continue to grow certain crops in spite of continually declining market prices," Mrs Mwesigye added.
Uganda's agro-commodity market is characterised by high transaction costs, is poorly structured and experiences extreme fluctuations in prices the annual marketing seasons.
Intermediaries are accused of giving low farm prices to farmers, feeding them on inaccurate market information, thereby increasing transaction costs and lengthening the marketing channel. The UCE is expected to provide a market mechanism through which trade in agro-commodities can be conducted efficiently.
It will also shorten the marketing chain between the farm and the market, leading to a drop in transaction costs. At the UCE, buyers and sellers meet to set the terms of sale contracts for agro-commodities.
The commodity is then listed on the exchange. On the demand side, the buyers send information to the exchange about what they intend to buy. This information is then sent to farmers' groups or listed on the exchange for every potential supplier to bid. The buyer pays a listing fee of $3.
A food market in Kampala. Lack of market information has been a significant impediment to market access for farmers

Original_Search_Title: Government Urgently Needs US $832,000 for Farms
Headline: Central African Republic: Government Urgently Needs US $832,000 for Farms
Body_Text:
Bangui — The Central African Republic (CAR) government has asked for some US
$832,000 to purchase crop seeds, fertiliser and other agricultural materials
for at least 40,000 farmers, the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO)
reported on Monday.
It said the money was needed to buy 450 mt of maize, 1,933 mt of
groundnuts, 91 mt of rice, 69 mt of sorghum, 285 mt of squash, 10 mt of
beans and 1.98 billion cassava cuttings before the start of the planting
season in April-May. They would be planted on 31,000 ha of farmland
previously held by the rebels loyal to the former army chief of staff,
Francois Bozize.
Since 13 February, the government army has been engaged in a massive
offensive to flush out the rebels from the north and centre of the country,
which are areas of intensive agricultural activity.
The FAO said it would work with the UN World Food Programme in providing
hungry farmers with food aid so that they would not eat the seed crops.
Food prices in the CAR capital, Bangui, have risen considerably because
the farming areas had been cut off from it. Farming ground to a halt as
residents fled the fighting between Bozize's men and government
troops. Early in February, the FAO said it was concerned about farmers'
ability to plant crops this season as they were reported to have eaten their
seed crops.

Original_Search_Title: Pay More to Puff Cigars Or Fly, But Less for a Small Car
Headline: South Africa: Pay More to Puff Cigars Or Fly, But Less for a Small Car
Body_Text:
Cape Town — The Cuban-cigarsmoking, large-car-driving executive who flies a lot is
in trouble Finance Minister Trevor Manuel has imposed a massive increase in
duty on cigars, made small cars cheaper and increased airport departure
tax.
In imposing duty on tobacco products, Manuel has maintained the policy
that the consumption-tax burden on tobacco products should be 50% of the
retail selling price of the most popular brand for each category.
This means that cigarettes go up 38c for a packet of 20, or 11%;
cigarette tobacco R1,08 for 50g, or 21%; and pipe tobacco about 15c for a
25g-packet, or 11%.
Cigars go up a whopping 39%, with the duty on 23g of cigars increasing
from R17,63 to R24,52.
The ad valorem duty on motor cars is to be adjusted through the use of a
formula that distinguishes between the type and weight of vehicles,
translating into a lower excise duty on lower-priced cars. The decision was
taken after discussions with the motor vehicle manufacturing industry.
Airport departure tax has not been adjusted for inflation since its
introduction in 2001. The departure tax for destinations in Botswana,
Lesotho, Namibia and Swaziland will go up 10%, bringing it to R55. For all
other international destinations, a similar 10% increase is applied,
bringing the tax to R110.
The departure taxes will be implemented on July 1.
The duty on all alcoholic drinks with the exception of sorghum beer,
which retains the same duty of 7,8c/l goes up 10%-11%, or roughly last
year's inflation rate. This is about 3% more than the projected 7,7%
inflation rate for this year. It translates into about 4c more on a can of
beer, 9c/l for wine, 18c/l for sherry and port (fortified wines), 25c/l for
sparkling wine and R1,15 more on a bottle of spirits.
Manuel has also moved to close loopholes in the definitions of clear
beer and alcoholic fruit beverages.
"This could potentially be exploited with a view to paying lower excise
duties applicable to traditional sorghum beer in respect of clear lager beer
that contains sorghum as an ingredient.
"The revised definitions for alcoholic fruit beverages will also clarify
any existing uncertainty as to the correct duty applicable for news
alcoholic beverages released on to the market," the budget review says.
With the exception of the departure tax, all of the other duties come
into effect on April 1. All existing ad valorem duties, with the exception
of cars and computers, remain the same.
Distell corporate affairs director Andre Steyn said that while the
higher taxes on alcohol and spirits were almost expected, he applauded the
new taxing formula, which would eliminate the volatility of the price
increases.
Tobacco Institute CEO Francois van der Merwe said an increase of 38c in
excise was regrettable. Over a period of nine years, excise duties had
increased nearly 400%.
He said that although government had not deviated from its excise tax
policy on tobacco products, the increase placed further pressure on the
disposable income of consumers.
This could lead to consumers moving away from legal tobacco products to
illegal, untaxed products. This would not only affect the legal tobacco
industry's volumes negatively, but would slowly erode government's tobacco
tax base.
National Council Against Smoking director Peter Ucko said Manuel did a
"good job" in raising taxes on tobacco products as higher prices were one of
the most useful means to get people to stop smoking.

Original_Search_Title: Smokers, Drinkers Hit Where It Hurts
Headline: South Africa: Smokers, Drinkers Hit Where It Hurts
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Smokers and drinkers have again been hit where it hurts by this
year's Budget - in their pockets.
As has come to be expected from the Finance Ministry, it has targeted
cigarettes and alcohol as increased sources of revenue again in this
year's national Budget. The changes will bring in roughly an extra
R907m to government coffers.
From 1 March, a box of cigarettes will cost the smoker 11% more,
increasing the excise tax to R3.89 per pack of 20. The total tax on
tobacco products has been maintained at 50% of the retail price of
the most popular brand of each of cigarettes, cigarette tobacco,
cigars and pipe tobacco. Among those products, cigars have been
targeted the most heavily, with a 39% hike in the excise duty.
Drinkers of unfortified and sparkling wine will see the highest
increase among the alcohol products - an 11% rise, while sorghum beer
and flour duties have been left unchanged for the second year in a
row.
Taxes on fortified wine and spirits, as well as beer and cider have
been increased by 10%.
The SA Receiver of Revenue (SARS) said the taxation of alcohol
products as it was now structured, was the result of a two-year
process of consultation with stakeholders. On average, 43% of the
retail price of spirits is paid on tax, 33% of beer and 23% of wine.

Original_Search_Title: North to Sell $8m Food
Headline: Uganda: North to Sell $8m Food
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE World Food Programme (WFP) has launched a programme to buy food for
relief from eight districts in northern Uganda.
The Food for Assets and Market Support Programme will buy over 9,000
metric tones of maize, beans, sorghum and millet from the region for over
three years.
The food would be procured at cost of over US$8m projected to go
directly to about 52,000 farmers.
WFP programme officers Elvis Odeke and Dorothy Kanyomozi introduced the
programme to Lira district leaders recently.
They said the programme would enable poor families gain assets and help
households depending on degraded natural resources to get food for more
sustainable livelihoods.
Sixty farmer groups will also supply food to WFP under the
programme.

Original_Search_Title: Smokers, Alcohol Drinkers to Dig Deep Into Their Pockets
Headline: South Africa: Smokers, Alcohol Drinkers to Dig Deep Into Their Pockets
Body_Text:
Pretoria — Finance minister Trevor Manuel has told imbibers and smokers they will
be coughing up more, as taxes on alcohol and tobacco products will be
increased further this year.
In his budget speech in Cape Town today, the minister said increases in
some of the indirect taxes would bring about additional revenue of
approximately R907-million into the country's reserves.
The Minister said the cost of beer and ciders will be raised by ten
percent, bringing the excise duty to 48 cents per 340ml or an average
increase of 4.4 cents per can.
According to Minister Manuel, duties on unfortified and sparkling wine
would be raised by 11 percent and duties on fortified wine would increase by
ten percent while duties on spirits would also increase by ten percent, to
bring the average total duty to R13 per bottle.
Meanwhile, the Minister also revealed that, for the second year running,
sorghum beer and sorghum flour duties would not be increased.
Minister Manuel further explained that taxes on tobacco products would
go up by 11 percent, thus increasing the excise tax to R3, 89 per packet of
20s cigarettes.
The minister said South Africa's economy registered improved growth last
year and that projections indicated that the economy would continue to
experience growth.
'The 2003/2004 budget gives priority to reducing poverty and
vulnerability,' he said.

Original_Search_Title: New Armyworm Outbreak Feared
Headline: Zimbabwe: New Armyworm Outbreak Feared
Body_Text:
WHILE a recent outbreak of armyworm has been contained in most areas, there are still fears that the deadly plant eater will re-emerge soon as long there are dry weather conditions to promote its occurrence, an official in the Department of Agriculture Research and Extension (Arex), has said.
Dr Shadreck Mlambo, Arex director of research said in an interview: "We are on top of the situation now as the pest has gone into pupation, a stage where it is inactive. Our fear is, however, that the pest will soon emerge as an adult fly, giving rise to a new generation of armyworm which will attack young plants."
The worm, which moves in large numbers, destroys pastures and young maize, rapoko and millet sorghum plants.
Mlambo said it was likely that there would be a new outbreak especially in the lowveld as the current temperatures were conducive to an armyworm outbreak.
He said: "There is danger that the new generation of armyworm will attack young plants. It looks like armyworm outbreaks would continue until May in the highveld and June in the lowveld."
He said the department continued to receive reports of more outbreaks of armyworm in Harare.
Mlambo said: "We have remained in a high state of preparedness for more outbreaks. We have distributed chemicals to strategic points in case there are more outbreaks."
The government last week set aside $42 million for equipment and chemicals to wipe out the menace.
The armyworm, first reported this season in Guruve last month, last week invaded almost all provinces in the country except Matabeleland.
More than 400 hectares under maize and 1 000ha of pasture were destroyed by the armyworm.
There are fears that the armyworm outbreak makes crops more vulnerable as they are already under severe threat from drought.
The armyworm, which originates from Mozambique and Zambia, is the pupa stage of the noctud insect which only breeds in Zimbabwe in drought years. The last serious armyworm outbreak was in 1991/1992 farming season but the worst in the history of Zimbabwe was in the 1981/1982 year.

Original_Search_Title: Cholera Under Control, But Crop Fears Continue
Headline: Mozambique: Cholera Under Control, But Crop Fears Continue
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Humanitarian workers in Mozambique are cautiously optimistic that a
cholera outbreak is under control, following a drop in recorded cases since
the beginning of this year.
World Health Organisation (WHO) disease control officer Pierre Kahozi
told IRIN there had been 10 deaths since the beginning of this year,
compared with at least 20 for the same period last year.
Case numbers peaked during the recent heavy rains to an average of 450
per month, affecting the provinces of Cabo Delgado, Nampula and Niassa in
the north and Sofala and Manica in the centre of the country.
"The situation is not normal because cholera is never normal, but it is
under control," Kahozi said.
He said NGOs and the government were still worried about the disease and
were providing clean water and increasing the number of cholera treatment
centres.
World Vision (WV), an NGO working with the government to control the
spread of the disease, said that a recent report noted that only 37 percent
of Mozambique's population had access to potable water.
The organisation said that seven new boreholes it had constructed
through Food for Work programmes in Mutarara district in the centre of the
country had become vital to the communities faced with the difficulty of
accessing fresh water.
However, while health authorities have breathed a sigh of relief at the
reduction in cholera cases, the heavy rains have brought renewed fears for
next year's crop in the north.
WV said that provinces in the north were bracing themselves for even
more rains, with up to 100,000 people said to need help with food and
shelter in both Nampula and Zambezia provinces.
WV's agriculture department said a large section of the target
population for its food security project in Nampula lost their cashew trees,
and roads and bridges were destroyed.
WV Mozambique is currently looking at funds for road repair and
rehabilitation, as one of the gravest concerns was access to these
communities and their market routes.
Conversely, in the south of the country, drought has led the Ministry of
Agriculture to concede that the next planting season has been
compromised.
"In the recently readjusted disaster contingency plan, the National
Institute for Disaster Management fears that the number of people affected
by 'pockets of hunger' could double from the current 700,000," WV said.
A recent report by the Famine Early Warning Systems Network said that
even drought-tolerant crops such as sorghum and millet, and recently planted
cassava, were showing signs of stress. Inhambane, Gaza and Maputo provinces
in the south have been the most severely affected and parts of Sofala, Tete
and Manica also faced a severe reduction in first season production.
The World Food Programme aims to reach 440,000 of the 655,000 people
identified for food aid in recent assessments.

Original_Search_Title: Opec Donates Funds for Food Crisis
Headline: Southern Africa: Opec Donates Funds for Food Crisis
Body_Text:
London — The United Nations World Food Programme
(WFP) has received a further 400,000 US dollars for its emergency
work in Mozambique from the organisation of petroleum exporting
countries (OPEC).
The WFP announced that it has received 9.2 million dollars
from the OPEC Fund for International Development for emergency
food aid operations in southern Africa, broken down as follows:
Mozambique 400,000 dollars; Lesotho 400,000; Malawi 2.1 million;
Swaziland 200,000; Zambia 1.8 million; Zimbabwe 4.3 million. It
has also received money from OPEC for its operations in West
Africa (300,000 dollars) and Eritrea (1.4 million dollars).
According to WFP the donation will be used to buy critical
supplies of vegetable oil and corn soya blend (CSB) to give to 15
million vulnerable people in southern Africa.
The OPEC donation follows the announcement from Britain that
is has given about 3.4 million dollars to WFP emergency work in
Zambia. The WFP appeal has now been promised 72 percent of its
507 million dollar appeal to deal with the hunger crisis in
southern Africa.
However, even if WFP manages to carry out its full programme
in Mozambique, this will not be sufficient to cope with the
growing emergency needs in the country.
The Famine Early Warning Systems Network, FEWS NET, has
recently issued a dire analysis that insufficient and erratic
rains over the last three months will result in an extremely poor
harvest throughout southern Mozambique, and in parts of the
central provinces.
FEWS NET has warned that the staple crop of maize will be
largely lost in drought affected areas, and beans and groundnuts
will also be severely affected. Even drought tolerant crops such
as sorghum and millet, and recently planted cassava, are showing
signs of stress.
According to FEWS NET, there is a large gap between food aid
needs and the planned response. WFP had planned to get food aid
to 440,000 Mozambicans in January, but only managed to help
283,202 people. This is just 43 percent of the current estimate
for the number of people in need, which stands at 654,865.
Predictions for the near future are even more worrying. The
current projections are that, in the commercial year that begins
in April 2003, the number of people in need of food aid will rise
to 1.475 million.
Meanwhile, the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO)
has warned that so far it has raised less than half the 25
million dollars it needs to improve self reliance through
supplying tools, seeds and know-how to households that
are suffering from hunger in southern African.
FAO has warned that drought, food shortages and AIDS have
cut the possibilities of a good harvest, threatening the lives of
15 million people in Zambia, Malawi, Mozambique, Zimbabwe,
Swaziland and Lesotho. Yet donors have only provided 10 million
dollars for its work in the region.

Original_Search_Title: State Injects $42m to Fight Armyworm
Headline: Zimbabwe: State Injects $42m to Fight Armyworm
Body_Text:
THE government yesterday released $42 million to purchase chemicals and equipment to spray maize crops and pasture invaded by armyworm.
Almost all provinces in the country except Matabeleland North and South are infested by the deadly plant eater.
The worm was spotted in the country for the first time in Guruve last month from where it has now spread to most parts of the country.
The worm, which moves in large numbers, destroys plants such as maize, rapoko, millet sorghum as well as pasture.
According to the Plant Protection Unit of the Department of Agricultural Research and Extension (Arex), more outbreaks of the armyworm were being reported with latest invasions in Gokwe and Harare this week.
Dr Shadreck Mlambo, Arex research acting director said yesterday: "The outbreak is now being treated as a national problem but not yet a disaster. The $42 million will be used to buy chemicals, sprayers and protective clothing to wipe out the menace."
Mlambo said the $42 million was enough to deal with the current problem and that the government would release more money should the situation worsen.
"We want to make sure that no food deficit will happen as a result of the armyworm. We do not see the problem persisting beyond three weeks as we will have wiped out the armyworm within this period.
"Today (yesterday) provincial Arex heads came to collect pesticide and equipment to spray affected fields and pastures."
Mlambo said the department would spray all infested areas in communal, new resettlement areas and at large-scale commercial farms.
This week the department had been quoted as saying it would only spray in communal areas and in farms occupied by A1 farmers (those resettled under the villagised model) only while large-scale commercial farmers and farmers resettled under the commercial farming scheme were encouraged to purchase their own chemicals.
On wether suppliers of chemicals had enough stocks to control the pest, Mlambo said: "We have already made invoices enough for our requirements so far."
There are concerns that the worm, which only attacks plants below knee level will wipe out the early planted maize crop resulting in serious food shortages.
The maize crop is already under severe threat from drought. To date the armyworm has destroyed more than 400 hectares of maize and 1 000ha of pasture.
Considerable damage has also been inflicted on young sorghum, rapoko and millet plants. The worm, which originates from Mozambique and Zambia, is bred by an insect called Noctud and it is only bred in Zimbabwe in drought years.
The last serious armyworm outbreak was in 1991/1992 farming season but the worst in the history of Zimbabwe was during the 1981/1982 year.

Original_Search_Title: Famine spreads across Africa
Headline: Africa: Famine spreads across Africa
Body_Text:
New York —  With 38 million facing starvation, "business as usual will not do"
On a scale not seen in Africa in nearly two decades, famine is once
again stalking the continent. According to estimates by the UN's World Food
Programme (WFP), as many as 38 million Africans are living under the threat
of starvation, and many will succumb if emergency relief does not reach them
in time. As of mid-2002, famine conditions were concentrated mainly in
Southern Africa, but by the end of the year they had emerged just as
severely in the Horn of Africa, and on a lesser scale in several countries
in West and Central Africa .
"This is an unprecedented crisis, which calls for an unprecedented
response," WFP Executive Director James Morris warned the UN Security
Council on 3 December, during a session devoted to considering Africa's food
crisis as a threat to peace and security. "The magnitude of the disaster
unfolding in Africa has not been fully grasped by the international
community.... An exceptional effort is urgently needed if a major
catastrophe is to be averted. Business as usual will not do."
Mr. Morris and other participants in the Security Council debate pointed
to a variety of factors contributing to the current crisis:
- drought and other difficult weather conditions in many of the affected
countries, bringing low harvests and driving up the price of food
- the debilitating impact of HIV/AIDS, which leaves those infected less
able to stave off the ravages of hunger and weakens local farming systems by
killing off millions of Africa's most productive farmers
- armed conflict or political strife, as in CÃ´te d'Ivoire, the
Democratic Republic of Congo, Sudan and Zimbabwe, and the difficulties
confronting countries only recently emerging from conflict, including
Angola, Ethiopia, Eritrea and Sierra Leone
- inadequate economic policies, especially in agriculture, which in many
affected countries have brought too little investment in farming inputs,
rural infrastructure or essential social services ? problems compounded by
the poor prices African farm exports fetch on the world market.
For relief organizations, the most immediate challenge is mobilizing
enough food, medical care and other assistance to prevent massive loss of
life in the famine-stricken countries. This will not be easy, and pledges
have been lagging well behind needs. Yet, as WFP Deputy Executive Director
Jean-Jacques Graisse emphasized on 16 December, during the launch of an
international "Africa Hunger Alert" campaign, "Progress is possible, if the
political will is there."
The underlying factors contributing to Africa's recurrent cycles of
famine also highlight the need for greater attention to long-term strategies
to promote development and peace. "Just shipping in food is not enough," UN
Secretary-General Kofi Annan stated in a 9 December address at New York's
Columbia University that focused on women, AIDS and the Southern African
famine.
Drought and infection
In the seven most severely affected countries of Southern Africa, nearly
16 million people are in urgent need of food aid. Drought is the most
immediate reason. Earlier hopes that sufficient rain would fall in time for
the 2002/03 planting season, after poor harvests in early 2002, have now
been dashed.
According to the Famine Early Warning Systems (FEWS) Network of the US
Agency for International Development, important grain-producing areas of
South Africa, Zimbabwe and Mozambique experienced "substantially inhibited"
rainfall during the last months of 2002. Although South Africa itself is not
threatened by famine, the UN regional office in Johannesburg noted that
inadequate rain for the country's maize, wheat, sunflower, sorghum and soya
crops will have a serious impact beyond its borders, since it is the main
food exporter to the rest of the region.
In his 9 December address, Mr. Annan noted that most of the Southern
African countries now hit by drought are also battling serious AIDS
epidemics. "This is no coincidence: AIDS and famine are directly linked."
One way they are linked, he pointed out, is through the role of Africa's
women, who provide most agricultural labour and have long been at the centre
of communities' efforts to adapt to famine conditions. Now, however, "as
AIDS is eroding the strength of Africa's women, it is eroding the skills,
experience and networks that kept their families and communities going."
Therefore, Mr. Annan stated, the international community "will have to
combine food assistance and new approaches to farming with treatment and
prevention of HIV/AIDS." Among other things, this will require integrating
HIV and famine early-warning and analysis systems, the introduction of new
agricultural techniques appropriate to depleted workforces, renewed efforts
to wipe out the stigma of HIV, and innovative and large-scale efforts to
care for and support the most vulnerable, especially orphans and other young
people in AIDS-stricken communities. "Above all," Mr. Annan said, "this new
international effort must put women at the centre of our strategy to fight
AIDS."
'Zero tolerance'
Political strife has further complicated the situation. In Southern
Africa, Zimbabwe accounts for the greatest number of people affected, 6.7
million, due to a staggering cereal deficit of 1.5 mn tonnes. Although
drought has been the main cause of Zimbabwe's poor harvests, analysts have
also pointed to the impact of political tensions and the government's
controversial land reform policies. After members of Zimbabwe's ruling party
seized some WFP food stocks in October for distribution to party supporters,
Mr. Annan reaffirmed the UN's "zero tolerance" policy against distributing
food on the basis of political affiliation.
In Angola, the signing of a peace agreement in April 2002 has brought a
dramatic decline in that country's long civil war. Ironically, however, this
has led to an increase in the amount of food and other relief assistance
required, since hundreds of thousands of Angolans previously beyond the
reach of relief agencies can now be assisted. At the beginning of 2002, the
WFP was feeding about 1 million Angolans, a number that climbed to 1.8
million by early December. By the turn of the year, an additional 100,000
Angolan refugees were expected to begin returning home from Zambia and the
Democratic Republic of Congo.
The WFP warns that relief pledges have not kept pace with Angola's
mounting requirements. In October, the agency appealed for $241 mn to help
feed about 1.5 million beneficiaries at that time. As of late December, only
about a third of the amount had been pledged. With no further pledges, the
food in the WFP's pipeline will run out by March. And by that point, the
number of Angolans needing food aid could well climb to between 2.1 and 2.4
million.
'Poverty is at the root'
Serious famine conditions have also developed in the Horn of Africa,
principally Ethiopia and Eritrea, just two years after the end of a
devastating war between the two countries. The UN, Ethiopian government,
relief agencies and non-governmental organizations (NGOs), after assessing
the full impact of Ethiopia's inadequate and erratic rainfall, estimate that
some 11.3 million people require more than 1.4 mn tonnes of food aid through
mid-2003, with another 3 million in need of close monitoring (out of a total
population of 67 million). A joint UN-Ethiopian appeal, issued on 7
December, warned that the crisis could reach the magnitude of Ethiopia's
1984/85 famine, which claimed around 1 million lives.
A subsequent FEWS assessment noted that conditions may actually be worse
than in Ethiopia's last major famine. Some 3-5 million poor rural Ethiopians
are chronically unable to feed themselves, even in good years.  Many others
have very low household grain stocks, following previous poor harvests. As a
result, many more people now need food aid than in 1984/85, when 8 million
required relief. As elsewhere in Africa, notes the FEWS report, the high
prevalence of HIV/AIDS is increasing destitution, lowering labour
productivity and eroding traditional coping mechanisms.
Ethiopia's high external debt of around $6 bn also hampers commercial
food imports, requiring annual debt servicing payments of more than $160
mn. In addition, private commercial creditors are demanding some $500 mn
from Ethiopia, much of it for businesses nationalized under the previous
military regime. "Whether these claims are legally right or wrong, Ethiopia
can't afford to pay," argues Mr. Justin Forsyth, head of policy for Oxfam,
which has joined with other non-governmental organizations to press for
greater debt relief to help the country through its emergency.  On top of
these problems, the UN-Ethiopia appeal observed that "a drop in the
international price of Ethiopia's main cash crop ? coffee ? has reduced the
government's ability to provide additional cash resources to the crisis."
Ethiopian Prime Minister Meles Zenawi, while announcing the joint
emergency appeal, emphasized the importance of tackling such fundamental
deficiencies "not after the emergency has passed, but in conjunction with
addressing the emergency. We need to develop strategies to fight poverty,
which is at the root of the problem."
'Stark reality' in Eritrea
In neighbouring Eritrea, an estimated 1 million people need emergency
food aid, nearly a third of its total population of 3.3 million. Again, the
most immediate factor is a severe, prolonged drought, the worst since the
country gained its independence from Ethiopia in 1993. There has been a
near-total crop failure in Barentu, which normally produces about 80 per
cent of the country's sorghum, Eritrea's staple cereal. WFP officials in
Asmara, the Eritrean capital, report that child malnutrition and school
dropout rates are increasing, and that in some districts up to one-fifth of
the livestock has already died.
In October, UN Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and
Emergency Relief Coordinator Kenzo Oshima toured camps for internally
displaced people who cannot return home because their land has been mined or
their homes were destroyed during Eritrea's recent war with Ethiopia.
"Right now, Eritreans need to commit many of their resources to coping with
the residual effects of war, but can't because of the drought." Therefore,
he said, food aid must be complemented by programmes for post-war
reconstruction, the safe return of refugees and internally displaced people,
the removal of landmines and poverty reduction. "If issues like these are
not addressed, we may well find ourselves with a similar emergency on our
hands in a few years," Mr. Oshima warned.
In November, the UN issued a $163 mn relief and rehabilitation appeal
for Eritrea, to cover the country's projected needs in 2003. Some $56 mn of
that amount would be earmarked for repatriation, landmine removal, shelter,
health, education, water, HIV/AIDS and other programmes. Fulfilling those
needs may be an uphill struggle, however. As of late December, only a bare
$9 mn had been pledged towards the request for $105 mn in food aid ?
usually the portion of an appeal that donors are most willing to support.
"The prospect of thousands starving is a stark reality," commented Mr.
Patrick Buckley, the WFP's representative in Asmara. "Ships carrying food
aid from abroad take months to arrive ? considering the magnitude of the
crisis at hand, each day is critical."
War, and more war
Conflict has been a major factor in a number of Africa's other
emergencies.  In neighbouring Sudan, two decades of civil war have left some
2.9 million Sudanese dependent on food aid. If an October 2002 agreement
between the government and the main southern rebel group leads to a
cease-fire, then a "new chapter" could open up for humanitarian assistance
in Sudan, says Mr.  Oshima, by making it possible for relief agencies to
reach previously inaccessible groups of people. This, in turn, will raise
aid requirements further.
In Central Africa, where drought generally has not been a factor, large
groups of refugees or internally displaced people also need relief aid,
including in the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda and Congo Republic.
In West Africa, there are two clusters of countries afflicted by famine, for
distinctly different reasons. Five countries in the arid Sahel zone (Cape
Verde, Gambia, Mali, Mauritania and Senegal) have a combined total of more
than half a million people suffering from the effects of drought. The WFP
estimates that another 791,000, mainly refugees and internally displaced
people, require emergency food aid in four countries along the southwestern
coast that have been mired in conflict: Liberia, Sierra Leone, Guinea and
CÃ´te d'Ivoire.
This, however, may underestimate the full impact of the Ivorian crisis,
which erupted suddenly and massively into virtual civil war in
September.
According to an interagency "flash appeal" issued by the UN Office for
the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) in late November, there may
be as many as 1.5 million internally displaced people in CÃ´te d'Ivoire, plus
another 1.2 million "war affected" Ivorians. These figures do not count
foreign residents (mainly from Burkina Faso and Mali) who had to be
evacuated back home or refugees who had previously fled to CÃ´te d'Ivoire
because of conflicts in their own countries (mainly Liberia and Sierra
Leone). If all categories of people affected by conflict in CÃ´te d'Ivoire
and its neighbours are included, OCHA estimates that the total could well
surpass 4 million, although not everyone would require international food
aid.
Mobilizing opinion
The escalation of emergency relief needs in Africa comes at a time of
increasing difficulty in securing the necessary funds and food aid. The
WFP's director, Mr. Morris, notes that in the 1980s the agency was able to
devote three-quarters of its operations to long-term agricultural
development programmes. But as donors pulled back from financing such
activities - and the number of conflicts in Africa proliferated - the
emphasis shifted largely to emergency relief, which now accounts for about
80 per cent of the WFP's budget.
Yet, Mr. Morris adds, funding for such relief operations has itself been
declining. Out of a total budget of $1.4 bn for the agency's activities in
Africa over the next year, only about half was pledged as of early
December. By the end of the month, new donations from Japan, Germany, Canada
and the African Development Bank brought the response to the agency's
Southern Africa appeal (excluding Angola) to $317 mn, but this was still a
third short of the region's total requirement through March 2003.
With the goal of stimulating greater international solidarity with
Africa, the WFP has been working with other relief agencies and NGOs to
highlight the continent's critical food situation. In early December, the
WFP, US Agency for International Development and 15 US humanitarian relief
organizations met in Baltimore. They launched a global campaign to help the
millions of Africans today facing famine. "We appeal to governments,
citizens' groups, private voluntary organizations, religious institutions
and individual citizens to recognize the enormity of the crisis confronting
Africa and to join in a massive and urgent response."
The WFP's "Africa Hunger Alert," launched on 16 December, is only part
of that campaign. A concert, dedicated to the African famine and attended by
1,200 people, was held in Tokyo on 9 December. Colleges and secondary and
primary schools in Ontario, Canada, the US state of Montana and other
__cpLocations have organized vigils, fasts and similar actions to generate
support for Africa. Lobbying efforts have been launched in Hong Kong to
press local governments to contribute to famine relief.
At the moment, these are isolated expressions of concern, notes the
WFP's Mr. Graisse. But he expects them to intensify and spread as the scale
of the African crisis becomes more apparent. "If we are to avert starvation
in Africa, ordinary citizens have an important role to play," says Mr.
Graisse. "It's critical they join the campaign and urge their governments to
address the needs of the hungry now before it is too late."

Original_Search_Title: The Economy in 2003
Headline: Zimbabwe: The Economy in 2003
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — During the course of 2003, Zimbabwe promises to provide
a classic case study on whether economic necessity forces political
change. There are two distinct viewpoints at the start of the year.
The business and economic community cannot believe that an economy
whose GDP has shrunk by 27 per cent in just four years can survive a
fifth year of such punishment.
But there are many in the political establishment, in some of the
banks and in businesses close to the ruling Zanu-PF party and even in
the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe, to judge from its public statements,
who take a very different view.
They believe that the worst effects of land resettlement have now
passed and that the "new" policies outlined in the November 2002
budget, mark the turning point in the economy.
There is little hard evidence to support the view that the economy
has reached a turning point. Without exception, all the macroeconomic
indicators (see table) paint a picture of not only a rapid but also
an accelerating economic decline. Hopes of a great leap forward in
agriculture are being contradicted by declining sales of seed and
fertilisers; by anecdotal reports of the smaller area planted with
crops; and by the forecast of drought conditions in the second half
of the 2002/03 growing season.
There is no purely economic solution to the Zimbabwe crisis. At
independence in 1980, President Robert Mugabe's government inherited
a going concern, frequently termed the "jewel of Africa" because of
its diverse natural resource base, its strong infrastructure and
institutions and, certainly by African standards, a well-educated and
well-trained workforce. But the government failed to deliver on
economics - so much so, in fact, that per capita income is lower now
than at independence.
The table below shows that the economy actually performed better
under economic sanctions (1965-1980), including the period of the
liberation war (1973-1980), than during the post-independence period.
It shows too that the economy grew twice as fast during the 1980s
when the government pursued command economy policies than in the
period of structural reform (1990-1998).
The 1997 watershed Four events in 1997/98 marked a watershed in the
Zimbabwean economy, by setting in motion the forces responsible for
the steep decline in output that began in 1999 (see table):
The unbudgeted Z$5,2 billion payout to "war veterans" The
announcement of a policy of compulsory land acquisition without
compensation for the expropriation of the land, to replace the
previous "willing buyer, willing seller" scheme Zimbabwe's entry to
the civil war in the DemocraticRepublic of Congo in support of the
government of President Laurent Kabila The adoption in late 1999 of
the Millennium Economic Recovery Programme (MERP) that signalled the
end of a largely unsuccessful period (1991-1998) of half-hearted
macroeconomic and structural reforms, which were supported by the
donor community, including the World Bank and the International
Monetary Fund.
Both the new land resettlement strategy, which became the Fast Track
Land Reform in 2000, and the war veterans' payments were politically
inspired.
By the late 1990s, the Mugabe administration had concluded that its
ruling Zanu-PF party was rapidly losing popularity. The government's
defeat in the February 2000 constitutional referendum and its
subsequent narrow victory in the general election in June 2000
convinced President Robert Mugabe that victory in the March 2002
presidential poll depended crucially on both accelerating and
expanding the land redistribution programme.
The end of economic reform It soon became evident that no economic
price was too high for the government's political survival.
Accordingly, the government's ambivalent and half-hearted commitment
to macroeconomic and structural reforms, embodied in the flawed
Economic Structural Adjustment Programme (ESAP) of 1991-1995 and its
successor ZIMPREST (1996-1999), was replaced by a phased return to
controls and a command economy.
The government had never really had a whole-hearted commitment to
ESAP.
After an initial burst of liberalisation in trade, price, wage and
interest and exchange-rate policies, the flawed reform programme was
soon blown off course by the severe drought in 1991-92 and growing
recognition that the government had simply undertaken too grandiose a
reform programme for its limited and rapidly deteriorating
institutional capacity.
The fact that ESAP itself, as well as two subsequent standby
agreements with the IMF in 1998 and 1999, had remained on the policy
agenda for as long as they did, was a tribute to the efforts of a
handful of government technocrats.
It also reflected the delusional approach of the Bretton Woods
institutions
that, as late as 1998-99, continued to insist that reform was "on
track".
Since 1999 the government has been unable to design a coherent
economic strategy. Political survival, through land redistribution,
has topped the agenda. Structural reform, apart from land
resettlement, has all but been abandoned, though the administration
still pays lip service to privatisation and commercialisation.
Privatisation is invariably seen as a vehicle for "indigenisation"
(black economic empowerment).
Though successive finance ministers have committed themselves to
restoring macroeconomic stability, their ability to do so has been -
and still is - severely constrained by the demands made initially by
the military involvement in the DRC, and by the subsequent
imperatives of political survival and land resettlement.
Easy money fuels inflation The implications for economic policy are
devastating. The 2003 budget, presented in November 2002, maintains
that triple-digit inflation (175% year-on-year in November 2002) will
be cured by a combination of massively negative real interest rates,
an overvalued exchange rate, fiscal laxity and sweeping price
controls. The finance minister maintained that this policy stance
would deliver double-digit inflation by the end of 2003 and
single-digit after that (see graph).
The budget promised "some increase in the general structure of
interest rates" to contain monetary growth to levels consistent with
these inflation goals.
To this end, the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe announced a two-tier
monetary policy that would make exporters eligible to borrow from
banks at a 5% interest rate, while the "productive sector" borrowed
at 15% from a revolving fund of Z$25 billion.
Borrowers outside these two categories could access funds from the
banking system at market-determined rates. However, because the
authorities have continued to pump liquidity into the system, the
promised "general increase" in rates has not materialised. In fact,
as inflation has escalated, real interest rates have become more, not
less, negative.
This is a classic catch-22 situation for the authorities. A tight
monetary policy with substantially higher interest rates is
politically unthinkable.
The new farmers would be starved of credit - more so than they are
already; government's domestic debt-service costs would become
unmanageable; many highly geared businesses would face bankruptcy;
and the recent surge in black empowerment-leveraged buyouts, often by
the politically well-connected, would be threatened.
The alternative is rampant growth in the money supply, which will
fuel hyperinflation, create asset price bubbles in the equity and
real-estate markets, and lead to unremitting downward pressure on the
exchange rate in the parallel market.
Events since the November 2002 budget suggest that, given the
dominance of politics, the government has chosen hyperinflation.
Currency in circulation increased by 153% in six months, rising to
Z$89 billion in November 2002 from Z$35 billion in May. Over the same
period, the year-on- year inflation rate accelerated to 175,5% from
122,3%.
By November the quarterly inflation rate (annualised) had risen to
450%, suggesting that the IMF's forecast of 522,2% average inflation
in 2003 could turn out to be conservative.
Price controls extended These developments left the authorities with
two inadequate instruments for controlling inflation - price controls
and an overvalued exchange rate. In the final weeks of 2002, the
ministry of Industry and International Trade gazetted price controls
covering hundreds of items, ranging from basic necessities (maize
meal, sugar, salt, bread, meat and milk) to school uniforms and
children's books.
The business response was predictable. Some firms have simply ignored
the order and paid spot fines to price-control inspectors; others
have withdrawn products which continue to be traded in the
underground market. The staple food, maize meal, sells informally at
between Z$1 500 and Z$2 500 for ten kilograms - ten times the
official price. One of the country's largest supermarket chains
estimates that in six weeks alone, it incurred losses of over Z$100
million because of the price controls. The two largest supermarket
groups have withdrawn products such as beef from their outlets
because of the losses they would incur by selling the product at the
controlled price.
Price controls will make published consumer inflation figures
increasingly meaningless, enabling the authorities to claim that the
policy is working.
Official prices will be used to calculate inflation, even in cases
where products are not available or where they are being traded at
much higher prices in the black market.
The cost of such a strategy is certain to be devastating, resulting
in the widespread closure of businesses and retrenchments. For this
reason, business executives say the price freeze, in its present
form, is unlikely to last beyond the first quarter of 2003. The
assumption is that the authorities could agree to replace it with a
system of approved margins and mark-ups, that would delay but not
prevent price increases.
Accordingly, as long as fiscal and monetary policy fuel and
accommodate inflation, the price freeze is likely to be no more than
a short-lived respite from price and wage increases.
Exchange controls tightened Similar comments apply to the "new"
exchange rate policy unveiled in the 2003 budget. In an effort to
shut down the parallel market - where the Zimbabwe dollar is now
(early in 2003) trading at around Z$1 700 to the US dollar as opposed
to the official rate of Z$55 - the Reserve Bank decreed that all
exporters' foreign currency accounts (FCAs) would be transferred from
commercial banks to the central bank.
In terms of the new regulations, exporters are required to sell half
(previously 40%) of their export proceeds to the Reserve Bank of
Zimbabwe (RBZ) at the official rate. The balance may be held in an
FCA with the central bank, on the understanding that it will be used
within 60 days to finance essential imports. After that period the
funds held in FCA accounts must be sold to, or through, the RBZ at
the official rate.
These new regulations mean that an exporter (excluding tobacco and
gold exporters for whom there are special arrangements) who, prior to
the budget, could have earned Z$922 for every US dollar of export
revenue, can now earn only Z$55. The Z$922 calculation assumes a
parallel market rate of Z$1 500 to the US dollar and a 60-40 split
between retentions and the sale of foreign currency to the central
bank.
It is hardly surprising therefore that several of the country's
leading exporters - in mining, horticulture and manufacturing - have
warned the authorities that if these regulations are enforced to the
letter, their businesses will no longer be viable. Like the
price-control regulations, these exchange rate measures are unlikely
to be maintained for any length of time and will probably be amended
or abandoned by the end of March 2003.
This clearly indicates that the existing stance on monetary policy,
exchange rates, prices and fiscal policy is unsustainable. The only
question is the nature and timing of the inevitable U-turn. By early
January, the Confederation of Zimbabwe Industries had drawn up
compromise proposals, including a plan for a substantial devaluation
in the official exchange rate and the relaxation of price controls.
This highlights the ongoing and growing conflict between political
survival and economic rationality. Once it becomes obvious that the
"new" policies announced in the 2003 budget (presented in November
2002) have backfired, the political pressures on the authorities will
intensify, especially if, as seems probable, it also becomes clear
that the policy centrepiece on which the government's political
fortunes hinge, has failed.
Fast track land resettlement Land reform was a key political goal of
the first ZANU-PF administration that took office in April 1980. At
that time, the administration set a target of resettling 162 500
families by 1985 on a "willing buyer, willing seller" basis, using
the funding of £40 million promised by the British government. By
1985 fewer than 30 000 families had been resettled and many of the
farms acquired for resettlement were lying derelict.
Over the next 15 years a further 40 000 families were resettled so
that by 1999 the total area resettled stood at 3,5 million hectares.
Yet only 100 000 ha of the resettled land had been planted with maize
in that year compared with 1,1 million ha in the traditional communal
areas and 207 000 ha in commercial areas. In the resettlement areas,
the area under maize had barely increased from 96 000 ha in 1993 to
100 000 ha six years later.
It was against this background of failure to deliver land to the
people and the accelerating erosion of its political power base, that
the Mugabe administration launched its fast track land resettlement
plan in 2000. At that time the target was to resettle 150 000
families on 5 million ha of land acquired from commercial farmers,
without compensation for the land, but with payment for improvements.
At the outset of the fast track land resettlement programme,
commercial farmers owned 11,2 million hectares of land - 28,2% of
Zimbabwe's total arable land area of 39,1 million ha. By August 2002,
6 022 commercially owned farms, covering 10,69 million ha or 95% of
total commercial land, had been listed for acquisition.
There is no accurate figure for the number of families resettled, but
government spokesmen have put it at between 300 000 and 350 000.
Available data on seed and fertiliser sales as well as anecdotal
reports of the extent of land tilled and planted, suggest that only a
small proportion - less than 40% of the land actually listed - is
being cultivated in the 2002/03 season.
Maize plantings are estimated at a maximum of 1,1 million hectares -
the lowest such figure since the great drought of 1992 and 18% below
the ten-year average.
Yields are expected to be down sharply, partly reflecting adverse
weather conditions but also the input shortages of the new settler
farmers and the shift from high-technology commercial farming to
low-technology small-scale production. Consequently the forecasts
made in December 2002 point to a maize harvest of around 700 000
tonnes. This is less than 40% of the country's annual consumption of
1,8 million tonnes, implying that maize imports of at least 1,2
million tonnes will be needed to tide the country over until the
April 2004 harvest. Moreover a further 500 000 tonnes of maize will
have to be imported in 2003/04 to rebuild the now depleted strategic
grain reserve.
Such targets are unrealistic. Zimbabwe does not have the foreign
currency to finance imports at that level, especially as, with a
reduced crop in South Africa, maize will have to be imported from
overseas at a higher cost.
Furthermore, the continuing maize imports for Malawi and Zambia as
well as Zimbabwe in 2003/04 will further congest an already
overextended rail transport system that is currently unable to cope
with the demand.
The number of people in need of emergency food assistance doubled
from 3,6 million in mid-2002 to 8 million by the end of the year. The
World Food Programme has received less than 60% of the funds it
requires to provide approximately one-third of the needed assistance.
Government efforts to bring in a further third - about 600 000 tonnes
of grain - have been hampered by a lack of foreign currency and
transport constraints.
The private sector, earmarked to bring in the remaining third, has
been effectively excluded from the operation by the government's
insistence that all imports have to be handled by the state-owned
Grain Marketing Board.
The forecast is that the food supply situation will deteriorate
further in the first quarter of 2003, but may be alleviated
temporarily from April onwards when the 2003 crop is harvested.
However, because there is likely to be a shortfall of at least one
million tonnes of maize and 150 000 tonnes of wheat, import demand in
2003 will be even stronger than in 2002.
Beyond the short term The medium and long-term effects of land
resettlement cannot be realistically assessed at this juncture. An
initial survey by the Commercial Farmers Union (CFU) in 2000
projected declines of 25% in both output and exports and the loss of
150 000 jobs on commercial farms - about 45% of the total workforce
in the formal agricultural sector.
This estimate was based on the assumption that five million hectares
would be acquired for resettlement. However, the actual figure is
more than double the estimate, suggesting that the declines in
production and export are likely to be closer to 50%.
Current estimates also suggest a loss of up to 250 000 jobs on the
farms - about 18% of total formal sector employment throughout the
economy in 1999.
The CFU projection did assume that some of the output and export
