losses of the commercial sector would be made good by the new
farmers. Estimates of this effect vary widely, usually depending on
the political affiliations of the forecaster.
Five concerns stand out:
Smallholder yields are between one-quarter and one-fifth of those on
commercial farms. On the face of it, this implies that planting would
have to increase at least threefold and probably more, if the
resettlement areas are to match commercial production. Supporters of
fast track land resettlement, often citing World Bank evidence,
insist that because peasant growers have been confined to less
fertile land with erratic and lower levels of rainfall than in the
main commercial areas, such a quantum leap in planting will not be
needed.
They argue that provided credit and input constraints can be
overcome, smallholders will match, if not exceed, the yields achieved
on large-scale commercial farms.
There is bound to be a shift in output structure from high
value-added commercially produced products - high-grade tobacco,
fruit, horticulture, soya beans, wheat, livestock, game ranching and
eco-tourism - to lower value-added maize, cotton, low-quality
tobacco, sorghum and groundnuts.
In recent years, in line with global trends, large-scale commercial
farming has become increasingly capital-intensive and
knowledge-intensive. It is unlikely that peasant farmers, with very
limited access to credit and expertise, or the new black Zimbabwean
elite to whom the much larger A2 commercial farms were handed out
(popularly known as "weekend" or "cell phone" farmers) will match the
commercial farmers' yields.
A substantial proportion of commercial agriculture's capital stock,
especially its cattle herd, but also irrigation and other equipment,
has been damaged or destroyed since 2000. This will have a material
impact on agriculture's performance over the next five years, and is
likely to mean that the livestock sector, in particular, will be
recovering lost ground instead of expanding.
All segments of agricultural industry are finding it increasingly
difficult to finance working capital. Commercial farmers who are
still working their land cannot borrow from commercial banks, unless
the authorities have delisted them officially. Some new A2
(commercial) farmers are managing to raise credit by using the title
deeds for commercial or residential properties as collateral.
The government's grandiose plan to raise Z$60 billion in bills and
bonds for on-lending to smallholders, was stillborn: only Z$6 billion
had been raised by early 2003.
Solving Zimbabwe's unemployment problem Ministers and policy makers
see land reform as the solution to Zimbabwe's unemployment problem.
But this ignores two realities - the loss of at least 250 000 jobs in
the commercial sector, and the fact that as economies develop,
agriculture's share in total employment declines. The table shows
that in industrialised countries, agriculture's share in total
employment has declined dramatically, reflecting greater capital
investment and more intensive skills, rising productivity and, very
often, the consolidation of smallholdings into larger, more viable
commercial properties. This is just one more instance where, in
seeking to use land to achieve political goals, the Mugabe government
has given priority to political survival rather than to a rational
development strategy.
The informal economy as the lead sector Not only is development
characterised by a decline in agriculture's share in total
employment, but also by a catch-up in farm productivity as the sector
sheds excess labour. Since 1950 agricultural productivity in leading
OECD economies has grown annually by 3,5% compared with 2,5% for
industry and only 1,1% in services. Although the productivity gap has
narrowed between agriculture, industry and services, output per head
in 1987 was 58% higher in industry than in agriculture, while in
services it was 26% higher than in agriculture (Maddison, 1991).
The belief that the informal sector is a dynamic source of economic
growth is at variance with the facts. Broadly speaking, the larger
the informal sector, the more backward the economy. By its very
nature, the informal sector excludes and isolates people from the
markets that are the chief source of dynamism.
Here too there is a political explanation for the Zimbabwe
government's enthusiasm for the informal sector. Its existence and
expansion have mirrored the decline in the number of people in formal
sector employment as a ratio of the population, allowing the
politicians to minimise the impact of rising unemployment and
mounting poverty by sweeping the problem under the informal sector
carpet (see graph).
There are no reliable unemployment data available for Zimbabwe. The
figures cited in the media, that 60% to 70% of the workforce is
unemployed, are clearly exaggerations. Formal sector employment is
estimated to have peaked in the late 1990s at around 1,4 million
people, declining to an estimated one million by the end of 2002,
mainly as a result of retrenchments on commercial farms. Prior to the
1999 downturn, unemployment was estimated at around a third of the
workforce. The subsequent loss of around 400 000 jobs means that
unemployment is now in the region of 45%.
The graph understates the severity of the situation in one respect.
The preliminary results of the 2002 census show a population of 11,6
million - some 2,7 million less than earlier official estimates based
on the 1992 census.
The lower figure can partly be explained by the impact of HIV/ Aids.
After Botswana (at around 33%), Zimbabwe has the second-highest
HIV/Aids prevalence rate in the world. As a result, the population
growth rate has apparently slowed down from 3,1% a year in 1992 to
just over one per cent ten years later.
This may be an exaggeration though, because there are estimates that
as many as two million Zimbabweans live and work outside their home
country. At least one million are in South Africa, mostly as "illegal
immigrants" and a further 500 000 are estimated to be in the UK,
again mostly as illegal immigrants.
This has cushioned the impact of unemployment in two key respects.
Almost without exception, the Zimbabwean emigrants are earning
substantially more offshore than they could at home. Many of them are
also repatriating substantial sums, especially from the UK, which has
contributed to the asset price bubble, especially in the property
market.
Zimbabwean expatriates are sending back sterling, rands and pula to
be converted into Zimbabwe dollars through the parallel market.
Estimates of remittance inflows vary widely. Because the inflows come
through the parallel market, there is no way of measuring them
officially.
Furthermore, it is not known what proportion of the inflow is a
conduit for capital flight, i.e. where capital exporters in Zimbabwe
make payments in local currency to beneficiaries inside the country
but bank the pounds or rands abroad.
Some analysts put the gross inflow from expatriate Zimbabweans at
between US$500 million and US$600 million in 2002 - equivalent to
more than one-third of exports of goods and services. Had such an
amount actually come into the country, the foreign currency crisis
would be far less severe than it is, implying either that this figure
is an exaggeration or, more probably, that the bulk of it is merely
financing capital flight.
The balance of payments The projections suggest that by the end of
2003, exports will have virtually halved from their peak 1996 levels.
Imports fell steeply until 2001 but recovered strongly in 2002,
reflecting the need to import food worth US$350 million.
The export figures understate the actual level of transactions. There
is a considerable volume of unrecorded cross-border trade, including
the smuggling of price-controlled items such as sugar, but also gold,
into neighbouring territories for sale in hard currency and at higher
prices than in Zimbabwe.
There is also a substantial volume of unrecorded exports by
day-shoppers crossing from neighbouring countries to buy consumer
goods from Zimbabwean supermarkets and clothing retailers. The
deficit on the current account of the balance of payments, which
averaged US$835 million in 2001/02, has been funded by unrecorded
capital inflows - presumably expatriate remittances - and the
build-up of arrears. The arrears had reached an estimated US$1,8
billion by the end of 2002 and are forecast to reach US$3 billion at
the end of 2003.
Zimbabwe's GDP is estimated at US$4,75 billion in 2002, and with
external debt, including arrears, is put at over US$5 billion,
underscoring the necessity for the country's internal debt to be
bailed out at some future date.
Manufacturing industry Though the visible signs of an unsustainable
balance of payments situation are obvious - shortages of fuel, food,
raw materials, essential input items for industry, mining and
agriculture, and the collapse of the exchange rate in the parallel
market - the invisible effects are far more difficult to gauge. The
slump in domestic savings, estimated at 9% of GDP, combined with the
foreign currency bottleneck means that Zimbabwe is fast running down
its capital stock. Since 2000, if not earlier, capital consumption
(depreciation) has exceeded gross fixed capital formation. The
consequences include a deteriorating infrastructure, declining
employment and lost competitiveness as Zimbabwe lags further and
further behind in technology and productivity.
The United Nations Industrial Development Organisation (UNIDO) in its
2002/03 Industrial Development Report, highlights the country's
decline. Zimbabwe, once the most industrialised country in
sub-Saharan Africa after South Africa, failed to build on "inherited
advantages". UNIDO asserts: "Part of the reason for this is
political, but structural factors also explain why its enterprises
have failed to cope with international competition."
This de-industrialisation started a decade ago - well before the
present crisis - but it contributed to that crisis because of the
manufacturing sector's failure to generate jobs and foreign currency.
UNIDO states that between 1985 and 1998, the share of medium and
high-technology products in Zimbabwe's manufacturing value-added
production declined along with manufactured exports. Manufacturing
value-added production peaked in 1996, and since then it has declined
by 27% to its lowest level since pre-independence days (1979).
The present policy stance - specifically price controls and the
thrust to achieve a low-tech, low-productivity small-scale
agricultural sector - will probably accelerate the
de-industrialisation process. There are strong linkages between the
manufacturing and commercial agricultural sectors, as industry relies
on farming for almost half its input. A substantial element of
manufacturing production either adds value to farm production or
targets final consumption in the agricultural sector - farm
machinery, irrigation equipment, vehicles, chemicals, fertiliser,
clothing and, of course, food and beverages.
The steep Manufacturing value decline in agricultural production in
the early years of the 21st century will have far-reaching adverse
consequences for both large and small-scale manufacturing.
Mining The mining sector ought to have been least affected by the
national economic downturn. Although President Mugabe has threatened
nationalisation on more than one occasion in recent times, the
industry has rightly dismissed this as political rhetoric that is
unlikely to be pursued. In fact, as recently as November 2002, the
chief executive of the country's largest platinum mine, Zimplats,
described Zimbabwe as "a great place to do business".
His view is not shared by colleagues in the industry. Two of these
companies, Rio Tinto Zimbabwe and Bindura Nickel Corporation, owned
by Anglo American plc, issued stock market cautionaries about the
impact of the government's foreign exchange policies on their
profitability, with Rio Tinto saying it would no longer be viable.
The main cause of the 33% decline in mining production since 1996 is
the 40% fall in gold output - a casualty of the relatively weak
bullion price, rampant domestic inflation and an overvalued exchange
rate. Official efforts to subsidise the industry, through the central
bank's gold support price, have kept mines afloat that would
otherwise have gone under. But the December 2002 warnings of
non-viability from both Rio Tinto and Falcon Gold suggest that the
authorities will have to increase the subsidy sooner rather than
later.
Though little investment is taking place outside the platinum sector,
mining appears to have a brighter future than manufacturing or
commercial agriculture.
Zimbabwe has the resources to become Africa's second-largest platinum
producer after South Africa, and the Rio Tinto group has a rich
diamond find that will be developed over the next few years. In the
short term, however, the industry is faced with constraints on
skills, input, fuel and foreign currency, that will prevent it from
exploiting its full potential.
Tourism Tourism has been hard hit. Foreign currency earnings have
slumped to an estimated US$50 million in 2002 from US$232 million in
1996. Political uncertainty, television and other media images of
unrest on commercial farms and the travel warnings issued by some
western governments have taken their toll on the number of overseas
visitors, which declined sharply from almost half a million in 1999
to an estimated 275 000 in 2002.
Official figures show that this decline has been largely offset by
increased regional arrivals, but the truth is that many - perhaps
most - of these represent day-shoppers crossing into Zimbabwe from
Botswana, Mozambique, South Africa and Zambia to change their
currency in the parallel market and buy cheap, price-controlled
products - ironically including petrol, which is in very short supply
in Zimbabwe.
Exchange rate strategy The exchange rate liberalisation adopted in
ESAP in 1991has been gradually eroded. Though no attempt has yet been
made to return to the full regime of exchange controls prevailing
until the early 1990s, the pegged-rate policy first embraced in
January 1999 has been maintained at a time of rapidly escalating
inflation.
The peg was adjusted from Z$38 to Z$50 to the US dollar in August
2000.
During the following three months, it was devalued further to Z$55,
which was still the official rate in January 2003. Consumer prices in
Zimbabwe have increased sixfold since the rate was pegged at Z$55. As
a result, the real exchange rate has appreciated considerably (see
graph) to levels where an official devaluation to at least Z$650 to
the US dollar and probably closer to Z$800 would be required to
restore some stability.
Official reluctance to devalue is both ideological and pragmatic. The
Zimbabwe dollar is substantially under-valued in the parallel market,
where it is now (January 2003) trading at around Z$1 700 to the US
dollar, causing a dramatic impact on prices and wages. A devaluation
of the official rate would therefore be disinflationary, stabilising
the market at about half the parallel rate of Z$1 700.
Despite this, the authorities have opposed devaluation partly for
ideological reasons - devaluation is seen as an admission of defeat -
but also because the fixed exchange rate enables the government to
maintain much lower food, fuel and electricity prices than would
otherwise be possible.
On the pragmatic side, there is also recognition that the supply-side
response to official devaluation would be constrained by the land
resettlement that has undermined agricultural exports and by the
input constraints arising from the shortage of foreign currency.
"Drought of good governance" The overvalued exchange rate has
undermined competitiveness. In the 2002 Global Competitiveness Report
(GCR), Zimbabwe is ranked 79th out of a total of 80 countries, with
only Haiti below it. The GCR demonstrates that the threat to
Zimbabwe's competitiveness is far broader than that posed by an
overvalued exchange rate. In the GCR's macroeconomic environment
index, Zimbabwe is ranked last below Nicaragua (79) and Turkey (78).
It is also ranked lowest for macroeconomic stability, investment,
inflation, money supply growth, wage and employment policy, and
country credit risk.
The survey makes particularly grim reading in the realm of governance
- with a ranking of 75th out of 80 for judicial independence, 77th
for property rights and 75th for favouritism in decisions made by
government officials. The GCR's findings are borne out by
Transparency International's 2002 Corruption Perceptions Index. When
first ranked in the corruption index in 1998, Zimbabwe was 43rd with
a score of 4,2. By 2002 the score had deteriorated to 2,7 and
Zimbabwe was in the joint 71st position out of a total of 102
countries.
It is a similar story in the Heritage Foundation/Wall Street Journal
Index of Economic Freedom, where Zimbabwe's ranking has deteriorated
steadily since the mid-1990s. In the current (2003) edition, Zimbabwe
is listed as "a repressed state" with a ranking of 153 out of 155
countries. Only Cuba and North Korea score lower.
The moral of all this is clear. Zimbabwe's economic deterioration is
multi-faceted. It is not merely the result of land resettlement, nor
the collapse of the rule of law. Instead it is the consequence of
what Botswana's President Festus Mogae has graphically described as
"a drought of good governance". Socio-economic policies have been
driven not by the will of the people or even what is deemed by the
political leadership to be in the best interests of the people, but
by the personal interests of the ruling elite.
The social consequences are captured in the Human Development Index
(HDI) compiled annually by the United Nations Development Programme.
Zimbabwe is just one of a tiny handful of countries whose people are
no better off now - in terms of human development - than they were a
quarter of a century ago. The latest index number (for 2000) shows
that Zimbabwe has an index 0,551 - down 11% on its peak figure
attained in 1985 and 4% below the level recorded at independence in
1980 (see graph).
The HDI is calculated as a combination of income per head, education
levels and life expectancy. The main reason for Zimbabwe's
deterioration is the sharp decline in life expectancy resulting from
the country's high prevalence of HIV/Aids. But it also reflects the
impact of falling per capita income and deteriorating standards of
health and education.
Given the severity and breadth of the economic crisis, the odds are
heavily stacked against the survival of the Zimbabwe government,
unless it is prepared to make far-reaching policy, structural and
institutional reforms.
Realpolitik suggests that such reforms would simply be incompatible
with the continuation of the status quo. The pressures arising, not
from "smart" sanctions imposed by some Western governments, but from
the acute shortages of foreign currency, food and fuel and the
ravages of hyperinflation, poverty and unemployment, will force a
change of direction, if not a change of government.
No economist can hope to predict what form the change process will
take, though one can state with confidence, if not certainty, that
the longer the status quo continues, the more radical and painful the
recovery programme.
Lessons for Africa - including South Africa Many lessons can be
learned from Zimbabwe's 23 years of independence. One of the key
maxims of the New Partnership for African Development (NEPAD) is that
poor governance undermines even the best economic policies.
Good governance is not an optional add-on, but a sine qua non for
efficient development policies.
Secondly, "fast track" land reform is a contradiction in terms. There
are no quick fixes in development. Recent African history abounds
with examples of IMF/World Bank "miracles" and role models that have
run out of steam - Kenya and the Republic of CÃ´te d'Ivoire in the
1980s, Ghana in the 1990s and Zambia, as well as Uganda up to a
point, in recent years.
If economic reform is to succeed, it has to be sustained over a
decade and longer - not just a few years. It has to achieve critical
mass, extending beyond the often superficial 3-year macroeconomic
stability programmes fostered by the IMF and World Bank. Successful
land reform cannot take place in an environment of weak institutions,
deep-seated corruption, macroeconomic instability and the collapse of
the rule of law.
At independence in 1980, Zimbabwe had a sound physical
infrastructure, a skilled, educated population and strong
institutions. Many of the skills have since emigrated; the
infrastructure is decaying visibly and its institutions - the public
service, parastatals, the judiciary, the health and education
delivery systems and the police - are also deteriorating.
The lesson is that institutional decay takes decades to remedy. South
Africa, as the one African country that has maintained strong
institutions, cannot afford in any respect to follow the example set
by its northern neighbour.
Given its strong institutions and its achievements in restoring
macroeconomic stability, South Africa is well placed to implement
land reform at a pace and in a manner consistent with maintaining
business and investment confidence.
In any event, land resettlement alone is not a viable strategy for
alleviating poverty. It can contribute to poverty reduction - both in
South Africa and Zimbabwe - but the fact that the dramatic advance of
agricultural productivity globally, and especially in industrialised
countries, has been driven by rapid technological progress, is a
constant reminder of the trade-off between growth and equity.
Above all, it is not possible to ring-fence radical policies,
particularly unsuccessful ones. Few doubt that President Mugabe has
brought contagion to southern Africa and possibly to sub-Saharan
Africa as well. It would be absurd to argue that Zimbabwe's policies
do not pose a challenge to NEPAD - not only in the sense of the peer
review mechanism, but also because the country's policies run counter
to the rational, orthodox thrust of NEPAD's economic development
strategy.
Professor Tony Hawkins, Director: Graduate School of Management,
University of Zimbabwe. This was first published by the Absa Group's
Economic Perspective First Quarter 2003

Original_Search_Title: Chinese Win Big Tender to Grow Food for Zimbabwe
Headline: Zimbabwe: Chinese Win Big Tender to Grow Food for Zimbabwe
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — AS ZIMBABWE's food crisis worsens, the government has awarded a Chinese company, China International Water & Electric Corporation, a tender to develop 100000ha of underutilised land.
The company was given the goahead by the government tender board last week to develop the Nuanetsi irrigation project, which is expected to restore Zimbabwe's food self-sufficiency. The project will focus on maize and sorghum.
The move came as government is trying to lure back the commercial farmers whom it displaced from the land during farm invasions that started two years ago.
Government claims it has drafted a memorandum of understanding, which awaits signing, but farmers say nothing has materialised from the talks that began a few weeks ago.
As part of the deal, government wants to give back to white farmers some of the properties it seized in exchange for equipment to help resettle subsistence farmers. Farmers have rejected the offer, saying the government is not sincere.
Zimbabwe is experiencing a widespread food shortage caused by a combination of drought and the effects of chaotic land reforms.
According to the official Sunday Mail, it is estimated that the Nuanetsi project can yield a minimum of 700000 tons of maize, three times a year. This means that it can produce about 2,1-million tons of maize a year.
The Nuanetsi Irrigation project was evidently an initiative of the staterun agricultural and rural development authority.
Under the project, the Chinese company will be responsible for the entire development. This includes clearing all trees and woodland within the perimeter, ripping, levelling and ploughing. The Chinese firm will also build roads and other essential infrastructure in the area.
The government is also said to be considering plans to expand irrigation schemes in the country to be able to withstand drought in future.
It plans to establish at least one irrigation scheme in each of the country's 59 districts.

Original_Search_Title: Looming Food Crisis Predicted
Headline: Mozambique: Looming Food Crisis Predicted
Body_Text:
Maputo — As the Mozambican government prepares
a meeting with its cooperation partners later this week on the
drought hitting parts of the country for the second year running,
the Famine Early Warning Systems Network, FEWS NET, has issued a
warning that the 2003 harvest is likely to be extremely poor
throughout the southern third of the country, and in parts of the
central region.
Hundreds of thousands of people in southern and central
Mozambique have been suffering the results of the poor 2002
harvest. But insufficient and erratic rainfall has dashed hopes
of a recovery this year. Exactly the same areas hit by drought in
2002 are also likely to face miserably low harvests this year.
According to FEWS NET, the staple crop of maize will be
largely lost in drought affected areas, and beans and groundnuts
will also be severely affected. Even drought tolerant crops such
as sorghum and millet, and recently planted cassava, are showing
signs of stress.
The FEWS NET findings show that in Inhambane, Gaza and
Maputo provinces virtually all districts are facing near crop
failure. Parts of Sofala, Tete and Manica, in the central region,
also face a severe reduction in the first harvest. 
 On the positive side, crop prospects remain good for the
fertile northern region, despite the January storms that caused
some infrastructure and crop damage. However, while this northern
production may improve the national food balance, production in
the north normally does little to lessen shortages in the south
due to transport constraints.
The staple maize crop has been most affected throughout the
southern region. Even in some normally productive lowland areas,
maize is performing very poorly due to a lack of rainfall and
soil moisture. For maize it is too late for further rainfall to
improve the yield, as most of the maize crop has reached the
maturation phase.
The crop loss was a result of a 30 day dry-spell during the
critical stage of crop development. In southern districts the
maize harvest is expected to drop by between 50 per cent and 90
per cent. Some other crops such as groundnuts, cassava and beans
are surviving, but high temperatures and the intensifying drought
are threatening them. In a few districts, such as Buzi and
Chibabava, in Sofala, and Mandlakazi and Chibuto in Gaza,
additional rainfall this month could lead to a slight improvement
in the overall situation.
The irrigation schemes along the Limpopo are at risk of
drying up as the river level is very low and discharges from the
Massingir dam are minimal. The report warns that, if there is no
additional rainfall in the areas of South Africa feeding the
Elefantes, the main tributary of the Limpopo, water will become
scarce for the irrigation scheme at Chokwe.
The report calls for seeds to be distributed for planting in
the second season, which will mainly be vegetables. However, it
notes that even a successful second season would not have a major
impact on food availability because of the quantity and types of
crops produced.
It warns that there is a large gap between food aid needs
and the planned response. The World Food Programme (WFP) had
planned to get food aid to 440,000 people in January. However,
problems with food aid supplies from donors, milling
requirements, and a lack of NGO partners to carry out
distribution, left it only able to help 283,202 people. This is
just 43 percent of the number of people estimated to be in need
of food aid. That number currently stands at 654,865.
It is expected that matters will continue to worsen in the
south. The general pattern of rainfall distribution during
January shows that nearly all of northern Mozambique had rainfall
in excess of 250 mm, which is well above normal. Meanwhile, much
of southern and central Mozambique registered below normal rains.
Despite the gloomy prospects, the report found that maize
prices in Maputo are stable, even though prices normally rise at
this time of year before the harvest. According to the
Agricultural Markets Information System (SIMA), the markets in
Maputo are fully supplied with maize from Nhamatanda and
Gorongosa in Sofala. Nhamatanda is also a major supplier of white
maize for markets in Beira.
SIMA also reports that yellow maize is available in small
quantities in Maputo, which helps some people as it is sold at 60
per cent of the price of white maize.
FEWS NET bases its analysis on field assessments and a
detailed analyses of satellite data. FEWS NET worked with the
Ministry of Agriculture and the National Disaster Management
Institute (INGC), and carried out a crop assessment between 20 to
30 January. The assessment covered 17 districts in Sofala,
Inhambane, Gaza and Maputo provinces.

Original_Search_Title: Kony Ambushes Red Cross Team
Headline: Uganda: Kony Ambushes Red Cross Team
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Uganda Red Cross Society (URCS) has suspended its operations in the northern Uganda districts of Pader, Kitgum and Gulu, following an ambush on its convoy on Saturday at Paiula in Pader district.
Joseph Kony's Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) rebels injured six URCS volunteers.
The rebels took personal effects and communication equipment which included two HF radios and four radio handsets.
In a statement issued yesterday, the URCS chairman, Tom Buruku, said, "the suspension will continue until security is conducive to the resumption of operations."
Buruku said, "The URCS would like to deplore and protest the attack on its clearly marked vehicles and volunteers operating on a humanitarian mission."
The six volunteers were returning from Pader town council after assessing the needs of the recenly displaced persons.
Army spokesman Major Shaban Bantariza regretted the attack and said the LRA were desperately in need of food and medicine.
He said the army had recently advised the URCS to allow their convoys to be escorted by the army.
"We urge the Red Cross to revisit their position and accept to be escorted," Bantariza said yesterday.
"We told them the LRA is a terrorist group which targets anybody, but they have refused to listen to us. We told them that they can operate as a neutral body between belligerents and unescorted only where the rebel group is sensible and has a good cause," he said.
Buruku said URCS was a neutral, independent and impartial organ committed to improving the lives of the vulnerable people in Uganda.
He said Red Cross vehicles and personnel do not carry arms but are protected under the International Humanitarian Law which requires combatants to respect war victims and their property.
Bantariza said on Saturday the rebels were intercepted by the UPDF at Olamunyung in Anaka, Gulu district. He said they fled and dropped bags of peas and sorghum.
Meanwhile, yesterday the rebels ambushed a passenger vehicle at Ligiligi, Kitgum district, and killed 11 people, among them three soldiers. Seven others were injured.

Original_Search_Title: Impending Food Crisis in the South
Headline: Mozambique: Impending Food Crisis in the South
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — A famine early warning agency has warned that southern Mozambique faces
yet another poor harvest this year, which was likely to lead to a "dramatic
increase" in food insecurity.
In a report based on field assessments and analysis of satellite
imagery, the Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS NET) said that
insufficient and erratic rains over the last three months has resulted in
extremely poor harvest prospects throughout southern Mozambique, and in
parts of the central region.
"First season maize production, which accounts for the majority of the
annual production of this staple crop, will be largely lost in the affected
areas. Beans and groundnuts are severely affected as well. Even
drought-tolerant crops such as sorghum and millet, as well as recently
planted cassava, are showing signs of stress," the US-funded early warning
unit said.
For a few parts of the central region, additional rainfall in February
could lead to a slight improvement in crop prospects. In most of the
southern region, the season is largely over and rainfall now will do little
to improve the crop outlook. The poor outlook for first season crops is
likely to cause a dramatic increase in food insecurity in the affected
zones, said the report, which was released on Friday.
FEWS NET noted the situation was especially worrying as this was the
second consecutive year of drought in the affected areas, while the food aid
response to the 2001/02 drought has been significantly lower than assessed
needs. At the national level, planned food distributions by the World Food
Programme (WFP) for January will only meet 43 percent of the latest
estimated needs of 654,865 people, a figure which "is certain to increase
due to the current climate conditions".
Inhambane, Gaza and Maputo provinces are the most severely affected,
with virtually all districts facing near crop failure, the report
said. Parts of Sofala, Tete and Manica also face a severe reduction in first
season production. Maize was expected to suffer losses of between 50 and 90
percent. Some crops such as groundnuts, cassava and beans are still
surviving, but due to high temperatures and the intensifying drought,
additional rainfall is needed within the next several weeks to save the
crops.
"Food security problems will be compounded by the fact that this is the
second year of drought, since households have already been maximising their
coping strategies for a year. Food aid distribution programmes are providing
some relief and are well received but the quantities are too small to have a
major impact," the report said.
FEWS NET said crop prospects remain good for the productive northern
region. Flooding, brought about by excessive rainfall, has caused some
infrastructure and crop damage in the north, but away from the flood areas,
crop growth was excellent. While northern production could improve the
national food balance, "production in the north normally does little to
ameliorate shortages in the south due to transport constraints".
FEWS NET, in coordination with the Ministry of Agriculture (MADER) and
the Disaster Management Institute (INGC), carried out the crop assessment
from 20 to 30 January. It covered 17 districts of southern Mozambique in the
provinces of Sofala, Inhambane, Gaza and Maputo. Formal food production
estimates will be produced by MADER in the next few months.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Defends Evictions From Kaptagat Forest
Headline: Kenya: Govt Defends Evictions From Kaptagat Forest
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government yesterday defended the on-going eviction of people from Kaptagat forest, in Keiyo District, saying the occupation was illegal.
A spokesman for the Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources, Mr D M Mbao, said the Government had a legal mandate to protect forest s from further destruction.
"The unabated destruction of forests is frustrating Government efforts towards poverty reduction, sustainable agriculture and provision of water," Mbao said in a statement.
Human rights groups from Keiyo District have condemned the eviction of those settled in the forest claiming it was politically motivated.
Area Kanu activist, Mr Paul Kibet, had alleged that the evictions were meant to "punish Keiyos for their loyalty to Kanu and Biwott."
The Executive Director of Eldoret-based Centre for Human Rights and Democracy, Mr Ken Wafula, had also condemned the raid, saying residents should have been given enough time to vacate.
But the Government defended the move saying those who had encroached on the forest were settled illegally.
"It is clear most of the forest occupied has been acquired illegally by the occupants," said Mbao.
He said the Ministry was working with other arms of the Government to determine how the forest occupation was effected before taking necessary action.
"In the event that such occupation was done without due legal process, the al__cpLocations will be revoked," said the official.
Mbao said it was "totally unacceptable" that Kenya's forest cover which stood at four per cent in 1963 had been reduced to less than 2 per cent.
All illegally acquired land will be repossessed and arid areas reclaimed to ensure Kenya achieved a 10 per cent forest cover in accordance with international standards, he said.
Meanwhile, Kisii DC, Mr Osman Warfwa, yesterday ordered the local Municipal Council to evict land grabbers from the Daraja Mbili market.
Warfwa, who toured the area together with the entire District Security Committee and the council chief officers, said the plots would be used by the open air market traders.
"This is the only means by which the orders by the Local Authorities Minister Karisa Maitha can be effected besides decongesting the Kisii/Migori highway because hundreds of the traders have been doing business on the roadside," the DC said.
He told the town engineer, Mr M Mungania, to effect his order within 14 days saying those who had constructed food kiosks or garages should be relocated.
In West Pokot, former Assistant Minister for Finance Mr Christopher Lomada urged the Government to curb human encroachment on the Lelan forest.
Lomada said unknown number of squatters had invaded the forest for habitation and livestock grazing.
He said the forest acts as water catchment for most rivers, which are used for irrigation by organisations such as Weiwei Integrated Development Project (WIDP).
He said WIDP initiated by Italian entrepreneurs are engaged in overhead irrigation for crops such as maize, beans, simlaw and sorghum.
He said the organisation had also generated employment opportunities for local residents leading to decreased poverty levels among area residents.

Original_Search_Title: US Donates 30,000 Tonnes of Relief Food
Headline: Zambia: US Donates 30,000 Tonnes of Relief Food
Body_Text:
THE United States has donated 30,000 tonnes of relief food worth US$15 million to distribute to the most vulnerable Zambians, especially HIV/AIDS victims.
US ambassador to Zambia Martin Brennan yesterday handed-over the first shipment of 450 tonnes of the cereal to the World Food Programme (WFP) and three Zambian Non-Governmental Organisations (NGOs).
Mr Brennan said 10,000 tonnes of sorghum and bulgur wheat would be distributed by the WFP while Care International, World Vision and Catholic Relief Services (CRS) would handle the remaining tonnages.
He said the donation represented his government's commitment to helping Zambians, but emphasised that the donation was designed to reach the most vulnerable.
A greater part of the 20,000 tonnes that the network of the three NGOs called C-SAFE Zambia had received would be targeted at HIV/AIDS patients, orphans, the less privileged, pregnant and breast-feeding women and under five children.
"I am proud that the US government has been able to find food during this period when there has been tremendous demand for food relief.
"I am particularly proud that the food will be given out to people who are not only vulnerable, but also living with HIV/AIDS," Mr Brennan said.
He said the US strongly endorsed the commitment and focus that the Zambian Government had in prioritising agriculture.
He said as partners, the US, working with WFP and other NGOs, were spearheading the implementation of good agriculture practices and also helping to link farmers to markets.
The envoy challenged Zambia to use her vast natural resources to produce enough food for itself.
He said he would be pleased to see Zambia exporting to other countries instead of asking for relief food.
And WFP country director Richard Ragan said the world food body was facing a difficult challenge in its third phase of the relief mitigation programme.
"We are getting to people that are most vulnerable. We have the most difficult two months coming in our operations."
CRS country representative Michele Broemmlsiek said the gesture was the first focused donation that would go towards supplementary feeding of HIV/AIDS affected households.
Ms Broemmlsiek said the initiative of the NGOs in C-SAFE Zambia grouping was helping the people to translate relief service into development.
She said C-SAFE Zambia had targeted 394,000 highly vulnerable beneficiaries and that they would need $20,686,129 to buy and distribute 10,000 tonnes of sorghum, 10,000 of bulgar wheat and 3,500 of pinto beans.

Original_Search_Title: Mutare Police to Step Up Operations Against Touts
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mutare Police to Step Up Operations Against Touts
Body_Text:
THE police in Mutare yesterday said touts risk being arrested if they continue harassing passengers at the Sakubva long distance bus terminus.
Francis Mubvuta, the officer in charge of traffic police, Mutare district, said: "The touts are committing an offence daily and should be on guard as the police will be out in full force after them.
"It is an offence that attracts a fine of up to $3 000,"
The warning comes at a time when the Mutare City Council has also stepped up efforts to remove touts from the long distance bus terminus.
The passengers, mostly from rural areas, and traders have in the past called on the police to evict the touts.
The touts verbally abuse passengers and force them to board buses against their choice. The touts have been accused of harassing rural traders who bring fresh farm produce for sale in the city, and robbing them of their money and goods.
Augustine Chikwanha, a councillor for Ward Three in Sakubva, told a recent community services, housing, health and education committee meeting that touts were disregarding the law and continued to operate illegally at the terminus.
"Those touts are charging rural traders and other travellers exorbitant fees for loading and offloading their luggage," Chikwanha said.
He said people no longer felt protected because of the prevalence of harassment by the touts at the terminus.
Touts at the terminus charge between $1 000 and $1 500 to load or offload a 50 kilogramme bag of maize, wheat, sorghum or millet.
Chikwanha said most passengers would seek assistance from the touts after failing to get help from loaders and conductors of the respective bus companies. "Bus operators are abandoning passengers leaving them at the mercy of the touts," Chikwanha said.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Rams Go Up
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Rams Go Up
Body_Text:
Jigawa — Prices of livestock have risen in Jigawa State in anticipation of the
usual high demand for livestock for the eid- el- kabir festival,
which is less than eight days away.
An investigation by the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) at Sara Market
in Gwaram Local Government Area of the state, showed that the prices,
which had been static for several months have risen significantly.
An average bull now attracts N60,000 as against N50,000 in the last
six months, while an average ram, which attracted N6,000 now goes for
more than N8,000.
NAN reports that a medium size sheep is sold for N5,000 and a goat
for less than N4,000, as against N4,000 and N2,600, respectively.
Similarly, the prices of chicken have gone up by about 20 per cent,
selling for about N250, while guinea fowl goes for N300 and cock
sells for N400 as against N200, N250 and N300, respectively.
The prices of food items have, however, remained steady since last
December, with sorghum, millet and maize selling for N2,600 per bag
and N65 per measure.
The prices of rice have, however, skyrocketed to N240 per measure as
against N200, while the local variety goes for N140 as against N110
and flour goes for N140 and sugar, N200 as against N120 and N160 per
measure, respectively.
A cattle dealer at Sara Market, Ibrahim Isa told NAN that the prices
would further go up due to the increase in demand the commodities in
the next few days.
"Though the prices have shown little improvement, but it is
encouraging, compared to the poor patronage in the previous months,"
he said.

Original_Search_Title: US Makes Non-GM Food Donation
Headline: Zambia: US Makes Non-GM Food Donation
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The US government on Thursday donated 30,000 mt of non-genetically modified (GM) sorghum and bulgur wheat to Zambia, struggling to cope with widespread food shortages.
The US $15 million donation comes at a time when the Zambian government had appealed for more relief food. In the national budget presented last week, Finance and National Planning Minister Emmanuel Kasonde set aside US $14 million for the commercial purchase of maize.
US ambassador Martin Brennan said the food aid was intended to "assist Zambia's most vulnerable people during the current food shortage". Close to three million Zambians are facing food shortages following two consecutive poor harvests, and concern has been raised over the impact of a possible drought this year.
The donation of sorghum and bulgur (partly cooked and dried wheat grains) follows a wrangle last year over the government's refusal to accept US-supplied GM maize on health and environmental grounds.
The World Food Programme (WFP) will distribute 10,000 mt of the US shipment, while their partners CARE International, and the church-run World Vision and Catholic Relief Services will distribute the remainder.
The US donation has beefed up existing stocks of relief food. "What I can say right now is that we have a healthy food pipeline, we are receiving thousands of tonnes of relief food every other week," WFP spokeswoman Sibi Lawson told IRIN.
Wheat and sorghum are generally accepted by Zambians, despite the fact that white maize is the staple food consumed by most households.

Original_Search_Title: Prospects for 2003 Agricultural Season
Headline: Zimbabwe: Prospects for 2003 Agricultural Season
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE has an agro-based economy, hence the significance of the
agricultural sector in the economy's development. According to the Reserve
Bank of Zimbabwe (RBZ) statistics, agriculture accounts for 15,9% of Gross
Domestic Product (GDP), an average of 40% to export earnings and 26,3% of
formal employment
Performance of the sector also has a direct bearing on manufacturing
sector output, accounting for at least 60% of the sector's input
requirements given strong backward and forward linkages. The agricultural
sector has recently gone through radical transformation through land reform,
making available at least 11 million hectares of former commercial
agricultural land to indigenous farmers. The 2003, national budget
prioritised the sector as an anchor for the turnaround of the economy
Recent performance The sector's performance has recently come under
threat owing to drought, which saw the sector shrinking by 20,8% in 2002,
necessitating imports in excess of 1,5 million tonnes of cereal to cover to
feed 6,7 million people. There was a reverse in the sector's growth in 2001
after averaging 5% per annum in the five years to the year 2000. The sector
still has to ward off the transitional costs of land reforms, which will
take some time to absorb. Prospects for the sector's performance this year
have been hampered by the unfolding drought, and escalating costs of inputs
which have severely compromised cropping arrangements
Outlook for 2002-2003 season According to the SADC Regional Early
Warning Unit, by early November Zimbabwe had received more than 80% of
normal rainfall following significant and well distributed rains since late
October. However as the rainy season progresses, signs of drought have
emerged
In the first half of 2002-2003 rainy season moderate to poor rainfall
was received in most parts of the country. According to the FEWSNET Report
for the season to 16 December, rainfall received countrywide during the
period October to December has ranged from 40 to 60% of normal. Hot and dry
weather was recorded throughout the country during the second half of
November and the first week of December, resulting in the wilting of the
early crop
Severe crop wilting has been recorded in the districts of Chivi, with
30% of the maize crop planted in October reaching permanent wilting, casting
doubts on the potential yields from these regions. These developments point
to the effects of the moderate El Nino phenomenon which had been projected
to take effect during the last quarter of 2002 and early 2003
The current drought signals have also been alluded to by the SADC
Drought Monitoring Centre's report for the period October to March 2003. The
centre submitted that the northern part of the SADC region is expected to
receive normal to above normal rainfall for the period October to December
2002, while the rest (Zimbabwe included) of the region is likely to
experience normal to below normal rainfall. During the period January to
March 2003, the Centre reported that there were high possibilities of normal
conditions across much of Southern Africa. However, there is a chance of the
rainfall sliding into the below normal category over the southern part of
the region. The countries to be affected are Zimbabwe, Botswana, Southern
Zambia, Southern Malawi, Central and southern Mozambique, Southern Namibia
and much of South Africa
The period from October to December 2002 had a high possibility of
normal rainfall (40%) for the Northern part of Zimbabwe. However, for
Southern Zimbabwe, there was a 35% chance of below normal rainfall this
season in comparison with the 25% chance of the same rainfall pattern in the
Northern region of the country. This rainfall pattern has been proved by the
inconsistent rainfall received by most parts of the country
Land utilisation Land preparation and planting during the current season
was constrained by state tillage bottlenecks and shortages of essential
inputs such as seed and fertiliser. High input costs, particularly for maize
seed whose prices have risen by 200%, have resulted in significant cost
escalations for newly resettled farmers
The country has experienced a persistent shortage of inputs,
particularly seed and fertiliser. As a result, most farmers had to plant
without any basal fertiliser. This, together with the erratic pattern of
rainfall and the uncertainty surrounding land redistribution, is likely to
see the country harvesting insufficient grain this season
Uptake for A2 plots has been below 50% at most, casting doubts on the
agricultural sector's capacity to expand this season. The agriculture
sector's financing woes have been worsened by confidence problems, which
have seen the Agribills floated through Syfrets Merchant raising $7,2
billion against a target of $25 billion. The facility would have lessened
government's financing requirements for agriculture, hence the outcome may
exert pressure on the domestic banking sector
FEWSNET estimates for the period to 4 December reveal that 37% of the
average area planted in the 1990s had been planted, with more planting
expected in the same month. The statistic implies a significant
underutilisation of capacity in the agro-sector, with negative effects on
the sector's earnings capacity. However, given the poor rainfall pattern,
not much planting activity was expected in December. Compared to the 1990s
figures for area under planted maize, sorghum, rapoko and pearl millet are
38, 13, 49 and 15% respectively. Hectarage for major cash crops ranges from
25 to 56% of the 1990s level. Cotton planted is at least 56% of the average
area, groundnuts 49% and tobacco 25%
The statistics point to a major strain in the sector which has impeded
land utilisation this season. There is no doubt that these production
bottlenecks will imply raw material supply bottlenecks to the manufacturing
sector, which draws 60% of its inputs from agriculture. Preliminary
estimates by the CFU point to a significant reduction in national agro
output this year, with maize estimated at 30% of annual output, groundnuts
35%,and soyabeans 87,4%
It will therefore not be surprising that a supplementary budget to cover
the sector's deficit will be tabled this year. A severe drought will put
pressure on inflation, the budget and the exchange rate in 2003. Commodity
stock outs will therefore become inevitable, depending on the severity of
the drought
Tobacco crop drops Tobacco contributes at least 30% of national export
earnings and a swing in output implies a significant shock on national
foreign currency reserves. According to the Zimbabwe Tobacco Association
(ZTA), provisional statistics point to a total of 528 commercial farmers who
have planted for the season on 21 000 hectares, of which 7 800 hectares is
irrigated. This crop hectarage implies a potential crop of 55-60 million kgs
of which 22 million kgs would be from the irrigated crop. The total crop is
however expected to be slightly higher due to an additional 50 new
commercial farmers who are still to be surveyed
Smallholder farmers encompassing both existing and newly resettled
farmers are estimated to have planted 30 000 hectares, with an estimated
yield potential of 25 million kgs this season. This year's crop is at least
48,7% lower than the preceding season's which totalled 165,5 million
kilograms, and was 58% lower than that for the 2000/2001 season. The current
crop size points to a significant decline in volumes, implying a critical
drop in foreign currency earnings. Zimbabwe will this year lose its third
position on the world tobacco producers chart to Brazil which has been
upping production in recent years. Brazil expects to produce 540 million
kilograms in the forthcoming marketing season
Tobacco grower base increase The biggest impact of land reforms has been
a steep rise in the participation of small scale growers in the tobacco
sector as shown by the graph
According to the ZTA, the number of small growers was 1264 in 1996,
rising to 12 000 in 2002 while at the same time the number of large scale
growers has dropped significantly from 1 653 to an estimated 1 400 in
2002. Yields per hectare for small scale growers has been static at an
average of 821,57 kg/ ha over the period. The steep rise in the number of
small scale growers does not necessarily imply a large yield, unless
sufficient productivity growth is registered. The need to increase
productivity can therefore not be overstated if competitive and sustainable
yield levels are to be guaranteed in the sector
Challenges The biggest threat to viability for the sector would be
macroeconomic instability which has caused huge input supply bottlenecks
during the current season. Price controls have also dented confidence in
agro-based business, and hence a relook at this policy will have an impact
on the role of agriculture in Zimbabwe
Another glaring challenge is the capacity of the resettled farmers to
sustain the productivity levels of previous tenants, as well as safeguard
the sector's contribution to national export earnings
It is also imperative to ensure that the sector moves away from
producing commodity based output towards value added production, or products
which are less susceptible to international price fluctuations. Although
agricultural output may grow, what is more desirable is the quality of the
output and on farm processes that may transform the value chain. The foreign
currency earnings capacity of the sector will therefore be
enhanced. Currently, producer prices are set primarily on social accounting
parameters, which have hampered viability. It is therefore important that
producer prices are reviewed periodically to reflect the cost of production,
in order to stimulate investment in the sector. Government also needs to
quickly audit the signals of the looming drought and engage in forward
arrangements to minimise effects of the looming cereals deficit on societal
welfare.

Original_Search_Title: LRA On Food Grabbing Spree
Headline: Uganda: LRA On Food Grabbing Spree
Body_Text:
Kampala — Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) rebels on Thursday raided a Kitgum town suburb, two days after they had sowed mayhem in another part of the district.
The rebels hit a shop at Lulojo, just half a kilometre outside Kitgum town, in a fashion similar to what they did Jan. 28 in Ayul, West Ward -- breaking-in and grabbing whatever they could lay their hands on.
The same day another group was reported to have operated in Kalibong, Pugwo and Pukoda parishes in Nam Okora sub-county, Chua county.
In the past few weeks, the LRA have busied themselves raiding shops and any place they can grab foodstuffs and clothes in what seems to be a deliberate stocking-up exercise.
This kind of activity has been reported in various parts of Gulu and Pader districts. It's harvest season now in Acholi sub-region.
Army spokesman Maj. Shaban Bantariza yesterday said "there is no doubt about that" when Sunday Monitor asked him if the rebels are re-stocking.
"They (the rebels) are desperate. At the base we attacked in south Sudan we only found one jerrycan of pigeon peas and one a half bags of sorghum," he said.
Bantariza also said statistics now show that the army is recovering more empty magazines of bullets than full ones " How can a man with an rifle fight with an empty magazine? He asked.
Gulu RDC Max Omeda told Sunday Monitor "the UPDF should be very vigilant now because the rebels are not only looking for food but also want to attack isolated army units".
The army has not been sitting by, except in one incident in Pajule where the rebels spent an entire night in the area before a UPDF unit forced them out. Government forces on Thursday surprised the rebels at Pukoda and rescued 24 abductees whom the rebels had locked in a hut while they cooked their food.
"These are their tactics. They lock up abductees as they cook. In this particular case, the UPDF attacked them, forcing them to abandon those they had abducted," Kitgum RDC Lt. Santos Okot Lapolo told Sunday Monitor, Saturday over phone.
He praised the army for promptly responding to this latest rebel infiltration..
Lapolo said the rest of Kitgum is peaceful but warned that a food crisis is looming.
Travelling by road between Kitgum and Gulu is still risky with many people preferring to fly by Eagle Air, which is charging $100 for a return ticket.

Original_Search_Title: Teso Welcomes Eagles
Headline: Uganda: Teso Welcomes Eagles
Body_Text:
Kampala — Nile Breweries new project unleashes sorghum revival
UNTIL late last year, Teso people had no cash crop, following the collapse of the cotton sector in the 1980s. Today, a farmer in Teso can earn as much as sh6m from one planting season by growing Epuripur sorghum.
Epuripur is a high yield sorghum variety, developed and presently being promoted by Nile Breweries Limited (NBL). The crop is used to brew their new beer brand called, Eagle Lager. Borrowing from popular fiction, the eagle has landed in Teso and taken the region by storm.
The high yielding Epuripur is not only for brewing beer. It is also favoured for local bread, commonly known as atap. The porridge is also referred to as 'Eagle Porridge'.
In an interview with Business Vision last week in Serere, Mr. Robert Elangot 66, a former Bank of Uganda Governor and a founder Bank Secretary in 1966, said the crop has become very popular in Teso and is quickly transforming peoples' standards of living.
Elangot delivered about 1,860kg of sorghum last planting season. He said the crop has increased people's purchasing power for basic commodities. Also to have money left over to buy a bottle of Eagle beer, which costs sh800.
Consider the figures. Under good management conditions, the crop which grows in 120 days can yield a farmer 700kg per acre. If a farmer has 30 acres, this can yield 21,000 kgs and if a kilo is at sh300, the farmer can earn sh6.3m.
This is very different from what the region was following the insurgency in the early nineties.
"In church, I could see clean and proper pressed shirts. Collections have also improved tremendously. I used to see them put sh50 or sh500 and then look in to the same basket for balance of sh450. Today they contribute sh500 then walk away. They have also become more generous during sad occasions," Elangot told Business Vision.
Grace Ebyau said epuripur does not require much to grow. She said it is easier to grow, because it is a mitigation of the existing red variety of sorghum. All they have to do, is maintain the required standard by Nile Breweries. No stones and it should not be mixed with other varieties of sorghum.
Her only fear is that in future the whole of Teso is likely to embark on growing epuripur. This could lead to a glut and then forcing the price per kilo to drop. Presently a kilo of epuripur goes for sh300 compared to the sh150 for red sorghum.
"It is a blessing that we now have a crop with a ready market. If the brewery doesn't take all, I am sure World Food Program (WFP) will take the rest and it can also be consumed," said Elangot.
He averted fears that people might abandon other crops in favour of the white sorghum.
He said Iteso are conservative and have learnt to be laid back, and are taking up epuripur with caution, following an earlier disappointment with the Serena variety of sorghum.
John Ebyau, Scientist from Serere Agricultural Research centre, the man behind the development and promotion of epuripur said the project has already taken off and cannot be abandoned.
NBL are said to be receiving requests from politicians and NGOs with similar interests to grow sorghum.
However, to protect the rural farmers from being out competed, Nile Breweries is now encouraging farmers to form groups, so as to raise a minimum of at least 30 acres of land for those who can't raise it single handedly. About 50 farmer groups have registered, with each consisting of a minimum of twenty members.
In this way NBL expects to regulate production by buying from registered farmers. This is also aimed at ensuring there is no glut and sustainability of good prices among small farmers.
Production is regulated in away that, seeds for planting are supplied by NBL in collaboration with the institute. The amount is often fixed to cover the expected acreage in a particular planting season.
On a sober note however, areas around Lake Victoria are said to be hot and humid. "We recommend those districts not to take up the production of epuripur because the crop will be affected with malt," said Ebyau.
The "Eagle story" story started in August 2001 with the development of an idea to brew a quality beer for Ugandan market based on local ingredients.
Beer in Uganda is relatively expensive in relative terms, because all ingredients bought are US dollar or euro denominated and are imported primarily from Europe.
The weakness and volatility of the shilling, high transport and production costs affect pricing. The tax structure in Uganda also adds considerably to the cost of a beer.
Given the financial barriers to entering this market only the privileged few have the opportunity to enjoying a high quality lager beer in Uganda. As such, the size of the beer market is very small at around 4 litres per capita compared to South Africa at 65 litres and even Tanzania at around 15 litres.
The underlying idea behind the "Eagle" was to lower the financial barrier to people entering the market by finding a suitable local ingredient to substitute the costly barley malt which has traditionally formed the basic ingredient for Nile products.
Following a successful discussion with Ministry of Finance Planning and Economic Development officials, NBL was given an indefinite tax break for as long as they continue sourcing raw materials from the within the country.
"We have however, been given permission to import some sorghum from South Africa, so as not to slow down the project. But by end of December 2003, we should be self sufficient and stopped importing sorghum," Ian McKintosh, Eagle Beer brand manager told Business Vision recently.
This tax break has meanwhile raised some concern from the competitors. The query is that NBL is only paying sh1.5b annually for sorghum yet the tax break is worth sh20b.
Mike Olsen, UBL marketing manager said there is legislation in Uganda allowing products produced with raw materials sourced within the country to be given a tax break.
"When we presented our project, government blessed it and gave us an exemption. There is no definitive time and exact amount. It is just a tax break that can last forever," said Olsen. Of course much depends, on Teso people drinking Eagle.

Original_Search_Title: SA Explores Ways of Helping Zim On Food Security
Headline: Zimbabwe: SA Explores Ways of Helping Zim On Food Security
Body_Text:
Harare — SOUTH AFRICA is exploring ways of assisting Zimbabwe on food security matters through various ways that include the purchase of fertiliser, seeds, machinery and veterinary vaccines.
This came out of a meeting between officials from both countries' Ministries of Agriculture held in Harare yesterday to review progress made since the idea was put forward last year.
Sources who attended the meeting said there was a call for both governments to work together to ensure there was food security in the two countries and the region.
The meeting was held within the context of the Zimbabwe-South Africa Joint Economic, Technical, Scientific and Cultural Co-operation formed to enhance the exchange of ideas in various fields between the two countries.
"The South African officials said they were looking at all instruments available within their country that they could access for the purpose of helping Zimbabwe," said the source.
"They agreed to look at priorities such as the purchase of seeds for the winter crop and the bringing in of machinery to clear land for irrigation purposes."
The Zimbabwe officials told their South African counterpart that the country was experiencing shortages of seed and fertilizers that were available in South Africa.
At the second session of the joint commission held in South Africa last year, it was agreed that Zimbabwe would need help from that country amounting to at least R2,2 billion to purchase various agricultural inputs.
The inputs included seed for white maize, wheat, soya-bean, sunflower, groundnut, sugar-bean, sorghum and compound D and top dressing fertilisers.
Equipment that included tractors and bulldozers, combines and heavy lorries were also to be part of the deal.
The sources said the officials shared information on the cropping season and agreed to work together in various agricultural projects.
They said the Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement Cde Joseph Made, briefed the South African delegation on the land reform process.
Cde Made said the fast-track land reform programme had ended, saying at least 5 868 farms amounting to 11 million hectares were acquired under that programme.
The South African delegation, led by Lands and Agriculture Minister Ms Thoko Didiza, arrived in the country yesterday afternoon for the discussions.
Ms Didiza met President Mugabe at his offices at the Zanu-PF Headquarters late in the afternoon where she said there was need to resolve the unequal distribution of land in the southern African region.
She said her country had always understood that the problem of land was not peculiar to Zimbabwe alone, as many countries in the region, including Malawi, South Africa and Namibia, faced the same problem.
"Last year, we had Sadc Ministers of Agriculture and Lands meeting in Namibia where the issue of land was discussed and agreed that this is a matter that has to be resolved," said Ms Didiza.
She said she briefed President Mugabe on efforts made in fulfilling agreements reached between Zimbabwe and South Africa on the need for agricultural support and the need for animal disease control.
Ms Didiza brought a number of farmers on her delegation who would tour some resettled farmers in the country today for first hand experience on the situation in Zimbabwe.
On the progress made by her government in correcting the land imbalance in her country, Ms Didiza said at least 36 000 people out of the targeted number 63 000 people had so far been resettled.
The South African delegation was expected to leave the country today after touring a number of farms in Mazowe district.
Early this month, South African Labour Minister, Mr Membathisi Mdladlana visited the country to discuss the harmonisation of labour issues between the two countries.
He held meetings with Public Service, Labour and Social Welfare Minister, Cde July Moyo within the framework of the joint commission.
The two ministers also visited some resettled farmers in the Mazowe district before drafting a memorandum of understanding on the labour issues that was expected to be signed before June.
They also visited farms in the South African Northern Province to assess the conditions of Zimbabweans working on those farms.
The Zimbabwe-South Africa Joint Commission for Economic, Technical, Scientific and Cultural Co-operation is expected to meet in June to review progress in various areas.

Original_Search_Title: Country Faces Food Crisis - Usaid
Headline: Nigeria: Country Faces Food Crisis - Usaid
Body_Text:
Kano — The United States Aids for International Development (USAID) has said that Nigeria is facing food crisis resulting from low investment in agriculture.
Speaking at the launching of the strategic seed reserve project in Kano, the Agriculture Development Officer of the agency, Mr. Andrew Levin, said agricultural productivity had stagnated and food importation had risen sharply in the country.
"Over 10 years, fertilizer availability has declined by one-half, and it is estimated that only 10 per cent of the planting seed available to farmers are improved varieties," he added.
Mr. Levin further noted that agricultural export from Nigeria had fallen.
All these, he said, necessitated the need for serious investment in agriculture in the country.
USAID in collaboration with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) launched a strategic seed reserve project with a budget of $500,000 (about N70 million) to help in funding the sector.
The project among other things aimed at catalysing farmer to farmer seed diffusion of selected improved, early maturing varieties of cowpea, millet and sorghum permitting strategic seed reserve development at household level.
Speaking at the launching ceremony, the IITA Kano Station Manager, Dr. B.B. Singh, disclosed that the project proposal was submitted to USAID by IITA for funds to develop strategic seed reserves in few of the states as pilot programme.
He said that the project launched in Kano State has just been approved by USAID with a budget of $500,000 for two years.
Two-pronged developing approaches involved in the project, he said, are the strategic seed reserve development at household level and catalysing the formation of seed growers associations to establish strategic seed reserves at the state levels.
This he said would ensure ready availability of improved seeds to farmers and will help to increase their productivity as well as to provide them the potential flexibility needed to adjust their cropping system in the wake of erratic rainfall in any given year.
Kano State Governor, Dr. Rabiu Musa Kwankwaso, speaking at the launching disclosed that the Federal Government has approved the completion of 7,000 hectares of land for irrigation at Kano River.
He added that the government had also agreed to complete Gari irrigation projects in Kunchi and Kazaure areas of Kano and Jigawa States. When completed, he said, the Kano river project would improve the economic situation in Kano and provide job opportunities to youths in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Dependence is an Enemy to Development - Nielson
Headline: Zambia: Dependence is an Enemy to Development - Nielson
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Dependence is an enemy to development, visiting Euro-pean Commissioner for Development Co-operation and Human-itarian Aid Poul Nielson has said.
Addressing villagers in Sekute village, 60 kilometres from Livingstone on Friday, commissioner Nielson said the EU were following the drought situation not only in Zambia but the whole Southern Africa region.
He said there was need for systematic support to the rural communities and political mobilisation of resources to provide food security and avoid high dependence on the donor support.
"We should avoid getting this country to a situation that erodes and deteriorates into a kind of situation we have seen like Ethiopia where people year in year out, they expect to be fed rice from donors," Nielson said. "That culture of dependence is a big enemy of development."
He said there was need to develop a long term policy to address the crisis.
Nielson said for many years the EU had been supporting people in such situations and pledged its continued assistance.
He called for diversification of crops saying it was dangerous to continue practising mono agriculture because of the adverse weather conditions.
"The better solution is to change the way things are done to reduce vulnerability," Nielson said. "We will continue to help where people are suffering but we have to engage in long term solutions."
He said all the relief food brought into Zambia by the EU was bought from within the region, particularly, South Africa, Tanzania, Mozambique and Malawi.
Nielson said it was important for the EU that the maize brought in was not coming from Europe where agriculture was subsidised so that Africa did not appear to be a dumping ground.
He expressed gratitude that Zambians were living in peace in spite of the economic and social problems they were going through.
And Care International regional director Brenda Cupper said the hunger crisis was serious and had continued to worsen over the last months.
Briefing commissioner Nielson and his delegation in Kazungula on Friday, Cupper said families and communities were in desperate need of food between now and March.
Cupper said the problem would not end after the 2002/2003 harvesting season because there were already indications of crop failure.
"Care believes this is a long time crisis, we see an acute food crisis," Cupper said. "Families have no maize in the granaries and there is no food in households."
She said Care International had a huge responsibility of ensuring that the 500,000 people they were providing for in Zambia's Southern, Western and Central provinces did not die from starvation.
Cupper said half of the 500,000 people they were providing for were in a critical situation with most of areas becoming inaccessible.
She said with indications of crop failure this year because of poor rains the situation was likely to worsen in the coming harvest season.
Cupper said there was need to embark on long term programmes which included diversification to improve the food security.
He expressed concern that the current food crisis if not seriously addressed would increase malnutrition prevalence rates throughout the country.
"The people are severely compromised in health, malnutrition and food security," said Cupper.
Care International representative for Southern Province Robby Mwiinga said it was difficult to change farmers' attitudes from growing the traditional cereal crop to cassava or sorghum.
Mwiinga said the diversification project could only succeed if farmers changed attitudes with the creation of elaborate schemes and markets for the produce.

Original_Search_Title: Soft Drinks Production Up 20%
Headline: Zimbabwe: Soft Drinks Production Up 20%
Body_Text:
DELTA Corporation says that soft drinks production capacity has increased by about 20% since the commissioning of the $2 billion bottling plant in November last year.
DELTA Corporation says that soft drinks production capacity has increased by about 20% since the commissioning of the $2 billion bottling plant in November last year.
The plant has a capacity to produce 42 000 bottles per hour.
The group said the canning line at its Southerton plant has been modified through an investment of some $200 million to give it capability to can soft drinks which were previously imported from Namibia.
"Local canning of soft drinks fully commenced in December last year and we expect improved contributions from the plant," said corporate affairs executive, George Mutendadzamera. He said the biggest constraint faced by the soft drinks division was the unavailability of glass containers as a direct result of the collapse of the only clear furnace at Zimglass last year.
Mutendadzamera said more recently the major constraint in this business has been the shortage of sugar arising from the inability of the Zimbabwe Sugar Refineries to get timely deliveries of raw sugar and coal, both of which are a result of logistic problems facing the National Railways of Zimbabwe.
"The situation is set to improve as a result of initiatives that are currently underway," he said.
He, however, could not be drawn into shedding light into what initiatives the company had taken.
He said the lager division had performed exceptionally well for the year to date, adding that volumes were significantly ahead of budget, driven by the spectacular growth of the 750ml value pack which has gained "exceptional market acceptance".
Delta allayed fears of a beer shortage, saying that there was sufficient production capacity and raw materials to satisfy any increase in demand for beer.
"Although we will need to import a little bit of barley for our malt export requirements, sufficient crop has been brought in from last year's winter harvest to meet all our barley malt export requirements," Mutedzamera told businessdigest.
He said the company was on course with the thrust on operational efficiencies and overhead cost reduction. This effort is assisted by the implementation of the World Class Manufacturing Strategy (WCM) which Delta is implementing with the assistance of SAB Miller who are the world class leaders on such projects.
"It is our expectation that we will achieve significant improvements in operating efficiencies for the next two to three years as a result of this initiative," said the Delta executive.
He revealed that Delta's strategy to enter new markets has been launched and the company recently made entry into the Angolan market in partnership with Zimtrade.
If this model succeeds, the group hopes to roll it out to other significant markets.
Volumes in the Chibuku division are also ahead of forecast and budget driven by an upsurge in consumer demand, the company said. The supply of critical raw materials such as maize and sorghum has been stable through a combination of local supply and imports. Delta said significant contracts have been made by farmers for the 2002-2003 season for both maize and sorghum.
"Despite the bad weather pattern of the season to date, it is our expectation that the contract scheme will play a significant role in giving assurance of raw materials supply through to the next season," Mutendadzamera said.
He explained that this is particularly so because the contract scheme has been structured more in favour of sorghum which is drought resistant.
Delta said the maltings business continued to perform exceptionally well with malt exports significantly ahead of last year.
The company also revealed that the packaging business at Ruwa has performed ahead of expectations in both revenue and volume terms for the nine months to December.
