Delta shares closed at $162 on Wednesday, a six percent gain on the week.

Original_Search_Title: US Govt Pledges $114m to 3 Humanitarian Organisations
Headline: Zambia: US Govt Pledges $114m to 3 Humanitarian Organisations
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The US government has pledged US $114 million emergency aid grant to three humanitarian organisations to fight severe food crisis in Zambia, Malawi and Mozambique.
Catholic Relief Services finance and administration manager Muzunza Musumbulwa yesterday said the grant would provide emergency and supplementary food contributions, agriculture support and development training in Malawi, Zambia and Zimbabwe, three countries most hit by the current crisis.
Musumbulwa said Care, Catholic Relief Services and World Vision had joined forces to provide a co-ordinated response to the food shortage and the related complexities of the existing HIV/AIDS pandemic which had exacerbated the crisis.
"The three year grant will provide for 160,000 metric tonnes of food in the first year. Once the programme is fully operational, Consortium for Southern Africa Food Security Emergency (C-SAFE) is expected to support more than two million people each month primarily women and children," Musumbulwa said.
She said the organisations would be distributing sorghum, bulgar wheat, maize, maize meal, pinto beans and vegetable oil.
Musumbulwa said C-SAFE programme would involve a combination of free general food distribution, food-for-work and supplementary feeding.
She said it was expected that 15 million people in Angola, Switzerland, Lesotho, Malawi, Mozambique, Zambia and Zimbabwe would need food aid up to March.
She said the Catholic Relief Services would take care of C-SAFE programmes in Zambia while Care and World Vision in Malawi and Zimbabwe respectively.
And Steve Goudswaard, regional C-SAFE programme co-ordinator, said the co-ordinated effort would establish a food pipeline to complement emergency responses already in place and lay the groundwork for a lasting recovery in the region.
"Our immediate goal is to save lives by getting food to the people as quickly as possible, especially those communities decimated by HIV/AIDS pandemic," said Goudswaard.

Original_Search_Title: Humanitarian Organizations Unite to Fight Starvation
Headline: Southern Africa: Humanitarian Organizations Unite to Fight Starvation
Body_Text:
Johannesburg —  Coalition receives $114 million USAID grant to assist nearly 2 million
people per month
The U.S. government has pledged to
World Vision, CARE and Catholic Relief Services a $114 million emergency aid
grant for a joint response to the severe food crisis in Southern Africa.
The grant will provide emergency and supplementary food distributions,
agricultural support and development training in Malawi, Zambia and
Zimbabwe, the three countries hardest hit by the current crisis.
The Consortium for Southern Africa Food Security Emergency (C-SAFE)
represents an unprecedented collaboration among three leading humanitarian
organizations. CARE, Catholic Relief Services and World Vision have joined
forces to provide a coordinated response to the food shortage and the
related complexities of the existing HIV/AIDS pandemic, which has
exacerbated the crisis.
The three-year grant will provide for 160,000 metric tons of food in the
first year. Once the program is fully operational, C-SAFE is expected to
assist nearly 2 million people each month, primarily women and children.
The agencies will be distributing sorghum, bulgur wheat, corn/maize,
cornmeal/maizemeal, pinto beans and vegetable oil.
"Our immediate goal is to save lives by getting food to people as
quickly as possible, especially those communities decimated by the HIV/AIDS
pandemic," said Steve Goudswaard, regional program unit manager, C-SAFE.
"This coordinated effort will establish a food pipeline to complement
emergency responses already in place and lay the groundwork for a lasting
recovery in the region."
Poor rainfall, political instability and the HIV/AIDS pandemic have
combined to create the most severe food shortage in more than a decade for
Southern Africa. It is expected that more than 15 million people in Angola,
Lesotho, Malawi, Mozambique, Swaziland, Zambia and Zimbabwe will need food
aid at least until March.
The C-SAFE program will involve a combination of free general food
distributions, food-for-work projects, and supplementary feeding. The
program will complement ongoing activities conducted by the three agencies
in Malawi, Zambia and Zimbabwe.
C-SAFE's objectives are to meet immediate needs and develop long-term
programs to strengthen resilience to future food shortages among vulnerable
communities and households. These objectives will be met through food
distributions and improved access to health services; training in
agricultural productivity, improved water availability, and seed fairs; and
training to improve communities' own early warning systems for future
crises. All programs will include an HIV/AIDS component.
C-SAFE will be managed by the three organizations from a base in
Johannesburg, South Africa, with each agency taking the lead in specific
countries: CARE in Malawi, Catholic Relief Services in Zambia, and World
Vision in Zimbabwe.

Original_Search_Title: Chieftaincy Disputes Block Food Aid
Headline: South Africa: Chieftaincy Disputes Block Food Aid
Body_Text:
Mbabane, Swaziland — Chieftaincy disputes are blocking attempts to disperse urgently
needed food aid to thousands of impoverished rural communities in
drought-stricken Swaziland.
So serious is the situation that police have been deployed to
supervise food distribution.
Chairman of the National Disaster Task Force (NDTF) Dr. Ben Nsibandze
has issued a strong warning to the feuding chiefs to stop using food
relief as a weapon in chieftaincy disputes.
He further cautions them not to politicise food relief because
international donors like the World Food Programme (WFP) and United
Nations (UN) could withhold the much-needed food assistance.
"Unless our chiefs and senior community leaders show appreciation of
the food aid provided by the international and local donors for our
starving people, we are bound to lose this important food
assistance," he warned.
Nsibandze also urged communities who don't face famine to stop
demanding food aid from non-governmental organisations distributing
the aid.
Some communities believe they're entitled to food aid because they
think the supplies come from absolute monarch King Mswati III.
"I'm very much disappointed by the growing tendency of greedy
communities who claim that they must also be getting this food
relief, yet they don't deserve because they are not classified as
hunger victims. This food is not donated by the King, but by the WFP
and local donors strictly for the starving people," he said.
One chieftaincy dispute stalling food relief falls in the Malindza
chieftaincy area, where two Tsabedze brothers, Mabutseka and Gede,
are pitted against each other in a battle over succession.
Worried member of parliament Mahlaba Mamba said food stores for the
chieftaincy were rotting at the Mpaka warehouse because the NGOs
could not distribute in the area.
NDTF logistics manager Absalom Dlamini refuted the allegation of
rotting food, but conceded that the chieftancy feuds in the two
drought-stricken areas of Malindza and Mpolonjeni were blocking the
food distribution exercise.
He said there was a similar problem at Mpolonjeni where Chief
Sibengwane Ndzimandze told the Swaziland Red Cross not to distribute
food parcels to subjects who did not pay allegiance to him.
Principal secretary in the deputy prime minister's office, Andreas
Mathabela, had to deploy police to the area to ensure food was
delivered to the hungry.
The chief was held at Siteki police station until the NGO had
finished distributing food.
"Chiefs, as custodians of Swazi culture, are not supposed to use the
food aid to fight their senseless political wars. I was trying to
show other chiefs that's a crime and government is not going to
tolerate it," said Mathabela.
World Vision Relief Programme Manager, Mandla Maduna, said the Mnjoli
and Likima chieftaincy dispute in the Lubombo region also stopped the
distribution of food aid for a while.
Controversy was sparked when a resident of Likima offered to store
food aid in his unused shop. The Mnjoli people thought World Vision
was siding with Likima and that they would not get food.
Maduna said there were similar incidents at Macetjeni and Malindza,
but they had been resolved.
"Disputes affect us in the distribution as some people perceive us as
being biased if we take certain decisions," he said. "We are neutral
and only offer food relief as a humanitarian act. Before starting the
distribution we appeal to the community leaders not to politicise the
exercise, but some continue to do so. It is painful to see people
using the food aid to fight their political wars at the expense of
the hungry and poor."
Food relief parcels include 12kg of yellow maize, 1.8kg beans and
750ml cooking oil per head. Food security parcels include 12kg maize
seed, 4kg bean seed, 4.5kg cowpeas seed, 3kg sorghum and 30kg
fertilizer.
According to the latest report by the Swaziland Vulnerability
Assessment Committee in conjunction with the Southern African
Development Community (SADC) and the WFP, the number of hunger
victims in Swaziland has grown from 144 000 in June last year to 297
000.
The rapid rise has been attributed to the inability of poor people to
cope with the drought situation as they already battle grinding
poverty, HIV/Aids, a poor economy, unemployment, as well as dwindling
food reserves.

Original_Search_Title: Sorghum Wins Teso's Hearts
Headline: Uganda: Sorghum Wins Teso's Hearts
Body_Text:
Kampala — -- "I have no regrets growing it especially given its ready market," says Esiru
John Francis Esiru 'grows beer'. He has cultivated enough sorghum on 30 acres of land in Serere, Soroti, to produce 12,600 crates of beer.
Esiru is one of the 170 farmers growing the newly released Epuripur sorghum variety, which is used for brewing beer under the trade name Eagle lager. By the end of January, he will have reaped 21,000 kg of sorghum, which is enough to produce 315,000 bottles of beer.
"I find the crop very easy to grow and it is not labour intensive compared to other crops like cotton and cassava. It is a crop you can grow extensively and don't feel fatigue because you only weed once after planting," Esiru says.
He adds that the sorghum (Epuripur variety) has a very high yield compared to the local variety. "I have no regrets growing it especially given the ready market it has," Esiru says.
John Ebiyau, a researcher with National Agricultural Research Organisation (NARO), who developed the variety says the crop has a yield ranging between 700-800 kg per acre, given good management.
It matures in 110 days and grows to a height of about 150 cm.
"The grains have a high sugar and starch content making it sweetish compared to the local variety," Ebiyau explains.
He adds that Epuripur can be tolerant to drought and the notorious striga weed that stunts cereal crops.
He observes that the high starch and sugar content makes the sorghum ideal for brewing although it could also substitute wheat in baking bread.
Mrs. Max Omeda, also a farmer, says she finds no problem with abandoning other crops for Epuripur because of the ready market and premium price of sh300 per kilogramme. The local variety goes for only sh120 a kilogramme.
"We are encouraging the planting of the crop in rows from the traditional broadcasting method, for better yield and I am proud to mention that some farmers have adopted the method," Ebiyau says.
He says, the crop is likely to lead other food and cash crops in Teso given the conditions that have proven ideal for its growth.
Ian Mackintosh, the technical director, Nile Breweries Ltd, the firm purchasing the sorghum says they plan to buy up to 3,500 tonnes this year. This will cost the company sh1.2b.
"It is a high quality variety and we applaud NARO for such wonderful research," Mackintosh said. He noted that Epuripur is producing high quality Eagle Lager beer.
He says they have ready cash for the sorghum purchase and encourages farmers to grow more. However, Mackintosh insists that all intending farmers must first register with the firm before growing it.
"This will help us to avoid excessive growing of the crop, because it might affect the current prices," he explains.

Original_Search_Title: Soroti Farmers Bowled Over By Sorghum Cash
Headline: Uganda: Soroti Farmers Bowled Over By Sorghum Cash
Body_Text:
Kampala — Farmers in Soroti have received their first payment from Sorghum sales to Nile Breweries Ltd.
The crop is being used to brew the latest beer on the Market, Eagle Brand.
The farmers sell the sorghum at Shs 300 per kilo.
National Agricultural Research Organisation and Serere Animal and Agricultural Research Institute developed the sorghum (locally known as Epuripur) for commercial brewing.
The Technical Director NBL Ian Mackintosh while handing over the eight million shillings to more than ten farmers Jan. 7, in Serere Sub-county, Soroti said "the company is looking at purchasing sorghum grown on over 2,000 acres of land."
He added that this season the company is looking at getting over 800-900 tonnes of sorghum.
"More than 250 local farmers in Soroti and Kumi districts could earn around Shs 300m from their initial harvest," Ian said.
He added that later this year the company expects 3,500- 4,000 tonnes of sorghum for the brew this year.
This will cost the company about two billion shillings by the end of year.
Esiru John Francis one of the farmers who was growing cotton and cassava before planted more than 20 acres of the crop.
When NBL and NARO started sensitisation about the crop late last year he did not hesitate to try out the new breed of sorghum.
He was not sure of the crop, but the excited Esiru said the crop is less labor intensive, compared to cotton and cassava.
"One only needs to weed once or you don't at all and you still get good harvests," Esiru said.
The crop takes only three months to mature and can be harvested twice a year unlike other crops.
"I hope to increase the sales and make enough savings to sustain my family," Esiru said.
He has a family of seven with of his children in boarding secondary schools.
However all is not rosy for the farmers.
The farmers have to shout their lungs out to scare birds off their plantations.
"We also hire children to throw stones at the birds, or tie scarecrows around the trees to scare away the birds," Esiru said.
Birds like this sorghum because it is sweet and has a milky flavour.
Perhaps the bigger threats are the monkeys.
" We use harsher methods to beat them away from the plantations," Esiru said.
Ian told the farmers to form co-operatives and sign contracts with Nile Breweries to help them raise the standards of living and more organised marketing.
He said through these co-operatives, formal agreements will be signed with the farmers to make the transactions easier between the company and the farmers.
The farmers for now collect the sorghum together and deliver it to (SAARI) where NBL vehicles collect it from once a week.
He added that forming co-operatives also regulates over production, which could affect the prices of the Sorghum.
Nile Breweries could not have been any wiser, the more Eagle Lager you take, the more you contribute to the development of society in Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Cottco Acquires 34pc Stake in Seed Co
Headline: Zimbabwe: Cottco Acquires 34pc Stake in Seed Co
Body_Text:
Harare — The Cotton Company of Zimbabwe has silently wrestled control of Seed Co in one of the major transactions marking meaningful entry by indigenous people into the development and marketing of certified seed.
Diversified financial services group Old Mutual has been dislodged as the controlling shareholder in the 63-year-old company through the multi-million dollar transaction cobbled through Interfin Mer-chant Bank of Zimbabwe Limited.
In no time at all, Cottco has amassed shares constituting a 34,75 percent majority in Seed Co through the purchase of the stock on the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange.
The chief executive officer of Seed Co, Mr Patrick Deve-nish, confirmed the deal.
"It is correct that Cottco has acquired a 34 percent stake and will now have a meaningful role to play in the company . . . there is no secrecy on that one. We are all delighted that we have this new partner," he said.
The managing director of Cottco, Mr Sylvester Nguni, could not be reached for a comment at the time of going to press as he was said to be in South Africa on business.
Sources privy to the deal said Cottco acquired Seed Co shares through Msasa Stock-brokers, a subsidiary of Interfin. The deal caught the market by surprise because both listed concerns did not issue cautionary statements to shareholders as required under ZSE regulations.
Major changes may soon occur to reflect Seed Co's new shareholding structure.
Mr Nguni is likely to replace Mr Keith Kirkman as Seed Co chairman, while Interfin is expected to second one of its directors. Changes within the company's management cannot be ruled out.
Interfin acquired a 10 percent stake in the seed concern after Seed Co's rights issues suffered an under-subscription late last year.
The merchant bank, which has masterminded a number of high-profile transactions locally, invited Cottco to acquire shares in Seed Co towards the end of last year when the stock market took a severe battering.
Interfin, which, as the lead underwriter of the $1,1 billion rights issue opened by Seed Co between November 19 and December 7 last year, could not stomach losing money and needed a powerful institution like Cottco to unlock shareholder value.
It is suspected that apart from using its investment company to get into Seed Co, Cottco also sought the services of several companies linked to it.
"At the end of the day, its shareholding in Seed Co easily added up to 34,75 percent," a local stockbroker said.
The deal will enable Cottco to diversify from its core business of providing services at every stage of cotton production and sales into the development and marketing of certified seeds.
Cottco will have an added advantage of reducing its exposure by dealing in crops grown in different regions. At the moment, Cottco business is too dependent on one crop and can easily catch a flue should a cold sweep through local and international markets.
The growth in the farming community brought about by the agrarian reform offers an opportunity for both companies. The existence of price controls remains the biggest threat.
Mr Nguni and his team have in the past chased opportunities in a number of unrelated sectors.
Only recently, Cottco tried to put a foothold into TSL Limited but abandoned the negotiations after the Minister of Finance and Economic Development, Dr Herbert Murerwa, and the Governor of the Reserve Bank, Dr Leonard Tsumba, came up with a national budget and new monetary policy that militated against the merger.
Cottco is expected to build on the relationship between Seed Co and its United States- based research company, Sygenta. It is, however, still unclear whether Cottco will choose to make an offer to minority shareholders.
Seed Co may have to be de-listed from the ZSE should the reconstituted board choose to go that route. The board may as well choose to maintain Seed Co on the high profile bourse.
The Government has a 25 percent shareholding in Cottco, followed by NSSA and Old Mutual at 18,8 percent and 11,16 percent respectively.
Seed Co's sales are primarily of hybrid seed maize. Other seed sales include wheat, so-yabean, barley, sorghum and groundnut.

Original_Search_Title: Nile's Eagle Offers Soroti Sh1.2b Cash Potential
Headline: Uganda: Nile's Eagle Offers Soroti Sh1.2b Cash Potential
Body_Text:
Kampala — NILE Breweries Limited may spend up to sh1.2b to buy sorghum from farmers in Soroti district this year, the Technical Director Ian Mackintosh has said. However this will depend on the demand for its new Eagle beer.
The Epuripur sorghum variety was developed locally and has a high yield of 800-900 tonnes per acre It currently sells for sh300 per kilogram as compared to the local red variety, which goes for sh120.
Mackintosh said, "The sorghum grains are of a higher starch and tanne content, which is ideal for brewing. We will be paying a premium price for it,"
Mackintosh was on Tuesday supervising farmers' payments at Serere.
"Eagle lager has already assumed a 20% market share on our products after one month since it was introduced and we are hoping that the brand will be the best brand in Uganda," Mackintosh told The New Vision.
Therefore, as sales grow, we will also buy more sorghum from farmers," Mackintosh said.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Welcomes Contribution From Orthodox Tewahido Church in Nairobi
Headline: Ethiopia: WFP Welcomes Contribution From Orthodox Tewahido Church in Nairobi
Body_Text:
The United Nations World Food Programme announced today a 132,000 birr contribution from the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahido Church in Nairobi, Kenya, to help feed millions of Ethiopians threatened by serious food shortages this year. The money was collected through a fund-raising activity by followers of the faith and will be used to buy enriched food for malnourished children and women.
The donation was handed over to WFP Country Director in Ethiopia, Ms Georgia Shaver, by Melake Hiwot Abba Stephanos, Administrator of the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahido Church in Nairobi and Mrs Manyahleshal Kebede, member of the Church community.
"It is a wonderful sign of compassion to see how Ethiopian communities have come together to assist their fellow Ethiopians. WFP is privileged to be the recipient of such generosity," said Georgia Shaver.
With a serious drought worsening in many regions of Ethiopia, the number of people needing food aid this year is estimated at over 11 million people.
Due to drought and erratic rainfall, maize and sorghum crops have failed for many farmers. These crops are the staple foods for most rural people, accounting for over 40 percent of the total cereal production in Ethiopia.
The drought has also caused numerous livestock deaths, with remaining animals only just surviving. Particularly affected are the pastoralist region of Afar, eastern parts of Oromia, several areas of the Southern Nations, Nationalities and Peoples Region and the northern Somali Region.

Original_Search_Title: Open Up More Land for Irrigation
Headline: Zimbabwe: Open Up More Land for Irrigation
Body_Text:
Harare — AS the spectre of a second consecutive drought looms in Zimbabwe, fears abound whether the economy can survive a similar battering it went through over the last year.
With severe shortages of wheat and maize having characterised the just-ended year, Zimbabweans are wondering whether adequate measures have been put in place to mitigate against the effects of another drought.
On its part, the Government has set aside $3 billion for irrigation to ensure continuous agricultural production irrespective of the rains to save the nation from starvation.
The southern African region is faced with a severe drought, which has been attributed to the El-Nino phenomenon, caused by temperature anomalies in the Indian Ocean.
El Nino is the name give to the phenomena in the Pacific Ocean when parts of that ocean become warmer than normal.
The temperature anomalies modify global weather patterns, with some areas receiving more rain while others, including southern Africa, receiving less.
The Sadc Drought Monitoring Centre issued its first drought warning in December last year for the current season, which forecast a below normal rainy season for countries in the region.
In its warning, the Drought Monitoring Centre said while different areas in the region would be hit with different degrees of weather patterns, the areas that were likely to be worst affected were the whole of South Africa, Lesotho, Swaziland, south-west Zimbabwe and south-east Botswana.
Matabeleland South is likely not to harvest anything as it is expected to lose most of its crop before harvest time.
Already most crops in this region are now in a poor condition, while crops in other parts of the country, including Mashonaland Central, which usually receive above normal rainfall, are suffering from severe moisture stress.
With effects of drought already being felt in the country, there is need for co-ordinated planning and implementation involving all sectors to come up with a solid contingency plan.
The first move that stakeholders may need to undertake is to open up more land for irrigation in the seriously affected areas, and beef up resources with a view of initiating similar projects in other parts of the country.
Farmers, through Government assistance, could make use of the recently established irrigation department within the Ministry of Rural Resources and Water Development to put a specific hectarage under irrigation for the production of main crops like maize.
The Masvingo winter maize crop initiative is one such project, which could be used as an example to ensure food security during drought periods.
The Masvingo Food Initiative has so far distributed 6 100 tonnes of maize to various districts in the province.
The maize was harvested from 1 800 hectares of winter maize put under irrigation in the sugar estates of Chiredzi.
The initiative, which is a brainchild of Masvingo provincial governor Cde Josiah Hungwe was started last year as a solution to chronic food shortages that have been haunting the province for years.
Impressed by the success story of the winter crop initiative in the Lowveld, Zambian president Mr Levy Mwanawasa ordered immediate planting of 2 000 hectares of winter maize in Mazabuka, the Zambian version of the Zimbabwe's sugar estates in Chiredzi.
This followed a visit by President Mwanawasa and other representatives from neighbouring countries to the Lowveld last year in April.
The adoption of the Masvingo Food Initiative by other Southern African countries, including the drought-ravaged Zambia, is an indication that if implemented on a large scale, the perennial food shortages in the region could be a thing of the past.
The recently launched multi- billion-dollar supplementary irrigation programme in Chegutu is another initiative which is poised for growth if given adequate support from all stakeholders.
The programme will see selected resettled farmers being provided with capital to start irrigation schemes on identified farms with dams and boreholes sometime this week.
The Chegutu district administrator's office has already identified 20 farmers through a training process and they would be deployed to their respective farms in due course.
In the same vein of propping up resources to mitigate effects of drought, the Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettle-ment, Cde Joseph Made, was recently quoted saying the Government had issued a tender to open up 100 000 hectares under irrigation. It is, however, not yet clear whether the tender has been taken up.
Now that it has become apparent that the country is now prone to more droughts than ever before, owing to the ever-changing climatic conditions, the Government may need to allocate a portion of the national budget to the agriculture ministry specifically for drought contingency.
In the event of a drought, this al__cpLocation to the agriculture ministry would then be used to buy food as well as boost irrigation projects by procuring more equipment.
Contingent measures like these should not only be effected when there is an anticipation of a severe drought as with the current scenario, but should be a continuous and a systematic process.
The Government may also need to look at homegrown short solutions that include promoting production of drought-resistant crops such as millet, sorghum and rapoko on a large scale.
Both small and large scale farmers could embark on this. If successfully implemented, the cereal crops would ensure adequate food for the nation.
While the Government is looking at how best it should improve food security in the country, a concerted effort is also needed from various sectors in the country, including the corporate world.
In his 15th State of the Nation address at the end of last year, President Mugabe appealed to the corporate citizens to play a visible, responsible and meaningful role to complement Government's effort in feeding the nation.
Zimbabwe requires about two million tonnes of maize annually to meet its domestic needs. Another drought in the face of current food shortages is set to worsen the plight of many families.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Moves GM Food to Malawi
Headline: Zambia: WFP Moves GM Food to Malawi
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THE World Food Programme (WFP) is moving the Genetically Modified food from Zambia to Malawi, the Southern Africa complex food security crisis situation report by the USAID has revealed.
The report dated January 3, 2003 states that the WFP continues to move biotech food commodities from Zambia to Malawi following government's rejection of the biotech food assistance.
"Mozambique and Malawi have expressed concerns over the environmental effects of biotech food, but are accepting such food assistance as long as it is milled before distribution," the United States Agency for International Development (USAID) stated. "However, the government of Malawi has publicly stated its intention not to disrupt the distribution of humanitarian corn if milling is not possible."
The report stated that December maize prices in Zambia averaged between US $240 and US $260 per metric tonne, three times more than the highest recorded maize prices in 2000 and 2001. "Prices are likely to continue to increase as a result of the limited prospects for commercial imports in early 2003. However, WFP expects the pipeline for January and February to be relatively strong.
WFP estimates that 79,676 metric tonnes of cereals will arrive during these two months," the report said. The report stated that from the beginning of 2002 to date, the US government has provided or pledged more than US $278 million in emergency humanitarian assistance in response to the food security crisis. The report stated that in the fiscal year 2002, USAID and Office of US Foreign Disaster Assistance (OFDA) provided more than US $10 million in non-food programs that were currently underway in Zimbabwe, Malawi, Zambia, and Lesotho. On the latest shipment of US food assistance to the region, the report stated that to date, the US government has delivered over 400,000 metric tonnes of food to the region.
"The balance of approximately 100,000 metric tonnes will arrive in the region by the end of February. The Magna Energy, with 15,000 metric tonnes of bulk sorghum, recently arrived in Dar es Salaam. WFP will receive 5,000 metric tonnes of this cargo," the USAID stated.
"The remaining 10,000 metric tonnes will be given to the NGO consortium, C-SAFE.
The entire tonnage has been allocated for Zambia." The report stated that this year, however, regional stocks were exceptionally low, as they were drawn down to fill the previous year's food shortages, and surplus commodities within the region were limited. USAID is also sponsoring a SADC regional workshop on biotech and food insecurity in Gaborone, Botswana, in February 2003.
Participants would include technical experts from around the world and policy makers from the affected countries. The meeting will address the technical and policy issues related to biotech food assistance.

Original_Search_Title: Food Insecure Population Set to Hit 2.2 Mln
Headline: Zimbabwe: Food Insecure Population Set to Hit 2.2 Mln
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Zimbabwe Vulnerability Assessment Committee has projected an
increase in the food insecure rural population at a time when food aid is
expected to dry up.
The committee said the food insecure rural population would increase to
2.2 million between July and September 2003.
A Famine Early Warning Systems Network (Fews-Net) monthly update warned
that unless a government response to the UN consolidated appeal was made
"extremely rapid", and food obtained quickly from South Africa, where an
estimated maize surplus of about 3.76 million metric tonnes exists, the
chances of the food aid pipeline break are very high.
"Food security conditions in Zimbabwe have the potential to remain
critical for the foreseeable future as a number of factors will continue
affecting the agricultural industry and hence the country's capacity to
produce food and foreign currency for importing the food," Fews latest
report said.
The government made an official appeal for 600 000 metric tonnes of
maize to the United Nations, representing 100 percent of the government's
estimated maize deficit for the 2003/2004 marketing year.
The supply of major staple cereals - maize, sorghum and millet -
continues to improve as the 2002/2003 harvest continues to reach the market,
Fews said.
"Bread, cooking oil and milk supplies continue to improve in most
markets throughout the country, but their prices are prohibitive for most of
the poor people. Consumers are still dependent on the highly priced parallel
market for their supply of maize meal and sugar," it said in its broad
report on the situation in the country.
The report said that staple cereal supplies in the southern districts of
the country remained critically low, and prices were, as a result,
relatively higher than elsewhere in the country.
"As food aid is scaled back, the majority of households in these
districts are left to depend on the relatively expensive parallel market and
irregular supplies from the Grain Marketing Board whose prices have
increased to $264/kg from the heavily subsidised $14/kg early this month."
Zimbabwe is currently grappling its worst ever-economic crisis in
history, with inflation hitting 364.5 percent year on year for June.
Annual food inflation for June was put at 434 percent, gaining 99.4
percentage points on the may rate of 334.6 percent.
"Market access to food is becoming the constraint to food security," the
Fews report said.
The government stipulated minimum wage for industrial and commercial
workers remains at about $50 000 per month, covering just 28 percent of the
estimated cost of a minimum expenditure basket for a low income urban
household of six in June 2003.

Original_Search_Title: Pork Business : Woman Makes Name
Headline: Cameroon: Pork Business : Woman Makes Name
Body_Text:
Women now go for masculine jobs to contain the hurdles of
poverty.
Pork business has for a long time remained a "business for men," both in
rural and urban areas. The courage to spill the blood of the animal and the
ability to withstand its last kicks makes it a man's job. But few women in
Yaounde have taken the challenge. They now compete with men in slaughtering
pigs and selling barbecued pork. Among the few, is Mrs Jiomeni Valentine,
who serves queues of men and women every night, at Carrefour Obili,
(Yaounde) with roast pork. Customers say she does better than any man.
Mrs Jiomeni is three years old in the barbecued pork business. She says
she served as an apprentice to a pork dealer for some months. Reason why she
now has the courage to withstand the act of killing and the sight of blood
at the slaughter house. The next problem was how to do a job meant for
men. "I started wondering what my friends will think about me, and how I was
to become a public food servant in a city, especially in the night," she
recalled. But all began well. She says many, especially men, curiously
patted her with words of encouragement. Her husband, who is a township taxi
driver, however, did not feel comfortable, particularly among his friends
who, at times, provoked him by associating the pork business with abject
poverty and misery. She says it soon came to pass.
The lady acknowledges that pork business is actually a man's activity
due to the constrains and the risk involved. The most tedious part of her
job is slaughtering and cleaning the meat which must be ready for the fire
by 6pm. She returns home as from 1pm, sometimes braving dangerous encounters
with bandits, who have twice made away with her money and dishes. Her
husband now accompanies her home after sale.
Mrs Jiomeni, has for these past three years, not faced any difficulties,
which she formerly encountered, in sending her five children to school,
feeding and clothing them. "I do not rely on my husband either for my basic
needs or social commitment any longer," she revealed. She also guarantees
the quality of the meat, though she revealed that sanitary officials simply
collect CFA 500 from each seller annually and instantly issue medical
certificates of fitness. A chunk costs CFA 500 and could amount to CFA 700,
if eaten with plantains. Most men and women stop at Carrefour Obili for
supper.

Original_Search_Title: Kcc Eviction Notice Worries Nakawa
Headline: Uganda: Kcc Eviction Notice Worries Nakawa
Body_Text:
Kampala — SOME of them were born and raised there, but within the next few weeks,
they have to vacate the area. Kampala City Council issued eviction notices
to tenants of Naguru and Nakawa housing estates to vacate the over 50-year-
old estates, to pave way for re-development.
In an October 28 notice, KCC ordered Naguru estate tenants to vacate
within 180 days, while those in Nakawa must leave in 90 days effective
November 1.
When I visited the estates on a chilly Saturday w, hordes of children
were playing in groups, oblivious of what is yet to befall their
families.
Mainly adult women were talking among themselves, pondering over what
the future holds in light of the developments. Since the notice to vacate
was issued, many of them gather in small groups discussing their fate.
For many, it is a hopeless and uncertain future. The area looks
congested and residents are suspicious of any stranger. Many of the units
are dilapidated.
Mzee Alfred Olanya, 64, one of the pioneer tenants of Naguru housing
estate, formerly employed by the Post Office said the situation is more
serious than meets the eye and that it should be handled humanly.
"If the worst comes to the worst, I will shift to the slums and live
like a pauper. I can't go back to Gulu," the frail father of four said
adding, "Evicting us will greatly affect the market as well, 80% of the
Nakawa market is run by tenants."
Olanya's abode is a replica of a small village homestead. With a garden
spade in hand, he painfully bends to weed his small garden of vegetables. A
stone throw away was a small garden of plantains, which he says KCC cut down
in October, citing security reasons.
"My children and some of their grandchildren have grown up here. This is
the 'ancestral' home," says Olanya, dressed in tatters and rubber sandals
with cut grass in hand. It is a tough moment for Olanya and many other
families in the area especially those headed by single mothers.
"Its common knowledge that many of the homes are headed by women who
have limited means to sustain themselves. I don't know whether KCC has taken
the initiative to find out," said Norah Nawandyo the LC1, chairman village
5, Naguru housing estate.
The tenants say KCC should give them priority to acquire and develop the
houses, but those interviewed openly admitted they don't have the money to
do it overnight.
"They should give us more time. I don't know where we shall go. I am a
low-income earner and a widow struggling to pay fees for my children," says
Mary Tumushabe, 45.
Others doubt the seriousness of KCC and insist they will not vacate. "I
think they are just threatening us, they won't do anything, otherwise where
will they put us, unless they want to establish a settlement camp for us," a
furious Nakawa tenant said.
However, for some tenants, staying in the houses was like eating from a
silver plate. "We were paying peanuts to KCC. A serious person by now would
have taken advantage of the situation. Some of us will definitely relocate
to personal houses," a tenant in Naguru said.
Following the eviction notice, Kampala Mayor Ssebaana Kizito told the
New Vision that all tenants are to be paid a year's rent to enable them to
relocate to other places. Ssebaana further said, "People should know that
those houses and land belong to KCC. We have a tenant and landlord
relationship,"
"They should have called us to the community centre where we usually
meet or send us memos. They have no respect for us. Running adverts in
papers! Is that the landlord -- tenant relationship the Mayor is talking
about," a tenant said.
Walufu Rhona, a mother of two says. "Give us two years and we shall get
where to go. The period KCC has given us to vacate is short."
Obot Lily, 48, a tailor and a widow says: "It is an ambush, a few months
notice to vacate is not enough. This is a matter of life and death. They
should treat us like Ugandans, we are not foreigners."
Odong Moses,a shopkeeper in Nakawa trading centre says: "The estates are
accommodating many of the people who fled their areas due to insecurity,
including those from Gulu, Lira and Kasese."
Walter Mwesigwa, 46, a social worker: "Government and KCC should go back
to the drawing table and critically analyse the implications of their
decision. Thousands of people cannot get alternative accommodation in such a
short period of time."
Josephine Ayitso, an employee with Britannia and a widow: "I have no
alternative. Many of us are widows with limited means. The president should
intervene."
John Musita, a tenant and shoeshiner: "We agree with KCC that
development is a necessity, but they should implement their policy in good
faith without hurting us.
Mukasa Henry, a journalist says: "KCC is right. these people should
live. They can't afford to buy and redevelop the area. Many of them are low
income earners."
"KCC developed and adopted the strategic framework for reform as a way
of improving service delivery to its residents. It therefore resolved among
other things to divest it-self from unproductive assets, which include all
its housing stock," the eviction notice read.

Original_Search_Title: More Money For Plantain Farming
Headline: Cameroon: More Money For Plantain Farming
Body_Text:
Two protocol agreements signed last Thursday would lead to increase in output.
What appeared last June in Ebolowa like another wishful thinking when the Minister of State for Agriculture, Augustin FrÃ©dÃ©ric kodock officially relaucnhed the plantation sector may gradually become reality. During that occasion, the Ministry of Agriculture committed itself to ensure that banana and plantain production are significantly stepped up. Among the engagements taken was the putting at the disposal of small scale farmers 10 million quality plants for 7000 hectares of banana. To concretise this, a convention and a protocol agreement were both signed with the African Banana and Plantain Research Centre (CARBAP) and the International Institute For Tropical Agricultural (IITA) respectively last Thursday in Yaounde.
While the convention signed with nursery farmers sets out to promote production at initial stage, the protocol on its part sets the ball rolling for researchers to carry out research and above all quickly put the results for use on the field. The African Banana and Plantain Research Centre (CARBAP) and the International Institute For Tropical Agricultural (IITA) have offered to furnish modern agricultural tools and train the staff of the Ministry of Agriculture as well as nursery workers, an offer that will cost over 22 million Frs.
The protocol falls in line with the research-development approach adopted by IITA. According to the Representative of IITA, Aboubakar Yakoubou, the approach lays emphasis on rapid and efficient publication of research results to peasant farmers. To the personnel of the two structures (CARBAP and IITA), the Minister of State, called for scrupulous respect the terms of collaboration which include: quality control, refresher courses for nursery workers and other producers, supply of basic seeds to nursery farms and nursery farmers, and technical follow up and control of projects such as nursery farms. To nursery workers, he exhorted them to equally respect the terms of contract and produce good quality banana and plantain plants.

Original_Search_Title: 10,000 Displaced By Skirmishes in Nimba County
Headline: Liberia: 10,000 Displaced By Skirmishes in Nimba County
Body_Text:
At least 10,000 civilians fleeing skirmishes between former government
fighters and MODEL rebels in Nimba County in north central Liberia, have
sought shelter in the relatively unscathed town of Saclepea, relief workers
said on Monday.
They told a UN assessment mission visiting Saclepea by helicopter that
the displaced people had arrived over the past week.
They said the civilians had fled fleeing fighting between forces loyal
to the former government of Charles Taylor, which control Saclepea, and
MODEL rebels advancing towards the town from Tapeta, 60 km to the
south.
The fighting flared up at the end of October, but the relief workers in
Saclepea, once a bustling market town of 15,000 people, said it finally died
down on 4 November.
"We have a very serious problem coping with the daily influx of the
displaced. Shelter, food and medication are their immediate needs," Cyrus
Saye, a relief worker with the Liberian Red Cross Society said.
"More than 3,000 weary and distressed IDPs [internally displaced
persons] are in Loryee village, one the biggest villages south of Saclepea
on the road linking to Tapeta," he added.
Relief workers said Saclepea, 285 km northeast of the capital Monrovia,
was the only town in Nimba county which had not been heavily damaged by
fighting earlier this year.
Clashes erupted in February when fighters of MODEL (Movement for
Democracy in Liberia) appeared on the scene and began to rapidly capture all
of southern and eastern Liberia. LURD (Liberians United for Reconciliation
and Democracy), a longer established rebel movement, meanwhile began
attacking Nimba Country from the west
Although UN peacekeepers have not yet been deployed to Saclepea, there
was no sign of gunmen in the town when the UN humanitarian mission visited
on Monday. Residents could be seen selling cigarettes, batteries, plantain,
and even used clothing at street stalls.
They said some of the goods had come from Diecke, a border town in
neighbouring Guinea, 60 km to the northwest.
An IRIN correspondent who accompanied the UN mission saw more than 100
displaced persons being registered at the Johnny Voker School in the town
center.
Its corridors and classrooms were crowded with people who had fled their
homes to escape the recent fighting. Most were old women and children who
said they slept at night on the bare school floor.
Mather Dahn, an elderly woman from Graie, a small town on the main road
to Tapita, said she walked in the forest for four hungry days before
reaching Saclepea. "I and my three children spent four days walking in the
bush without eating and we only lived on creek water," she said.
Moses Korwee, a teacher displaced from his home in Tapeta, said: "We are
tired running around. Let the UN talk to the fighters to stop killing us."
General Tommy Biddle, the commander of the pro-Taylor forces in
Saclepea, told IRIN that Graie was a buffer zone between his forces and
MODEL. But he insisted that the skirmishes in that area had ceased.
Graie, about four hours drive south of Saclepea on a pot-holed dirt
road, was the scene of intense battles at the beginning of November.
The scale of the clashes prompted General Daniel Opande, the head of the
UN peacekeeping force in Liberia, to visit the area by helicopter last week,
accompanied by senior government and MODEL commanders, to make peace.
Biddle said: "For us, it will be good for UNMIL to deploy between
Saclepea and Tapeta. In my own military analysis, if the troops are
deployed, there would be no more fighting in that entire region."
"Helicopter patrol is not sufficient. We want the UN to deploy and
patrol on land because they would be able to detect any troop movement and
their presence would weaken the spirit of fighters who may want to take up
arms," he added.
However, Opande has only 5,000 troops on the ground at present and he is
reluctant to stretch them too thinly on the ground. The United Nations
Mission in Liberia (UNMIL)does not expect to reach its full strength of
15,000 men until March.
Opande told reporters last week after visiting Saclepea that UN
peacekeepers patrolled the road from Monrovia to the nearby town of
Sanniquellie every two days, but he could not afford to deploy men
permanently in Nimba County at present.
Biddle said despite the warning by UNMIL to MODEL rebels to withdraw
from Graie to Tapeta, MODEL fighters still occupied the town and were
refusing to retreat.
The warring parties in Liberia signed a peace agreement in August that
paved the way for businessman Gyude Bryant to take power at the head of a
broad-based transitional government on 14 October. But LURD, MODEL and
soldiers and militiamen loyal to former president Taylor still control most
of the interior.
Trucks and buses have resumed plying the road from Saclepea, although
continuing insecurity in the area means that fares have more than doubled
from what they were in February before the road was cut by fighting between
government troops and LURD fighters near Ganta.
On Monday a 21-seater mini-bus could be seen in Saclepea loading up with
plantain and palm oil before heading for the capital.
"Before the war in Nimba, the cost of travel from Monrovia to Saclepea
was about 650 Liberian dollars (US $15). Now it is 1,500 Liberian dollars
(US $35)," Joseph Mehn, a resident of Saclepea said.

Original_Search_Title: Group Donates to Police Brutality Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Group Donates to Police Brutality Victims
Body_Text:
Enugu — A NON-GOVERNMENTAL Organisation, Women's Aid Collective (WACOL) has
donated N300,000 to 30 women in the Niger-Delta area of the country to aid
the rehabilitation and reintegration of women victims of Police/Military
brutality of August 8, last year.
Presenting the money to the beneficiaries at the end of a two-day Self
Esteem, Assertion and Empowerment workshop for victims of the state torture
in Enugu, Executive Director of EACOL, Mrs. Joy Ezeilo said that the women
received N10,000 each to enable them start off a small scale business.
She urged the women to invest the money wisely in order to be
financially empowered while contributing 10 per cent of the credit towards
the Victim Support Club (VSC) managed solely by them, to enable other
victims benefit from te scheme.
Receiving the donation, the first batch of women from Itsekiri, Ijaw and
Ilaje thanked WACOL for the gesture and promised to put the money to good
use. The women lamented their untold hardship occasioned by environmental
degradation of their communities by oil companies in the Niger Delta.
The women who spoke through their leaders, Mrs. Rose Orila (Ijaw),
Mrs. Baby Ade (Ilaje) and Mrs. Kate Ajagbawa (Isekiri), said they were yet
to recover from the physical and psychological injuries they suffered in the
hands of state agents (police and soliders) when they went on peaceful
demonstration to Shell and Chevron in August last year to demand for
compensations. According to them, the situations that led to the
demonstration had not abated, adding that both the multi-national oil
companies and the Federal Government had refused to address their
problems.
"The situation that informed our demonstration have not been addressed
by either the oil companies or Federal Government and many of us are yet to
recover from the police/military brutality during the demonstration," the
women said. One of the victims, a nursing mother now breast-feeds on only
one breast as the second is yet to heal from injuries sustained when the
security operatives physically injured her, they lamented.
Continuing, the women said, "our lives revolve around fishing and
farming. Even those of us who trade, trade on fish or farm produce. Now,
there are no fish to catch. Our farmlands have become infertile. All because
of oil spillage. We used to grow cassava and plantain. But they don't grow
anymore.
"We do not have pipe-borne water or boreholes. The river, which is our
only source of drinking water, has been polluted. Gas flaring has made
drinking rainwater unsafe. Consequently, our health suffers and there are no
health facilities. Our husbands and youths are jobless." They however called
on the Federal Government to provide social amenities while the oil
companies should compensate them and provide scholarship for their children
up to the tertiary level as well as employ their children who are
graduates.

Original_Search_Title: Consumer Price Index Rises By 4.1% in July
Headline: Nigeria: Consumer Price Index Rises By 4.1% in July
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The Federal Office of Statistics (FOS) today in Abuja said a Composite
Consumer Price Index (CPI) of 109.5 was recorded in July.
FOS in the July edition of its Statistical News stated that this showed
a significant increase of 4.1 per cent over the June cpi figure of
105.2.
It stated that the increase in the CPI figures for July was caused by
the rise in the price of some food items including, clothing materials,
fuel, transport fares and other services.
According to FOS, the urban and rural indices rose by 6.6 and 3.3 per
cent respectively when compared to June 2003 indices.
FOS said that in the twelve months to July 2003, the average consumer
price level went up by 12.9 per cent adding that this was lower than 14.0
per cent observed by making comparison with the levels in June.
"The urban and rural indices showed increase of 19.1 and 10.8 per cent
respectively over the same period", the statistics office stressed.
It said the percentage change in the average composite CPI for the
twelve months period ending July 2003 over the average CPI for the previous
twelve months period was 10 per cent.
"This means that a fixed market basket of consumer goods and services
purchased with N100 during the twelve months period ending July 2002 sold
for N110 on the average for the corresponding period ending July 2003", it
explained.
FOS news said the percentage change also indicated a small decrease when
compared with 10.1 per cent observed by making a similar comparison in June
this year.
The FOS also stated that composite food index increased by 0.2 per cent
in July adding that the marginal rise in the index was due to the slight
increase in the prices of staples and proteins such as rice, plantain, beef
and fish as well as edible oils.
"The level of the composite food index was higher than the corresponding
level a year ago by 0.7 percent ", it stated. FOS further said that the
average annual rate rise of the index was 5.4 per cent for the twelve months
period ending July 2003.

Original_Search_Title: Refugee Farmers Seek Banana Shoots, Cassava Cuttings
Headline: Central African Republic: Refugee Farmers Seek Banana Shoots, Cassava Cuttings
Body_Text:
Molangue — Refugees farmers from the Democratic Republic of the Congo living in
Camp Molangue, about 120 km south of the Central African Republic (CAR)
capital, Bangui, have asked the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO)
to provide them with banana shoots and more cassava cuttings for
cultivation.
The refugees made their request on Tuesday to a FAO delegation that was
inspecting farming projects at the camp, for which the FAO had provided
maize, cassava and groundnuts over the last two years.
The refugees fled fighting in their country from 1997-8 and have been
living in Camp Molangue since 2001.
One of them, Faustin Mabele, is an agronomist. He said at least 1,000
families had received 536 more hectares to cultivate during the current
September-October planting season.
"Most of us want to have plantain shoots to plant in that additional
land, " Mabele told the FAO mission, which included Roger Larribe, a
consultant for the agency on a visit to the CAR.
Larribe told IRIN on Tuesday that with US $12,000 of the $128,000
refugee-farming project left, the FAO would before 30 October distribute at
least 10 banana shoots to each refugee family. He said the shoots would be
bought locally.
With the same funds and within the same period, Larribe said, 400
refugees who were transferred from Bangui to the camp in December 2002, and
who received neither seeds nor farming tools, would be provided these
items.
"Each family is to receive about 500 cuttings and farming tools," he
said.
The FAO office in Bangui, he said, still had 1,400 hoes, 1,400 machetes
and 500 axes. The remaining tools would be distributed as rewards to those
of the 30 refugee farming cooperatives that produced the largest quantity of
food last season.
Mabele said the refugees produced about 543 mt of food last season. The
Office of the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) gave 18 million
francs CFA ($30,000) in August to buy 90 mt of maize from the refugee
farmers, which will be distributed to 750 vulnerable refugees.
Jean De Dieu Ndjopou, an employee of the Italian NGO, Cooperazione
Internazionale (COOPI), working in the camp told IRIN that UNHCR, through
COOPI, had already bought 22.4 mt of maize and 5.6 mt from nationals who,
together with refugees, form farming cooperatives.
Targeting 58 pregnant women, 78 nursing women, 150 elderly, 38
malnourished children, 400 transferred refugees and people suffering from
different chronic diseases, the food aid would last until December. On
Tuesday, the beneficiaries received their first ration made up of maize
grains, palm oil bought from refugee and national farmers, as well as salt,
sugar and beans purchased in Bangui.
The refugee farm project is aimed at enabling the refugees to survive
and make money. Some, who have limited food storage facilities, have asked
the FAO to find buyers for their produce.  FAO-Bangui Programme Officer
Etienne Ngounio Gabia said the agency was considering how it and the
National Livestock Federation of the Central African Republic (Federation
Nationale des Eleveurs Centrafricains) could purchase some of the maize for
another FAO pig and chicken rearing project that is about to be
launched.

Original_Search_Title: Death in the Slow Lane - The Tragic Story of Farming as an Industry in Ghana
Headline: Ghana: Death in the Slow Lane - The Tragic Story of Farming as an Industry in Ghana
Body_Text:
Accra — Is Agriculture or farming a dying industry? An expert in the area says
the country's agriculture needs a vigorous shake up to keep it awake.
"It is though not sick but also not well," he said
Dr. V.K Nyanten an Agricultural Economist with the Institute of
Statistical Social and Economic Research (ISSER) of the University of Ghana,
Legon says Ghanaian farmers have treated agriculture as a way of life and
not business over the years.
"Any body who takes a cutlass and cultivates less than an acre of land
calls himself a farmer," he said adding "some of them even refuse to borrow
money to expand their farms when funds are available and even if they do,
they borrow for consumption and not for expansion."
This explains why loan repayment is quite low among such farming
communities.
Farmers in this category also use very old farming implements like the
cutlass and the hoe and relies solely on household labour . They also appear
very poor-making farming unattractive to their children.
Dr. Nyanten who has been teaching at the University for about 30 years
and who retires at the end of the month called for a redefinition of who a
farmer is in Ghana to carve a new image for the business.
Ghana's agriculture which is said to be a backbone of the economy
contributes about 40 percent of the country's Gross Domestic Product
(GDP).
It also employs about 6o percent of the economically active people while
bringing in 42 percent of foreign exchange.
Agriculture also accounts for 80 percent of food supply in the
country. It again constitutes about 70 commodities of non-traditional
exports.
Agriculture is expected to grow between six to eight percent before the
country can reach a middle-income status by the year 2020.
Ghana has had several agricultural programmes in the past.
These included the famous Operation feed yourself by the Acheampong
Government in the 70s as well as Kwame Nkrumah's Socialist agricultural
plans.
Recent ones include the President's special initiative on Cassava
etc.
Unfortunately, Dr. Nyanten said many of the programmes were not carried
out to their logical conclusion and no proper evaluations have been done for
any of them.
The main source of funding for agricultural programmes have been from
the country's donor partners.
Dr. Nyanten said irregular donor inflows have affected the country's
agricultural production and implementation programmes.
Dr. Nyanten said such farmers should be people who can diversify their
business when farming fails that is if they are unable to make money in
their bid to become farmers.
Such people should be business inclined and look for improved technology
for higher yield.
He should be eager to borrow for expansion as well as respond to
production incentives.
A business inclined farmers should also operate a medium to large scale
enterprise and make money from his business.
Dr. Nyanten said the bulk of Ghanaian farmers fall within the range of
those who see farming as a way of life.
Problems of Ghanaian farmers are varied.
Most farmers are above the age of 50 years and this is a threat to food
security if nothing is done about the situation.
Also the collapse of produce price at harvest periods have rendered most
farmers poor.
This is evident during the last plantain harvest season where the
country experienced a high glut.
Several tons of plantain got rotten at the Agbogbloshie market since
farmers could not find buyers.
Dr. Nyanten was also worried that the country's industries are not well
developed to absorb huge farm produce such as what happened during the last
plantain season.
The Minister for Food and Agriculture Major (Rtd) Courage Quashigah at
his appearance with the media at the Meet the Press series in Accra said the
government has commissioned scientists to research into how perishable goods
like plantain could be preserved for use in the lean seasons.
The practice in Accra is that many men and women in the business bring
the plantain from the hinterland some as far as Techiman in the Brong-Ahafo
Region only to meet low prices because supply during the bumper season
usually outstrips demand. Not even the traders at the Agbogbloshie and
Kantamanto who hail from Apedwa in the Eastern region whose plantain is
usually in high demand as a result of its special taste were spared the drop
in prices this year. They therefore called for mechanisms to preserve
plantain to ensure that prices are stable all year round.
According to the traders what made the situation worse was the fact that
the glut coincided with the increase in fuel prices.
"We paid more for our Transport and made huge losses," said Ama
Amankwaa, A trader from the Juaboso Bia area of the Western Region.
There is also the issue of lack of irrigation facilities for dry season
farming making farming a seasonal profession.
Most young men are idle during the dry season because there is no water
for farming activities.
Although some young farmers have acquired some water pumping machines
which enable them to pump water from nearby streams to aid dry season
farming Dr. Nyanten believes a lot still needs to be done.
Ministry of Food and Agriculture earlier in the year said in spite of
the food glut experienced this year as a result of abundant rains; the
country cannot continue to rely on rain fed agriculture.
It has therefore decided to rehabilitate obsolete irrigation equipment
in various parts of the country to ensure the availability of food all year
round.
To this effect, an amount of 900 million cedis has been committed
towards the rehabilitation of the Vea and Tono irrigation projects.
The Minister for Food and Agriculture Major Courage Quashigah (Rtd) said
his administration has repaired a broken down valve, which had led to the
loss of water enough to irrigate about 50 hectares of land daily for the
past years at Botanga at the cost of 30 million cedis.
Farmers are also just using more land to the neglect of modern
techniques in farming.
Only 11 percent of land will be suitable for farming by the year 2020 if
nothing is done about the state of land use for farming activities in the
country.
Meanwhile many environmental activists have kicked against the use of
Biotechnology as a means of increasing production worldwide.
Biotechnology is defined as "any technique that make use of organisms
(or parts thereof) to make or modify products to improve plants or animals
or to develop micro-organisms for specific purposes."
Rights activists worldwide have been fighting against biotechnology
being used to produce genetically modified food.
Seeds from such plants though give higher yields are unable to geminate
again and farmers always have to go back to special shops and countries to
buy them at an expensive price.
There are also fears that genetically modified food may lead to health
hazards over a long period of time.
Research is not conclusive on this.
Dr. Nyanten also said one of the problems of Ghana's agriculture has
been the removal of subsidies on agriculture in 1990 and the Trade
Liberalization Policy.
"While developed countries are subsidizing theirs, we have cut down
drastically on our subsidies for farmers," he said.
This issue of farm subsidies is one of the main problems that led to the
collapse of the just ended World Trade Organisation Meeting in Cancun
Mexico.
Trade Experts say in 2001 alone, America's 25,000 cotton farmers
received $3.6 billion in government support, three times US aid to
Africa.
Each year industrialized countries provide over $300 billion in support
to agricultural producers.
The controversial US Farm Bill which came into effect last year did not
only increase budget support for farmers but also strengthened the links
between agriculture and production.
Lower world prices in cotton caused by American subsidies mean that
desperately poor households have seen their incomes fall with attendant
consequences for poverty.
According to a trade expert Kevin Watkins, the price decline in Benin
translates into a four percent increase in poverty or 250,000 people falling
below the poverty line.
Excluded from rich country markets, small country markets also suffer in
domestic markets. In their development rhetoric, most northern governments
recognize that small holder agriculture is vital to poverty reduction, yet
they underminelocal markets of food producers through subsidized
dumping.
Trade Liberalisation is taking a toll on the rice and poultry industries
in Ghana.
Poultry farmers have complained that the dumping of poultry from
developed countries and the high cost of poultry production in the country
has made it difficult for them to make profit.
Ghana Government's attempt to impose a 20 percent import tariff on
poultry and a five percent tariff on rice had to be reversed as a result of
international pressure.
Again rice production in the country almost collapsed until it was
resuscitated not long ago as a result of dumping of rice from advanced
countries especially the US.
Most local foods such as Waakye which was prepared with the local paddy
rice is now being prepared with imported perfumed rice from the Rice Master,
a popular American Rice Trade Mark.
With a recent research finding that Kenkey, a local maize meal may
contain Aflatoxins, a cancer causing agent if the maize is not dried well,
some Ghanaians have changed their diet from local dishes to rice dishes such
as fried rice which is normally sold along side fatty imported chicken
thighs.
Health authorities have warned against the consequences of the continued
consumption of such high fat meals.
Head of the Ghana Health Service Prof. Agyeman Badu Akosah who is also
the chief pathologists has said on several occasions that shift in diet of
some Ghanaians is contributing in no small way to the upsurge of fat related
diseases such as hypertension in the country.
Dr. Nyanten said before agriculture could make strides in the country,
policies need to be reviewed to provide incentives to farmers.
He said the Ministry of Food and Agriculture(MOFA) lacks adequate staff
to cover all the 110 districts of the country to supervise farming
activities.
He said there is the urgent need to conduct research in District
Assemblies agricultural activities and training of staff.
According to the Ghana Poverty Reduction Strategy, although there are
options that can be employed to achieve the set goals, consultations with
other interest groups indicate that there is a consensus that the five
objectives be achieved through private sector led production, promoting
Agri-business as opposed to subsistence farming and Agro-based industrial
expansion.
The rest are Government should facilitate strategic interventions where
markets fail, investments in social services that complement agricultural
and agro-industrial production, accelerated decentralisation to facilitate
policy implementation and public sector reforms to leverage private sector
productivity.
According to the Minister for Trade and Industry Alan Kyerematen, the
Ministry intends to use tariff and other non-tariff measures as instruments
to promote domestic activities.
A lot of water has passed under the bridge since government withdrew
tariffs on poultry and rice, but poultry farmers have threatened to drag the
government to court over the matter.
Many Ghanaians believe that tariff barriers could solve part of the
problems of dumping of goods on our markets.

Original_Search_Title: Shiroro: in the Wake of the Great Flood
Headline: Nigeria: Shiroro: in the Wake of the Great Flood
Body_Text:
Once in a while nature seems to make a golden turn in favour of those
who are in need.
Galkogo is one community where such remarkable truism is being played
out on a daily basis. Gold abounds in the area, and many have been thrust
into great wealth on this account. On the way there, by means of a
motorcycle, which traversed a narrowing and muddy path, Daily Trust
encountered young girls of primary school age panning for gold in a
stream.
Perhaps they should have been in school, but here they were in life's
own golden school. They had spades and all the vital patience which the task
requires. In Galkogo itself those who have succeeded in mining have bought
motorcycles and generators. To a degree the quality of life has radically
altered. Here the people seem to be a bit more enthusiastic about life, and
this is quite natural.
In an interview with Daily Trust, Comrade Mohammed Erena, the Secretary
to the Niger state government stated that mining for gold in the area is an
illegal activity, but he said that solid minerals belong to the federal
government. In his words: "The solid minerals belong to the federal
government, and the state cannot go into it, but we can hold these areas in
trust for the federal government.
We know there is gold. The United Nations once sent a team of geologists
last year, and they did a survey, and though their survey was on water and
water related issues, in the process of doing that, they came upon gold, and
they informed us that the gold we have around this area is of commercial
magnitude. According to them if real mining commences there, what obtains in
South Africa would be a child's play."  There is a mass video centre in
Galkogo too, and it seems to illustrate the new self-confidence inspired by
a number of mining activities in the area.
Most of the nine flooded areas covered by this reporter are Gbagyi
speaking communities and the inhabitants are largely farmers, and a number
of hunters and fishermen are present too. One community which graphically
illustrates the crisis which manifested in the wake of the floods is Jiko. A
trip by Land Rover gets one to Guni, from where a boat ride is necessary to
get to Jiko and the neighbouring village of Manta. Boat rides are the
regular, means of transportation and your pilot may fall asleep sometimes as
mine did. Jiko provides evidence of much devastation. There are scenes of
flooded farms and withered crops.
Maize, Banana and Plantain fields have all been destroyed, and in some
cases it is necessary for the farmer to carry out an emergency
harvest. Indeed all crops here look quite sad and forlorn. The villagers
complain about the dam which has turned out to be a curse rather than a
blessing. At Manta, the local situation even before the flood, left much to
be desired. The village clinic is in poor health, and it looks unbelievably
inadequate. There are two beds there. One has no pillow case or bed sheet,
while the other is made of poor cane. The walls are badly in need of paint,
and another section of the clinic has a ceiling which looks positively
rotten. On the whole the clinic does not seem fit enough to carry out its
role.
Clinics in the valley generally fit this description. The nearby primary
school was recently renovated by the Emirate Tax Fund, and a former block of
classrooms had collapsed, Daily Trust is told, which justified the
renovation exercise in the first instance. The District Head of Manta
lamented that there is poor communications between the Shiroro dam and his
domain. He stated that they were informed by NEPA that in two weeks time
waters would be released from the dam, but he was surprised to see that the
waters came much earlier than expected. It also seems that the few islands
within the valley are significant to the numerous communities of farmers and
fishermen, but the villagers complain that these little islands come under
much buffeting from the dam waters, and sometimes these islands are
thoroughly inundated. Jiko and Mantu provide grim pictures of the flood and
its impact within the Shiroro valley.
Perhaps one sign of the neglect the environment has endured, is the
plaque of the foundation stone for a bridge which is supposed to link the
communities in the valley. Laid by General Abdulsalami Abubakar in May 1999,
no work has yet taken place there. In fact the plaque was surrounded by
weeds, and grass had to be cut so that a picture could be taken. Community
Action for Popular Participation (CAPP) an Abuja based NGO some years ago
did a thorough study of this valley and their observations are documented in
the work titled 'Damned by the Dam.'
However, in a number of places one came across young farmers busy at
work. There is a conscious effort to keep hope alive here. Then there is the
unpleasant sight of sticks raised to support buildings which had been
buffeted in the wake of the flood, but which today are still standing,
almost miraculously.
There is no sign of electricity in any of the communities visited. Roads
are non-existent, and even the effort to improve one self is limited by the
quality of communication facilities. For instance pupils at the primary
school at Manta have to go to Gurmana, some 50 kilometres away whenever they
have to write the Common Entrance Examination. Sometimes they need to go to
Erena which is a total of 70 kilometres further. The situation is always
worsened if there is the slightest problem with their boats, and this is the
basic means of transport and communication in the valley.
Of course if there was electricity supply, this would mean the
possibility of e-mails and information, which means that some journeys many
not have be undertaken. The words of the District Head of Manta are quite
helpful in this matter.
"I was born here but I have never seen a flood as serious as this
one. Floods have changed so many things for us. Now we no longer harvest
fish in abundance as in the early days. Formerly, our markets used to burst
with fish. We then had fish in excess of the local demand, and then we used
to transport the remaining fish to Minna and then these were great in
number. Today, this is not so. The government should please help us."
Comrade Mohammed Erena, secretary to the Niger State Government shed
light on recent events in these communities when he told Daily Trust that
most of the communities living downstream are very close to the river bank,
and this is because some of the people find it difficult to leave the very
special spots where their ancestors had once lived.
On the resettlement of communities which played host to the dam
initially, he said "We have a situation where majority of the communities
that were resettled, were not even provided with basic amenities like
electricity and water supply. Even schools for the children of these
resettled communities were ignored. Even in the so-called resettled areas,
you would find a lot of contradictions in the sense that you would encounter
a resettled community not able to enjoy facilities provided.
For instance, as for the NEPA Secondary School in Shiroro, not all
members of the community have the opportunity to send their children to that
school. We battled with that aspect for long, and it was just recently that
NEPA began to live up to expectation. We have just started talking to NEPA
about the electrification of some of the communities that were resettled
many years ago, and nobody bothered about linking them with electricity, not
to talk of a portable water supply."
He decried the absence of roads in the area. Indeed this reporter was
able to get to many of the communities because the Secretary to the state
government was kind enough to provide a Land Rover, a driver and an amiable
guide in the person of Sanusi Mohammed, Special Assistant to the SSG.
Commenting on communications within the valley, Comrade Mohammed Erena
had this to say "Even with the Shiroro dam, you cannot talk of a telephone
linkage as such, talk less of the other communities. But recently the local
government ministry decided to tackle head on the problem of communications,
at least in terms of telephone lines. The wish is to have a rural telephone
system.
We are hoping that in the very near future, most of these remote local
governments would have access to rural telephone lines, so that we would be
able to communicate with them easily, because there is an obvious
communications problem." Indeed much property would have been saved if
electricity supply and its benefits which include televisions, computers etc
had been a regular feature of life in the valley. A better informed and
ready citizenry would have been living in the valley.
Given the accumulated and complex problems suffered by communities
located close to where hydro-electric power structures exist, there has been
a call for the establishment of a body which would look into the problems
suffered by these areas. Communities in the states of Niger, Kwara, Kebbi
and Kogi are the special areas to be focused on by this body. This body is
to be known as the Hydro-Electric Power Producing Areas Development
Commission (HYPPADEC). The National Assembly has passed a Bill in this
respect, and it is hoped that President Olusegun Obasanjo would endorse the
Bill shortly. If these floods, bad roads, poor communication facilities and
years of official neglect etc would lead to the establishment of such a
unique structure, which has serious implications for life, development and
communications in the area, then the flood itself, terrifying as it was, may
have been ringed by beams of hope.

Original_Search_Title: UNDP Partners State Govts On Community Projects, Schemes
