Headline: Nigeria: UNDP Partners State Govts On Community Projects, Schemes
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Calls for integration of state branches
The United Nations Development Programme, the development arm of the
United Nations has initiated moves to ensure greater efficiency concerning
its projects and schemes in Nigeria, through integration with the various
state administrations all over the country.
Under the arrangement, which is expected to take place nationwide before
the end of the year 2003, the donor organisation has asked all its states'
branches to work together with their host state governments to avoid
duplication of efforts and engender wider spread of activities and
projects.
The body, which has just concluded its last country programme in after a
five year programme, running from 1997 to 2002, is set to begin a fresh set
of projects aimed at enhancing community development, skills acquisition,
poverty alleviation, and capacity building in the country.
The directive, which issued from the national head office, explained
that all external aid programmes should be integrated into development plan
of each state before December 2003. Another focus of the plan is to ensure
the host states and affected communities have a sense of ownership of the
various schemes under implementation in the states, thus engendering greater
participation of the people and sustainability of the projects.
Several states have taken the directive seriously, and moves are being
made to ensure compliance. Among the states include Akwa-Ibom, where there
are various UNDP-assisted projects on ground, and Adamawa states. In
Akwa-Ibom, the officials of the agency are to work closely with the state
government under the auspices of the Ministry of Economic Development for
project implementation and supervision.
Based on the target priorities of the organisation, which include
capacity building, skills acquisition, poverty alleviation, community
development, and financial empowerment, state officials are expected to
implement projects which will better the lives of the rural poor and the
productive urban populations.
On a recent visit to Akwa-Ibom, THISDAY observed various projects
targeted at development of the citizenry and infrastructure. These were
implemented under a five-year rolling plan which allegedly gulped more than
N131million under UNDP assistance. Officials of the group claimed the funds
were provided in partnership with concerned communities and the state
government to help reduce the level of poverty in the state.
One of the major and latest projects, which is yet to be commissioned by
the agency and the state government, is the Information and Communication
Technologies Center at Uyo. The centre, which boasts of more than one
hundred and eighty-three students, has three different programmes. There is
the three month certificate programme, the six-month diploma programme, and
the executive programme for working students on part-time training.
The project is being implemented by the University of Uyo, Yocom
Technologies (which provided 40 computer systems worth around N5.5m); the
Akwa-Ibom State Government (which provided accommodation and the sum of
N1.4m); and the UNDP, which also provided computers and N2.5m. All in all,
over N10m has been expended on the centre, where students are taught
computer appreciation, programming, and desktop skills.
The centre, which attracts various categories of individuals, serves as
an important tool for skills acquisition and empowerment, and there are
plans to make sure similar centres are opened all over the state. The centre
is also self-sustaining as students pay for their certificate
programmes.
Another important project, which has made rapid progress at development
and sustainability in the state is the Entrepreneurs' Village at
Akpan-Andem, which has over five thousand stalls spread over a large expanse
of land in the state capital, Uyo.
The project, which was constructed in 1999 and commissioned in 2000,
allegedly gulped more than N179million. The entrepreneurs themselves, who
are the direct beneficiaries of the programme put up the sum of N94m, the
state government contributed N45m, while UNDP assisted with N40m. The large
complex of bungalows houses various trades, vocations, professions, and
businesses. Among them are the vocational training school, which has
students who undergo skills acquisition training programmes for varying
length of time before taking up any of the vocations like sowing,
hairdressing, barbing, and adult literacy classes.
Other units in the village included the motor mechanics' workshop, where
apprentices are taught intricacies of mechanical engineering; open market
stalls where market women who have benefited from the micro-credit scheme
display and sell their wares, especially foodstuffs and other staples;
retail and wholesale outlets in lock-up shops; banks; offices of the
micro-finance institutions (MFIs, in this case, the Peace Development
Centre); police station; fire station; distribution stalls; meeting places
of traders' associations; restaurants; and borehole projects, among
others.
Other projects included the Integrated Community Development Project at
Iboko, which has formed the bedrock of rural development in the
state. There, rural people from all over the state, especially women, gather
to learn skills in agro-processing and vocational training. The successful
graduates then export the same knowledge to their various local communities,
and spread the knowledge through application and further training at the
local level.
The Iboko project (pictured above), is a combined project of the Federal
Government (who provided funds); the Akwa-Ibom State Government (also
funding); the National Directorate of Employment (NDE, which provided the
bore-hole/overhead tank; the local communities (Mboho, Nkaiso, Ndito, and
Iboko communities), which provided the land; the UNDP, which provided the
equipment used at the centre; and the United Nations Industrial Development
Organisation (UNIDO), which provided training for the individuals and groups
who frequent the project __cpLocation.
Among the activities at the centre include the grains mill, the borehole
project to service the community, frying pot for cassava; rabbitry; fish
farms, cassava sieves; fabricator units where various unique farm implements
like the two-wheeled barrow are fabricated; plantain plantation; the oil
palm mill, which has boilers, extractors, kernel cracking machines; fish
smoking kiln; and maize plots among others; all under the supervision of
Mr. Felix Inyang.
The various projects mentioned above, which are self-sustaining and
development-focused, are still being coordinated by the State Programme
Manager, Mr. Effiong Akpan, who, along with Dr. Akan, the Director of
International Cooperation, are to work under the overall supervision of the
top officials of the state Ministry of Economic Development, with Dr. Ebebe
Ukpong as the Commissioner, and Dr. Valentine Attah as the Permanent
Secretary of the ministry. Incidentally, Dr. Attah was the immediate past
state supervisor of UNDP in the state before joining the ministry, with vast
experience in developmental efforts.

Original_Search_Title: Should Ghana Accept Genetically Modified Products?
Headline: Ghana: Should Ghana Accept Genetically Modified Products?
Body_Text:
Accra — Although the issue of Biotechnology and genetic engineering is being
hotly debated in advanced countries and even in southern Africa, not much
has been said about it in Ghana.
The technical nature of the subject has not made it possible for the
attention of Ghanaians to be drawn to the issue and also join the debate to
decide whether Ghana should opt for it or not.
Media reports indicate that Ghana is in the middle of a difficult
decision-that is to accept genetically modified seeds or not.
While the Americans are persuading Ghana to accept them, the European
Union on the other hand is asking Ghana not to accept them.
Rights activists worldwide have been fighting against biotechnology
being used to produce genetically modified food.
Seeds from such plants though give higher yields are unable to geminate
again and farmers always have to go back to special shops and countries to
buy them at an expensive price.
There are also fears that genetically modified food may lead to health
hazards over a long period of time. Research is not conclusive on this.
Several European countries are helping their farmers to grow organic
food while consumers will have nothing to do with Genetically Modified
Food.
However, American citizens are not as worried as Europeans about their
food choices and opposition to GM foods are not as strong as in Europe
although many of them will opt for organic grown foods.
When the Minister for Food and Agriculture Major Courage Quashigah met
the press in Accra earlier on, he explained that Ghana has not yet decided
on the use of genetically modified seeds in agriculture.
He said Ghanaian scientists are still researching into the pros and cons
of such seeds and its impact on the country's agriculture.
The former Minister for Environment and Science, Prof. Dominic Fobih in
a speech read for him at a National Roundtable discussion on Biotechnology
under the theme "Environmental Degradation-Biotechnology to the rescue."
said Ghanaians need to engage in discussions on development issues driven by
science and technology.
He said it is important for Ghanaians to recognize the potential of
science and technology as critical tools for socio-economic development and
the need to understand the benefits and risks of specific technologies such
as Biotechnology.
Biotechnology is defined as "any technique that make use of organisms
(or parts thereof) to make or modify products to improve plants or animals
or to develop micro-organisms for specific purposes."
It was a third in a series after the first two on health and
agriculture.
The meeting was aimed at promoting dialogue on the risks and benefits of
Biotechnology for a developing country like Ghana.
"I am aware of the potential of Biotechnology as a tool for cleaner
environment," the Minister said adding "biotechnology processes and products
can be deployed in biomediation and microbial treatments of polluted water
and soils, industrial and domestic wastes as well as the development of
diagnostics to enhance environmental monitoring, assessment and
management.
There has also been claims that the mining sector in Ghana is making
"breathtaking advancements" in heap mining technologies using bioleaching
techniques in mining activities.
The Ashanti Goldfields Company in particular has been cited as having
installed a BIOX plant believed to be one of the largest in the world. This
BIOX plant is said to enhance the recovery of gold from tailings, which are
normally discarded with negative impact on the environment.
The bacteria in the BIOX plant depend on arsenates to survive and
therefore metabolise the arsenates from the tailings.
This natural characteristic of the bacteria eliminates arsenates from
the tailings -leading to the release of a clean air in mining areas, which
suffered pollution for years.
Biotechnology processes and products have also been widely deployed in
the world for treatment of wastewater, which can be recycled.
Again modern biotechnologies are also being exploited to convert
agricultural wastes into biodegradable plastics.
According to some scientists, the developments of biodegradable plastics
will reduce the proportion on non-biodegradable and ozone depleting plastic
bags in mountainous waste dumps considering the fact that plastics have
become the packaging material for virtually all street foods and drinks and
water in particular.
Scientists also say the conversion of agricultural waste and sludge into
biogas is a technology process and has already been demonstrated in Ghana,
specifically- Appolonia, a farming community near Accra, Pokuase, and
Achimota School among others.
"If this technology is efficiently applied in small communities,
hospitals and schools, it could reduce the nation's heavy dependence on
conventional sources of energy notably petroleum" Fobih said at that
meeting.
Biotechnology he said can also be used to convert agricultural wastes
into bio fertilizers, which are environmentally clean.
Although the biotechnology has its positive sides, it has its negative
sides as well.
Perhaps it is against this background that the Minister said "The goal
of government of Ghana is to ensure the safety of Ghanaians and our
environment," adding, "it appears I am highlighting only on the benefits of
biotechnology but then it is through the appreciation of these benefits that
will stimulate a thorough investigation of the technology to identify
potential risks and challenges associated with its deployment."
He said the Ministry of Environment and Science is exploiting
opportunities to enhance the development of technical capability for the
safe and environmentally sound management of Biotechnology applications in
Ghana.
Ghana ratified a protocol on Biosafety known as the Cartagena Protocol
last December.
Biosafety is also defined as the development of policies and procedures
adopted to ensure the environmentally safe application of modern
biotechnology.
This protocol also seeks to assist parties develop and implement
environmentally sound management of Biotechnology at both the international
and local levels.
"I am happy to announce that the ratification of the protocol paved the
way for Ghana to join the global community in the implementation of the
protocol for sound environmental management.
He also said Ghana is currently among some 100 countries participating
in a United nations Environment Programme (UNDP) project in collaboration
with the Global Environment Facility (GEF).
The project is to assist countries develop their National Biosafety
policy frameworks and build local capacity in the sound management of
biotechnology.
At a meeting in Accra last Wednesday to launch official consultations
programme of the Framework development process under the theme
"Biotechnology for sustainable development in Ghana, the current Minister
for Environment and Science Prof. Kassim Kasanga has this to say, "We have
formulated a national science and technology policy, which among other
things endorses the use of innovative and pervasive technologies, including
biotechnology as tools for development."
"I must admit that Biotechnology has become a key issue in the
international debate on sustainable development, my ministry is therefore
developing the technical capability to ensure the safe and environmentally
sound management of biotechnology in Ghana.
According to Kasanga, the government through the Ministry of Environment
and Science has been a firm advocate of the precautionary principle which
clearly states that "When embarking on something, we should think very
clearly about whether it is safe or not and we should not go ahead if we are
not convinced."
Farmers in Africa have been warned to be wary of the supply of cheap
genetically engineered seeds which already have components of pesticides in
them until they lose their traditional methods of seed supply, soil and pest
controls as well as disease management systems.
Seeds would eventually fall in the hands of big multinational companies
who are now patenting various seeds and other living things and they would
dictate the price if western countries and their companies manage to go
ahead with various proposals currently under negotiations at the World Trade
Organisation (WTO).
The history of genetically modified food is a long one.
It all started in the industrialised countries of Europe and North
America with the patenting of mechanical inventions.
The idea behind the patenting was that if a person invents a new
mechanical innovation, he or she could monopolise the use of that invention
for a period of about 15 to 25 years.
All other people who would want to use that invention then would have to
pay royalties to the one who invented it.
As to how it came to apply to living things, it was the British
government who wanted to ensure that the seed used for agricultural
production was of high quality.
At the end of the Second World War Britain instituted a system for
registering good quality seeds. Seed companies were then required to sell
only registered seed varieties they bred. Afterwards the British government
thought of rewarding individuals who improved seeds further and gave them
legal rights which came to be known as "Breeders Rights."
This gave the breeders the right to levy royalties on the new seed they
bred.
Later four developments took place originating from the desire to
control the market which even distorted the intellectual property rights
given to those who invented machines and equipment.
Firstly, the royalties became insignificant compared to expenses
incurred in legal fees used in registering the rights as well as spying on
what other seed companies were coming out with. This discouraged individuals
in the business leaving oil in the hands of big companies.
Secondly, the area of seed production and marketing, the seed companies
dropped good seeds that were not protected by any rights and focused only on
the registered seeds.
Seed production in Britain therefore moved from the hands of the farmers
to the companies.
Thirdly, breeders rights became more and more restrictive until the 1991
version of the Union for the Protection of New Varieties of Plants(UPOV)
became very similar to patent law.
Fourthly because the restrictive breeders rights laws prepared the
ground, the patenting of living things became accepted, first in the USA and
then in Europe.
One of the major issues that has attracted public disgust and criticism
is moves by the two biotechnology giants in the world the US company
Monsanto and the British Company Zeneca is their decision to programme death
in the second generation into seeds they sell so that farmers will be forced
to buy new patented seed from it every year.
"The Monsanto Technology has now become known as the "Terminator" while
the Zeneca Technology is known as the "Verminator," said Dr. Tewolde Berhan
Egziabher of the Ethiopian Environmental Protection Agency.
"These companies can force their technologies on farmers because over
decades, the European and American and even many developing farmers have
lost their traditional varieties and are entirely dependent on the seeds
these corporations bring to the market."
The health implications of eating food grown with genetically engineered
seeds are not clear with so many people fearing it might have grave health
consequences.
It has also been established by experts that some of the seeds are
programmed in such a way that they are not friendly towards other plants and
such plants may die if a genetically modified seed is grown near it.
It is against this background that the Organisation of African Unity now
African Union developed a model law on Plant Breeders Rights and Regulation
of Biological Resources in 1999.
The council of Trade Ministers in 2000 encouraged all member states to
enact their own national provisions into legislation to protect the right of
local communities, farmers and breeders as well as for the regulation of
access to biological resources in their countries.
The Ministry of Justice and Attorney General in conjunction with the
Third World Network has already organised a consultative meeting in Accra to
deliberate on the draft law known as "Protection of Plant Varieties bill,
2002."
The Chairman of the Ghana national Biodiversity Committee, Prof. A. A
Oteng Yeboah at that meeting said biological resources are natural capital
and their conservation should be an investment that would yield benefits
locally, nationally and globally for the present and future
generations.
"Benefits accruing from the utilisation and conservation are to be
shared equitably and fairly among all stakeholders," he said adding, "The
use of traditional and customary knowledge of biological diversity will also
be equitably shared with those from whom the knowledge has been acquired."
Speaking on Biotechnology at one of the meetings in Accra, the Director
for the Centre for Scientific and Industrial Research Prof. E. Owusu Bennoah
said Biotechnology could be developed and deployed to solve critical
national problems such as environmental degradation.
"We feel science must be brought to the doorstep of Ghanaians," he said
adding, "It is pertinent to harness the human capital which abounds in our
research institutions and universities for our common good."
He said Ghanaian scientists have been in the forefront in the use of
tissue culture for crop improvement while researchers have developed
protocols for crops such as cassava, plantain, paw paw and pineapple.
Research on the use of Sterile Insect technique to control the
population of tsetse flies in the northern region is also on going. "The
successful applications of this technique should control sleeping sickness
in both animals and humans, improve health, increase human productivity and
reduce poverty," Prof. Bennoah noted.
He also said Ghanaian scientists have acquired considerable expertise in
the application of radiation processing to reduce post harvest loss, improve
food safety and also for sterilization of medical products.
While certain aspects of biotechnology could be arguably good, the issue
of genetically modified food and seeds should not be encouraged and all
Ghanaians are welcome to the debate.

Original_Search_Title: The Shame of Post-Harvest Losses
Headline: Ghana: The Shame of Post-Harvest Losses
Body_Text:
HARVEST SEASON is here and a lot of food is now on the market. Prices have dropped and festivals are being celebrated. We are all content.
But in a few months from now when the rains are over and we have consumed most of what was produced, another lean season will arrive. Prices will shoot up and then the problem of food scarcity will be with us.
This pattern of abundance and scarcity has been with us since time immemorial. Does it have to be so? Earlier this year, tomato prices per crate shot up to almost Â¢1.5 million compared to the current Â¢60,000. That is just one example.
Italy, with a short growing season of just three months, produces enough tomatoes to be leading net exporters in the world, all year round. And the secret? Food processing and preservation!
The economy of this country is based on agriculture and yet we cannot produce enough to feed ourselves.
We spend millions of dollars each year importing food to meet local demand. Rice alone absorbs over $100 million whilst sugar takes up an equal amount, if not more.
For a country blessed with vast swathes of rich soils complemented with a huge reserve of fresh water (the Volta lake) is it not shameful, indeed criminally negligent, for such a situation to be allowed to exist?
Over 70% of our population earns their living off the land. All things being equal Ghana should be the breadbasket of the West African sub-region. Sadly, due to bad policies over the years we have allowed our next door neighbour, La Cote d'Ivoire, to overtake us in cocoa production and almost all other agricultural produce. We now import food like bananas and plantain from there.
Despite this sorry state of affairs, we still allow the little we produce to go to waste. Statistics show that over 25%, a quarter no less, of what we produce, is wasted through post-harvest losses.
The Chronicle believes it is now time for the government to sit up and embark on a serious programme to drastically reduce this unacceptably high rate of post-harvest losses.
Not only will such a reduction save us scarce foreign exchange but it will also show our development partners that we are serious about national development. This is especially important in a situation where 40% of our budget depends on donor funding.
Food processing and preservation should be top priority in the agricultural sector. We need not re-invent the wheel to achieve this. The appropriate technology for all these already exists. All that is needed is the political will.
This paper calls on the NPP government to arise and champion this good cause. It is only when the vicious cycle of annual food abundance and scarcity is broken that the good people of Ghana will see that yes, indeed, there is a positive change in their lives.
A stitch in time

Original_Search_Title: Okyenhene Has a Good Case
Headline: Ghana: Okyenhene Has a Good Case
Body_Text:
If the saying that 'we borrowed the land from the unborn generation but
not our ancestors" is anything to go by, then, Osagyefo Amoatia Ofori-Panin
II, the Okyenhene is fighting a good cause.
A trip to Atiwa Forest in the Eastern part of Ghana depicts the gross
encroachment of individuals, farmers and especially chainsaw operators in
the seemingly diminishing virgin forest that shelters the watershed of three
main rivers; Birim, Ayensu and Densu derive their source.
It is said every borrowed thing is supposed to be returned without
blemish. But it seems, the generation today has more questions than answers
if the next generation summoned them before court. It was very refreshing
when the forest guard drove us through the forest along the mountain
slopes.
Nature was identifying itself in different manner, from brooks to
rivers, valleys to gorges and climbers to trees. Hardly could I hear any
noise but the throb of my heart. We then got into the deepest part of the
forest where once stood the Butterfly Sanctuary. But surprisingly, someone
had nicodemously cleared that portion and cultivated plantain and cocoyam,
forcing the insects to migrate one kilometre ahead from their original
abode.
Comparatively, Kakum Park in the Central part of the country has
similarity with Atiwa Forest. This is because while Kakum has a canopy-
walkway installation, Atiwa has a natural canopy-walkway that enables
tourists view tops of trees as one works across the serpentine road.
Kakum Park, which was near depletion, was saved by the timely
intervention of the late Ato Austin, the then Central Regional Minister in
the then Provisional National Defence Council (PNDC) Government. By then,
Takoradi Veneer and Lumber Company (TVLC) was vigorously exploiting timbers
logs.
The bold decision he took has earned the reserve recognition as one of
the best tourists attractions on the continent. Thousands of people visit
this site including school children that have had opportunity to grope
themselves with the work of nature.
Analytically, mining also play major role in the development of the
nation. It offers employment and contributes immensely to Gross Domestic
Product (GDP) in the form of corporate taxes and royalties.
The recent announcement by Mrs. Cecilia Bannerman, Minister of Mines
that EPA has been tasked to scrutinize the environmental impact of any
concession granted within forest reserves is very laudable. Also, the
additional 0.6% payment of royalty to meet the cost of monitoring these
guidelines is most welcome.
But the question is, can EPA sustain this? Won't the 0.6% additional
payment of royalty serve as incentive to mining companies to shirk their
reclamation responsibilities, since it is envisaged that the government
would be required to reclaim the land if an investor fails to honour such
obligation?
It was rather unfortunate for the minister to say that most of the
so-called forests reserves had already been degraded so the best available
option was to give them out as concessions to mining companies to do their
business. Does it mean that there can be no systematic measures to curtail
the activities of those encroachers?
As Okyenhene said his stance on exploiting bauxite deposit in the Atiwa
was neither anti-government nor anti-development but for the sustainability
of area through good ecosystem practice, it is very important to link the
rate at which our 8.4 million hectares forest which, have been diminished to
1.8million hectares are depleted and the consequences that are likely to
confront the country in future.
One can't rule out completely that there are not defects associated with
ecosystem. There are problems of wildlife-human conflict, prostitution among
other things.
Even though I'm not an anti- mining, the situation prevailing in Akwatia
and its surrounding towns is very pathetic. My six months stay in the
diamond city depicts gross human rights abuse, indiscriminate defecating by
the so-called 'galamsey operators' which made it difficult for the farmers
to weed under their farms. Pits are left uncovered and one could hardly run
through the bush in case there is a danger. The pits serve as death
traps. In extremely cases, getting good drinking water becomes problem
because most these rivers are polluted.
The assertion by the Okyehene that proceeds generated from eco-tourism
annually globally amounts to $280 billion and Ghana hadn't realized much
from this, opens a new chapter for those in the helm of affairs to shift
from the old paradigm of over reliance on mining as one of the major
contributors to the GDP.
It is on record that the government is doing everything possible to
attract about two million tourists annually into the country by the year
2015. The question is what actually is the government developing as
tourists' scenes? It is very amazing to have heard from the Minister of
Tourism and Modernization of the Capital City, Mr Jake Obetsebi- Lamptey
stressing on the fact that, the country was yet to take advantage of her
huge tourism potentials he described as a 'Goldmine.'
TheMinister indicated that Ghana was expected to earn $1.5billion from
this year to 2007. This will creates jobs for almost 30,000 people. If this
assertion is anything to go by, then what the Okyenhene is trumpeting must
be seriously looked at.
For instance, the co-existence of two species of monkeys, namely
Campell's Mona and Geoffroy's Pied Colobus currently numbered about one
thousand in Buabeng and Fiema reserve in the Nkransa district of Brong Ahafo
Region is one the most attractive scenes in the country. Also the tourist
site has about 40 species of butterfly and about 450 different kinds of
trees.
Kenya and other East African countries mostly depend upon tourism, which
doesn't face price fluctuations like gold, diamond and other minerals do on
the international market.
Therefore, it would be suicidal, if the country would continue to
sacrifice her remaining 1.4 million hectares forest reserves for
mining.
Okyenhene's action might seem odd to some Ghanaians taking into account
the advantages derive from mining. But in future when Atiwa attains the
status like that of Kakum Park, there people would hail him for his
stance.

Original_Search_Title: Fair Trade Here and There...Should the Producer Subsidize the consumer?
Headline: Africa: Fair Trade Here and There...Should the Producer Subsidize the consumer?
Body_Text:
Caythorpe Court is a very lovely place, like any other farming district
in Europe. The Court is near Caythorpe village which lies midway between
Grantham, the home town of Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher, and Lincoln,
the capital of Lincolnshire County in the Midlands of England.
A typical farmer in the advanced countries is regarded as a king or
queen. With an average of a thousand acres the farmer has well constructed
farm houses, which include living quarters for the family and farm hands,
barns and workshops for tractors and other equipment.
The production cycle of a typical poultry farm is as follows: The farmer
picks up the phone and calls the firm that washes poultry houses,
warehouses, etc. Armed with jet spares and other equipment and inputs they
come and wash down the buildings.
The farmer then calls the firm that deals with sawdust and they rush and
fill the poultry house with adequate sawdust.
The farmer then calls the company that deals in fumigation and they rush
down to ply their trade.
The farmer then calls the feed mill for starter rations, and he is
promptly supplied.
The farmer then calls the hatchery and requests for a quantity of
day-old chick, and he is promptly supplied with well-examined and clinically
certified healthy chicks, with an excess of the requested number. This is to
make up for losses during the transportation.
The farmer calls in the vets and they come to inoculate the birds. This
is the general routine, with the farmer sitting down at the farm and calling
service providers by phone and having them running over each other to render
their services.
At the end of the production cycle, some company is at the farm to pick
the birds and eggs at the appropriate time. Proceeds are paid by cheque
directly to the farmer or through bank transactions into his accounts. Here
is a typical production cycle for a Ghanaian poultry farmer: He can hardly
afford to wash down his poultry farm and equipment.
He goes to a carpentry shop for sawdust. He wakes up early and queues at
the hatchery for day-old chicks. He would be lucky to have his al__cpLocation
which he paid for exactly twenty-one days earlier. He may even be told that
the cost of day-old chicks has gone up and he has to pay the difference, a
sort of cash and carry. At the end of a very frustrating session he is given
birds that are fairly weak and quite a number do not make the trip to the
farms. Earlier, he had to struggle for poultry feed. So all his vocational
life as a farmer, he has to struggle for this and that and accept whatever
he is given, whether satisfied or not.
Meanwhile, in Caythorpe, as well as other farming districts in the
U.K. and rest of the advanced world, the farmer is held in high esteem and
companies struggle to beat each other to provide nothing less than the best
for the farmer. While his colleague in the Third World subsidizes the
consumer, the Western farmer is heavily subsidized and even at times he is
paid well to produce nothing less to create a glut on the market. It may
interest readers to know that farmers in the Western communities do not
bother themselves to visit the big cities. They are very comfortable on
their farms and make treks to the villages only to top up their provision
baskets. Even petroleum fuel is supplied them.
In the Third World, the farmer is in a hurry to go to the big cities for
imaginary gold plated jobs. So while only less than a tenth of the American
population is feeding U.S.A. and at least a third of the world, over eighty
per cent of nationals of a typical Third World country are farmers who
cannot feed more than a third of the nation.
Agriculture is so advanced in the West that in the U.S., for example,
one crop season a year of maize yields almost five times more maize than a
two crop season a year in a place like Ghana on the same acreage. The issue
today is that the poorer nations are calling for removal of subsidies for
farming in the West, and the West is saying that is impossible.
I would not fight with the West on this issue. It seems that we are
taking the fight where we need not. How do we regard our farmers? Do we pay
realistic prices for their produce? How is that wholesalers and retailers of
farm produce are millionaires while the farmers get poorer and poorer each
year? How come our farmers are not able to repay their loans even at reduced
interest rates and the agro-business person can contract commercial for
agro-business and pay off without sweat?
The Westerners know very well that the farmer is the most valuable asset
in a nation, so therefore they treat him right. They are seriously
protecting the interest of agriculture. Those of us on this part of the
world regard farming with contempt. I remember when Agricultural Science was
introduced in the school, some seniors protested saying that the government
wanted them to do farming, and had only coined a beautiful word for it.
The West are saying that they will not remove subsidy on agriculture and
they dare us to subsidize agriculture if we feel we are we. Some may say it
is not fair. Well, if we had succeeded in running down our economy and
chosen to run cup in hand to the West for funding, we must accept their
terms, no matter how bitter.
So what can we do? I will suggest the following:
There must be a fund to be named Agriculture Enhancement Fund
(AEF). This could be funded from corporate taxes and levies. Since we all
agree that we eat, corporate bodies must be made to put in say five per cent
of their annual profits into the AEF. Such bodies should include the
financial institutions, with Agriculture Development Bank not
exempted. Government could raise an imaginary levy of a Â¢10,000.00 a month
on every Ghanaian.
So for eighteen million citizens (men, women, children and babies), we
can get Â¢180,000,000.00 a month or Â¢2 .16 trillion a year for the AEF.
The imaginary levy would be raised from taxes and not direct levy on the
citizen.
Government and Parliament must set a ceiling for VAT. So therefore an
increase of VAT by another 2Â½ % for the AEF should be properly debated and
accepted into a bill. Government must endeavour to spread the tax net to
cover every economic and business venture. This would enhance the al__cpLocation
to the AEF. We are asked by the West not to subsidize our farmers. No
problem. Funds from the AEF may be used to finance farmers at a very low or
no interest rate. The Fund could buy agricultural inputs and equipment and
sell to farmers at give-away prices.
The Fund could be used to establish insurance companies to insure our
farming ventures and make them less risky to go into.
The Fund can be used to set up cottage industries in the farming areas
for the primary processing of farm produce.
Meanwhile, the Ghana Government must initiate action to protect farmers
and fishermen at all times. Commercial fishing vessels must be asked to go
further away from the continental shelf, so as to give the local canoe
fishermen a protected scope for their fishing expedition.
Let us bear in mind that the Western farmer cannot grow cocoyam,
plantain and cassava for us. Let us give our farmers and fishermen the right
prices for their wares and help agriculture to grow.
I hear that some people went on demonstration called 'Kafo Didi.' Well,
we know that even the indebted person must eat, however well, next week we
shall take a look at this "the Indebted Person Must Eat Issue."
Meanwhile, let us give fair trade to our food producers before we
challenge the West to do the same.

Original_Search_Title: Data On Agro Processing Company A Promising Future
Headline: Ghana: Data On Agro Processing Company A Promising Future
Body_Text:
Sefwi Juaboso-Bia District would soon be taking its place in an emerging
agro-processing industry through greater public, private sector
partnership.
The district is endowed with human and material resources, a large
variety of food corps, in an area with unlimited agro opportunities.
The publication of its 30 -page investment and trade brochure, has
exposed its hidden potential with interested investment partners having
started searching for opportunities.
Datano Community Agro-Processing Company, a prospective local company is
on the verge of hitting the international market with its three income
generating projects, namely,plantain chip processing, mushroom production
and cannery and grasscutter meat processing.
Mr. Maxwell Agyeman, 30- year-old graduate of the University of Ghana,
who is the Chief Executive of the company, taking advantage of the abundance
of plantain, one of the major food crops in the area which was being wasted
away, went into processing to set the stage.
With the objective of creating an alternative source of income and
increase the income derived from plantain, he also sought to add value to
the crop, embark on export drive and promote local sales of plantain chips
in supermarkets.
With the assistance of Mr. R. F. Knibbe, a Netherlands Management
Cooperation Programme [N. M.C. P] expert, Datano Community Agro-Processing
Company has succeeded in entering into a contract with Fair Trade-Trading
Cooperation in Holland to export the product.
On the local market the company's well packaged product is receiving
good patronage in retail shops and super markets in Accra and
Kumasi. Arrangements have also been concluded to have the product exported
to Korea and Holland by November this year.
In order to meet the increasing demand, against the present low capacity
of production especially with the poor local techniques of production, the
company, according to Mr. Maxwell Agyeman is seeking investment partners, to
widen its scope and to meet the challenges.
The investment being sought is in the areas of improved processing
factory, procurement of processing machinery - steam automatic stainless
steel machines and conveyor fryers as well as distribution vans and
containers , and re-designing of packaging materials.
MUSHROOM PRODUCTION AND CANNERY
A Memorandum of Understanding [MOU] has been reached with two Canadian
firms - Double 'T', mushroom growing equipment dealers and Lehmann
Enterprise, a technical consulting group which specialises in growing,
processing, preservation and marketing of the product in the area of the
mushroom industry.
Based on a proposal prepared by DACAPC Ghana and Canadian Experts led by
Mr. Oscar Lahmann, a renowned agricultural chemist, the Canadian
International Development Agency (CIDA) through its industrial programme has
accepted to fund the project with an initial grant of $7.8 million.
A consultant is expected in the country soon for a viability study, site
__cpLocation and other relevant advise for the project to take off.
The mushroom industry had been identified in Australia and some
developed parts of the world as the most valuable fresh vegetable crop and
one of the three fastest growing crop industries.
GRASSCUTTER AND MEAT PROCESSING INDUSTRY
As its third project, the Datano Community Agro-Processing Company in
partnership with its Netherlands Management Cooperation programme expert
Mr. Fredrick Ranger Knibbe have produced a working document on the
grasscutter and meat processing industry.
The document, a five-step strategies implementation plan have been
accepted by the Social Investment Fund [SIF] whose architects at Fumesua, in
Ashanti, are designing structures for the project.
The proposed local block of housing would have the capacity for 700
animals.

Original_Search_Title: The Agriculture Alternative
Headline: Nigeria: The Agriculture Alternative
Body_Text:
One thing, amongst many, which the many successive governments of
Nigeria from Obasanjo through to Obasanjo again all have in common is the
expression of the desire that the economy be more diversified. Then one
commodity--crude petroleum--was suddenly beginning to cause more trouble
than bargained for. As oil income rose, so did government, until there came
a point at which one could not meet the other.
Of course, there had been a point, when there had been no crude oil
exploration, and Nigeria had survived. Those were the days of the famous
pyramids of the north, and what not. Nigeria had, in fact, not only
survived, but prospered.
The Western region, in the hands of the late Awolowo had recorded
particular success in agriculture and the administration of it, and
continues to be a reference point. Then, as the wailing tale goes so often,
it has become a source of irritation rather than a legend, we began to
explore oil. The more the investment in oil --foreign investment which we
sought then even as we continue to do so now, sparing no expense--the less
attention went into agriculture and all other sectors which were of far less
significance, anyway.
As things stand today, our country is in near desperation in trying to
go back to the way things were. Cassava chips, we are told, has the
potential of bringing in at least five billion dollars extra every year. If
this is so, the burden of debt--the reason, real or imagined, for our
continued underdevelopment--would soon become a thing of the distant
past. It was the same thing we were told when the NLG project was in the
offing with its good tidings and all, and we are hard pressed to see any
difference. That is only one example.
Such 'failures' of the past can be attributed not to the failure of the
project to rake in the expected revenue (sometimes even more than expected)
but to the absence of the culture of accountability. Our budgets of the last
twenty years, for instance, fall short of reflecting the figures OPEC claims
we have made out of oil so far.
Yet, it is no reason not to swamp us down with cassava cuttings and
imported fertilizer in the reasonable hope that one day in the not too
distant future, we might have a cassava revolution or even become the sixth
largest producer of cassava chips in the world.
It is not only the president that shares this hope. In nearly every
forum where over dependence on oil is being discussed, the solution is
always bound to be said to be agriculture. If it has happened before, it can
happen again.
And indeed there is hope in agriculture, but only if certain factors are
taken into consideration. Agro crusaders--and indeed agro
practitioners--would do well to realize that the world agric scene today is
completely different from what it was in the days of the great groundnut
pyramid. It is rare the country whose economy can afford to depend on its
agricultural exports the way world agriculture has been re- structured. If
it is a third world African country, depending on agric exports is near
suicide, as countries such as Cote d'Ivoire must realize by now. The
importers on the other end discourage imports of finished products by
increasing tariffs on products according to the degree of finishing, as the
United States currently does. The huge cash grants to EU and US farmers,
which has rocked more than a few boats lately and is generating a fair
amount of controversy with the upcoming Trade meet in Mexico next week
expected to be the crescendo.
Our future in agriculture as a foreign exchange earner is therefore
dependent, to a large extent, on how well the playing grounds can be leveled
on the international scene, a hope whose realization is bound to take a good
deal of time.
More significant than the pursuit of a level playing field in the
international market is its pursuit in the local market, where imported
agricultural products, both raw and processed, are pushing locally produced
equivalents out of the local markets. The importation of apples and roses,
for instance, may not sound as problematic as the importation of plantain
into a country which has enough capacity even as it stands. How then could
we talk of exporting food, when at the moment we still can not store our
surpluses and get food to the people who need them? Increased agricultural
production will of course be of immense benefit to local industry.
All talks about agric as an alternative to petroleum always end with the
inevitable question of food insecurity at home, and the challenge for
feeding those at home before agriculture can be invested in as a foreseen
forex earner; and the former can not happen unless infrastructure, which is
crucial to the agric and food distribution sectors, are first heavily
invested in.

Original_Search_Title: South West : EDCA Spearheads Development In Essoh-Attah
Headline: Cameroon: South West : EDCA Spearheads Development In Essoh-Attah
Body_Text:
The Essoh-Attah Development and Cultural Association (EDCA) has gained a
wide acclamation for spearheading development in this most eastern village
of Lebialem Division
Another giant step taken by EDCA recently was the maintenance of the 10
kilometre portion of the overall 28 kilometre Dschang-Ngundeng-Pia-Essoh
Attah road to disenclave the 20,000 populated area.
In a recent drive, the members of EDCA nation-wide have invested CFA 2
million to take the area out of the severe disenclavement. EDCA's president
general Dr. Stephen Formin told Cameroon Tribne that maintenance work kicked
off May 12 on the Pia-Essoh Attah stretch of the road. This part of the road
been completely damaged and rendered access to the village by car impossible
in recent times. It is expected that many other surrounding villages like
Njoagwi and Fonke in the neighouring Nguti Sub Division would benefit from
the road opening.
Access to Essoh Attah has been for the past five years a nightmare
especially as the other road from Njentche through Essoh Attah Njoagwi and
Mbetta had not been rehabilitated despite government having disbursed CFA 25
million in funds. In a recent correspondance to the EDCA president general,
the Minister of public works, Dieudonne Ambassa Zang affirmed that the
Njentche-Essoh Attah-Njoawi-Mbetta rural road was under technical study so
that it could be rehabilitated.
Following the minister's letter dated December 11, 2002 the people of
Essoh Attah and Noagwi are jumping for joy in the full hope that they could
sonner than later be linked to the rest of Cameroon through a motorable
road.
It this road is constructed, the two chiefdoms totalling over 30,000
people would evacuate their high-yield cash crops like cocoa, coffee and
palm produce. Equally, the heavily demanded food crops from the area like
cocoyams, plantains and cassava shall gain access to neighbouring markets of
Dschang (Menoua) and Menji (Lebialem).

Original_Search_Title: Gov Shekarau Set to Boost Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Gov Shekarau Set to Boost Agriculture
Body_Text:
In an attempt to boost farming activities in Kano State, the state government has expressed its intention to engage the services of the National Horticultural Research Institute, located at Bagauda.
The institute is expected to offer expert advice to farmers on how to boost vegetable and fruit cultivation and production.
This was disclosed by the Kano State deputy governor, Alhaji Magaji Abdullahi, during a courtesy visit paid on him by the executive members of the institute.
Alhaji Magaji, however, enjoined the institute to enlighten and encourage farmers in the state to establish cooperative bodies, to enable them benefit from the activities of the institute.
He then pledged the state government's readiness to assist the institute in solving its problems.
Said he: "Because of the priority accorded to the agriculture sector by the present administration, the Kadawa fruit processing company would be rehabilitated and put into full operation. This would serve as encouragement to the people of the area to embark on farming."
Director of the institute, Prof. J.O. Babalola, had earlier explained that the institute operates in collaborative research activities with some national and international institutes such as Asian Vegetable Research and Development Centre in Taiwan.
The centre, he said, provides the institute with nursery facilities for the propagation of mango, citrus, pineapple and plantain seedlings.
He further disclosed that the institute has organised training courses for staff of the state ministry of agriculture and other agricultural agencies in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Solgas'll Break the Ajaokuta Jinx, Says Vice Chairman
Headline: Nigeria: Solgas'll Break the Ajaokuta Jinx, Says Vice Chairman
Body_Text:
Kaduna —  The $3.6 billion contract awarded to SOLGAS incorporated, a United
States-based energy company stirred up several controversies in the country,
especially among stakeholders in the iron and steel industry in the
country. Series of questions were raised over the technical know-how of the
company in the steel business; its financial capacity to resuscitate
Ajaokuta, as well as the circumstances leading to the sealing of the
deal.
However, in this interview with our Group Business Editor, Ahmed
I. Shekarau and Deputy Business Editor, Emma Yashim, Executive Vice Chairman
of SOLGAS Nigeria Limited, a subsidiary of SOLGAS (US), Chief Seun A,
Oyefeso said his company will surprise Nigerians by breaking the age-old
myth that Ajaokuta will never work. Excerpts:
Weekly Trust: The contract your company signed with the federal
government of Nigeria on the rehabilitation, completion, commissioning and
operation of the Ajaokuta Steel Company Limited is stirring up
controversy. What does SOLGAS have to offer?
First of all, it is a contract, an agreement definitely, but it is not a
situation whereby the government is going to pay us. The contract is for
$3.6 billion. But the government is not going to pay us a penny out of this
amount. It represents the amount of investment that is supposed to be made
over a period of time. What we did in a situation that we have right now, is
- you have a steel plant that has been lying there for so long until we came
along. At one time, they said 98 per cent of the project had been
completed. At some point, they said it was 95 per cent. But you know also
that if you build a house and you leave it uncompleted, after sometime,
there would be some degree of deterioration. So in a situation like that,
one cannot actually determine at this point what percentage of this plant
requires completion. But we've done a new work on it and we believe that
with the kind of people we're bringing in on this project, we will be able
to determine what the percentage is exactly. We'll find out as we go
along. When you ask what we have to offer, I'll say that what we have to
offer is a unique project that is supposed to help in the take-off of the
project.
In looking at steel, there are three factors that affect the cost of
production. First is labour. Number two is the raw materials. And number
three is the cost of energy. We all know that energy is probably one of the
biggest problems this country has. How do you generate electricity? Energy
is our problem. Now, if you fire off that blast furnace, and you start
production, the last thing you would want to happen is to have power
failure. We do not have enough power in this country right now to do
whatever it is you want to do and that is one of the things we needed to
consider in designing this particular project package.
So what we did was that we brought together some of our own parent
companies and affiliates. We brought some other people to
participate. SOLGAS is the lead company in a consortium of companies that
are coming to work on the project. It is a three-point package. When you
look at labour, you will realise that it is considerably cheap in Nigeria
when you compare it to other countries. Most of the raw materials we require
are already in Nigeria except coking coal and maybe one or two things that
we may need to bring in. So we came up with this design whereby we would be
able to generate our own energy, our own power. How do we do that? We looked
at the available resources. There is nothing we're doing that is outside of
the resources that are immediately available to us. And we looked at the
pipeline. There is a natural gas pipeline that comes from the Delta region
and the northernmost tunnels into Ajaokuta. There is a thermal power plant
of 110 megawatts that was supposed to be built to general power so that it
could also sell power to NEPA. And I believe that that was done even before
the shutdown of the plant finally.
So what we did was to look at what we had on the ground. The natural gas
comes from the Delta region. We added the components. There's wet gas being
flared - associated gas. All we needed to do was put a gas processing plant
at that point, go into a deal and have a gas supplier - maybe with Shell or
any of the majors, with the NNPC assisting in making it possible one way or
the other. Then with the Nigerian Gas Company (NGC) to use the pipeline. We
put the processing plant up there. What to do in this situation is that we
would get the best offer out of those we have received who would supply
us. We already have an arrangement whereby we want to get the best for
Ajaokuta. We'll gather gas at a particular point and process it.
In the arrangement we have, 40 per cent of the liquid fuels - propanes
and butanes and probably automotive gas oil-would now belong to the Ajaokuta
Steel Company. It is a 40-60 arrangement. The revenue from these liquid
fuels would now go there. It is now their own. The arrangement is for
Ajaokuta Steel Company. 60 per cent is taken by the vendors who are
partners. It is almost like a new NLNG kind of situation. Now, the dry gas,
which is the by-product of what you get from the processing, would be made
to pass though the pipeline to Ajaokuta to fire the furnaces and to power
the turbines. The dry gas is supposed to be a waste product. Now, we need a
reservoir. We don't want a situation whereby vandalism or anything would
disrupt the flow of gas while you're making steel. So what we have decided
to do is to take part of the 40 per cent of the liquid fuels and store in
Ajaokuta for about two to three months so that even if there's a problem, we
can have a systematic shut-down of the plant that would not create the risk
of the country losing millions and millions of dollars of investment. Then
of course you make money also when you can sell electricity. So we need to
generate electricity. We are going to have one of the biggest power and
steel plants and then sell. We're going to expand the thermal plant, have
enough capability there to be able to handle all our operations and at the
same time sell to NEPA. There is money to be made particularly if you have
control over the cost of your gas and how you get your energy. So we
designed an Independent Power Project (IPP) - part of our design. We'll put
a hundred megawatts each in Kano, Kaduna, Bauchi, Yola. We'll also put 50
megawatts - depending on the size of the city. And what do you need when you
put all these turbines in those places? Liquid fuels to run them. If you
have to depend on the diesel, the PMS that are being imported and are not
even enough, you'll end up in serious problems.
That was why the federal government and the Bureau of Public Enterprises
(BPE) loved the package we offered. You put all those things there and
basically what you'll have is socio-economic development in those areas. If
the need for more electricity arises, then what you'll need is just to add
more will be able to do it. And also, the kind of power we're planning to
generate will be from smaller turbines from about 10 megawatts to maybe
about 25 maximum. This is so that if you put a hundred megawatts in a
particular __cpLocation, and you have to service the turbines, you will be able
to shut down one at a time. It is not going to be the kind of situation
whereby you will put in 300 megawatts and the day you want to service it,
you have to turn down all the power. This is the problem we've always had in
the past in the country. It makes it difficult for turn-around
maintenance.
So we've looked at all the kinds of problems that we've had in this
country and the best way to tackle them. And we're bringing in the most
prominent companies in the world as you'll find out to handle their various
aspects of the project. That is what we're bringing into the Ajaokuta
project. A team of people, a team of companies, a consortium that is going
to make the project successful.
WT: We understand that in a steel plant like Ajaokuta, constant
supply of power is needed until such a time turn-around maintenance is to be
done. But is this enough reason for power generation to take precedence over
the actual production of steel?
Yes. Because even on a national scale, power dominates everything. That
is why government is trying to ensure that there is uninterrupted supply of
power. It has to take precedence because energy and these things I pointed
out represent, for any business, probably about two-thirds of the cost of
production and even maintaining them for any business. I don't care if it is
just selling plantain by the roadside. When you start, it'll have its
moment. It'll take time for it to stabilise in any kind of business. The
investment ultimately belongs to the federal government, not SOLGAS, not any
company. These are value-added situations you're bringing into the plant
because the revenue from the electricity will go back into the Ajaokuta
Steel Company.
By the time you start, you'll look at a situation where there is a 60
per cent levy on the importation of steel products. We are not planning to
ask the government to increase import levy to protect us. Because in the
first instance, we're not going to be able to start at 100 per cent capacity
utilisation. We cannot meet the demand of the Nigerian public. So the best
way to do this is to design a package that could be business-like so as to
make us competitive. Because if you have a protectionist policy in place and
you cannot meet local demand, automatically, the price of all these steel
products will soar. Who suffers? The average Nigerian.
So what is why we said that with the kind of measures that we're putting
in place, we can produce steel at 55 per cent the cost of steel
worldwide. It is a question of having certain businesses to support the
project. We're planning to get the rolling mills to start producing in
another four months by God's grace. By the time you do the rehabilitation
and start up these things, you'll discover some other problems that you were
never aware of before, and you take care of them. The last thing you would
want to do is now depend on a situational register, then you stop. So we're
trying to reduce as much as possible the cost of energy that is so vital to
the operation.
In the contract you signed with the federal government, we saw that
you will be spending over 67 per cent of the finances on generation of
energy for a project that is basically supposed to be for steel
production. How is SOLGAS going to reconcile this with the fact that it is
not a steel expert yet it wants to begin to produce within four months?
It is true that SOLGAS is not a steel expert. But the answer, like I
said, lies in the fact that it is a consortium. For instance, the Russians
who are the originator of the company - we are working with the
Tiajpromexport (TPE) and the Ukrainians who built the thermal power plant to
be able to produce to make that place work. So contrary to what a lot people
have said, we went into contract, a signed a Memorandum of Understanding
(MoU) with these companies and recently we gave a letter to the TPE and I
met with them again a few days afterwards. Since then, we've been meeting
regularly to discuss how to complete the project. We're not bringing in a
bunch of Americans to just take over-like people have said about the Russian
technology being obsolete. If a technology is obsolete, it is obsolete. But
the situation is that 70 per cent of the world's steel producers still use
the blast furnace method. The Russians are the ones who are going to finish
their work and you can confirm that from them.
All that is there is that we have consultants that work with us and are
also involved in what is happening so that they would ensure that whatever
investments are made into the project, are being channelled properly.
It is widely believed that the Ajaokuta project SOLGAS is embarking
on now is like a dash from the federal government.
A lot of people have said that. But it is because for the greater part
of the time of our beginning operation, we have been working with the
federal government for almost four years in participating in the development
of the economy. As a matter of fact, the former minister of power and steel,
Dr Agagu, sent letters around to a lot of companies that are steel experts
to come and participate the best way they could in the project - VAIS Alpine
of Austria, Kobe Steel of Japan - all of them. And in fact, a committee was
formed about a year and a half ago, headed by the former managing director
of Ajaokuta, Mr Tom Miachi to evaluate and look at all the different
proposals and make recommendations to the government. And everything was
done above board. We had to discuss with the various arms of government and
we also had to go and defend the project even with the Bureau of Public
Enterprises (BPE), which wanted to know how we could make the steel project
work. Typical of the BPE, they wanted to know how we could make it
work. They wanted to know the figures, the numbers, how all these things
would add up. And a lot of people did not know what was going on behind the
scene.
Somebody said in your article that everything about the agreement was
done in three days. It was not done in three days. The first agreement we
signed was in May 2002. That was the first agreement - a joint venture
agreement. If you had a copy of the agreement, you would see that it said
clearly that two other agreements would be signed concurrently - the
financing agreement and the management agreement. Unfortunately, when we
signed the joint venture agreement, the first problem that we had was a
situation whereby some people raised some issues and we now went back into
further discussions and negotiations that ended up in the signing of the
financing agreement in November 2002. It took a lot of negotiations again,
because there were a lot of people who were concerned in this country - even
the staff of Ajaokuta too - that things should be done properly. Ministry
officials, the BPE, the Debt Management Office (DMO) had to come in when we
were discussing guarantees. The Ministry of Justice screened the agreement,
the Finance Ministry also screened the agreement and sent us to the DMO -
all kinds of things were done to make sure that the country was not getting
a raw deal in the agreement.
They hired a private law firm to harmonise all the positions and come up
with a document. It came up with a document and it was not the perfect of
all documents. It was that document that we deliberated on for the changes
to be made in the three or four days that somebody was talking about in your
paper. You can discuss an amendment within one hour since you already have a
document. That also depends on the volume of the amendments. Those
amendments were issues that were raised by the BPE to ensure that the
project is successfully implemented. All these things were done, but a lot
of people did not know. And the BPE only pointed out that rather than have
the agreements in bits, it was better to merge all the documents that would
end up spelling out for us which way forward, or what to expect.
The issue of funding. You said it is not the government that is
going to finance the project. Is it then going to guarantee whatever loans
you are going to get?
No. The government as a matter of fact has refused to guarantee any
loan, and this is the beauty of the project. As far as he is concerned, no
guarantees for situations like this. The government has infrastructure on
ground. Come and look at it and if you feel that you can make it work,
employ Nigerians. If it yields revenue for the federal government and people
are able to have some kind of livelihood from it, take it. But as he said,
"I am not putting one penny into that place."
WT: What about the issue of negotiations for the loan you're going to
source? Will the government be a party to it?
Oyefeso: No. As a matter of fact, there is a monitoring committee that
is going to be set up. And together, we will discuss issues on the way
forward. In whatever we do, we'll be working with Nigerians. The transition
committee - the team we're trying to put together now - will involve the
BPE, officials of the power and steel ministry, Ajaokuta Steel Company
officials and ourselves. And then probably one or two outsiders that we feel
would be in a position to do something. As a matter of fact, I have been
trying to get in touch with the Nigerian Society of Engineers (NSE) so that
we can actually put some of these professional bodies in some of the
committees to look at what we are doing because this has to be
teamwork.
There is the allegation that SOLGAS did not conduct any technical
evaluation of the Ajaokuta Steel Company before signing the agreement with
the federal government. We learnt from the National Electric Power Authority
(NEPA) that it was not a party to your plans to generate energy at Ajaokuta
and sell to it (NEPA) even when it should have made input into the
project.
In the first place, it is not true that we did not bring in anybody. We
brought in consultants. We brought in Schueck Steel of the United States,
which has built eleven out of the eighteen steel plants in America. But yes,
we did not bring people in to do the kind of technical evaluation that
government paid for TPE do because the TPE has already done a technical
auditing, which we have decided to work with. As regards to NEPA, the
authority may have forgotten that while the current Minister of Power and
Steel, Senator Liel Imoke was at NEPA as chairman of its technical board, we
had actually made presentations to NEPA. But what NEPA said to us at the
time was that the turbines that we were considering using at that time were
too small and that it would have been a little funny to have so many tiny
turbines littering the landscape of the country and so on. Yes, we did get
in touch with them. The only thing is that we have not actually gone to them
except for when I took IBM Global to them. The authority is right. We have
not gone into sufficient details with them.
WT: We learnt that some of the directors of SOLGAS are serving governors
- one in the South-West and the other in the North-West and that even the MD
of SOLGAS is a cousin to one of the influential women in the present
government. Did this situation influence your getting the Ajaokuta contract?
I have heard a lot of things, but this is another angle I am hearing
with regards to this. Fortunately, there is the Corporate Affairs
Commission. It is an open registry. So if for any reason you imagine that
what I am telling you is not true, you can always verify. As a matter of
fact, most of the people on the board of SOLGAS are Americans, Britons and
Russians.
They could be fronts.
You're absolutely right. But all this hullabaloo is because all our past
leaders have not given us reason to trust them because they have let us down
so many times. So if people react the way they are reacting, that is
understandable. What made our partners interested in staying this long was
actually the integrity of the process.
I want to believe that you did a lot of homework before undertaking
this project at Ajaokuta. Could you then give us an honest assessment of the
Ajaokuta project? Can Ajaokuta Steel Company produce steel or at best
generate energy alone?
It can do both. As a matter of fact, this is why the package we designed
is attractive. It can produce steel. But when do you break even without
electricity. You want to depend on taking so much from NEPA, when it is
currently still in the process of making sure there is adequate power supply
in the country? That is why even in the original design, the Russians
included a thermal power plant because they know the importance of energy to
their project and that it needed to be expanded. There's no way that you'll
go into a venture like this without thinking and planning about energy. We
designed this package to make sure that Ajaokuta has close to 100 per cent
operation.
What would you then say to the advice the World Bank gave to Nigeria
saying it should convert Ajaokuta to a 100 per cent energy-generating plant
because it cannot produce steel?
You see, it comes down to what I just said to you. That is, if you look
at the Ajaokuta project with blinders on, and you see only steel. Of course,
the World Bank did not say so, because they did not want Nigeria to produce
steel. If, however, that was part of their plans - I don't want to speculate
- I am aware of the Hatch Associates report, which said Ajaokuta was not
viable. And when we had this meeting with the BPE, I remember the World Bank
representatives and some people saying that even upto 2012, there was no way
Nigeria could absorb 1.3 million tonnes of steel. And I told them that they
were wrong. They were a little amazed at first and they asked me where I got
my statistics from. You know we still have problem of gathering statistics
in Nigeria. We are just beginning to do that. You know, Vodacom said they
could not come to Nigeria for the GSM business because the figures did not
add up. But look at what is happening in the GSM market today. A lot of
people who buy cars these days don't even have bank accounts. So in the same
way, I told them - when they asked me how I got my own statistics - that I
got it from the customs.
We learnt that SOLGAS hasn't got the wherewithal to finance the
project. How do you hope to get the funds?
I told you that SOLGAS is the lead company in a consortium. We don't
need $3.6 billion to be able to finance this project. We already have
funding for some of the works there. I don't want to go into details right
now. We are not planning to retrench people as people have been
saying. Companies around the world have done bigger things. SOLGAS as a
company does not have $3.6 billion, but as a consortium, it has much
more.

Original_Search_Title: Public Schools Pupils Learn Under Yoke of Malnutrition
Headline: Nigeria: Public Schools Pupils Learn Under Yoke of Malnutrition
Body_Text:
MOST of pupils and students who attend public primary, secondary and tertiary institutions in the sprawling Lagos metropolis are malnourished.
Majority of children attending primary and secondary schools can not afford mid-day meals during break time.
That the situation of feeding in higher institutions is as pathetic for most of the students studying there.
Vanguard conducted an investigation into the process of getting food and the nutritional provisions existing in public educational institutions.
For good health and growth, the right nutrition and diets are essential, moreso in the development of cognitive domain.
In a country without a reasonable food security for its citizens, where poverty is widespread, high rate of unemployment, per capita income of about 2 dollars a day, and agriculture grossly neglected, people can hardly afford one good meal a day. No surprise therefore that this prevailing situation of hunger in the land has impacted seriously on public education system.
Compared with private institutions, government-owned schools are far worse off in terms of feeding and nutrition for pupils and students.
In a recent study to find out the feeding situation in schools without boarding facilities like public primary and secondary schools, Vanguard went to three day colleges and four primary schools.
From all indications, over findings invariably reflected a general trend most likely to exist in most schools across Lagos State.
In the primary schools, a significant number of pupils between the ages of 5-11 year do not bring money along when coming in the morning. Usually from low-income earning parents, petty traders, job-seekers, retrenched people without means of earning a living, labourers, artisans, unskilled and semi-skilled, they just managed to provide a little food for the children to eat before leaving home for school.
These pupils are given a small quantity of garri with salt or sugar to drink. The more fortunate ones may be given beans (ewa) with garri spread on top, rice without meat or fish, bread without butter, eba or amala with small soup, to serve as breakfast, before they leave for school. A few could even be put on the road to school without anything in the stomach.
In all the primary schools visited, a lot of them could not patronise the food vendors in the premises.
When asked why are they not buying food like a few of their colleagues, the answers given seemed to be the same.
"I don't have money; daddy did not give me anything to buy food; I've eaten at home; when we close, I'll eat when I reach house; give me money to buy buns or biscuit; I want to drink 'sobo,' I didn't eat this morning." These were some of the responses from primary school pupils, when Vanguard cornered them at break time.
Yet, there are others with money, between N5 and N10 who throng food vendors, hawkers of pure water, fried plantain, packaged coloured-sweetened water, and even iced cream.
Instead of having mid-day meals during break-time, public school pupils merely roam about in the compound, jump and play, often in a rough manner, until the bell rings, summoning them back to the class rooms for the last session, ending at 1.30 p.m.
What is obtaining in public schools shows a distinct contrast with that of privately owned schools. Because of their elitist, middle-class, and even upper class backgrounds, children who go to expensive fee-paying private schools come with food packs apart from taking some kind of breakfast at home.
At break time, the pupils are given mid-day meals and milk. Afterwards, they display vigour, vitality and strength, that are not found with their counterparts in public schools.
Children in public schools lack the food they need, making them vulnerable to illness, and intellectually disabled.
At Aguntasolo Primary School, Ijeshatedo, Surulere, Lagos, most of the children cannot buy food to each during break period. They resort to playing around with football, and running all over the place until the break time is over. The children managed with whatever food already taken at home, and stay on till afternoon when the school closed for the day.
Almost similar situation at Ajangbadi Primary School in Okokomaiko, Onike Primary School, Iwaya, Yaba, and Olowogbowo Primary School, in Lagos Island.
Our investigation revealed that thousands of children in public schools suffer from malnutrition. They are not to blame for being in that condition.
Malnutrition emanates from undernourishment due to lack of one or more nutrients like vitamins and minerals. Primarily, it is caused by a deficiency in the intake of nutrients by the cells of the body. Insufficient intake of proteins, calories, vitamins and minerals could eventually lead to malnutrition.
Every organ - the heart, brain, stomach, kidneys, intestines, lungs may be affected. The effects are impaired mental development, poor scholastic and intellectual performance.
According to United Nations Children's Fund, UNICEF, "the depletion of human intelligence on such a scale - for reasons that are almost entirely preventable, is a profligate, even criminal waste."
In a recent report of the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FOA), an organ of the United Nations, titled, The State of Food Insecurity in the World 2001, while acknowledging there has been a slowdown in the reduction of undernourished in the world, however admits that, "the number of undernourished has increased considerably in the majority of developing countries."
It must be borne in mind that malnutrition is not limited to children. The WHO report of 2001 amply stated:
"Malnutrition casts long shadows, affecting close to 800 million people - 20 per cent of all people in the developing world." This means that 1 out of every 8 people in the world suffers from malnutrition.
But Africa has the highest percentage of undernourished people in the world.
Lack of food has been identified as the direct cause of malnutrition, which has become threatening to the public school system of Nigeria. Deeper economic, social, cultural and environmental factors are also responsible. Easily the principal factor is poverty, which afflicts millions of Nigerians.
Unequal distribution of wealth and mindless corruption by the leadership and elite political class ensured that children of economically subjugated masses are herded into largely impoverished public schools without food and nutrition to sustain their education.
